full_text: FUEL PROCESSING TECHNOLOGY ELSEVIER Fuel Processing Technology 52 (1997) 145-153 Whole cell biocatalysis for an oil desulfurization process L. Setti a, * , G. Lanzarini b, P.G. Pifferi a Department of Industrial Chemistry and Materials, V.le Risorgimento 4, 40136 Bologna, Italy b Dipartimento di Biologia, Difesa a Biotecnologie Agroforestali, Universitdella Basilicata, Via Nazario Sauro 85, 85100 Potenza, Italy Abstract Various attempts have been made to develop biotechnological processes based on microbiological desulfurization employing aerobic and anaerobic bacteria. In order to obtain a biodesulfurization process competitive with the chemical-physical one of hydrodesulfurization (HDS), a biotechnological process has to follow the three main refinery steps: 1) separation, entailing some pretreatment of crude oil; 2) conversion, i.e. biocatalytic transformation where the biocatalyst favours a selective desulfurization process without destroying useful products; 3) finishing, in which the crude oil is separated from the biocatalyst and the byproducts. Biocatalysis may be carried out using whole cells or isolated enzymes in the free or immobilized form. The use of isolated enzymes is advantageous since it avoids the formation of undesirable byproduct mediated by contaminating enzymes as in the case of the aerobic biodesulfurization, which is not a selective process. In fact, the aerobic microorganisms may degrade almost all the compounds which make up the heavy oil. However, despite this advantage, extraction and purification of the enzyme is costly and, frequently, enzymes catalyzing oxidation-reduction reactions require enzyme cofactors which must be regenerated after the reaction. For these reasons, metabolic conditions can often be designed by using whole cell biotransformations to promote cofactor regeneration, thus avoiding the problems associated with cofactor recycling and regeneration. Today, the main limitations for the industrial application of a biodesulfurization process are associated with the high cost of the biocatalyst and with the volumetric ratio between the organic phase and the aqueous one. In order to overcome these problems, cell immobilization is one of the most promising approaches in terms of treatment costs and in finishing step times when compared to a continuous stirred tank bioreactor for the biodesulfurization process. 1997 Elsevier Science B.V. Keywords: Oil biodesulfurization; Organic sulfur; Cell immobilization; Pseudomonas . Corresponding author. Fax: +39-51-6443673. 0378-3820/97/$17.00 1997 Elsevier Science B.V. All rights reserved. P11 S0378-3820(97)00023-4 146 L. Setti et al. /Fuel Processing Technology 52 (1997) 145-153 1. Introduction The concept of `biorefining' in petrochemistry is increasingly becoming a viable reality thanks to the low costs of biological technology. In fact, as organic sulfur removal in oil fossil fuels is an important goal for reducing a source of heavy-sulfurous gas emissions to the atmosphere during combustion, various attempts have been made to develop biotechnological processes based on microbial desulfurization. Biocatalytic desulfurization (BDS) is not yet a commercial technology, but conceptual and practical engineering analyses have shown that the approach is very promising. BDS technology may be applied to various stages of the refining process. In particular, it can be combined with the conventional chemico-physical process of hydrodesulfurization for the treatment of middle distillates. Usually, conventional technology avoids aqueous treatments because of possible corrosion and formation of deposits in the reactors. For this reason, the BDS technology is particularly suited to streams, such as the direct desulfurization process of crude oil which, however, is generally not taken into consideration. Moreover, crude oil has to be stored in tanks for a long time before refining. For a biodesulfurization process to be competitive with the chemico-physical one of hydrodesulfurization (HDS), three main refinery steps need to be followed, namely: 1) separation, entailing some pretreatment of crude oil; 2) conversion, i.e. biocatalytic transformation where the biocatalyst favours a selective desulfurization process without destroying useful products; 3) finishing, in which crude oil is separated from the biocatalyst and the byproducts. Conversion and finishing are affected by a number of crucial factors, namely: 1) biocatalyst specificity; 2) biocatalyst stability; 3) biocatalyst activity; and, 4) bioreactor design, in which the volumetric ratio between the oil phase and the aqueous medium represents the main limiting factor for an industrial application of the biotechnological process. 2. The choice of enzymes or whole cells as biocatalysts of the biodesulfurization process The microbial degradation of dibenzothiophene, DBT, which represents a model compound for the most of the organic sulfur compounds present in crude or heavy oils, has been described by several researchers, various metabolites having been detected depending on the strains and culture conditions. The degradation pathways can be classified as either non-destructive or destructive of the sulfur aromatic molecule (Fig. 1). The non destructive pathway appears promising due to its selectivity which removes the organic sulfur without the loss of carbon atoms. Cleavage of the C-S bond is achieved through hydrolytic, reductive or oxidative mechanisms. The reductive mechanism holds considerable interest for industrial applications as the process is similar to conventional HDS and, moreover, because both the aliphatic and aromatic compounds are not highly degraded by the sulfur reducing bacteria [1]. Nevertheless, reducing microorganisms are not capable of degrading all the sulfur aromatic compounds, in particular DBT derivatives which are the most abundantly found in crude and heavy oils G. Desulfovibrio sp. Pseudomonas sp. Fig. 1. Destructive and non-destructive desulfurization pathways of dibenzothiophene (according to Refs. [3,17,18]). [2,3]. The oxidative non destructive mechanism of DBT desulfurization, called 4S pathway, is obviously a prerogative of aerobic microorganisms which can nevertheless also degrade a large number of aliphatic, aromatic and sulfur aromatic compounds. An example of this mechanism at work can be seen in the batch experiments of heavy oil desulfurization in which Rhodococcus rhodochrous IGTS8 is used and where non destructive desulfurization 1 is followed by the complete degradation of the aliphatic fraction which constitutes 60% of the oil (Fig. 2). Destructive biodesulfurization is even less convenient. In fact, it is characterized by the cleavage of carbon-carbon bonds as a consequence of oxidative steps leading to a complete mineralization of the organic molecule. The comparison between aerobic and anaerobic processes with the use of a Pseudomonas sp. [4] and a Desulfouibrio desulfuricans [1], respectively, reveals a conflicting situation (Fig. 3). In fact, although aerobic microorganisms can remove high amounts of organic sulfur, the sulfur percentage in the residual heavy oil may increase as a consequence of simultaneous aliphatic compound biodegradation. Today, only one strain of Brevibacterium sp. DO is known to perform selective aerobic desulfurization in crude oil [5], but no further information has been reported as to the capacity of this strain to degrade different sulfur aromatic compounds. On the other hand, in the anaerobic process, heavy oil composition before and after microbial treatment does not really differ, while the organic sulfur percentage is significantly decreased, but only in terms of benzothiophene derivatives. This last process has proven to be effective only in the treatment of middle distillates such as kerosene containing a high amount of benzothiophenes. The limitations of aerobic biodesulfurization deriving from its low specificity could be overcome by using cell-free extracts. It may be assumed that the conversion of DBT occurs via a multienzyme system judging from the possible intermediates previously Data not reported. 148 L. Setti et al. /Fuel Processing Technology 52 (1997) 145-153 Time (min.) 60 60 Fig. 2. Gas chromatograms showing the n-alkane distribution in a deasphaltenated crude oil after a microbial desulfurization treatment by using Rhodococcus rhodochrous IGTS8: (a) control and (b) after 3 days. described. On this way, the enzyme systems catalyzing the conversion of DBT to 2-hydroxybiphenyl (2-HBP) are attractive. Oshiro et al. [6] have described the enzymatic desulfurization of DBT by using cell free extract from Rhodococcus erythropolis D-1, with DBT being the sole source of sulfur. They have shown that NADH is essential for the desulfurization process. The participation of enzyme cofactor in the biotransformation reactions presents a disadvantage in term of its regeneration. Cellular biotransformation systems are, instead, especially advantageous because some metabolic conditions can often be designed to promote cofactor regeneration. Moreover, the free-cell extracts exhibit a lower activity, in the order of 0.01 g of DBT removed per gram of protein per hour, as compared to that obtained with aerobic and anaerobic microorganisms (0.4 g/g (biomass)/h) [7] and (0.1 g/g (biomass)/h) L. Setti et al. / Fuel Processing Technology 52 (1997) 145-153 149 Fig. 3. Deasphaltenated oil composition before and after the microbial treatment by using (a) a mixed culture N3 of sulfate reducing bacteria [11 and (b) a pure culture of Pseudomonas sp [41 in anaerobic and aerobic conditions, respectively. [8], respectively. In view of these considerations, the extraction and purification procedures for preparing the enzymatic extract may be costly. In conclusion, the use of whole cells seems to be more profitable than that of the enzyme. The undesirable metabolic pathways due to the presence of other enzymes in the whole cell can be minimized or eliminated by repression of the enzymatic activity, mutation of the cells, or denaturation of the undesired enzymes. In this way, some of the genes of the DBT degradative pathway have been isolated and characterized [9] and more recently, Denome et al. [ 10] have isolated a set of genes from Rhodococcus rhodochrous IGTS8 which confer a specific desulfurization phenotype to mutants of IGTS8 and to a related organism, Rhodococcus fascians D188-5, that is normally unable to desulfurize DBT. 3. Mechanism of dibenzothiophene microbial uptake A wide range of water-miscible and immiscible organic solvents have been used with biocatalysts both as isolated enzymes or as whole cells. There are many factors affecting the microbial desulfurization mechanism which need to be considered. Agitation, aeration, medium conditions and substrate characteristics (solubility, viscosity, surface tension, partition coefficient, etc.) play a very important role in hydrocarbon assimilation by the microorganisms. Bioavailability depends on the hydrocarbon uptake mechanism 150 L. Amount of dibenzothiophene removed from three different organic phase (101 mM) by a Pseudomonas sp. after 5 days degradation (n-dodecane, C12; n-hexadecane, C16; 2,2-4,4-6-8,8 heptamethylnonane, HMN) and the respective microbial adherence towards the organic phase with the oil/water volumetric ratio equal to 50% (according to Ref. [13]). which occurs either directly by contact between the cells and the organic phase or by hydrocarbon solubilization in the aqueous medium. Our studies on the biodegradation of DBT dissolved in n-alkanes (n-dodecane and n-hexadecane) and in poorly biodegradable solvents (2,2-4,4-6-8,8 heptamethylnonane and di-n-octylphthalate) by an aerobic strain of Pseudomonas sp have shown that three concomitant factors are rate limiting, namely: 1) microbial adherence to the hydrocarbons, 2) diffusional mass transfer and, 3) co-assimilation mechanisms [1 I]. In particular, we have observed that the DBT biodegradation rate increases when the microbial adherence to the organic phase increases [12] (Fig. 4). These results show that DBT assimilation by the microorganisms is located at the interphase between the organic and the aqueous phase. 4. Bioreactor design Recently, we have suggested a whole cell immobilization reactor for an oil desulfurization process [13] that it offers a number of advantages for industrial application as compared to the conventional continuous stirred tank bioreactor [14], namely: 1) biocatalyst recovery; 2) increase of the oil/water volumetric ratio to 90% with respect to the 30-50%o of a continuous stirred tank bioreactor; 3) disposal of a lower amount of exhausted medium after treatment; 4) easy separation of the oil from the biocatalyst; 5) low risk of microbial pollution. Even though the most common method is entrapment [15], cell immobilization was made by us through an adsorption method employing hydrophilic natural supports. This technique is not limiting on the hydrocarbon uptake mechanism; in fact, the ideal L. Setti et al. / Fuel Processing Technology 52 (1997) 145-153 151 conditions for hydrocarbon biodegradation come to be restored at the interphase between the oil phase and the hydrophilic support surface. Immobilization also seems to retain the water around the catalyst, which is essential for biocatalytic function. Hydrophilic supports may however compete with the biocatalyst for the available water in the reaction system, especially when the water is also a reactant in the DBT biodegradation pathway so that complete depletion of water from the reaction system results in no biocatalytic activity. The use of hydrophilic supports permits a process in which a decanter unit downstream of the bioreactor is not required as the water remains entrapped in the support. This factor represents an advantage in terms of finishing costs and times. In order to be a good desulfurizing immobilized whole cell, a microorganism must possess the following properties: 1) high desulfurizing activity (aerobic microorganisms usually exhibit a higher activity than anaerobic ones), 2) high solvent tolerance (among the bacteria tested, the Pseudomonas group showed highest solvent tolerance while the tolerance of some species within the genus Corynebacterium, Breuibacterium and Rhodococcus was relatively lower), 3) an activity which is not affected by co-metabolic compounds, i.e. the microorganism has to be able to grow on DBT as the only carbon and sulfur source. Pending the development of a better biocatalyst, the choice of the strain for the immobilization process falls on the Pseudomonas sp., as it satisfies the three main properties described above. Owing to the low specificity of the microorganism, it was decided to dissolve the sulfur compound in a poorly biodegradable solvent such as 2,2-4,4-6-8,8 heptamethylnonane. Thanks to this strategy, it was possible to study an ideal biodesulfurization process employing a non-selective desulfurizing microorganism in conditions of obliged desulfurization selectivity. The activity of the immobilized cells was seen to be significantly lower, up to ten times, than that of the cell-free, being in the order of 0.01-0.04 g/g (biomass)/h, a rate which depends on the kind of support used. The immobilized cells mantained an operational stability for about 100 h, a time which is similar to that reported for some cell-free bioconversions in n-alkane (Table 1). Water bioavailability is one of the main factors which still needs to be investigated in order to improve the performance of the immobilized biocatalyst which is still not as of today competitive with the physico- Table 1 Operation times of some bioreactors used for bioconversions in n-alkane carried out by cells (according to Ref. [ l 81) Reactor type Biocatalyst Organic phase Operation time (days) Stirred-tank R. minuta n-heptane 55-63 (half-life) Packed-bed Mycobacterium n-hexadecane 1-3 (half-life) Stirred-tank N. corallina n-hexadecane 10 Stirred-tank P. oleouorans n-octane 2 Fed-batch P. oleouorans n-octane 8-12.5 Loop R. arrhizus n-heptane 12.5 152 L. Setti et al. / Fuel Processing Technology 52 (1997) 145-153 chemical process (0.4-18 g/g (catalyst)/h) [16]. The hydrophobicity-hydrophilicity balance is derived from the intrinsic properties of the polymers and is affected by the water content of the gels. 5. Conclusions Although much work has been published on biocatalysis utilizing whole cells in non-conventional media, practical applications are as yet scarce. The prospects for a biodesulfurization process are linked to the concept of biorefining, cell immobilization technology representing possibility for designing a continuous biodesulfurization reactor which is less expensive and more efficient than a stirred tank bioreactor. Improvements in the specificity and the activity of the biocatalysts as well as studies on the physico-chemical factors which can lead to an ideal biodesulfurization process are essential for BDS technology to be able to find practical applications in the near future.
title: Whole cell biocatalysis for an oil desulfurization process

reaction_catalog.transformation: desulfurization of organic sulfur compounds in oil

procedure_catalog.name_reported: whole cell immobilization reactor for an oil desulfurization process

steps.method_details: cell immobilization through an adsorption method employing hydrophilic natural supports

full_text: Bio-crude oil from lignocellulosic biomass has gained increasing attentions as a substitute for the depletion of fossil fuel, due to the fact that it can be refined into various of transportation fuels that can be used as drop-in biofuels [1]. Typically, hydrothermal liquefaction (HTL) in sub- and supercritical water and alcoholysis liquefaction in supercritical alcohols are the two common direct liquefaction processes to produce bio-crude oil from lignocellulosic biomass [2–4]. A significant advantage of HTL technology relates to its feedstock flexibility. In a HTL process, biomass, especially high-moisture feedstocks such as macroalgae and microalgae, can be directly converted into liquid products without an energy consuming in the dewatering process [1]. For alcoholysis liquefaction, ethanol has been widely used as both reaction substrate and liquid hydrogen donor for biomass conversion under supercritical conditions due to its lower critical point (240.75°C, 6.14MPa, 0.276g/cm3) than water (373.95°C, 22.06MPa, 0.322g/cm3). It was revealed that ethanol could not only reduce acid content in the bio-crude oil through esterification reaction, but also prevent the condensation, cyclization, and re-polymerization of the intermediates [5]. However, in most studies, low yield of water-insoluble bio-crude oil with low heating values of the resulting bio-oil was obtained in case of utilizing water as the solvent for liquefaction of lignocellulosic biomass [6]. To enhance water-insoluble bio-crude oil yield with lower oxygen content, hydrogen donor (HD), HD solvents, and reducing with high-pressure hydrogen agent have been utilized in the upgrading process. HD was widely used in the co-liquefaction process (e.g., plastic waste & wheat straw) to improve the H/Ceff of feedstocks; at the same time plastic waste also could supply hydrogen [7]. The use of HD solvents is becoming popular and the most common donors that have been considered are tetralin and light alcohols such as methanol and ethanol [8]. These previous investigations showed that the HD solvents had better results in reducing the tar or coke formation as well as the hydrogenating performance via donate in situ H+ to hydrogenate the biomass fragments [9]. A critical step is via hydrodeoxygenation (HDO) in the presence of reducing agents (mainly H2) to remove the oxygen of bio-crude oil in the form of water where valuable biofuels can be produced [10]. Usually, HDO processes are accompanied by two main routes, depending on the hydrogen source generated. One is called a direct injection hydro-liquefaction approach where hydrogen is a processing gas performed usually under a high H2 pressure [11–13]. Another is called an in situ reduction approach, instead of external hydrogen gas, the hydrogen is generated in situ [14]. Although the conversion ratio of biomass as well as the quality of bio-crude oil was increased in the presence of high pressure H2, this approach reveals its economics to be unfavorable unless hydrogen is readily available [15–17]. In this regard, the in situ reduction approach has received a lot of attention in recent studies. Xiong et al. [14] reported that hydrogen could be generated in situ through formic acid (FA) and effectively hydrogenating the bio-crude oil. Moreover, instead of using FA, calcium formate as a more environmental-friendly hydrogen donor has been used as an in situ source of hydrogen to improve deoxygenation and the quality of bio-crude oil obtained both in pyrolysis and HTL processes [18–20]. More recently, Li et al. [21] reported the application of nano-Cu catalyst in a glycerol-methanol-water solvent to generate hydrogen in situ for the liquefaction of Miscanthus whereby methanol was effectively decomposed to H2, CO, and CO2 by the catalysis of Cu. Similarly, very recently work from Ma et al. [22] indicated that the hydrogen generated from the aqueous phase reforming of methanol over Raney Ni could offer hydrogen source for the upgrading of bio-crude oil model compounds and the raw bio-crude oil. So far, the hydrogen generated in situ was mainly attributed to the pyrolysis of hydrogen enriched bodies or alcohols reforming, but they were always accompanied by some undesired side reactions with side products which could reduce the selectivity of H2. Another means was by using metal hydrogen carriers based on oxidation reduction reactions. Fedyaeva et al. [23] studied the hydrogenation of bitumen in situ in supercritical water (SCW) with the addition of zinc and aluminum (Al). Zinc and aluminum showed excellent activity in the conversion and hydrogenation of bitumen due to the hydrogen evolution during the oxidation of metals by SCW. Al is a rather promising hydrogen carrier [24], and the in situ formed γ-Al2O3 during the oxidation of Al also could play a catalyst for the ethanol reforming reactions (such as ethanol dehydration [23,25]) to produce additional H2 [26]. However, Al2O3 suffers from possible conversion to AlO(OH) by water. To eliminate the adverse effect of water on the catalytic performance of Al2O3, an initial study of the conversion of lignocellulose biomass with the assisted of Al in supercritical ethanol (SCE) was carried out. The aim of this study is to investigate the synergistic effects of esterification and HDO reactions for bio-crude oil upgrading in one-pot process with the assisted of Al. The pathway for the hydrogen generation by in situ via the oxidation of Al coupled with ethanol reforming was presented by control experiments. The sample of rice stalk (RS) used in this study as the biomass feedstock was collected from Shenyang, China as it was without any pretreatment before use but sieved to particles size smaller than 30mesh (99.0% purity) was supplied by Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. The ethanol and acetone used were analytical grade. Ethanolysis of RS was performed in a 500mL stainless autoclave. Typically, a fixed 15g of RS and 150mL anhydrous ethanol were loaded into the autoclave based on our previous studies [27]. Three different Al to RS mass ratios (1:3, 2:3, and 3:3) were used in the experiments. Ethanolysis of Al at three different ratios (Al to ethanol ratio of 1:30, 1:15, and 1:10g/mL) was also performed under the same conditions aimed to know the reactivity of ethanol and Al. For this study, a total of 7 runs were considered, as shown in Table 2 . In a typical run, the autoclave was heated from room temperature to reaction temperature (300°C) for ~60min and held at the reaction temperature for 60min. Once the reaction was completed (after 60min), the reactor was cooled to approximately 30°C by a fan in 160min. The gas that had not been condensed was vented and rest of the products were poured into a 500mL beaker for separation. In addition, the residues inside the autoclave were washed several times with ethanol to ensure that all of them were recovered and then dumped into the same beaker. The products in the beaker were filtered to separate the solid and liquid phase (ethanol soluble). The solid products were rinsed with acetone to obtain the acetone phase. The bio-crude oil defined as the combined mass of ethanol and acetone soluble fraction after they were evaporated by rotary evaporator. The acetone insoluble material was dried in an oven at 105°C for 4h to obtain residue solid. Each run was performed in triplicate and standard deviation is reported. (1) Yield of bio − crude oil wt % = Mass bio − crude oil / Mass feed × 100 % where Massfeed included two parts: 1. the weight of initial rice stalk fed; 2. the ethanol converted into the bio-oil, which was calculated through ethyl esters according to our previous studies [28]. Gas composition was determined using an online infrared syngas analyzer (Wuhan Cubic Optoelectronics Co., Ltd., China; accuracy≤±1%, resolution: 0.01%, error≤2%). The gas was filtered with silicon before injected into the unit at a flow rate of 1.0mL/min. It should be note that adequate gases were needed till a constant value was recorded because the response time may be longer. In addition, low molecule non-condensable gaseous hydrocarbons (i.e., C2H4, C2H6, C3H6, and C3H8) could not be directly detected by online infrared analyzer, which were represented by CnHm. The relative contents of the components (CO, CO2, CH4, CnHm, and H2) were calculated by normalization method. The chemical composition of the bio-crude oils was analyzed by a gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS, Agilent 6890N/5975, USA) equipped with a HP-5 capillary column (30m×0.25mm×0.25μm) and a quadrupole analyzer operated in an electron impact (70eV) mode. The carrier gas was He at a flow rate of 1.0mL/min. The initial column temperature was set at 50°C (hold 3min), and followed by a heating rate of 5°C/min to 180°C and then increased at a constant heating rate of 10°C/min to 280°C for 15min. The mass range scanned was from 20 to 500amu. The compounds were identified by comparison with library spectra supplied by the NIST08. The elemental compositions (C, H, N, and O) of the bio-crude oil were determined by a CHNS/O elemental analyzer (Eurovector EA 3000, Italy; accuracy: CHNS ≤0.1%). The higher heating value (HHV) of the bio-crude oil and solid residue were determined using an oxygen-bomb calorimeter (Changsha Kaiyuan, China; accuracy ≤0.2%). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of solid samples were performed by an X-ray diffractometer (X'Pert Pro MPD, PANalytical B.V., Netherlands) with Cu Kα radiation (λ=0.15406nm) operating at 40kV and 40mA. The scattering angle 2θ was varied from 20 to 90°. Thermal analysis tests (thermogravimetry, TG; differential thermogravimetry, DTG; and differential scanning calorimetry, DSC) for bio-crude oils were conducted on a STA 449F3 simultaneous thermal analyzer (NETZSCH, Germany). For each test, approximately 10mg of oil sample was placed into a platinum crucible and heated from 30 to 800°C at a heating rate of 20°C/min under N2 atmosphere at a flow rate of 60mL/min. The energy recovery (ER) was defined as the total heating value of bio-crude oil to total heating value of feed (RS and ethanol), the calculation equation was shown below: (2) ER % = HHV bio − crude oil × Mass bio − crude oil / ∑ HHV feed × Mass feed Herein, the ethanol consumption contained two parts: 1. the part converted into the bio-oil; 2. the part involved in the reforming reactions, which was approximately calculated through the amount of hydrogen. The functional groups of solid residues after liquefaction were characterized using a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 FT-IR, resolution: 0.5cm−1). FT-IR spectra were recorded in transmittance mode in the range of 4000–400cm−1 with an attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode. Gas compositions after ethanolysis of Al with and without the presence of RS are shown in Fig. 1 . It can be observed that as Al to ethanol ratio increased from 1:30 to 1:15g/mL, the yield of hydrogen increased significantly and then increased slightly as Al to ethanol ratio increased to 1:10g/mL. Theoretically, approximately 277.78mmol/g Al of the hydrogen can be obtained according to the reaction equation (Al+3C2H5OH→Al(C2H5O)3 +3/2 H2). Here, hydrogen yield increased by 10.9, 78.9, and 20.2% of the theoretical yield with the Al to ethanol ratio of 1:30, 1:15, and 1:10g/mL, respectively, indicating that some side reactions occurred in the process. The methane formation indicated that CC bond was cleaved, and the hydrocarbon formation also suggested re-polymerization reactions occurred in the Al-SCE system (Fig. 1b). As the Al to ethanol ratio increased from 1:30 to 1:10g/mL, the hydrogen in the gaseous product obtained from RS-Al-SCE system increased from 42.39 to 67.28 and then to 248.95mmol compared with that of 9.49mmol obtained from RS-SCE system as shown in Fig. 1a. When the Al to ethanol ratio increased from 1:15 to 1:10g/mL, the yield of hydrogen obtained in the Al-SCE system slightly increased; however, the hydrogen in the gaseous product obtained from RS-Al-SCE system significantly increased. It was postulated that the hydrogen consumption decreased with the increase of Al loading. Herein, it can be conclude that the Al to ethanol ratio of 1:15g/mL was proper for the RS-Al-SCE system. The XRD patterns of the solid residues from ethanol and Al powder reaction with and without the presence of RS at 300°C for 1h are illustrated in Figs. 2 and 3 . According to the XRD analysis data, Al was partially oxidized by SCE and various forms of Al2O3 were formed. As shown in Fig. 2, the sample obtained from Al-SCE system contains the unreacted Al powder and the η-, κ-, θ-, and α-Al2O3. In addition to the unreacted Al, only trace amounts of oxidized aluminum (θ- and α-Al2O3) were detected in the RS-Al-SCE system (Fig. 3). Plausible reaction pathways during the investigation of ethanol and Al system were proposed, as shown in Fig. 4 . According to wet corrosion mechanism, Al can corrode in ethanol to form Al(C2H5O)3, and H2 is generated [29]. The Al(C2H5O)3 is unstable, which can be transformed to white-colored Al(OH)3 via hydrolysis [30]. Boehmite (AlOOH) and the ultimate product alumina (θ- and α-Al2O3) were formed by multileveled thermal dehydration. Additionally, it was observed that the stable α-Al2O3 was formed through a serious of metastable transition alumina phase γ-AlOOH→γ-Al2O3 →δ-Al2O3 →θ-Al2O3 →α-Al2O3 [26]. The formation of gaseous products, such as methane, carbon dioxide, carbon monoxide, and other hydrocarbons (Fig. 1b), indicated that side reactions occurred during Al ethanolysis process. It was explained that ethanol reforming reactions occurred during the oxidation of Al, because γ-Al2O3 can act as a catalyst for the processes of ethanol reforming [23,25], resulting in the formation of CO, CO2, H2, and CnHm. This could explain why the hydrogen generation mmoles were higher than that of the theoretical calculation as depicted in Fig. 1a. Notably, the mass of Al used in the present study was relatively higher despite the fact that it can be recycled from aluminum electrolysis. Interestingly, almost half of the mmoles of H2 was generated from ethanol reforming via Al2O3 in Run 2 as depicted in Fig. 1a, on the assumption that Al was completely transformed. It was evident that the actual consumption of Al was low as it was seen from XRD analysis (Figs. 2 and 3). Therefore, the addition of Al in the liquefaction process would be a feasible choice for bio-oil upgrading. The actual use of Al to instead external hydrogen is an attractive way in HDO process since it leads to the decreased of additional costs such as hydrogen storage and delivery as well as enhancement of safety. However, further attention should be given to the efficiency of H2 generation and recirculation of the unreacted Al so as to evaluate the economical feasibility. The effect of Al loading on the bio-crude oil yield along with their corresponding ER during RS liquefaction in SCE is shown in Fig. 5 . It can be seen that the bio-crude oil yield went through a maximum of 33.45wt% at the Al to RS ratio of 2:3. Because reducing gases are effective in stabilizing the fragmented products and hence reduce char formation [9], it is reasonable that the bio-crude oil yield increased with the Al to RS ratio. However, when the Al to RS ratio was increased to 3:3, their competition to ethanol might be more intense, and there would not be sufficient time to allow ethanol to act as solvent for the degradation of RS, resulting in a decrease of the oil yield. The ER variation trend was consistent with that of oil yield, and a maximum ER of 75.09% was achieved with the Al to RS ratio increased to 2:3, which was increased by around 53% than that of without the addition of Al. When the Al to RS ratio was further increased to 3:3, the reduction in ER was significant, where an ER of 45.01% was observed due to the decreased bio-crude oil yield as shown in Fig. 3. Literature values showed that the ER of bio-crude oil was increased by 0.90–4.0 fold after hydrotreated in situ but still had a far too low ER of <50% [18,19]. It should be noted that as for the lignocellulosic biomass like rice husk, rice stalk, wheat straw, etc., they all exhibit a relatively low ER when compared to algae due to the different compositions of the feedstock [31]. A direct way to evaluate HDO process is through elemental analysis. Table 3 shows elemental and HHV analysis of bio-crude oils obtained under different conditions. The HHVs of the bio-crude oils were increased with the Al to RS ratios, and the maximum of HHV was 36.07MJ/kg, which occurred at the ratio of 3:3, concluded that the effect of HDO was observed with an enhanced HHV of the bio-crude oil. The increase in HHVs could be due to the corresponding low O/C and high H/C ratios as shown in a van Krevelen diagram (Fig. 6 ), from which the bio-crude oil at the Al to RS ratio of 3:3 had the lowest O/C ratio of 0.11 and highest H/C ratio of 1.54. Compared to the raw material and non-hydrogenated bio-crude oil, the O/C and H/C ratios of the bio-crude oil were improved greatly after upgrading. Fig. S1 presents the total ion chromatograms (TICs) of the liquid products obtained from the reaction of ethanol and Al powder. The reactivity of ethanol was verified by heating ethanol and Al powder under the same conditions. A total of 4 molecules, including ethyl acetate, butanol, 1-ethoxy butane, and 1,1-diethoxy ethane were detected in the product, and molecules obtained from the reaction of ethanol and Al powder were only formed to a very small extent (Fig. 3a). Some product molecules including ethyl acetate, butanol, and 1,1-diethoxy ethane were also detected to verify the reactivity of ethanol by heating ethanol and catalyst (α-MoC1−x/AC) during catalytic ethanolysis of Kraft lignin at 280°C [32]. Therefore, it further verified that ethanol reforming reactions occurred during the oxidation of Al as depicted in Section 3.3. The bio-crude oils obtained from RS-Al-SCE system were also detected by GC–MS, and the products classification is listed in Table 4 . The detailed distributions and yields of the chemical species are listed in Table S1. In all runs, the oil components was classified into phenols, ketones, esters, alcohols, acids, nitrogen compounds, aldehydes, ether, hydrocarbons, and acid anhydrides. Based on peak areas, phenols, ketones, and esters were the dominant components, which accounted for around 50% of the total integrated GC peak area for all the detected compounds. The total percentage area for identified compounds from Run 4 was around 86%, where it was remarkably decreased to around 57% with the increasing Al/RS ratios. A possible explanation was that more polarity compounds formed in the bio-crude oil obtained from Runs 5–7, which GC–MS technique cannot obtain [33]. The volatile fraction (excluding ethanol and acetone) that had been removed by rotary evaporator was also detected by GC–MS. It was observed that the major composition of the volatile fraction were alcohols (methyl alcohol, propyl alcohol and 1-butanol), esters (ethyl acetate, propanoic acid ethyl ester and butanoic acid ethyl ester), furans (2-methyl-furan), ethers (3-ethoxy-2-methyl-1-propene) and aldehydes (1,1-diethoxyethane), which were low molecular compounds with carbon numbers from C1–C6. As shown in Table S1, the most abundant compound in the bio-crude oil obtained from Run 4 was 2-methyl-propanoic acid anhydride (13.37%), while it was decreased to 2.15% in Run 5, and was not identified in Runs 6 and 7. Similar results were reported by Xu et al. [34] on liquefaction of woody biomass in sub- and supercritical ethanol in the presence of H2. It could be concluded that the H2 involved reaction inhibited the formation of acid anhydride. The most abundant compounds identified in the oils obtained from Runs 5–7 were hexadecanoic acid ethyl ester (3.6%), tetrahydro[2,2′]bifuranyl-5-one (6.73%), and tetrahydro[2,2′]bifuranyl-5-one (4.05%), respectively. In this sense, the RS-Al-SCE system appeared to promote the formation of ketones. Esters were the most abundant compounds when the ethanol was used as solvent, similar results were also reported by Kosinkova et al. [35] and Brand et al. [36]. On the other hand, the acidity of oil would be expected to be much lower because of the esterification reactions between ethanol and acids. The content of esters first increased from 29.64% (Run 4) to 34.47% (Run 5) and then decreased to 20.86% (Run 6) and 19.38% (Run 7), while the vary of hydrocarbons content was opposite. The hydrocarbons included mainly naphthalene and long-chain alkanes. It suggested that hydrogenated reaction system could promote ester compounds to form hydrocarbons [37]. Aldehyde and ketone compounds were mainly derived from holocellulose conversion in SCE, due to occurrence of dehydration reactions [38]. It could be suggested that dehydration and aromatization of the structural units of holocellulose occurred in the presence of Al, resulting in the higher yield of ketones. Besides, ketones may transform between alcohols and acids, and thus they were less stable under hydrothermal conditions [39]. Results in Table 4 also showed that yields of alcohols and acids decreased in Runs 5–7, which might be explained as a result of the increase in ketones. The phenolic compounds mainly included phenol and alkylated phenols, and its content was somewhat decreased with the addition of Al, however the types of phenols identified was increased. The nitrogen-containing compounds were decreased in the RS-Al-SCE system, which may be due to the hydrodenitrification reactions [40]. The TG, DTG, and DSC curves of bio-crude oils obtained from RS ethanolysis with/without the presence of Al are shown in Fig. 7 . Heating the bio-crude oils under an inert atmosphere to 800°C resulted in a mass loss of 86–94wt% (Fig. 7a). The residue left after evaporation/thermal decomposition of bio-crude oils obtained from Runs 4–7 were 12.63, 13.54, 6.11, and 6.70wt%, respectively. This showed that bio-crude oil obtained from Runs 6–7 had higher volatility and less potential coke [41]. The reason was that hydrogen avoided the repolymerization reactions and suppressed tar or coke formation, thus improving the volatility of the bio-crude oil. In addition, oil obtained from Run 6 had the lowest decomposition peak temperature (188°C) with the highest mass loss rate, about 50–80°C less than other those of the oils. The boiling point distribution of the bio-crude oils could be assessed by TGA [42]. The boiling point distribution of the bio-crude oils and diesel is presented in Table 5 . Only 55.39 and 51.95wt% of the bio-crude oil from Runs 4 and 5 had a boiling point <300°C, respectively. In contrast, about 65.09wt% and 71.98wt% of the bio-crude oil from Runs 6 and 7 had a boiling point <300°C, indicating that a large fraction of the bio-crude oil was of lower molecular weight. It was concluded that the addition of Al resulted in a significant increase in the fraction of bio-crude oil with lower boiling point (<300°C) except for that from Run 5. Compared with a typical diesel fuel approximately 97% of which has a boiling point <300°C, the boiling point of the bio-crude oil obtained was mainly in the range of 100–400°C, indicating that the obtained bio-crude oils were much heavier [43]. Due to the endothermic volatilization and decomposition, a corresponding endothermic peak appeared on the DSC curve. From Fig. 7b, there is an endothermic peak of water evaporating from 30 to 150°C in the DSC curves of the oils obtained from Runs 4–6 except for that from Run 7. When the temperature was higher than 225°C, the DSC curve of the oil obtained from Run 4 kept a continuous increasing, while the curves of the oils obtained from Runs 5–7 increased first and then followed by a decrease when temperature was higher than 560°C. Obvious exothermic peaks were observed on the DSC curves in the temperature range of 150–240°C for the oils obtained from Runs 4–6. This might be caused by the re-polymerization of some small molecular compounds in the oils obtained from Runs 4–6. Solid residue is the second yield product during hydrothermal liquefaction. Currently, solid residue can be used as solid fuel or other applications. To better understand its composition, residues derived with different Al to RS ratios were studied through elemental analysis and FT-IR analysis. The ultimate and proximate analysis of the solid residues is presented in Table 6 . The volatile matters was significantly decreased with the addition of Al, and thus probably contributed to higher bio-crude oil yield, especially in Run 6 with RS/Al of 2/3. There was also a significant decrease of O/C in the runs with the addition of Al, resulting in higher HHVs. It may be caused by dehydration reactions [44], which were accelerated by the in situ generated hydrogen. FT-IR analysis of the solid residue was studied to identify the chemical functional group absorption type and corresponding chemical species. As can be seen from Fig. 8 , the aromatic skeletal vibrations at 1604cm−1 was attributed to the CC stretching vibration from lignin [45]. OH plane deformation (1319cm−1) and CO stretching (1066cm−1) were the characteristic absorption of holocellulose [44]. The absorbance peak at 782cm−1 represented CH bending indicated the presence of aromatic compounds and their derivatives [46]. The four peaks mentioned above decreased or disappeared completely with the addition of Al, indicating the degradation of RS was enhanced. Additionally, mineral matter may exist in the residue proved by the absorbance around 456cm−1 [47]. Liquefaction of RS was carried out in SCE with the addition of Al to enhance deoxygenation and the quality of the bio-crude oil obtained. The oxidation of Al in SCE could offer in situ hydrogen for the bio-crude oil upgrading. In situ formed Al2O3 could also act as catalyst to the ethanol reforming reactions to produce additional hydrogen. The H/C molar ratio was increased from 1.41 to 1.54 while the O/C molar ratio was decreased from 0.29 to 0.11 with the Al to RS ratio increased from 1:3 to 3:3. A maximum energy recovery around 75% with oil yield approx. 33wt% was achieved at the Al to RS ratio of 2:3. After hydrogenated, aldehyde, acid, and nitrogenous groups in the oil were decreased while bicyclic phenolics, ketones, and hydrocarbons were increased. Besides, the hydrogen involved reactions resulted in significant increase in the volatile oil yield with boiling point <300°C. Solid residue obtained after liquefaction has potential to be used as solid fuel due to its relatively higher carbon content and HHV, and its HHV was also increased with the addition of Al. We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51576135) and the Aeronautical Science Foundation of China (No. 2015ZB54006). Supplementary material Image 1 Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.fuproc.2017.07.013.
abstract: Bio-crude oil derived from sub- and supercritical liquefaction of lignocellulosic biomass has received considerable interests, but it cannot be directly used as a fuel because of its high oxygen content. In this work, a novel hydro-liquefaction process with the hydrogen generated in situ during the oxidation of aluminum by supercritical ethanol was investigated. X-ray diffraction analyses showed that η-, κ-, θ-, and α-Al2O3 mixtures were formed as a result of aluminum oxidation. Proposed reaction pathways revealed that ethanol reforming reactions occurred with additional hydrogen produced. A maximum energy recovery around 75% from feedstock to bio-crude oil was achieved with the addition of aluminum. Investigation on the chemical composition showed that liquefaction of rice stalk in the presence of aluminum decreased the yield of aldehyde, acid, and nitrogenous groups, and increased the yield of bicyclic phenolics, ketones, and hydrocarbons in the oil. In addition, the volatile fraction of the oil was increased. Overall, aluminum assisted liquefaction significant increase in the degree of deoxygenation, and may be a promising way to convert lignocellulosic into biofuels.
title: Hydro-liquefaction of rice stalk in supercritical ethanol with in situ generated hydrogen
