full_text: The purpose of this work was to obtain an understanding of the fundamental mechanisms governing mercury speciation in coal-fired power plants (other than the mechanisms involved in mercury oxidation across SCR catalysts) and the relative importance of differences found in coal-fired power stations, in terms of their effect on mercury speciation. In order to do this, it was necessary to conduct this research in a semi-industrial scale facility with actual coal-derived flue gas, using a temperature-time history similar to that of the full-scale units, to ensure that the mechanisms investigated were the most relevant. Although it would be unfeasible to traverse every operating condition and coal type available, the coal types tested ranged across the most significant differences in rank, and the operating conditions traversed the range of most low-sulfur PC-fired plants in the United States. The mechanisms governing mercury speciation have been described in this paper, particularly emphasizing the role of each parameter involved, insofar as they have been elucidated in this work and are now known. The predominant forms of mercury in coal-fired flue gas are elemental (Hg0) and oxidized (HgCl2) [1–3]. The fraction of oxidized mercury in the stack effluent of a particular power plant depends on the coal type, combustion efficiency, and the pollution control equipment used. Essentially all of the mercury entering the furnace with the coal is vaporized and exists in the elemental form until the flue gases cool below ∼1000 °F (538 °C) [1–3]. The oxidation and capture of mercury in coal-fired boiler systems is kinetically limited [1–3]. In addition to the trace nature of mercury in coal-fired boilers, favorable reactions for mercury oxidation and capture have short temperature/time windows. Consequently, the extent of mercury oxidation and capture is highly dependent on catalytic processes. A system of reactions, which include significant chlorine-speciation reactions, has been proposed to describe homogeneous Hg-oxidation [4]. This set of governing reactions allows direct oxidation of Hg0 to HgCl and HgCl to HgCl2 by the following four chlorine species with different reaction rates: Cl, Cl2, HCl, and HOCl [4]. This system of equations has been shown to effectively predict mercury speciation for specific homogeneous systems [2]. However, the homogeneous model alone consistently underpredicts the oxidation of mercury from coal-fired boilers [5]. Niksa et al. [6] suggested a possible mechanism whereby unburned carbon (UBC) may catalyze mercury oxidation, as follows: (1) UBC+HCl⇔UBC.Cl+H and UBC.Cl+Hg0 ⇔HgCl+UBC As will be shown, there is a synergistic relationship between the above UBC-based mechanism and the presence of calcium (inherent in the flyash or added through sorbents) that enhances mercury capture by flyash and sorbent in the duct and across baghouses and electrostatic precipitators (ESPs). This paper presents the results of an investigation of the relationship between flue-gas parameters and mercury oxidation and capture across a pilot-scale baghouse utilizing full-scale bag technology, across a pilot-scale ESP, and in the duct upstream of a particulate collection device, under conditions representative of full-scale boilers. In addition, fundamental mechanisms responsible for mercury removal enhancement by calcium in high-calcium ash or injected calcium-based sorbents are elucidated. Experiments were conducted in the Combustion Research Facility (CRF) at Southern Research Institute (SRI) in Birmingham, AL, a 1-MW t semi-industrial-scale, coal-fired facility, which mimics the thermal profile of a full-scale boiler from the burner through the economizer. Fig. 1 shows a two-dimensional sketch of this facility. The furnace is a vertical, up-fired, 8.5-meter high cylinder, with an inner diameter of 1.07-meters (see Fig. 1). This allows gas velocities of 3 to 6-meters per second and residence times of 1.3 to 2.5 s, depending upon the firing rate. The furnace is both refractory lined and water cooled, in order to obtain furnace temperatures that are both representative of full-scale units and that are stable and reproducible. As shown in Fig. 2 , the temperature/time history of the CRF mimics that of full-scale power plants from the burner through the economizer. Table 1 contains a description of the main coal types used in this work. A combination of extractive methods, such as Ontario Hydro and carbon traps, and the most advanced semi-continuous mercury speciation monitors (combined with state-of-the-art spike and recovery validation systems) were used to obtain the flue-gas mercury species concentrations in this work [7]. There isn't space to present all data or findings obtained from this research. However, the findings presented were chosen to illustrate the quantitative and relative impact of each of the most important parameters observed affecting mercury speciation. Some parameters, such as H2O, CO, or NO concentration, iron in ash, and many others, were indeed examined but were found to have little impact on mercury speciation. In an effort to demonstrate the observed mechanisms from different types of experiments and test conditions, results of measurements across a baghouse are presented, followed by results of tests across an ESP, and then some interpolative mapping of Hg-speciation response to the most important parameters are presented. Fig. 3 compares the relationship between Hg-oxidation across a baghouse and chlorine concentration when the flue-gas HCl concentration was varied either by injecting chlorine gas through the burner while firing Powder River Basin (PRB) coal (a low-chlorine, high-calcium ash, sub-bituminous coal) or by blending PRB with higher-chlorine bituminous coals. Although the form of chlorine can have a significant impact on its mercury oxidation potential, the form of chlorine injected through the burner is relatively insignificant, since it is converted to HCl by the time the flue gas reaches temperatures where mercury oxidation occurs [1,5,7]. A mass balance on coal-mercury suggested that the majority of mercury at the baghouse inlet was in the gas phase for all blend conditions. As shown, chlorine injection alone (i.e., resulting in HCl addition in the back pass) had some impact on mercury oxidation. However, much more mercury oxidation was realized as a result of coal blending, even though there was less chlorine; adding bituminous coal yielded more UBC in the ash, which appeared to catalytically enhance mercury oxidation. Fig. 3 also suggests that increases in UBC levels above some moderate threshold had less effect on mercury oxidation, while increases in chlorine (primarily HCl) concentration continued to impact mercury oxidation across the baghouse, for the full range of chlorine concentrations investigated. It should be noted that all these chlorine and UBC levels are considered low relative to the levels produced from most eastern bituminous coals. Table 2 illustrates the synergism that results from blending bituminous coals with PRB sub-bituminous coal, ostensibly to combine both UBC and calcium for enhanced mercury capture by the flyash. The UBC was altered both by blending (the bituminous coal fraction produces more UBC than the PRB fraction) and by changing the amount of excess air used for combustion. Table 2 also illustrates the relative ineffectiveness of chlorine injection alone at increasing mercury capture by PRB flyash. The UBC was more important to the enhancement of mercury capture by flyash than the increase of total chlorine content (predominantly in the form of HCl in the back pass of the facility). As shown in Table 2, coal blending generated significant Hg-capture enhancement in the presence of relatively little chlorine, yet the UBC fractions were relatively small compared with those observed for power plants burning 100% bituminous coal. Parameters considered included the full spectrum of bulk flue gas and flyash compositions, as well as temperature, residence time, and heating rate. An extensive exploration of Hg-oxidation and Hg-capture correlations with each parameter affected by coal blending was performed. The exploration revealed that flue-gas components other than calcium, UBC, and HCl (the predominant form of chlorine in the back pass of coal-fired power plants) had little effect [1]. Changes in heating rates in the range of differences found in the existing fleet of U.S. coal-fired power plants was not significant, and temperature and residence time were of course very important. Based on the finding of this exploratory investigation, subsequent tests were designed to further quantify the impact of these constituents, which were found to be most important. One approach to combine the ash components necessary for enhanced mercury removal is to inject ash from one coal type into the baghouse of a power plant that burns another coal type. Either a high-calcium ash (i.e., PRB) may be injected into the baghouse of a power plant burning a coal that produces significant quantities of UBC, or a bituminous ash may be injected into the baghouse of a plant burning a high-calcium coal. The latter was attempted in this investigation and was performed in such a way so as to effectively isolate the impact of ash composition from changes in flue-gas composition that occur during coal blending. Ash collected in the CRF baghouse while burning Choctaw America, a low-chlorine bituminous coal, was later re-injected into the baghouse while firing PRB sub-bituminous coal. Table 3 contains the analysis of the ash injected. The Choctaw America ash was fairly low in calcium and had a UBC content of ∼3.5%. The specific surface area of the injected ash was similar to that of the PRB ash produced in the furnace (see Table 3). Fig. 4 illustrates the result of injecting the Choctaw America coal ash (with 3.5% UBC) into the baghouse while burning PRB coal in the CRF. In addition to 100% flyash, several mixtures of hydrated lime and ash were injected into the baghouse. The injection rate of material was approximately equal to half that of the ash loading in the duct and was consistent for all three injection conditions shown in Fig. 4. Mercury oxidation and removal across the baghouse increased with increasing bituminous ash concentration, as shown in Fig. 4. In fact, twice the total removal of the baseline condition was achieved while injecting 100% bituminous ash. Hydrated lime has approximately an order of magnitude higher internal surface area than coal ash, but essentially no carbon. Consequently, the hydrated lime appeared to have little effect on mercury removal in the baghouse other than to dilute the concentration of bituminous ash being injected. The baseline calcium concentration was already high, from the contribution of the PRB flyash. Fig. 5 , which contains additional data, supports the suggestion that the dominant parameter of importance to enhance mercury oxidation through coal blending is the UBC in the ash. For the blend data, UBC was increased by blending 10% to 20% bituminous coal by mass with the PRB coal. However, mineral matter in the ash also increased along with UBC for coal blending. In order to isolate the effects of UBC on Hg-oxidation, combustion modifications were used to increase the UBC while burning PRB coal only. Combustion in the furnace was modified so as to increase the UBC from coal char and avoid generating soot, which would have different properties than typical UBC. Specifically, the airflow and swirl at the burner were maintained so as to continue to consume the volatiles and prevent the formation of soot. Char UBC was increased by lowering the overall air/fuel ratio and reducing the swirl through the overfire-air ports such that the furnace exit oxygen was lower and char burnout was less efficient. The results of the UBC isolation tests are shown in Figs. 5 and 6 . The mercury oxidation increase from coal blending appears to be completely accounted for by the increase of UBC in the flyash. As shown in Fig. 5, the curve of oxidation versus UBC was somewhat of a continuum for the two conditions – (1) PRB only and (2) blending – suggesting that UBC was the dominant parameter affecting the flue-gas Hg-oxidation-state. Similarly, Fig. 6 illustrates the relationship between mercury removal and UBC for data taken while blending 20% or less of two different bituminous coals with PRB coal. The far left data point is for PRB only. The increase of mercury removal for PRB only tests as a function of UBC concentration is much steeper than for the coal blends and has a zero y-intercept. Blending bituminous coal with PRB coal enhances mercury removal by providing more UBC. The most likely explanation for the greater amount of Hg-removal induced from combustion-modification-produced UBC than from coal-blending-produced UBC is the nature of the carbon generated. The reason why the concentrations of UBC produced from PRB coals are typically small is that the char produced from firing PRB coal is much more reactive than the char produced from bituminous coal [8], and PRB coal char has a higher internal surface area than bituminous-derived char [8,9]. In addition, bituminous-derived UBC tends to be more graphitic and turbostratic (Hurt and Gibbins) than PRB-derived char. PRB-derived char is much more like activated carbon than turbostratic char [1,8,9,10] and, therefore, likely to be more effective at enhancing mercury removal than the UBC derived from the bituminous coal. In other tests, hydrated-lime injection into the baghouse increased Hg-removal from 25% to nearly 80%, when firing a low-chlorine bituminous coal (∼3.5% UBC in the ash), with a much lower calcium content than PRB coal. Other parameters, such as the SO x concentration, were unchanged by the hydrated-lime injection. However, it was not clear whether or not calcium oxide or hydrated lime alone would effectively capture oxidized mercury or if the calcium simply enhanced the mercury capture by UBC. In order to further clarify the role of UBC and calcium (either in flyash or in sorbents) in synergistic mercury capture by baghouse filter cakes, two baghouses were used in series. Fig. 7 illustrates the configuration of the baghouses during the test. Choctaw America coal was chosen because of its low-chlorine content and because its ash (generally producing about 4% UBC in the CRF) had been shown to be effective at enhancing the oxidation of mercury through baghouses. The first baghouse was used to collect and remove the flyash from the flue-gas stream and provide a filter cake that would enhance mercury oxidation. Hence, the flue gas leaving the first baghouse was free of particulate (including UBC) and yet contained very little chlorine and a mercury concentration that was over 80% oxidized. Some mercury was removed across the first baghouse, although the removal was not more than 40%, partly because the Choctaw America coal ash was low in calcium. The flue gas at both the inlet and outlet of the second baghouse contained 82–83% oxidized mercury, and the baghouse removed 28% of the total mercury entering it (see Fig. 8). While the second baghouse was fairly clean, some residual ash remained on the bags from previous test runs, and this is potentially why some mercury was removed across this baghouse. However, when hydrated lime was injected into the second baghouse, mercury removal did not increase, and oxidation levels entering and leaving the baghouse did not change. As shown in Fig. 8 , when the bituminous coal full-ash loading was allowed to enter the baghouse continually, the addition of calcium via hydrated-lime injection was very effective at increasing mercury removal. For this case, hydrated-lime injection increased the mercury removal from ∼25% to ∼80%. Hence, the synergistic relationship between calcium and UBC was more than just for UBC to enhance the oxidation of mercury prior to contact with calcium. The synergistic relationship required UBC and calcium (high-calcium ash or calcium sorbents) to be present together in the dust cake for the most effective mercury removal, under the conditions here investigated. It is important to understand that the only difference between the two conditions just described is that in one case the flyash was allowed to mix with the hydrated lime, and in the other case, it was not. Also, no measurable SO3 was generated in the flue gas and acid-gas concentrations, such as SO2, were unchanged. Therefore, the mechanism of calcium/carbon synergism was unrelated to poisoning prevention of the UBC by acid gases. Three extensive coal-blending investigations were conducted to observe the inherent relationship between mercury speciation, unburned carbon from different coals, and calcium from Powder River Basin (PRB) sub-bituminous-coal flyash. In these three separate investigations, PRB coal was fired on the first day of the investigation. On subsequent days, the concentration of bituminous coal in the blend was increased, until on the final day of testing, 100% bituminous coal was fired. North Antelope Rochelle Complex (NARC) PRB coal was used for all three investigations. A different bituminous coal was used for each blend investigation, two eastern bituminous coals (Blacksville and Choctaw America) and a western bituminous coal (West Elk). Depending on the test week, either the chlorine concentration or the furnace exit oxygen (FEO) was altered once during each test day. The differences in chlorine concentration in the range investigated had little impact on mercury speciation. Data obtained from these tests have been plotted in the ternary diagram in Fig. 9 , which plots mercury speciation at the 325 °F (163 °C) location (see Fig. 2), specifically the percent of the total mercury found in each of three categories — (a) vapor-phase elemental mercury, (b) vapor-phase oxidized mercury, and (c) particulate mercury. The ternary plot provides a complete picture of mercury speciation, where each location on the graph defines a completely unique and fixed mercury speciation point. Given any two values (i.e., the oxidized and particulate mercury fractions), the third (i.e., elemental mercury fraction) is also known, because the sum of the three components must always add up to 100%. If you are unfamiliar with ternary plots, consider each axis independently until you get used to the concept. Position the base of the triangle in front of you, such that the axis of interest is on the right of the base. Then the lines that should be used to connect the data points with the axis of interest are those parallel to the base horizontal axis. For the left axis (the Hg0 axis in Fig. 9), follow the line parallel to the axis on the right (the HgCl2 axis in this example), while for the bottom axis (Particulate Bound Mercury), follow the lines parallel to the left axis (the Hg0 axis in this example). Several examples are provided for clarity. The 100% Choctaw data point indicates ∼63% Hg0, ∼37% HgCl2, and ∼0% particulate mercury. The 75% PRB/25% Blacksville data point indicates ∼73% Hg0, ∼5% HgCl2, and ∼22% particulate mercury. Note: all of the data in Figs. 9 and 10 are based on a constant total mercury value, particulate mercury being defined as the total amount of mercury removed from the vapor phase. As shown in Fig. 9, for all conditions tested with a western bituminous (West Elk) blend, the elemental mercury concentration was far less than for the eastern bituminous blend at the 325 °F (163 °C) location. There was slightly more mercury removal for the western bituminous blends and vastly more mercury oxidation upstream of the ESP than was observed during the eastern bituminous blend tests. This result was expected and is consistent with the more active carbon type known to be produced from the western bituminous (West Elk) coal than from the eastern bituminous (Blacksville or Choctaw America) coals [8]. Consistent with the more active carbon type, more mercury was adsorbed on the carbon, which was a precursor to both increased mercury removal and mercury oxidation. For the West Elk blends, the greatest mercury removal was observed for the 2.5% furnace exit oxygen (FEO) condition, presumably because the amount of carbon produced was greater at the lower FEO level. Fig. 10 contains mercury speciation data collected at both the inlet and outlet of the ESP, while firing the western bituminous/PRB blends. As noted previously, there was a significant fraction of particulate-bound mercury prior to the ESP (25–55%), and mercury continued to be captured by the particles across the ESP (either in-flight or on the ESP plates). This process removed between 80 and 90% of the mercury by the time the flue gas reached the ESP exit (measured as total loss of mercury from the 550 °F (288 °C) duct location to the ESP exit), for the optimum blend and firing condition. The UBC concentration for this optimum removal condition at the ESP inlet was 11.6 lb/MMacf (0.186 g/Nm3). While the 2.5% FEO condition (higher UBC) resulted in significantly more removal of vapor-phase mercury on the particulate than the 4.5% FEO condition (lower UBC) at both the ESP inlet and exit, it is interesting to note that UBC (as influenced by FEO) had little effect on the elemental mercury concentration at the ESP inlet. This may suggest that the carbon type was reactive enough that changes in the amount of carbon, beyond a certain level, had little effect on mercury oxidation in the dispersed flue gas, for the short time (1 or 2 s) before the flue gas reached the ESP. Based on the mechanisms previously proposed [1], only a small amount of carbon is needed to effectively oxidize mercury, while mercury removal may be significantly enhanced by much larger amounts of carbon in the flue gas. On the other hand, the FEO concentration (i.e., UBC level) had a significant effect on the fraction of elemental mercury at the ESP outlet. Differences in transport phenomena may play a significant role in altering kinetics where interaction of vapor-phase mercury with carbon on the ESP plates occurs. Hence, the effect of the carbon on mercury speciation is expected to differ depending on whether it is dispersed in the duct upstream of the ESP or deposited on the plates of the ESP. The data in Fig. 10 indicate that for some conditions oxidized mercury was preferentially captured across the ESP and at other conditions oxidized mercury was primarily reduced to the elemental form, while at still other conditions, some oxidized mercury was reduced and some was captured. The only parameter that correlates somewhat with this is carbon content. In general, the oxidized mercury at the ESP inlet was preferentially captured across the ESP for the conditions with the highest carbon content, and the oxidized mercury was primarily reduced for the conditions with the lowest carbon content. Another point to take from the graph in Fig. 10 is that mercury removal was greatest for the coal blends containing both carbon and calcium in the ash. A least-squares multiple regression was performed on the data from these tests to yield an interpolative correlation of mercury removal as a function of carbon and calcium concentration in the flue gas. The mercury removal data for both the ESP inlet and ESP exit were referenced to the carbon and calcium-oxide concentrations at the ESP inlet, because the levels of these constituents at the inlet determine mercury removal in the ESP. Further, virtually all the carbon and calcium oxide were captured in the ESP and, therefore, not present in the outlet flue gas. Mercury removal across the ESP is a function of both the speciation of mercury entering the ESP and the ash composition on the ESP plates. The latter, in turn, is directly dependent on the composition of the ash entering the ESP. These correlations are response-surface presentations and mapping of actual data. These are not theoretical predictions or extrapolations, but rather representations of actual data. Eq. (2) is an empirical quadratic model, derived from response-surface concepts, which was used to record and describe the data correlation of mercury removal with CaO and C concentrations in the flue gas: (2) Hg Removal (%)= b 0 + b 1 C C + b 2 C CaO + b 3 C C 2 + b 4 C CaO 2 + b 5 C C C CaO where C C and C CaO represent the concentration of C and CaO respectively in the flue gas in lb/MMacf. Table 4 contains the constants determined by the least-squares regression of the data taken at the inlet and outlet of the ESP during the West Elk/PRB blend test, as well as at the ESP exit from the eastern bituminous/PRB blend investigations. As shown in Table 4, the R 2 values of the correlations indicate relatively effective fits to the data. A comparison of the partial derivatives (or sensitivity index, [11]) of the correlation for mercury removal (Eq. (2)) with respect to each concentration, indicates which concentration change will have the largest effect on mercury removal. For the entire range of data from which the constants in Table 4 were derived, the sensitivity index obtained for carbon concentration was greater than that for the CaO concentration on an absolute basis, indicating that for the same mass addition carbon would have a greater impact on mercury removal than calcium addition, as has been observed experimentally. However, after normalizing by the range of carbon and calcium-oxide concentrations in the flue gas, the indices were similar across the range of conditions from which the mercury removal data were obtained, indicating (as shown in subsequent contour plots) that C C and C CaO were of comparable importance to mercury removal, for the range of flue-gas concentrations investigated. Specifically, the ratio of indices at the extremes (within the range of conditions examined) showed that carbon had 3 times more impact than calcium on mercury removal at one extreme and that calcium had 2 times more impact than carbon on mercury removal at the other extreme. A comparison of calculated and measured data, presented in Fig. 11 , indicates reasonable agreement. The quadratic model provides a good interpolation of data within the bounds of the data range used to obtain the bi constants. However, predictive extrapolation with the quadratic model is not recommended. Nevertheless, within the constraints on this tool, the correlation has been used to develop mercury removal contour plots as a function of carbon and calcium in the flue gas. These contour plots provide a simple illustration of the data obtained describing the relationship between mercury removal and carbon and calcium in the flue gas that is easy to see and interpret. The scale of the axes and the limitation on the Hg-removal lines shown are based on the range of conditions tested and the resulting Hg-removal data obtained. Figs. 12–14 respectively contain Hg-removal contour plots for (1) the ESP exit location data obtained from the eastern bituminous tests, (2) the ESP exit location data from the West Elk blend test, and (3) the ESP inlet location data from the West Elk blend test. The C and CaO concentrations are plotted as lb/MMacf and g/Nm3 to show the results in a generic metric that might allow extrapolation to applications elsewhere, independent of total ash loading, recognizing that pilot combustors inherently have lower particulate concentrations in their back pass than full-scale boilers. As shown, a given mercury removal percentage may be obtained either with a lot of carbon and a little calcium or with a lot of calcium and less carbon. Fig. 13 contains the broadest range of information for the ESP exit (of the two ESP exit graphs, i.e., Figs. 12 and 13). Fig. 13 indicates a slight increase in distance between contour lines, as mercury removal increases. This phenomenon is expected to exponentially increase as higher and higher mercury removal percentages are sought, because the nature of reaction kinetics are such that the reaction rate slows as the concentration of vapor to be scavenged (i.e., Hg vapor) decreases. Another important item illustrated by the contour plots shown in Figs. 12–14 is the slope of the contour lines as they approach the axes of the graphs. For lower mercury removal targets, the slope of the lines approaching the CaO-concentration axis level out parallel to the axis, indicating a minimum amount of carbon necessary to remove the mercury, regardless of the amount of calcium present. The contours in Figs. 13 and 14 also suggest that large amounts of carbon might be needed to remove high percentages of mercury from the flue gas, unless some significant quantity of calcium was present. Treated carbons address this problem in a somewhat different way, specifically by adding stronger sorption sites through bromine impregnation. It appears from Fig. 14 (ESP inlet location) that mercury removal by the carbon in the ash upstream of an ESP or baghouse is even more susceptible to diminishing returns for carbon concentration as the desired mercury removal increases. The contour plots described above were presented without using chlorine concentration as a parameter. In the range of chlorine concentrations generated by the coals used in the investigation (e.g., 2 to 50 ppmv), the mercury oxidation and removal was fairly insensitive to differences in chlorine (i.e., HCl) concentration changes [7]. However, it is known that chlorine concentration does have some effect on mercury adsorption [2,4,7,12]. Therefore, mercury removal across an ESP at a particular power plant may yield slightly different mercury removals than predicted by the contour plots, especially if the chlorine concentration in the flue gas is quite high (i.e., 300–400 ppmv of HCl). Fig. 15 contains a SEM micrograph of isokinetically-sampled ash and sorbent, taken while firing PRB coal and injecting activated carbon. Significant mercury removal was obtained upstream of the ESP during this test condition. Significantly more mercury removal was observed during this test condition that fired PRB coal (with high-calcium ash) than for a condition that fired bituminous coal (low-calcium ash), with the same activated-carbon injection rate. The angular and typically larger particles are the carbon particles. Some of the calcium material appears to be adhering to the surface of the carbon particles. Yet in terms of the total carbon and calcium surface area, there is only a small amount of contact between the high-calcium ash and carbon surfaces. In spite of the relative lack of connectedness, the condition from which this sample was taken yielded significant enhancement of mercury capture by the presence of calcium and carbon. Therefore, the non-connectedness of carbon and calcium apparent in the ash suggests that intimate contact between carbon and calcium is not necessary for mercury removal enhancement by calcium in coal-fired flue gas (assuming the flue gas contains the necessary proportions of both materials — about 3 to 10 times as much calcium as carbon). Chlorine in coal is released in the furnace and is almost entirely transformed into HCl by the time the flue gas reaches the back pass of utility boiler systems, where thermochemical driving forces allow the alteration of the elemental mercury vapor within the flue gas [1,5,7]. Activation-energy barriers prevent HCl from reacting directly with mercury in the gas phase to form oxidized mercury, under typical flue-gas conditions. Eqs. (3)–(7) describe how unburned carbon (UBC) can effectively catalyze mercury oxidation by reacting with HCl to form chlorinated-carbon sites, which then react with mercury on the surface of the carbon to form adsorbed HgCl, which finally desorbs as oxidized mercury. The oxidized mercury may in turn re-adsorb onto open UBC sites. Eq. (3) describes the formation of chlorinated-carbon sites. (3) HCl+C⇔Cl.C+H Eq. (4) allows for desorption of chlorine, thus providing some control of chlorine accumulation on the carbon surface. (4) 2Cl.C⇔Cl2 +C Eq. (5) describes the capture of elemental vapor-phase mercury by reaction with chlorinated-carbon sites located on the surface of ash particles. (5) Cl.C+Hg0 ⇔HgCl.C Eq. (6) allows for the desorption of oxidized mercury. (6) HgCl.C⇔HgCl+C Various homogeneous pathways can convert HgCl to HgCl2, such as reaction of HgCl with Cl− or Cl2. The conversion is assumed to be very fast, and thermochemical equilibrium calculations predict nearly all mercury to be in the HgCl2 form in flue gas downstream of the airheater [1,7]. Eq. (7) allows for the re-adsorption of oxidized mercury onto open carbon sites. (7) HgCl2 +C⇔HgCl.C+Cl− This mercury adsorption and desorption pathway provides a basis for Ca-enhancement mercury capture, or calcium/carbon synergism, as discussed in this paper. It is known [13] that the desorption rate of HgCl from carbon at a given temperature decreases with increasing available carbon sites, as the desorption rate depends primarily on the HgCl concentration deposited on the carbon surface and the concentration of vacant carbon sites per unit mass of carbon. Furthermore, for a given temperature, the adsorption rates of Hg0 and HgCl2 increase with an increase in the number of sites available for sorption — i.e., the UBC sites for activation by HCl and adsorption of HgCl2 (i.e., through the mechanisms described in Eqs. (3) and (7), respectively) and the Cl·C sites (see Eq. (5)) for adsorption of Hg0. Hence, the speciation and removal of mercury by particulate, produced by a given coal and firing condition, depend on the amount (and surface activity) of the carbon (in the flue gas) available to adsorb mercury, keep it adsorbed, and to re-adsorb oxidized mercury. With sufficient carbon (i.e., high enough C/Hg ratio), the overall adsorption rate of mercury will be overwhelmingly greater than the overall desorption rate, in which case significant mercury removal will be observed. On the other hand, if sufficient carbon (activated carbon or UBC) is present to obtain significant mercury adsorption on chlorinated-carbon sites, but not enough carbon is available to slow the desorption of HgCl and to allow significant re-adsorption of HgCl2, then extensive mercury oxidation may be observed without significant mercury removal. This mechanism is consistent with bench-, pilot-, and full-scale Hg-adsorption and speciation data, which have shown a direct relationship between mercury adsorption and carbon concentration [1,7,12]. At low carbon concentrations, the rate of Hg-adsorption is of the same magnitude as the rate of mercury desorption. As the carbon concentration increases, assuming at least some chlorine in the flue gas, mercury adsorption begins to dominate over mercury desorption, until most of the mercury is observed to be removed from the flue gas. The mechanism is observed even more clearly for mercury speciation. At very low concentrations of carbon in the flue gas (i.e., PRB-fired power plants with high carbon burnout), the mercury will neither be oxidized nor adsorbed. For slightly higher concentrations of UBC (i.e., up to about 4.0% in the ash) in the absence of much calcium, extensive mercury oxidation will be observed (see Figs. 8, 9 and 11), and for still higher concentrations of UBC, mercury removal will dominate even without much calcium present (as observed at power plants with high levels of UBC in the ash). As suggested by the data shown above, there is a synergistic relationship between UBC and calcium (inherent in the flyash or added through sorbents) that enhances mercury capture by flyash and sorbent across baghouses and electrostatic precipitators. The mechanisms described in this paper were observed in low-Sulfur flue gas, where SO3 (or H2SO4) were not a factor. Under such low-S conditions, perhaps calcium enhances mercury capture by preventing the desorption of oxidized mercury after it has adsorbed onto chlorinated-carbon sites, when only moderate amounts of carbon are present. Another possibility would be for calcium to enhance mercury capture by reacting with the intermediate species desorbing off of carbon (i.e., HgCl) before it was homogeneously oxidized to form HgCl2, as described in Eq. (8). (8) HgCl+Ca-Site⇔HgCl.Ca-site In addition to the mechanisms suggested above, calcium compounds in the ash can reduce the concentration of H2SO4 that may affect the ability of carbon to capture mercury. However, this mechanism was found to be absent, for the low-sulfur flue gases examined in this research. A combined EPRI/DOE project was conducted in Southern Research Institute's Combustion Research Facility with the goal of improving understanding of the mechanisms causing mercury to be in the oxidized form and to be removed by fly ash. The following conclusions were drawn. • Increased UBC in ash appears to be the primary component responsible for enhancement of mercury oxidation and capture when blending bituminous coal with PRB coal. • Calcium and UBC synergistically enhance mercury capture. The UBC provides chlorinated-carbon sites to scavenge Hg0 from the vapor phase, and, subsequently, sorption sites for HgCl2. During Hg0 adsorption and oxidation, calcium (if present in sufficient quantity) may shift the overall fraction of vapor-phase oxidized mercury to adsorbed HgCl. For moderate concentrations of UBC in the flue gas, and in the absence of much calcium, elemental mercury adsorbs onto chlorinated-carbon sites and desorbs as oxidized mercury, resulting in high levels of oxidized mercury without a lot of mercury capture. For large concentrations of UBC, mercury adsorption may be independent of the calcium concentration. • During coal blend tests with PRB and western bituminous coals (where calcium from the PRB ash was shown to have enhanced mercury capture by the UBC from the bituminous coal), as much as 85% of the mercury in the flue gas was removed by the fly ash (i.e., at the ESP outlet) with a UBC level equivalent to 11.6 lb/MMacf (0.186 g/Nm3) of carbon in the flue gas (no carbon injection). • While calcium has been shown to significantly enhance mercury capture on carbon in the flue gas, the optimum ratio of carbon to calcium appears to be influenced by the type of carbon in the flue gas — i.e., by the number of mercury-active sites available in the carbon, which is partly related to the total internal surface area of the carbon. • The carbon produced from West Elk (western bituminous) coal was more active for mercury removal and oxidation than that produced from eastern bituminous coals, such as Choctaw America and Blacksville. • Contour plots were developed that represented some of the data obtained from this investigation. These plots may be used to suggest coal-blending or sorbent-injection approaches for achieving a desired mercury removal across an ESP, although plants containing significantly different flue-gas chlorine concentrations than those under which the tests were conducted (i.e., 2 to 50 ppmv of HCl), might obtain deviations from the data presented in the contour plots. These plots are based on a least-squares multiple regression analysis that produced constants for a quadratic model which fit the data; the resulting model describes these data with R 2 values of 0.74 to 0.89 and is applicable primarily within the range of these data. Plants containing significant concentrations of SO3 (i.e., 10 ppmv or greater) might also benefit from higher calcium concentrations than shown in the contour plots for removal of this pollutant (and carbon poison). Increased UBC in ash appears to be the primary component responsible for enhancement of mercury oxidation and capture when blending bituminous coal with PRB coal. Calcium and UBC synergistically enhance mercury capture. The UBC provides chlorinated-carbon sites to scavenge Hg0 from the vapor phase, and, subsequently, sorption sites for HgCl2. During Hg0 adsorption and oxidation, calcium (if present in sufficient quantity) may shift the overall fraction of vapor-phase oxidized mercury to adsorbed HgCl. For moderate concentrations of UBC in the flue gas, and in the absence of much calcium, elemental mercury adsorbs onto chlorinated-carbon sites and desorbs as oxidized mercury, resulting in high levels of oxidized mercury without a lot of mercury capture. For large concentrations of UBC, mercury adsorption may be independent of the calcium concentration. During coal blend tests with PRB and western bituminous coals (where calcium from the PRB ash was shown to have enhanced mercury capture by the UBC from the bituminous coal), as much as 85% of the mercury in the flue gas was removed by the fly ash (i.e., at the ESP outlet) with a UBC level equivalent to 11.6 lb/MMacf (0.186 g/Nm3) of carbon in the flue gas (no carbon injection). While calcium has been shown to significantly enhance mercury capture on carbon in the flue gas, the optimum ratio of carbon to calcium appears to be influenced by the type of carbon in the flue gas — i.e., by the number of mercury-active sites available in the carbon, which is partly related to the total internal surface area of the carbon. The carbon produced from West Elk (western bituminous) coal was more active for mercury removal and oxidation than that produced from eastern bituminous coals, such as Choctaw America and Blacksville. Contour plots were developed that represented some of the data obtained from this investigation. These plots may be used to suggest coal-blending or sorbent-injection approaches for achieving a desired mercury removal across an ESP, although plants containing significantly different flue-gas chlorine concentrations than those under which the tests were conducted (i.e., 2 to 50 ppmv of HCl), might obtain deviations from the data presented in the contour plots. These plots are based on a least-squares multiple regression analysis that produced constants for a quadratic model which fit the data; the resulting model describes these data with R 2 values of 0.74 to 0.89 and is applicable primarily within the range of these data. Plants containing significant concentrations of SO3 (i.e., 10 ppmv or greater) might also benefit from higher calcium concentrations than shown in the contour plots for removal of this pollutant (and carbon poison). The authors would like to sincerely thank the United States Department of Energy for funding this work through the following cooperative agreements: DE-PS26-02NT41183 and DE-FC26-04NT41900. We also acknowledge the primary funding support of EPRI and its members. We would like to specifically acknowledge the support of Southern Company, through the use of their Combustion Research Facility at Southern Research Institute. In addition, we would like to thank Southern Company Services, MidAmerican Energy Holdings Company, Dynegy Midwest Generation, Arkansas Electric Cooperative, American Electric Power, and Tennessee Valley Authority for their financial contributions to this work.
abstract: This paper summarizes the results of a significant research and development investment to understand the fundamental mechanisms governing the speciation of mercury in coal-fired power systems. An extensive experimental search was conducted through all the parameter variations characterizing the majority of full-scale U.S. coal-fired power plants, in an effort to quantify the impact each parameter variation had on mercury oxidation and removal from the gas phase. As a result of this extensive investigation, the mechanisms responsible for mercury speciation differences observed for different coal types, power plants, and pollution control devices were elucidated. Specifically, unburned carbon was found to catalytically enhance mercury oxidation, while the actual differences in chlorine concentration from plant to plant were found to be of secondary importance to carbon. In addition, synergistic enhancement of mercury capture by carbon and calcium in flyash was quantitatively described for various coal-fired configurations, such as coal blending and sorbent injection, including development of contour plots of mercury removal at an ESP inlet and exit as a function of carbon and calcium concentration at the ESP inlet.
title: Mechanisms governing the fate of mercury in coal-fired power systems

reaction_catalog.transformation: Hg0 to HgCl2 and removal from flue gas

metrics.notes: removed by fly ash at ESP outlet

full_text: The simplest of molecules, hydrogen, is in principle, the lightest and the cleanest of all fuels because its combustion results in essentially zero emissions and therefore it is expected to play a major part in the energy supply of the future. Hydrogen can be used in internal combustion engines or more favourably in proton-exchange-membrane fuel cells (PEMFC) to generate power so that the exhaust emission is simply water vapour [1,2]. Regardless of the source of hydrogen, a complete switch to hydrogen fuel cell vehicles in the transportation sector would likely lead to a significant improvement in health, air quality and climate. The benefits are derived from a complete elimination of common vehicle exhaust. This does not, however, address the effect of greenhouse gases when hydrogen is derived from fossil fuels [3]. From an environmental perspective, renewable sources such as solar power and biomass are required as conventional methods of producing hydrogen from fossil fuels invariably produce carbon dioxide (green house gas) [4]. As raw fossil feeds are diminishing resources, chemical and energy-related industries are examining alternative renewable feedstocks. The conversion of renewable materials to hydrogen, which can be used for fuel cells, assists in the utilization of renewable energy sources, while conversion to commodity chemicals facilitates the replacement of petroleum by renewable resources. For a long period, it has been proposed to use renewable resources for the production of transportation fuels, especially hydrogen [5–9]. Glycerol, which is the by-product of biodiesel production by transesterification of vegetable oils (triglycerides) and methanol, is a potentially important hydrogen source [10–13]. The production of 10 kg of biodiesel yields approximately 1 kg of crude glycerol. Glycerol is non-toxic, non-volatile, and has high energy density [14]. Furthermore, this biomass-derived glycerol is considered to be renewable and CO2 neutral. Although the global production of biodiesel is still limited, the market price of glycerol has dropped rapidly. If the production of biodiesel increases as predicted, the supply of glycerol will be in excess of demand [15]. Hence, exploitation of such a renewable material for energy production is gathering strong interest. Several studies have been carried out on glycerol steam reforming for hydrogen production. Various catalysts were investigated for glycerol steam reforming, including noble metal–based [16–20] and nickel-based catalysts [21–25]. Noble metal catalysts are well known for their high catalytic activities, whereas nickel-based catalysts have been more extensively studied in reforming reactions because of their high capacities in cleavage of C–C bonds and high selectivity towards hydrogen production combined with a relatively low cost [26]. Several thermodynamic analyses of glycerol reforming were also performed to complement the properties of this topic [27–33]. One of the difficulties, however, associated with the utilization of the synthesis gas produced by steam reforming for energy production is the high CO2 and CO contents. For all fuel cell systems especially, large CO2 content of the fuel gas greatly drops the efficiency of the system, whereas CO has very strong poisoning effects on the catalyst of PEMFC [34]. For example, in terms of fuel cell applications, a CO concentration of less than 10ppm is required for low temperature proton exchange membranes and alkaline fuel cells [35]. Furthermore, the cost of separating H2 from a H2-rich gas with impurities incurs major cost penalties [36,37]. In order to enhance the H2 concentration in the product gas, a CO2 sorbent, such as calcium oxide (CaO), can be used in situ to capture CO2 immediately when it is produced. The removal of the CO2 from the reaction system alters the equilibrium composition of the product gas and promotes the production of a H2-rich gas. Besides the capacity of removing CO2 to a very low concentration, CaO may be derived from a range of abundant and inexpensive precursors, which makes it attractive from an economic perspective. Additionally, heat generated from the exothermic gas-solid absorption reaction is available to drive the endothermic reforming reactions [38,39]. With regards to in situ removal of CO2 in glycerol steam reforming, only Dupont and co-workers have investigated a commercial Ni-based catalyst and a calcined dolomite sorbent in sorption-enhanced steam reforming [40]. Their experimental results showed that the effect of temperature in 673–973K on the products selectivity is strong, with a H2 selectivity that increases with increasing temperature. It is demonstrated that 773K was an optimum temperature with the longest CO2 breakthrough time and the highest H2 purity of 97%. Dupont et al. also reported a thermodynamic analysis of adsorption-enhanced steam reforming of glycerol and concluded that the most favourable temperature for steam–glycerol reforming is between 800 and 850K in the presence of a CO2 adsorbent, which is about 100K lower than that for reforming without CO2 adsorption [41]. In their study, they investigated the effect of in situ removal of CO2 on equilibrium compositions but other effects from CO2 sorbents, sorption equilibrium and kinetics were not considered. As introduced above, heat generated from the exothermic gas–solid absorption reaction can drive the endothermic reforming reactions, which can alter the reaction equilibrium too [38,39]. Based on the method used in Dupont's study, they have not taken this heat effect into account. Nevertheless, this can be solved by using equilibrium reactor module of commercial software and indicating the CO2 sorbent, e.g. CaO. A search through the open literature has failed to unearth any study using CaO as a CO2 sorbent in hydrogen production by glycerol steam reforming. The objective of this work is to study the impact of CaO as a CO2 sorbent on glycerol steam reforming in terms of both thermodynamic analysis and experimental work. In this article we first study product compositions at equilibrium employing the total Gibbs free energy minimization method. Next, hydrogen purities under experiments are investigated over a Ni/ZrO2 catalyst. The kinetics and/or catalysts will play a very important role in the product compositions; therefore finally we present for the first time the comparison of experimental results and thermodynamic reaction equilibrium. The method of thermodynamic analysis by minimization of Gibbs free energy was introduced in our previous publications [31–33] and was also introduced in detail by other groups [41–43]. At low pressure and high temperature, the system can be considered as ideal [44,45]. The total Gibbs function for a system is given as follows: (1) G t = ∑ i = 1 N n i G i _ = ∑ i = 1 N n i μ i _ = ∑ n i G i 0 + R T ∑ n i  ln f i ^ f i 0 For reaction equilibria in gas phase, f i ^ = ϕ i ^ y i P , f i 0 = P 0, and since G i 0 is set equal to zero for each chemical element in its standard state, ΔG 0 = ΔG f i 0 for each component is assumed. The minimum Gibbs free energy of each gaseous species and that of the total system can be expressed as Eqs. (2) and (3), with the Lagrange's undetermined multiplier method. (2) Δ G f i 0 + R T ln ϕ i ^ y i p p 0 + ∑ k λ k a ik = 0 (3) ∑ i = 1 N n i ( Δ G f i 0 + R T ln ϕ i ^ y i p p 0 + ∑ k λ k a ik ) = 0 With the constraints of elemental balances: (4) ∑ i = 1 N n i a ik = A k When a solid is considered in the system, Eq. (5) is used in the calculations. (5) n s Δ G fs 0 = 0 In almost all the thermodynamic analyses of glycerol steam reforming, the authors only considered primary species such as hydrogen, carbon monoxide, carbon dioxide, methane, water, glycerol and solid carbon [27–33]. According to the literature, however, methanol, ethanol, acetaldehyde, acetone and ethene were also detected in the experiments [22,46,47]. Therefore, all the different species mentioned above have been taken into account for thermodynamic calculations in this work. Calcium oxide and calcium carbonate were also involved when calcium oxide is used as a CO2 sorbent. The thermodynamic equilibrium calculations were accomplished with the use of Outokumpu HSC Chemistry 4.0, a chemical reaction and equilibrium software, using the extensive thermochemical database delivered in the software package [48]. Gibbs module of this software is usually used to directly calculate product compositions at equilibrium employing the total Gibbs free energy minimization method. The initial amount of glycerol is assumed to be 1mol with/without 3mol of CaO in the system. Thermodynamic analysis was performed between 700 and 1100K at atmospheric pressure with a water to glycerol ratio (WGR) of 3, 6 and 9, respectively. Hydroxide precursors of ZrO2 were prepared from co-precipitation of ZrOCl2·8H2O (Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China) and ammonia solution (Tianjin Jiangtian Chemical Technology Co., Ltd., China). The formed white precipitate was washed thoroughly with deionized water until it was free of chlorine and exchanged with ethanol several times. The precipitate was then filtered, dried at 393K and calcined at 923K in a N2 flow. The active phases were impregnated by the wet impregnation method with a given amount of nitrate aqueous metal solution on zirconia supports. Nitrate precursor salts were Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (Tianjin Yingda Sparseness & Noble Reagent Chemical Factory, China). The impregnated catalyst samples were dried overnight at 393K and then calcined at 923K. Metal loading of the catalyst was set as 15% in weight for Ni. Catalytic performance tests were conducted in a quartz fixed-bed reactor, in which 0.2ml of catalyst with/without 0.4ml of CaO was diluted with inert quartz particles. Prior to the catalytic reaction, all catalysts were reduced in a H2 flow (10% in N2) at 823K for 1 hour. The carrier gas N2 was kept constant at 50ml/min by mass flow controller. All experiments were run with WGRs of 6 and 9 under atmospheric pressure with a GHSV of 10,200h−1. The active tests without the presence of CaO were carried out at 923K, whereas, in order to investigate the influence of CaO addition, the experiments with CaO were performed at 850K. The reactor effluent was passed through a condenser, in which the condensable products were separated from the non-condensable products. Non-condensed gases were analyzed on-line by a gas chromatograph (BeiFen, SP2100), equipped with a TCD detector, TDX-01 packed column. In order to calculate the glycerol conversion, condensable products were separated with a capillary column (INNOWAX) and analyzed off-line with a gas chromatograph (Agilent, 4890), equipped with a FID detector. Irrespective of in situ CO2 adsorption, complete conversion of glycerol was obtained for the range of temperature and WGR considered in both thermodynamic and experimental parts of this study (not shown). With regards to the various species which may be generated in the reactions, as a matter of fact, methanol, ethanol, acetaldehyde, acetone and ethene do not exist in equilibrium according to the equilibrium calculations. With respects to carbon formation from a thermodynamic perspective, no carbon was formed with WGRs of 6 and 9 regardless of the temperature in the case of glycerol steam reforming without CaO. Only a small amount of carbon (< 0.2mol) was produced under 825K at WGR=3 [31]. With the presence of CaO, carbon formation was further inhibited as thermodynamically no carbon was formed over the entire range of temperature and WGR considered here. The reactions of glycerol steam reforming can be expressed as follows. (6) C 3 H 8 O 3 ↔ 4 H 2 + 3CO  Δ H 298 K = 251.2 kJ/mol (7) CO+H 2 O ↔ H 2 + CO 2 Δ H 298 K = − 41.2 kJ/mol (8) CO+3H 2 ↔ CH 4 + H 2 O  Δ H 298 K = − 206.1 kJ/mol (9) CO 2 + 4H 2 ↔ CH 4 + 2H 2 O  Δ H 298 K = − 164.9 kJ/mol (10) CO 2 + CH 4 ↔ 2 H 2 + 2CO  Δ H 298 K = 247.3 kJ/mol (11) H 2 + CO ↔ C+H 2 O  Δ H 298 K = − 131.3 kJ/mol (12) CH 4 ↔ 2 H 2 + C  Δ H 298 K = 74.9 kJ/mol (13) 2 CO ↔ CO 2 + C  Δ H 298 K = − 172.4 kJ/mol Fig. 1 depicts dry product composition and moles of hydrogen at different temperatures and WGRs at equilibrium. The temperature affects the equilibrium concentrations of the products significantly. The number of moles of hydrogen increases with an increasing temperature when WGR is 3. For WGRs of 6 and 9, hydrogen production increases as temperature increases, before reaching a maximum at around 925K, where almost no methane is produced, and then begins to decrease at higher temperatures. The greatest quantity of hydrogen (6 mol) is produced at 925K with a WGR of 9. Moles of hydrogen increase steadily with increasing WGR. Although high WGR enhances the reforming performance significantly, it is believed that higher WGRs require higher reactor volume because of higher steam volumetric flow, and consume higher input heat duty because of higher vaporization energy. With regards to the dry product compositions, hydrogen and CO concentrations increase with increasing temperature, whereas the concentrations of CO2 and CH4 decrease with an increase in temperature. For all the WGRs (except 3) considered in this study, the concentrations of hydrogen reach a maximum and tend to be stable above 850K. However, the highest hydrogen concentration of the product of glycerol steam reforming is only 67% (achieved at 925K and WGR of 9), which is far from the basic requirement of hydrogen purity for PEMFC. The distribution of carbon compound at thermodynamic equilibrium was also studied. CO2 is the predominant product (over 55%) that includes carbon at all temperatures with a WGR of 9 as shown in Fig. 2 . CH4 production is favourable at low temperatures while high temperature promotes CO generation. The adsorption of CO2 in situ is of great significance as a maximum of 73% of CO2 is achieved. The total conversion of glycerol was obtained from the experiments (not shown). The complete decomposition of glycerol is also consistent with the prediction from thermodynamics. However, high glycerol conversion may or may not be caused by an active catalyst in glycerol steam reforming as thermal decomposition of glycerol may achieve 98% conversion above 533K under N2 flow [49]. Fig. 3 shows the comparison between experimental results and thermodynamic equilibrium. The product compositions are close to the thermodynamic equilibrium values over the investigated reaction conditions as indicated in Fig. 3. The concentrations of H2 and CO2 with a WGR of 9 are, however, 64% and 20%, respectively, which are lower than the thermodynamic prediction. In addition, 13% of CO and 3% of CH4 are higher than equilibrium. Fernando et al. also reported that experimental results over a Ni/MgO catalyst were far from thermodynamic equilibrium [21]. The difference between the experimental results and thermodynamic analysis is primarily due to the influence of catalyst. With highly selective catalysts, the product composition can be quite similar to the thermodynamic equilibrium. Navarro et al. compared the performances of nickel catalysts supported on Al2O3 modified by Mg, Zr, Ce or La. The results showed that the product distribution for the steam reforming of glycerol was strongly dependent on the catalysts. The Ni/Al2O3 (modified by Zr) sample was the most selective catalyst with a gas composition after steam reforming similar to the values predicted by equilibrium [23]. Rihko-Struckmann et al. have shown that with a La0.3Ce0.7NiO3 catalyst, the hydrogen yield was close to the thermodynamic equilibrium yield over the whole investigated temperature range (773–973K) [25]. As expected, a decrease in WGR can weaken steam reforming of glycerol since the proportion of H2 and CO2 decrease when WGR declines from 9 to 6. Moreover, thermodynamic equilibrium could still not be achieved with this catalyst since the concentrations of CH4 and CO are higher than that at equilibrium. It may be attributed to the following reasons. Firstly, decomposition of glycerol to CH4 is highly favourable during the reforming process [18,50]. Secondly, in the reaction equilibrium of the water gas shift reaction, Eq. (7), the conversion of CO is depressed at high temperatures [21,25]. The last reason is that the Ni/ZrO2 catalyst used in this study may not be active enough to convert CH4 and CO to H2 and CO2 effectively under selected conditions. Disregarding these issues, hydrogen purity from glycerol steam reforming without the presence of CaO is still significantly far from the standard for PEMFC. When glycerol steam reforming is coupled with in situ CO2 capture using CaO, the following reaction takes place. One of the advantages of conducting glycerol steam reforming with the presence of CaO is that the heat generated by Eq. (14) can drive the reforming reactions, which makes an energy-efficient route for high-purity hydrogen production. With the presence of CaO, glycerol conversions were also 100% in both thermodynamic analysis and experiments, which is similar to the results without the use of CO2 adsorption. (14) CaO+CO 2 ↔ CaCO 3 Δ H 298 K = − 178.8 kJ/mol Fig. 4 illustrates dry product composition and moles of hydrogen at different temperatures and WGRs with CaO present. This is consistent with the trends in glycerol steam reforming without CaO present, as temperature has a significant effect on the equilibrium concentrations of the products. Throughout the range of WGRs considered in this study, moles of hydrogen produced per mole of glycerol with CaO present increase slightly with increasing temperatures and then decrease with an increase in temperature. It is apparent that high WGRs are more favourable for hydrogen production. More than 6 mol of hydrogen can be produced with WGRs of 6 and 9 below 1000K. In terms of the dry product composition, the addition of CaO in the system can increase hydrogen purity dramatically. As shown from Fig. 4, hydrogen purities are over 95% below 925K for WGRs of 6 and 9, and furthermore, almost no CO2 or CO is produced with the only impurity being CH4. It is noteworthy that incorporating CaO to the steam reforming substantially diminishes the CO concentration. Hydrogen concentration, however, declines rapidly between 900K and 1025K. It can be attributed to two main reasons, firstly due to the deactivation of the CaO sorbent. This occurs due to the higher reaction temperatures favouring the endothermic decomposition of CaCO3, according to Eq. (14) [38,39]. The second reason is due to the high reaction temperature suppressing the proceeding of water gas shift reaction, Eq. (7), which can be supported by the increased amount of CO and H2O (not shown) when the temperature is above 900K. When the temperature is above 1025K, CaCO3 cannot be formed at all. The equilibrium gas compositions are equivalent to those predicted when CaO is not present (as shown in Fig. 1). High WGRs can increase both moles and concentrations of hydrogen as shown in Fig. 4. Comas et al. have reported a thermodynamic analysis of ethanol steam reforming using CaO as a CO2 sorbent [51]. Our results are in noticeably good agreement with theirs in terms of the trend of hydrogen production and the behaviour of CaO in the system. Interested readers may find that our present results above 900K are different from the existing report by Chen et al. [41]. In their study, the trend of numbers of hydrogen produced does not change above 900K. This is because their calculation was based on the ideal assumption that a certain amount of CO2 can be adsorbed (or removed) completely from the system (no practical sorbent was indicated). However, we have used CaO as a sorbent and considered the fact that its capacity of CO2 adsorption decreases when temperature is higher than 900K. Fig. 5 describes carbon compound distribution at thermodynamic equilibrium with the presence of CaO as a function of temperature with a WGR of 9. Almost all the carbon element exists in CaCO3 when temperatures are below 900K. After the sorbent deactivates completely, however, CO and CO2 are main products besides hydrogen with the latter one predominant. Fig. 6 shows the difference between the experimental results and the results from thermodynamic analysis. For WGR of 6, the hydrogen concentration can reach 87% together with 9% CH4, 3% CO and only 1% CO2, which are quite different from the thermodynamic prediction. In the case of WGR of 9, 95% of hydrogen can be obtained in the experiments with only 5% CH4 as impurity. The concentration of CH4 is higher than the thermodynamic value again indicating the catalyst may not be active enough for the conversion of excess CH4 to hydrogen. Although the experimental results of hydrogen purity are still far from thermodynamic equilibrium, it has been shown that the addition of CaO as a CO2 sorbent can obviously enhance hydrogen purity significantly. No CO2 or CO was detected in our experimental tests which may be owing to the trace amount produced. Finally, it must be noted that when using the CO2-sorbent and due to the fixed amount of sorbent in an experimental run, the CO2 concentration in the product gas rises rapidly once the sorbent reaches a certain capacity [40]. Thermodynamic analysis and experimental tests of glycerol steam reforming with/without CaO as a CO2 sorbent were performed and compared in this work. Methanol, ethanol, acetaldehyde, acetone and ethylene do not exist in equilibrium conditions according to the equilibrium calculations. In the absence of CO2 removal, thermodynamic predictions show that a maximum hydrogen concentration of 67% can be obtained at 925K with a water to glycerol ratio of 9. In the experiments, the Ni/ZrO2 catalyst fails to catalyze the reactions to thermodynamic equilibrium under the selected conditions as the highest hydrogen concentration obtained is 64%. With in situ CO2 removal, thermodynamic analysis shows that hydrogen purity exceeding 95% can be achieved between 700 and 925K with WGRs of both 6 and 9. Additionally, CaCO3 cannot be formed above 1025K, and then the function of CaO as a CO2 sorbent disappears entirely. Although our catalyst is not active enough to reach the thermodynamic equilibrium again with the presence of CaO, a hydrogen purity of 95% with only 5% CH4 in the dry gas composition can be reached at 850K with a WGR of 9 in the experimental tests. Experimental results over Ni/ZrO2 catalyst are still far from thermodynamic equilibrium, which may be because the Ni/ZrO2 catalyst is not active enough to convert excess CH4 to hydrogen. This may leave room for development of effective and efficient catalysts for hydrogen production by glycerol steam reforming. The addition of CaO to the system shows promising results by greatly enhancing the hydrogen production while reducing the CO concentration. There is, therefore, room for investigations of hydrogen production from glycerol steam reforming with in situ CO2 removal. a ik number of atoms of the kth element present in each molecule of species i A k total mass of kth element in the feed f i ^ the fugacity of species i in system f i 0 the standard-state fugacity of species i G i _ partial molar Gibbs free energy of species i G t total Gibbs free energy G i 0 standard Gibbs free energy of species i ΔG f i 0 standard Gibbs function of formation of species i ΔG fs 0 standard Gibbs function of formation of solid n s mole of solid N number of species in the reaction system P pressure of system P 0 standard-state pressure of 101.3 kPa R molar gas constant T temperature of system y i mole fraction of each substance in gas products ΔG Ο standard Gibbs function of molar reaction Greek symbols λ k Lagrange multiplier μ i _ chemical potential of species i ϕ i ^ fugacity coefficient of species i number of atoms of the kth element present in each molecule of species i total mass of kth element in the feed the fugacity of species i in system the standard-state fugacity of species i partial molar Gibbs free energy of species i total Gibbs free energy standard Gibbs free energy of species i standard Gibbs function of formation of species i standard Gibbs function of formation of solid mole of solid number of species in the reaction system pressure of system standard-state pressure of 101.3 kPa molar gas constant temperature of system mole fraction of each substance in gas products standard Gibbs function of molar reaction Lagrange multiplier chemical potential of species i fugacity coefficient of species i The financial support from the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University (NCET-04-0242) and the Program of Introducing Talents of Discipline to Universities (B06006) are gratefully acknowledged.
abstract: Thermodynamic analysis and experimental tests of glycerol steam reforming with/without calcium oxide (CaO) as a carbon dioxide (CO2) sorbent have been performed and compared in this work. Methanol, ethanol, acetaldehyde, acetone and ethylene do not exist in equilibrium conditions according to the equilibrium calculations. Without CaO present, thermodynamic predictions show that a maximum hydrogen concentration of 67% can be obtained at 925K, with a water to glycerol ratio (WGR) of 9. In the experiments, the Ni/ZrO2 catalyst fails to catalyze the reactions to thermodynamic equilibrium under the selected conditions as the highest hydrogen concentration obtained is 64%. With the presence of CaO, thermodynamic analysis implies hydrogen purity exceeding 95% can be achieved below 925K at WGRs of 6 and 9. However, CaCO3 does not exist at temperatures greater than 1025K. In the experiments, a hydrogen purity of 95% with only 5% CH4 as impurity can be reached at 850K with a WGR of 9. The Ni/ZrO2 catalyst is not active enough to convert excess CH4 to hydrogen in glycerol steam reforming as CH4 concentrations are usually higher than the equilibrium values. The addition of CaO to this system greatly enhances the hydrogen production while reducing the CO concentration.
title: Hydrogen production by glycerol steam reforming with/without calcium oxide sorbent: A comparative study of thermodynamic and experimental work
