full_text: Sulfur dioxide (SO2) and nitrogen oxides (NO x ), mainly emitted from fossil fuel combustion, are major air pollutants. Sulfur dioxide is generally accepted to be the most important precursor to acid rain [1]. Nitrogen oxides contribute a lot to photochemical smog, acid rain, ozone depletion and greenhouse effect [2,3]. China produces about 24 million tons SO2 and 7.7 million tons NO x per year, which leads to more than 13.3 billion dollars loss [4]. The new policy against pollution further appeals to the desulfurization and denitrification of flue gas. Therefore, desulfurization and denitrification of flue gas attracted much more attention. Many technologies have been developed to remove SO2 and NO x from flue gas. Among them, flue gas desulfurization (FGD) and selective catalytic reduction (SCR) are most effective for SO2 and NO x removal respectively. Compared to the individual control techniques, simultaneous desulfurization and denitrification is advantageous due to less equipment demanded. Simultaneous SO2 and NO x removal from stationary sources can be achieved with high efficiency using copper on alumina catalyst sorbents (CuO/Al2O3) [5]. SO2 and NO x from flue gas are simultaneously removed by a dual bed of potassium-containing coal-pellets and calcium-containing pellets [6]. Microwave has been widely used in environment protection [7]. Microwave is also applied to a pyrolytic carbon such as activated carbon and char, enhancing the reaction of sulfur dioxide (SO2) and nitrogen oxides (NO) with carbon [8]. The simultaneous treatment with the accelerated electronic beams and the microwaves can increase the removal efficiency of NO x and SO2, about 80% of NO x and more than 95% of SO2 were removed by precipitation with ammonia [9]. It was reported that the reaction efficiency of microwave reduction of NO x could be up to 98% when microwave energy was applied continuously [10]. SO2 and NO x emissions from coal combustion were simultaneously reduced by calcium magnesium acetate [11]. In this study, experimental investigations were conducted to simultaneous desulfurization and denitrification from stimulated flue gas by the novel microwave reactor with potassium permanganate and zeolite. The study evaluates the influence of microwave power, empty bed residence time (EBRT), inlet concentration, microwave and catalyst on the performance of the microwave reaction system, and the mechanism for microwave induced catalytic SO2 and NO x oxidation was elicited. An understanding of the role of microwave and catalyst on simultaneous desulfurization and denitrification can help evaluate the potential of applying this novel method as an effective SO2 and NO x emission control strategy for the flue gas cleaning. The experimental flow loop used in the study was shown schematically in Fig. 1 A constant input power of 119–280 W was used and the microwave frequency was 2450 MHz. The microwave reactor consisted of quartz tube (o.d. 25 mm and 85 mm long) with the potassium permanganate (KMnO4) and zeolite was set up to study the removal of SO2 and NO x from waste gas. The SO2 and NO x supplied from the compressed-gas steel cylinders, was first diluted with the air from a compressor, passed through air mixture bottle and flowed upwards through the microwave reactor. The flow meter and the valve were used to monitor the gas flow through the reactor. The SO2 and NO x concentrations were monitored by the analysis device of S2000 flue gas, and the rate of the gas flow was monitored by the rotameters and the mass flow controllers. In the process of the experiments, the simulated SO2 and NO x -containing flue gas were supplied to the microwave reactor, at a flow rate of 200–500 l.h−1 (empty bed residence time (EBRT), 0.214–0.536 s). The periodic measurements of the gas concentration from sampling ports in the quartz tube and the gas flow of the quartz tube in the microwave reaction system were carried out by using the following devices. S2000 flue gas device was used for the analysis of sulfur dioxide (SO2) and nitrogen oxides (NO x ). The rate of the gas flow was measured by ‘Model LZB-1’ flow meters with the units of 0.01 m3 h−1. Fig. 2 a shows the effect of concentration of SO2 in inlet on desulfurization using potassium permanganate (KMnO4) or zeolite. The “PP”, “Z” and “PPZ” profile are represented by potassium permanganate, zeolite, potassium permanganate and zeolite respectively. Fig. 2a indicates the purifying efficiency of SO2 is gradually decreased when the concentration of SO2 in inlet is increased, the effect of desulfurization using Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) only is very low. Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) addition to zeolite increased the SO2 removal efficiency about 10%. The possible reason for the shape of the curve “PPZ” could be that high humidity in stimulated flue gas increased the SO2 removal efficiency in the inlet concentration of SO2 at 1000 mg m−3, 1230 mg m−3. For comparison, the desulfurization effect using potassium permanganate and zeolite together are higher than that using potassium permanganate or the zeolite only. Zeolite could adsorptive SO2 potassium permanganate could oxidative SO2 to sulfate. The use of both zeolite and potassium permanganate would induce SO2 oxidizing catalyst reaction using zeolite as catalyst. Fig. 2b shows the effect of concentration of NO x in inlet on denitrification using potassium permanganate (KMnO4) or zeolite. Denitrification is generally in the order: (PPZ)>(PP)>(Z). It has no effect of the denitrification with zeolite only, the possible explanation for this is that the zeolite is not adsorptive NO x . Fig. 2b indicates the effect of denitrification using potassium permanganate is very low but not more than 10%. When potassium permanganate and zeolite are added to reactor simultaneously, NO x removal efficiency increases from 57% to 87%, reaching from 66.7% to 90.3%. The possible reason for this could be that the use of both zeolite and potassium permanganate would induce NO x oxidizing catalyst reaction to nitrates using zeolite as catalyst under our experimental conditions. Fig. 3 a and b shows the effect of simultaneous desulfurization and denitrification by microwave reactor with different microwave power using potassium permanganate (KMnO4) as oxidizing agent and zeolite as catalyst. The “PP–MW”, “Z–MW” and “PPZ–MW” profile are represented by potassium permanganate, zeolite, potassium permanganate and zeolite under the microwave respectively. The conversion of desulfurization purifying efficiency increases from 33.3% with 119 W microwave power to 95% with 280 W, and the conversion of denitrification increases from 85.6% with 119 W to 98.2% with 280 W, showing excellent desulfurization and denitrification effect of the microwave power with the potassium permanganate and zeolite. The reason for over 259 W microwave power the shape of the “PPZ-MW” increases could be that high temperature keeps the catalytic surface reaction of microwave desulfurization and leads to the enhancement of SO2 removal efficiency up 0.7%. The experimental results showed that the optimum microwave power on desulfurization and denitrification is supposed to be 259 W. Compare Fig. 3a with Fig. 2a, and compare Fig. 3b with Fig. 2b, in the presence of Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) and zeolite, SO2 and NO x removal efficiency, with/without microwave irradiation in flue gas treatment process, increased by 7.2% and 12.2% under the conditions of microwave power of 259 W, inlet concentration of 800 mg m−3 SO2, and inlet concentration of NO x 180 mg m−3, and more than 94% of SO2, 97% of NO x was oxidized at microwave power of 259 W. The additional use of microwave energy to Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) and zeolite is very effective in the pollutants oxidation. For comparison, the microwave desulfurization and denitrification effect using potassium permanganate and zeolite together are much higher than that using potassium permanganate or the zeolites only, which are generally in the order: ( PPZ – MW ) > ( PP – MW ) > ( Z – MW ) As are shown in Fig. 3a and b, the simultaneous presence of microwave potassium permanganate and zeolite increases from 16.5% to 43.5% the microwave removal efficiency for SO2, and the NO x removal efficiency from 85.6% to 98.2% than the microwave potassium permanganate only. The additional use of potassium permanganate to the microwave zeolite leads to the enhancement of SO2 removal efficiency up from 53.9% to 95%, and denitrification efficiency up from 85.6% to 98.2%. Based on the experiments as above, we assumed that SO2 and NO x from flue gas can react with potassium permanganate to produce SO4 2−, NO3 − when potassium permanganate and zeolite are used together under microwave, which is critical to desulfurization and denitrification. The potassium permanganate does not absorb microwave energy and hence microwave would not induce SO2 and NO x oxidation without microwave absorbent such as zeolite [12]. Zeolite does absorb microwave energy but require the oxidizing agent such as potassium permanganate. The use of both zeolite and potassium permanganate combined with microwave energy would induce SO2 and NO x oxidizing catalyst reaction significantly. Significant synergetic effects of microwave and catalyst treatment were observed. We could speculate that microwave accentuates catalyst treatment, and increases the removal efficiency of SO2 and NO x [8–13]. Zhang [1] verified on the existence of hot spots in the catalysts heated by microwave irradiation. Thus, a major mechanism for microwave-induced SO2 and NO x oxidation can be described as the microwave induced oxidizing catalyst reaction between SO2 and NO x and potassium permanganate with zeolite being the catalyst and microwave absorbent. The microwave induced catalytic oxidation reaction between SO2 and NO x from the flue gas and potassium permanganate can also proceed in a different way, giving rise to the product sulfate, nitrates with zeolite acting as catalyst. Therefore, we assumed that the mechanism of the reactions might be followed as below: (1) KMnO4+NO→KNO3+MnO2 (2) 2KMnO4+3SO2+2H2O→K2SO4 +2H2SO4 +2MnO2 (3) 2KMnO4→ K2MnO4 +MnO2 +O2↑ (4) 2SO2 +O2→2SO3→SO4 2− (5) 2NO+O2→2NO2→NO3 − The effect of empty bed residence time (EBRT) on simultaneous desulfurization and denitrification is presented in Fig. 4 , under the conditions of microwave power of 259 W, inlet concentration of 800 mg m−3 SO2 and inlet concentration of NO x 180 mg m−3 with potassium permanganate and zeolite. As keeping the inlet concentration of SO2 and NO x at 800 mg m−3, 180 mg m−3 respectively, the flow meter in the process should be changed step by step to make the mass loading of SO2 and NO x changed proportionally, and the system should be stabilized for 3 min after every adjustment. In our experiment, 6-step adjustment was conducted to increase the empty bed residence time (EBRT) from 0.214 s at 0.5 m3 h−1 air flow rate to 0.536 s at 0.2 m3 h−1 air flow rate. Fig. 4 shows that the desulfurization efficiency increases from 86.5% to 96.8%, whereas the NO x removal efficiency increases from 93.2% to 98.4% with empty bed residence time (EBRT) increasing. Fig. 4 demonstrates that SO2 and NO x were rapidly oxidized at microwave power of 259 W, when potassium permanganate and zeolite are added simultaneously. SO2 was oxidation to sulfate and NO x was oxidation to nitrates rapidly by potassium permanganate under microwave irradiation. This indicates the longer residence time benefits on the removal of SO2 and NO x , in case the residence time is too short to oxidize SO2 or NO x to sulfate or nitrates before release. The type of zeolites and the length of the quartz tube with catalyst and oxidizing agent are the key elements. From Fig. 4, in our experimental conditions, we can assume the optimum empty bed residence time (EBRT) is 0.357 s in the desulfurization and denitrification system, and about 95.2% sulfur dioxide or 98.1% nitric oxide in the gas stream is converted. Keeping the microwave power (259 W) and the empty bed residence time (EBRT) (0.357 s) fixed, the influence of concentration of SO2 and NO x in inlet on simultaneous desulfurization and denitrification with potassium permanganate and zeolite are presented in Figs. 5 and 6 separately. Figs. 5 and 6 show that the conversion of desulfurization reduces from 99.5% with 200 mg m−3 to 82.8% with 4400 mg m−3 SO2 concentration, whereas the denitrification increases from 40% to 96.3% within the increasing inlet concentration of NO x from 30 mg m−3 to 270 mg m−3. SO2 removal efficiency decreased with increased SO2 concentration, while the NO x removal efficiency increased with NO x concentration increasing, the possible explanation for this is that those denitrification reactions are involved in the NO x surface absorption catalytic oxidation by using microwave in the presence of potassium permanganate with zeolite as catalyst. More than 92% SO2 was oxidized at 259 W for less than the initial concentration of 1000 mg m−3 SO2. The high rate of SO2 oxidation is also achieved when the concentration of SO2 further increases. This experimental result is because sulfur dioxide (SO2) is oxidized to sulfate by potassium permanganate with zeolite as catalyst under microwave irradiation. Part of NO was oxidized to NO2 below 130 mg m−3 NO x . NO x removal efficiency reached 96.3% at 270 mg m−3 NO x (see Fig. 6). This illustrates that the microwave reactor is efficient in purifying the waste gas whose SO2 concentration is between 200 mg m−3 and 4400 mg m−3 and whose NO x concentration is between 30 mg m−3 and 270 mg m−3. When microwave power of 259 W, SO2 and NO x concentration in inlet gas were 600 mg m−3, 170 mg m−3 separately and the empty bed residence time (EBRT) was 0.214 s, the purification efficiency of SO2 and NO x could reach 96.2% and 93.6% respectively. The use of microwave induce catalytic oxidation proves to be an efficient way to achieve desulfurization and denitrification. This work has demonstrated that the microwave reactor with potassium permanganate and zeolite can be used for simultaneous removal of sulfur dioxide (SO2) and nitrogen oxides (NO x ) from flue gas. Microwave accentuates catalyst treatment, and microwave addition can increase the SO2 and NO x removal efficiency. The microwave desulfurization and denitrification effect using potassium permanganate and zeolite together are much higher than that using potassium permanganate or the zeolite only. The mechanism for microwave-induced SO2 and NO x oxidation can be described as the microwave induced catalytic reaction between SO2 and NO x and potassium permanganate with zeolites being the catalyst and microwave absorbent. Microwave induced catalytic technology is a viable and promising method for flue gas cleaning in view of the reduction of power consumption of the gas treatment process. The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (04300554).
abstract: Simultaneous sulfur dioxide (SO2) and nitrogen oxides (NO x ) removal from flue gas can be achieved with high efficiency by microwave with potassium permanganate (KMnO4) over zeolite. The experimental results showed that the microwave reactor could be used to oxidation of SO2 to sulfate with the best desulfurization efficiency of 96.8% and oxidize NO x to nitrates with the best NO x removal efficiency of 98.4%. Microwave accentuates catalytic oxidation treatment, and microwave addition can increase the SO2 and NO x removal efficiency by 7.2% and 12.2% separately. The addition of zeolite to microwave potassium permanganate increases from 16.5% to 43.5% the microwave removal efficiency for SO2, and the NO x removal efficiency from 85.6% to 98.2%. The additional use of potassium permanganate to the microwave zeolite leads to the enhancement of SO2 removal efficiency up from 53.9% to 95%, and denitrification efficiency up from 85.6% to 98.2%. The optimal microwave power and empty bed residence time (EBRT) on simultaneous desulfurization and denitrification are 259 W and 0.357 s, respectively. SO2 and NO x were rapidly oxidized in microwave induced catalytic oxidation reaction using potassium permanganate with zeolite being the catalyst and microwave absorbent.
title: Simultaneous removal of SO2 and NOx by microwave with potassium permanganate over zeolite

full_text: Rapid development of air-breathing aerospace engines has led aircraft speeds to increase from supersonic to hypersonic. However, hot air generated by the ram effect cannot cool the engine at these higher Mach numbers. Therefore, it is necessary for the fuel itself to be used as a coolant. The thermal cracking of hydrocarbon fuels is endothermic and can significantly contribute to the consumption of excess heat while also producing smaller hydrocarbon molecules that are beneficial in the subsequent combustion process. However, thermal cracking also generates solid products such as coke deposits, which can adversely affect heat transfer and thermal and combustion efficiencies, potentially shutting down the engine [1]. Coke formation is an inevitable side reaction in the thermal cracking of hydrocarbon fuels and was initially studied by researchers in the chemical industry. Albright et al. [2] summarized that three types of coke formation occur in the hydrocarbon fuels: filamentous coke can be formed from metal-catalyzed reactions; condensation coke can be formed from the polymerization and dehydrogenation of simple aromatics; and deposition coke can be formed from the reactions of small-molecular hydrocarbon with free radicals on the existing coke surface. Geem et al. [3] demonstrated that coke was deposited on alloy surfaces primarily via a metal-catalyzed pathway and a free-radical mechanism. Wickham et al. [4] found that condensation coke was the main product of Diels–Alder reactions between different dienes. Other researchers highlighted that polyolefins and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) are precursors for coke formation in the thermal-cracking process [5,6]. Yang et al. [7] adhered Al2O3 powder to the surface of a metal tube to decrease the production of filamentous coke. An alumina passivating layer was deposited inside the channels of stainless steel reactors by Gong et al. [8] to deactivate the metal surface for the purpose of coke suppression. Trimm [9] found that adding sulfide to the surface of nickel-based alloy was very effective at suppressing coke formation. Dhuyvetter et al. [10] found that coke formation could be minimized in the process of naphtha steam cracking by continuous dimethyl disulfide (DMDS) addition into the feedstock. Wickham et al. [4] confirmed that 2, 4-diaminotoluene decreased coke deposition during n-heptane cracking by suppressing the Diels–Alder reactions of butadiene and ethylene. Emily et al. [11] found that some hydrogen donor molecules increased the high-temperature stabilization of jet fuels and similar hydrocarbon mixtures. Croswell et al. [12] showed that fuel-hydrogenation decreased the production of aromatic and alkene species during thermal cracking and limit coke deposition at high temperatures. However, although these works show that additives can decrease coke formation during thermal cracking, they cannot eliminate it, especially at surfaces that are exposed to high heat fluxes. Attempts to decrease coking by employing endothermic catalytic reforming of hydrocarbon fuels to produce high levels of hydrogen production has recently attracted significant attention [13–16]. Korabelnikov et al. [17,18] and Kuranov et al. [19] studied the catalytic conversion and output characteristics of steam reforming based on methane, heptane, and decane. Those works indicate that coke deposition can be alleviated with the addition of steam-reforming catalyst in a thermal cracking regime. Hou et al. [20,21] reported on the benefits of endothermic catalytic steam reforming of aviation kerosene. Despite the range of work that has demonstrated that the catalytic reforming reaction can significantly inhibit coke formation and decrease coke deposition, mechanistic explanations of these phenomena have remained uncertain. There is also little work published that details the factors influencing catalytic steam reforming coke formation during the thermal cracking of kerosene. This work, therefore, experimentally investigates the effect of catalytic steam reforming on alleviating coke formation in the thermal cracking process of kerosene. A series of results reveal the variation of coking deposition rate and characteristics of gas and liquid products under different operating conditions and leads to an analysis of mechanisms for coke suppression. The catalytic reforming reaction takes place between hydrocarbon vapor and steam on the catalytic surface as follows: (1) C n H m + nH 2 O → H 2 CO CH 4 ⋯ , Because this reaction is endothermic, it serves as a thermal protection measure against coke formation. The reaction also generates easily combustible gaseous species, such as hydrogen, carbon monoxide, and light hydrocarbons [21]. All of the experiments are carried out in the setup shown in Fig. 1 . High purity nitrogen gas is used to remove residual fuel before and after every experiment. RP-3 Chinese kerosene (critical pressure and temperature of 2.5MPa and 404°C, respectively), which is almost entirely made up of cycloalkanes and paraffins, is used as the test fuel. Water and kerosene, together with 0.3% emulsifier, are fed into the fuel tank in advance and mixed to form an emulsion fuel using a gear pump and a static in-pipe mixer. The in-pipe mixer contains static mixer elements and intermediate chambers. The static mixer element contains an 180° twisted blade. Owing to the multi-dimensional movement (horizontal, tangential and radial), the mixing of the emulsified fuel is further improved. A metering pump then pressurized the fuel, which is directed into a pre-heater and then the reactor. A Coriolis mass flow meter (capacity pressure 10MPa; flow rate limit of 3g/s; measurement error±0.2%) measures and controls the fuel flow rate. The electrically heated reactor consisted of a stainless steel plate with a curving channel as shown in Fig. 2 . The path through the reactor consists of five loops with a total length of 2865mm and a rectangular cross-section of 4mm×2mm. A total of eleven thermocouples measures the fuel temperatures at the reactor inlet and outlet and the outer wall temperature along the reactor flow path. For the catalytic tests, a nickel-based steam reforming catalyst (Sinopec Qilu Company, PR China) is coated onto the inner wall of the reactor by plasma spraying, which is controlled to within 0.2mm. Elemental nickel is added as an active component using calcium aluminate as the carrier using an impregnation method. The two sides of the reactor are covered with electric heating boards with heat fluxes simulated by gradually regulating the power output voltage. Most of the heat is absorbed by fuel, which promotes thermal cracking and catalytic reforming reactions. After exiting the reactor, the reaction products are cooled in a condenser and sent to a gas-liquid separator. Gaseous products are analyzed by gas chromatography (GC SP-3430; Beijing Analysis Instrument Co. Ltd., PR China) while liquid products are collected and analyzed with a gas chromatography-mass spectrometer (GC–MS DSQ, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.). Identification of each compound is based on retention times and matching the mass spectrum recorded with those in the spectral library (NIST-11). The yield of each gaseous product is calculated on a per-unit-of-fuel basis as follows: (2) Y i = ω i / 100 × V i / m oil × 1000 / 60 , where Y i is the yield of gaseous component i (L/kg); ω i is the content of gaseous component i as measured by GC; m oil is the fuel mass flow rate (g/s); and V i is the volumetric flow rate of all gaseous products (L/min). The coke deposited on the inner surface of the reactor is analyzed quantitatively using a pressure difference transmitter (ROSEMONT 3051S, pressure drop limit of 60kPa, error<±0.025%) based on Eqs. (3) and (4). The pressure drop under cold conditions is measured before and after hot experiments. Because under cold conditions the fluid flow is laminar, the friction coefficient is not influenced by the roughness of the wall and any variation in pressure drop can be assumed to be only caused by coking. The amount of coke deposition, m coke , is therefore determined by the change in equivalent hydraulic diameter derived from the pressure drop, ΔP, before and after the hot test. (3) Δ P = 8 × λ × L × q m 2 ρ fuel × π 2 × D 5 , (4) m coke = ρ coke × π × L × D 2 − D 1 4 , where λ is the coefficient of friction; L is the reactor channel length(2865±1mm); q m is the fuel mass flow rate(1±0.02g/s); ρ fuel is the fuel density; ρ coke is the coke density which is assumed to be 1g/cm3 according to [22]; and D 1 and D 2 are the equivalent hydraulic diameters of the inner channel before and after the hot test, respectively. A coking rate, ω coke , is defined to quantitatively compare the impact of various reaction parameters on coke formation: (5) ω coke = m coke t R × S in , where t R is the accumulated reaction time (min) and S in is the total surface area of the inner channel. The pressure drop across the reactor is also continually monitored during heating to provide an indication of the rate of coke formation in the reactor as the reaction progressed. As listed in Table 1 , cases 1 and 2 allow comparison between coke formation with and without catalytic steam reforming, respectively. Cases 2 and 3 compare the coke formed when the catalyst is used without and with water, respectively, while cases 4–7 investigate the effects of reactor temperature on coke formation. Comparison between cases 2, 3, and 5 also allows an investigation into the impact of varying the amount of water present in the fuel. For all experiments, a fluid mass flow rate of 1g/s and a reactor pressure of 3MPa are used. As shown in Fig. 3 , under catalytic conditions with 3% water, the differential pressure remains below 5kPa even after 60min. Conversely, without the catalyst, the differential pressure increases to a maximum value of 60kPa in less than 5min and then fluctuates considerably for a further 3min. After 8min, the constant value of 60kPa signifies that the inner channel is restricted by coke deposits. In Case 2, the differential pressure also increased to a maximum of 60kPa after 15min where it again leveled off. This indicates that the catalytic coating on the wall's metal surface can resist the metal-catalyzed nucleation and the growth of coke deposits, partially alleviating the formation of filamentous coke. However, a lack of water limits the extent to which coke formation can be suppressed, suggesting that significant suppression requires both water and catalyst. In such a situation, catalytic steam reforming consumes the coke precursors (CxHy) as follows: (6) C x H y + xH 2 O = xCO + x + y 2 H 2 , where the generation of hydrogen also inhibits the polymerization of aromatics, which are produced by the cracking reactions, to poly-aromatics. As listed in Table 2 , the coking rate is the fastest for the uncatalyzed condition (Case 1: 4040μg/(cm2·min)), significantly lower when the catalyst is sprayed on the channel's inner surface (Case 2: 302μg/(cm2·min)), and even lower when water is added (Case 3: 52μg/(cm2·min)). The high coking rate observed in Case 1 results from the simultaneous formation of coke from the metal wall, and condensation and deposition pathways. The decrease of the coking rate in Case 2 is caused by a catalyst being sprayed on the inner wall of the channel forming a barrier and preventing the formation of metal-catalyzed coke. Potash, which covers the acid site, is added in the catalyst as a resistant to carbon formation. Adding water to the catalytic reaction causes the proportion of H2 in the gaseous products to increase nearly threefold between Cases 2 and 3 (Table 2). At the same time, the concentration of small hydrocarbons resulting from the thermal cracking of kerosene observed in Case 2 is higher than that in Case 3. The proportion of aromatics is also higher in Case 2. Together this suggests that hydrogen is detrimental to coke formation, while the presence of ethylene, propylene, and aromatics favors coke deposition. Indeed, the coking rate (decreasing from 302 to 52μg/(cm2·min)) appears closely correlated with the proportion of polycyclic aromatics (which decrease from 5.7 to 3.2wt%). Correspondingly, polycyclic aromatics are mainly derived from the polymerization of monocyclic aromatics (Eq. (8)). Monocyclic aromatics can be derived from alkenes such as ethylene and propylene via the Diels–Alder reaction in the presence of butadiene (Eq. (7)): (7) (8) Limiting the extent of these two reactions decreases the formation of polycyclic aromatics (and therefore coke formation). Because H2 is a product in both of these reactions, increasing its local concentration (via catalytic steam reforming) shifts the above equilibria to the left for a given reaction temperature, decreasing the production of polycyclic aromatics and coke formation via condensation reactions. As illustrated in Fig. 4(a), the coking rate decreases from 302 to 53 and 34μg/(cm2·min) as the water content increases from 0 to 3 to 10wt%. The initial addition of water rapidly suppresses the coking rate but the rate of suppression decreases as more water is added. As shown in Fig. 4(b) and (c), the mass ratios of cycloalkanes and paraffins increase as the water content increases. At water contents of 0, 3, and 10wt%, the respective proportion of aromatics are 29, 18, and 3wt%. Similar trends are observed for monocyclic aromatics and naphthalene. From the gaseous products as shown in Fig. 4(d), the yields of hydrogen and carbon monooxide demonstrate the rising trend with the increasing water content. The rising content of water as a reactant promotes the catalytic steam reforming of kerosene. From Eq. (1), it is also obvious that the mole ratio of hydrogen to carbon monooxide is (n+m/2):n. Therefore, the rising rate of hydrogen should be faster than that of carbon monooxide with an increase in water content. However, it can be seen from Fig. 4(d) that, when the water content increases from 3wt% to 10wt%, the yield of carbon monooxide increases from 0.11L/kg to 0.44L/kg, and its rising rate is 4 times. The yield of hydrogen increases from 1.12L/kg to 2.25L/kg, and its rising rate is 2 times. Actually, the rising rate of hydrogen is lower than that of carbon monooxide. From Eqs. (7) and (8), olefins and/or aromatics polymerization generate hydrogen. In contrast, the reverse of the polymerization reaction, called hydrogenation, consumes hydrogen. The higher the concentration of hydrogen in the reactants is, the faster the hydrogenation will occur. Hence, the hydrogen generated from the catalytic steam reforming of kerosene is consumed by the hydrogenation reaction. As illustrated in Fig. 4(c) and (d), the yields of ethylene and propylene decrease, and the mass ratios of aromatic and naphthalene also decrease with the increase of water content. Therefore, part of the hydrogen generated from the catalytic steam reforming reaction is used in the hydrogenation reactions thus suppressing the formation of olefins and aromatics. As a result, the coking rate decreases because less aromatics are generated with the water addition. As demonstrated in Fig. 5(a), the coking rate increases exponentially with increasing temperature: at 500°C, the coking rate is 14μg/(cm2·min) while at 750°C it reaches 449μg/(cm2·min). At 750°C, the pressure drop increases dramatically and reaches the maximum value in a very short reaction time. Such a rapid increase prevents the timely collection of gaseous or liquid products for the sake of security. Table 3 , Fig. 5(b) and (c) therefore present results for the temperature range of 550–700°C. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the aromatics such as benzene, toluene and p-xylene increase because the higher temperature favors the forward Diels–Alder and polymerization reactions as the temperature increases. As the increased yield of gaseous products in Fig. 5(c) shows, increasing the temperature also promotes thermal cracking. In particular, the yield of CH4 begins increasing from 600°C onwards and continues to increase with increasing temperature. The yield of H2, C2H4 and C3H6 also increases, but the rate of increase slowed once the temperature increases above 630°C. At 600°C the only PAH detected is naphthalene (Table 3). At temperatures of 600°C, 630°C, 680°C, 700°C, the proportion of naphthalene are 1.54wt%, 13.26wt%, 19.4wt%, 25wt%, respectively. Biphenyl, fluorene, anthracene, pyrene are not detected at 600°C, while phenanthrene, 11H-benzo[a]fluorene, benz[a]anthracene and triphenylene are detected only at 700°C. Below 680°C, biphenyl and poly-aromatics containing more than 3 aromatic rings account for less than 1wt%. But at 700°C, all poly-aromatics containing 3 aromatic rings and 4 aromatic rings such as pyrene account for more than 1wt%, and poly-aromatics containing more than 4 aromatic rings, such as 11H-benzo[a]fluorene, benz[a]anthracene and triphenylene, are also detected in the liquid products. At a temperature above 680°C, the reaction rate of polymerization exceeds that of hydrogenation, which therefore increases the proportions of poly-aromatics significantly. As a result, as seen in Fig. 5(a), the coking rate from 680°C to 750°C is much faster than that at a temperature below 680°C. To reduce the coking rate at temperatures exceeding 700°C, higher water content is needed. Although the ability to suppress coke formation decreases with increasing temperature, a comparison between Cases 1 (temperature of 600°C without catalyst) and 7 (temperature of 750°C with catalyst) confirms that the addition of catalytic material decreases the rate of coke formation and deposition. The coke formation with and without catalytic reforming and water have been compared. Spraying a catalyst on the reactor wall decreases coke formation by preventing the formation of filamentous coke on the metal surface. The decrease of coke precursors consumed by catalytic steam reforming results in the decline of a polymerization condensation. The hydrogen produced from the catalytic steam reforming of kerosene suppresses the polymerization condensation of unsaturated hydrocarbons. Higher content of hydrogen promotes the reverse reaction of Diels-Alder and polymerization reactions. The effects of varying water content and reactor temperature on coke formation have been investigated during the thermal cracking of kerosene with catalysis. The coking rate decreases with an increase in water content. However, the suppression of coking become weaker as the fuel temperature increases. Financial supports provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project No. 51176093) and the Tsinghua University Initiative Scientific Research Program (20131089265) are gratefully acknowledged.
abstract: A new method is developed to decrease coke formation during the thermal cracking of kerosene with applications in air-breathing jet engines. Endothermic catalytic steam reforming of kerosene is tested to investigate the potential for consuming excess heat and decreasing coke deposition rates. A plate reactor is used to simulate an engine-cooling channel and to compare the coke deposition with and without use of a reforming catalyst. The reactor temperature and the blending proportions of water fed to the reactor are varied across experiments. Spraying catalyst on the channel's inner surface prevents the production of filamentous coke, the production of which is catalyzed by the metal surface. The generation of a certain amount of hydrogen from the catalytic steam reforming of kerosene favors the reverse direction of the Diels–Alder and polymerization reactions, consuming the aromatic coke precursors and decreasing the coke formation rate. The effects of varying water content and reactor temperature on coke formation have been investigated during the thermal cracking of kerosene with catalysis. The coking rate decreases with an increase in water content. However, the suppression of coking become weaker as the fuel temperature is increased.
title: Coke suppression of kerosene by wall catalytic steam reforming

steps.method_details: Elemental nickel is added as an active component using calcium aluminate as the carrier using an impregnation method.
Catalyst is coated onto the inner wall of the reactor by plasma spraying, which is controlled to within 0.2mm.
Water and kerosene, together with 0.3% emulsifier, are fed into the fuel tank in advance and mixed to form an emulsion fuel using a gear pump and a static in-pipe mixer.

The in-pipe mixer contains static mixer elements and intermediate chambers. The static mixer element contains an 180° twisted blade. Owing to the multi-dimensional movement (horizontal, tangential and radial), the mixing of the emulsified fuel is further improved.
High purity nitrogen gas is used to remove residual fuel before and after every experiment.

full_text: Fischer–Tropsch (FT) synthesis is one of the main methods of utilizing natural gas, and has gained significant industrial attention as an alternative to the use of limited crude oil resources [1]. The application of FT synthesis is expected to increase and result in a shift towards utilizing natural gas derived liquid fuels for transportation purposes. However, FT synthesis is non-selective and produces a wide range of light gases and distillates, including olefins, paraffins and oxygenated products [2]. The diversity in composition of FT products reflects a complex set of surface reactions [3], whereby CH2 units formed by the hydrogenation of CO are “monomers” in a stepwise oligomerization process. At each stage of growth, the adsorbed hydrocarbon species may be desorbed, hydrogenated to form primary FT products, or react with another monomer to continue chain growth [1–3]. In order to develop catalysts that increase product selectivity, additional information is required regarding FT reactions and the role of hydrogen in controlling product selectivity. The synthesis of high molecular weight hydrocarbons is the main objective of FT synthesis [4], and two important variables that affect the activity and selectivity of the products are feed gas composition (H2/CO ratio) and residence time of species in the catalyst bed of the reactor [1,2]. The change of H2/CO ratio affects the hydrogen concentration in the catalyst bed. Hydrogen as a reactant in CO hydrogenation plays important roles in this catalytic process. The adsorption kinetics and energetics, as well as interactions of hydrogen with surface CO/carbon species, strongly affect the activity/selectivity behavior of FT catalysts [5]. In regard to hydrogen concentration in FT synthesis, the following conclusions have been made: the reaction rate is generally proportional to the concentration of hydrogen in the gas phase, as well as the fractional coverage of hydrogen on the catalyst surface (H2/CO ratio) [6]; hydrocarbon product selectivity is determined by the ability of a catalyst to promote chain propagation versus chain termination steps, and the chain termination rates are generally more dependent on hydrogen concentration than are chain propagation rates; the order of hydrogen concentration in the reaction rate is higher for the production of paraffins than for olefins [5]. The residence time variation also affects the product distribution, where the effects have been attributed to the alteration in contact time of various species in the catalyst bed [7]. For example, previous results indicate that for long residence times secondary reactions occur with a decrease in olefin content and an increase in the formation of higher molecular weight hydrocarbons by chain growth [8]. Previous studies have optimized the flow and composition of the feed rates at the reactor entrance to control the effect of H2/CO ratio and residence time in FT reactions. In this work, for the first time hydrogen was distributed along the catalyst bed axis to reduce the selectivity to methane (as an undesirable product) and enhance the selectivity to distillates. A special experimental fixed-bed reactor was constructed in which hydrogen could be injected along the length of the reactor to control the H2/CO ratio and the residence time of species change along the bed simultaneously. Due to high activity, high selectivity to distillates, and low water–gas shift (WGS) reaction, a supported cobalt catalyst was selected for this investigation [9,10]. These characteristics as well as a higher resistance to oxidation demonstrate that cobalt-based catalysts may be considered as the preferred catalysts for FT synthesis [11]. A 22.2 wt.% of Co dispersed on SiO2 catalyst was prepared by wet impregnation of silica (Davisil Silica gel, 60–100 mesh, 300 m2 g−1) with cobalt nitrate solution. The support was impregnated in two steps to achieve a final metal loading of 22.2 wt.%. First, an aqueous solution of 57.1 wt.% cobalt nitrate was prepared and used to impregnate the silica. After the first impregnation step, the sample was dried for about 6 h at room temperature, 2 h at 343 K and for 5 h at 393 K in a vacuum oven. This process was repeated a second time to achieve a final loading of 22.2 wt.% cobalt. The sample was then calcined for 2 h at 573 K, 3 h at 723 K and 1 h at 773 K with a heating rate of 3 K min−1 and an air space velocity of 2000 h−1. The details of the catalyst preparation and characterizations have been reported elsewhere [12]. The catalyst was reduced in situ in a H2 flow of 120 mL min−1 (STP), while the temperature was raised at 5 K min−1 to 673 K and maintained at 673 K for 12 h. Principal components of our experimental apparatus are shown in Fig. 1 . The setup includes a gas feeding, reaction, and products analysis sections. The H2 and CO passed through two separate sets of moisture and oxygen traps. A bed of quartz beads at 573 K was used downstream of the traps along the CO feed stream to decompose possible metal carbonyls. CO and H2 input gases were individually regulated by five electronic mass flow controllers, thereby hydrogen feed could be introduced at various points along the catalyst bed. The reactor was a 1/4-in. OD stainless steel tube packed with 2 g of the catalyst. A series of 1/16-in. OD stainless steel tubes were used for hydrogen feed at equal distances along the reactor axis. The reactor was immersed in a bath of molten sodium nitrite and nitrate and potassium nitrate salts. A stirrer was used to obtain a uniform temperature along the reactor length. Reactor temperature was measured by a thermocouple on the wall located a mid of the catalyst bed, and a PID controller adjusted the salt bath temperature. Four different strategies for hydrogen feeding were employed. The CO was introduced into the entrance of the reactor, while the total stream of hydrogen was either introduced to the reactor inlet (usual co-feed strategy) or distributed along the catalyst bed at equal distances from the reactor inlet. Fig. 2 shows a schematic diagram of the strategies employed in these experiments. In the co-feed strategy (referred to as C), the total stream of hydrogen along with CO was introduced at the inlet of the reactor. In the decreasing strategy (D), the total hydrogen flow rate was divided into four streams of 36.7%, 26.7%, 20% and 16.6% and were introduced at the inlet of the reactor, and first, second, and third side stream inlets. For the increasing strategy (I), the percentages of the hydrogen flow rates introduced into the inlet and side stream inlets were reversed to 16.6%, 20%, 26.7% and 36.7%. In the equal strategy (E), equal amounts of hydrogen were entered into these inlets. The effluent from the reactor contains un-reacted CO and hydrogen, as well as low and high molecular weight hydrocarbons and water vapor. The effluent was transferred through a 1/8-in. OD stainless steel tube, the temperature of which was adjusted at a few degrees higher than the reactor temperature to prevent possible condensation of the heavy hydrocarbons and water vapor. A modified multi-valve multicolumn Varian CP-3800 refinery gas analyzer (RGA) from Varian-Chrompack was used for the analysis of the reactor effluent. The RGA was equipped with an FID and a TCD. The TCD was connected to the FID via a methanizer, in which the CO is converted to methane and is accurately quantified in FID. The signals from the FID and TCD were conditioned, digitized, and processed (Star Chromatography Workstation software). Using the hydrocarbon standards, RGA peaks were identified and calibrated to calculate the molar flow rate of different products. This was used to obtain product distribution (selectivity of CH4, C2–C4, C5+), CO conversion, olefin to paraffin (Ole/Par) ratio for C2 and C3 hydrocarbons, and chain growth probability (α). The peak areas for the reactants and products were normalized by an external standard. All of the collected data were obtained after at least 4 h on stream FT synthesis to ensure steady-state conditions. The basis for the selectivity calculation was the number of moles of carbon present in a given product and the total number of moles of CO converted. The residence time was calculated based on the total feed flow rate measured at standard conditions. Prior to and during data collection for each set of experimental runs, the catalyst was under specific test conditions to ensure the stability and reproducibility of the catalyst performance. The conditions for this test were as follows; T=483 K, H2/CO=2.0 and residence time=2.0 s. The catalyst performance at the test conditions in the course of the experiments was quite similar. As mentioned previously, two different feeding strategies, co-feed and hydrogen distributed strategies, were studied. The results for each strategy are presented in the following sections. The Co/SiO2 catalyst was tested at 3 different temperatures, 3 residence times, and 3 H2/CO ratios. The effect of these parameters on CO conversion, α, Ole/Par ratio and CH4, C2–C4, C5+ selectivities were investigated. The main aim of this set of experiments was to utilize the results as a basis for comparison with those obtained under hydrogen distribution strategy. The operating parameters and the results are presented in Table 1 . Increasing the reaction temperature results in an increase in CH4 and C2–C4 selectivities (light hydrocarbons) and CO conversion, whereas C5+ selectivity, α, and Ole/Par ratio decrease. At higher reaction temperatures, olefins are preferentially hydrogenated and chain propagation is suppressed. These observations are consistent with previously reported studies indicating that a higher operating temperature results in a shift in selectivity towards lower carbon number products (low α) and to more hydrogenated products [1] for many FT synthesis catalysts. These observations are also consistent with previously published studies on the influence of temperature on the performance of cobalt catalysts [13–18]. A study of carbon monoxide hydrogenation on 15 wt.% cobalt on alumina revealed that an increase in temperature would cause an increase in hydrogenation activity and production of light hydrocarbons [13]. On the other hand, CO conversion increased when the chain growth probability decreased [13]. Das et al. [14] reported that on rhenium-promoted Co/Al2O3 catalyst, the methane selectivity increased by an increase in the reaction temperature. Other investigators have reported similar results [15]. The impact of H2/CO ratio was explored by changing the ratio from 1.0 to 3.0 (Table 1). At a higher H2/CO ratio, higher CO conversion and higher CH4, C2–C4 selectivities were obtained. For instance, at a H2/CO ratio of 3.0, the C5+ selectivity (i.e. heavier products), Ole/Par ratio, and the chain growth probability were lower compared with those obtained at lower H2/CO ratios. Several research groups studied the performance of different cobalt catalysts under different H2/CO ratios [15–19]. Rohr et al. [16] observed that on 10 wt.% Co/Al2O3 catalyst promoted with zirconia, the C5+ selectivity, Ole/Par ratio and CO conversion dropped when the H2/CO ratio increased from 2 to 9. Kuipers et al. [19] showed that the methane selectivity improved while the chain growth probability (α) decreased with an increase of H2/CO ratio. Our results also indicate the Ole/Par ratio decreases exponentially with chain length. Other studies revealed that an increase in H2/CO ratio leads to the enhancement of CO conversion and production of the lighter hydrocarbons while suppressing the Ole/Par ratio [20]. The effect of decreasing olefin incorporation with increasing H2/CO ratio is consistent with a set of reaction pathways for olefins in which hydrogenation and incorporation both consume α-olefin competitively. Thus, at a higher H2/CO ratio, more olefins are hydrogenated and consequently less are incorporated into the growing chains [18]. Table 1 indicates that with an increase in residence time, CO conversion increases and the Ole/Par ratio decreases. This result confirms that for long contact times, secondary reactions occur with a decrease in olefins content and an increase in the formation of higher molecular weight hydrocarbons by chain growth [8]. At short residence times, olefins have not enough time to undergo secondary reactions and almost primary product distributions can be obtained [17]. The selective α-olefin readsorption and chain initiation, instead of secondary hydrogenation, can account for the observed effects of bed residence time on the Ole/Par ratio [18]. Also, at higher residence times, the extent of olefin readsorption on the surface improved and further hydrogenation of the readsorbed olefins resulted in a lower Ole/Par ratio [8]. Kuipers et al. [19] studied a poly-crystalline cobalt foil and Iglesia et al. [21] a TiO2-supported ruthenium catalyst, and observed an enhancement of Ole/Par ratio at shorter residence times. Table 1 also shows that hydrocarbon selectivity, including methane and chain growth probability, were not strongly affected by residence time in the range of residence times investigated in the present study. Other researchers observed a similar pattern [15,18,22]. Das et al. [14] observed that on rhenium promoted Co/Al2O3 catalyst, CO conversion increased almost linearly with increasing residence time. Hurlbut et al. [15] reported that over magnesium promoted cobalt catalysts, the chain growth probability was not affected by changing the residence time. The catalyst activity and selectivity under different hydrogen distribution strategies was studied at atmospheric pressure and 483 K. The H2/CO ratio calculated for the entire bed was 2.0, and the residence time based on the total flow injected to all feed entries including the reactor inlet was 2.0 s. The results of CO conversion, different products selectivities, and Ole/Par ratio for all of the feed strategies investigated (i.e. equal (E), increasing (I) and decreasing (D)) were normalized with respect to their corresponding co-feed (C) results. Fig. 3 presents normalized CH4, C2–C4 and C5+ selectivities for the above-mentioned strategies. Using the different hydrogen distribution strategies, the C1 and C2–C4 selectivities (lighter hydrocarbons) are suppressed to some extent whereas C5+ selectivity (higher molecular weight hydrocarbons) is improved. In the equal strategy (E), the extent of suppression for CH4 and C2–C4 selectivities were about 11% and 12%, respectively, while C5+ selectivity improved by about 9%. However, the different strategies did not indicate a significant difference in these regards. The hydrogen distribution results in two important effects on the bed conditions: (1) with a hydrogen distribution strategy, as compared to a corresponding co-feed strategy, the H2/CO ratio is lower throughout the catalyst bed and increases gradually down the bed to nearly the same value for the co-feed strategy at the last quarter of the bed; (2) the residence time of the reactants and products also change along the catalyst bed due to the change in feed strategy. Under a hydrogen distribution strategy, the residence time is higher at the beginning parts of the bed and gradually increases to the same value for the co-feed strategy. This opposes the changes in H2/CO ratio. The effect of changes in both H2/CO ratios and residence time causes a significant change, as compared to the usual co-feed strategy in the performance of the FT synthesis along the catalyst bed. It is believed that in typical fixed-bed reactors, the rate of reaction is high at the inlet and decreases down the axial length of the reactor [23]. In H2 distribution, the syngas consumption rate is low due to the lower H2/CO ratio, and increases near the reactor outlet. This difference between the co-feed and hydrogen distribution strategies is suggested to be the main reason for the behavior observed. With a low H2/CO ratio at the beginning of the bed, the conditions are suitable for chain growth of the primary products. At lower hydrogen concentrations, the relative rate of chain termination decreases with respect to chain propagation [4,24]. This causes the selectivity of the higher molecular weight (C5+ selectivity) to increase at the expense of lower selectivity for lighter hydrocarbons (C2–C4 selectivity) because the reactive primary hydrocarbons formed at the beginning of the bed are readsorbed further down the bed and either undergo hydrogenation to form paraffins or are polymerized to form higher molecular weight hydrocarbons [8]. It is also believed that the longer residence time favors the improvement of heavy hydrocarbon selectivity, similar to the effect of low H2/CO ratios [4,8,24]. With regard to methane selectivity, it has been reported that large amounts of methane are usually formed at the first part of the fixed-bed reactors [8,23], and it is suggested that a higher concentration of hydrogen favors the reactions forming methane. Thus, it appears that lowering the hydrogen concentration, or in the other words lowering H2/CO ratio, at the beginning of the bed using hydrogen distribution inhibits the formation of methane to some extent. Fig. 4 indicates a significant improvement in the normalized Ole/Par selectivity ratios using the hydrogen distribution strategies. The most significant improvement occurred for the increasing (I) strategy. In this strategy, the H2/CO ratio is the lowest at the beginning of the bed as compared to C, D and E strategies. Due to the lower H2/CO ratio, the formation of larger amounts of olefins especially at the beginning of the bed is more likely. Schulz et al. [3] have shown that the major (primary) products of FT synthesis on cobalt-based catalysts are linear olefins and paraffins. They believed that these compounds are formed by desorption of alkyl groups, namely associative desorption for paraffins and dissociative desorption for olefins. In the present study, it may be argued that the hydrogen distribution strategy increases the partial pressure of CO. Higher CO partial pressure leads to higher surface coverage, which can lead to a higher probability of chain growth and a lower probability of the desorption of n(–CH2–) species. On the other hand, at lower hydrogen partial pressures the probability of propagation of the surface species to olefins increases [4,21,24]. Thus, one could expect that as the H2/CO ratio decreases, the selectivity would shift towards heavier and unsaturated hydrocarbons [8]. Fig. 5 shows the normalized chain growth probability (α) for different feed strategies. The results indicate that α has increased under H2 distribution, which is consistent with selectivity results (Fig. 3), i.e. lower methane selectivity and higher C5+ selectivity. The figure predicts the maximum α for E strategy. Fig. 6 presents the CO conversion for different feed strategies. As expected, conversion decreases with the hydrogen distribution strategy as compared to the co-feed strategy. Comparing the C to D strategy, the hydrogen partial pressure decreases which in turn leads to a lower CO conversion. Among the D, E and I strategies, the latter one corresponds to the lowest hydrogen partial pressure which causes the CO conversion to minimize. Table 2 presents the results of the hydrogen distribution effect (E strategy) at three different values of H2/CO ratios (1.0–3.0), residence times (1.0, 2.0 and 4.0 s) and temperatures (473, 483 and 493 K). All of the results have been normalized with respect to their corresponding co-feed conditions. The results indicate that methane selectivity decreases for all the experiments compared to their co-feed results. However, the extent of reduction was the largest for run no. 9, in which the residence time was 4.0 s. For this run, methane selectivity decreased by about 20%. The C5+ selectivity also improved for all of the experimental runs. The maximum improvement in C5+ selectivity (i.e. 16%) was observed for run no. 9. Additionally, the Ole/Par ratios are enhanced for all the runs. Similar to C5+ selectivity, the maximum enhancement (i.e. 1.82 times) occurred for run no. 9. Finally, the CO conversion under hydrogen distribution was decreased for all of the runs, as expected. This is mainly due to the H2/CO ratio, which is lower for this feed strategy as compared to its corresponding co-feed system. Interestingly, the extent of reduction in CO conversion is not so large for run no. 9, which revealed some promising results. In summary, at a temperature of 483 K, H2/CO ratio of 2.0 and a residence time of 4.0 s, the hydrogen distribution strategy led to significant improvements in the performance of the catalyst with regard to selectivity. Specifically, this strategy produced lower methane selectivity, higher C5+ selectivity, and higher Ole/Par ratio while the extent of reduction in CO conversion was still acceptable. In the present investigation, the effects of hydrogen distribution on Co/SiO2 FT synthesis catalysts using a fixed-bed reactor were examined. As compared to a typical co-feed strategy, the distribution of hydrogen enhanced the FT synthesis selectivity to C5+ hydrocarbons while decreasing the selectivity to methane. These effects were attributed to the changes in H2/CO ratio and residence time of the reactants along the catalyst bed. While C5+ selectivity increased by 16% for a H2/CO ratio of 2.0, methane selectivity decreased by about 20%. Moreover, the Ole/Par ratio improved by a factor of 1.6 to 1.8. The different hydrogen distribution strategies did not exhibit significant differences among themselves; however, in some cases the equal (E) distribution strategy appeared to produce the most favorable results. The authors wish to acknowledge the Iranian Petrochemical Technology and Research Company for their partial financial support.
abstract: This work investigates the effect of hydrogen distribution in a fixed bed on the activity and selectivity of Co/SiO2 (22.2 wt.% Co) Fischer–Tropsch (FT) catalysts. Hydrogen is introduced from 4 equally spaced axial locations and the flow rates are adjusted with 3 different strategies; the so-called decreasing, equal, and increasing flow strategies. Distribution of hydrogen results in different H2/CO ratio along the reactor, which in turn controls the product selectivity. As compared to the co-feed strategy, the decreasing strategy suppressed methane selectivity by 20%, while enhancing C5+ selectivity by about 16% and increasing chain growth probability to a limited extent. The olefin to paraffin selectivity ratio also shows an improvement by a factor of 1.6–1.8, depending upon the type of strategy employed. The dependence of product selectivity on the operating conditions such as temperature, overall H2/CO ratio, and feed residence time was also explored.
title: Enhancement of distillate selectivity in Fischer–Tropsch synthesis on a Co/SiO2 catalyst by hydrogen distribution along a fixed-bed reactor
