full_text: The term of SOx means mainly sulfur dioxide SO2 — which is generated in the process of sulfur oxidation, and sulfur trioxide SO3. Natural sources of those gasses are volcano eruptions, decomposition processes occurring in organic matter, fires of woodlands and steppes or soil erosion. As far as anthropogenic sources are concerned, the biggest share in the emission of sulfur oxides in the air has fossil fuels; when burnt, they will release as oxides the sulfur contained in them, and this sulfur is emitted into the air, and this constitutes approximately 85% of total SO2 emission. Another poisoner is metallurgical industry with a 10% share of air poisoning with the gas said. In Poland the main source of sulfur dioxide air pollution is mainly power industry; however, we must not forget about the role of chemical and metal industry (mainly copper branch) [1]. High SO2 emissions in the air result from the predominant share of coal as energy winning raw material and relatively high sulfur contents in home coal deposits (on an average, the sulfur level is approximately 1.3% S). Be stressed that due to the application of new technologies and sulfur removal installations, the volume of emitted sulfur dioxides has been diminishing. In 2000, the volume was around 1511thousand ton, whereas in 2008 it fell down to 999thousand ton, which means that Poland complied with the assumptions of the II Sulphur Protocol of Geneva Convention in which Poland had promised to bring the SO2 emission down to 1400thousand ton [1]. The volume of sulfur oxide emitted in the air in Poland has been decreasing in the latest years. More than 99% of emission is attributed to stationary sources [1]. Sulfur oxides are a serious danger to the natural environment [2,3]. This negative effect means acidification of lakes and rivers where sulfur dioxide reacts in the air with ˙OH in the presence of oxygen according to the reaction: ˙OH+SO2 +(O2, H2O)→H2SO4 +˙OOH The resulting product – sulfuric acid (VI) with precipitations – causes the acidification of surface waters, which is a very dangerous phenomenon because acid rains may occur thousands miles away from the sources of SO2 emissions. In addition, sulfur dioxide destroys tree foliage, which is best seen nearby large industrial plants, like power plants or metallurgical works. As far as living organisms are concerned, a deleterious effect of SO2 can be observed at concentrations as low as 1–2ppm, whereas the man can detect a level of 3–5ppm. This gas attacks mainly the respiratory tracts and vocal cords. Inhalation of SO2 causes the bronchia to contract. Sulfur dioxide pervades first into the walls of the respiratory tracts, then into blood and the entire body; it is accumulated in the walls of trachea, bronchia, liver, spleen, brain and lymphatic nodes. A high SO2 in the air may lead to corneal lesions. Most endangered to direct action of SO2 are buildings with limestone or marble finish, which are destroyed in the reaction: SO2 +CaCO3 →CaSO3 +CO2 It was also found that sulfur dioxide has a bad effect upon sandstones [3]. Moreover, such pollutions affect buildings because of acid rains [3]. Direct effect of SO2 causes corrosion of such structural parts, like steel, zinc, copper or aluminum [3]. Long time research done into this item has been crowned with various technologies which allow one to reduce emission of sulfur compounds on all three stages of coal combustion. Methods limiting SOx emissions from combustion of fossil fuels can be divided into three groups [4–9]: — fuel desulfurization — limitation of SO2 emission in the combustion processes; sulfur is bound by appropriate substances, — desulfurization of exhaust gasses after coal burning. fuel desulfurization limitation of SO2 emission in the combustion processes; sulfur is bound by appropriate substances, desulfurization of exhaust gasses after coal burning. The removal of coal sulfur (occurring as pyrite) is complex from the viewpoint of physics and chemistry [10–13]. In physical methods utilized are differences in densities (the density of pyrite is approximately 5g/cm3, of other materials around 2g/cm3), size and shape of particles as well as in surface, electric and magnetic properties [14]. Chemical methods are based upon oxidation (oxidant — air, oxygen, chlorine) [10,11,15–18] and caustification (solutions or molten NaOH, KOH, Ca(OH)2) [19–24]. A disadvantage of the method said is a modification to coal properties in the purification process. A factor that impedes the development of chemical methods of desulfurization is expenditure which depends on the required degree of purification, waste volume, need of washing, coal properties etc. A new promising method is microbiological coal desulfurization [9] which consists mainly in oxidizing pyrite sulfur to sulfate sulfur which is easy to be removed. The said process is run by sulfur bacteria, is very complicated and includes a series of reactions redox, hydrolysis and complexing. Practically, the coal pyrite is subject to the action of air and humidity, which will lead to sulfuric acid [VI] and iron [III] sulfate [VI] which participates in the chemical oxidation of pyrite to another portion of sulfate, and elementary sulfur. Elementary sulfur is oxidized by bacteria to sulfuric acid [VI]. Pyrite sulfur is preferably removed by the strain Thiobacillus ferrooxidans which can bring the pyrite contents by around 90%. For the removal of organic sulfur, other microorganism strains must be used — Sulfolobus can eliminate only 20%, and Pseudomonas (which decompose tiophenes) — approximately 50% of organic sulfur contained in coal. An advantage of the method said is a high desulfurization selectivity which does not alter the carbon skeleton; hence, there are no losses and no changes in calorific value. In addition, coal can be purified of heavy metals and ashes contents can drop as well. A flaw is instead a slow rate, which means using high volume reactors. The use of desulfurized coal considerably simplifies its preparation for combustion, and the costs of desulfurization of exhaust gasses are also cut. The main rule of desulfurization of exhaust gasses is transforming SO2 in a substance which is easily removed from the gas and cleaning system. Most typical methods are sorption processes in conjunction with SO2 oxidation; for the recovery of sulfur as commercial goods or production of sulfur wastes, mentioned can be regeneration and waste [not regeneration] processes which can be run as wet or dry. Such terms can be referred to the final product, use or no use of liquids and moisturizing and cooling of cleaned gasses. Most common are the following methods of exhaust gas desulfurization [25–29]: wet methods (calcium and limestone, magnesium waste-free method, double alkaline method, Wellman–Lord's or ammonia methods); then, there are dry and semi-dry methods (SO2 sorption on solid sorbents SO2s injection into a gas stream, atomization drying desulfurization or desulfurization in the fluidized bed). Currently, several methods are used and implemented on a large scale; anyway, each selection should be preceded by a detailed feasibility study to regard the given conditions. One should also take into consideration the issue of NOx emission, since in some cases a part of those pollutions can be eliminated. In the wet process, exhaust gasses contact directly the water solution of ligand. An advantage of that process is high yield. A flaw is, instead, a reduction of temperature of gasses which need reheating, and means extra investment costs. As far as the dry process is concerned, the temperature of gasses does not fall below the condensation point of vapor. A relatively high temperature is an advantage, since there is no need of reheating exhaust gasses before they are emitted in the air. A flaw of this method is a lower efficiency, the necessity of extra devices (e.g. electrofilters) and of solid wastes utilization. Investigations into the catalytic reduction of sulfur dioxide in the presence of ammonia aimed at desulfurization of exhaust gasses of stationary sources have been led since the 1960s. Methods aimed at limiting SO2 emissions in the combustion process consist in mixing solid, dry sorbent with fuel or in blowing sorbent in the furnace chamber over dust burners [30–34]. Most used sorbents are calcium carbonate and calcium hydroxide. When mixed up with exhaust gasses at high temperatures, those compounds are transformed into oxides. This method has an efficiency of 60–80%. The application of calcium sorbents allows using cheap, generally available deposits of limestone. Generally applied are two solutions to limit SO2 emissions during the combustion process. The first consists in injecting dry sorbent in dust-bearing exhaust gasses before an efficient deduster. This is a very economical option. The other method, considerably more expensive, needs another deduster (cloth filter). However, finally the efficiency of exhaust gas desulfurizing is higher and less sorbent is consumed. Also, a combined option is applied. Additionally, practiced is the activation of sorbent carried in the exhaust gasses, viz. CaO or Ca(OH)2; applicable are sorption solutions or water. Due to water, the humidity of exhaust gasses is increased, dust resistivity falls and the volume of SO2 is also reduced thanks to higher reactivity of sorbent. An alternative solution to the methods said is DESOX, elaborated by Nazimek [35,36]. In this method, the emission of sulfur oxides is reduced as early as in the coal combustion process. This method can be considered an improved option of SO2 emission reduction, referred to in the preceding chapter. Anyway, the latter method completely changes the approach to the reduction of SOx volumes, since the desulfurization of exhaust gasses occurs on the coal grain subject to combustion. DESOX materials used in this method are dry additives added to coal under burning. They bind sulfur compounds in furnace residuum. Due to such additives, the desulfurization process is shifted from the gaseous phase to the solid phase. There are no gaseous pollutions because desulfurization occurs in the combustion process, which eliminates the use of a series of technological lines to remove new pollutions (sulfur oxides, NOx). DESOX allows one to separate desulfurization processes from the boiler type and efficiency. The efficiency of the processes depends only on coal types and conditions in which coal feed is burnt. The principle of operation of the DESOX catalyst was presented by Nazimek in 1996 [36]. The DESOX catalyst was an intermediate system in which performed were fundamental reactions of coal combustion and SO2 removal. This is indispensable because the equilibrium of the oxidation reaction from SO2 to SO3 is strongly shifted leftwards due to its exothermic character (the achieved equilibrium fraction reacted is approximately 1%). Hence, in order to increase the fraction reacted, the resulting SO3 should be converted into sulfates, which means its elimination from the reaction environment. The DESOX catalyst is aimed at developing an active phase in the combustion occurring on the coal grain surface (system of coal alloy and catalyst), in which burnt is coal and other substances. DESOX is competitive to traditional methods in view of technology and economy as well. Nevertheless, it is necessary to adjust the additive composition to the coal used. The degree of elimination of sulfur dioxide from exhaust gasses – when the said catalyst is applied – depends on the following factors: preparation methods, catalyst contents, Al2O3 contents in the catalyst, quantity of metal added to the additive (e.g. vanadium, zinc in the case of MgO–ZnO catalysts), carrier quantity, calcium contents in coal, quality of coal burnt. Another factor enhancing the efficiency of such a system is the addition of some dolomite, which causes a fall in the reaction rate and intensifies the catalyst's operation, and at the same time, the mechanism of SO2 elimination from the system remains unchanged. The size of specific surface of additives does not affect the degree of removal of sulfur dioxide from exhaust gasses. The main advantages of using DESOX catalyst are: increased process efficiency (earlier fuel combustion), reduction of SOx emission (40–80%) according to air excess, reduction of NOx emissions (85–95%), dust emission as well as prevention of low and high temperature corrosion. In patents Nazimek at al. [37–39], the authors described the evolution of development of DESOX catalysts. The first DESOX catalyst, labeled by Nazimek ‘prototype’, was the system presented below: MgO (82–86.5%), ZnO (7–10%), graphite (>3%), V2O5 (3.5–5%), CaO (Mont-V64>Mont-V16>Mont-V32>Mont-V4. Tests with montmorillonite-cobalt systems were carried out like the tests with vanadium containing samples. Mont-cobalt additives removed 35–53% SO2 weight from exhaust gasses generated in the combustion of less sulfurised WJ coal (Table 2). For the ‘Bogdanka’ coal the results were almost twice that good. To sum up, the type of hard coal, first of all sulfur level, is decisive for the activity of montmorillonite-cobalt additives (Fig. 3 ). Unlike montmorillonite-cobalt additives for materials of series D (containing nickel ions), there was observed no such a drastic dependence between the degree of sulfur dioxide removal and the type of hard coal WJ (Table 2). The activity seems to be in this case better, since those additives remove 83–90% SO2. Therefore, those materials proved to be more attractive than montmorillonite-cobalt ones (Fig. 3). Neither was there observed any relation between the degree of SO2 emission and percentage contents of active material. Those materials remove 60–73% sulfur dioxide in the process of burning hard coal WJ (Table 2). It was observed that an additive containing nickel ions leads to the elimination of 60% SO2, whereas the other catalysts eliminate practically an identical quantity of SO2 (73%). On the final stage of reducing the sulfur dioxide emission in the process of hard coal emission, it is desirable to obtain furnace residuum enriched in sulfates. Therefore, performed were analyses of forms of sulfur contained ion ashes. XRF tests yielded no unequivocal results; hence, phase analyses were executed by means of X-ray diffraction (Fig. 4 ). Those tests corroborated the absence of relatively big, crystalline SO4 2− forms (measurable with XRD). In the figure presented is an example diffraction pattern for ashes obtained from co-combustion of ‘Julian’ hard coal with Mont-V8. In the ashes found were SiO2 (quartz), Al6Si2O13 (mullite), Fe2O3 (hematite) and Ca3Al2O6 (Fig. 4). Ashes were subject to further analyses. With a scanning electron microscope provided with a detector of back scattered electrons (SEM-BSE) some pictures were made (for example Fig. 5 ), and EDXS analyses (Fig. 6 ) for selected areas were performed. Only scanning microscope investigations confirmed the occurrence of sulfates in ashes; taking into consideration the results of XRF and XRD, it could be stated that those compounds occur as highly dispersed crystallites. To sum it up: — As a result of using diversified preparatory procedures on montmorillonite additives DESOX obtained were materials with different physicochemical properties. — While comparing a series of montmorillonite and a zeolite series, it can be seen that the former exhibited a slightly lower activity in the reaction of removal of sulfur dioxide from exhaust gasses in the combustion process of ‘Julian’ main hard coal; yet, an asset of the montmorillonite carrier is its re-utilization as a production waste (after sulfur recovery) unlike Sipernat which is a synthetic and more expensive material. — There was performed an optimization of the montmorillonite-vanadium additive through selecting the most advantageous time of carrier's contact with the promoting solution. It was proved that the laid active material, like in the case with zeolite carriers, occurs as vanadium ions with the fourth or fifth degree of oxidation. — In the case of montmorillonite-vanadium additives applied was the double impregnation method — DIM, in order to lay nickel ions on aluminum oxides, zeolites or molecular screens. In the said investigations for the first time applied was the DIM with an EDTA solution for impregnation of montmorillonites. It was proved that this method can be used successfully for those carriers, and at the same time, it was also proposed for the impregnation with ions of nickel and cobalt. — The introduction of cobalt ions on the montmorillonite carrier yielded the best catalytic results of an order of 85% for co-combustion of high level sulfur hard coal from ‘Bogdanka’ mine. — Nickel-montmorillonite DESOX additives also exhibited good efficiency in the limitation of the sulfur dioxide emission into the air. — There was prepared and investigated in detail a series of additives with a carrier of developed texture (based upon acid activated montmorillonite and supported with zircon oxide). Ions of nickel, copper and manganese were laid classically by adsorption from water solutions of proper nitrates (CIM). Those catalysts exhibited in the DESOX process a catalytic activity comparable with the materials described before. — Sulfate forms obtained after the combustion process with addition of montmorillonite additives are amorphous and very well dispersed. As a result of using diversified preparatory procedures on montmorillonite additives DESOX obtained were materials with different physicochemical properties. While comparing a series of montmorillonite and a zeolite series, it can be seen that the former exhibited a slightly lower activity in the reaction of removal of sulfur dioxide from exhaust gasses in the combustion process of ‘Julian’ main hard coal; yet, an asset of the montmorillonite carrier is its re-utilization as a production waste (after sulfur recovery) unlike Sipernat which is a synthetic and more expensive material. There was performed an optimization of the montmorillonite-vanadium additive through selecting the most advantageous time of carrier's contact with the promoting solution. It was proved that the laid active material, like in the case with zeolite carriers, occurs as vanadium ions with the fourth or fifth degree of oxidation. In the case of montmorillonite-vanadium additives applied was the double impregnation method — DIM, in order to lay nickel ions on aluminum oxides, zeolites or molecular screens. In the said investigations for the first time applied was the DIM with an EDTA solution for impregnation of montmorillonites. It was proved that this method can be used successfully for those carriers, and at the same time, it was also proposed for the impregnation with ions of nickel and cobalt. The introduction of cobalt ions on the montmorillonite carrier yielded the best catalytic results of an order of 85% for co-combustion of high level sulfur hard coal from ‘Bogdanka’ mine. Nickel-montmorillonite DESOX additives also exhibited good efficiency in the limitation of the sulfur dioxide emission into the air. There was prepared and investigated in detail a series of additives with a carrier of developed texture (based upon acid activated montmorillonite and supported with zircon oxide). Ions of nickel, copper and manganese were laid classically by adsorption from water solutions of proper nitrates (CIM). Those catalysts exhibited in the DESOX process a catalytic activity comparable with the materials described before. Sulfate forms obtained after the combustion process with addition of montmorillonite additives are amorphous and very well dispersed. The author wishes to acknowledge the financial support from the Polish Ministry of Science and Higher Education, under project AGH no. 11.11.210.52.118. The author specially thanks A. Marcewicz-Kuba and D. Nazimek for their cooperation and assistance. The author also expresses her thanks to Mr Adam Gaweł from the AGH for the SEM photos and EDXS analysis, and XRD paterns.
abstract: Series of modified with vanadium, cobalt, nickel, manganese and copper montmorillonite were compared as additives for desulfurization during combustion of hard coal. Samples of coal with added montmorillonite were subject to a 2h a flow reactor in the air atmosphere, at 1173K. The weight ratio of montmorillonite:coal was 1:500. Changes in sulfur dioxide contents in flue gasses caused by the additive were investigated by means of an exhaust gas analyzer. During the co-combustion of coal with montmorillonite modified with vanadium was removed 58–84% of SO2 from flue gasses. Application of Co and Ni led to a reduction in SO2 emissions by 35–53% and 83–90%, respectively. For additions of Ni, Cu and Mn was observed to reduce emissions of SO2 by 60–73%. As a result of using diversified preparatory procedures on montmorillonite additives DESOX obtained were materials with different physicochemical properties. Sulfate forms obtained after the combustion process with addition of montmorillonite additives are amorphous and very well dispersed.
title: Application of modified montmorillonite for desulfurization during the combustion of hard coal

full_text: Biomass gasification is a very important technique to apply biomass energy. Air gasification is an easier way to gasify the biomass among the current varied gasification technologies [1]. Air gasification can be realized through the auto-thermal gasification. It is a partial oxidation of the biomass, which provides the needed heat source during the gasification process. This process does not need external heat sources to maintain the gasification temperature and to keep the gasification reactions happening. As a result, the equipments and operation requirements of biomass gasification are relatively lower when air is used as a gasification agent. The calorific value of the syngas produced by air gasification is obviously lower than that of pure oxygen or steam gasification [2–4]. The nitrogen, which enters into the gasification process, may reduce the calorific value of the syngas. The calorific value of air gasification syngas is generally lower than 6MJ/Nm3, which limits the application of air gasification in some industrial fields [5]. Many researchers have investigated on the air gasification, especially on the gasification influence factors and the gasification technologies. They have made a multi-angle analysis of biomass gasification in order to improve the carbon conversion efficiency and the quality of the syngas [6–8]. Reducing the concentration of nonflammable gas and improving the conversion efficiency of biomass are the main purpose of the air gasification researches. Rich oxygen gasification and high temperature gasification are the effective ways. Reducing the concentration of nonflammable gas N2 and CO2 can effectively improve the calorific value of the syngas [4,9]. Recently, many researchers adopt rich oxygen air or pure oxygen as gasification agent to reduce the influence of N2. The rich oxygen gasification, due to the low nitrogen, can effectively improve the syngas calorific value, enhance the gasification rate, increase the gasification temperature and restrain the tar [10,11]. However, the high cost of oxygen generation technology limits the application of rich oxygen gasification. Therefore, the rich oxygen gasification needs a thoroughly economical evaluation before its industrial application. Some researchers focus on the dual-bed gasifier. One bed is blown with air to burn char to produce heat and steam that are used in the other bed to gasify the biomass. N2 and CO2 are the emissions of the first bed and the high calorific value syngas are the emissions of the other bed. This mode of gasification is suitable for a large-scale application [11,12]. High temperature gasification is the other way to get high conversion efficiency and high calorific value syngas. High temperature gasification agents can effectively promote the carbon conversion efficiency [10]. Most researches concentrate on the external heat sources to heat the gasification agents [10–12]. This process needs a lot of energy to heat the agent to a higher temperature, which also needs to be economically evaluated. Without the external heat sources, increasing partial oxidation of the biomass can promote the gasification temperature, but the calorific value of syngas may drop because the air brings more N2 in the syngas. In conclusion, air gasification not only needs to improve gasification temperature, but also reduce air equivalent ratio. An effective way to realize the goal is recycling the sensible heat of the syngas to heat the gasification agent. According to Buljit Buragohain's research, nearly one-fourth of the energy is lost in the forms of sensible heat of syngas and the heat content in the ashes [13]. The sensible heat of syngas can be used to heat the gasification agents and promote the gasification temperature. Most researches focus on the gasification performance of the high temperature gasification agent [3,4], but the gasification agent is heated by some external heat sources not the exhaust heat of the gasifier. In the industrial application, the heat can be recycled to heat the gasification air, such as the downdraft gasifier in Vera's research [14]. The syngas is cooled from 1073K to 905K and the gasification air is heated to 573K. The heat recycle efficiency is not high. Few gasifiers, bench scale or even larger scale, recycle the heat of the syngas [15], for it is difficult to recycle the heat of the syngas with surface-type heat exchanger. This paper focuses on recycling the sensible heat of the syngas to heat gasification agent, improve the gasification temperature, promote the syngas calorific value and reduce the tar content. To recycle the sensible heat of the syngas, the gasifier needs an effective heat exchanger. The heat exchanger must be not only small and high heat exchanging efficiency, but also can bear high temperature. Surface-type heat exchanger is widely used in many fields, but it may not be appropriate to recycle the heat of the gasifier. Firstly, it needs large heat exchange area to reach high heat exchange efficiency. Secondly, it needs a special material to bear the high temperature of the syngas (may be higher than 1173K). Thirdly, the syngas contains water vapor and tar, which may contaminate the heat exchange surface and make the thermal resistance increasing. The technical requirements of gasifier on exchanger volume, the bearing temperature and the preventing contamination make the surface heat exchanger not appropriate for the gasifier. High temperature air combustion technique (HTAC) is widely used in industrial furnace. The key components are the ceramic honeycombs in the system. Two identical ceramic honeycombs are symmetrically installed on the two sides of the combustion chamber, but they are not in the same operation mode at the same time. In one process, one side ceramic honeycomb is used as the air inlet and the other side ceramic honeycomb is used as the exhaust gas outlet. The inlet side ceramic honeycomb releases thermal energy to heat the air and the outlet side ceramic honeycomb absorbs sensible heat of the high temperature exhaust gas. In the next process, a four-way valve switches the inlet and the outlet, and switches the operation mode of the two ceramic honeycombs. As a result, the sensible heat of the exhaust gas can be recycled through the two ceramic honeycombs absorbing the heat and releasing the heat alternately. The ceramic honeycombs as a heat exchanger can reach the requirements of recycling the heat of the gasifier. This paper introduces the ceramic honeycombs into the gasifier to recycle the heat of the syngas. The gasifier with ceramic honeycombs can be stated as the regenerative gasifier. The layout of the regenerative gasifier is different from HTAC. In HTAC, the fuel is generally gas fuel or atomized liquid fuel. This gasifier is used to gasify the solid biomass; the scheme of the gasifier needs to be newly designed. The scheme of the regenerative gasifier is shown in Fig. 1 . The biomass is fed from the top of the gasifier and moves down into the main gasification zone at the lower part of the gasifier. Two ceramic honeycombs are symmetrically installed on the two sides of the gasification zone. The two ceramic honeycombs provide the high temperature gasification agent and vent the syngas alternatively. It is necessary to prevent the ash or the carbon particles going into the ceramic honeycombs. Therefore, two porous baffles are set on the two sides of the gasification zone in this gasifier. The gasification residue is expelled from the bottom of the gasifier. In HTAC, the operation mode of the two ceramic honeycombs can be switched directly for the newly entered air can mix with the residual flue gas in the tube. However, in this gasifier, air enters into the gasifier through one ceramic honeycomb and flammable syngas vents from the other ceramic honeycomb. When the four-way valve shuts off the pathways, some syngas remains in the outlet side tube. If the four-way valve switches air and syngas pathways at this time, the residual syngas in the tube will mix with the newly entered air. The mixture of flammable syngas and air enter into the high temperature gasification zone may be ignited and cause deflagration, which may increase the pressure of the reaction chamber. In order to prevent the situation mentioned above, this paper introduces a tube purging system to prevent the air mixing with the syngas. As shown in Fig. 1, this gasifier has been set two four-way valve A and B. Valve A is used to control the air and the syngas pathways and valve B is used to control the steam and the syngas pathways. When it is the time to switch the air and the syngas pathways, it needs to purge the residual syngas in the tube firstly. The operating procedures are described below. For example, when air enters the gasifier from the left side tube and the syngas vents through the right side tube, the gasification direction is from left to right. It needs two steps to switch the gasification direction (from right to left). Firstly, turning the valve A to shutoff the pathways in valve A, the air and the syngas cannot go through valve A. Secondly, turning the valve B to connect the pathways in valve B, steam enters the right side tube and pushes the residual syngas in the right side tube into the gasifier. At the same time, the newly produced syngas goes through the left side tube and vents from the valve B. After the residual gas in the right side tube is purged by the steam, turn the valve A to connect the air pathway. The air enters the gasifier from the right side tube to maintain the auto-thermal gasification. At this time, the state of the valve B determines that the process is air gasification or air/steam gasification. It is similar to the other gasification direction. The gasification system is shown in Fig. 2 . Biomass is fed from a hopper above the gasifier. Air is supplied with an air compressor and measured with an air flow meter. A peristaltic pump is used to supply water and control the flow rate. Water is evaporated completely and superheated in a constant temperature heater. Moreover, the steam is sent to the four-way valve B through a trace heating tube. When the pressure of the steam is higher than 0.2MPa, a relief valve can open to control the steam pressure. The tar sampling system is extracting the syngas close to the ceramic honeycombs when syngas just vents from the ceramic honeycombs. The advantages of the heat recycle system in the gasifier are shown as follow. Firstly, ceramic honeycombs can effectively recycle heat to rise up the temperature of the gasification agent. High temperature agent can effectively improve the gasification efficiency, improve flammable composition and reduce the tar concentration in the syngas. Secondly, the special porous structure of ceramic honeycombs makes it not only a heat exchanger, but also an ideal catalysts carrier. The ceramic-honeycombs-type automobile catalyst is wildly used in the engine exhaust system of an automobile [16]. Therefore, this paper uses this kind of catalysts carrier to deal with the tar in the syngas. When the syngas goes through the ceramic honeycombs, the catalysts loaded on the inside surfaces of ceramic honeycomb holes can crack the tar at appropriate temperature. The ceramic honeycombs are in a wide range of temperature, which means the catalysts can do the best work. Thirdly, the operation mode of the ceramic honeycombs is helpful to prevent the deposition of carbon on the catalysts. When catalysts crack tar, the catalytic activity of the catalysts may reduce for the carbon deposition on catalyst surface. According to the current literatures, many researchers have developed a lot of coking resistance catalysts to deal with tar [17]. In this gasifier, the common catalysts may be inactivated by the carbon deposition, but the catalysts can be regenerated when switching gasification direction. The switching operation mode of the ceramic honeycombs makes the catalysts contact the syngas and the gasification agent alternatively. The gasification agent reacts with the carbon deposition and regenerates the catalysts. Therefore, the catalytic activity can be longer and stable [18]. This paper uses ceramic honeycombs produced by cordierite (2MgO·2Al2O3·5SiO2) and adopts the sol–gel method to deposit Al2O3 coated on cordierite ceramic honeycomb [19,20]. After the high-surface area Al2O3 coating is finished, the ceramic honeycombs are impregnated in the aqueous solution of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O for 2h. Ceramic honeycombs are dried at 383K for 12h and calcinated at 1073K for 6h under the air atmosphere [21,22]. Loading of Ni is expressed in a weight ratio of Ni/(Ni+Supporter). Components and component concentrations reflect the quality of the syngas and the performance of the gasifier. A small amount of syngas is sampled and analyzed. The component concentrations fluctuate with time for the gasification agent temperature fluctuates with time. Therefore, the sampling system extracts the syngas for several minutes with a small flow rate. The sampled gas is collected in a gasbag. Before the gas is analyzed in gas chromatography, tar and water vapor are removed with low-temperature treatment and drying treatment. The concentrations of H2, CO, CH4, CO2 and N2 in the syngas are gotten through a gas chromatography (SC2000) equipped with TDX-01column (3mm×2m) and TCD. Tar is an unwanted gasification product. It needs special technology to deal with in the gasification process [23,24]. The concentration of the tar in the syngas has a great impact on the application of the syngas. This paper adopts the tar sampling system shown in Fig. 3 . The syngas is extracted by a vacuum pump and measured by a volume flow measurement. The tar is absorbed in a series of impinger bottles (400ml) containing isopropanol (200ml), which is considered to be the most suitable solvent for tar absorption [25]. The impinger bottles are all immersed in ice water. The syngas sampling point is close to the outlet of ceramic honeycombs, for the tar can be sampled with little condensation. The gas sampling pipeline is electric heated to keep the gas temperature. A particle filter in the pipeline is used to get rid of the particles in the syngas. The syngas goes through the ceramic honeycombs intermittently; the sampling system must extract the syngas at the time when syngas vents from the ceramic honeycombs. After sampling is completed, the isopropanol is collected. The syngas contains some water vapor, which is absorbed in the impinger bottles and dissolved in the isopropanol. It needs to remove the water and isopropanol before the measurement of the tar mass can be done gravimetrically [26]. This paper dries the solution in a sealed drier (308K, with desiccant to keep air humidity less than 15%) for 48h, then gets rid of isopropanol with mean of solvent distillation (353K), and measures the tar mass at last. The gasification is discontinuous and unsteady because the gasification direction must be switched to recycle the heat. Gasification agent temperature decreases with time for ceramic honeycombs release heat to the agent. Therefore, the center temperature of the gasifier fluctuates with the time. The center temperature of the gasifier is tested by a thermocouple and recorded by a data acquisition system (DAS). This paper analyzes the temperature data and gets the mean temperature of each gasification process. This paper has also detected the temperature of syngas vented from ceramic honeycombs. This temperature also fluctuates with the time, and the temperature cannot be too high (>513K), because the four-way valve might be damaged when the syngas is higher than 513K. Another reason to monitor the temperature is that the temperature reflects the heat recycle efficiency. The high outlet temperature means low heat recycle efficiency. Meanwhile, the temperature cannot be too low (<463K) because the gasifier must prevent tar condensation in the ceramic honeycombs and the gas tubes. According to the thermal power and the heat capacity of the ceramic honeycombs, the switching time is set 40 seconds and the syngas temperature can meet the requirements. This paper uses pine as experiment samples, chemical analysis and calorific value of the pine are shown in Table 1 . Steam is necessary in this gasification system because the steam must be used to purge the tubes. According to the tube size, about 0.15kg/h (283K, 0.1MPa, 5 times of the tube volume) will be enough to purge the tubes. The pine is made into pellets of 0.5cm in mean diameter. The pine pellets are fed from a hopper above the gasifier. The feeding rate of the pine pellets is 5kg/h. Air flow rate is 4.8Nm3/h–8.4Nm3/h and the steam flow rate is 0.15kg/h–3.2kg/h. The gasification direction switching time is fixed at 40 seconds in all the experiments. The loading of Ni catalysts is 4.5% (wt.%). ER influences not only the gasification temperature, but also the syngas. The main reactions of biomass gasification are listed as follows: (1) Oxidation  :  C ( s ) + O 2 , ( g ) → CO 2 , ( g ) △ H R = − 4 0 5 : 8 kJ / mol (2) C ( s ) + 1 / 2O 2 , ( g ) → CO ( g ) △ H R = − 11 0 : 7 kJ / mol (3) Water − gas :  C ( s ) + H 2 O ( g ) ⇌ H 2 , ( g ) + CO ( g ) △ H R = + 131 . 5  kJ / mol (4) Boudouard :  C ( s ) + CO 2 , ( g ) ⇌ 2CO ( g ) △ H R = + 172 . 5 kJ / mol (5) Water − gas shift :  CO ( g ) + H 2 O ( g ) ⇌ CO 2 , ( g ) + H 2 , ( g ) △ H R = − 4 0. 9  kJ / mol (6) Steam reforming :  CH 4 , ( g ) + H 2 O ( g ) ⇌ CO ( g ) + 3H 2 , ( g ) △ H R = + 2 0 6 .0  kJ / mol (7) Methanation :  C ( s ) + H 2 , ( g ) ⇌ CH 4 , ( g ) △ H R = − 87 . 4  kJ / mol Fig. 4 shows that temperature (point C in Fig. 1) is greatly influenced by ER. Increasing ER enhances the oxidation reaction (reactions (1),(2)), which produces more heat and increases the center temperature. The temperature is not linearly proportional with ER. Firstly, the increasing temperature may lead to much heat loss of the gasifier. Secondly, the higher ER means more air and syngas, which reduce the heat recycle efficiency of the ceramic honeycombs. Thirdly, higher gasification temperature strengthens endothermic reactions and inhibits exothermic reactions. The main exothermic reactions, such as the water–gas reaction, the boudouard reaction and the steam reforming reaction, absorb heat and inhibit the temperature rising of the gasification zone. The temperature can reach 1300K with ER=0.35. Fig. 5 shows the effect of ER on the concentration of the main gases. H2 concentration decreases with ER because the oxygen may react with the H2. Higher gasifying temperature and low steam concentration inhibit the water–gas shift reaction and enhance the boudouard reaction rate, this is why the CO is the main product as the H2 and CO2 reduce their yield. At the same time, the low steam concentration can also inhibit water–gas reaction and steam reforming reaction. In addition, the gasification temperature is not high enough to ignite the steam reforming reaction [27,28]. The methanation reaction is an exothermic reaction, but it does not play an important role because the temperature cannot convert CH4 into H2 and C quickly [29]. The decrease of CH4 can be regarded as CH4 is partially combusted and produces more CO. N2 concentration slightly increases with ER, which may reduce the concentration of the flammable gas and the calorific value. Another important reason for the calorific value decrease is the reduction in H2 and CH4 with ER. In some high temperature gasification researches, the calorific value of syngas simply increases with the agent temperature, if the agent is heated by some external heat sources [3,4]. In this gasifier, the heat recycle system can promote the agent temperature. As a result, this gasifier can reach higher reaction temperature and higher calorific value with a relatively lower ER. Fig. 6 shows that the calorific value of the syngas can reach 5.4MJ/Nm3, which is higher than the gasifier with no heat recycle. Steam is another gasification agent in this gasification system. This paper studies air/steam gasification with S/C range from 0.04 to 0.9. Fig. 7 shows that the center temperature of the gasification zone decreases with S/C. This is because steam absorbs more heat as S/C increases. In addition, high steam concentration enhances the water–gas shift reaction, which absorbs heat and decreases the temperature. Another reason mentioned in subsection 4.1 is that the increasing flow rate of gasification agent (air and steam) and syngas (syngas and unreacted steam) reduces the heat recycle efficiency. The gasifier loses more sensible heat. It can be seen that the temperature drops about 60K when S/C increases from 0.04 to 0.9 in Fig. 7. Fig. 8 shows the effect of S/C on the concentration of the main gases. H2 concentration increases with S/C. High steam concentration and dropping temperature strengthen the water–gas shift reaction and produce more CO2 and H2. Meanwhile, temperature also affects the boudouard reaction. Steam concentration and temperature both affect water–gas reaction. The joint impact is that CO concentration first lightly increased and then decreased. Steam reforming reaction and methanation reaction have little effect on the result because the temperature is not high enough [27–29]. Fig. 9 shows the calorific value of the syngas of air/steam gasification. The calorific value first lightly increases and then decreases, which can also ascribe to the joint impact of S/C and temperature. Small amount of steam strengthens the gasification reactions and promotes calorific value, but a large amount of steam decreases the gasification temperature and inhibits the gasification reactions. The best S/C is about 0.3 because the calorific value of syngas reaches the highest 5.6MJ/Nm3. Table 2 shows the tar concentration in the syngas with different gasification conditions. Ni catalysts loaded on the ceramic honeycombs can effectively reduce the tar concentration in the syngas. The tar concentration without catalysts is about 1g/Nm3, but with the catalyst, the tar concentration is about 0.5g/Nm3. Tests 3 and 6 are with the same gasification conditions. The results, by contrast, show that the catalysts crack more than half of the tar exhausted from the gasifier. 573K–873K is the favorable temperature range for Ni catalysts to crack tar [21,30]. In the gasifier, the temperature of the ceramic honeycombs is one side high and the other side low. The higher temperature side is close to the gasification zone and the temperature is higher than 1000K. The lower temperature side is about 473K. The ceramic honeycombs can provide the favorable temperature range for Ni catalysts to crack tar. This regenerative gasifier can recycle the sensible heat of the syngas to heat the gasification agent. Compared with no heat recycle gasifier, this gasifier can effectively promote the gasification temperature and reduce ER. It can effectively promote the calorific value of the syngas. At the same time, the higher gasification temperature and catalysts loaded on the ceramic honeycombs can reduce the tar concentration in the syngas and further improve the quality of the syngas. This paper develops a generative gasifier and completes some experiments of the gasification. The conclusions are as follows: (1) This paper applies ceramic honeycombs to recycle the heat of syngas to heat the gasification agents. The gasifier can reach higher gasification temperature with lower ER. This paper introduces the tube purging system to prevent the air mixing with the syngas. Two four-way valves are set to provide the air and steam respectively. (2) The recycled heat promotes the gasification temperature and promotes the endothermic reaction. More flammable syngas is produced in this gasifier. The calorific value of the syngas can reach 5.4MJ/Nm3. (3) The steam is produced in an electric steam generator and is used as gasification agent. The gasification temperature drops with S/C. The calorific value first lightly increases and then decreases. The best steam to carbon ratio is 0.3, and the calorific value of syngas reaches the highest 5.6MJ/Nm3. (4) This paper introduces the tar catalytic cracking system. The ceramic honeycombs are used as catalysts carrier. The combination of the gasification chamber and the catalytic converter make the gasifier more compact. The ceramic honeycombs can provide the best catalytic cracking temperature for the Ni catalysts. The gasification mode prevents the carbon deposition and keeps the catalytic activity of the catalysts for a longer time. This paper applies ceramic honeycombs to recycle the heat of syngas to heat the gasification agents. The gasifier can reach higher gasification temperature with lower ER. This paper introduces the tube purging system to prevent the air mixing with the syngas. Two four-way valves are set to provide the air and steam respectively. The recycled heat promotes the gasification temperature and promotes the endothermic reaction. More flammable syngas is produced in this gasifier. The calorific value of the syngas can reach 5.4MJ/Nm3. The steam is produced in an electric steam generator and is used as gasification agent. The gasification temperature drops with S/C. The calorific value first lightly increases and then decreases. The best steam to carbon ratio is 0.3, and the calorific value of syngas reaches the highest 5.6MJ/Nm3. This paper introduces the tar catalytic cracking system. The ceramic honeycombs are used as catalysts carrier. The combination of the gasification chamber and the catalytic converter make the gasifier more compact. The ceramic honeycombs can provide the best catalytic cracking temperature for the Ni catalysts. The gasification mode prevents the carbon deposition and keeps the catalytic activity of the catalysts for a longer time. The experiments were conducted at the Low Grade Energy Technology and System Laboratory (Chongqing University), Power Engineering Department, Chongqing University. The experiments were supported by a 211 “Third Period” Construction Project (s-09101).
abstract: Ceramic honeycombs are applied in the biomass regenerative gasifier to recycle the sensible heat of the syngas to heat the gasification agents, and ceramic honeycombs are used as Ni catalysts carrier to crack the tar in the syngas. Experiments on gasification of pine with different air equivalence ratio (ER) and steam/carbon mole ratio(S/C) have been carried out in this paper. Experimental results show that the heat recycle of the ceramic honeycombs can effectively promotes the gasification temperature and the syngas calorific value, and the Ni catalysts can effectively crack tar. Meanwhile, the gasification method of the gasifier can keep the activity of the catalysts for a long time.
title: High temperature gasification of woody biomass using regenerative gasifier
