full_text: The duple threats from the shortage of fossil fuels and the increasing pollution during their utilization urge the exploration of new energy source [1]. Biomass, the sole renewable carbon-containing source on the earth and characterized by carbon neutrality or even carbon negativity, has been attracted a lot of research interest [2,3]. In view of the long-term dependence of human being on liquid fuels, conversion of biomass to bio-oil is extremely preferred [4,5]. Among various biomass conversion technologies, pyrolysis is the simplest method to rapidly obtain bio-oil from nearly all types of biomass [6]. In the past few decades, most researchers have been committed to maximizing the yield of bio-oil. It is reported that the yield of bio-oil can be as high as 75 wt% under an optimized condition [7]. However, the resulting bio-oil with such high yield can be just used as a low-grade fuel to be burned for heat or steam generation. The most important reason why bio-oil is difficult to use is mainly because of its high oxygen content, which can reach 50% in some bio-oils obtained from fast pyrolysis [8]. In addition, oxygen is usually present as polar compounds in bio-oil, such as aldehydes, ketones, sugars, carboxylic acids, and phenols, and most of them have propensities to form coke during the downstream applications [9,10]. Hydrodeoxygenation (HDO) is a highly effective method to remove oxygen as water from bio-oil. However, the harsh conditions (high temperature and pressure) as well as high costs of catalysts (e.g., Pt, Pd, Ru, etc.) and hydrogen used for this route severely restrict its application [11,12]. Alternatively, catalytic pyrolysis at atmospheric pressure can reduce oxygen content in bio-oil to a certain extent with little hydrogen consumption. Although the resulting bio-oils derived from catalytic pyrolysis still miss the requirements of standard liquid fuels, they are quite suitable as feedstocks for some popular refining processes, such as dual-stage hydrotreating of bio-oil [13,14]. Undoubtedly, the catalyst plays the most important role in the above-mentioned catalytic pyrolysis. Zeolites, the most common catalysts, especially ZSM-5, enable the production of hydrocarbons from biomass pyrolysis because of its unique pore structures [15–18]. The low yield of liquid products and serious coke formation during reactions, however, are the main drawback for zeolite catalysts [19]. Ceria (CeO2) has been employed for oxygen elimination from a series of carbonyl and hydroxyl compounds, such as acids, aldehydes, and alcohols, to produce ketones. In this regard, Almutairi et al. [20] compared the performance of several metal oxides during the ketonization of acetic acid, and concluded that CeO2 had the highest catalytic activity. This was related to the abundant weak base sites on the surface. Orozco et al. [21] proposed that the ketonization of aldehydes over CeO2 was through an acidic intermediate pathway, rather than an aldol condensation mechanism. Shao et al. [22] proved that CeO2 was effective for producing ketones from acetic acid, n-butyraldehyde, n-butanol and ethyl acetate. CeO2 has a higher lattice energy, and thus the catalytic ketonization reactions of carbonyl groups could not only occur on its surface but also in the bulk, giving higher activity [23]. The ketonization of those multi-functionalized compounds in bio-oil significantly improves the stability of bio-oil. Moreover, the ketonization pathways are usually based on condensation of two or more small molecules (e.g. precursors of gas products), suggesting that more liquid products can be obtained via the ketonization of cracked small molecules from bio-oil [24]. Taken this into consideration, CeO2 as a catalyst for catalytic pyrolysis of biomass has high potentials in simultaneous production of high quality and high yield of bio-oil. The samples for ketonization over CeO2 are generally carbonyl group containing compounds, where α-H is believed to be responsible as the key to initiate the reactions by combining with the basic sites on the catalyst surface [25]. However, rare studies can be found for the deoxidation of non-carbonyl compounds, such as sugars which are very abundant in bio-oil derived from cellulose and hemicellulose pyrolysis. In theory, there are also extraordinarily rich α-H available in sugars. The polarization of non-carbonyls could be quite different from that of carbonyls, which might also influence the reaction with nano-CeO2. In this study, xylose derived from hemicellulose was thus chosen as the sample for ketonization over nano-CeO2 with different catalyst to feedstock ratios at varying pyrolysis temperatures, and the mechanism of the catalytic pyrolysis was also proposed and discussed. Raw material D-(+)-xylose (CAS# 58-86-6, purity 98%) was purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. Nano-CeO2 powder (purity = 99.99%, average particle size = 10 nm, specific surface area = 35–70 m2·g−1) obtained from US Research Nanomaterials Inc. was used as catalyst. To reduce the resistance of the catalyst bed to the volatiles, particle CeO2 was prepared using a simple method. In particular, CeO2 was dissolved in double distilled water (DDW), and the suspension obtained was heated at 60 °C and stirred continuously until the formation of a slurry. Then the slurry was transferred to an oven and dried at 105 °C for 24 h. The acquired bulk solid was smashed and sieved to produce fine particles within 0.18–0.25 mm (60–80 mesh). The ketonization of xylose and the subsequent online vapor analysis were carried out in a pyrolyzer (Py) coupled with a gas chromatograph/mass spectrometry (GC/MS). For each catalytic pyrolysis experiment in the Py reactor, a quartz tube (i.d. = 2 mm, length = 24 mm) was used as sample holder, as shown in Fig. 1 . Xylose with a mass of 0.50 ± 0.02 mg was placed at the center of the quartz tube, and an appropriate amount of CeO2 was located evenly on both sides of the sample. Using this method, sufficient catalytic reactions could be ensured, and the gas channeling could be avoided. Quartz wool was used to separate the catalyst from the raw material as well as to fix the position of CeO2. The quartz tube was heated by a platinum coil assembled on a pyroprobe at an extremely high heating rate (nominally 20,000 °C/s). Before each pyrolysis run, the pyroprobe was heated externally from ambient temperature to 270 °C at ~10 °C/s. Temperatures of the valve box and the transferring line were both set at 275 °C. Volatiles from Py reactor was injected directly into the inlet (maintained at 280 °C) of the GC/MS. Separation of the volatiles was performed using a VF-1701 ms capillary column (30 m in length, 0.25 mm in i.d., 0.25 μm in film thickness). The oven was initially preserved at 40 °C for 2 min. Then the temperature was increased to 200 °C in a ramp of 5 °C/min, and further to 280 °C in 10 °C/min. The final temperature was held for 3 min. The separated gas was introduced into the MS through an interface kept at 280 °C. MS was operated in the EI (electron ionization) mode, where the voltage of the ionization source was 70 eV. The temperatures of the ionization source and the quadrupole were 230 °C and 150 °C, respectively. Analysis of the obtained total ion chromatogram (TIC) was performed using the NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) database. To investigate the catalytic performance of nano-CeO2, catalytic pyrolysis of xylose was carried out at different CFRs (catalyst to feedstock ratios, determined by volume of nano-CeO2 to mass of xylose ratios, in μL/mg). The pyrolysis temperature was 600 °C. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the non-catalytic pyrolysis of xylose (CFR = 0:1) produced aldehydes, furans and sugars as the major product groups, with the peak areas of 0.67 × 109, 0.61 × 109 and 0.80 × 109, respectively. The peak area of ketones was as low as 0.24 × 109. Fig. 2(b) indicates that the typical components of these three groups were hydroxy acetaldehyde, furfural, and xylose, respectively. These results are also supported by Hu et al. [26], who reported that the main pyrolysis products of xylose were hydroxy-acetaldehyde and furfural. Fig. 2(b) also shows a relatively great peak area of xylose (0.68 × 109), indicating the incomplete decomposition of xylose at 600 °C because of an extremely short residence time. Regarding the selectivities of ketonic products plotted in Fig. 2(c), 2-butanone and 1-hydroxy-2-propanone accounted for 97.63% in total when xylose was pyrolyzed without catalyst. The above results indicate that xylose is relatively difficult to decompose at 600 °C. Moreover, the decomposition of xylose produces multi-functional oxygenated compounds like hydroxy acetaldehyde and furfural, resulting in a low quality of the pyrolysis liquid products. Upon the addition of nano-CeO2, as illustrated in Fig. 2(a), the peak area of ketones increased dramatically, while those of aldehydes, furans and sugars decreased obviously. The highest peak area of ketones was observed at a CFR of 5:1, showing a 5-fold increase when comparing with the non-catalytic pyrolysis. Conversely, with the increase in CFR from 0:1 to 20:1, the peak areas of aldehydes, furans and sugars decreased gradually from 0.67 × 109, 0.61 × 109 and 0.80 × 109 to 0.18 × 109, 0.28 × 109 and 0.26 × 109, respectively. Interestingly, the peak area of acid increased from 0.07 × 109 for non-catalytic pyrolysis to 0.17 × 109 for CFR = 3:1, and then decreased to (0.04–0.07) × 109 at higher CeO2 dosages. Besides, a small amount of hydrocarbons began to show up when CFR was 5:1, and elevated to ~0.09 × 109 as CFR increased to 20:1. The presence of hydrocarbons indicates a complete deoxygenation of the oxygenated compounds. This may be one of the reasons leading to the linear reduction in ketone yield as CeO2 to xylose ratio rises from 5:1 to 20:1. With respect to the detailed components (Fig. 2(b)), the peak area of xylose decreased to the minimum of 0.20 × 109 along with the increasing CFR. It is indicated that xylose can be effectively decomposed by CeO2, especially at higher dosages. Similarly, hydroxy acetaldehyde and furfural were also converted by using CeO2. As a result, the peak areas of these two compounds decreased from 0.42 × 109 and 0.49 × 109 for non-catalytic pyrolysis to 0.07 × 109 and 0.17 × 109 for CFR = 20:1, respectively. On the contrary, as the CFR rose from 0 to 20:1, the peak areas of acetone and 2-butanone climbed up from 0 and 0.17 × 109 to the maximum of 0.31 × 109 (CFR = 10:1) and 0.32 × 109 (CFR = 8:1), respectively. Based on the above results, it could be reasonably speculated that hydroxy acetaldehyde and furfural are mainly converted to mono-functional linear ketones—especially acetone and 2-butanone—over the catalysis of nano-CeO2. The deoxygenation ability of nano-CeO2 is verified by the dramatic reduction of xylose and furfural and the remarkable increment of mono-functional linear ketones. Fig. 2(c) illustrates the selectivity of ketonic products at different CFRs. As shown, the use of a small amount of CeO2 (CFR = 3:1) led to a significant decrease in the selectivity of 2-butanone from 70.91% for non-catalytic pyrolysis to 24.60%. This is because that more ketone compounds are detected for catalytic pyrolysis. Further increase in CFR resulted in a minor fluctuation of the selectivity of 2-butanone between 23.62% and 26.47%. Meanwhile, when CFR increased from 0 to 20:1, the selectivity of 1-hydroxy-2-pentanone descended from 26.71% to 7.15%. In contrast, the selectivity of acetone increased along with the rise of CFR. For the other major ketonic products, their selectivities increased as CFR increased from 0 to 5:1 and varied slightly at higher CFRs. The changes in selectivities of ketonic compounds imply that the CeO2 dosage has a minor effect on the selectivities of most ketonic compounds (except acetone and 1-hydroxy-2-propanone) when CFR exceeds 5:1. A CFR of 10:1 preferably produced higher selectivity of acetone, which was beneficial to selective production of mono-functional linear ketones. As has been summarized in previous literature, there are two key properties which contribute the most to the ketonization ability of CeO2 [25]. The first is the abundant surface basic sites, which will initiate the ketonization reaction by abstracting α-H from the reactant. Then the exceptional redox properties of CeO2 combine the generated proton and the hydroxyl group from another reactant, and thus maintain the ketonization activity. Temperature plays a key role during the pyrolysis process. Taking this into consideration, the catalytic pyrolysis of xylose at different temperatures within 500–700 °C was performed at a CFR of 10:1. As displayed in Fig. 3(a), the peak area of ketones reached the highest of 1.21 × 109 at 550 °C and then slightly reduced with further increase in pyrolysis temperature. This result is relevant to the elimination of multi-functional ketonic products (shown in part in Fig. 3(b)), as will be discussed in the coming part. The peak area of furans decreased from 0.38 × 109 to 0.22 × 109 when the pyrolysis temperature increased from 500 to 600 °C and kept steady at higher temperatures. Besides, the yield of aldehydes increased from 0.12 × 109 at 500 °C to 0.19 × 109 at 550 °C, and decreased slightly to 0.15 × 109 as pyrolysis temperature rose to 700 °C. Moreover, it was noteworthy that the peak area of hydrocarbons increased with the rise of temperature, signifying that high temperature was beneficial to the thorough deoxygenation. The distribution of major products shown in Fig. 3(b) illustrated relatively high peak areas of furfural (0.21 × 109) and xylose (0.26 × 109) at 500 °C. The increase in pyrolysis temperature resulted in the reduction of these two components. Meanwhile, the peak area of acetaldehyde also showed a decreasing trend with the increasing temperature. Correspondingly, the peak areas of acetone and 2-butanone reached the highest at 600 °C (0.31 × 109) and 550 °C (0.27 × 109), respectively. A minor drop of 2-butanone was observed when the pyrolysis temperature exceeded 550 °C. Moreover, higher peak areas of 3-pentanone (0.11–0.15 × 109) were also found at elevated pyrolysis temperatures (550–700 °C) in comparison to that at 500 °C (0.05 × 109). Based on these results, it could be concluded that the pyrolysis temperature of 550–600 °C is optimized for producing mono-functional linear ketones. Fig. 3(c) exhibits that the effects of pyrolysis temperature on the selectivity of ketonic compounds were not as intensive as those of CFR (shown in Fig. 2(c)). The most significant changes were observed for 3-pentanone and multi-functional 2-cyclopenten-1-ones. With the rise of pyrolysis temperature, the selectivity of the former product increased from 5.56% (500 °C) to a maximum of 14.60% (650 °C), while that of the later compounds decreased from 18.83% (500 °C) to a minimum of 10.50% (600 °C) and kept constant at higher temperatures. The decrement in selectivity of multi-functional 2-cyclopenten-1-ones further proves that higher temperature is beneficial to removing the oxygenated functional groups from ketonic products. However, as one of the prominent defunctionalized products, 2-cyclopen-1-one showed a marginally change in selectivity within the studied temperature range. This result may imply that 2-cyclopenten-1-one has been transformed to other products through ring-opening, CC cleavage, etc. According to the results obtained in this study and other literature reports, we summarized the reaction pathway of major products during catalytic pyrolysis of xylose over CeO2. Fig. 4 depicted the reaction routes of major products including acetone, 2-butanone, 3-pentanone and furfural. As has been proven in literature [27] and in this study, xylose could be degraded into multifunctional products like hydroxy acetaldehyde and furfural. With the addition of CeO2, these compounds could be converted to mono-functional products through ketonization. During this process, three principal reaction pathways could be identified. Pathway 1: According to Wang et al. [9,10], xylose will most likely undergo ring-opening reactions at the C(1)-O position due to its lowest Mayer bond order value. As has been verified by Huang et al. [28], the ring-opening intermediate undergoes a complete ring-opening reaction, producing small-molecule products formaldehyde and ethylene glycol. The later product will play a crucial role in the subsequent reactions. Pathway 2: Ring opening of xylose produces an unstable intermediate (an acyclic carbonyl compound). Hu et al. [26] stated that this intermediate underwent CC bond scission and produces 2,3-dihydroxy propanal and ethylene glycol. These two compounds play as important intermediates and provide oxygen-containing functional groups in the following reaction chains. Orozco et al. [21,29] revealed that over the catalysis of nano-CeO2, the aldehyde compound could be easily oxidized to the corresponding acidic product, then the acid would undergo both cross- and homo-ketonization reactions. On one hand, the cross-ketonization of 2,3-dihydroxy-propanoic acid with hydroxy acetic acid will produce multi-hydroxy 2-butanone. This product could further be hydrogenated to generate 2-butanone. On the other hand, the homo-ketonization of 2,3-dihydroxy-propanoic acid produces multi-functional 3-pentanone, which could be further converted into 3-pentanone. According to the book chapter by Dooley [30], ethylene glycol, produced during the initial steps of pathway 2, could be converted to glycolaldehyde via isomerization and further to hydroxyacetic acid via oxidation with water. Then this acidic compound could be converted into 1,3-dihydroxyacetone through a classic homo-ketonization pathway. The followed hydrogenation of dihydroxyacetone will eventually lead to the production of acetone. Pathway 3: The unstable acyclic containing-carbonyl isomer formed in pathway 2 could also be dehydrated to multi-hydroxy aldehyde intermediate with CC bonds, which has been confirmed by Huang et al. [28]. Li et al. [31] further discovered that the intermediate would dehydrate between the hydroxy on C(2) and the hydrogen atom of the hydroxy group on C(5), and then cyclized through C(2)-C(5) bond formation. As a result, furfural is produced. Through the above mechanism, products distribution under different conditions can be explained. With the increase in CeO2 dosage or at appropriate temperatures, pathway 2 is promoted. As a result, the yield of linear ketones, including acetone, 2-butanone and 3-pentanone, increases obviously. Meanwhile, the competing reaction pathways 1 and 3 are inhibited by CeO2, leading to the reduced yields of furfural and formaldehyde. From the above mechanism, the multi-functional xylose could be converted to mono-functional linear ketones, implying an outstanding deoxygenation ability of nano-CeO2. Meanwhile, the short-chain intermediates (including C2-C3 aldehydes and alcohols) have been reformed to C3-C5 ketones, indicating an increase in carbon chain length. Both above advantages will contribute greatly to the improvement of bio-oil quality. In this study, the ketonization of xylose over nano-CeO2 was investigated. The addition of nano-CeO2 effectively converted the multi-functional oxygenated products (hydroxy-acetaldehyde and furfural) from xylose pyrolysis into mono-functional linear ketones (principally acetone and 2-butanone). As a result, the yield of ketones increased to the highest at a catalyst to xylose ratio of 5:1, while those of aldehydes, furans and sugars decreased with the increasing catalyst dosage. In terms of pyrolysis temperature, the highest yield of ketones was obtained at 550 °C, where linear ketones were also the most abundant. Moreover, the carbon chain length of C2-C3 products from xylose pyrolysis was also increased to C3-C5 by nano-CeO2. Based on the experimental results, mechanism on the theoretical reaction pathways was also proposed. In this mechanism, xylose is first decomposed into intermediates including alcohols and aldehydes. Then, these intermediates are further converted into ketones via homo- and cross-ketonization reactions. Results from this study provide a solid foundation to produce valuable fuels and chemicals from renewable biomass resources. Kuan Ding: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Hao Zhou: Investigation, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Yiling Wan: Data curation, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Yong Huang: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Yuming Zhang: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Xun Hu: Writing - review & editing. Hong Zhang: Resources, Supervision. Shu Zhang: Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition. None. This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants 51876093) and an internationally collaborative project (BRICS2019-040) under BRICS STI Framework Programme with government funding organizations of Brazil CNPq (402849/2019-1), Russia RFBR (19-58-80016), India DST (CRG/2018/004610, DST/TDT/TDP-011/2017), China MOST (2018YFE0183600), and South Africa NRF (BRIC190321424123).
abstract: Ketonization of biomass-derived oxygenates provides an efficient way for bio-oil upgrading. However, little information on the ketonization of oxygen-rich sugars has been reported, leaving the deep deoxygenation of biomass an unsolved challenge. To address the problem, this paper investigated the catalytic pyrolysis of xylose over nano-CeO2 using an analytical pyrolyzer. Results showed that upon the addition of CeO2, multi-functional oxygenated compounds from xylose pyrolysis were majorly reformed to mono-functional ketonic products, resulting in a 5-fold increase in peak area of ketones. Among them, acetone and 2-butanone presented the highest selectivity of 51% with the CeO2 to xylose ratio of 10:1 at the temperature of 600 °C. Meanwhile, the short-chain oxygenates, i.e. C2-C3 aldehydes and alcohols, had been converted into C3-C5 ketones, indicating an outstanding ability of chain-increasing over CeO2. Both the catalyst dosage and the pyrolysis temperature play a vital role during the ketonization process. The reaction mechanism was proposed according to the experimental results, in which three major reaction pathways were analyzed. This study demonstrates that ketonization of biomass-derived sugars is a promising method for oxygen removal and carbon chain increase, thus improving the bio-oil quality.
title: Ketonization of xylose over CeO2 to produce mono-functional ketones

steps.method_details: CeO2 was dissolved in double distilled water
the suspension was heated and stirred continuously until formation of a slurry
the slurry was transferred to an oven and dried
the acquired bulk solid was smashed
sieved to produce fine particles within 0.18–0.25 mm (60–80 mesh)
xylose was placed at the center of the quartz tube, and an appropriate amount of CeO2 was located evenly on both sides of the sample
quartz wool was used to separate the catalyst from the raw material as well as to fix the position of CeO2

full_text: The objective of this research is to develop improved transportation fuels by producing higher ethers and oxygenates for use as additives and blending agents in reformulated gasolines and ultra-clean diesel fuels. The addition of oxygenates to gasoline and diesel fuels raises combustion temperatures, improves engine efficiencies, and causes the fuel to burn more cleanly, resulting in lower levels of carbon monoxide and unburned hydrocarbons in the exhaust stream [1]. Ethers are the favored oxygen-containing additives for reformulated gasoline since they produce high octane gasolines which burn cleanly. Methyl-tert-butyl ether (MTBE) has been extensively utilized in the fuel oxygenates market for years due mainly to favorable physicochemical properties. However, its significant solubility in water evokes environmental concerns and stimulates the development of alternatives [2]. For the product ethers to be acceptable additives in motor fuels, the ethers must be of sufficient carbon number to boil in the same range as the motor fuel to which they are being added. From an environmental perspective, the ethers should have low water solubility and low vapor pressure. In this study, the ethers selected to be produced were of carbon number C7 and C9. The focus of this research is to develop a single-step liquid phase process to produce higher ethers from synthesis gas. The conventional method of producing ethers is first to produce methanol from synthesis gas and then to convert the methanol to an ether. For example, this process is typically used to produce dimethyl ether (DME) from methanol by either a gas phase or liquid phase reaction [3–6]. The conversion of syngas to methanol can be increased by removing, either physically or chemically, the alcohol as it is formed, because this removal lowers the thermodynamic constraints and promotes syngas conversion [7]. A single-step, liquid phase DME synthesis reaction has been developed that produces DME directly from synthesis gas using a bifunctional catalyst system that promotes both methanol synthesis and methanol dehydration to DME [8]. Performing this single-step reaction in a liquid phase offers the advantage of heat dissipation, thereby allowing a lower temperature to be maintained. The liquid phase reaction results in less catalyst deactivation and longer catalyst lifetime [9]. The ultimate objective of this research is to develop a catalytic reaction system to produce higher ethers from synthesis gas and olefins using a single-step liquid phase process. In this system, as methanol is produced from syngas, the methanol would react with olefins to produce higher ethers. The thermodynamic constraint to methanol synthesis can thus be overcome by removing the methanol in an ether. The methanol synthesis reaction is exothermic and therefore the equilibrium production of methanol is favored by low temperatures. A number of processes have been developed which produce methanol from syngas at temperatures from 60 to 120 °C and 1–5 MPa [7,10]. These conditions were found to be quite suitable for the etherification reaction also, as discussed below. One study has been found in which synthesis gas was etherified with isobutene in a single step reaction [11]. A dual catalyst system was used with Pd/SiO2 as the methanol synthesis catalyst and a zeolite as the etherification catalyst. The yield of ether from this reaction, which was operated at 175 °C and 7 atm, was very low. This result was most likely caused by the choice of catalyst. Not only is Pd/SiO2 a methanol synthesis catalyst, it also serves as an effective hydrogenation catalyst for the added olefin—in this case, isobutene—and yields of butanes were correspondingly high. In the current work, HZSM-5, which consists of CuO/ZnO on alumina, was used as the methanol synthesis catalyst and dry Amberlyst 15 was used as the etherification catalyst. The reaction temperatures ranged from 60 to 100 °C. A comprehensive literature review has been performed to determine the state of the literature for single-step synthesis reactions producing ethers from synthesis gas and olefins. The patent literature pertaining to DME formation and the production of higher ethers, along with the processes for synthesizing methanol at lower temperatures, plus the article by Kazi et al. [11] indicate the feasibility of developing this process. For the initial investigation, the study was divided into two parts: the first part was an analysis of ether production from the reaction of C6 and C8 olefins with alcohols, and the second part is the development of a single-step process where both alcohol and ether synthesis reactions are performed simultaneously. The work described herein evaluated the degree of reactivity of C6 and C8 olefins with methanol and the selectivity for the production of higher ethers. In a companion paper, we present our evaluation of the degree of reactivity of C6 and C8 olefins with ethanol and 2-butanol [12]. The initial parametric conditions were selected on the basis of the conditions expected to be encountered in the methanol synthesis reaction. An excess of olefin over the stoichiometric amount required would help shift the etherification equilibrium to ether and increase methanol consumption. This in turn would help drive the methanol synthesis reaction by consuming the product thereof. For this reason, the molar ratio of olefin to methanol for the initial experiments was chosen as 2:1. Karinen and Krause [13] produced C9 ether compounds from C8-olefins and methanol. They demonstrated that longer carbon chains decreased the rate of etherification. The rate of etherification depended strongly on the characteristics of the reactants, such as the bulkiness of the olefins. Higher carbon ethers are readily synthesized by the reaction of methanol with a branched olefin over an ion exchange resin catalyst [13–15]. In this study, commercially available Amberlyst 15 catalyst was used to produce high carbon number (C7 and C9) ether compounds from (C6 and C8) olefins and methanol. The effects of process variables such as molar ratio between olefin and methanol, reaction temperature, reaction pressure, reaction time, reaction medium, and catalyst loading are presented for etherification in a batch reactor. A series of four reactions was performed to determine the efficacy of ether production from the reactions of olefins with methanol. Those reactions were thermal with olefin only (to demonstrate olefin stability); thermal with olefin and methanol; catalytic with olefin only (again, to demonstrate olefin stability); and catalytic with olefin and methanol. The first and third sets of reactions were conducted to establish baseline reactivity of the system without methanol, that is, to investigate any rearrangement of the olefinic structure. The reactions were performed using 25-cm3 stainless steel batch reactors, which were immersed and agitated at 100 cpm in a temperature-controlled sand bath. In general, duplicate trials were conducted at each of the experimental conditions by utilizing side-by-side batch reactors in the same sand bath. For any given test, the analytical results presented in Table 1 represent an average of the results obtained from the individual reactors. The only exception to this rule was in the case of the determination of the effect of changing a single process variable, such as pressure, on the process yield, all other parameters being held constant. A description of the experimental apparatus (Tubing Bomb Microreactor system) appears elsewhere [16]. The reactions were performed at temperatures of 60 to 100 °C and reaction times from 15 min to 24 h using a pressure of 1.5 MPa H2. Hydrogen was chosen as a blanketing gas as it represents a less toxic alternative to synthesis gas. One experiment was performed at an elevated pressure of 5.5 MPa H2 to investigate the effect of pressure on etherification. The olefins used in this study were straight-chain olefins, 1-hexene, 1-heptene and 1-octene, and the branched olefins, 2,3-dimethyl-1-butene (23DM1B), 2,3-dimethyl-2-butene (23DM2B), 2,4,4-trimethyl-1-pentene (244TM1P) and 2,4,4-trimethyl-2-pentene (244TM2P). The reactions with the straight-chain olefins were conducted at 100 °C while the reactions with the branched olefins were conducted at temperatures ranging from 60 to 100 °C with most of the experiments being conducted at 70 °C. Reactions were initially performed at a 2:1 molar ratio of olefin (C6=1.4 g; C8=1.44 g) to methanol (for C6=0.26 g; C8=0.2 g) and then the molar ratio was changed to a 1:8 ratio of olefins (C6=0.48 g; C8=0.53 g) to methanol (for C6=1.5 g; C8=1.2 g) so that an excess of methanol would be present in the reactor. The olefins were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical (97% pure) and used as received. The methanol was obtained from Fisher Scientific (99.9% pure, ≤0.05% water) and was dried over molecular sieves prior to use. The etherification catalyst used in the reactions was Amberlyst 15 (0.2 g) and was initially charged to the reactor at 10 wt.% of the total charge. Catalyst studies were conducted in which dry Amberlyst 15 was compared to wet Amberlyst 15 and to crushed and extruded HZSM-5. Only the dry Amberlyst 15 promoted any reactivity and hence was used for the rest of the experiments. Additional reactions using 23DM1B and 244TM1P were performed in which the amount of dry Amberlyst 15 was halved to 5 wt.%, doubled to 20 wt.%, and tripled to 30 wt.% of the total charge; the Amberlyst 15 was also crushed and charged at 10 wt.%. Additionally, HZSM-5 was used alone and HZSM-5 and Amberlyst 15 were used together to test the effect of the HZSM-5 on the etherification. Both dry and wet Amberlyst 15 were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical and used as received. The HZSM-5 was obtained from United Catalysts, and activated prior to use. The pretreatment consisted of heating the HZSM-5, under a nitrogen flow, for 2 h at 200 °C, followed by 2 h at 400 °C. Reaction products were analyzed by gas chromatography (GC) using a Varian model 3400 equipped with a J&W Scientific DB-5 phase capillary, 25 m×0.32 mm I.D. with 0.52-μm film thickness. The injector was maintained at 200 °C and the flame ionization detector was maintained at 210 °C. The initial column temperature was 50 °C and the column was maintained at that temperature. 2,2,4-Trimethylpentane was used as an internal standard. Response factors were obtained for all available reactants and products. Response factors for those products which were not commercially available were estimated by obtaining the response factors for structurally similar available compounds. For this reason, some of the product distributions presented in Table 1 may not sum to 100%. In etherification, there are a number of reaction variables that determine the extent of reaction and product distribution. In this study, reaction temperature, reaction time, amount of catalyst loading, molar ratio of olefin to methanol, and concentration of reactants were selected for investigation. Table 1 presents the experimental conditions used in 50 etherification experiments performed in batch reactors. The product distributions are reported on a methanol-free basis. Table 1 also presents the conversions for methanol and the α-olefins as well as the selectivity to ether production. The initial experimental work focused on determining the reactivity and selectivity of straight-chain olefins when reacted with methanol using dry Amberlyst 15 catalyst. The reaction sets performed were thermal reaction conditions with olefin only, catalytic reaction with olefin only, and thermal and catalytic reactions with olefins and methanol. In reactions using 1-hexene, 1-heptene, and 1-octene, performed at 100 °C, no reactivity was observed under thermal conditions, i.e., in the absence of catalyst, with or without methanol. Rearrangement of the olefins was observed in the presence of a catalyst regardless of whether methanol was present; while in the presence of methanol, a small amount of unidentified higher boiling material was produced, but no ethers were observed in the product mixture. The next phase of the experimental work involved reactions of branched C6 (23DM1B and 23DM2B) and C8 (244TM1P and 244TM2P) olefins with methanol using 10 wt.% dry Amberlyst. Baseline reactions were performed at 80 °C in the absence of methanol to determine the amount of rearrangement that occurred under thermal and catalytic conditions with the 1- and 2-pentene isomers of 2,4,4-trimethylpentene and 1- and 2-butene isomers of 2,3-dimethylbutene. The reaction scheme is presented in Fig. 1 . In the catalytic reaction, the 1-pentene isomer rearranged forming 22% of the 2-pentene isomer, while the 2-pentene isomer rearranged yielding 80% of the 1-pentene isomer. After reacting for 2 h, the product distributions from both isomers were similar. Similar results were obtained for the C6 olefin. After 2 h of catalytic reaction, regardless of the initial isomer, the reaction composition was similar, yielding ∼90% 23DM2B. When the 2-pentene isomer was reacted in the presence of methanol, 3 mol% of the ether (run P2), 2-methoxy-2,4,4-trimethylpentane (OME; octyl methyl ether), was formed while the 1-pentene isomer yielded 7 mol% of OME (run P1). Similarly, the reaction with 2-butene yielded 11 mol% (run B2) 2-methoxy-2,3-dimethylbutane (HME; hexyl methyl ether), while the 1-butene isomer yielded 27 mol% of HME (run B1). In both cases, the reaction of the α-olefin isomer with methanol was favored. Hence, once the 2-butene or 2-pentene isomer was formed, the reaction proceeded very slowly yielding much less ether than the 1-butene or 1-pentene in an equivalent amount of time. It may be noted that the α-olefin is structurally more stable than the α-olefin and is, consequently, less reactive in etherification [17]. In many experimental studies of the synthesis of higher ethers from mixtures of methanol and olefins, ether compounds have been the primary products along with small amounts of alcohols [13,17,18]. In this study, the formation of 2,3-dimethyl-2-butanol (23DM2BOH) from C6 olefin and water was detected as a side reaction in all the C6 olefin runs. Under identical conditions, no hydration of the C8 olefin was observed. The presence of a higher alcohol in the products from the C6 olefins reactions indicates a small amount of water may have been present in the reactants, or may have adsorbed onto the surface of the Amberlyst during weighing and loading. Pretreatment of the Amberlyst by drying in a vacuum oven at 90 °C overnight did not reduce the amount of higher alcohol formed, so the pretreatment process was abandoned and the Amberlyst was used as received thereafter. The effect of reaction temperature and time on the production of ethers from 244TM1P and 23DM1B was evaluated. The production of OME from 244TM1P in reactions performed at 70, 80, and 100 °C (runs P3–P16) is shown in Table 1. The reactions performed at 70 °C from 1 to 24 h (runs P3–P7) showed an increase in ether production from 2.5 to ∼10 mol%. The reactions performed at 80 °C (runs P8–P12) and 100 °C (runs P13–P16) produced similar amounts of ether as the 70 °C reaction but at shorter reaction times (Fig. 2) . The amount of rearrangement of the 1-pentene isomer to the 2-pentene isomer increases as both temperature and reaction time are increased. Ether production from 23DM1B after 2-h reactions at 70, 80, and 100 °C (runs B4–B6) were not greatly affected by temperature, with all the reactions yielding 30 to 32 mol% ether (Fig. 3) . However, a substantial increase in ether production from 16 to 27 mol% was observed between 60 and 70 °C (runs B3 and B4). The effect of longer reaction times of 2, 4, 6, 8, and 24 h was minimal on ether production, which ranged from 30 to 36 mol% (runs B4, B7–B10). In all of these reactions, the amount of rearrangement to 23DM2B was substantial, yielding nearly 60 mol% of the 2-butene isomer after 6 h (runs B8–B10), as shown in Fig. 4 . The effect of temperature on the isomerization of C6 olefins is more significant than it is on C8 olefins. The reactivity of 23DM1B increases greatly (from 36% to 86%) in the temperature range from 60 to 70 °C (runs B3 and B4) but increases only slightly (from 86% to 93%) in the temperature range from 70 to 100 °C (runs B4–B6), as shown by the conversion of 23DM1B (Fig. 5) . The conversion is approaching a limiting value at temperatures over 70 °C. This partially explains why there is such a dramatic increase in ether production in the temperature range from 60 to 70 °C, but no temperature effect is observed after 70 °C. It should also be noted that the selectivity to HME production drops markedly in the range from 60 to 80 °C. The lower temperature gave good selectivity but low yields due to the low conversion. Increasing the temperature to enhance the reactivity resulted in too much α-olefin being converted to the less reactive β-olefin. Therefore, the choice of an optimum temperature for ether synthesis must involve a trade-off between α-olefin conversion and selectivity to ether production. From Fig. 5, the optimum temperature for HME synthesis seems to be about 70 °C. Similar results were obtained for the production of OME, with the optimum temperature being about 80 °C. However, so that direct comparisons could be made between the reactivities of C6 and C8 olefins, a temperature of 70 °C was used for the reactions of both olefins in subsequent experimentation. Another important reaction condition, the molar ratio between olefin and methanol, was also investigated because it affects the mechanism in the course of etherification [19] as well as the product distribution. The reactions discussed heretofore used a 2:1 olefin to methanol ratio. This ratio was selected because the methanol, which is ultimately to be derived from synthesis gas, would most likely react as soon as it is formed. The amount of ether produced using the 2:1 olefin to methanol ratio was only about 35%. In order to increase the yields of ethers from olefins, it was decided to employ a large excess of methanol in subsequent reactions. The molar ratio of olefin to methanol selected was 1:8. Several parametric factors may have affected the conversion of olefin to ether. Fig. 6 shows that changing the ratio between olefin and methanol from 2:1 to 1:8 has a significant effect on product yields at both 1-h (run P3 and P17) and 2-h (run P4 and P18) reaction times when 244TM1P reacts with methanol. Similar experimental results were obtained for the reaction of 23DM1B with methanol at 2 h. In the 1-butene reaction at a 1:8 ratio, an additional as yet unidentified product was formed. The amount of ether formed using the 1:8 olefin to methanol ratio was nearly double that obtained in the reaction with the 2:1 ratio (runs B4 and B11). In the 1-pentene reaction, the increased ratio had the effect of more than tripling the ether formation (runs P3 and P17, P4 and P18), indicating that the reaction had been limited for the methanol reactant. This result is in good agreement with the results of other researchers [13,20]. The conversion of 23DM1B increased slightly (less than 10%) when the molar ratio of olefin to alcohol was changed from 2:1 to 1:8, while the selectivity to HME production increased by a factor of 2.5 (runs B4 and B11). The enhanced selectivity due to the presence of excess methanol explains the increased ether production. In the case of 244TM1P, both olefin conversion and selectivity were significantly increased (runs P3 and P17, P4 and P18). The effect of catalyst loading on the conversion of 23DM1B and 244TM1P was also determined. The initial catalyst loading (single loading, Amberlyst 15 dry, as received) was 10 wt.% of the total reactor charge. Tests were also conducted using one-half, double, and triple the single loading, or 5, 20, and 30 wt.% of the total reactor charge, respectively. In addition, the single loading was crushed and tested to ensure that the process was not diffusion limited. In the case of the C6 olefin, crushing the Amberlyst had very little effect on the olefin conversion, but a significant effect on the selectivity to HME production (runs B11 and B15). The etherification yield was increased by about 20%, indicating that the methoxylation may be diffusion limited. Curiously, no such limitation was observed in the case of the C8 olefin, with the results obtained for conversion, selectivity, and ether production all being identical with the values obtained using an equal weight of uncrushed catalyst (runs P18 and P21). For the C6 olefin, the one-half, single, and double loading of catalyst all gave ether yields in the 54 to 58 mol% range at a reaction temperature of 70 °C and reaction time of 2 h (runs B16, B11, and B13). The triple loading increased the etherification yield to 66 mol%, an increase of about 20% (run B14). For the C8 olefin, the yield of ether from the single loading was 18 mol% (run P18). Both the double and triple loading of catalyst increased the ether production from 244TM1P (runs P19 and P20), but the yield seems to have reached a maximum at about 22% by the time the double loading was reached. As in the case of the C6 olefin, the increase in yield from tripling the catalyst loading was about 20%. From a process economics standpoint, doubling or tripling the catalyst loading to effect a 20% increase in product yield would probably not be justified because of the cost of maintaining the catalyst in the reaction system. Therefore, the optimum catalyst loading seems to be in the 5 to 10 wt.% range. Since it may be necessary to employ high pressures to drive the conversion of synthesis gas to methanol in the single-step etherification reactor, the effect on etherification yields of an increase in reaction pressure was also investigated. The reactions described above were performed under a 1.5-MPa hydrogen blanket. Increasing the pressure from 1.5 to 5.5 MPa hydrogen in the reaction that utilized a 2:1 ratio of 23DM1B to methanol and reaction time of 2 h had very little effect on the etherification yield (runs B4 and B12). Since the single-step etherification reactor will likely utilize a slurry phase design, the effect of employing an inert diluent on etherification yields was also investigated. In the reactions described above, the reactants were introduced into the reaction vessel and allowed to react, i.e., no diluent was employed. In order to investigate the effect of an inert reaction medium, decalin (cis/trans=50:50) was employed as diluent in the following reactions. In the case of the C6 olefin, when the reactants were diluted with decalin at a 1:1 combined reactants to solvent mass ratio, the amount of ether produced decreased due to the reduction in concentration of the reactants (runs B11 and B18, runs B13 and B20). This concentration effect is even more evident when the ratio between reactants and solvent is decreased to 1:2 (runs B18 and B19, runs B20 and B21). This effect of dilution was also observed in the reaction with the C8 olefin (compare runs P18, P24, and P25, and P19, P26, and P27), as shown in Fig. 7 . For 23DM1B, doubling the amount of Amberlyst in the 1:1 diluted reaction mixture gave a slight increase (<5%) in the yield of HME (runs B18 and B20), while doubling the amount of Amberlyst in the 1:2 diluted reaction mixture gave an increase of over 25% in the yield of HME (runs B19 and B21). For 244TM1P, doubling the amount of Amberlyst in the 1:1 diluted reaction mixture gave an increase of over 45% in the yield of OME (runs P24 and P26), while doubling the amount of Amberlyst in the 1:2 diluted reaction mixture gave an increase of over 35% in the yield of OME (runs P25 and P27). It is interesting to note that while the amount of diluent and catalyst employed affected the conversions of α-olefins, the selectivity to ether production remained fairly constant. For 23DM1B, the selectivity to HME production stayed at about 1.1, while for 244TM1P, the selectivity to OME production stayed at about 2.0. The effect of employing dual catalysts of both Amberlyst 15 and methanol synthesis catalyst on etherification yields was also investigated. The methanol synthesis catalyst itself, HZSM-5, showed no etherification activity, nor did it promote isomerization of the olefin (run P23). In mixed catalyst testing, at mass ratios of 1:1.5 (run B17) and 1.33:1 (run P22) of Amberlyst 15 to HZSM-5, the methanol synthesis catalyst had little or no effect on etherification yields. The statistical experimental design method of ANOVA was applied as a means of interpreting the degree of importance of catalyst loading and reaction medium on the production of hexyl methyl ether and octyl methyl ether. Production of hexyl methyl ether was performed at six different combinations of catalyst loading (10 and 20 wt.%) and reaction medium (200 psig H2, 1:1, and 1:2 reactants to decalin) at 70 °C, 2-h reaction time, and 1:8 ratio of 23DM1B to methanol (runs B11, B13, B18–B21). Synthesis of octyl methyl ether was also performed at six different combinations using the same catalyst loadings and reaction media under the same condition applied in HME production (runs P18, P19, P24–27). A reaction time of 2 h, temperature of 70 °C, and a ratio between olefin and methanol of 1:8 seem to be reasonable reaction conditions to produce the higher carbon ether compounds. Two reaction parameters, catalyst loading and reaction medium, were selected, and their effect on the production of hexyl methyl ether and octyl methyl ether was determined. Tables 2 and 3 show the results of the ANOVA. A residual mean square was created by pooling sums of squares and degrees of freedom from sources of variation that were obviously statistically weak or insignificant to conduct the ANOVA statistical tests. The details of these calculations are available elsewhere [21]. The ANOVA presented in Table 2 shows that catalyst loading and reaction medium were significant at the 25% and 10% level for the production of hexyl methyl ether, respectively. The ANOVA presented in Table 3 indicates that catalyst loading and reaction medium were significant at the 2.5% and 1% level for the production of octyl methyl ether, respectively. The feasibility of producing the higher ethers 2-methoxy-2,3-dimethylbutane (hexyl methyl ether) and 2-methoxy-2,4,4-trimethylpentane (octyl methyl ether) from olefins (2,3-dimethyl-1-butene; 2,3-dimethyl-2-butene and 2,4,4-trimethyl-1-pentene; 2,4,4-trimethyl-2-pentene, respectively) and methanol has been demonstrated at temperatures between 70 and 100 °C. Branched olefins have a greater reactivity than straight-chain olefins and are better candidates for producing higher ethers for use as fuel additives. β-Olefins are structurally more stable than α-olefins and are, consequently, less reactive in etherification. Increasing the amount of methanol available for the reaction raised the ether production to a reasonable level. Temperatures in the range from 70 to 100 °C were found to be suitable for ether production. However, the rate of isomerization between α- and β-olefins increases with increasing temperature, yielding more of the less reactive β-olefin. Therefore, serious consideration must be given to both the etherification reaction and the isomerization reaction when choosing the optimum temperature at which to conduct this synthesis. The concentration of reactants played a key role in determining the rate of formation of ether compounds. The reactivity of C6 olefins in etherification with methanol was greater than that of C8 olefins. The authors fully appreciate that there are conflicting thermodynamic constraints here. On the one hand, the concentration of methanol should be kept to a minimum in order to drive the methanol synthesis reaction. On the other hand, high concentrations of methanol help shift the etherification equilibrium to ether. These conflicting constraints present just one of the challenges to the single-step etherification reactor design. The next challenge will be the selection of the low-temperature methanol synthesis process which is the most compatible with the etherification reaction. The optimization of the various reaction parameters (temperature, pressure, reaction time, catalyst loading, mass ratio of etherification catalyst to methanol synthesis catalyst, molar ratio of synthesis gas feed to olefin feed, recycle ratio of unreacted reactants, etc.) should provide promising avenues for future research. The authors are grateful to Professors Christopher B. Roberts and Mark O. Barnett for their helpful discussion. We would also like to thank DOE for funding the project (DE-FC26-99FT40540).
abstract: Higher carbon ether compounds (HME; hexyl methyl ether and OME; octyl methyl ether) are produced from olefins (C6 olefins: 23DM1B; 2,3-dimethyl-1-butene and 23DM2B; 2,3-dimethyl-2-butene and C8 olefins: 244TM1P; 2,4,4-trimethyl-1-pentene and 244TM2P; 2,4,4-trimethyl-2-pentene) and methanol in the presence of commercially available Amberlyst 15 ion exchange catalyst. Stainless steel batch reactors are used and the experiments are conducted from 333 to 373 K. It appeared that α-olefins are much more reactive to produce the corresponding ether compounds than β-olefins are. Probably due to steric hindrance, the reactivity of C8 olefins seems to be less than that of C6 olefins. Analysis of variance (ANOVA) shows that the amount of catalyst and reaction medium is significant at 2.5% and 1% levels for the production of octyl methyl ether and at 25% and 10% levels for the production of hexyl methyl ether, respectively. The implications of the current results to the design of a continuous process for the preparation of higher carbon ethers from olefins and synthesis gas in a single-step etherification reactor are discussed.
title: Synthesis of higher carbon ethers from olefins and alcohols

full_text: Higher alcohol synthesis (HAS) from nature gas or coal via syngas was considered to be a promising alternative route for the production of clean fuels and chemicals in view of both utilization of resources and environmental protection [1]. Among various catalysts, alkali-doped molybdenum sulfide (ADM) catalysts have attracted much interest due to their higher selectivity to alcohols, higher activity for water–gas shift reaction and excellent resistance to sulfur poisoning for feed gas [2–5]. Previous studies revealed that for K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst, the addition of F–T element Co could enhance the selectivity to higher alcohol. However, the studies of other F–T elements such as Ni and Fe were fairly rare [6,7]. Nickel was generally considered to be active towards methanation. Recent investigations on group VIII elements including Co, Fe and Ni catalysts revealed that, in some cases, nickel showed an excellent ability for CO insertion, which has an important significance for higher alcohol synthesis [8]. Especially, nickel could keep high activity after sulfur treatment [9]. As a result, Ni-contained catalyst may be a potential for higher alcohol synthesis. In the present paper, modified Ni/K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst and the performances of higher alcohol synthesis were investigated. Conventional K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst was prepared by thermal decomposition of mixture of (NH4)2MoS4 and K2CO3 at 500 °C under N2. (NH4)2MoS4 was obtained through the reaction of (NH4)2S and (NH4)2Mo7S24 as described elsewhere [10]. Ni/K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst was prepared by co-precipitation of Ni(C2H4O2)4 and (NH4)2MoS4 solutions (Mo:Ni molar ratio=3:1). The precipitates were mixed with K2CO3 (K/Mo molar ratio=0.7) followed by thermal decomposition at 500 °C under N2. The BET surface areas of K2CO3/MoS2 and Ni/K2CO3/MoS2 catalysts were 10.4 m2/g and 9.6 m2/g, respectively. The carbon monoxide hydrogenation reactions were carried out in a stainless fixed-bed reactor with I.D. of 5 mm with a catalyst loading of 1 ml and the reaction conditions were as follows: temperature=280–340 °C, H2/CO=2.0, GHSV=3000 h− 1 and a pressure of 10.0 MPa. Syngas obtained from decomposition of methanol was introduced into the reactor after purification and preheating. The products were analyzed by gas chromatographs: H2, CO, CH4 and CO2 were analyzed by TCD equipped with a TDX01 column; the liquid products were analyzed by FID equipped with a Porapake-Q column. The mass balance was based on carbon-atom and the error of the balance of oxygen and hydrogen was within 5%. The activity of synthesis of alcohols was expressed as space-time yield (STY), gROH/mlcat. h and the alcohol selectivity was based on carbon mole selectivity on a CO2 free basis. Fig. 1 illustrates the performances of K2CO3/MoS2 and Ni/K2CO3/MoS2 catalysts for higher alcohol synthesis from syngas. It can be seen that the addition of Ni improved the catalytic reactivity clearly. At 320 °C, the CO conversion and the alcohol STY over K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst were 15.17% and 0.18 g/ml h, respectively. But for Ni/K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst, the CO conversion and the alcohol STY were 25.15% and 0.3 g/ml h, respectively. The addition of Ni resulted in a higher selectivity to hydrocarbons (from 13.48 C-atom % to 24.10 C-atom %), which increased monotonously with the temperature over the two catalysts (see Fig. 2 ). However, with increasing temperature, the selectivity to hydrocarbons over Ni/K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst tended to be close to that of K2CO3/MoS2. For K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst, the maximum space-time yield of alcohols was 0.23 g/ml h at 340 °C, however, the maximum space-time yield of alcohols 0.3 g/ml h was obtained at 320 °C over Ni/K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst. Similar to K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst, the major products of alcohols over Ni/K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst were C1–C4 linear alcohols. For the catalyst modified by nickel, the percentage of ethanol increased obviously. From Fig. 3 it can be seen that the addition of Ni resulted in an apparent deviation of ethanol yield from the traditional linear Anderson–Schulz–Flory (A–S–F) distribution of K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst, which indicated that Ni exerted a promotion effect on the chain propagation for the step of C1 to C2. The influences of temperature on the distribution of alcohols over Ni/K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst are shown in Fig. 4 . It was found that the percentages of methanol and ethanol decreased while propanol and butanol increased as the temperature increased. Higher temperature was favorable in the formation of higher alcohols. Considering that the undesired hydrocarbons were easily formed at higher temperature, the optimum temperature range was 320–340 °C for Ni/K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst. A stability test of Ni/K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst at 300 °C, 10.0 MPa, 3000 h− 1 was conducted for about 200 h and the results indicated the catalyst showed good stability under reaction conditions (Fig. 5 ). The nature of nickel promoter on the catalytic performance of Ni/K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst is under research. According to the mechanisms of carbon chain growth by CO insertion of K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst [6], it seems that the promoting effect for the step of C1 to C2 may be due to the strong ability of CO insertion of Ni promoter to form higher alcohol [8]. On the other hand, nickel also exerts a function to accelerate the dissociation of CO to produce more precursors of CH x , which is favorable to the production of higher alcohols with CO insertion. For traditional K2CO3/MoS2 higher alcohol synthesis catalyst, nickel was an efficient promoter, which could promote both catalytic activity and selectivity to higher alcohols (especially ethanol). Compared with K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst, the selectivity to hydrocarbons over Ni/K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst was close to that of K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst in the temperature range of 320–340 °C, however, the STY of alcohols was much higher. Ni/K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst showed good stability during a running of 200 h. As a result, nickel appeared to be a potential promoter for K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst.
abstract: Ni modified K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst was prepared and the performance of higher alcohol synthesis catalyst was investigated under the conditions: T =280–340 °C, H2/CO (molar radio)=2.0, GHSV=3000 h− 1, and P =10.0 MPa. Compared with conventional K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst, Ni/K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst showed higher activity and higher selectivity to C2 +OH. The optimum temperature range was 320–340 °C and the maximum space-time yield (STY) of alcohol 0.30 g/ml h was obtained at 320 °C. The selectivity to hydrocarbons over Ni/K2CO3/MoS2 was higher, however, it was close to that of K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst as the temperature increased. The results indicated that nickel was an efficient promoter to improve the activity and selectivity of K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst.
title: The performances of higher alcohol synthesis over nickel modified K2CO3/MoS2 catalyst

full_text: Gasification is an effective technology for the thermochemical conversion of biomass to energy or synthesis gas. The gasification temperatures in the conventional process have been above 800°C, as a higher gasification rate is expected at such temperatures. However, the high gasification temperature induced problems due to the formation of ash with low melting temperatures and heavy refractory hydrocarbons [1]. Low-temperature gasification (LTG) of biomass is an attractive alternative for producing hydrogen and synthesis gas, especially from biomass with low calorific values, such as livestock manure compost [2] and waste activated sludge [3–5]. LTG is more advantageous than high-temperate gasification (HTG) as the process avoids ash-related problems, such as sintering, agglomeration, deposition, erosion and corrosion [6]. Furthermore, the LTG process is easier to operate and is more efficient [7]. However, if the LTG produces excessive tar, then the product gas contains tar, which blocks and fouls engines and power-generating turbines. Tar is defined as condensable organic molecules containing six of more carbon atoms [8], and its composition is quite complex. Catalytic gasification of tar in the product gas (catalytic hot gas cleaning) is a practical solution to the problem and is the most important issue in the conversion of low calorific biomass to energy. A cheaper and long-life catalyst is desirable, and nickel loaded on brown coal shows promise as a catalyst for gasification of biomass [9–11]. Another issue to be considered in achieving a long-life catalyst for biomass conversion is the deactivation of gasification catalysts during the LTG process. One of the major mechanisms in this process is carbon deposition [12–15], which results in a decrease in the active surface area of the catalytic metal particles. The strategy to prepare catalysts with a tolerance for carbon deposition is to use a basic support material, achieve a high dispersion state of metal particles, and introduce promoters to the catalysts [16]. Surrogate compounds of tar have been employed in the studies on carbon deposition on catalysts. In these studies, the surrogate compounds employed were mainly aromatic hydrocarbons, such as toluene, benzene, naphthalene, anthracene, and pyrene, with the assumption that the compounds are formed at temperatures above 750°C [17] or 700°C [18]. Among these compounds, benzene has been revealed to be the most inactive compound for cracking [19,20]. However, the tar components in LTG are expected to be different from the ones in HTG. Lighter hydrocarbons, such as cyclohexane [21] and n-heptane [22–25], and oxygen compounds, such as phenols [26], have been employed as the surrogate compounds of tar in LTG. Morishita et al. revealed that the tar in the LTG of biomass is made up of aliphatic compounds, rather than aromatic ones [27]. For example, acetic acid, levoglucosan, and 1-hydroxy-2-propane have been obtained from wood by supercritical water gasification at 450–550°C [28], whereas aliphatic compounds have been produced from meat and bone meal at 350–500°C [29]. The objective of the present study is to understand how the different surrogate hydrocarbons influence the carbon deposition on a commercial Ni/Al2O3 catalyst using four different C6 hydrocarbons with different chemical structures, since C6 is the lightest component of tar from biomass [30]. The catalyst used in the present study was a commercial Ni/Al2O3 catalyst (20wt.%Ni, C13-4, Süd-Chemie, AG), which was crushed and sieved to retrieve fine particles less than 250µm in diameter. Carbon deposition behaviors were studied by using thermogravimetry, with the equipment shown in Fig. 1 . The four C6-hydrocarbons selected as the carbon sources, n-hexane, 1-hexene, cyclohexane and benzene, were purchased from Wako Chemical Co. Ltd. and utilized without further purification. The compounds were placed one at a time in a bubbler placed immediately before the thermobalance equipment (TGD-9600, ULVAC-RIKO) and the carbon source compound vapor was directed to the reaction zone by a nitrogen gas stream. The partial pressure of the compounds was fixed at 35kPa by setting the appropriate temperature of the bubbler using a thermostat. The set temperatures were 33°C (for n-hexane), 28°C (for 1-hexene), and 43°C (for cyclohexane and benzene). Immediately after pre-reduction in a hydrogen/nitrogen (20% H2 in N2) stream at 600°C for 1h [12], the catalyst was placed in a thermobalance for the carbon deposition measurement. The catalyst was first heated to 100°C in a nitrogen flow (10°C/min) after the residual air in the reaction system was purged. When the temperature reached 100°C, the catalyst was exposed to the carbon source compound vapor by switching to the line connected to the bubbler. The catalyst was further heated to the preset temperatures 500°C or 950°C in order to observe the carbon deposition behaviors. The carbon deposition behaviors were observed in terms of the changes in mass during the heating process. After the used catalyst has been cooled in a nitrogen stream, it was removed from the equipment for further characterization. Experiments with the support, Al2O3, were also conducted, as controls. The metal dispersions and morphologies of the deposited carbons were studied using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2010, JEOL) operated at an acceleration voltage of 200kV. The fresh or used catalysts were lightly crushed with an agate mortar and pestle and were then dispersed in methanol under ultrasonication. Subsequently, the TEM specimen was prepared by applying drops of a dispersion solution to a carbon evaporated Cu-micro grid and leaving it to dry. X-ray diffraction measurement was performed on the fresh nickel catalyst to determine its crystalline phase and particle size. Powder X-ray diffractograms were obtained using an X-ray diffractometer (RINT 2000/PC, Rigaku Corp.) with Cu-Kα X-ray source operated at 40kV×32mA. The diffraction angle was continuously scanned over the range of 5°–90° in 2θ at 1.0°min−1. The chemical states and surface composition of the fresh catalyst were studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCA5600, Perkin Elmer) with a Mg–Kα X-ray source operated at 15kV and 400W. The ability of the catalyst to adsorb the C6-compounds was also examined using the same thermogravimetry (TG) setup under dynamic conditions. After heating the reduced Ni/Al2O3 to 300°C in a nitrogen stream, it was exposed to a flow of C6-compounds containing N2 (partial pressure: 35kPa). The adsorption uptake was determined as the increase in weight until weight became constant, which was normally observed within 0.5h after the introduction of the compounds to the TG setup. The definition of mass gain is given below: Δ w T = 100 m T − m 0 m 0 where Δw(T): mass gain at temperature T (%), m(T): total mass detected by TG (mg), and m0: catalyst weight (mg). Fig. 2(a) and (b) shows the mass gain curves and their corresponding first derivative curves with respect to the reaction time. It was shown that all C6-compounds exhibited a two-stage behavior in their weight increase with respect to temperature. These increases were found to be due to the carbon deposition, as evidenced by the TEM images obtained at 500°C (Fig. 2) and 900°C (Fig. SD-2). The two stages are referred to as low-temperature (LTS; 300–650°C) and high-temperature (HTS; above 650°C) stages. Since we are interested in the carbon deposition in the low-temperature gasification of biomass, hereinafter, we will consider carbon deposition in LTS. The description of HTS is available in the supporting data (SD-2). The derivative curves of all four C6-compounds showed similar shapes, but at close inspection, the maximum rates were different depending on the compound type. The temperature at which the maximum rate is observed (T max) is a parameter indicating the tolerance of catalyst deactivation, while the maximum rate (r max) represents the catalytic activity toward thermal decomposition of the hydrocarbon. 1-hexene showed the lowest T max and the highest r max, which makes it the most influential component to deactivate the catalyst. Benzene had the similar T max, although it also had a lower r max. The saturated hydrocarbons, n-hexane and cyclohexane showed higher T max, among which n-hexane showed a higher r max. Herringbone-type carbon nanofibers (HB-CNF) were obtained in LTS, as shown in Fig. 3 . HB-CNF are distinguished by their characteristic arrangements of hexagonal layers in the graphite structure; the carbon hexagonal layers are slantingly aligned to the fiber axis. This type of carbon nanofibers have been prepared from the combinations of carbon source compounds and catalysts, such as CO/CO2/N2 +H2 with Ni/SiO2–Al2O3 [31], CH4 or CO/H2, and C2H4/H2 with Ni/SiO2 [32], where hydrogen and nickel catalysts have been exclusively employed. Thermal decomposition of n-hexane and cyclohexane resulted in the formation of serpentine HB-CNF with rough surfaces, as shown in Fig. 3(a) and (e). The enlarged images of the carbon fibers shown in Fig. 3(b) and (f) clearly exhibited the features of HB-CNF. The rough surfaces were due to the characteristic alignment of the carbon layers to HB-CNF, i.e., the layer terminated at their fiber surfaces. The diameters of the fibers obtained from n-hexane and cyclohexane were also similar, i.e., 15–25nm. 1-hexene produced two typical forms of nanosized deposits: straight fibrous shapes and round structures, as shown in Fig. 3(c). The former structures had diameters of 20–30nm and displayed well-organized stacking of the hexagonal plane for long lateral distances. The latter was revealed to be curled CNFs, which were apparent from an enlarged TEM image, as shown in Fig. 3(d). Benzene produced both HB-CNF and granular nanocarbons, as shown in Fig. 3(g). Smaller amounts of granular carbons having shell-like structures were also formed; the nickel particles were encapsulated by stacked carbon layers. The carbon deposition mechanism consists of the decomposition of a hydrocarbon at the surface of catalyst metal particles, the diffusion of carbon atoms through the catalyst particles, and the deposition of carbon fiber from the other sides of catalyst metal particles. The deposition of carbon from the opposite sides of the particles continuously forms carbon fibers [33]. There have been different explanations on the rate determining steps of the catalytic carbon deposition; possible steps include diffusion through the Ni particle [33], the preceding carbon formation at the top surface of the metal particles [34], or certain surface chemical reactions [35]. The reaction continues until the bare surface of nickel particles located at the top of the fiber is covered by graphitic layers. Since the carbon deposition occurs on the same nickel catalyst surface in the present study, the decomposition of hydrocarbons is considered to be intrinsic to the nature of the hydrocarbons. Hence, the ease of decomposition of the compounds into the constituent elements, i.e., hydrogen and carbon, and the surface concentration of the reactant would govern the carbon deposition activity. The following decomposition reactions were assumed in the study of their thermodynamic feasibility. (1) n − hexane  C 6 H 14 = 6 C + 7 H 2 (2) 1 − hexene and cyclohexane C 6 H 12 = 6 C + 6 H 2 (3) Benzene C 6 H 6 = 6 C + 3 H 2 The calculated standard Gibbs energies for the reactions are shown in Fig. 4 as functions of the reaction temperature; the Gibbs energies were obtained from thermodynamic databases [36,37]. The values at temperatures ranging between 450–500°C, where the maximum rates were attained, were compared. Consequently, it was found that the most reactive compound was 1-hexene, while the most stable compound was benzene. The saturated hydrocarbons, n-hexane, and cyclohexane showed similar reactivity rates, which were between that of benzene and 1-hexene. Similar thermodynamic discussions have been presented by Li et al., who considered the relationship between the thermodynamic feasibility and the formation of high-purity single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWNT) [38]. The adsorption uptakes of the compounds are listed in Table 1 in terms of the mass of the compounds per unit mass of the catalyst. Benzene showed the highest adsorption uptake among the four C6-compounds, followed by 1-hexene. Both n-hexane and cyclohexane showed similarly low adsorption uptakes. The difference in the adsorption uptakes may originate from the different adsorption mechanisms of the hydrocarbons. Alkanes are adsorbed on Ni as 1, 2-diadsorbed species [39]. On the other hand, alkenes such as ethylene, are adsorbed on Ni in π-coordination and diσ-coordination [40]. Ab initio calculation revealed that benzene is found to be adsorbed molecularly, parallel to the surface, at the threefold site and bonding primarily through its π-electron systems [41]. Tilted adsorption of benzene on Pt (110) 1×2 surface was observed experimentally [42]. From the standpoint of thermodynamic and adsorptive properties, 1-hexene is found to be the most effective compound to decompose at the nickel surface to produce carbon atoms, resulting in the highest r max and the lowest T max among the four hydrocarbons studied. Although benzene is thermodynamically stable, its high ability of adsorption resulted in a lower T max. The comparatively higher T max of the saturated hydrocarbons, n-hexane and cyclohexane, was due to their moderate thermodynamic feasibilities and low surface concentrations. This study clarified that alkenes should also be recognized as a carbon source to deactivate nickel catalysts besides benzene, which has been recognized as a surrogate tar compound. Also, we developed useful criteria for discerning the deactivation trend of hydrocarbons by carbon deposition, i.e., the thermodynamic feasibility of decomposition and the surface concentration. This study was financially supported by the Japan Science and Technology Agency (JST) under the contract of “Gunma Prefecture Collaboration of Regional Entities for the Advancement of Technological Excellence,” by Grant-in-Aid for Scientific Research (KAKENHI) (Project No. 20560718) and by Low-Carbon Research Network Japan (LCnet, MEXT, Japan). The authors are grateful to Professor Takayuki Takarada, Dr. Kayoko Morishita, Li Liuyun (Gunma University), and Professor Masahiko Arai (Hokkaido University) for their useful and sincere inputs on the subject. Supplementary materials Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.fuproc.2012.04.021.
abstract: C6-hydrocarbons, namely, n-hexane, 1-hexene, cyclohexane, and benzene, were selected as surrogate tar in low-temperature gasification (LTG) of biomass in the study of carbon deposition of organic compounds on a Ni/Al2O3 catalyst. All the compounds showed a two-stage weight increase due to carbon deposition; the two stages were the low-temperature stage (LTS: 300–650°C) and high-temperature stage (HTS: above 650°C). The LTS is the focus of this study. The susceptibility of the catalyst to carbon deposition was measured by the temperature (T max) giving maximum rate of weight increase (r max). 1-hexene was found to be the most influential compound with the lowest T max and the highest r max, followed by benzene. The saturated hydrocarbons, n-hexane and cyclohexane, were the least influential compounds for carbon deposition. The morphology of the deposits was found to be mainly herringbone-type carbon nanofibers, determined by transmission electron microscopy. Thermodynamic calculation revealed that 1-hexene was the most reactive compound for carbon deposition, while benzene was the most stable compound. The factors determining the carbon deposition of hydrocarbon compounds were explained in terms of both thermodynamic and adsorptive properties of the compounds.
title: Carbon deposition on a Ni/Al2O3 catalyst in low-temperature gasification using C6-hydrocarbons as surrogate biomass tar
