full_text: Glyerol is the simplest trihydric alcohol which has IUPAC name of propane-1,2,3-triol. It is also commercially known as glycerin, 1,2,3-propanetriol, 1,2,3-trihydroxypropane, glyceritol or glycyl alcohol. Glyerol can be used in food and beverages, feedstock for raw chemicals and pharmaceutical applications [1]. In the past years, the demand for biodiesel increases sharply and glycerol forms as a byproduct of transesterification reaction of natural oils with alcohols (C1–C4). Stoichiometrically, glycerol is produced by 10wt.% of total biodiesel production. The mass production of biodiesel in Europe and US would entail surplus glycerol production which has 80–88% purity [2]. The conventional application and current market of glycerol could not cope with the excess production that need further costly purification step to meet the purity of crude glycerin of industrial grade (98% purity) [3]. The transformation of glycerol into fuel oxygenates by etherification [4,5] and esterification methods [6–8] are among chemical reactions that draw great interest of many researches since they are economically beneficial to the production of glycerol byproduct and biodiesel process. Higher glycerol ester and acetyl glycerol have been identified as potentially valuable additives for diesel, gasoline, biodiesel, and blend biodiesel–diesel that assist to decrease in particles, hydrocarbons, carbon monoxide and unregulated aldehydes emissions. These derivates can act as cold flow improvers and viscosity reducer for biodiesel, antiknock additives for gasoline, and octane boosters for gasoline, as an alternative to commercial trialkylethers (MTBE and ETBE) [9]. The preparation of the esters of glycerol by the acetylation of glycerol is one of the possibilities of glycerol utilization [7,8]. This process may be realized via the reaction of glycerol and acetic acid in the presence of a suitable acidic catalyst. Traditionally, the esterification of glycerol with acetic acid is carried out over mineral acids as catalysts. However, the effluent disposal leads to environmental problems and economical inconveniences. Homogeneous catalysts such as sulfuric acid, hydrofluoric acid and paratolunesulfonic acid are toxic, corrosive and often hard to remove from the products. These problems can be overcome by the use of heterogeneous catalysts. Additionally, they have been found to offer better yield of the desired products compared to homogeneous catalysts [7,10]. Zeolites [7], mesoporous silica with sulfonic acid groups [8], ion exchange resins [10], niobium-zirconium [11], niobium-SBA-15 [12], heteropolyacids loaded inorganic materials [6,13] have been used as solid catalysts in the esterification of glycerol with acetic acid. Among them, cation-exchange resins showed excellent activity as well as selectivity toward higher esters in the esterification of glycerol with acetic acid [7,14]. The acetic acid to glycerol molar ratio and the reaction temperature are the most influential parameters on the esterification process [8,12]. Melero et al. [14] observed that glycerol conversion was enhanced with the increasing acetic acid concentration for all temperatures, whereas the detrimental effect of temperature increment was identified at low concentration of acetic acid [8]. Recently, nearly 100% selectivity of triacetin was achieved in high molar ratio (acetic acid:glycerol 24:1) and high pressure (200bar) using Amberlyst-15 catalyst [15]. However, such a high molar ratio is not applicable for the industrial process due to the difficulty of the separation of the unreacted reactants. The aim of this work was to investigate the influence of the acetic acid to glycerol molar ratio and temperature on the product distribution of the glycerol acetylation over Amberlyst-15 in a slurry reactor, and to optimize the reaction conditions and estimate the kinetic parameters for this reaction. In agreement with the kinetic analysis, the product distribution was expressed as the yield of different products. The commercial strong acid ion-exchange resin (Amberlyst 15) from Sigma–Aldrich was used as esterification catalysts. The Amberlyst in a dry form (moisture capacity 300, 240, and 200min at AA:G molar ratio 9:1, 6:1, and 3:1, respectively (Fig. 1). These results indicated that the glycerol concentration (instead of acetic acid concentration) in the reactant mixture was the main factor which had significant influence on the equilibrium time of the product distribution because acetic acid was excess in this reaction. In a typical 5h experiment, almost complete conversion of glycerol was achieved at reaction temperature above 95°C and the initial AA: G ratio 9:1 (Table 2). At lower AA: G ratios, the complete conversion of glycerol was not achieved even at 110°C. It is interesting to note that the higher conversion is not necessary accompanied by the higher yield of DAG and TAG because the formation of DAG and TAG takes a longer time. After 45min of reaction time, the glycerol conversion was around 70% for both 6:1 and 3:1 of AA:G molar ratio, however, 29.38% of combined yield of DAG and TAG at the AA:G molar ratio 6:1 was almost double of that (15.03%) at the AA:G molar ratio 3:1 (Fig. 1(b)–(c)). Both the glycerol conversion as well as the combined yield of DAG and TAG increased with increasing the AA:G molar ratio. The higher AA:G molar ratio facilitates the shift of the distribution of the esters towards higher substituted species, and thus higher glycerol conversion and yield of DAG and TAG could be achieved. The consecutive reaction mechanism of glycerol acetylation could be confirmed from the product distribution as shown in Fig. 1 (and the Suppl. Fig.1). The yield of MAG decreased with the reaction time, while that of DAG passed a maximum value before its decrease. However, the yield of TAG increased steadily, although only a relatively small amount of TAG in the final products. As more MAG converted to DAG and TAG, its yield decreased, while the yield of DAG and TAG increased. Similarly, as more DAG converted to TAG in the latter stage of the reaction, the yield of DAG decreased, whereas the yield of TAG still increased. It is worth mentioning that in this figure we did not distinguish the two different monoacetin or diacetin isomers in the products, formed by the reaction on the primary and secondary hydroxyl group of glycerol. The consecutive reaction mechanism of glycerol acetylation could be confirmed from the product distribution as shown in Fig. 1 (and the Suppl. Fig.1). The yield of MAG decreased with the reaction time, while that of DAG passed a maximum value before its decrease. However, the yield of TAG increased steadily, although only a relatively small amount of TAG in the final products. As more MAG converted to DAG and TAG, its yield decreased, while the yield of DAG and TAG increased. Similarly, as more DAG converted to TAG in the latter stage of the reaction, the yield of DAG decreased, whereas the yield of TAG still increased. It is worth mentioning that in this figure we did not distinguish the two different monoacetin or diacetin isomers in the products, formed by the reaction on the primary and secondary hydroxyl group of glycerol. The high fraction of monoacetin and diacetin under the experimental conditions indicated that the stoichiometric amount (even excess amount) of acetic acid was insufficient to accomplish the complete substitution of glycerol to triacetin. We note, however, that the conversion of acetic acid was 28–74% in these experiments, which means that the available acetic acid was not totally consumed. Conventionally, the esterification reaction is equilibrium limited [19]. An alternative method to overcome the thermodynamic limitation is the application of reactive distillation. Recently, the application of reactive distillation for the etherification of glycerol with C4–C5 alcohols (e.g. iso-butyl alcohol, TBA and iso-amyl alcohol) with or without the presence of fatty acids has been patented [20]. In this process, water produced from the etherification (or esterification in the presence of fatty acid) is removed from the reaction section as a distillate, thereby enabling higher conversions to be achieved. The results in Table 2 showed that the AA:G molar ratio was also the more influential factor on the combined yield of DAG and TAG than the temperature, although increasing the temperature also has a positive influence on yield. As shown in Table 2, the combined yield of DAG and TAG increased by 45–56% with AA:G molar ratio increasing from 3:1 to 9:1 with the same temperature. However, this value only increased by 7–18% with temperature increasing from 80 to 110°C with the same AA:G molar ratio. The optimization of molar ratio of reactant towards selectivity was studied intensively by Luque et al. [21] using Starbon®-400-SO3H catalyst under microwave irradiation condition, keeping the amount of catalyst and temperature constant, but simultaneously varying the reaction time and irradiation power. They found that the formation of monoacetyl glycerol was favored at lower AA:G molar ratio (1:1), shorter reaction time (10min), and lower irradiation power (100W). However, higher AA:G molar ratio, longer reaction time and higher irradiation power would favor the formation of diacetyl glycerol and triacetyl glycerol [22]. Similar results were found in our experiments, the results in Table 2 showed that more DAG and TAG products were formed at higher AA:G molar ratio and higher temperature. The influence of temperature on the glycerol acetylation was studied with the constant AA:G molar ratio (9:1) and the temperature varying from 80 to 110°C. The results (Fig. 2 ) showed that with increasing temperature the reaction rate increases, and thus, the product distribution reaches constant in a shorter time. As shown in Fig. 2, it takes more than 300min for the reaction to reach stable at AA:G molar ratio 6:1 and 3:1, but only 170min for that at AA:G molar ratio 9:1. Moreover, the reaction time for the conversion of MAG to DAG, and DAG to TAG also became earlier, this could be clearly seen from the yield curves of DAG, the maximum yield of DAG occurred on the time of 210, 70, and 40min at the temperature 80, 90, and 110°C, respectively. The increase in the temperature improved the reaction rate significantly and also facilitated the distribution of the esters toward higher substituted species [23]. The most appropriate temperature for DAG formation in the final products was 80°C (AA:G molar ratio 9:1) with 49.59% of the DAG yield (Table 2 and Fig. 2 (c)), but with increasing temperature the yield of DAG in the final products decreased. However, the most appropriate temperature for the glycerol acetylation was 110°C because both fast reaction rate and high conversion of glycerol and yield of DAG and TAG esters could be achieved. The effects of the temperature on the product distribution of glycerol acetylation at AA:G molar ratio 6:1 and 3:1 are shown in Figs. 3 and 4 , respectively. It could be seen from these figures that the equilibrium time for the product distribution was shortened significantly at the high temperature. Moreover, the yield of TAG is increasing with temperature, indicating that high temperature facilitates the formation of TAG. At AA:G molar ratio 6:1, the glycerol conversion increased by 7.5% with the temperature increasing from 80°C to 110°C, However, the high temperature did not significantly improve glycerol conversion at the lower AA:G molar ratio (3:1). Glycerol conversion in reaction with acetic acid seemed to enhance with the increasing acetic acid loading for all temperature. Melero et al. [8] found that at low acetic acid loading, the detrimental effect of increasing temperature was significantly observed. They concluded the optimal condition for highest glycerol conversion was obtained with high AA:G molar ratio and low–medium temperature of 100–125°C. From their research work too, they observed that high molar ratio and high temperature quashed the formation of MAG, while high molar ratio and high temperature would influence the formation of TAG. The similar pattern was observed in our work. MAG was preferentially formed at lower temperature while TAG was preferentially formed at higher temperature. In terms of glycerol conversion, reaction rate, and the combined yield of DAG and TAG, the optimal values are the highest molar ratio (9:1) and the highest temperature (110°C). Under these conditions the glycerol conversion was 97% and a high total yield (90%) for DAG and TAG could be achieved after 120min. In the absence of the catalyst (Fig. 5 ), the esterification only gave MAG as the main product with the yield of more than 60%, and only a small amount DAG with the yield of<5% at AA:G molar ratio 9:1 and 110°C. However, the conversion of glycerol can still reach more than 70% after 70min even without a catalyst. In comparison with the product distribution in the presence of Amberlyst-15 (Table 2), in this case the esterification led to DAG and TAG as the main product with the total yield of about 90%, and only a very small amount of MAG with the yield of<8%. Therefore, the main function of the catalysts in this reaction should be to increase the yield of the desired products (DAG and TAG). It is well known that the acid sites of the catalysts have significant influence on the catalytic activity and selectivity. Theoretically, the proton from Amberlyst-15 attaches on the lone pair of the oxygen which is double-bonded to the carbon, and this causes the carbon obtain a fair amount positive charge. It is followed by the nucleophilic attack of a hydroxy group of glycerol, accompanied by the leasing of water molecule that eventually leads to the deprotonated ester formation, yielding the respective mono-, di-, tri- glycerol esters (Scheme 2 ) [24,25]. In addition, the pore structure of the catalysts also plays an important role on the product distribution. It is evident that DAG and TAG are space demanding, however, their formation and diffusion in Amberlyst-15 is not difficult because Amberlyst-15 has enough space in the pore structure for the diffusion of DAG and TAG. The comparison of glycerol aceltylation over different catalysts is shown in Table 3 , it could be seen that Amberlyst-15 showed high activity as well as high yield (or selectivity) for DAG and TAG compared to other catalysts such as acid zeloites (HZSM-5 and HUSY) and Niobic Acid. The high activity of Amberlyst-15 for glycerol acetylation may be due to two reasons: firstly Amberlyst-15 has high catalytic acid sites, enhancing the formation of the reactive electrophilic intermediate and resulting in high conversion of glycerol; Secondly Amberlyst-15 has enough pore space and excellent swelling ability, facilitating the formation of bigger molecular such as DAG and TAG. The water content in the final products was found to be 2.28–2.77%, 3.03–3.43%, and 5.02–5.31% at AA/G molar ratio 9:1, 6:1, and 3:1, respectively (Table 2). Park et al. [26] reported that the water resulting from the esterification of oleic acid with methanol severely inhibited the esterification reaction. The activity of Amberlyst-15 decreased rapidly due to the direct poisoning of acid sites by water. To deduce whether water had similar effect on the acetylation of glycerol, the wet Amberlyst-15 with 3.20 wt.% of the moisture content was also investigated for glycerol acetylation and the results are also shown in Table 3. The lower activity of the wet Amberlyst-15 (compared to dry Amberlyst-15) was attributed to the poisoning of acid sites by water in the resins, as well as the poor accessibility of reactants to acid sites due to the presence of water in the resins [27]. The laboratory measurements of BET surface area and pore volume for the Amberlyst 15 samples before and after the drying process (Section 2.1) were carried out using Autosorb-1 from Quantachrome Instruments. It was found that the BET area increased from 42.64m2/g to 46.35m2/g and the pore volume increased from 0.324cm3/g to 0.348cm3/g, and the BJH adsorption pore diameter slightly increased from 30.96 to 32.03nm after drying. As discussed earlier, the water presented in the Amberlyst 15 would inhibit the catalytic activity of Amberlyst 15 for glycerol acetylation, leading to the decrease of both glycerol conversion and selectivity toward DAG and TAG (Table 3). The general reaction rate expression for the acetic acid esterification could be expressed in the form of Langmuir–Hinshelwood–Hougen–Watson (LHHW) model with surface reaction as the determining step [28,29]. It assumes that the main species in the reaction mixture are weakly adsorbed on the catalyst surface, then the resistance term of the LHHW model could be ignored. Moreover, since the rate of forward reaction of glycerol acetylation is much faster than the backward reaction for the formation of MAG, DAG and TAG products before equilibrium [23], we can ignore the backward reactions in each step for the convenience of kinetic calculation. Finally, the amount of acetic acid was excess and could not be completely converted, especially at high AA:G molar ratio. For this reason we can assume that enough acetic acid are available for the catalytic active sites and thus the concentrations of acetic acid in these sites are constant. Then homogeneous first-order consecutive reaction could be used to describe the glycerol acetylation; (1) A → k 1 R → k 2 S → k 3 T Where A=glycerol, R=mono-acetyl glyceride (MAG) ester, S=di-acetyl glyceride (DAG) ester, and T=tri-acetyl glyceride (TAG) ester. For a batch reactor, this simple scheme yields the following unsteady-state material balance (under isothermal conditions). The reaction rate of different species can be expressed as; (2) − r A = − d C A d t = k 1 C A (3) r R = − d C R d t = k 1 C A − k 2 C R (4) r S = − d C S d t = k 2 C − R k 3 C S Where r A, r R, and r S are the reaction rate (mol/(min·L)) of species A, R, and S; C A, C R, C S and C T are the concentrations (mol/ L) of species A, R, S, and T at any time in the reactor, respectively. And if it assumes that A is the only source of R, S and T, then for an initial (at t =0) concentration of A, termed, CA0, the balance at any time is: (5) C A 0 = C A + C R + C S + C T The integration of Eqs. (2) to (4) provides (6) C A C A 0 = e − k 1 t (7) Y R = C R C A 0 = k 1 k 1 − k 2 e − k 2 t − e − k 1 t (8) Y S = C S C A 0 = k 1 k 2 k 1 − k 2 e − k 2 t − e − k 3 t k 3 − k 2 − e − k 1 t − e − k 3 t k 3 − k 1 and combining with Eq. (5) yields (9) Y T = C T C A 0 = 1 − e − k 1 t − k 1 k 1 − k 2 e − k 2 t − e − k 1 t − k 1 k 2 k 1 − k 2 e − k 2 t − e − k 3 t k 3 − k 2 − e − k 1 t − e − k 3 t k 3 − k 1 We can then use the transient data to carry out nonlinear regression exercise using Polymath 7.0 to obtain the rate constants, k 1, k 2 and k 3 for each run by minimizing the sum of squares of the difference between the experimental and the predicted concentrations. The results are shown in Table 4 . The results of Table 4 show that the k values are depended on both the temperature and the AA:G molar ratio. It is well known that the temperature dependency of the rate constant, (k 1, k 2, and k 3) can be expressed as Arrhenius equation. Actually, the k values at a certain AA:G molar ratio in Table 4 are well fitted well with Arrhenius equation (R 2 =0.9536–0.9872 for the linear equations: lnki =lnA i − E i /(RT), where A i is the pre-exponential factor). For example, the temperature dependency of the rate constant (k 1, k 2, and k 3) as well as its corresponding activation energy (E 1, E 2, and E 3) at AA:G molar ratio 9:1 are: k 1 min − 1 = exp 25.07 − 10534 / T , E 1 = 87.58 kJ / mol , R 2 = 0.9853 k min − 1 2 = exp 11.49 − 5467 / T , E 2 = 45.45 kJ / mol , R 2 = 0.9775 k 3 min − 1 = exp − 0.436 − 1857 / T , E 3 = 15.44 kJ / mol , R 2 = 0.9548 However, according to the rate law, k-values should be independent of the concentrations of the species involved in the reaction [30]. In contrast, different k-values were obtained for reactions conducted using different initial acetic acid to glycerol molar ratio. For instance, reactions at higher AA:G molar ratio (6:1 and 9:1) have larger k 2 values than those at 3:1. The fact that different k values are obtained when initial reactant concentrations are changed suggests that there are other physical interactions occurring in this study. The impacts of these other parameters are imbedded in the k values shown in Table 4. Of all the physical interactions occurring, probably the most important one is the phase behavior (the water phase of acetic acid and the oil phase of glycerol and glycerol esters) and the fact that phase behavior and other physical properties might also be changing as reaction proceeds due to the formation of different products such as MAG, DAG and TAG. Apparently more research work is required to enhance our fundamental understanding in the future. However, the potential of a larger process for glycerol acetylation over Amberlyst-15 to synthesize bioadditives is promising. In consideration of the effects of both AA:G molar ratio (β) and the temperature (T) as shown in Table 4, the overall apparent rate constants (k 1, k 2, k 3) can be expressed as [30]; (10) k i = k i 0 β n exp ( − E i R T ) or ln k i = ln k i 0 + n i ln β − E i R T Then we can use the linear equation ln(k i ) versus (lnβ and 1/T) to get the kinetic parameters (k i0, n i , E i ). The overall apparent rate constants (k 1, k 2, and k 3) as well as its corresponding activation energy (E 1, E 2, and E 3) were found to be; k 1 = 2 . 07 × 10 6 β 0.274 exp − 6890 T , E 1 = 57.26 kJ / mol k 2 = 18 . 66 β 1.82 exp − 3830 T , E 2 = 31.87 kJ / mol k 3 = 1 . 16 β − 0.474 exp − 1670 T , E 3 = 13.90 kJ / mol It is noted that the activation energy values (13.90–57.26kJ/mol) calculated from Eq. (10) are close to the values (15.44–87.58kJ/mol) calculated from Arrhenius equation at AA:G molar ratio 9:1. These results indicate that the activation energy values for glycerol acetylation over Amberlyst-15 are within the range of reasonable estimates. The comparative results of the k values (k i,cal) based on Eq. (10) and the k values (k i,exp) in Table 4 are shown in Fig. 6 . The fact that all the k values are close to diagonal lines indicates that there is no obvious derivation between k i,cal and k i,exp values. The influences of the molar ratios of acetic acid to glycerol and the temperature on the product distribution of the glycerol acetylation over Amberlyst-15 were investigated. The consecutive reaction mechanism of glycerol acetylation could be confirmed from the product distribution with different time. The equilibrium time of the product distribution prolonged at the higher AA:G molar ratio but shortened at the higher temperature. It was found that the conversion of glycerol increased with both the AA:G molar ratio and the temperature. However, the AA:G molar ratio was the more influential factor on the combined yield of DAG and TAG than the temperature. The main function of the catalysts in this reaction was to increase the yield of the desired products (DAG and TAG). In terms of glycerol conversion, reaction rate, and the combined yield of DAG and TAG, the optimal values are the highest molar ratio (9:1) and the highest temperature (110°C). Under these conditions the glycerol conversion was 97% and a high total yield (90%) for DAG and TAG could be achieved after 120min. Experimental data were fitted with the homogeneous first-order model. The overall apparent rate constants (k 1, k 2, and k 3) as well as its corresponding activation energy (E 1, E 2, and E 3) were found to be; k 1 = 2 . 07 × 10 6 β 0.274 exp − 6890 T , E 1 = 57.26 kJ / mol k 2 = 18 . 66 β 1.82 exp − 3830 T , E 2 = 31.87 kJ / mol k 3 = 1 . 16 β − 0.474 exp − 1670 T , E 3 = 13.90 kJ / mol The following are the supplementary data related to this article. abstract: Glycerol is a byproduct of biodiesel synthesis and it can be converted into high value-added bioadditives. The heterogeneous acetylation of glycerol with acetic acid was conducted over Amberlyst 15 using a slurry reactor. The influence of reaction conditions such as the temperature and the molar ratio of acetic acid to glycerol (AA:G) on the product distribution was investigated. The results indicated that glycerol acetylation was a consecutive reaction. The equilibrium time for the product distribution shortened with decreasing AA:G molar ratio but with increasing temperature. It was also shown that the equilibrium conversion of glycerol as well as the combined yield of di- and triacetylated glycerols (DAG+TAG) increased with the AA:G molar ratio. Moreover, the AA:G molar ratio was the more influential factor on the yield of DAG+TAG than the temperature, although increasing the temperature might also have a positive influence on this yield. Reasonable yields of mono-, di- and triacetylated glycerols could be obtained by varying the reaction time, the AA:G molar ratio, and the temperature. The kinetic model based on homogeneous first-order consecutive reaction showed that the overall apparent rate constants (k 1, k 2, and k 3) as well as its corresponding activation energy (E 1, E 2, and E 3) were found to be; k 1 = 2 . 07 × 10 6 β 0.274 exp − 6890 T , E 1 = 57.26 kJ / mol k 2 = 18 . 66 β 1.82 exp − 3830 T , E 2 = 31.87 kJ / mol k 3 = 1 . 16 β − 0.474 exp − 1670 T , E 3 = 13.90 kJ / mol
title: The acetylation of glycerol over amberlyst-15: Kinetic and product distribution

full_text: Fuel Processing Technology, 2 (1979) 155--159 155 Elsevier Scientific Publishing Company, Amsterdam -- Printed in The Netherlands CATALYTIC HYDROTREATING OF 1-METHYLNAPHTHALENE AS A SCREENING TEST S.T. BRAMMER* and S.W. WELLER Department of Chemical Engineering, State University of New York at Buffalo, Amherst, N.Y. 14260 (U.S.A.) (Received August 7th, 1978) ABSTRACT Catalytic hydrotreating of 1-methylnaphthalene, a component of most recycle vehicles in coal liquefaction processes, can provide a useful screening test for three catalytic functions: ring hydrogenation, hydrodemethylation (HDM), and ring hydrocracking to give substituted benzenes. Detailed information is obtained from the pressure change on reac- tion and from gas chromatographic analyses of both the product gas and the product liquid. A commercial Co/Mo/Al203 catalyst tested at 450 shows very little incremental HDM compared with a no-catalyst control, no ring hydrocracking, but subtantial ring hydro- genation. At 500, all reactions are more extensive. A low-temperature ash from Kentucky coal is relatively inactive at 450 except for HDM. INTRODUCTION Substituted naphthalenes are important constituents of recycle process vehicles in such coal liquefaction processes as SRC, H-Coal, Synthoil, and, presumably, the Exxon donor solvent process. Since the primary liquefaction step occurs in the presence of some catalyst -- externally added or derived from the coal mineral matter -- it is important to understand the behavior of recycle vehicle constituents in the presence of typical catalysts, elevated tem- perature, and elevated H2 pressure. The present work is intended as a contribu- tion toward such understanding, with the use of 1-methylnaphthalene as a model compound. The test method also appears promising for screening cata- lysts with regard to three catalytic functions: ring hydrogenation, hydrode- methylation, and ring hydrocracking. Catalytic hydrotreating of monomethyl- and dimethylnaphthalenes was studied by Ipatiev and Orlov as early as 1929 [1]. In the presence of alumina-- ferric oxide catalyst, an initial hydrogen pressure of 70 atm, and temperatures of 440--465, monomethylnaphthalene gave a reaction product mixture de- scribed as "benzene hydrocarbons, hydronaphthalenes, methylhydronaphtha- lenes, naphthalene, and unchanged material", with about 50% hydrodemethy- lation. Hall, as part of the comprehensive investigations at the British Fuel * Present address: The Procter and Gamble Co., Cincinnati, Ohio, U.S.A. 156 Research Board, in 1935 reported further data on the "hydrogenation-crack- ing" of 1- and 2-methylnaphthalene over a charcoal-supported ammonium molybdate catalyst [ 2]. Between 350 and 400 the principal reaction was ring hydrogenation to the corresponding methyltetralin. At 450 or 500, the methyltetralin suffered both hydrodemethylation (2-methyltetralin more slowly than 1-methyltetralin) and ring hydrocracking to give m-alkyltoluenes and alkylbenzenes. In the absence of catalyst, little reaction of 1-methylnaph- thalene occurred at 450; 61% conversion to naphthalene occurred after 2 hours at 500. Attention has been focussed in the present work on a commercial Co/Mo/ Al203 catalyst, like those that might be used in H-Coal or Synthoil processing, and a low-temperature (400) ash, obtained from Western Kentucky coal, which might be typical of coal mineral matter in an SRC-II operation. EXPERIMENTAL Equipment Batch autoclave experiments were carried out in a l-liter stirred reactor (Autoclave Engineers Model MB-1005 Magne Dash). A glass liner was used to minimize potential catalyst memory or reactor wall effects. Product gas analyses were obtained with an on-line gas chromatograph (Perkin-Elmer Model 810) for light hydrocarbon analyses. The chromatograph column was 7 ft. 1/8 in., 80--100 mesh Porapak Q; the operating tempera- ture was 80, with helium carrier gas flow rate of 45 ml/min. Liquid product samples were analyzed with a Varian Aerograph Model 1420 gas chromatograph. This instrument was equipped with 5 ft. X 1/8 in. columns packed with 1.5% OV-101 on 100--120 mesh Chromosorb G HP. The column was operated at 125 for 3 min after each sample injection, followed by a programmed temperature rise of 20/min to a maximum of 270. Helium carrier gas flow rate was 25 ml/min. Data were analyzed by a Varian CDS-111 data system, and chromatograms made by a Leeds and Northrup Azar recorder. Materials The 1-methylnaphthalene was reagent grade obtained from Eastman Kodak or Aldrich Chemical Co. The Co/Mo/A1203 catalyst used was Harshaw Type 0402T, containing approximately 15 wt.% MoO3 and 3 wt.% CoO on silica-stabilized alumina (5% SIO2). This hydrodesulfurization catalyst was --60 mesh size and had an average pore diameter of 60 A [3] .The coal ash catalyst, used as received, was obtained through the courtesy of Mr. David Burden at Peabody Coal Co. The ash had been prepared by burning a sample of coal (90% Kentucky No. 9 and 10% Kentucky No. 14) in an open crucible in a 400 oven for 16 hours. The ash, 18.6% of the original coal sample, was --100 mesh size. 157 Procedures Experimental procedures for this work were essentially those described in refs. 3 and 4. Initial hydrogen gas pressure of about 1000 p.s.i.g, at room temperature was employed in all experiments. In each run reactor agitation during heatup and the 1 h reaction period at temperature (450 or 500 + 5) was at a constant dasher frequency of 180 per minute. Reactor con- ditions for the experimental runs are tabulated in Table 1. Liquid charge to the autoclave was always 215 g of 1-methylnaphthalene. The amount of Co/Mo/A1203 catalyst in Runs 2 and 4 (Table 1) was 0.75 g; the amount of low-temperature coal ash in Run 5 was 17.3 g. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION Table 1 summarizes the results of three runs at 450 and two at 500. A control experiment with no catalyst was conducted at each temperature. In addition, (a} the catalytic effect of a commercial Co/Mo/A1203 (Harshaw 0402T) was studied at each temperature at a level of 0.35 wt.% (based on 1-methylnaphthalene charged), and (b) the catalytic effect of low-temperature ash from Western Kentucky coal was studied at 450C at a level of 8.0 wt.% (based on 1-methylnaphthalene). The product gas contained only H2, CH4, and, occasionally, small amounts of C2H6. Gas chromatographic peaks in the analysis of liquid product were identified from the correspondence of observed retention times (see Table 2) with those of known compounds available to us. The exact identity of "methyltetralin-l" and "methyltetralin-2" has not yet been established. The peaks for these appear in a composite sample, containing methyltetralins, kindly furnished by Mobil Research and Development Corp.; a sample of 6-methyltetralin, obtained through the courtesy of Suntech, Inc. has a reten- tion time intermediate between those listed in Table 2 for "methyltetralin-l" and "methyltetralin-2". These compounds appear in relatively large quantity only in the runs with Co/Mo/A12Oa catalyst. Separate autoclave experiments (not shown) indicate that these two appear in increasing amount as the time of hydrogenation at 400 is increased. Moreover, the ratio of peak areas for these peaks in the chromatogram remains roughly constant with increasing extent of hydrogenation. The two compounds, therefore, are probably 1-methyl- tetralin and 5-methyltetralin, formed with constant selectivity in the presence of Co/Mo/A1203. A minor contaminant in the charge, listed as Unknown in Tables 1 and 2, appears in all product samples as well. The "blank" run, No. 1, at 450 with no catalyst resulted in very little reaction of any kind, in agreement with Hall [2]. Only a small amount of hydrodemethylation (HDM) occurred, as shown by both the gas and the liquid analyses. The HDM reaction involves no change in number of moles of gas (one CH4 produced per H2 consumed), so that HDM entails no pressure change. By contrast, a final (cold) pressure [designated pf (cold) in Table 1] much less 158 TABLE 1 Hydrotreating of 1-methylnaphthalene Run No. 1 2 3 4 5 Temperature, 450 450 500 500 Catalyst None Harshaw* None Harshaw* Catalyst weight, g 0 0.75 0 0.75 pf (cold), p.s.i.g. 988 665 928 725 Gas analyses, mole % H~ 97.8 96.9 73.4 58.5 CH4 2.2 3.0 26.6 39.5 C2 Ha -- trace trace 2.1 Liquid analyses *** (in order of increasing retention time), area % 460 Coal ash** 17.3 892 96.8 3.2 Alkylbenzenes 0.2 -- 1.6 6.8 0.6 Tetralin -- 0.8 1.0 5.0 0.3 Naphthalene 4.5 2.7 31.4 28.6 7.0 "Methyltetralin-1" trace 7.9 -- 1.6 1.8 "Methyltetralino2'' -- 12.8 -- 5.6 0.2 1-Methylnaphthalene 93.5 73.0 63.7 51.4 88.8 Unknown 1.8 1.3 1.7 1.0 1.4 * Harshaw 0402T Co/Mo/AI203 (SiO2-stabilized) ** Coal ash obtained by burning Western Kentucky No. 9 coal at 400 in an open crucible for 16 hours. Particles pass a 100 mesh sieve. *** Compositions given are based on peak areas; so-called "area %'. Alkylbenzenes are lumped. TABLE 2 Retention times for liquid product components Compound tR*, min Alkylbenzenes (various) 0.28--1.05 trans- Decalin 1.08 cis- Decalin 1.38 Tetralin 1.48 Naphthalene 1.70 "Methyltetralin-l" 2.10 "Methyl tetrali n- 2" 3.05 1-Methylnaphthalene 3.40 Unknown 4.60 * Observed retention time less retention time of air. 159 than 1000 p.s.i.g, is a good indication that ring hydrogenation has occurred. The "blank" run at 500, Run No. 3, resulted in much more HDM, as evi- denced by much CH4 in the gas and much naphthalene in the liquid; this is again consistent with Hall [ 2]. Since HDM occurred quite cleanly, there was very little drop in final (cold) pressure, as expected. When Co/Mo/A1203 catalyst is present even in small amount, HDM at 450 (Run No. 2) is as small as in the "blank", but ring hydrogenation is very dX- tensive. This is indicated by the continued high H2 content of the gas (97%), low naphthalene content of the liquid, relatively high content of methyltetra- lins in the liquid, and low final gas pressure (665 p.s.i.g.). The use of Co/Mo/A1203 at 500 (Run No. 4) results in even more HDM than for the 500 "blank" (Run No. 3), substantial ring hydrogenation again (tetralin and methyltetralins in the liquid), and, for the first time in these ex- periments, appreciable ring hydrocracking (C2H6 in the gas, alkylbenzenes in the liquid). Finally, the use of a quite large amount of low-temperature Kentuqky coal ash at 450 (Run No. 5) results in only very modest activity for ring hydro- genation and HDM. The catalytic activity of this ash for the reactions studied is far less than that of Co/Mo/A1203, per unit weight. On the basis of the results so far obtained, this model system -- 1-methyl- naphathalene as charge, pressure change on reaction, and gas chromatographic analyses of product gas and product liquid -- appears to be a promising one for easy catalyst screening for the distinguishable catalytic functions of ring hydrogenation, hydrodemethylation, and ring hydrocracking. ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS The authors wish to express their gratitude to the Department of Energy for financial support of this work, to Mr. Gary Sellers for helpful discussions, and to Mr. David Burden for providing the sample of low-temperature coal ash.
abstract: Catalytic hydrotreating of 1-methylnaphthalene, a component of most recycle vehicles in coal liquefaction processes, can provide a useful screening test for three catalytic functions: ring hydrogenation, hydrodemethylation (HDM), and ring hydrocracking to give substituted benzenes. Detailed information is obtained from the pressure change on reaction and from gas chromatographic analyses of both the product gas and the product liquid. A commercial Co/Mo/Al2O3 catalyst tested at 450°C shows very little incremental HDM compared with a no-catalyst control, no ring hydrocracking, but subtantial ring hydrogenation. At 500°C, all reactions are more extensive. A low-temperature ash from Kentucky coal is relatively inactive at 450°C except for HDM.
title: Catalytic hydrotreating of 1-methylnaphthalene as a screening test

full_text: The overwhelming majority of the scientific community is convinced that man-made greenhouse gas emissions are the main cause of global warming [1–4]. The harmful effects of the steady rise in temperature since the beginning of industrialization are already having a strong impact on humanity and nature [5–8]. In addition, there is a constantly increasing global energy demand of about 1% per year, which is mainly due to a growing world population and an expanding economy. At the same time, the share of fossil energy sources was still 81% in 2018 [9]. The emission of vast amounts of carbon dioxide among other greenhouse gases due to the use of fossil fuel consumption has been raising serious environmental concerns. The change in global energy supply from fossil fuels to renewable energy sources is therefore an unavoidable step. In this context, the establishment of an energy system based on hydrogen can contribute significantly to the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions [10]. Main advantages are its abundance on earth, continuously replenished resources and zero, or near to zero emissions during end-use [11]. As a secondary energy carrier, the ecological footprint of hydrogen is largely depending on its primary energy source and the carbon dioxide emissions during the production. An economy based on hydrogen using fuel cells as efficient energy converters is a promising means for diminishing the harmful effects of global warming [12]. One major challenge in the widespread use of hydrogen as energy carrier is its production, wherefore primary energy resources like water or hydrocarbons must be employed. Currently, hydrogen for industrial applications is mainly produced from fossil fuels such as natural gas, heavy oils and naphtha [13]. Although, the production out of renewable feedstock is currently playing a minor role in the world's hydrogen production, it is expected to play the major role in the near future [14]. Although electrolyzers are technologically mature, the high necessary demand of electrical energy for splitting water molecules electrochemically is still preventing its establishment as the major hydrogen production source [15]. Therefore, the central large-scale steam reforming or partial oxidation of hydrocarbons is still the most widespread hydrogen production method [16]. Centralized hydrogen production has the further disadvantage of large transport distances for hydrogen distribution to the consumer using tube-trailers. The consequences thereof are a significant rise in greenhouse gas emissions, costs for post-production hydrogen compression and transportation and large space-demand for storage on-site at the customer. Chemical looping is considered as very promising technology for future sustainable energy conversion for heat generation, hydrogen production and carbon capturing [17,18]. The oxygen carrier (OC) works as a medium for the transport of pure oxygen without nitrogen dilution from the oxidizing to the reductive stream. Chemical looping hydrogen uses water as oxidant of choice, releasing pure hydrogen in the separated oxidation step. Two reactor designs for chemical looping hydrogen systems are investigated in literature, the fixed bed and the fluidized or moving bed reactor. Operation of the fixed bed reactor system is realized in batch-mode in which the reaction gases are switched between the reduction and oxidation stage of the process. In the fluidized bed setup, the active material is circulated in powder form between the fuel- and the air reactor. The fixed bed reactor setup has the advantage of simple operation and enables a compact and inexpensive construction of the system for the decentralized application [19]. Continuous hydrogen production in fixed-bed mode is possible by the employment of at least two reactors, one being in reduction mode in which the fuel is utilized and the other in oxidation mode in which hydrogen is produced. The concept of the reformer steam-iron cycle (RESC) offers a simple process layout for the generation of high-purity hydrogen based on fixed bed chemical looping [20]. The process combines a reformer unit and a chemical-looping section within a single reactor enabling the production of high-purity hydrogen for the use in polymer-electrolyte fuel cells [21] (Fig. 1 ). Previous investigations showed the successful use of artificial biogas as feedstock for the RESC [22,23]. Furthermore, up to 100 bar hydrogen production pressures without an additional gas compression step and the possibility of carbon dioxide separation while pressurized hydrogen production could be shown [24–27]. Desired properties for oxygen carrier materials involve a high oxygen exchange capacity (OEC), high reactivity, structural stability as well as feasible material and production costs [28,29]. As previous experiments showed, the oxygen carrying materials are subjected to multiple stresses simultaneously. These include (I) high thermal loads due to process temperatures of 800–900 °C, (II) significant thermal gradients between exothermic oxidation and endothermic reduction, (III) mechanical stresses caused by phase changes during the reduction and oxidation reactions (e.g. swelling) [20,21,23,29–32]. Due to the high process temperatures and constant chemical conversion from the oxidized to the reduced state, stabilizers have to be added to the material that are either high melting metal oxides or mineral structures that incorporate the active material. Due to the importance of the oxygen carrier material, international research groups are concerned with improvements that are mainly implemented by optimizing the composition or optimizing the particle structure [29]. Because of its favorable thermodynamic properties almost exclusively iron-based oxygen carriers are characterized in the scientific literature concerning the chemical looping hydrogen methods [29]. (1) Fe 2 O 3 / Fe 3 O 4 + H 2 / CO → 3 FeO + H 2 O / CO 2 ∆ H R , 298 = + 74.7 / + 33.6 kJ (2) FeO + H 2 / CO → Fe + H 2 O / CO 2 ∆ H R , 298 = + 25.4 / − 15.7 kJ (3) 3 Fe + 4 H 2 O → Fe 3 O 4 + 4 H 2 ∆ H R , 298 = − 151.1 kJ According to literature the main advantages of iron based oxygen carriers are their high activity to the reduction and oxidation reactions (Eqs. (1)–(3)), the environmental compatibility of its oxides Fe3O4, Fe2O3 and FeO as well as the lower price compared to other oxides such as NiO or CuO [27,29,33,34]. Pure Fe2O3 was studied as OC with high oxygen exchange capacity in the first cycle, however a fast decrease of the activity could be investigated due to significant particle agglomeration [35]. Since pure metals do not meet the requirements listed above, inert components such as Al2O3, ZrO2, TiO2 and SiO2 are used as additives for improving the mechanical and chemical properties [36]. The described investigations are limited primarily to cyclic material stress tests using thermogravimetric analysis in milligram-scale or small-scale fixed bed reactors with gram-scale powder. Literature studies in larger scale are mainly conducted in fluidized or moving bed mode [29,37]. Also, in the recent scientific literature the characterization of pelletized oxygen carriers for fixed bed chemical looping hydrogen is rarely found. The following lines are intended to describe some exemplary recently published studies on OC development in fixed-bed chemical looping. A short overview of the main results and the experimental setup, especially regarding the dimensions, is given. Hosseini et al. [38] investigated the behavior of Ca2Fe2O5 oxygen carriers for H2 production out of methane in a thermogravimetric system and a small-scale fixed-bed reactor (l = 400 mm, id = 10 mm) using ~100 mg of oxygen carrier material. Their research showed that doping Ca2Fe2O5-based oxygen carriers with Cu or Ni improves the redox performance significantly. Moreover, they found that the reversible exsolution of Ni or Cu nanoparticles and their reincorporation into the OC structure is necessary to avoid particle sintering and deactivation. Huang et al. [39] used a small-scale fixed-bed reactor system (l = 800 mm, id = 15 mm) with an oxygen carrier mass of ~1.5 g in order to characterize NiO, Fe2O3, synthetic NiFe2O4 and mechanical mixtures of NiFe2O4 with alumina oxygen carriers for hydrogen production characteristics out of CO feedstock. They found that due to the dispersibility between the Fe and the Ni atom the introduction of the inert alumina can effectively inhibit collapse of the spatial structure and sintering of the single NiFe2O4, which improved the reactivity and recyclability of the OC particles. The H2 production capacity of the mixed NiFe2O4 + Al2O3 OC was stable for 20 redox cycles. Ma et al. [40] used a small-scale fixed-bed reactor system (l = 750 mm, id = 22 mm) in order to compare composite Fe2O3/CeO2 oxygen carrier materials with core shell-structured Fe2O3
abstract: The successful application of fixed bed chemical looping hydrogen in industrial scale demands highly stable oxygen carrier pellets. The goal of this work was the realistic long-term characterization of the pelletized oxygen carriers in a fixed bed reactor environment. TGA characterization of an iron-based powder sample containing 5 wt% Al2O3 for 100 cycles showed fast reaction speeds and high oxygen exchange capacity. Characterization of the same sample as pellets in a fixed bed reactor revealed a high temperature gradient, decreased oxygen exchange capacity and a rapid increase in system pressure due to powder formation. The pellet lifetime was prolonged to 100 redox cycles when the material was stored in reduced state at the respective operating temperature. The increased stability of the reduced material could be attributed to the higher crushing strength of the iron and wüstite phase in contrast to magnetite. The increase of the inert portion and of the pellet calcination temperature led to an improved starting material durability but did not influence the mechanical stability after 100 cycles. The results of this study indicate that the long-term redox cycling seems to outweigh the impact of oxygen carrier preparation methods that may be overvalued in the long run.
title: The impact of manufacturing methods on the performance of pelletized, iron-based oxygen carriers for fixed bed chemical looping hydrogen in long term operation

full_text: Data will be made available on request. Roughly, 20% of global CO2 emission is due to the burning of fossil fuels in the heavy chemical industries such as iron and steel (31%), cement (27%), refineries (10%) and other petrochemical industries (32%). These heavy chemical industries release 10 GtCO2 into the atmosphere annually [1]. Steel production is predicted to exceed 2200 Mt. a year in 2050, causing a considerable rise in energy consumption and CO2 emissions. In these conditions, even the best possible energy-efficiency techniques would not be able to reduce the emission of CO2 to the level set for the future (carbon neutral by 2050) [2]. Therefore, there is a need to develop and implement CCUS techniques for CO2 reduction in the steel sector. In steel industry, the two major routes responsible for steel production are; (i) the blast furnace (BF) and basic oxygen furnace (BOF) integrated route (BF/BOF), which represents nearly 65% of the world steel production and (ii) electric arc furnace (EAF) route, which contributes nearly 35% of the world steel production [3,4]. The blast furnace gas (BFG) has very low heating value (LHV = 2–4 MJ kg−1), and a very high carbon content (> 45% by vol.). Therefore, it is important to develop CO2 capture technologies to decarbonize existing steel plants and when possible, upgrade BFG into a valuable low carbon-fuel for energy and chemical uses. In the present scenario, three routes/viable solutions have emerged as the best possible routes for the zero carbon emission in steel industry [5] namely: (i) switching from coke as a reducing agent to H2, (ii) implementation of carbon capture and storage (CCS) technologies and (iii) using biomass rather than coal as a reducing agent and source of energy. Several CCS technologies are sufficiently mature and ready to use with ideally zero carbon emission from steel industries. Pre- and post-combustion CCS technologies are widely used for retrofitting of existing plants. The monoethanolamine (MEA) based post-combustion process is a chemical absorption process widely used in industrial sector to reduce the level of CO2 in the off-gases. However, the high content of CO2 in the BFG demands for a high consumptions of steam, required for the regeneration of the absorbent, hence, causes a limited application in the steel industries [6–8]. The pre-combustion capturing of CO2 with methyldiethanolamine (MDEA) requires more modification in the existing plant as compared to the post-combustion with MEA. However, with this process nearly 90% CO2 can be avoided in the off-gases [9]. The amine-based sorbents having a high CO2 capture capacity and improved durability have been developed during the VALORCO project [10], and this technology is being tested at Technology readiness level-7 (TRL7) for steel mill off-gases in the framework of the ongoing 3D Project [11,12]. In the H2020 STEPWISE project, the low heating value BFG is transformed into a H2-rich gas having >30% vol. H2 by reacting with steam through a water-gas shift process (WGS), whereas the CO2 produced during the reaction is captured by the hydrotalcite-based sorbent [13]. The demonstration of this technology up to TRL6–7 has been carried out during the consecutive EU programmes CACHET, CAESAR, and STEPWISE [14]. A wide range of additional emerging CO2 capture technologies have been proposed to target gas streams in the iron and steel industries, either as post-combustion or as a top gas recycle concepts (advanced solvents [15], membranes [16], new solid sorbents for Pressure Swing Adsorption and Vacuum Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA/VPSA) [17], chemical looping and calcium looping [18,19]). All these technologies present issues regarding stability of functional materials, role of impurities, energy consumption, retrofitability and need of rigorous quantitative estimation of cost and overall process efficiencies. The Ca-based materials have also been widely proposed as CO2- sorbents in various pre-combustion applications [20,21] [22], they theoretically allow process intensification by conducting several reactions steps in a single reactor and can yield high theoretical energy efficiencies because their operating conditions at suitably high temperatures. However, the development of these processes have encountered serious issues, as a very high temperature (~900 °C) is required during the regeneration of the used sorbent to produce a CO2 rich gas stream. Despite of having an advantage of recovering thermal energy input during the carbonation and heat removal stages, there is still a main challenge of providing a thermal heat during the regeneration stage. To counter this issue, one feasible solution is to merge the sorption-enhanced process and the chemical looping combustion (CLC) of metal oxides. During the CaCu chemical looping process, the reduction of CuO using a various gaseous fuel such as CH4, CO, or H2 provides the necessary heat for the calcination of the carbonated sorbent resulted in producing CO2 and H2O without the need of any air separation unit (ASU) [23]. In EU ASCENT project, a considerable development was made on reactor modelling [24,25] with the preparing and characterization of CaCu materials [25], process integration [26,27], and lab-scale to TRL5 scale validation of packed beds reactor [28–30]. The application of CaCu chemical process in steel-mills to produce H2 and CO2 rich using interconnected fluidized bed reactors have been widely proposed in literature [18,31]. The arrangement of interconnected fluidized bed reactor not only helps in assisting the heat management in the highly exothermic redox stages, but it also results in a continuous production of desirable product streams. However, this setup has a pressure restrictions and needs an extra unit to separate the Ca- from the Cu-based materials once the sorbent has been completely regenerated. In a previous work [32], a variant of these processes has been proposed using packed bed reactors to conceptually design the Calcium Assisted Steel-mill Off-gas Hydrogen (CASOH) production process (Fig. 1 ) based on the concept of pre-combustion capture with Calcium Assisted WGS to capture the CO2 at the downstream of BF in a steel plant. In the proposed CASOH process, BFG is transformed into a H2-rich stream free of CO2 with higher Lower Heating Value (LHV) while producing a large amount of high-temperature heat. The process is carried out in dynamically operated packed-bed reactors following a set of three main reaction stages: CASOH, Cu oxidation and regeneration as shown in Fig. 1. The main reactions considered in the CASOH process are illustrated in Table 1 . The conditions of temperature and pressure are changed to facilitate the conditions of the solids bed in each stage, following well established principles of PSA/TSA and regenerative heat-transfer processes [32]. In the present work, we have used the combined CaCu based material in a packed bed reactor to study the main reaction stages (CASOH stage, Cu-oxidation stage and reduction/calcination stage) occurring during the CASOH process. Prior to the CASOH experimentation, the individual Ca- and Cu-based materials are tested under the various temperature (400–850 °C) and pressure (1–5 bar) conditions for over 300 h of operation. The CASOH experiments are carried out in a packed bed reactor under the different conditions of temperature (600–870 °C), pressure (1–5 bar), flowrate of feed (10–20 NLPM) and inlet gas compositions. A lot of research has been carried out on sorption enhanced WGS and sorption enhanced reforming, as well as chemical/Calcium looping technology applied to H2 production. Despite the research proposed in that area does not refer to integrated steelmill [30,33–41]. The pseudo-continuous CASOH process in a lab-scale packed bed reactor under high-pressure conditions is demonstrated first time in the literature. Apart from the testing of the gas-solid combined chemical/calcium looping above atmospheric pressure and flowrate which has never been presented in literature confirming several of the hypotheses previously formulated but never demonstrated, this work is also looking at the process operation by using existing material paving the way to the further scale-up and demonstration. In the CASOH stage, the 20% vol. CO present in the BFG reacts with the steam through the WGS reaction (R7) and produces a H2/N2 fuel gas. The Cu acts as a catalyst during the WGS reaction and the CaO reacts with CO2 forming CaCO3 (R1). The continuous removal of the formed CO2 from the gas phase shifts the WGS equilibrium towards a higher H2 production (about 34%, on a dry basis), therefore, it is feasible to have a higher CO conversion under the operating conditions of 600 °C to 650 °C and 5.0 bar, and consequently  7.5 min), the reactor acts as a simple WGS unit and a steady state product gases composition profile is achieved (i.e. 11% H2, 4% CO, 29% CO2 and 56% N2 by vol.). Fig. 9 shows the conversion of CO breakthrough curve and conversion profile during the CASOH stage. During the pre-breakthrough period, where the CaO-based sorbent is completely active, 100% CO conversion is achieved. In contrast, this conversion drops down to 95% in the post-breakthrough period where only WGS process is taking place. The results indicate that the system CaO/CuO is capable of converting a gas with typical BFG composition in a H2/N2 gas stream. The H2-rich gas obtained during CASOH stage could be used in the steel mill for power generation or sent to PSA to produce pure H2 (at industrial grade). Other applications of this H2/N2 stream could also be the production of additional sponge iron by fuelling an auxiliary Direct Reduction of Iron, as proposed in [31], the production of NH3, as recently proposed in the H2020 INITIATE [44], or gasoline and other liquid fuel or olefins, as proposed in H2020 FRESME [45] and SPIRE COZMOS [46]. Fig. 10 shows the CO2 composition breakthrough curves at the exit of the reactor during the CASOH stage after 8 cycles of CASOH process under the operating conditions of 600 °C, 5.0 bar and S/CO molar ratio of 2.0. The results from consecutive cycles indicate that although the sorbent is progressively reducing its CO2 carrying capacity [47], it is still capable of maintaining its reactivity while there is active CaO in the bed. In this way, gas product composition is not determined by the amount of CaO active in the bed, but the CASOH duration is affected. Once the CASOH stage is completed, most of the reactor bed is still at a very high temperature (approximately at 690 °C). Since the Cu oxidation is an exothermic reaction, a further increase of the solid temperature would start to release CO2 along with the N2, thus reducing the CO2 capture efficiency of the overall process. In order to avoid it, a heat removal (HR-1) stage is required to remove the excess amount of accumulated heat in the reactor. Therefore, a flow of 5.0 NLPM of N2 at 600 °C is fed into the reactor and the exit gas is cooled downstream. The amount of heat recovered through the exit gas in this stage can be used at a large scale as a heat source for other units of the steelwork or it can be used to produce high-pressure (HP) steam that can later be used for the power generation. Once HR-1 has been accomplished, (i.e. the bed is at 600 °C), the oxidation stage is tested at high pressure (5.0 bar) using various O2 concentration (10% - 21%) in the feed, as listed in Table 2. At the start of this stage, the CaO based sorbent is carbonated and the Cu-based material is present in the reduced form. The objective of the oxidation stage is to oxidize the Cu without decomposing the CaCO3. Fig. 11 (a) shows the O2 and CO2 breakthrough curves during the Cu-oxidation over 30 consecutive cycles performed at 600 °C, 5.0 bar and 10% O2 in the feed. The O2 and CO2 profiles are highly reproducible, which demonstrates the great stability of the Cu-based material. The high pressure during the oxidation of Cu to CuO limits the leakage of CO2 by partial calcination of CaCO3. A maximum of 0.6% of CO2 is observed in the product gas after 5 min of operation. The amount of CO2 measured during the oxidation indicates that only 3.5% of the calcium carbonate present in the bed is calcined. As the O2 is totally converted, the product gas during the pre-breakthrough period consists almost of pure N2. Fig. 11 (b) illustrates the temperature profile along the length of the reactor during the Cu-oxidation. The highly exothermic reaction of Cu oxidation causes a rise in temperature of the bed to 712 °C. This temperature increase is due to the 15% by wt. of active Cu present in the reactor. The O2 and CO2 breakthrough curves for various temperature (600–650 °C) and pressure (1–5 bar) is presented in Fig. 12 . Due to the considerable heat losses and the effect cooling of the inlet gases, the temperature across the solid bed is not uniform, with the difference between first TC and the initial bed temperature being 50–100 °C. The O2 breakthrough time at 600 °C and 650 °C is identical but the amount of CO2 released at 650 °C is higher (1.6% vol.) as compared to the amount of CO2 released at 600 °C (0.6% vol.) as shown in Fig. 12 (a). The higher leakage of CO2 at 650 °C is the consequence of the maximum temperature (767 °C) achieved as compared to the maximum temperature (712 °C) achieved at 600 °C oxidation as shown in Fig. 12 (b). The decomposition of CO2 is favourable at high temperature and low pressure as it can also be seen in Fig. 13 ; more CO2 is released during the oxidation stage as we decrease the pressure from 5.0 bar to 1.0 bar under the same operating temperature and flowrate conditions. Moreover, an increase in pressure causes a later O2 breakthrough, mainly due to 1) the decrease in gas velocity with an increase in pressure, thus resulted in increase in the gas residence time inside the reactor and 2) the strong dependency of the pressure on the kinetics [48,49]. Fig. 14 illustrates the effect of O2 content in feed (10%, 13%, 19% and 21%) on the O2 and CO2 breakthrough curves during the oxidation at 600 °C, 5.0 bar and 10 NLPM conditions. As can be seen, with the increase in O2 content, the O2 breakthrough time decreases. Moreover, as the concentration of O2 in the feed increases, the CO2 concentration at the reactor outlet also increases due to the increase in the temperature in the bed, which favours the CaCO3 decomposition. When feeding 21% O2 the product gas contains 1% vol. of CO2. However, only 0.6% vol. of CO2 is measured when the inlet gas contains 10% of O2. Once Cu is completely oxidized to CuO, the reactor is purged for 5 min with N2 gas (5 NLPM) to eliminate any trace of O2 in the bed and the pressure is reduced to almost atmospheric pressure to facilitate the calcination of CaCO3 at temperatures below 900 °C. To avoid the calcination of CaCO3 during the heating up of the reactor after the oxidation to the operating temperature of the reduction/calcination, reactor bed is heated up to the reduction/calcination temperature in 100% vol. CO2 at atmospheric pressure. Once the calcination/reduction temperature is reached, CO2 is tuned off and the reduction feed is introduced for the reduction and calcination reactions to take place. In these conditions, the Cu/Ca molar ratio present in the solids bed is 1.4. In the rig used in this work, an initial solid temperature profile of 840–850 °C is required to achieve a final regeneration temperature of 870 °C. Different mixtures of reducing gases and steam have been used during the reduction/calcination tests, as listed in Table 2. Fig. 15 (a) shows the molar composition of the product gases at the outlet of the reactor during the reduction/calcination stage. When a gaseous mixture composed of 20% CO, 20% CO2, 60% N2 and 1.5 S/CO molar ratio is fed into the packed-bed at 800 °C a mere 10% CaCO3 is calcined according to the amount of CO2 released during the experiment. During the first 4 min of the operation, the product gas is mainly composed of 40% CO2 and 60% N2. Once the reduction/calcination front reaches the last portion of the bed, the CuO-based material approaches total conversion to Cu, and CO along with H2 starts appearing in the product gas as the reactor acts as a single WGS unit. In these conditions, the temperature of the solids is insufficient and the complete calcination of CaCO3 can only be achieved by heating the bed using an electric furnace. Fig. 15 (b) shows the temperature profile with time along the length of the reactor. The sorbent calcination takes place in the region of the bed whose temperature is well above 840 °C, according to the carbonation/calcination equilibrium (i.e. PCO2eq, 840 °C = 0.4). To observe the effect of initial temperature on CaCO3 calcination, the reduction/calcination stage has been performed between 800 °C and 870 °C at atmospheric pressure. Fig. 16 shows the product gases evolution profile at different initial temperatures in the bed (800–870 °C). The outlet composition of product gases is mainly comprised of CO2 and N2, as shown in Fig. 16. As the operating temperature increases, the amount of CO2 released during the reduction/calcination is higher. In this way, the amount of CO2 released at 870 °C corresponds to 80% calcination of CaCO3 as compared to 10% and 50% calcination at 800 °C and 850 °C, respectively. These results show that the calcination of CaCO3 is favourable at higher temperature. During calcination/reduction, the heat is transferred from CuO-Cu to CaCO3-CaO and the final temperature of the system is 870 °C. the heat transfer from particle-to-particle occurs fast to generate a single reaction and heat front in the reaction as shown in Fig. 15 and Fig. 16 (for the first 4 min). The same conclusions were also achieved in two previous study from CSIC [50]. Total calcination of the sorbent can be achieved by prolonging the subsequent purge with N2 carried out at temperatures above 850 °C by further heating using electric furnace (i.e. for t 95% vol.) If CH4 or other gases with low content of N2 such as coke oven gas (COG) are used instead of BFG during the reduction/calcination stage, the CO2 purification can be simplified as the only products obtained are CO2 and H2O. However, this scenario also causes a large additional CH4 consumption [32]. For this reason, in this work, a gas stream containing 20% CH4 in N2 (CASE 1) and a gas mixture containing 20% CO, 20% CO2, 20% CH4 and 40% N2 (CASE 2) along with steam (S/CH4 = 3) has also been used as reducing gases for the sorbent regeneration stage. Fig. 18 (a – b) shows higher concentrated CO2 gas streams at the reactor exit for CASE 1 and CASE 2. According to the mass balance, about 26% CaCO3 is calcined using the gas mixture in CASE 1 under the operating conditions of 800 °C and 1.0 bar. On the other hand, 64% CaCO3 is calcined using the CASE 2 gas mixture operating at 870 °C and 1.0 bar. The composition of product gases at the exit of reactor during each stage is shown in Fig. 19 . As discussed earlier, CASOH stage involves the production of H2-rich stream (17% H2 on dry basis). H2-rich stream is intended as a gas that would be substantially pure H2 (>99%) on dry and N2 free basis. The CO2 captured in CASOH stage is mainly released during the regeneration stage as CaO-based sorbent is regenerated by the calcination of CaCO3. In the regeneration stage, the outlet gas rich in CO2 (46% CO2 and 54% N2) would necessitate an additional capture step. This could be possible by employing CO2/N2 adsorption process or cryogenic separation. Fig. 20 shows the one complete CASOH cycle temperature profile starting from heating the bed to the cooling down the reactor after the reduction/calcination stage under the optimum operating conditions for each stage in the CASOH process. At the end of the experimental campaign, the structural characterization of the CaCu material after the different stages in the CASOH process is observed using the Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) data for the fresh and spent materials (Ca-based and Cu based material). The SEM images of fresh and spent Cu material show that there is a change in the structure of Cu material after multiple cycles of CASOH process. The overall surface area of Cu material has increased as can be seen in more magnified images as shown in Fig. 21 . On the other hand, the SEM images of fresh and spent Ca-based material show that there is no change in the structure of the material after multiple cycles of CASOH process as shown in the Fig. 22 . In this paper, the three main reaction stages of the process were examined under the various operating conditions of temperature, pressure, flowrate and feed composition. The results show that the system CaO/CuO is capable of converting a gas with typical BFG composition in a H2/N2 gas stream (H2/N2: 17%/82%, <1% unconverted CO and leakage of CO2) under the operating conditions of 600 °C feed temperature, 5.0 bar and S/CO of 2.0. The maximum temperature attained during the Cu-oxidation stage can be restricted below 700 °C, which causes only 3.5% calcination of the CaCO3 available in the bed. The calcination of CaCO3 increases from 3.5% to 8.0% as the initial solids bed temperature increases from 600 °C to 650 °C at 5.0 bar operating pressure. The amount of CO2 released during the oxidation increases from 0.6% vol. to 1.6% vol. with the decrease in pressure from 5.0 bar to 1.0 bar. The regeneration stage is carried out with different gases available in a steel mill and is expected to lead to a complete calcination of the CaCO3 present in the bed, giving as product a gas with different CO2 content. The CuO reduction, using BFG, at 800 °C, 850 °C and 870 °C causes 10%, 50% and 80% calcination of CaCO3 respectively. This shows a temperature higher than 850 °C is preferable for the sorbent regeneration. The CO2 content starting from 46% diluted in N2 (on dry basis) in case of only BFG at 870 °C, 33% in CASE 1 (20% CH4 and 80% N2 in feed) at 800 °C and 50% CO2 in CASE 2 (20% CO, 20% CO2, 20% CH4 and 40% N2 in feed). This makes necessary a subsequent purification of CO2 in a CPU unless a N2-free fuel such as NG is used. In terms of energy outputs, the results demonstrate that it is possible to convert a low quality BFG stream into a H2 rich gas and pure CO2 stream with a very low energy penalty. Syed Zaheer Abbas: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Validation. José Ramón Fernández: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Alvaro Amieiro: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Monisha Rastogi: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Johan Brandt: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Vincenzo Spallina: Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.
abstract: In the present work, a lab-scale packed bed reactor has been used to decarbonize mixtures of inlet gases simulating the typical composition of blast furnace gases (BFG) and convert them to H2-rich streams by means of the CaCu chemical looping concept. The reactor was packed with 355 g of Cu-based oxygen carrier (OC) supported on Al2O3 and natural Ca-based sorbent. The three main reaction stages; namely (i) Calcium Assisted Steel-mill Off-gas Hydrogen (CASOH), (ii) Cu oxidation and (iii) Regeneration of carbonated Ca-based sorbent were examined. In CASOH stage, BFG is converted into H2-rich stream (17% by vol.) under the experimental conditions of 600 °C, 5.0 bar and S/CO molar ratio of 2.0. A controlled oxidation causes a mere 3.5% of CaCO3 to decompose during the Cu-oxidation stage. This resulted in a nearly pure N2 stream at 600 °C and 5.0 bar operating conditions. During the regeneration stage, BFG and mixture of BFG and CH4 is used as a reducing fuel. To ensure the amount of heat needed for the decomposition of CaCO3 during the reduction of CuO, a 1.4 CuO/CaCO3 molar ratio has been used. It resulted in 46% CO2 in N2 at the end of the reduction/calcination stage.
title: Lab-scale experimental demonstration of Ca Cu chemical looping for hydrogen production and in-situ CO2 capture from a steel-mill
