full_text: After excavation, the coal exposed to air will absorb oxygen and release heat which accumulates in the coal under appropriate conditions and accelerates the oxidation reaction process of coal, producing more energy and eventually leading to coal spontaneous combustion. The combustion not only causes waste of resources and pollution to the environment, but also acts as the ignition source in many gas explosion accidents. Thus, it is very important to prevent coal spontaneous combustion during coal mining [1–3]. Inhibitors, which are simple and convenient to use for preventing coal spontaneous combustion, are widely applied in coal mines. However, previous researches on inhibitors mainly focus on the inhibitors' physical and chemical properties such as their water absorption and moisture retention properties and their influences on the coal spontaneous combustion are rarely studied, leaving defects of low inhibitory efficiency and short inhibitory time [4–7]. Hence, it becomes particularly important to make an in-depth study on an efficient inhibitor that can suppress coal spontaneous combustion from the perspective of coal's self-ignition factors. The internal factors causing the occurrence of coal spontaneous combustion include the physical and chemical properties, lithofacies distribution and mineral composition of coal [8–11]. Mineral inorganic salts in coal contain abundant metal ions that can pose different influences on the combustion. As we know, the spontaneous combustion tendency of coal increases with the increase of pyrite content, and the Fe2+/3+ can remarkably promote the combustion. On the contrary, NaCl, CaCl2 and MgCl2 can be used as inhibitors to retard the spontaneous combustion of coal in goaf. Na+, Ca2+ and Mg2+ in these substances can exert a certain inhibitory effects on the combustion. Relevant scholars have conducted in-depth researches on different effects of metal ions on coal spontaneous combustion, as well as their corresponding mechanisms. Wang [12], who studied the inhibition mechanism of Ca2+ on coal spontaneous combustion based on the theory of quantum chemistry, revealed that nitrogen, phosphorus, sulfur, oxygen and other active groups contained in coal formed coordination chemical bonds and ligands with metal Ca2+, thus lowering the activity of active groups to react with oxygen. Tang [13] proved that manganese ions could accelerate coal spontaneous combustion by studying the effects of three kinds of manganese salts on the combustion; meanwhile, he found via infrared spectroscopy that manganese ions could promote the decomposition of CO and alkyl, and obtained via a thermal analysis instrument that manganese could promote the oxidative decomposition to release more heat; moreover, he also pointed out the possible mechanism for its catalysis on the coal oxidation. After studying the process in which different metal ions accelerated the oxidation of Shenfu coal sample, Jiang [14] revealed that rare metal ions Co2+, Ni2+ and La3+ had a more prominent catalytic effect on oxidation by analyzing the product structure via infrared spectroscopy. Azik et al. [15] found that during the process of oxidation, iron (II) sulfate was formed in 24 h; aliphatic CH2 and CH3 groups barely changed during the formation of iron (II) sulfate; then, after the formation, aliphatic CH2 and CH3 groups started to oxidize. The above studies show that metal ions (such as Ca2+ and Mg2+) in the halide salt can inhibit coal spontaneous combustion to some extent, while transition metal ions (such as Fe2+,Co2+ and Ni2+) can promote the occurrence of coal spontaneous combustion to a large extent. People often prevent coal spontaneous combustion by adding halide salts in production applications, yet there are very few researches on the prevention of coal spontaneous combustion by means of suppressing metal catalysis. Many oxidation processes take place when metal ions participate in the transfer of electrons during the change of the valence state to shorten the chain initiation period. Therefore, the occurrence of coal spontaneous combustion will be restrained to some extent, if the transition metal ions with a catalytic effect can be reduced and the activation energy of coal oxidation can be increased. EDTA, as a very strong metal ion chelating agent, can combine with metal ions in the form of coordination bond to form stable six-membered ring chelates, thus inactivating metal ions and raising the activation energy of organic oxidation. This property of metal chelating agents is widely used in the remediation of heavy metal in soils and the extraction of heavy metals in plant waste streams [16–19]. In the food-processing industry, it is used to stop metal ions from catalyzing the oxidative deterioration of food [20–22]. In industries of polymer products like rubber, it is applied to the prevention of occurrence of catalytic aging for maintaining the service life and performance. In the medicine field, it is used as anticoagulant to inactivate calcium ions, so that blood can be stopped from coagulating [23]. Nevertheless, the application of EDTA in coal industry is very limited, as it is used for the titration of metal ion to determine the contents of constant metallic ions in coal. In this paper, a highly effective inhibitor for coal spontaneous combustion control was studied from the perspective of inactivating transition metal ions. A new idea of inactivating metal ions in coal and increasing activation energy of coal oxidation was proposed considering internal factors that affect coal spontaneous combustion instead of just the nature of inhibitor. The transition metal elements and their contents in coal, the mass and heat release of coal before and after the inhibition, the gas composition and changes of functional groups on coal surface at different temperatures were tested and analyzed using techniques such as XRF, thermal analysis, infrared spectroscopy and gas chromatography. In addition, the apparent activation energy of coal oxidation was calculated. Four kinds of coal samples were selected for the experiment, namely Wu Lan coal sample, Bu Lianta coal sample, Kou Zidong coal sample and Da Fosi coal sample. The proximate and metamorphic degree of the coal samples are shown in Table 1 . The newly exposed coal samples were vacuum-sealed underground and then brought to the laboratory, after which they were crushed in the protective atmosphere of nitrogen and sieved to a particle size range of 0.180–0.380 mm. After 30 g of EDTA water solution with a mass fraction of 10% was prepared, 50 g of coal sample was separately added into it and stirred evenly for inhibitory immersion of 48 h. Then, the samples were dried in a vacuum drying box at 40 °C. The comparative tests were carried out by adding distilled water of the same mass. An obvious knot phenomenon appeared after the treatment of coal samples. The transition metal elements in coal can promote its spontaneous combustion. In this experiment tested, the composition and contents of chemical elements in coal was tested on the X-Ray Fluorite Spectroscopy S8 Tiger instrument produced by Bruker Company, Germany, so as to describe the possible transition metal elements and their contents in coal. First, the four coal samples were subjected to ashing treatment in a muffle furnace and the ash samples were ground to below 74 μm. Next, it was made into a 6 cm3 flake for the test and analysis. The contents of transition metal elements in coal ash are listed in Table 2 . The low-temperature oxidation experimental device consists of a programmed temperature device and a chromatographic analysis device (see Fig. 1 ). The programmed temperature device is a 2000-type temperature oven for coal spontaneous combustion. The chromatographic analysis device, a FULI9790 chromatographic analyzer made in China, includes three channels and can simultaneously analyze the concentrations of gases such as CO, C2H4, CO2 and O2 at the outlet. When the margins of error of gas concentration both lie within 0.5% for two measurements, the peak areas of gases at the outlet were measured, based on which the concentrations were calculated. Heat generation and accumulation is the most direct cause of coal spontaneous combustion [24, 25]. Besides, mass and heat changes are also a concomitant phenomenon during physical and chemical reactions. The raw coal and EDTA-inhibited coal sample was heated in the atmosphere of dry air on a NETZSCH STA 409C thermal analyzer produced in Germany. To eliminate mass loss caused by water evaporation and gas desorption in coal during the heating, the TG-DSC of low-temperature pyrolysis of coal in the protective atmosphere of argon was carried out as a reference. The analysis of mass and heat changes was limited within the temperature range of 25–240 °C, because the inhibition experiment mainly focuses on characteristic parameter variation of the inhibitor during the low-temperature oxidation. The heating rate and the flow rates of both gases were set as 5 K/min and 50 ml/min, respectively; a set of experimental data was recorded every 0.01 min. The changes of gas concentration and crossing point temperature before and after the inhibition can directly reflect the occurrence degree of coal oxidation [26], so the paper studied changes in gas composition and crossing point temperature of coal samples before and after the EDTA treatment by performing programmed temperature experiments. 50 g of each prepared coal sample was placed in the sample tank within the programmed temperature oven (see Fig. 1). After a check of gas paths, the programmed heating experiment was initiated at the air flow rate and heating rate of 50 ml/min and 0.5 °C/min, respectively. Within the range of 30–180 °C, the gas taken at the duct end via a 20 ml syringe was sent to the gas chromatograph every 10 °C temperature rise. The generated image was processed; compositions and concentrations of gases at the outlet were calculated; and changes of oven temperature and coal temperature were recorded. The WL raw and EDTA-inhibited samples were ground to below 0.075 mm and heated to 40 °C, 80 °C, 120 °C and 160 °C respectively in the atmosphere of dry air at a flow rate of 25 ml/min. After being oxidized for 30 min, the samples were prepared for the experiment. The experiment was performed on a Vertex 80V Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy produced by Bruker Company, Germany, and the KBr pellet method was adopted in the spectroscopy experiment. 2 mg of raw coal and 2 g of dried KBr analytical reagent were ground in a grinding machine for about 10 min to enable them to mix well. 200 mg of the mixed sample was evenly spread on a press mould of 13 mm in diameter. The sample was pressed under 10 MPa pressure for 1 min into an even flake for the spectroscopic measurement. The experimental wave number range was 400–4000 cm−1, and the sample was scanned 64 times. The experimental thermogravimetric analysis of coal samples before and after EDTA treatment within 25–240 °C was made. To better eliminate the interference of irrelevant factors and analyze the reaction state in each stage, the heating experiment of raw coal in the protective atmosphere of argon was carried out as a comparison. The change curves of mass and heat of coal samples with temperature are shown in Fig. 2 . The mass and heat of raw, inhibited and pyrolyzed coal samples with temperature in the temperature range of 25–240 °C can be directly observed from Fig. 2. The test [27] proves that the coal sample has already started physical oxygen adsorption process at −20 °C. Hence, before the temperature reaches 70 °C, both raw and inhibited coal samples undergo oxygen adsorption, gas desorption and water evaporation during the temperature rise. Since oxygen mainly adsorbs on coal physically at low temperature, only a small amount of oxygen is adsorbed, while the gas desorption amount and water evaporation amount are very large. In this stage, the mass of coal declines rapidly and then the decline slows down as a result of the three processes. After the temperature exceeds 83.12 °C, chemical oxygen adsorption on coal sample develops, and the mass of oxygen adsorption gets higher than that of gas and lost water in coal. This is a mass increase stage where the mass of coal sample rises slowly on a macro level. Compared to the raw coal, the inhibited sample possesses a smaller mass at the same temperature. The masses of samples exhibit downward trends, showing no mass gains. Moreover, with the rise of temperature, the gap between mass percentages widens gradually, with the gas being up to 3.30% at 240 °C. From the TG curve of pyrolyzed sample, it can be observed that the pyrolyzed sample undergoes a similar mass gain to the raw coal within 110.1–149.3 °C where the mass of the pyrolyzed sample increases by 0.23% due to the adsorption of argon on coal. Subsequently, it decreases rapidly to 87.61% at 240 °C, which is much lower than 93.31% of raw coal and 89.99% of inhibited sample. It can be obtained from the DCS curves that all the three samples absorb heat before they release it in the whole heating process (from 25 °C to 240 °C). Among them, the raw and EDTA-inhibited coal samples share basically the same change curves, while the pyrolyzed sample merely possesses a basically the same heat change curve in the early stage of temperature rise. The separation appears at about 135 °C, as the heat release amount and rate of pyrolyzed sample get lower than those of the other two. The analysis of changes in each temperature range and in TG curves reveals that all the three samples have experienced an endothermic phase from 25 °C to 75 °C, while their TG curves fall rapidly, primarily because of the water evaporation in coal and the gas desorption on coal. The heat release amounts of the three samples decline to zero at around 75 °C, indicating the ending of endothermic process and the beginning of exothermic process. Fig. 3 shows the generation amounts of CO, CO2 and C2H4 and the consumption amount of oxygen at the outlet in various reaction stages (from 30 °C to 180 °C) before and after the coal sample was treated with EDTA. As can be observed from Fig. 3, in the beginning stage, namely, between 30 °C and 50 °C, the oxidation degree of coal is relatively low with no CO release. With the rise of coal temperature, CO and CO2 concentrations of the raw and treated coal samples both show an exponentially ascending trend, and the generation amount and rate of the raw coal are both significantly higher than those of the treated coal sample. The lower the temperature of C2H4 (a product of coal spontaneous combustion to a certain extent) is, the more likely the sample will combust spontaneously. The C2H4 emission in Fig. 3 demonstrates that after the treatment, the initial temperature of C2H4 is delayed from 110 °C to 130 °C, and the C2H4 production amount and rate are much lower than those of raw coal at the same temperature. The analysis of oxygen content at the outlet suggests that the oxygen content of the treated coal sample is significantly higher than that of the raw coal, which is consistent with the decline of oxygen adsorption amount of the treated coal sample in the TG curve. Crossing point temperature is one of the kinetic parameters for determining coal spontaneous combustion [28, 29]. The coal sample temperature is lower than the tank temperature in the initial stage, and then the coal sample temperature increases gradually affected by heat transfer and heat release from coal oxidation. The point when coal sample center temperature exceeds the oven temperature is defined as the crossing point [30, 31]. The crossing point temperatures before and after the treatment are presented in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4 demonstrates that the temperature of coal sample within the programmed temperature oven rises linearly with the passage of time, which is presented as a straight line in the figure. The red solid line and the blue dotted line represent the raw coal sample temperature monitored by the temperature probe at the center of the coal sample and the coal sample temperature after the EDTA treatment. The temperature of the coal sample tank crosses at about 232 min and 266 min in the two curves. At this point, the crossing point temperatures of raw and treated samples are 145.6 °C and 165.1 °C, respectively, indicating that the crossing point temperature is increased by 19.5 °C and its appearance is delayed by 34 min only under the same external conditions. The infrared spectroscopic data of coal can reflect the content and distribution of active functional groups on coal surface [32]. To describe the change of active functional groups on coal surface before and after EDTA inhibition, the spectroscopic experiments were carried out under different temperature conditions. The experimental data are exhibited in Fig. 5 . As can be seen clearly from Fig. 5, the overall absorbance curve of the treated coal sample is much lower than that of the raw coal. That is, the number of active functional groups in the treated coal increases extremely slowly during the heating. Even the absorbance strength of functional groups on the treated coal at 160 °C is far lower than that in the raw coal at 40 °C. This suggests that compared with the raw coal, the treated coal sample owns significantly decreased active functional groups, indicating a good inhibitory effect. The treated coal sample can well restrain hydroxyl groups, carboxyl groups, alkyl groups, aldehyde groups, etc. The spectroscopic curve of each band was peak fitted using peak software, so as to quantitatively describe the change trend and amount of the active functional groups during the temperature rise more accurately. Hydroxyl groups are greatly affected by moisture during heating and are not discussed here. According to the previous researches [33, 34], the wavenumber range is divided into two parts: 1500–1850 cm−1 and 2800–3000 cm−1. The changes of the structures of alkyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups in the coal are compared before and after the inhibition. The accurate positions corresponding to the absorbance peak and shoulder peak of original spectrum were found according to the peak and valley of the second order derivative, after which peak fit treatment was carried out. Fig. 6 is a detailed peak fitting of coal samples at two different wavenumbers. Among them, 2955 cm−1 and 2922 cm−1 are the asymmetrical vibration of methyl group and the asymmetrical vibration of methylene group; 1710 cm−1 belongs to the carboxy-attributed peak, and 1648 cm−1 belongs to the carbonyl assigned peak. Fig. 7 was plotted from the attribution and area of characteristic peaks of specific functional groups obtained after the peak fit. The changes in active functional groups can be visually seen from Fig. 7. With the rise of temperature and the increase of chemical oxygen adsorption on coal, CO, the product of coal-oxygen compound reaction begins to accumulate. The areas of its corresponding functional groups in the raw and inhibited coal samples keep increasing, but compared with the raw coal, the inhibited sample has a far smaller content of functional groups as well as a much slighter increase. Overall, the content of CH2 and CH3 in the raw and inhibited coal samples decrease in the heating process. The content of CH2 increase abruptly at 80 °C and then decrease notably at 120 °C. The reason may be that the breakage of some unstable chemical bonds in coal leads to the grown number of groups, and both the oxidation and consumption rates of active groups are accelerated increasingly with further rise of temperature, resulting in a decrease in numbers of the two groups. The total content of CH2 and CH3 in the inhibited coal sample are obviously smaller than those in the raw coal. The analysis of COOH in the coal shows that as the temperature rises, the content of carboxyl groups grows before the inhibition and falls after the inhibition. The combination of EDTA with metal ions inhibits the catalytic effect of metal ions on the reaction of functional groups in coal. Thus, the rate of coal oxidation is lowered, which is reflected in the greatly decreased content of functional groups in the inhibited coal sample compared to that of the raw coal. Activation energy is an important kinetic parameter for the study of low-temperature oxidation of coal. The combustion reaction of coal is a complex multistage reaction process, so apparent activation energy is used to describe the energy required for the reaction [35–37]. Apparent activation energy for the low-temperature oxidation of coal is calculated in accordance with the oxygen consumption during coal oxidation. This study measured the changes of oxygen consumptions of the coal samples before and after the inhibition with rising temperature through a programmed temperature experiment, and then found the difference of activation energy before and after the treatment through linear treatment according to the Arrhenius equation. Unlabelled Image From the Arrhenius equation, the rate of coal oxidation at any temperature is: (1) V Ti = V O 2 = AC O 2 n exp − E / RT where V is the reaction rate; T i represents the thermodynamic temperature of coal, K; A is the pre-exponential factor; n standards for the reaction order which equals 1 the low-temperature oxygen adsorption stage; E is the activation energy, J/mol; and R is the molar gas constant, 8.314 J/(mol·K). In the heating experiment, the air flow was assumed to move along the axial direction of the coal sample. Then, the reaction rate of O2 at a section dx taken from any axial is as follows: (2) S ⋅ V O 2 dx = − V g dC where S is the cross-sectional area of coal sample tank, m2; V(O2) denotes the consumption rate of O2, m3/(m3/s); V g is the air flow rate, m3/s. By substituting Eq. (1) into Eq. (2), the Eq. (3) can be obtained: (3) Sdx ⋅ AC O 2 exp − E / RT = − V g dC Eq. (4) can be acquired by integrating both ends of Eq. (3): (4) ∫ 0 L S V g A ⋅ exp − E / RT = ∫ C in C out 1 C O 2 dC where L represents the height of the coal sample tank, m; C in is the concentration of O2 at the inlet of coal sample tank; C out is the concentration of O2 at the outlet of coal sample tank. (5) ln C in C out = V V g ⋅ A ⋅ exp − E / RT (6) ln ln C in C out = − E R ⋅ 1 T + ln VA V g When the gas flow rate is constant, ln ln C in C out corresponds linearly with 1/T. The apparent activation energy in the reaction phase can be obtained by calculating the slope of the straight line. Fig. 8 shows that the activation energy in the whole temperature range, which is not a continuous straight line, can be divided into two temperature intervals (30–70 °C and 80–180 °C) for linear fitting. The activation energy data obtained from the fitting are given in Table 3 . Before 70 °C, the reduction in active functional groups decides that the energy required for coal oxidation is higher, so the apparent activation energy gets larger with the rise of temperature. After 70 °C, as the coal sample starts to release heat, the active functional groups increases and less energy is required for coal oxidation, so that activation energy is reduced. The apparent activation energy of coal samples before and after EDTA reduction can be visualized by the data in Table 3. The apparent activation energy in the temperature range 30–70 °C increases from 43.24 kJ/mol of raw coal sample to 51.18 kJ/mol of treated coal sample by 7.94 kJ/mol; accordingly, the apparent activation energy in the range of 80–180 °C increases from 35.14 kJ/mol to 43.42 kJ/mol by 8.28 kJ/mol. The change of apparent activation energy data shows that EDTA can promote the activation energy of coal oxidation, and thus it has a strong inhibitory effect on coal spontaneous combustion. The concentrations of CO and CO2 are considered to be most effective indexes reflecting the low-temperature oxidation of coal. In order to study the effects of EDTA on different coal samples, the concentration of CO and CO2 of samples before and after the inhibition in the heating process was compared using the method introduced in Section 2.3. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 9 . The amounts of gases produced by the three different samples before and after the inhibition demonstrate that the inhibited samples produced CO and CO2 of significantly lower concentrations, showing the good inhibitory effect of EDTA on different coal samples. To quantitatively evaluate the inhibitory ability of EDTA, the inhibiting efficiency, R (%), was calculated from the measurements: R = 1 − B / A × 100 % where A — CO concentration produced by raw coal sample at 100 °C (ppm), and B — CO concentration produced by inhibited coal samples at 100 °C, ppm. According to the CO production of raw coal and EDTA-inhibited coal samples, the corresponding inhibition efficiency of the four coal samples can be calculated, as shown in Table 4 . Table 4 exhibits the good inhibitory effects of EDTA on four kinds of coal samples, with the efficiency lying between 58.76% and 71.73%. The difference in inhibitory effect may result from the different amounts and catalytic abilities of transition metal elements in the samples. Among numerous metal ions in coal, transition metal ions contain empty d-orbits which enable them to accept pairs of electrons and conjugate bonds or revert to an empty orbit. This property of transition metal ions can reduce the activation energy for reaction, thereby endowing them with a strong catalytic effect on the combustion. Transition metal ions in coal can catalyze the activation of CH bonds, the oxidation of methyl/hydroxyl groups and the decomposition of carboxyl groups to form reactive radicals. All the catalytic effect facilitates coal oxidation. A large number of studies have shown that metal ions existing in coal catalyze the oxidation of organic compounds as follows: First, the activation of CH bonds by transition metal ions [38, 39]; Unlabelled Image where X/R represents radical groups such as H, Me, MeO, Cl and NO2. The reaction can oxidize methyl groups into carboxyl groups and oxidize hydroxyl groups into aldehyde groups under a certain conditions, which increases the activity of functional groups. Second, the oxidation of methyl/hydroxyl groups by transition metal ions [40]; Unlabelled Image Unlabelled Image where X/R represents H, Me, MeO, Cl, NO2 and other radical groups. Though the reaction, methyl groups can be oxidized into carboxyl groups, and hydroxyl groups into aldehyde groups respectively under a certain conditions, which increases the activity of functional groups. Third, the catalysis of carboxyl groups by transition metal ions [41]; Unlabelled Image where M stands for metal ions. Under the catalytic action of metal ions, carboxyl groups undergo Fenton-like reactions to generate active free radicals which do not require activation energy for reaction and can quickly attack other organic substances to release a lot of heat. It can be concluded on the basis of free radical reaction mechanism of coal spontaneous combustion [42] that the transition metal ions in coal can catalyze the functional groups of activated coal by reducing the activation energy for reaction. Under the catalytic effect of metal ions on alkyl side chain in coal, CH bond gets activated, which initiates the chain reaction of free radicals. Then, the active free radicals generated in the reaction combine with oxygen chemically to generate oxygen-containing free radicals such as aldehyde groups, carboxyl groups. Oxygen-containing free radicals can have a decarboxylation reaction to form CO2 and new free radicals at room temperature, remove aldehyde groups to form CO and new free radicals, or remove alkyl groups to form C2H4 and new free radicals under certain temperature conditions. During the reaction, the stronger the catalytic capacity of metal ions, the greater the amount of gas and the number of active functional groups generated in the reaction. Therefore, the inhibitory effect of EDTA on coal spontaneous combustion can be determined in accordance with the generated amount of gases such as CO, CO2 and C2H4 as well as the number of active functional groups on coal surface. As a common metal ion chelating agent, EDTA can combine with almost all divalent metal ions except alkali metal to form soluble metal chelates, so that transition metal ions lose their reactivity. The structural formula of EDTA is: Unlabelled Image An EDTA molecule contains four carboxylic acid moieties and two amine moieties, all of which boast a good coordination capacity and can be used as a ligand tooth for combining with metal ions. After EDTA is added into the coal sample, its six ligand teeth combine with metal ions in coal in the form of coordination bonds to form stable pentacyclic clathrates. The process in which EDTA reacts with metal ions (represented by M2+) to produce metal chelates is as follows: Unlabelled Image After the metal chelating agents combine with metal ions in coal, the strong coordination between ligands and central ions (transition metal ions) forces the d orbits in transition metal ions to lose their ability to transfer electrons. In this way, with the activation energy of coal oxidation increased, coal oxidation is more difficult to occur. In the TG curve, this point of view is reflected by the phenomena that the oxygen adsorption amount of the inhibited coal sample is obviously low, and that there is no mass gain caused by oxygen adsorption in the raw coal throughout the heating process. Macroscopically, it can be demonstrated by three phenomena: First, CO, CO2 and C2H4 generation and O2 consumption of the inhibited coal are both lower than those of the raw coal in the low-temperature oxidation process; second, the crossing point temperature of the inhibited coal sample is remarkably increased compared with that of the raw coal; third, oxygen-containing functional groups of the inhibited coal are greatly reduced and meanwhile its apparent activation energy also increased. Metal chelating agent and transition metal ions in coal form chelates together by means of coordinate bond, which lowers the catalytic effect of transition metal ions in coal and increases the activation energy for the chemical reaction between the active free radical groups on coal surface and oxygen. In this way, the reaction between the active free radical groups on coal surface and oxygen slows down or gets suppressed, thus greatly inhibiting the occurrence of coal spontaneous combustion. Further research can be conducted on choosing a metal chelator with a lower cost and a better effect on the basis of this study. In this paper, the effect of EDTA on coal spontaneous combustion was analyzed after performing a series of experiments. The specific conclusions were drawn as follows: 1) The mass and heat changes of raw, EDTA-inhibited and pyrolyzed samples were analyzed via the TG-DSC technique. Compared with the raw coal, the EDTA-inhibited sample adsorbs significantly reduced oxygen adsorption in the low-temperature oxidation stage. 2) The changes of functional groups on coal surface before and after the inhibition were analyzed by infrared spectroscopy at 40 °C, 80 °C, 120 °C and 160 °C. It is found that the numbers of active functional groups, especially oxygen-containing functional groups, are remarkably lowered after EDTA treatment. Therefore, EDTA can reduce the oxidation capacity of active functional groups in coal. 3) EDTA has an inhibitory good effect on coal spontaneous combustion, because the coordination and combination between EDTA and metal ions reduces the catalytic activity of transition metal ions and increases the activation energy of coal oxidation. The mass and heat changes of raw, EDTA-inhibited and pyrolyzed samples were analyzed via the TG-DSC technique. Compared with the raw coal, the EDTA-inhibited sample adsorbs significantly reduced oxygen adsorption in the low-temperature oxidation stage. The changes of functional groups on coal surface before and after the inhibition were analyzed by infrared spectroscopy at 40 °C, 80 °C, 120 °C and 160 °C. It is found that the numbers of active functional groups, especially oxygen-containing functional groups, are remarkably lowered after EDTA treatment. Therefore, EDTA can reduce the oxidation capacity of active functional groups in coal. EDTA has an inhibitory good effect on coal spontaneous combustion, because the coordination and combination between EDTA and metal ions reduces the catalytic activity of transition metal ions and increases the activation energy of coal oxidation. This research was supported by “the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2018BSCXA05)” and the “Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province”.
abstract: The inorganic salts in coal contain a certain number of metal ions, among which transition metal ions can exert a certain catalytic effect on coal spontaneous combustion by reducing the activation energy of coal oxygen reaction. Therefore, the addition of metal chelating agent in coal will definitely reduce the catalytic effect of metal ions, so as to inhibit coal spontaneous combustion. In this paper, the inhibitory effect of a common metal chelating agent ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) on coal spontaneous combustion was studied. Four coal samples were selected in this study. First, basic data such as the types and contents of transition metal elements in coal samples were measured via X-ray fluorescence (XRF). Then, thermal analyses, infrared spectrum experiments, low-temperature oxidation experiments and crossing point temperature experiments were performed on WL coal samples before and after EDTA treatment. The experimental results show that there is no mass gain caused by oxygen adsorption in the EDTA-treated coal sample throughout the heating process. In addition, the treated coal sample exhibit a great reduction in its content of oxygen functional groups, a decrease in the concentration of gas products and a rise of the crossing point temperature, indicating that EDTA has a strong inhibitory effect on coal. The calculation of the apparent activation energy reveals that the activation energies of the inhibited coal sample were 51.18 kJ/mol and 43.42 kJ/mol in the temperature ranges of 30–70 °C and 80–180 °C, respectively, which are 7.94 kJ/mol and 8.28 kJ/mol higher than those of the raw coal. This proves that the metal chelating agent EDTA is able to inhibit coal spontaneous combustion by chelating transition metal ions to increase the activation energy. Finally, experiments were carried out to study the inhibitory effects of EDTA on different coal samples, which suggests that EDTA can exert good inhibitory effects on different coal samples.
title: Experimental study on the inhibitory effect of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) on coal spontaneous combustion

full_text: Biomass is an attractive option for the production of renewable liquid fuels. Pyrolysis converts biomass into bio-oil. Because bio-oil is very unstable, acidic and has high oxygen content and high concentrations of heavy components, bio-oil needs to be upgraded in order to be used a fuel in vehicle engines [1–3]. The catalytic hydrotreatment of bio-oil under mild conditions (150–450°C and pressure>50bar) is one of the main routes to upgrade the bio-oil. Various types of reactions could take place during the hydrotreatment of bio-oil. The composition and properties of the hydrotreatment products would strongly depend on reaction conditions. The formation of coke, as an undesirable product, and the consequent blockage of the reactor are the main challenge in the hydrotreatment of bio-oil, which can also depend on reaction conditions [4,5]. Therefore, the knowledge about the effects of reaction conditions on coke formation and product distribution is essential for the development of a bio-oil hydrotreatment technology. It is believed that the formation of coke, including the formation of large aromatic ring systems that could be considered as the precursors of coke, during hydrotreatment could be at least partly due to the slow heating up of bio-oil where the bond breakage in bio-oil is not matched by the availability of active hydrogen whose supply from the relatively cold catalyst is rather limited [6]. In other words, the bonds in bio-oil can break at a very low temperature level at which the catalyst is not sufficiently active to produce the active hydrogen required to stabilise the broken bonds (free radicals) [6]. Different types of reactions including hydrogenation, hydrocracking, hydrodeoxygenation and recombination can take place between radicals. All these reactions could occur simultaneously. The provision of active hydrogen could reduce the formation of coke as well as large aromatic ring systems that can be the precursors of coke. In our recent work, a variety of reaction conditions including bio-oil feeding rate and temperature were studied [7,8]. The results demonstrated that the feeding rate of bio-oil and reaction temperature could drastically alter the formation of coke and its precursors (including large aromatic ring systems) in the product [7,8]. Further fundamental studies are required to understand the effects of process conditions on the coke formation and product composition. Various parameters including catalyst activity and availability of hot active hydrogen to stabilise radicals produced from the bond breakage of big molecules in bio-oil should be studied in details. Unfortunately, insufficient direct data have been reported in literature on this matter. Fixed-bed reactors are commonly used for the hydrotreatment of bio-oil [4,5]. In conventional fixed-bed reactors, temperature gradient cannot be avoided, especially at the feeding point where cold bio-oil and hydrogen are introduced into the reactor. Bio-oil would be gradually heated up as it flows along the reactor length. For catalysts such as NiMo, the bond breakage in the bio-oil would become significant before the bio-oil can come into contact with a hot catalyst at temperatures at which catalyst is sufficiently active to generate active hydrogen to stabilise the broken bonds (radicals). The recombination of radicals could thus lead to coke formation in the reactor or larger molecules in the products. It is hypothesised that one way to overcome this undesirable temperature profile is to heat up the hydrogen and injecting the hot hydrogen into the reactor so that the bio-oil would come into contact with the catalyst that is already at high temperature and thus active. It is believed that the enhanced supply of active hydrogen could decrease the formation of coke (and its precursors) inside the reactor and also stabilise the broken bonds (free radicals) [6]. Therefore, the purpose of this paper is to demonstrate the effects of the injection of hydrogen at different temperatures to the upper section of the reactor, where bio-oil is injected, on the stabilisation of the radicals and consequently the amount of coke formation inside the reactor. For a better understanding of coke formation, efforts have been made to monitor the deterioration of hydrotreatment products, especially the formation of large aromatic ring systems, during the hydrotreatment of bio-oil. The bio-oil used in this study was obtained from the pyrolysis of mallee wood in a grinding pyrolysis reactor at 450°C [9]. Commercial pre-sulphided NiMo/Al2O3 catalyst (hereafter referred as NiMo catalyst) supplied by Eurecat was used for the hydrotreatment. The catalyst was ground and sieved to obtain the particles in the size range of 600–800μm for the hydrotreatment process. Other chemicals were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. The hydrotreatment of the mallee wood bio-oil was carried out in a fixed-bed reactor. More details on the system have been reported elsewhere [10]. The reactor was made from 1-inch stainless steel 316 straight tubing (0.125″ wall thickness) with a length of 30cm (as shown in Fig. 1 ). During the experiment the reactor was immersed inside a hot fluidised sand bath. The bio-oil sample was fed into the reactor at a specified liquid hourly space velocity (LHSV) of 1h− 1 on the basis of organics in the inlet bio-oil. A simple way to heat up the bio-oil feed rapidly was to feed the bio-oil directly into the hot catalytic bed in the reactor. The bio-oil to be hydrotreated was mixed with 2L/min hydrogen gas before it was fed into the reactor. In other words, the liquid bio-oil was entrained with hydrogen before it was carried through a thin tube (1/8in. or 1/4in.) into the hot catalyst bed in the reactor (at the sand bath level, Fig. 1). The entrainment of liquid bio-oil by the hydrogen gas ensured that the bio-oil would spend a very short period of time (around 0.6ms) in the thin tube before it met the hot catalyst particles, during which the bio-oil would not have been heated up appreciably. The linear velocity of the bio-oil/hydrogen in the feeding line at room temperature was 8.1m/s. This can effectively reduce (not yet eliminate, see below) the coke formation inside the feeding tube. Based on our hypothesis (see Introduction), the upper section of the reactor was kept hot to make the catalyst active enough to stabilise the radicals from the bond breakage of the molecules present in bio-oil. One way to keep the upper section of the reactor hot is to bring the heat from an external source. In these experiments, to keep the upper section of the reactors hot, 4L/min hydrogen was pre-heated (to various temperatures – see below) and fed from the top of the hot zone (Fig. 1). The heater was made of 1in. tubing filled with 600–800μm NiMo catalyst particles and immersed in a separate heated fluidised sand bath. It should be noted that the reactor shown in Fig. 1 was used for this fundamental research. A reactor configuration for practical application can be found in Ref. [6]. The hydrotreated bio-oil product, together with hydrogen and other gases such as steam and hydrotreating gaseous products (CO, CO2, CH4 etc), would exit the reactor via a central thin tube (1/4in.) positioned in the centre of the reactor with an inlet at the bottom of the reactor. This minimised the time the hydrotreated bio-oil would spend at high temperature without being in contact with the catalyst. Moreover, it transferred some heat back to the upper section of the reactor from the bottom. All experiments were done by setting the temperature of the fluidised-bed bath (Tsand) at 390°C and the outlet pressure (Poutlet) at around 70bar. In each experiment the temperature at 3cm into the hot zone (i.e. 3cm below the sand bath level, Fig. 1) was kept at different temperatures and recorded continuously. The temperature at this location can be used to distinguish the direct effect of hot hydrogen on the temperature of the initial (top) section of the reactor. As isothermal operation in the top of the reactor was impossible practically, the equivalent isothermal temperature (from the beginning of the feeding to the time when 820mL of bio-oil was fed in, see below for further explanation) was calculated and used to name each experiment [11]. The products were collected into time-resolved product fractions (samples), normally at an interval of 1h, for product yield quantification and analysis. The experiments were stopped when the reactor blockage happened, which is evidenced by the rapid increases in the pressure at the reactor inlet. The UV-fluorescence spectra of bio-oil and its hydrotreated products were acquired with a Perkin-Elmer LS50B spectrometer. The samples were diluted with methanol [Uvasol for spectroscopy; purity (GC): ≥99.9%] to 4 ppm by weight (on the basis of sample to be analysed). The synchronous fluorescence spectra were recorded with a constant energy difference of −2800cm−1. The slit widths were 2.5nm and the scan speed was 200nm/min [12]. The light components of the products and bio-oil were quantified by using an Agilent GC–MS (a 6890 series GC with a 5973 MS detector) with a capillary column (HP-INNOWax) (length, 30m; internal diameter, 0.25mm; film thickness, 0.25μm of crosslinked polyethylene glycol) [12,13]. The yields of formic acid, acetic acid, propionic acid, butanoic acid, pentanoic acid, hexanoic acid and heptanoic acid were calculated and added together to be considered as “acid yield”. The compounds including phenol, 2,6-dimethyl-phenol, 3,4-dimethyl-phenol, 4-(1-methylpropyl)-phenol, 2,4,6-trimethyl-phenol, 2,4-dimethyl-phenol and 3-methyl-phenol were summed and reported as “phenolics”. In the category of “benzene” compounds, the following components were considered and summed: ethylbenzene, 1,2-dimethyl-benzene, 1,4-dimethyl-benzene, propyl-benzene, 1-ethyl-2-methyl-benzene, 1,2,3-trimethyl-benzene and 1-methylpropyl-benzene. A sample was heated up in a thermogravimetric analyser (TGA, TA Instruments Q600) from 25 to 500°C at a heating rate of 10°Cmin−1 in nitrogen (at a flow rate of 50mL/min) to determine the coke formation tendency of the bio-oil and the hydrotreated bio-oils [14–17]. Fig. 2a show the temperature at 3cm inside the hot zone of the reactor (see Fig. 1) as a function of time on stream that is expressed as the accumulative amount of bio-oil fed into the reactor. An exothermic peak was observed at the early stage of each of these experiments, which is in agreement with previous findings [7,8]. The temperature then stabilised at different levels depending on the extent of preheating of hydrogen. As is shown in Fig. 1, two streams of hydrogen were fed into the reactor. The first stream at room temperature was mixed with bio-oil to entrain the bio-oil into the reactor. The second stream of hydrogen was pre-heated in a separate fluidised sand bath and fed into the upper section of the reactor. This second stream of hydrogen brought heat into the upper section of the reactor to heat up the catalyst and bio-oil in the section. The temperature in Fig. 2a represents that at a particular point in the upper section of the reactor. With the reactor set up shown in Fig. 1, once the reacting bio-oil mixture including hydrogen reached the bottom of the reactor, the mixture would exit the reactor via a central tube. The hot products including hydrogen would thus heat up the upper section of the reactor. Therefore, some extents of heating of the upper section of the reactor by hot hydrogen in the exit stream was always achieved. However, the heat transfer between the hydrotreatment products and the reactants in the upper section would depend on the status of the products. As was shown in our previous work [10], the heavy bio-oil species (tending to be liquid) would tend to have much longer residence time in the reactor than the corresponding light species (tending to be vapour). If the hot products are gases (similar to hydrogen), their residence time in the exit tube with limited heat transfer area (i.e. the time for heat transfer from the products to the inlet reactants) would be short. This exit tube could be in a form with large heat transfer area in a practical application [6]. On the other hand, if the products are liquids under high pressure, their velocity in the exit tube would be slow, giving sufficient time for the heat transfer from the products to the reactants. As will be seen below, less pre-heating of the second hydrogen stream would tend to produce a heavier product. This explains why the temperature at 3cm into the reactor increased after about 820mL of bio-oil had been fed into the reactor and when the heavy species started to come out of the reactor. In order to evaluate the effects of the pre-heated hydrogen stream on the coke formation and product distribution, in isolation from the enhanced heating of the upper section of the reactor by the liquid products, the equivalent isothermal temperature was calculated [11] by considering the temperature from the beginning of the bio-oil feeding to the time when 820mL of bio-oil had been fed into the reactor. These calculated equivalent isothermal temperatures (182, 255, 263 and 369°C) are used to label the temperature profiles in Fig. 2a. Fig. 2b shows the pressure measured at the feeding pump, which is close to the pressure at the reactor inlet, as a function of the time on stream (i.e. the accumulative amount of bio-oil fed into the reactor). When the pressure started to increase rapidly due to coke formation, the experiment had to be terminated. This rapid pressure increase did not take place at the same time for different temperatures at 3cm into the hot reactor zone. In order to have a better understanding of where the coke/blockage formed, the reactors were opened and inspected carefully. The observations can be summarised as follows: • For the experiment with T
abstract: This paper reports the effects of hydrogen and bio-oil inlet temperature on the coke formation and product distribution during the hydrotreatment of bio-oil. A bench scale continuous hydrotreatment fixed-bed reactor set-up was used with pre-sulphided NiMo/γ-Al2O3 as the catalyst. The temperature of hot fluidised sand bath in which the hydrotreatment reactor was immersed was set at 390°C while the pressure at the reactor exit was kept at around 70bar. An LHSV of 1h− 1 (on the basis of organics in the bio-oil feed) was used. Our results show that the presence of hot hydrogen in the injection point of bio-oil to the reactor reduced the coke formation and reactor blockage for prolonged catalyst activity. This was due to the enhanced cracking and minimised polymerisation of bio-oil fragments when hot hydrogen was used to heat and activate the catalyst at the injection point. Moreover, lighter products with less coking propensity and smaller aromatic ring systems were formed when the injection point was maintained at a higher temperature with the use of hot hydrogen. These results indicate that the coke formation during hydrotreatment is at least partly because of the slow heating up of the bio-oil and the resulting bond breakage not being matched by the supply of active hydrogen from the catalyst.
title: Importance of hydrogen and bio-oil inlet temperature during the hydrotreatment of bio-oil

full_text: Waste plastic, a common petroleum-based waste material, is a source of hydrocarbons that is not being used effectively. Only ∼2% of the waste plastics are currently being recycled [1], with the remainder being disposed of in landfills. An increase in the usage and consumption of plastics is considered to be inevitable because of their versatility, their myriad uses, and the relatively small amount of energy required for their production compared to that of other materials [2]. A number of problems are associated with the recycling effort, including the presence of contaminants such as antioxidants and colorants [3], the difficulty of convincing consumer households of the necessity to recycle, separating the waste plastics effectively for primary recycling of the plastic back to the monomer, and having sufficient waste plastic for processing, particularly in areas distant from centers of population [4]. Layman [5]suggests that tertiary recycling of plastics to produce fuels will become profitable if a fee of US$150–190 per ton can be charged for processing. Tertiary recycling will provide an additional source of hydrocarbon fuels and chemical feedstocks. The addition of other hydrocarbon sources such as our most abundant US hydrocarbon resource, coal, will provide a stable supply as well as an additional source of hydrocarbons. The direct coprocessing of coal and waste plastics is difficult because of the chemical and processing incompatibility of the materials [6–9]. Typical household plastic waste consists of roughly 63% polyethylenes (high and low density polyethylene, i.e., HDPE and LDPE), 11% polypropylene, 11% polystyrene, 7% PET and 7% PVC [10], causing the wastes to be highly aliphatic. By contrast, coal is ∼70% aromatic. These differences in chemistry result in the two materials being incompatible during liquefaction. The liquefaction decomposition products from polyolefinic waste plastics are typically saturated and are, thus, a poor solvating medium for coal. In addition, researchers have shown that aliphatic and alicyclic compounds do not provide the hydrogen needed for hydrogen transfer reactions to coal [11–13]. Since coprocessing of waste plastics with coal suffers from both the incompatibility of the reactants and the lack of hydrogen donation from the saturated species to the coal, effective coprocessing requires the development of alternative processing methods to promote their mutual dissolution. Multiple catalysts may be introduced to promote desirable reactions of both species, or a solvent such as resid may be added to provide a dissolution medium for both components. In this research, these two methods were combined. Different catalysts were used to promote the reactions of both coal and waste plastics, that would lead to increased conversion in the presence of a resid, and were selected based on previous research which examined the coprocessing of coal and waste plastics [6–9, 14, 15]. Coal requires hydrotreating catalysts, such as alumina supported NiMo or CoMo, and slurry phase hydrotreating catalysts, that promote coal conversion and hydrogenation of the coal-derived liquids [16, 17]. Waste plastics require hydrocracking catalysts such as super acid catalysts [18, 19], zeolites [8, 9, 14, 15, 20], and fluid catalytic cracking catalysts [8, 9]that break the long polymeric chains and result in liquefied products. In recent research, coprocessing reactions of coal with waste plastics showed that the introduction of hydrocracking catalysts promoted the overall conversion and production of a lighter product slate than that obtained with a hydrotreating catalyst alone [8, 9]. Since the desirable end products are liquids and not gases, the conditions under which these hydrocracking catalysts are used must be carefully controlled to limit the amount of cracking that occurs. Two-stage liquefaction of waste plastics with coal has been shown to be beneficial in terms of tailoring the catalysts and the reaction conditions specifically to the reactant, thereby minimizing gas production and maximizing the liquid product [21]. Hydrocracking and fluid cracking catalysts were used in the first stage to promote the cracking of the polymeric chains in the waste plastics, and hydrotreating catalysts were used in the second stage to promote coal conversion. Heavy petroleum resid provides a solvent and a dissolution medium that is effective for waste plastics. The thermal cracking of waste plastics with heavy resids or heavy oils was evaluated by Canadian researchers [22]who utilized post-consumer plastics containing polyethylene, polypropylene, and polystyrene to determine if dual processing could be used to produce transportation fuels. Mutual thermal cracking of the polyolefins and the resid increased the H/C ratio of the product, reduced retrogressive reactions, and helped to suppress coke formation. In another study, Marsh et al. [23]evaluated the pyrolysis of polystyrene in the presence of a petroleum heavy oil. Pyrolysis conditions of 400°C decomposed waste polystyrene completely and was an acceptable pyrolysis temperature for heavy oil and heavy bottoms, although not for decant oil or FCC slurry oil. The distillate contained products that were obtained from the polystyrene as well as from the heavy oil. Joo and Curtis [6]have shown that heavy petroleum resid acts as an effective bridging solvent when added to coprocessing reactions of coal and waste plastics, and provides a medium for their mutual dissolution [6, 7]. A commercial hydrotreating catalyst, NiMo/Al2O3, was used to promote the conversion of the solid reactants and the subsequent hydrogenation of the coprocessing products. Joo and Curtis [24]also examined the effect of reaction time on the coprocessing of LDPE, coal, and resid using NiMo/Al2O3 as the catalyst and found that longer reaction times of 5 and 6 h produced high conversions and increased the production of lighter boiling products. The solid reaction products from the ternary coprocessing system consisted primarily of LDPE. The research reported here evaluated the effect of four different catalysts on the conversion and product distributions obtained from the coprocessing of coal, heavy petroleum resid, and waste plastics. The plastic used in this study was LDPE, since it and its higher density form have been shown to be especially difficult to coprocess with coal [6–9]. Since both hydrogenation and hydrocracking selectivities are needed to produce desirable reaction products from coprocessing reactions containing LDPE, coal, and resid, four different catalysts were employed: presulfided NiMo/Al2O3, presulfided NiMo/zeolite, HZSM-5, and Zeolyst Z-753, a hydrocracking catalyst. The addition of a 10 wt.% hydrocracking catalyst, either Z-753, HZSM-5, or NiMo/zeolite, to presulfided NiMo/Al2O3 was also examined to determine if adding some additional hydrocracking capacity to the hydrotreating catalyst NiMo/Al2O3 would increase the conversion and production of lighter products of the individual components of the coprocessing systems. The effect of different reaction parameters on catalytic coprocessing was evaluated by using an analysis of variance (ANOVA) [25, 26]. A 2 k factorial design analysis, which involved two levels of each of the three parametric factors of temperature (400 and 430°C), time (30 and 60 min), and catalyst (Z-753 and presulfided NiMo/zeolite), was performed to determine the factor or combination of factors that most affected the conversion of the reactants to desirable products. The parameters found to be most effective were used in the individual component and coprocessing reaction systems. The model plastic, LDPE, was obtained from Aldrich Chemical and used as received. Blind Canyon DECS-17 bituminous coal was obtained from the Penn State Coal Sample Bank. The proximate analysis of the coal was 45% fixed carbon, 45% volatile matter, 6.3% ash, and 3.7% moisture, and the ultimate analysis was 82.1% C, 6.2% H, 0.4% S, 1.4% N, and 0.12% Cl. The resid used in the coprocessing reactions was Manji, and was obtained from Amoco. The analysis of the Manji resid was 85.1% C, 10.8% H, 0.7% N, 2.6% S, 231 ppm V, 220 ppm Ni, and 23 ppm Fe. The solvents used for fractionation of the reaction products were HPLC grade hexane, toluene, and tetrahydrofuran (THF) from Fisher Scientific. Carbon disulfide (CS2) was used as the solvent for simulated distillation and was obtained from Aldrich, who also supplied the biphenyl for use as a standard for determining the amount of material recovered from the simulated distillation column. The catalysts tested in this study were NiMo/Al2O3 (supplied by Shell, with 2.72 wt.% Ni and 13.16 wt.% Mo), NiMo/zeolite (from Akzo, with 500°C in the HXs fraction and in the total product were calculated. Standard deviations for these weight percentages were calculated using standard deviations obtained in the product distributions and from simulated distillation recovery. All analyses were duplicated. A combined boiling point analysis involving the results from solvent fractionation and the simulated distillation of the HXs fraction was calculated. The solids produced during the catalytic reactions were analyzed to determine the LDPE content. The method involved washing the reaction solids with THF, washing the solids with hot decalin, dissolving the LDPE into hot decalin, and then recovering the LDPE as described previously [24]. Baseline experiments were performed with known amounts of LDPE in systems of LDPE alone, LDPE and coal, and LDPE, coal, and resid. In each case, LDPE recoveries of more than 95% were obtained. The effect of different catalysts on the reactivity of LDPE at coprocessing conditions and on the conversion and product distributions obtained from the ternary system composed of LDPE, coal, and resid was investigated. The catalysts used were a hydrotreating catalyst, designed to promote reactions in hydrocarbonaceous mixtures such as coal and heavy resid, and hydrocracking catalysts, developed to promote cracking of long chain hydrocarbons. The catalysts used were NiMo/Al2O3, NiMo/zeolite, Zeolyst Z-753, and HZSM-5. All of these catalysts have some propensity for promoting hydrocracking although NiMo/Al2O3 shows much less hydrocracking activity than the other three. The measures used to evaluate the effectiveness of the catalysts for both conversion and reactivity in the reaction systems studied were conversion of the solids to THF solubles and product distribution in terms of HXs, TOLs and THFs. The boiling point distribution of the HXs was obtained by simulated distillation and was combined with the product distribution from the solvent fractionation to obtain a boiling point distribution of the total product slate. These combined distributions were calculated for the different catalytic species and compared. Reactions of LDPE with presulfided NiMo/zeolite and pretreated Z-753 were performed at four different combinations of reaction time and temperature: 400°C, 30 min; 400°C, 60 min; 430°C, 30 min; and 430°C, 60 min. The conversions and product distributions presented in terms of gas, HXs, THFs, and IOM obtained from these reactions are given in Table 1 . Both conversion and production of HXs were affected by the severity of the reaction conditions. The higher reaction temperature and longer reaction time yielded the highest reactivity for both catalysts. The conversions in reactions with Z-753 increased from 35.7% at 400°C and 30 min to 94.2% at 430°C and 60 min. The HXs yields increased in a similar manner from 16.5% to 69.7%, respectively. Similarly, the conversions in the LDPE reactions with NiMo/zeolite increased from 32.1% at the lowest severity condition to 93.1% at the highest. The increase in HXs yields was highest with Z-753, going from 5.1 to 69.7%, with NiMo/zeolite showing a slightly smaller increase from 6.5 to 56.0%. However, the amount of gas produced was greater with NiMo/zeolite than with Z-753. The recoveries were low at the higher severity conditions, due to the loss of volatiles during product workup. The results of the 2 k factorial design analysis were evaluated for the two selected catalysts and are given in Table 2 Table 3 . The analysis was performed by assigning each factor or source of variation (SV) a letter and subscript. For example, the reaction temperature was letter A and subscript i, reaction time was letter B and subscript j, and catalyst type was letter C and subscript k. Each fraction (gas, HXs, and conversion) from the product distribution was assigned a designation of y ijk . The product distribution results were split into factor level combinations (FLCs) which were the sum of the values (SVs) of the same source being investigated as shown in Table 2. Degrees of freedom, sum of the squares, and the mean square were then calculated for each SV and sum of SVs. The df for the individual factor are equal to one less than the total number of variations (n) for each SV: (2) df SV =n SV −1 SS were calculated according to the following equation: (3) SS= abs(level 0−level 1) number of variations where abs is the absolute value and the number of variations is 8 for this study. The mean square was calculated by dividing the SSSV by the df SV for each SV, as shown in the following equation: (4) MS SV = SS SV df SV F ∞, the F distribution value, was determined by dividing the MS for each SV by the MSerror, which is the mean square of the error for each source value. The values calculated for F 0 were then compared with tabular F x values [25, 26]to identify the values that passed the F test for a given level of certainty. For example, if F 0 was greater than tabular F 0.10, the SV would show an effect, but there would be a 10% probability that no effect existed. Similarly, if the F 0 value was greater than the tabular F 0.01 value, then the SV would have an effect at the 99% confidence level and there would be only a 1% probability that there was no effect. The ANOVA presented in Table 3 showed that reaction temperature was significant at the 1% level for converting LDPE and producing HXs materials, as was the interaction between reaction temperature and reaction time. Reaction time alone affected conversion and HXs at the 2.5% significance level. Reaction temperature and catalyst type were significant at the 5% level for producing gas during the LDPE reaction. The effect of four different catalysts and combinations of those catalysts on coprocessing reactions of LDPE/coal/Manji resid for 60 min at both 400 and 430°C was evaluated. The catalyst combinations consisted of 10% of a hydrocracking catalyst, either Z-753, HZSM-5 or NiMo/zeolite, in NiMo/Al2O3. In addition, reactions were performed with LDPE and LDPE/coal using NiMo/Al2O3, Z-753, NiMo/zeolite, and 10% Z-753 in NiMo/Al2O3. In all of these reactions, the effect of the catalyst on the conversion obtained, the product distribution from solvent fractionation, and the total boiling point distribution was determined. Evaluation of the product distributions given in Table 4 showed that the reaction temperature strongly influenced the conversion obtained from the LDPE and LDPE/coal reactions, while the conversions from coprocessing reactions with resid were much less affected. The conversions from LDPE ranged from 69.6 to 94.2% at 430°C, but only from 16.1 to 28.5% at 400°C. A similar but smaller difference was observed for the LDPE/coal reactions, where the conversion ranged from 41.3 to 43.5% for the 430°C reactions but were lower, ranging from 24.4 to 33.1%, for the 400°C reactions. The overall conversion in the ternary coprocessing reactions increased compared to LDPE/coal at 430°C and also compared to LDPE and LDPE/coal reactions at 400°C. This increase can be related directly to the THF solubility of the resid. The range of conversions for the ternary LDPE/coal/Manji system overlapped at the two reaction temperatures, ranging from 61.7 to 74.9% at 430°C, and from 57.3 to 62.0% at 400°C. The effect of reaction temperature on the product distributions obtained from the catalytic reactions was similar to that observed with conversion, as shown in Table 4. The higher reaction temperature affected the production of gases and HXs materials in both the LDPE and LDPE/coal reactions, with the LDPE reaction system being the most sensitive. At 430°C the production of gas and HXs from LDPE was high and the recoveries were low, indicating that many volatile compounds were produced during the reaction that escaped during product workup. At 400°C the amount of HXs produced from the LDPE reaction was very low and the recovery was high, indicating that low boiling compounds were neither produced nor lost. The LDPE/coal reaction system also produced less HXs at 400°C than at 430°C, although the differences were small compared to the LDPE system. Differences in the product distributions at the two reaction temperatures were also observed in the LDPE/coal/resid systems, where the HXs were higher and the THFs were lower at the higher reaction temperature. The effect of different catalysts on the conversions and product distributions was also apparent for each reaction system at both reaction temperatures; however, since the reactions at 430°C showed much greater reactivity, the catalyst's effect was much more obvious and will be discussed at that temperature. The LDPE reaction system was the most sensitive to catalyst type. The hydrocracking catalysts, Z-753 and NiMo/zeolite, were most effective for increasing LDPE conversion and HXs yields at 430°C. The addition of 10% Z-753 to NiMo/Al2O3 promoted conversion and HXs yields compared to NiMo/Al2O3 alone, but was still much less effective than the use of hydrocracking catalysts alone. For the LDPE system, Z-753 produced the highest conversions and yields of light materials and was the most effective of the catalysts studied. The effect of the catalyst on the LDPE/coal systems was minimal at 430°C but not at 400°C (Table 4), where the conversion and HXs of the LDPE/coal system were highest with Z-753. The coprocessing reactions of LDPE/coal/Manji responded favorably to NiMo/zeolite and Z-753 at 430°C, while at 400°C, NiMo/zeolite was most effective for producing HXs and conversion. The products from the catalytic reactions of all three reaction systems were analyzed in terms of their total boiling point distribution, as given in Table 5 . The total boiling point distribution was developed by performing a simulated distillation of the HXs material and then combining the results with the data from the solvent fractionation. The source of the weight percent of the fractions given in Table 5 are represented in Fig. 1 , where the ibp to 500°C portion was made up of the material eluting at temperatures above 500°C, the material remaining on the GC column, and the TOLs and THFs from the solvent fractionation. The IOM was the solid material obtained during product distribution and was presumed to boil above 500°C. The most apparent result from this total boiling point analysis was that the material produced was either very light, in the ibp to 100°C range, or very heavy, in the >500°C range or the IOM. The production of light materials occurred primarily in the LDPE system at reaction temperatures of 430°C, while the production of heavy materials was most evident in the LDPE/coal system. The amount of material that boiled between 100 and 500°C was quite small in all three reaction systems, but was highest when resid was present in the coprocessing system. The products from the 400°C reactions of all three reaction systems were considerably heavier, as shown by their predominance in the >500°C and IOM fractions, than the products from the 430°C reactions. The catalyst that produced the most material boiling in the range of 100 to 500°C in the ternary system was 10% HZSM-5 in NiMO/Al2O3 at 400°C, and HZSM-5 alone at 430°C (Table 5). The effect of the catalysts on the compositions of the solids produced in the coprocessing reactions of LDPE/coal and LDPE/coal/resid was analyzed by determining the amount of LDPE and coal and/or resid in the solid reaction products obtained from reactions performed at 430°C and 60 min. These reactions were performed several months after those presented in Table 4, and the amount of IOM produced agreed quite well. The data presented in Table 6 are reported in terms of decalin solubles, which represents the amount of LDPE present in the reaction products, while the amount of decalin insoluble material represents the amount of coal and/or resid in the reaction products. The amount of LDPE that remained unconverted in both reaction systems was quite high and was dependent on the presence or absence of resid and on the type of catalyst used (Table 6). A higher percentage of the reaction solid was composed of LDPE in the LDPE/coal/resid reactions than in the LDPE/coal reactions. However, a higher percentage of the reaction products were solids in the reactions without resid present than with resid. The catalysts that promoted LDPE conversion in both systems were Z-753 and HZSM-5, with HZSM-5 being the more effective. The effect of catalyst loading and reaction time on the conversion and product distribution of the ternary coprocessing system of LDPE, coal, and Manji resid was investigated and the results are shown in Table 7 . The product distributions and conversions were obtained for catalyst loadings of 1, 3, and 10 wt.% NiMo/zeolite in reactions at 400 and 430°C, but the catalyst loading was found to have little effect at each reaction temperature. The conversion for the three loadings at 430°C ranged from 74.8 to 76.3%, although the HXs increased somewhat from 45.2 to 55.3% with increased loading. At 400°C, increased catalyst loading did not result in either increased conversion or HXs yields. By contrast, increasing the reaction time from 60 to 300 min in reactions containing 1 wt.% NiMo/zeolite reacted at 430°C resulted in substantial increases in both conversion and HXs yields. The conversion increased from 74.9 to 92.8% when the reaction time was increased from 60 to 300 min, with the conversion of 92.8% indicating that almost all of the LDPE was converted, and the HXs yields increased from 45.2 to 72.6%. The total boiling point distributions in Table 8 clearly show that reactions at longer times resulted in the production of lighter materials in the ibp to 100°C range, as well as in the 100 to 500°C range. The material boiling at >500°C was also reduced in the 3 and 5 h reactions compared to the 1 h reactions with higher catalyst loading levels. The results from this investigation showed that catalytic coprocessing of LDPE, coal, and resid was affected by the type of catalyst used. NiMo/Al2O3, the catalyst with least hydrocracking propensity, also produced the lowest amount of conversion and light products. The introduction of 10 wt.% of a hydrocracking catalyst to the NiMo/Al2O3 increased conversion and improved the overall product slate. The hydrocracking catalysts themselves were most effective for converting the solid reactants to THF soluble material and producing the lighter boiling products. This effect was particularly evident in reactions with LDPE alone. LDPE was substantially hydrocracked with Zeolyst 753 and NiMo/zeolite. By contrast, the different types of catalysts gave very similar results in LDPE/coal reactions and did not produce substantial conversion. Reaction temperature was very important in converting LDPE. When a reaction temperature of 430°C was used, the hydrocracking catalysts promoted reactions that converted LDPE to lighter products. At the lower reaction temperature, the catalysts affected the reactivity of LDPE but did not yield more than 30% conversion. The higher reaction temperature, in conjunction with the hydrotreating and hydrocracking catalysts, resulted in higher conversion and lighter products in both the LDPE/coal and LDPE/coal/resid reactions. The reaction time strongly affected the overall conversion and product slate in the coprocessing of LDPE/coal/resid at 430°C using a NiMo/zeolite catalyst. At the 1 wt.% catalyst loading level, increasing the reaction time from 1 to 5 h increased the conversion from ∼74.5 to 93% and resulted in a substantial increase in the production of lighter products. By comparison, increasing the catalyst loading from 1 to 10 wt.% had only a minimal effect on the conversion of the reactants and production of lighter materials. When LDPE was in a reaction environment with heavy hydrocarbonaceous material such as coal and resid, the hydrocracking catalysts were not as active for converting LDPE and producing lighter products. This reduced activity was most probably due to competition and poisoning by the heavy, heteroatom-laden coal and resid molecules. Hence, longer reaction times and longer contact time with the catalyst were required for the LDPE to convert and to produce lighter products in catalytic reactions. The supply of DECS-17 Blind Canyon coal from the Penn State Coal Sample Bank, the NiMo/Al2O3 catalyst from Shell, NiMo/zeolite from Akzo, Zeolyst Z-753 from Zeolyst, and HZSM-5 from United Catalysts is gratefully acknowledged. We also appreciate the help of Gary Robbins from CONSOL in the development of the method for reclaiming LDPE. We also thank our staff, in particular, Becca McVey, Joanne Haaland, Michael Hornsby, Henry Cobb, and Joseph Aderholdt, for their assistance and support. The discussions and assistance provided by Ying Tang, Felicia Belyeu, and Mingsheng Luo are sincerely appreciated.
abstract: Catalytic coprocessing of low density polyethylene (LDPE) with coal and heavy petroleum resid was investigated using four different catalysts that included both hydrotreating and hydrocracking catalysts. Reaction systems that were evaluated included LDPE alone; LDPE with coal; and LDPE, coal, and resid. The catalysts used were NiMo/Al2O3, a hydrotreating catalyst with some hydrocracking activity, and the hydrocracking catalysts Zeolyst 753, NiMo/zeolite, and HZSM-5. These catalysts were reacted individually or in combinations of 10 wt.% of each hydrocracking catalyst in NiMo/Al2O3. The catalytic reactions were performed at two temperatures, 400 and 430°C, using 1 wt.% of each catalyst or a combination of catalysts on a total feed basis. The effects of the different catalysts on the reaction products were measured in terms of solvent fractionation and total boiling point distribution. Reactions at the higher reaction temperature of 430°C resulted in substantially higher conversion and production of lighter products than the reactions at 400°C. The LDPE reaction system was sensitive to the catalyst type, and yielded increased conversion and lighter products when Zeolyst 753 and NiMo/zeolite were used. By contrast, the conversion and product slate obtained from the LDPE and coal systems were low and showed no effect due to the different types of catalyst. Introduction of resid to the LDPE/coal system increased the reactivity of the system and allowed the catalysts to have a larger effect. The hydrocracking catalysts were the most active in producing more conversion and hexane soluble material. Comparison of the effect of increasing the reaction time up to 5 h with 1 wt.% catalyst loading to the effect of increasing the catalyst loading from 1 wt.% to 10 wt.% for a reaction time of 1 h showed that increased reaction time was much more effective than catalyst loading in converting the solid LDPE to liquid reaction products.
title: Catalytic coprocessing of LDPE with coal and petroleum resid using different catalysts
