full_text: Increasing concerns about climate change and increasing demand for energy as a result of wide economic development, including that in rural and remote regions, have stimulated the development of various renewable energy technologies. Biomass holds a special position because biomass is the only carbon-containing renewable resource that can be used to produce liquid fuels to replace the petroleum-derived conventional ones. Pyrolysis of biomass would produce gases, biochar and bio-oil with their yields strongly depending on the feedstock and pyrolysis conditions [1–3]. Compared with the bulky biomass, bio-oil is a liquid that can be transported relatively easily and economically. This allows for the pyrolysis to be carried out in a modular and “distributed” mode, saving the costs to transport the wet bulky biomass over a long distance and greatly improving the economic competitiveness of biofuel production. However, bio-oil is acidic and contains water and high molecular mass components [3–5]. Therefore, bio-oil cannot be used directly as a replacement of petrol and diesel. Bio-oil must be upgraded, e.g. via hydrotreatment [6–15]. During the hydrotreatment of bio-oil, a significant fraction of its oxygen will be removed in the forms of H2O, CO and CO2. The hydrotreatment could also result in decreases in molecular mass [13–14]. In order to improve the commercial feasibility of the hydrotreatment of bio-oil, the liquid hourly space velocity (LHSV) must be high enough so that the hydrotreatment reactor size can be reduced. The pressure of hydrogen should be as low as possible. LHSV, i.e. the rate at which bio-oil is fed into the hydrotreatment reactor, can significantly affect the formation of coke on the hydrotreatment catalyst, which would ultimately result in the deactivation of the catalyst. Unfortunately, little information is available in the literature about the effects of LHSV on the product quality and coke formation, lagging behind the requirement of technology development. As a product from the random thermal breakdown of macromolecular networks and other species in biomass, bio-oil has an inherently complicated composition with abundant reactive functional groups. More importantly, the bio-oil components would have a very wide molecular mass distribution with light species such as formic acid and heavy species that are the products from the partial thermal breakdown of the polymeric structures in biomass. During hydrotreatment, the residence time for bio-oil species could vary over an extremely wide range [15]. While some heavy bio-oil species would exist in the liquid phase in the hydrotreatment reactor, some would become vapour on entering the reactor. The overall LHSV value does not describe in any way the true residence time of various species in the reactor. This situation is worsened when operation is carried out at low pressures that is preferred to reduce the costs of biofuel production. This study aims to investigate the behaviour of bio-oil during the hydrotreatment in a continuous reactor using a commercial pre-sulphided NiMo/Al2O3 catalyst at a moderate temperature (375°C) and a relatively low hydrogen pressure (70bar). The study is focused on the effects of the overall LHSV on the hydrotreatment behaviour of lighter and heavier species in bio-oil. The hydrotreated products (termed as biofuel) were characterised with a wide range of analytical techniques in order to gain insights into the important processes taking place during hydrotreatment. Bio-oil was produced in a grinding pyrolysis pilot plant [16,17] from the pyrolysis of mallee wood (Eucalyptus loxophleba, ssplissophloia) grown in the wheat belt of Western Australia [18,19]. Briefly, a mixture of wood chips having a wide range of particle sizes from microns to centimetres was continuously fed into a rotating reactor at 450°C in which the pyrolysis and particle size reduction took place simultaneously. After the separation of biochar particles in two cyclones, bio-oil vapour was condensed to give the liquid bio-oil sample used in this study. The bio-oil sample was stored in a freezer (−18°C) until use. The bio-oil was filtered (20–25μm) before the hydrotreatment experiments. The hydrotreatment of bio-oil was carried out in a U-shape continuous pack-bed reactor, as is shown in Fig. 1 . The reactor was made of stainless steel 316 and had a diameter of 3/4in. with a total reactor length of 40cm. The reactor was partly (about half, see “the sand bath level” shown in Fig. 1) immersed in a hot fluidised sand bath that was heated to 375°C. The U-shaped reactor design made it easier to heat up the reactor in a sand bath. The packed-bed reactor contains two zones of catalysts. In the first zone (10cm), 5% palladium supported on activated carbon (Pd/C, Bioscientific) catalyst was used. It was outside the sand bath. This section would have undergone a temperature transition ranging from room temperature to 110 bars), the experiments were terminated. Contrary to the reports in the literature [20,25] that the Pd/C catalyst could stabilise the bio-oil to reduce coke formation, these experiments demonstrated that the stabilisation of bio-oil using the Pd/C catalyst was rather limited, certainly not to the extent to ensure long-term continuous operation using the NiMo catalyst, at least under the current experimental conditions. In fact, separate experiments [15,17] also showed that the use of Pd/C alone (i.e. without the NiMo catalyst) would also result in the blockage of reactor and the deactivation of catalyst. As is shown in Fig. 1, two thermocouples were placed in the NiMo catalyst bed: one at 5cm into the NiMo catalyst bed in the fluid flow direction and another one at 15cm into the bed. The bed temperatures measured at 15cm into the NiMo catalyst bed for the three different LHSV values are shown in Fig. 2a. The x-axis refers to the total amount of bio-oil that had been fed into the reactor, which facilitates a better comparison of the experiments with different bio-oil feeding rates. It is an indirect indication of the time that has passed since the start of the feeding of bio-oil. Fig. 2b shows the temperature profiles measured at 5 and 15cm into the NiMo catalyst bed at the same LHSV value of 3h−1. The most striking feature of Fig. 2 is the presence of huge exothermic peaks. Temperature increases as high as 80°C were observed. Under the present experimental conditions (>350°C and >70bar), many light species (e.g. acetic acid with a critical temperature of 319.6°C) would exist in the gas/vapour phase. Carried by the excess supply of hydrogen, the residence time of these light species could be at the order of seconds, in fact 400mL of bio-oil had been fed into the reactor. This appears to coincide with the exothermic peak shown in Fig. 2a: by extrapolation, the exothermic peak would appear at the end of the NiMo bed at >400–500mL. Even at the end of that experiment, the concentration of acetic acid in the product was never as high as its concentration in the raw bio-oil. This is taken to mean that the destruction of acetic acid can take place both at the hyperactive sites and at the “normal” active sites of the catalyst. However, the occupation of the reactive sites by heavy liquid species did greatly reduce the accessibility of these active sites to acetic acid. When the LHSV was increased to 2h−1, acetic acid started to appear in the product stream just after 350mL of bio-oil had been fed into the reactor. At the LHSV value of 3h−1, acetic acid appeared in all product samples except in the product in the first trap. These again correspond to Fig. 2a that the exothermic peaks appeared earlier with increasing LHSV. These results confirm the importance of availability of active sites to the destruction of acetic acid, which could be occupied by the heavy species. Fig. 6b shows the yield of phenolics including phenol and substitutional phenols. Fig. 6c shows the yields of benzene and substituted benzenes, shown as “benzene compounds”. Bio-oil is rich in phenol structures both as light components and as heavy components, including lignin-derived oligomers [3,4,21]. However, many phenol structures in bio-oil were embedded in large molecules that would not have gone through the GC column to be quantified. Therefore, light phenolics could be converted, e.g. to produce benzene and substitutional benzenes, or formed from the breakdown of lignin-derived oligomers. Indeed, the content of GC-quantified light phenolics in bio-oil was higher than the yield of phenolics in the oil phase products produced at the earlier stages at LHSVs of 1 or 2h−1 but lower than the yields under all other conditions. The data in Fig. 6b and c indicate that the fresh NiMo catalyst at the initial periods of experiments was active in converting light vapour phenolics (Fig. 6b) into benzene compounds. At the later periods of experiments, this conversion was a lot less effective. This must again have been due to the occupation of the catalyst active sites by the heavy species. In some cases, e.g. LHSV of 2h−1, when the catalyst was significantly deactivated at later stages of experiments, the observed yields of GC-quantified phenolics decreased, apparently owing to the reduced conversion of phenol structures in large molecules into GC-quantified light phenolics. In the case of LHSV of 3h−1, the low yields of GC-quantified phenolics must have been due to the low activities of the catalyst that were in contact with abundant bio-oil liquids even at the earlier periods of experiments. To produce high yields of benzene compounds, the catalyst must be sufficiently active to produce light phenolics and also convert light phenolics into benzene and substitutional benzenes, explaining the trends in Fig. 6c. For example, at a LHSV value of 3h−1, the active sites were not sufficiently available to convert the phenol structure in large molecules into light phenolics (Fig. 6b) or to convert the light phenolics into benzene compounds, with the exception at the beginning of the experiment. Substituted cyclopentanes and cyclohexanes are the hydrogenation products. As is shown in Fig. 6d, their production was favoured at the fresh catalyst surface, mostly from the hydrogenation of light species in the gas/vapour phase, and decreased with the occupation of the catalyst by liquid and the deactivation of the catalyst. Thermogravimetric analysis was used to characterise the thermal properties of the hydrotreated products. A small amount of the oil phase product was heated up in a TGA to 500°C at a heating rate of 10°C/min. The weight loss was a result of combined physical (evaporation) and chemical (decomposition) processes, which in turn is partly related to the molecular mass distribution (see below). The residue at 500°C was termed as “potential coke”, reflecting the potential amount of coke that would form when the oil is heated to 500°C. Fig. 7 shows the typical DTG curves and the potential coke yields of the hydrotreated oil products (in the oil phases) in comparison with those of the raw bio-oil. The TGA was carried out only with the oil phase products because of the difficulties in getting accurate data with the aqueous phases that had very high water contents. The data in Fig. 7a show that, at a LHSV value of 2h−1, the product collected initially (after only 205mL of bio-oil was fed) was relatively light, all evaporated at 400°C in TGA. However, caution must be exercised in interpreting the DTG data at high temperatures (e.g. >300°C). Bio-oil is exceedingly reactive and will polymerise once it is heated up to elevated temperatures [26]. At high temperatures, these species would tend to polymerise instead of being evaporated, giving very high potential coke yield. On the other hand, many O-containing functional groups responsible for the high reactivity of bio-oil would have been hydro-deoxygenated. Therefore, the data in Fig. 7 indicate that the hydrotreated bio-oils, even at the later stages of experiments when the catalyst has been partially blocked or even partially deactivated, have much less tendency to polymerise than the raw bio-oil. Some species in the hydrotreated bio-oil could still evaporate at >450°C instead of forming coke. The data in Fig. 7b indicate that the potential coke yield determined in TGA was always low for the LHSV value of 1h−1. Increasing LHSV resulted in rapid increases in the potential coke yield. This is at least due to two reasons. Firstly, the residence time decreased with increasing LHSV, giving less time for hydrotreatment reactions to take place. Secondly, the concentration of heavy liquid in the reactor increased with increasing LHSV, limiting the access of active sites to hydrogen. In the absence of abundant active hydrogen, the relative importance of polymerisation would increase over the hydrogenation and hydrocracking reactions, favouring the formation of heavy species and coke. Fig. 8 shows the synchronous spectra of hydrotreated bio-oils (oil phases). As was stated in Experimental, the fluorescence intensity has been expressed on the basis of moisture-free bio-oil to allow for comparison under different experimental conditions. The spectrum for the raw bio-oil is shown for comparison. At the initial stages of hydrotreatment (Sample 1, Fig. 8a), the fluorescence intensity was generally very low. Little intensity was observed at wave lengths longer than 320nm, signalling the absence of ring structures with more than 2 (equivalent) fused benzene rings. The lack of oxygen in the hydrotreated bio-oil also would not give high quantum yields, contributing to the observed low intensity. These data are taken to indicate that the gas-phase-dominated hydrotreatment product was well hydrotreated. This is in agreement with the visual observation that these samples were lightly coloured. With the progress of experiments (e.g. Sample 2 in Fig. 8b), the fluorescence intensity increased, at least partly due to the appearance of liquid that had travelled through (most of) the catalyst bed. In particular, at the LHSV value of 3h−1, there was a significant increase in fluorescence intensity at wavelengths longer than 300nm, most likely due to the aromatic structures with more than 2 (equivalent) fused benzene rings. At the later stages of experiments (Samples 3 and 4 in Fig. 8c and d), the observed fluorescence intensity of the hydrotreated bio-oils were similar to or higher than those of the raw bio-oil. However, the similarities in the spectral features between the raw and hydrotreated bio-oils (e.g. the shoulder peaks at around 385nm) indicate the similarities in their aromatic structure features. The explanation of these data must consider the importance of intra-molecular energy-transfer to the observed fluorescence intensity for this type of samples [27]. Due to the intra-molecular energy transfer, very large aromatic ring systems in large molecules in bio-oil are not well represented by the observed fluorescence [27]. As these large molecules are broken down as a result of thermal or hydrocracking or removal of oxygen, the efficiency of intra-molecular transfer is lowered to result in a better representation of these large aromatic ring systems in the observed fluorescence. This explains why the fluorescence intensity of hydrotreated bio-oil can be higher than that of the raw bio-oil, but having similar spectral features. The possible formation of additional aromatic structures during the later stage of hydrotreatment cannot be ruled out but our data do not give conclusive evidence for this possibility. The UV-fluorescence data in Fig. 8 further support the discussion above in that the lighter species have behaved differently from the heavier species. The catalyst became increasing less effective in hydrogenating the aromatic structures. The continuous hydrotreatment of bio-oil in a packed bed catalytic reactor using a presulphided NiMo/γ-Al2O3 catalyst was carried out under mild conditions (375°C, 70–80bar). The aim was to investigate the hydrotreatment behaviour of the light and heavy components as a function of LHSV and catalyst time-on-stream. Our results indicate that the lighter and heavier components in the same bio-oil could behave very differently. The overall bio-oil liquid hourly space velocity can drastically affect the hydrotreatment process. While the residence time of the light species that evaporate instantly could be very short, the residence time of heavy species could be very long as they passed through the catalyst bed in the form of liquid. The initial contact of heavy bio-oil species with the pre-sulphided NiMo/Al2O3 catalyst could result in very significant exothermic peaks but did not create a thermal runaway situation, owing to the rapid deactivation of the hyperactive sites in the catalyst. The NiMo catalyst used was less active in hydrotreating the heavier bio-oil species than in hydrotreating the lighter bio-oil species. The potential coke yields of the hydrotreated bio-oils, even at very low extents of hydrotreatment, were drastically reduced. This project received funding from ARENA as part of ARENA's Emerging Renewables Program and the Second Generation Biofuels Research and Development Grant Program. The study also received support from the Government of Western Australia via the Low Emissions Energy Development Fund and via the Centre for Research into Energy for Sustainable Transport (CREST).This research used large samples of mallee biomass supplied without cost by David Pass and Wendy Hobley from their property in the West Brookton district.
abstract: This study aims to investigate the hydrotreatment of bio-oil in a continuous packed-bed reactor at around 375°C and 70bar. The bio-oil was produced from the grinding pyrolysis of mallee wood in a grinding pyrolysis pilot plant. Our results indicate that the lighter and heavier components in the same bio-oil could behave very differently. Their behaviour can be affected very significantly by the overall bio-oil liquid hourly space velocity. While the residence time of the light species that evaporate instantly could be very short, the residence time of heavy species passing through the catalyst bed in the form of liquid could be very long. When a commercial pre-sulphided NiMo/Al2O3 catalyst came into contact with the heavy bio-oil species, significant exothermic reactions would take place, which result in the deactivation of hyperactive sites in the catalyst. The NiMo/Al2O3 catalyst used was less active in hydrotreating the heavier bio-oil species than in hydrotreating the lighter bio-oil species. However, even at very low extents of hydrotreatment, the bio-oil structure and properties, e.g. coking propensity, could be drastically improved.
title: Different reaction behaviours of the light and heavy components of bio-oil during the hydrotreatment in a continuous pack-bed reactor

full_text: Compared with petroleum-based diesel, biodiesel has no remarkable differences in engine durability or in carbon deposits, but it has many favorable advantages, such as biodegradable, nontoxic, low emission and renewable [1,2]. Therefore, it gains more and more attention all around the world [3–5]. Researchers are always looking for the suitable materials to produce biodiesel on a larger scale [6]. Oil such soybean oil [7,8], rice bran oil [9], sunflower oil [10], jatropha oil [11], waste edible oil [12,13] and canola oil [14] as feedstock for enzymatic production of biodiesel have been reported previously. Different immobilized lipases also have been employed as biocatalysts [15], such as Lipozyme TL IM [16,17], and Novo 435 [17,18]. But enzyme-catalyzed methods have not been applied in industrialization mainly due to the relatively high price of lipase and the short operational life caused by the negative effects of excessive methanol and by-product glycerol. Novozym 435 is often applied in the biodiesel production because it is a nonspecific lipase and can migrate acyl groups from sn-1,2,3 of triglycerides. But the high price ($ 2270/Kg) brings about some difficulties in the industrialization appliance. While Lipozyme TL IM could only migrate acyl group from position sn-1,3 and the activity of transesterification is lower than that of lipase Novozym 435, its price ($ 139/Kg) is 16 folds lower than that of Novozym 435. Moreover, the high positive synergic effect and excellent operational stability were found between Novozym 435 and Lipozyme TL IM in our previous study [19]. So the combined biocatalysts were applied in the biodiesel preparation from stillingia oil. Stillingia oil was used as substrate in the study because its seed contains oil as high as 45% and the amount of unsaturated fatty acid is up to 90% (seen in Table 1 ). Therefore, it is very suitable for biodiesel preparation. The seed output is very large every year in China and the Chinese government has made a decision to develop biodiesel on a large scale based on stillingia oil raw material. Traditional view thinks that enzymes only play a catalytic role in aqueous solution, its configuration will be changed or most of its critical surface water molecules will be stripped in organic solvent system, then resulting in inactivity [20]. With the research deepening, Klibanov et al. found that catalytic activity of some enzymes increased and the stability was greatly improved in nearly anhydrous environment in 1980s [21]. Thereafter, transesterification-biocatalyzed in organic phase has received gradual attention. In transesterification reaction, methanol was chosen as the other substrate because of the cheap price. But it is insoluble in oil and the immobilized lipases are easily inactivated by contacting with methanol droplets. By-product glycerol is hydrophilic and also insoluble in the oils, and it is easily adsorbed onto the surface of the immobilized lipase and reduces lipase activity and operational stability. In previous literature, the t-butanol was proved to be a good solvent in transesterification [22,23]. As reaction media, some organic solvents were chosen for application to the compound-lipase catalyzed biodiesel preparation in this research. The effect of different pure organic solvents and co-solvent mixtures on the preparation of biodiesel was compared, and an optimal co-solvent medium which could dissolve both methanol and glycerol to eliminate the negative effects thoroughly was found and applied in the stability experiments of the compound-lipase. Novozym 435 and Lipozyme TL IM were bought from Novo Nordisk Bioindustrials Inc (Denmark). Stillingia oil was purchased from a refinery factory of Dawu country, Hubei Province, P. R. China, and its composition was shown in Table 1. Methyl palmitate, methyl stearate, methyl oleate, methyl linoleate, methyl linolenate were got from Sigma (USA). All other chemicals were also obtained commercially and of analytical grade. Under standard conditions, reactions were carried out in 5ml of pure organic solvent or co-solvent mixtures (v/v), containing 5.12 molar ratio of methanol to stillingia oil, 4% (w/w) of compound-lipase (1.96%Novozym 435+2.04% Lipozyme TL IM) in a 50-ml flask stoppered with a plug at 40°C and 200rpm for 20h. These conditions were used except when otherwise stated in the text. The experiments were carried out in triplicate. When the reactions ended, samples of 100µl were drawn out and centrifuged, and 10µl upper layer was mixed with 290µl n-hexane and 300µl standard solution, and then were analyzed by gas chromatography. The ME yield in the reaction mixture was assayed with a GC9790 gas chromatography equipped with a 19091N-133 INNOWAX capillary column (30m×0.25mm×0.25µm, Agilent Technologies Inc. USA). The column temperature was held at 160°C for 2min, heated to 210°C at 5°C/min, then increased to 230°C at 3°C/min and kept for 1min. Nitrogen was used as the carrier gas at 7.5ml/min. The injector and the detector temperatures were set at 230°C and 280°C, respectively. Some independent factors would bring significant effect on the yield of ME in the transesterification reaction. Therefore, according to the yield of ME, two-level experiments were designed to pick out the significant factors, and the Box–Behnken design was further adopted to optimize the experimental condition. Triplicate experiments were done for all design points except for the center one (0, 0, 0), and all experiments were conducted in a random order. Experimental design and data analysis were done with software SAS version 9.0 (SAS Institute Inc., Cary, NC, USA). Based on the experimental data, polynomial model for the ME yield was regressed (Eq. (1), in terms of coded factors), and was further testified experimentally under the optimal reaction conditions. (1) Y = β 0 + ∑ i = 1 3 β i X i + ∑ i = 1 3 β ii X i 2 + ∑ i = 1 2 ∑ j = i + 1 3 β ij X i X j where Y (%) is the yield of biodiesel, X i are the independent factors, and β 0, β i, βii, βij are the intercept, linear, quadratic and interaction constant coefficients, respectively. Glycerol is viscous and adsorbs onto the surface of the enzyme particle, so the contact ratio between substrate and lipase decreased. So the compound-lipase was retrieved before reuse. The retrieval procedure is: 1, after 12h reaction, the compound-lipase was separated from the reaction system and added into 5ml of acetonitrile (60%) and t-butanol (40%), incubated in a 50-ml flask stoppered with a plug at 40°C and 200rpm for 10min; 2, the co-solvent was filtrated and extracted. Glycerol and water was separated and heated until the water was vaporized totally; 3, the residue was diluted and determined [24] until the glycerol could not be measured; and 4, the compound-lipase was desiccated in vacuum, and was used in the next cycle. The methanol was added once. Infrared (IR) spectrum of the viscous sample obtained from the washing liquid of enzyme after transesterification was determined on VERTEX 70 (Bruker Company, German). The free glycerol in the reaction mixture was measured according to the method of Bondioli and Della Bella [24]. Laane reported the relationship between the log P of organic solvents with the reaction rate, and solvents with a log P value below 2.0 have not been considered appropriate as biocatalyst media [25]. Therefore, different polar or non-polar solvents were studied in the transesterification of stillingia oil with methanol. Higher conversion rates were observed in t-butanol, acetonitrile, t-pentanol, tehrahydrofuran and 1, 4-dioxane whose log P values from −1.52 to 1.4 were below 2.0, and lower biodiesel yield was attained in the solvent with higher hydrophobicity, such as tetraloromethane, n-hexane and n-heptane (Table 2 ). This result could explain that these organic solvents, such as t-butanol, could dissolve methanol well. Good dissolution could make two substrates contact adequately and accelerate the rate of reaction, but hydrophobic solvents dissolved little methanol, and excessive insoluble methanol deactivated the lipase gravely. In theory, polar organic solvents could deprive the lipase molecules of their essential water, thus deactivate the biocatalyst and make it unable to reuse. This is the most important reason why lipase was limited in the industrialization of biodiesel production. The co-solvent which is mixed with a strong hydrophobic solvent and a low hydrophobic solvent was considered as an alternative. Based on the above analysis, dioxane, acetonitrile tehrahydrofuran, t-butanol and t-pentanol were chosen for the mixture to investigate the effects on biodiesel yield. The log P c was calculated by following Eq. (2) [26]. (2) log P c = x 1 log P 1 +   x 2 log P 2 where log P c is the log P of the co-solvent; x 1 and x 2 are the mole fractions of two solvents mixed; log P 1 and log P 2 are the pure component values. Table 3 indicates that the biodiesel yields were higher in co-solvent than those of the pure organic solvents and the biodiesel yield in acetonitrile system was higher than in dioxane system. The highest yield (90.57%) was found in the co-solvent with 60% acetonitrile and 40% t-butanol (v/v), much higher than those of pure t-butanol (80.4%) and acetonitrile (57.88%), respectively. And the biodiesel yield kept steady in the co-solvent media along with the volume rate of two organic solvents which changed from 9:1 to 1:9. From the data, for each co-solvent, there is a consistent trend: the biodiesel yield increased with the value of log P c was added, and the biodiesel yield began to decrease when the value of log P c exceeded the point of the solubility limit of the methanol. The cause maybe was that the hydrophobicity of the co-solvent became strong and essential water of the lipase molecules was lost and affected the lipase activity (Fig. 1 ). However, some adjacent log P c value indicated different biodiesel yields. For example, the biodiesel yield was 51.38% in a mixture of 30% dioxane and 70% t-pentanol (v/v) with log P c value 0.65 and 83.89% in a mixture of 10% dioxane and 90% t-butanol (v/v) with log P c value 0.61 (Table 3). The reason maybe was that, on the one hand, the log P of a pure organic solvent could have a relationship with the enzyme activity (Table 2), but the log P of co-solvent is simply an average of the log P values of the individual components and could not describe the relation between the log P and lipase activity well. On the other hand, in addition to log P, perhaps there was other factors that could affect the lipase activity and biodiesel yield in co-solvent medium, for example the polarity of solvent could change the activity-central-conformation of lipase in some degree, and affected the combination of biocatalyst with substrates, causing lipase activity to decrease and the configuration of production changed. In addition, the functional group structure of organic solvents or interaction between the functional groups of different solvents may affect the activity of lipase by changing its selectivity, such as substrate selectivity, location selectivity or enantio selectivity. The cause why solvent engineering method is applied in transesterification reaction catalyzed by lipase is that organic solvent can accelerate solubility of methanol in oil and eliminate the toxicity of methanol on lipase. Theoretically, the molar ratio of methanol to oil is 3:1, but the ratios from 1:1 to 9:1 were all experimented [27]. From Table 3, the biodiesel yield exceeded 80% with volume rate of acetonitrile and t-butanol from 9:1 to 1:9, and the highest biodiesel yield (90.57%) was observed in co-solvent with 40% t-butanol and 60% acetonitrile (v/v). So, co-solvents with 40% t-butanol and 60% acetonitrile and pure t-butanol were chosen as reaction media to investigate the tolerance of compound-lipase to methanol in the co-solvent. After 20 reaction, Fig. 1 shows the tolerance of compound-lipase to the methanol. Biodiesel yields maintained above 89% when the molar ratio of methanol to oil were changed from 3:1 to 9:1, especially at 6:1, as high as 96.64%, higher than the biodiesel yield in pure t-butanol, respectively. The result accounted for the co-solvent could better mitigate the harm of methanol on the enzyme and enhance the tolerance of lipase to the methanol than the pure t-butanol. According to the Plackett–Burman design, experimental results showed that the molar ratios of methyl to oil, enzyme dosage and the amount of molecular sieve were the three significant factors affecting the yield of ME (P <0.05) (reaction conditions: 40°C, at 200rpm for 20h in a co-solvent system). RSM experiment (Box–Behnken design) was designed based on these three independent factors, and their effects on the yield of ME were presented in Table 4 . It shows the various yields of ME under different reaction conditions and the differences of experiment and prediction. Fifteen experiments were performed in triplicate. Table 5 shows the analysis of variance (F-test) for the experiments, and the coefficient of determination (R 2) was shown as 98.86%, indicating that the accuracy and general availability of the polynomial model were very good, and the response trends could be analyzed compatibly and well by the model. The data in Table 5 also indicate that the linear and quadratic constant coefficient was bigger than the interaction constant coefficient. Thus, we can conclude that the linear effect between the independent variables was significant, and conversely the interaction between the three independent factors was insignificant. The polynomial model for the ME yield is shown below (in terms of uncoded factors): (3) Y = − 132 . 151 + 3 0. 4 0 583 X 1 + 26 . 83875 X 2 + 28 . 3 0 917 X 3 − 4 . 511667 X 1 2 + 0. 575 X 1 X 2 + 0. 88 X 1 X 3 − 2 . 567917 X 2 2 + 0. 665 X 2 X 3 − 3 . 3 0 5417 X 3 2 Eq. (3) (the regression model) indicates the linear terms X 1, X 2 , X 3 and quadratic terms X 1⁎X 2, X 1⁎X 3, X 2⁎X 3 (positive coefficients) have positive effects on the increase of Y. But, X 1 2, X 2 2 and X 3 2 have negative effects. Response contour plot described by the regression model was shown in Fig. 2 , which exhibited the effects of three independent factors on the yield of ME and the significance of the mutual interactions between the independent factors. When the molar ratio of methanol to oil was kept at ≤6:1, an increase in ME yield was observed with the increasing of the amounts of enzyme and molecular sieve, but the trend reversed when the amounts of enzyme and molecular sieve reached up to a certain value. It could be interpreted that, under a certain value, the enzyme and molecular sieve could speed the reaction rate, but superfluous enzyme (over 4.32% (wt/wt)) tended to reunite and reduced its catalytic activity, and superfluous molecular sieve (over 5.5% (wt/wt)) would absorbed the essential water, which could maintain the active configuration of enzyme, and caused enzyme denaturation. Lipases possess the unique feature of acting at the interface between an aqueous and an organic phase, and the activity generally depends on the available interfacial area. Similarly, the effect of molar ratio of methanol to oil on production of ME was significant. With the increase of molar ratio of methanol to oil, firstly an increase and subsequently a decrease were observed for the yield of ME. The reason could be that, under a certain amount (molar ratio of methanol to oil=6.4:1), the methanol could improve the solubility of water in oil and the reaction mixture could be well mixed. Therefore, with the increase of methanol amount, ME firstly increased and then decreased as a result of the reduction of enzyme activity caused by the damage of excessive methanol. The optimal reaction conditions calculated through the regression model were as follows: 6.4:1 molar ratio of methanol to oil; 4.32% (wt/wt) compound-lipase and 5.5% (wt/wt) molecular sieve. Following the above optimal conditions, triplicate experiments were conducted to validate the precision of the regression model under 40°C, 200rpm for 20h. The average value of the yield of ME was up to 96.38%, which coincided well with the predicted value (97.52%). Therefore, the model was considered to be accurate and available in predicting the yield of ME. One of the most important characteristics of an immobilized enzyme is the stability and reusability over an extended period of time. The primary factors which affect stability and reusability of immobilized lipase are toxicity of methanol on lipase and the depression of by-product glycerol. In organic solvent medium, the solvent increased the solubility of methanol in oil and reduced the toxicity of methanol on immobilized enzyme. In addition, the by-product glycerol dissolved better and the adsorption of glycerol onto the surface of immobilized vector was avoided. The operational stability of compound-lipase was investigated in the co-solvent (40% acetonitrile and 60% t-butanol) and pure t-butanol system. From Fig. 3 , there was nearly no loss in the activity of compound-lipase after being recycled for 30 times in both two media, but the biodiesel yield was higher in the co-solvent. At the same time, the freezing point of pure t-butanol is so high (25°C) that pure t-butanol is solid state under normal temperature and brings some difficulties to the industrialization. The lower freezing point (−43.8°C) and the cheaper price of acetonitrile make the co-solvent better in industrial application. Different oils were applied to research extensive applicability of compound-lipase in the co-solvent (60% acetonitrile and 40% t-butanol) and the pure t-butanol at the same reaction conditions (Fig. 4 ). All test oils except waste oils attained very high yield. As known, the acid value of waste oils is higher than other substrate oil, and a great deal of water will be produced as by-product in the transesterification reaction. With increasing of water, excess water may also stimulate the competing hydrolysis reaction. The yield of biodiesel would decrease. This demonstrates that the co-solvent can be used as a potential medium for lipase catalyzed biodiesel production, which provides a strong theoretical foundation for industrial application of the compound-lipase technology. Because some organic solvents, for example acetonitrile and t-butanol, were added in the reaction system, the motive of analysis of glycerol under Infrared (IR) spectrum is to observe the change of functional groups in glycerol. If the change happened, the organic reagent should participate in the transesterification reaction and is not a good solvent. By-product glycerol which was extracted and purified from the reaction mixtures was analyzed by Infrared (IR) spectrum. The peaks at different wavenumbers represent different functional groups in the samples. Fig. 5 shows the infrared spectra of the two viscous samples had nearly 100% similarity to that of pure glycerol, and concluded the functional groups of the two viscous samples are the same with pure glycerol. It also illuminates that acetonitrile and t-butanol in the study did not participate in the transesterification reaction but only as solvents. The amounts of viscous samples were measured in a spectrophotometer set at 410nm [24]. And the amount of glycerol in the co-solvent was 417.6mg glycerol (ca 0.00454mol), more than that of pure t-butanol, 333.0mg glycerol (ca 0.00362mol). The reasons maybe were, on the one hand, methanol dissolved in the co-solvent better than in pure t-butanol solvent and the damage of methanol to lipase alleviated, and made for the addition of by-product. On the other hand, that glycerol dissolved better than in the co-solvent, and the amount of glycerol increased. Lipase Novozym 435 is applied in the research extensively because it is a nonspecific enzyme and exhibits high activity in biodiesel preparation. But it is not applied in industrial application because of the high price. Lipozyme TL IM is a specific enzyme (migrate acyl groups sn-1 and 3 of glycerol) and has lower activity than Novozym 435, but it is cheaper than Novozym 435. Combining the two lipases and applying them in the biodiesel preparation could decrease the cost of industrial production. t-butanol is a good media in transesterification. But higher freezing point (25°C) of t-butanol brings some troubles in industrial production. When pure t-butanol was substituted by co-solvent combined with t-butanol and acetonitrile (−43.8°C and cheaper than t-butanol) in the study, both the freezing point of media and the cost of production would decrease. In this study, solvent engineering method acted well in protection of lipase from methanol toxicity and glycerol absorption on the surface of immobilized vector in transesterification and the reaction conditions was optimized with RSM. The study also indicates that the tolerance to methanol and the stability and reusability of the compound-lipase were greatly enhanced, which suggests that co-solvent mixture as a catalyst medium for biodiesel preparation catalyzed by compound-lipase can be a promising way. This work is financially supported by the National High Technology Research and Development Program of China (Nos. 2006AA020203, 2007AA05Z417, 2007AA100703 and 2009AA03Z232), Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University (No. NCET-07-0336), and Science and Technology Research and Development Program of Wuhan City (No. 200720422138).
abstract: Besides high cost, the most important reasons that immobilized lipases are limited in industrialization of biodiesel production are the toxicity of methanol and the adsorption of glycerol onto the surface of immobilized vector. Solvent engineering method was employed to the reaction where compound-lipase with synergistic effect, Novozym 435 and Lipozyme TL IM, catalyzed preparation of biodiesel from stillingia oil with methanol. The treatment accelerated the solubility of methanol in oil and dissolved glycerol, which helped maintain lipase activity. It is found that the yields of biodiesel in co-solvent exceeded those in the pure organic solvents. The mixture system of co-solvent with 60% acetonitrile and 40% t-butanol (v/v) was proved to be an optimal one, and RSM was used to optimize the reaction factors and the optimal conditions: methanol/oil molar ratio 6.4:1, compound-lipase 4.32% (wt/wt) and molecular sieve 5.5% (wt/wt). R 2=98.86% showed good coincidence between predicted and experimental values. There was nearly no loss inactivity of compound-lipase after being recycled for 30 times. Other oils were also investigated in the mixture system, and we got the same results, which indicated that the mixture system could be an ideal prospective medium applied to biodiesel production.
title: Biodiesel preparation catalyzed by compound-lipase in co-solvent
