full_text: Bio-oil is a liquid product from the fast pyrolysis of biomass and has been recognized as a renewable feedstock for the production of fuels for transportation. However, raw bio-oil has a number of undesirable properties such as corrosiveness and instability, largely caused by the carboxylic acids in bio-oil [1–4]. Thus, bio-oil has to be upgraded to eliminate the carboxylic acids. Esterification is a method developed and investigated intensively recently to neutralize the carboxylic acids in bio-oil with alcohols [5–17]. The traditional mineral acids such as H2SO4 and HCl are not suitable to catalyse the esterification of bio-oil due to the difficulty to separate and recycle these acids and due to the high corrosion of these acids to the equipment. Thus, most studies focused on the development of the solid acid catalysts for bio-oil esterification. Solid acid catalysts are different from the liquid ones as the active sites were not dispersed freely in the reacting solution but on/in the local surface of the catalyst. Due to the limitation of the specific area and pore sizes of the solid acid catalysts, the accessibility of the active sites for the reactants in bio-oil is a question of concern. In addition to acetic acid, which is commonly present in bio-oil, a lot of other acids with quite different molecular structures are also present in bio-oil. These acids compete for the same active sites on/in catalysts. Thus, one question was raised herein. What is the effect of the molecular structures of the carboxylic acids on their conversion over the solid acid catalyst? Understanding this is important for us to develop the solid acid catalysts to effectively convert the various acids in bio-oil. However, the knowledge about catalytic effects of molecular structures on the conversion of carboxylic acids over solid acid catalysts is still scarce. Heavy esters were also found in the bio-oil from mallee leaves. Under the esterification conditions, the transesterification of these heavy esters may occur, which undergo a similar reaction mechanism with the esterification of carboxylic acids. Thus, do the molecular structures of the esters also affect their conversion over the solid acid catalysts? In addition to acids and esters, olefins are also present in bio-oil. Is it possible to remove them via only esterification? Moreover, some oligomers were also presented in the bio-oil due to the incomplete decomposition of the poly-structure of leaves during fast pyrolysis. Does the decomposition of the oligomers continue in the presence of acid catalysts in the alcohol medium? This study tried to answer the above questions which are important to understand the catalytic behaviours of the main compounds in bio-oil during esterification. The leaf bio-oil was obtained from the pyrolysis of the leaves of mallee eucalypts (E. loxophleba ssp. gratiae) at 500°C. The experimental procedures for the pyrolysis of the leaves were presented elsewhere [18], while the typical composition of the bio-oil from leaves can be found in reference [19]. All chemicals used in this study were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. Amberlyst 70 (Rohm & Haas), a commercial solid acid catalyst with the maximum operating temperature of 190°C and the concentration of acidic sites≥2.55eq/kg, was used without further pretreatment. The detailed physical properties of Amberlyst 70 can be found in reference [20]. The Hastalloy batch autoclave reactor (Autoclave Engineers, Division of Snap-Tite Inc.) was used to perform the esterification of the leaf bio-oil. The experimental procedures were similar to our previous study [21]. Briefly, the reaction mixture and the catalyst were loaded into the reactor at room temperature. Nitrogen was used to purge the autoclave before it was heated up by an external furnace to the desired reaction temperatures in 24min. The autoclave was not pressurized before heating up but the pressure generally goes up ca. 20bar at 170°C due to the formation of vapours. Once the required reaction temperature was reached, samples were taken periodically at the required reaction temperatures at 15min intervals for 120min. The products were analysed using an Agilent GC–MS (6890 series GC plus a 5973 MS detector) with a capillary column (HP-INNOWax) (length, 30m; internal diameter, 0.25mm; film thickness, 0.25μm of crosslinked polyethylene glycol). The acetone solution containing ca. 15wt.% of sample was injected into the injection port set at 250°C with a split ratio of 50:1. The column was held at 35°C for 1.8min and then heated to 260°C at 10°C/min and held for 10min. The identification of each compound was achieved by matching its mass spectrum with that in the spectral library and was further confirmed by injecting the standard when available. It was difficult to obtain standards for some compounds identified in the GC–MS chromatograms. In these cases, the peak area of the compound was used to measure the relative changes of its concentration during reaction. In the leaf bio-oil, a number of acids were identified. These acids differ in their alkyl substituted groups (the size of the linear aliphatic acids), the structures of the alkyl substituted groups (the branched chain), and the type of the carbon chains (aliphatic acids versus aromatic acids). The difference in their structures may cause varied steric hindrance during their conversion into esters. Fig. 1 shows the conversions of a series of carboxylic acids in bio-oil with linear alkyl substituted groups as function of reaction time. The acids with a short carbon chain such as acetic acid can be converted much faster than the acids with a long carbon chain such as n-hexadecanoic acid. The esterification of the n-hexadecanoic acid only started after the reaction temperature reached 170°C. However, the conversion of n-hexadecanoic acid reached conversion levels similar to those of acetic acid when the reaction time was long enough (e.g. 120min). Clearly, the length of the carbon chain drastically affects the esterification rate of the acids. Amberlyst 70 is a macroporous resin with the basic styrene unit bearing SO3H group [22]. In a polar solvent such as methanol employed in esterification, it will swell significantly to increase its surface area and pore size to improve the accessibility of the SO3H group in the inner surface by the reactants [22]. The hydrogen ions on/in the catalysts were not dispersed in the whole solution but were concentrated on the local surface of the catalyst. Small amounts of hydrogen ions are distributed near the outer surface of the catalyst but most are located deep in the inner. To access these hydrogen ions (i.e. the catalyst), the acids have to diffuse into the deep pores of the catalyst. Acetic acid has a small molecular size, and it can easily access the hydrogen ions in both big and small pores of the catalyst. In converse, the long carbon chain in n-hexadecanoic acid creates some steric hindrance difficulty in accessing the hydrogen ions in the catalyst. In addition, the small acids and big acids would also compete for the same hydrogen ions on the outer surface of the catalyst. The branched carbon chain would also significantly affect the esterification of the acid in bio-oil. 3-Methyl-butanoic acid and pentanoic acid have the same number of carbons. However, the conversion of 3-methyl-butanoic acid was much slower than that of pentanoic acid under the same conditions, as is shown in Fig. 2 . Hexanoic acid, having a longer carbon chain than 3-methyl-butanoic acid, could also be converted much faster. It was possible that the steric hindrance created by the branched carbon chain of 3-methyl-butanoic acid slowed down its esterification rates. Aromatic acids were also identified in the leaf bio-oil, which have lower pKa values than the aliphatic acids. For example, benzoic acid is a typical aromatic acid in the leaf bio-oil, having a pKa of 4.20, which is even lower than that of acetic acid (4.74). However, the conversion of the aromatic acids in bio-oil (Fig. 3 ) was much slower than that of acetic acid or even slower than that of the aliphatic acids having big substituted groups (e.g. nonanoic acid and n-hexadecanoic acid). The long carbon chain in an aliphatic acid is flexible and thus capable of accessing the hydrogen ions even in the small pores from one direction. In comparison, the aromatic acids contain the rigid aromatic rings, creating somewhat bigger steric hindrance to access the hydrogen ions, and consequently lower the reaction rates. Only the lower concentration of hydrogen ions near the catalyst outer surface can be available to catalyse the esterification of the aromatic acids. The increase of a methylethyl group in 4-(1-methylethyl)-benzoic acid further slowed down the reaction rate. Hence, longer time is needed to convert the aromatic acids into the esters. Twelve acids were identified in the raw bio-oil. However, more than thirty esters were detected (Table 1 ) in the esterification products. The majority of them were not present in the raw bio-oil and therefore they were the products of esterification or other reactions. The abundance (GC–MS response) of the small esters could reach the plateau value much more rapidly than the big ones. Typical esterification reactions are shown in Scheme 1 . Not all esters came from the esterification of the carboxylic acids. For example, methyl levulinate was produced from methanolysis of the C6 sugars present in the bio-oil [23–25]. In addition to these methyl esters, some lactones such as butyrolactone, E-7-isopylidene-3-oxabicyclo[3.3.0]octan-2-one, and 7-methylene-6,6-dimethyl-3-oxabicyclo[3.3.0]octan-2-one were also detected. These lactones might be produced from the intramolecular esterification (Scheme 1). The conversion of the polar and reactive carboxylic acids and sugars into the neural and volatile esters could improve the quality of bio-oil as the esters, especially the fatty esters, are good biodiesels [26–28]. In addition to the esterification reactions, the transesterification of the high molar esters also produced the smaller esters and alcohols. Some high molar mass esters were present in the raw bio-oil from leaves. In the methanol-rich medium and in the presence of an acid catalyst, the transesterification between esters and methanol would transform the high molar mass esters into smaller methyl esters. The transesterification of some esters in the bio-oil from mallee leaves is shown in Scheme 2 . It is interesting that the abundance of isoamyl isovalerate (Fig. 4 ) did not decrease initially with the increase of the reaction temperature to 170°C (Fig. 4a). Conversely, its abundance even increased to some extent (i.e. some negative initial conversion). Meanwhile, isoamyl isovalerate reacted with methanol, producing isopentyl alcohol and methyl isovalerate (see Fig. 4b and c). Apparently, isoamyl isovalerate must have produced simultaneously from the decomposition of some oligomers in bio-oil. The reaction rates of the esters with methanol over Amberlyst 70 were quite different. 1-Acetoxy-2-butanone could be completely converted after 120min (Fig. 5a). In comparison, the conversion of 4-methyl-2-pentyl acetate was ca. 80% (Fig. 5c), which might be related to their different molecular structures. Both 1-acetoxy-2-butanone and 4-methyl-2-pentyl acetate are acetate esters and their molecular sizes are similar (Scheme 2). However, two methyl groups (branched carbon chain) are present in 4-methyl-2-pentyl acetate and one of them is close to the ester group, which would create some steric hindrance for the interaction of the ester group with methanol and/or accessing the catalyst. Isoamyl isovalerate also contains the branched carbon chain. Moreover, Isoamyl isovalerate has even bigger molecule in size than 4-methyl-2-pentyl acetate, and it was converted much slower than 4-methyl-2-pentyl acetate. As discussed in Section 3.1, the molecular structures of the carboxylic acids or steric hindrance significantly affect their conversion. This effect also applies to the transesterification of heavy esters as the transesterification and esterification are similar processes to convert the heavy esters/carboxylic acids into esters with alcohols in the presence of an acid catalyst. These heavy esters could be precursors for the formation of carboxylic acids in bio-oil, as upon heating up in the absence of methanol these esters would react with the water in bio-oil to be hydrolysed into the carboxylic acids. In comparison, in the methanol-rich medium, these high molar mass esters could be converted into smaller methyl esters via the transesterification. The small methyl esters are relatively more volatile, which contributes to the improvement of the quality of the bio-oil as fuels. Olefins are important compounds in the bio-oil from leaves. During esterification in the presence of methanol, they could undergo the methanolysis to produce methyl ethers. One example was the methanolysis of styrene, as shown in Scheme 3 and Fig. 6 . Some styrene was already present in bio-oil. With the increase of the reaction temperature to 170°C, more was produced. The precursor for styrene was not identified and it was possible that the decomposition of some oligomers in the bio-oil produced more styrene. With the progress of the reaction, the methanolysis of styrene dominated, producing the ether, (1-methoxyethyl)benzene (see Fig. 6b). The olefins are not stable and are prone to polymerize under thermal conditions [29]. Thus, the elimination of the olefin via the methanolysis would contribute to the stabilization of bio-oil. GC/MS can only detect the very volatile/small compounds in bio-oil. The heavy compounds or the oligomers in bio-oil cannot be detected. At elevated temperatures for prolonged reaction time and in the presence of an acid catalyst, these oligomers may react to form smaller ones. During esterification, the abundance of some compounds increased significantly at the elevated temperatures while their possible precursors were not identified with GC/MS (Fig. 7 ). These compounds have their origins in the oligomers in the bio-oil. For example, hydroxyl acetone (Fig. 7a) could be produced from the degradation of the C6 sugars [30]. Hence, it should come from the decomposition of some sugars or sugar oligomers in bio-oil. β-Methyl-4-(1-propenyl)-benzeneethanol (MPBE) is an alcohol with the aromatic group (Fig. 7b). Cardinene (Fig. 7c) has the characteristic structure of the terpenoids while 2-heptadecanone (Fig. 7d), like the fatty acid in bio-oil, has the long carbon chain. The exact precursors for the formation of these compounds are not yet known. The fast pyrolysis of the mallee leaves to produce the bio-oil was performed in a fluidised-bed reactor at 500°C [19]. However, the residence time of the pyrolysis vapour in the reactor was quite short (<3s) to produce some intermediate products such as the oligomers. During esterification, although the temperature (170°C) was much lower than that during pyrolysis (500°C), the long residence time (120min) and the presence of an acid catalyst and methanol would facilitate the various acid-catalysed reactions of the oligomers. The further reactions of big oligomers into smaller ones could improve the volatility and stability of bio-oil. The molecular structures of acids in bio-oil significantly affect their acid-catalysed conversion in methanol. The increases in the molecular size significantly decrease the reactivity of the acids, due to the steric hindrance limiting their access to the hydrogen ions in the deep pores of the catalyst. The esterification rate of the carboxylic acid with the branched carbon chain was lower than the corresponding linear ones, while it is even more difficult to esterify the aromatic acids with rigid aromatic rings. The development of any solid acid catalysts for the esterification of bio-oil has to consider the different catalytic behaviours of these acids. Preferably, the solid acids with big pores for increased hydrogen ions accessibility are expected to be more efficient to convert the acids of high molecular masses. The molecular sizes of the heavy esters also affect their transesterification into the small esters. In addition, the methanolysis of the olefins and other reactions of the oligomers in bio-oil occurred simultaneously during the acid-treatment of bio-oil in methanol, which together with other reactions significantly modified the composition of the bio-oil. The following are the supplementary data related to this article. Table S1 The carboxylic acids identified in the leaf bio-oil. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.fuproc.2012.09.028. Australian Government funding through the Second Generation Biofuels Research and Development Grant Program and the International Science Linkages (ISL) Program support this project. The study also received support from the Government of Western Australia via the Centre for Research into Energy for Sustainable Transport (CREST).
abstract: Acid-treatment of the bio-oil from the pyrolysis of mallee leaves in methanol over a solid acid catalyst Amberlyst 70 has been investigated in this study. The reactivities of the carboxylic acids with methanol were quite different over the solid acid catalyst, which was closely related to their distinct molecular structures. The linear alkyl substituted groups, the branched alkyl substituted groups, and the aromatic carbon chain of the acids created the different steric hindrances, which negatively affected their accessibility to the catalytic sites on/in the solid acid catalyst. Similar steric effects were found in the conversion of the heavy esters into small esters via transesterification. In addition to the esterification/transesterification, the methanolysis of the olefin and decomposition of the oligomers in bio-oil occurred simultaneously during the acid treatment, which converted the reactive components into the relatively volatile and stable ones and improved the quality of the bio-oil.
title: Acid-catalysed treatment of the mallee leaf bio-oil with methanol: Effects of molecular structure of carboxylic acids and esters on their conversion

reaction_catalog.transformation: heavy esters + methanol → small methyl esters + alcohols

steps.method_details: loaded catalyst into reactor at room temperature
purged autoclave with nitrogen

full_text: The close and unavoidable fossil fuels exhaustion, the energetic dependence on traditional energy sources, the environmental problems associated with their exploitation and the increasing energy demand are reasons more than enough to think that it is imminent that a change on the worldwide energy system has to occur. The potential environmental effects derived from the continual increase in CO2 concentration have moved the international community to act [1]. As a consequence, the Kyoto protocol and other international agreements have been signed in order to secure an international commitment to reduce the global CO2 emissions. The alternative energy resources such as nuclear power and renewable energy sources are nowadays being employed to generate electricity. In the transport field (which is responsible for the 18% consumption of primary energy worldwide) there are several alternatives able to substitute the fossil fuels role, such as biofuels. However, the great demand on energy requires more alternatives at medium-long term and H2 PEM fuel cell (FC) is considered as an attractive alternative to the conventional internal combustion engine that may become the engine of the future transports provided the problems of H2 storage can be solved. FCs could also be used to transform energy into electricity and heat. In this way, electricity production would be shared between nuclear, renewable and hydrogen sources and energy supply to transport would be covered almost totally by hydrogen (with a certain contribution of biofuels). This clean hydrogen-based energy system, supposed to take place in next decades, is known as “Hydrogen economy” [2]. Many chemical processes can be employed to produce hydrogen such as fossil fuel reforming (currently causing 98% of hydrogen production), water electrolysis, photoelectrolysis, biomass gasification, ion exchange membranes, photobiological production, ethanol and methanol steam reforming [3]. The obtention of H2 from biomass or from electrolysis using renewable systems such as wind, hydraulics or photovoltaic power has arisen the interest of researchers worldwide [4]. Although its combustion causes just H2O as emission, as an energy carrier, hydrogen will be as clean as the method employed for its production. The transformation of biomass into hydrogen-rich gas is a suitable source for energy production due to the advantages typical of biomass (renewable character, net CO2 emissions below zero, possibility of using residues generated in agrarian exploitation plants…). Moreover, European Community has committed to the utilization of biomass in order that its contribution to primary energy in Europe would reach around 9% by year 2010. Having into account that nowadays biomass represents only 3% of primary energy consumption in industrialized countries, it is evident that intense research focused on improving biomass conversion into energy (and hydrogen) has to be done. Regarding hydrogen production by means of biomass gasification processes, although some research on the various equilibria taking place has been done [5–7], there is a lot of work to do on it, studying the influence of variables such as pressure, temperature, steam/biomass ratio, steam flow, presence of catalysts, particle size…etc. Some research [8,9] has also been made concerning the production of hydrogen from carbonaceous materials using CaO as CO2 sorbent (in the same reactor) in order to remove CO2 from the gas produced (via the reaction: CaO+CO2 →CaCO3). It is also very important to study the reforming of the tar generated during the biomass gasification processes since it means a very important obstacle in the utilization of these processes for power generation or hydrogen production. Tar derived from pyrolysis/gasification processes will be condensed as temperature is lower than its dew point, then block and foul process equipments like fuel lines, filters, engines and turbines. The current tar reduction or destruction methods can be broadly divided into five main groups [10]: mechanisms methods (such as scrubber, filter, cyclone and electrostatic precipitators), self-modificators (influence of the operating parameters during biomass gasification, including two stage gasification processes), thermal cracking, catalysts cracking and plasma methods. The next step in our future works will be to investigate tar removal using thermal and catalyst cracking, once the production of hydrogen has been optimized. This work intends to offer a clear discussion on the influence of temperature in steam (diluted in air) gasification, in order to understand the degree at which the equilibria involved take place, focusing on the obtention of an hydrogen-rich gas. Since, as we reported in previous works [11], low temperature gasification procedures led to high tar content in the gasification products, in order to obtain large gas yields, high temperatures were employed (700–900 °C). Identical experiments were carried out without steam (just air gasification) in order to determine the several equilibria produced by steam it self. A large number of catalytic substances are employed in biomass steam-gasification processes to enhance the yield of fuel gas and reduce its tar content by cracking and reforming the high molecular weight organic components [12]. The use of dolomite as a catalyst in biomass gasification has attracted much attention because it is inexpensive and abundant, but it is significantly active only above 800 °C; also, it is possible to use it in bed as a primary catalyst as well as in secondary, downstream reactor [13,14]. Other alternatives to dolomite in biomass gasification or pyrolysis can be olivine [13,15] and ZnCl2 [16,17]. In this work, the presence of two catalysts (ZnCl2 and dolomite) was studied in steam gasification. Olive oil waste was used as raw material since it is a residue that is extensively generated in Spain (approximately, 2.2 million t/yr of orujillo are generated in our country, which means 30% of world total production) [6]. Many authors have already proven the efficiency of the residues generated by olive oil manufacturing for the obtention of energy by means of gasification [6] or pyrolysis processes [16]. The gasification of olive orujillo was carried out at atmospheric pressure on a bench scale cylindrical reactor integrated in an experimental installation that has been described elsewhere [18]. The gasifier, with an inner diameter of 4 cm (outter diameter of 4.3 cm) and total height of 75 cm was placed inside an electrical furnace, which provided the heat for reactions. Once the furnace had achieved the target temperature, the biomass (contained in a basket in the reactor) was displaced towards the inner of the gasifier and the process was initiated. The steam was produced in a coil by another electrical furnace and the steam flow rate was controlled by a water pump supplying water to the generator. The gas produced was made to pass through a quenching system (glass recipients covered by ice), where the tars and condensable liquids were collected. Next, the gas flow rate was measured with a flowmeter. The composition of the gas produced; mainly H2, CO, CO2, CH4, and O2 (and small traces of C2H2, C2H4 and C2H6), was analysed by a 4000 HRGC KONIK gas chromatograph provided with two thermal conductivity detectors, connected to two columns: Porapack Q and Carboxen-1000 (15 ft length, 1/8 in. diameter). In future works, once the gasification process has been optimized, the authors will include a second reactor (next to the main one) in which the gas produced will be subjected to high temperatures in the presence of catalysts such as olivine or calcined dolomite in order to enhance the tars cracking, and thus increasing H2 production. The experiments were carried out by three experimental series: a) air gasification (100 cm3min−1), varying the temperature (700–900 °C); b) steam/air gasification, using steam (0.41 g min−1) diluted in air (100 cm3 min−1) for the same range of temperature; c) catalyzed steam gasification employing ZnCl2 and dolomite (5% w/w referred to cation) at 800 and 900 °C (using the same air and steam flows). ZnCl2 was added by impregnation (the orujillo was introduced in a 5% w/w ZnCl2 stirring solution for 24 h, then it was dried before being used) and crushed dolomite was added by physical blending with biomass. All experiments were carried out isothermally and an initial mass of 20 g of raw material was employed. Nitrogen (200 mL min−1) was used as inert gas during the warming and cooling periods. Although most of the research on steam gasification found in the literature is made using N2 [5,11], or O2 [19] as carrier gas, in this work we used a low flow of air as steam diluting agent since it could be more convenient for industrial applications because it is available and free. Also, comparing air and steam/air gasification processes is interesting in order to clarify the processes that are occurring due to steam. The olive waste (orujillo) was used as it is generated in industrial plants, with a particle size of 0.4–2 mm. The gasification time employed was equal to 60 min. The proximate analysis of the residues was carried out according to standard norms. The determination of moisture, volatile matter and ashes was performed following the norms UNE/32-001-81 (1981), UNE/32-019-84 (1984), UNE/32-004-844 (1984), respectively [20–22]. The fixed carbon was calculated by difference to 100%. The determination of the ultimate analysis was made using a LECO CHN 1000 elemental analyser (EA 1108). The higher heating value (HHV) of the olive waste was determined with a Parr 1351 calorimeter bomb (norm ISO 1928). These parameters are displayed in Table 1 ; one can see that orujillo has a high content of volatile matter and low content of ash, nitrogen and sulfur, which is interesting with respect to its applications in gasification and pyrolysis processes. The low content of S diminishes the possibility of acid species formation which can produce ‘‘acid rain’’ or corrode the metallic parts of the gasification installation. The low N content ensures that thermal NO x formation during the gasification process is negligible. Table 2 shows the solid yields obtained for the experiments corresponding to this series (also the ones of steam gasification processes, which will be discussed later on). The results show clearly that the increase in temperature led to lower solid yields due to enhanced char reaction with the gasifying medium. Fig. 1 shows the evolution of the H2 concentration (mole L−1) with time over the range of temperatures (700–900 °C). It can be appreciated that, due to the pyrolytic character of this hydrogen, the velocity at which it is generated is related to temperature. The residence time corresponding to the maximum value of H2 molar fraction is displaced to shorter times as temperature is increased. Thus, at 900 °C the highest molar fraction of H2 (0.27) was achieved at a residence time lower than 1 min; at 850 and 800 °C, the values obtained for this fractions (0.56 and 0.54, respectively) were both observed in the range of time of 1–3 min. At 750 °C, the maximum H2 fraction (0.51) appeared at 3–5 min and at 700 °C, the residence time yielding the highest XH2 (0.47) was equal to 7 min. Since the residence times corresponding to the highest XH2 were so different and temperature-dependent, we thought that it would be more interesting to compare the molar fractions of each component in the whole gas generated after one hour (which is the duration that we established for the experiments). In Fig. 2 , the composition (molar fraction of each component: H2, CO, CH4 and CO2) of the gas produced after the whole process (60 min) has been plotted. The rise in temperature from 700 to 850 °C was found to slightly increase X H2 from 0.32 to 0.41, while further increases in temperature did not seem to increase this concentration (possibly due to the fact that the release of other components was bigger at higher temperatures). Also, CH4 decreased from 0.16 to 0.11, which could be due to a possible contribution of methane reforming reaction (CH4 +H2O→3H2 +CO), favored with temperature. The increase found in CO concentration at higher temperatures may be due on the one hand, to a higher contribution of partial gasification of carbon (which has an absolute value of enthalpy higher than the total oxidation reaction), and on the other hand, to the endothermic character of Boudouard equilibrium (C+CO2 →2CO) which does also justify the decrease in CO2. At a given pressure the equilibrium gas composition for this reaction is only a function of the temperature employed [23]. This increase in CO/decrease in CO2 can be clearly observed if the mean ratio CO/CO2 is plotted vs temperature for each experiment (Fig. 3 ). As it can be seen, this value varies from 0.85 for the temperature of 700 °C to 2.7 in the case of 900 °C. Also, it can be observed that two different tendency lines exist: one in the range 700–800 °C, with a slope of 0.0067 and R 2 =1, and another one for higher temperatures, in which the slope value is almost the double (0.0113), with a R 2 of 0.9925. This indicates the predominance attained by Boudouard reaction at temperatures higher than 800 °C. Table 3 shows the gas production (mol/kg biomass) corresponding to this series. These values confirm the hypothesis made above. All the experiments belonging to this series (700–900 °C) were made using a steam/biomass ratio of 1.2 w/w. Steam gasification results in the participation of several equilibria (apart from pyrolytic and oxidation reactions present in air gasification) from which the following reactions are the most important: Water gas reaction C+H2O→CO+H2 ΔH =118.9 kJ mol−1 Hydro gasification reaction C+2H2 →CH4 ΔH =−87.4 kJ mol−1 Water gas shift reaction CO+H2O→H2 +CO2 ΔH =−40.9 kJ mol−1 Steam reforming reaction CH4 +H2O→3H2 +CO ΔH =206.3 kJ mol−1 Changes in pressure or temperature within relatively narrow limits can produce significant shifts of the equilibrium composition towards either the starting materials or the end products [24]. Having a look at the solid yields corresponding to this experimental series (see Table 2), some points can be regarded: the decrease in solid yield with temperature is more abrupt than the one observed in air gasification, and the increase of the molar productions of the gases produced (except for methane), is also more important (see Table 4 ). This may be due, as in air gasification, to the fact that higher temperatures favour the elimination of volatile compounds in pyrolysis steps and the cracking of liquid phase heavy hydrocarbons, and also to the participation of secondary reactions common of steam gasification (as water-gas reaction), promoted by the rise of temperature, specially above 750 °C. Figs. 4–7 show the effect of temperature on the evolution of the concentration of H2, CH4, CO and CO2, respectively. Fig. 8 shows the molar composition of the final gas produced after 60 min at each temperature studied. The production of hydrogen (see Fig. 4) was clearly favored with temperature, and maintained quite important concentrations after the pyrolytic process (these results are very interesting from the point of view that the production of a stable H2 current would be useful for PEM Fuel Cells feeding). The concentration attained in the pyrolytic process was found to increase after this period for temperatures higher than 850 °C. Thus, differently from air gasification, the value of the maximum H2 molar fraction found in almost all the cases is very close to the H2 molar fraction found in the overall process (Fig. 8) which is very interesting with views of continuous feeding systems. At 700 °C, the highest H2 molar fraction (0.51) was found at 20 min; the average value of it (for gas generated during 60 min) is equal to 0.44. For the range 750–900 °C, the maximum X H2 was achieved in a residence time between 7–10 min and was equal to 0.60 (at 750 °C), 0.65 (at 800 °C) and 0.67 (at both 850 °C) and 0.70 (at 900 °C). One can see the similarity between these values and the ones shown in Fig. 8. Having a look at this figure, it can be observed how the rise in temperature clearly increased H2 formation from 0.44 (at 700 °C) to 0.65 (at 900 °C). This tendency (higher temperatures produce higher H2 concentrations) has been found by most of the researchers [5,19,25] and seems to be the same, independently of the particle size of the samples belonging to the series employed [25]. The determination of the relationship between this increase in H2 concentration and the various equilibria taking place in steam gasification is complex, but some statements can me made, taking into account the variations in the concentrations of the other gaseous components. Fig. 5 shows the evolution of the concentration of CH4 with time. The decrease in CH4 concentration as temperature is increased may be due to various factors but the fact that higher temperatures favour methane oxidation might be the most important. Some contribution of methanation reaction (3H2 +CO→CH4 +H2O, ΔH =−206.3 kJmol−1) could be a source of methane formation at the lowest temperatures, although it does not seem to participate in a significant extent since big amounts of CO are observed at 700 °C for times up to 60 min (see Fig. 8, a CO global molar fraction of 0.21 was obtained at 700 °C, higher than the one corresponding to slightly higher temperatures, except for 900 °C). Also, if we compare the CH4 molar fraction obtained in the case of air gasification for the temperatures 700–750 °C (0.16–0.13, respectively) we find that these values are higher than the ones found at these temperatures in steam gasification (0.08–0.06, respectively), which supports the hypothesis that steam reforming reaction is taking place rather than methanation reaction, which is in agreement with the results reported by Turn et al. [19] in their study about steam gasification of sawdust. The concentration of CO exhibits a particular feature with increasing temperature. At 700 °C, it has a value of 0.21 (see Fig. 8). Then, it decreases slightly at 750 °C and then increases moderately with temperature (from 0.19 at 750 °C to 0.21 at 900 °C). This effect (higher CO concentrations at lower temperatures, then decrease of CO and final tendency of increasing CO with temperature) has also been obtained by other authors when steam gasifying other biomass residues [5]. First, temperatures lower than 700 °C favour the partial gasification of carbon. As stated by Schilling [24], in steam/air gasification, the oxygen is first converted to carbon dioxide in a shallow combustion zone. The decomposition of steam and the formation of CO occur to an appreciable extent when the coal and gas have become hot. If we observe the shape of the curve corresponding to 700 °C (Fig. 6), the concentration of CO reaches its maximum value after 25–30 min, which is consistent with the theory exposed before. This CO concentration is not maintained for higher temperatures (750–900 °C) but it decreases in a variable extent after the pyrolytic stage (about first 5–6 min). This decrease is more abrupt as temperature lowers, yielding lower CO concentrations. Possibly, at 750 °C there is CO that is being formed (as at 700 °C), but it may be converted to CO2 via water-gas shift reaction (yielding lower quantities of CO, and increasing H2 concentration). There might also be a slight contribution of Boudouard equilibrium since the CO2 molar fraction found at this temperature has decreased a little. Also, there might be some contribution of steam reforming reaction, as it has been stated above. At temperatures about 800–850 °C, although some contribution of water–gas shift reaction may take place, other reactions that consume CO2 and/or produce CO may become dominant (Boudouard equilibrium and water- gas reaction). This explains the increase in CO and H2 occurred in this range. Finally, at highest temperatures (850–900 °C) the prominence taken by water-gas reaction and Boudouard equilibrium is clear. Steam reforming reaction (and in general reforming and cracking reactions) could also be a source of CO and H2 formation at highest temperatures. In Fig. 9 the mean ratios CO/CO2, CO/H2 and CO2/H2 have been plotted for the different temperatures. Having a look to these curves it is easy to guess which of the reactions are being dominant for each range of temperature. CO/CO2 values decreased with temperature rise up for the range 700–750 °C, then increasing with temperature as thermodinamically favoured (specially from 800 °C on). The value of H2/CO ratios shows a clear tendency: at lower temperatures (700–750) it increases due to the prevailing role of water–gas shift reaction; then, it decreases since the contribution of this equilibrium is less important (the increase observed in this value at 900 °C is not due to a decrease in CO but to a great increase in H2 production). With respect to H2/CO2 ratio, it is obvious that it increases in the whole range of temperature studied. The fact that this rise is much more sharply for higher temperatures indicates the increasing prominence taken by water-gas reaction. It is important to notice that in this range (800–900 °C) the value of the slope of the corresponding tendency line for CO/CO2 plot (0.0106) is very close to its value in air gasification (0.0113, shown in Fig. 3). This indicates that even at high temperatures some CO2 is being formed from water–gas shift reaction since if this did not happen (and only water-gas reaction was taking place) this slope should be bigger. The great amount of hydrogen formed at 900 °C may be the consequence of the simultaneous presence of these two equilibria: although water–gas reaction is thermodynamically favoured, the important amounts of CO produced may displace water–gas shift process to the right, yielding CO2 and more hydrogen. At the same time, the presence of Boudouard equilibrium (in a similar extent than in air gasification) may produce CO from the CO2 present in the medium. The effect of ZnCl2 and dolomite as catalysts was studied for the temperatures 800 and 900 °C. Table 5 shows the solid yields obtained for these reactions (the solid yields corresponding to the non-catalytic processes have been included). One can see that the value of this parameter for each temperature is similar when both catalysts are used and lower than the corresponding uncatalyzed one. The evolution of the concentration of the hydrogen produced (mol L−1) vs time for these series at 800 °C and 900 °C have been plotted in Figs. 10 and 11 respectively (in order to compare the results corresponding to air and steam processes, these curves have also been included). Table 6 shows the molar productions per kg biomass obtained for these series. Having a look at Fig. 10, it can be seen that the presence of these catalysts clearly favours the production of H2 at 800 °C, especially in the case ZnCl2. With this catalyst, the residence time at which the highest X H2 is achieved (0.69) is shorten to 5 min, whereas with steam and dolomite it occurs at 13 min and reaches a value of 0.65 and 0.58, respectively. With respect to the H2 molar concentration of the overall gas, it was increased for both catalysts (see Fig. 12 ). Some authors have obtained similar H2 concentrations [14] or slightly lower [19] when steam gasifying biomass at 800 °C, employing dolomite as a downstream catalyst. This increase in H2 production at 800 °C might be due to a more prevailing predominance of water-gas reaction since, although CO has decreased a little, the quantity of CO2 is much smaller than that corresponding to the uncatalyzed one. One advantageous point about the results obtained in our case is that H2 concentration (Fig. 10) seems to be maintained and even increased after the pyrolytic stage, which did not occur in uncatalyzed experiments. The utilization of these catalysts is therefore positive in the sense that both of them (especially ZnCl2) allow the process to take place at lower temperatures, even increasing the hydrogen production (and maintaining a very stable H2 concentration, which is interesting for industrial applications). At 900 °C, none of the catalysts improved H2 production as it can be seen in Fig. 11 and in Table 6. The presence of these catalysts did not affect the residence time at which the maximum H2 molar fraction was found either. On the other hand, as it happened in non-catalyzed experiments, water-gas reaction is promoted at high temperatures, which is consistent with the tendencies shown if we compare the analogous experiments at 800 and 900 °C (CO increases and CO2 decreases). The increase found in CO2 production at 900 °C in the case of dolomite catalyzed reaction might be attributed to dolomite calcination. In order to evaluate the energy content of the gases produced the following parameters were determined: gas yield (GY, that is the volumetric production of gas per gram of biomass, LN g−1 biomass), high heating value (HHV, which is the volumetric gross calorific value of the gas, MJ NL−1), energy yield (EY%, defined as the ratio between the kcal/kg of gas produced and kcal/kg of initial biomass) and carbon yield (CY%, that is the relation between the carbon content in the gas produced and the one present in the biomass). These parameters have been plotted in Fig. 13 (influence of temperature in non-catalyzed steam gasification). It can be seen that the gas yield increased with temperature, because, as it has been stated before, greater temperatures favoured the production of the gas. For the same reason, the rise in gasification temperature resulted in higher conversion of Energy (parameter also referred to mass of initial biomass). The decrease observed for the values of HHV (referred to volume of gas produced) with the increasing temperature (from 700 to 750 °C) can be attributed to the fact that higher temperatures produce gases with less quantities of CH4 (molecule with the highest HHV) and, on the other hand, although H2 content has increased, this rise is not enough to make this parameter higher. However, from 750 to 900, this value increases moderately since the compromise between these effects (less CH4, more H2) results positive. With respect to carbon yield (%), it can be seen that this parameter is initially increased with temperature (until 800 °C) and then decreases, mainly due to the important decrease in CO2. The results obtained so far seem to suggest that the optimized gasification temperature could be 900 °C or above, because the gas obtained have greater content of hydrogen and less hydrocarbons and tars; this temperature would also favour gas yield and energy conversion. The main disadvantage of using higher temperatures could be the high operational costs involved in the need of indirect heating [5]. In the case of catalyzed reactions (Fig. 14 ) very different effects can be observed for each temperature. At 800 °C, HHV, GY and EY are improved with both catalyst and CY remains almost constant (slightly lower for ZnCl2). This is explained on the basis that both catalyzed reactions gave rise to higher H2 concentrations. The slight increase in carbon yield of dolomite in comparison to ZnCl2, might be a consequence of dolomite calcination. At 900 °C, no improvement on HHV, GY or EY was observed as it was expectable from the results discussed before. The high value of the carbon yield for the ZnCl2 experiment at 900 °C, is a consequence of the high productions of CO and CO2 found on it. Biomass steam gasification could be a source of hydrogen production for fuel cells feeding providing that their contents in tars, CO and CO2 are properly removed. In order to understand the equilibria taking place in this process, olive waste residue (orujillo) was gasified with water steam (diluted in air) in this work comparing the results obtained with those corresponding to air gasification reactions. The results obtained confirm that increasing temperature results positive in the production of H2 (a molar fraction of 0.70 was obtained for the experiment carried out at 900 °C at a residence time of 7–10 min). This high value of molar concentration attained is not only important due to the great amount that it means but also for the stability of the concentration of the H2 produced in this reaction with time. The use of ZnCl2 and dolomite as catalyst (5% w/w) resulted positive at 800°C and did not seem to improve H2 concentration at 900 °C. Further research should be done analyzing the influence of other variables such as pressure or steam/biomass ratio. Future works made on this line will focus on the increase of Hydrogen production by a two stage process: one consisting of steam gasification at the optimal conditions found here, and a second process consisting on the removal of the tars contained in the gas produced by thermal and/or catalytic cracking of them. Later on, we will work on the elimination of CO and its conversion to CO2 by means of water-gas shift reaction. Finally, we will try to remove the CO2 from the gas studying the presence of sorbents such as CaO and other methods such as membrane technologies or amines treating. The authors express their gratitude to “Junta de Extremadura” for the financial support through the project 2PR04B016. S. Román would like to thank the “Junta de Extremadura” for the research grant received.
abstract: Hydrogen could be the energy carrier of the next world scene provided that its production, transportation and storage are solved. In this work the production of an hydrogen-rich gas by air/steam and air gasification of olive oil waste was investigated. The study was carried out in a laboratory reactor at atmospheric pressure over a temperature range of 700­900 °C using a steam/biomass ratio of 1.2 w/w. The influence of the catalysts ZnCl2 and dolomite was also studied at 800 and 900 °C. The solid, energy and carbon yield (%), gas molar composition and high heating value of the gas (kJ NL−1), were determined for all cases and the differences between the gasification process with and without steam were established. Also, this work studies the different equilibria taking place, their predominance in each process and how the variables considered affect the final gas hydrogen concentration. The results obtained suggest that the operating conditions were optimized at 900 °C in steam gasification (a hydrogen molar fraction of 0.70 was obtained at a residence time of 7 min). The use of both catalysts resulted positive at 800 °C, especially in the case of ZnCl2 (attaining a H2 molar fraction of 0.69 at a residence time of 5 min).
title: Investigation on the reactions influencing biomass air and air/steam gasification for hydrogen production

full_text: Gas-To-Liquid (GTL) technology has attracted significant research interest due to the exploitation of small-scale undeveloped gas resources as an alternative to oil depletion. For this reason, GTL technology has been a major business item by major oil companies. GTL synthetic fuels prepared by Fischer–Tropsch (FT) synthesis contain extremely low sulfur and aromatic compounds. In addition, emissions of carbon monoxide (CO), nitrogen oxide (NOx) and hydrocarbons are very low. Therefore, GTL synthetic fuels are considered to be clean fuels [1]. Recently, the GTL-FPSO (floating production, storage and offloading) process has attracted considerable attention for the production of clean synthetic fuels from stranded gas fields and associated gas in oil fields on ships [2]. The GTL process consists of three processes: reforming, FT synthesis and upgrading process. The reforming process involves the conversion of methane from natural gas to syngas, which is composed of hydrogen and carbon monoxide. FT synthesis is a reaction to obtain high boiling point hydrocarbons as the main species. Subsequently, a clean synthetic fuel can be produced through an upgrading process. In general, reforming is needed to produce syngas with a H2/CO molar ratio that is suitable to the FT synthesis reaction over Fe or Co-based catalyst. Co-based catalysts show the optimum activity from a H2/CO mole ratio of 2 [3]. The reaction process of auto-thermal reforming was easy but requires an air separate unit (ASU), which is too bulky and very expensive [4]. Partial oxidation requires an additional process to adjust the syngas molar ratio for FT synthesis and carries an explosion danger caused by the high process temperature [5]. In addition, similar to partial oxidation, dry reforming (DR) and steam reforming (SR) require an addition process to adjust the syngas molar ratio for FT synthesis [6,7]. On the other hand, the steam-CO2 reforming (SCR) of methane is a combination of steam reforming and dry reforming, and the syngas (H2/CO) molar ratio for FT synthesis can be controlled by the steam to CO2 molar ratio [8]. Water and CO2 can be obtained from submarine gas fields. The water can be obtained by desalination process of the seawater and the CO2 of 15–80% is included in the submarine gas fields [9]. Therefore, SCR is the most suitable reforming process for GTL-FPSO. The SCR of methane reaction consists of three main reactions; SR, water-gas shift reaction, and DR (Eqs. (1)–(3)). Additional reactions are involved including CO2 methanation, CO methanation, methane decomposition, boudouard reaction, and coke gasification (Eqs. (4)–(8)). (1) CH 4 + H 2 O ↔ 3 H 2 + CO , ΔH 298 0 = 206 kJ / mol (2) CO + H 2 O ↔ CO 2 + H 2 , ΔH 298 0 = − 41 kJ / mol (3) CH 4 + CO 2 ↔ 2 H 2 + 2 CO , ΔH 298 0 = 247 kJ / mol (4) CO 2 + 4 H 2 ↔ CH 4 + 2 H 2 O , ΔH 298 0 = − 164.9 kJ / mol (5) CO + 3 H 2 ↔ CH 4 + H 2 O , ΔH 298 0 = − 206.1 kJ / mol (6) CH 4 + C ↔ 2 H 2 , ΔH 298 0 = 75 kJ / mol (7) 2 CO ↔ C + CO 2 , ΔH 298 0 = − 171 kJ / mol (8) C + H 2 O ↔ CO + H 2 , ΔH 298 0 = 131 kJ / mol . In general, the reforming reaction employs noble metals, such as Pt, Pd and Rh, and non-noble metals, such as Co, Ni and Fe [10–13]. The catalytic activity is strongly dependent on the reaction temperature, which is an important factor determining the reformer performance. On the other hand, the widely used pellet supports show low thermal dispersion because of its low thermal conductivity. Therefore, cold or hot spots are formed on the catalyst surface during reforming, particularly as the reformer scale is increased. Hot spots can damage the catalyst and cold spots can decrease the catalytic activity. Therefore, a uniform temperature distribution through the SCR reaction is very important for the SCR reactor design [14]. Recently, metallic foam catalysts with high thermal conductivity, uniform thermal dispersion, and high mechanical strength have been studied widely. The metallic foam is defined as a porous metal inside which many pores are formed. The metallic foam is classified into two types: open cell and closed cell [15,16]. The closed cell-type metallic foam has pores that are not interconnected, whereas the open cell-type metallic foam has pores that are interconnected. Therefore, a fluid can pass easily through the metallic foam. Consequently, the open cell-type metallic foam has been used widely in a range of industrial fields [17]. The cell size is indicated as pores per inch (PPI). Mostly, 5–100PPI foams have been used, of which the porosity is approximately 80–97% [18]. As a catalyst support, the metallic foam can improve the catalytic activity because of its high specific surface area. In addition, the metallic foam can improve heat and mass transfer, and can minimize the pressure drop because of its porous structure [19]. In particular, the metallic foam can remove the hot and cold spots on the catalysts in exothermic and endothermic reactions [20]. The metallic foam does not have high reactivity itself as a catalyst. Therefore, a porous catalyst should be coated on the surface of the metallic foam. The catalyst is difficult to coat directly on the metallic foam. As an alternative, the surface of metallic foam is pretreated by a wash-coating with a high porous material. Two methods are used widely for wash-coatings: sol–gel method and slurry method. The sol–gel method uses coating materials with organic agents, which are dissolved in distilled water. The pH of the aqueous solution is controlled by adding an acid. A sol is obtained after drying for a few hours at room temperature [21]. On the other hand, the slurry method uses a coating material with binder agents, which are dissolved in distilled water. A slurry in a liquid phase is obtained [22]. In both methods, the metallic foam can be wash-coated using either a sol or slurry. Using the sol–gel method, a uniform wash-coated layer on the surface of metallic foam can be obtained. On the other hand, the loading for one coating is so low that the wash-coating should be repeated to obtain a sufficient catalyst loading. In contrast, the slurry method can provide a high loading in a single coating but the wash-coated layer is not uniform and the metallic foam is coated layer by layer. In addition, the pores can be clogged with an accumulated lump and cracks can occur easily on the non-uniform and thick wash-coated layer. In the present study, a hybrid technique of sol–gel and slurry methods was used to obtain the optimal conditions for wash-coating the metallic foam [23]. Subsequently, the wash-coated metallic foam was activated by loading the Ni catalysts using the wet impregnation method. The metallic foam was wash-coated under a variety of conditions according to the composition of the wash-coating sol and the number of wash-coating repetitions. The uniformity and adhesion strength of the wash-coated layer were examined by a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and by measuring the fall-off rate after exposure to ultrasonic cleaner. The 2.27g of aluminum isopropoxide (AIP) and 0.5g of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), which were purchased from Alfa Aesar Co., were dissolved in distilled water heated to 65°C. The pH of the aqueous solution was adjusted to 2.0 by adding HNO3 (JUNSEI). A transparent sol was obtained after drying for 1.5h at room temperature. Subsequently, 3μm Al2O3 powder (Alfa Aesar. Co.) was added to the transparent sol to increase its viscosity and Al2O3 content. After aging for 1h, the Al2O3 hybrid sol for the wash-coating was prepared. In this step, the Al2O3 powder to AIP molar ratio (Al2O3/AIP) is an important factor that determines the coating quality in terms of uniformity and adhesion on the surface of the metallic foam. In the present study, the Al2O3/AIP molar ratio was changed from 1 to 9. Before wash-coating with the prepared sol, Ni foam was washed in an ultrasonic cleaner with ethanol for 1h. After a pretreatment in ethanol, the Ni foam was dried in a convection oven at 100°C for 1h. The Al2O3 sol was wash-coated on the pretreated Ni foam using a dip coating method for 7min. Subsequently, the redundant sol was blown off from the Ni foam to prevent pore blocking using an air-blower. The wash-coating process was repeated to obtain the desired weight fraction. The weight fraction of the Al2O3 layer to the Ni foam was adjusted to 20wt.%. The wash-coated Ni foam was dried in a convection oven at 100°C for 12h, and pre-calcined at 300°C (2.5°C/min) for 2h. Consequently, a γ-Al2O3/Ni foam was obtained. The prepared γ-Al2O3/Ni foam was coated with the Ni catalyst using a wet-impregnation method. The catalyst precursor was prepared by dissolving Ni(NO3)2 in distilled water (nitrate solution). The γ-Al2O3/Ni foam was immersed in a nitrate solution at 50°C for 4h. The weight fraction of the Ni catalyst to the γ-Al2O3 supported on the wash-coated Ni foam was 20wt.%. After impregnation, the catalyst sample was dried at 100°C for 12h and calcined in air at 800°C for 5h. Fig. 1 presents a photograph of the prepared metallic foam catalyst, Ni/γ-Al2O3/Ni foam. The wash-coated surface of the prepared catalysts was analyzed by a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Hitachi S-4800). The coating quality of the catalyst layers that had been coated using the wash-coating solution at different Al2O3/AIP molar ratios was evaluated. The adhesion strength of the wash-coat layers with the Ni foam was evaluated by measuring the fall-off rate [24]. Ultrasonic bath (HWASHIN Powersonic 510) was used to provide physical damage to the wash-coated Ni foam. The frequency and nominal power of the ultrasonic were 45kHz and 500W, respectively. The fall-off rate was defined as the weight loss after the fall-off test. Five samples of the wash-coated Ni foam prepared at different Al2O3/AIP ratios were tested and their weights were measured 10 and 50h after initiating the fall-off test using a high resolution balance (AND GF-600). The Nusselt number was used to evaluate the improvement in heat exchange between the wash-coated Ni foam and gaseous reactants. The Nusselt number is defined as Eq. (9) [25]. (9) Nu = h e D k c where he , kc and D are a convective heat transfer coefficient, thermal conductivity of the reactants and characteristic length, respectively. Fig. 2 shows the experimental setup for measuring the temperatures of the reactor to obtain the Nusselt number of radial heat transfer. A 1in. diameter tubular reactor made of the stainless steel (SUS316) was used, and thermocouples were located at the inlet (Ti ), middle (Tm ), outlet (To ) and wall (Tw ). The wash-coated Ni foam was packed into the reactor. The γ-Al2O3 pellets with the same volume were also packed for comparison. N2 was used as a feed gas and the flow rate was controlled to 100–500mL/min using a mass flow controller (MFC, NFS TSC-210). The wall temperature was fixed to 200°C in the present study [18]. In the configuration of the reactor packed with the wash-coated Ni foam, he was obtained by measuring the wall temperature (Tw ) and the centerline temperatures of the reactor inlet (Ti ) and outlet (To ). The heat is transferred from the wall to the center in the tubular reactor. The Nusselt number for the radial heat transfer was calculated by integrating the local heat transfer rate across the whole bed of the reactor as Eq. (10) [25,28]. (10) Nu = ρ C p r o 2 u k c L ln T w − T i T w − T o where ρ and Cp are a density and specific heat of the fluid in the reactor, respectively. r0 , L and u are the radius, length of the reactor and the flow rate, respectively. Tw , Ti and To are temperatures located at the wall, inlet and outlet, respectively [26–28]. Before examining the reactivity of the catalysts, the optimal feed composition for the SCR reaction was determined by using ASPEN Plus Simulation [29]. A Gibbs reactor was used to simulate the SCR reaction, and a heater and mixer were provided in the front of the reactor. The simulation was performed by varying the molar ratio of the feed gases (CH4:H2O:CO2) to obtain a H2/CO molar ratio optimized to the FT reaction. The H2O and CO2 compositions were varied in the range of 0.8–1.1 and 1.7–2.1 at a fixed CH4 composition (1.0mol), respectively. The reaction temperature was fixed to 800°C. Fig. 3 presents the experimental apparatus for the steam-CO2 reforming of methane on the Ni-Al2O3/Ni foam catalyst. A SUS316 tube, 1.27cm in diameter and 40cm in length, was used for the reaction. The prepared Ni-Al2O3/Ni foam catalyst was packed in the reactor and the catalyst weight was 0.5g. The reaction temperature and pressure were fixed to 800°C and 1bar for all reactions. All catalysts were reduced at 800°C for 2h in a H2 atmosphere with a flow rate of 50mL/min. The CH4 and CO2 were supplied by a MFC and H2O was supplied by a liquid piston pump. The CH4 and CO2 were mixed with H2O after vaporizing at 300°C. The space velocity was fixed to 10,000h−1. The dry product gases after separating the liquids in the products through a liquid trap were analyzed by GC (gas chromatography) equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a flame ionized detector (FID). The GC was calibrated with a standard gas to determine the concentration of CH4, CO2, CO, H2 and N2. The CH4 and CO2 conversions were calculated from the product composition under each reaction condition. The carbon balance was correct within 10% at all experiments. Table 1 lists the number of wash-coating repetitions and the weight fraction of the wash-coat according to the Al2O3/AIP molar ratio. The coating was repeated for all samples to be an equal weight fraction to ~20wt.%. When the Al2O3/AIP molar ratio was 1, the coating was repeated 13 times. On the other hand, only 2 coatings were sufficient to obtain the same weight fraction when the Al2O3/AIP molar ratio was 9. This is because the viscosity of the coating sol increased with increasing Al2O3/AIP molar ratio. Fig. 4 shows the weight fraction of the Al2O3 wash-coat layer to the Ni foam as a repetition of the wash-coating. The weight fraction of the Al2O3 wash-coat layer per wash-coating increased with increasing Al2O3/AIP molar ratio. When a high Al2O3/AIP molar ratio was used, the number of repetitions of the wash-coating can be reduced to obtain the desired weight fraction of the catalyst to the Ni foam. If 20wt.% Al2O3/Ni foam is required, e.g. 13 times, the wash-coating should be repeated with a Al2O3/AIP molar ratio of 1.0. The weight fraction of Al2O3 wash-coat increased in direct proportion to the number of wash-coatings. The average increase of the weight fraction at Al2O3/AIP ratio of 1.0 was 1.46wt.% per wash-coating. The Al2O3/AIP molar ratio should be increased by 5.0 to obtain a 20wt.% Al2O3/Ni foam within 7 wash-coatings. Fig. 5 presents SEM images showing the coating quality of the wash-coat layers when the coating was performed using the wash-coating sol at different Al2O3/AIP molar ratios. The as-prepared samples have no cracks on the surface and the aggregation of catalyst particles. Cracks formed after calcining at 800°C. The wash-coat peeled off from the Ni foam at an Al2O3/AIP molar ratio of 5 because the aggregated Al2O3 particles were coated thick on the surface of the Ni foam. The catalyst layer coated with an Al2O3/AIP molar ratio of 5 had no cracks after calcination. Table 2 lists the fall-off rate of the wash-coated Ni foams with different Al2O3/AIP molar ratios after 10 and 50h. During the fall-off test for 10h, the weight loss of the wash-coat layer was 0.96% in the case of a Al2O3/AIP ratio of 5. In other cases (Al2O3/AIP=1, 3, 7 and 9), the weight loss was greater than 1.0%. After 50h, the weight loss was the highest at an Al2O3/AIP ratio of 3, whereas the weight loss was less than 2.0% in others. The weight loss in cases of an Al2O3/AIP ratio of 7 and 9 occurred mostly within 10h. After 50h, however, the weight loss was relatively low because the weight losses were similar to the case of an Al2O3/AIP ratio of 5. That is because cracks formed on the wash-coating in cases of an Al2O3/AIP ratio of 7 and 9, and the cracked wash-coat layers fell off after 10h. After the cracked layers were removed from the Ni foam, the remaining wash-coat layer showed low weight loss after 50h. Considering the fall-off test and SEM images, the optimal Al2O3/AIP ratio was determined to be 5 because of the uniform surface without cracks and the lowest fall-off rate. Fig. 6 shows the Nusselt numbers of the reactor packed with γ-Al2O3 pellets and the wash-coated Ni foam, respectively. The Nusselt number of the wash-coated Ni foam was higher than that of the γ-Al2O3 pellets over the entire range of flow rates. The improvement in the heat exchange rate of the Ni foam, which means the difference in the Nusselt number between the Ni foam and pellets, increased with increasing flow rate. Fig. 7 shows the inlet and outlet temperatures as a function of the flow rate at the γ-Al2O3/Ni foam and γ-Al2O3 pellets. The temperature difference between the inlet and outlet became large through the reactor as the flow rate was increased. On the other hand, the temperature difference of the γ-Al2O3/Ni foam was much lower than that of the γ-Al2O3 pellets. Furthermore, the outlet temperature of the γ-Al2O3/Ni foam was maintained for gas feeding. This means that the wash-coated Ni foam has a more uniform temperature distribution along the reactor. The simulation of the SCR reaction was performed by using ASPEN Plus Simulation including the product molar ratio, CH4 and CO2 conversion, and H2/CO molar ratio at various feed compositions. The H2/CO molar ratio was adjusted to 2.0 when the CH4:H2O:CO2 composition was 1.0:1.9:1.0. A previous experimental study [23] reported that the H2/CO molar ratio decreased slightly because CO2 conversion increased more than the simulated value. The H2/CO molar ratio could be adjusted to 2.0 by increasing the H2O supply [30]. Therefore, the feed composition selected was CH4:H2O:CO2 =1.0:2.0:1.0 in the present study. At this feed composition, the conversions of CH4 and CO2 were 99.7% and 30.8%, respectively. Fig. 8 shows the CH4 and CO2 conversions of the bare Al2O3, bare Ni foam, Al2O3/Ni foam and Ni/Al2O3/Ni foam. First, there was no conversion in the empty reactor, which means that a homogeneous reaction did not occur. On the other hand, the bare Al2O3 and bare Ni foam showed reactivity even if the Ni catalyst was not loaded, and the bare Ni foam showed slightly higher CH4 conversion than the bare Al2O3. In general, the Ni foam plays the role of a structuring material for the catalysts to improve heat and mass transfer of the catalyst bed in the reactor. The Ni foam could also have reactivity (CH4 conv.=18.9%, CO2 conv.=10.2%) because Ni is active on the SCR reaction. After wash-coating the Al2O3 layer on the Ni foam, the CH4 and CO2 conversions increased by 69.1% and 34.0% compared to the bare Ni foam. These conversions were higher than those of the bare Al2O3, bare Ni foam and their combinations. This is because a small amount of Ni nitrate was contained in the wash-coating sol coated over the surface of the Ni foam [31]. Ni nitrate could be formed by nitric acid that was added to adjust the pH of the wash-coating sol. After the Ni catalyst was coated, the Ni/Al2O3/Ni foam showed high reactivity: the CH4 and CO2 conversions were 99.7% and 44.0%, respectively. Fig. 9 shows the syngas flow rate and H2/CO molar ratio of the bare Al2O3 bead, bare Ni foam, Al2O3/Ni foam and Ni/Al2O3/Ni foam. The syngas flow rate showed a similar tendency to the CH4 and CO2 conversions. On the other hand, the H2/CO molar ratio had a different result. The H2/CO molar ratio of the bare Al2O3 bead and bare Ni foam was less than 2.0. The Ni/Al2O3/Ni foam only had a H2/CO molar ratio of ~2.0. Catalyst supported on metallic foam that was developed to improve the performance of the methane SCR reactor for GTL-FPSO, because the metallic foam catalyst has good thermal properties compared to the conventional pellet catalysts. First, metallic foam catalysts were prepared under a range of wash-coating conditions, such as the composition of wash-coating sol (Al2O3/AIP molar ratio). The prepared metallic foam catalyst was evaluated by SEM, fall-off test and SCR reaction. In addition, the radial heat transfer was characterized in terms of the Nusselt number and was compared with the pellet catalyst to verify the improvement in heat transfer of the metallic foam catalyst. The optimal composition of the wash-coating sol was determined. When the Al2O3/AIP molar ratio was 5, surface cracks were not observed and the weight fraction of the Al2O3 wash-coat layer on the Ni foam was 20wt.% when wash-coated 5 times. The fall-off test revealed the highest adhesion at an Al2O3/AIP molar ratio of 5. As a result of the heat transfer characteristics, the radial heat transfer of the metallic foam catalyst was improved. The Nusselt number of the metallic foam catalyst was higher than that of the conventional pellet catalyst, and the difference between both catalysts increased with increasing space velocity. Consequently, the metallic foam catalyst can maintain a uniform temperature distribution through the reactor with a high space velocity (either an increased gas feed rate or decreased reactor size). Therefore, the metallic foam catalyst is more suitable for GTL-FPSO applications in terms of the reformer compactness and the mechanical strength of the catalyst.
abstract: A metallic foam catalyst was used to improve the heat transfer of a reactor for the steam-CO2 reforming (SCR) of methane in the GTL-FPSO (gas to liquid-floating production storage and offloading) process. The metallic foam catalyst was prepared and examined by scanning electron microscopy, fall-off test and SCR reaction. The radial heat transfer was characterized in terms of the Nusselt number and was compared with the pellet catalyst to confirm the improvement in the heat transfer of the metallic foam catalyst. As a result of the fall-off test, the adhesion strength was highest at an Al2O3/AIP molar ratio of 5. The Nusselt number of the metallic foam catalyst was higher than that of the conventional pellet catalyst. Therefore, the reformer had a uniform temperature distribution along the catalyst-bed when the metallic foam catalyst was used.
title: Preparation and evaluation of a metallic foam catalyst for steam-CO2 reforming of methane in GTL-FPSO process
