full_text: It is costly to separate CO2 and atmospheric N2 from the flue gas of conventional air combustion due to the high energy penalty of gas–gas separations. In general, the gas–solid separations are much easier than the gas–gas separations. If the separation of N2 and CO2 can be substituted by gas–solid separations, the cost can be potentially reduced. Chemical looping combustion (CLC) utilizes two gas–solid separations to replace the CO2–N2 separation of the conventional air combustion, as shown in Fig. 1 [1]. Two essential reactors are involved in a CLC system: an air reactor and a fuel reactor. The oxygen carrier is circulated between these two reactors during operation. The first gas–solid separation aims to separate N2 and the oxidized oxygen carrier from the air reactor, where the gaseous O2 in air is “stored” into the oxygen carrier in the form of lattice oxygen. The second gas–solid separation is designed for CO2 and the reduced oxygen carrier from the fuel reactor, within which the lattice oxygen transfers from oxygen carrier to fuel to produce CO2. With this arrangement, fuel can be burned by air while the CO2 and atmospheric N2 are inherently separated. Recently, CLC of solid fuels has gained an increasing interest [2–6]. In terms of CLC of coal with an iron-based oxygen carrier, three approaches are available: coal direct CLC, syngas-CLC, and in-situ gasification CLC (iG-CLC) [5,7]. An advantage of coal direct CLC is the relatively high oxygen transport capacity (OTC) as the Fe2O3 can be reduced to FeO/Fe [5]. However, a disadvantage is the low reaction rate, especially when the Fe2O3 is deeply reduced [8]. To overcome this disadvantage, syngas can be used to reduce the oxygen carriers, with either ex-situ or in-situ gasification approach. With syngas-CLC, significant extra energy input is needed for the air separation unit (ASU) [9]. To avoid the requirement of ASU, iG-CLC was proposed, where the fuel particles are in-situ gasified by CO2 and/or H2O in the presence of oxygen carriers [7,10]. Compared to coal direct CLC, the reduction rate can be improved by iG-CLC, but the OTC is decreased because the reduction of Fe2O3 is thermodynamically limited to the form of Fe3O4 [11,12]. The solid inventory of a CLC process is directly affected by the reaction rate and the OTC of the oxygen carrier. For the reactivity enhancement of iG-CLC and coal direct CLC, alkali metal catalysts are usually used. Gu et al. found that the reduction rate of iron ore oxygen carrier in iG-CLC could be improved by K2CO3 loading, and suggested that this improvement was mainly caused by the accelerated char gasification and water–gas shift reaction [13]. The solid inventory of a CLC process is expected to be significantly decreased if the high reaction rate and high OTC of oxygen carrier are simultaneously satisfied. Our previous research indicated that the reduction rate of iron oxide oxygen carrier of coal direct CLC could be remarkably enhanced by the addition of alkali metal carbonates (high reactivity); meanwhile, the Fe2O3 can be reduced to an oxidation state of FeO/Fe (high OTC), suggesting that catalytic CLC was an effective method of coal combustion with a low solid inventory [14]. Even though there are some researches focusing on the effects of alkali metal on iron-based oxygen carrier, the catalytic mechanism has not been addressed in more detail, especially in the following aspects: (1) how the alkali metal initially loaded on oxygen carrier catalyzes the char gasification; (2) the catalytic effect of reduced iron oxide itself on char gasification in the presence of alkali metal addition; and (3) the maintained catalytic effect in the multicycle operation. Hence, this work aims to investigate these aspects of the catalytic CLC of activated carbon (AC) with an iron-based oxygen carrier modified by K2CO3. The oxygen carrier used in this work consists of 70wt.% Fe2O3 and 30wt.% ZrO2 (denoted as Fe70Zr30), which was synthesized by a co-precipitation method at room temperature using the following procedures: First, 85.14g of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (98.5%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China) and 25.00g of Zr(NO3)4·5H2O (99.0%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China) were mixed in the de-ionized water. Then, this solution and NH3·H2O were added to 100mL of de-ionized water dropwise under vigorous stirring. The pH during precipitation was kept at a value of 9. After precipitation, the product was filtered and the precipitate was washed using the de-ionized water and then air dried overnight at 110°C. Finally, the precipitate was calcined at 1000°C for 8h in muffle furnace. The obtained oxygen carrier was crushed and sieved to a particle size below 180μm. The K2CO3 modified Fe70Zr30 oxygen carrier was prepared by impregnation as follows: a desired amount of K2CO3 (99.0%, Tianda Chemical Reagent Factory, China) was dissolved in de-ionized water and then this solution was slowly added to Fe70Zr30 particles; after ultrasonic treatment for 60min, the slurry was air dried at 110°C for 12h. The loading ratios of K2CO3 were varied from 0, 2, and 6wt.% in terms of K2CO3/Fe2O3. The K2CO3 loaded Fe70Zr30 was referred to Kx-Fe70Zr30, where x represents the loading amount of K2CO3. The oxygen carrier was physically mixed with AC (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China) with a mass ratio of 9/1 (Fe70Zr30/AC). The proximate and ultimate analyses of AC are listed in Table 1 . The reactivity of oxygen carrier was investigated by a Setaram Setsys TGA instrument. About 25mg of the mixture was heated in a platinum crucible under N2 atmosphere (100mL/min) from 30°C to 850°C with a rate of 10°C/min. Then, the mixture was held at 850°C for 40min before the carrier gas was switched to air (100mL/min) for a 10min oxidation. The multicycle CLC tests were simulated in a fixed-bed reactor (as shown in Fig. 2 ). The experimental procedures are briefly described as follows: About 1.2g of mixture was loaded into a cylindrical alumina crucible (35mm i.d.×10mm height). Then, the crucible was placed into the cooler chamber of the reactor. After flushing the reactor with a continuous stream of N2 for 60min, the crucible was lowered quickly into the reaction zone for 40min under N2 atmosphere. Then, the carrier gas was switched to air for a 10min oxidation. Finally, the crucible was lifted rapidly to the cooler chamber where the crucible was quenched under a N2 stream. The reaction temperature was set to 850°C and all the flow rates of the purge gases were kept at 200mL/min. After the crucible was cooled, the residue was taken off and mixed with another dose of AC to prepare the next redox cycle. Meanwhile, about 25mg of the newly prepared mixture was transferred to the TGA to assess the redox behavior of oxygen carrier in every two cycles. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the oxygen carriers were collected on a RIGAKU D/max-rB X-ray powder diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (40kV, 100mA, Kα1=0.154 08nm). A JEOL field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, JSM-7001F) coupled with an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS, BRUKER) was used to characterize the surface morphology and the elemental mapping of the oxygen carriers. The element contents of K were analyzed by an inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometer (ICP-AES, iCAP 6300, Thermo Fisher). Before the mechanism investigation, the verification tests of reduction rate enhancement by K2CO3 addition were conducted by TGA. Fig. 3 compares the reduction curves of K0-Fe70Zr30, K2-Fe70Zr30, and K6-Fe70Zr30 by AC. Without K2CO3 (K0-Fe70Zr30), the weight loss is only 5.33wt.% in a long residence time of 800min, while with 2wt.% (K2-Fe70Zr30) and 6wt.% (K6-Fe70Zr30) K2CO3 addition, the weight loss can be reached at 26.38wt.% and 28.24wt.%, respectively, even though the reduction time is much shorter (123min). From this figure, it can be deduced that the reduction of Fe70Zr30 oxygen carrier by AC was significantly improved by K2CO3 addition. It is generally accepted that the reduction of iron oxide with carbon mainly occurs via the indirect reductions by gaseous intermediates (CO and CO2), which proceeds through a chain reaction as follows [15–17]: (1) CO+Fe x O y →CO2 +Fe x O y −1 (2) CO2 +C→2CO where reaction (2) is the rate-limiting step. According to this mechanism, the significant enhancement of the reaction rate (as described in Fig. 3) should be mainly ascribed to the improvements on the C–CO2 reaction step. As for the reduction of potassium decorated iron-based oxygen carrier with AC, the rate-limiting step (C–CO2 reaction) could be catalyzed by either K2CO3 or iron oxide itself. It is interesting to know how the K2CO3 initially loaded on oxygen carrier, not on carbon, effectively catalyzes the C–CO2 gasification step. To study this phenomenon, SEM–EDS mapping of the mixtures was conducted, as shown in Fig. 4 . Before reduction, the K element (green dots) was mainly on the oxygen carrier (Fig. 4a), which implies that the mechanical mixing process did not cause remarkable migration of potassium, while after 90s of reduction, significant K enrichment can be found on the surface of carbon (Fig. 4b), indicating that the active species had migrated from the oxygen carrier to the surface of carbon. This observation is similar to the conclusion of Keller et al., who found the transfer of potassium from the Brazilian manganese ore to solid fuel [18]. Hence, it is reasonable to infer the effective occurrence of catalytic C–CO2 gasification by potassium after migration. Some potassium remained on the surface of the oxygen carrier (Fig. 4b). According to Bao et al., the residual potassium could also improve the reaction rate of iron oxide with CO (reaction (1)) [19]. However, this improvement should not be the main reason of the significant enhancement as depicted in Fig. 3, because the reaction of iron oxide with CO (reaction (1)) is not the rate-limiting step. In addition to the reduction curves in Fig. 3, the reduction rates were also analyzed, as shown in Fig. 5 . The profile of reduction rate of K0-Fe70Zr30 presents a downward trend without the peak being identified even with a long residence time in 850°C (Fig. 5a). This downward trend of reduction rate accords with the view that the reduction rate is very slow when Fe2O3 is deeply reduced to Fe [8]. However, it can be inferred that this trend will be changed when K2CO3 is added because there are reduction peaks for K2CO3 doped oxygen carriers. As to K2-Fe70Zr30 and K6-Fe70Zr30, the reduction peaks appear in the isothermal zone (Fig. 5b) and ramp zone (Fig. 5c), respectively. Furthermore, the reduction residue which corresponded to the reduction peak of K2-Fe70Zr30 was also analyzed by XRD. As shown in Fig. 6 , FeO and Fe (the deeply reduced products) were observed, which validates that the deep reduction of K2-Fe70Zr30 proceeded with a high reaction rate. The reduction peak of K2-Fe70Zr30 in isothermal phase also suggests that the autocatalytic reduction occurred. The justifications are expanded in the following paragraph. Temperature, reactant concentration, and catalyst amount are the main factors that affect the reaction rate. In terms of the reduction peak of K2-Fe70Zr30 illustrated in Fig. 5b, the temperature and potassium content can be considered to be constant, and the reactant concentration decreases as the reduction proceeds. These three factors could not contribute to the increase of reduction rate. However, the reduction product content increases as the reduction proceeds. The iron oxide is not only the reduction product of oxygen carrier, but also the catalyst of the rate-limiting step (C–CO2 reaction). Furthermore, the catalytic effect of iron oxides on C–CO2 reaction increases approximately in the order of Fe2O3, Fe3O4, FeO, Fe [20]. Because autocatalysis means catalysis of reaction by one of its products, the increase of reduction rate of K2-Fe70Zr30 in isothermal phase can be ascribed to the increasing catalytic effect of reduction products, while the decrease of reduction rate can be attributed to reactant concentration decreasing when the reactant concentration becomes the controlling factor. The reduction peak of K6-Fe70Zr30 in Fig. 5c could not exclude the influence of temperature, because it is possible that the rate increases with the temperature rising firstly in the ramp zone and then decreases with reactant concentration decreasing. If the autocatalytic effect exists, the reduction peak of K6-Fe70Zr30 should be also found in an isothermal phase. To confirm this speculation, the isothermal temperature was reduced. After lowering the temperature in isothermal phase of operation to 780°C, the reduction peak of K6-Fe70Zr30 can be also found in the isothermal zone (Fig. 5d), which verifies the speculation of autocatalysis by iron oxide itself. In this work, the autocatalysis was only observed for the potassium modified iron-based oxygen carrier. It seems that the potassium has a promoting effect on the catalysis of iron oxide itself. However, it needs further investigations. One of the important characteristics of an oxygen carrier is the stability under repeated redox cycles at high temperatures [8]. As for catalytic CLC of AC with an iron-based oxygen carrier modified by potassium, it is essential to understand whether the catalytic effect could be observed in the subsequent redox cycles. Gu et al. have conducted the cycle experiments with the K2CO3-decorated iron ore, and found a relatively stable catalytic effect in the CLC process [13]. However, these results cannot be simply applied to our experiments because of the differences of reaction conditions. One of the significant differences is the reduction degree of Fe2O3. In the cycle experiments of Gu et al., the reduction of Fe2O3 was thermodynamically limited to Fe3O4 [13], while the Fe2O3 was reduced to FeO/Fe in this study. The catalysis of FeO/Fe on carbon gasification is much higher than that of Fe3O4. Fig. 7 demonstrates the activity change of K6-Fe70Zr30 oxygen carrier during a 20-cycle CLC test. From Fig. 7a, it can be seen that there is no significant difference in the activity of the oxygen carrier before the 6th redox cycle. This is different from our previous conclusion, where a decreasing activity was observed for K2CO3 added Fe2O3 with coal char due to the ash accumulation [11]. To explore why the catalytic effect was maintained, SEM–EDS mapping of the oxygen carrier before and after air oxidation was performed, which is shown in Fig. 8a and b, respectively. As shown in Fig. 8, no AC particles were found, and the enrichments of K element on the surface of oxygen carriers were observed, suggesting that the active species of potassium traveled back to the surface of oxygen carriers from carbon particles. In the next redox cycle, the potassium on the oxygen carrier could migrate again to the surface of carbon. Therefore, the migration of potassium species between the oxygen carrier and carbon might be the main reason of the persisting catalytic effect observed in multicycle experiments. Fig. 7a also shows a remarkable decrease of activity in the 8th cycle. Table 2 lists the K contents of the fresh K6-Fe70Zr30 oxygen carrier and the residue after the 9th redox cycle. K content loss was found after the 9th redox cycles, which indicated that potassium loss might be a primary factor of the activity decrease. To verify this, 6 wt.% K2CO3 was added to the oxygen carrier after the 9th cycle to see if the subsequent reactivity can be increased. From Fig. 7b, it is evident that the reactivity was improved again, which confirms the surmise on potassium loss. Fig. 7b also suggests that the makeup of K2CO3 may be an effective way to maintain the durable catalytic activity for catalytic CLC of solid fuels. This work investigated the catalytic mechanism of reduction and multicycle tests for CLC of AC with an iron-based oxygen carrier modified by K2CO3. The main conclusions of this paper can be summarized as follows: (1). The potassium initially loaded on oxygen carrier can migrate to the surface of AC to catalyze the C–CO2 reaction (rate-limiting step) during reduction. (2). The reduced iron oxide shows significant autocatalytic effect on reduction in the presence of K2CO3 addition. (3). The potassium could travel back to the oxygen carrier after reduction, which might be the main reason of the maintained catalysis during multicycle operation. (4). The potassium loss is a primary factor of the reactivity decrease, and the makeup of K2CO3 appears to be an effective way to maintain high reactivity for catalytic CLC of solid fuels. The potassium initially loaded on oxygen carrier can migrate to the surface of AC to catalyze the C–CO2 reaction (rate-limiting step) during reduction. The reduced iron oxide shows significant autocatalytic effect on reduction in the presence of K2CO3 addition. The potassium could travel back to the oxygen carrier after reduction, which might be the main reason of the maintained catalysis during multicycle operation. The potassium loss is a primary factor of the reactivity decrease, and the makeup of K2CO3 appears to be an effective way to maintain high reactivity for catalytic CLC of solid fuels. This work was financially supported by Shanxi Natural Science Foundation Committee (no. 2012021005-4) and Strategic Priority Research Program of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (no. XDA07050100). Thanks go to Professor Yang Wang for his helpful discussion and Pengfei Liu for his valuable editing to make this a better paper.
abstract: One of the major obstacles of chemical looping combustion (CLC) with solid fuels is the low reaction rate of solid fuels with an oxygen carrier. Alkali metal catalysts can be used to enhance the reaction rate. In this work, the catalytic reduction mechanism of a K2CO3 modified iron-based oxygen carrier by activated carbon (AC) and the multicycle CLC tests were investigated. The results confirm that the deep reduction of K2CO3 added iron-based oxygen carrier occurred at a high rate. The energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) analysis shows that the catalytically active species of K2CO3 migrated from the oxygen carrier to the surface of AC during reduction, which could then catalyze the rate-limiting step (C–CO2 reaction) of the reduction process. Besides the catalytic effect of K2CO3, a remarkable autocatalytic effect of iron oxide itself on the reduction was also observed. The active species could also travel back to the oxygen carrier after reduction, which guaranteed the persisting catalytic effect in the multicycle tests. The loss of potassium primarily contributed to the deactivation of the oxygen carrier, but this deactivation can be partly retrieved and improved by the supplement of K2CO3.
title: Catalytic chemical looping combustion of carbon with an iron-based oxygen carrier modified by K2CO3: Catalytic mechanism and multicycle tests

full_text: Mercury (Hg) in flue gas discharged from coal-fired boilers has been recognized as a major concern because of its volatility, persistence, and bioaccumulation [1]. The US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) recently issued the first national standard for mercury pollution from power plant [2]. Accordingly the emission of mercury together with other toxic air pollutant like arsenic, acid gas, nickel, selenium, and cyanide from coal-fired power plant must be in compliance with the new standard. Mercury in coal combustion flue gas is often classified into three chemical forms: elemental mercury (Hg0), oxidized mercury (Hg2+), and particle-associated mercury (HgP). Among them, Hg0 is insoluble in water and thus is most difficult to be captured in conventional pollutant control equipments such as a wet-type desulfurization unit (FGD) [3]. Catalytic mercury oxidation is under consideration as a useful and cost-effective option to reduce Hg emission. Over the V2O5 based SCR catalyst, Hg0 has been found to be oxidized to Hg2+ by a small amount of hydrochloric acid [4–6]. By incorporation of the SCR catalyst into mercury reduction strategies, Hg0 can be transformed to Hg2+ and easily captured by wet-type FGD. Because Hg0 oxidation over the V2O5 based SCR catalyst is greatly promoted by the presence of hydrochloric acid, the reaction is considered to proceed by the following equation: (1) Hg0 +2HCl+1/2 O2 →HgCl2 +H2O Recent studies have revealed that Hg0 oxidation may proceed through the Eley–Rideal type mechanism [7–9]. In this mechanism, Hg0 reacts with chlorinated surface active species from gas phase or as a weakly adsorbed species. Another mechanism in which both Hg0 and chlorine react as the adsorbed species on the catalyst active sites has been also proposed [10]. In both cases, the adsorption of chlorine is interfered with the presence of ammonia which is a necessary reductant for the NO reduction. The reduction of NO with NH3 proceeds according to the following equation: (2) NO+NH3 +1/4 O2 →N2 +3/2 H2O When NH3 is present in the gas stream, HCl competes with NH3 for surface active sites but the adsorption of NH3 always dominates over the HCl adsorption in conventional reaction conditions, consequently the Hg0 oxidation efficiency is reduced. To achieve higher Hg0 oxidation efficiency, an appropriate control of NH3 injection is important. Another important issue on the catalytic Hg0 oxidation is a catalyst life time in the coal combustion flue gas. Although it is one of the key factors in practice, only limited information on the aging process of the V2O5 based honeycomb catalyst as Hg0 oxidation catalyst has been available so far. The SCR catalysts are normally placed upstream of electrostatic precipitator (ESP) and are exposed to high concentrations of fly ash derived from coal combustion. Because fly ash is composed of several inorganic materials including alkali, alkaline earth metals, Si, Al and sulfur containing compounds, the ash deposition on the catalyst surface deactivates the catalyst activity [8,11–13]. The purpose of this study was to examine how the SCR catalyst which can be served as a mercury oxidation catalyst as well deactivates in the actual coal combustion flue gas where abundant fly ash exists. The catalyst, before and after a service, was evaluated for both Hg0 oxidation and NO reduction. The characterization was carried out with the assistance of a focused ion beam (FIB) fabrication and field emission-type SEM (FE-SEM) observation to clarify the ash accumulation process on the catalyst under an industrial condition. Using FIB technique, the cross-section of the honeycomb wall covered with the accumulated fly ash can be revealed with the fine and fragile structure of the accumulated ash layer being preserved for a successive FE-SEM observation. The commercial V2O5–WO3/TiO2 SCR catalyst with a honeycomb structure used in this study was manufactured over a nearly decade ago by then-current state-of-the-art technology and has been in service for about 10years at a coal-fired power plant located in Japan. Coal burnt in the plat was bituminous type and mostly produced in Australia and Indonesia. The content of poisonous materials such as alkaline, alkaline earth metals and phosphorous in coal was relatively low. The catalyst was designed to have a high NO x removal efficiency with depressed SO2 oxidation activity and to be durable both catalytically and mechanically. The length of the cell opening of the honeycomb structure is about 6.0mm and the wall thickness is about 1.4mm. Honeycomb catalysts which have been exposed to the coal combustion flue gas for about 65,000h were taken from an SCR rector to investigate the change in catalytic activity, physicochemical properties, and fly ash accumulation on their surface. Because fly ash deposition is most severe at the front-end of the honeycomb catalyst and is mitigated along with gas flow direction, the monolithic samples A and B were cut from the frond-end and the back-end of the honeycomb catalyst (1m length) respectively to see the effect of the amount of fly ash deposition on the catalytic activity (see Fig. 1 ). FE-SEM (Hitachi High-Tech., S-500) observation was performed on the monolithic samples A and B. To observe the structure of accumulated layer of fly ash on the surface, the FIB fabrication (Hitachi High-Tech., FB-2000A) was performed with Ga ion source prior to the SEM observation. Before the fabrication by Ga ion beam, the sample surface was pretreated by a wolfram deposition. EDX analysis was also performed on the cross-section of the honeycomb wall using an EDAX genesis. BET analysis was performed on a piece of honeycomb wall by nitrogen adsorption at 77K (Quantachrome instruments, Autosorb 1-MP), after degassing at 373K for 10h. Pore size distribution of the catalyst was measured by a mercury porosimeter (Quantachrome instruments, Autoscan-33). The monolithic sample consisted of one channel of honeycomb with a length of about 10.7cm. Measurements of the Hg0 and NO conversions were carried out under steady-state conditions in a continuous gas flow reactor at a total flow rate (STP) of 1524cc/min, atmospheric pressure, and a reaction temperature of 623K. High space velocity (15,000h−1) was employed to see a change of catalytic activity clearly. Reactant concentrations were 30ppm HCl, 200ppm NO, 200ppm SO2, 2% O2, and 10% H2O, with the balance being N2. Hg0 concentration (STP) in the reactant stream was set at approximately 22μg/m3 throughout the experiments. The concentration of NH3 was set to 160ppm corresponding to a NH3/NO molar ratio 0.8. Detailed experimental setup is shown in the literature [8]. The mercury concentration in the reactant and product streams was carefully monitored by a mercury analyzer (Nippon Instruments, MS-1A and DM-6B), which allowed a continuous measurement of both Hg0 and Hg2+ concentrations. The concentration of mercury in the effluent was determined by cold vapor atomic absorption spectroscopy. Appropriate materials such as Teflon and quartz ware chosen for the gas sampling line, and, to ensure accurate analysis, the temperature was maintained above 423K to avoid adsorption of mercury onto the equipment. Hg0 oxidation rate was calculated based on the Hg0 concentrations at the inlet and the outlet of the catalyst: (3) Hg0 conversion=[C Hg 0 ,in − C Hg 0 ,out]/C Hg 0 ,in ×100 where C Hg 0 ,in and C Hg 0 ,out are the Hg0 concentration of the inlet and outlet streams, respectively. The NO concentration in the reactant and product streams was determined by chemiluminescence (Horiba, PG-200). The NO conversion was determined by (4) NO conversion=[C NO,in − C NO,out]/CNO,in ×100 where C NO,in and C NO,out are the NO concentrations in the inlet and outlet streams, respectively. Fig. 2 shows typical SEM pictures on the surface of both (a) fresh and (b)–(c) spent samples taken from the front-end and back-end of the honeycomb catalyst, sample A and B, respectively. On the fresh catalyst, submicrometer particles which may originate from raw material of the catalyst such as TiO2 powder were observed. Micro-cracks with less than 1μm in width and a few μm in length were often observed on the surface. These micro-cracks may be formed during a drying and a calcination process while manufacturing. On the aged catalyst, numerous numbers of fine particles with different sizes ranging from several 10nm to several 100nm in diameter were observed. Since typical coal combustion fly ash has a wide particle size distribution from submicrometer to more than 10μm in diameter [3], majority of fly ash is considered to be carried away from the SCR reactor by a flue gas stream, leaving their smallest fraction on the catalyst surface. Micro-cracks were seldom observed on the aged catalysts. They may be covered by abundant fly ash particles. Fig. 3 shows a typical example of the FIB fabricated cross-section of the spent catalyst (sample A). By scanning the surface repetitively by the focused ion beam, the fragile fly ash deposition layer was successively revealed for the observation. On top of the catalyst surface, the ash deposition layer with relatively uniform thickness was observed. Thickness of the fly ash deposition layer was observed to vary by its position of the honeycomb catalyst. The ash deposition layer was the thickest at the front-end of the catalyst and decreased in thickness along with the gas flow direction. Average thickness estimated by SEM picture was 3.1μm for sample A and 0.95μm for sample B. Fig. 4 shows the results of the EDX analysis at selected positions of both ash deposition layer and the catalyst (shown in Fig. 3). Main constituent of the catalyst was confirmed to be Ti and Si. Other metals such as vanadium and wolfram could not be observed clearly because of their lower contents. The fly ash deposition layer was observed to consist mainly of Si, Ti, Ca, Al, and sulfur which were typical constituent elements of a coal ash. Table 1 shows the BET surface area (S BET) and the pore volume (V p) of the samples. S BET was observed to decrease from 76m2/g of the fresh catalyst to about 50m2/g of the spent one. S BET of sample A and B was essentially coincident. Because the thickness of the ash deposition layer was several micrometer, which is about 1/1000 times thinner than the wall thickness of the honeycomb structure, the decrease of S BET could not be due to the ash deposition. The reduction of S BET may be caused by a sintering of the TiO2 particles which is thermally induced during the operation. Fig. 5 shows an example of the results of Hg0 oxidation experiments over the fresh catalyst at 623K. At the inlet of the sample, about 2μg/m3 of Hg2+ which was less than 10% of total mercury concentration was confirmed to exist. Since similar values were observed for both the fresh and the spent catalysts, this small fraction of Hg2+ may stem from the Hg0 oxidation reaction in the preheater zone upstream of the reactor. Once the Hg speciation measurements at the outlet of the catalyst started, both Hg0 and Hg2+ concentrations gradually reached the steady-state values. In the absence of NH3 in gas phase, Hg2+ concentration at the catalyst outlet was about 12μg/m3; this means about 56% of the inlet Hg0 was converted to Hg2+. When NH3 was added to the reactant (NH3/NO ratio 0.8), the concentration of Hg0 increased and accordingly Hg2+ decreased with maintaining the sum of Hg and Hg2+ concentrations nearly constant. The steady-state value of the sum of Hg0 and Hg2+ concentration was about 22μg/m3, which was consistent with the one observed at the catalyst inlet. This suggests that the Hg speciation was accurately measured in this study by avoiding such as the adsorption of mercury on the sampling materials. As for the NO reduction, the concentration of NO was observed to decrease from 170ppm to 74ppm for the fresh catalyst when NH3 was added to the reactant (not shown in figure). Fig. 6 shows the Hg0 and NO conversions over the fresh and the spent catalysts, sample A and B, at 623K. Compared to the fresh catalyst, both NO and Hg conversion for the spent catalysts were observed to decrease. The decrease was more apparent for the sample A compared to the sample B, suggesting that the front-end of the honeycomb had suffered severely from the deactivation. The deactivation mechanism strongly depends on the type of fuel, consequently on flue gas properties, and on the operation conditions. Compared to the deactivation rate observed in this study, for example, a deactivation caused by alkali sulfate and/or phosphate seems to proceed at much higher deactivation rates [12,13]. A rate of 8% loss in activity over 1000h was observed in flue gas derived from co-firing of biomass and coal [12]. Another example, that is an average deactivation rate of 18% over 1000h for the catalyst installed in a slipstream reactor in a utility boiler co-firing wood waste and Powder River Basin (PRB), was reported [13]. This extremely high deactivation rate was reported to be caused by the pore blocking by combined alkali and calcium sulfate deposition and their growth in the pores [13]. The average decreasing rate of the NO and Hg0 conversion was calculated to be 0.8% and 1.1% for sample A and 0.1% and 0.3% for sample B over 1000h, respectively. The deactivation process of this catalyst is much slower than those reported in the literatures [12,13]. Since the coal burnt in this study was bituminous type and potential poisonous materials such as alkaline, alkaline earth metals and phosphorous were much lower than those observed for PRB and biomass containing fuels. As a result, the alkaline concentration in the ash deposited on the surface was very low and the plugging was not observed. The deactivation of this catalyst is considered to be induced by the ash deposition layer which composed of mainly inert silica and alumina. This deactivation mode is different from the rapid deactivation by the poisoning and the plugging. The Hg0 oxidation reaction over the V2O5 based catalyst is considered to proceed through the Eley–Rideal mechanism in which the surface active chlorinated species reacts with gaseous Hg0 [7–9]. In this study, we assumed the first order kinetics according to Hg0 in order to calculate the rate constant of the Hg0 oxidation reaction, k Hg 0, as a first assumption. Similarly a first order kinetics was also assumed for the NO reduction reaction which is considered to proceed through the reaction between adsorbed NH3 species and gaseous NO to calculate the rate constant for NO reduction, k NO. Fig. 7 shows the calculated k Hg 0 and k NO as a function of the thickness of the ash deposition layer which is calculated from the observed cross-sectional SEM pictures. It can be seen that both k Hg 0 and k NO decrease proportionally with the thickness of the deposition layer. Interestingly the thickness of the deposition layer at which the rate constants, k NO and k Hg 0, become nearly zero is nearly same, about 4μm thickness. This result clearly shows that the gas diffusion through the porous ash deposition layer is the rate limiting step for both the Hg0 oxidation and the NO reduction for the spent catalyst. For monolithic honeycomb catalyst, the rate of the overall process involves two steps in series: (i) transport process of the reactants such as Hg0, HCl, NO, and NH3 between the bulk flow and the surface of the catalyst walls of the channel and (ii) the simultaneous diffusion and reaction inside the porous wall [14]. As the aging of the catalyst proceeds, the additional transport process of the reactants in the porous wall becomes significant. At the beginning of the service, fine fly ash particles whose particle size is several 10nm may be stuck to the active sites of the outer surface of the catalyst. This may partly block the progress in the reaction on the active sites. As the decay of the catalyst proceeds, the fly ash particles continue accumulating on the outer surface. Consequently the porous deposition layer consisted of the fly ash is built up as we observed in Fig. 3 and this porous layer hinders the gaseous reactants access to the surface active sites. The gas diffusion though the porous deposition layer is a prevailing rate limiting step which is a main factor of the deactivation. The decay mechanism of the V2O5/TiO2 honeycomb catalyst in the coal combustion flue gas was studied. Fly ash particles were observed to deposit and form a uniform ash deposition layer on the catalyst surface. The ash deposition layer was porous and composed of mainly fine silica and alumina particles. The catalytic activity, both Hg0 oxidation and NO reduction, was found to decrease proportionally with the ash deposition thickness. The diffusion of the reactant through the porous ash deposition layer is the prevailing rate limiting step which is the main factor of the deactivation.
abstract: Decay mechanism of the V2O5/TiO2 commercial catalyst in the coal combustion flue gas was studied. Both fresh and spent catalysts with honeycomb structure, which were exposed to a coal combustion flue gas in a coal-fired boiler for over 65,000h, were examined. The catalysts were characterized by BET analysis, FE-SEM observation with EDX analysis, and catalytic activity of Hg0 oxidation and NO reduction was measured. Fly ash particles were observed to deposit on the spent catalyst and form the layer-like porous structure. The thickness of the ash deposition layer was 3.1μm at the front-end of the catalyst and observed to have decreased to 0.9μm at the back-end. The activity loss was observed to be more pronounced at the front-end of the catalyst where the most severe ash accumulation was confirmed than at the back-end of the catalyst for both Hg0 oxidation and NO reduction. The catalytic activity, both Hg0 oxidation and NO reduction, was found to decrease proportionally with the ash deposition thickness. The diffusion of the reactant through the porous ash deposition layer is the prevailing rate limiting step which is the main factor of the deactivation.

full_text: As a renewable, green, and carbon-neutral resource, biomass is considered as the alternative fuels with reduced environmental pollution arise from fossil fuels. Pyrolysis is recognized as one of the most prevailing and promising routes for utilization of biomass resources investigated for converting biomass into liquid fuels or chemicals [1]. However, large-scale application of bio-oil is unsuitable because of its unfavourable properties i.e., high oxygen content, low energy density, thermal instability and high corrosiveness [2–4]. Among all bio-oil upgrading technologies, catalytic pyrolysis is one of the most efficient options for bio-oil upgrading in the presence of heterogeneous catalysts, which overcomes the limitations to the use of bio-oil [5–8]. In addition, biomass pretreatment for removing part of oxygen from raw biomass prior to pyrolysis is another method for bio-oil upgrading [2,9,10]. Although many types of catalysts have been used in catalytic pyrolysis, ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst is still one of the most effective catalysts for bio-oil upgrading with improving the selectivity of desired compositions [11,12]. There have been many efforts that aim at the modification of zeolite catalysts to enhance their catalytic deoxygenation and increase the selectivity for hydrocarbons in bio-oil. Metal-modified ZSM-5 zeolite catalysts, such as Pb, Ni, Zn, Mo, Ga and Co, have been used to improve the selectivity for hydrocarbons production while reducing coke formation during catalytic pyrolysis of biomass feedstock [13–17]. Recently, relevant studies have found that in-situ Fe contained in biomass feedstock enhanced the yield of hydrocarbons during catalytic pyrolysis in the presence of ZSM-5 due to the formation of new active sites when Fe was deposited on the catalyst [18]. Therefore, modification of ZSM-5 with adding Fe for upgrading bio-oil during catalytic pyrolysis would be an attractive option both from the perspective of economy and environment. Torrefaction has been reported to have the potential for improving the bio-oil quality during the process of pyrolysis due to the reduction of the oxygen content of biomass feedstock and structural transformations in cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin [19–23]. Recently, a number of studies have suggested that torrefaction also has the effect on enhancing the selectivity for hydrocarbons production over ZSM-5 from catalytic pyrolysis. Neupane et al. found that mild torrefaction of pine resulted in a significant increase in hydrocarbons yields when compared to that of non-torrefied pine [9]. Zheng et al. found that torrefaction caused the obvious increase in BTX (benzene, toluene and xylenes) selectivity from catalytic pyrolysis of torrefied hemicellulose, lignin and corncobs [24,25]. Chen et al. found that combination of torrefaction and HZSM-5 during catalytic pyrolysis was a promising method for pyrolysis products upgrading [26]. Zhang et al. investigated the two-step catalytic pyrolysis of pinewood with a fluidized-bed reactor, and has demonstrated that the hydrocarbons yield was improved and the coke deposition on ZSM-5 was reduced from catalytic pyrolysis of torrefied pinewood [27]. However, previous studies have indicated that most inorganic species still remained in torrefied biomass, and which had an inhibiting effect on the formation of hydrocarbons and also had the potential for catalyst deactivation during catalytic pyrolysis [28–30]. Washing pretreatment for removal of the inorganic species can increase the yield of hydrocarbons apparently in the catalytic pyrolysis process. To date, limited information on the upgrading of bio-oil by catalytic pyrolysis with combined washing-torrefaction pretreatment of biomass resources has been reported. Thus, in this study, rice husk underwent combined washing-torrefaction pretreatment and pyrolyzed with ex-situ catalytic pyrolysis mode via a two-stage fixed-bed reactor in the absence and presence of different Fe-modified ZSM-5 zeolite catalysts. The specific objective was to investigate the effects of combined washing-torrefaction pretreatment and Fe-modified ZSM-5 zeolite on upgrading of bio-oil for hydrocarbons formation from catalytic pyrolysis. Rice husk (RH) was provided by Jiangxi Jinkang Advanced Material Sci-Tech Co., Ltd., China. Before being used, rice husk sample was first screened to remove coarse contaminants, and then dried. Fe(NO3)3·9H2O with analytical grade and ZSM-5 zeolite catalysts (SiO2/Al2O3 = 30) were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagents Plant, China and Catalyst Plant of Nankai University, respectively. Fig. 1 gives the schematic of combined washing-torrefaction pretreatment of rice husk sample as reported by previous studies, which evaluated the effects of combined washing-torrefaction pretreatment of biomass resources on the pyrolysis products [31,32]. The raw rice husk sample was first washed for removal of the inorganic species, and then subjected to torrefaction process for further upgrading. Washing process was performed as follows: 20 g raw rice husk was leached in 200 mL washing agent (acetic acid solution at pH = 2.0 for simulated torrefaction liquor) with a proportion of 1:10 at 30 °C for 4 h in a water bath, and was separated by filtration and then washed with deionized water to neutral. Afterward, wet rice husk was dried after washing to obtain the yield of washing process. Next, washed rice husk (W-RH) was subjected to subsequent torrefaction process by torrefying at 210, 240 and 270 °C corresponding to light, mild, and severe torrefaction conditions for 60 min in the torrefaction reactor, which can be denoted as WT-RH210, WT-RH240 and WT-RH270, respectively. The detailed description of torrefaction process could be obtained as described in our previous studies [33,34]. In this study, Fe-modified ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst was prepared by wet impregnation method. The stoichiometric quantities of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O and ZSM-5 zeolite powders were dispersed in 50 mL deionized water, stirred for 2 h at room temperature and then dried overnight. After that, the mixture was calcined at 600 °C for 4 h under the air atmosphere to obtain the Fe-modified ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst. For different Fe-modified ZSM-5 zeolite, the Fe loading of 0.5 wt%, 1 wt%, 2 wt%, 4 wt% and 8 wt% within ZSM-5 zeolite catalysts were prepared, which were denoted as 0.5%Fe/ZSM-5, 1%Fe/ZSM-5, 2%Fe/ZSM-5, 4%Fe/ZSM-5 and 8%Fe/ZSM-5, respectively. Catalytic pyrolysis experiment was conducted in a two-stage fixed-bed reactor, which was presented in Fig. 2 . Catalytic pyrolysis experiment system contained of nitrogen cylinder, mass flow controller, sample feeder, thermocouple, two-stage fixed-bed reactor, heating furnace, temperature controller, condenser, gas dryer and gas sampling bags. The two-stage fixed-bed reactor was made of quartz material, which was divided into pyrolysis stage with inner diameter of 20 mm and catalytic upgrading stage with inner diameter of 24 mm. Before each experiment, 15 g of catalyst powder was loaded into the middle of catalytic upgrading stage and 200 mL/min of N2 used as the carrier gas was supplied to the system for 30 min to provide an oxygen-free atmosphere before the start of pyrolysis. To prevent the catalyst powder from being entrained in the condenser by the pyrolysis gas, a quartz cotton pad was placed under the catalyst layer. The target pyrolysis and catalytic upgrading temperatures were both 550 °C in this study. Once the reactor was stabilized at the programmed temperature, 3 g of rice husk samples were added through a specially designed sample feeder with the catalyst/rice husk sample mass ratio equal to 5 during the experiment. During reaction, the pyrolysis vapor was condensed with three condensing bottles and then gas product was collected by the gas sampling bag. After pyrolysis reaction was completed (reaction time of 5 min), the two-stage fixed-bed reactor was removed from the heating furnace and cooled by a fan to room temperature, and then the biochar was weighed. In addition, the mass of bio-oil was calculated from the difference of the mass of three condensing bottles before and after the reaction. Meanwhile, the non-condensable gas product yield was obtained by deducing the yields of biochar and bio-oil from 100%. In each experiment, the collected catalyst was calcined at 600 °C for 4 h under air atmosphere to obtain the yield of coke. In order to ensure the experimental error within the acceptable range (less than 3%), each catalytic pyrolysis experiment was repeated at least 3 times. Physical adsorption instrument (ASAP 2020 M Micromeritics, United States) performed by nitrogen adsorption at 77 K was used to determine the pore structural parameters of catalyst. The surface morphology of catalyst was analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, LEO1530VP LEO, Germany) coupled with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS, OXFORD INCA X-Sight, UK). The crystal properties of catalyst was analyzed with X-ray diffractometer (XRD, X'TRA ARL, Switzerland) via Cu Kα radiation with a scan rate (2θ) of 4°/min between the range of 5–55°. Bio-oil obtained in this study was analyzed by gas chromatograph/mass spectrometer (GC/MS, 7890A/5975 Agilent, United States) with a HP-5MS capillary column (0.25 mm × 0.25 μm × 30 m). National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) mass spectral library was used for the compounds identification of bio-oil. The fuel characteristics of raw and pretreated rice husk samples are presented in Table 1 . It was found that ash content and fixed carbon content decreased firstly after washing process and then further increased after combined washing-torrefaction pretreatment, while the volatile content showed the opposite trend. An obvious decrease in the atomic ratios of O/C and H/C could be observed by combined pretreatment, due to the dehydrogenation reaction (dehydration and deacetylation) and deoxygenation reaction (decarboxylation and decarbonylation). In addition, the HHVs were also increased from 16.25 MJ/kg for raw rice husk to 17.36–19.42 MJ/kg for rice husk sample after combined pretreatments. Meanwhile, Table 1 presents the change of inorganic species concentration depending on different pretreatment conditions. The results indicated that the inorganic species concentrations were significantly affected by combined washing-torrefaction pretreatment. Specifically, the relative concentrations of most inorganic species (K, Mg, Ca, P, S and Cl) were drastically reduced after washing process, whereas a slight increase trend could be observed after subsequent torrefaction process. However, it can be seen that the inorganic species concentrations in rice husk sample after combined pretreatment were much lower than that of raw rice husk. The SEM images of raw and pretreated rice husk samples as shown in Fig. 3 indicated that combined washing-torrefaction pretreatment removed most ash particles attached to both of inner and outer surfaces of raw rice husk, and also destroyed the polymer structures of raw sample. It was found that no significant destruction was observed on the outer surface of rice husk sample with the increasing of torrefaction temperature due to the presence of silica protective film on the outer surface. While, more cracks and cavities appeared on the inner surface of pretreated rice husk sample when torrefaction temperature increased, indicating the destruction in the polymer structures of rice husk sample under severe torrefaction condition. Fe-modified ZSM-5 zeolite catalysts with different Fe loading have been prepared to investigate their effects on catalytic pyrolysis behaviors of pretreated rice husk samples. The pore structure parameters of catalysts are presented in Table 2 . For different catalysts, microporous volume (Vmic) and microporous area (Smic) accounted for up to 64.51%–72.89% and 75.08%–78.15% of total pore structure, respectively. The increase of Fe loading resulted in the reduction of specific surface area (SBET), total pore volume (Vt), microporous volume (Vmic) and microporous area (Smic), which were mainly due to the blockage of the microporous channels caused by the dispersion of active metal component on the surface or channels of catalyst [14]. The XRD patterns of Fe-modified ZSM-5 zeolite catalysts are shown in Fig. 4 . There was no obvious change in the main crystal structure of different zeolite catalysts. However, a gradual decrease trend in the peak intensity could be observed with the increase in Fe loading, which may be due to the adverse effect of Fe loading on some characteristic peaks of ZSM-5 [35]. Moreover, no characteristic peaks of Fe in the spectra could be found, suggesting that Fe was highly dispersed on ZSM-5 zeolite catalysts without forming the large-particle crystal structure. The XRD results were in good agreement with the images obtained from X-ray mapping of 4%Fe/ZSM-5 as shown in Fig. 5 , which further confirmed that Fe was dispersed uniformly throughout the Fe-modified ZSM-5 zeolite catalysts. Fig. 6 shows the yields of pyrolysis products obtained from catalytic pyrolysis of raw rice husk sample in the absence and presence of different Fe-modified ZSM-5 zeolite catalysts. In fact, the biochar yield of around 32.1% was hardly affected by the presence of Fe-modified ZSM-5 because the catalyst has the effect on pyrolysis vapours without directly contact with rice husk sample in the two-stage catalytic pyrolysis system. It was found that the bio-oil yield decreased and those of non-condensable gases and coke gradually increased as the increase in Fe loading. This suggested that, the loaded Fe on ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst further promoted the catalytic cracking and reforming reaction of pyrolysis vapours, which resulted in the decrease of bio-oil and increase of gas product. Fig. 7(a) shows the effects different zeolite catalysts on the relative contents of chemical compositions in upgraded bio-oil from catalytic pyrolysis of raw rice husk. According to the functional groups, the main chemical compositions in bio-oil can be classified into seven groups, i.e., acids, esters, ketones, furans, phenols, hydrocarbons and sugars. As observed, the relative contents of oxygenated compounds obviously decreased, and that of hydrocarbons in bio-oil from catalytic pyrolysis with ZSM-5 significantly increased when compared to that without catalyst. Due to the presence of ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst, the pyrolysis vapours including of many oxygenated compounds contacted with the active sites on the surface and channels of zeolite catalyst, which resulted in the formation of hydrocarbons through a series of cracking, dehydration, decarbonylation, decarboxylation, and oligomerization reactions followed by combination, aromatization, and cyclization reactions [15,36]. It has been reported that the main chemical compositions with different functional groups in the pyrolysis vapours had different reaction path when passed through the ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst. During the catalytic upgrading process, acids and ketones mainly occurred dehydration, decarboxylation and decarbonylation reactions for converting into olefins and aromatic hydrocarbons due to those high reactivity [37]. As shown in Fig. 7(a), a small amount of acids and ketones can still be detected in the upgraded bio-oil, which was due to the cracking and fragmentation reactions of macromolecular components such as levoglucosan during catalytic upgrading process. The furans were mainly derived from the dehydration reaction of anhydrosugars formed from pyrolysis of hemicellulose and cellulose. During catalytic upgrading process, the furans have undergone decarbonylation to form allene and CO or Diels-Alder condensation to produce benzofuran and water, the allene then underwent oligomerization, aromatization and cyclization reactions to form hydrocarbons. Meanwhile, the decarbonylation reaction of benzofuran leaded to the formation of benzene, CO, and coke [13]. The phenols, which accounted for a large proportion of the pyrolysis vapours, have low reactivity on ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst. In addition to the coke formation by acid-dehydration of phenols, the cracking, oligomerization and aromatization of phenols also resulted in the formation of aromatic hydrocarbons [38,39]. As the increase in Fe loading of Fe-modified ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst, the relative content of hydrocarbons was enhanced obviously from 44.14% with the presence of ZSM-5 to 49.97% with the presence of 4%Fe/ZSM-5. There was no significant increase in the relative content of hydrocarbons when further increasing the loading of Fe (8%Fe/ZSM-5), indicating that the optimum Fe-modified ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst should be 4%Fe/ZSM-5. As exhibited above, the overloading of Fe decreased the surface area of the catalyst, corresponding to the increase of the mass transfer resistance [36]. Fig. 7(b) shows the hydrocarbons selectivity for different catalysts. Here, the hydrocarbons were classified into eight groups, i.e., benzene, toluene, xylenes, ethylbenzene, 1,2,4-trimethylbenzene, naphthalene, naphthalene derivatives (2-methyl-naphthalene and 2,6-dimethyl-anthracene) and PAH (phenanthrene, fluorine and anthracene). One can see that the selectivity for benzene, toluene, naphthalene and naphthalene derivatives significantly increased, while the selectivity for xylenes, ethylbenzene and 1,2,4-trimethylbenzene decreased. These results indicated that the Fe loading exhibited a significant effect on the secondary reactions for hydrocarbons production, which facilitated the further aromatization to form naphthalene and naphthalene derivatives instead of the alkylation to form benzene derivatives [40]. The yields of pyrolysis products obtained from catalytic pyrolysis of raw and pretreated rice husk sample in the absence and presence of 4%Fe/ZSM-5 are presented in Fig. 8 . It is worth mentioning that the bio-oil yield showed the similar trends with the change of pretreatment condition in the absence and presence of 4%Fe/ZSM-5 catalyst. For combined washing-torrefaction pretreatment, bio-oil yield increased firstly by washing process and then decreased by subsequent torrefaction process. In addition, for rice husk sample after combined pretreatment at the severe torrefaction condition (WT-RH270), the bio-oil yield was obviously lower than that for raw rice husk with or without catalyst. The results indicated that the change of the bio-oil yield was mainly attributed to the initial pyrolysis stage. On the other hand, the coke formed by undergoing polymerization reactions over 4%Fe/ZSM-5 catalyst was an important issue during catalytic pyrolysis process. As shown in Fig. 8(b), the coke yield accounted for about 3.6–5.8 wt% with respect to the rice husk sample with different pretreatment processes. It was found that washing process slightly increased the coke yield, and it decreased gradually as the torrefaction temperature increased. Washing process has the promoting effect on bio-oil production, especially macromolecular components, i.e., sugars. These macromolecular components could not easily pass through the microporous structure of catalyst, resulting in coking over the surface of 4%Fe/ZSM-5 [41]. Moreover, the decrease in bio-oil yield as the increase in torrefaction temperature resulted in a reduction in the amount of coke during catalytic upgrading process [27]. Fig. 9 shows the chemical compositions in bio-oil from catalytic pyrolysis of raw and pretreated sample in the absence and presence of 4%Fe/ZSM-5. It can be seen from Fig. 9(a) that combined washing-torrefaction pretreatment obviously promoted the production of sugars and inhibiting the production of acids, esters, ketones, and furans. For upgraded bio-oil with 4%Fe/ZSM-5 catalyst, Fig. 9(b) shows that washing process obviously increased the relative contents of hydrocarbons and sugars compared to that of raw rice husk. The increase of sugars was mainly due to its high relative content in initial bio-oil after washing process (28.62%), which resulted in residual sugars components in upgraded bio-oil. Furthermore, the increase in the relative content of hydrocarbons by washing process can be explained by two reasons. On the one hand, the removal of most inorganic species by washing process has the effect on suppressing reactions that would convert sugars to biochar and gas products, which resulted in the increase of organic components that could be converted to hydrocarbons in the initial bio-oil [18,29]. On the other hand, the accumulation of inorganic species (especially K) on zeolite catalyst resulted in the decrease of the active sites of catalyst and reduced the deoxygenation effect during catalytic pyrolysis process for raw rice husk sample [28,42,43]. Thus, the selectivity for hydrocarbons in upgraded bio-oil from washed rice husk was higher than that from raw rice husk. For combined washing-torrefaction pretreatment, the relative content of hydrocarbons increased gradually with the increase of torrefaction temperature. It has been reported that most hemicellulose was decomposed by torrefaction process, resulting in a comparatively higher fraction of cellulose in pretreated sample [28,42,43]. Compared to hemicellulose and lignin, cellulose would produce more hydrocarbons [38]. Moreover, combined washing-torrefaction pretreatment promoted the production of phenols with no side branches due to the cleavage of ether linkages and demethoxylation in lignin structure as shown in Table 3 , which could form hydrocarbons by a dehydration reaction in the presence of zeolite catalysts [9,20]. The hydrocarbons selectivity in the upgraded bio-oils with 4%Fe/ZSM-5 is shown in Fig. 10 . It was found that washing process increased the selectivity for BTX (benzene, toluene and xylenes) at the expense of other hydrocarbons components, which was due to the influence of low concentration of inorganic species in the washed rice husk sample [30,44]. Furthermore, for combined washing-torrefaction pretreatment, the selectivity for BTX was further increased as the increase of torrefaction temperature. As mentioned above, the comparatively higher fraction of cellulose and lignin in torrefied sample could be obtained. The rank order of BTX selectivity among biomass components by catalytic pyrolysis was lignin > cellulose > hemicellulose [24,25]. Therefore, as the increase of the fractions of cellulose and lignin in pretreated rice husk, the selectivity for BTX increased gradually. Furthermore, torrefaction process also attributed to changes in the lignin structure, which promoted the production of the monocyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, i.e., benzene, toluene and xylenes [9]. According to the above results, it was found that during catalytic pyrolysis with 4%Fe/ZSM-5, combined washing-torrefaction pretreatment had an obvious effect on promoting the production of hydrocarbons and further increasing the selectivity for BTX, while severe torrefaction condition could significantly decrease the yield of upgraded bio-oil. Therefore, combined washing-torrefaction pretreatment at mild torrefaction condition (210 and 240 °C in this study) was more suitable for upgrading bio-oil from catalytic pyrolysis over Fe-modified ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst. Combined washing-torrefaction pretreatment and Fe-modified ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst had important effects on upgrading of bio-oil during catalytic pyrolysis. With the increase of Fe loading in Fe-modified ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst, the yield of upgraded bio-oil was decreased, while the relative content of hydrocarbons was enhanced obviously. The optimum Fe-modified ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst should be 4%Fe/ZSM-5 because excessive loading of Fe could affect the yield of upgraded bio-oil. Furthermore, it was found that during catalytic pyrolysis with 4%Fe/ZSM-5, combined washing-torrefaction pretreatment had an obvious effect on promoting the production of hydrocarbons and further increasing the selectivity for BTX, while severe torrefaction condition could significantly decrease the yield of upgraded bio-oil. Therefore, combined washing-torrefaction pretreatment at mild torrefaction condition was more suitable for upgrading bio-oil from catalytic pyrolysis over Fe-modified ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst. The authors are grateful for financial supports provided by the National Science Technology Support Plan Projects of China (No. 2014BAA05B01) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2242016K41041).
abstract: Catalytic pyrolysis of rice husk sample after combined washing-torrefaction pretreatment over Fe-modified ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst for bio-oil upgrading was investigated. This study shows the effects of Fe-modified ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst with different Fe loading and different pretreatment conditions from an integrated process of pretreatment and catalytic pyrolysis to improve the quality of bio-oil. The results indicated that with the increase of Fe loading, the yield of upgraded bio-oil decreased, while the relative content of hydrocarbons was enhanced obviously. The optimum Fe-modified ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst should be 4%Fe/ZSM-5 because excessive loading of Fe could affect the yield of upgraded bio-oil. Furthermore, it was found that during catalytic pyrolysis process with 4%Fe/ZSM-5, combined washing-torrefaction pretreatment had an obvious effect on promoting the production of hydrocarbons and further increasing the selectivity for BTX (benzene, toluene and xylenes), while severe torrefaction condition could significantly decrease the yield of upgraded bio-oil. Generally, combined washing-torrefaction pretreatment at mild torrefaction condition (210 and 240 °C in this study) was more suitable for upgrading of bio-oil from catalytic pyrolysis over Fe-modified ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst.
title: Upgrading of bio-oil from catalytic pyrolysis of pretreated rice husk over Fe-modified ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst
