full_text: Coal will continue to be an important energy source contributing to the world's energy system in the foreseeable future, but its utilization has faced serious environmental issues [1,2]. To alleviate such problems, great efforts have been made to develop new technologies for clean and efficient utilization of coal. Gasification therein has been recommended to be an attractive choice as it is capable of efficiently converting coal into either clean energy, i.e. heat and electricity, or high value-added chemicals [3]. In particular gasification of low-rank coal under mild condition (atmosphere pressure and temperature lower than 1000°C) has received great attention recently in consideration of operation cost, energy recuperation and ash-related problem [4,5]. Gasification with steam as gasification agent is a well-known process for hydrogen-rich gas production [6]. The process is highly endothermic and generally has to introduce air or oxygen as a part of gasification agent to maintain the process autothermic. It intrinsically includes a series of reactions, e.g. fuel pyrolysis, char gasification, tar destruction and combustion of carbon residues and combustible gases. In traditional gasification technologies, all the above-mentioned reactions occur in a single reactor. Hence, the reactions are closely interrelated with each other and against to be individually regulated to facilitate gasification performance, adapt fuel property and match downstream applications. Specifically, when air is used, the product gas will be seriously diluted by nitrogen introduced with the air. In contrast, decoupled gasification separates these reactions into at least two reactors or zones, and thus provides an effective solution to promote the desired reactions or suppress those unexpected to facilitate the gasification performance [7,8]. Typically, in order to break the interaction between combustion and gasification to improve quality of product gas, the so-called dual bed gasification has been intensively investigated [9–12]. The gasification system separates the gasification and combustion reactions into two isolated reactors, i.e. a gasifier where fuel gasification occurs with steam as gasification agent, and a combustor where char combustion takes place with air as combustion agent. Bed material as solid heat carrier is circulating through the two reactors to carry the heat from the combustor to the gasifier. In this case, the flue gas from the combustor and the product gas from the gasifier are separated, and so that the hydrogen-rich product gas without dilution by nitrogen and combustion-generated carbon dioxide can be available. Typical example of the system is the so-called fast internally circulating fluidized bed (FICFB) gasification at Vienna University of Technology, Austria [13]. The system combines a bubbling fluidized bed reactor as fuel gasifier and a fast fluidized bed reactor as char combustor, which has been successfully demonstrated in Güssing [14] and Oberwart [15], Austria, respectively. Some similar designs can also be found in literatures [16–20]. Despite of great advantage for nitrogen-free and hydrogen-rich gas production, the system suffers from tar formation in product gas during coal gasification under mild condition, which will impose restriction on the end-use application of the gas as well as block downstream equipment [21]. In order to minimize the impact of such tar-related problems, various gas cleanup approaches have been attempted to decrease tar content in product gas [22]. Among these methods, in-bed catalytic destruction is a preferred option because of thermal integration and high tar conversion even at a low temperature [23]. Several kinds of in-bed catalysts for tar removal in dual bed gasification, e.g. natural minerals, alkali and alkali earth metals and supported transition metals, have been investigated [24–28]. Despite of inferior activity in tar removal, olivine, a naturally occurring iron‑magnesium mineral with high attrition resistance and mechanical strength, is widely employed in dual fluidized bed gasifier [29]. In typical dual bed gasification system, the extent of tar destruction is limited even using in-situ tar destruction catalyst as bed material due to short residence time of volatiles in the gasifier and inadequate contact between the volatiles and the catalyst. That is essentially due to the fact that fuel gasification and tar destruction reactions are still interacted in the same space, making it impossible to enhance tar destruction for efficient tar removal. In this respect, decoupling tar destruction from the fuel gasification could provide a solution to optimize reaction condition of tar destruction. Accordingly, Xu et al. [30] proposed a two-stage dual fluidized bed gasification system, in which the single-stage bubbling fluidized bed gasifier is substituted by two stage fluidized bed gasifier. The product gas from the first stage is upgraded further in the second stage, which is favorable for increase of gasification efficiency and decrease of tar content. Göransson et al. [20] installed an in-situ reformer above the dense zone of the fluidized bed gasifier but under the hot bed material return position to intensify the contact of volatiles and catalytic bed material for efficient tar reforming. Our group [31] has developed a decoupled triple bed gasification (DTBG) system to improve tar destruction. In the system, tar destruction, fuel gasification and char combustion reactions are decoupled into three separated reactors, i.e. a reformer, a fuel reactor and a combustor, which are connected in series with the help of circulating solid heat carrier. In this way, tar destruction can be strengthened under optimized reaction conditions. Nevertheless, the coal gasification performance of the DTBG is still inferior to that of other gasification systems [13,17] in regards of gas yield, carbon conversion and cold gas efficiency due to limited coal gasification under mild condition. The key issue is that the fuel reactor temperature is restricted by the reformer temperature in one single circulation loop and then against for coal gasification. In order to modify the DTBG, a dual loop gasification system (DLG) composed of three decoupled reactors and two bed material circulation loops has been proposed to improve both fuel gasification and tar destruction under mild condition. The two circulation loops, i.e. one char combustor-fuel gasifier loop for fuel gasification and the other char combustor-tar reformer loop for tar destruction, share the single combustor. In this way, the fuel gasifier and the tar reformer could be controlled individually, which provides a solution to strengthen both fuel gasification and tar destruction to benefit the performance of coal gasification. The system has already been employed to increase tar destruction in steam gasification of biomass [32]. This study aims to validate the feasibility of the DLG for steam gasification of low-rank coal under mild condition. With calcined olivine as both solid heat carrier and in-situ tar destruction catalyst, the effect of reaction condition on steam gasification performance of two low-rank coals has been investigated. The principle and lab-scale facility of DLG have been described in detail in previous publication [32]. Briefly, the system consists of three separated reactors, i.e. a gas-solid countercurrent moving bed gasifier where fuel is gasified with steam and/or oxygen, a gas-solid radial cross flow moving bed reformer where tarry product gas from the gasifier radially pass through the bed and further cracked and reformed, and a fast fluidized bed combustor where residual chars from the fuel gasifier and deposited cokes on the surface of the circulating bed material particles from the reformer are combusted with air. Two circulation loops, i.e. the char combustor-fuel gasifier loop between the gasifier and the combustor and the char combustor-tar reformer loop between the reformer and the combustor, are in parallel and share the single combustor. A cyclone following the combustor is used to separate the circulating bed material from the flue gas of the combustor. Sealing legs are separately set at the top joint zones between the cyclone and the gasifier or the reformer and the bottom between the combustor and the gasifier or the reformer to prevent the undesired gas leakage, and so that the hydrogen-rich product gas and the flue gas are isolated. The distribution of bed material into the gasifier and the reformer was achieved by three mechanical valves separately set on the top sealing leg of the gasifier and the bottom sealing legs of the gasifier and the reformer. All the reactors are made of 310S stainless steel and externally heated by independent electrical furnaces to compensate heat loss. The temperature of each reactor is recorded by a K-type thermocouple placed at the middle part of the reactor. Manometers are placed at various points of the reactors to indicate the pressure profiles. Specifically in the reformer, a differential manometer is installed to monitor the gas pressure drop through the lateral particle layer and to insure the operation to be normal. The specific operating conditions of the experiments are shown in Table 1 . In this study, a Shenmu bituminous coal (SB) was adopted as feedstock, and an Inner Mongolia lignite (IL) was used for comparison (see Section 3.3). Before each experiment, the coal was crushed and sieved to an average particle size of 0.38–0.83mm, and then dried at 105–110°C for 3h. Table 2 presents the results of the proximate analysis, ultimate analysis and lower heating value of the coals. Olivine from Yichang, China, was used as the bed material and catalyst. Before test, the olivine was crushed and sieved to an average particle size of 0.38–0.83mm, then calcined in muffle at 900°C for 3h under air atmosphere. The result of chemical composition of the olivine particle analyzed by X-ray fluorescence (XRF) is shown in Table 3 . Quartz sand with the same particle size as that of the olivine was used as inert bed material in blank test for comparison. Each experiment was conducted for 2h to ensure every parameter obtained at a stable reaction condition. After about 1h when the system reached to the steady state, the product gas was sampled every 15min in gas bag. The composition of the product gas was detected by a gas chromatograph GC7900 equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a flame ionization detector (FID). The final gas composition presented in this paper is a dry gas composition excluding nitrogen, and averaged by at least three samplings with respect to time on stream. After each test, the outlet pipelines and condensers were washed with solvent tetrahydrofuran. The condensed products including tar, water and the solvent were filtered to remove dust, and then evaporated at 40°C to remove the solvent under reduced pressure. The water was separated by extraction using ethyl acetate, and the tar was obtained by rotary evaporation at 45°C under reduced pressure. Details about tar sampling and analysis method were described in our previous works [31,32]. The reaction parameters are defined as follows: (1) Dry gas yield Nm 3 / kgdaf = Volume of the dry product gas Nm 3 Mass of biomass of dry ash − free basis fed into the system kg (2) Tar yield % = Mass of tar collected in the test g Mass of coal of dry ash − free basis fed into the system g × 100 (3) Tar content of product gas g / Nm 3 = Mass of tar collected in the test g Volume of the dry product gas Nm 3 (4) Cold gas efficiency % = Lower heat ing value of the product gas kJ / Nm 3 × Gas yield Nm 3 / kg Lower heat ing value of coal fed into the system kJ / kg × 100 (5) Carbon conversion % = Mass of carbon in the product gas kg Mass of carbon in coal fed into the system kg × 100 (6) Water conversion % = Mass of water introduced into the system g − Mass of water collected in the test g Mass of water introduced into the system g × 100 (7) Steam to carbon mass ratio S / C = Mass of steam introduced into the system kg Mass of carbon in coal fed into the system kg (8) Air equivalence ratio ER = Oxygen to fuel weight ratio used in the test Oxygen to fuel weight ratio for stoichiometric combustion (9) Gasification circulation ratio C / F = Mass of bed material into the gasifier per hour kg / h Mass of coal fed into the system per hour kg / h (10) Reforming circulation ratio C / F = Mass of bed material into the reformer per hour kg / h Mass of coal fed into the system per hour kg / h In the configuration of DLG, the reformer is decoupled from the gasifier, and so that the volatiles from the gasifier could be further cracked and reformed in the reformer under optimized reformer temperature and in the presence of catalytic bed material. In order to validate the advantage of the system for tar removal, experiments were conducted at varied reformer temperature and bed materials, maintaining the gasifier temperature at 800°C, S/C 1.2 and C/F 10. As shown in Table 4 , the gasification performance obtained under lower reformer temperature (run 1) was poor even with olivine as bed material in regards of H2 concentration, carbon conversion, water conversion and cold gas efficiency as well as tar content and tar yield. Specifically, the tar content and tar yield were as high as 165.3g/Nm3 and 5.0%, respectively. It suggests that the tar destruction was limited to a great extent in the gasifier due to short residence time of the volatiles and poor contact between the volatiles and the olivine catalyst in the upper free space of the gasifier. With the increase of reformer temperature (run 2 and run 3), the dry gas yield, carbon conversion, water conversion and cold gas efficiency increased slightly. H2 concentration increased evidently, and those of CO, CO2 and light hydrocarbons (C2–C3) decreased gradually. Notably, the tar content and tar yield significantly dropped by about 10 and 7 times respectively with increasing the reformer temperature from 700°C to 850°C. It indicates that the tar destruction was surely strengthened at higher reformer temperature. The comparison between run 3 and run 4 shows that the olivine had a better catalytic activity for tar destruction, which gave rise to higher H2 concentration and lower tar content and tar yield. As discussed above, separation of the reformer from the gasifier is of great help to extend residence time of the volatiles and improve contact between the volatiles and the catalyst, and thus allows for further tar destruction in the reformer under appropriate temperature and in the presence of the olivine catalyst. Nevertheless, the tar conversion has a little contribution to the gasification performance of the coal in terms of dry gas yield, carbon conversion and cold gas efficiency due to limited devolatilization of the coal. In order to increase the coal gasification performance, the pyrolysis and gasification of the coal should be strengthened. In the DLG, the reactions would be enhanced by increasing gasifier temperature and S/C, still introducing a part of oxygen as a gasification agent. The steam gasification of coal was conducted at varied gasifier temperature from 750°C to 850°C with 50°C interval, holding reformer temperature at 850°C, S/C 1.2 and C/F 10. As seen in Fig. 1 , the gasifier temperature had a significant influence on gasification performance but little on dry gas composition in product gas. With the increase of gasifier temperature, the dry gas yield dramatically increased from 0.43Nm3/kg daf to 0.62Nm3/kg daf. At the same time, the carbon conversion, water conversion and cold gas efficiency also increased evidently. Nevertheless, no significant change was observed in the tar yield. In product gas, the concentrations of H2 and CO2 had little changes. The CO concentration increased slightly and that of CH4 decreased slowly. It indicates that coal pyrolysis, steam gasification and Boudouard reaction were strongly promoted with the gasifier temperature but the tar destruction was inhibited to some extent by gradually shortened residence time of the volatiles from pyrolysis and steam gasification of the coal. The H2/CO ratio descended evidently as a function of the gasifier temperature, revealing that the reverse water gas shift reaction played an important role in the investigated gasifier temperature range. The S/C was varied from 0.2 to 2.2 by changing steam flow rate, keeping constant of gasifier temperature at 800°C, reformer temperature 850°C and C/F 10. As shown in Fig. 2 , with the increase of the S/C, the dry gas yield, carbon conversion, water conversion and cold gas efficiency increased but the tar yield exhibited a little increase tendency. It suggests that the steam gasification was strengthened with the S/C. Nevertheless, tar destruction reaction was restricted because of the residence time of the volatiles in both the gasifier and the reformer was greatly shortened by unreacted steam and increased product gas. H2 concentration increased sharply while those of CO and CH4 decreased as a function of the S/C, which caused a dramatic increment of the H2/CO ratio. It could be explained by the promoted water gas shift reaction. Notably, the tar yield and tar content in product gas at lower S/C were still at a low level, indicating that tar could be self-reformed with H2O and CO2 generated from coal pyrolysis. The results are in accordance with that reported by Min et al. [33]. In order to further promote the coal gasification and provide a secondary heat source for steam gasification of the coal, oxygen was introduced into the gasifier as a part of gasification agent. The experiments were performed by varying ER in a range of 0–0.3, fixing gasifier temperature at 800°C, reformer temperature 850°C, S/C 1.2 and C/F 10. As expected in Fig. 3 , with the increase of ER, the dry gas yield, carbon conversion and cold gas efficiency remarkably increased as results of the intensified oxidation reaction of the coal. The tar content in product gas was sharply reduced but the tar yield remained at a relative constant value with the ER, suggesting that the tar destruction in the gasifier has not been evidently affected by the oxygen introduction. The reason is that the gasifier operated in a gas-solid countercurrent moving mode. The water conversion increased slowly with the ER, indicating that the steam gasification was promoted in the presence of oxygen. That phenomenon could be explained by the fact that the exothermic oxidation reactions provided additional heat to enhance the exothermic steam gasification. At higher ER, the concentrations of CO and CO2 ascended evidently whereas those of H2 and CH4 showed an opposite trend, which was attributed to the promoted oxidization reactions. The increase of CO concentration and decrease of H2/CO ratio displayed that the oxidization reaction of the coal played an important role in the investigated ER range. Comparison of gasification performance between SB and IL were conducted at gasifier temperature 800°C, reformer temperature 850°C, S/C 1.2 and C/F 10. As seen in Table 5 , at the same reaction condition, the steam gasification performance of IL in terms of the dry gas yield, tar yield, carbon conversion, water conversion and cold gas efficiency was much better than that of SB. The H2 concentration in product gas was in the same level as that of SB. The CO and CO2 were a little higher, and the CH4 and tar content were slightly lower. That could be attributed to the higher volatiles content and gasification reactivity of IL. In order to validate the advantages of DLG, the coal gasification performance of DLG was also compared with that of DTBG [31]. It can be seen from Table 5 that the gasification performance of DLG in regards of H2 concentration, dry gas yield, carbon conversion, water conversion and cold gas efficiency was superior to that of DTBG. That was mainly attributed to the promoted steam gasification in the gasifier of the DLG, which was achieved by operating the gasifier at higher temperature It is noticed that the tar contents and tar yields of both DLG and DTBG were in low level. The tar content and tar yield of DTBG was lower, which could be attributed to the longer residence time of the volatiles (lower gas yield) and the more catalytic bed material olivine contacted with the tar (at higher C/F) for efficient tar destruction. As discussed above, in the DLG, the configuration that separation of the reformer from the gasifier and arrangement of them into two independent bed material circulation loops provides an effective way to individually optimize both coal gasification and tar destruction at appropriate reaction conditions. Nevertheless, the water conversion of steam gasification of coal in the DLG was at the low level, because it was operated under mild condition as were in other steam gasification systems [34,35]. Specifically, negative values of water conversion were obtained at the lower reformer temperature and S/C (as shown in Fig. 2). That could be partially because the water generation from the coal pyrolysis is more than water consumption from steam gasification and tar reforming under these conditions. Moreover, the reduction of Fe2O3 on the surface of the regenerated olivine particles [36,37] in both the gasifier and the reformer could consume part of hydrogen in product gas and further produce the water. The feasibility of the DLG for steam gasification of Shenmu bituminous coal under mild condition has been validated with calcined olivine as both solid heat carrier and in-situ tar destruction catalyst. Tar destruction was significantly enhanced by increasing reformer temperature and using calcined olivine catalyst as bed material, which resulted in the decrease of tar yield. In comparison, the increase of gasifier temperature, S/C and ER promoted coal gasification, and thus facilitated gasification performance of the coal in terms of dry gas yield, carbon conversion and cold gas efficiency. H2 concentration in product gas also increased with the S/C but decreased at higher ER. The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 50776013) and the National High Technology Research and Development Program (“863”Program) of China (No. 2008AA05Z407).
abstract: A dual loop gasification (DLG) system consisting of three separated reactors and two bed material circulation loops has been proposed for steam gasification of low-rank coal to obtain hydrogen-rich gas with low tar content. The system decouples the gasification process into fuel gasification, tar destruction and residual char combustion, which occur in three independent reactors correspondingly, i.e. a gasifier, a reformer and a combustor. Both the gasifier and the reformer are separately interconnected with the single combustor, forming two bed material circulation loops in parallel. In this way, both the gasifier and the reformer could be operated individually under optimized conditions. With Shenmu bituminous coal as feedstock and calcined olivine as both solid heat carrier and in-situ tar destruction catalyst, the performance of the system for the steam gasification of the coal has been investigated. It has been found that the tar was effectively removed under higher reformer temperature and in the presence of the olivine catalyst, and the coal gasification was promoted at higher gasifier temperature, S/C and ER.
title: Gasification of low-rank coal for hydrogen-rich gas production in a dual loop gasification system

full_text: Tar formation remains an unsolved problem in most biomass and agricultural waste gasification processes under development for power generation. Tar dealing or disposal is also a choice for other sources like oil residues, coke production, industrial effluents and municipal solid waste treatment, recycling of waste plastics and other processes which presently have different goals and markets. The nonvolatile components present in tars clog process lines, soil equipments and contaminate the residual streams making hot gas purification a relevant step of most gasification processes. Among the proposed technological alternatives, catalytic tar gasification with steam seems to be attractive. The enhanced fuel-gas formation that results from the addition of a tar gasification step would contribute to the process efficiency. Tar decomposition has been extensively studied. The catalytic effect of cheap materials like limestone and dolomite in tar decomposition has been demonstrated through studies using model compounds [1–3] as well as by using real tars [4–12]. Work carried out with model compounds have shown that tar gasification takes place through the intermediate formation of solid carbon, deposited on the catalyst surface, which subsequently is partially gasified with steam thus increasing the yield of gaseous products. This gasification step is the rate-determining step of the whole process. Because of the high heterogeneity of tars, the present level of understanding does not allow to predict confidently the gasification behaviour of one tar based on experimental data obtained for other tars. An extensive characterization of three tars from very different sources was reported previously [13] and in this work the predicting capability of that characterization is tested against actual experimental data on the catalytic gasification of those tars. The characterization included standard techniques like TGA, GC, GC/MS, UV–fluorescence, as well as less frequently used techniques such as planar chromatography, size exclusion chromatography in NMP, solution state 1 H and 13 C -NMR spectroscopy, solid state 13 C -NMR spectroscopy and FT-IR spectroscopy [13]. In this work the gasification behaviour of those three tars from different sources is also compared as a way to: provide experimental data relevant to the design of process equipments and plant operation; extend the results about mechanisms obtained from previous works with model compounds; propose a kinetic model able to predict tar conversion to gaseous products as a function of operating parameters from a flowing reactor. One of the tars (Petrox, abbreviated as PDR in figures and tables) corresponds to a crude oil flash column residue, obtained at a local oil refinery. This product is partly converted to heavy and light oils in a catalytic cracking unit; however, 40% remains as a heavy residue. Another tar (Huachipato, abbreviated as COT) originates from coke production out of a mixture of Canadian and Australian bituminous coals at the Huachipato Steel Mill, located near Concepción, Chile. This tar corresponds to the condensable products from pyrolysis, removed so as to prevent contamination of the gas lines. The third tar (Gasco, abbreviated as CGT) is an undesirable byproduct in the coal gasification process by which town gas is partially supplied to the city of Concepción. It is made up of condensable products found in the pyrolysis zone of the (Lurgi type) integral gasifier which operates at alternating cycles of combustion and gasification [14]. Fig. 1 shows a diagram of the equipment. Argon (AGA, 99.998%), measured and controlled by a mass flowmeter, was used as carrier gas; it was flowed through a steam saturator (3) formed by a double jacket coolant; steam concentration was regulated by adjustment of the circulating water temperature (4). The tar was fed through a capillary tube located inside the reactor (7); its flow was controlled by an injection pump. The tar was mixed with the steam–argon stream and fed to the catalytic bed reactor. The 40 mm length and 30 mm diameter stainless steel reactor had a perforated plate for the catalyst support. All input and output lines were covered by heating pads so as to avoid condensation of water and volatile gaseous products. The reactor temperature was regulated by an electric oven equipped with a programmable temperature controller. The effluent stream flowed through an ethanol/CO2 (T=213 K) trap where the condensable products were collected. The gaseous products were analyzed by on-line gas chromatography (Perkin-Elmer, 8700) equipped with a packed column (Porapak R). For each residence time, the experimental run extended until stationary state, which was assumed established when constancy of the measured gas concentrations was reached (approximately 30 min). The experimental runs were carried out keeping the tar feed flow rate constant while varying the steam to tar ratio (R V o, g/g) and the residence time τ (s), the latter by adjustment of the carrier gas flow rate. Some pyrolysis assays were also performed for comparison. In this case, argon was directly fed from the cylinder (2) to the reactor inlet (5) through a bypass line. Previously activated CaO (S BET: 10 m2/g, 1–2 mm particle size) was used as catalyst in a 40-mm height bed. Activation (internal surface area generation) was performed by limestone (from Guarelo Island, XII Region, Chile) calcination at 800°C for 3 h. The chemical composition of the raw limestone, determined by atomic adsorption spectrometry, is shown in Table 1 . Table 2 shows the elemental analysis and other properties of the three selected tars; feeding molar flow rates were calculated from the elemental composition. Table 3 presents the boiling point distribution of the tar samples, as determined by TGA [13]. In a first series of experiments, pyrolysis of the three tar samples was performed. Fig. 2 (curves obtained by smoothing of the experimental data) shows the yields of the gaseous products, H2, CH4 and CO, evolved at 720°C. The higher yields obtained during pyrolysis of the Petrox tar are in agreement with its chemical nature as determined by GC and GC-MS analysis [13], which showed the presence of highly boiling aliphatic compounds, being these mainly alkanes. On the other hand, 1 H NMR analysis showed that 97% of the hydrogen content of the Petrox tar corresponds to aliphatic H2. Pyrolysis studies of different hydrocarbons have shown a greater tendency of aliphatic chains to evolve as gaseous products, as compared with compounds of aromatic nature [15–18]. This is the case for the present work (see Table 2) as the Petrox tar with 16% has far lower content of aromatic components than either the Huachipato and Gasco tars, with 57% and 94% carbon aromacity, respectively. The CH4 yields are also in agreement with the characteristics of the Petrox tar as CH4 is known to be a significant product during thermal decomposition of aliphatic or highly alkyl-substituted organic compounds, which are predominant in this tar [16–18]. CO formation also follows the same order (Petrox>Huachipato>Gasco), in agreement with the decreasing contents of oxygen-containing groups encountered in these tars. The decay of the gas yields from pyrolysis with increasing values of soaking time is attributed to catalyst saturation due to carbon deposition, a phenomenon which was observed during pyrolysis of the three samples. Carbon formation during pyrolysis has been observed in a previous study using model compounds [19] and its effect upon surface characteristics and activity of the CaO catalysts is being currently investigated. The larger decay observed for the CO yield curves might be explained by the occurrence of the shift reaction, which is included in the mechanism proposed later and it is consistent with other experimental results, as well. In conclusion, pyrolysis results confirm the greater thermal stability presented by the Gasco tar, as a result of its high aromatic ring system with extensively dealkylated aromatic structures and of its small contents of oxygenates [13]. These structural differences in the characteristics of the three tars are a consequence of their thermal `history'. The Gasco sample is the tar obtained under the most harsh thermal treatment and chemical environment. Table 4 shows the range of operating conditions selected for the gasification assays. Carbon conversion, X c, that is, the carbon fraction of the feed tar that is converted to gaseous products, was calculated from the molar flow rates of the C-containing gases, determined from the measured concentrations. Fig. 3 shows the values of X c, obtained at different gasification temperatures as a function of the steam to tar ratio of the feed, R V o. In all cases, the only gaseous products detected were H2, CH4, CO and CO2. At the lower assayed temperatures (T750°C) steam gasification of deposited carbon takes place increasing carbon conversion and gas production [8]. The role of the gasification temperature may be inferred from the conversion curves of Fig. 3. As temperature increases, greater maximal conversion were obtained and total conversion to gaseous products was attained at the higher temperatures (T>750°C) and ratios of 3.5 g of steam/g of tar, or greater. These results are in agreement with the above described sequence, as at temperatures higher than 750°C, steam can presumably gasify the deposited solid carbon at a faster rate than its rate of formation. At lower temperatures, gasification is much more slower, requiring larger steam/tar ratios for complete, or larger conversion of the tar feed. This can be observed for the Petrox tar gasified at 720°C, for which the maximal conversion that could be reached was around 0.8. As thermodynamics favors the formation of gaseous products from tars, the latter results confirm that gasification of the deposited solid carbon is the rate-controlling step. As shown in the previous pyrolysis results of the Petrox tar, the prevailing aliphatic nature resulted into a faster decomposition, as compared to the other tars. Hence, for this tar, catalytic saturation by carbon deposition is enhanced at the lower essayed temperatures. At temperatures higher than 750°C, both decomposition and gasification rates are similar therefore steady state conditions may be achieved, particularly for steam to tar ratios greater than 3.5 g/g. The reactivity of the tars can be assessed by comparison of the minimum steam to tar ratio (R Vmin o) required for 90% conversion. For example, the most reactive tar at a gasification temperature of 770°C is the one from Petrox (R Vmin o≈2.8) followed by the Gasco tar (R Vmin o≈3.5) and the Huachipato tar (R Vmin o≈4.5). The higher reactivity presented by the Petrox tar is attributed to the lower thermal stability of this tar, which results from its previously discussed structural characteristics (higher content of aliphatic chains, smaller proportions of polycyclic compounds). In spite of its higher thermal stability to pyrolysis, the Gasco tar shows higher reactivity to gasification than the Huachipato tar. This result will be analyzed next on the basis of the relative weight of the gasification reactions upon the product yields. Fig. 4 Fig. 5 show the yields of gaseous products (mol of product/g tar) at two of the four assayed temperatures, as a function of the steam to tar ratio. As the yield of H2 increases almost linearly with R V o, that is proportional to the steam concentration in the reactive ambient, it shows that, beyond pyrolysis, steam gasification of the deposited carbon is taking place. At 750°C the H2 yield appears to be independent of the tar type (composition), while at 790°C the Huachipato and Gasco tars generated slightly higher quantities of H2 in the gaseous product (as well as of CO at both temperatures) than the Petrox tar. These differences should be ascribed to a larger weight of gasification, which takes place only at higher temperatures, with respect to pure pyrolysis. That is, at temperatures where gasification may occur significantly, the formation of H2 and CO must be attributed mainly to gasification of deposited solid carbon, which was expected to be higher for the Gasco and Huachipato tars due to their higher fixed carbon content and aromaticity (see Table 3) and lower hydrogen and oxygen contents, as determined in the characterization study [13]. Methane yields were independent of feed ratio R V o at both temperatures, thus showing that CH4 produced at the initial step of gasification (i.e., the pyrolysis), does not react with steam as its reforming (CH4+H2O→CO+3H2) is favoured at high pressure. On the other hand, methane formation decreases in the order Petrox>Huachipato>Gasco, in agreement with the decreasing content of aliphatic compounds of these tars. Figs. 4 and 5 additionally show that CO2 yields increase with R V o with no significant differences among the three samples. This result, taken together with the lack of formation of this gas during pyrolysis of the tars, points to the occurrence of the shift reaction: (1) CO+H2O→CO2+H2 As mentioned, studies of pyrolysis and catalytic gasification of model compounds [1–3, 15], have shown that gasification can be viewed as taking place in two steps: (a) dissociation of the naphthalene molecule on the catalyst surface, and (b) gasification of deposited solid carbon, with additional occurrence of the shift reaction. The latter increases as the fed steam to tar ratio increases, as shown by the larger CO2 concentrations in the gaseous products. The tendency of the curves to level off is consistent with complete conversion of the deposited carbon and the equilibrium established by the shift reaction. Hence, the yield curves shown in Figs. 4 and 5 are in agreement with the above sequence and presence of events. If the reactive sequence just described for the thermal decomposition of naphthalene and aromatic compounds is applied to tar gasification, the latter can be described by the following proposed mechanism: (2a) A+X→C–X+gases (CH4 and others)+Light tar (2b) H2O+X→O–X+H2(g) (2c) C–X+O–X→CO–X+X (2d) CO–X→CO(g)+X (2e) CO–X+O–X→CO2(g)+2X Steps (2a) and (2b) describe the adsorption of solid carbon from thermal cracking of the tar (A) and the adsorption of oxygen generated from dissociation of the water molecule, on the active sites of the catalyst (X), respectively. CO formation would be the result of competition between desorption from the carbon–oxygen complex as described by step (2d) and its consumption due to the shift reaction, partially represented by reaction (2e). The experimental results for tar gasification described above are in agreement with the proposed mechanism for gasification of model compounds; however further studies are required for a detailed verification of the mechanism involved in gasification as well as for the understanding of the role of the catalyst in the different steps of this reaction. It is known that thermal decompositions (cracking) are carried out at given preferential active sites whereas dissociation of the water molecule requires sites of different nature. Work is in progress in our laboratory concerning the surface characterization of the catalysts used in this work, in order to explore these subjects. Carbon conversion during tar gasification may be modelled with respect to the steam to tar ratio as: (3) dX c dR o V =k 1−X c Integration of Eq. (3)leads to the following exponential-type relation which, under the experimental conditions of this study, i.e., constant tar and variable carrier gas flow rate, represents the behaviour of the conversion curves shown in Fig. 3: (4) X c =1−exp −kR o V The kinetic parameter k may be considered as a measure of reactivity for tar gasification. Table 5 shows values of k obtained by adjustment of Eq. (4)to the curves in Fig. 3. As shown, the tars exhibited the same increasing order of reactivity, i.e., Petrox>Gasco>Huachipato. A relation between carbon conversion and the residence time in the reactor is obtained from the definition of this parameter: (5) τ= V eff v = V eff N oT V ̃ where the total molar flow rate into the reactor, N oT, is given by the sum of the reagents flow rates of steam N oV, tar N oA and the carrier gas flow rate N oAr: (6) N oT =N oV +N oA +N oAr Under the experimental conditions of this work, the flow rate of tar was negligible as compared with the steam and the argon flow rates; therefore: (7) N oT ≈N oV +N oAr From the ratio of mass flow rates R V o=(F oV)/(F oA), it follows that: (8) N oV = R o V F oA M V =FR o V Where F=F oA/M v, was a constant in the experimental assays. Under the experimental conditions of constant tar flow rate, constant steam concentration and variable argon flow rate, there is a linear correlation between the latter and the steam to tar ratio of the form: (9) N oAr =AR o V Replacement of Eqs. (8) and (9)into Eqs. (7) and (5)leads to: (10) τ= V eff (A+F) V ̃ R o V = M R o V With M=V eff/(A+F)Ṽ, a constant. Fig. 6 was obtained from the experimental values of residence time and R V o applied in this work. As shown, all experimental points fit the above relationship. Constant M was adjusted from the data as 8.3679 (min g tar/g steam). From Eqs. (4) and (10), (11) X c =1−exp − 8.3679k τ which represents the relationship between carbon conversion and the residence time when tar gasification is carried out at constant tar and steam flow rates. Fig. 7 shows that the agreement with the experimental data is good when values of k from Table 5 are replaced into Eq. (11). Curves in Fig. 7 correspond to assays carried out at temperatures of 720° and 790°C. The catalytic steam gasification of three tars from completely different sources: oil refinery (Petrox), coal gasification (Gasco), and coking (Huachipato), using CaO (10 m2/g) as catalyst was carried out at bench scale. The data thus generated is relevant to the design and operation of plants, and consistent with both, a simple kinetics and the structural characterization of the tars. It was possible to represent the catalytic gasification of these tars by a two step reactive sequence: (a) thermal decomposition of the tar, which generates solid carbon deposition on the catalyst; (b) steam gasification of deposited solid carbon, with partial occurrence of the shift reaction. Gaseous yields results show good agreement with this mechanism. At the higher temperatures assayed (T>750°C) and feed rate R V o greater than 3.5, the gasification rate is higher than the rate of solid carbon generation, so complete conversion to gaseous products is observed. At temperatures below 750°C, the rate and extension of the gasification reaction are lower, the catalyst surface suffers solid carbon deposition, and the gas yields vary accordingly. Extensive characterization, particularly structural characterization of the tars [13] proved to be a useful tool in the prediction of pyrolysis and gasification behaviour. Thus, we obtain the following. Under pyrolysis conditions, the thermal stability of tars decreases in the order Gasco>Huachipato>Petrox, which correlates with the higher aromaticity, low contents of alkylated and oxygenated compounds of the Gasco tar, as well as with the higher presence of aliphatic compounds, in the inverse order. Under significant gasification (T>750°C), the reactivity of tars decreases in the order: Petrox>Gasco>Huachipato. However H2 and CO yields were higher for the less reactive tars showing that gasification of deposited carbon is the predominant factor. Structural characterization of the tars determined more aromaticity for the less reactive tars thus predicting a larger amount of deposited carbon that may be directly gasified by steam to H2 and CO. Finally, a proposed kinetics plus mass balances in a flowing reactor lead to an expression by which carbon conversion can be related with a simple exponential-type function to the feeding ratio of steam to tar and the residence time in the reactor. F oA tar feed mass flow rate (g tar/min) M v steam molecular weight (18 g/mol) N oT total feed molar flow rate (mol/min) N oV steam feed molar flow rate( mol steam/min) N oA tar feed molar flow rate (mol tar/min) N oAr argon molar flow rate (mol/min) R V o feed mass ratio (g steam/g tar) v volumetric feed flow rate (cm3/min) V eff effective volume of reaction (cm3) Ṽ ideal gas volume at 20°C (24,465 cm3/mol) X c carbon conversion τ residence time in the reactor (s) This work was possible through the support of The Chilean National Science and Technological Foundation (FONDECYT). One of the authors (N.A.A.) gratefully acknowledges a fellowship granted by the Andes Foundation. The helpful assistance of Ms. Paola Concha is also gratefully acknowledged.
abstract: Catalytic gasification of three tars from different sources (oil refinery, Petrox; coking, Huachipato; coal gasification, Gasco) was carried out at a laboratory scale fixed bed reactor using CaO as catalyst, obtained by limestone calcination. Gasification runs were performed keeping constant the tar feed flow rate, as well as the steam concentration. The residence time, τ, was regulated through the carrier gas (argon) flow. For steam to tar ratios, R V o (g/g) equal to, or larger than 3.5 and temperatures over 750°C, total conversion to gaseous products was obtained; solid carbon deposition was observed at lower values of R V o. Gas yields and product distribution can be explained and partially predicted by extensive tar characterization, reported elsewhere. A kinetic model is proposed, which coupled with mass balances in a flowing reactor led to an expression of carbon conversion to gaseous products as a function of the operating variables, R V o and τ. The data for the catalytic gasification of these tars corroborate the mechanisms proposed at earlier studies of catalytic gasification, using naphthalene as a model compound.
title: Steam gasification of tars using a CaO catalyst

full_text: With the increasing concerns on energy crisis and global warming, more strict requirements have been progressively established for energy saving and CO2 emission reduction. The technology of lean burn, which can greatly improve the economic utilization of fossil fuels and reduce the emission of CO and hydrocarbon, has been received wide attention. However, the conventional three-way catalysts are ineffective in the NO x reduction to N2 due to the presence of excessive oxygen under lean burn condition. Hence, the catalytic removal of NO x in the presence of O2 becomes a challenge in the field of catalysis. Catalysts for the removal of NO x in the presence of O2 include mainly non-noble metal oxides, molecular sieves and noble metals. Among these catalytic systems, noble metal catalysts have drawn numerous interests, owing to their high activity at low temperature and strong resistance to poisoning by SO2 and H2O. Recently, more and more attentions have been put on the activity of iridium catalysts [1–18]. Wang et al. [6,8] reported that among Pt, Pd, Rh and Ir catalysts, Ir/ZSM-5 catalyst exhibits the highest activity for the NO reduction by CO in the presence of excess O2 and H2O. Haneda et al. [9,11,17] discovered that Ir/SiO2 shows marked catalytic activity for the NO reduction with CO in the presence of O2, H2O and SO2. Supported [3,4,7,18] and unsupported [5] Ir-based catalysts also display high catalytic activity and N2 selectivity when NO reduces with hydrocarbon in rich oxygen environment. Furthermore, Fujitani et al. [19] investigated the adsorption and reactions of NO over the clean Ir (111) surfaces and found that the adsorbed NO dissociates to N2 at 200 and 300°C. Chen et al. [20,21] studied the adsorption and decomposition of NO on the clean and O-covered planar Ir (210) and nanofaceted Ir (210) and found that both Ir (210) surfaces are very active for the NO decomposition with high selectivity to N2 and faceted Ir (210) still remains active toward the NO decomposition upon increasing the oxygen coverage to 0.7 ML O. However, few researches have been reported on the direct catalytic reaction of NO over Ir-based catalysts under oxygen-rich conditions. In this work, we investigated the NO reactions over Ir-based catalysts under oxygen-rich conditions and discussed the effects of Ir content and support materials on the NO reactions. The catalysts were prepared by iso-volumetric impregnation with aqueous solutions of H2IrCl6.6H2O (Platinum Group Metals China) and the Ir content is 0.02wt.%, 0.1wt.%, 1wt.% and 5wt.%, respectively. The support materials used were ZSM-5 (Nakai University, Si/Al=50), active γ-Al2O3 (Dalian Luming Nanometer Material Co., Ltd, 200–500nm, 160m2·g–1) and SiO2 (Qingdao Haiyang Chemical Co., Ltd, 0.125–0.425mm, 460m2·g–1). The impregnated catalyst precursors were dried at 110°C for 12h and finally calcined at 500°C for 3h in air. The powder catalysts were pressed at 10 Mpa into pellets, crushed and sieved to obtain grains of size 0.250–0.425mm for catalytic activity measurement. For comparison, 5wt.% Pt/γ-Al2O3 was prepared by the iso-volumetric impregnation of γ-Al2O3 with an aqueous solution of H2PtCl6.6H2O (Platinum Group Metals China). The amount of chemisorbed H2 was measured with a self-made pulse chemosorb apparatus. TEM analysis was performed with JEOL JEM-2010 at an acceleration voltage of 200keV. Catalytic activity was conducted with a fixed-bed continuous flow reactor by temperature programmed reaction (TPR) technique. The catalyst granules (0.05g) were carefully held in a tubular quartz reactor (6mm i.d.). The reaction gas, containing NO (420ppm) and O2 (4.4vol.%) diluted in He as the balance gas, was fed through the catalyst bed at a rate of 100mL·min−1. The reaction temperature was monitored by a thermocouple that was directly inserted into the region of the catalyst bed and controlled by a PID-regulation system (Bachy, CKW-2200). The reaction temperature was raised at a rate of 4°C·min–1 from 100°C to 750°C. The concentrations of NO, NO2 and NO x (=NO+NO2) were continuously detected by Nitrogen Oxides Analyzer (Australian Ecotech, EC-9841B). In order to evaluate the catalytic performance of iridium catalysts in the NO reaction, the maximal conversion ratio and the corresponding conversion temperature were adopted. For the NO oxidation, the maximal oxidation conversion ratio (η O) was estimated by the ratio of the maximum concentration of NO2 formed to the concentration of NO added originally, and the corresponding oxidation conversion temperature (T O) was the temperature at the maximal NO2 formation. For the NO reduction, the maximal reduction conversion ratio (η R) was estimated by the ratio of the maximum concentration of NO x reduced to the concentration of NO added originally, and the corresponding reduction conversion temperature (T R) was the temperature at the maximal NO x reduction. Temperature programmed desorption (TPD) was done after the TPR experiment. The catalyst was cooled to room temperature in the same gas stream as the TPR experiment and the catalyst bed was then purged with Helium for about 1h. When the signal of NO and NO2 from the detector dropped to zero, the TPD experiment was initiated in a Helium stream at the same volume flow rate and heating rate as that in the TPR experiment. Fig. 1 shows the TPR results of NO reaction over Ir/ZSM-5 with different Ir contents. From Fig. 1(a), it can be seen that the concentration of NO2 begins to increase at 300°C while NO decreases simultaneously over 0.1wt.% of Ir/ZSM-5, suggesting that NO is oxidized to NO2 (Eq. (1)). The concentration of NO2 reaches the maximum at 391°C and then decreases as the temperature increases, which is limited by the thermodynamic equilibrium among NO, NO2 and O2 [22]. From Fig. 1(b), it can be seen that the concentration of NO x over 0.1wt.% of Ir/ZSM-5 decreases from 300°C, reaches the lowest at 391°C and then increases as the temperature continues to increase. In contrast Fig. 1(a), it is obvious that the NO x reduction is related to the oxidation of NO to NO2 over 0.1wt.% of Ir/ZSM-5. Wang et al. [8] conducted a research on TPD of NO after co-adsorption of NO and O2 over 0.1wt.% of Ir/ZSM-5 using GC-MS technique and found that N2 and N2O formed near 395°C. In our work, although the concentration of N2 and N2O were not determined, the temperature at which NO x reached the lowest concentration is consistent with that at which N2 and N2O formed in Wang's study [8], which implies that the NO x reduction over Ir/ZSM-5 is transformed into N2 and N2O. Due to the NO x reduction associated with the NO2 formation, the NO x reduction can be attributed to NO2 decompose to adsorbed NO (NO(ad)) (Eq. (2)) [23,24] and then decompose to N2 (Eq. (3)) [8,19–21] or disproportionate into N2O (Eq. (4)) [8,25,26]. Thus, the oxidation of NO to NO2 occurs over Ir-based catalysts, simultaneously, the reduction of NO to N2 or N2O also takes place. (1) NO + O 2 → NO 2 (2) NO 2 → NO ( ad ) + O ( ad ) (3) NO ad → N 2 + O ad (4) NO ad → N 2 O + NO 2 Figs. 1 to 3 show the TPR profiles of NO, NO2 and NO x over Ir/ZSM-5, Ir/SiO2 and Ir/γ-Al2O3 with different Ir contents, respectively. The results of the catalytic activity are listed in Table 1 . Compared with Ir-based catalysts, blank supports show negligible activities. Over the same support material, the oxidation activity and the reduction activity of NO both increase with the increase of Ir content. With the same Ir content, Ir/ZSM-5 is more effective for the NO oxidation and Ir/γ-Al2O3 is more conducive to the NO reduction. Therefore, Ir-based catalysts play important roles on the NO reactions and the Ir content and support material have important effects on the activities of Ir-based catalysts. In Fig. 2(a), with the increase in Ir content over Ir/SiO2, η O increases, T O moves toward low temperature and the temperature window of NO oxidation becomes broad. These results indicate that a higher Ir content is more beneficial for the oxidation of NO to NO2, which is similar to that reported by Xue et al. [27]. Xue et al. [27] found that the oxidation activities of NO over Pt/SiO2 increase with the Pt loading increasing and thought that would be due to the increase in the exposed Pt atoms increase. Table 2 lists the results of hydrogen chemisorption over Ir/SiO2 with the Ir content of 1wt.% and 5wt.%. These results show that the exposed Ir atoms on the surface increase with the increase in Ir content. In Fig. 2(b), with the increase of Ir content, η R increases, T R moves toward low temperature and the temperature window of NO reduction becomes broad. Such results indicate that a higher content of Ir metal is more effective for the reduction of NO, which is same as the results of NO oxidation. Hence, the exposed Ir atoms on the surface promote the NO reactions over Ir/SiO2 and the activities of oxidation and reduction are both improved by the increase in the Ir content. T O and T R are same (in Table 1), which further demonstrates the association between the NO x reduction and the NO2 formation. From Figs. 1 and 3, it is can be seen that, with the increase in Ir content, the activities of NO oxidation and reduction both increase over Ir/ZSM-5 and Ir/γ-Al2O3, which are similar to that over Ir/SiO2. Compared with the NO reactions over Ir/ZSM-5 and Ir/SiO2, an obvious discrepancy over Ir/γ-Al2O3 is the strong desorption of NO and NO2 in the temperature range from 100°C to 250°C. This is due to the adsorption of NO and NO2 at low temperature, which coincides with that reported by Xue et al. [27]. From Table 1, it can be seen that the values of η O over blank supports decreases in order of ZSM-5>γ-Al2O3 >SiO2 and the values of η R over all blank supports are zero. These results indicate that support materials exhibit a little effect on the NO oxidation and no effect on the NO reduction. The results of NO oxidation are same as that reported by Xue et al. [27], which depend primarily on the characteristics of support materials. In Table 1, for the NO oxidation with the Ir content of 1wt.%, the order of η O is Ir/ZSM-5>Ir/SiO2 >Ir/γ-Al2O3 and the T O is Ir/ZSM-5Pt/γ-Al2O3, which is similar to our result. However, this result is different from that obtained over blank supports, suggesting that the difference in catalytic activities should be derived from the discrepancy of Ir dispersion on the support surface [28]. The TEM images of 1wt.% Ir over different supports shown in Fig. 4 demonstrate that Ir particles are well dispersed over the surface of ZSM-5 and are accumulated over the surface of γ-Al2O3. As a result, Ir particles accumulated are not conducive to the catalytic oxidation of NO to NO2. For the NO reduction with the Ir content of 1wt.% (in Table 1), the η R order is Ir/γ-Al2O3 >Ir/SiO2 >Ir/ZSM-5 and the T R is Ir/ZSM-5Ir/SiO2 >Ir/ZSM-5, which is consistent with the η R trends. This result implies the adsorbed-decomposition of NO2 plays an important role in the NO reduction. With the Ir content of 0.02wt.%, the η O order is Ir/ZSM-5>Ir/γ-Al2O3 >Ir/SiO2, which is similar to that over blank supports(in Table 1). The values of η R are small and the trend is not obvious. These results are due to the characteristics of support materials that play important roles on the catalytic activities when the Ir content is very low. With the Ir content of 0.1wt.% and 5wt.%, the η O order is Ir/ZSM-5>Ir/SiO2 >Ir/γ-Al2O3 and the η R order is Ir/γ-Al2O3 >Ir/SiO2 >Ir/ZSM-5, which are similar to that of Ir-based catalysts with 1wt.% Ir content. To confirm the effects of support materials on Ir dispersion over the support surface, the amount of chemisorbed H2 over Ir/ZSM-5 and Ir/γ-Al2O3 with 5wt.% of Ir content was measured (results also shown in Table 2). The results show that the exposed Ir atoms decrease in order of Ir/ZSM-5>Ir/SiO2 >Ir/γ-Al2O3, which is in agreement with the trends of TEM in Fig. 4 and confirms that the well dispersion of Ir on the surface of support materials are conducive to the catalytic oxidation of NO to NO2. Fig. 6 shows the TPR profiles of NO, NO2 and NO x over 5wt.% Pt/γ-Al2O3 and Table 1 also lists the results of catalytic activity. As shown in Table 1, T O is at 361°C and η O is 58.8% over Pt/γ-Al2O3, which are consistent with that reported by Xue et al. [27]. Compared with 5wt.% Ir/γ-Al2O3, although the maximal conversion activity of NO oxidation over Ir/γ-Al2O3 is similar to that over Pt/γ-Al2O3, the maximal conversion activity of NO reduction over Ir/γ-Al2O3 is higher than that over Pt/γ-Al2O3. Hence, Ir/γ-Al2O3 is more effective for the NO reduction than Pt/γ-Al2O3, which is also observed by Wang et al. [8]. Over Ir-based catalysts under oxygen-rich conditions, the oxidation of NO to NO2 and the reduction of NO to N2 or N2O occur. Ir content and support material both exhibit effects on the NO reactions. The activities of oxidation and reduction of NO increase as the Ir content increases. When the Ir content is lower than 0.1wt.%, the NO oxidation activity depends mainly on the characteristics of support materials and decreases in order of Ir/ZSM-5>Ir/γ-Al2O3 >Ir/SiO2. When the Ir content is higher than 0.1wt.%, the NO oxidation activity relies on the Ir dispersion over support materials and decreases in order of Ir/ZSM-5>Ir/SiO2 >Ir/γ-Al2O3, while the NO reduction activity relies on the adsorbed-decomposition of NO2 over Ir-based catalysts and decreases in order of Ir/γ-Al2O3 >Ir/SiO2 >Ir/ZSM-5. Compared with Pt catalyst, Ir catalyst has a similar activity for the NO oxidation but is more effective for the NO reduction.
abstract: A series of experiments with various Ir-based catalysts for the NO reactions under oxygen-rich conditions have been conducted using a temperature programmed reaction (TPR) technique. The results indicate that the oxidation of NO to NO2 and the reduction of NO to N2 or N2O occur over Ir-based catalysts under oxygen-rich conditions. The catalytic activity for the NO reactions increases with the increasing of Ir content. Furthermore, support materials also have important effects on the catalytic activity. When the Ir content is lower than 0.1wt.%, the catalytic activity for the NO reactions is dependent on the characteristics of support materials and decreases in order of Ir/ZSM-5>Ir/γ-Al2O3 >Ir/SiO2. When the Ir content is higher than 0.1wt.%, the catalytic activity for the NO oxidation is correlated with Ir dispersion on the surface of support materials and decreases in order of Ir/ZSM-5>Ir/SiO2 >Ir/γ-Al2O3, while the catalytic activity for the NO reduction is attributed to the adsorbed-decomposition of NO2 and decreases in order of Ir/γ-Al2O3 >Ir/SiO2 >Ir/ZSM-5. Compared with Pt catalyst, Ir catalyst has a similar activity for the NO oxidation but is more effective for the NO reduction.
title: NO reactions over Ir-based catalysts under oxygen-rich conditions
