full_text: Biomass pyrolysis/gasification process plays a significant role in the energy fuel system due to the convention of the low-value feedstocks to the high-quality combustible gases which consist mainly of H2, CO, CH4, CO2, and N2 in various proportions [1–3]. The typical application of the gas products is directly burning used with electricity generation or turning into liquid transportation fuels. However, is there any other method by using the gases produced from biomass pyrolysis/gasification process for a high-value application? From the component view of the gas, CO, CO2, and CH4, as the rich carbon source gas, can be used for the high-value solid carbon material preparation process. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) exhibited the high mechanical strength, excellent thermal conductivity and electronic characteristics [4] which are applied widely in the energy and environmental shields and viewed as the target materials in this study. According to the literature, CH4 decomposition [5,6], CO disproportionation [7,8] and methane reforming [9,10] processes for CNTs synthesis were drawn much attention recently. To expand the carbon source gas field, we established a novel approach for CNTs produced from the conventional biomass-based gasification process. There were few researchers considered the composition of the pyrolysis/gasification gas for CNTs synthesis. Maghrebi et al. [11] found that the additional gas with oxygen element can effectively inhibit the deactivation of the catalyst in the carbon mixture gases. Nishii [12] synthesized a large number of CNTs with the coal gasification syngas (CO and H2) as the carbon source gas at 800–900 °C. CNTs production considered by the component of CO and CH4 both simultaneously has hardly any research; therefore, this research focused on the component with CO and CH4 for CNTs production from conventional biomass-based gasification process. In this study, commercial NiMg/Al2O3 (atomic ratio Ni/Mg = 1/1) catalysts were used for determining the decomposition characteristic of the mixture gas of CO and CH4. A thermogravimetric (TG) analyzer was adopted for obtaining the reaction temperature range and carbon deposition feature. The catalytic decomposition processes of carbon source gas were created in a fluidized bed reactor. In the fluidized bed, the synthesis process can be reached maximization procedure on the catalyst surface which was limited in the TG reactor. Meanwhile, the essential operation parameters were measured to master the mechanism during a decomposition process. Gas components (CH4, CO, and CO/CH4), bed temperature (450C, 550 °C, and 650 °C) and various partial pressure of the gas (0.1 atm, 0.2 atm, and 0.4 atm) were investigated to determine the characteristic of carbon formation in the fluidized bed. The carbon products were confirmed as nanotubes through the measurements of the scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM) and Raman spectroscopy. The activity tests over the NiMg/Al2O3 catalyst for the CO/CH4 mixture gases were performed in a thermogravimetric analyzer (TG, WCT-2A). Before the reaction, to avoid spatial limitations, 5 mg reduced catalyst (by H2 in 30 min) samples were loaded into the crucible and deposited in the furnace. Typically, a mixture gas of carbon source gas and N2 with a total flow of 70 mL/min was explored as the reaction gas from room temperature to 800 °C with the rate of 10 °C/min at atmospheric pressure. The N2 was a carrier gas to balance the total gas flow. The partial pressure ratio of CH4 was as a constant of 0.1 atm. The effect of the partial pressure ratio of CO/CH4 on the final carbon deposit rate was investigated. The weight gain changes during the reaction were measured consecutively. The CNTs synthesis experimental system was a lab-scaled fluidized bed as shown in Fig. 1 . The reactor was made of a quartz tube with a total length of 1250 mm and an internal diameter of 30 mm. The distributor with lots of uniform distribution pores for the high resistance force was plated in the middle of the fluidized bed. In each experiment, a certain amount of catalysts (kept the same ratio of carbon source to catalyst equal to 4.41 L·h−1 g−1, which was the best condition for carbon source decomposition) were located upon the distributor before the reaction started. Firstly, the temperature was heated from room temperature to 500 °C with a rate of 15 °C/min in an N2 atmosphere, then the gas translated into the mixture of H2/N2 gas for 30 min at 500 °C to reduce the catalyst from the oxidation state to the reduced state. Secondly, the mixture gas was switched to the pure N2. Meanwhile, after warming or cooling to the preset reaction temperature, the carbon source gas was introduced and maintained 60 min. The generated gaseous products were passed through the filter device and dryer, then analyzed by the MRU gas analyzer with the data recording consecutively. The solid products were weighted after cooling in the N2 atmosphere for 30 min. In the carbon source decomposition processes, carbon sources (CH4 or CO) must be “cracked” or broken into the CH links or CO links to produce the carbon products. The performance of this operation evaluated by using the conversion rate as follows: (1) X i , t = F in , i , t − F out , i , t / F in , i , t × 100 where i represents CH4 or CO, F is the amount (mL/min) of CH4 or CO with time, the subscript of in or out is the input amount or output amount. The yield of hydrogen (H2 yield, expressed as Y H 2 ) was determined as followed: (2) Y H 2 = F out , H 2 , t / 2 F in , CH 4 , t × 100 where F out, H2, t represents the output amount (mL/min) of the H2, and F in, CH4, t is the input amount (mL/min) of CH4. The total amount of solid carbon products (Carbon weight gain, expressed as C dep /C cat in figures) was measured by using the following equation: (3) C dep / C cat = W t − W cat / W cat × 100 where W t and W cat represent the total and initial weight (g) of the catalyst after the reaction and after reduced by hydrogen, respectively. C dep /C cat represents carbon weight gain per unit mass catalyst. Due to the analysis of Gas Chromatography (GC) for the gaseous products of methane decomposition process, carbon nanotubes were the only carbon production from methane decomposition process without the other kinds of gaseous carbon products. Commercial calcined catalyst has adopted the NiMg/Al2O3 catalysts with the same loading amount of Ni and Mg, and Al2O3 as the supporter. NiO species in the primary catalysts which have been calcinated in the air were reduced to Ni species by H2 gas. Due to the addition of Mg, the interaction between Ni and support material was strong enough to form solid solutions (NiAl2O4, MgAl2O4, and Ni-Mg solid solution) with the improvement of Ni dispersion [13] that resulted in increasing the activity of the catalysts [14]. The crystal domain size of Ni was calculated to 15.5 nm calculated by the Scherrer formula, which was beneficial to the CNTs production with its small size. The morphology of deposited carbon products was measured by SEM (LEO1530VP) and TEM (H7650). Raman spectroscopy (Labram HR800) was undertaken on the carbon deposit product under various carbon sources to determine their graphitic quality. Raman spectroscopy was applied with a wavelength of 514.5 nm at Raman shifts between 300 and 3500 cm−1. To achieve the activity of the catalysts and the temperature range of the decomposition process, the mixture gas of carbon source gas was decomposed in the TG reactor. Results of activity tests are illustrated in Fig. 2 under different partial pressure ratio of CO/CH4 with the same amount of CH4. As presented in Fig. 2(a), the sharp weight rise was observed at 425 °C for pure CH4 gas. However, in the case of the presence of CO composition, the temperature of initial carbon weight gain (C dep /C cat ) points down to 350 °C. The carbon weight gain increased slowly with the temperature enhancement, when the temperature reached around 600 °C, the carbon weight gain maintained stable regardless of the amount of CO added in CO/CH4 gas. The final carbon weight gain was increased with the CO partial pressure enhancement. Compared with DTG curves in Fig. 2(b), the temperature of maximum carbon deposited rate was decreased from 575 °C to 450 °C with the CO addition. Moreover, the temperatures of the maximum carbon deposited rate and the stable decomposition stage were brought forward by the enhancement of a partial pressure ratio of CO/CH4. (R-1) CH 4 → C + 2 H 2 Δ H 298 Θ = 75 kJ / mol (R-2) 2 CO → C + CO 2 Δ H 298 Θ = − 172.4 kJ / mol (R-3) CO + H 2 → C + H 2 O Δ H 298 Θ = − 131.3 kJ / mol The main reason for the above phenomena is that the addition of CO will lead to the reactions of (R-2) and (R-3) reacted, especially for the disproportionation reaction (R-2), the react temperatures for this kind of reactions are lower than that of (R-1) reaction. Due to the carbon products rapidly deposited during the CO disproportionation process under low temperature, carbon products covered the catalysts surface quickly. Therefore, the contact between CH4 and catalyst was not enough for CH4 decomposition with a high degree, even though the temperature improved to a high temperature, which was suitable for CH4 decomposition process. However, under a low temperature, the (R-3) reaction almost not occurred due to the small amount of H2 produced from (R-1) reaction. Thus, the degree of CO disproportionation process played a dominant role in the mixture gas in the temperature increasing process. Before the decomposition of mixture gas, the feature of pure gas decomposition must be comprehended firstly. The CH4 decomposition process is an endothermic reaction as the reaction (R-1) shown. Fig. 3 illustrates the CH4 decomposition process for various reaction temperatures over NiMg/Al2O3 catalyst with a gas velocity (u) of 0.043 m/s and CH4 partial pressure (P CH4 ) of 0.4 atm. The CH4 conversion rate (X CH4 ) as a function of reaction time and carbon weight gain calculated after the reaction are shown in Fig. 3(a) and (b). It is observed that the initial X CH4 increased with the increment of the reaction temperature. Nevertheless, the X CH4 decreased to 3% after 50 min (600 °C) and 28 min (650 °C), respectively. The high temperature was not in support of X CH4 maintained the high stable value for a long time. We can also find a similar trend for the carbon weight gain as shown in Fig. 3(b). The highest carbon weight gain was obtained at 550 °C with 160%, which was more than twice the other temperatures. Due to the low X CH4 limited by thermodynamics under the low temperature, the value of carbon weight gain was small. Dunker et al. [15] also found that the effect of temperature on the CH4 decomposition did not emerge the linear relationship in the temperature range of 835 °C–930 °C. Furthermore, there were two reasons for the high temperature against for carbon weight gain. On the one hand, CH4 decomposed rapidly in a high temperature, and it caused a large amount of deposited carbon covered the surface of catalysts in a short time. It means that the catalytic activity was impeded by the carbon products, which caused the decline of X CH4 after a while. On the other hand, high temperature caused the deactivation of catalysts since the selective with temperature which was proved in a TG reactor in our previous research [14]. Importantly, low temperature and high temperature were not beneficial for the final carbon deposited. CO as the common precursor in CNTs synthesis process was also taken out in the fluidized bed. Fig. 4 illustrates the effect of temperature on the CO conversion rate (X CO) and carbon weight gain are the same. The X CO and carbon weight gain decreased with the enhancement of temperature, which was in accord with thermodynamics. The result of 550 °C was slightly less than that of 450 °C. When the reaction temperature raised to 650 °C, the X CO and carbon weight gain were just 1/5 for the other conditions in CO disproportionation process. On the contrast of CH4 decomposition process, CO disproportionation reaction was an exothermic reaction as the reaction (R-2) shown which due to the low reaction temperature (below 650 °C) on the thermodynamic limitation [16,17], and the high temperature was not beneficial for the process going on. As previously mentioned, the thermodynamics of CH4 decomposition process and CO disproportionation process presented the different characteristic. To achieve the feature of mixture gas decomposition, Figs. 5 to 8 display the results of CO/CH4 mixture gas decomposition in the fluidized bed. CO has taken a much higher proportion (30 to 40% of carbon source) than CH4 (10%) in the pyrolysis/gasification gas [18], thereby, the partial pressure ratio of CO/CH4 was selected from 0 to 4 in this study. The average conversion rate and H2 yield in the following sections were calculated with the average results in the stable stage for the reaction time of around 20 min. The effect of various temperatures and partial pressure ratios of CO/CH4 on the conversion rate and H2 yield can be known well by comparing the three figures in Fig. 5. Firstly, based on the effect of temperature, X CH4 and Y H2 increased with the temperature increment regardless of the partial pressure ratio of CO/CH4. The improvement degree of X CH4 was uniform with the temperature increment of 20%, but a little improvement of H2 yield with 13% for the temperature increment each 50 °C. In the pure CH4 decomposition process, it was uniform for X CH4 and Y H 2 (as the details shown in Fig. 6); however, in the mixture gas decomposition process, the value of them and improvement degree were not conformed. X CO reduced slightly when the temperature increased from 450 °C to 550 °C but dropped to a low value as the temperature increased to 650 °C. Secondly, taken account of the partial pressure ratio of CO/CH4. There was a minor impact on X CH4 and Y H 2 with increasing the ratio of CO/CH4 in each temperature. The effect of the increment of the ratio of CO/CH4 on X CO was tiny under low temperature. However, a high partial pressure ratio of CO/CH4 was beneficial for X CO, from 12% up to 46% at 650 °C. CO/CH4 decomposition results compared with the pure CH4 decomposition process and CO disproportionation process with a same partial pressure of reagent under three temperatures are shown in Figs. 6 and 7. According to Fig. 6, X CH4 improved obviously with the adjunction of CO. It was mainly displayed in high reaction temperature (650 °C) case, the X CH4 enhanced about 50%. X CH4 still enhanced by 15% even at 450 °C which temperature was not suitable for CH4 decomposition process. However, X CO and Y H 2 just increased less than 10% in the mixture gas decomposition process. Fig. 7 illustrates the variation of carbon weight gain under decomposition processes of different carbon source gases. In CO/CH4 mixture decomposition process, the carbon weight gain produced under 550 °C appeared the highest level regardless of the ratio of CO/CH4, which was different with the change trend of CH4 and CO conversion rate shown in Fig. 5 (the average data in 20 min). With the increment of a partial pressure ratio of CO/CH4, the carbon weight gain improved obviously, especially at 550 °C (15% increased for each situation), which was accorded with the TG results. There was a huge increment for carbon weight gain compared with the pure CH4 decomposition process and CO disproportionation process. The carbon weight gain was eight times under the partial pressure ratio of CO/CH4 equal to 2 than that of pure CH4 decomposition and CO disproportionation processes at 550 °C. Compared with the Figs. 6 and 7, it was found that the addition of CO was a benefit for X CH4 and carbon weight gain, but not given much positive effect on the Y H 2 . The outlet amounts of H2 was not in proportion to X CH4 . The primary gas product of the CH4 decomposition is H2, to confirm the effect of H2 on X CO and carbon weight gain, Fig. 8 compares the carbon formation results from the CO/H2 and CO in the fluidized bed. X CO and carbon weight gain produced from the CO/H2 gas were higher than that of CO gas. However, compared with the results of the mixture gas of CO/CH4, the increasing range of X CO was less, and carbon weight gain was high in CO disproportionation process. Theoretically, X CO was enhanced by providing a high partial pressure of CO. Nevertheless, there was a decreasing tendency of X CO with the growth of partial pressure in both reagents. The reason was that the reaction time between reagents and catalysts was not long enough in the fluidized bed reactor, even though the total amount of CO increased by high CO partial pressure. However, the increment of handling capacity for per unit mass catalyst by high partial pressure caused by improving carbon weight gain more than double from 0.2 atm to 0.4 atm regardless of the addition of H2. The carbon weight gain mainly synthesized from the CO disproportionation process, even in the mixture of CO/H2. Due to the presence of H2, the reaction of (R-3) was promoted to the forward reaction; therefore, the X CO raised 25% and the carbon weight gain raised 30% contemporary. Compared to the data of mixture gas of CO/CH4 in Fig. 6, due to the presence of CH4, the reactions in the process were more complexity, not only related to the H2. (R-4) CO 2 + 2 H 2 → C + 2 H 2 O Δ H 298 Θ = − 90 kJ / mol (R-5) CH 4 + CO 2 → 2 CO + 2 H 2 Δ H 298 Θ = 247 kJ / mol (R-6) CO 2 + 4 H 2 → CH 4 + 2 H 2 O Δ H 298 Θ = − 165 kJ / mol (R-7) CO + H 2 O → CO 2 + H 2 Δ H 298 Θ = − 41 kJ / mol (R-8) CO + 3 H 2 → CH 4 + H 2 O Δ H 298 Θ = − 206.2 kJ / mol Therefore, we assumed that H2 as the medium, and elucidated the mechanism of the mixture carbon source gas of CO and CH4 in this research as Fig. 9 shown. The thickness of the line indicates the strength of the reactions. Reactions of (R-1) and (R-2) played the leading roles in the mixture gas of CO and CH4 which were provided in the last section. According to the CO2 distribution form the mixture gas decomposition outgases, from 450 °C to 650 °C, the efficiency conversion (release the accounted for N2) was 11.2%, 8.4%, and 1.8%, respectively. Based on the weight changes of the dryer in the fluidized bed process, H2O also accrued in this mixture gas decomposition process. Therefore, the other reactions (R-4) to (R-8) occurred successively due to the presence of H2, CO2, and H2O. The trend of X CH4 and Y H 2 changed with the temperature in the mixture gas due to the endothermic reaction of (R-1), and the tendency of X CO varies with temperature was mainly related to (R-2) reaction. Due to the complex system in the reactor, high temperature was a benefit to the initial conversion of CH4, but it was easy to cover the catalyst with carbon products in a short time. The carbon deposited quickly at 650 °C by CH4 decomposition caused the carbon weight gain was lower than 550 °C, which was similar to the data shown in Fig. 3(a). It must also be mentioned that the catalyst will be deactivated even at 550 °C in a TG reactor [14]. However, in the fluidized bed, the reaction rate maintained well more than 60 min at the same temperature. It gives a possibility for large production in the fluidized bed due to the continuous flow system. Production of H2 from CH4 decomposed was not enough to react with CO in low temperature (which also can be confirmed from the TG data); meanwhile, at high temperature, the other exothermic reactions were not with an optimum behavior. The ratio of CO to CH4 was a benefit for CO conversion; however, a high ratio of partial pressure inhibited the tendency of Y H 2 which was identified with CH4. The more addition of CO, the more H2 was consumed. Compared with the pure gas decomposition process, X CH4 , X CO, Y H 2 and carbon weight gain were increased significantly. In conclusion, CH4 and CO played a synergistic role (especially at 550 °C) in the decomposition process of CO/CH4 hybrid carbon source gas, with the H2 as the medium to promote the decomposing reactions further for each other. Fig. 10(a) to (d) present SEM results of the carbon products from various reagents decomposition processes. All figures show that carbon products are fiber type of with well top closure, and the wall surfaces are smooth with no excessive bending and breaking. Carbon products from CH4 decomposition were short and straight relatively, which were a kind of wool flocculated carbon materials. Compared with the carbon products of CO disproportionation at the same temperature as Fig. 10(b) presented, the fibrous structure was presented as twisted with an irregular direction, and the part of the pipe walls had a spiral type as the arrow pointed with a long length. Addition of CO into CH4 expanded the length of carbon products. The high partial pressure ratio of CO/CH4 enhanced the length of carbon products with the flocculent style. The macroscopic TEM images of the synthesized products after acidizing of our experiments are illustrated in Fig. 11 . Bamboo growth pattern can be found from the circle part in TEM images. As indicated by the arrows in the images, the tops of the carbon nanotubes showed a distinct triangular opening, which was caused by the pretreatment of the sample by acidification. The carbon products presented a structure of hollow tube bundle, which was a typical type of the CNTs. The CNTs from CH4 decomposition (Fig. 11(a)) were smooth with a uniform diameter. In addition to the standard smooth tube bundles, there were many kinds of carbon nanotubes with helical and arcs, which illustrated the typical S-type and G-type carbon nanotubes from CO disproportionation process (Fig. 11(b)). Due to the anisotropic carbon deposition from different crystal facets [19], it was possible to form the S-type and G-type CNTs in a short time, which was obviously in CO and CO/CH4 gas phases. With the increase of the partial pressure ratio of CO/CH4, the wall of CNTs was going to smoother and more even (Fig. 11(c) and (d)). Fig. 12 shows the Raman spectrum of CNTs obtained after acid treatment from different reagents decomposition process. Four strong peaks were observed in the vicinity of 1350 cm−1,1580 cm−1, 2700 cm−1 and 2950 cm−1, which corresponding to the carbon nanotube characteristic peaks, D peak, G peak, D' peak and G' peak, respectively. D peak and G peak were the evaluation indexes for carbon products, which correspond to disordered sp2-hybridized carbon atoms and sp2-bonded carbon atoms, respectively [20]. The relative intensity ratio of ID/IG indicates the quality of the carbon nanotubes with the meaning of the graphitization of carbon nanotubes or degree of disorder in the structure. In this study, the ID/IG ratio changed from 1.03 to 1.65 for several CNTs, which indicates that the CNTs were very well graphitized. The ID/IG ratio increased with the increase of the partial pressure ratio of CO/CH4, which on behalf of the graphitization degree enhanced caused by long carbon nanotubes wall for bending graphite accumulation. The presence of D' peak suggested that the formation of single wall nanotubes (SWCNT) also obtained in the CNTs. The intensity of D' peak enhanced with the addition of CO to CH4. This study focused on studying the decomposition process of mixture carbon gas of CO and CH4 in a TG reactor and a fluidized bed, compared with the results of the pure gas decomposition processes. The main conclusions are as follows. 1) It was feasible to synthesize the CNTs from the mixture gas of CO/CH4, which were the main component in biomass-based gasification gas. 2) TG results showed that carbon weight gain increased under low temperature the mixture gas of CO/CH4 than pure CH4 decomposition. The final carbon weight gain was increased with the CO partial pressure enhancement. 3) X CH4 , X CO, Y H 2 and carbon weight gain were increased significantly in the mixture gas of CO/CH4 than pure CO and CH4 gas decomposition process. The carbon weight gain increased significantly at 550 °C (more than five times from mixture gas than that of CO absent). The CNTs produced with well graphitized from CO/CH4 were longer and smoother than that of pure gas decomposition. 4) CH4 and CO played a synergistic role in the CO/CH4 hybrid carbon source gas decomposition process, with the H2 as the medium to promote the decomposing reactions further for each other. It was feasible to synthesize the CNTs from the mixture gas of CO/CH4, which were the main component in biomass-based gasification gas. TG results showed that carbon weight gain increased under low temperature the mixture gas of CO/CH4 than pure CH4 decomposition. The final carbon weight gain was increased with the CO partial pressure enhancement. X CH4 , X CO, Y H 2 and carbon weight gain were increased significantly in the mixture gas of CO/CH4 than pure CO and CH4 gas decomposition process. The carbon weight gain increased significantly at 550 °C (more than five times from mixture gas than that of CO absent). The CNTs produced with well graphitized from CO/CH4 were longer and smoother than that of pure gas decomposition. CH4 and CO played a synergistic role in the CO/CH4 hybrid carbon source gas decomposition process, with the H2 as the medium to promote the decomposing reactions further for each other. We recognize financial support from the China Scholarship Council through a Ph.D. scholarship to Miss Zhang.
abstract: The gas product which synthesized from the biomass-based gasification process is a kind of rich carbon source gas. To establish the high-value applications, the synthesis process of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) from biomass-based gasification gas is established in this study. This study focused on the CNTs production process from the mixture of CO and CH4, which were primary components of biomass pyrolysis/gasification gas, in a thermogravimetric and a fluidized bed. The reaction temperature and the partial pressure of the essential role of the mixture gas were investigated. Meanwhile, in the fluidized bed, results of the decomposition of mixture gas (CO/CH4) were compared with the results by pure gas (CH4 decomposition process and CO disproportionation process). Results showed that the initial temperature for carbon weight gain increased in the mixture gas decomposition process was lower than that of the pure CH4 decomposition process in the TG reactor. In the fluidized bed, the conversions of CO and CH4 were promoted in the mixture gas decomposition process. CO and CH4 played a synergistic role with the H2 as the medium in the CO/CH4 decomposition process. With the increase of CO/CH4 partial pressure ratio, the yield and quality of CNTs increased significantly. The CNTs produced from CO/CH4 were longer and smoother than that of CH4 and CO decomposition. When the partial pressure ratio of CO/CH4 up to 2, the yield of carbon weight gain was eight times than that of same partial pressure of CH4 decomposition. Therefore, it is feasible for the decomposition of biomass-based gasification gas for the CNTs formation process.
title: Synthesis of carbon nanotubes from conventional biomass-based gasification gas

full_text: Chemical looping combustion (CLC) with fossil fuel has emerged as a promising technology for heat and power generation with inherent CO2 capture [1]. A CLC unit usually consists of two interconnected fluidized-bed reactors, the air reactor (AR) and the fuel reactor (FR). Employing a metal oxide material named an oxygen carrier, volatiles (CH4, etc.) and syngas (CO and H2) produced by the pyrolysis and gasification of solid fuel are oxidized to CO2 and H2O by the active lattice oxygen in the oxygen carrier. The oxygen carrier reduced in the FR is transported to the AR, where it is reoxidized by the air and goes to the next cycle. In this regard, the CLC process is an inherent CO2 separation technology with lower energy expended for the capture process [2]. Continuous operation is closely related to the development and scaleup of the chemical looping process. To date, the continuous operation of chemical looping plants has been focused on the range of 0.3 kW to 3 MW with gaseous, liquid, and solid fuels. Several CLC units with solid fuels or gas fuels,such as Chalmers 10 and 100 kW [3,4], CSIC1.5 and 50 kW [5,6], SINTEF 150 kW [7], SEU 3 and 20 kW [8,9], and Utah 225 kW [10] have been erected and operated. More details about the CLC units have been reviewed in the literature [1,11]. Moreover, a 1-MW CLC pilot unit has been autothermally operated using ilmenite as the oxygen carrier and coarse coal as the fuel [12]. Relevant studies on different kinds of solid fuel (e.g., biomass, coal, petroleum coke) have already been conducted in 0.5–100 kW CLC units [6,9,10,13–15]. Research on CLC with coal and petroleum coke as fuel indicated a rate-limiting step in terms of char gasification by CO2 and H2O [1,11,15]. Some unconverted char particles may be transported to the AR from the FR in the oxygen carrier stream, and thus CO2 produced by char combustion will be released in the AR outlet. Finally, a low carbon capture efficiency is undesired. To solve this problem, a carbon stripper (CS) implementation is proposed to separate the char particles from the stream of solids between the FR and AR and return them to the FR [16]. Operation with a high-efficiency carbon stripper has been effectively proven in ICB-CSIC 50 kW and Chalmers 100 kW to obtain a higher carbon separation efficiency compared with that in the 1 MW CLC (no carbon stripper is installed) of Darmstadt [6,12,15]. To reduce the escaped char slipping into AR and achieve high carbon capture efficiency, an annual CS has been proposed and verified in a cold model apparatus [17]. Furthermore, a CLC system for solid fuels coupled with the annular CS was built and operated using ilmenite as the oxygen carrier, and previous studies indicated that a 90%–95% char separation efficiency was obtained by the annular CS [18]. Actually, the time needed for char gasification is closely related to the type and size of the solid fuel. The smaller the particle size used, the faster gasification rate will be realized due to the higher ratio of surface to volume. For instance, although the elutriated char decreases when coarse coal is fed, the mean residence time has to increase to ~23 min for gasification [19]. Lyngfelt et al. proposed a suitable fuel size (~100 μm) for the CLC process [20]. According to Berguerand and Lyngfelt, operation with petroleum coke has a rather low carbon capture efficiency of 60–85% due to the low reactivity of this fuel [14]. Indeed, the effect of fuel size and type on behalf of the gasification rate in FR on the char separation efficiency of CS and carbon capture efficiency should be investigated further. One of the crucial cornerstones of CLC technology is oxygen carrier materials. Natural ores and synthetic materials are the mainstream types such as Ni-based [21], Cu-based [22], Fe-based [23], and Mn-based oxides [24]. The synthetic oxygen carriers presented an obstacle to large-scale preparation due to high costs and unexpected loss. Natural low-cost ores such as ilmenite [25], iron ore [26], copper ore [27], etc., have been popular in CLC with solid fuels, Recently, manganese ore as an oxygen carrier has attracted significant attention; for example, researchers from Chalmers University of Technology and other institutes have focused on evaluating different manganese ores as oxygen carriers [28,29]. Manganese ores have been tested and compared with ilmenite and showed better performance due to enhanced volatiles and syngas conversion [30]. However, studies have shown that most manganese ores showed poor mechanical strength, resulting in a shorter lifetime (99–284 h) and fluidizing properties [31]. Our previous work has proven that the manganese ore promoted by kaolin could increase its attrition index and melting points [32], which makes manganese ore more suitable as an oxygen carrier. A deeper study on the impact of different oxygen carriers and solid fuels on the operational performance of the CLC system is required. Therefore, a kaolin-promoted manganese ore was applied as the oxygen carrier material in this work. The overall aim of this paper is to evaluate the conversion performance of solid fuels in a 30 kW chemical looping combustion unit with an annular CS using lignite and petroleum coke as fuels and kaolin-promoted manganese ore as the oxygen carrier. The effects of the varying fuel size and residence time in the FR on the char separation efficiency of the annular CS and carbon capture efficiency were studied. A model was applied to describe and predict the carbon capture efficiency influenced by the char separation efficiency of the CS, fuel types, and residence time. Additionally, the kinetics and SEM analysis of the fresh and tested oxygen carriers were compared. The solid fuels used in this research are a lignite and a petroleum coke. The lignite and petroleum coke were dried (95 °C for 4 h) before being crushed and sieved. Table 1 shows the proximate and ultimate analysis results of the lignite and petroleum coke. The lignite was sieved to different sizes for testing, including 106–212 μm, 212–500 μm, 500–600 μm, and 600–1000 μm. The petroleum coke was sieved to 106–212 μm. The oxygen carrier is a manganese ore supported by kaolin and manufactured by rolling granulation, as described in our previous work [32]. Kaolin, acted as an efficient inert support, was used to enhance the mechanical strength of oxygen carrier particles. The oxygen carrier was selected based on its high melting temperature, strong crushing strength, and fast reactivity [32]. Prior to the experiments, the oxygen carrier was sieved to 106–300 μm. Its chemical and physical properties are listed in Table 2 , of which the crushing strength was determined by using a WDW 3020 equipment, and the final crushing strength is the average of 10 independent measurements [32]. A series of experimental campaigns was carried out in the 30 kW unit of chemical looping combustion. Fig. 1 and Table 3 show the layout and main dimensions of the CLC unit, respectively [18]. The CLC unit is composed mainly of an air reactor (AR) and a fuel reactor (FR), as well as an annular carbon stripper [18]. The AR was designed to be a fast-fluidized bed with a riser and a dense bed. To increase the bed inventories and residence time in the FR, the FR was designed to have three independent bubbling beds, i.e., bubbling bed I (BBI), bubbling bed II (BBII), and bubbling bed III (BBIII). The BBI and BBII are positioned in the vertical direction, connected with a vessel [18]. The BBII and BBIII are located in the horizontal direction. The oxygen carrier particles flow into the BBII and BBIII by overflow. It should be noted that the current three bubbling beds in the lab scale could be merged to just one bed in the full scale [18]. An annular fluidized bed with a center riser acted as the carbon stripper (CS) to realize the efficient separation of char from the stream of solids [17,18]. The mixtures of oxygen carrier and char particles are carried from the center riser to the upper section. Thereafter, the heavy oxygen carrier particles are carried down into the annular bed by gravity and sent to the AR. The light char particles are still carried up by fluidization gas and then return to the FR. The whole system is placed into an electric furnace. A detailed description of this 30 kW CLC unit can be found in a previous publication [18]. For a typical test, after adding enough oxygen carrier particles into the reactors, the CLC unit was heated from room temperature to the reaction temperature under air as the fluidizing gas. The gas velocity and bed inventories are listed in Table 3. The flow rates of the fluidizing gas were adjusted according to the gas expansion ratio and controlled by mass flow controllers. When a stable solid circulation was achieved among the AR, the FR and the CS, steam, N2, and Ar were switched into specified locations of the FR, the CS, and the loop seals, respectively. The solid circulation rate between the AR and FR was measured by a stacked method, as described in our previous work [18]. After achieving a stable solid circulation and uniform temperature distribution, the solid fuels were added into the fuel reactor to start the CLC experiments. The gas species (O2, CO2, N2) in the AR flue gas were measured by using a gas analyzer (7008 MGA, Signal Group Limited) and a gas chromatograph (GC, Shimadzu GC-2014). The gas species (CO2, CO, CH4, H2, SO2, NO, NO2, N2) from the FR were analyzed by FTIR (Thermo Nicolet, Nexus 670) and gas chromatography. To evaluate the char separation efficiency of the CS, a solid sampling apparatus was applied to obtain the solid particles in the BBII and the loop seal (CS-AR), as mentioned in our previous work [18]. The hot solid was cooled down under an Ar atmosphere to avoid an unexpected reaction with air. The carbon mass fraction in the solid was measured using an elemental analyzer (EURO EA). The oxidation and reduction kinetics of the fresh and tested oxygen carrier particles were investigated by using a microfluidized bed thermogravimetric analysis apparatus (MFB-TGA) [33]. An electric furnace, a bubbling bed reactor, a gas supply system, and a measurement system compose of the MFB-TGA, as shown in Fig. 2 . The reactor with 30 mm i.d. is made of quartz. The fluidizing agents flowed into the reactor bottomby switching magnetic valves. The reactor was put on the mass transducer whose accuracy is 1 mg. The temperature in the bed was measured by using a K-type thermocouple. The stability and more details can be found elsewhere [33,34]. ~ 16 g of silica sand (300–350 μm) was filled as the bed material in a typical test. After heating to 900 °C under a N2 atmosphere, approximately 450 mg oxidized oxygen carriers were added into the bubbling bed. Redox cycles were done by alternating the fluidizing gases between oxidizing gas (21 vol% O2) and reducing gas (10 vol% H2). In addition, a scanning electron microscope (SEM, GEMINISEM 500) was applied to analyze the morphology of the fresh and tested oxygen carriers. To obtain the char separation efficiency (φ CS) of the CS, the carbon mass fraction of the solid sampled from the BBII and the CS-AR loop seal was analyzed. Eq. (1) was used to describe the char separation efficiency (φ CS), indicating that the reduction of the carbon mass fraction in the CS-AR loop seal (c C,LS) was comparable to the carbon mass fraction in the FR dense phase (c C,FR). (1) φ CS = c C , FR − c C , LS c C , FR The carbon capture efficiency (η CC, e, %) by experiments is defined as the amount of the carbon-containing gas stream from the FR ( V ̇ C , FR , mol/s) with respect to the total carbon converted to gas in both the FR and AR ( V ̇ C , FR + V ̇ C , AR , mol/s), as described in Eq. (2). (2) η CC , e = V ̇ C , FR V ̇ C , FR + V ̇ C , AR × 100 = 100 V ̇ FR c CO 2 , FR + c CO , FR + c CH 4 , FR V ̇ FR c CO 2 , FR + c CO , FR + c CH 4 , FR + V ̇ AR c CO 2 , AR where c i, FR and c i, AR are the gas concentrations in the FR and AR flue gas (dry basis, %), respectively. V ̇ C , FR (m3/s) and V ̇ C , AR (m3/s) represent the flowrates of the FR and AR flue gas, respectively, and their values can be obtained according to the nitrogen balance, see Eqs. (3) and (4). (3) V ̇ FR = V ̇ N 2 , FR + V ̇ N 2 , CS c N 2 , FR (4) V ̇ AR = V ̇ N 2 , AR / c N 2 , AR where V ̇ N 2 , FR (m3/s), V ̇ N 2 , CS (m3/s), and V ̇ N 2 , AR (m3/s) are the nitrogen flowrates into the FR, CS, and AR, respectively. Gaseous species in the FR flue gas contain mainly unconverted reducing gases, including CH4, H2, and CO. The oxygen demand (Ω OD, %) is defined to describe the oxygen lacking to achieve complete combustion of the unconverted gases in the FR flue gas; see Eq. (5). y i, FR(mol%) is the molar fraction of gas i in the FR flue gas. Φ0(kg-O2/kg-fuel) is the ratio of oxygen required for combustion to the C of the fuel (per kg). (5) Ω OD = 0.5 y H 2 , FR + 0.5 y CO , FR + 2 y CH 4 , FR Φ 0 y CO 2 , FR + y CO , FR + y CH 4 , FR × 100 The mass transducer recorded the real-time mass signal, m(t), during the experiment. R o,m is the oxygen transport capacity. The conversion levels were described as Eqs. (2) and (3) for the reduction (X r) and oxidation (X o), respectively. m o referred to the mass of the added samples, which was in the full oxidation state, while m r is the fully reduced one. (6) X r t = m o − m t R o , m m o (7) X r t = m t − m r R o , m m o During the experimental tests, the pressure drops and temperatures at different positions of the CLC unit were measured in real time. Stable solid circulation between the AR and FR can be achieved and monitored. As a representative example (see Fig. 3 ), the pressure drop in BBI would decrease when the fuel was fed, since the pyrolysis and gasification of solid fuel increased the gas velocity and decreased the solid volume fraction in the BBI. Approximately 20 min are needed to achieve a new dynamic stability after feeding fuel to the BBI. For the other reactors such as BBII and AR the pressure drops remained stable. Simultaneously, the temperature in the AR dense phase increased by 8–15 °C. The reduced oxygen carrier particles were oxidized, and the slipped char was burned in the AR dense phase, which led to heat release. Approximately 20 min were needed for the temperature to reach a stable state after feeding fuel into the fuel reactor. The concentrations of gas species in the flue gases from FR and AR were measured and analyzed during the operation processes. From Fig. 4(a), when the operation was stable, the concentrations of the main carbon-containing gases, CO2, CO, and CH4, were ~ 82%, ~14%, and ~ 4%, respectively. It should be noted that the inert gases and H2 were excluded in Fig. 4(a), since the used gas analyzers cannot measure H2 content online. The concentrations of the main pollutant gases, SO2, NO, and NO2, were ~ 32 ppm, ~30 ppm and ~ 5 ppm, respectively (see Fig. 4(b)). The gas concentrations from the AR are shown in Fig. 4(c). Approximately 2% CO2 was emitted from the AR, since some amount of char was carried to the AR by the stream of solids. After feeding solid fuel, the O2 concentration was decreased from ~19.6% to ~15%. The consumed oxygen was used to reoxidize the oxygen carrier and burn the char from the CS. The carbon capture efficiency (η CC, e, %) and oxygen demand (Ω OD, %) are presented in Fig. 4(d). The fuel feeding rate was maintained at ~18 g/min in this case. The carbon capture efficiency (η CC, e, %) and oxygen demand (Ω OD, %) were stable at ~70% and ~ 12%, respectively. In this study, twenty experiments in total were conducted in the CLC unit (see Table 4 ). These experiments were carried out using lignite and petroleum coke with different particle sizes, thermal inputs, residence times, etc. Therefore, five series of operational campaigns were obtained to study the effect of various operating conditions, and the total operating time was more than 160 h. Series I-IV aimed to evaluate the influence of the fuel particle size on the char separation efficiency, carbon capture efficiency, and oxygen demand. For each fuel size, the effect of the solid circulation rate and residence time in the FR on the operating performance was designed in series I-IV as well. The residence time is calculated according to the ratio of bed inventories in the fuel reactor to the solid circulation rate between the air reactor and the fuel reactor. Series V was used to compare the carbon capture efficiency and oxygen demand between the lignite and petroleum coke. Petroleum coke char is well known to have a lower activity than lignite char. The thermal input (P fuel, kW) represents the feeding rate of fuel to the fuel reactor. Φ is the oxygen carrier to fuel ratio. This work focus on the evaluation of the effect of fuel size and residence time under a similar temperature of operation. The effect of thermal input on the carbon capture efficiency (η CC, e, %) and oxygen demand (Ω OD, %) were investigated for different fuel sizes, as shown in Fig. 5(a) and (b). The solid circulation rate was controlled at 30 ± 5 g/s as much as possible in these cases. According to Fig. 5(a), the carbon capture efficiency (η CC, e, %) decreased with increasing fuel feeding rate. The carbon capture efficiency (η CC, e, %) had an obvious decrease when increasing the fuel size from 106 to 212 μm to 600–1000 μm. The carbon capture efficiency (η CC, e, %) was ~88% for the 106–212 μm lignite, which was decreased to ~54% for the 600–1000 μm lignite. Usually, the density of char particles is lower than the density of oxygen carrier particles. The larger the char particle is, the lower the char separation efficiency of the annular carbon stripper, according to the fluidization theory and our published work [17]. Therefore, more char transported to the AR led to a lower carbon capture efficiency (η CC, e, %). For the oxygen demand (Ω OD, %), there was a sharp drop when comparing the 212–500 μm lignite with the 106–212 μm lignite (see Fig. 5(b)). The oxygen demand (Ω OD, %) was increased from 25% to 29% when improving the thermal input. The result was caused by the decrease in bed inventory (kg/MW), as found in CSIC 1.5 kW [19]. However, the oxygen demand (Ω OD, %) was hardly impacted in the range of 212–1000 μm, and the result is the same as the research [19]. The highest oxygen demand as the lignite size at 106–212 μm may cause by the fast volatiles release before fuel reaching the bed. In the result, an effective gas- solid contact between the pyrolysis gases and oxygen carrier particles cannot occur in the fuel reactor. The effect of residence time in FR on the carbon capture efficiency (η CC, e, %) and oxygen demand (Ω OD, %) was also investigated, as shown in Fig. 6(a) and (b). The results in Fig. 6(a) show that the carbon capture efficiency (η CC, e, %) was dependent on the residence time and the fuel particle size. The carbon capture efficiency (η CC, e, %) decreased as the residence time decreased and the fuel particle size increased. For the 106–212 μm lignite, the carbon capture efficiency (η CC, %) could reach 97% when a long residence time was applied. The decreasing carbon capture efficiency (η CC, e, %) with the shortening of the residence time was related to the increase in the solid circulation rate between the FR and AR, which led to more ungasified char slipping to the AR. How the fuel particle size impacted the carbon capture efficiency (η CC, e, %) has previously been explained. The carbon capture efficiency (η CC, e, %) was 47% for petroleum coke at 106–212 μm, even if operating at a longer residence time. Petroleum coke is well known to have a slow gasification rate. The unconverted petroleum coke in the FR was transported to the AR with the stream of oxygen carriers, resulting in a low carbon capture efficiency (η CC, e, %). Minor differences were found for the oxygen demand (Ω OD, %) with different solid circulation rates (see Fig. 6(b)). High oxygen demand (Ω OD, %) occurred only when the 106–212 μm lignite was used as fuel. For other fuel size ranges, the oxygen demand (Ω OD, %) was ~10–15%. For petroleum coke at 106–212 μm, the low oxygen demand (Ω OD, %) was 11%. The low oxygen demand (Ω OD, %) was related mainly to the small amounts of unconverted gaseous products, i.e., CO, H2, and CH4 from pyrolysis in the FR, due to the lower volatile content in the petroleum coke. The solid particles sampled from the BBII and CS-AR loop seal were measured and used to calculate the char separation efficiency of the annular CS. Selecting Test 1, Test 6, Test 12, Test 17, and Test 20 as representative of the respective series, the measured carbon mass fraction and calculated char separation efficiency are shown in Fig. 7 . For the larger lignite particle size, the char separation efficiency was lower. The char separation efficiency was 0.89 for 106–212 μm and 0.69 for 212–500 μm and decreased to 0.57 for 500–600 μm and 0.40 for 600–1000 μm. For the 106–212 μm petroleum coke, the char separation efficiency was calculated to be 0.91. Magnetic method has been proposed to recycle the oxygen carrier from ash [35]. To test the performance of the oxygen carrier being tested, the magnetic method was used to separate the oxygen carrier particles from the sampled solid particles (see Fig. 8 ). The separated oxygen carrier (tested oxygen carrier) was measured in the MFB-TGA for the determined redox reactivity, compared with the fresh oxygen carrier, as seen in Fig. 9 . Both the oxidation and reduction kinetics decayed after operation in the CLC unit. The critical conversion (X o) for the oxidation reaction was ~80% regardless of the fresh and tested oxygen carrier (see Fig. 9(a)). However,~5 s for the fresh oxygen carrier and ~ 8 s for the tested oxygen carrier were required to reach the critical conversion. The required time for the 80% conversion level of the reduction reaction was ~60 s for the fresh oxygen carrier and ~ 90 s for the tested oxygen carrier (see Fig. 9(b)). The decay in both oxidation and reduction processes might be attributed to the decrease in of the tested oxygen carrier particles [32]. Fig. 10 shows the SEM results of the fresh and tested oxygen carriers. The surface exhibited a very dense morphology after testing in the CLC processes, indicating that the pore space had a decay, which is not in favor of gas diffusion inside the oxygen carrier particles. One of the important indicators of CLC is the carbon capture efficiency. In the CLC process, there is always some char following the stream of oxygen carriers and slipping to the AR, which can decrease the carbon capture efficiency. To prevent the slipping of char, there are two strategies: 1. separate char particles from oxygen carrier particles with a carbon stripper (CS). The CS can effectively extract char from the mixture of oxygen carrier particles and char particles and enhance the carbon capture efficiency, such as Chalmers 10 kW [3], Chalmers 100 kW [4], CSIC 50 kW [6] and our previous work [18]. 2. guarantee sufficient residence time for char particles in the FR to gasify the char as much as possible. The residence time is related to the solid circulation rate and bed inventories. For a pilot-scale CLC unit, the cyclone could not realize a high separation efficiency [32], leading to some char not being caught and returning to the FR. Therefore, some elutriated char left the FR. Figs. 11 and S1 (seen in the supporting information) illustrate the migration route of carbon in the CLC unit. The carbon conversion in the char (ζ) depends on the separation efficiency of the FR cyclone. The carbon capture efficiency (η CC, m, %) can be described as: (8) η CC , m = 100 − 100 m ̇ C , AR m ̇ fuel x C 1 − β + ζβ where m ̇ C , AR (g/s) devotes the carbon mass transported to the AR and m ̇ fuel (g/s) is the feeding rate of the solid fuel.x C(%) is the carbon mass fraction in the fuel.βis the mass fraction of fixed carbon (x FC, %) to all carbon content in the fuel (x C, %). Finally, the carbon capture efficiency (η CC, m, %) can be described as a function of char separation efficiency (φ CS), residence time in the fuel reactor (t FR) and the carbon stripper (t CS); see Eq. (9). A detailed process of formula derivation from Eq. (8) to Eq. (9) is included in the supporting information. abstract: Chemical looping combustion (CLC) of solid fuels was studied in a 30 kW dual fluidized-bed system coupled with an annular carbon stripper for char separation by using a kaolin-promoted manganese ore oxygen carrier. Two solid fuels, a lignite and a petroleum coke, were tested in the CLC unit. The CLC unit was operated continuously for more than 160 h. The effects of the fuel size and residence time in the fuel reactor on the carbon capture efficiency and oxygen demand were investigated. The char separation efficiency of the annular carbon stripper reached 90%, and a maximum carbon capture efficiency of 97% was achieved for the 106–212 μm lignite. The carbon capture efficiency was affected by the fuel particle size and residence time. When >212 μm lignite particles were used, there was a ~ 12% oxygen demand. The carbon capture efficiency in the experiment with petroleum coke was only 45% due to the slow gasification rate. A model was applied to analyze the influence of key factors on the carbon capture efficiency. The redox kinetics of the tested oxygen carrier were studied by a micro-fluidized bed thermogravimetric analysis method.
title: Conversion characteristics of lignite and petroleum coke in chemical looping combustion coupled with an annular carbon stripper

full_text: Biodiesel has been recognized as a clean and renewable fuel. The promising biodiesel of fatty acid methyl esters (FAMEs) has received much attention in recent years [1]. FAMEs can be produced from the transesterification and esterification with short-chain alcohol in the presence of acid or base catalysts [2–4]. In the processes of biodiesel production, the homogeneous acid and alkaline catalysts are often hindered by corrosion and additional purification [5]. Meanwhile, with a heterogeneous catalytic system, the catalysts are recyclable and easily separable, which simplify the steps of biodiesel purification [6,7]. In addition, solid acid catalysts are excellent alternatives to other catalysts, owning to their simultaneously catalyzing the esterification and transesterification reactions with low-grade, highly-acidic and water-containing oils without soap formation [8,9]. It is observed that solid acid-catalyzed biodiesel production reactions are slow and need a longer reaction time and a higher temperature to obtain relatively high reaction rate and better biodiesel yield [10,11]. However, the application of heterogeneous acidic catalysts in industrial production of biodiesel is limited by minimal energy consumption principle in manufacturing. Thus, the preparation of highly active heterogeneous acidic catalysts under moderate catalytic conditions is critical for the efficient application. Recently, many types of heterogeneous acidic catalysts, such as heteropolyacids, ion exchange resins, zeolites and sulfated metal oxide etc. [8,12], have been developed to meet requirements as described. Among the solid acid catalysts, sulfated zirconia showed superior performance in biodiesel production with its plenty of strong acid sites [13]. Conventional sulfated zirconia has been usually prepared by two-step (precipitation-impregnation) method with the sulfating agent of sulfuric acid or (NH4)2SO4 solution [14]. The conventional sulfated zirconia is commonly used by many researchers as heterogeneous catalyst in the production of biodiesel. For example, Kiss et al. [15] reported the esterification of dodecanoic acid with 2-ethylhexanol catalyzed by the sulfated zirconia. It exhibited the best esterification conversion of 70% at 160°C. Jitputti et al. [16] also reported the transesterification of crude palm kernel oil with methanol catalyzed by the catalyst. The highest fatty acid methyl ester yield was over 90% at 200°C. Obviously, the conventional sulfated zirconia has displayed good catalytic performance on relatively high reaction temperature. In addition, Zhang et al. [17] studied the activity of modified sulfated zirconia prepared by chlorosulfonic acid impregnation, showing complete conversion of simulated crude rice bran oil into FAMEs biodiesel at low reaction temperature (120°C). However, the catalyst was synthesized by complicated multi-step and hazardous chemical. Recently, one-pot modified preparation method has attracted considerable attention, due to its simple and facile synthesis procedure. Sang et al. [18] reported that a one-step route was used to synthesize the mesoporous sulfated zirconia nanoparticles using the aqueous solution of sodium dodecyl sulfate and Zr(OC3H7)4. The catalyst exhibited the highest yield of fatty acid esters (98%) in transesterification of soybean oil with methanol at 120°C. However, it is an intricate process, which involves the hydrolysis of organic zirconium in the precursor solution. Lately, we have reported effect of a simple one-pot route of synthesizing nanocrystal zirconia [19]. The preparation of the nanocrystal zirconia was obtained by vapor phase hydrolysis using anhydrous alcohol solvent, which could efficiently control the hydrolysis of organic zirconium precursor. However, whether the corresponding modified sulfated zirconia can be directly synthesized by one-pot route with an effective sulfated agent - ammonium persulfate [20] is still to be discussed. What is more, the catalytic activity of this catalyst in the conversion of expired oil to biodiesel under mild conditions has not been explored. In this work, a direct synthesis of solid super acid catalyst (S2O8 2−/ZrO2) by one-pot method is proposed. The X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscope (TEM), and NH3-temperature programmed desorption (NH3-TPD) analyses have been employed to connect the physical characteristics and acid property of the catalysts with their performance in the transesterification of expired oil. The best performance of the solid acid catalyst was also studied by optimizing the reaction parameters, including those of reaction temperature, reaction time and molar ratio of oil to methanol. Finally, recyclability of the catalyst was evaluated under the determined optimum conditions. Zirconium n-propoxide (Zr(OC3H7)4) with 80wt.% in n-propanol was purchased from Aladdin. Benzyl alcohol, methanol, ethanol and ammonium persulfate ((NH4)2S2O8) were supplied by Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Institute. S2O8 2−/ZrO2 used in this study was prepared in a modified method based on the procedure reported by Shi et al. [19], but with the addition of ammonium persulfate as sulfur source. In a typical procedure, 0.959g zirconium n-butoxide was dissolved into a 12mL benzylalcohol and stirred for a while. Then the solution was poured into the weighing bottle with 1.14g ammonium persulfate, which was placed in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with deionized water and heated to 140°C for 24h. On completion of the synthesis period, the precipitate was obtained using centrifuge, washed twice with ethanol and dried in an oven at 60°C overnight. The samples were calcined in muffle furnace at various temperatures (500°C, 600°C and 700°C) for different calcination duration (1h, 3h and 5h) to determine the optimum calcination condition. The catalysts were labeled as S2Zx-y, where “x” stands for the calcination temperature and “y” the calcination duration. For example, S2Z500-1 stands for the catalyst calcined at 500°C for 1h. The XRD patterns of sulfated zirconia catalysts were recorded on Shimadzu XRD-6000 with Cu Kα radiation at 20°–70°, 2θ in steps of 8°. TEM images were taken on a JEM-2100F high resolution transmission electronic microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The acidity of the catalyst was measured with NH3-TPD experiment. Thermal conductivity detector (TCD) was used for the ammonia desorption profile. In a typical run, 0.1g of the catalyst was heated in an helium flow at 300°C for 1h and then cooled down to 120°C. Next, the sample was allowed to saturate with NH3 at 120°C. Subsequently, it was treated with dry helium for 30min to eliminate the weakly adsorbed NH3 until then the temperature increased to 600°C at a rate of 10°C·min−1 and kept constant for a given time period. The total sulfur content was measured by using a YX-DL 8300 type compact sulfur detector (Youxin Equipment Manufacture Co., Ltd., China) with measuring accuracy of 0.001%. The reactions were performed according to batch catalytic experiment in a 50mL stainless steel autoclave with Teflon wall and autogenous pressure with constant stirring (600rpm). The total volume of the reaction mixture and typical catalyst concentration were kept constant, being at 20mL and 3wt.% of the weight of the oil, respectively. The predetermined amounts of catalysts, the commercial edible grade oil (an expired soybean oil) and methanol were sealed in the reactor. When the set temperature was reached, the stirrer began working and it was timed. After a preset reaction time, the reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature and filtered to separate the solid catalyst. The methanol was then removed by a rotary evaporator, so glycerol could be separated by gravity filtration. Consequently, the remaining liquid was diluted with pyridine and analyzed for its compounds content. To optimize the reaction condition, parameters such as reaction time, reaction temperature and molar ratio of the reactants were adjusted. Studies of the processes were conducted in the reaction time from 1h to 6h, at temperature ranging from 90°C to 150°C, by varying the molar ratio of methanol to oil in the range of 6:1 to 30:1 (with excess of methanol). The reference products were analyzed and quantified using gas chromatograph equipped with an AS-200 automatic injection, a flame ionization detector and an Agilent DB 5ht capillary separation column. The column oven temperature was programmed from 70 to 190°C (at the rate of 15°C·min−1) and then raised to 260°C at 7°C·min−1 and to 380°C at 30°C·min−1 and maintained at this temperature for 6min. The injector and detector temperatures were set at 360°C and 380°C, respectively. In this experiment, the conversion of oil based on the amount of the oil collected in the product was calculated according to the following formula: Conversion of oil % = 100 - mass of unreaction oil init i al mass of oil × 100 The XRD patterns of the sulfated zirconia prepared with different calcination processes were shown in Fig. 1 . Clearly, the XRD pattern of S2Z500-1 presented broad peaks in the 2θ regions of 20°–40° and 40°–70°, indicating the existence of amorphous structure, which were similar to the sulfated zirconia reported by Garcia et al. [21]. However, pure ZrO2 with low intensity XRD composed of tetragonal nanocrystals was synthesized in the presence of benzyl alcohol solvent, as had been reported earlier [19]. Meanwhile, the TEM image of S2Z500-1 (Fig. 2 ) showed the presence of ill-defined network aggregated particles, in which small and randomly distributed patterns of fringes were observed. Hence, the amorphous structure could be interpreted as relating to the existence of tetragonal zirconia microcrystallites, which was in agreement with the observation reported by Morterra [22]. It can be concluded that the growth and orderliness of the crystals were influenced by the factor of addition of sulfur source in the synthesis process, while, the calcination duration did not have a significant impact on the crystalline structure, which could be seen in the XRD patterns. As the calcination temperature (600°C) increased, the intensity of the tetragonal phase obviously increased with the growth of the crystalline in the framework, but the width of the peaks were much broader, which suggested that zirconia particles in S2Z600-1 were still small. Relevant researches reported that further increase in the calcination temperature leaded to instability of tetragonal zirconia phase due to the decomposition of sulfate groups or persulfate ions [23,24]. In our elemental analysis (Table 1 ), obvious loss of sulfur was noticed in the series of samples and the crystalline phase transformed into monoclinic phase when the calcination temperature reached 700°C. NH3-temperature desorption is usually used as a measurement of total acidity and acid strength distribution in the solids. Fig. 3 illustrated the acid sites distribution for the S2Z calcined for 1h at different temperatures. The sample calcined at 500°C exhibited two groups of temperature peaks, at about 380°C and 600°C, corresponding to the medium and the super acid sites on the surface of zirconia. Similar types of intermediate and strong acid strengths were reported for sulfated zirconia by others [25]. Noticeably, the acid sites in S2Z500 showed unexpectedly intense peak at >500°C, suggesting the presence of strong Brønsted acidic sites with large amount of effectively combined sulfate ions [3]. However, the intensity of super acid sites peaks decreased significantly when the calcination temperature increased to 600°C, corresponding to the decomposition of the active component sulfate groups [26], which was in accord with the result of sulfur content measurement as shown in Table 1. Moreover, the disappearance of the peak in S2Z700 as a result of the removal of sulfate ions and the existence of monoclinic phase was evident in the XRD patterns. For S2Z700, only one broad peak remained which belonged to inter-mediate strength acid on the surface of zirconia. NH3-TPD profiles of S2Z500 calcined for different duration were illustrated in Fig. 4 . Clearly, the calcination duration only affected the amount of the super acid. As the calcination time extended, the super acid content gradually decreased. Compared with the two NH3-TPD figures (Figs. 3, 4), it was found that the calcination procedure not only affected the acid quantity, but also changed the surface character of the catalyst, resulting in a distinct change in the acid strength distribution. Hence, different catalytic reaction conditions and consequence from the existent results were expected. To obtain better reaction conversion and find a suitable catalyst among the prepared sulfated zirconia, transesterification of oil and methanol was carried out under specific conditions of methanol to oil molar ratio 20:1, 6h and at different reaction temperatures, the results of which were summarized in Table 1. It was clear that S2Z700 showed an unsatisfactory conversion of 15.6% even at 150°C due to its owning of monoclinic phase and lacking of super acid. Although, S2Z600 composed of common tetragonal phase and mainly medium acid sites showed a high conversion of 93.7%, the reaction temperature of 150°C was still the limitation. Remarkably, the S2Z500 containing quantities of super acid kept a constant conversion of 100%, even when the reaction temperature was reduced to 120°C. Taking into account the previous NH3-TPD results, we could easily observe that the catalytic activity is dependent on both the content of the strong acid and the structure properties. Hence, the optimum calcination temperature was found to be 500°C in all the cases. In order to further optimize the catalyst, the effect of calcination duration and reaction temperature on the conversion of oil with S2Z500 was investigated, as shown in Fig. 5 . Although the activities of the three S2Z500 catalysts calcined for different duration were similar at the temperature of 110–120°C, remarkable difference in conversion effect appeared as the reaction temperature was reduced. Especially, when the reaction temperature was 100°C, the conversion with S2Z500-5 and S2Z500-3 were about 45% and 78%, respectively. But, the performance of S2Z500-1 at 100°C was much better than that of others, which was in accordance with the content of acid of the catalysts [27]. Three S2Z500 catalysts were not active for the transesterification of oil at a temperature below 100°C. Our experiments confirmed that higher quantity of strong acid of the catalysts was influencing the transesterification reaction of lower reaction temperature. Normally, the decreasing of viscosity of the oil and the lowering of the mass transfer limit at higher reaction temperatures favor the methanol nucleophilic collision on the triglyceride, resulting in an increase in reaction rate [28,29]. In our experiment, it was clear that, for S2Z500-1 (Fig. 5), the oil conversion increased sharply from 40.8% to 97.4% when the reaction temperature rose from 90°C to 100°C. When the temperature increased further, the increasing tendency of the oil conversion, now being 100%, was not remarkable. Considering the minimal energy cost and maximum conversion, 110°C was considered as an optimal temperature and was used for further studies. As is commonly held, the methanol to oil molar ratio should be higher than that of stoichiometric ratio in the transesterification of oil in order to drive the reaction towards completion and produce more methyl esters as product [30]. From Fig. 6 , the oil conversion rate was evidently higher when smaller amount of methanol was used in the initial period, which here could be explained by the competitive adsorption of reactants on the catalyst and the fact that the active sites were mainly dominated by triglyceride molecules. Especially, in this experiment, when the molar ratio of methanol to oil was 6:1, the conversion in the first hour was up to 68%, although only improved slightly as the time increased. As is usually observed, methanol functioned as a reactant as well as a solvent to extract the production in this type of reversible reaction [31], so a modicum of methanol should have also limited desorption of the product from the catalyst surface. Although excessive methanol reduced the initial reaction rate, with the extension of reaction time to 2h, the reaction proceeded quickly in the ideal direction due to the high concentration of reactants and the gradually generated FAMEs [32]. This greatly increased the solubility of the mixed system, so that the reaction rate in this period was the fastest in the whole reaction as seen in Fig. 6. As most of the reactant turned into the FAMEs, the reaction rate gradually slowed down. The maximum conversion was obtained in the shortest time of 4h, when the molar ratio was close to 20:1. In addition, excessive methanol lowered the concentration of soybean oil in the mixture and thus lowered the reaction rate [10]. The reaction rate decreased in the beginning 3h when the molar ratio was increased to 30:1, but the conversion could still remain 100% after 4h of reaction. Therefore, it could be concluded that to promote the conversion, an excess methanol feed was favored, to a certain extent. Recyclability tests were conducted with S2Z500-1 in the transesterification of oil. The catalyst recycling studies were carried out by separating the used catalysts after 4h of reaction and reusing them without pretreatment in a subsequent reaction cycle. The results of the tests carried out with the reused S2Z500-1 were shown in Table 2 . It could be observed that the activity developed in the second use of the sample was lower although it remained 67.4% of conversion. In the successive runs, its activity dropped at every reuse of the catalyst. The conversion in the fourth transesterification run was only 25.6%. Moreover, a comparative test (see Table 2) confirmed that the catalyst was active for the simultaneous esterification of oleic acid and the transesterification of soybean oil with a yield of 100%. According to Kiss et al. [5], the catalyst deactivation was due to hydrolysis of sulfated zirconia in aqueous media, but there was no leaching of sulfate groups when only a small amount of water was present in the transesterification phase. However, Garcia et al. [21] showed a quick deactivation of sulfated zirconia when only small amounts of water was present, and it was suggested that the small amount of leaching sulfate in the reaction mixture was not the only cause for the catalyst rapid deactivation. Moreover, Suwannakarn et al. [33] studied the activities and stability of sulfated zirconia in for the liquid-phase transesterification and reported that the catalyst deactivated with subsequent reaction cycles due to the removing of sulfate ion species from catalyst surface as sulfuric acid. Therefore, in order to evaluate the stability of the catalyst, we also analyzed the total content of sulfate groups in the reaction product mixture and glycerol simultaneously with the recycle tests. The sulfate content was detected by high temperature calcination-coulomb titration method. According to the results, the sulfate was not detected in all reaction product mixture, but found in the glycerol. This meant that the sulfate had been dissolved into this polar media and not into the non polar media (biodiesel). It is due to that monoalkyl hydrogen sulfate and dialkyl sulfate, formed by methanol and sulfuric acid in solution [33], are soluble in the polar media, but hardly soluble in the aliphatic hydrocarbons. These indicate that the leaching of sulfate is another important reason for the catalyst deactivation. The S2O8 2−/ZrO2, prepared via a simple synthesis route, which was treated with (NH4)2S2O8, exhibited excellent performance for the biodiesel production from expired soybean oil. A complete conversion of oil catalyzed by S2O8 2−/ZrO2, calcined at 500°C for 1h, was obtained when the methanol to oil molar ratio was 20:1, reaction temperature 110°C and reaction time 4h. The existence of microcrystallines with tetragonal phase, as proved by the results of TEM, and plenty of S2O8 2− produced by one-pot method, were important factors contributing to the formation of considerable super acid in the catalyst, which worked for the high catalytic activity under lower reaction temperature. Moreover, this experiment brought about an effective method to synthesize stable sulfated zirconia with high activity. This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51172154, 51451002) and Shanxi Province Science Foundation for Youths (201302 1008-3). Supplementary material Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.fuproc.2016.01.021.
abstract: A solid super acid catalyst, S2O8 2−/ZrO2, was prepared by a new synthesis route of one-pot method with ammonium persulfate via vapor phase hydrolysis to improve biodiesel production using expired soybean oil as reactant. Catalysts were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscope (TEM), and NH3-temperature programmed desorption (NH3-TPD) to determine the optimum pretreatment conditions and understand the role of the active sites for transesterification. S2O8 2−/ZrO2 calcined at 500°C, which showed an amorphous phase by XRD characterization, was identified as composed of tetragonal phase ZrO2 microcrystallites by TEM examination. The microcrystallites which anchored more S2O8 2− had abundant super acid and displayed high catalytic activity in the transesterification under mild reaction conditions. The reaction parameters over the catalyst were optimized and both the conversion of soybean oil and selectivity of biofuel reached 100% under the conditions of 110°C of reaction temperature, 4h of reaction time and 20:1 of methanol to oil molar ratio. Moreover, the catalyst showed high stability in the reactions and no sulfur leaching was found in the biodiesel products in cyclic utilization.
title: Mild synthesis of biofuel over a microcrystalline S2O82−/ZrO2 catalyst
