full_text: As most of the hydrogen used in fuel cell vehicles today, comes from carbonaceous raw material, especially from natural gas and naphtha [1–3], it is desirable to reduce the carbon footprint by using renewable fuels. Various options for renewable hydrogen have been considered including fuels such as biogas, biomass producer gas, methanol, ammonia and more complex biofuels like vegetable oil [4–6]. Biodiesel, the monoalkyl esters of vegetable oils or animal fats [7], is one such fuel, widely applied in diesel engines [8]. The biggest advantage that biodiesel has over gasoline and petroleum diesel is its environmental friendliness [7,9,10]. Since biodiesel is made entirely from vegetable oil, it contains little sulfur, aromatic hydrocarbon and metal. Further the higher heating values (HHVs) of biodiesels are relatively high [11]. The HHVs of biodiesels (39 to 41MJkg−1) are slightly lower than that of gasoline (46MJkg−1), petro diesel (43MJkg−1) or petroleum (42MJkg−1), but higher than coal (32 to 37MJkg−1) [12,13] with reasonable cost and availability [14]. Here we consider the use of biodiesel as a fuel for internally reforming solid oxide fuel cells (SOFCs). In previous studies of biofuels in SOFCs, Saunders [15] reported that certain mixtures of isooctane, water, alcohol and surfactant produced beneficial results with remarkably low carbon deposition, which is less than 1% of the molecular carbon in a SOFC operated at 800°C. Preece [16] studied the electrical performance of liquid ethers in micro tubular SOFCs, showing that C―O bonds were better than C―C bonded molecules in reducing damage caused by carbon deposition. Murray et al. [17] investigated the use of direct SOFCs with DME, DME plus air, and DME plus N2 using Ni/YSZ anodes. Addition of oxygen promoted DME decomposition into primarily H2, CO, and CH4 at temperatures as low as 550°C. The decomposition was 50 to 100°C lower than that of pure DME, thereby improving the low temperature performance of DME as an SOFC fuel. A maximum 0.75Wcm−2 was recorded with 33% DME at 650°C. Zhou et al. [18] studied the direct utilization of waste cooking oil over ceria, ceria-Cu, and ceria-Rh based SOFC's. Pure ceria, ceria-Cu fuel cells exhibited power densities of 0.60 and 0.10Wcm−2, respectively. Power density increased from 0.1 to 0.250Wcm−2 with addition of Rh to pure ceria anodes. Ceria-Rh fuel cell exhibited stable operation over 100h with waste vegetable oil without dilution. Power density of 0.250Wcm−2 was obtained at fuel supply rate of 1μl/min; voltage and current density did not change up to 0.8Acm−2. McPhee et al. [19] reported power density of 0.117Wcm−2 and current density of 0.217Acm−2 with the use of liquid tin anodes operated on biodiesel flow rates of 100μl/min. From the literature it is clear that oxygenated hydrocarbons like methanol, dimethyl ether, biodiesel and waste cooking oil can be directly utilized in SOFCs. Addition of oxygen has a positive effect on utilization of hydrocarbons. Further increasing the oxygen content of hydrocarbons decreases the carbon deposition on the nickel cermet [20]. Biodiesel is one of such oxygenated hydrocarbons with limited investigations as fuel for internal reforming solid oxide fuel cells (SOFCs). The objective of this study was to show that certain biodiesel formulations could improve the performance of internally reforming SOFCs. Addition of water to biodiesel formulations would increase hydrogen production by steam reforming of biodiesel and help in the removal of carbon formed on the anode surface, formed by various complex reactions. The following section describes the preparation of fuel cell and the construction of SOFC test rig. Fig. 1 shows the various steps in the preparation of SOFCs. The basic cells used in the testing (Fig. 1, 1.1), were obtained from Adaptive Materials Inc, USA. The cell consists of NiO/YSZ supported micro-tubes of 5.5cm in length, 0.22cm diameter. Two layers of cathode were painted on the raw tubes. The cathode was necessary to provide adequate reaction sites for the electrochemical reduction of oxygen to its ions. The porosity, tortuosity and pore sizes were taken into consideration in order to produce an optimized cathode layer. To do this, two distinct cathode layers were synthesized; (a) a 50%/50% — LSM/YSZ layer and (b) 100% — LSM layer. The composition of cathode formulation is tabulated in Table 1 . The powders of the two mixtures were weighed out and mixed in separate bottles according to the compositions specified in Table 1 and acetone was used as the mixing solvent. The two compositions were then milled in a vibro-miller for 24h after which 2g of Terpinol was added into the 100% LSM mixture followed by additional vibro-milling for 10min. The cathode formulation is followed by painting on the raw tubes. About 30mm of the 50%/50% — LSM/YSZ cathode ink is painted on the raw tubes and allowed to dry under atmospheric conditions for 24h. This is followed by painting the 100% — LSM cathode ink on the previously painted 50%/50% — LSM/YSZ ink with 12h drying time (Fig. 1, 1.2). Cathode layers were painted in such a way that there was space on either sides for the manifold and the injector (Fig. 1, 1.2) on which the fuel cells are suspended. The cathodes painted cells were sintered in a Carbiolite™ furnace under a programmed temperature conditions. . Four distinct stages were undertaken during the sintering process, the first stage consisted of a slow ramp up at 1.5°C/min to 500°C during which the binder and solvent were burnt out. The second is the rapid ramp up at 12.5°C/min to the sintering temperature. The third phase was the sintering at 1150°C for 2h so that the two cathode layers adhere to the tube. The final phase was the cooling at 20°C/min to room temperature, it should be noted that the micro-tubes are very robust which is why they can be cooled at this high rate, other fuel cell geometries are not able to withstand such rapid cooling [21]. The cooled tubes coated with cathode were reduced with 20ml/min of hydrogen for 45min. Silver ink (Alfa Aesar) was used to improve the electrical conductivity of the cathode; it was painted in four rings along the circumference of the micro-tube with one long stripe connecting the four bands (Fig. 1, 1.3). The silver rings were made as thin as possible so as to minimize masking the cathode area with silver paint. Approximately 40% of the cathode area was covered by the silver paint. About 10cm of silver wire was left un-twined for attachment to the potentiostat. For the bulk of this study, the anode current collection was achieved by carefully polishing off the thin electrolyte, with the aid of a fine abrasive files to expose the Ni surface (Fig. 1, 1.3). Approximately 7mm was polished off one of the ends of the micro-tube and care was taken not to exert too much force as the cells were quite brittle. Subsequently the Ni surface was wiped off with clean cloth to remove the abrasive sediments and silver ink was painted so as to enhance conductivity between the silver wire and the Ni anode. The painted silver was allowed to dry for 6h. Subsequently, silver wire (Good fellow 99.9% pure, 0.25mm diameter) was wound firmly around the silver bands for current collection. The length of the silver wire used was 50cm. The silver wires were wound four times on each silver ring and then carefully twinned along the stripe line to the next ring. Passing the wire along the stripe line helped to maximize the cathode area for effective oxygen reduction reaction. The silver wire was wound around the whole painted section for maximum contact and approximately 10cm of wire was left unwound for connection to the potentiostat. Cell manifolds were prepared using Macor blocks, cut into cubes (1cm×1cm×1cm) as shown in Fig. 1, 1.4. Perpendicular holes were drilled on two faces of the cubes of 3mm and 6mm diameters. An alumina tube of length 15cm was inserted in the 6mm hole and was sealed with cement. The assembly was dried for more than 24h and was used as a manifold for connecting the cell to a vaporizer. Reduced tubular SOFCs were inserted into the 3mm hole of manifold and fixed in place with FortaFix AL/CS adhesive. These cells with current collectors were placed in the manifolds as shown in (Fig. 1, 1.4). The microstructure of raw and the reduced microtubular SOFC are shown in Fig. 2 . SEM characterization of the sintered layer showed a cathode thickness of approximately 50μm. The hand painting of the cathode does not give a uniform microstructure due to natural imperfections; this makes the cathode thicker in certain areas and thinner in other. However, the difference in performance between cells prepared in this manner was not significant. The SOFC test rig along with the furnace used for the evaluation of the cell performance is represented in Fig. 3 . The test rig (Fig. 3, 3.1) consisted of a furnace and a gas supplying system constructed by using Swagelok 1/8″ stainless steel tubing, connections and valves. The twin-chamber furnace (Fig. 3, 3.2) used in the testing was purpose-built using Thermal Ceramics insulating fire bricks and nichrome ribbon (Advent Ni80/Cr20 1.5mm×0.13mm) elements, and could accommodate the fuel evaporator and a manifolded fuel cell The Nichrome ribbons were wound around ceramic rods which were embedded in the fire brick (Fig. 3, 3.2). Power was supplied to the furnace by Eurotherm™ 2404 controller. All gasses were delivered via mass flow controllers (Unit Instruments 7300) to the SOFC test rig. Gasses were not preheated, as they were passed through heated evaporation chamber. Biodiesel formulations were pumped to SOFC test rig using peristaltic pumps as shown in Fig. 3, 3.1. The formulations were evaporated in an evaporator maintained at 400°C (Fig. 3, 3.2). Helium was used as a carrier gas. The evaporated mixture was then feed to the fuel cell maintained at 800°C. The exhaust gasses from the fuel cell could be sent to analytical tools like mass spectrometer for analysis, or vented if analysis was not required. The electrical performance of SOFC's was measured with a purpose-built potentiostat capable of varying electrical load as required. Current and voltage values were varied and logged using the Lab VIEW program. Transesterification process for producing biodiesel was selected due to its simplicity and industrial acceptance for preparation of biodiesel. Fig. 4 represents the experimental setup used in the preparation of biodiesel and test emulsion formulations. Methyl ester was synthesized in a batch type reactor (Fig. 4, 4.1) using sodium hydroxide as catalyst as per the method given by Lang et al. [22]. The reaction was carried out using 100% excess alcohol, i.e. molar ratio of methanol to oil of 6:1 and catalyst concentration of 1%. Sodium methoxide was prepared by dissolving 3.4g of sodium hydroxide in 200ml methanol, which was added to hot oil and the reaction was carried out in the reaction flask with temperature maintained between 45 and 50°C. For branched chain alcohols like butanol we need to heat the reaction flask in order to dissolve the alkaline catalyst inside butanol. This heating process was not necessary in methanol usage. Gravity separation was used to separate glycerol after completion of the reaction. The catalyst was removed by hot water washings and complete removal of the catalyst was checked by phenolphthalein indicator. Ethyl and butyl esters of rapeseed oil were prepared in a similar fashion as methyl ester. The transesterification was carried out at 70°C for 2h with vigorous agitation. In case of ethyl esters with low purity alcohol (96%) and very high alcohol and oil ratio to 20:1, does not yield esters [23]. The purity of ethanol to be used in the reaction must be as high as 200 proof in order to obtain high conversion. To avoid the emulsion formation after the transesterification reaction, 0.1% aqueous tannic acid was used as a washing solution to remove catalyst [24]. After the washing process, it was observed that the separation of water from ethyl and butyl esters takes longer time in comparison with methyl ester. This result is in accordance with the literature. Higher alcohols viz ethanol and n-butanol have a tendency to absorb more water during the washing process than methanol, as expressed in the literature [22]. With mild agitation in a flask, the alcohol and most of the soap could be removed by three washes. Mix esters were prepared in the same reactor at 25°C with vigorous agitation for 40min. Different molar ratios of methanol and ethanol (such as 3:3,5:3 and 3:5) were used for the transesterification reaction, keeping the molar ratio of alcohol to oil as 6:1 to prepare mix esters. In the paper from this point forward, mix esters with methanol/ethanol ratio 3:3, 5:3 and 3:5 is represented by M/E=1, M/E=5/3 and E/M=5/3 respectively. n-Butanol was used to prepare sodium butoxide in case of butyl ester preparation. The formation of butoxide ion was very slow as compared with ethoxide and methoxide. Therefore the formation of butyl esters was very slow compared to all the other forms of esters. Various combinations of formulation were prepared using a high shear mixer (4 LRT Silverson, UK) shown in Fig. 4, 4.2. Two types of emulsifying agents were used i.e. sodium lauryl sulfate (SLS) and SPAN 80. The amount of emulsifier was varied from 0.1 to 10% of the dispersed phase. In order to prepare 20% biodiesel formulations, 80g of biodiesel along with 8g of SPAN 80 was accurately measured in a beaker. To this mixture 320g of distilled water was added and the contents of the beaker were mixed for 15min in a high shear mixture. The mixture was mixed at 6000rpm for 10min and gradually the speed was increased to 8000rpm for the last 5min. The samples were placed in centrifuge tubes and were observed for about 6 to 8h. Similar procedure was adopted for making emulsions from methyl and M/E=5/3 mix esters. Same procedure was adopted for the preparation of 50% emulsion, but with higher amount of emulsifier 15g. It was observed that emulsions made from SPAN 80 were stable as compared with emulsions made from SLS or combination of both emulsifiers. Ethyl ester, M/E=1 mix ester and E/M=5/3 mix ester formulations formed stable emulation without emulsifiers, while butyl, methyl and M/E=5/3 mix ester formulations required emulsifiers. The following section discusses the formation of various esters prepared from rapeseed oil using ethyl, methyl and butyl alcohols. There were various parameters which are determined in the characterization of biodiesel like mono, di, tri glycerides content, free and total glycerol content, sulfur content, iodine number, acid value, ash content, water content [25]. But the most important parameter affecting the performance of the fuel cell operated on biodiesel formulations is the ester content. Hence the formation of various esters is studied with respect to time. Fig. 5 shows the formation of esters with respect to time. The ester contents increased rapidly at the start and as time progressed, there was not much difference in the ester content. Oleic acid was the major component of the ester (around 60%). Formation of ester was as low as 60% in most of the cases till 30min and with time the ester content increases to about 90%. Due to the slow reactivity of the alkoxide ion the formation of ethyl esters was slower as compared to methyl esters. On same grounds the formation of butyl ester was slower as compared to methyl and ethyl esters. Therefore the formation of butyl ester was not shown in Fig. 5. In the transesterification reaction the reactivity of the alkoxides ion is the rate determining step. Reactivity of methoxide is more than the ethoxide anion. The reason for this is, as the length of the carbon chain increases, nucleophilicity of the alkoxides anion decreases leading to a decrease in the reactivity of alkoxide anion [24]. This causes the slower reaction rate of ethanol than methanol, when mix alcohol is used for the preparation of biodiesel. Due to the non polar nature of rapeseed oil and polarity of ethanol, rapeseed oil is more soluble in ethanol as compared with methanol. Therefore ethanol contributes to better solubility of oil as compared to methanol resulting in less mass transfer limitations, which results in high rate of formation of esters. But the rate of formation of ethyl esters was slower in the mix alcohol biodiesel [24]. In case of M/E=1 mix ester the formation of ethyl esters was 50% than that of the methyl esters. Even though the molar ratio of methanol to ethanol was 1, the formation of ethyl esters was much lower than the methyl esters due to reason mentioned above. As the molar ratio of methanol to ethanol was increased from 3:3 to 5:3, the formation of ethyl esters decreased. The use of higher methanol to ethanol molar ratio 5:3, resulted in the lower formation of ethyl esters [26]. Since biodiesel using butanol could not be produced by alkaline transesterification process for 48h, acid (H2SO4)-catalyzed transesterification reaction was carried out. Biodiesel could not be produced with 6:1 molar ratios of 1-butanol to oil with 3% H2SO4 at 110°C for 24h, however when the reaction time was extended to 48h, glycerol phase separation could be obtained. Biodiesel essentially is an alkyl ester obtained by transesterification (alcoholysis) of fat or oil with an alcohol, with an alkali or acid as a catalyst to improve the reaction rate and yield. Excess alcohol is used to shift the equilibrium to the products side due to the reversible nature of the reaction. Oil or fat contains saturated and unsaturated fatty acids like palmitic, stearic, myristic, oleic, linoleic, and linolenic [27]. During transesterification process fatty acids are converted to alkyl esters of fatty acids methyl/ethyl depending upon the type of alcohol used in the transesterification. The major component of beef tallow, olive oil, canola, peanut and sunola oil is oleic acid [28]. While linoleic acid is the major component in rapeseed and crambe oil [12]. After transesterification of triglycerides, the products are a mixture of esters, glycerol, alcohol, catalyst and tri-, di- and mono glycerides. Obtaining pure esters was not easy, since there were impurities in the esters, such as di- and mono glycerides [29]. Typical composition of rapeseed methyl and ethyl ester was C16:0 =2.7%, C18:0 =11.4, C18:1 =21.9%, C18:2 =13.1% and C22:1 =50.9%. There are two reactions occurring in the fuel cell steam reforming and decomposition (cracking). At high temperatures the unreacted oil cracks to form CO, CO2, CH4, and C2H4. The typical gas phase composition obtained by thermal cracking of vegetable oil are CO 14.6%, CO2 9.6%, CH4 13.6%, C2H2 2.5%, C2H4 40.8, C2H6 4.4%, C3 + 14.5%. Thermal cracking of vegetable oils starts at 250–350°C by cleavage of the ester bonds to yield free fatty acid chains. Fatty acids will be liberated by cleavage of the ester bonds, a fraction of the fatty acids will decompose, and the cracking products will be steam reformed simultaneously with the remaining fatty acids to produce hydrogen and carbon monoxide. The predominant route for the conversion of fatty acids to hydrogen will depend on the relative rates of the processes (i.e., cracking of the fatty acids and steam reforming of the cracking products vs. direct reforming of the fatty acids) [30]. Hydrogen is also produced by proton extraction from processes such as the formation of cycloolefins and aromatic hydrocarbons as well as coke formation by polymerization of olefins and aromatics, polycondensation of oil, and splitting of hydrocarbon molecules into its elements. It is also produced by dehydrogenation of olefins to diolefins and acetylenic hydrocarbons [31]. Decomposition of the triglyceride molecule of oil, produces long-chain fatty acids (RCOOH), ketones (RCOR′), aldehydes (RCHO), and esters (RCOOR′). Carbon monoxide is formed from the decarbonylation of oxygenated hydrocarbons such as ketones, aldehydes, fatty acids, and esters [32]. Carbon dioxide is also produced by decarboxylation of saturated acids such as stearic and palmitic acids as well as unsaturated acids [32,33]. Some of the saturated free fatty acids obtained (such as stearic and palmitic acids) are released from the parent triglyceride molecule without any subsequent decomposition of the fatty acid [31]. At higher temperatures, fatty acids decompose by decarboxylation and decarbonylation to form saturated and unsaturated hydrocarbons. These hydrocarbons are then further converted by ethylene elimination, isomerization, and hydrogen-transfer reactions to yield ethylene, propylene, and other small hydrocarbons [31]. C2H4 is the main cracking product detected in the gas and its concentration increased as the temperature was increased [34]. C2H4 is associated with rapid coke formation in the diesel autothermal reforming process [35]. Short-chain oxygenated hydrocarbons are formed by the C―C scission of the unsaturated fatty acid chains, cycloolefins appear as a result of cyclization reactions, and aromatics also originate at high cracking severity [36]. Cracking of methyl ester produces gaseous and liquid products. The possible products are H2 4.6%, CI–C4 (CH4, C2H4, C2H6, C3H6, C3H 8, C4H8, C4Hl0, and traces of C2H2) 45.1%, C5–C9 (linear 1-olefins and n-paraffins) 12.6%, C10–C14 cut 1%, C15–C18 cut 0.2%, aromatics (benzene and toluene) 11.6%, C3;1–C3;8 esters 4.1%, C9;1–C16;1 esters 0.5%, saturated methyl esters 3.1%, CO 3.8%, CO2 1.6%, coke 3.1% and other products 13.1% [37]. At high temperature, C2H4 is the major component of C1–C4 cut with 30% selectivity. At high reaction temperatures large amounts of C2–C4 olefins are obtained from cracking of oil and biodiesel. Theses light olefins oligomerize to yield olefins in the C2–C10 range. Both aliphatic (mostly cyclic) and aromatic hydrocarbons are eventually produced as a result of cyclization and aromatization reactions of C2–C10 olefins [38]. Aromatic products (benzene, methyl and ethyl benzene, etc.) and linear hydrocarbons (mainly olefins) are abundant at 750°C [35]. Aromatics deactivate the fuel cell by filling of the pores of anode with polyaromatic compounds formed by gas-phase, free-radical reactions [39]. Coke is formed either due to the condensation of the oil molecules or by polymerization of large aromatic hydrocarbons and heavy oxygenated hydrocarbon molecules inside the pores of anodes [38]. Other route for coke formation is decomposition of long-chain hydrocarbon radicals into its elements i.e. carbon and hydrogen [33]. Polyaromatic hydrocarbons are the intermediates in the formation of coke from aromatic hydrocarbons. Extensive removal of hydrogen from these hydrocarbons results in coke formation [31]. It can be concluded that with increase in unreacted oil composition of the formulations, fuel cell performance would be greatly affected. The formation of coke via various reactions as explained above would result in the deactivation of the fuel cells. Introduction of steam decreases the concentration of aromatic hydrocarbons such as benzene and toluene with increasing steam/oil ratio [31]. When pure biodiesel was used for testing, the cell deactivates within seconds due to cracking of biodiesel producing ethylene and aromatics, due to the above mentioned reasons. Due to lack of steam for reforming reactions of ester and hydrocarbons, formed by decomposition of esters, cell performance is largely affected. Two sets of experiments were performed using 20 and 50% formulations. Various formulations of butyl, methyl and ethyl esters were prepared with water and emulsifier. The electrochemical performance of the cell was measured in terms of current output from the fuel cell. The cell voltage was held at 0.5V and the corresponding current was measured via potentiostat leads. The cell was operated at 800°C and the evaporator was maintained at 400°C, with helium as carrier gas. The optimum evaporation temperature was obtained by varying the evaporator temperature, in order to ensure complete fuel vaporization and prevent the cell blockage. Prior to the loading of the fuel cell open circuit voltage (OCVs) was measured and were in the range of 0.95–1V, which was less than the theoretical value of 1.2V. The difference between the theoretical value and the measured value is mainly due to the various fuel cell over potentials as expressed by the Nernst equation [40]. The OCVs were substantially lower than that of H2 but were higher than that reported for jet fuel [41], waste cooking oil [18], toluene [42] and gas hydrocarbon fuels such as butane [43]. In case of butyl ester formulation OCV was lowest. But introduction of air helped to increase the OCV and help in removal of the carbon deposits [44]. As time progressed, decrease in the reforming activity was observed with decrease in the current being produced, which can be accounted for the amount of air being used. This amount of air was insufficient to remove the carbon deposited and thereby improve the catalytic activity of the anode. Significant carbon deposition was observed on the inlet of the cell and on the injector. A 66% air–helium mixture was required to obtain a stable OCV. In case of methyl and M/E=1 mix ester formulations a 45% air–helium mixture was required for stable operation. Since the ester content of the formulations was higher and the methyl ester was the major component the amount of air was similar to pure methyl ester formulations. Fig. 6 exhibits the V–I and P–I performance curves for the fuel cell operated at 800°C, which was fueled with 50% biodiesel formulations. Maximum power densities of 0.1564, 0.1372, 0.1330, 0.129Wcm−2 were obtained for, methyl, ethyl, M/E=1 mix ester and butyl formulations respectively which were lower as compared to 0.71Wcm−2 for the hydrogen shown in Fig. 7 . Power density reached maximum at current density of 0.212 Acm−2, before declining at 0.23Acm−2, for butyl ester formulations. The slight better performance was shown by the methyl ester formulation fuelled fuel cell. Power density reached maximum at current density of 0.26Acm−2 in contrast to 0.212Acm−2 before declining at 0.279Acm−2 instead of 0.23Acm−2, in case of butyl formulation. Maximum power density was obtained at 0.266Acm−2 and 0.228Acm−2 current densities, before declining at 0.277Acm−2 and 0.233Acm−2, for ethyl and M/E=1 mix ester formulations respectively. Zhou et al. reported a power density of 0.2Wcm−2 for waste cooking oil fuelled SOFC with Ce–Cu anodes [18]. The higher power density can be attributed to better reforming activity of Ce–Cu anodes as compared to Ni/YSZ anode used in this investigation [45]. Similarly ethanol fuelled fuel cell with Cu–CeO2 two layer structure anodes reported a higher power density of 0.442Wcm−2 at 800°C [46]. From the formulations tested, it is clear that with increase in the chain length of alcohols, the cell performance decreases. In terms of power density, methyl ester based formulation showed the best performance as compared with ethyl and butyl based formulations. In comparison to all the formulations examined, the power density for hydrogen reaches to maximum at higher current density of 0.75Acm−2 shown in Fig. 7. Fig. 8 shows a comparison of current generated with respect to time at 0.5V for hydrogen and various 50% biodiesel formulations. The current generated by hydrogen was higher as compared with other fuels because hydrogen is a clean fuel and does not contain any carbon atoms. The decrease in performance of hydrogen is only due to the sintering of anode [47]. Typical SOFC anode is a cermet of Ni/YSZ. At high temperature the Nickel atoms become extremely mobile and have the tendency to agglomerate thereby causing a reduction in electro-active area [48]. In case of biodiesel formulations, the performance of the cell depends on various parameters like steam/carbon (S/C) ratio and triglyceride content. In case of methyl, butyl esters and M/E=1 mix ester formulations performance was also influenced by oxygen/carbon (O/C) ratio. The performance of the butyl ester formulation was unsatisfactory possibly due to the increase in triglyceride content, which in turn causes an increase in carbon deposition. The higher amount of tri, di, and mono glycerides content in butyl ester formulation, in comparison with the other formulations, might lead to higher amount of aromatics and ethylene formation. It is possible that the anode pores were filled with tar-like molecules formed due to decomposition of aromatics (toluene), which were not removed by air [39]. The large amount of carbon deposition on the injector inlet confirms the hypothesis of formation of high amount of aromatics and ethylene. As butyl ester was prepared from butyl alcohol which is a long chain hydrocarbon, will have a tendency to deposit more carbon in comparison with methyl or ethyl esters. The performance of ethyl ester formulation was best due to lower triglycerides content as compared to butyl ester formulation. In comparison to butyl ester, ethyl ester formulation is prepared from short chain alcohols thereby decreasing the amount of carbon deposited. The performance of methyl ester formulation was better compared to butyl and ethyl formulations, on similar grounds. The ethyl ester formulation fuelled fuel cell lasted longer than most other formulations. Ethyl ester formulation lasted for 60min; possibly due to its better emulsion forming ability. Further the lack of emulsifiers in ethyl ester formulations, which can be an additional source of carbon formation in the case of methyl and butyl formulations, can affect the performance of ethyl ester formulation fuelled fuel cell. Fuel cell fuelled with butyl formulation did not last longer than 30min followed by methyl formulation which lasted for 40min. The life of M/E=1 mix ester formulation was similar to pure methyl ester formulation, as the content of methyl ester in M/E=1 mix ester formulations, was higher in comparison to ethyl ester exhibiting similar life time. Compared to hydrogen fuelled fuel cell, biodiesel formulations operated fuel cells showed considerable decrease in current formation over the period of time. In order to investigate the cell performance and the effect of concentration of formulation, on power and life time of the cell, several 20% formulations were prepared and tested. The power generated by M/E=1 mix ester formulation was similar to that of methyl ester formulation and had similar life time. In order to investigate the effect of mix esters on the performance of the fuel cell and emulsion formation, esters with varying methyl and ethyl alcohols ratios were prepared and tested. V–I and P–I performance curves for the fuel cell which was fueled with 20% biodiesel formulations at 800°C, are represented in Fig. 9 . The OCV recorded with 20% formulation was slightly lower than the OCV obtained with 50% formulations. This was probably due to lower ester concentration in 20% ester formulation as compared with 50% ester formulation. Higher ester concentration in 50% formulations means higher hydrogen being produced by steam reforming reaction and thus higher hydrogen partial pressure in the fuel cell and thus increasing the OCV of 50% ester formulation operated SOFC's. Similarly higher triglyceride content in 50% formulation would increase carbon deposition. Some of the carbon will be gasified to form hydrogen and carbon monoxide thus increasing the hydrogen partial pressure. OCVs measured were in the range of 0.9 to 0.95V. Similar to 50% butyl ester formulation, 20% butyl ester formulation fuelled fuel cell required air in order to obtain stable OCV. Increasing the water content of the formulations, increases the power for butyl ester formulation fuelled fuel cell. This may be due to increase in steam reforming activity of the hydrocarbons, formed due to the decomposition of oxygenated hydrocarbons and carbon removal from the anode surface. Power density for 20% butyl ester formulation reached maxima 0.137Wcm−2 at 0.212Acm−2 current density before declining at 0.292Acm−2. Maximum power density of 0.1368 and 0.133Wcm−2 was observed for 20% methyl and ethyl formulations at 0.342 and 0.25Acm−2 current densities, before declining at 0.383 and 0.26Acm−2 respectively. The power density of 20% formulations was lower as compared to 50% formulations. This can be attributed to lower ester content of the formulations as a result of dilution due to higher steam. Similar to 50% formulation 20% methyl formulations exhibited the highest power density. The performance of the mix esters in terms of V–I and P–I curves is shown in the Fig. 10 . Power densities of 0.1417 and 0.1327Wcm−2 at 0.236 and 0.249Acm−2 current densities, was obtained for M/E=5/3 and M/E=1 mix ester formulations respectively. The power density for these mix ester formulations starts declining at current densities of 0.265 and 0.301Acm−2. As compared to pure methyl ester 20% formulation, the power density for M/E=1 mix ester formulations was slightly lower. While M/E=5/3 mix ester formulation exhibited higher power density in comparison to pure methyl ester 20% formulation. When M/E=1 mix ester formulation were prepared using mix alcohols the methyl ester content of the formulation was higher as compared to ethyl ester, hence the power density was similar to pure methyl ester 20% formulation. The higher power density of M/E=5/3 formulation could be due to the better emulsion forming properties of M/E=5/3 mix ester in comparison to pure methyl and M/E=1 mix ester formulations. Further the higher percentage of methyl ester in M/E=5/3 ester formulation would suggest better performance. Similar to methyl esters formulations the performance of ethyl esters formulations depended on the composition of the ester present in the formulation. Power density of 0.133Wcm−2 at current densities of 0.266 and 0.264Acm−2, was obtained for 20% pure ethyl ester and E/M=5/3 mix ester formulations respectively. Fig. 11 shows the performance comparison of various ester formulations with hydrogen in SOFCs operated at 800°C loaded at 0.5V. The performance of the formulations in comparison with hydrogen, in terms of current produced was considerably low. The current generated by hydrogen fuelled fuel cell was stable over the period of investigation while the performance of the formulations degraded with time. The life of fuel cell operated with 20% formulations was longer as compared to that of 50% formulations. The decrease in concentration with increase in S/C ratio results in increase in the life of the fuel cell. Kendall et al. [49] indicated that there were a number of chemical reactions that could take place within the anode of SOFC fuelled by methane and steam reforming is one of the reactions. The increase in steam with decrease in concentration of the formulations reduces the requirement of air for 20% ester formulations. A 45% air–helium mixture was required for 20% butyl ester formulation. While in case of methyl and M/E=5/3 ester formulations 30% air–helium mixture was required to obtain a stable fuel cell operation. Butyl ester formulation performance was least satisfactory as compared to all other formulations. A decrease in activity of the reforming of the anode was observed with respect to time. With the increase in the amount of tri, di, mono glycerides from the unreacted oil, coke formation increases due to the formation of aromatics and ethylene. Cracking of emulsifier can also be one of the reasons of coke deposition. This caused the blockage of the cell at the inlet and breakage due to fracture. The fuel cell showed steady current generated for no more than 40min. But after 40min the cell performance deteriorated rapidly. The supplied air was not sufficient to remove the coke deposited. The excessive coke deposited resulted in cell blockage and breaking due to fracture. It is possible that the anode pores are being filled with tar-like molecules formed due to decomposition of aromatics (toluene) which were not removed by air [39]. The performance in terms of life of the cell, M/E=5/3 ester formulation behaved in similar fashion to pure methyl ester formulation. The life of the M/E=1 ester formulation fuelled cell, lasted longer as compared to pure methyl and M/E=5/3 ester formulation. The M/E=1 ester formulation contained 50% ethyl ester resulting in stable emulsion formation in comparison to pure methyl ester formulation. With increase in methanol to ethanol molar ratio 5:3 in preparation of mix ester, the formation of ethyl ester decreases. As a result emulsifier is required to obtain stable formulation for mix M/E=5/3 ester formulation. The absence of emulsifier could be the possible reason for M/E=1 mix ester formulation fuelled cell to last longer in comparison to M/E=5/3 mix ester formulation. The performance of formulation was also dependent on the presence of emulsifier; hence ethyl ester formulation performed satisfactorily as a result of the absence of emulsifier as compared to other ester formulations. The life of E/M=5/3 mix ester formulation was slightly lower as compared to pure ethyl ester formulation. The presence of methyl component of the ester could have affected the life of the cell. The rapid decline in power densities for the all the formulations might be as a result of concentration resistance. The flow rate maintained is very low and in addition to that the pulsating nature of the pumps can causes fluctuations in the fuel delivered. Further decrease in the formulation concentration from 50 to 20% at low flow rates would decrease the fuel concentration and would limit the current obtained [41]. The life of the fuel cell operated with the various ester formulations was considerably lower as compared to the fuel cell operated on waste cooking oil [41]. The Rh anodes used in the evaluations have greater reforming activity and carbon resistance as compared to conventional Ni/YSZ anode [50]. Further the use of ceria has a significant contribution to resist carbon deposition formed due to the complex reactions involved in decomposition of ester triglycerides etc. [51,52]. The utilization of biodiesel formulations in a SOFC operated at 800°C was examined. The performance of the fuel cell was measured in terms of V–I and P–I curves. In comparison to pure biodiesel operated SOFCs [19], the SOFCs running on biodiesel formulations exhibit better performance and generate higher power. This is could be due to the increased hydrogen production as a result of the steam reforming of biodiesel and effective carbon removal from anode surface by gasification. Two sets of biodiesel formulations namely 20 and 50% were prepared for testing using a high shear mixer. The formulations based on methyl and butyl esters required the use of emulsifiers to prepare stable emulsions, while ethyl esters were able to form stable emulsions without emulsifiers. Maximum power densities of 0.1564, 0.1372, 0.1330, 0.129Wcm−2 were obtained for 50% methyl, ethyl, M/E=1 mix ester and butyl ester formulations respectively. Power densities of densities of 0.1417, 0.1368, 0.1341, 0.1337Wcm−2 were obtained for 20% formulations of M/E=5/3 mix ester, methyl, M/E=1 mix ester, butyl ester formulations respectively. While ethyl and E/M=5/3 mix ester formulations exhibited similar performances. A power density of 0.1330 was observed for ethyl and E/M=5/3 ester formulations. Air was required to be supplied for 50% butyl, methyl and M/E=5/3 mix ester formulations for stable OCV generation. The decrease in concentration of emulsions reduced the power densities for methyl formulations while increased the power density for butyl ester formulations. The decrease in the concentration of formulation did not have any effect on the power density of the ethyl ester formulation. The 20% formulations fuelled fuel cell lasted longer than 50% formulations in all the formulations tested as a result of increase steam carbon ratio resulting in effective removal of carbon deposited on the anode surface. Butyl ester formulations exhibited the worst performance in both type of formulations examined. The best performance in terms of life of the cell was exhibited by 20% ethyl ester formulation, but 50% methyl ester formulations exhibited the highest power density. Mix ester M/E=5/3 formulation showed better performance in comparison to pure methyl ester. M/E=1 mix ester showed better emulsion formation properties in comparison to pure methyl and M/E=5/3 mix ester. Formulation prepared using 50% M/E=5/3 mix ester required air for obtaining stable OCV while 20% M/E=5/3 mix ester did not. The increase in the triglyceride content of formulations can be considered as the cause for the deactivation of the cells. Further work needs to be carried out in terms of optimization of anode. Cracking pattern of methyl esters as well as triglycerides needs to be studied in details which are important in order to determine the factors affecting the carbon deposition and the performance of the cell. Steam and autothermal reforming of biodiesel must be studied in detail to understand the reactions occurring in the fuel cell. Use of noble metals based anodes like Rh must be examined as it has been shown to be effective with the use of waste cooking oil. The authors would like to thank Adaptive Materials Inc., USA (AMI), for their cooperation and supply of raw NiO/YSZ tubes.
abstract: Biodiesel (alkyl ester of rapeseed oil) is prepared using various, methyl, ethyl and butyl alcohols through the transesterification process. Sodium hydroxide and sulfuric acid are used as catalyst for methyl alcohol, ethyl alcohol and butyl alcohol respectively. Biodiesel–water formulations are formulated using water and emulsifiers like sodium lauryl sulphate (SLS) and SPAN 80 in a high shear mixer. The formulations are tested at 800°C as fuel for internal reforming in solid oxide fuel cells (SOFCs). The formulations based on methyl and butyl esters require the use of emulsifiers to prepare stable emulsions, while ethyl esters are able to form stable emulsions without emulsifiers. The decrease in the biodiesel concentration of formulation does not have any effect on the power density of the ethyl ester formulation. Fuel cells fuelled with 20% formulations lasted longer than 50% formulations in all the formulations tested as result of increase in steam carbon ratio resulting in effective removal of carbon deposited on the anode surface. Butyl ester formulations exhibited the worst performance in both types of formulation tests. The best performance was exhibited by 20% ethyl formulation in terms of life of the cell but 50% methyl ester formulations exhibit the highest power density.
title: Biodiesel formulations as fuel for internally reforming solid oxide fuel cell

reaction_catalog.reaction_name_reported: internal reforming in solid oxide fuel cells

aliases: Ni/YSZ anode used in this investigation

steps.method_details: weighed out powders for two cathode mixtures
mixed powders in separate bottles with acetone as solvent
milled mixtures in a vibro-miller for 24h
added Terpinol to 100% LSM mixture and vibro-milled for 10min
painted 50%/50% LSM/YSZ cathode ink on raw tubes and dried for 24h
painted 100% LSM cathode ink on previous layer and dried for 12h
sintered painted tubes in a furnace with programmed temperature stages
reduced tubes with hydrogen flow
painted silver ink on cathode for electrical conductivity
polished off electrolyte to expose Ni surface for anode current collection
painted silver ink on Ni surface and dried for 6h
wound silver wire around silver bands for current collection
dissolved sodium hydroxide in methanol to prepare sodium methoxide
added sodium methoxide solution to hot oil and maintained temperature
carried out transesterification reaction
separated glycerol by gravity separation
washed with hot water to remove catalyst
mixed biodiesel, emulsifier, and distilled water in a high shear mixer

full_text: The catalytic conversion of syngas to liquid hydrocarbons fuel and higher alcohols is a potential route for providing clean fuel and petrochemical feedstock [1,2]. Production of ultra clean fuel from coal and biomass derived synthesis gas (CO+H2) has received significant attention due to limited oil reserves, with increasing populations and economic developments. Renewable energy could be a sustainable and significant route to meet the future energy targets. The major part of gas-to-liquids (GTL) technology is Fischer Tropsch synthesis which converts syngas into liquid fuel with a wide range of liquid hydrocarbon fuel and high-value of added chemicals. Several types of catalysts have been used for the reaction [2]. However the process provides a wide range of hydrocarbons (C1–C60). Iron and cobalt based catalysts along with MFI Zeolites (ZSM-5) has been reported for potential commercial aspects because of their high catalytic activity and coke built tolerance [3–5]. A combination of an iron-based FT catalyst has shown high selectivity to olefins and oxygenates in presence of MFI zeolites or HY zeolites [2,4]. This activity enhanced the gasoline selectivity and an increased concentration of high-octane branched and aromatic hydrocarbons by promoting oligomerization, cracking, isomerization, and aromatization reactions on the MFI zeolites (Si/ Al=35) [2–6]. FT product distributions are generally controlled by the Anderson–Schulz–Flory (ASF) polymerization kinetics, resulting in a nonselective formation of any hydrocarbons [3–6]. In other dimension first step is to convert syngas to methanol over a methanol synthesis catalyst and subsequently polymerize methanol to hydrocarbons over MFI Zeolite [7–9]. Many investigations have demonstrated the advantages of the one-stage processes by using bi-functional catalysts compared with the two-stage and three stage processes of synthesis gas conversion to gasoline. Bifunctional catalysts have more potential to deliver better performance for syngas to higher hydrocarbons [10–14]. Zeolite has the acidic function most commonly used, due to its properties like activity, selectivity, limited deactivation, and thermal stability [15–21]. However, Cu–Co-based catalysts have been found effective for synthesis of higher alcohols produced from syngas [22–24]. In this study application and characterization of the CuO–CoO–Cr2O3 (CAT-A) mixed with MFI Zeolite catalyst was studied for conversion of synthesis gas to higher hydrocarbon fuel. The catalyst was characterized by using thermo gravimetric analyser (TGA), Surface area and pore volume analyser, X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), Temperature-programmed reduction (TPR), H2-chemisorptions, Scanning electron microscope (SEM), and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The effects of the operating variables on the catalytic performance of the (CAT-A+MFI zeolite) bifunctional catalysts were examined in a high-pressure fixed bed reactor. All the chemicals used for catalyst preparation were of high purity and procured from Merck (Germany). Catalyst CuO–CoO–Cr2O3 (25:50:25wt.%, CAT-A); was prepared by mixing a known amount of chromium nitrate (Cr (NO3)3 ·6H2O), copper nitrate, (Cu (NO3)2 ·6H2O), and cobalt nitrate, (Co (NO3)2 ·6H2O), in KOH solution. Mixing of these three different metal precursors was carried out in mechanical shaker; with deionized water. The solution was kept under constant stirring, at 65°C for about 30min. To this prepared solution, citric acid was slowly added as a chelating agent and heated at 80°C for 1h followed by drying at 105±5°C for 12h. The addition of citric acid helps in dissolution of precipitated metal hydroxides and thereby resulting a homogeneous solution, because the organic ligand has a strong affinity towards Cu and Co ions during the solution preparation [11,12,17]. The foam-like solid thus obtained was calcined at 420±5°C for 3h in a muffle furnace. Blackish fine powder of CuO–CoO–Cr2O3 (1: 2: 1 mass ratio) was finally mixed with MFI zeolite powder. The MFI zeolite (Si/Al=35) procured from Süd-Chemie India (P) Ltd, was dried overnight at 105±2°C and followed by crushing and sieving to 0.01±0.005mm particle size. This MFI zeolite powder was physically mixed with the CAT-A powder (dry mixing). The catalyst mixture thus obtained was a mixture of CAT-A and MFI zeolite (Si/Al=35) in equal ratio and is designated as CAT-B. Finally the powder was pelletized followed by crushing to desired particle size for kinetic experiments. The fixed bed reactor set up used was supplied by Autoclave Engineers (BTRS). A schematic diagram of the experimental set up is shown in Fig. 1 . The reactor is a stainless steel chamber of 12.6mm internal diameter and 292.4mm length, with a volume of catalyst up to 5cm3 and is electrically heated. 3–5g of the catalyst along with inert material silicon carbide, (SiC) was loaded in the reactor. Prior to the reaction the reactor was kept under N2 pressure for the leak test, then the system was purged by flowing H2 for 30min, followed by pre-reducing catalyst in a stream of H2–N2 for 4h. Thereafter syngas was introduced into the reactor. The flow rates of H2 and CO were adjusted in the range of 40–100 (ml/min) using mass flow controllers. No temperature gradient was observed in the catalyst bed throughout the reduction process, but during the syngas conversion process, there was some temperature rise observed due to the exothermic reaction. The analysis of the hydrocarbon was carried out with a Nucon gas chromatograph (AIMIL) provided with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a flame ionization detector (FID). The flow rates of liquid and gaseous products were measured at regular intervals. The analysis of gaseous products like H2, CO, N2, CH4 and CO2, was done by Carbo-sphere column using Ar as carrier gas. All hydrocarbon gases were analyzed by using a Porapak-Q column (i.d.: 2mm, length: 1.8m). The liquid hydrocarbons were determined by using a capillary column (petrocol fused silica column, L=60m, i.d.=0.25mm fused silica), on a FID mode. The oven, injector and detector temperature were maintained at 60, 120, and 220°C respectively. Overall mass balance and carbon balance were performed based on inlet/outlet flows of gas composition analysis, and liquid product weight and runs with more than 95% carbon balance were considered for the analysis of the data. Final metal loading of the catalyst was determined by SEM–EDX analysis using new ZEISS EVO Series Scanning Electron Microscope EVO 50 and X-ray analysis. SEM EDX results are quantitatively presented in Fig. 2 (A1, A2, B1) in which metals are identified by different peaks indicating the final weight % of metal loading on the catalyst. The amount (wt.%) of metal loading of the CAT-A was: Cu: Co: Cr (22:47:17). The metal content of the MFI mixed (1:1 w/w) catalyst was also determined by SEM-EDX and the metal ratio was Cu–Co–Cr 13:24:10 (rest Si and Al) due to the presence of Al and Si in MFI mixture. The surface areas and pore volumes of the fresh and used catalyst (CAT-A and CAT-B) were analyzed using N2 adsorption. The BET surface area, total pore volume and pore size distribution of the catalyst was determined from nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms measured at −196°C using Micromeritics ASAP 2010 apparatus [23,25]. Prior to gas adsorption measurements the catalyst was degassed at 180°C under high vacuum for a period of 6h. The total pore volume was calculated at a relative pressure of approximately 0.99. TGA was used to analyze weight loss during heat treatment and to estimate the amount of coke deposited on the catalyst surface after reaction using a SDT Q600 thermal analyzer of the precalcined catalysts. The TPR spectra were recorded using the Micromeritics Pulse Chemisorption Chemisorb 2720. TPR experiments were carried out at ramp of 10°C/min. The reactive gas compositions were H2 (10vol.%; balance Ar). For the temperature-programmed reduction experiments, the catalyst sample was (0.5g) placed in a quartz reactor and heated up to 750°C. The metal surface area and metal dispersion was measured by H2 chemisorption using Micromeritics Pulse Chemisorption Chemisorb 2720. For pulse chemisorptions 0.5g of sample was taken in a quartz reactor, placed in a furnace. Then the temperature of the furnace was raised for reduction at ramp of 10°C/min under flow of 30ml/min, using 9.9% H2 and rest Ar. After the reduction temperature was reached, under the flow of gas mixture, heating was stopped and the temperature was further cooled down to 40°C, and reduced catalyst sample was flushed with Ar, at a flow of 30ml/min to remove specifically adsorbed H2. The reactor was cooled to 30°C. At this temperature, 0.4ml pulses of H2 injected periodically in the stream of nitrogen in order to find out the total H2 uptake. The cobalt surface area was calculated by assuming the adsorption stoichiometry of one H2 molecule per each fresh Co–Cr–Cu surface atom which can be shown in Eqs. (1) and (2) [26]. (1) Percentage of Dispersion= H 2 uptake ∗ atomic weight ∗ stoichiometry Percentage of Metal (2) = number of Co 0 atoms on surface ∗ 100 total number of Co atoms in sample ∗ fraction reduced XRD pattern was obtained using a Bruker-model operated at 40kV and 20mA using Cu Kα radiation with a wavelength of 1.54A°. The diffraction angle was varied from 20° to 80° at a scan rate of 0.02°/s. The average particle size of CoO in the calcined catalyst (CAT-A) was estimated from Scherrer's equation using the most intense reflection of CoO crystallites at different value of 2θ. The CoO, CuO and Cr2O3 particle size in the calcined sample were then converted to individual metal diameter in the reduced catalyst by considering the relative molar volumes of Coo and Co3O4 using the Eqs. (3–5) and following the concept for other metals [16–18,23]. (3) d Co 0 = 0 .75 ∗ d Co 3 O 4 , . The average size of Co3O4 particle was calculated according to the Scherrer's equation [22]. (4) d= 0 .89 ∗ λ / B cosθ ∗ 180 0 / π where d is the average crystallite diameter, λ is the wavelength of radiation, and B is the full width half in degrees. Then the Coo metal dispersion was estimated from diameter of (Coo) by assuming a spherical geometry of the metal particles with uniform site density of 14.6 atoms per nm2, using the following equation [10,16,26]. (5) D = [ 96 / d ] where D is the % Coo dispersion and d is the mean particle size of Coo in nm. The morphology of the catalyst sample was investigated using SEM equipped with a new ZEISS EVO Series Scanning Electron Microscope EVO 50. The ZEISS EVO 50 is a versatile analytical microscope with a large specimen chamber. Scanning Electron Microscope EVO 50 having the resolution 2.0nm at 30kV (SE with LaB6 option) with magnification range up to 1,000,000×. TEM was performed using PHILIPS CM12 microscope operated at an accelerating voltage of 100kV [17,18]. The surface area, pore volume, and average pore diameter of MFI Zeolite (Si/Al=35), CuO–CoO–Cr2O3, (CAT-A) and CuO–CoO–Cr2O3 +MFI Zeolite, (CAT-B) are reported in Table 1 . The BET surface areas of MFI Zeolite, CAT-A, fresh CAT-B, and used CAT-B are 276.6, 29.9, 138.5, and 124.2m2/g, respectively. The surface area of the catalyst, CAT-B decreased after the reaction indicating that metal crystallite size increased slightly during the reaction [23,25]. The dried fine powder of pre-calcined CAT-A was analyzed using a SDT Q600 thermal analyzer to determine the weight loss as a function of temperature. These TGA results are shown in Figs. 3 and 4 . The reduction pattern indicates that between 310 and 520°C variation in wt.% was approximately 0.95%. Up to 460°C calcinations it was sufficient to get oxidized and usage of CAT-A compound with MFI Zeolite (Si / Al=35) mixture. A total of 12% weight loss obtained during calcination indicates the decomposition of Cr(NO3)3 at 170±5°C, and Cu(NO3)2 at 225±5°C, and also due to the formation of CuCrO4 phase [27]. During calcinations it is easy to get different alloy shell and Co/ Cr spinel which is in agreement with the published literature [28–30]. Baker et al. [28] suggested that both copper and cobalt are required for higher alcohol synthesis and the presence of metallic cobalt is necessary either pure or alloyed with copper and chromium. The reducibility of catalyst was examined by temperature programmed reduction. Prior to temperature-programmed reduction experiments, each catalyst sample was activated under flowing O2 (10vol.%; balance He) at 600°C for 60min. Fig. 5 illustrates the TPR profiles of two catalysts where a large peak centered at 334°C in CAT-A, and at 336°C in CAT-B was observed. Such a large peak can be attributed mainly to the reduction of Co3O4 species. Another reduction peaks was observed for CAT-B could be attributed to the reduction of Co3O4 to CoO and CoO to metallic CoO [16,23]. Incorporation of Cu metal on the catalyst increased the reduction temperature from 250°C to 334±2°C. Tien-Thao et al. [23] suggested that the copper provides an active site for higher alcohol synthesis, to activate cobalt reduction, and possibly to modify the cobalt by alloying. After reduction of highly dispersed CuO and CoO aggregates, formation of small Cu–Co clusters occurs. Xiaoding et al. reported that Cu–Co alloy is responsible for higher alcohol synthesis [11]. Baker et al. proposed that the active centre is comprised of metallic cobalt atoms on copper clusters and the active catalyst consisted of copper particles supported on Co/Cr spinel [28–31]. The copper metal in these catalysts is less sensitive to atmospheric oxidation, as there is no separate peak at lower temperatures for the reduction of copper however the copper metal remains in ionic states during reduction step and reaction step in presence of CO and H2 [2]. The adsorption capacity of fresh CAT-A was examined during temperature programmed reduction. Prior to temperature programmed reduction experiments and before chemisorptions, argon was passed for 1h followed by cooling from 750°C to 40°C at 30ml/min. After reaching 40°C, H2 gas circulated in the analysis port for dosing purpose for TCD graphs [10,16,26]. The total metal dispersion as determined from the chemisorptions study was 3.6%. The average particle diameter of the metal crystallite as determined by H2 chemisorptions and XRD was approximately 20±3nm. The intrinsic activities (TOF) for the FT synthesis at 275°C for the two catalysts (CAT-A and CAT-B) were 7.4×10−3 s−1 and 18.2×10−3 s−1 respectively. The values are in good agreement with (TOF=28.5×10−3 s−1) reported by Martinez et al. [16]. It must be stated that the TOF of the catalyst mostly depends on the preparation method, different particle sizes and the overall dispersion to achieve active metal sites. Fig. 6 (A and B) shows the XRD pattern of catalyst samples. The XRD peaks at (A) corresponding to Cu (JCPDS-04-0836) at 2θ of 43.44° and 50.44°. Peaks at 38.79° and 74.15° corresponds to Co3O4, which were observed (JCPDS-74-1657) in the fresh catalyst sample [2,13]. Cobalt and chromium were found as Co3O4 and Cr2O3 crystallites in the fresh and used sample as per (JCPDS-82-1484), Cu2O at (2θ) 73.96° and 59.8° (JCPDS-03-0892) were clearly detected by XRD in the used catalyst samples in Fig. 6B XRD of used samples indicates that Co3O4 was converted to CoO after reaction, whereas the reflection lines of Cr2O3 are unchanged after reaction. Fig. 6B indicates that Cr2O3 was not reduced under different reaction conditions [13,18,23]. Some phases like CrO3, CuCrO4 and CoCr2O4 [29,30] were also formed in fresh and used catalyst getting conformation from (JCPDS-77-0125, 77-2474 and 78-0711) respectively. Fig. 7 (A–B) shows a typical SEM photograph of the calcined catalyst before and after reaction respectively. It is found that the catalyst surface is covered with small crystallites of cobalt oxide (Co3O4), copper and chromium particles, which is in agreement with the reported literature [17]. It is apparent that both CAT-A and zeolite in the fresh catalyst sample are uniformly distributed, i.e., zeolite particles are evenly distributed and covered by porous CAT-A oxides. In addition, the size of these small crystallites grew larger due to crystallite agglomeration with MFI zeolite (Si/Al=35) during pelletization and heat temperature during reaction. Therefore the agglomeration of individual Cu–Co–Cr metal oxide particles with MFI zeolite and the formation of larger Cu particles may be a factor of decreasing the catalytic performance, which is in agreement of the published data [17]. Thus high temperature causes agglomeration of small crystallites, which leads to catalyst deactivation under high temperature reaction [31,32]. Cu–Co–Cr and MFI zeolite were well mixed in the fresh conditions and resulting particles were both porous and nano-sized. TEM images of fresh CAT-A after calcination and the used catalyst CAT-B (after 120h of reaction under 225 to 325°C) are depicted in Fig. 8 (A and B) respectively. In these micrographs, the morphology of the particle was observed spherical. The particles of fresh CAT-B are fairly uniform with an average diameter of 18±3nm and whereas for the used CAT-B it was 25±3nm in size. The TPR results indicates only CuO has reduced at temperatures below 423°C; thus it is hypothesized that these distinctive particles are reduced metal copper which has partially separated from the Co–Cr oxides after 120h of reaction [17]. Fig. 8 (A–B) shows both metal oxide phases are well fitted into each other and metal grains are in the range of 15–30nm. TEM Figures clearly illustrates the crystalline nature of the sample. The samples are spastic structures, not single-crystalline structures, respectively. These are located close to the surface, their outer surface being coated by an amorphous or a poorly crystallized oxide which protects them from rapid oxidation in the presence of hydrogen at higher temperature. This tendency with catalyst may be due to the composite material combination with MFI zeolite. The performance of the catalyst was investigated for syngas conversion to liquid fuel. Effect of temperature, reaction pressure, gas hourly space velocity (GHSV) and CO/H2 ratio was studied in the fixed bed reactor set up [25]. The experiments were carried out at different temperatures in the range 225°C to 325°C. The total pressure of the reaction was varied from 28 to 38atm pressure with GHSV kept within 457 to 850h−1 and the mole ratio of CO/H2 at 1 to 2. CO conversion and product selectivity (%) on carbon basis was calculated using Eqs. (6) and (7) respectively [24,33]. (6) CO conversion % = Moles ​CO in - Moles CO out Moles​ CO in × 100 (7) Selectivity of product % = Moles of product Moles CO Reacted × 100 CO conversion increased from 28% at 225°C to 72% at 275°C and decreased to 68% at 325°C. Although temperature favors the kinetics of hydrocarbon formation reactions, according to the thermodynamics only the light hydrocarbons, like methane, shows higher stability in these conditions. It shows the higher temperature up to 275°C is favorable for the CO conversion. The decrease in conversion, as temperature increased above 275°C is probably due to the thermodynamic restrictions of the exothermal reaction and copper metal surface sintering, which leads to partial loss of catalyst activity [9]. The hydrocarbon concentration in the effluent increased with temperature. Hydrocarbon distribution toward gasoline (C10+ to C15+) first increased as the reaction temperature increased from 225 to 275°C, and then decreased after 275°C [24]. The amount of coke deposited on used catalyst was also determined after 120h of run time. The deposited coke on the catalyst was estimated to be 2.8% by TGA analysis. The CO / H2, molar ratio plays a vital role in higher hydrocarbon synthesis. Conventional syngas has high CO to H2 ratio as compared to natural gas synthesized syngas. It can affect both reaction rates and activity as lower H2/CO ratio tends to decrease CO conversion while increasing higher hydrocarbon selectivity. The feed H2/CO molar ratio of 2:1 was kept and its effect on CO conversion and hydrocarbon distribution has investigated. In Fig. 9 the CO conversion and hydrocarbon formation are compared with a H2/CO molar ratio of 1 where CO conversion was 72% at 275°C and then it decreased when H2/CO molar ratio increased to ~2. Also significant changes in product were obtained. A change in the ratio also affects the partial pressure of H2 and CO under constant total pressure, thus the stoichiometry will be different for each reaction. Higher hydrogen partial pressure reduces the coke formation, thus better activity of the catalyst was obtained. Also at high carbon monoxide partial pressure, the formation of high molecular weight compounds increased. At lower H2/CO ratio, CO insertion and C–C chain growth are more favorable, which enhanced the production of higher hydrocarbons [4,33]. Therefore an optimum H2/CO ratio is desired for the liquid fuel hydrocarbon production. Fig. 9 compares (C5+ range hydrocarbon) product distributions at two different molar ratios of H2/CO. Higher H2/CO ratio favored the formation of straight chain paraffin products where as at lower H2/CO ratio of ~1, the formation of higher C/H ratio products (aromatics) favored. At higher H2/CO molar ratio the C5+ fraction increased at the expense of the lighter fractions in the product stream. This is probably due to the fact that a decrease in the H2/CO molar ratio favors the condensation steps over the hydrogenation steps [9,20]. Consequently products of higher range of liquid hydrocarbons were formed and selectivity, toward hydrocarbon increased. Also olefin formation suppressed producing higher paraffin and iso-paraffin. At (H2/CO=1–1.5) aromatic formation gradually increases, which is in accordance with the results reported in literature by Li. et al [7,20]. The effect of temperature on CO conversion and product (C1+ to C15+) distribution are presented in Figs. 10 and 11 respectively. The experimental conditions were as follows: P (28–38atm), GHSV (457 to 850h−1) at molar ratio of H2/CO of 1: 1. As shown in Fig. 11 the hydrocarbon distribution was affected by the change in temperature, higher hydrocarbons (mainly C5+) being favored at relatively moderate temperatures. At low temperature formation of lower hydrocarbons favored mainly methane. The higher hydrocarbons are more sensitive to temperature, and therefore undergo cracking and de-alkylation reactions. The identification of different key components synthesized during reaction was analyzed by GC-MS (Model: Perkin–Elmer clarus-600). These distinct identified peaks are summarized in Table 2 and programmed GC-MS graph has shown in Fig. 12 . Selectivity toward gasoline (C5+ fraction) first increased up to 275°C and decreases thereafter. It is reported that, the activation energy values for condensation steps are smaller than those for hydrogenation of light hydrocarbons [20]. With respect to the higher hydrocarbons and aromatic products (toluene, xylene, 1, 2, 3-trimethylbenzene, and 1, 2, 4, 5-tetramethylbenzene), the C5+ fractions increased and the C8+ fraction reach a maximum value at 275°C and then decreased with increase of temperature [34–36]. Experiments were carried out at different GHSV ranging from 457 to 840h−1 with other operating conditions were kept constant at 275°C, 38atm. and molar ratio of CO/H2 ~1. The effect of the space velocity on CO conversion and hydrocarbon distribution indicates that as space velocity increases CO conversion decreases, which has shown in Fig. 13 . It is observed that the hydrocarbon selectivity increased up to a space velocity of 585h−1 and decreased thereafter with further increase in space velocity. The liquid hydrocarbons fraction (C8+) progressively decreased with increasing space velocity. Lower GHSV favored higher C/H ratio products, especially the aromatics like ethylbenzene, o-xylene, 1-methyl-2-ethylbenzene, naphthalene, styrene, n-decane, n-dodecane etc. According to the thermodynamics an increase in total pressure increases the equilibrium towards the product side, and thus increases the conversion. Pressure is always favorable for Fischer–Tropsch bi-functional catalysts [1,2]. The change in Gibbs free energy is always negative when pressure increases. Fig. 13 depicts the effect of change in total pressure and GHSV on CO conversion. Experiments under pressure of 28, 32, 35 and 38atm., has been carried out in the present study. The other operating conditions were as follows: temperature=275°C; CO / H2 molar ratio of 1; and gas space velocity=585h−1. The increase in pressure favored the formation of C8+ to C11+, while methane and C1–C3 gaseous hydrocarbons decreased with an increase in pressure. The primary reaction during FT synthesis is the formation of α olefin which hydrogenates in consecutive step to the corresponding paraffin, so pressure has significant effect on gaseous olefin to paraffin ratio. Furthermore the change in total pressure did not affect the water gas shift reaction but changed the equilibrium of product distribution and CO conversion in CO-hydrogenation. Thus bifunctional transition metal oxide with MFI Zeolite catalysts have been found capable of converting syngas to liquid fuels in a single reaction step as they contain both CO hydrogenation function (Cu–Co–Cr oxides) and hydrocarbon synthesis function. The liquid product obtained also include some groups of alcohols, ketones, and aldehydes as identified by GC-MS. The basic reaction for the formation of these hydrocarbon products is –CH2-group. Furthermore, a change in total pressure does not affect the water gas shift reaction but changes the equilibrium product distribution [1,4,22]. In general there are two steps involved in the conversion of syngas to hydrocarbons over a bifunctional catalyst. First, alcohols are synthesized from the syngas over an oxide catalyst. In the second step zeolite catalyst dehydrates these alcohols into olefins and rearranges the remaining hydrogen and carbon atoms into a concentrated high energy fuel [1–4]. Effect of temperature revealed that aromatic fractions increased with temperature. Thus there is a tradeoff between two trends: the CO hydrogenation reaction yielding alcohols, mainly due to the Cu–Co–Cr metal oxides, with an optimum temperature of 250–300°C and the acid function-based reaction i.e., conversion of oxygenates to hydrocarbons or aromatization, mainly due to the MFI Zeolite catalyst [21]. The synthesis rates for both alcohols and hydrocarbon are dependent on the syngas pressure over Cu–Co–Cr metal oxide catalyst. Therefore, increasing syngas partial pressure results in an increased productivity of almost all products except of CO2, which is not very sensitive to the change in syngas pressure. In the present study, a stoichiometric H2/CO ratio of one seems to be an appropriate syngas composition for the synthesis of hydrocarbons [25,26]. Cu–Co–Cr based catalysts are utilized for the production of higher alcohols. The chain growth of higher alcohol formation is favored at high cobalt concentration or low copper content. Mainly the cobalt ions in combination with metallic Cu or the interaction phase are important to oxygenate synthesis, as it is important to carry out reduction with H2 at 250°C, before syngas passing inside Cu–Co interaction, which are the active site for the reaction. XRD results revealed that metallic copper and CoO and Cr2O3 are active components for CO hydrogenation. In higher alcohol synthesis, copper sites are responsible for hydrogen dissociative chemisorption and CO associative adsorption since these have been reported as the main elements in methanol synthesis [1,2,4,13]. CuO–CoO–Cr2O3 (CAT-A) mixed with molecular shape selective acid function zeolite (Si/Al-35) has been found effective for the conversion of synthesis gas to liquid hydrocarbons. Use of citric acid as complexant during catalyst preparation, has partly reduces the samples and decrease the particle sizes. High yield of liquid hydrocarbons was observed over (CAT-A) catalyst mixed with MFI zeolite during syngas conversion. Temperature and pressure are the most significant variables affecting the C5+ yield and selectivity. CO conversion increases from 28% at 225°C to 72% at 275°C; but decreases thereafter. Selectivity towards gasoline (C5+ fraction) range of hydrocarbons also increased in the temperature range from 225 to 275°C. Higher the H2/CO ratio is favorable for formation of products of lower C/H ratio (straight chain of paraffin), while lower H2/CO ratio is favorable for higher C/H ratio products (aromatics). The financial support provided by Department of Science and Technology, Government. of India for sponsoring this research project is gratefully acknowledged.
abstract: CuO–CoO–Cr2O3 mixed with MFI Zeolite (Si/Al=35) prepared by co-precipitation was used for synthesis gas conversion to long chain hydrocarbon fuel. CuO–CoO–Cr2O3 catalyst was prepared by co-precipitation method using citric acid as complexant with physicochemical characterization by BET, TPR, TGA, XRD, H2-chemisorptions, SEM and TEM techniques. The conversion experiments were carried out in a fixed bed reactor, with different temperatures (225–325°C), gas hourly space velocity (457 to 850h−1) and pressure (28–38atm). The key products of the reaction were analyzed by gas chromatography mass spectroscopy (GC-MS). Significantly high yields of liquid aromatic hydrocarbon products were obtained over this catalyst. Higher temperature and pressure favored the CO conversion and formation of these liquid (C5–C15) hydrocarbons. Higher selectivity of C5 +hydrocarbons observed at lower H2/CO ratio and GHSV of the feed gas. On the other hand high yields of methane resulted, with a decrease in C5+ to C11+ fractions at lower GHSV. Addition of MFI Zeolite (Si/Al=35) to catalyst CuO–CoO–Cr2O3 resulted a high conversion of CO-hydrogenation, which may be due to its large surface area and small particle size creating more active sites. The homogeneity of various components was also helpful to enhance the synergistic effect of Co promoters.
title: Liquid fuel production from syngas using bifunctional CuO–CoO–Cr2O3 catalyst mixed with MFI zeolite
