full_text: In the new environmental legislations, elimination of heteroatoms including sulfur, nitrogen, oxygen and metals from transportation fuels has become a central issue for effective fossil fuels processing. In this regard, to reduce the adverse impacts of fossil fuels consumption, diesel sulfur content is restricted to be 99.4% in purity), ammonium heptamolybdate tetrahydrate ((NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O, >0.994 g/g in purity) and nickel (II) nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, for analysis grade) were used as supplied by Merck KGaA. Sodium aluminate (Na2O 41%, Al2O3 53% and H2O 6%) was used as supplied by VWR company. Tetramethylammonium hydroxide pentahydrate (TMAOH·5H2O 98% in purity) was used as supplied by Alfa Aesar GmbH & Co KG. Pseudoboehmite (PB) was prepared through neutralization of NaAlO2 solution with CO2, developed and extensively studied in our previous studies [36]. Based on our previous work [35], to synthesize the support with the optimum average pore size, a mixture of N2 and CO2 gases should be used in PB synthesis step. 0.1 M NaAlO2 solution was neutralized with a gas mixture containing 50 vol% CO2 which entered the semi-batch membrane dispersion microreactor with flow rate of 100 mln/min. Temperatures of the reaction solution and the gas mixture were controlled preciously to be 10 °C. As the pH of the solution decreased to 8, the gas flow and consequently the reaction was stopped and the resulting suspension was aged at 90 °C for 2 h. PB powder was obtained after filtering, washing and drying of the obtained suspension from aging step. Details of the synthesis route can be found in our previous work [36]. 31.71 g of NaOH and 28.21 g NaAlO2 were added to the solution of 134.4 g of TMAOH in 374.5 g of deionized water under rigorous stirring. After obtaining a clear solution, the resulting mixture was added to 130.9 g of silica solution. The synthesis gel with composition of 4 Na2O:1 Al2O3:6 SiO2:200 H2O:5 TMAOH was aged for two weeks. By placing the PTFE autoclave containing the gel in an oven pre-heated to 100 °C the NaY zeolite was synthesized after the residence time of 48 h. The white powder obtained after cooling, vacuum filtering, washing and drying at 110 °C is the synthesized NaY zeolite. [37]. The ion exchange process was carried out at 90 °C for 1 h using 2 N NH4Cl solution while the solution to zeolite ratio was 10 ml/g. After filtering, washing and drying, the treated zeolite is ready for steaming. Based on the findings in our previous work [38], to maximize the Si to Al ratio of the synthesized USY zeolite through steaming of the ion exchanged HY zeolite, temperature and duration of steaming, steam flow, steam partial pressure, steaming repetition, and washing EDTA molarity should be set to 700 °C, 5 h, 50 SCCM, 100 kPa, 3 repetitions and 0.3 mol/lit, respectively. Steaming was performed while these conditions were applied. Mesoporous γ-alumina and USY/γ-Al2O3 composite supports were prepared through peptization of the synthesized PB powder or mixture of the USY zeolite and the PB powder. Three supports were prepared to have zero, 5 and 10 wt% of USY zeolite, in all of which, HNO3 to AlO(OH) molar ratio, total liquid to solid weight ratio, NH4OH to HNO3 weight ratio were 0.01, 2 and 0.25, respectively. To obtain a uniform paste, all required nitric acid was diluted with three quarters of the required water and added to the powders dropwise under kneading. The remaining water was added to ammonium hydroxide and the resulting solution was slowly added to the paste under kneading. The paste was then aged at 25 °C for 2 h. The catalyst supports were prepared by extrusion of the paste. The resulting extrudates were dried at 80 °C for 6 h and 110 °C for another 6 h and finally calcined at 550 °C for 4 h. All temperature ramps were 3 °C/min. Porefilling method was used to prepare NiMo catalysts [39]. 27 catalysts were prepared with different USY/γ-Al2O3 support compositions, total metal (Ni + Mo) loading, Ni/(Ni + Mo) and EDTA/Ni ratios as summarized in Table 1 . Catalyst preparation was started by dissolving EDTA in ammonium hydroxide aqueous solution at pH ∼ 10, If it was dictated by the experimental design that EDTA should be used as the chelating agent in the preparation of the sample. Otherwise, the pH of required amount of water was increased to 10 by adding ammonium hydroxide. Afterwards, (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O was added to the solution. By complete dissolution of Mo source, pH decreased slightly. Then Ni(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in the obtained solution. The final pH of the solution was adjusted to 9. The impregnated γ-alumina supports were dried at 80 °C for 6 h and 110 °C for another 6 h and finally calcined at 500 °C for 4 h. Characterizations were carried out using Philips 1840 X-ray diffractometer with Co-Kα X-ray tube and an input voltage of 40 kV while 2θ varied from 5 to 90° with scan rate of 1°/min, and 2θ intervals of 0.02°, Mira 3-XMU FESEM apparatus (TESCAN, Czech Republic) and Zeiss Sigma VP variable-pressure SEM (Carl Zeiss Inc., Germany) fitted with ED/EBSD system (Oxford Instruments, UK), TriStar-II-Series surface area analyzer (Micromeritics Instrument Corporation, USA) with degassing conditions of 300 °C for 3 h, TPD/TPR analyzer (TPD/TPR 2900, Micromeritics Instrument Corporation, USA) with a thermal conductivity detector in the temperature range of 60–1000 °C with constant heating ramp of 10 °C/min while using He gas with flow rate of 40 cm3/min as the carrier and Perkin Elmer 2380 Atomic Absorption Spectro Photometer. Elemental analyzer (multi EA® 5000 for Micro-Elemental Analysis) was used to evaluate sulfur content of the feed and hydrotreated samples, according to ASTM D5453 standard. Besides, this apparatus was used to evaluate sulfur content of the sulfided catalyst. A trickle bed setup with flow scheme shown in Fig. 1 , the details of which were described elsewhere [35], was used to study activity of the synthesized catalysts in hydrodesulfurization of the cracked and the atmospheric gasoil blend. Catalysts activities were evaluated in hydrodesulfurization of a blend of 0.05 g/g visbreaker gasoil (VisGO) and 0.95 g/g atmospheric gasoil (AGO). Properties of the atmospheric gasoil (AGO), visbreaker gasoil (VisGO) and the blend are summarized in Table 2 . Prior to activity tests, all catalysts were activated during presulfiding process using mixture of 10 vol% H2S and H2 gases at 400 °C [9,40]. It takes about 2 h to reach the final sulfiding temperature with heating temperature ramp of 3 °C/min and the catalysts were sulfided at that temperature for 5 h. A typical presulfiding process takes 7 h to complete. The gas mixture was passed through the catalyst bed with flow rate of 300 mln/min. By establishing the steady state conditions, reliable results can be obtained. Steady state conditions were obtained after one operating day during which atmospheric gasoil was hydrotreated. After that the blend of 0.05 g/g visbreaker gasoil and 0.95 g/g atmospheric gasoil were used. Sulfur content evaluation was performed after washing the obtained sample with 1 M caustic solution to completely remove dissolved hydrogen sulfide and ammonia. Model development and analysis of the obtained results were conducted using RSM, which is a combination of statistical and mathematical methods [41]. The effects of four parameters that are potentially important in HDS catalyst synthesis were studied, and the catalyst performance was maximized by optimization of the obtained response surface. Using this methodology, the relationship between these parameters and the resulting response surfaces were quantified [42]. RSM was used in combination with a Box-Behnken design which is a fractional design method. Adoption of Box-Behnken design results in considerable reduction in number of experimental sets compared with common fractional design methods while the accuracy of the optimization is remained unaffected [43]. All the independent synthesis parameters (x i ) are measurable, so the answer of the system (y) and consequently the response surface can be expressed as: (1) y = f x 1 x 2 x 3 x 4 It was assumed that the independent variables were continuous and controllable with negligible errors. To optimize the response (y), a suitable approximation of the functional relationship between independent variables (x i ) and the response surface was considered, and the best one was defined to be a second-order model. (2) y = β 0 + ∑ i = 1 k β i x i + ∑ i = 1 k β ii x i 2 + ∑ i = 1 k − 1 ∑ j = 1 k β ij x i x j + ε β 0 , β i , β ii and β ij are constants which were determined in the second-order model. ε is a random error. The least square method was used to find these constants. Analysis of variance (ANOVA) is a decision-making tool of statistical studies to identify any possible differences in average performances of studied independent parameters. Sum of squares and F statistics are used to determine the relative importance of the studied parameters and the measurement of the errors. SEM micrographs of the synthesized catalyst supports are illustrated in Fig. 2 . The micrograph corresponding to the mesoporous γ-Al2O3 support is Fig. 2(a). As can be seen, macro and mesopores of γ-Al2O3 catalyst supports were maintained somehow unmarked compared with PB powders. However, by introducing the USY zeolite into the catalyst support, the texture of the support was changed and the difference from mesoporous γ-Al2O3 support was intensified by increasing the percentage of the USY zeolite. XRD patterns obtained for PB and USY zeolite powders and mesoporous composite USY/γ-Al2O3 supports are demonstrated in Fig. 3 . Considering the XRD spectrum represented for synthesized PB sample, all peaks referred to (020), (120), (031), (051), (200), (231), (002) and (251) planes are present which confirms the orthorhombic structure of the AlOOH with standard card coded as JCPDS 21-1307. The broad XRD peaks of this sample represent Nano-scale AlOOH crystallites. In addition, these broad peaks represent a low crystallinity and high water content in the synthesized PB powder [44]. In the XRD pattern of the synthesized USY zeolite all corresponding peaks to (111), (533), (331), (555), (642), (440), (220), (333), (660), (840), (664), (733), (311) and (620) planes are present, according which, prepared zeolite powder can be indexed as cubic NaY zeolites with standard JCPDS card number of 00-043-0168. Due to sharpness of the peaks, the obtained zeolite can be considered to have high crystallinity. In XRD patterns obtained for the three mesoporous supports, all peaks correspond to (311), (400) and (440) planes are present, representing the cubic gamma alumina [45]. The presence of broad XRD peaks can be attributed to the Nano-scale crystallite size and low crystallinity of the gamma alumina phase. In the XRD patterns of the supports containing USY zeolite, peaks corresponding to the zeolite phase are present and increasing concentration of USY zeolite increases intensity of these peaks. The results of nitrogen adsorption-desorption experiments, including BET surface area, average pore size, BJH meso-pore volume calculated from adsorption and desorption branches of the isotherm, are summarized in Table 3 . The surface area of the prepared supports is in the range of 250 to 300 m2/g. The pore volume of these supports is in the range of 0.68 to 0.75 cm3/g. The average pore size, calculated by the BJH method based on the adsorption and desorption branches of the isotherm is in the range of 98 to 108 and 85 to 91 Å, respectively. Based on our previous study [35], HDS catalyst supports with these average pore sizes are appropriate to be used in preparation of catalysts with maximum activity in HDS of cracked and atmospheric gasoil blend. Isotherms of nitrogen gas adsorption and desorption experiments are shown in Fig. 4 . As clearly seen, all synthesized supports show adsorption-desorption isotherms of type IV. Based on the hysteresis rings and isotherm type, multi-layer N2 adsorption, followed by capillary condensation can be considered for all three prepared supports which confirm presence of micro and meso pores. Pure γ-Al2O3 and composite supports pore size distribution curves, estimated using BJH method, are presented in Fig. 5 . The variation in pore size distribution curves of the supports could be attributed to the presence of USY zeolite and its concentration. The NH3-TPD results for the prepared supports are shown in Fig. 6 . In these TPD curves, two peaks are detectable at around 220 °C and 675 °C. The amount of desorbed NH3 per gram of the catalyst support according to each peak and the total amount of released ammonia are summarized in Table 4 . By increasing the zeolite content of the composite support, desorbed NH3 and consequently, the acidity of the support increases slightly, which could be related to higher acidity of the USY zeolite compared with PB powder. Results of elemental analysis of three synthesized catalysts (cat-00-20-33-0, cat-00-20-33-2 and cat-10-20-33-0) are illustrated in Fig. 7 . Considering sample “cat-00-20-33-0” as a reference, samples cat-00-20-33-2 and cat-10-20-33-0 were prepared with different USY/γ-Al2O3 support compositions and EDTA/Ni molar ratios, respectively. The nominal active phase loadings for all these three catalysts were 4.12 wt% of NiO and 15.88 wt% of MoO3. Results of Atomic absorption spectroscopy revealed that NiO and MoO3 loadings varied in ranges of 3.9 to 4.2 wt% and 15.1 to 16.0 wt%, respectively. In the other words, the active phase loading was performed with error which is under 6%. As can be seen in Fig. 7, surficial distribution of the active phase metals (Ni and Mo) is homogeneous and both species are distributed over the supports' surface equally. The H2 TPR profiles of the NiMo catalysts coded as cat-00-20-33-0, cat-00-20-33-2 and cat-10-20-33-0 are shown in Fig. 8 . Reduction profiles of these catalysts were similar with well-defined low temperature reduction peaks located around 420 °C which are corresponding to reduction of octahedrally coordinated multi-layer Mo (Mo6+ → Mo4+) and nickel (Ni2+ → Ni°). These peaks were followed by weak high temperature peaks located around 800 °C which could be assigned to reduction of the tetrahedral Mo species as well as deep reduction of all other Mo species which may have stronger metal-support interactions. The low temperature peaks of cat-00-20-33-0, cat-00-20-33-2 and cat-10-20-33-0 were located at 419, 411 and 427 °C, while the high temperature peaks were located at 832, 771 and 781 °C, respectively. In case of cat-00-20-33-2, the low temperature reduction peak is slightly shifted to lower temperatures, as well as the high temperature reduction peak which is moved from 833 to 771 °C; indicating that metal support interactions are weakened by using EDTA during catalyst synthesis. In sample cat-10-20-33-0 which is prepared using the composite support, the intensity of the high temperature peak is significantly reduced compared with that of the other two samples prepared using the pure Al2O3 support. Besides, the high temperature peak was moved from 833 to 781 °C. These two phenomena indicate that Mo is present mainly as easily reducible species like poly-molybdates that provide weak metal-support interactions. However, the reduction of poly-molybdates occurred at slightly higher temperatures in comparison with cat-00-20-33-0. The reduction behaviors are consistent with observations for NiMo catalysts supported on SiO2-Al2O3 [46] and Al2O3 [47]. It can be inferred that introducing USY zeolite into the catalyst support and using EDTA as the chelating agent in catalyst synthesis can result in ease of reduction of active phase metals and reduces the metal-support interaction. The results of N2 adsorption-desorption experiments for the prepared catalyst, including BET surface area, average pore size, and volume of micro and mesopores calculated from absorption and desorption branches of the isotherms using BJH method are summarized in Table 5 . The surface area of the prepared catalysts is in the range of 199 to 277 m2/g, the pore volume of these catalysts is in the range of 0.50–0.63 cm3/g while the average pore size calculated by the BJH method based on the absorption and desorption branches of the isotherm are respectively in the ranges of 88 to 107 and 75 to 90 Å. Based on our previous study [35], catalysts with an average pore sizes in these ranges have the most suitable pore size to achieve the highest activity in HDS of used AGO and VisGO blend. The surface areas of the prepared catalysts are all in the range of ±16% of the average value of 236 m2/g. In the case of pore volume and average pore size, the ranges of variations around the average values were calculated to be ±11% and ±9%, respectively. The average pore volume and adsorption (desorption) average pore size of the prepared catalysts were 0.56 cm3/g and 97 (83) Å, respectively. According to the obtained results reported in our previous study [35], it could be expected that diffusion of organic sulfur compounds which are present in the feed and the desulfurized species could be carried out without any limitations within the prepared catalysts. In other words, it can be assumed that the catalyst pore size is not a limiting factor in the reaction rate and it is possible to study the direct and interactive effects of USY/γ-Al2O3 support composition, total active phase loading, promoter to active phase and chelating agent to promoter ratios on catalyst performance, and determine the most important and dominant ones. During activation process, molybdenum and nickel oxides were undergone sulfiding reactions as described in Eqs. (3) and (4). The sulfur contents of the synthesized catalysts were measured experimentally and predicted using the developed model and are summarized in Table 6 . (3) MoO 3 + 2 H 2 S + H 2 → MoS 2 + 3 H 2 O (4) NiO 2 + H 2 S + H 2 → NiS + H 2 O The activity of sulfided catalysts was studied in hydrodesulfurization of the cracked and atmospheric gasoil blend. Sulfur content of the processed hydrocarbon mixture was measured and the obtained results are summarized in Table 6. To select the most adequate models and estimate the models' coefficients, statistical surveys of developed models for the sulfur content of the catalyst and the hydrotreated gasoil blend were performed, and the results are summarized in Tables 7 and 8 . According to the results, Quadratic models can be announced as the best models for both sulfur content of the synthesized catalysts and the processed hydrocarbon blend. Despite the higher R2 and comparable R2 adj values of Cubic models compared with the Quadratic ones, Cubic models could not be introduced as the best models in both cases. The reason is that the Cubic models are aliased, that is, the effects of each variable that generates different signals are not recognizable. Regarding the values of R2 and R2 adj calculated for the linear models and the models that merely consider the interactions of two different parameters (2FI), it is evident that none of these models are efficient and appropriate. According to the results of the statistical analysis, the developed Quadratic models were selected for fitting to the experimental data. The relationship between the sulfur content of the activated catalysts and the four independent studied parameters is shown as follows: (5) Catalyst Sulfur Content = 69.283 − 2.897 × USY zeolite − 2.787 × Ni + Mo − 117.2 × Ni / Ni + Mo − 12.77 × EDTA / Ni + 0.064 × USY zeolite × Ni + Mo + 4.59106 × USY zeolite × Ni / Ni + Mo − 0.2369 × USY zeolite × EDTA / Ni + 3.41927 × Ni + Mo × Ni / Ni + Mo + 0 × Ni + Mo × EDTA / Ni + 26.6708 × Ni / Ni + Mo × EDTA / Ni + 0 × USY zeolite 2 + 0.04747 × Ni + Mo 2 + 0 × Ni / Ni + Mo 2 + 2.77769 × EDTA / Ni 2 The relationship between the processed hydrocarbon sulfur content and the four independent studied parameters is shown as follows. (6) Treated Gasoil Sulfur Content = 160.659 + 0.275 × USY zeolite − 9.641 × Ni + Mo − 213.5 × Ni / Ni + Mo + 10.224 × EDTA / Ni + 0 × USY zeolite × Ni + Mo − 9.258 × USY zeolite × Ni / Ni + Mo + 0 × USY zeolite × EDTA / Ni + 4.765 × Ni + Mo × Ni / Ni + Mo + 0.715 × Ni + Mo × EDTA / Ni − 68.605 × Ni / Ni + Mo × EDTA / Ni + 0.129 × USY zeolite 2 + 0.173 × Ni + Mo 2 + 315.531 × Ni / Ni + Mo 2 − 2.1 × EDTA / Ni 2 R2 is defined as the ratio of variations that can be justified by the model to the total variation, and can be used as a measure of fitting quality. The minimum value of R2 for a good model is 0.8. This means that both of the developed models can accurately predict the sulfur content of the activated catalysts and the processed hydrocarbon. R2 (R2 adj) values of the developed models for sulfur content of the activated catalysts and the processed hydrocarbon are 0.98 (0.96) and 0.97 (0.94), respectively with a confidence level of 95%. Given the calculated values for F and p parameters, it can be concluded that the developed models are very significant. Tables 9 and 10 show the analysis of variance (ANOVA) results for the developed models. ANOVA is an analytical method used to identify the significance of the model and its parameters. Larger F values and small p values indicate parameters with greater influence and significance. The coefficients for total active phase loading and USY/γ-Al2O3 support composition are very large, which means that these factors have great influences on the sulfur content of the sulfided catalysts. It is worth mentioning that, all second-order effects are very small compared with self-interaction effects of the EDTA/Ni molar ratio, which indicates that there is an optimum point within the studied range for this parameter. The model's coefficient for USY/γ-Al2O3 support composition is very large and significant. In the case of three other variables, the coefficients are large and significant. Bearing in mind, it can be concluded that all factors had significant effects on the sulfur content of the processed hydrocarbon mixture, especially the USY/γ-Al2O3 support composition, which has the largest coefficient. In order to ensure and validate the proposed models' suitability, it is necessary to check their accuracy. The accuracy of the developed models was studied by comparing experimental and predicted sulfur contents of activated catalysts as well as the processed hydrocarbon blend. Figs. 9 and 10 show the linear relationship between predicted sulfur content and measured values for activated catalysts and the processed hydrocarbon mixture. Clearly, all the experimental data and the corresponding predicted values are distributed around the line within a narrow range. It could be concluded that both models are valid. Model optimization was performed considering the scope of this study which is preparation of the most active catalyst for hydrodesulfurization of the cracked and atmospheric gasoil blend as the optimization target. The proposed model for the catalyst activity predicts sulfur content of the processed hydrocarbon mixture in the range of 50.4 down to 2.3 ppm. In order to achieve the maximum catalyst activity and thus the minimum amount of sulfur remaining in the processed hydrocarbon blend, the independent variables of USY/γ-Al2O3 support composition, total metal (Ni + Mo) loading, the ratio of the loaded promoter to the active phase as Ni/(Ni + Mo) and the ratio of chelating agent to promoter as EDTA/Ni should be respectively adjusted to 10 wt%, 18 wt%, 0.417, and 2. To validate the predicted optimal response, an additional catalyst was prepared considering the optimum conditions. The activity test was performed and the amount of sulfur remaining in the hydrocarbon blend processed by this catalyst was measured as 3.1 ppm which is in good agreement with the results of the model. The sulfur content of the catalyst prepared under the optimal conditions was predicted to be 16.07% using the developed model, which was measured experimentally as 16.50. Considering the obtained results, it could be concluded that the proposed models are valid and accurate. Referring to the contents mentioned in the model development section and the results reported in Table 10, it is clear that the sulfur content of the processed hydrocarbon blend, in addition to the direct dependence of the studied parameters, is a function of the interactions between USY/γ-Al2O3 support composition and Ni/(Ni + Mo) molar ratio, total metal loading and Ni/(Ni + Mo) molar ratio, total metal loading and EDTA/Ni molar ratio, Ni/(Ni + Mo) molar ratio and EDTA/Ni molar ratio. Therefore, in order to depict the dependence of the processed hydrocarbon sulfur content to independent factors, four contour diagrams in each, one of the mentioned interactions are illustrated have been used to describe independent factors effects on the sulfur content of the processed hydrocarbon mixture. Considering that there are 4 independent variables, to draw each interaction contour, 2 of them should be adjusted and maintained constant. The optimum condition values were used for 2 independent parameters which were constant in each interaction contour. Fig. 11 illustrates effects of the USY/γ-Al2O3 support composition as well as the interactions between the USY/γ-Al2O3 support composition and Ni/(Ni + Mo) molar ratio. By increasing the amount of zeolite in the composite support, the sulfur content of the activated catalyst is reduced. This decrease is more evident when the Ni loading share of the total loaded metals is 25%. According to Fig. 11, it is clear that with increasing Ni/(Ni + Mo) molar ratio, the reducing effects of zeolite on the sulfur content of the catalyst loses its power and the slope of the variation decreases. The reason for this phenomenon is that in the presence of zeolite the possibility for formation of an active NiMoS phase increases due to the reduced interaction between the active phase and the support, and NiMoS phase replaces the NiS and MoS2 phases. This is consistent with the results of H2 TPR studies. The high temperature peak in H2 TPR profile of the prepared sample with the composite support, is significantly weaker and located at lower temperatures in comparison with the γ-Al2O3 supported catalyst. This indicates that, the interactions of metals with the composite supports are weaker than pure Al2O3 support. The formation of a NiMoS phase with less sulfur content compared with NiS and MoS2 phases reduces the sulfur content of the activated catalyst. In 18% total metal loading, for samples with small Ni/(Ni + Mo) molar ratios, significant decrease in the sulfur content of the activated catalyst by increasing the presence of zeolite in the support can be attributed to the high sulfur content of the MoS2 phase compared with NiS and NiMoS. In this case, with increasing the zeolite content of the support and consequently increasing the tendency toward formation of the NiMoS phase, the changes in the sulfur content will be much more severe. The modeling results also show the same trend. By increasing the amount of zeolite in catalyst support, sulfur content of the hydrotreated gasoil decreases, which indicates an increase in catalyst activity. The textural properties of mesoporous zeolite result in a good distribution of the catalyst active phase. The acidic properties of the support with a higher zeolite content increase the catalyst activity in the hydrogenation pathway [18,19]. Due to the heavy sulfur species present in the feed, the hydrogenation pathway provides a better route for hydrodesulfurization. On the other hand, by increasing the amount of zeolite, the interaction of the active phase and the support decreases; consequently, favoring formation of the NiMoS active phase, which has higher activity in direct hydrodesulfurization pathway compared with other phases, enhances direct HDS, and eventually the entire desulfurization reaction is enhanced. The results are consistent with the findings of Yin et al. [11,16]. Fig. 12 shows effects of total metal loading on the activated catalyst and the processed hydrocarbon sulfur contents as well as the interactions between total metal loading and Ni/(Ni + Mo) molar ratio. By increasing the total metal loading, sulfur content of the activated catalyst increases. This phenomenon can be linked to an increase in the number of sites which are active in the sulfiding process. According to Fig. 12, by increasing total metal loading, a decrease in sulfur content of the processed hydrocarbon blend can be seen which is followed by an increase. The trend of reducing product's sulfur content and increasing the catalyst activity by increasing the metal loading to about 20%, can be justified by the increase in the number of active sites. However, it can be seen that beyond this amount of metal loading catalyst activity was reduced, due to the active sites' accumulation, zeolite surface covering by the active phase and hydrogenation reaction pathway limitation. The Effects of Ni/(Ni + Mo) molar ratio on the sulfur content of the activated catalyst and the hydrotreated gasoil blend are depicted in Fig. 13 . Besides, interactions between EDTA/Ni and Ni/(Ni + Mo) molar ratios are shown in this figure. The effects of Ni/(Ni + Mo) molar ratio on the sulfur content of the activated catalyst and the processed hydrocarbon were investigated considering two different cases (EDTA/Ni = 0 and EDTA/Ni = 1 or 2). In the case of zero EDTA/Ni molar ratio, due to high interaction between the active phase and the support, no significant effects of Ni/(Ni + Mo) molar ratio on the sulfur contents of the activated catalyst and the processed hydrocarbon can be announced. However, when EDTA/Ni is equal to 1 or 2, due to the presence of the chelating agent in the impregnation solution and the formation of a metal complex, a better distribution of metals on the support surface and in active sites can be expected; which eventually, results in a catalyst which exerts suitable distribution of the active phase on its surface. Availability of these active sites during sulfiding boosts the activated catalyst's sulfur content and significantly enhances the catalyst performance in HDS reaction which can clearly be seen in Fig. 13. Fig. 14 depicts direct and interactive effects of the EDTA/Ni molar ratio and total metal loading on the sulfur content of the hydrotreated gasoil blend. By increasing the EDTA/Ni molar ratio in the impregnation solution, enhanced distribution of the active sites on the catalyst support and favored formation of NiMoS type II active phase [29,30] could be achieved as well as a catalyst with lower active phase-support interactions [22]; which enhance the catalyst activity and leads to reduction of the remained sulfur in the hydrotreated gasoil blend. Higher catalytic activity and weaker metal-support interaction of the catalyst prepared using EDTA as the chelating agent is consistent with the results of H2 TPR studies. The low and high temperature reduction peaks of the catalyst prepared in presence of EDTA was shifted to lower temperatures, indicating that metal support interactions are weakened. Improved HDS activity of the catalyst prepared in presence of EDTA is in agreement with results reported by Rana et al. [20], Sundaramurthy et al. [21] and Badoga et al. [22]. The catalyst initial stability test was performed using the most active catalyst among the 27 prepared catalysts. After presulfiding and stabilizing the catalyst using AGO feed during the first day of the process, hydrodesulfurization reaction conditions were maintained for 48 h. At the end of each 8 h, sampling was performed and the sulfur content of the processed hydrocarbon was measured. The obtained results are presented in Fig. 15 . The data points are in the range of 9.74 to 11.16 ppm with the average of 10.76 ppm. The results fluctuations and variations are in a narrow range with no meaningful trend. Thus, it can be concluded that the prepared catalysts are stable under considered hydrodesulfurization reaction conditions. This paper seeks to find the most active catalyst for hydrodesulfurization of the cracked and atmospheric gasoil blend through optimization of the active phase and composite catalyst support. In this research, it has argued that NiMo catalyst performance is governed by the direct and interactive effects of USY/γ-Al2O3 support composition, total metal loading as well as Ni/(Ni + Mo) and EDTA/Ni molar ratios. γ-Al2O3 and USY/γ-Al2O3 composite supports were prepared using the pseudoboehmite and the USY zeolite which were respectively synthesized through neutralization of NaAlO2 solution with CO2 and steaming of the ion exchanged NaY zeolite at 700 °C. 27 NiMo catalysts were prepared through impregnation of synthesized supports with different metal loadings and Ni/(Ni + Mo) and EDTA/Ni molar ratios. Experimental results showed that: 1- Synthesized supports are completely suitable for HDS catalyst preparation due to their surface area, pore volume and adsorption (desorption) average pore sizes in the ranges of 250 to 300 m2/g, 0.68 to 0.75 cm3/g and 98 (85) to 108 (91) Å, respectively. 2- BET surface areas, pore volumes and absorption (desorption) average pore sizes of the catalyst supports are in the ranges of 199 to 277 m2/g, 0.50–0.63 cm3/g and 88 (75) to 107 (90) Å, respectively; which are the most favorable textural properties to achieve the highest activity in hydrodesulfurization of a blend of 0.05 g/g VisGO into AGO. 3- The sulfur contents of the hydrotreated blend of 0.05 g/g VisGO into AGO were in range of 10.84 to 37.66 ppm, indicating high HDS activity of all prepared catalysts. Synthesized supports are completely suitable for HDS catalyst preparation due to their surface area, pore volume and adsorption (desorption) average pore sizes in the ranges of 250 to 300 m2/g, 0.68 to 0.75 cm3/g and 98 (85) to 108 (91) Å, respectively. BET surface areas, pore volumes and absorption (desorption) average pore sizes of the catalyst supports are in the ranges of 199 to 277 m2/g, 0.50–0.63 cm3/g and 88 (75) to 107 (90) Å, respectively; which are the most favorable textural properties to achieve the highest activity in hydrodesulfurization of a blend of 0.05 g/g VisGO into AGO. The sulfur contents of the hydrotreated blend of 0.05 g/g VisGO into AGO were in range of 10.84 to 37.66 ppm, indicating high HDS activity of all prepared catalysts. Two quadratic models were developed to study presulfiding behavior and hydrodesulfurization activity of the prepared catalysts. These models were optimized considering the hydrodesulfurization performance as the optimization target, and the optimum catalyst synthesis conditions were determined. The most important results obtained in the statistical studies could be summarized as: 1- The models predicted sulfur content of the synthesized catalysts and the processed hydrocarbons in ranges of 8.64 to 20.83 ppm and 11.24 to 38.60 ppm, respectively. 2- The optimum value of Ni/(Ni + Mo) ratio was determined to be 0.417 for USY/γ-Al2O3 composite support containing 5 and 10 wt% USY zeolite. 3- The most active catalyst was prepared by loading of 18 wt% metal oxides on USY/γ-Al2O3 support containing 10 wt% USY zeolite, while molar ratios of Ni/(Ni + Mo) and EDTA/Ni are adjusted to 0.417, and 2, respectively. 4- Investigated factors could be prioritized based on their effectiveness in HDS of the blend feedstock as 1) USY/γ-Al2O3 support composition, 2) EDTA/Ni molar ratio, 3) total metal loading and 4) Ni/(Ni + Mo) molar ratio. The models predicted sulfur content of the synthesized catalysts and the processed hydrocarbons in ranges of 8.64 to 20.83 ppm and 11.24 to 38.60 ppm, respectively. The optimum value of Ni/(Ni + Mo) ratio was determined to be 0.417 for USY/γ-Al2O3 composite support containing 5 and 10 wt% USY zeolite. The most active catalyst was prepared by loading of 18 wt% metal oxides on USY/γ-Al2O3 support containing 10 wt% USY zeolite, while molar ratios of Ni/(Ni + Mo) and EDTA/Ni are adjusted to 0.417, and 2, respectively. Investigated factors could be prioritized based on their effectiveness in HDS of the blend feedstock as 1) USY/γ-Al2O3 support composition, 2) EDTA/Ni molar ratio, 3) total metal loading and 4) Ni/(Ni + Mo) molar ratio. BET surface areas, pore volume and absorption (desorption) average pore sizes of the optimum catalyst were 269 m2/g, 0.55 cm3/g and 92 (79) Å, respectively. Using this catalyst, sulfur content of the gasoil blend was reduced from 11,500 to 3.1 ppm. Introducing mesoporous USY zeolite into the conventional γ-Al2O3 support matrix can significantly enhances the catalyst activity through favoring hydrogenation and isomerization reactions. Based on the H2 TPR studied, the presence of EDTA during impregnation, leads to improvement of the HDS catalysts activity. It could be related to the reduction in metal-support interactions and postponing nickel sulfide formation to temperatures at which Mo sulfides were formed. Considering promising hydrodesulfurization activities of prepared catalyst in HDS of cracked and atmospheric gasoil blend, a catalyst was introduced which was prepared to overcome and reduce the environmental impacts of using unconventional sources of hydrocarbons such cracked gasoil as transport fuels by reducing their sulfur content to meet euro V standard requirements. This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.
abstract: The effects of active phase and composite support compositions on activity of the HDS catalyst were investigated. Direct and interactive effects of USY/γ-Al2O3 support composition, total metal (Ni + Mo) loading, molar ratios of Ni/(Ni + Mo) and EDTA/Ni on activated catalysts sulfur content and hydrodesulfurization activity were investigated using response surface methodology (RSM). 27 catalysts with surface area, pore volume and BJH adsorption (desorption) average pore sizes in the range of 199 to 277 m2/g, 0.50 to 0.63 cm3/g and 88 (75) to 107 (90) Å were prepared. The concentration of sulfur remained in the hydrocarbon product, treated with different catalysts, was measured and results were in the range of 10 to 37 ppm. Based on the developed models, the most active catalyst was determined to be the one with composite support containing 10 wt% USY zeolite, loaded with 18 wt% metal oxides with Ni/(Ni + Mo) and EDTA/Ni molar ratios of 0.417, and 2, respectively. The lowest sulfur content of the hydrotreated gasoil blend was predicted to be 2.3 ppm, which was experimentally validated to be 3.1 ppm.
title: Ultra-deep hydrodesulfurization of cracked and atmospheric gasoil blend: Direct and interactive impacts of support composition, chelating agent, metal and promoter loadings

age the paste at 25°C for 2 h

age gel for two weeks

carry out ion exchange at 90°C for 1 h using 2 N NH4Cl solution with solution to zeolite ratio 10 ml/g

full_text: Metallic cobalt is an excellent catalyst for CO hydrogenation, yielding higher hydrocarbons in F–T synthesis [1]. The activity of supported cobalt catalyst in the F–T synthesis should be proportional to the area of the exposed metallic cobalt atoms [2,3]. The high dispersion of the cobalt metal is usually done by deposition of a cobalt salt on high surface area supports, such as silica and alumina, and subsequent reduction. The support with large surface area, however, usually contains small pore size, which results in poor intra-pellet diffusion efficiency of reactants and products, especially in multi-phase reactor. Slow transportation of reactants to and products from catalytic sites often controls the rate of primary and secondary reactions even on small catalyst pellet [4]. Meanwhile, the pore size of catalyst also affects product selectivities, due to the spatial effect of support. In contrast, the bimodal pore structure support, which contains large pores and small pores at the same time, contributs to higher dispersion of supported cobalt crystallites by the small pores, which enlarged the surface area of the support. Furthermore, it is able to diminish the diffusion resistance by its large pores. A support with a distinct bimodal pore structure has excellent advantages in solid catalysis reaction because the large pores provide pathways for rapid molecular transportation and small pores serve a large area of active surface, contributing to high diffusion efficiency and dispersion of supported metal simultaneously, as theoretically expressed by Levenspiel [5]. Until now, several preparation methods were reported to form bimodal pore catalyst support. But these methods used very corrosive reagents such as aqua regia, and the size of large pores of the obtained bimodal structure was as high as several hundred nanometer that the effect of bimodal structure was not obvious [6,7]. Furthermore, the reported methods are only effective for one specific oxide support, such as Al2O3 or SiO2. A general method to prepare bimodal structure, especially containing hetero-atom structure, is expected. To find a simple, clean, and general preparation method to form tailor-made bimodal pore structure, it is proposed here a new method to introduce silica or zirconia sols into large-pore silica gel directly to form the bimodal pore supports. As an application of this kind of bimodal pore support, it was used for liquid-phase FTS reactions where cobalt was supported. Meanwhile, as a promoter of Co/SiO2 FTS catalyst, zirconia should promote the catalytic activity as well [8]. The bimodal support was prepared by incipient-wetness impregnation of a commercially available silica gel (Cariact Q-50, Fuji Silysia, specific surface area: 70 m2 g−1, pore volume: 1.2 ml g−1, pellet size: 74–590 μm and mean pore diameter: 50 nm), with zironia sol (Seramic G 401, Nibban Institute, paticle size: 1.7–2.4nm, content: 21 wt.%, alcohol solution) or silica sol (Snowtex XS, Nissan Chemicals, particle size: 5 nm, content 20 wt.%, aqueous solution). After the impregnation, the support was calcined in air at 673 or 873 K for 2 h directly without drying, for zirconia or silica bimodal, respectively. Cobalt-supported catalyst with 10 wt.% metal loading was prepared by incipient-wetness impregnation of different supports, including the bimodal support, with cobalt nitrate aqueous solution. The catalyst precursors were dried in air at 393 K for 12 h, then calcined in air from room temperature to 673 K with a ramping rate of 2 K min−1 and kept at 673 K for 2 h. After calcination, the catalysts were activated in flowing hydrogen at 673 K for 10 h, and at last, passivated by 1% oxygen in nitrogen at room temperature for 1 h. FTS reaction was carried out in a semi-batch autoclave, slurry-phase reactor with the inner volume of 80 ml. The passivated catalyst (1.0 g, under 149 μm) and 20 ml liquid medium (n-hexadecane) were loaded in the reactor. During the reaction, effluent gas released from the reactor was analyzed by on-line gas chromatography. CO and CO2 were analyzed by using an active charcoal column equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD). The hydrocarbons were also analyzed using FID for C1–C5 (Porapak Q, on-line) and for C6–C20 (SE-30, uniport), respectively. Argon was employed as an internal standard with concentration of 3% in the feed gas. The reaction conditions were P (total)=1.0 MPa, CO/H2=1/2, W/F (CO+H2+Ar)=10 g h mol−1, T=513 K. Pore size distribution, BET surface area and pore volume were determined by a measuring instrument for surface area and pore size of porous materials (Shimadzu ASAP 2000) where nitrogen was used as absorbent. Supported cobalt crystalline size of the passivated catalysts was detected by TEM (TOPCON EM-002B, acc. volt: 200 kV, Point resolution: 0.18 nm, line resolution: 0.14 nm) and XRD (Rigaku, RINT2000). The supported cobalt crystalline average size was calculated by ∑n i d i 3/∑n i d i 2 for TEM data and by L=Kλ/Δ(2θ)cosθ 0 for XRD data, where L is the crystallite size, K is a constant (K=0.9–1.1), λ is the wavelength of X-ray (CuKα =0.154 nm), and Δ(2θ) is the width of the peak at half height. In this study, the 2θ of 44° for two kinds of bimodal catalysts and 42° for Q-3 catalyst were used to calculate the supported cobalt crystalline size. Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted using a Shimadzu DTG-60 instrument. The samples before calcination were placed in the furnace and heated in air from room temperature to the desired temperature, 873 K, at a heating rate of 10 K min−1 and then held at final temperature for 2 h. Temperature-programmed reduction (TPR) experiments were carried out in a quartz-made microreactor using 0.2 g calcined catalysts. The gas stream, 5% H2 diluted by nitrogen as reducing gas, was fed via a mass flow controller. After the reactor, the effluent gas was led via a 3 A molecular sieve trap to remove the produced water. The pore size distributions of silica gel Q-50 and bimodal pore supports are shown in Fig. 1 . It is clearly proved that the obtained silica bimodal support contained 6 and 45 nm pores meantime, unlike uniformly distributed silica gel Q-50. In calcination step, it is considered the small particles from silica sols formed 6 nm small pores through dehydration occurred between the surface OH-groups of silica sol particles, in the large pores of silica gel. The large pore of silica gel Q-50 slightly decreased from original 50–45 nm in bimodal structure. The BET surface area significantly increased from 70 to 132 m2 g−1 for silica bimodal due to the newly formed small pores, as compared in Table 1 . As the loaded particles of silica sols formed silica porous structure inside the large pores of Q-50, the pore volume of obtained bimodal support decreased. The BET surface area increased and pore volume decreased with the increased loading of silica, as shown in Table 1 also. Concerning the pore distributions, the obtained bimodal pore support distinctly exhibited two kinds of pores according to bimodal pore structure. The increased BET surface area and decreased pore volume indicated that the particles of silica sols entered into the original large pores indeed, and deposited on the inner surface of Q-50 to form the small pores, as illustrated in Fig. 2 . The pore size formation of bimodal pore support depended on newly formed silica porous structure inside the large pores of Q-50. If the sol-derived silica particles blocked the pore structure of Q-50 and did not form the new small pores inside the large pores, the BET surface area would decrease remarkably. Based on these findings, it is able to conclude that the obtained bimodal support formed according to the designed route, as shown in Fig. 2. It was reported that the particles of silica sol aggregated easily when vapor coexisted in calcination step, because the silanol on the surface of silica particles was easy to produce, contributing to movement of the particles of silica sol [9]. Based on this, the samples of silica bimodal supports were calcined at 873 K for 2 h directly without drying after impregnation to improve the aggregation of silical sols particles with the coexisting vapor and formation of new small pores. Concerning the TGA of the calcination step of the silica bimodal support sample, as shown in Fig. 3 , an endothermic weight loss was found at 355 K, due to the vaporization of water. After removal of solvent water, the dehydration of silanol occurred on the surface of the silica particles, resulting in aggregation of the silica particles to form the 6 nm small pores inside the large pores of silica Q-50. The endothermic weight loss between 727 and 800 K was attributed to this dehydration of silanol groups of the silica sols particles. There was no weight loss when the samples were held at 873 K for 2 h. For a mass of agglomerate of spherical particles, specific surface area (S) is given by following equation: (1) S=3/ρr where r and ρ are the radius and density of the particles, respectively. Based on this, the specific surface area of silica bimodal pore support can be determined by the particle size of silica sols, namely one can choose a kind of sol with favorite particle size to form a desired pore size distribution for bimodal pore support. Furthermore, if the diameter of a primary particle in sol is D, the pore size, produced by this kind of primary particles, is determined by diameter D and the agglutination of primary particles [10]. It will be 0.155D, 0.414D and D, when the primary particles aggregate as closest packing, primitive cubic lattice and diamond lattice, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2. In this study, the primary particle size of silica sol was 5 nm, and it formed new small pores inside the large pores of silica gel Q-50 as 6 nm. The more important point was that for obtained silica bimodal pore supports, the newly formed small pore size was 6 nm, irrespective of the increased silica loading, as exhibited in Table 1. These findings indicated that the primary particles of silica sol aggregated as diamond lattice mainly, inside the large pores of silica Q-50 in the calcination step. If the primary particles mainly aggregated as closest packing or primitive cubic lattice, the size of newly formed small pores would be smaller than the diameter of primary particle. If the primary particles at first formed the secondary particles mainly, which the latter formed new small pore, the size of newly formed small pores would vary with the changed silica loading. Furthermore, it is confirmed that the specific surface area and pore structure of porous silica support were thermally stable until 1200 K. Therefore the specific surface area and pore structure of obtained bimodal supports were not influenced by the calcination temperature, which was 873 K in this study. Based on these findings, it was considered that the silica bimodal pore support with desired large pores and small pores was able to be formed by choosing a silica gel with suitable large pores and a silica sol with favorite particle size. Meanwhile, a lot of oxides, such as zirconia, were used as promoter to synthesize highly active silica-base FTS catalyst [11]. By introducing different sols other than SiO2 sols into silica pellets, it is considered that the multi-function bimodal pore support can be formed, where besides spatial effect of bimodal pore structure, new chemical phenomenon or effect might appear with the hetero-atom formation between different oxides. In this study, the zirconia-silica bimodal pore support was formed by using zirconia sols and silica pellets. For the obtained zirconia bimodal support the specific surface area increased from 70 m2/g of silica gel Q-50 to 201 m2/g. And pore volume decreased from 1.2 ml/g of Q-50 to 0.41 ml/g of zirconia bimodal pore support, as shown in Table 1. The pores of the obtained zirconia bimodal pore support distinctly distributed as two kind of pore size, as 3.2 and 45 nm, according to bimodal structure, as exhibited in Fig. 1. To investigate the promotional role of the bimodal supports, it was applied to liquid-phase Fischer–Tropsch synthesis reaction. Liquid-phase Fischer–Tropsch synthesis reaction has advantages in temperature control, wax extraction and catalyst lifetime extension, compared to the common gas-phase reaction [12]. But the main drawback of the liquid-phase FTS reaction was the slow diffusion rate of the syngas as well as the formed hydrocarbons. To release the overall reaction from possible diffusion-controlled regime and obtain maximum hydrocarbon yield, silica bimodal support with 11.2wt.% silica loading and zirconia bimodal support with 16.8wt.% zirconia loading were used to prepare the cobalt supported catalysts, which were tested in liquid-phase FTS reaction. The reaction performance of the catalysts prepared from the bimodal support or Cariact Q-50, Q-3 was compared in Table 2 . Cariact Q-3 was an analogy to Q-50 but with average pore size of 3nm. Their properties were compared in Table 1. Catalyst prepared from Q-3 support had the largest surface area but the smallest pore diameter. It exhibited low catalytic activity and the highest methane selectivity. For the catalyst prepared from Q-50 support, which had the lowest surface area and the largest pore size, the CO conversion, CH4 and CO2 selectivity were the lowest. For the catalyst prepared from silica bimodal support, the CO conversion was higher than that of catalyst derived from Q-50 and Q-3, and meanwhile selectivities of CH4 and CO2 were as low as to those of the catalyst prepared from Q-50. For the zriconia bimodal catalyst, the CO conversion was significantly increased from that of silica bimodal catalyst as the highest in this study, and the selectivities of CH4 and CO2 were almost the same as those of silica bimodal catalyst. It has been already pointed out that the propagation of the carbon–carbon chain occurred more easily on the catalyst with lower specific surface area where metallic particle size was larger [13,14], which naturally decreased methane formation. When the pore size was larger, the transportation of the primary product was more effective and relevantly methane formation rate from secondary hydrocracking of olefins was lower. Also lower BET surface area of large-pore support determined larger metallic size and suppressed methane formation. On the other hand, the activities and selectivities of the FTS catalysts are markedly depending on their pore structure. The catalyst having a small pore size tended to produce lighter hydrocarbons. However, the product distribution of large-pore catalyst was wider and the proportion of the heavy hydrocarbon was high [15,16]. Based on these reasons, the methane selectivity was the lowest for the catalyst prepared from Q-50. Due to the larger pore existing in the catalyst prepared from bimodal support, the CH4 selectivity was also low. The hydrocarbon products of all catalysts were under the Anderson–Schulz–Flory products distribution. The chain growth probability and C5 + selectivity did not change significantly. The chain growth probability was 0.86, 0.86, 0.87and 0.84 of the catalysts derived from Q-50, silica bimodal, zirconia bimodal and Q-3, respectively, as shown in Table 2. On the other hand, FTS rates on cobalt catalyst can be improved by increasing the dispersion of supported cobalt crystalline. Generally, the metal dispersion is increased with the increased surface area and the decreased pore size of support. The supported cobalt crystalline average size was calculated by the data of TEM, as 37, 22.6, 21.6 and 1.4 nm for Q-50 catalyst, silica bimodal pore catalyst, zirconia bimodal pore catalyst, and Q-3 catalyst, respectively, as presented in Table 2. For Q-3 derived catalyst, its small pore and slow diffusion efficiency determined high methane selectivity. But its CO conversion was not the highest, regardless of its highest metal dispersion in this study. The supported cobalt crystalline size was detected also by XRD. The XRD spectra of various catalysts were shown in Fig. 4 . This clearly displays that the catalysts prepared from bimodal supports had smaller particle sizes than that of the catalyst prepared from silica gel Q-50. In Table 2, the metal particle size calculated by XRD data is generally in accordance with that from TEM. The supported cobalt dispersion was determined by H2 chemisorption at 373 K also, as shown in Table 2. It was clear that the dispersion of two kinds of bimodal pore catalysts was higher than that of Q-50 catalyst and lower than that of Q-3 catalysts. Based on these findings, it was proved that the dispersion of the supported cobalt was improved by the bimodal pore structure. The average pore size increased very slightly for various Co/SiO2 catalysts, compared to the corresponding bimodal pore support. Since the supported cobalt crystalline blocked some smaller pores of support, but the bimodal pore catalysts still kept bimodal structure with two kinds of pore size of 8 and 47 nm for silica bimodal catalyst, as well as 5 and 47 nm for zirconia bimodal catalyst, as shown in Fig. 5 . Bimodal catalyst, having higher metal dispersion due to its larger BET surface area, and accelerated diffusion rate derived from the bimodal structure, exhibited the highest CO conversion and low methane selectivity. It has been claimed that in low-pressure FTS, addition of zirconia to the Co/Al2O3 catalyst support led to markedly increased activity and C5+ selectivity but this phenomenon was not due to the increased cobalt dispersion and a change in the reducibility [17]. Withers et al. found an increase in activity for FTS with increasing Zr/Co ratio for Co on ZrO2/SiO2 support using a slurry-phase reactor [10]. Ali et al. reported that cobalt on ZrO2/SiO2 did not exhibit such a high dispersion effect and had the same reduction degrees as the un-promoted catalyst. But zirconia addition to Co/ SiO2 significantly increased the specific reaction rate of FTS [18]. In this study, the reduction property of various catalysts was detected by TPR. In TPR spectra of various catalysts, as shown in Fig. 6 , two peaks existed for the Q-50 catalyst, which located at 612 and 673 K. The two peaks have been identified as conversion Co3+ to Co2+ followed by the conversion of Co2+ to Co, and the broad region that was located above 800 K indicated the existence of several species reduced at approximately the same temperature [19]. For the Q-3 catalyst, the first peak located at 610 K and the second peak was rising from 700 K, while the H2 consumption continuously increased until 1073 K, indicating that some Co could not be reduced at lower temperatures. For silica bimodal catalyst, there was only one wide peak from 500 to 820 K. On the other hand, the TPR spectra of zirconia bimodal catalyst exhibited three peaks, which located at 610, 673 and 752 K. Especially, the first peak was more intensive than the second and the third peak. Comparing the reduction degrees of various catalysts, which were calculated by TPR data from 323 to 1073 K, the Q-50 catalyst showed the best reducibility as 98.6% due to its largest supported cobalt particle which had lighter interaction with silica support. For Q-3 catalyst, due to its smallest supported cobalt particles, which had the strong interaction with silica support, the reducibility was the lowest as 62.3%. Both of two kinds of bimodal catalysts showed almost the same reducibility, as 86.2% for silica bimodal pore catalyst and 88.1% for zirconia bimodal pore catalysts. In this study, it is considered that zirconia, which formed the new small pores of bimodal support, influenced the reduction performance due to some chemical effects, but did not improve the reduction degree of supported cobalt crystalline. Furthermore, on bimodal pore catalyst, the dispersion of supported cobalt crystalline was improved due to enlarged BET surface area by the newly formed zirconia porous structure inside original large pore, not attributed to chemical promotional effect of zirconia. A number of authors proposed that on zirconia modified cobalt silica catalysts, CO dissociation was facilitated, leading to an increase in reaction rate without a change in H2 activity [20,21]. The TOF of different catalysts was calculated in this study also. As shown in Table 2, the TOF of zirconia bimodal catalyst was significantly higher than that of silica bimodal catalyst, even though their reduction degree and crystalline size of supported cobalt were almost the same. Hence, the results suggest that the activity of Co sites might be increased due to the presence of ZrO2 in zirconia bimodal catalyst. ZrO2 might form an active interface with Co which was responsible to some extent for the enhancement in Co activity. From described above, it is clear that building up micropores using zirconia particles inside silica large pores realized two functionality, chemical promotion and spatial promotion, at the same time to enhance the activity for liquid phase FTS reaction. One was the bimodal structure with both large and small pores in the supports, which improved the diffusion rate of syngas and products, and the dispersion of active metal particles, respectively. The other was the presence of zirconia in the bimodal supports, which itself acted as a catalytic promoter for FTS reaction. These results indicate that a new, simple and general method for preparing bimodal support is developed by manipulation of zirconia nano-particles from its sol inside the pores of silica gel. By this method, the multi-functional bimodal pore support with both large pores of the desired size and micropores can be easily prepared and the large pore size of the bimodal pore structure is successfully controlled. Furthermore, building small pores using A oxide like a “brick” via its sol inside large pores of B oxide can easily extend to prepare a lot of kinds of bimodal supports if varying A and B. Besides spatial effect of bimodal pore structure, new chemical phenomenon or effect might appear with the hetero-atom formation between A and B. This method can be expanded to prepare various bimodal structure catalyst supports with different chemical compositions as it is possible to change the combination of large-pore gel and sol. For instance, Al2O3–SiO2 (large pore: Al2O3, small pore: SiO2), SiO2–SiO2 or SiO2–Al2O3 (large pore: SiO2, small pore: Al2O3) bimodal structure is also possible. The method of introducing oxide sols into the large pore silica pellet, to form tailor-made bimodal catalyst support, was developed. The distinctly distributed two kinds of pores, the increased BET surface area and the decreased pore volume of the obtained bimodal support proved that the particles from the sol indeed entered the uniformly distributed intrinsic large pores of silica pellet to ensure bimodal structure. The Co/SiO2 catalysts derived from silica bimodal supports were tested in slurry phase FTS. It showed higher activity and favorable selectivities, due to its improved dispersion of supported cobalt crystalline by bimodal structure, as proved by XRD and TEM, and fastened diffusion efficiency inside catalyst pellet with bimodal structure. Furthermore, besides the spatial effect from bimodal structure as shown in silica–silica bimodal catalyst, significantly enhanced activity was realized using ZrO2-silica bimodal support, as ZrO2 inside the large pores of SiO2 not only formed small pores but also intrinsically promoted FT synthesis.
abstract: A simple preparation method of bimodal pore supports was developed by introducing SiO2 or ZrO2 sols into large pores of SiO2 gel pellets directly. The pores of the obtained bimodal pore supports distributed distinctly as two kinds of main pores. On the other hand, the increased BET surface area and decreased pore volume, compared to those of original silica gel, indicated that the obtained bimodal pore supports formed according to the designed route. The obtained bimodal pore supports were applied in liquid-phase Fischer–Tropsch synthesis (FTS) where cobalt was supported. The bimodal pore catalysts presented the best reaction performance in liquid-phase Fischer–Tropsch synthesis (FTS) as higher reaction rate and lower methane selectivities, because the spatial promotional effect of bimodal pore structure and chemical effect of the porous zirconia behaved inside the large pores of original silica gel.
title: New bimodal pore catalysts for Fischer–Tropsch synthesis
