full_text: With fast increasing demand in liquid transportation fuels and limited petroleum resources, the production of transportation fuels from direct coal liquefaction (DCL) technologies has been an important option to lessen the deficiency in petroleum supply, especially for China which is a fast developing country with limited petroleum reserves [1]. But the liquid product obtained from DCL process has low hydrogen to carbon ratio and contains a considerable amount of heteroatom compounds that can cause many troubles such as instability of fuel during its transportation or storage [2]. The challenge in DCL technology includes design better catalysts for product upgrading to meet all the current specifications for transportation fuels, especially for feeds with high aromatic and nitrogen contents [1]. But nitrogen compounds are an inhibitor in hydrotreatment and cracking processes [3,4], and they can lead the catalysts used in the downstream secondary processing (such as fluid catalytic cracking and hydrocracking) of these stocks deactivate faster. Thus the higher content of nitrogen compounds present in the coal-derived heavy oil should be removed prior to further processing. N, S and O contents in heavy oil can be removed to the acceptable levels in petroleum refining by sulfided Ni–Mo, Co–Mo or Ni–W catalysts. The aromatic nature of DCL products is another challenge although there exist several catalytic processes to remove the aromatic content in several distillation cuts. Advanced refining technologies such as HDA must be implemented in order to fulfill the limits for aromatic contents. Cetane number can also be increased by the HDA process. The hydrogenation of polyaromatics is the main issue in upgrading of coal-derived liquid due to the various types and amounts of aromatic species present in the oil as well as the complexity of the reactions [5]. Considerable studies have been conducted to investigate the hydrotreatment of coal-derived product on various catalysts [6–9]. In order to improve the activity of hydrotreating catalysts, many approaches like (i) different preparation method, (ii) support modification and (iii) altering the active component have been followed in recent years. These approaches are important, especially those having an impact on the active phase. For example carrier acidic nature, strength and number can impact the catalyst activity, especially HDN and asphaltenes conversion, which may depend on the catalyst preparation protocols [10]. Among different supports and their combinations, ordered mesoporous silica supports such as MCM-41 and SBA-15 have attracted attention in recent years due to their high surface areas, large volumes of ordered mesopores (pores with diameters between 2 and 50nm), and diverse morphology. Some applications of these materials in catalysis have already been published [11–19]. SBA-15 is a silica-based mesoporous material with uniform hexagonal channels ranging from 3 to 50nm with narrow pore size distribution. It is one of the most attractive catalyst supports with high hydrothermal stability and large surface area of 600–1000m2/g, allowing for the dispersion of a large number of catalytically active species and the entrance of the large feed molecules. Also, SBA-15 has been used as support for NiW [16]; CoMo and NiMo sulfides [17,18]; and their hydrotreating activities have been explored using model compounds such as thiophene [17] and 4,6-dimethyldibenzothiophene [19]. The above studies evidence the superior activity of SBA-15 supported catalysts than the conventional γ-Al2O3 supported catalysts. Thus, SBA-15 possesses the characteristics of a potential support for the preparation of a commercial coal-derived heavy oil upgrading catalyst with high activity. However no any report is available on the hydrotreating of coal-derived liquid from pilot plant on the SBA-15 supported catalysts, especially on SBA-15 supported catalysts with different pore sizes. In this investigation aseriesofNi–WsupportedonSBA-15mesoporouscatalysts were prepared,characterizedandtested for upgrading of DCL heavy oil under the online hydrotreating conditions. The pore sizes of the catalysts werevariedand the effects of preparation method and pore structure of SBA-15 supports on hydrotreating activities (HDN and HDA) of Ni–W catalysts were evaluated. The information obtained is useful for the development of better catalysts for upgrading of coal-derived liquids. The Shengli coal-derived heavy oil (HO) used was generated from direct liquefaction of Shengli coal in a 0.1ton/day continuous unit at China Coal Research Institute, Beijing. The DCL reactor was operated at 455°C and 19MPa in the presence of Shenhua Fe-based catalyst (1wt.% Fe loading on daf coal basis). The catalyst is a highly dispersed ultra-fine FeOOH prepared by a procedure reported in the literature [20,21]. The products from the DCL unit were separated by passing through two separators, atmospheric and vacuum distillation columns. The HO used in this study was the narrow fraction of Shengli coal-derived liquid (distillation temperature: IBP-450°C), which distillation temperature was in the range of 320–340°C and density is about 0.97g/ml. The element analysis of HO is shown in Table 1 . SBA-15 molecular sieves with different pore sizes were prepared using Pluronic P123 (EO20PO70EO20, MAV=5800, Aldrich) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, AR) under acidic conditions following the method reported previously [22]. The narrow pore size silica, labeled as SBA-15-1, was synthesized without any hydrothermal treatment. The detailed synthesis route was as follows: P123 (10g) and 2M HCl (350mL) were mixed at 35°C to obtain a clear solution. Subsequently, TEOS (21g) was gradually added to the solution and stirred for 48h. The materials were obtained by filtration and washing with deionized water. Finally, the obtained materials were transferred to a muffle furnace and calcined at 500°C for 5h. The preparation of SBA-15-2 is similar to that of SBA-15-1 except for hydrothermal treatment at 130°C for 24h before filtration and washing with deionized water. The third silica, labeled as SBA-15-3, was obtained as the same as that of SBA-15-2 except for the addition of 3mL 1,3,5-tri-methylbenzene (its role is to expand the pore size) prior to TEOS in order to increase of the pore size of SBA-15-2. The loading of Ni–W was carried out by incipient wetness impregnation of the support using mixed solutions of ammonium metatungstate and nickel citrate. The impregnated sample was dried and calcined at 500°C for 3h. These calcined precursors were then sulfided at 400°C with a CS2/ H2 gas (10/90%, 60ml/min) for 2h followed by degassing at the same temperature in stream of H2 for 1h. The sample was then cooled to room temperature and then passivated in stream of 1% O2 for 6h. The prepared Ni–W catalyst contains 5wt.% Ni and 15wt.%W. All catalysts were ground to pass through a 200 mesh screen, stored under nitrogen atmosphere before use. BET surface area of the sulfided catalyst was determined by nitrogen adsorption at −196°C using an ASAP2000 volumetric adsorption analyzer. Total pore volume was calculated from the amount of nitrogen adsorbed at a relative N2 pressure (P/P0) of 0.99. The average pore diameter was assumed to be 4V/BET [23]. All the samples were degassed at 150°C for 12h before the measurement. Small angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) measurements were performed using a Bruker D8 diffractometer equipped with a Cu-Kα source. X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) of sulfided catalysts were obtained on a Rigaku computer-controlled D/max 2500× using Cu-Kα as the radiation source. During the analysis catalysts were scanned from 10 to 80° at a speed of 4°/min. The hydrogenation experiments of Shengli coal-derived heavy oil were carried out batch-wise in a 30ml tubing reactor shaken vertically. 3.0g of heavy oil was charged into the reactor together with 0.15g sulfided catalyst, which simulates typical online hydrotreating conditions for heavy liquids in DCL with an initial H2 pressure of 5MPa and a temperature of 400°C. After loading the sample the reactor was sealed and flushed 3 times with hydrogen followed by turning the system to the desired initial pressure of 5MPa H2. The reactor agitated vertically at 120rpm was submerged into a eutectic salt bath, which had been heated to the desired temperature and maintained for 60min. After the reaction, the reactor was quenched in cold water and the gases in the reactor were vented. The liquid and catalyst in the reactor were poured out and separated by centrifuge. Nitrogen analysis was performed using an Elementar Vario EL III. The liquid samples in elemental analysis were sealed in the capsule. Air in the capsule was forced out via a gas stream of oxygen. HDN activity was calculated by determining the nitrogen content of the HO and its reaction product after the hydrogenation. Aromatic distribution analysis was carried out on Shimadzu LC-2000 high performance liquid chromatography with an UV detector (λ=254nm). A Sepax NH2 (30cm length, 0.8cm i.d.) separation column was operated isothermally at 25°C. Hexane was used as the mobile phase with a flow rate of 1.2ml/min. The identification of aromatic components (biaromatic and triaromatic) was carried out by the retention time of selected representative components of the mono-, di- and triaromatic groups (toluene, naphthalene, anthracene and phenanthrene). The BET surface area, pore volume and pore size distribution of three sulfided catalysts are shown in Table 2 . Table 2 shows that the surface area, the pore volume and average pore diameter of Ni–W/SBA-15-2 are slightly higher than those of Ni–W/SBA-15-1. Ni–W/SBA-15-3 had a smaller surface (about 133m2/g) compared to Ni–W/SBA-15-2 sample. But the difference of pore volume between Ni–W/SBA-15-2 and Ni–W/SBA-15-3 was very small, which is caused by the difference of the pore size of the two samples. The observed textural differences may result in the differences of the metal dispersion and catalytic activity for the three supports. The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of sulfided Ni–W/SBA-15 catalysts are shown in Fig. 1 . All the sulfided Ni–W/SBA-15 catalysts exhibited the typical type IV isotherms according to BDDT classification, which exhibits the condensation and evaporation step characteristic of periodic mesoporous materials. The pore diameter distribution curves of the samples are shown in Fig. 2 . It clearly shows that sulfided Ni–W/SBA-15-1 and Ni–W/SBA-15-2 catalysts exhibit unimodal distributions of pore size with a specific range (3–7nm and 7–8nm, respectively). It could be seen that, compared with the Ni–W/SBA-15-1 and Ni–W/SBA-15-2 catalysts, the pore size of Ni–W/SBA-15-3 was broad (diameter ranging from 5 to 80nm). In the small angle X-ray scattering (SAXS, Fig. 3 ), the as-synthesized SBA-15-1 showed the strong (100) peak and very weak (110) and (200) signals, corresponding to a highly ordered hexagonal mesoporous silica framework, confirming the formation of highly ordered mesoporous structure. On the other hand, the intensity of the prominent reflection (100) as well as the (110) and (200) reflections of SBA-15-2 significantly decreased compared to that of SBA-15-1, indicating the loss of long range order. No scattering signals were detectable for the SBA-15-3, suggesting less organized pore structures of this sample. The disordered XRD pattern indicates that only amorphous phase isformedorverysmallcrystallinephases. This indicates that using 1,3,5-tri-methylbenzene as auxiliary chemicals leads to phase formation from highly ordered hexagonal mesostructure to mesostructural cellular foams, which is agreed with Jana and Schmidt-Winkel's findings [24,25]. Fig. 4 shows the SAXS profiles for sulfided Ni–W/SBA-15 catalysts. After introducing the Ni–W sulfide species, the diffraction peaks for SBA-15-1 was kept, indicating that supported Ni–W sulfide species had a small impact on the long range ordering and mesoporous structure of the support. But for SBA-15-2, the intensity of these diffraction peaks significantly decreased after introducing the Ni–W sulfide species, suggesting that the mesoporous structure of SBA-15-2 maybe partially destroyed. Fig. 5 shows the X-ray diffraction patterns of the three sulfided Ni–W/SBA-15 catalysts. The XRD patterns of sulfided Ni–W/SBA-15-1 catalyst displayed only XRD peaks of the SBA-15 support, and no characteristic peaks of NiS2 (2θ: 27.4, 35, 39, 45.2, 53.8), WS2 phases (2θ: 14, 33.5, 39.5), NiO (2θ: 43.4, 37.4, 63.0) and WO3 (2θ: 23.1, 23.6, 24.3), metallic nickel (2θ: 44.5, 51.9, 76.4)/tungsten phase (2θ: 40.6, 59.1, 74.0) were observed. This result indicates that the Ni and W species are either completely amorphous or composed of crystallites smaller than 4nm under preparation conditions [26]. Also the absence of diffraction lines of nickel or tungsten sulfide indicates that these phases have a very small particle size or that they are inserted into the structure of WS2 or MoS2, forming a well dispersed Ni–W(Mo)–S solid solution[27]. But for Ni–W/SBA-15-2 or Ni–W/SBA-15-3, which has the smallest surface area and disordered mesoporous structure, the WS2 phases can be confirmed. This result indicates that the catalyst preparation method and pore diameter impact the active phase, which may be due to the small surface area of the support. Fig. 6 shows the HDN activities of the three sulfided Ni–W/SBA-15 catalysts. HDN conversions of sulfided Ni–W/SBA-15-1,-2 and -3 catalysts were 15%; 22% and 39% respectively. This indicates that the HDN of HO is not an easy task using this type of catalysts. The HDN activity increased with the increase of pore size of Ni–W/SBA-15 catalyst. Ni–W/SBA-15-3 had larger pore size as shown in Table 2 and it gave the highest HDN conversion among the three Ni–W/SBA-15 catalysts. This may indicates that the catalyst with larger pore size has higher HDN activity towards coal-derived heavy oil hydrogenation. For hydroprocessing of heavy oils, it has been found that the catalyst pore size may have a significant effect on activity, because intrapore diffusion in this situation is an important factor. Catalyst pore structure decides if the feed molecules can easily access the interior surface of the catalyst. The prevailing explanation for pore size effects in hydrotreating of coal liquids is of steric hindrance of large molecules. It anticipates that the smaller pores will contribute to HDN of smaller molecules and the larger pores promote the reactions of larger molecules [28,29]. This implies that the heavier materials containing N in HO are usually larger in molecular weights and sizes. Murti [7] found that pyridines, anilines, quinolines and indoles were the major nitrogen-containing compounds in South Banko coal liquid distillate and anilines were the most dominant nitrogen species after denitrogenation at 400°C. Determination of the changes in the type of nitrogen compounds before and after HDN should be useful for further understanding of the effects of catalyst pore structure. Corresponding works will be done in our later researches. The gravity of product obtained from hydrotreatment on Ni–W/SBA-15-3 is about 0.93g/ml. The difference of the density between feedstock and product indicates that the composition of heavy oil has been significantly changed. Fig. 7 shows the aromatic ring distributions of the feedstock (HO) and its hydrogenation products in the presence of the three sulfided Ni–W/SBA-15 catalysts. The percentile decrease of aromatic content was determined and defined as HDA activity, which was calculated by the difference of the peak area of aromatic hydrocarbons before and after the hydrotreatment. It shows that HO mainly contained biaromatics (naphthalene and its derived) and triaromatics (anthracene and phenanthrene). After hydrogenation the amounts of aromatics significantly decreased for all the catalysts. Table 3 shows the HDA activities of three sulfided Ni–W/SBA-15 catalysts. It shows that the HDA activities of sulfided Ni–W/SBA-15-1,-2 and -3 catalysts were 50%, 55% and 61% respectively. With the increase of pore size the HDA activities increase. For sulfided Ni–W/SBA-15-3 catalyst the HDA activities of triaromatics were higher than that of Ni–W/SBA-15-2 by 9%. This indicates that the larger pore of catalyst can significantly increase HDA activities of triaromatics. In order to get some more insights into the reactivity of HO, the HDA activity of triaromatic (anthracene and phenanthrene) were calculated in Table 3. It can be seen that hydrogenation of HO on the sulfided Ni–W/SBA-15-1 results in 47% and 56% decreases of anthracene and phenanthrene respectively. With the increase of pore size all of the conversions of anthracene and phenanthrene over Ni–W/SBA-15-2 increased by 3% compared to that in the presence of Ni–W/SBA-15-1. Especially over Ni–W/SBA-15-3 the conversions of anthracene and phenanthrene increased by 6% and 19% respectively compared to that in the presence of Ni–W/SBA-15-1. This indicates that pore size can significantly affect the hydrogenation activities of Ni–W/SBA-15. Also Song et al. found that asphaltene conversion for Ni–W/Al2O3 with large pore size was higher than that for Ni–W/Al2O3 with small pore size during upgrading heavy coal liquids [28,29]. It is recognized that support's nature can affect the catalyst activity and selectivity by changing the active phase morphology and dispersion, interaction between the active phase and the carrier, the electronic and acidic properties of the catalyst [10,16–18]. Based on results of catalyst characteristics mentioned above it can be seen that the active phase is impacted by the catalyst preparation method. Also, the number and type of active sites are certainly modified. Nevertheless HDA and HDN activities determined show that the larger pore size catalyst (Ni–W/SBA-15-3) has highest HDA and HDN activities compared to those of the other two catalysts. Therefore the HDN and HDA activities of Ni–W/SBA-15 maybe related with the pore diameters of Ni–W/SBA-15. As shown in Fig. 8 it can be seen that the HDN and HDA activities (especially tri– and phenanthrene) increased with the pore diameter up to 15.7nm in our studied range. Apparently the presence of very large pores does offer an advantage for upgrading of coal-derived heavy oil. This indicates that the pore size of catalyst affects the HDA and HDN activities for upgrading of coal-derived heavy oil, which favors the diffusions of reactants and products especially for the diffusion of large molecules such as coal-derived heavy oil [28,29]. Also the modification of support such as SBA-15 doped with Al, Zr or Ti will increase either the activity or thermal stability since these additives have positive effects such as: promoting a stabilization in the impregnate solution, increasing the catalyst dispersion and avoiding the Nickel lost in the support [18,19]. Further detailed study is carrying out in our laboratory to further increase the activity of the catalyst. A series of Ni–W/SBA-15 catalysts with different pore sizes have been prepared and used for hydrotreatment of coal-derived heavy oil at 400°C with initial H2 pressure of 5.0MPa. Small angle X-ray scattering showed that the initial highly ordered hexagonal mesostructure with long range order of support lost partially and finally turned to mesostructural cellular foams with the increase of pore size. The XRD patterns of a series of Ni–W/SBA-15 catalysts with different pore sizes indicated that the catalyst preparation method affected the metal sulfide cluster size. The catalytic activities tests revealed that NiW/SBA-15 catalyst with 15.7nm of pore diameter had the best catalytic HDN and HDA activities in this study. The catalyst preparation method and the pore size of the support have a significant influence on the active phase and hydrogenation activities.
abstract: A series of Ni–W catalysts supported on SBA-15 with different pore sizes were prepared by incipient wetness impregnation method and characterized by N2 adsorption–desorption and X-ray diffraction. The hydrogenation of heavy oil (distillation temperature: 320–340°C) derived from the direct coal liquefaction process using Shengli coal in the presence of sulfided Ni–W/SBA-15 catalysts with different pore sizes were evaluated at 400°C and initial H2 pressure of 5.0MPa. The results showed that the catalyst preparation method and the pore size of the support had a significant influence on the Ni/W crystallite size, hydrodenitrogenation (HDN) and hydrodearomatization (HDA) activities of coal-derived heavy oil. The larger pore could cause the Ni–W/SBA-15 to form larger Ni–W crystallite. The catalysts with largest pore in the range studied displayed highest HDN and HDA activities for upgrading of the coal-derived heavy oil.
title: Hydrotreatment of heavy oil from a direct coal liquefaction process on sulfided Ni–W/SBA-15 catalysts

full_text: The utilization of low rank coal by means of pyrolysis is an important method to obtain valuable products including gas, char and coal tar [1,2]. Among these products, coal tar has characteristic of high viscosity, thermal instability, corrosiveness, high content of phenols, and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [3–5]. The color of coal tar is brown or black. As compared to the conventional crude oil, coal tar is denser and poor quality [6]. Therefore, the conversion of tar into valuable chemicals or light fuels is still required [7,8]. Tar can be subjected to thermal cracking or catalytic cracking to produce light fuels or useful chemicals [7], which is based on the decarbonization and hydrogenation. Traditional hydrocracking can produce high-quality clean fuels [9,10]. Meng et al. prepared NiMo catalysts supported Beta-KIT-6 catalysts and applied to hydrocracking of low-temperature coal tar to produce gasoline oil [10]. Li et al. used nickel catalyst supported on microfiber attapulgite to investigate the hydro-conversion of coal tar model compounds [11]. However, hydrogen is expensive [12,13] and hydrocracking process usually requires severe operational conditions, increasing the cost in capital and operation [14]. Compared to the typical hydrocracking process, the partial oxidation using transition metal oxide, particularly of Fe2O3, for upgrading of heavy oil has received much attention [15]. Fumoto et al. developed Fe2O3-based catalysts for upgrading of vacuum residue (VR) [12]. Lee et al. synthesized ZrO2-impregnated red mud, rich in Fe2O3, for the partial oxidation of VR [16]. The partial oxidation could be applied to the coal tar upgrading. Sonoyama et al. reported that the utilization of Fe2O3-based catalysts could upgrade Loy Yang coal tar to produce useful chemicals; moreover, the conversion of heavy tar is around 97.0 wt% [8]. Considering the wide application of Fe2O3 in the upgrading of heavy oil, an integrated process of coal tar upgrading and in-situ reduction of Fe2O3 was proposed in this study. The effect of reaction temperature on the product distribution and composition was investigated. Changes in physical and chemical properties of Fe2O3 were characterized by various characterization methods. Finally, coke deposited on the spent Fe2O3 samples was used as reducing agent to reduce spent Fe2O3. The co-precipitation method was used to prepare Fe2O3. Generally, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O and 2 M NaOH were used as metal salt precursor and precipitator, respectively. Similar synthetic procedure has been reported in the previous literature [17]. The resulting Fe2O3 was screened to obtain a particle size of 380–830 μm. Low temperature coal tar was used as feedstock and the basic properties are shown in Table 1 . Coal tar upgrading was performed on a fixed bed reactor as shown in Fig. 1 . The detail description of reaction system and procedure can be found in previous works [17,18]. The loading of Fe2O3 and the mass flow rate of coal tar are 1.00 g and 2.50 g/h, respectively. The effect of temperature including 500, 600, 700, and 800 °C was investigated. After 1 h of reaction, liquid and gas products were separated and collected by means of ice trap and gas bag, respectively. Liquid product contains tar and water, which are separated on the basis of ASTM D95-05e1 (2005). Carbonaceous residue (CR) including coke and residue was calculated according to the difference in weight of reactor before and after reaction. The gas yield in weight was determined from the gas volume and gas composition according to the ideal gas state equation. It should be noted that the active oxygen species in Fe2O3 can participate in the reaction and contribute to the generation of CO and CO2, resulting in the total product yield higher than 100 wt% in all the tests. The increment (Y actual total yield − 100 wt%) can be seen as the oxygen amount from Fe2O3. Fraction distribution was measured on SICON 540. It was divided into gasoline (500 °C). Elemental analysis was conducted on VARIO EL cube. Gas component was determined on GC 7890 II. Coal tar composition was analyzed on Agilent 7890A coupled with Agilent mass detector. Crystal phase transition of Fe2O3 was detected by X-ray diffraction (XRD) on D/Max 2400 diffractometer. Thermogravimetry (TG) analysis was performed on a Mettler Toledo TGA/SDTA851e to record weight change behavior with respect to temperature. Textural properties were obtained on JK-BK 122 W by measuring the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms. Morphology was imaged on QUANTA450. XPS spectra were obtained on Thermo Scientific ESCALAB™ 250Xi+. The product distribution from different reaction temperature is shown in Fig. 2a. When the reaction temperature is 500 °C, tar is the main product with the yield of 72.0 wt% and the gas yield is only 11.3 wt%. With the increase of temperature, yield of tar decreases and, conversely, that of gas increases. For example, yield of gas and tar at 800 °C is 50.0 and 34.7 wt%, respectively. In the case of water and CR yields, no obvious change is noted. The above results show that the increase of temperature mainly promotes the conversion of tar into gas rather than water or CR. Fig. 2b shows the change in gas composition with temperature. When the reaction temperature is 500 °C, the main composition in gas stream is H2 being 73.9 vol%, following by CO2 and CH4 with the content of 13.0 and 7.7 vol%, respectively. The content of H2 and CO2 decreases with the increase of temperature, and is 26.3 and 3.9 vol%, respectively, at 800 °C. In contrast, the content of CH4 increases obviously as the reaction temperature increases. The main gas composition is CH4 with 40.5 vol%, followed by H2 and CO being 26.3 and 14.3 vol%, respectively, at 800 °C. Reactions involved in the tar upgrading are complex. Nguyen-Huy and Shin suggested that the thermal cracking and catalytic cracking could occur simultaneously in the heavy oil upgrading [19]. When the metal oxide with the capability of releasing active oxygen species is used, partial oxidation and complete oxidation may occur in the upgrading of coal tar. If the complete oxidation is the major reaction as shown in Eq. (1), the CO2 and H2O are the final products [20,21]. However, the yield of H2O shows no obvious change with the increase of reaction temperature, suggesting that the complete oxidation is not the major reaction. If the contact between hydrocarbon molecules and Fe2O3 is not good enough, partial oxidation as shown in Eq. (2) will be the major reaction rather than the complete oxidation. The partial oxidation can produce gas [22,23] and liquid product [24]. In addition of oxidation reactions, other potential reactions including steam reforming, steam dealkylation, hydro-cracking, hydro-dealkylation, dry reforming, cracking, and carbon formation have been reported during tar decomposition [25] as shown in Eqs. (3)–(9). (1) Complete oxidation : 2 C n H m + M x O y → 2 nCO 2 + mH 2 O + M x O y ‐ 4 n ‐ m (2) Partial oxidation : C n H m + M x O y → CO + H 2 + M x O y ‐ 1 + C i H j + C n − i − 1 H m ‐ j ‐ 2 (3) Steam reforming : C n H m + nH 2 O → nCO + n + 0.5 m H 2 (4) Steam dealkylation : C n H m + H 2 O → C n ‐ 1 H m + H 2 + CO (5) Hydro ‐ cracking : 2 C n H m + 4 n ‐ m H 2 → 2 nCH 4 (6) Hydro ‐ dealkylation : C n H m + H 2 → C n ‐ 1 H m ‐ 2 + CH 4 (7) Dry reforming : 2 C n H m + 2 nCO 2 → 4 nCO + mH 2 (8) Cracking : C x H y → C x ‐ 1 H y ‐ 4 + CH 4 (9) Carbon formation : 2 C x H y → 2 xC + yH 2 Fig. 3 shows the distillation fractions of tar. The diesel fraction is the main component, and its content is higher than 50 wt% at different temperature. With the increase of temperature, the diesel content increases firstly, reaching the maximum being 62.0 wt% at 600 °C, after that it decreases to 51.5 wt% at 800 °C. The change of gasoline content is the same as that of diesel fraction, but the change of VGO shows the opposite trend. Among these fractions, the heavy oil has the lowest content and it increases to 8.0 wt% at 800 °C. The elemental analysis of tar was conducted, and the change of C, H, N, S, O, and H/C ratio is shown in Fig. 4 . The C and H shows a monotonous change with the increase of temperature, but their changes are exactly the opposite. The C content increases and H content decreases with the increase of temperature. The content of C and H is 80.57 wt% and 11.12 wt% at 500 °C, and becomes 87.97 wt% and 5.96 wt% at 800 °C, respectively. The H/C ratio decreases from 1.66 to 0.81 as temperature increases from 500 to 800 °C. For the heteroatoms, the O has the highest content, followed by N and S. The O content decreases with increase of temperature, but the change of N and S is not significant. The elemental analysis revealed that the variation of H/C ratio in tar with temperature is obvious, which indicates that the chemical compounds in the tar could be changed. To determine tar composition, the GC/MS analysis was carried out. As shown in Fig. 5 , the total ion chromatogram (TIC) indicates that tar composition is complex. The main peak position within 50 min remains almost unchanged, but the peak intensity changes, suggesting the change of its content. Peaks with the retention time exceeding 50 min can be clearly observed at 500 °C. These peaks are mainly attributed to the aliphatic compounds including alkanes and alkenes. The intensity of these peaks decreases at 600 °C. Moreover, the peak positions and intensity at 700 and 800 °C are different from those of 500 and 600 °C, suggesting that the chemical compounds in the tar product have changed. The chemical compounds in tar are classified into aliphatics (alkanes and alkenes), BTX (benzene, toluene, and xylene), phenols (phenol and its alkyl-substituted phenols), and PAHs (polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons). Fig. 6 shows the content of chemical compounds in tar at different temperature. The aliphatics show the highest content being 47.1 wt% at 500 °C. Its content decreases with the increase of temperature. The aliphatic molecules can be hardly detected at 700 and 800 °C. Generally, the aliphatic compounds have a high H/C ratio. The decrease of aliphatic compounds content leads to the decrease of H/C ratio in tar, which explains why the H/C ratio decreases from 1.66 to 0.81 when the reaction temperature increases from 500 to 800 °C. The aliphatic compounds are not stable under the reaction temperature and will be cracked into smaller molecules such as CH4. The cracking of aliphatic compounds is a highly endothermic reaction; therefore, the increase of temperature is contributed to the high conversion, leading to the decrease in aliphatic compounds content. From Fig. 2b, the CH4 content increases with the increase of temperature. Hao et al. reported that the formation of CH4 at the temperature range of 550–800 °C is occurred from the cracking of methyl (CH3 ) and methylene (CH2 ) in the aliphatic molecules and alkyl side chains bounded to aromatic rings and naphthenic rings [26]. The combination between CH3 and H leads to formation of CH4 [26]. Therefore, the cracking of aliphatic compounds results in the production of CH4. The content of BTX increases from 5.6 to 13.2 wt% as the temperature increases from 500 to 800 °C. Among these, the increase of toluene is the most obvious. It increases from 2.8 to 6.3 wt% when the reaction temperature increases from 500 to 800 °C. The content of phenols increases first and then decreases to 4.8 wt% at 800 °C. The content of other PAHs including naphthalenes, tricyclic and tetracyclic compounds increases as the temperature increases. With respect to naphthalenes, the content of naphthalene increases from 1.4 to 17.2 wt% when temperature increases from 500 to 800 °C. PAHs with 3, 4 or more aromatic rings show a low content at 500 and 600 °C, while the sum of their content increases to 35.6 wt% at 800 °C. Moreover, benzo[ghi]perylene and indeno[1,2,3-cd]pyrene with high boiling point (> 500 °C) can also be detected in tar from 800 °C. This explains why the heavy oil content increases to 8.0 wt% at 800 °C (see Fig. 3). It also reveals that the decrease of H/C ratio in tar is attributed to the increase in PAHs content. The above results show that the increase of temperature is contributed to the formation of aromatic compounds. C and H are the main elements in coal tar, which could be considered as reducing agent during the coal tar upgrading. For Fe2O3, it can be reduced to low-valence iron oxide (Fe3O4, FeO) or metallic iron (Fe) under reducing atmosphere [27–29]. The spent Fe2O3 samples obtained from different reaction temperature were characterized by XRD. The spent Fe2O3 samples are indicated as Fe2O3-500, Fe2O3-600, Fe2O3-700, and Fe2O3-800, suggesting that the reaction temperature is 500, 600, 700, and 800 °C respectively. As shown in Fig. 7 , the fresh Fe2O3 is a typical hematite structure. The Fe2O3 is reduced after reaction, and the reduction degree is closely associated with the reaction temperature. The crystal phase on the Fe2O3-500 and Fe2O3-600 is mainly assigned to magnetite (Fe3O4). The diffraction peaks on Fe2O3-700 are mainly assigned to the FeO and other peaks with weak intensity are mainly attributed to Fe3O4. The diffraction peaks of Fe2O3-800 are basically the same as that of Fe2O3-700. Moreover, Fe3C can be observed on Fe2O3-800. A broad peak around 26o is ascribed to carbon, suggesting that more carbon is deposited on the Fe2O3-800. Uddin et al. found that Fe2O3 transformed into Fe3O4 during the catalytic decomposition of biomass tar [30]. Therefore, it can be concluded that Fe2O3 can be in-situ reduced during the coal tar upgrading. Coal tar is a reducing agent and Fe2O3 is considered as an oxidant by supplying active oxygen species to react with coal tar. Generally, these oxygen species on the surface of Fe2O3 can be classified as lattice oxygen and chemically adsorbed oxygen, respectively [24,31]. When these two surface oxygen species are consumed and oxygen vacancies are generated on the surface, the lattice oxygen in the bulk will be relocated and move to the surface due to the concentration gradient. The loss of surface and bulk oxygen eventually leads to the crystal phase transition [32]. As the reaction temperature increases, the oxygen transfer is enhanced, thus more bulk oxygen is consumed, leading to the formation of FeO. The gas volume of CO and CO2 is 1.0 and 11.2 mL/g.coal tar at 500 °C, and increases to 63.5 and 17.4 mL/g.coal tar at 800 °C, respectively. The increase in formation of CO and CO2 confirms the supply of active oxygen from Fe2O3. The possible reactions for the formation of CO2 or CO can be expressed as [33]: (10) Direct reduction : 3 Fe 2 O 3 + C → 2 Fe 3 O 4 + CO (11) Direct reduction : Fe 3 O 4 + C → 3 FeO + CO (12) Indirect reduction : Fe 3 O 4 + CO → 3 FeO + CO 2 (13) Boudouard reaction : C + CO 2 → 2 CO As shown in Fig. 8 , the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of the fresh and spent Fe2O3 samples are attributed to the type IV isotherms and they show a jump at relative pressure P/P0 at 0.8, which can be seen as mesoporous structure [34]. The hysteresis loop in N2 adsorption isotherms is at the high relative pressure being 0.8. All the samples show a H4 hysteresis loop, which could be the effect of capillary condensation in the pore structure [35]. The adsorbed volume on the used Fe2O3 is smaller than that on the fresh Fe2O3, and it decreases further as the reaction temperature increases, which is similar with the reported work by Zhang et al. [36]. The decrease of adsorbed volume suggests the decrease of specific surface area (SBET) [36]. Table 2 shows the textural properties fresh and spent Fe2O3 samples. The fresh Fe2O3 has the largest SBET and pore volume being 23.0 m2/g and 0.091 cm3/g, respectively. The SBET and pore volume of the spent Fe2O3 are smaller than those of fresh Fe2O3 and decrease with the increase of reaction temperature. The decrease of SBET and pore volume could be due to the sintering and the aggregation of particle size [37]. XRD analysis showed that the spent Fe2O3 is in the form of reduced state as well as the deposition of coke. When the used samples are treated under air condition, the spent Fe2O3 is oxidized and coke can be burnt off. To understand weight loss behavior of spent Fe2O3 samples under air atmosphere, TG analysis was conducted and the weight change with temperature was recorded and shown in Fig. 9 . Fig. 9a shows that the weight change can be divided into two stages: weight increase at low temperature and weight decrease at high temperature. At low temperature, the weight increase is due to the oxidation of reduced iron oxide (Fe3O4 and FeO) to Fe2O3 as shown in Eqs. (14), (15). As the tar upgrading temperature increases, the weight increase stage shifts to high temperature, but it is difficult to distinguish this weight increase in the case of Fe2O3-700 and Fe2O3-800 because coke removal occurs simultaneously, which eliminates the increase of weight. At high temperature, a significant weight loss can be noted, which is due to the combustion of coke as shown in Eq. (16). According to the XRD analysis shown in Fig. 7, the Fe3C was detected on Fe2O3-800. The reaction between Fe3C and oxygen can be indicated in Eq. (17). The total weight loss of the spent Fe2O3 samples follows a sequence of: Fe2O3-500  Fe2O3-600 > Fe2O3-700 > Fe2O3-800. As shown in Fig. 12b, the peak of weight loss rate shifts to a high temperature with the increase of tar upgrading temperature. Fe2O3-800 has the largest coke deposition as shown in Fig. 9, but it shows the least weight loss under N2 atmosphere and requires the highest reduction temperature. This suggests that the weight loss under N2 atmosphere is related to the oxidation state of Fe. It has been reported that the reduction of Fe3O4 or FeO requires a high temperature as compared to that of Fe2O3 [46]. The reduction of Fe2O3 to form metallic Fe is a stepwise reduction process: Fe2O3 → Fe3O4 → FeO → Fe [47]. This means that the FeO bond in FeO is stronger than that in Fe3O4. Thus, the removal of [O] in FeO requires a high temperature. The XRD analysis was conducted to understand the crystal phase transition after using coke as reductant as shown in Fig. 13 . The reduced samples are expressed as R500, R600, R700, and R800, respectively. The diffraction patterns are mainly attributed to Fe3O4, and some peaks associated with FeO are detected on R500. For R600, the main crystal phase is the same with R500, but the peak intensity at 36o attributed to FeO is stronger than that of R500. The diffraction peaks on R700 and R800 are different from those of R500 and R600. The main diffraction peaks are attributed to the Fe3C, and no peaks corresponding to Fe3O4 or FeO can be detected on R800. Moreover, peak assigned to C can still be detected on R800. Fig. 14 shows the SEM images of the reduced samples. Compared to morphology of fresh or spent Fe2O3 samples, the particle of reduced samples shows a severe sintering and agglomeration. In the case of R500 and R600, the particle size is large. In contrast, some small particles can be observed on the surface of R700 and the morphology is irregular. R800 is formed by the agglomeration of small regular pellets. The particle size of R800 is about 1 μm, which is smaller than other samples. The upgrading of coal tar in the presence of Fe2O3 at different temperature was investigated, and the product distribution and composition were analyzed. It is shown that the increase of reaction temperature is contributed to the formation of H2 or CH4 rich gas, and the decrease of tar yield. The content of aliphatic compounds decreases and that of PAHs increases, resulting in the decrease of H/C ratio in tar. The naphthalene content is 17.2 wt% at reaction temperature of 800 °C. The characterization of spent Fe2O3 revealed that in-situ reduction of Fe2O3 can be achieved, and the reduction degree of Fe2O3 is related to the reaction temperature. FeO and Fe3C can be formed at 800 °C. The treatment of spent Fe2O3 under N2 indicated that coke can be served as a reducing agent to further reduce the spent Fe2O3. In the case of R800, Fe3C with small regular pellets is formed. The authors acknowledge the financial support from Joint Fund of Coal-based Low Hydrocarbons by NSFC and Shanxi Provincial Government of China (No. U1710105).
abstract: Producing light fuels or valuable chemicals from coal tar is an important strategy to achieve the high value-added utilization of coal tar. This study proposed an integrated process of coal tar upgrading and in-situ reduction of Fe2O3. The effect of reaction temperature on the product distribution, coal tar component, and crystal phase transition of Fe2O3 were investigated. It is shown that increase of reaction temperature leads to increase production of H2 or CH4 rich gas and decrease of tar. Tar analysis revealed that more polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) are formed along with the decrease of H/C ratio. The crystal phase transition from oxidation to reduction state can be detected on spent Fe2O3. The reduction degree of Fe2O3 is closely associated with the reaction temperature. Coke deposited on the spent Fe2O3 can be used as reducing agent, producing Fe3C when it was treated under N2 atmosphere. This study provides an integrated method of producing valuable aromatic compounds from coal tar as well as reduction process of Fe2O3 for steelmaking industry.
title: Integrated process of coal tar upgrading and in-situ reduction of Fe2O3

full_text: Biomass is a renewable and abundantly available feedstock for the production of biofuels and value-added chemicals. Hydrolysis is a process to depolymerize biomass, producing free C5 and C6 sugar monomers and/or oligomers [1,2]. These sugar monomers and oligomers are mixed and can be acid-treated together to produce the platform chemicals such as furfural, 5-(hydroxymethyl)furfural (HMF) and levulinic acid [3,4]. These C6 and C5 sugars share many similarities. They all have multiple hydroxyl groups and they can dehydrate and polymerize [5,6], but their reaction pathways and propensities towards polymerization are different [7]. It is difficult to separate them via distillation as they are very polar and have no specific boiling points. However, via acid-treatment these mixed sugars can be converted into the volatile products such as furfural [8–15] and methyl levulinate [16–20], which can then be separated from each other easily via distillation. This is one practical way to utilize the mixed sugars derived from the hydrolysis of biomass. When the mixed sugars are acid-treated together, one fundamental question is raised. Do these sugar monomers/oligomers react/interact with each other? During hydrolysis of glucose, it is known that the intermediates such as HMF could react with glucose to form cross-polymer [21], while furfural also can cross-polymerize with xylose [22]. Thus, the different sugars may also react/interact with each other in some way and affect their following conversion. However, little effort has been made to explore their potential interaction, understanding of which can help to optimize the process for production of biofuels from biomass. Useful information also can be obtained for developing selective solid acid catalyst for conversion of the different sugars. In this study, the acid-treatment of the single and mixed sugar monomers/oligomer including glucose, fructose, xylose and raffinose was performed to understand their potential interactions. The chemicals used in this study were obtained from Sigma Aldrich and are of analytical grade. A commercial solid acid catalyst, Amberlyst 70, was obtained from Dow Chemical Company. The catalyst was used in its original wet form, which contains ca. 55wt.% water. A Hastalloy batch autoclave reactor (Autoclave Engineers) with a maximum volume of 130mL was used to perform the acid-catalyzed conversion of the sugars. Typically, sugar(s), Amberlyst 70, and water or dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were mixed and loaded to the autoclave at room temperature. After assembling the autoclave, residual air in reactor was removed by purging nitrogen for three times. The reactor was then heated up to 180°C with a stirring rate of 500rpm in 12min. A sample was taken immediately when the temperature reached the set temperature and others were taken at a certain time interval, which was specified in the figures. The reaction was maintained at 180°C for 100min. At the end of the tests, the reactants were cooling down immediately with the cold water running through a cooling coil inside the reactor. Residual solution together with the used catalyst was collected and separated via filtration. The filtered solid residual was then repeatedly washed with acetone until the residual solution is colorless. The washed solid was dried in an oven at 105°C for 4h to constant weight to measure the insoluble polymers formed. Total weight of the used catalyst deducted by the weight of the fresh catalyst (dry basis) is the amount of solid polymer formed. Agilent GC/MS (6890 series GC with a 5973 MS detector) with a capillary column (HP-INNOWax) was used to analyze the products. The procedure was detailed in Ref. [23]. Briefly, the temperature for injection port was set at 250°C, and the temperature for column was programmed from 40 to 260°C at 10°Cmin−1 with helium (2.0mLmin−1) as the carrier gas. The samples were diluted in acetone before the injection for analysis. The GC/MS library (version: NIST MS Search 2005) was used to identify the products. The standards, if available, were used to confirm the identification and calculate concentration of the compound of interest. Concentrations of acidic sites on/in catalyst were determined by a back titration method following that in the literature: [24]. FT-IR spectra of the fresh/used catalyst were characterized with a Perkin-Elmer Spectrum GX FT-IR/Raman Spectrometer with a spectral resolution of 4cm−1. The spectrum represents an average of 10 scans. The UV-fluorescence spectra of the soluble polymers were recorded with a Perkin-Elmer LS50B spectrometer using a constant energy difference of −2800cm−1 with the scan rate of 200nm/min. Before analysis, the samples were diluted with methanol to 800ppm. The excitation intensity is proportional to abundance of the soluble polymer at this concentration.. Yields of products are defined by the formula: Yields mol % = Moles of the compound in products / Theoretical moles of the compound from the sugar loaded . × 100 % Fig. 1 shows the distribution of the typical products from the acid-treatment of single glucose and single xylose as well as the mixed glucose and xylose versus reaction time. A much higher yield of levulinic acid (52% versus 35% in Fig. 1a) was obtained from single glucose than from the mixed sugars. HMF is the main reaction intermediate, which was almost completely converted in the acid-treatment of single glucose but not in that of the mixed sugars (Fig. 1b). Apparently, degradation of the HMF from the acid-treatment of single glucose is more efficient. Although xylose contributes to levulinic acid formation, the amount produced from xylose is minimal [25]. Furfural is the major product from xylose but it is not stable in the presence of acid catalyst. With the prolonged reaction time furfural polymerized (Fig. 1c). In addition, levoglucosan, which is formed from the dehydration of glucose and further dehydrates to HMF/levulinic acid, also maintained a higher abundance in the acid-treatment of the mixed sugars (Fig. 1d). These phenomena together indicate that the formation of levulinic acid and the degradation of HMF/levoglucosan were slowed down in the acid-treatment of the mixed sugars. There seem some interactions between xylose and glucose, slowing down their conversions. Cross-polymerization is assumed as the primary reason for the changed behaviors of xylose and glucose. This is because dehydration of both xylose and glucose produces the reactive intermediates [26,27], which may react with each other, preventing their further conversion into furfural or levulinic acid. The solid polymer formed in terms of weight and percentages to sugar(s) loaded was shown in Fig. 2 . The solid polymer formed from the acid-treatment of xylose is almost doubled that from glucose. Unexpectedly, the amount of polymer formed in the acid-treatment of the mixed sugars is almost the same as the sum amount of polymers from the single xylose and the single glucose. Clearly, the acid-treatment of the mixed sugars did not lead to the formation of more solid polymer. The cross-polymerization between xylose and glucose may not exist or at least does not result in the formation of more insoluble polymer. However, the behaviors of glucose and xylose in terms of kinetics did change, which may be related to the catalyst. In the acid-treatment of the single sugar or mixed sugars the same amount of catalyst was used. However, the amount of polymer formed is very different (4.0g for glucose, 7.4g for xylose, 11.5g for mixed sugars). The formation of the significant amount of polymer in the acid-treatment of the mixed sugars may deactivate the catalyst, slowing down conversion of the sugars. Acid densities of the used catalysts were shown in Fig. 3 . The acid density of the used catalyst decreased significantly and that of the mixed sugars is the lowest. It is understandable as the polymer formed in the mixed sugars is the most significant. Amberlyst 70 is a solid acid resin catalyst. In polar solvent like water it can swell and make the inner acidic sites accessible [28]. The polymerization reactions are catalyzed by these acidic sites [29]. Once polymer forms in the inner pores, they may not be able to come out due to their increased sizes. As a consequence, the pores will be gradually filled up and the acidic sites will not be available to the reactants outside the pores. This explains why conversion of the sugars was slowed down if significant amount of polymer is formed. In addition, it should be noted that a back titration method was used to determine acid density of the catalyst [24]. In this method excess amount of NaOH was used to neutralize the acidic sites. The OH− might penetrate some blocked pores to react with the acidic sites due to its small size, however, these acidic sites may not be accessible to the sugars due to their much bigger sizes. Therefore, the acidic sites that can really be used to convert sugars may be much less than that determined by the back titration method. In addition, in the acid-treatment of the mixed sugars the total concentration of the sugars (33.3wt.%) is much higher than that (20wt.%) of the single sugars, however, similar amount polymer was formed. This seems to contradict to the expectation that the more the sugars loaded, the more the polymer formed in terms of yields, as high concentration of sugars in the reaction medium will create more chance to collide and condense. The acid-treatment of the mixed sugars with a lower concentrations (total 20wt.%, 10wt.% of each sugar) of glucose and xylose was further performed to confirm this. The ratio of solid polymer formed to the sugars loaded is ca. 40%, which is again very similar to that (ca. 39%) from the high sugar loading (33.3wt.%). The use of high concentration of sugar does not give higher yields of insoluble polymer. This is possibly because if concentrations of sugars exceed a threshold value, the effect of sugar concentration starts to become unimportant. DMSO is a polar aprotic solvent and it has many unique features when compared to water. Interaction of xylose and glucose in DMSO may be different. Typical results from the acid-treatment of glucose/xylose were summarized in Table 1 . High yield of HMF from glucose was reported in DMSO [30]. However, the experimental conditions used here (180°C, 17wt.% of catalyst loading) are may be too harsh to selectively convert glucose to HMF. Instead, some HMF derivatives were produced, as is shown in Scheme 1 . Decomposition of DMSO produces substantial amount of dimethyl disulfide, which reacts with HMF, forming 5-[(methylthio)methyl]-2-furancarboxaldehyde. HMF also underwent oxidation to produce 2,5-furandicarboxaldehyde and decomposition to produce levulinic acid. The solid polymer formed (Table 1) is unexpectedly similar to that in water. DMSO is very reactive to decomposition at the experimental conditions employed and its ability to suppress the polymerization is compromised. It is also interesting that acid-density for the used catalyst is almost the same as that in water. Apparently, acid density of the used catalyst is independent of solvent but directly relates to the amount of polymer formed. Unlike that of glucose, xylose gave much higher yield of furfural and correspondingly less solid polymer. The acid density is also relatively higher than that in water due to the less polymer formation. Furfural does not have the hydroxyl group and thus it could not react with the decomposition products from DMSO or be oxidized by DMSO, and higher yield was obtained. In the acid-treatment of the mixed sugars, the yields of HMF and levulinic acid were doubled (Table 1). Interestingly, the amount of the polymer formed in the acid-treatment of the mixed sugars almost equals to the sum of that of the single sugars. These results indicate that in DMSO the interaction of xylose and glucose, if exists, does not lead to more insoluble polymer formation. The acid density of the used catalyst became half of that of the single sugar. With the deactivation of the catalyst the decomposition of DMSO was suppressed and the formation of the derivatives of HMF also decreased (not shown). As a result, HMF would have more chance to stay in the reaction medium or continue to decompose to levulinic acid. The formation of more levulinic acid is also related to the significant increase of water content in the medium of the mixed sugars (to ca. 18wt.%). The water came from both the wet Amberlyst 70 catalyst (water content: ca. 55wt.%) and dehydration of the sugars. The presence of water does not favor the formation of furfural as furfural is very reactive towards polymerization in water [31]. To eliminate the effects of water on furan production, the acid-treatment of the mixed sugars with dry Amberlyst 70 was also performed in DMSO. As expected, more HMF and furfural are produced. Levulinic acid production decreases but not significantly. Although the catalyst is dry, dehydration of the sugars still produces water. The polymer formed is almost exactly the same with that using wet catalyst. Water played a negligible role in the polymerization of sugars in DMSO but can change the product distributions. The furans especially furfural is sensitive to the presence of water in DMSO. Fig. 4 shows the main product distribution in the acid-treatment of fructose, raffinose, and fructose/raffinose. After heating up to 180°C the yield of levulinic acid from fructose has already reached ca. 43% while that from raffinose is just ca. 4%. Clearly, the size of raffinose does matter. Amberlyst 70 is a porous resin catalyst with acidic sites concentrated in local surface of the inner pores [28]. The sugars with big size may have some difficulty to access the acidic sites in some small pores, slowing down their conversion. Despite the slower conversion of raffinose, the solid polymer formed is very similar in both sugars (Fig. 5 ). Apparently, hydrolysis of big sugars does not necessarily lead to the formation of more insoluble polymer. In addition, in the acid-treatment of fructose HMF is the intermediate, which was converted not only to levulinic acid but also to other products such as polymer. As is shown in Fig. 6 , ca. 56% of HMF was converted into the targeted levulinic acid. The rest of HMF was converted to other by-products or probably polymerized, which is a major way for polymer formation in fructose conversion. In the acid-treatment of the mixed fructose and raffinose, a little more polymer is formed. It is possible that the HMF from fructose reacts with the intermediates from raffinose, forming some additional polymer. In addition, the formation of levulinic acid from both the mixed sugars and single fructose levels off in 20min, while that in single raffinose continues to increase (Fig. 4a). The dehydration of fructose is relatively quick. The polymer formed from fructose and/or raffinose prevents raffinose to access the catalytic sites, and consequently the formation of levulinic acid was significantly slowed down. Furfural is the major product from C5 sugars and it may react with the C6 sugars due to its nature of high propensity towards polymerization. The acid-treatment of glucose/furfural and fructose/furfural was further investigated. Compared with that in single glucose, levulinic acid yields in glucose/furfural were much lower (Fig. 7a). Similarly, the degradation of HMF was also slowed down (Fig. 7b). Furfural was completely converted but majority of it went to the polymer (weight ratio of polymer to furfural loaded: ca. 78%), which leads to the significant deactivation of catalyst. As a consequence, the conversion of glucose was slowed down, but the acid-treatment of glucose and furfural did not produce more insoluble polymer (Fig. 8 ). In the acid-treatment of fructose and furfural, the yield of levulinic acid is also remarkably higher in single fructose (Fig. 9a), but the yield of HMF is relatively higher in fructose/furfural (Fig. 9b). This is mainly due to the deactivation of the catalyst by furfural, which slowed down the conversion of HMF. In addition, in the degradation of HMF, only ca. 47% of the HMF was converted into levulinic acid in the acid-treatment of furfural/fructose while 56% of HMF was converted to levulinic acid in single fructose (Fig. 6). Clearly, HMF underwent some co-polymerization reactions with furfural. The polymer formed in the acid-treatment of furfural and fructose is also a little higher than the total polymer formed in the acid-treatment of furfural and fructose (Fig. 10 ). Soluble polymer is the primary intermediate/product from condensation of sugars and also precursor for insoluble polymer, characterization of which provides insight into the interaction of different sugars from another angle. A UV-fluorescence method has been developed in our previous studies to characterize relative abundance of the soluble polymers formed in the acid-treatment of sugars [32]. Fig. 11a shows the excitation spectra of single glucose, xylose, and the mixed sugars. Excitation intensity of the soluble polymer in either glucose or xylose is small, but the amount of insoluble polymer formed is significant. This is mainly because solubility of the soluble polymer in water is small, resulting from lower polarity of the soluble polymer. During dehydration/polymerization sugars will lose some polar hydroxyl groups and form conjugated π bonds. As a consequence, solubility of the polymer will be reduced in the polar solvent like water and eventually the polymer precipitates. In the mixed sugars, although profile of the excitation does not change much, the intensity, which has a linear relationship with abundance, increased substantially. Apparently, the strong excitation at 380nm is not simply an add-up of that of glucose and xylose, but a direct evidence that glucose and xylose interact with each other during the acid-treatment, forming more soluble polymer with the excitation at 380nm. Fig. 11b shows the excitation spectra of single glucose, furfural, and glucose/furfural. Furfural shows a stronger excitation at 296 and 380nm than that of glucose. In comparison, the excitation for the mixed furfural/glucose is much stronger at 380nm, indicating that the acid-treatment of glucose/furfural, like that of glucose/xylose, also forms more soluble polymer. In addition, the excitation shifts from 296nm for furfural to 317nm for furfural/glucose, indicating that conjugated π bonds of the soluble polymer grow bigger. Clearly, furfural reacts/interacts with glucose, forming different soluble polymers with an excitation wavelength at 317nm and more soluble polymer at 380nm. In addition, the interactions between xylose/glucose and furfural/glucose are different. Xylose can directly interact with glucose, forming the soluble polymer with the wavelength at 306 and 380nm, or dehydrate to furfural, which interacts with glucose and forms the soluble polymer with the excitation wavelength at 317 and 380nm. Regardless of xylose, furfural, and glucose, the soluble polymer formed shares a similar wavelength at 380nm, which roughly equals to the polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon with 3 to 4 rings (ca. 7 to 9 conjugated π bonds) [33]. The soluble polymer can grow up to this size in water and then precipitate to insoluble polymer. Fig. 11c shows the excitation spectra of single fructose, furfural and mixed fructose/furfural. Similar to that of glucose and furfural, the interaction between fructose and furfural also exists. Strong excitations at 296 and 380nm were observed in the mixed furfural/fructose. In addition, the interaction between furfural and fructose has some difference with that between furfural and glucose in terms of excitation at a low wavelength (296nm for furfural/fructose versus 317nm for furfural/glucose). This may be because of the different product distributions in the acid-treatment of fructose and glucose. HMF is the major intermediate in fructose conversion while it is the minor one in glucose conversion. It has multiple reactive functional groups and also very reactive towards polymerization. Fig. 11d shows the excitation spectra of single raffinose, fructose and mixed raffinose/fructose. The excitation spectrum of the mixed fructose/raffinose is very similar to that of raffinose. Fructose does not play a role in the formation of the excitation spectrum. This is possibly because raffinose molecule contains already a fructose unit, and hence the addition of fructose will not change the excitation pattern. In short, xylose and furfural interact with glucose or fructose and form different soluble polymers. However, although these sugars interact with each other, the interaction does not necessarily lead to the formation of significantly more insoluble polymer. Fig. 12 shows the FT-IR characterization of the fresh and used catalyst in the acid-treatment of the single and mixed sugars. Strong absorbance of CO group and CC double bonds was observed for all the used catalyst. The polymerization of sugars involves the dehydration of hydroxyl groups to form the carbonyl groups and the carbon double bonds. When the solubility of the soluble polymer falls below the threshold value, they will precipitate and form insoluble polymer in/on the catalyst. Interestingly, the adsorption patters for glucose and fructose are similar and the adsorption of carbonyl group is mild. In comparison, the used catalyst in xylose or furfural showed a strong adsorption of carbonyl group and carbon double bonds, which is related to the formation of significant amount of insoluble polymer in the acid-treatment of xylose or furfural. In summary, in water the interaction or cross-polymerization of xylose/glucose, fructose/raffinose, furfural/glucose, and furfural/fructose does exist, producing more soluble polymer. However, the interaction does not lead to the formation of significantly more insoluble polymer. The amount of the insoluble polymer formed from the acid-treatment of the mixed sugars almost equal to the simply add-up of the insoluble polymer formed from the single sugar. The formation of the significant amount of polymer especially in the acid-treatment of the mixed sugars deactivates the solid acid catalyst and slows down conversion of the sugars/intermediates. In addition, the different sugars and furfural present very different propensities towards polymerization in water. Yields of the insoluble polymer from the sugars (furfural) increase in the order: fructose~raffinose<glucose<xylose<furfural. In DMSO, the insoluble polymer produced in the acid-treatment of xylose/glucose is also similar to the add-up of the polymer formed from the single sugars. HMF can react with DMSO or its decomposition products via oxidation or etherification, while furfural can be preserved by DMSO. The formation of the significant amount of insoluble polymer is a big challenge for the acid-treatment of the mixed sugars from hydrolysis of biomass, which can deactivate the solid acidic resin catalyst and lower the efficiency of the process. How to develop the solid acid catalyst with good tolerance or resistance towards insoluble polymer formation is one of the key challenges for the efficient production of biofuels/value-added chemicals from biomass. This project is supported by the Commonwealth of Australia under the Australia–China Science and Research Fund as well as by the Curtin University of Technology through the Curtin Research Fellowship Scheme.
abstract: Carbohydrates are the intermediates/products during hydrolysis of biomass, which can be converted to value-added chemicals/biofuels via further acid-catalysis. The interactions between the typical carbohydrates/furans including glucose, fructose, raffinose, xylose, and furfural during their acid-catalyzed conversion in water/dimethyl sufoxide were investigated in this study. The insoluble polymer formed from the carbohydrates was measured and characterized with FT-IR, while the soluble polymers were characterized with a UV-fluorescence spectrometer. The interaction or cross-polymerization of xylose/glucose, fructose/raffinose, furfural/glucose, and furfural/fructose does exist, producing more soluble polymer but not significantly more insoluble polymer. The insoluble polymer deactivates the solid acid catalyst, slowing down conversion of sugars/intermediates. In addition, different sugars have very different propensities towards polymerization in water, which follows the order: fructose~raffinose<glucose<xylose<furfural. In addition, the acid-treatment of glucose in DMSO and in water produces the similar amount of insoluble polymer, while the insoluble polymer formed in the acid-treatment of xylose is less in DMSO than in water at the experimental conditions employed.
title: Acid-treatment of C5 and C6 sugar monomers/oligomers: Insight into their interactions
