full_text: Gasification of coal or any other carbonaceous materials under moderate reaction conditions can improve the cold-gas efficiency as well as save capital and maintenance costs, especially for small scale systems which features high surface/volume ratio and thus significant heat loss. Besides the syngas (mainly H2 and CO), tarry materials as the undesirable products will be inevitably generated from the gasification process [1,2], particularly at medium reaction temperatures and pressures. The tar contents in product gas are higher when lower rank fuels (e.g. brown coal or biomass) are used as feedstocks due to the high yields of volatile that are tar precursors. Tar elimination has been and will continue to be a very hot research area because it can somehow determine the feasibility and competency of newly-developed gasification technologies. Wet scrubbing is a widely adopted approach to condense the tar components from hot gas mixture at the outlet of gasifier [3,4], of which oil is a better absorbent than water as tarry materials are largely hydrophobic. However, wet scrubbing produces large amounts of waste liquid containing poisonous and even cancerous chemicals. With increasingly stringent requirements on environment protection, people now become more reluctant to utilize the wet scrubbing method to remove tars from gasification system. Thermal cracking has also been intensively examined to decompose the large hydrocarbons, which normally requires temperatures above 1200 °C (even higher) to achieve satisfied effects. Besides, coke formation is always a concurrent process during the thermal reactions of tar [5]. Introducing oxygen to oxidize/destroy the tar compounds has also been considered as another option. Ahrenfeldt et al. [6] reported that the primary tar from the pyrolysis of wood pellets in a two-stage gasifier could be effectively converted into small molecules and PAHs. The tar reduction by oxidation had to be at a cost of the decrease in syngas heat value, and thus reducing cold gas efficiency. Catalytic reforming has received a great attention for removing tar from both pyrolysis and gasification of coal, biomass and other carbonaceous materials [7–9]. A variety of catalysts such as dolomite, olivine and Ni-Al2O3 for tar reforming have been tested under various experimental conditions [10–12]. Natural minerals such as dolomite and olivine are cheap and easily accessible, but with relatively poor catalytic activity and weak strength. Ni-based synthesized inorganic catalysts showed very excellent catalytic performance for tar reforming. It was suggested that Ni based catalysts could not only effectively eliminate tar, but also show activity for water gas shift reactions (thus adjusting the ratio of H2 and CO) [13,14]. However, the weak strength and easy deactivation by coke formation were the fatal disadvantage that prevented the Ni based inorganics from being used as catalysts for tar reforming in a commercial scale. Chars derived from pyrolysis and gasification of coal and biomass have seen a very promising future as a candidate for catalytic tar reforming [7,15,16]. Chars as tar reforming catalysts have been well demonstrated in both fundamental and pilot-scale studies [7,16,17]. It is economically viable and could be prepared on site. The most striking feature is that the spent char catalyst could be recycled to the reactor to be gasified, leaving zero environmental footprints. Activity of char as catalysts is attributed to its inherent catalytic AAEM (alkali and alkaline earth metallic) species as well as the defected carbon structural units in chars [1,17,18]. The carbon structure including O-containing functional groups would not only show catalytic activity themselves, but also largely govern the roles and fates of AAEM species and thus its catalytic performance. Therefore, it is of paramount importance to investigate the effects of carbon structure on chars' catalytic performance for tar reforming. As the first step, the effects of carbon structure should be determined without the disturbances from AAEM species, which will provide very basic and fundamental information for this series of studies. Different carbonaceous materials (i.e. biomass, different ranks of coals and graphite) were used in this study so as to formulate different char catalysts with various carbon structures. The raw carbon materials were acid-washed to remove AAEM species before being used for preparing char catalysts. Therefore, the objective of this work was to examine the effects of the structures of char on its activity for reforming tar that was generated from pyrolysis/gasification of Shengli brown coal in a bench scale quartz reactor. Chars prepared from five kinds of acid-washed carbon materials were used as catalysts. The results indicate that the difference in carbon structure (and thus catalytic reactivity) has apparently led to the variations in tar yields and tar compositions, as well as has interestingly changed the role of steam on tar reforming. A Chinese brown coal from Shengli mining site was used as feedstock to generate volatile (tar precursors) in a two-stage quartz reactor in this work. It was ground and sieved to obtain the particles ranging from 98 to150 μm, and its proximate and ultimate analyses are listed in Table 1 . The different ranks of carbon materials including wood (Natural oak chips), brown coal (Shengli), bituminous coal (Datong), anthracite (Jincheng) and commercial graphite (99.99%) were firstly milled to prepare the samples with the particle sizes of 98–150 μm. To remove the AAEM species, approximately 30 g of each sample was soaked in acid mixture of HCl (38.0 wt%) and HF (40.0 wt%) (1:1 by volume) for 8 h. The samples inside the solution were then filtered out and washed using double-distilled water before evaporating the water in oven at 70 °C for 24 h. The moisture contents and ash yields of the different ranks of carbonaceous materials before and after acid washing are summarized in Table 2 . The impurity of graphite was negligible, and thus was not subjected to the acid washing process. After the acid washing treatment, the ash yields for all the carbon materials were in the range of 0.6–2.6 wt%. The remained inorganics were not dissolvable in acids and generally inert. The dried sample (H-form raw materials) was further sieved to ensure that its size distribution was within 98–150 μm, which was then sealed and stored in a fridge. To prepare H-form char catalysts, 10 g of each H-form raw material was loaded into the bottom section of the two-stage quartz reactor as is shown in Fig. 1 .1 L/min argon was continuously supplied into the reactor via its bottom to maintain the inert atmosphere. The reactor was then quickly dropped into the electrically-heated hot furnace at 900 °C and held for 30 min for a complete devolution. Subsequently, 15% steam in argon was provided into the reactor so as to activate the char for 10 min. At the end of experiments, the reactor was lifted out of the furnace and naturally cooled down to room temperature with the constant argon flow to maintain the inert atmosphere. The prepared chars were then collected and ready to be used as tar reforming catalysts. Tar reforming experiments were also conducted in the same two-stage quartz reactor. Brown coal as feedstock to generate tar was always dried in an oven at 70 °C overnight. To begin with, 1 g char catalyst prepared above was evenly distributed on the top quartz frit. The reactor with char catalysts was then heated up in the furnace to 800 °C under the protection of argon (0.65 L/min). After the temperature was stabilized, 1.8 g brown coal was fed into the bottom stage at a feeding rate of 180 mg/min through a water-cooled probe with further 10 min holding time. Therefore, the volatile released during heating up was forced to pass through the char catalyst bed, hence being catalytically reformed in situ by the catalyst. Blank experiments in the absence of char catalysts were also carried out for the purpose of comparison. The pyrolysis or gasification conditions were realized by supplying only argon or adding 15% steam in argon, respectively, via the bottom tube of the quartz reactor shown in Fig. 1. During the feeding time, four washing bottles connected in series were used for capturing tar vapor (Fig. 1, Part II) at the exit of quartz reactor [7]. Dry ice was inserted into the first two bottles to condense the majority of tar and all the steam. In the meantime, the organic solvent mixture (analytically pure chloroform and methanol with volume ratio of 4:1) was loaded into the latter two bottles to ensure no escape of organic vapors. The volume of the blended solvent in each bottle was 50 mL. A dry ice bath was used to accommodate the four bottles to guarantee the low temperature inside the bottles. After experiments, the clean solvent in the fourth bottle was normally used to wash other three bottles and the outlet of reactor. Some additional fresh solvent was always used for a thorough cleaning. A part (accurately weighed) of collected tar solution was dried at 35 °C in an aluminum tray for 8 h to ensure a complete evaporation of the moisture and solvent. Finally, the yield of tar was calculated by Eq.(1). (1) Y tar = M 1 M 2 M S M C × 100 % Y tar —tar yield, wt%; M 1 , M 2 —the weight of tar solution used for the analysis before/after drying, g; M S —the total weight of tar solution, g; M C —the weight of coal sample, g. The composition of tar was determined using gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC-MS-4000, VARIAN) as previously reported [7], and the components were quantified with area normalization method. A column (VF-5 ms, 60 m × 0.25 mm) was adopted and its initial temperature was 50 °C. After holding for 6 min, it was heated up to 290 °C at a rate of 5 °C/min, and then kept for 30 min. Ion source in the MS was electron ionization (25 μA of the filament current, 50–1000 μ of the scanning range with 1.64 s). 5 μL of sample (tar dissolved in the chromatographically pure chloroform) was injected every time. The carbon structure was characterized by using a 193 LAS-NY532/50 confocal microprobe Raman spectroscopy. The apparatus and analytical method have been detailed elsewhere [19–21]. Briefly, the original Raman spectra of char samples were recorded in the range of 800–1800 cm−1, which was then curve-fitted using the GRAMS/32 AI software with 10 Gaussian bands [20,21]. Each of sub-peaks represented certain typical structures in char, particularly from low rank coals and biomass. D (1300 cm−1) band here mostly represented large aromatic ring systems with no <6 fused rings, while the three bands: Gr (1540 cm−1) band, Vr (1380 cm−1) band, Vl (1465 cm−1) band mainly reflected smaller aromatic ring systems as well as the semi-circle breathing of aromatic rings. Therefore, the difference in the areas of different sub-peaks could denote the variation in the structural characteristics of char samples. Hereafter, IGr, ID and I(Gr+Vl+Vr) referred to the band area of Gr, D and (Gr + Vl + Vr) respectively. The ratios of IGr/ID and I(Gr+Vl+Vr)/ID could indicate the ratios between the large aromatic ring systems (≥6 fused rings) and the small aromatic ring systems (3–5 fused rings) [9,20,21]. The porous properties of the chars were determined by an automatic volumetric sorption analyzer (Quantachrom, Autosorb-1). The measurement was conducted by N2 adsorption at 77 K. The total specific surface area of the samples was estimated using the multilayer adsorption model developed by Brunauer, Emmett, and Teller (i.e. the BET method). Fig. 2 shows the char yields as a function of raw materials that were pyrolyzed at 900 °C, followed with 10 min activation by steam. To mainly examine the effects of carbon matrix on the catalytic reforming reactions, all the raw carbon materials were acid-washed before being used to minimize the disturbance of inorganic matters in this study. As expected, the char yields gradually increased with increasing ranks of carbon material from biomass to graphite. Compared to the high rank carbon materials, the low rank ones contain high amounts of volatile matter which can be quickly liberated into gas phase upon to the high temperature treatment. For instance, the biomass showed a very low char yield of 22.6%, while graphite did not see an obvious mass loss at 900 °C. For the given experimental condition, the difference in char yields among the carbon materials indeed reflected the different ease at which the chemical bonds can be broken/dissociated. The different chemical bonds (thus different carbon structure) will be partially inherited by the resultant chars (catalysts). The effects of carbon structure on tar reforming reactions could thus be examined using the prepared chars from the various carbon materials as tar reforming catalysts. The tar yields as a function of char catalysts were shown in Fig. 3 . The “Net difference” in Fig. 3 was the difference in tar yields between pyrolysis and gasification conditions. The tar yields apparently increased regardless the supply of steam with increasing ranks of parent carbon materials used for preparing the char catalysts, of which the tar production from blank experiment (without using any catalysts) showed the highest value on the top right of Fig. 3. The trend indicates that the char catalysts from low rank carbon materials (e.g. biomass) performed much better for tar reforming reactions than those from high rank carbon materials (e.g. graphite). As the raw materials have been acid-washed before being used, the difference in catalytic activity of different chars mainly came from its surface area and/or carbon structure as well as the functional groups in the chars. The chars derived from low rank carbon materials (such as biomass and brown coal) would feature highly porous structure as a large portion of its matrix has been thermally decomposed and gasified by steam during the char catalysts preparation. Despite that the high porosity is always advantageous for improving catalyst performance, there was always discrepancy on the relationship between the surface area and the catalytic activity of chars so far [17,22]. The large surface area did not always lead to high activity for the char catalysts as carbon structure (the chemical structure) could play a dominant role in some circumstance [17]. In this study, the surface area of brown coal char and anthracite char was measured and the results show that the surface area (287 m2/g) of brown coal char was a lot higher than those (2.3 and 1.0 m2/g) of anthracite char and bituminous coal char. Clearly, the difference in their activities as shown in Fig. 3 was much smaller than that in their surface area. Again, the surface area of chars has no well-built correlation with their catalytic performance although the high surface area is always desirable for any catalysts. As a matter of fact, the surface area (or the porous structure) can only provide possibility for chemical reactants to contact, and the nature of chemical structure on the surface however thermodynamically determines if the catalytic reactions could take place or not. Chars from low rank carbon materials contain abundant functional groups and highly disordered carbon structure. The O-containing functional groups and amorphous carbon structure could be acting as active sites for tar reforming reactions. The comparison of chemical structure of the char catalysts is a main focus in this study and will be introduced later. The effect of steam supply during the tar reforming reaction could be observed by comparing the black and red columns, and their difference was calculated as shown in Fig. 3 as well. In the cases of blank (no catalysts) and using graphite as catalysts, the presence of steam has obviously reduced the tar yields, while the difference was not observable when other char catalysts were used. The reaction pathways between steam and tarry compounds could likely occur as follows: 1) The primary volatile directly reacting with steam produced light gases and secondary volatile which will be further reformed when passing through the bed of char catalysts; 2) The steam can be disassociated into radicals of H and OH on char catalysts. The oxidative radicals of OH could facilitate to transform the tarry compounds into light gases while the single H radicals with high mobility could initiate the condensation/polymerization of large aromatic fused rings, thus forming coke and eliminating tar at the same time. The blank experiments with/without steam shown in Fig. 3 strongly suggested the significant reactions directly took place between steam and volatile in the gas phase. Using graphite as catalysts, the tar yields have slightly dropped compared to the blank experiments. It is unlikely for the ordered carbon structure in graphite to possess any catalytic activity for tar reforming. Probably some active species could be formed when tarry compounds (particularly O-containing functional groups) interacted with graphite. Additionally, the highly ordered carbon can always react with steam to form O-containing intermediates [23] which can enhance the bond cleavages of tarry compounds and reduce tar production. The presence of steam did not lead to the change in tar yields when char catalysts were prepared from low rank parent carbon origins. In the presence of char from low-rank fuel (e.g. biomass/brown coal), the relatively unstable structures may be cracked by char itself regardless the presence of steam, remaining the more refractory tar (e.g. naphthalene) which is difficult to be reformed by steam. For blank and graphite char which had minor catalytic effects on tar reforming, steam may preferentially reform relatively unstable tar components, such as aliphatic/O-containing structures in tar. Therefore, the effect of steam on tar reforming seems significant in the cases of blank and using graphite char as catalysts. However, the chemical compositions of tar from pyrolysis (without steam) to gasification (with steam) were obviously altered, even for the char catalysts from low rank carbon materials. Fig. 4 shows the changes in chemical compositions (based on wt% of feedstock) of tars collected after being reformed by the different char catalysts during the pyrolysis and gasification experiments at 800 °C. As is shown in Fig. 4(a), the tar from the pyrolysis consisted primarily of 2–4 fused aromatic rings, and the change in the contents of 3–4 fused aromatics was more significant than that of 2 fused ones. The large aromatic rings were more likely to develop complicated molecular structures in three dimensions, providing multiple contacting points with char catalysts [7,24]. Furthermore, the average bond energy of large aromatics was low partially due to the stereo-hindrance effect, leading to the strong reactions between char catalysts and large aromatics in tar [7,24]. The char catalysts from low rank carbon materials contained abundant functional groups. The O-containing functional groups on char surface could form conjugated system and reduce the electron density of π bonds [25]. The various types of functional groups originated from different chars may selectively remove different tar components. Fig. 4(b) shows the changes in compositions of tar from the gasification experiments. The main components of tar collected were 3–4 fused aromatic rings with some discernible 5 rings. In contrast to Fig. 4(a), the tar from gasification experiments contained larger aromatic compounds. The difference in tar compositions due to the supply of steam could be possibly attributed to two reasons: 1) The steam has consumed more small aromatic ring systems than large ones, thus leaving high percentage of large aromatics in the reformed tar; 2) The small aromatics have been transformed into large ones, which was facilitated by H radicals in the presence of steam. Considering the observation beforehand that large aromatics were consumed easier than small ones, it appears that the large proportion of large aromatics in the reformed tar in the presence of steam should be caused by the transforming/condensation reactions. Actually, the occurrence of 5 fused aromatic rings could well support the speculation. Fig. 5 shows the curve fittings of Raman spectra for the char catalysts prepared from 5 different carbonaceous materials. In terms of the original shape and area of Raman spectra, the vast differences could be immediately seen among the biomass char, coal chars and graphite. The graphite has a very regular carbon structure and does not contain electron-rich structural units (e.g. oxygen containing groups etc.), thus giving a very low response to Raman scattering. The char catalysts from anthracite shows obvious lower Raman intensity that the chars from brown coal and bituminous. The total Raman areas were calculated and shown in Fig. 6 . As mentioned before [20,21], the total Raman area was mainly determined by the extent of aromaticity and contents of electron-rich unit structure (e.g. O-containing functional groups). The increase in function groups could significantly enhance the Raman intensity, while the fused aromatic rings would favor the absorbance of lights and reduce the Raman scattering ability. The former factor is much more critical than the latter one, and therefore the Raman total area of char catalysts could reflect the contents of oxygen contents to a great extent [26,27]. As is shown in Fig. 6, the total Raman area decreased with increasing rank of parent materials used for preparing the char catalysts. The change in the total Raman area could be mainly ascribed to the change in the oxygen content in raw materials. The biomass contains the highest oxygen content, whereas the oxygen in graphite was negligible. The oxygen contents in the char catalysts are presented in Table 3 . It seems that the oxygen abundance in chars was well proportional to the total Raman intensity of chars. The oxygen contents in the carbon matrix (thus in the resultant char catalysts) played an important role in the catalytic performance for tar reforming reactions. Song et al. [16] has investigated the effects of volatile-char interactions on tar reforming using chars from acid-washed coal and raw coal respectively. It indicates that the char from acid-washed coal showed much better activity than that from raw coal, which was mainly due to the high content of oxygen in the char from acid-washed coal [16]. Fig. 7 shows the ratios of IGr/ID和I(Gr+Vl+Vr)/ID for the char catalysts from different carbonaceous materials. They can represent the ratio of small and large aromatic ring systems in the char catalysts. Clearly, the ratios of IGr/IDand I(Gr+Vl+Vr)/ID decreased with increasing ranking of carbon materials, whereas the biomass derived char shows exclusively high values of the ratios. It suggests that the abundances of small aromatic ring systems in the char catalysts from the low rank raw carbon materials (especially biomass) were relatively high. The small aromatic ring systems (amorphous carbon structure) tended to enclose more defected carbon structure (reactive sites). As is shown in Fig. 8 , the carbon structure of high rank coal with little side chains and functional groups is much more condensed and regular than that of low rank coal. Thus the char catalysts from high rank fuels were more stable and of low activity. The analysis of carbon structure for the char catalysts from different rank fuels well elucidated the results above that the chars from low rank fuels gave high reforming ability. Chars prepared from five kinds of acid-washed carbon materials, including biomass, brown coal, bituminous coal, anthracite and graphite, were used as catalysts for tar reforming in this study. The char structure (and thus its activity) was considerably inherited and affected by the parent carbonaceous materials. High contents of O-functional groups and disordered carbon structure in the char catalysts from low rank fuels were greatly contributing to the catalytic activity for tar reforming. Compared to the small aromatic compounds in tar, the large aromatic rings were preferentially reformed. In the presence of char catalysts from low rank fuels, the gasification condition during the reforming experiments did not result in lower tar yields than the pyrolysis condition, while the tar reduction was obviously enhanced by the supply of steam in the cases of blank experiments and using graphite as catalysts. The char catalysts derived from low rank fuels could crack the unstable structures (aliphatic and O-containing structure) without steam. The transformation of small aromatics to large ones (e.g. five aromatic fused rings) clearly occurred in the presence of steam. The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from 12th Five-Year Plan of National Science and Technology Support (2012BAA04B02) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (21406264).
abstract: The carbon structure of char as catalysts is very crucial as it greatly governs the catalytic activity of char for tar reforming reactions. More importantly, the char structure also plays an important role in affecting the interactions between the inherent metal species and carbon atoms, thus affecting the catalytic performances. This study aims to investigate the effects of char structure on the reforming of tar derived from the gasification and pyrolysis of Shengli brown coal. Chars prepared from five kinds of acid-washed carbonaceous materials, including biomass, brown coal, bituminous coal, anthracite and graphite, were used as tar reforming catalysts in this work. The different effectiveness of H-form char catalysts from the different ranking carbon materials have clearly shed light on the effects of char structure on tar reforming. Results show that the chars from biomass and low rank coals featured obviously higher catalytic activity than those from high rank fuels. The variation in char structure that was revealed by FT-Raman has greatly exerted impacts on tar yields as well as tar compositions. Interestingly, the supply of steam (in the gasification condition) did not always see the reduction in tar yields, very much depending on the type of char catalysts used in the experiments.
title: The catalytic reforming of tar from pyrolysis and gasification of brown coal: Effects of parental carbon materials on the performance of char catalysts

full_text: No data was used for the research described in the article. With the depletion of fossil fuel energy and the impact of the greenhouse effect on the global climate, renewable resources are the focus of current research on sustainable development [1]. As a rich food crop, peanuts are produced in more than 100 countries in the world. It has been estimated that the global production of peanuts exceeded 46.6 million tons in the 2017–2018 season, of which approximately 36.7% was produced by China [2,3]. Therefore, peanut shell is an abundant and inexpensive biomass resource and has great potential for biomass energy raw materials because of its high contents of lignin and cellulose (Table S1). Lignin, the most abundant natural polyphenol in nature, has three basic structural units, guaiacyl (G), syringyl (S) and hydroxyphenyl (H), which are connected by C-O-C and CC interunit bonds [4,5]. Due to this unique structure, the production of high value-added aromatic chemicals from lignin has attracted great attention [6,7]. Nevertheless, the unique three-dimensional network structure and the high bond energy of lignin limit its degradation efficiency [8]. To date, multiple strategies for lignin depolymerization have been investigated, including pyrolyzis, hydrogenolysis, acid/base hydrolysis, reduction and oxidation [1,9–12]. Catalytic hydrogenolysis is considered a potential strategy to effectively depolymerize lignin into aromatic monomers due to its excellent selectivity for lignin ether bond cleavage [13–15]. The process usually involves noble metals or nonnoble metals catalysts. Noble metal catalysis (Ru, Rh, Pd or Pt, etc.) usually load nanoparticles onto a variety of carriers and are regarded as highly effective catalysts of lignin depolymerization processes due to their excellent hydrodeoxygenation properties [16–21]. Ruthenium, a relatively low-cost noble metal, exhibits high selectivity and is widely used as a catalyst for lignin hydrogenolysis [22–24]. For example, lignin and corn straw were converted to phenolic compounds and alkanes in a water-ethanol mixture using ruthenium on activated carbon, in which the Ru/C catalyst exerted excellent hydrogenolysis efficiency. After catalytic hydrolysis, the Ru/C catalyst still showed good activity after separation and recovery under the same reaction conditions [19]. Nonprecious metals are mainly Cu, Fe and Ni. Ni-based catalysts have been reported to exhibit high selectivity for phenol monomers and high activity for the selective cleavage of CO bonds [25–27]. Sergeev et al. demonstrated that Ni/C converts a large amount of lignin into propylguaiacol and propylsyringol due to its high activity and selectivity [28]. Recently, the Ru/Ni bimetallic catalyst has proven to be a promising catalyst because it exhibits better catalytic activity and stability through the interaction of two metal components. Zhang et al. reported a RuNi bimetallic catalyst for hydrogenolysis of organic solvent lignin under a mild pure water reaction system and assessed the hydrogenolysis efficiency of CO bonds in lignin [29]. The results revealed that the RuNi bimetallic catalyst was not only effective in overcoming the limitations of single component catalysts but also showed high activity in the hydrogenation of CO bonds and could reduce the material cost. However, the synthesis of catalysts faces many challenges [29]. For example, the aggregation of metal nanoparticles (NPs) with high surface energy often leads to serious degradation of the catalytic activity and reusability [30,31]. Compared with other synthesis methods, electrospinning can load nanoparticles onto carbon nanofiber carriers through simple steps, which can effectively avoid the aggregation of nanoparticles and construct unique nanostructures [32,33]. Metal/CNFs have the advantages of a high specific surface area, many active sites and uniform morphology [34]. In addition, electrospinning can introduce heteroatoms into carbon materials to improve catalytic activity, such as nitrogen doping. Li et al. researched a Ru
abstract: Peanut shell, as a widespread agricultural waste, is a biomass resource rich in lignin that has not been fully utilized. Herein, a novel and efficient hydrogenolysis process was provided for the selective decomposition of peanut shell lignin. Ru and Ni nanoparticles embedded within N-doped nanofibers were synthesized by a simple electrospinning technique and carbonized to obtain a bimetallic catalyst (RuxNiy-NCNFs). With the optimized catalyst of Ru2Ni1-NCNFs, 74.46 wt% of lignin oil was obtained with a char formation rate lower than 2.44 wt% at 300 °C for 3 h without an external hydrogen source. 2D-HSQC-NMR demonstrated that representative interunit linkages, such as β-O-4, β-5, and β-β, were considerably degraded during the depolymerization process. The obtained lignin oil has a narrow molecular weight distribution, low molecular weight and high burning value, indicating that it has great potential to become a high-quality aromatic fuel through subsequent upgrade processing. This study opens a novel avenue toward the exploration of highly efficient but low-cost bimetallic catalysts for lignin hydrogenolysis.
title: RuNi nanoparticles embedded in N-doped carbon nanofibers as a bimetallic catalyst for the hydrogenolysis of peanut shell lignin

full_text: Oxidative coupling of methane (OCM) has attracted much attention since it was first reported by Keller and Bhasin in 1982 [1] because more useful chemicals such as ethylene and ethane can be obtained directly from methane. However, OCM processes have not been commercialized mainly because of the limited yield of C2+ hydrocarbons. Although various OCM catalysts have been developed [2–4], further catalyst improvement is desired to increase the C2+ yield. Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 is a promising catalyst for OCM, achieving a C2 selectivity of 66.9% at 33.7% methane conversion with long-term stability [5]. The OCM reaction over the Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst was found to proceed through CH4 activation by gas-phase OH radicals that are formed catalytically from the H2O–O2 reaction [6–9]. In addition to elucidating the reaction mechanism over the Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst [6–12], research has also been devoted to improving this catalyst [11]. The effect of preparation method has been investigated [13–15], as well as substitution of the components [16–23] and addition of promoters such as oxides of Sn, Ce, and La [15, 24–27]. Alkali chloride-promoted Mn oxide catalysts [28–31], first reported by Otsuka and Komatsu, have been proven effective for OCM. A desirable feature of these catalysts is the high productivity of ethylene, which is the more preferred product than ethane. A total hydrocarbon yield of 30.6% and ethylene yield of 25.7% have been achieved with the NaCl/Mn oxide catalyst [28]. The contribution of chlorine radical was presumed to explain the high yield of ethylene [32]. Although alkali chloride is a promising promoter for the Mn-based catalysts, the influence of the type of alkali chloride on the activity and selectivity have not been investigated thoroughly. Herein, we systematically investigated the effect of the addition of a promoter on the catalytic performance of Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 for OCM using a series of alkali chlorides (MCl, M=Li, Na, K, Cs, and Na+K). In particular, the physical properties of MCl that influence catalyst activity and durability were examined. Finally, the phase state of MCl was discussed based on the results of activity tests and high-temperature X-ray diffraction. Silica gel (Davisil® grade 646) and manganese(II) nitrate hexahydrate (Mn(NO3)2·6H2O) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co, LLC. Lithium chloride (LiCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), cesium chloride (CsCl), sodium tungstate(VI) dihydrate (Na2WO4·2H2O), and quartz sand (600–850μm: >50%) were purchased from Wako Pure Chemical Industries Ltd. Helium-diluted methane (20.05%) and helium-diluted oxygen (4.91%) gases were purchased from Sumitomo Seika Chemicals Co., Ltd. MCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalysts were prepared by physical mixing and subsequent heating. LiCl, NaCl, KCl, CsCl, and an equimolar mixture of NaCl and KCl were used as MCl. Silica gel (1.42g) and Mn(NO3)2·6H2O (0.616mmol) were mixed in a mortar for 10min. Na2WO4·2H2O (0.288mmol) and MCl (6.59mmol) were then added and mixed for another 10min. The molar ratio of SiO2/Mn/Na2WO4/MCl was adjusted at 1.00/0.0261/0.0122/0.279. The mixture was subsequently heated in air at 1123K for 3h. Catalysts without MCl and/or Na2WO4, i.e., Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2, NaCl–Mn/SiO2, and Mn/SiO2, were also prepared for comparison using the same procedure described above. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the catalysts were obtained using a SmartLab diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation at 293K. High-temperature XRD (HT-XRD) patterns were corrected on a D8-ADVANCE Vαrio-1 powder diffractometer (Bruker AXS, Japan) with a modified Debye–Scherrer geometry using Cu Kα1 radiation. This diffractometer equips with a capillary heater stage (Material Research Instruments, CHS) and a 1D position-sensitive detector (VÅNTEC-1). The sample was packed into a quartz capillary tube with an inner diameter of 0.5mm. Catalytic activity for OCM was investigated using a fixed flow reactor at atmospheric pressure. The catalyst was diluted with quartz sand (catalyst/quartz=1/10) and placed in a quartz tube reactor (8mm inner diameter). A mixture of methane (5.7%), oxygen (3.5%), and helium (90.8%) was fed into the reactor at a total flow rate of 35cm3·min−1. The reactor was then heated to the desired temperature. Unreacted methane, coupling products (ethane, ethylene, propane, and propylene) and byproducts (carbon monoxide and carbon dioxide) were analyzed using gas chromatographs with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) (GC-3200, GL Science Inc., Japan) and a flame ionization detector (FID) (GC-8A, Shimadzu Co., Japan) equipped respectively with active carbon and Porapak Q columns. Carbon balance was more than 98%. The activities of the catalysts were evaluated 30min after the reactor temperature reached the desired value. Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of the catalysts at 293K. The XRD pattern of the Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst shows peaks attributable to α-cristobalite, Mn2O3, and Na2WO4, which agrees with previously reported XRD data for this system [13,33]. The crystalline silica phase is observed in the XRD patterns of the MCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 and NaCl–Mn/SiO2 catalysts. The LiCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst contains α-quartz while the other catalysts contain α-cristobalite. Such phase transition of SiO2 from amorphous to crystalline by heat treatment is known to be important in obtaining a high yield of coupling products over the Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst [33]. However, no crystalline silica phase is observed for the Mn/SiO2 catalyst, indicating that the phase transition of silica gel from amorphous to crystalline requires alkali metals [33]. The Mn2O3 phase is observed for all catalysts, with the exception of the CsCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst. In the CsCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst, the CsCl phase was clearly observed (Fig. 1h) because the large portion of this catalyst is CsCl at the weight ratio. Results of OCM at 1023K are presented in Table 1 . A higher conversion of methane is obtained with MCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalysts than with the Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst, indicating that MCl enhances the activation of methane. Furthermore, higher yields of coupling products, especially of ethylene, are obtained with MCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalysts. The addition of MCl promoters results in higher methane conversion and ethylene yield in the order NaCl>NaCl+KCl>KCl>CsCl>LiCl. At 1023K, the most active catalyst, NaCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2, gives an ethylene yield of as high as 31%, although the high yield is partly because of low methane pressure of the reactant (5.8kPa) [7,28]. The obtained ethylene yield is higher than that with the NaCl/Mn oxide catalyst (25.7% yield under 6.1kPa of methane at 1023K) reported by Otsuka and Komatsu [28], although a strict comparison is not possible because of the difference in experimental conditions. The ethylene yield with the NaCl–Mn/SiO2 catalyst (27.3%) is slightly lower than that with the NaCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst. However, it is much higher than that with the Mn/SiO2 catalyst (0.6%). These results show that the combination of NaCl and manganese is the main contributor to the high ethylene yield. Activity tests were conducted with different amounts of the NaCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst to investigate the relation between methane conversion and selectivity (Fig. 2 ). The total selectivity to coupling products (C2 +C3) reaches 88% at 12% methane conversion. It decreases with an increase in methane conversion, which is accompanied by an increase in the selectivity to byproducts (carbon monoxide and carbon dioxide). These results indicate that methane is converted predominantly to the coupling products and carbon monoxide and carbon dioxide are formed mainly from the coupling products consecutively. The selectivity to ethylene increases initially as methane conversion increases whereas the selectivity to ethane decreases, suggesting that ethylene is formed consecutively from ethane [6]. In Fig. 2, selectivity with other catalysts is also shown for comparison. The selectivities with various MCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalysts are found to be similar except for the LiCl-promoted catalyst, indicating that the type of alkali metal does not influence the selectivity of these catalysts. The low ethylene selectivity of the LiCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst compared with that of other MCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalysts is ascribed to the higher volatility of LiCl, as will be discussed later. The ethylene selectivity of the NaCl–Mn/SiO2 catalyst is slightly lower than that of the NaCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst, suggesting that the addition of Na2WO4 has a positive effect on ethylene formation, as will also be discussed below. However, a significant improvement in ethylene selectivity is observed for MCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalysts compared with the Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst, although ethane selectivity does not increase with the addition of MCl. The addition of MCl is thought to promote the consecutive formation of ethylene from ethane. Fig. 3a–c shows the time course of OCM at 1023K with the NaCl–, KCl–, and CsCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalysts. Catalyst deactivation is observed with the durability of the MCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalysts decreasing in the following order: NaCl>KCl>CsCl. In particular, the CsCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst deactivates within 1.5h, after which the conversion and selectivity become constant. The selectivity after 1.5h is similar to that of the chloride-free Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst (Table 1), which implies the loss of CsCl. The vapor pressures of MCl are not negligible at 1023K (NaCl (16Pa [34])KCl>CsCl>LiCl). However, this order differs at lower temperatures and is reversed at 873K (LiCl>CsCl>KCl>NaCl). It is considered that the effect of MCl vaporization lessens at lower temperatures. On the basis of the observed relative activities at 873K, it can be assumed that the physical property of MCl most likely to be important to catalytic activity is the melting point. To determine the influence of the melting point of MCl, the yield obtained with the (NaCl+KCl)–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst was compared with that obtained with the NaCl– and KCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalysts. This comparison was done based on the fact that the melting point of the equimolar NaCl–KCl mixture (930K [37]) is lower than that of KCl (1049K) and NaCl (1074K), although the vapor pressure of the mixture is between that of NaCl and KCl [38]. At a reaction temperature of 873K, the order with respect to the yield is (NaCl+KCl)>KCl>NaCl (Fig. 5). Thus, a lower melting point for MCl leads to a higher yield of the coupling products. This result suggests that the melting point of MCl is an important factor for OCM catalyzed by MCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2. The melting points of MCl would be lowered by the presence of other salts such as Na2WO4. To investigate the effect of Na2WO4, the yield and selectivity to coupling products with the NaCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 and NaCl–Mn/SiO2 catalysts are compared at various temperatures (Fig. 6 ). At 923K, the yield and selectivity with the NaCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst are about twice as high as those with the NaCl–Mn/SiO2 catalyst, although the differences become small at 1023K. These results are consistent with the hypothesis that molten MCl species promote the reaction and suggest that the promoting effect of NaCl in the NaCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst becomes more manifest at lower temperatures compared with the NaCl–Mn/SiO2 catalyst. The phase behavior of the catalysts around reaction temperatures was investigated using HT-XRD. Fig. 7 shows the HT-XRD patterns of the NaCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst at temperatures of 773–1023K. Within this temperature range, the silica support is transformed to β-cristobalite from α-cristobalite at 293K. Peaks of unknown phase(s) are observed at 2θ =16.4, 23.2, 28.6, 33.2, 37.0, 40.9, and 47.7° at 773K, which can be ascribed to a form of tungsten bronze (NaxWO3) [39,40]. These peaks are not found at 973K, indicating that the tungstate phase is melted below 973K. Peaks of NaCl are observed at 31.0 and 44.5° at 773K, with decreased intensities at elevated temperatures of 773–1023K. In the HT-XRD patterns of the NaCl–Mn/SiO2 catalyst (not shown), β-cristobalite and NaCl were detected, and the peak intensities of the latter gradually decreased with elevated temperatures. Fig. 8 shows the change in the relative peak intensity of the 200 diffraction peak of NaCl (2θ =31.0° at 773K) with increasing temperature. Although the relative intensity decreases at elevated temperatures for both the NaCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 and NaCl–Mn/SiO2 catalysts, the decrement is greater for the former at temperatures higher than 923K. In general, the thermal vibration of atoms in crystals increases at high temperatures, decreasing the XRD peak intensity. However, this cannot explain the difference in the decrement of the peak intensity of NaCl between the two catalysts. Therefore, the NaCl crystals in the NaCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst were probably partially melted at temperatures higher than 923K. Fig. 9 shows the HT-XRD patterns of the (NaCl+KCl)–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst. Similar to the HT-XRD patterns of the NaCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst in Fig. 7, an unknown phase, presumably tungstate, is observed at 773K. This phase disappears at 873K, which is lower than the temperature observed for the NaCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst (973K). This suggests that NaCl and KCl interacts with the crystalline tungstate phase and lowers its melting point. Moreover, the molten tungstate phase is supposed to dissolve a part of NaCl and KCl. At 773K, a Na-rich NaxK1−xCl phase (30.6 and 43.7°) and a K-rich NaxK1−xCl phase (28.6 and 40.9°) are observed. These phases are transformed to a single NaxK1−xCl phase (29.0 and 41.4°) at 823K. This indicates that the migration of the NaCl and KCl species on the (NaCl+KCl)–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst surface occurs between 773 and 823K. At temperatures higher than 973K, no crystalline chloride phase is observed, which is reasonable because the melting point of the equimolar mixture of NaCl and KCl is 930K. The HT-XRD data above suggest that MCl on the catalysts is not rigid, even at temperatures lower than the melting point. NaCl crystals on the NaCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst melt partially above 923K. In addition, a molten phase composed of tungstate, NaCl, and KCl is formed on the (NaCl+KCl)–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst at 873K. These temperatures (923K for NaCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 and 873K for (NaCl+KCl)–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2) are roughly consistent with those at which the oxidative coupling reaction began. The MCl species in the molten state, which is formed more easily from MCl crystal with lower melting points, is considered to promote the oxidative coupling reaction. The addition of MCl (LiCl, NaCl, KCl, and CsCl) to the Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst enhanced methane activation and ethylene formation from ethane. The highest ethylene yield of 31% was obtained with the NaCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalyst at 1023K. At this temperature, the Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalysts containing MCl with lower vapor pressure exhibited higher activity (NaCl>KCl>CsCl>LiCl). This was attributed to catalyst deactivation caused by the evaporation of MCl from the catalysts. However, the order of activity was inverted at 873K (LiCl>CsCl>KCl>NaCl). Molten MCl is considered to promote the oxidative coupling reaction.
abstract: We investigated the effect of alkali chloride (MCl=LiCl, NaCl, KCl, CsCl, and equimolar mixture of NaCl and KCl) on catalytic performance of MCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalysts for oxidative coupling of methane. The MCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalysts exhibited higher methane conversion and ethylene selectivity than the catalyst without MCl, with the highest ethylene yield (31%) obtained with the NaCl-promoted catalyst at a reaction temperature of 1023K. However, catalyst deactivation occurred because of the evaporation of MCl from the catalysts. Activity tests and high-temperature X-ray diffraction showed that molten MCl species promote the oxidative coupling reaction. Thus, the vapor pressure and melting point of MCl have a significant influence on the catalytic activity of the MCl–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalysts.
title: Oxidative coupling of methane over alkali chloride–Mn–Na2WO4/SiO2 catalysts: Promoting effect of molten alkali chloride

full_text: activated carbon attapulgite powder aromatic rings alkyl-substituted phenols branched alkanes bridged linkages benzene ring condensed ARs catalytic hydroconversion Di(1-naphthyl)methane filter cake gas chromatograph/mass spectrometer hydrocarbons initial hydrogen pressure iron pentacarbonyl 1-methylnaphthalene molar ratio methyltetralin normal alkanes non-CHC nitrogen-containing organic compounds Naomaohu lignite non‑nitrogen ketones Ni nanoparticles nickel tetracarbonyl oxygen-containing organic compounds residue 1 from thermal dissolution of NL residue 2 from NCHC of R1 residue II from CHC of R1 reaction mixture room temperature sulfur-containing organic compounds soluble portion the SP 1 from thermal dissolution/ethanolysis of NL the SP from NCHC of SP1 the SP from CHC of SP1 the SP 2 from NCHC of R1 the SP II from the CHC of R1 specific surface area strong covalent bonds thermal dissolution TD/ethanolysis X-ray photoelectron spectrum Lignites are abundant in natural resources over the world [1,2]. In spite of their large-scale exploitation and low transportation costs, they are not used as widely as higher-rank coals owing to their high contents of moisture, mineral matter, and organic oxygen [3]. Lignites are also rich in aromatic rings (ARs), most of which are connected by bridged linkages (BLs). Since many aromatics are value-added chemicals, cleaving the BLs facilitates producing the aromatics and achieving the value-added utilization of lignites. Thermal dissolution (TD), especially TD in an alkanol, is an important approach for obtaining more soluble portion (SP) [4] from lignites compared to room-temperature (RT) extraction. In fact, at elevated temperatures, TD in an alkanol involves in both TD and alkanolysis. However, such a SP is still rich in macromolecular species, including heteroatom-containing components, in which many ARs could be connected by BLs. Therefore, for value-added use of the SP, cleaving the BLs and removing the heteroatoms are needed. On the other hand, different from both lignites themselves and their derived SP, organic matter in the residues from the TD/alkanolysis of lignites only consists of insoluble macromolecular species. So, using a residue as the reactant can avoid the disturbance of inherent soluble species on the product analysis. Catalytic hydroconversion (CHC) of coals in non-polar solvents at low temperatures attracts wide attention in recent years [5–7]. Transition metals, especially precious metals [8–10], are applied for catalyzing AR hydrogenation at lower temperatures [11], but the high price and difficulty in recycling limit the practical application of precious metals. A series of sulfided bimetallic catalysts, such as nickel‑tungsten and nickel‑molybdenum sulfides, were used for hydrocracking, hydrodearomatization, and hydrodesulfurization [12–16]. Nickel tetracarbonyl (NTC) proved to be an important precursor for preparing highly dispersed metallic nickel loaded on a support [17], while attapulgite powder (AP) is an unusual support owing to its microfibrous morphology, high specific surface area (SSA), remarkable thermal stability, and low price [18,19]. Similar to NTC, iron pentacarbonyl (IPC) can also be thermally decomposed in situ on a support for preparing highly dispersed metallic iron loaded on a support. So, in-situ thermally decomposing both NTC and IPC on AP could prepare an active bimetallic catalyst. Di(1-naphthyl)methane (DNM) contains 2 naphthalene rings connected by 2 Car-Calk bonds, which are strong covalent bonds (SCVBs). So, DNM is well used as a coal-related model compounds and H transfer proved to be an effective approach for cleaving the SCVBs, especially over a metal‑sulfur system [20,21] or an activated carbon (AC)‑sulfur system [22]. Taking the above consideration into account, we tried to prepare Fe-Ni/AP by in-situ thermally decomposing both NTC and IPC on AP. Using the catalyst with sulfur addition, we examined DNM hydrocracking under different conditions and compared the CHC and non-CHC (NCHC) of both SP and residue from TD/ethanolysis (E). AP was added into a 500 mL round flask with 0.5 mol L−1 aqueous HCl solution with agitation for 4 h followed by centrifugating the mixture with deionized water several times to remove unreacted HCl. Then the AP was calcined in a tubular furnace at 350 °C for 4 h and pulverized to pass through a 200-mesh sieve followed by desiccation in vacuum at 80 °C for 24 h before use. IPC and NTC were synthesized in a 100 mL stainless-steel autoclave. Iron or nickel powders were added into the autoclave. After filling CO to 8 MPa, the autoclave was magnetically stirred at 100 rpm for 8 h at 120 °C. Fe-Ni/AP was prepared by thermally decomposing IPC and NTC onto AP. In detail, 2.0 g AP, 20 mL ethoxyethane, and different molar ratios (MRs) of IPC to NTC were added into the autoclave, which was stirred at 100 rpm for 1 h at RT after displacing air with nitrogen, heated to 100 °C and kept at 100 °C with agitation at 250 rpm for 2 h, and then cooled to RT in a water bath to release CO. The steps above were repeated to make the loading sufficient. Both AP and each catalyst were characterized with multiple instruments described in the Supplementary Material. As demonstrated in Figs. 1, 2 g NL and 20 mL ethanol were added into an autoclave. After replacing air with N2, the autoclave was heated to 300 °C and kept at 300 °C for 2 h with continuous magnetic agitation at 200 rpm. The SP 1 (SP1 ) and residue 1 (R1) were obtained by filtrating the reaction mixture (RM) after cooling the autoclave to RT. The same operation was repeated 3 times. About 1 mmol DNM, 1 g R1, or 1 g SP1, 20 mL cyclohexane, 0.1 g or 0.2 g a catalyst, and 25 mg sulfur were added into the magnetically stirred autoclave, which was heated to a prescribed temperature after displacing air with N2 and being pressurized with H2 to 5 MPa. NCHC was carried out as CHC without catalyst. The filtrate and filter cake (FC) from the CHC of R1 were obtained by filtrating the RM taken out from autoclave. The catalyst recovery from the FC was conducted using a magnetic iron. The FC was repeatedly extracted under ultrasonic irradiation with 100 mL acetone at RT until the extract solution became colorless to obtain residue 2 (R2 ) from the NCHC and residue II (RII ) from the CHC. Table S1 shows the ultimate analysis of NL and R1. SP 2 (SP2 ) and SP II (SPII ) are the SPs from the CHC and NCHC of R1, respectively. The yield (Y SP) of each SP was calculated as the mass ratio of the SP (m SP) to NL (m NL, daf ) on dry and ash-free basis, i.e., Y SP = m SP1 / m NL, daf . As observed in Figs. 2 and 3 , the surface of AP is very smooth, while the surfaces of Fe/AP, Ni/AP, and Fe-Ni/AP (Fe:Ni = 2:1) are rough. Besides, according to the analysis with inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer, the contents of Fe and Ni in Fe-Ni/AP are 21.1% and 9.0%, respectively (Table S2), indicating that Fe and Ni nanoparticles (NPs) are supported on the AP surface. As for AP, the diffraction peaks (DPs) of Si-O-Si crystal layer at 8.5o and 16.9o and the DPs of quartz at 35.1o, 20o, and 26.7o can be observed in Fig. 4 , while all the DPs from Fe-Ni/AP are weak. The DP of Ni is observed at 44.1o and the DPs of Fe appear at 30.3o, 57.1o, and 62.7o. The results indicate that Fe and Ni were successfully loaded on AP. As exhibited in Fig. S3, the N2 adsorption isotherms of AP and Fe-Ni/AP show representative type-H3. The pore diameters of AP and Fe-Ni/AP range from 0.6 to 25 nm. The SSA (90.3 cm3 g−1) of Fe-Ni/AP is significantly smaller than that (211.7 cm3 g−1) of AP (Table S3). Similarly, the total pore volume decreased to 0.4 cm3 g−1 in Fe-Ni/AP from 0.8 cm3 g−1 in AP, implying that some NPs of Fe and Ni were loaded onto some pores in AP. As Figs. 5 and S4 exhibits, elements Ni, Mg, Fe, Ni, Si, O, and C exist in Fe-Ni/AP. The typical peaks in the ranges of 846–864 eV and 705–740 eV are ascribed to Ni 2p and Fe 2p, respectively. There are no obvious differences in X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XRPES) between the fresh and recycled Fe-Ni/AP. The peak at 853.6 eV is the satellite peak of Al3Ni and the peaks at 855.8, 857.1, and 861.2 eV are attributed to higher hydroxylation of NiO, while the peak at 858.3 is assigned to the chemical bond of Ni(OH)+ [23–26]. Similarly, the peaks at 710.3, 711.4 eV, and 724.3 eV are attributed to the satellite peak of Fe3+, while the peaks at 714.2 eV and 726.8 eV are assigned to the satellite peaks of Fe2+, and the peak at 709.0 eV is ascribed to chemical bonds of metallic Fe. There are obvious peaks of NiS and FeS in XRPES of Fe-Ni-S/AP after recovering the recycled Fe-Ni/AP from the RM of Fe-Ni/AP and sulfur (Fig. S5). Both the fresh and recycled Fe-Ni/AP can be recovered, although the saturated magnetization value (17.5 emu g−1) of the Fe-Ni/AP recovered 3 times is obviously lower than that (28.5 emu g−1) of the fresh Fe-Ni/AP (Fig. 6 ). As summarized in Fig. S1, the activity of Fe-Ni-S/AP for DNM hydrocracking under the same conditions is significantly higher than that of Fe-Ni-S/AC, Fe-Ni-S/mordenite, Fe-Ni-S/ZSM-5, or Fe-Ni-S/γ-Al2O3. DNM conversion is ca. 97.7% over Fe-Ni-S/AP under 5 MPa of initial hydrogen pressure (IHP) at 300 °C for 4 h. The high catalytic activity of Fe-Ni-S/AP should be related not only to its large SSA but also to the interactions of AP with sulfided Fe and Ni. As shown in Fig. S2, the activity of Fe-Ni-S/AP with Fe:Ni = 2:1 for DNM hydrocracking under the same conditions is higher than that of Fe-Ni-S/AP with other MRs of Fe to Ni. DNM conversions over Fe-S/AP and Ni-S/AP are 54.2% and 70.0%, respectively, significantly being lower than 97.7% of DNM conversion over Fe-Ni-S/AP under the same conditions (IHP 5 MPa, 300 °C, 4 h). AP itself is almost not active for DNM hydrocracking under the same conditions (Fig. S6). So, we paid main attention to Fe-Ni-S/AP with Fe:Ni = 2:1. As summarized in Fig. 7 , DNM conversion along with the yields of tetralin, 1-methyltetralin (1-MT), and 5-methyltetralin (5-MT) increased monotonously with either prolonging time or raising temperature, while the yields of naphthalene and 1-methylnaphthalene (MN) increased with prolonging time up to 3 h or raising temperature up to 280 °C. Without catalyst, DNM was almost not converted under the reaction conditions due to the absence of active hydrogen species. DNM conversion is only 40.0% over Fe-Ni/AP without sulfur addition under 5 MPa of IHP at 300 °C for 4 h (Figs. S6 and S7). The stability and recyclability of a catalyst are very important for the practical application of the catalyst. Thus recycling experiments were done to evaluate the stability of Fe-Ni/AP with sulfur addition under 5 MPa of IHP at 300 °C for 4 h. As Fig. 8 displays, DNM conversion decreased slowly with increasing recycle times but is still 82.3% after recycling the catalyst 3 times. The reason for the small decrease in DNM conversion could be ascribed to the possible aggregation of Ni and Fe NPs on the AP surface. Transition metals contribute to the formation and transfer of biatomic active hydrogen (H…H) [27,28], which is active for hydrogenating ARs, while metal‑sulfur system proved to be effective for the formation and transfer of hydrogen radical (H) [29]. As Scheme 1 demonstrates, H addition to the ipso-position of DNM induces DNM hydrocracking. H· addition to the resulting naphthalene produces tetralin and to the resulting MN generates both 1-MT and 5-MT. Neither decalin nor methyldecalins were detected, indicating the absence of H…H over the catalyst and ineffectiveness of H for completely hydrogenating ARs. For example, H· preferentially abstracts benzylic hydrogen in tetralin rather than adding to benzene ring (BR) in tetralin due to the low superdelocalizability of each carbon atom in BR, similar to the catalytic hydrogenation of anthracene [28]. As shown in Fig. 9 , the yields of SP1, SP2, and SPII are 56.0%, 2.3%, and 11.2%, respectively. In total, 378 compounds were detected in the SPs with gas chromatograph/mass spectrometer (GC/MS). They can be classified into 4 classes: hydrocarbons (HCs), oxygen-containing organic compounds (OCOCs), nitrogen-containing organic compounds (NCOCs), and sulfur-containing organic compounds (SCOCs), and further classified into 64 group components (Tables S4–S68 and Fig. S13). As shown in Figs. 10 and S13 along with Tables S4-S67, the distribution of group components in SPNCHC is quite different from that in SPCHC , while the distribution of group components in SP1 is similar to that in SPNCHC. Arenes (Tables S13–S19) are the most abundant in SPCHC, accounting for 21.9%, while the most abundant group component in SP1, SPNCHC, and SPII are non‑nitrogen ketones (NNKs) (Table S31–44), accounting for 20.0%, 20.0%, and 26.6%, respectively. Most of the NNKs in SPII contain ARs. SPCHC is rich in alkyl-substituted benzenes (ASBsI) in addition to normal alkanes (NAsI ), branched alkanes (BAsI ), cyclanes, and alkyl-substituted phenols (ASPsII ), while more NNKs and esters (Table S47–58) were detected in SPNCHC. The precursors of ASBsI and ASPsII could be alkyl-substituted tolyl groups and alkyl-substituted phenoxy groups, respectively, connected to condensed ARs (CARs). H addition to the ipso-position of the CARs releases alkyl-substituted tolyl and alkyl-substituted phenoxy radicals followed by H transfer to the radicals to produce ASBsI and ASPsII, respectively (Scheme 2 ) [30,31]. As summarized in Fig. 11 , the absorbances around 3731 and 3431 cm−1 are assigned to the phenolic hydroxyl group of the SPs. The absorbances around 1142 and 1101 cm−1 are ascribed to >C-O-C< bridged bond vibration. The absorbances around 2959, 2927, and 2865 cm−1 of aliphatic moieties from SPCHC and SPII are significantly stronger than that from other samples. The absorbance around 635 cm−1 results from the C-H in-plane bending vibration of ARs. The results of the analysis with Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIRS) is in accordance with that of the analysis with GC/MS. As Table S1 lists, the contents of oxygen, sulfur, and nitrogen in R1 are significantly lower than those in NL, perhaps due to the high yield of SP1 from the TD/E of NL. Fe-Ni-S/AP proved to be active for catalyzing the cleavage of BLs connecting ARs and has a good stability and recylability. By the TD/E of NL and subsequent CHC of the resulting SP1 and R1 over Fe-Ni-S/AP, the total yield of the SPs, i.e., SP1 and SPII reaches 67.2%. More NAsI, BAsI, cyclanes, arenes (especially ASBsI), and ASPsII exist in SPCHC, while NNKs and esters are abundant in SPNCHC and most of the NNKs contain ARs. Mei-Ling Xu, Xian-Yong Wei, De-Wu Meng, Feng-Hai Li, Yun-Peng Zhao, Yu-Hong Kang, Zhi-Min Zong, Guang-Hui Liu, Sheng Li, Yan Xue, Fang-Jing Liu, Xing Fan, Jing-Pei Cao, Wei Zhao, Feng-Yun Ma, and Jing-Mei Liu declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper. This work was financially supported by the Key Project of Joint Fund from National Natural Science Foundation of China and the Government of Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region (Grant U1503293) and the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant 2018YFB0604602). Supplementary material Image 1 Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fuproc.2020.106388.
abstract: Fe/attapulgite powder (AP), Ni/AP, and Fe-Ni/AP were prepared by thermally decomposing iron pentacarbonyl (IPC), nickel tetracarbonyl (NTC), and different molar ratios of IPC to NTC onto AP in ethoxyethane, respectively. Their activities for the catalytic hydrocracking of di(1-naphthyl)methane (DNM) were compared under the same conditions. As a result, the activity of Fe-Ni-S/AP (Fe:Ni = 2:1) for DNM hydrocracking is significantly higher than that of other catalysts used. DNM conversion is near 100% over Fe-Ni-S/AP with Fe:Ni = 2:1. Over the catalyst, H2 can be effectively split to H, which attacks the ipso-position of DNM to cleave the Car-Calk bridged bond in DNM. Naomaohu lignite was subjected to thermal dissolution/ethanolysis in ethanol to afford soluble portion 1 (SP1, yield 56.0%) and residue 1 (R1) followed by non-catalytic hydroconversion (NCHC) and catalytic hydroconversion (CHC) of SP1 and R1 at 300 °C using Fe-Ni-S/AP with Fe:Ni = 2:1 as the catalyst to afford SPNCHC, SPCHC, SP2 (yield 2.3%), and SPII (yield 11.2%). According to gas chromatography/mass spectrometric analysis, arenes are the most abundant in SPCHC, accounting for 21.9%, while the most abundant group component in SPNCHC are non‑nitrogen ketones, accounting for 20.0%.
title: Catalytic hydroconversion of derivates from Naomaohu lignite over an active and recyclable bimetallic catalyst
