full_text: Many researches have been devoted to reduce catalyst deactivation by coking during catalytic hydroprocessing of petroleum residues. Among various approaches, understanding the mechanism of coking is an important topic. It is generally recognized that coking on catalyst starts with adsorption of asphaltenes on the surface and in the pore of a catalyst, which is followed by condensation of asphaltenes to coke [1]. The asphaltenes may also precipitate from the oil phase onto the catalyst if their quantity reaches a critical concentration [2]. The coking may also involve dehydrogenation of small fragments generated from thermal craking of residues in the catalyst's pores, which is followed by coupling and condensation reactions in the pores if activated hydrogen is insufficient [3]. It has been reported that coking on catalyst is also influenced by other factors, including the properties of feed and catalyst and operating conditions. For example, Castaño and Gutiérres et al. [4,5] showed that in hydrocracking of a light-cycle oil the coking on a PtPd catalyst was promoted by molecules with sulfur and nitrogen and the acidity and the BET surface area of the fresh catalyst. De Jong et al. [6,7] showed that in catalytic hydroprocessing of a vacuum gas oil (VGO), addition of a small amount of Mo to Al2O3 significantly decreased coke formation, and the higher the Mo loading was, the higher the catalyst activity was, even at a higher coke build-up. Gualda et al. [8] showed that in catalytic hydroprocessing of a Kuwait atmospheric residue (AR), the amount of coke on a commercial NiMo/Al2O3 catalyst increased over time to a maximum, and the higher the temperature was, the higher the maximum was and the higher the coking rate became. Furthermore, a higher H2 pressure decreased the coke build-up and increased the H/C ratio of the coke. Hydrogen donor media may act in a similar way as H2 does. Nakamura and Kubo et al. [9,10] showed that the coke formed on a NiCoMo/Al2O3 catalyst and the hydrogen consumption during hydroprocessing of a residue decreased significantly in hydrogen donor media. Liu [11] suggested that the catalytic hydroprocessing of a residue at a high H2 pressure may be simulated at a low pressure in a hydrogen donor solvent, because the form of activated H2 on a transition metal sulfide is M-S-H (M stands for a transition metal and S is sulfur), which can be readily formed in hydrogen donor solvents. Researches were also carried out to analyze coke on catalyst. Matsushita et al. [12] studied coke on a sulfided Mo/Al2O3 catalyst after being washed by tetrahydrofuran (THF), and categorized the coke in two types based on temperature-programmed oxidation (TPO). One type was termed soft coke, including coke precursors such as aromatics and asphaltenes in the feedstock, which adsorbed on the catalyst reversibly and could be removed from the catalyst by oxidation at temperatures around 300°C. The other type was termed refractory coke, which adsorbed strongly on the support surface and could be removed only by oxidation at temperatures higher than 400°C. Similar research was conducted by Castaño et al. [5] who deconvoluted the TPO curve of coke into three peaks at 450, 500 and 550°C, representing three types of coke of different condensation levels and different locations, such as on the external surface, on the metallic sites, and inside the micropores of the catalyst. Hauser et al. [13] studied H/C ratio of THF insoluble matters formed on a Mo/Al2O3 catalyst and showed a decrease in H/C ratio from 1.55 to 1.0 during a 240h operation. Fonseca et al. [14] characterized coke on a CoMo/Al2O3 catalyst with the solid state 13C NMR and reported dealkylation and condensation of aromatic rings during reaction, which resulted in a gradual increase in aromaticity of coke. It should be noted that besides the complex behaviors of coking discussed above, the coking on catalyst is also affected by or interrelated with the residues' reaction in the liquid phase, including the formation of coke precursor and/or even fine coke particulates, which has not been well studied. Furthermore, it is generally recognized that the coking process, no matter on the catalyst surface or in the liquid phase, follows the radical mechanism [7,15–18]. The mechanism starts by cleavage of weak covalent bonds in the residues to generate radical fragments and is followed by reaction of radical fragments. The large radical fragments usually contain more than one unpaired electrons. The coupling and condensation of the large radical fragments form heavy products including coke precursors and coke embedded with unpaired electrons that cannot move freely and react with each other [19]. The weak covalent bonds in petroleum residues include mainly those associated with aliphatic carbon (Cal), such as Cal Cal and Cal-X (X stands for a heteroatom) bonds, which crack usually at temperatures higher than 327°C [18]. When the temperature rises to 400–500°C, covalent bonds associated with aromatic carbon (Car), such as Car-X and maybe Car Cal, would also crack [20]. Coke is a group of complex heavy organic matters that cannot be fully understood by characterization using a single method or a few methods, such as spectroscopic techniques and TPO commonly seen in the literature. It has been reported that heavy organic matters, including coals and their pyrolysis tars, crude petroleum and its refining residues, contain stable radicals. These radicals are confined in large structures and inaccessible by other radicals. Therefore, they are measurable by electron spin resonance (ESR) [19,21,22]. Furthermore, thermal reaction of coals and coal tars can be monitored by changes in radical concentration, g value and the linewidth of ESR signals, which reflect the formation of coke precursors (asphaltenes and preasphaltenes) and coke [21,23]. The active free radicals however couple quickly and cannot be detected by ESR [24]. This capability of ESR has not been used to characterize coke formed on the catalyst's surface. The radicals behaviors accompanied with the evolution of coke structure on the catalyst surface should be a useful addition to the current knowledge. To better understand coking and radical behaviors during catalytic hydroprocessing of a petroleum residue, this paper studies hydroprocessing of an atmospheric residue on a commercial sulfided catalyst in hydrogen donor media in the absence of H2. The emphases are products distribution, coke formation on the catalyst, and the radical concentration associated with coke formation. The properties of Maoming atmospheric residue (AR) and NiMo/γ-Al2O3 catalyst studied are shown in Tables 1 and 2 , respectively. The catalyst was ground to sizes of 60–100 mesh and pre-sulfidated in a fixed-bed reactor. The sulfidation was carried out using 3.0g catalyst under a temperature programmed heating, from room temperature to 200°C at a rate of 10°C/min in a N2 atmosphere, and then from 200°C to 400°C at the same rate and maintained at 400°C for 30min in 15%H2S in H2 at a flow rate of 50mL/min. The AR was heated to 90°C and sampled 5.5±1mg by a glass capillary of 1.1mm in diameter. The sample loaded capillary along with the catalyst was placed into a glass tube of 2mm in diameter and 30mm in length at a catalyst to AR mass ratio of 1:5. The glass tube was purged with N2 and sealed by a blast burner. In some experiments, tetrahydronaphthalene (tetralin or THN), a common hydrogen donor solvent, was also introduced in the glass tube at a THN to AR ratio of 1:2. The hydroprocessing and control experiments were carried out by placing the sample loaded glass tube mentioned above into a quartz tube of 4mm in diameter and 250mm in length that was inserted in a furnace preheated to 400, 420, 440 and 460°C. The furnace has 20 slots allowing 20 experiments being simultaneously performed, and the temperature differences among the slots were no more than ±1°C. It took approximately 2min for the sample to reach the designated temperature without overshooting. The reaction time was 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 5.0, 10.0, 15.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0 or 60.0min. After reaction, the quartz tube with the heated sample was removed from the furnace and cooled to the room temperature in air. The quartz tube, with the sample loaded glass tube, was directly inserted into the resonance cavity of an ESR (e-scan, Bruker, Switzerland) for radical analysis at room temperature. The ESR was operated at 9.5GHz and 1.578mW with a central magnetic field of 3485G, a modulation amplitude of 1.0G, a sweep width of 100G, sweep time of 20.97s, and a time constant of 0.041s. The radical concentration data were calibrated by DPPH (1,1-Diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl, purity over 98%) of known radical concentration. The radical concentration of per-unit-mass sample (R) is calculated by Eq. (1a)(1b), where NDPPH is the number of radicals in DPPH which is known, DDPPH and Dsample are the numbers of radicals recorded by ESR, and msample is the mass (g) of sample. (1a) N DPPH / D DPPH = N sample / D sample (1b) R = N sample / m sample = N DPPH × D sample / D DPPH × m sample The capillary, glass tube and quartz tube showed little influence on the samples' ESR results. The control experiments with the catalyst alone or THN alone under the hydroprocessing conditions showed no radical signals. The experimental errors were less than 5% and average data of at least 2 runs are reported. The glass tube was cut open and weighed (m1), and then immersed in 0.5mL CS2, corresponding to a CS2 to AR mass ratio of 125:1, for 24h. CS2 is a good solvent with a density of 1.3g/cm3, which is higher than that of AR (0.9–1.1g/cm3) but lower than that of the catalyst (1.6g/cm3). Therefore, it extracted CS2 soluble fraction, CS2-S, out of the glass tube and kept the catalyst and CS2 insoluble fraction (CS2-IS or coke) in the tube. The glass tube with the remained catalyst and coke were dried and weighed (m2). The difference between m1 and m2 is the mass of CS2-S. The mass of coke was quantified by oxidation, which was carried out by sealing the glass tube with the remained catalyst and coke into an oxygen-filled quartz reactor and heating it to 500°C for 10min to burn the coke into CO2. Since the amount of oxygen in the reactor was in excess of the coke, the combustion was complete. The quantity of CO2 in the reactor was determined by gas chromatography (GC, Agilent 7890B, TCD detector), which is equivalent to C in coke in mole. The mass of gas was the difference between the mass of AR and the total mass of CS2-S and coke. Repeated experiments showed experimental errors of less than 10%, including those in weighing, integration of GC and other uncontrollable factors. The main product of THN conversion is naphthalene, because peaks other than THN and naphthalene were not observed in the products by gas chromatography (GC). Carbon balance is greater than 97.5% by comparing the GC peak area of THN in the initial sample with sum of the GC peaks of THN and naphthalene in the samples subjected reaction. It can be seen in Fig. 1 that THN conversion in 30min increases with an increase in temperature, from 0.4% at 400°C to 8.7% at 460°C in the absence of catalyst, indicating that the CH bonds in THN dissociated at these temperatures to generate small amounts of hydrogen that may be donated to AR if it presents in the reaction. The catalyst promotes THN conversion as shown by 54.3% at 400°C and 77.7% at 460°C in 30min, suggesting that the catalyst may promote hydrogen donation of THN in the presence of AR. It is generally believed that S ions at the corner and edge of MoS2 are readily removed under hydroprocessing conditions, resulting in coordinatively unsaturated sites (CUS) and/or sulfur vacancies which are able to absorb and dissociate H2 via the formation of Mo-S-H and MoH bonds. These bonds are low in bonding energy and active for shuttling hydrogen to hydrocarbon radicals [25–27]. Since the bonding energy of CH bond in THN (347kJ/mol) is much lower than that of HH bond (436kJ/mol), H in THN is easier to be activated by the catalyst than H2, suggesting that the presence of THN may be used to simulate hydroprocessing process at high H2 pressures. It should be noted that the carbon balance of THN in the reactions is greater than 97% based on the GC analysis of CS2-S since THN and naphthalene are CS2 soluble. The yields of CS2-S of AR+Catalyst+THN in Fig. 2 therefore are determined by subtracting the amount of THN added. As shown in Fig. 2, the reactions proceed slowly at 400 and 420°C. In the case of AR+Catalyst, the yields of CS2-S, coke and gas in 60min are 95.2, 3.8 and 1.0%, respectively, at 400°C and 87.9, 5.8 and 6.2%, respectively, at 420°C. THN has little influence on the product yields at these two temperatures, within the experimental errors. It is noted in Fig. 3 , however, that the THN conversions in 60min are actually quite large, approximately 57.4% at 400°C and 79.9% at 420°C, indicating generation of large amounts of hydrogen at these temperatures. These phenomena may indicate that the AR is either inactive at these temperatures or active but its reactions are limited to conversion of some CS2-S to other CS2-S, because in both cases the yields of coke and gas are low. It is worth to note that the THN conversions at 400 and 420°C in the presence of AR (Fig. 3) are lower than those of THN in the absence of AR (Fig. 1, THN+Catalyst). For example, its conversions in 30min are 45.9 and 56.4% for the former while 54.3 and 63.1% for the latter. This behavior may suggest that AR suppresses the dehydrogenation of THN probably by reducing its contact with the catalyst. Fig. 2 shows that the reactions of AR are fast at temperatures of 440 and 460°C, leading to obvious decrease in CS2-S yield and increases in coke and gas yields. As in the case of AR+Catalyst, the CS2-S, coke and gas yields in 60min are 61.2, 16.1 and 22.7%, respectively, at 440°C and 33.9, 23.3 and 42.8%, respectively, at 460°C. THN influences the yields of the products but its effect is not significant until 20min at 440°C and 10min at 460°C. After these initial periods, THN slows down the decline of CS2-S yield and the increase of coke and gas yields. For example, the CS2-S yield in 60min at 440°C is 85.3% in the presence of THN, which is 24 percentage points higher than that in the absence of THN. Correspondingly, the coke and gas yields decrease by 14 and 10 percentage points, respectively. These data indicate that, from the yield point of view, the reactions of AR become significant at a temperature between 420 and 440°C, and the condensation reactions for coke and gas formation in the initial 20min at 440°C and the initial 10min at 460°C are mainly inter-molecule structural condensation of large molecules, which do not involve generation of radical fragments. Furthermore, the reactions of radical fragments produced from cracking of AR in the initial periods are those between the radical fragments or those between the radical fragments and H donated by THN, both form new CS2-S, although the former is heavier than the latter. This is supported by the large conversion of THN in the initial periods, 74.7% in 20min at 440°C and 60.5% in 10min at 460°C as shown in Fig. 3. This indicates that the quantities of THN participating in the reaction in the later period are small, and the reduced decline in CS2-S yield as well as the reduced increases in coke and gas yields in the later period may be ascribed to the reaction of lighter CS2-S formed in the initial period, which results in less condensation reaction. Fig. 4 shows the radical concentration of the samples in the glass tubes, including the liquid, coke and gas, obtained in reactions shown in Fig. 2. Two data points in Fig. 4(d) exceed the vertical scale, which are 6.2 and 10.5μmol/g-AR at 460°C in 40 and 60min, respectively. It is seen that the radical concentrations in the case of AR+Catalyst decrease in the initial 3min, but then rise linearly afterwards at rates promoted by temperature. The increases in radical concentration can be expressed by the Arrhenius Equation with activation energy of 236.9kJ/mol and a correlation coefficient of 0.978. This behavior shows that the reaction of AR is observable at 400°C, and the reaction involves cracking of AR to generate radical fragments and coupling of the radical fragments to form heavy products, which inevitably confine some unpaired electrons detectable by ESR. It can be seen in Fig. 4 that in the case of AR+Catalyst+THN the radical concentration at 400, 420 and 440°C declines over time with similar rates, lower than 0.3μmol/g-AR in 5min. This suggests that the radical fragments generated from cracking of AR are hydrogenated by THN, which results in little condensation reaction. This phenomenon supports the discussion presented earlier that the CS2-S formed in the presence of THN is mainly hydrogen-rich light product, which cannot be observed in Fig. 2. It is noted that the radical concentration at 460°C also declines initially, to 0.2μmol/g-AR in 10min, but then starts to rise. The rising rate speeds up after 40min and reaches 1.1μmol/g-AR in 60min. It suggests that the quantities of radicals generated at this temperature are so large that the amounts of hydrogen donated by THN are not sufficient to meet hydrogenation demand after 10min, leading to more condensation reaction between the AR radical fragments. It is noted that the coke yields in Fig. 2 include coke on the catalyst and the coke suspended in the liquid phase. To quantify the coke on the catalyst, the catalyst separated out by CS2 extraction after the reaction was washed by ethanol and water. The results in Fig. 5 show that in the absence of THN (AR+Catalyst), the yield of coke on catalyst increases with increases in temperature and time, and the rates at 400, 420 and 440°C are linear after the initial 3min. The trends are similar to that of radical concentration in Fig. 4 except in the initial 3min, during which the yield of coke increases quickly but the radical concentration decreases slightly. These different behaviors in coke yield and radical concentration in the initial period may suggest that the catalyst quickly absorbs AR's heavy components (CS2-IS or coke) initially due to their increased mobility upon heating, which also promoted coupling of radicals. The coking rate at 460°C also increases linearly with time but the rate reduces somehow after 20min. This trend agrees with the phenomenon in Fig. 2(d) but is slightly different from that in Fig. 4(d) for radical concentration. Compared with that in AR+Catalyst, Fig. 5 shows that the hydrogen donation of THN in AR+Catalyst+THN reduces the rate of coke formation on the catalyst, although the rate still increases linearly with an increase in temperature. The linear trend of coking on the catalyst in Fig. 5 after the initial 3min may be approximated by the zero-order kinetics with the slope being the rate constant k. It should be noted that there are 8 sets of experiments in Figs. 5, and 7 of them can be represented by a straight line after the initial 3min heating up period. The only set of data that may be expressed by two consecutive straight lines is in Fig. 5(d) for the case of AR+Catalyst, where the line in 3–20min is used along with other 7 lines in the Arrhenius equation. This is because that the oil compositions in this stage are more close to that in other 7 cases than those in the latter stage, which are probably low in heavy components due to condensation and precipitation reaction at the earlier stage. Based on Arrhenius Equation, Eq. (2), where R is the gas constant and T the temperature, the frequency factor k 0 and the apparent activation energy E a can be obtained by the regression shown in Fig. 6 . It can be seen that the rate constants of coking are much higher in the absence of THN than that in the presence of THN, and the difference becomes larger with increasing temperature. The activation energy is 208.8kJ/mol in the absence of THN, similar to that reported by He [21] for coking in coal tars (199–218kJ/mol) and that reported by De Jong [18] for coking of VGO on a CoMo/Al2O3 catalyst in a trickle-bed reactor (210kJ/mol). The activation energy is 134.5kJ/mol in the presence of THN, which is lower than that in the absence of THN as well as coking on the CoMo/Al2O3 catalyst studied by De Jong [18]. This further supports the hypothesis that the hydrogen donation by THN converts heavier CS2-S to lighter CS2-S, which does not change the yield of CS2-S but decreases the coking rate even when THN is mostly consumed. Clearly, the smaller E a in the presence of THN may suggest that the coking rate does not increase with increasing temperature as much as that in the absence of THN because the hydrogen donation rate of THN also increases with increasing temperature, which slows down the increase in condensation rate. (2) ln k = ln k 0 − E a / RT Fig. 7 shows that the radical concentration of coke on the catalyst increases over time and the increase is faster at a higher temperature. This indicates that the structure of coke becomes more condensed over time due to cleavage and release of its lighter constituents, including the relatively light aliphatic side chains. Compared with that in the absence of THN, the radical concentration of coke on catalyst in the presence of THN is much lower under all the conditions, suggesting that the hydrogen donation of THN greatly hinders the condensation of coke, especially at a higher temperature. This behavior in radical concentration is supported by the corresponding changes in H/C molar ratio of coke on the catalyst, which vary from 1.15–1.12 at 400°C to 0.38–0.36 at 460°C in 40–60min in the absence of THN, while from 1.29–1.21 at 400°C to 0.60–0.41 at 460°C in 40–60min in the present of THN. It is noted that the increase in radical concentration of coke on the catalyst can be approximated by a two-stage linear growth. The initial stage is relatively fast and short, 3min in the absence of THN and 10min in the presence of THN. This suggests that the condensation of coke is faster in the absence of THN but slower in the presence of THN, which further indicates that the asphaltenes is quickly absorbed by the catalyst in the initial stage [1,12,28] and undergoes a faster condensation in the absence of THN or the hydrogen donation of THN inhibits the condensation of asphaltenes. The slower increase in radical concentration in the later stage may indicate that the coke formation on the catalyst is mainly a result of surface reaction between the coke already formed on the catalyst and the radical fragments from cracking of AR rather than direct precipitation of asphaltenes. It can be seen in Fig. 8 that the radical concentration generally increases with an increase in coke content, indicating structuring of the coke from light to heavy on the catalyst via continued condensation. This trend is faster at a higher temperature in the absence of THN (AR+Catalyst, Fig. 8(a)) as indicated by the slopes, 0.8, 1.6 and 2.8μmol/g-coke at 400, 420 or 440, and 460°C, respectively, where the points represented by the lines are in the first stage. The trends in the second stage are scatter but still show a lower slope than that of the first stage, indicating that the coking rate is slow which encapsulates with fewer radicals. Fig. 8(b) shows that THN weakens the effect of temperature and the overall slope is approximately 1.3μmol/g-coke, which is lower than that at 420, 440 and 460°C in the absence of THN, suggesting again that the hydrogen donation of THN inhibits the condensation of coke. As mentioned above, the radicals exist mainly in coke but also in the solvent soluble fractions in the form of heavy components suspended. The data in Fig. 9 are derived from the differences in quantity of radicals between the sample and the coke on catalyst. It is found that the radical concentrations of CS2-S are low, generally less than 1μmol/g of CS2-S, about a few percent of that in coke. After an obvious decrease in the initial 3min, attributable to the rapid adsorption of heavy components in AR by the catalyst, the radical concentration of CS2-S increases in the absence of THN, indicating cracking and condensation of AR in the liquid phase. The trend is similar to that in Fig. 4 under the same conditions but the rate is much small due probably to precipitation of heavy products onto the catalyst. The presence of THN significantly reduces the condensation of radicals in CS2-S. This paper studies the reaction of Maoming AR on a sulfided NiMo/γ-Al2O3 catalyst in a temperature range of 400–460°C in the presence and absence of THN. It is found that the changes in radical concentration are more sensitive than the changes in yields of solid, gas and liquid, and radical concentration is an effective parameter to characterize the reaction, especially the condensation reaction that generates heavy products such as coke. The radical concentration shows that the reaction of AR is significant at 400°C, resulting in the formation of coke (CS2 insoluble matter) and CS2 soluble matters (CS2-S) that are different from that originally in the AR. The yields of CS2-S, coke and gas do not change significantly until the temperature is within 420–440°C. The coke formation on the catalyst is initially (around 3min) fast, which is ascribed to adsorption of heavy component in AR onto the catalyst. This initial coke formation is accompanied with fast increase in radical concentration, indicating condensation of light coke to heavy coke. The hydrogen donation of THN reduces the coking rate and the activation energy of coking kinetics due to increased hydrogen donation rate at high temperatures, which inhibits the condensation reaction. The radical concentration of CS2-S is also reduced by THN, indicating the presence of less heavy components. The work is financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (21506009) and China Petrochemical Corporation (Sinopec Group, 114080).
abstract: Coking on catalyst during hydroprocessing of petroleum residues is an important issue that should be addressed for better catalyst performance. This paper studies reaction and coking behaviors of Maoming atmospheric residue (AR) over a sulfided commercial NiMo/γ-Al2O3 catalyst in the presence and absence of a hydrogen donor solvent, tetrahydronaphthalene (THN), in a micro-reactor at temperatures of 400–460°C and reaction time of 0–60min. It reports the changes in yields of CS2 soluble matter (CS2-S), CS2 insoluble matter (coke) and gas, as well as the changes in quantity of coke on the catalyst and in radical concentration of the reaction system, coke on catalyst and CS2-S. It is found that compared with the changes in yields, which are significant at temperatures higher than 420°C, the changes in radical concentration are significant at 400°C. The catalyst promotes hydrogen donation of THN, which lowers the amounts of coke formed in the liquid phase, on the catalyst, and the activation energy of coking on the catalyst.
title: Coke and radicals formation on a sulfided NiMo/γ-Al 2 O 3 catalyst during hydroprocessing of an atmospheric residue in hydrogen donor media

reaction_catalog.transformation: atmospheric residue → CS2 soluble matter + coke + gas

steps.method_details: ground to sizes of 60–100 mesh
temperature programmed heating in a fixed-bed reactor
sampled 5.5±1mg by a glass capillary
placed capillary with catalyst into glass tube at catalyst to AR mass ratio of 1:5
glass tube purged with N2 and sealed by a blast burner

override_steps.method_details: introduced THN in the glass tube at a THN to AR ratio of 1:2

steps.method_details: sealed glass tube with remained catalyst and coke into oxygen-filled quartz reactor and heated to 500°C for 10min to burn coke into CO2
inputs: glass tube with catalyst and coke

results.value: temperature programmed heating from room temperature to 200°C at 10°C/min in N2, then from 200°C to 400°C at same rate and maintained at 400°C for 30min in 15% H2S in H2 at 50 mL/min

results.notes: coke burned to CO2 at 500°C for 10min, determined by GC

results.value: increases over time, faster at higher temperature

common_conditions.reactor_type: glass tube in quartz tube furnace
common_conditions.feed_or_medium: Maoming atmospheric residue (AR), with/without tetrahydronaphthalene (THN) as hydrogen donor solvent
common_conditions.catalyst_amount: catalyst to AR mass ratio of 1:5
common_conditions.other: catalyst pre-sulfidated, THN to AR ratio of 1:2 when used, reaction times: 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 5.0, 10.0, 15.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0, 60.0 min

full_text: The unique physical and chemical properties of nanocarbon materials have attracted great attention and created a broad application market for them [1–6]. Among the nanocarbon materials, carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have a wide range of applications in electrode materials [7], supercapacitors [8], energy storage materials [9] and composite materials [10] due to their excellent mechanical, electrical and chemical properties. The existing preparation technologies for CNTs mainly include chemical vapor deposition (CVD), arc discharge, and laser ablation [11]. As these methods have high requirements for the purity of raw materials (the CVD method generally uses pure hydrocarbon gas as raw material) [12] or require high reaction temperatures (the maximum temperature of arc discharge and laser ablation methods is about 3000–4000 °C) [13,14], the preparation cost of CNTs is high, which greatly limits their practical applications. Therefore, research on CNTs preparation technology is currently focused on the use of inexpensive raw materials and relatively mild preparation conditions [11,15–19]. Coal resources are characterized by abundant reserves and low prices, and coal is an excellent carbon source for the preparation of CNTs [11,15,16]. As the main constituent element, carbon in coal has an important influence on the activity of coal. Wang et al. [20,21] found that coal as a raw material for the preparation of CNTs is more reactive than graphite. The reason is that coal is a macromolecular solid formed by polymerization of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons via aliphatic phenyl, alkyl, ether linkages or other bridging functional groups. In the process of arc discharge, coal is cracked and releases a large number of fragments of active hydrocarbon molecules, such as alkynes and aromatic substances, which are the basic materials for the formation of CNTs. Moreover, their activity is higher than that of carbon atoms ionized from graphite. The content of carbon also has an important effect on the preparation of coal-based CNTs. Qiu et al. [22] used arc discharge method to prepare CNTs with coke-rods prepared from natural coke, long-flame coal and anthracite as anodes. The content of C element (daf, dry-ash-free) in these three coke-rods were 94.2%, 96.9% and 97.5% respectively, and the corresponding yields of CNTs were 1.44 wt%, 2.72 wt% and 8.17 wt%, respectively. The carbon content in the coke-rod was not linearly related to the yield of CNTs, but with the increase in carbon content, the yield of CNTs increased [22,23]. In addition, some researchers have used coal pyrolysis gas or city gas as raw materials to prepare CNTs by CVD method [24–26]. It was found that CH4 and CO in the raw gas are the main carbon sources for the growth of CNTs, and they have synergistic effect. Although there are many reports on the preparation of CNTs with coal as raw material, these studies mostly focused on arc discharge method and CVD method. The conversion processes of the above two methods are the high-temperature conversion of solid carbon source (3000–4000 °C) [13,14] and the low-temperature conversion of gas carbon source [26], respectively. The new method of preparing CNTs by K-catalyzed pyrolysis of coal proposed by the research group is different from the two processes mentioned above, and it involves the low-temperature conversion of solid carbon source. The latest research of author's group found that in the process of K-catalyzed coal pyrolysis, the carbon source needed for the growth of CNTs mainly comes from the macromolecular solid carbon structure in coal or coke, while the carbonaceous gas generated in the process of coal pyrolysis has limited effect on the growth of CNTs. The results show that there are two significant differences between the K-catalyzed pyrolysis of coal to produce CNTs and the two existing technologies(arc discharge and CVD): 1. The carbon source structure required for the growth of CNTs in this method is similar to that of arc discharge, but the conversion process of these carbon structures is different from that of arc discharge; 2. The solid-phase carbon source required for the growth of CNTs in this method is different from the gas-phase carbon source such as CH4 and CO in the CVD method, and the transformation process of solid-phase carbon structure is obviously different from that in the CVD method. Up to now, there are no reports about the structure of carbon source and its transformation mechanism for the preparation of CNTs by K-catalyzed pyrolysis of coal. Therefore, it is urgent to study the preparation of CNTs by K-catalyzed pyrolysis of coal, especially the mechanism of the transformation of the main carbon structure in coal and its effect on the growth of CNTs. This will provide new ideas for the clean and high-value utilization of coal resources. In order to investigate the evolution of carbon structure of macromolecular solids in coal during pyrolysis and its influence on the growth of CNTs, this paper studies the evolution of carbon structure during the non-catalyzed pyrolysis and K-catalyzed pyrolysis of low metamorphic bituminous coal and its pyrolytic coke. SEM, TG-DTG, FTIR, XRD, and Raman analysis techniques were used to investigate the surface morphology, CNTs content, functional groups, crystal structure, and carbon structure of the pyrolysis products. Based on the results, the probable growth mechanism of carbon nanotubes in the pyrolysis process was proposed. Raw coal sample (RC) was obtained by grinding air-dried Shanxi bituminous coal to below 74 μm. An appropriate amount of RC was placed in a tube furnace, and the RC sample was heated to 900 °C and kept for 90 min under nitrogen atmosphere. Then, the heating was stopped and nitrogen atmosphere was maintained until the temperature dropped to room temperature. Thus, the coal coke sample (CC) was obtained. The results of proximate and ultimate analyses of RC and CC are shown in Table 1 . The KOH catalyst was loaded on the coal samples as follows: 15.0 g of KOH was dissolved in a mixture of distilled water (30.0 ml) and ethanol (10.0 ml), and then 30.0 g of RC or CC was added to the KOH solution. The mixture was magnetically stirred for 24 h. Then, the samples were dried at 60 °C for 1 h and dried at 105 °C for 5 h respectively. After cooling, a KOH-loaded coal sample was obtained, which was named RC-K or CC-K. The KOH and ethanol used in the experiment were of analytical grade and purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (China). About 8.0 g of sample (RC, RC-K, CC or CC-K) was placed in a horizontal tubular furnace. Then, the reaction device was connected and the air in the device was exhausted with nitrogen. The tubular furnace was then heated to 900 °C at the rate of 10 °C/min and held for 0, 30 or 90 min. Then, heating was stopped and the remaining samples were taken for standby when the temperature of tubular furnace dropped to room temperature. In addition, appropriate amounts of RC-K and CC-K pyrolysis residual samples were pickled with dilute hydrochloric acid (1 M), then washed with distilled water until neutral. The washed samples were dried at 105 °C for 5 h, and finally cooled in a dryer for 30 min to obtain the required samples, which were named RC-K-X or CC-K-X. The pyrolysis product of RC or CC without pickling or distilled water washing was named RC-X or CC-X. The X represents the residence time. The products obtained by the respective materials at different residence times were labeled as shown in Table 2 . Thermo scientific Nicolet iz10 infrared spectrometer was used to analyze the changes in functional groups of each sample. The detector scanning range was 400–4000 cm−1, and the instrument resolution was 0.4 cm−1. The sample was prepared by KBr mixed tablet method with 2.0 mg and 100.0 mg mass of the sample and KBr, respectively. The crystal structure of the sample was measured using a Japanese RIAKU Ultima IV diffractometer. The scanning range was 2θ: 5–90°, the scanning speed was 4°/min, and the step size was 0.02°. The Renishaw RM2000 laser Raman spectrometer (equipped with a 50 × short-focus optical microscope) was used to analyze the carbon structure of the sample. The excitation wavelength of the instrument is 514 nm, the grating is 1800 g/mm, the slit is 50 μm, the exposure time is 10 s, the spectral resolution is 1 cm−1, and the spatial resolution is 0.5 μm horizontally and 2 μm vertically. The diameter of the laser beam reaching the sample is about 1 μm. The first-order bands were recorded in the range of 600–2300 cm−1. The surface morphology of the sample was observed by a Japanese HITACHI SU8020 field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM), and the acceleration voltage was 20 kV. The weight loss process of the sample was analyzed by a Japanese SHIMADZU TA-60WS thermal analyzer under air atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C/min. A GC950 gas chromatograph (with thermal conductivity detector) was used to detect the precipitation of carbon-containing gas components during the pyrolysis of RC. High purity H2 (99.999%) was used as the carrier gas, and the gasification chamber temperature was 50 °C. The column/furnace temperature was 40 °C. The results of SEM analysis of the products of RC, RC-K, CC and CC-K at 900 °C for 0, 30 and 90 min respectively are shown in Fig. 1 . It can be seen from Fig. 1a–c, g–i that the surface of the RC and CC pyrolysis product particles was relatively flat and no CNTs were present. It can be seen from Fig. 1d that a small amount of CNTs appeared in the RC-K-0 sample, and a large number of CNTs appeared in RC-K-30 (Fig. 1e) and RC-K-90 (Fig. 1f). A trace amount of CNTs appeared in the CC-K-0 sample (Fig. 1j), and a small amount of CNTs appeared in the samples of CC-K-30 (Fig. 1k) and CC-K-90 (Fig. 1l). The CNTs in the RC-K and CC-K pyrolysis products were about several tens of micrometers long and had a relatively uniform diameter. The above results show that the addition of KOH was directly related to the formation of CNTs. In addition, the content of CNTs in the catalytic pyrolysis products of RC was higher than that of CC under the same conditions, indicating that the carbon source content suitable for the formation of CNTs in RC was higher. The results of TG-DTG analysis of the products of RC, RC-K, CC and CC-K are shown in Fig. 2 . From Fig. 2a, b, e, f, it can be seen that there was a main weight loss peak in the interval of 400–700 °C for RC pyrolysis products and CC pyrolysis products. The weight loss ratio of each sample was about 90 wt%, and the maximum weight loss rate was in the intervals of 604–619 °C and 606–630 °C, respectively. The maximum weight loss rate of CC pyrolysis products (CC-0, CC-30 and CC-90) was higher than that of RC pyrolysis products (RC-0, RC-30 and RC-90) under the same reaction conditions. The reason is that after the reheating treatment, the degree of condensation of CC pyrolysis products was stronger than that of RC pyrolysis products (single heat treatment). The internal hetero atom (such as O atom) content was decreased and the carbon content was increased, so the reactivity of the pyrolysis product was lowered [27]. According to Fig. 2c and d, RC-K pyrolysis products had two weight loss peaks within the interval of 360–700 °C, among which the main weight loss peak was in the temperature interval of 360–600 °C, and the maximum weight loss rate appeared within the interval of 567–581 °C. The weight loss interval of the secondary weight loss peak was 600–700 °C, and the maximum weight loss rate appeared near 626 °C. According to SEM analysis (Fig. 1d–f), RC-K pyrolysis products mainly consisted of activated carbon and CNTs, and activated carbon was the main component. Therefore, the main weight loss peak in TG-DTG analysis corresponded to the oxidation weight loss process of activated carbon in the sample, while the secondary weight loss peak was due to the oxidation weight loss process of CNTs. According to Fig. 2c, the content of CNTs in RC-K-0, RC-K-30 and RC-K-90 were 1.98 wt%, 6.85 wt% and 10.16 wt%, respectively. From Fig. 2g, h, it can be seen that there were two weight loss peaks in the interval of 250–650 °C. The main weight loss peak weight loss temperature interval was 250–500 °C, and the maximum weight loss rate appeared in the interval of 378–407 °C. The weight loss interval of the secondary weight loss peak was 450–650 °C, and the maximum weight loss rate appeared in the interval of 507–520 °C. Compared with the weight loss process of CC (Fig. 2e, f), the weight loss process of CC-K significantly changed, because the added KOH etched the ordered carbon structure of CC at high temperature [28,29], resulting in the increased content of internal amorphous carbon. Finally, the carbon component in the pyrolysis products of CC-K was divided into two parts: the amorphous carbon component and the residual relatively ordered carbon component [30]. Therefore, the sample showed two obvious weight loss peaks during the weight loss process. With the increase in residence time, the content of amorphous carbon produced by KOH etching CC increased gradually. As shown in Fig. 2g, the content of amorphous carbon in CC-K-0, CC-K-30 and CC-K-90 samples were 42.25 wt%, 59.62 wt% and 70.05 wt%, respectively. In contrast, the content of relatively ordered carbon structure in CC decreased gradually, and the weight loss ratios were 39.05 wt%, 22.20 wt% and 13.97 wt%, respectively (Fig. 2g). In addition, as the CNTs content was low, the oxidation weight loss process of the CNTs is not shown in the figure. Fig. 3 shows the FTIR spectra of the samples. It can be seen from Fig. 3 that the infrared absorption patterns of RC, RC-K, CC and CC-K pyrolysis products were similar, and absorption peaks appeared near 600, 1060, 1600, 2854, 2918 and 3430 cm−1. In addition, the RC pyrolysis products showed a shoulder near 1380 cm−1. The FTIR absorption peaks and the functional group assignment are shown in Table 3 . All samples showed a distinct -OH stretching vibration peak in the range of 3000–3700 cm−1, which was attributed to intermolecular hydrogen bonding, free -OH or -OH groups at the end of the coal aromatic cluster [31]. In addition, all samples had weak absorption peaks around 2916, 2848 and 600 cm−1. The asymmetric stretching vibration peak of aliphatic -CH2 at 2916 cm−1 in the sample was slightly stronger than the symmetric stretching vibration peak of aliphatic -CH3 at 2848 cm−1, indicating that -CH2 existed in the form of relatively long side chain [29,32]. The absorption peak near 600 cm−1 was attributed to the SiO and Si-O-Al groups, which are related to the Si and Al minerals in the sample. It can be seen from Fig. 3a that with the extension of residence time, the deformation vibration of aromatic CC and CH and the stretching vibration absorption peak intensity of ether bond C-O-C corresponding to 1060 cm−1 gradually increased during the pyrolysis of RC, indicating that the pyrolysis of RC promoted the formation of aromatic carbon CC, CH and C-O-C groups. It can be seen from Fig. 3b, d that the absorption peak intensities of the CC, CH and C-O-C groups of the RC-K and CC-K pyrolysis products gradually decreased as the reaction progressed. The reason is that KOH was gradually converted into K-containing substances such as K2CO3, K2O, and K under high temperature conditions, and these substances etched the carbon structure in RC or CC, resulting in destruction of the skeleton carbon structure in RC or CC [28,33]. It can be seen from Fig. 3c that with the extension of residence time, the absorption peak intensity of aromatic CC, CH and ether bond C-O-C structure of the pyrolysis product of CC decreased gradually, indicating that the reheating may destroy the original aromatic CC, CH and ether bond C-O-C structure in the CC. Comparing Fig. 3a with b, c with d, it was observed that the CC deformation vibrations of aromatic rings at 1600 cm−1 of pyrolysis products of RC-K and CC-K were stronger than those of RC and CC (Fig. S3), which indicates that the CC content of aromatic structure in samples increased when KOH was added for pyrolysis. According to some existing studies, K-containing substances such as K2CO3, K2O and K, which are transformed from KOH, can destroy the carbon structure of coal [28,33], resulting in the reduction of the aromatic CC absorption peak intensity in the active carbon products. In this experiment, the product was a mixture of activated carbon and CNTs, and the aromatic CC absorption peak strength of the product increased after the addition of KOH pyrolysis. Therefore, the enhancement of CC absorption peak in RC-K and CC-K pyrolysis products was due to the formation of CNTs in the process of KOH catalyzed RC/CC pyrolysis. According to Fig. 3b, d, with the extension of residence time, the absorption peak strength of RC-K and CC-K gradually decreased at 1060 cm−1, indicating that CC, CH and C-O-C structures in the raw materials were transformed in the process of K-catalyzed pyrolysis. According to the results of SEM (Fig. 1) and TG-DTG (Fig. 2) analyses, the content of CNTs in the sample gradually increased with the extension of residence time. When the temperature was lower than 900 °C, no CNTs were found to be generated in the RC-K pyrolysis products (Fig. S1). In addition, before the temperature rose to 900 °C, the carbon-containing gases such as CH4, C2H6 and C3H8 produced by coal pyrolysis were no longer generated (Fig. S2). Therefore, it can be speculated that the carbon source needed to generate CNTs during K-catalyzed coal pyrolysis mainly came from the skeleton carbon structure in coal, especially the aromatic CC, CH carbon structure and ether bond C-O-C carbon structure. Fig. 4 shows the XRD analysis results of the sample. As shown in Fig. 4a, c, with the increase in residence time, the 002 diffraction peak intensity of the pyrolysis products of RC and CC increased gradually, indicating that the order degree of amorphous carbon structure in the sample increased gradually. Some samples showed a CaO diffraction peak, which is the result of decomposition of calcite from RC [29]. In addition, the intensity of quartz diffraction peak (2θ = 26.4°) increased with the increase in residence time. This is because the weight loss degree of sample increased with the increase in residence time, and the relative content of residual quartz increased gradually. When KOH was added, it reacted with quartz, kaolinite and other minerals in the sample to form silicate, aluminosilicate and other substances [29], which were removed in the subsequent pickling process [38]. Thus, the quartz diffraction peak in the sample disappeared, as shown in Fig. 2d (CC-K-0). As can be seen from Fig. 4b, when the temperature reached 900 °C, a graphite (002) diffraction peak appeared for the sample RC-K-0 (2θ = 26.1°), which may be due to the formation of a small amount of CNTs in the sample. When the residence time was changed from 0 to 30 min, the intensity of the graphite (002) diffraction peak of RC-K-30 rapidly increased. When the residence time was increased further, the intensity of the graphite (002) diffraction peak of RC-K-90 slightly increased compared with RC-K-30. This may be because the effective convertible carbon structure in RC was rapidly converted into CNTs under etching action of K components within 0–30 min, so the CNTs content increased rapidly. When the effective carbon content was reduced, the CNTs growth process was slower and the growth rate of CNTs content decreased. As can be seen from Fig. 2d, when the temperature reached 900 °C, no graphite (002) diffraction peak appeared in CC-K-0. When the residence time was 30 min, a significant graphite (002) diffraction peak appeared in CC-K-30. When the residence time was extended further, the intensity of the graphite (002) diffraction peak of CC-K-90 slightly increased compared with CC-K-30. The reason may also be that the content of CNTs in CC-K increased rapidly first and then slowly. The above results are consistent with the results of SEM (Fig. 1) and TG-DTG (Fig. 2) analysis discussed earlier. In addition, all samples showed (101) diffraction peaks at (2θ = 44°), and the peak shape showed a broadening trend, indicating that some graphite microcrystalline carbon was present in the sample [39]. As can be seen from Fig. 2c, as the reaction time increased, the intensity of the (002) diffraction peak of CC gradually increased and no shift occurred. As can be seen from Fig. 2d, as the reaction time was prolonged, the (002) diffraction peak of CC-K gradually shifted to a lower angle, indicating that there was more disordered carbon in the sample [40,41]. The results showed that the addition of KOH can destroy the ordered carbon structure in CC, and increase the disordered carbon content in the pyrolysis products of CC-K. This result is consistent with the previous TG-DTG analysis results (Fig. 2g, h). In addition, the relative content of crystalline carbon structure and amorphous carbon structure in samples can be obtained by using the ratio of graphite (002) diffraction peak area/(amorphous carbon peak area + graphite (002) diffraction peak area) after Gaussian fitting. Then, combined with the actual content of CNTs (TG-DTG analysis results), the relationship between XRD peaks and the actual content of CNTs can be constructed. The specific process is described in the calculation method of CNTs content in the supplementary materials (Figs. S4, 5, Table S1). According to the Gaussian fitting results (Fig. S6, Table S2), the content of CNTs in the pyrolysis products of CC-K-30 and CC-K-90 were 1.07 wt% and 1.67 wt%, respectively, nearly equivalent to that of RC-K-0 (1.98 wt%). These results are consistent with the previous SEM analysis results (Fig. 1). Raman spectroscopy has a high sensitivity to carbon structure, which can effectively reflect the degree of graphitization of carbon materials and the degree of internal defects or disturbances [42]. As can be seen from the Raman spectra (Fig. 5 ), all samples exhibited two significant bands in the first-order Raman analysis. Among them, the D band at ~1350 cm−1 indicates the defect structure caused by the internal vacancies of the sample or other non‑carbon atom substitution [29], as well as the defect structure caused by the aromatic cluster of some amorphous structure [43]. This band corresponds to the disordered model A1g, reflecting the degree of disorder in the carbon structure [35,44]. The G-band at ~1590 cm−1 represents the plane stretching vibration frequency of sp2 hybrid C atom in the sample. This band represents the E2g stretching vibration mode of aromatic lamellae in the graphite crystal structure and reflects the ordered degree of graphite structure in the sample [29,45]. By comparing the intensities of G band and D band, the graphitization degree or crystal order degree of the sample can be obtained. The larger the ratio IG/ID is, the stronger the degree of graphitization is. On the contrary, the smaller IG/ID is, the higher the disorder degree of the sample is. Fig. 5a shows that IG/ID values of RC pyrolysis products RC-0, RC-30 and RC-90 were 1.13, 1.15 and 1.16, respectively, which are very similar, indicating that residence time had little influence on the graphitization degree of RC pyrolysis products. As can be seen from Fig. 5b, the IG/ID values of the RC-K pyrolysis products RC-K-0, RC-K-30 and RC-K-90 were 1.14, 2.48 and 3.90, respectively. IG/ID (RC-K-30) and IG/ID (RC-K-90) were 1.18 and 2.42 times of IG/ID (RC-K-0), respectively. This result indicates that the degree of graphitization of the sample increased significantly as the residence time increased. Unlike RC, KOH was added to RC-K. K-containing substances such as K2CO3 or K2O, which are transformed from KOH at high temperature, often lead to a decrease in the degree of graphitization of samples [28,33]. Therefore, the increase in graphitization degree of pyrolysis products of RC-K was probably due to the formation and increase in content of CNTs. It can be seen from Fig. 5a and c that when there are no CNTs in the sample, the G band wave number of the sample is about 1600 cm−1, which is larger than that of high oriented graphite (1582 cm−1). It can be seen from Fig. 5b that the G band positions of RC-K-0, RC-K-30 and RC-K-90 are 1578 cm−1, 1575 cm−1 and 1575 cm−1, respectively. Compared with the wave number of high oriented graphite G band 1582 cm−1, the corresponding wave number of the three samples all redshifts to the low wave number in varying degrees, which is the characteristic of multi walled carbon nanotubes (MCNTs) [46,47]. In Fig. 5d, the G band wave number of CC-K sample is basically the same as that of RC (Fig. 5a) and CC (Fig. 5c). That's because the content of CNTs in CC-K sample is low, and the Raman analysis results mainly show the carbon structure of the active carbon component in the sample. In addition, from Fig. 5b and d, it can be seen that there is a weak shoulder peak on the right side of the G band of the pyrolysis products of RC-K and CC-K, which makes the G band shape of the sample show a weak asymmetry, which is a typical G peak feature of MCNTs Raman analysis [48]. In addition, as mentioned above, Raman analysis technology has high sensitivity to the carbon structure of the sample matrix, but the sample has poor light transmittance, and there are many CNTs on the surface, which changes the roughness of the sample surface [49]. Therefore, the results of Raman analysis deviate from the actual sample structure information, but it reflects the transformation process of carbon structure and the formation process of CNTs in the process of K-catalyzed coal pyrolysis, which provides information for the analysis of the formation mechanism of CNTs. It can be seen from Fig. 5c that the IG/ID values of CC pyrolysis products CC-0, CC-30 and CC-90 were 1.14, 1.06 and 0.96, respectively, showing a downward trend. This indicates that the reheating treatment reduced the degree of CC graphitization, likely because the reheating treatment destroyed the original relatively ordered carbon structure in CC. As can be seen from Fig. 5d, the IG/ID values of CC-K pyrolysis products CC-K-0, CC-K-30 and CC-K-90 were 1.01, 1.05 and 1.11, respectively, showing an increasing trend. Comparing the results in Fig. 5b and d, it can be seen that the graphitization degree of the pyrolysis products of CC-K was much lower than that of RC-K with the increase in residence time. This indicates that the content of CNTs in the pyrolysis products of RC-K was much higher than that of CC-K at the same residence time. The above results are consistent with the results of SEM (Fig. 1), TG-DTG (Fig. 2) and XRD (Fig. 4) analyses. In order to further understand the relationship between different carbon structures in the sample and their content of CNTs, the Raman spectra of RC-K-0, RC-K-30 and RC-K-90 were deconvoluted with the Peakfit software according to the existing methods [50–52]. The Raman spectra were deconvoluted into 1 Gaussian band (D3 band) and 4 Lorentzian bands (G, D1, D2, and D4 bands). The results are shown in Fig. 6 . The G band (1575 cm−1) represents the tensile vibration mode (E2g symmetry) of the ideal graphite crystal structure [50,53]. The D1 band (1345 cm−1) represents the vibration mode of the disordered graphite lattice, and its planar defects are mainly caused by heteroatoms and other defects [50,54]. The D2 band (1620 cm−1) usually appears with D1 due to the vibration mode (E2g symmetry) of the graphene layer on the surface of the disordered graphite lattice [50,55]. The D3 band (1535 cm−1) of amorphous carbon attributed to the sp2 hybrid form includes organic molecules and functional group fragments [50]. The D4 band (1250 cm−1) is attributed to an organic functional group, particularly aromatic alkyl ether and an aromatic ether bond [35]. According to the results of deconvolution, the areas of G, D1, D2, D3 and D4 bands can be obtained. The ratio of D-band to G-band (ID1/IG, ID2/IG, ID3/IG and ID4/IG) and the ratio of G-band to total area (IG/IALL) can be used to quantitatively analyze the ratios and evolution of various carbon structures. As shown in Fig. 7a, ID3/IG, ID4/IG and(ID3 + ID4)/IG displayed a negative linear correlation with the content of CNTs in the sample (R2 > 0.97), indicating that the amorphous carbon (including organic molecules and functional group fragments) in sp2 hybrid form and ether bond carbon structure in the sample were strongly correlated to the generation of CNTs. From the FTIR analysis (Fig. 3), it was determined that the main functional groups participating in the reaction during the K-catalyzed coal/coke pyrolysis process were aromatic CC, CH and ether bond C-O-C structures. Therefore, changes in the ID3/IG, ID4/IG, and (ID3 + ID4)/IG ratios can indicate changes in the content of aromatic CC, CH, and ether bond C-O-C structures in the sample. The linear negative correlation indicates that the carbon structures represented by the D3 and D4 bands in the sample were the main carbon source required for CNTs growth. It can be seen from Fig. 7b that ID1/IG and ID2/IG gradually decreased with the increase in content of CNTs in the sample, but the linear relationship was poor (R2  0.99). Therefore, by comparing the variation patterns of G-band (Fig. 5) and CNTs content in the sample (Figs. 1 and 2), it can be inferred that the enhancement of the G band of RC-K was mainly due to the increase in content of CNTs in RC-K. Based on the results presented above, it was determined that the carbon source required for CNTs growth in the K-catalyzed pyrolysis process is the aromatic carbon structure (CC, CH) and ether bond carbon structure (C-O-C) in coal. Combined with the results of previous studies, the catalyst required for CNTs growth in this process is mainly the primary mineral Fe (pyrite) in coal [38]. The specific process is as follows. First, under high temperature conditions, the aromatic CH structure in coal undergoes a condensation reaction, as shown in Eq. (1) [56]. (1) 2 C − H → C − C + H 2 ↑ In the process of coal pyrolysis, the added KOH mainly exists in the form of K2CO3 (Fig. 8 ), which is consistent with the existing research results [38,57]. However, at high temperature, K2CO3 decomposes to produce K2O and CO2, as shown in Eq. (2) [58–60]. (2) K 2 CO 3 → K 2 O + CO 2 ↑ K2O produced from K2CO3 can be reduced to metal K by aromatic CC and H2, as shown in Eqs. (3) and (4) [61]. (3) 2 K 2 O + C − C → 4 K + 2 CO ↑ (4) K 2 O + H 2 → 2 K + H 2 O ↑ In addition, K2CO3 can be directly reduced to form metal K via the aromatic CC group in coal, as shown in Eq. (5) [59]. (5) K 2 CO 3 + C − C → 2 K + 3CO ↑ Previous studies showed that when graphite was used as raw material for pyrolysis instead of coal, after KOH catalytic pyrolysis, there was obvious etching on the surface of the sample, but no CNTs were generated [38]. This indicated that the aromatic CC in the sample could not be directly converted into CNTs. Therefore, the main role of aromatic CC structure consumed in the process of coal catalytic pyrolysis may be the reduction of K component, as shown in Eqs. (3) and (5). Then it can be inferred that the carbon source required for CNTs growth during K-catalyzed coal pyrolysis is the ether bond carbon structure C-O-C. Since metal K has strong reducibility, it can react with oxygen-containing functional groups in coal [62]. Therefore, the metal K generated by Eqs. (3)–(5) may react with C-O-C in coal, as shown in Eq. (6). (6) 2 K + C − O − C → K 2 O + 2C Metal K could react with C-O-C in coal through a one-step reaction, as shown in Eq. (6), or a two-step reaction, as shown in Eqs. (7) and (8). (7) K + C − O − C → C − O − K + C (8) K + C − O − K → K 2 O + C The carbon atoms produced by Eqs. (6)–(8) are the carbon sources needed for the growth of CNTs. These carbon atoms are converted into CNTs by the action of Fe catalyst in coal [38], as shown in Eq. (9). Fe catalyst is obtained from the reduction of primary mineral siderite in coal by solid carbon structure in coal and reductive substances such as CO, H2 produced by coal pyrolysis [38]. (9) C + C + C + … → Fe CNTs In the K-catalyzed coal pyrolysis product, the KOH added before the reaction mainly existed in the form of K2CO3 and K2CO3·1.5H2O (Fig. 8). K2CO3·1.5H2O may be generated through absorption of water by K2CO3 in the sample during sampling or XRD analysis. According to Xia et al. [28,33], K2O exists in the process of KOH catalytic coal pyrolysis under high temperature. Therefore, it can be assumed that K2O obtained by Eqs. (6)–(8) was converted into K2CO3 by the reaction represented by Eq. (10) after completion of the pyrolysis reaction [58]. (10) K 2 O + CO 2 → K 2 CO 3 In summary, the probable mechanism of CNTs formation in the process of K-catalyzed coal pyrolysis is: aromatic CC and CH in coal convert K components such as K2CO3 and K2O into metal K. Then, the metal K converts the carbon in ether bond structure C-O-C into single carbon atoms or carbon clusters. These carbon atoms or clusters are then transferred from the K component to the Fe catalyst particles. With the continuous integration, the carbon atoms or clusters inside the Fe catalyst particles gradually become supersaturated, and finally precipitate in the form of CNTs on the surface of the Fe catalyst particles. The specific process is shown in Fig. 9 . The Fe catalyst is derived from the primary siderite in coal, which is reduced by CO, H2 or carbon in the coal during pyrolysis. In addition, some carbon atoms or carbon clusters may be directly converted into CNTs under the action of the K components [63]. In this work, the formation mechanism of CNTs during K-catalyzed pyrolysis of low metamorphic bituminous coal was investigated. It was found that the addition of KOH was directly related to the formation of CNTs in the RC or CC pyrolysis process. The content of CNTs in K-catalyzed RC/CC pyrolysis products increased with the extension of residence time. When the residence times were 0, 30, and 90 min, the yields of CNTs in RC-K pyrolysis products RC-K-0, RC-K-30 and RC-K-90 were 1.98 wt%, 6.85 wt% and 10.16 wt%, respectively. When the residence times were 30 and 90 min, the content of CNTs in CC-K pyrolysis products CC-K-30 and CC-K-90 were 1.07 wt% and 1.67 wt%, respectively. The main carbon structures in RC, RC-K, CC and CC-K which were converted during the pyrolysis process were aromatic CC, CH structure and ether bond C-O-C structure. These carbon structures were the main carbon sources for the growth of CNTs during K-catalyzed pyrolysis of RC or CC. The formation of CNTs and the subsequent increase in their content were the main reasons for the appearance and enhancement of graphite (002) diffraction peak in the XRD patterns of pyrolysis products of RC-K and CC-K, and the enhancement of G-band in Raman spectra of pyrolysis products of RC-K and CC-K. The probable growth mechanism of CNTs in K-catalyzed coal pyrolysis process is: aromatic CC and CH in coal convert K components such as K2CO3 and K2O into metal K. Then, the metal K converts the carbon in the ether bond C-O-C structure into single carbon atoms or carbon cluster. Finally, these carbon atoms or carbon clusters form CNTs under the action of Fe catalyst particles in the coal. This study successfully demonstrates the conversion of inexpensive coal resources into high-value CNTs, providing a new idea for the utilization of coal resources as well as an efficient method for preparing CNTs at a macro-scale and low-cost. Tiankai Zhang:Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.Yongfa Zhang:Resources, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.Qi Wang:Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.Xuemei Lv:Data curation.Yunhuan Luo:Data curation. The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Number 51774211).
abstract: In this paper, the changes in solid-phase carbon structure and the formation of carbon nanotubes in the process of K-catalyzed pyrolysis of bituminous coal were investigated. The results showed that the content of carbon nanotubes in K-catalyzed coal pyrolysis products increased with the extension of residence time. When the residence times were 0, 30 and 90 min, the yields of carbon nanotubes in the coal pyrolysis products were 1.98 wt%, 6.85 wt% and 10.16 wt%, respectively. It was found that the aromatic carbon CC, CH and ether carbon C-O-C structures in coal were the main carbon sources for the growth of carbon nanotubes. The probable growth mechanism of carbon nanotubes in the process of K-catalyzed coal pyrolysis was proposed to be as follows: aromatic CC and CH structures in coal convert K components such as K2CO3 and K2O into metal K, and then metal K converts carbon atoms in the ether bond C-O-C structure into carbon atoms or clusters, which generate carbon nanotubes under the action of Fe catalyst particles in coal. This method provides a new strategy for the simultaneous high-value utilization of coal resources and macroscopic, low-cost preparation of carbon nanotubes.
title: Mechanism of K-catalyzed transformation of solid carbon structure into carbon nanotubes in coal

reaction_catalog.transformation: solid coal carbon structure → carbon nanotubes

steps.method_details: Dissolve KOH in a mixture of distilled water and ethanol

parameters.concentration: 15.0 g in 30.0 ml water and 10.0 ml ethanol

steps.method_details: Add coal sample to KOH solution and magnetically stir
Dry the sample at 60 °C
Further dry the sample at 105 °C

Add CC to KOH solution and magnetically stir

procedure_catalog.name_reported: Preparation of coal coke (CC) from raw coal (RC)

steps.method_details: Grind air-dried Shanxi bituminous coal to below 74 μm
Heat RC in a tube furnace under nitrogen atmosphere
Stop heating and maintain nitrogen until cooling to room temperature
Place sample in a horizontal tubular furnace
Exhaust air in the device with nitrogen
Heat the tubular furnace at a ramp rate to 900 °C and hold
Stop heating and cool to room temperature under nitrogen
steps.output_intermediate: cooled pyrolysis product (RC-X, RC-K-X, CC-X, or CC-K-X)

procedure_catalog.name_reported: Pickling and washing of pyrolysis residues

steps.method_details: Pickle pyrolysis residual samples with dilute hydrochloric acid
Wash with distilled water until neutral
Dry the washed sample at 105 °C
Cool in a dryer for 30 min
steps.output_intermediate: final sample (RC-K-X or CC-K-X after pickling)
