full_text: Lignin is the main component in waste black-liquor which is a by-product of the pulping process in the pulp and paper industry [1]. Basically, lignin is supplied as a low-grade fuel for generating heat and power through its combustion. Lignin has a natural aromatic structure consisting of three phenylpropane alcohol units (coniferyl, para-coumaryl, and sinapyl alcohol) cross-linked by various C-O-C (β-O-4, α-O-4, 4-O-5) and CC (β-1, β-β, 5-5) bonds [2,3]. Moreover, lignin has a high molecular weight, various reactive functional groups, and a high-energy content. Another outstanding feature from its unique properties is that lignin has a high potential to be converted through depolymerization into phenolic, aromatic, acid, alcohol, and low-molecular weight compounds as a replacement for fossil fuel resources. Even with all these outstanding qualities, it has been challenging to use lignin as a starting material for the bio-fuel and bulk chemicals production even though this line of processing has been of interest in the last few decades. It has been observed that <5% of lignin is commercially utilized to manufacture polyurethane foams, epoxy resin, biodispersants, binding material or high-value products [4]. The main approach in lignin depolymerization is to determine the method as well as an effective catalyst that will result in the selective scission of CO and CC bonds leading to depolymerization [5]. Various depolymerization technologies have been studied for producing value-added chemicals from lignin, such as gasification, pyrolysis [6,7], liquefaction, hydrolysis [8–10], oxidation [11–13], hydrodeoxygenation [14], and hydrogenolysis [15–17]. At present, hydrogenolysis appears to be the most promising process because it leads to higher lignin conversion, higher selectivity and yields of monomer products, and smaller char formation as compared to other thermochemical degradation processes [18,19]. Various supported noble metals such as Pt/C, Pd/C, Ru/C, Rh/C [15,20–22] and non-noble metal catalysts (e.g., Ni-based catalysts, a-MoC1−x/AC, W2C/AC, and CuMgAlOx) [23–25] have been evaluated in the catalytic hydrogenolysis of lignin. Moreover, the synergy of dual catalysts between S2O8 2−-KNO3/TiO2 (acid-based catalyst) and Ru/C (noble metal catalyst) can achieve high monomeric and dimeric product yield in lignin depolymerization [26]. Among these catalysts, Ni-based materials e.g. Raney Ni [27], Ni/SiO2 [28], Ni/HZSM-5, Ni/C [29], Ni/H-beta [30], have been the preferred catalysts for lignin conversion into low-molecular weight products. This is because Ni atoms show a high catalytic activity, and are especially highly selective in cleaving the CO bonds of α-O-4 and β-O-4 linkages which are the most abundant bonds in the lignin structure as well as promote the hydrogenolysis and hydrolysis of the aromatic ether bonds in parallel reactions [31]. So, Ni-based catalysts have demonstrated that they can be used to promote high lignin conversion to produced more lignin-derived products. Many studies on the development of efficient Ni-based catalysts have concentrated on the activity of the Ni metal phase and their stability (including coke formation). It is also known that catalyst activity and stability depend on the nature of the selected support [32,33]. The drawback of some of the support materials (e.g. SiO2 and Al2O3) when operated in strong basic-acid medium is their chemical instability property. In the case of carbon materials, the drawback is that it cannot be isolated from the solid residue obtained from lignin depolymerization. Among the widely used supports, ceria-zirconia (CeO2-ZrO2) demonstrates the advantage of possessing high oxygen vacancies, reversible oxygen storage capacity, coke prevention, and good chemical and thermal stability. Recently, CeO2-ZrO2 has been considered for application as a support for catalysts for lignocellulosic biomass conversion, such as the conversion of cellulose [34,35], lignin-model compounds [36], and lignin itself [37]. Hence, if the potential synergy between advantages derived from Ni metal and CeO2-ZrO2 support can be exploited, then this can be used to enhance the lignin conversion to obtain higher yields of lignin-depolymerized products. Furthermore, the solvent system has an effect on lignin depolymerization. The literature has reported that alcohols such as methanol, ethanol and 2-propanol are widely used as a solvent or co-solvent for the depolymerization of lignin due to their ability to provide increased lignin solubility as well as acting as a hydrogen donor solvent which helps to promote the hydrogenolysis reaction [38–42]. The present work aims to investigate the catalyst performance of a synthesized catalyst that uses Ni metals supported on CrO2-ZrO2. It will evaluate the method of synthesis of the catalyst on the depolymerization of lignin into value-added chemicals. Specifically, this work intends to introduce a one-step activation by reduction of the Ni species supported on CrO2-ZrO2 whereby Ni(NO3)2 is reduced to Ni as it is impregnated on CrO2-ZrO2 support as opposed to Ni(NO3)2 impregnation on calcined CrO2-ZrO2 support, drying, calcination and eventual reduction of the calcined supported catalyst. The catalysts preparation and characterization will be evaluated to establish possible relationships between the physical-chemical properties of catalysts, the method of synthesis, and their catalytic activity in the depolymerization reaction. The effects of depolymerization conditions such as reaction temperature, reaction time, and solvent system are also studied, and then used to further improve the lignin depolymerization efficiency in terms of lignin conversion, product yield, and product distribution. In addition, the difference in the properties between the original lignin and residual lignin after depolymerized are characterized and used to explain the lignin structure changes during the reactions. The commercial Alkaline lignin (CAS NO: 370959) was used as the starting material for the lignin depolymerization studies. Ethyl acetate (EtOAc, anhydrous, 99.8%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%), methanol (HPLC grade), and tetrahydrofuran (THF, ≥99.9%, anhydrous, inhibitor-free) were used in the reaction and product separation processes. For catalyst preparation, the metal precursors were: cerium (III) nitrate hexahydrate (CeN3O9·6H2O, 99%), zirconium (IV) oxynitrate hydrate (N2O7Zr·xH2O, 99%), nickel (II) nitrate hexahydrate (N2NiO6·6H2O, 99.999%), and sodium borohydride powder (NaBH4). These were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Canada). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and potassium hydroxide (KOH) were purchased from EMD chemical Inc. and Fisher Scientific, respectively. Standard phenolic compounds: phenol, guaiacol (2-methoxy phenol), 4-methyl guaiacol (4-methyl-2-methoxy phenol), 4-ethyl guaiacol (4-ethyl-2-methoxy phenol), vanillin (4-hydroxy-3-methoxybenzaldehyde), acetovanillone (1-(4-hydroxy-3-methoxyphenyl)-ethanone), homovanillic acid (4-hydroxy-3-methoxy benzeneacetic acid), and syringol (2,6-dimethoxy phenol) were used as analytical standards. All standard chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Canada) and Wako Pure Chemical Industries Ltd. (Japan). Ultra-high-purity (UHP) helium (99.999%) for purging the reactor and gas chromatography analysis was purchased from Praxair Canada Inc. The CeO2-ZrO2 support was synthesized by the co-precipitation method. The composition of CeO2-ZrO2 system was 80 and 20 equivalent mol% of Ce and Zr, respectively. For the preparation step, the required amount of CeN3O9·6H2O and N2O7Zr·xH2O were dissolved in deionized water with a total concentration at 0.25 M. A 20 wt% KOH solution was added drop-wise to the solution at 80 °C with constant stirring to reach a pH around 11–12. The precipitates were digested and aged at 80 °C for 72 h. Then, the precipitates were filtered and subsequently washed with distilled water several times to remove KOH before air-drying at 100 °C overnight. The obtained sample was calcined at 400 °C for 6 h. The Ni doped on CeO2-ZrO2 catalyst was prepared by a chemical reduction method using NaBH4 as the reducing agent. This catalyst is designated as Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red. The loading amount of Ni was varied in the range of 1–10 wt%. In this method of catalyst preparation, a pre-determined amount of N2NiO6·6H2O was dissolved in deionized water. After that, CeO2-ZrO2 support was added into metal precursor solution and stirred at high-speed for 2 h at room temperature. Sonication for 1 h was used to assist and enhance the substrate dispersion. Then, NaBH4 solution (excess 10 times the mole of metal precursor) was dropped into previous solution and continuously stirred for 2 h to complete the reduction process. The obtained catalyst was filtered, washed with deionized water until there was no change in the pH, subsequently dried in an oven overnight. Meanwhile, the Ni doped on CeO2-ZrO2 catalyst (referred as Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-ipm) was prepared by a wet-impregnation method and used to study the catalyst performance in the second preparation method. The loading amount of Ni was fixed at 10 wt%. A pre-determined amount N2NiO6·6H2O was dissolved in deionized water and stirred at room temperature for 30 min. The CeO2-ZrO2 support was added in the solution and subsequently heated and dried by evaporation at 80 °C for 4 h. The obtained catalysts was dried in an oven overnight and calcined at 500 °C for 2 h. The third preparation method was the subsequent reduction of Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-ipm in 5%H2 (balance N2) at 500 °C for 2 h. This was designated as Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-ipm-red. The powdered X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique was carried out to study the phase transformation and crystalline structure of the synthesized catalysts using a D8 Discover diffractometer (Bruker AXS, USA) which employed a general area detector diffraction system (GADDS) with Ni filtered Cu Kα radiation at a wavelength of 1.54006 Å. The scanning angle of 2θ was measured from 10 to 80° with a scanning speed of 0.05° s−1. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area, pore volume, and pore size including adsoption-desoption isotherms of prepared catalysts were determined by N2-physisorption measurements performed at liquid nitrogen temperature 77 K (−196 °C) using a BELSORP-mini II (BEL Inc., Japan). All samples were degassed at 150 °C under a N2 atmospheric prior to the start of the adsorption process. Meanwhile, the reduction behaviors of the CeO2–ZrO2 supports and the Ni-based catalysts were determined by H2-temperature programmed reduction (TPR) to verify the interaction between metal and support. A Quantachrome ChemBET-3000 plusar TPR/TPD instrument was used to analyze in this experiment. About 50 mg of sample was placed in U-quartz tube and preheated at 150 °C for 2 h under He flow. For the analysis, the temperature was ramped from 25 to 900 °C at a rate of 10 °C m−1 in 5 v/v % H2 balanced in N2 mixture (Praxair, Canada) with flow rate 30 mL min−1 (STP). The gas leaving the reactor was analyzed using a thermal conductivity detector (TCD). A TPR profile was plotted between the reducibility temperature and the amount of H2 consumed or TCD signal. The depolymerization of lignin was performed in a 600 mL high-pressure reactor (Parr 5500 series, SS 316) equipped with a temperature and motor speed controller (Parr 4848). Typically, the system contained 2 g of alkaline lignin, 1 g of catalyst, 25 mmol of NaOH to ensure lignin solubility, and 200 mL of a water-methanol mixture. The reactor was closed and purged with high-purity helium gas several times to check for leaks and to remove any air inside the reactor. The reactor was heated to the desired temperature and kept for the desired reaction time. At the end of reaction, the reaction was stopped and cooled down to room temperature by cooling water circulation. The procedure for separation of products after depolymerization is shown in Fig. 1 . Firstly, the gaseous product was collected in a 0.5 L gas-sampling bag. The rest of the reaction product mixture was filtered to separate liquid fraction from the solid fraction. The solid fraction, which included catalyst and char, was washed several times with deionized water and methanol, and subsequently dried at 100 °C until a constant weight was obtained. Unconverted lignin in filtrate was obtained by acidified precipitation with HCl until pH <2. Recovered lignin was filtered, washed with deionized water until the Cl− was completely removed, and dried at 100 °C until a constant weight was obtained. The phenolic products in the liquid phase was extracted using liquid-liquid extraction with 3 times the volume of EtOAc with vigorous shaking. After the extraction was complete, the EtOAc phase was collected and evaporated using a rotary evaporator to recover the phenolic products in terms of lignin oil. The gas chromatography-mass spectrometer (GC–MS) analysis of the phenolic products was conducted on an Agilent 7890 GC with an Agilent 5975 inert mass-selective detector (Agilent Technologies Inc., USA) equipped with a HP-INNOWAX capillary column (Polyethylene glycol (PEG), 30 m × 250 μm × 0.25 μm, Agilent Technologies Inc., USA) and identification was according to the NIST library. The temperature program was as follows: oven temperature started at 60 °C and held for 2 min followed by heating to 250 °C at a rate of 10 °C min−1 and then held for 10 min. The injector temperature was set 280 °C with split mode (5:1), and MS detector source was set at 230. The quantitative analysis of these chemicals was determined by internal standard calibration method using syringol as an internal standard compound. The conversion of lignin, the yield of lignin oil, residual lignin (unconverted lignin as well as high-molecular weight product), and char were calculated according to Eqs. (1)–(4), respectively. Additionally, the yields of the main selected phenolic monomer products; phenol, guaiacol, 4-methyl guaiacol, 4-ethyl guaiacol, vanillin, acetovanillone, and homovanillic acid were evaluated according to the following Eq. (5). (1) Lignin conversion wt . % = weight of initial lignin g ‐ weight of residual lignin g weight of initial lignin g × 100 % (2) Lignin oil wt . % = weight of lignin oil g weight of initial lignin g × 100 % (3) Residual lignin wt . % = weight of residual lignin g weight of initial lignin g × 100 % (4) Char wt . % = weight of solid residue g ‐ weight of catalyst g weight of initial lignin g × 100 % (5) Phenolic monomer yield of i wt . % = weight of phenolic monomer i g weight of initial lignin g × 100 % The molecular weights of lignin samples before and after depolymerization were determined by the gel permeation chromatography (GPC) technique. The GPC analysis was performed using a Waters e2695 separation module (Waters cooperation, USA) equipped with PLgel 10 μm mixed B two columns (MW resolving range 500–10,000,000) and a refractive index detector (RID) model 3580 (Viscotek). Analysis was carried out at 35 °C using THF as the eluent at a flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1. The average molecular weight was calibrated according to the external standard method using polystyrene compounds as a standard (Mw = 1220–1,214,000 g mol−1). The Proton nuclear magnetic resonance with respect to hydrogen-1 nuclei (1H NMR) was used to determine the structure changes of lignin after depolymerization as compared with the initial alkaline lignin. The lignin samples were dissolved in deuterated dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO‑d 6), and then introduced into a 1H NMR spectroscopy analysis using a Bruker Advance III HD 400 MHz spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was also used to interpret functional groups of lignin structure before and after depolymerization. Infrared spectra of lignin were obtained using an Equinox 55 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker Ltd., USA) using potassium bromide (KBr) pellet method. The measurement resolution was set at 4 cm−1 with 64 co-added scans in the range of infrared spectrum 4000 to 400 cm−1 with mercury cadmium telluride (MCT) detector. Elemental analyses of initial lignin and residual lignin were performed on a CHN elemental analyzer (JM10, JS-science Lab, Japan) at combustion temperature of 850 °C under helium atmosphere. Carbon (C), hydrogen (H), sulfur (S) contents were quantitatively determined by elemental analysis on dry ash free basis and the oxygen (O) content was calculated by difference. The XRD patterns of CeO2-ZrO2 support and Ni-supported CeO2-ZrO2 catalysts are shown in Fig. 2 . The XRD pattern of CeO2-ZrO2 support show peaks corresponding to 28.8°, 33.4°, 47.9°, 56.9°, 59.6°, 70.1°, 77.5°, and 79.9° which are assigned to a typical cubic fluorite structure of Ce(0.80)Zr(0.20)O2 (ref. PDF 01–074-8064). No separate phase was found for reaction between CeO2 and ZrO2 in the synthesized CeO2-ZrO2 support, which can be ascribed to the formation of smaller zirconium ions in the cubic lattice of CeO2 resulting in a homogenous phase of the CeO2-ZrO2 structure [43]. The XRD pattern of Ni-supported CeO2-ZrO2 catalysts prepared by chemical reduction (Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red) method with different Ni loadings showed the same peak positions corresponding to the cubic fluorite structure of the CeO2–ZrO2 support [44]. The peak of nickel oxide (NiO) and Ni0 phases were not observed by XRD for Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red catalysts. This can indicate that the Ni particles prepared by chemical reduction method are finely dispersed on the surface of CeO2–ZrO2 support or there was the formation of an alloy between Ni and CeZr metals. However, the strong higher peak intensity of CeO2–ZrO2 shows an increase in the crystallite size of CeO2-ZrO2 with increasing Ni loading. The addition of Ni into the structure of CeO2–ZrO2 can increase the interstitial point defect and could form Ni-Ce-Zr-O on the surface [45]. On the other hand, the XRD pattern of Ni-supported CeO2-ZrO2 catalysts prepared by simple wet-impregnation method (Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp) had three new peaks corresponding to 37.3°, 43.3°, and 62.9°, which are characteristic peaks of bulk crystallites of NiO (ref. PDF 01-089-5881). The calcination step in simple wet-impregnation method affected the crystallite size of NiO which becomes larger due to sintering of NiO particle. After the activation of Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp catalyst by reducing in a H2 atmosphere at 500 °C for 2 h, which was defined as Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp-red catalyst, it was found to exhibit new peaks at 44.5° and 51.8° which correspond to the Ni peak. So, it can be concluded that the methods of catalyst preparation: chemical reduction and simple wet-impregnation methods have an influence on the phase transformation of Ni metal and metal dispersion. The surface area, pore volume and pore size of all synthesized CeO2-ZrO2 support and Ni-doped CeO2-ZrO2 catalysts are summarized in Table 1 . The specific surface area results of bare CeO2-ZrO2 support showed the value of 89.99 m2 g−1 with pore volume of 12.25 cm3 g−1. For the Ni-doped CeO2-ZrO2 catalysts prepared by chemical reduction method (Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red), it can be observed that the addition of low amount of Ni metal (1 and 4 wt%) into CeO2-ZrO2 support led to a decrease of the specific surface area of the support. This result is due to Ni particles forming and blocking the inside of the support pores resulting in the decreasing of the pore volume of the catalyst [44]. However, the increase of Ni loading up to 10 wt% results in an increase of surface area of the support. This could be due to the formation of small particles of Ni species which are well dispersed on the catalyst surface as well as the formation of its own porous structure. A comparison of all the synthesized Ni/CeO2-ZrO2 catalysts shows that the 10% Ni/CeO2-ZrO2 catalyst prepared by simple wet-impregnation method (identified as 10% Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp) has the lowest surface area (59.41 m2 g−1) and smallest pore volume (0.20 cm3 g−1). This could be due to sintering and agglomeration of NiO species which cluster on the surface area and inside the pore of the catalyst during calcination. This was earlier confirmed from the result in the XRD pattern for NiO peaks (Fig. 2). The TPR patterns of CeO2-ZrO2 support and the Ni/CeO2-ZrO2 catalysts are displayed in Fig. 3 . As shown in Fig. 3(a), the reduction peaks of the CeO2-ZrO2 supports are revealed in two temperature regions at 380–660 °C and 770–830 °C. The first peak at a lower temperature around 579 °C can be ascribed to the surface reduction of CeO2 because unsaturated oxygen ion at the surface can be easily removed at a low temperature. The higher temperature peak is attributed to the reduction of bulk CeO2. It was found that the synthesized mixed oxide CeO2-ZrO2 support showed a higher reduction temperature than the reduction characteristic of pure CeO2. Basically, the reduction characteristic of pure CeO2 was found in two reduction peaks at around 440 and 725 °C [46]. ZrO2 is an oxide material that is difficult to reduce under hydrogen environment [47]. So, the addition of ZrO2 into CeO2 can affect the reduction properties of CeO2 resulting in the reduction observed at low-temperatures being shifted to higher temperatures with the increase of Zr content [46,48]. The Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red catalysts show new lower temperature reduction peaks, first at around 387 °C which is mainly attributed to the reduction of bulk Ni species, and secondly at around 473 °C indicating Ni particle interacting with the surface of the CeO2-ZrO2 supports as shown in Fig. 3(b). The observed reduction peak at 789 °C seems to be the bulk reduction of CeO2-ZrO2 support. However, the Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp catalyst has only the sharp reduction peak at 450 °C (see Fig. 3(c)) that may be representing the strong interaction between NiO crystallites and CeO2-ZrO2 support which may be brought about as a result of calcination during catalyst preparation. After the Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp catalyst was reduced in hydrogen atmosphere, it was found that its reduction peak (i.e. Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp-red catalyst) appeared at a lower temperature (349 °C) than the Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp catalyst as presented in Fig. 3(d). The results show that not all the NiO species in Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp-red catalyst were actually reduced to the active Ni species after reduction. This can explain the lower activity of 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp-red catalyst compared with 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red in which TPR results show almost complete reduction to active Ni species. Interestingly, the TPR results for the 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp-red catalyst shows that reduction in a hydrogen atmosphere made the catalyst more reducible in which the TPR peak appeared at a lower temperature. This result corroborates the XRD results in which NiO peaks were only observed in the 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp and 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp-red catalysts' surface, and that NiO species were reduced to Ni metal according to the appearance of the Ni0 phase in their XRD patterns [49]. Lignin depolymerization was performed at 250 °C for 3 h in 50:50 v/v of H2O-methanol mixture over different synthesized catalysts first for screening the performance of the catalysts. The CeO2-ZrO2-based catalysts were evaluated in terms of lignin conversion, as well as yields of lignin oil, char, and residual lignin. These are shown in Fig. 4 . The depolymerization in the absence of catalyst (no catalyst) was set as the reference condition and this resulted in a low lignin conversion and lignin oil yield of 37.89% and 30.45%, respectively. Using only CeO2-ZrO2 support, the lignin conversion increased to 43.78% while the lignin oil yield increased slightly to 34.80%, indicating that CeO2-ZrO2 played a role in enhancing the depolymerization of lignin. The higher lignin conversion and increased lignin oil could be attributed to the ability of Ce to selectivity cleave the CC bonds [50] in lignin. Moreover, previous works have used Ce catalyst to depolymerize kraft lignin to produce bio-oil and this process obtained high yield of bio-oil [51,52]. It is obvious that Ce is a promising catalyst with sufficient efficiency and effectiveness to disrupt the chemical bonds in the lignin structure. The modified catalyst using a noble metal, especially Ni metal, which is a significant hydrogenation catalyst, exhibited better performance in lignin depolymerization as compared with cases of no catalyst and CeO2-ZrO2 support. Ni species efficiently cleave the various CO bonds in lignin structure resulting in higher monomeric yield production. In this work, the catalyst modification by doping with Ni using three different techniques were used to study their catalytic performance in the depolymerization of lignin. The three catalyst preparation techniques were: (1) chemical reduction (−red), (2) simple wetness-impregnation (−imp), and (3) wetness-impregnation followed by reduction under H2 atmosphere (−imp-red). They were used to investigate the effect of Ni active phase on lignin depolymerization. It is clearly seen that the catalytic activities of the Ni-based catalyst prepared by different techniques had some significant effect on lignin conversion. However, the most significant effect was observed for the properties of each catalysts on the lignin oil characteristics and yields. The yield of lignin oil was increased when Ni was loaded on CeO2-ZrO2 support for all the different catalyst synthesis methods. Among the synthesized Ni/CeO2-ZrO2 catalysts, it was observed that the highest amount of lignin oil (46.80%) was obtained from the catalytic depolymerization of lignin using 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red catalyst. This is as a result of the unique catalytic properties of 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red catalyst, including highest catalyst surface area and high-dispersion of Ni spices on catalyst surface. For 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp catalyst, it was found that the addition of Ni using the simple wet-impregnation method did not significantly enhance the deoplymerization activity because the Ni spices formed is NiO which is not the active phase of Ni. So, the reduction step under H2 atmosphere was used to activate NiO to the active phase of Ni0 vacancies. After reducing the 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp catalyst in 5% v/v of H2/N2 at 500 °C for 2 h, the catalyst is identified as 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp-red catalyst. It can be observed that the 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp-red catalyst shows a better catalytic performance and provided a higher lignin conversion and yield of lignin oil than the unreduced 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp catalyst. Thus, in addition to surface area and Ni dispersion, the phase transformation of Ni species has an effect on the catalytic activities in the depolymerization of lignin. The Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red catalysts with varying Ni loadings (1, 4, 7, and 10 wt% gNi g−1 support) synthesized by chemical reduction method were tested to determine the effect of Ni content on catalytic depolymerization of lignin. The lignin depolymerization was performed in a system of 50:50 v/v water-methanol mixture at a reaction temperature at 250 °C for 3 h using 1 g of catalyst. Fig. 5 shows the effect of Ni loading on lignin conversion and yield of lignin-derived products (oil, residual lignin, and char). It can be seen that increasing the Ni loading enhanced the catalyst activity in lignin depolymerization resulting in increased lignin conversion and yields of lignin oil with higher Ni content doped on CeO2-ZrO2 support. The 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red catalyst showed the highest catalytic activity which reached the maximum lignin conversion of 50.32% and highest lignin oil yield of 46.80%. This suggests that increased Ni loading can affect the chemistry of the active site on the catalyst, which is directly reflected on CO and CC bonds in lignin structure. The higher Ni active sites can greatly accelerate the cleavage of CO bonds by hydrogenation in the presence of hydrogen. Furthermore, the physical properties of 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red catalyst show the highest surface area when compared with other Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red catalysts. The effect of reaction temperature on lignin depolymerization was investigated in a system of 50:50 v/v water-methanol mixture using 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red as a catalyst with a constant reaction time of 3 h. The results obtained are shown in Fig. 6(a). The results show that temperature had a significant effect on lignin conversion and products distribution. As can be seen in the results, lignin conversion continuously increased from 37.64 to 65.87 wt% with an increase in reaction temperature from 200 to 300 °C. These results indicate that lignin degradation is an endothermic reaction. Considering the results in terms of product yield (oil, char, and residual lignin), it is observed that the oil yield increased from 32.85 to 46.80% for a reaction temperature increase from 200 to 250 °C. After that, lignin oil slightly decreased when the reaction temperature reached 300 °C. Simultaneously, lignin intermediates and lignin-degraded products, which are prone to further decomposition, condensation and/or repolymerization at high temperatures, give rise to a higher yield of char products (19.80%) and a low yield of liquid product as temperature increased up to 300 °C. According to previous studies, gas and char formation are promoted at high temperature due to the occurrence of further reaction of lignin-decomposed products [53–55]. Fig. 6(b) shows the effect of reaction time on lignin depolymerization. The depolymerization was carried out using 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red as the catalyst with various reaction times (e.g. 1, 3, and 5 h) at constant reaction temperature (250 °C). It was observed that the increase of reaction time did not significantly affect the lignin conversion. It however reveals that at a reaction time longer than 3 h, the reactions taking place only affected the oil yield and coke production. The yield of oil increased slightly with increasing reaction time from 1 to 3 h and then gradually decreased when operated at a prolonged reaction time. As the oil yield decreased, the char yield increased at the longer reaction times because of further reactions including cracking, condensation, and repolymerization of depolymerized products simultaneously occurring during lignin degradation [56,57]. The kinetic modeling of lignin depolymerization is still difficult to investigate because lignin molecule can be converted into various products. The kinetics in this work was determined by using a simplified model based on lignin conversion. The equation of the rate of reaction for lignin depolymerization was assumed to be first order as given in Eq. (6). The rate of reaction of lignin depolymerization related with lignin conversion is given as (5) d 1 − X dt = k 1 − X (6) d 1 − X = k 1 − X dt where X is lignin conversion obtained from experiments. The rate constant can be evaluated based the Arrhenius equation as shown in Eq. (8). (8) k = k 0 exp − E a RT Eq. (7) can be rewritten into Eq. (11) to determine the pre-exponential factor (k0) and the activation energy (Ea). (9) − dX = k 0 exp − E a RT 1 − X dt (10) − dX 1 − X = k 0 exp − E a RT dt (11) − 1 1 − X dX = k 0 exp − E a RT dt Integrating both sides of Eq. (10), (11) ∫ − 1 1 − X dX = ∫ k 0 exp − E a RT dt Then, by substituting dt = 3 and X = the lignin conversion at the reaction time of 3 h into Eq. (11), we obtain: (12) ln 1 − X = exp − E a RT 3 k 0 Taking the natural logarithm (ln) of both sides of Eq. (13), we obtain (13) ln ln 1 − X = ln 3 k 0 − E a RT According to Eq. (14), the estimated kinetic parameters for lignin depolymerization at all reaction temperatures are summarized in Table 2 . The plot of ln(ln(1 − X)) against 1/T, as shown in Fig. 7 , was used to determine the activation energy (Ea) and pre-exponential factor (k0) from the slope of curve and y-intercept, respectively. These yielded a pre-exponential factor of 11.75 h−1 and an activation energy of 17.1 kJ mol−1. It is well known that the activation energy of lignin depolymerization varies with the lignin source including the methods of isolating lignin from biomass. TOF in this study was defined as the converted lignin per Ni active site per time as shown in Eq. (15): (15) TOF h − 1 = mole of converted lignin mole of Ni active site × time h where Mw of lignin = 2092 g mol−1, and mole of Ni active sites was determined from mole of Ni loading on the catalyst. The mole of Ni active site was calculated as the number of moles available based on the mass of Ni used in each catalyst produced Ni atoms which were all exposed to the surface for reaction even though a larger mass and the catalyst preparation method will lead to lower exposure. This assumption is justified because, as shown in the XRD results in Fig. 2, we did not observe any Ni peak in the XRD pattern except for the one for 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp-red catalyst. This could mean very high dispersion of Ni or the Ni is in an amorphous phase on the non-impregnated catalysts. Either way, since this is a comparative TOF, it implies that the trend in TOF in terms of Ni loading will not change. Also, the value of TOF for 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp-red catalyst will be lower than that for 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red. The TOF value vs. Ni loading is presented in Fig. 8 . It can be seen in the figure that TOF decreases with an increase in Ni loading implying that not all the Ni loaded are effective for lignin depolymerization because the Ni dispersion and exposure for surface reaction decrease as the Ni loading increases. The quantitative yields of the main phenolic products in lignin oil obtained from depolymerization process under various prepared catalysts are shown in Fig. 9 . It was found that the addition of catalyst in the system had an effect on the total phenolic product yield. The Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red gave the highest yield of phenolic product (10.14%) compared to the absence of catalyst (6.60%). In the case of CeO2-ZrO2, the yield of phenolic products slightly increased to 7.10% especially, for proportions of vanillin and homovanillic acid. This result could be as a result from the oxygen reversible properties of CeO2-ZrO2 which leads to the promotion of the oxidation reaction resulting in higher concentration of oxygenated compounds. Furthermore, the active phase of Ni in synthesized catalyst had an influence on lignin depolymerization reaction resulting in the 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp-red catalyst catalyzing a higher phenolic product yield than the unreduced 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-imp catalyst. Among all catalysts, it can demonstrate that the 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red catalyst prepared by chemical reduction method had the best performance in this depolymerization process. Moreover, the yields of the main products in lignin oil obtained from depolymerization process with different reaction temperatures and reaction time are illustrated in Figs. 10 and 11 , respectively. At the low reaction temperature (200 °C), guaiacol and homovanillic acid were the main products in lignin oil. It was observed that guaiacol was dramatically decreased relative to the methyl-guaiacol as the reaction temperature increased. This can be explained on the basis that methyl-guaiacol were methylated from guaiacol due to the presence of methanol in the system. However, at the highest reaction temperature of 300 °C, the concentrations of all phenolic compounds were greatly decreased due to the fact that these phenolic products were easily decomposed and continually reacted further by secondary reactions such as hydrodeoxygenation, hydrolysis, repolymerization to unidentified products, or form dimers and larger species which could not be identified through GC–MS analysis. The results of the yield of the main phenolic compositions in lignin oil obtained from the depolymerization of lignin over 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red catalyst in 50:50 v/v of H2O-methanol mixture at 250 °C with varied reaction times are presented in Fig. 11. The figure shows that the yield of all phenolic products decreased with reaction time excepting 4-methyl guaiacol which increased when the reaction was operated for a prolonged time. This indicates that guaiacol was methylated with methanol into 4-methyl guaiacol. These reaction pathways can be confirmed with the greatly decreased concentration of guaiacol and higher yield of methyl-guaiacol in the products composition. Table 3 show a list of phenolic products and the percentage of peak area derived from GC–MS analysis of lignin oil obtained from the depolymerization of alkaline lignin over 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red catalyst with different experiment conditions. The distribution of the different phenolic products was highly dependent on the depolymerization conditions. It was observed that the phenolic product distribution was more diverse when performed at a higher reaction temperature. Among all experiments, guaiacol (2-methoxy- phenol in Table 2) was the primary and intermediate product, and the concentration of guaiacol increased with increasing reaction temperature and time. Moreover, the yields of methyl-substituted compounds such as 2,4-dimethyl-phenol, 2,4,6-trimethyl-phenol, and 2,3,5,6-tetramethyl-phenol increased with reaction temperature due to the enhancement of methylation reaction between intermediate compounds and methanol. For the effect of reaction time on the phenolic product distribution, the same group of products are present but have a difference in product yields. It can be concluded that the intermediate phenolic products can undergo further reaction or degradation to other compounds or to gas products. The changes in molecular weight between original alkaline lignin and residual lignin (obtained after reaction) were measured in order to investigate the relationship between depolymerization and repolymerization reactions. The results of weight-average molecular weight (Mw), number-average molecular weight (Mn) and polydispersity index (PDI) of the original lignin and residual lignin after depolymerization at different reaction conditions are listed in Table 4 . As observed, the molecular weight of all residual lignin samples from reactions performed at different reaction temperatures (entry 2–4) and retention times (entry 3, 5–6) showed significant changes relative to the original alkaline lignin (2092 g mol−1). It was found that the molecular weight of residual lignin samples degraded with reaction temperature. After the depolymerization of the original lignin at 200, 250 and 300 °C, the molecular weight of residual lignin decreased to 1926, 1640, and 1238 g mol−1, respectively. This can be explained on the basis that the effect of reaction temperature on catalytic depolymerization of lignin is to enhance the bond cleavage in the lignin structure resulting in decrease of the molecular weight. In particular, the depolymerization reaction is an endothermic reaction and the activation energy of depolymerization reaction is smaller than repolymerization reaction. As reported by Wang et al., who have been studied the kinetics of lignin depolymerization, it is concluded that the reaction can be divided into 2 stages; the first stage is depolymerization and the second stage is repolymerization of lignin-derived fragments [55]. It was found that the Ea of depolymerization stage is 33.67 kJ mol−1 which is smaller than the Ea of repolymerization stage (43.87 kJ mol−1). That is why increasing the reaction temperature promotes the depolymerization reaction resulting in greater lignin conversion and lignin-derived products, especially, lignin oil. In addition, the effect of reaction time of lignin depolymerization on molecular weight was also studied. In contrast to temperature effects, the molecular weight of residual lignin was continuously increased with increasing retention time of depolymerization from 1 to 5 h (1,479 < 1640 < 1797 g mol−1). These results agreed well with the previous studies of Huang et al. [58] and Brandt et al. [59]. It can be concluded that lignin was depolymerized into low-molecular weight fragment in the early state of reaction resulting in the initial decrease of the molecular weight of lignin. After that, the molecular weight of lignin continuously increased with prolonged time due to repolymerization and condensation reaction of unstable lignin fragments and original lignin taking place which made the residual lignin to have a higher molecular weight. Relatively, lignin oil decreased, while char formation increased with longer retention times of the depolymerization process. Therefore, this shows that a longer reaction time enhances repolymerization and condensation reactions. 1H NMR spectrometer and FT-IR techniques were used to investigate the structure changes of lignin during depolymerization reaction. Fig. 12 shows the 1H NMR spectra of original alkaline lignin and residual lignin after depolymerization over 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red catalyst at different reaction conditions. According to the reported literatures for identifying lignin structure [60,61], the chemical signals between 8.0 and 6.0 ppm are ascribed to the aromatic protons in guaiacyl and syringyl units. The appearance of signal at 3.55 to 3.95 ppm illustrate the chemical shift of methoxyl protons (-OCH3). The chemical shift of the signal between 2.0 and 0.8 ppm are ascribed to the aliphatic protons on the hydrocarbons of lignin. Fig. 12(A) shows the spectra of the lignin after depolymerization at different reaction temperatures (200, 250, and 300 °C) for a constant reaction time of 3 h. It was observed clearly that the aromatic proton and methoxy group decreased when the reaction was performed at higher temperatures and totally disappeared in the depolymerized lignin at 300 °C for 3 h. This result is attributed to the demethoxylation of the alkaline lignin occurring during the depolymerization step which is enhanced at a high temperature. Moreover, the increase of the intensity in the region of aliphatic proton with increased reaction temperature suggests the occurrence of transalkylation during the depolymerization reaction. Reaction time (Fig. 12(B)) does not have a significant impact on the structural changes of lignin when the reaction time is increased from 1 to 5 h as evidenced by the similarity in pattern for the different reaction times. However, they showed a decrease in the methoxy groups and an increase of aliphatic H when performed at longer reaction times. Furthermore, the FT-IR spectra also confirmed the changes of functional groups in lignin structure during the depolymerization process. The FT-IR spectrum of the alkaline lignin and the residual lignin after different conditions are summarized in Fig. 13 in which the FT-IR spectrum assignments are according to the literature [62,63]. The spectrum of the original lignin shows a broad absorption at the region of 3600–3200 cm−1 which is assigned to the stretching frequencies of phenolic or aliphatic -OH groups. The band at 2936 cm−1 and 2853 cm−1 are attributed to the vibrations of CH asymmetric and CH symmetric bonds in the methyl and methylene groups, respectively. The peak at 1701 cm−1 is assigned to the vibration of CO stretching of non-conjugated aromatic ring followed by the peaks at 1603 cm−1, 1514 cm−1, and 1427 cm−1 originating from aromatic ring vibration. Additionally, the bands of asymmetric and symmetric CH deformation in -CH3 of methoxy groups were found at 1463 cm−1 and 1368 cm−1, respectively. The peaks at 1271 cm−1 and 1216 cm−1 correlated to the vibration of guaiacly breathing with CO stretching and syringyl ring breathing. Simultaneously, the bands at 1128 cm−1, 1033 cm−1, 856 cm−1, 818 cm−1 were attributed to the CC or CO stretching vibrations, aromatic CH plan deformation of syringyl and guaiacyl type including CO deformation of primary alcohol. Fig. 13(A) and (B) shows the total changes of lignin structure that occurs with the increase of depolymerization temperature and reaction time, respectively. It was observed that the absorbance of functional groups decreased as compared with the original lignin. There was a decrease of the broad -OH stretching band at 3436 cm−1 when the reaction temperature and reaction time in depolymerization of lignin were increased which reflect the disappearance of hydroxyl groups in the lignin structure. Moreover, the intensity of the bands, especially at 1271 cm−1 and 1216 cm−1 (guaiacyl ring breathing with CO and syringyl ring), 1128 cm−1, and 1033 cm−1 (aromatic CH in plane formation of syringyl and guaiacyl type) were gradually reduced. This indicates that the lignin was depolymerized to monoaromatic units and lignin oil. This is in agreement with the analysis of the 1H NMR analysis. Meanwhile, the peak intensity of the CH deformations asymmetric in –CH3 of methoxy groups also decreased with reaction temperature and reaction time, indicating that demethoxylation reaction of lignin probably occurred during the depolymerization process. Elemental analysis was performed in order to obtain a better understanding of the chemical changes occurring in the lignin structure. The obtained results of the residual lignin isolated from the depolymerization process over 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red catalyst in 50:50 v/v of H2O-methanol mixture at 250 °C for 3 h compared with the initial alkaline lignin are shown in Table 5 . The original alkaline lignin shows an elemental (chemical) composition of 63.36% C, 6.69% H, 27.19% O, 1.99% of S, and trace amount of N (0.77%) on a dry and ash free basis. The sulfur contained in the alkaline lignin was due to the lignin isolation process which originated from the Kraft process that uses sodium hydroxide and sodium sulfide as the digesting reagent. As for the residual lignin after depolymerization at different conditions, a significant increase in C content and a decrease in O and H contents were observed with reaction temperature and reaction time. Moreover, the high heating value (HHV) of original lignin and residual lignin was calculated using Dulong's formula. It was observed that all the residual lignin samples after the depolymerization process exhibited higher high heating values (HHV) than the original lignin (26.13 MJ kg−1) and lignocellulosic biomass used currently as a solid fuel. The stability of catalysts is an important parameter for indicating the catalyst performance. Therefore, the stability of the catalysts was investigated in term of recyclability of the catalysts in this study. After the completion of the depolymerization step, the used catalyst was separated out from the lignin-derived products by filtration, and followed by washing with water and ethanol. After that, the separated catalyst was directly used in the next experimental depolymerization reaction without the regeneration process. Fig. 14 shows the recyclability test of the Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red catalyst for depolymerization of lignin at 250 °C for 3 h. It can be seen that the conversion of lignin was almost stable after four consecutive recycling runs. Regarding oil yield, the reused catalysts slightly decreased after the first recycling run. After four cycles, the oil yield dropped to 32.40% as compared with the 46.80% obtained from the fresh catalyst. The synthesized Ni/CeO2-ZrO2 catalysts prepared via the one-step chemical reduction method were provided a more effective and active catalyst for depolymerization of lignin into phenolic-based compounds. The active phase of Ni can be formed in a one-step reduction of this catalyst, and so, there is no need for the additional reduction step to activate Ni active sites. The highest catalyst performance was obtained at 10 wt% loading of Ni on CeO2-ZrO2 support. The influence of reaction temperature and reaction time on the lignin depolymerization process was investigated in this work for optimizing the catalytic activities and to obtain a better understanding of the structure changes of lignin during the depolymerization reaction. The reaction temperature was the major factor in giving high diversity of phenolic products and product yields. On the other hand, the reaction time of lignin depolymerization does not have a significant effect on the reaction only in terms of similarity of obtained products and the structure of residual lignin for all the reaction times. Char production and the molecular weight of residual lignin increased because of recondensation and repolymerization reactions when operated at higher reaction temperatures and longer reaction times.
abstract: Alkaline lignin obtained from paper manufacturing is a potential feedstock for upgrading to produce value-added chemicals. This study demonstrates the efficiencies of synthesized Ni supported CeO2-ZrO2 catalysts to convert this low-cost alkaline lignin into value-added phenolic-based chemicals for further commercial applications. Lignin depolymerization was performed under mild reaction conditions, namely, reaction temperature of 200–300 °C, and reaction time of 1–5 h. Three different catalyst preparation methods (simple wet-impregnation, wet-impregnation assisted reduction in H2 atmosphere and one-step chemical reduction) were investigated and used to evaluate possible correlations between the active Ni phase and catalytic performance in lignin depolymerization. The active Ni phase was successfully formed by one-step chemical reduction method which showed a significant improvement on product yield, especially lignin oil. The best result obtained in lignin depolymerization was by using 10%Ni/CeO2-ZrO2-red catalyst which resulted in producing a high yield of lignin oil (46.8%) from lignin feedstock under mild condition of 250 °C. The major phenolic compounds in lignin oil were: guaiacol, methyl-guaiacol, ethyl-guaiacol, vanillin, acetovanillone, and homovanillic acid. Furthermore, the reaction temperature affected the product yield and product distribution resulting in higher diversity of phenolic compounds in lignin oil.
title: Catalytic depolymerization of alkaline lignin to value-added phenolic-based compounds over Ni/CeO2-ZrO2 catalyst synthesized with a one-step chemical reduction of Ni species using NaBH4 as the reducing agent

full_text: One promising approach to mitigate global energy problems is the use of biomass bio-fuels, such as bioethanol and biodiesel, as abundant and environmentally friendly energy sources. Biodiesel is a non-petroleum based, alternative diesel fuel that is relatively clean burning, non-toxic, biodegradable and renewable [1–6]. It consists of a mixture of esters composed of mono-methyl esters of long chain fatty acid derivatives [7–9]. The combustion of biodiesel can decrease carbon monoxide (CO) emissions by 46.7%, particulate matter emissions by 66.7% and unburned hydrocarbons by 45.2% [9]. The most common method for producing biodiesel is via triglyceride (TG) transesterification from vegetable oils and animal fats in the presence of short-chain alcohols and homogeneous acid or base catalysts; these yield fatty acid methyl esters (FAME) or fatty acid ethyl esters (FAEE) and glycerol (also called glycerin) as products [10]. Every triglyceride molecule reacts with 3 equivalents of alcohol to produce glycerol and three fatty acid (methyl) ester molecules [11–13]. The alcohols (R-OH) used in the transesterification are normally methanol or ethanol, where methanol is the most common because of its low cost, low reaction temperatures, fast reaction times and higher quality methyl ester products [9–11]. The most commonly used method for triglyceride transesterification relies on the use of batch plants, where homogeneous base-catalyzed reactions are used [10,14]. NaOH, KOH or methoxides are typically used as the homogenous catalyst due to their high activity, among other advantages that make them economically superior over mineral acids and lipases [12]. In this catalytic method, the base catalysts are unrecyclable after the reaction and suffer from separation problems. Additionally, the current aqueous quenching practice, which is performed by acid quenching, results in saponification and emulsion formation. This process also results in an alkaline wastewater stream, which leads to the corrosion of equipment and high operating costs. Moreover, the recovered glycerol is normally impure due to the presence of salts, soaps, monoglycerides and diglycerides, and the glycerol purification process consequently adds an additional cost. Therefore, for both environmental and economic reasons, there is increasing interest in the possibility of replacing homogeneous bases by heterogeneous solid catalysts for triglyceride transesterification [2,5,9,15–19]. Different heterogeneous base catalysts have been investigated for the production of biodiesel [10]. Some of these solid catalysts include alkali-metal and alkaline earth metal carbonates and oxides (MgO, CaO, BaO and SrO [19–23]), lithium base ceramics (Li4SiO4 and Li2SiO3 [24,25]), Na2SiO3 [26], transition metal oxides (ZnO, PbOx, ZrO2 etc. [27,28]), basic ion-exchange resins [29], layered double hydroxides [30–33], supported bases (basic alumina, [34]), salts impregnated on Al2O3 and ZnO [35–37] and zeolites [38]. CaO has been one of the most widely used alkaline earth metal oxides as a catalyst for the transesterification reaction, as the transesterification reaction yields a 98% FAME efficiency during the first cycle [39]. Modifications of ZnO or CaO with lanthanum [40] and lithium [41] have attracted considerable attention for the production of biodiesel due to their high basicity, low solubility and low preparation cost [1,10]. Sodium zirconate (Na2ZrO3) has recently been examined for high temperature CO2 capture and has shown some interesting behavior [42–44]. It has been found that Na2ZrO3 is able to trap CO2 due to its lamellar crystal structure and because of its high surface basicity [42]. Therefore, the aim of the present work is to determine the influence of sodium zirconate as a catalyst in the transesterification reaction of soybean oil, where the parameters of temperature, time and reagent:catalyst molar ratios were examined. Sodium zirconate (Na2ZrO3) was prepared via a solid state reaction. Stoichiometric amounts of zirconium oxide (ZrO2, Sigma-Aldrich) and sodium carbonate (Na2CO3, Aldrich) were mixed and heat-treated at 900°C for 4h. The sample was then characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and nitrogen adsorption. X-ray diffraction patterns were obtained on a Bruker AXS D8 Advance diffractometer coupled to a copper anode X-ray tube. The resulting compounds were identified according to their corresponding JCPDS files (Joint Committee Powder Diffraction Standards). The microstructural characteristics of Na2ZrO3 were determined via N2 adsorption–desorption and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) measurements. For the N2 adsorption–desorption experiments, isotherms were acquired on a Bel-Japan Minisorp II at 77K using the multi-point technique. The samples were degassed at room temperature for 24h under vacuum before analysis. SEM experiments were performed on a JEOL JMS-7600F SEM. Transesterification reactions catalyzed by Na2ZrO3 were performed in a 25mL three-neck round-bottomed glass flask equipped with a mechanical stirrer, a reflux condenser and a thermometer. The batch reactions were performed with various stoichiometric methanol/soybean oil molar ratios. Analytical grade methanol (Sigma-Aldrich, 99.9% purity) was used along with pure Soybean oil, Nutrioli brand NOM-051-SCFI-SSA12010, that was purchased at a local food store (Mexico, D.F.). The fatty acid composition of the soybean oil was determined by gas chromatography (GC) analysis and is reported in the Results and discussion section. Various catalyst concentrations between 1 and 10% sodium zirconate under vigorous magnetic stirring at 65°C were considered. Additional experiments were performed at different temperatures (45, 65 and 85°C). A reflux condenser was employed to prevent the evaporation of alcohol. The progress of the transesterification reaction was monitored at various reaction times between 0.5 and 8h. After the corresponding reaction times, the samples were immediately quenched in an ice bath (~4°C) to control the conversion rate, and the different phases were separated for analysis. The top liquid layer was the FAME (fatty acid methyl ester) phase, whereas the bottom liquid phase was glycerol. Both liquid layers were separated, and the catalyst was recovered and washed several times with methanol. The components of the FAME and liquid samples as well as the purity of the biodiesel product were determined using infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR), proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) and gas chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry. For ATR-FTIR spectroscopy measurements, the samples were analyzed using a Nicolet 6700-FTIR spectrometer. Several 1H NMR analyses were performed using a Bruker Advance II spectrometer (200MHz) with CDCl3 (99.8%, Aldrich) as the solvent. A Hewlett Packard-5973 GC–MS was used to evaluate the conversion and selectivity of the product over the transesterification reactions. The mobile phase was hexane (HPLC grade, Sigma-Aldrich). The final flow rate was 0.9mL/min, and the sample injection volume was 1μL. The temperature of the detector was 250°C, and the temperature analyses were initially performed from 150 to 300°C at a rate of 20°C/min. All analyses were performed at room temperature. Finally, the catalyst was re-characterized by XRD after several transesterification cycles to evaluate the structural stability of the catalyst. The purity of Na2ZrO3 was analyzed by XRD. Fig. 1 shows the XRD pattern of the sample after thermal treatment at 900°C for 4h. The diffraction pattern was fitted to the JCPDS file 35-0770, which corresponds to Na2ZrO3 with a monoclinic structure. Therefore, Na2ZrO3 was obtained without the presence of any impurities at the XRD detection level (>3%). The Na2ZrO3 microstructural characteristics were determined using SEM and N2 adsorption. The morphology and particle size of the sample were analyzed by SEM (Fig. 2 ). The Na2ZrO3 particles exhibited a dense polyhedral morphology, with an average particle size of ~1μm. These particles produced large agglomerates with sizes greater than 15–20μm. The dense morphology was corroborated by the textural analysis performed using nitrogen adsorption–desorption experiments, in which a surface area of 1.3m2/g was determined using the BET model [45]. All microstructural results are in good agreement with the solid-state reaction synthesis method. Once the Na2ZrO3 was characterized, the material was used as a catalyst in the transesterification reaction of soybean oil and methanol. Initially, the objective was to investigate the influence of the amount of catalyst used on the conversion. The amount of catalyst was varied between 1 and 10wt.% (catalyst/oil weight ratio), and the transesterification reactions were performed at 65°C for 3h. These experimental conditions were established based on different
abstract: Sodium zirconate (Na2ZrO3) was tested as a basic catalyst for the production of biodiesel using a soybean oil transesterification reaction. Initially, Na2ZrO3 was synthesized via a solid-state reaction. The structure and microstructure of the catalyst were characterized using X-ray diffraction, scanning electron microscopy and N2 adsorption. Various transesterification reactions were then conducted using soybean oil and methanol under differing reaction conditions. The influence of some parameters, such as the reactant concentrations (molar ratios), catalyst percentage, reaction time, temperature and re-use of the catalyst, on the transesterification process in the presence of Na2ZrO3 was investigated. The maximum FAME conversion efficiency was 98.3% at 3h of reaction time and 3% of catalyst. Additionally, the produced biodiesel was characterized using infrared spectroscopy, gas chromatography coupled to mass spectrometry and proton nuclear magnetic resonance. The resulting biodiesel showed good purity, composition and degree of unsaturation in comparison to previous reports. According to these results, Na2ZrO3 could become an alternative solid base catalyst for scalable and cost-effective biodiesel production.
title: Sodium zirconate (Na2ZrO3) as a catalyst in a soybean oil transesterification reaction for biodiesel production
