full_text: Dramatic climate change, dwindling crude reserves, and increasing energy demand have spurred unprecedented global interests in the development of renewable and environment-friendly energy sources [1,2]. The alternative production of liquid fuel and fine chemicals derived from biomass is considered one of the most promising routes since biomass is an abundant and sustainable resource [3]. Among various types of biomass, microalgae are competitive materials for bio-oil production due to their impressive productivity and high carbon dioxide fixation capacity compared with terrestrial plants [4,5]. However, the bio-oils obtained from thermochemical conversion of microalgae biomass cannot be directly used as liquid transportation fuel because of their corrosiveness, thermal instability, high viscosity, and low heating value caused by high oxygen content [6,7]. Therefore, deoxygenation of microalgal bio-oil is a crucial process in production of high-grade transport fuel. As reported in previous studies [8,9], microalgal bio-oil contains a large amount of long-chain fatty acids, such as stearic acid (C18) and palmitic acid (C16), which are conventionally used as model compounds to investigate deoxygenation of microalgae-based fuels. In general, the main deoxygenation processes of fatty acids are shown as follows [10–12]. The hydrocarbon products obtained from decarbonylation and decarboxylation route are one carbon less than the reactants, whereas hydrodeoxygenation (HDO) yields hydrocarbon with the same chain length as the reactants [13]. Decarboxylation : R − COOH → R − H + CO 2 g Decarbonylation : R − COOH + H 2 → R − H + CO g + H 2 O Hydrodeoxygenation : R − COOH + 3 H 2 → R − CH 3 + 2 H 2 O Catalytic deoxygenation during bio-oil upgrading has been widely investigated in the past few decades. Conventional hydrotreating catalysts, such as sulfided NiMo and CoMo, deactivate rapidly due to sulfur leaching [2]. Among hydrotreating catalysts, noble metal-supported (Pt, Pd, and Rh) catalysts show superior activity and selectivity [14]. However, the exorbitant price of noble metals considerably hinders their large-scale utilization. Therefore, additional sulfur-free and inexpensive metal catalysts must be developed for upgrading microalgal bio-oils. Alternatively, Cu-based catalysts have been intensively investigated because of their high CO bond hydrogenation selectivity [15,16]. Gutierrez et al. [17] observed that nanoparticle (MCM-48)-supported highly dispersed Cu is selective toward CO bond activation in cinnamaldehyde hydrogenation due to the existence of Cu+ species. Gong et al. [18] revealed that utilization of Cu/SiO2 catalysts achieved high yield of ethanol at over 83% from hydrogenation production via syngas and observed that Cu-based catalysts exhibited remarkable stability and efficiency, which are attributed to the cooperative effect between surface Cu0 and Cu+. Although Cu-based catalysts show promise in selective hydrogenation reaction, the weak hydrogen dissociation capability and valence transition of cupreous species under reaction conditions severely impair their practical applications, especially in liquid-phase hydrogenation [19]. Studies have reported that the strong interaction between metal Cu and Co can be beneficial for synergistic catalysis [20], because Co-based catalysts feature strong hydrogenation capability, which generates an easy interaction between oxygen atoms in lattice and reactants [21]. Li et al. [22] prepared CuCo catalysts for selectively converting furfural to cyclopentanol (CPL), and a strong interaction between Cu and Co was observed. Co0 and Cu+ on the catalyst surface exhibited high hydrogenation activity with the highest yield of 68% CPL. Although specific hydrogenation reactions have been investigated for the CuCo catalyst, the catalytic conversion of long-chain fatty acids over CuCo catalyst has not been reported. In this work, carbon nanotubes (CNTs) were used as supporters for the nanoscale combination of Co and Cu nanoparticles. The advantages of using CNTs as supporters for dispersing metal catalyst particles have been demonstrated due to the richness in surface functional groups and defects [23]. Apart from their accessibility to the active phase and good chemical stability in aggressive media [24,25], the unique electronic structure of CNTs exhibits a large charge transfer, which decreases the diffusion resistance kinetically [26]. In addition, active components that strongly interact with CNTs can lead to structural defects in CNTs and further affect the transfer of reactants and products inside or outside CNTs [27]. In this study, the deoxygenation performance of CNT-supported CuCo catalyst for converting long-chain fatty acids was investigated for the first time. The catalyst was synthesized by wet impregnation. Considering the energy conservation and cost reduction, experiments were performed under lower H2 pressure and lower ratio of catalyst to reactant compared with those in our previous research on Mo2C catalyst [28]. Meanwhile, the reduction temperature of CuCo catalyst notably decreased during preparation in this study. This study aimed to evaluate the catalytic performance of CNT-supported CuCo catalyst for deoxygenation of stearic acid under mild conditions. A brief pathway for deoxygenation was proposed based on experimental and theoretical analyses, and the performances of CuCo catalysts between the oxidation and reduction states were compared under this catalytic system. In addition, the synergistic effect between Cu and Co was discussed. This study has been conducted using a single model compound as a first approach for the future use of such catalysts with actual feeds. Neutral CNTs (>95%) were provided from XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Stearic acid (98%), n-octadecane (≥99.5%), n-heptadecane (≥99.5%), octadecanal (≥99.5%) and octadecanol (≥99.5%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Decane as solvent (>99%) and Co(NO3)2·6H2O were purchased by Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Cu(NO3)2·xH2O (Mr = 187.56) was purchased from Macklin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were used as-received without further purification. The pristine CNTs (BET specific surface area: 150 m2·g−1) supported Cu, Co and CuCo catalysts were synthesized by the wet-impregnation method. For convenience, the calcined samples are denoted as Cu/Co-C or CuCo-C in the article, and the hydrogen-reduced catalysts are denoted as Cu/Co-R or CuCo-R. The CuCo-C-x min and CuCo-R-x min represent the samples reacted for x min in our reaction system. The detailed synthetic procedure and characterization of catalysts are shown in Supporting Information. The catalytic hydrogenation experiments of stearic acid conversion were carried out in a batch autoclave. The autoclave was heated with an external electrical furnace. The temperature was measured with a thermocouple controlled at ±1 °C. The final products were analyzed using a gas chromatograph (Agilent GC6820), equipped with flame ionization detector (FID) and a GsBP-Inowax capillary column (30 m × 0.32 mm × 0.25 μm). The detailed information had been shown in Supporting Information. The conversion of stearic acid and selectivity of products are calculated via following formulas. C = moles of converted stearic acid / moles of initial stearic acid × 100 % S e = moles of each product / moles of total product × 100 % where, C and S e denotes the conversion of stearic acid and selectivity of products respectively. The theoretical calculation was executed on the platform of Materials Studio (Version 6.1). The theoretical XRD spectrums of Cu-R, CuCo-R, Co-R and CuCo-C were obtained using Reflex module. The simulated spectrums were then processed to search the best fitness with experimental spectrums of CuCo-C, Cu-R, Co-R, and CuCo-R respectively based on the algorithm of multiple linear least-square regression. The parameters achieved were used as reference to analyze the combination of corresponding components for different catalysts. The model catalysts of Cu-R and CuCo-R were built by packing corresponding components in the CNTs compartments according to the corresponding ratios. The geometry optimization was implemented using DMol3 module. Then these catalysts were tested for the reactions of converting stearic acid to heptadecane, octadecane and octadecanol, and cracking products, respectively. The methodology was based on comparison of energy difference between reactants and products of each reaction in the catalyst system. The XRD patterns of as-calcined CuCo-C precursor, CuCo-R, Cu-R and Co-R catalysts at 350 °C, and CNTs carrier are shown in Fig. 1(a). The results indicate that the phases of CuCo-C precursor after calcination at 500 °C are mainly Co3O4 and CuO. Then the as-calcined CuCo-C and CuC samples are reduced at 350 °C in hydrogen atmosphere. Both XRD patterns of hydrogen-reduced CuCo-R and Cu-R catalysts show similar diffraction peaks at 2θ = 43.4°, 50.6° and 74.3°, which are assigned to the lattice planes of (111), (200) and (220) of metallic Cu with cubic structure in the 10–80° range respectively (JCPDS: 65-9743). The XRD patterns of as-calcined CuCo-C and CuCo-R catalysts confirm that Cu2+ cation is reduced to Cu0 during the hydrogen reduction process at 350 °C. Furthermore, the diffraction peaks intensity of metallic Cu gradually decrease with introducing Co into CuCo-R catalyst (Cu/Co atomic ratios = 2.6:1, according to EDS results in Fig. 2(b)). No diffraction peaks corresponding to Co or Co oxides are observed in investigated XRD region of CuCo-R catalyst, possibly because Co or Co oxides have been dispersed well on the surface of CNTs or Co atoms are doped into the Cu lattice [29,30]. Through carefully comparing the XRD patterns of CuCo-R and Cu-R catalysts in the 42–45° range (Fig. 1(b)), a slight shift to lower diffraction angle in comparison with pure Cu (111) can be observed. According to the Prague equation 2dsinθ = nλ, it can be deduced that the interplanar spacing d is enlarged. The lattice expansion takes place after introducing Co into pristine Cu catalyst, which is corresponding to the fact that Cu atomic radius is smaller than Co atomic radius (R Cu = 1.57 Å, R Co = 1.67 Å). The result also indicates that the solute Co atoms are doped into the Cu lattice, and replace some Cu atoms with larger Co atoms in lattice through solid solution process, ascribed to formation of CuCo solid solution. Thus a strong interaction between Co and Cu is confirmed, which can also be benefit for its catalytic activity. Through carefully comparing the XRD patterns of Co-R catalysts in the 30-60° range in Fig. 1(c), the phases of Co-R catalyst after reduction at 350 °C are mainly Co and CoO. As previously described, Co atom is incorporated into Cu lattice formed CuCo solid solution. In addition, the results above also confirm the CoO phase is dispersed well on the surface of CNTs, therefore no CoO phase is observed in CuCo-R catalyst by XRD characterization. The results of theoretical calculation are shown in Fig. 1(d). The simulation spectrums are in consistence with the experimental spectrums in Fig. 1(a). The simulated spectrums are then processed to search the best fitness with experimental spectrums of CuCo-R Co-R, Cu-R and CuCo-C respectively, and the parameters achieved were used as reference to analyze the corresponding components for different catalysts, the results are listed in Table 1 . The results show that the composition of Cu-R catalyst is pure Cu0, and compositions of Co-R catalyst are Co0 (41.78%) and Co2+ (58.22%) by theoretical calculation, which are in consistence with compositions of Cu-R and Co-R catalysts by experimental XRD results. When the Co is doped to Cu-R catalyst, the composition of CuCo-R are Cu0 (81.30%), Cu+ (3.10%), Co0 (7.70%) and Co2+ (7.90%) respectively by theoretical calculation. Compared to Cu-R catalyst, part Cu0 is converted into Cu+ in CuCo-R catalyst, which indicates the doping Co can attribute to coexistence of Cu0 and Cu+. No CoO and Cu2O phases are observed in CuCo-R catalyst by experimental XRD results, which further indicate these phase are dispersed well on the surface of CNTs. TEM-EDS analysis was carried out to investigate the surface morphology, particle size, and element content of CuCo-R, Cu-R, and Co-R catalysts. The results are shown in Fig. 2. The CuCo-R catalyst in Fig. 2(a) shows that CuCo nanoparticles uniformly dispersed outside the surface of CNTs, and portions of the particles were distributed inside the CNTs, as observed from the high-resolution TEM on the bottom right corner. The mean diameter of CuCo nanoparticles was approximately 16.4 nm according to statistical analysis. EDS point analysis of CuCo-R catalyst in the yellow circle further indicated that the black nanoparticles included Cu and Co, with the atomic ratio of Cu:Co reaching 2.6:1 (Fig. 2(b)). Fig. 2(c) shows the surface morphology and particle size of Cu-R catalyst. As shown in Fig. 2(c), Cu nanoparticles grew on the surface of CNTs. The mean size of Cu particles was approximately 23.7 nm, which is evidently larger than that of the CuCo-R catalyst. This result further confirms that doping of Co can be attributed to the dispersion of CuCo nanoparticles [31]. Fig. 2(d) displays the surface morphology and particle size of Co-R catalyst. The figure shows that Co nanoparticles also dispersed well on the surface of CNTs due to their outstanding dispersion characteristic; the mean diameter of Co nanoparticles was approximately 15.6 nm. No evident sintering occurred during preparation, and all catalysts exhibited well-dispersed morphology, thereby implying the unique structures and properties of CNTs as support to dispersion of active nanoparticles, such as CuCo nanoparticles [14]. The formation process of CNTs supported CuCo solid solution catalyst may be the gradual accumulation of Cu and Co atoms around surface structure defects of CNTs, which are formed during the preparation catalyst process, then the Co atoms are gradually doped into the crystal lattice of Cu and replace some Cu atoms affiliated due to the looseness of structure. Subsequently, the CNTs supported CuCo solid solution catalyst is formed. Therefore, the strong interaction between CuCo solid solution and CNTs can affect the physical properties and chemical behavior of CuCo catalyst. In addition, the CNTs supported CuCo solid solution catalyst possesses large hydrophobic surface that promotes water desorption, which may be much conducive to the HDO reaction [32]. To investigate the changes in the specific surface area and porous structure, N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and corresponding Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) pore size distribution plots of hydrogen-reduced catalysts and CNT carrier were measured, and the results are presented in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3(a) shows the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms. The shapes of all sample isotherms exhibited representative type IV curves, indicating that hydrogen-reduced catalysts and CNTs were typical mesoporous materials with the presence of H1-type hysteresis loop at relatively high pressures of 0.80–0.99, which is generally associated with the open-ended capillary condensation that occurs in mesopores [33]. As shown in Fig. 3(b), all samples possessed a large number of pores in the mesopore range, and larger pore sizes were approximately centralized at 20–40 nm. The pore sizes of supported catalysts presented differences, especially for CuCo-R catalyst, the pore sizes of which were approximately centralized at 24 nm. On the other hand, the pore sizes of other catalysts were larger than that of the CuCo-R catalyst at approximately 32 nm, and the gap was approximately 8 nm. Table 1 lists the textural properties of all the catalysts, including CuCo-C and hydrogen-reduced catalysts. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface areas and external surface areas of all samples increased, especially for CuCo-R catalyst, and this result can be ascribed to the effective dispersion of metal nanoparticles on CNTs or partial transport of metal nanoparticles into the pore structure of CNTs with no evident sintering. The textural properties of Cu-R catalyst indicate that the surface area of micropore hastily reduced from 40.34 m2 g−1 of CNTs to 9.53 m2 g−1. The volume of micropore and average pore size also decreased from 0.019 cm3 g−1 of CNTs to 0.004 cm3 g−1 and from 11.77 nm of CNTs to 9.08 nm, respectively, thereby indicating that impregnating metal Cu nanoparticles on CNTs can hinder porous channel of CNTs, especially blocking micropores. Cu-R catalyst possesses larger particle diameter from particle size distribution than Co-R and CuCo-R catalysts, further accounting for the most serious micropore blocking of Cu-R catalyst. For the Co-R catalyst, the surface area and volume of micropore showed no evident change compared with CNT carriers in Table 1, thereby implying that Co nanoparticles were rarely distributed in the micropore of CNTs. Compared with the slight increase in total pore volume and decrease in average pore size, a reasonable partial distribution of Co nanoparticles was observed on the surface of CNTs. Compared with pure Cu-R catalyst, the micropore volume of CuCo-R catalyst reduced, and the average pore size enlarged (Table 1), indicating that doping Co can restrain micropore blocking. Analysis of pore diameter distribution of CuCo-R catalyst indicated that the value decreased, implying that several CuCo particles entered the mesopores and macropores of CuCo-R catalyst. Table 1 summarizes the different sample compositions determined by inductively coupled plasma (ICP). Sample compositions are denoted by the theoretical weight percentage of Cu and/or Co impregnated on CNTs carriers. The reduction properties of the CuCo-C, CuC and CoC samples are determined by hydrogen temperature-programmed reduction (H2-TPR). As shown in Fig. 4 , all the catalysts exhibit broad H2 consumption peaks appearing above 575 °C, which is attributed to the gasification of oxygen-containing functional groups (such as –OH) on the surface of CNTs supportors due to the reductive activity of H2 at high temperature [34,35]. For the CoC sample, two obvious overlapped-peaks appear at the temperature range of 275–575 °C, which respectively assigns to the two reduction steps of Co3O4 to CoO, and CoO to metallic Co [36,37]. For the CuC catalyst, a shoulder at 275 °C overlaps with an intensive reduction peak at around 380 °C is observed, which can be attributed to the reduction of small CuO crystallite. The strong and overlapped reduction peaks can be assigned to the reductions of Cu2+ to Cu0 [38]. Compared to CuC sample, the reduction peaks of CuCo-C sample shift to lower temperature, especially for Cu species at the temperature range of 150–400 °C, which illustrates the addition of Co can lower the reduction temperature of CuC sample. As reported in the literatures [35,39], the particle sizes have great influence on reduction of catalysts. The mean diameter of CuCo-R and Cu-R catalyst are 16.4 nm and 23.7 nm respectively according to particle size distribution in Fig. 2. The larger Cu nanoparticles can block some micropores of CNTs, and the diffusion of reduction gas can be hindered by the blocked Cu species near the micropores of CNTs, which results in lower reducibility and higher reduction temperature of Cu sample. The detailed microporous blocking in Cu catalyst has been discussed in the section of N2 adsorption-desorption analysis. In addition, the H2-TPR profile of the CuCo-C sample exhibits two weak and broad reduction peaks at the range of 410–575 °C, which are assigned to the reduction of Co species, and the valence states of Co species exist stably in the form of CoO and metal Co (not Co3O4 according to XRD results). Compared to CuC sample, the decreased reduction temperature is beneficial for increasing the CuCo nanoparticles exposed area on CNTs carrier due to weaker sintering, which also benefits to its catalytic activity [40]. To obtain further insights into the surface electronic state and chemical composition, XPS was carried out for analyzing samples of CuCo-R, Cu-R, Co-R and CuCo-C catalysts. The survey spectra for Cu2p, Co2p, Cu LMM and O1s are displayed in Fig. 5(a). Fig. 5(b) shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Co2p. The binding energy observed at 780.46 eV with their satellite features confirms that the surface phase of reduced CuCo-R and Co-R catalysts is CoO [41]. No peak of metallic Co is found on the surfaces of two reduced catalysts, further implies that the metallic Co is incorporated into the Cu lattice, which is also consistent with XRD results. The CoO peak intensity in Co-R catalyst is higher than CuCo-R catalyst, which suggests that the introduction of Cu can affect the electronic state of CuCo-R catalyst, promoting the reduction of Co oxides to metallic Co. This result can be further proved by the theoretical results in Table 1, in which the ratio of Co+/Co0 is 1.39 for Co-R catalyst, but the ratio of Co+/Co0 is 1.03 for CuCo-R catalyst. For CuCo-C catalyst, the peaks corresponding to binding energy observed have slightly shifted from 780.46 eV to 781.67 eV, which can be assigned to Co3O4. The results are also consistent with XRD analysis. For the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Cu2p in Fig. 5(c), the binding energies of Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 peaks (approximately 933.7 eV and 953.7 eV respectively), accompanied by characteristic shake-up satellite peaks (942.2 eV) are identified as Cu2+ chemical state on the surface of CuCo-C catalyst [30,42]. For CuCo-R and Cu-R catalysts, the disappearance of shakeup satellite features and the binding energy of Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 shifting to a low value about 933 eV and 953 eV respectively verify that the surface copper species in the these catalysts are complete reduction of Cu2+ to Cu+ or/and Cu0 [43,44]. The precise values are shown in Table S1 in Supporting Information. The binding energy difference value between CuCo-R and Cu-R catalysts is about 0.08 eV at the first column in Table S1. The slight deviation of binding energy value indicates that the chemical environment of Cu changed, presumably owing to the interaction of Cu with Co species during the preparation process [45]. In addition, the slight shift of binding energy to higher energy of CuCo-R catalyst is related to presence of small nanoparticles [46], which has also been confirmed by XRD and TEM results. Because of the identical binding energies between Cu+ and Cu0, the Cu LMM XAES spectra was used to further distinguish the surface Cu+ and Cu0 species of reduced catalysts. As shown in Fig. 5(d), all the Cu LMM spectra of reduced catalysts present asymmetrical and broad peak, centralized at 570.4 eV and 575.3 eV, ascribed to Cu0 and Cu+ respectively, suggesting the stable coexistence of Cu+ and Cu0 on the surface of these reduced catalysts [47,48]. Considering the composition of Cu-R catalyst by theoretical calculation in Table 1, the Cu+ on the surface of Cu-R catalyst proved by XPS analysis attributes to oxidation of Cu0. The concentration of Cu+ and Cu0 on the surface of reduced catalysts can be determined by the ratio between their peak intensities in the Cu LMM spectra. The ratio of Cu0/(Cu++Cu0) in CuCo-R catalyst obtained from Cu LMM spectra is 0.92%, which is approximately in consistence with the ratio of Cu0/(Cu++Cu0) of 0.96% by theoretical calculation. The acidities of CNTs supported Cu-R, Co-R and CuCo-R catalysts were analyzed by NH3-TPD. As shown in Fig. 6 , no obvious NH3 desorption peak can be observed from Co-R catalysts, suggesting the high dispersion of Co and CoO particles prepared by incipient wetness method. Only weak acidity (at about 120–300 °C) is observed in Cu-R and CuCo-R catalysts, and the Cu-R catalyst exhibits more strong acid sites than CuCo-R catalyst. In addition, very subtle decrease, occurred in acidity of catalyst with the addition of Co metal, implied the introduction of Co is responsible for surface acidity of CuCo-R catalyst. Furthermore, the electronic environment variation of formed CuCo solid solution around CNTs can exert great influence on reaction activity and product selectivity, especially for the cracking performance of reduced catalysts [35]. To evaluate the catalytic activity of different catalysts, batch experiments on catalytic hydrogenation of stearic acid were carried out, and the reaction results are presented in Fig. 7 . As shown in Fig. 7, product distributions of reactions over different catalysts showed distinct differences, and the detailed results can be found in Table S2 in Supporting Information. The reduced Cu-R catalyst exhibited higher selectivity to cracking products (37.15%) compared with other catalysts, such as C5–C16, due to the cracking of long-chain alkanes and their oxides. This phenomenon can be attributed to the high number of acid sites on the Cu-R catalyst according to NH3-TPD results. In addition, large amounts of octadecanol, the intermediate product of hydrogenation of stearic acid, can be achieved when using Cu-R as catalyst, with the selectivity of octadecanol reaching 39.98%. No n-octodecane was found as the dehydration–hydrogenation reaction of octadecanol to n-octodecane requires high energy [14]. Although more acidic sites are conducive to the conversion of octadecanol, porous blocking, especially microporous blocking of Cu-R catalyst as described in N2 adsorption–desorption characterization, can affect the penetrability of reactant molecules and intermediate products, thereby further restraining the dehydration–hydrogenation pathway of octadecanol for n-octodecane production. A certain amount of n-heptadecane was detected for the Cu-R catalyst, indicating that this catalyst favors decarbonylation of octadecanal to produce n-hepadecane. Trace level of intermediate octadecanal was detected in the overall reaction after tracing the conversion of stearic acid over the Cu-R catalyst, confirming that the rate of octadecanal reduction to octadecanol occurred faster than hydrogenation of stearic acid to octadecanal. For the reaction results of Co-R catalyst, the selectivity of cracking products approximated 16.84%, which is lower than that of the Cu-R catalyst. The selectivity of octadecanol decreased significantly to 8.12%, accompanying the production of n-octodecane, the selectivity of which is approximately 7.73%. Through analysis of product distribution of Co-R catalyst, the selectivity of n-heptadecane reached 67.31%, indicating that the decarbonylation reaction of octadecanal to n-hepadecane is the major pathway. Considering the economy of carbon atoms, measures should be developed for inhibiting cracking of products. In this work, Co doping was performed to form CuCo solid solution for regulating the physical and chemical properties of catalysts. The reaction results of CuCo-R catalyst showed remarkable changes in product distribution. As expected, the cracking products evidently decreased with selectivity of 2.17%. The selectivity of long-chain alkanes, including n-heptadecane and n-octodecane, increased to 94.82% at 100% conversion. The aspects conducive to catalytic activity of CuCo-R catalyst were proposed according to characterization results. First, the reduced acid sites mainly caused cracking inhibition during Co doping, thereby enhancing the interaction between Cu and Co to form CuCo solid solution, inhibiting cracking, and resulting in alkane production. Second, several CuCo particles in mesopores and macropores enhanced the contact between CuCo particles and intermediates according to N2 adsorption–desorption analysis, thereby further accelerating the conversion of stearic acid. A blank test without catalyst was carried out to evaluate the contribution of catalysts at a reaction temperature of 260 °C, limited stearic acid conversion can be observed. The presence of catalyst plays a crucial role in hydrogenation of stearic acid. According to our previous work on Mo2C catalyst [28], the catalytic performance of CuCo-R catalyst is an alternative; the H2 pressure not only reduced from 4 MPa to 3 MPa, but the ratio of reactant and catalysts also improved from 2:1 to 5:1, and these conditions are conductive to energy conservation and show potential for large-scale industrial applications. To study in depth the catalytic performance of CuCo-R catalyst, the effect of hydrogen pressure on catalytic deoxygenation of stearic acid was further investigated. The results indicate that initial hydrogen pressure significantly affected the conversion of stearic acid and selectivity of liquid products. As shown in Fig. 8 , complete conversion of stearic acid was obtained with increasing hydrogen pressure to 3 MPa. The major liquid product is n-heptadecane with the highest selectivity of 89.44%, whereas the selectivity of n-octadecane enhanced slightly to 5.38%. The selectivity of octadecanol initially increased and then decreased with the increase in hydrogen pressure, indicating that high hydrogen pressure is conducive to shifting the equilibrium from octadecanol to octadecanal and leads to rapid decarbonylation of octadecanal to n-heptadecane. Thus, the selectivity of n-heptadecane was enhanced. The detailed reaction mechanism of deoxygenation of stearic acid over CuCo-R catalyst is discussed in the subsequent chapter. The models of Cu-R and CuCo-R catalysts were built by packing Cu, Cu2O, Co, and CoO in the CNT compartments according to the corresponding ratios in Table 1. The assumed reaction paths of converting stearic acid into heptadecane, octadecane, octadecanol, and cracking products are shown as follows, where the cracking alkane is n-hexane (assumption of n = 6). [Re1] stearic acid + H2 → n-alkane + (18-n) carboxylic acid [Re2] stearic acid + H2 → heptadecane + HCOOH [Re3] stearic acid + 3H2 → octadecane + 2H2O [Re4] stearic acid + 2H2 → octadecanol + H2O The methodology for estimating reaction paths was based on the comparison of energy differences between reactants and products of each reaction in the catalytic reaction system. A high energy difference is conducive to reaction. Fig. 9 shows the results of theoretical calculation. As shown in Fig. 9, the Cu-R catalyst presented a higher energy difference in the reaction path of [Re1] compared with the CuCo-R catalyst, indicating a much easier cracking toward the Cu-R catalyst. This result also proves that doping Co into Cu catalyst can inhibit cracking, consistent with previous experimental results. The energy difference in the reaction path of [Re1] in CuCo-R is negative, indicating that the [Re1] reaction of the catalyst difficultly occurs kinetically. However, multiple effects, such as acidity and textural properties of catalysts, can affect the cracking reaction of CuCo-R catalyst, as shown in previous experimental results. In addition, the energy difference of CuCo-R catalyst is higher than that of Cu-R catalyst in the reaction paths of [Re2], [Re3], and [Re4]. The enlarged figure related to the reaction paths of [Re2] and [Re4] is shown in the upper right corner in Fig. 9, which indicates that doping with Co can be attributed to the conversion of stearic acid and production of long-chain alkanes. The negative value of Cu-R in the reaction path of [Re3] suggests the difficult formation of n-octodecane by dehydration–hydrogenation reaction over Cu-R. These findings are also consistent with experimental results. To further illuminate the synergistic effect between Cu and Co of CuCo-R catalysts, the catalytic performance of calcined CuCo-C catalyst at different reaction time was evaluated to ascertain active valence state. The detailed reaction results are presented in Table S3 in Supporting Information. As shown in Table S3, the CuCo-C catalyst at the reaction time of 240 min reveals the similar catalytic performance compared to CuCo-R catalyst of 240 min. Both of them exhibit superior catalytic activity, and the conversion of CuCo-C catalyst is up to 98.26%. The product distribution is also comparable with CuCo-R catalyst. The major product is also heptadecane with selectivity of 86.67%, and the selectivity of cracking products is significantly low (about 4.51%). All of the results above reveal that the in-situ reduction occurs in our catalytic system. To further analyze the interaction between Cu and Co, the catalytic performance of reaction time of 10 min is also evaluated, and the results indicate the CuCo-C catalyst of 10 min exhibits a catalytic activity with the conversion of 27.12% only. In addition, the used catalysts of CuCo-C at different reaction time are collected for further characterization. The XRD characterization is used for analyzing the phases of used-calcined catalysts at different reaction time, and the results are listed in Fig. 10 . As shown in Fig. 10, the phases of used-calcined catalysts at different reaction time are metallic Cu with cubic structure in the 10-80° range, which confirms the fast in-situ reduction through reducing Cu2+ cation of calcined samples to Cu0 under the catalytic system. No diffraction peak corresponding to Co or Co oxides is observed in investigated XRD region of CuCo catalyst, due to well dispersion of Co or Co oxides on the surface of CNTs and dope of Co atoms into Cu lattice based on above results. Compared to reduced Cu-R catalyst, the diffraction peak in the 42–45° range of used CuCo-C catalyst of Cu (111) for 240 min shifts to lower diffraction angle, with a smaller movement than reduced CuCo-R catalyst, which further illustrates that a small number of Co is doped into crystal lattice of Cu for used CuCo-C catalyst of 240 min, and the residual Co is existed as CoO. The diffraction peak location in the 42–45° range for used-calcined catalysts of 10 min has no obvious change compared with pure Cu-R catalyst, which illustrates that the introduced Co are existed in the form of CoO, well dispersed on the surface of CNTs. In addition, the crystallinity of bimetal catalysts reduces obviously compared to pure Cu-R catalyst, which attributes to the effect of Co atoms. The different existed forms of Co, containing surface CoO and lattice Co atoms, have great effect on the catalytic activity, which has been confirmed above. Most importantly, the higher content of lattice Co atoms is conducive to the conversion of stearic acid, the detailed analysis are discussed below. The existed lattice Co atoms further affirm the strong interaction between Cu and Co, as ascribed in XRD analysis. To further analyze the surface valent and content of used CuCo-C catalysts for understand of the synergistic effect between Cu and Co, the XPS characterization was carried out for analyzing samples of used CuCo-C catalysts at different reaction time. The results are presented in Fig. 11 . Fig. 11(a) showed the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Cu2p. No characteristic shake-up satellite peak is found in detection region with the binding energies of Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2, focused on 933 eV and 953 eV respectively, verifying that the surface copper species in these catalysts are Cu+ or/and Cu0. Because of identical binding energies between Cu+ and Cu0, the Cu LMM XAES spectra were also used to further distinguish the Cu+ and Cu0 species of used-calcined catalysts. As shown in Fig. 11(b), all the Cu LMM spectra of used-calcined catalysts present asymmetrical and broad peaks, centralized at 570 eV and 575 eV, which are ascribed to Cu0 and Cu+ respectively, suggesting the stable coexistence of Cu+ and Cu0 on the surface of these used CuCo-C catalysts. The concentration of Cu+ and Cu0 on the surface of used CuCo-C catalysts can be determined by the ratio of their peak intensities in the Cu LMM spectra. The deconvolution of Cu LMM peaks is carried out, and the results are listed in Table 2 . The ratio of Cu0/(Cu++Cu0) in used CuCo-C catalyst increases from 0.73% for CuCo-C-10 min catalyst to 0.84% for CuCo-C-240 min catalyst, which implies more Cu2+ are reduced to Cu0 with extending the reaction time in our reaction system. According to the valent content of 0.92% of reduced CuCo-R catalyst, the Cu0/(Cu++Cu0) ratio order of different used CuCo-C catalysts and reduced catalysts are as follows: CuCo-R > CuCo-C-240 min > CuCo-C-10 min, with the same order of the stearic acid conversion. The uniformity between ratio of Cu0/(Cu++Cu0) and conversion illustrated the high ratio of Cu0/(Cu++Cu0) is beneficial for transformation of stearic acid. Fig. 11(c) shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of Co2p. The position about 781 eV of the main peak in the Co2p photoemission spectra shifts to a higher binding energy than elemental Co (~778 eV) with their satellite features, indicating the presence of cobalt oxides. However, the intensity ratio of Co2p1/2 satellite to its main peak is used to distinguish monoxide and spinel. It has been reported that the intensity ratio of Co2p1/2 to its mains peak is ~0.9 for cubic CoO and ~0.3 for Co3O4 [49]. From the spectra obtained from used CuCo-C catalysts in Fig. 11(c), this ratio is around 0.9, which clearly indicates the formation of CoO. The CoO intensity ratio of CuCo-C-240 min catalyst decreases 34.53%, compared to CuCo-C-10 min catalyst by calculating peak area, which implies much more lattice Co atoms existed in CuCo-C-240 min are conducive to conversion of stearic acid. Based on the results above, the synergistic effect between Cu and Co mainly focuses on promoting reduction progress, by which the ratio of Cu0/(Cu++Cu0) and lattice Co atoms have obvious difference. The higher ratio of Cu0/(Cu++Cu0) and lattice Co atoms leads to the better performance of conversion of stearic acid. To investigate the deoxygenation process of stearic acid over CuCo-R catalyst, experiments on catalytic hydrogenation versus reaction times were carried out. The results are shown in Fig. 12 . As shown in Fig. 12, conversion of stearic acid gradually increased with reaction time. The conversion of stearic acid reached 100% at a reaction time of 4 h. The selectivity for liquid hydrocarbons gradually increased with reaction time. The major liquid product is n-heptadecane with the highest selectivity of 89.44% reached after 3 h, whereas the selectivity for n-octodecane slightly enhanced from 1.13% to 5.38%, indicating that decarbonylation of octadecanal is the major reaction route during deoxygenation of stearic acid. As expected, a limited amount of octadecanal was detected, further verifying the decarbonylation pathway of octadecanal to n-heptadecane. The content of octadecanol sharply declined with the increase in reaction time. This phenomenon can explain that the initial deoxygenation reaction proceeded initially by rapid hydrogenation of carboxylic group to octadecanal. Then, the obtained octadecanal hastily underwent hydrogenation to produce octadecanol, further producing n-octodecane through subsequent dehydration–hydrogenation. Equilibrium existed between octadecanal and octadecanol, as confirmed by the varied selectivities of n-heptadecane and n-octadecene versus reaction time. No other products, such as esters or ketones, were detected during deoxygenation, indicating the good HDO performance of CuCo-R catalyst. CO may react with H2 to produce methane and water in the reaction. The reaction pathway of stearic acid over CuCo-R catalyst was proposed based on these results (Scheme 1 ). The reaction pathway proceeds through an initial hydrogenation of carboxylic group on the surface of CuCo-R catalyst to form octadecanal as an intermediate. Then, the octadecanal is either rapidly decomposed by decarbonylation to n-heptadecane (major route) or converted into octadecanol by hydrogenation. Subsequently, octadecanol undergoes sequential dehydration–hydrogenation to obtain n-octadecane (minor route). In addition, equilibrium between octadecanal and octadecanol is achieved instantly in the overall reaction, which can be notably influenced by reaction time, and further affects the selectivity of n-heptadecane and octadecanol. One interesting feature is that the formation of n-heptadecane is favorable at reaction time higher than 1 h, accompanying by the selectivity of octadecanol sharply declined. The selectivity for n-heptadecane and octadecanol can be tuned simply by regulating the reaction time. This indirect synthetic methodology using inexpensive catalysts under facile conditions is a promising process that has the potential to enable a sustainable n-heptadecane and octadecanol production. In this study, CuCo-R catalyst prepared by wet impregnation possessed excellent activity for stearic acid deoxygenation with selectivity to long-chain alkanes of 94.82% at 100% conversion. The catalyst of CuCo solid solution can inhibit cracking reactions and promote production of diesel-like hydrocarbons, consistent with theoretical calculations. Moreover, the CuCo catalyst in the oxidation state underwent rapid in-situ reduction at the beginning of deoxygenation experiment, and the catalytic performance was comparable to that of reduced CuCo catalyst. High ratio of Cu0/(Cu++Cu0) and lattice Co atoms can result in high catalytic activity for stearic acid conversion. In addition, the reduced H2 pressure and amounts of catalysts show potential for large-scale industrial applications. This non-noble catalyst with high activity opens up a new possibility to upgrade microalgae-based bio-oils into high-grade transportation bio-fuels.
abstract: In this study, a novel carbon nanotube (CNT)-supported CuCo catalyst was successfully synthesized by wet impregnation. Experimental and theoretical results on catalytic deoxygenation of stearic acid, a model compound of microalgal bio-oil, revealed that the CuCo catalyst can effectively inhibit cracking reactions and promote production of diesel-like hydrocarbons. CuCo particles in the pores of CNTs were conducive to conversion of stearic acid to long-chain alkanes, featuring a high selectivity of 94.82% at 100% conversion. CuCo catalyst in oxidation state underwent fast in-situ reduction at the beginning of deoxygenation reaction, and its catalytic performance was comparable to that of reduced CuCo catalyst. The improved catalytic conversion of stearic acid to diesel-like hydrocarbons can be achieved at a high ratio of Cu0/(Cu++Cu0) and lattice Co atoms. In addition, the reduced H2 pressure and amounts of catalysts show potential for large-scale industrial applications.
title: Improved conversion of stearic acid to diesel-like hydrocarbons by carbon nanotubes-supported CuCo catalysts

full_text: Biomass can be thermochemically converted to gases. Some of the main applications of the gases generated from biomass are power generation, hydrogen and synthesis gas production. Nowadays, the use of biomass is considered an attractive alternative to fossil fuels because of the advantage that it does not contribute to the net generation of carbon dioxide in the earth's atmosphere. Thermochemical processes such as pyrolysis and steam gasification are currently being investigated in order to obtain gases from biomass. We have also considered the study of biomass gasification with CO2 to be of interest for two main reasons. The first is related to the presence of CO2 in the atmosphere of gasification reactions, so that this study can contribute to the knowledge of the role of CO2 in biomass gasification reactions and the final composition of product gas. The second concerns the use of CO2 as a possible gasifying agent of biomass. It is possible to obtain a rich-hydrogen gas from steam gasification or by catalytic steam reforming of liquids from pyrolysis [1,2]. In these processes a CO2 residual stream is generated. The possibility of recycling CO2 from the product gas and converting it into useful gases appears to be a promising option. These streams with high CO2 content could be used as a gasifying agent producing a synthesis gas with a low H2/CO ratio. This gas can be useful in oxo-synthesis reactions [3]. Some works [4,5] focusing on the influence of the reaction atmosphere on the thermochemical treatment of biomass have shown the specific role of carbon dioxide in the process. According to the results of Minkova et al. [4], the use of a mixture of steam and carbon dioxide generates the highest degree of carbonisation. It was also found that the reactive agents contribute to the activation of the solid residual material to transform it into activated carbon, and the mixture of CO2 and H2O achieves the highest activities for most of the raw materials tested. Pindoria et al. [5] present coincidental results, the chars prepared under carbon dioxide showing a more open porous structure and higher reactivity than the chars prepared under other reactive gases (steam and hydrogen). However, our interest is centered on the product gas obtained from thermochemical conversion of biomass. The works of Simell et al. [6–8] studied the role of carbon dioxide focusing on tar-decomposition in the biomass gasification process. One of these papers [7] studied the effects of CO2 reforming reactions using benzene as a model compound. In another work carried out at 900°C, using toluene as a model compound, Simell et al. [8] analyzed the effects of different materials, among them nickel catalyst and reaction atmospheres. Both steam and carbon dioxide (dry) reforming reactions of toluene took place at high rates over the nickel catalyst, and the dry reforming reaction was slightly faster. This result corroborates the fact that dry reforming reactions become thermodynamically more favourable than steam reforming reactions at temperatures >830°C. Corella et al. [9] have also pointed out the contribution of CO2 (dry) reforming of tars, CH4 and small hydrocarbons on the gas composition. Arauzo et al. [10] performed some experiments of CO2 and CO2-steam gasification of biomass and suggested the participation of CO2 in the gasification of carbon deposits on the catalyst surface. Nowadays, carbon dioxide reforming of methane is of great interest because of the environmental implications since both are greenhouse gases. Also, this reaction can be used in energy storage due to its high endothermicity, and produces a synthesis gas with a lower H2/CO ratio. The review of Wang et al. [11] analyzes the thermodynamics, catalyst selection and activity, reaction mechanism, and kinetics of carbon dioxide reforming of methane. Mleczko et al. [12] have proposed a novel reactor design for gas synthesis production from this reaction. Knowledge of the research carried out in carbon dioxide reforming of methane is useful in carbon dioxide gasification of biomass because of similarities such as the endothermicity and the catalyst development needed for the process. In this context, the present work studies CO2 gasification of biomass at 700°C in a bench-scale experimental installation based on Waterloo Fast Pyrolysis Process (WFPP) technology. A Ni–Al coprecipitated catalyst is introduced into the fluidized bed reactor where the thermal decomposition of biomass takes place. The experimental installation and the operating conditions are similar to those used in previous works of pyrolysis and steam gasification [13,14]. A comparison of the results found in pyrolysis, steam gasification and CO2 gasification will allow us to know the specific role of CO2 in these processes. The experiments have been carried out in a bench-scale installation based on Waterloo Fast Pyrolysis Process (WFPP) technology [15,16]. The most relevant items of equipment are the biomass feeder and the fluidized bed reactor. The feeder allows a continuous flow rate of biomass between 5 and 100 g/h. A detailed description of this feeder can be found in the literature [17]. Two streams are introduced into the reactor. One is of nitrogen and transports the biomass from the feeder into the bottom of the fluidized bed, while the other is of mixtures of nitrogen and the gasifiying agent (CO2 in CO2 gasification experiments). The total flow rate was the same in all the experiments in order to work with a constant residence time of the gases in the reaction bed. The product gas is cleaned of char particles using a cyclone. The liquid products, tar and water, are retained in a system of two condensers and a cotton filter. The gas flow rate is then measured using a dry testmeter, and the CO and CO2 concentrations are continuously determined by an infrared analyzer. In addition, gas samples are taken at regular time intervals and analyzed by chromatography to determine the percentages of H2, CO, CO2, CH4 and C2 (C2H2, C2H4 and C2H6). Reaction temperature, total gas flow, and CO and CO2 concentrations are registered by a data acquisition system. A scheme of this experimental system with minor modifications can be found elsewhere [13,14]. The experiments were carried out at 700°C and atmospheric pressure. The reaction bed was composed of catalyst diluted with sand. These solids have the same particle size between 150 and 350 μm. Catalyst weight, W, ranged from 2 to 16 g and the same bed volume (34.39 cm3) was used in all the experiments. The inlet biomass flow rate, mb, ranged from 21.2 to 34.1 g/h. The catalyst weight/biomass flow rate ratio, W/mb, varied from 0.546 to 0.065 h. One experiment without catalyst was also carried out. The inlet CO2 content was around 15% for the majority of the experiments. The total flow rate into the reactor was around 1715 cm3 (STP)/min. Under the operating conditions, the theoretical minimum fluidization velocity was 2 cm/s and the experimental velocity used more than 10 times this theoretical value. No problems of attrition or segregation were observed. The biomass processed was pine sawdust with a moisture content of about 10% and a particle size of −350+150 μm. The results of the elemental analysis (in mass%) of the pine sawdust are carbon 48.27%, hydrogen 6.45%, nitrogen 0.09%, and oxygen (by difference) 45.19%. The Ni/Al catalyst was prepared in the laboratory by coprecipitation following the method described by Al-Ubaid and Wolf [18]. Nickel nitrate hexahydrate and aluminum nitrate nonahydrate were dissolved in distilled water and precipitated with ammonium hydroxide. The Ni:Al ratio used was 1:2. The catalyst was calcined in an air flow until the final calcination temperature of 750°C during 3 h. The precursor and the calcined catalyst were characterized by various techniques such as nitrogen adsorption, mercury porosimetry, X-ray diffraction (XRD), atomic emission spectrometry by inductively coupled plasma (ICP), thermogravimetric analysis, and temperature-programmed reduction (TPR). More details about the catalyst preparation and characterization can be found in a previous paper [19]. The catalyst employed was not reduced before the biomass reaction owing to the results obtained in previous works [14,19], in which the same catalyst was reduced by the reaction atmosphere in pyrolysis and steam gasification experiments. Table 1 presents the global experimental results of CO2 catalytic gasification. This table includes the values of some experimental variables such as catalyst weight, W, sawdust feeding rate, mb, catalyst weight/biomass flow rate ratio, W/mb, CO2/biomass ratio, CO2/B, and reaction time. The yields of gas, liquid and char have been calculated dividing the total amount of each product obtained by the total sawdust fed. The recovery has been calculated dividing the sum of gas, liquid and char products by the total sawdust and CO2 fed. The yields of different gases, H2, CO, CO2, CH4 and C2, are also presented. All these yields are calculated dividing the total amount obtained by the total sawdust fed. The C2 yield includes C2H4, C2H6 and C2H2 yields. This table also presents the average global gas composition (N2 and steam free). The total gas yield shows values higher than 1, due to the fact that CO2 is considered in the product gas but not when it is introduced as a gasifying agent. Liquid product includes tar and water. Some char yields are almost zero, which could be due to some experimental error because the weight of char is very small compared with the weight of the reactor. Although the sawdust feeding rate and reaction time were not the same for all the experiments, the analysis of these global results can show some tendencies. For instance, for the experiments with a CO2/B ratio close to 1 the general tendency shows a decrease in total gas yield and an increase in liquid yield when the W/mb ratio diminishes. Concerning gas yields, H2 and CO decrease while CH4 and C2 increase when the W/mb ratio diminishes. The tendency of the CO2 yield does not appear very clear, which could be due to the influence of the CO2/B ratio on the final gas composition. For two experiments with a similar CO2/B ratio, runs 1 and 10, the decrease of the W/mb ratio causes an increase in CO2 yield. The observed tendencies for gas yields are also observed for gas composition. Thus, H2 and CO contents decrease while CO2, CH4 and C2 contents increase when the W/mb ratio diminishes. Considering these results, the catalyst can be seen to have an important effect on CO2 gasification of biomass. The catalyst transforms liquid products (tars) into gas and modifies the gas composition, increasing H2 and CO yields and decreasing CH4 and C2 yields. The catalytic CO2 reforming reactions are involved in the final gas composition. These CO2 reforming reactions are: (1) Tar+CO 2⇒nH 2+mCO (2) CH 4+CO 2⇔2H 2+2CO (3) C 2 H 4+2CO 2⇔2H 2+4CO A comparison of runs 7 and 8, carried out with a W/mb ratio close to 0.16 h and similar sawdust feeding rates, shows that the increase of the CO2/B ratio increases total gas and liquid yields, and the CO yield also increases. The inverse water–gas shift reaction (Eq. (4)) can explain the increase in CO and liquid yields. (4) CO 2+H 2⇔CO+H 2 O The inverse water–gas shift also occurs as a side reaction in carbon dioxide reforming of methane [11,20,21]. The analysis of the global experimental results has shown some general tendencies, including the influence of the W/mb ratio on gas yields. In the light of these results, we decided to carry out a more detailed study to analyze the influence of the W/mb ratio with time for different gases. With this purpose, the results of the experiments with a CO2/B ratio close to 1 and W/mb ratios ranging from 0.505 to 0.065 h have been represented vs. time. The results of non-catalytic CO2 gasification have also been included for comparison. Run 1 with the highest W/mb ratio has not been represented because of some problems caused by bed agglomeration. The conversion of CO2 to other valuable gases is one of our objectives, and the percentage of CO2 conversion vs. time is represented in Fig. 1 . This percentage is calculated as the subtraction between the CO2 in the inlet gas and the CO2 in the exit gas divided by the CO2 in the inlet gas. As can be observed, a significant CO2 conversion is obtained in the catalytic process compared with non-catalytic CO2 gasification. In the non-catalytic process the percentage of CO2 conversion is almost zero. In the catalytic process, the increase of CO2 conversion can be observed when the W/mb ratio increases. The highest value of CO2 conversion is around 50% and is obtained for the experiment with a W/mb ratio of 0.505 h. The smallest values of CO2 conversion in the catalytic process are higher than 10%. For a given W/mb ratio, the decrease of CO2 conversion is observed when the reaction time increases. This could be due to the catalyst deactivation. However, at a short reaction time for W/mb ratios higher than 0.069 h, the percentage of CO2 conversion increases with time. This fact can be related to the activation of the catalyst by the reaction atmosphere. H2 and CO generated in the thermal decomposition of biomass is able to reduce the catalyst, as occurs in pyrolysis and steam gasification [14,19]. Figs. 2 and 3 represent H2 and CO yields, respectively, during the reaction time. The H2 and CO yields show similar tendencies: a decrease of gas yields when the W/mb ratio diminishes and a decrease of gas yields with reaction time. For the experiment with a W/mb ratio of 0.505 h, an increase of gas yields is observed at longer reaction time. The activation of the catalyst at short reaction time can also be observed. These results are common to those found in the analysis of CO2 conversion. However, a different tendency than that for CO2 conversion is observed when the yields of H2 and CO in the catalytic process are compared with those obtained in the non-catalytic CO2 gasification. For W/mb ratios ≤0.069 h, the yields of product gases after 40 min of experimental time are similar to the values of the non-catalytic process. In Fig. 3, it can be seen that CO yields are higher than 1 for W/mb ratios higher than 0.147 h. This result could indicate the conversion of the gasifying gas, CO2, to CO. The participation of CO2 reforming reactions (Eqs. (1)–(3)) and the inverse water–gas shift reaction (Eq. (4)) can explain this fact. For the experiment with a W/mb ratio of 0.505 h, H2 and CO yields after more than 80 min are close to those obtained at a short reaction time. This result indicates that under these operating conditions the catalyst is not deactivated. The use of high W/mb ratios could therefore be considered an important parameter in the design of an industrial application. The variation of H2 and CO yields with time for the experiment with a W/mb ratio of 0.505 h could be explained by some problems of bed agglomeration (similar to that for the experiment with a W/mb ratio of 0.546 h) and by the participation of the Boudouard equilibrium reaction (Eq. (5)). (5) CO 2+C⇔2CO In the experiment with a W/mb ratio of 0.505 h, it may be possible to regenerate the catalyst “in situ”. The coke deposited on the active sites of the catalyst could be gasified with CO2. The W/mb ratio used could favour this reaction. Fig. 4 represents the C2 yield evolution with time. An increase in the C2 yield can be observed when the W/mb ratio diminishes, and for a given W/mb ratio the C2 yield increases with time. The highest C2 yields in the catalytic process are obtained at high reaction time and for the smallest W/mb ratios are smaller than those obtained in the non-catalytic process. The CH4 yield shows a similar tendency to that for the C2 yield (Fig. 5) . However, the main difference is found for the W/mb ratios ≥0.373 h, where the CH4 yields are almost constant over time. Considering the results obtained, it can be concluded that the catalyst deactivation causes the decrease of H2 and CO yields and the increase of CH4 and C2 yields. CO2 conversion decreases with the loss of catalyst activity. The important effect of the W/mb ratio on catalyst deactivation can also be seen. A comparison of the gas yields obtained in the catalytic process when the catalyst is deactivated (at high reaction time and for the smallest W/mb ratios) with those corrresponding to the non-catalytic gasification has shown that the H2 and CO yields are similar, while the CH4 and C2 yields and the percentage of CO2 conversion are higher for the catalytic process. These results indicate that the catalyst is not totally deactivated and that the residual activity could be related to the CH4 and C2–CO2 reforming reactions. Fig. 6 represents the H2/CO (vol/vol) ratio vs. time for different W/mb ratios. It can be observed that at the beginning of the experiment the H2/CO ratio is around 0.7 for all the W/mb ratios studied. For a given W/mb ratio, the H2/CO ratio decreases with time and the general tendency shows this decrease when the W/mb ratio diminishes. An exception is found for the experiment with a W/mb ratio of 0.277 h, which shows the highest H2/CO ratios and values almost constant with time. This result could be related to the slightly higher CO2/B ratio used. In order to analyze the activity of the catalyst before deactivation occurs, the yield of gases at the initial time of the experiment has been obtained. The values of the yield of different gases over time have been extrapolated to zero time and compared with those obtained in the non-catalytic process (W/mb=0). Fig. 7 shows H2, CO, CO2, CH4 and C2 yields at initial time of the experiment vs. the W/mb ratio for the experiments carried out with a CO2/B ratio of around 1. A marked increase in H2 and CO yields can be observed while CO2, CH4 and C2 yields decrease very significantly when the W/mb ratio ranges from 0 to 0.065 h. This fact indicates that the Ni/Al coprecipitated catalyst used is very active for CO2 gasification of biomass. For W/mb ratios ≥0.3 h, no significant modification in gas yields at initial time is observed. It was considered useful to compare the experimental results with those corresponding to thermodynamic equilibrium. A good agreement can be observed between the experimentally obtained gas composition at the beginning of the experiment and that corresponding to thermodynamic equilibrium (Fig. 8) . The gas composition is represented on a N2 and steam free basis. Although for the equilibrium composition water is present in the gas, water was not considered in the experimentally determined composition as the water is condensed with tar and was therefore not analyzed. This fact could explain the small differences between the experimental and equilibrium results. Previous works [13,14] have described the performance of the catalyst in pyrolysis and steam gasification. It has been considered of interest to compare the initial yield of different gases vs. the W/mb ratio at a reaction temperature of 700°C for pyrolysis, steam gasification and CO2 gasification. Because a different performance is observed in the initial yields of H2, CO, CO2, CH4 and C2, figures for all gases are shown. The results represented in Figs. 9–13 correspond to steam gasification experiments carried out with a steam/biomass (S/B) ratio close to 1, and for the CO2 gasification experiments, the CO2/B ratio was around 1. In Fig. 9, H2 initial yields are represented. For the three processes the increase of H2 initial yields with the W/mb ratio is asymptotic, and for W/mb ratios ≥ 0.4 h no significant modifications are observed. It can be seen that the highest H2 yields are obtained in steam gasification, while very similar values are obtained in pyrolysis and CO2 gasification. The changes of the CO initial yield with the W/mb ratio are similar to those of the H2 initial yield, as is shown in Fig. 10. However, the highest initial CO yields are obtained in CO2 gasification with values up to 1.4 g CO/g sawdust. The initial CO yields of pyrolysis reach values higher than 0.8 g CO/g sawdust, while the highest values of steam gasification are around 0.6 g CO/g sawdust. The evolution of the CO2 initial yield with the W/mb ratio depends on the process (Fig. 11). For pyrolysis and CO2 gasification, the CO2 yield decreases when the W/mb ratio increases, while for steam gasification the CO2 yield increases when the W/mb ratio increases for W/mb ratios ≤0.4 h. At higher W/mb ratios the changes in the CO2 initial yields are not noticeable, the values obtained being smaller than 0.06 g CO2/g sawdust in pyrolysis, 0.6 g CO2/g sawdust in CO2 gasification and around 0.75 g CO2/g sawdust in steam gasification. The initial yields of CH4 and C2, represented in Figs. 12 and 13, respectively, decrease when the W/mb ratio increases. For high W/mb ratios, in all three processes, the initial C2 yield tends very clearly towards zero. The highest C2 initial yields for all W/mb ratios are obtained in pyrolysis while CO2 gasification and steam gasification present similar lower values of C2 initial yields. The CH4 initial yields generated in CO2 gasification are very similar to those corresponding to pyrolysis while the values for steam gasification present the lowest values. The H2/CO (vol/vol) ratio in the product gas can influence its posterior use. The highest values of H2/CO (vol/vol) ratio correspond to steam gasification, with values at the beginning of the experiment ranging from 4 to 2 when the S/B ratio decreases from 1.5 to 0.53. For a S/B ratio of 1 the H2/CO ratio is around 3. The initial H2/CO ratio in pyrolysis is around 1, and the smallest values are obtained in CO2 gasification (0.7). In order to explain the yields of the different gases, obtained in the three processes, we can consider a very simple two step model. The first step for each process is non-catalytic pyrolysis, where the biomass decomposes to gases, tars and char. In the second step, the catalyst and gasifying agents react with the products formed in the previous step, generating the final product distribution and gas composition. The results of gas yields obtained in catalytic steam gasification at 700°C [14] indicated the participation of the catalyst in steam reforming reactions of tars, CH4 and C2; the water gas shift reaction and the gasification of char with steam and CO2. (6) Tar+H 2 O⇒pH 2+qCO (7) CH 4+H 2 O⇔3H 2+CO (8) C 2 H 4+2H 2 O⇔4H 2+2CO (9) CO+H 2 O⇔H 2+CO 2 (10) C+H 2 O⇔H 2+CO To explain the results obtained in CO2 catalytic gasification, CO2 reforming reactions of tars (Eq. (1)), CH4 (Eq. (2)) and C2 (Eq. (3)) can explain the decrease of these compounds, the increase of H2 and CO yields and the increase of CO2 conversion when the W/mb ratio increases. The participation of the inverse water–gas shift reaction (Eq. (4)) has also been proposed. It can be considered that H2 production in catalytic pyrolysis is mainly due to the catalytic cracking reactions of tars, CH4 and C2. The similar H2 initial yields obtained in pyrolysis and CO2 gasification could indicate the participation of the inverse water–gas shift reaction (Eq. (4)) in the CO2 catalytic gasification. Steam reforming reactions produce higher hydrogen yields than CO2 reforming reactions. This can be observed by comparing reactions (2) and (7) for the CH4 conversion. The participation of the water–gas shift reaction in steam gasification of biomass and the inverse water–gas shift reaction in CO2 gasification influences the generation of higher hydrogen yields in steam gasification than in CO2 gasification. These reactions can explain the highest hydrogen yields being obtained in catalytic steam gasification. The water–gas shift reaction can explain the lower yield of CO in steam gasification than in pyrolysis. This reaction must also be involved in the significant influence that the S/B ratio in the feed exerts on the H2/CO ratio in the product gas. The important increase of the CO2 yield observed in steam gasification can also be explained by the water–gas shift reaction. Another interesting result is the different influence of the reaction atmosphere on CH4 and C2 yields. The experimental results indicate that CO2 reforming of CH4 is more difficult than steam reforming of CH4. This could be due to the lower reaction rate of carbon dioxide reforming of CH4, which is in accordance with the findings of other authors [20]. The participation of steam reforming reactions in the CO2 catalytic gasification of biomass could also be considered. Steam is generated in the inverse water–gas shift reaction and could react faster than CO2. However, the evidence for this possibility is not clear. Over a longer reaction time, the Boudouard equilibrium reaction (Eq. (5)) and the gasification of carbon deposits with steam (Eq. (10)) could also be involved in the final gas composition obtained. The participation of these reactions in CO2 catalytic gasification and steam catalytic gasification could contribute to the regeneration of the catalyst that would occur simultaneously with the biomass gasification process. CO2 gasification of biomass at 700°C and atmospheric pressure using a Ni/Al coprecipitated catalyst has been studied. The experiments have been carried out with W/mb ratios from 0.546 h to 0, in the case of the non-catalytic CO2 gasification. The CO2/B ratio ranged from 0.57 to 1.39. The influence of the reaction atmosphere has also been analyzed. The main conclusions obtained are as follows. (1) CO2, used as a gasifying agent, is converted into valuable gases in catalytic biomass gasification. (2) The W/mb ratio influences very significantly the product gas obtained. A decrease in the W/mb ratio diminishes H2 and CO yields, while CO2, CH4 and C2 yields increase. (3) For a given W/mb ratio, H2 and CO yields decrease with the reaction time, while CO2, CH4 and C2 yields increase. These gas evolutions are due to the loss of catalyst activity. The deactivation of the catalyst is faster when the W/mb ratio is smaller. (4) The Ni/Al coprecipitated catalyst used is very active for CO2 gasification of biomass. For W/mb ratios ≥0.3 h, the initial yields of several gases do not vary significantly. For these values of W/mb ratio, the experimentally determined gas composition of CO2 catalytic gasification is close to the corresponding thermodynamic equilibrium. (5) A significant influence of reaction atmosphere on gas product is observed when the pyrolysis, steam gasification and CO2 gasification processes are compared. The participation of steam reforming reactions of tars and light hydrocarbons together with the water–gas shift reaction can explain the results found in the steam gasification of biomass, while CO2 reforming reactions and the inverse water–gas shift reaction can explain the results of CO2 gasification of biomass. The authors express their gratitude to the Fundación Caja de Madrid for the research grant and financial support awarded to L. Garcia. The acknowledgement is also extended to the Ministerio de Educación y Cultura (Spain) for providing financial support for the study (Project 2FD97-0890).
abstract: Catalytic CO2 gasification of pine sawdust has been carried out at a relatively low temperature, 700°C, and at atmospheric pressure. The Ni/Al catalyst used was prepared by coprecipitation and calcined at 750°C for 3 h. The influence of the catalyst weight/biomass flow rate (W/mb) ratio on product distribution and gas composition was analyzed. Using a CO2/biomass ratio of around 1, the increase of the W/mb ratio increases H2 and CO yields while CH4 and C2 yields decrease. Deactivation of the catalyst was also observed under the experimental conditions employed. The influence of the W/mb ratio on the initial gas yields has been also analyzed. For W/mb ratios ≥0.3 h, no significant modifications are observed on the initial yields of different gases, and it is confirmed that under these conditions the initial gas composition is close to that for thermodynamic equilibrium. The influence of the reaction atmosphere on gas yields has also been carried out, analyzing the results obtained in pyrolysis, steam gasification and CO2 gasification.
title: CO2 as a gasifying agent for gas production from pine sawdust at low temperatures using a Ni/Al coprecipitated catalyst
