full_text: The rapid development of automobile transportation worldwide brings about the great increase of fuel consumption. As a consequence, air pollution caused by exhaust gas has become more and more severe. For this reason, governments all over the world have been tightening regulations on the compositions of gasoline, such as aromatic and olefin contents and sulfur-containing compounds content. In China, approximately 80% of gasoline comes from FCC processes in which the olefin content is usually as high as 50–65 vol.%. It is the high olefin content of the gasoline that causes the terrible automobile emission problem. At present, however, it is difficult to change the gasoline pool composition remarkably because of the lack of catalytic reforming, alkylation, isomerization and oxygenated compounds producing units. Therefore, lowering the olefin content in the FCC gasoline is a critical way to meet the governmental regulations. Various measures have been taken to control the olefin content of FCC gasoline. It has been found that by means of operation optimization of FCC units and the use of novel olefin reduction FCC catalysts, the olefin content could be decreased to around 35 vol.%, which is still much higher than the upper limit 20 vol.% regulated by the International Charter on clean fuels. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a novel technique to greatly reduce the olefin content in gasoline while preserving gasoline octane number. To achieve this goal, a FCC gasoline upgrading process with balanced hydroisomerization and aromatization performance is desired [1]. By this way, the loss of gasoline octane number due to olefin reduction can be compensated by the increased contents of isoparaffins and aromatics that have higher octane number. ZSM-5 zeolites are widely used not only in the oligomerization and aromatization of light olefins, but also in the transformation of light paraffins to aromatics [2–5]. In a previous study, we found that the parent HZSM-5 zeolite catalyst with crystals in nanoscale had stronger ability and better stability for olefin reduction than the microscale ones [6], and that more olefins were transformed into aromatics (from 2 to 64 vol.%) with a high RON using the FCC light fraction as feedstock. In order to further improve the catalytic stability in FCC gasoline upgrading process, modification of the parent nanoscale HZSM-5 was carried out. Hydrothermal treatment is usually employed to adjust the acidity of HZSM-5 zeolite and improve its stability [7]. In general, it has been shown that mild steaming conditions favor the generation of catalytic sites with specific efficiency. Catalysts dealuminated by means of steam treatment generate a secondary pore structure that can contribute to the enhancement of aromatization stability by reducing diffusion limitations [8]. The effect of hydrothermal treatment on microscale HZSM-5 has been studied widely in relation to their catalytic performances, while it is seldom reported for nanoscale HZSM-5. During hydrothermal treatment the steaming temperature is the most important factor to be considered. In this paper, nanoscale HZSM-5 zeolite was hydrothermally treated with a steam of ammonia at different temperatures and then loaded with lanthanum and zinc oxides which can improve the catalyst aromatization activity, isomerization activity and catalytic stability [9,10]. The catalytic performance of the prepared catalysts in FCC gasoline upgrading process was investigated. The compositions of FCC gasoline were shown as follows: olefins 49.6 vol. %, aromatics 11.4 vol.%, paraffins 39.0 vol.%, RON 92.2 and the ratio of i-paraffins/n-paraffins 5.1. The synthesis of nanoscale ZSM-5 followed the procedure described in a previous publication [11]. The molar ratio of SiO2/Al2O3 was 25.5. By extruding a mixture of 80 wt.% nanoscale NaZSM-5 with 20 wt.% γ-alumina (pseudo-boehmite), a strip form nanoscale NaZSM-5 catalyst was obtained (∅1 mm×2 mm). After it had been dried at 393 K for 12 h, then calcined at 813 K for 3 h, ion exchange (three times) was carried out with 0.4 mol/L ammonium nitrate, and then calcined at 813 K for 3 h. The obtained nanoscale HZSM-5 catalyst was labeled as Catalyst A. Catalyst A was hydrothermally treated with water containing 0.8 wt.% NH3 for 3 h (WHSV 0.2 h− 1) at different temperatures. Then, supported catalysts were prepared using an incipient wet impregnation method. The precursors of the active components La and Zn were lanthanum nitrate and zinc nitrate, respectively. Finally, the impregnated solids were dried at 393 K overnight and calcined at 813 K for 3 h. Table 1 lists the prepared catalysts. The crystal size and morphology of nanoscale HZSM-5 were determined with a JEOL JSM-6700F Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM). Silicon and aluminum content of the zeolites were obtained on a Bruker SRS 3400 spectrometer. The acid properties of the parent nanoscale HZSM-5 and the modified catalysts were measured with a Quantachrome CHEMBET 3000 using a method of temperature programmed desorption (TPD) of ammonia. Acid sites and acid type distribution were determined with infrared spectroscopy (IR) of chemisorbed pyridine. All spectra were recorded on an EQUINOX55 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer at a resolution of 4 cm− 1. The catalyst samples were finely ground and pressed into a self-supporting wafer (8–10 mg/cm2, diameter=15 mm), then placed into an infrared cell with KBr windows. An evacuation at 773 K (ca. 10− 4 Torr) for 4 h was carried out prior to the adsorption of pyridine. IR spectra were recorded after a subsequent evacuation at increasing temperatures from 423 to 723 K (1 h at each temperature). Catalytic activity measurements were carried out in a continuous-flow fixed bed reactor (ID 10 mm). Reaction conditions were as follows: temperature 643 K, hydrogen pressure 3.0 MPa, H2/gasoline (volume ratios) 600, weight space velocity of FCC gasoline 3 h− 1, catalyst loading 2 g. The composition of the liquid product was analyzed on a SRI 8610 gas chromatograph equipped with a flame ionization detector and an OV-101 capillary column (50.0 m×0.32 mm). Research octane number of the samples was calculated from PONA composition. Fig. 1 is the SEM picture of the nanoscale ZSM-5, which shows that the crystal size of ZSM-5 is about 50–80 nm. Fig. 2 presents the NH3-TPD patterns of both the parent and the modified nanoscale HZSM-5 catalysts. A decrease in acid sites quantity and strength was observed with increasing temperature of hydrothermal treatment. When steaming above 400 °C, strong acid sites on the modified catalysts decreased drastically, which could prevent them from coke deposition and improve their stability. In order to discriminate between Lewis and Brönsted acid sites, pyridine adsorption/desorption experiments were carried out followed by FT-IR measurements. As shown in Figs. 3 and 4 , the total Lewis/Brönsted acidity and the medium and strong Lewis/Brönsted acidity can be calculated after desorption at 300 °C and 450 °C, respectively. Typically IR bands at 1545 and 1452 cm−1 correspond to characteristic bands of chemisorbed pyridine molecules on Brönsted and Lewis acid sites, respectively, and the IR bands at 1491 cm−1 are ascribed to the synergistic effect between Brönsted and Lewis acids. Table 2 gives quantitative information about the total acidity and the ratio of Lewis to Brönsted acid of the catalysts. According to the data, it is clear that the total Lewis/Brönsted acidity and the medium and strong Lewis/Brönsted acidity of the catalysts increase notably after modification. It may be probably the results of dealumination and the creation of Lewis acidic external framework aluminum during steaming and introduction of Zn into HZSM-5 zeolites. This phenomenon was also observed by Motz et al. [12] and Smieskova et al. [13]. Fig. 5 shows the changes of olefin content in product oil on the modified catalysts in FCC gasoline upgrading process. It can be seen that Catalyst C had not only a strong olefin reduction ability but also the best stability in olefin reduction in FCC gasoline. The olefin content in FCC gasoline was lowered from 49.6 to 8.1 vol.% on this catalyst. Catalyst B also showed a strong ability of reducing olefin content in FCC gasoline at the very beginning, but the olefin content increased drastically with time on stream. Combining it with the NH3-TPD curves, the poor stability of Catalyst B in reducing olefin content can be attributed to the high acidity of Catalyst B. Fig. 6 illustrates the aromatization activity of the modified catalysts in FCC gasoline upgrading process. Since aromatics generally have high octane numbers, an appropriate increase of aromatic content can conduce to the compensation of the loss of gasoline octane number due to olefin reduction. It can be seen that Catalyst C showed a high aromatization activity and the best stability among the modified catalysts. The aromatic content increased from 11.4 to 29.1 vol.%. Aromatization requires not only Brönsted acid but also Lewis acid [14]. A suitable ratio of Lewis acid to Brönsted acid and their synergistic effects are critical for aromatization. Table 2 shows that the ratio of total Lewis acid to total Brönsted acid is 2.3 at which high aromatization activity and stability were achieved. The isomerization activity of the modified catalysts is shown in Table 3 . Isoparaffins also have high octane number and therefore, converting olefins in FCC gasoline into isoparaffins can offset the loss of gasoline octane number due to olefin reduction. From Table 3, it can be found that Catalyst C had the highest isomerization activity and the ratio of i-paraffins/n-paraffins increased from 5.1 to 6.7–7.0. The results above demonstrate that Catalyst C had excellent aromatization activity and isomerization activity to reduce the olefin content in FCC gasoline. To further study the stability of catalytic performance of Catalyst C, a 300 h time on stream evaluation was performed. The results are presented in Table 4 . It can be seen that the modified nanoscale ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst has not only a stronger olefin reduction ability but also better stability in FCC gasoline upgrading process. The olefin content in the FCC gasoline decreases from 49.6 to 12.8 vol.% on this modified nanoscale ZSM-5 catalyst. Meanwhile, its ability for olefin reduction did not change much during the 300 h time on stream. The aromatic content increased from 11.4 to 24.7 vol.%, the paraffin content increased from 39.0 to 62.5 vol.%, while the benzene content was about 1.0 vol.%. Also, its aromatization and isomerization activity were found to be stable. Its RON was preserved while the olefin content was reduced greatly. The reason that the RON of gasoline remains unchanged is that olefins in FCC gasoline is transformed into isoparaffins and aromatics which have high octane numbers. The NH3-TPD and IR results show that the acid strength of the parent nanoscale HZSM-5 is weakened and the strong acid sites are decreased significantly. From the point of view of the reaction results, Catalyst C has the most suitable acidity with a Lewis/Brönsted acid sites ratio of 2.3 which resulted from the combined-modification. The nanoscale HZSM-5 catalyst, hydrothermally treated with ammonia water at 400 °C and loaded with lanthanum and zinc oxides, exhibited excellent aromatization activity, isomerization activity and high ability of reducing olefin content in FCC gasoline without a RON loss. One of the modified catalysts showed excellent stability during a test run with 300 h time on stream. This catalytic process is competent of producing gasoline to meet the requirement of the Europe Union III standard. The authors are grateful to Prof. Chunshan Song and Dr. Shuguang Zhang for comments.
abstract: Nanoscale HZSM-5 zeolite was hydrothermally treated with ammonia water at different temperatures and then loaded with La2O3 and ZnO. The parent and the modified nanoscale HZSM-5 catalysts were characterized by SEM, NH3-TPD, IR and XRF. The performance of the modified HZSM-5 catalysts for FCC gasoline upgrading was evaluated in a fixed bed reactor in the presence of hydrogen. The results indicated that the modified catalyst which was hydrothermally treated at 400 °C exhibited excellent aromatization activity, isomerization activity and higher ability of reducing olefin content in FCC gasoline. Under the given reaction conditions, the olefin content in FCC gasoline could be decreased from 49.6 to 8.1 vol.%. The catalytic performance of the modified nanoscale ZSM-5 catalyst hardly changed within 300 h time on stream, and the research octane number (RON) of gasoline was preserved.
title: Effect of hydrothermal treatment temperature on FCC gasoline upgrading properties of the modified nanoscale ZSM-5 catalyst

full_text: Gasification of biomass to syngas, followed by catalytic conversion of synthesis gas to mixed alcohols is a promising route for the production of bioethanol [1]. Improvements in catalyst design and process development need to be achieved to make this route commercially viable [2]. To the best of our knowledge, presently there is only one thermochemical process, proposed by Enerkem company, operating at commercial scale to produce ethanol, besides methanol and other chemicals [3]. In this case ethanol is planned to be produced by an indirect route using methyl acetate as intermediate product. However, ethanol production is not expected until the second half of 2016. The reaction mechanism of mixed alcohol synthesis over alkali-doped molybdenum sulfide catalysts was studied by Santiesteban et al. [4], who concluded that ethanol is produced from methanol through a CO insertion mechanism. This means that in a mixed alcohol process, methanol produced in the reactor could be recycled back to the reactor along with syngas to further increase ethanol production. However, the literature where methanol co-feeding is experimentally studied is very scarce. Gunturu [5] ran experiments based on a fractional-factorial experimental design, in which 0, 2 and 4% mole concentration of methanol in the feed were evaluated. Results indicated that methanol co-feeding enhances CO conversion and the productivity of ethanol and higher alcohols but also hydrocarbons. Quarderer et al. [6] reported significant improvement in higher alcohol productivity using a K/MoS2 catalyst by injecting methanol up to 7.5% mole concentration in the feed. Portillo et al. [7] studied the effect of methanol addition at different reaction temperatures (280 to 300°C) and methanol concentrations in the feed (from 0% to 8.5%). The experiments revealed that carbon monoxide conversion, ethanol and higher alcohol productivities increase linearly with methanol content in the feed at any reaction temperature, while hydrocarbon productivity increases exponentially. Therefore, there is a trade-off between increasing alcohol productivity and the selective conversion of methanol to alcohols. The addition of methanol has a positive influence in the ethanol selectivity only at low methanol content in the feed so the optimum amount of methanol recycle to the reactor in an ethanol production process with this kind of catalyst should be determined by an economic analysis [7]. Recently, Rodrigo Suárez et al. [8] also studied the effect of methanol co-feeding for various mole concentrations of methanol in the feed (1.5% to 10%) at typical operating conditions for this kind of catalyst (71bar, 340°C, 6000NL/hgcat, H2/CO=1). The results indicated that under these conditions, methanol recycle is not a viable option to boost higher alcohol production because it results in an increase in methane yield, while the effect on higher alcohols was negative or not significant. Therefore, there is still not full agreement about the effect of methanol addition. Experiments with methanol co-feeding may be of interest to generate a model able to predict how the catalyst may perform as a result of significant methanol recycle in order to design a process that optimizes methanol recirculation. Several kinetic models have been developed for alkali doped MoS2 catalysts [9,10,11,12]. Of these, Gunturu [5] was the first to develop a kinetic model from an experimental design where methanol mole fraction in the feed (0–4%) was among the variables considered in the experiments. The kinetic model consisted of a system of Langmuir-Hinshelwood and power-law type reactions to describe the kinetic scheme proposed by Santiesteban [4]. Larsson et al. [13] slightly reformulated and refitted the parameters of this kinetic model and validated its prediction capability against experiments that did not feature methanol co-feeding. However, Philips et al. [14] used Larsson's kinetic model to study the effect of methanol recycle at different temperatures when designing a thermochemical ethanol production process from biomass. Later, Dutta et al. [15] continued the design work of Phillips et al., but in this case they used a kinetic model of promoted MoS2 catalyst developed by The Dow Chemical Company to optimize methanol recycle. Unfortunately, this model is proprietary of Dow Chemical and it is not available publicly. In this study, experiments with and without methanol co-feeding at different operating conditions and reactor scale (laboratory and bench) were carried out with the aim of generating and validating a kinetic model able to predict how the catalyst may perform as a result of significant methanol co-feeding, which is not available in the literature. An alkali-Co doped MoS2 catalyst produced by an industrial manufacturer based on a patented formulation was used (named as catalyst I in the patent) [16]. The contents of Mo, Co and K were determined using an ICP Horiba Jobin Yvon to confirm that the measured composition of the catalyst was that of the patent (Table 1 ). The BET surface of the catalyst was 6.56m2/g. Catalytic studies were carried out at lab and bench scale facilities. The lab scale experimental setup consisted of a high-pressure fixed bed reactor (inner diameter: 8.3mm, length: 330mm, stainless steel) where a bed of catalyst particles (5g) was placed inside the reactor supported with silicon carbide. Details of the experimental setup at lab scale have been published elsewhere [7]. Fig. 1 shows a diagram of the experimental setup at bench scale. Syngas (CO+H2), N2 and two mixtures of H2S and H2 were supplied from pressurized cylinders. Nitrogen was used to test leaks and to flush the system. A mixture of 10% H2S in H2 was used for pretreatment of the catalyst, while a mixture of 400ppm H2S in H2 was used to maintain 50ppm H2S concentration in the feed gas to the reactor and maintain the activity (sulfidity) of the catalysts. The syngas, with a molar ratio of H2/CO~1, was stored in a tank using a dual stage compressor. The total flow of syngas fed to the reactor was controlled with a mass flow controller (MFC3). In methanol co-feeding experiments, methanol was fed directly to the reactor using a HPLC pump. Before being fed to the reactor, the syngas passed through a carbonyl filter to prevent catalyst poisoning by Fe and Ni carbonyls. The reaction was carried out in a high pressure fixed bed reactor (3/4″ Sch 80 and 1500mm length). A bed of catalyst particles (100g) was placed inside the reactor supported with CSi. Five spiral electrical resistances distributed along the reactor supply heat to maintain the reactor temperature, which was measured by five thermocouples positioned perpendicular to the bed. Each thermocouple-resistance pair was associated with a PID controller that controls the temperature in a section of the reactor through a spiral resistance. Downstream of the reactor, the pressure was maintained with a pressure control valve. The reactor outlet stream was in vapor-phase and an aliquot was analyzed online using a gas chromatograph detection system (Agilent 7890A) equipped with a thermal conductivity detector and two flame ionization detectors, which use helium as carrier gas. The product separation system was comprised of a heat exchanger, a condenser, a storage tank for collecting liquid and a pressure control valve. The heat exchanger was a double pipe exchanger whose function was to cool and condense the gases down to 30°C. The unreacted synthesis gas and products circulate inside the inner tube. The flow of cooling water in the outer tube was controlled with a regulating valve. The condenser further cooled the gases down to 20°C with a Peltier cell and separated the gas-phase and the liquid-phase. To control the pressure in the condenser and prevent loss of ethanol in the gas-phase, a pressure control valve was used. The gas-phase stream can be purged or compressed with a single stage compressor and then be stored in a recycle storage tank. Once there is sufficient gas in the tank, it is analyzed, and can be fed to the reactor together with syngas in order to study the effect of the gas recycle to the reactor. A reaction scheme for higher alcohol synthesis over sulfide molybdenum catalysts was taken from the literature and a power law model was assumed for the reaction rate of compounds. Experiments at laboratory and bench scale reactors were carried out to obtain experimental data that allow regression and validation of the kinetic parameters, respectively. For the fitting, the differential mole balance equations in the tubular reactor for the main compounds (carbon monoxide, hydrogen, methanol, ethanol, propanol, methane, carbon dioxide and water) needed to be solved. For that purpose, an initial guess of the kinetic parameters was previously obtained with a simplified method that assumes constant molar flow along the reactor so the differential system can be transformed into an algebraic system, where the kinetic parameters are estimated using non-linear regression. Details of the methodology are explained below. The chosen reaction scheme is based on the CO insertion mechanism proposed by Santiesteban [4] and Park [9] as it has been successfully used to describe the kinetics of higher alcohols synthesis over MoS2 catalysts [4,10]: (I) CO + 2 H 2 → CH 3 OH (II) CH 3 OH + H 2 → CH 4 + H 2 O (III) CH 3 OH + CO + 2 H 2 → C 2 H 5 OH + H 2 O (IV) C 2 H 5 OH + CO + 2 H 2 → C 3 H 7 OH + H 2 O (V) CO + H 2 O ↔ CO 2 + H 2 All reactions are assumed irreversible except the water gas shift (WGS) reaction. The power-law model was utilized because it is simple and widely used in the literature to predict the reaction rate for the higher alcohol synthesis [10,12,17]. In this work, the gross reaction rates of the compounds are expressed as follows: (1) r MeOH = A 1 ⋅ exp − E a 1 R · T P CO A ⋅ P H 2 B (2) r CH 4 = A 2 ⋅ exp − E a 2 R · T P H 2 C ⋅ P MeOH D (3) r EtOH = A 3 ⋅ exp − E a 3 R · T P CO E ⋅ P H 2 F ⋅ P MeOH G (4) r PrOH = A 4 · exp − E a 4 R · T P CO H · P H 2 I · P EtOH J (5) r CO 2 = A 5 ⋅ exp − Ea 5 R · T P CO ⋅ P H 2 O − k x / K ws · P CO 2 · P H 2 where Ai is the pre-exponential factor, Eai is the activation energy, A to J are the partial reaction orders, Kws is the equilibrium constant of the WGS reaction calculated as Kws =exp(−ΔGWS ∘/(R·T)) where ΔG WS ∘(cal/mol)=−8514+7.71· T(K) [12], and kx is a parameter that takes into account the non-ideality of gases due to high operating pressures. Partial reaction orders in the WGS reaction have been assumed equal to 1 to reduce the number of parameters. The independent variables are the temperature and the partial pressures of reactants, while Ai, Eai, A to J and kx are the parameters of the kinetic model (overall 21 parameters). The parameters of the kinetic model were regressed from data of experiments carried out in the lab reactor with and without methanol co-feeding (Section 2.4). By assuming ideal plug flow for the lab reactor, the mass balances of the compounds are: (6) dF CO dW = ‐ r MetOH + r EtOH + r PrOH + r CO 2 (7) dF H 2 dW = ‐ 2 r MetOH + 2 r EtOH + 2 r PrOH + r CH 4 + r CO 2 (8) dF H 2 O dW = r EtOH + r PrOH + r CH 4 ‐ r CO 2 (9) d F MetOH dW = r MetOH − r EtOH − r C H 4 (10) d F EtOH dW = r EtOH − r PrOH (11) d F PrOH dW = r PrOH (12) d F C H 4 dW = r C H 4 (13) dF CO 2 dW = r CO 2 where W is the mass of catalyst and Fi the mole flow of compound i. The mass balances are expressed as a function of the five independent gross reaction rates (Eqs. 1–5). The fitting was carried out by minimizing an objective function (Je). According to the maximum likelihood principle, if the errors in the observations are independent, normally distributed, with constant variance for each dependent variable (homoscedasticity), and the covariance between dependent variables is negligible, the resulting objective function is defined as the sum of the squares of the residuals divided by the variance of the error for each dependent variable (Eq. 14) [18,19]. By using this maximum likelihood function, the variance of the fitting parameters is minimized in the regression [20]. (14) J e = ∑ i = 1 C 1 σ i 2 ∑ j = 1 N ( F ̂ ij − F ij ) 2 In the objective function, Fij is the mole flow of compound i at the outlet of the reactor for experiment number j; σi 2 is the variance of the experimental error for compound i, which is estimated as the variance of the residuals of compound i for all experiments; N is the number of experiments and C the number of compounds (carbon monoxide, hydrogen, methanol, ethanol, propanol, methane, carbon dioxide and water). For each experiment, the predicted mole flows at the reactor outlet were calculated by integrating the differential equations (Eqs. 6 to 13) along the reactor with ode15s routine in Matlab®. The function fminsearch of Matlab's Optimization Toolbox, which implements the Nelder-Mead algorithm, was used to minimize the error function Je by changing the parameters of the kinetic model. The variances of the residuals were updated in each iteration. To solve the differential mole balance mentioned above, an initial guess of the kinetic parameters is necessary. This has been obtained using a simplified method whereby the integration of the differential equations along the reactor is avoided. The method assumes that the total molar flow rate (F) remains constant along the reactor. Thus, the left-hand term of the differential equations, i.e. the net reaction rate, can be expressed as follows (Eq. 15): (15) d F i dW = d F y i dW ≈ Fd y i dW = d y i d W F where yi is the molar composition of component i at the outlet of the reactor and F the total mole flow at the inlet of the reactor. To calculate the net reaction rate several series of experiments were carried out at fixed temperature, pressure and inlet H2/CO ratio while varying the inlet molar flow rate to generate a range of W/F (see Section 2.4, Table 2 ). For each series of experiments at a fixed temperature the value of each yi was plotted against W/F (see for instance, Fig. 2 for ethanol). For a given W/F the slope of the curve is the net reaction rate of compound i at the outlet of the reactor. Thus, for each experiment the net reaction rates of five components (namely methanol, ethanol, propanol, carbon dioxide and methane) were calculated from the slope of the curves and then the values of the five gross reaction rates were determined using Eqs. 9–13. Next, the initial guess of the kinetic parameters was determined with non-linear regression by minimizing the sum of the squares of the differences between experimental and predicted gross reaction rates for each compound (Eq. 16). (16) S i = ∑ j = 1 N r ̂ ij − r ij 2 i = 1 … … 5 For Eq. 16, the partial pressures in the kinetic equations were evaluated at the outlet of the reactor. The kinetics equations for the reaction rates were the same as those used for the rigorous model (Eqs. 1–4) except the WGS reaction, which has been simplified (Eq. 17). The reason is that by using Eq. 5 for the WGS reaction, or the irreversible version, a good fit was not possible and negative values for the frequency factor were obtained. Therefore, in the simplified method Eq. 17 was used for obtaining a rough estimation of A5 and Ea5 in Eq. 5. (17) r CO 2 = A 5 ⋅ exp − Ea 5 R · T P CO c ⋅ P H 2 O d The aim of these experiments was to obtain experimental data for development of the kinetic model. In all experiments, there is neither internal nor external diffusion resistance, so the controlling resistance is the chemical reaction (see appendix A). Four groups of experiments were performed, groups 1, 2 and 3 at laboratory scale and group 4 at bench scale. Experiments of group 1 (Table 2) were performed to apply the simplified method as explained before. Experiments of group 2 and 3 were used for the fitting of the kinetic parameters with the rigorous method. Group 2 is an experimental design at different conditions of T, P, GHSV and H2/CO to obtain experimental data in a wide range of operating conditions, typical for this kind of catalyst (Table 3 ). Group 3 are experiments where methanol co-feeding was performed and were used to enhance the prediction capability of the kinetic model for syngas/methanol mixtures. Several series of experiments were carried out at fixed temperature, pressure, inlet H2/CO ratio and space velocity while varying the concentration of methanol in the feed (Table 4 ). Experiments of group 4 were used to validate the kinetic model. They were carried out in a bench scale facility with a double purpose: to verify the kinetic model at a larger scale and at operating conditions similar to an industrial process, that is, considering the recycle of byproducts such as CO2 and hydrocarbons. The experiments can be organized into 4 different subgroups depending on the composition of the gases fed to the reactor (Table 5 ). The parameters of the kinetic model calculated using the simplified method are shown in Table 6 . Experiments 6, 12 and 18 of group 1 were excluded for the fitting because the assumption of the simplified method (constant molar flow along the reactor) was largely violated, so the reaction rate could not be determined properly as the slope of the curve yi-W/F. The kinetic parameters from Table 6 were used as initial values for the rigorous method. The initial value of kx in Eq. 5 was assumed 1, which would be the case if the behavior of the gases were ideal. The parameters of the kinetic model calculated using the rigorous method are shown in Table 7 . With these parameters, the error function Je was reduced by 67% with respect to the initial parameter values. Fig. 3 shows the fit between experimental mole flows and those predicted by the model for carbon monoxide, hydrogen, methanol, methane, ethanol, propanol, carbon dioxide and water. The model shows a good fit with the experimental results so the chosen reaction scheme and the assumption of the reaction orders for WGS reaction are correct. Christensen et al. [17] recently suggested that alcohol coupling might be one of the possible routes over MoS2-based catalysts but the coupling mechanism is absent or negligible in our case because the scheme of CO insertion correctly predicts the experimental results. It is necessary to validate the assumptions of the regression model by residual analyses. The independency of the errors is fulfilled because experiments were performed randomly. To verify that the errors follow a normal distribution, the Shapiro-Wilk contrast test was used. The contrast test considers as null hypothesis (Ho) that the errors are normally distributed, and as alternative hypothesis (H1) that they do not. The decision rule, expressed in terms of the p-value is: Ho is rejected if p-value is less or equal to α (the significance level). As shown in Table 8 , the p-value is always higher than 0.05 (chosen significance level) except for propanol. Since propanol is a minor compound in the experiments, the model is accepted, even though the normality assumption for propanol is slightly violated. To validate the hypothesis of homoscedasticity, the estimations of the model were plotted against the residuals for each variable (Fig. 4 ). This figure demonstrates that the errors are randomly distributed, with a mean of zero and constant variance. Trends are also not observed, so for each compound the homoscedasticity hypothesis can be accepted. Table 9 shows a comparison between the activation energies of higher alcohol synthesis reactions over MoS2 based catalysts reported in the literature with those obtained in this work. Perhaps, the most striking feature of the kinetic data of this table is the lack of agreement between the values obtained in the studies. However, the reaction rates are affected by the composition, the structure of the active phase of the catalyst and the support and reaction conditions and this may explain the wide dispersion of results. Overall, the results show that there is a general agreement that the activation energy tends to increase with the chain length of alcohols [22]. Moreover, the activation energy of methane (main hydrocarbon) is approximately equal in the different studies and it is always higher than those of alcohols. Therefore, an increase in temperature favors the formation of hydrocarbons but also higher alcohols. To validate the kinetic model, its predictive performance was tested against a group of experiments that were not used for the fitting. These experiments were carried out in the bench scale facility (Group 4 experiments, see Section 2.4). Fig. 5 shows the comparison between the molar flow of compounds predicted by the model and the experimental data at bench scale. The results indicate that the model captured the intrinsic kinetics and there was not overfitting in determination of the parameters. The model slightly underpredicts hydrogen, probably due to the smaller accuracy in the quantification of hydrogen by gas chromatography at the bench scale plant than at the laboratory scale reactor (hydrogen atom balance versus internal standard, respectively). On the other hand, some experimental values for propanol are slightly overpredicted, possibly due to uncertainties in the measurements as the amount of propanol produced in the experiments was small. However, we can conclude that the kinetic model developed at lab scale can be used to predict the performance of the catalyst at a larger scale because, in general, all the compounds fit reasonably well with the experimental values. A question remains whether a model could correctly predict the effect of methanol co-feeding if its parameters were determined by fitting experiments where methanol co-feeding was not performed. To answer this question, the fitting of the kinetic parameters was carried out again only with experiments of group 2. Then, they were compared with the parameters of Table 7, and the relative change in the parameters is shown in Table 10 . The results indicate that there is significant variation in some parameters between both cases. With these new parameters the mean square error on the validation experiments (group 4) has doubled, however, the fit seems acceptable for prediction purposes (Fig. 6 ). Therefore, it is possible to predict reasonably the effect of methanol recirculation with a kinetic model that has been developed without experiments where methanol is co-fed, at the expense of a moderate increase in the uncertainty of the predictions. A kinetic model of an alkali-Co doped MoS2 catalyst for the higher alcohol synthesis reaction was developed and the capability of the model to predict the effect of significant methanol content in the feed was evaluated. A reaction scheme from the literature (reactions I-V), based on the CO insertion mechanism, was used to describe the kinetics of the higher alcohols synthesis and a power-law model was chosen for the reaction rate of compounds (Eqs. 1–5). Experiments at laboratory and bench scale reactors, which featured co-feeding of methanol, were carried out to obtain experimental data that allow regression (Table 7) and validation of the kinetic parameters, respectively. Methane, carbon dioxide, methanol, ethanol and n-propanol were the main products. For the fitting, the differential mole balance equations in the reactor for the main compounds were solved (Eqs. 6–13). An initial guess of the kinetic parameters was previously obtained using a simplified method. The hypothesis of the regression model was confirmed. Validation of the model with data from the bench scale reactor indicates that the model captured the intrinsic kinetics and there was not overfitting in determination of the parameters (Fig. 6). Moreover, the kinetic model developed at lab scale can be used to predict the performance of the catalyst at a larger scale. The accuracy of the kinetic model is expected to be high if the reaction conditions are such that formation of C4+ alcohols is negligible (for instance, operating at high space velocity where chain growth is not favored). Formation of C4+ alcohols was not included in the kinetic model as selectivity of C4+ alcohols in all the experiments was very low (below 1%). This situation is expected when operating conditions are chosen to favor ethanol selectivity. Finally, it has been concluded that a kinetic model could reasonably predict the effect of methanol co-feeding if its parameters were determined by fitting experiments where methanol co-feeding was not performed. This work has been carried out in the framework of the CENIT Project BIOSOS (CEN-20091040) funded by the Spanish Ministry of Science and Innovation and Abengoa Bioenergy New Technologies (ABNT). Their support is gratefully acknowledged. Reaction rates in the absence of these internal and external diffusion resistances are termed ‘intrinsic kinetics’ [12]. The absence of these effects in the tests was analyzed to verify that the chemical reaction is the controlling resistance. The diffusion of the reactants or products between the bulk fluid and the external surface of the catalyst is known as external resistance. This can be eliminated by increasing the velocity past the particle or using very small particles [21] so the worst case is the lowest gas velocity and the highest particle size. The particle size selected in this work is 310μm. The lowest velocity is 608h−1, corresponding to the case of W/F=152730kgcat·s/kmol. To evaluate the influence of external diffusion, the Mears criterion is used [22]. The Mears criterion states that external mass transport limitations are absent, if the following condition is met: (A.1) C Mears = − r obs ' · ρ b · d P · n A 2 · k c · C Ab < 0.15 If CMears ≪0.15 there is no external diffusion limitation and if CMears ≫0.15 the external diffusion limits the reaction severely [12,22]. The value of CMears was calculated at high velocity and at three different temperatures (290, 300, 310°C). r obs ' and CAb vary along the reactor so the output values have been used. The CMears values are of the order of 1·10−5 in the worst case, which is far <0.15. This confirms that external diffusion is negligible on the catalyst with the average particle diameter used. The Weisz-Prater criterion (CWP) is used to estimate the internal mass-transfer resistance in heterogeneous catalytic reactions. If CWP ≪1 there is no internal diffusion limitation and if CWP ≫1 the internal diffusion limits the reaction severely [12]. This criterion is given as follows: (A.2) C WP = − r obs ' · ρ c · r 2 D e · C As < 1 The value of CWP was calculated following the same methodology as Surisetty and Christensen in their thesis [22,23]. It was considered that the surface concentration of the component A was similar to that of bulk liquid (CAs =CAb) because there was no resistance to the external transfer. The CWP values are about 10−4, which is far <1. This confirms that internal diffusion is negligible on the catalyst with the average particle diameter used. Table B.1 Results of group 1 experiments. abstract: A set of experiments was run in laboratory and bench scale reactors over an alkali-Co doped molybdenum sulfide catalyst in order to develop and validate the kinetics of higher alcohol synthesis from syngas/methanol mixtures. The purpose is to generate a kinetic model useful to design a mixed alcohol process where methanol is recycled to the reactor to increase ethanol production. Experimental data from a laboratory scale reactor were fitted using a power-law model, considering a CO insertion reaction scheme. The model was validated with a group of experiments carried out at a bench scale reactor, consisting of experiments with methanol co-feeding and gas recycle. Finally, we also studied whether a model would be able to predict the effect of methanol co-feeding if its parameters were determined by fitting only the experiments where methanol co-feeding was not performed.
title: A kinetic model for the synthesis of ethanol from syngas and methanol over an alkali-Co doped molybdenum sulfide catalyst: Model building and validation at bench scale

full_text: Chemical products are derived primarily from petroleum, while coal and natural gas remain minor feedstocks. Chemical products derived from petroleum are seldom produced from coal or natural gas. The balance of supply and demand of crude oil inevitably strongly affects the prices of many chemical products. To stabilise their prices, it is necessary to diversity of the sources of these chemicals. Low-rank coals, such as brown coals and lignites, are not usually exploited in the world coal market because of their low calorific values, high moisture and oxygen contents, and propensity for spontaneous ignition. Technologies for upgrading such low-rank coals are needed to enhance the calorific value, remove moisture, and modify their surface properties. Mild heat treatment or pyrolysis at moderate temperature is one possible upgrade method, because it can greatly increase the calorific value of low-rank coal. Heating coal over 600K produces a substantial amount of liquid, often called tar and pitch, but this is not a problem if such liquid is effectively converted into valuable chemicals while producing upgraded coal. Tar and pitch can be subjected to processes such as distillation, hydrogenation, and thermal cracking. Distillation would be ineffective, due to the very low concentration of light liquids, such as mono-aromatics, phenols, and aliphatic oxygenates. Hydrogenation can consume the H2 produced by the gasification of coal or another existing process. Thermal cracking seems to be the simplest process for converting the light liquid from tar or pitch, but in practice it is difficult to achieve sufficient selectivity of the light liquid, even when the operating variables are optimised. Thermal cracking results in insufficient conversion of heavy liquids at lower temperatures or the loss of oxygenates and formation of polyaromatic hydrocarbons at higher temperatures [1,2]. Catalytic cracking is a potential method for converting tar or pitch into light liquids with high selectivity. The compounds in low-rank coal tar that arise from aggressive depolymerisation via C–C bond rupture are aromatics in the form of benzene, toluene, and xylenes (BTX), plus phenols and aliphatics. This study investigated the production of valuable chemicals, such as oxygenated compounds, from low-rank coal tar by pyrolysis and cracking, rather than gasification. Iron oxide catalysts containing zirconium have been reported to possess activity for degrading petroleum residual oil in atmospheres rich in steam [3–5]. Catalytic cracking over zirconium-bearing iron oxides has yielded phenols and ketones from sewage sludge and paper sludge-derived tar that contained hydroxyl and carbonyl groups [6,7]. Carbonaceous materials, such as petroleum residual oil, sewage sludge, and paper sludge-derived tar, passing over the catalysts are thought to be decomposed oxidatively by the lattice oxygen of the iron oxide matrix and fragments of the materials are hydrogenated and stabilised by active hydrogen species that are formed by the decomposition of H2O under the catalytic effect of zirconia [3]. Additionally, cerium has the ability to store and release of oxygen. Considering previous studies [6,7] and the ability of cerium, we postulated that an iron oxide catalyst containing cerium would be effective for converting low-rank coal tar into chemicals. In this work, we investigated the effect of adding cerium to an iron oxide catalyst on the catalytic activity cracking coal tar. Four different iron-based catalysts were prepared by coprecipitation from aqueous solutions of nitrates of iron, aluminium, zirconium, and cerium. Hereafter, a catalyst with Al2O3, ZrO2, and CeO2 contents of X, Y, and Z wt.%-catalyst, respectively, is denoted by Ce(X)–Zr(Y)–Al(Z)–FeOx. Four catalysts were prepared: FeOx, Zr(10)–Al(7)–FeOx, Ce(2.5)–Zr(7.5)–Al(7)–FeOx, and Ce(10)–Zr(10)–Al(20)–FeOx. The catalysts were pelletised without any binders, crushed, and sieved to yield particles 300–850μm in diameter. The structures of the catalysts were analysed using powder X-ray diffractometry. A Victorian brown coal from the Latrobe Valley, Victoria, Australia, was pyrolysed in a continuous reactor with a peak temperature of 873K and a mean particle residence time of 47s. The volatile products contained non-condensable gases, water and water-soluble organics, water-insoluble tar, and pitch with yields of 13.5, 11.6, 4.2, and 11.6wt.% on a dry coal basis, respectively. The total yield of tar and water-soluble organics was 5.0wt.% on a dry coal carbon basis. The pitch was separated using a silica fibre filter located between the reactor and water/tar condenser, and heated at 423K. A 10wt.% benzene solution of the tar was used as the feedstock for catalytic cracking. We confirmed that the toluene content in benzene (special grade, Wako Chemicals Ind., Ltd.) was 0.007mol-C% and no xylene was detected. The inertness of benzene with iron oxide catalyst containing zirconium was confirmed previously [3]. We considered that the addition of aluminium, zirconium, and cerium had the effect of the inertness of benzene, because they were highly dispersed in an iron oxide matrix and their iron-oxide solid solution was formed in this study [3]. Catalytic cracking of the tar in a steam atmosphere was performed at 773K and ambient atmospheric pressure in a fixed-bed reactor that was loaded with about 1g of one of the iron oxide catalysts. The feeding rates of the feedstock and steam were 1 and 1.5g/h, respectively. The feedstock was supplied to the fixed-bed reactor through a 1/16-inch stainless steel tube by a syringe and heated at 473K around an inlet of the fixed-bed reactor. A mixture of steam and nitrogen with partial pressures of 87 and 14kPa, respectively, was introduced into the fixed-bed reactor. A train consisting of a condenser and a gasbag was installed downstream. The liquid products were collected with the condenser and analysed by gas chromatography. Heavy tar in the liquid products was defined as the residue remaining after the liquid products were allowed to evaporate by thermogravimetry at 523K in an inert atmosphere. Non-condensable gases were allowed to pass through the condenser, collected in the gasbag, and analysed by gas chromatography. CO, CO2, CH4, alkens (C2–C5), alkanes (C2–C5), and H2 were referred to collectively as gas. For Ce(2.5)–Zr(7.5)–Al(7)–FeOx, the effects of the partial pressure of steam, P H2O, on the catalyst activity were investigated. The total flow rate of steam and nitrogen was kept constant, while the feed rate of steam was adjusted by altering the feed rate of nitrogen. Fig. 1 shows the cumulative yields of acetone, 2-butanone, toluene, xylenes, phenol, and cresols. The formation of toluene and xylenes suggests the formation of benzene, although this could not be examined because benzene was used as the diluent of the tar. The total fraction of these compounds in the feedstock was 12.8% on a tar carbon basis, which increased to 25.5, 27.8, and 41.2% on cracking with FeOx, Zr(10)–Al(7)–FeOx and Ce(2.5)–Zr(7.5)–Al(7)–FeOx, respectively. In contrast, the yield of the commodity chemicals decreased with Ce(10)–Zr(10)–Al(20)–FeOx. The metal promoter contents of the iron oxide catalyst were essential for obtaining the desired product. The formation of ketones (i.e., acetone and 2-butanone) appears to be a particular feature of our cracking process. Compared to FeOx, Zr(10)–Al(7)–FeOx yielded an increase in acetone and a decrease in monoaromatic compounds. Moreover, cracking with Ce(2.5)–Zr(7.5)–Al(7)–FeOx produced much more 2-butanone, but much less acetone, than with the other catalysts. Although the details are limited, the production of ketones likely resulted from the degradation of the tar or the dimerisation of carboxylic acids that are included in the tar or tar fragments. Cracking with Ce(2.5)–Zr(7.5)–Al(7)–FeOx gave more phenols (i.e., phenol and cresols) than with the other catalysts. Accordingly, we concluded that the cerium in Ce(2.5)–Zr(7.5)–Al(7)–FeOx suppressed the decomposition of oxygen-containing functional groups, ketones, and hydroxyls in the catalytic cracking process, such that the yield of oxygenated compounds increased. Compared with the initial heavy tar, the heavy tar in the liquid product decreased dramatically on cracking over the catalysts used in this study (Fig. 2 ). In particular, cracking with Ce(2.5)–Zr(7.5)–Al(7)–FeOx converted about 97% of the initial heavy tar into lighter products by mass. Consequently, Ce(2.5)–Zr(7.5)–Al(7)–FeOx was superior to the other catalysts in terms of the production of chemicals and the decomposition of the initial heavy tar. Fig. 3 shows the composition of gas produced by cracking. The gas composition was defined as the percentage of total volume of gases that were CO, CO2, CH4, alkens (C2–C5), alkanes (C2–C5), and H2. Hematite is an oxidative catalyst. In the cracking with FeOx, the CO2 concentration in the product gas exceeded 98mol%, and CO and H2 were not detected in the product gas. A zirconium-bearing iron oxide catalyst has been reported to produce H2 in a steam atmosphere [3]. In preliminary experiments, we confirmed that iron oxide bearing cerium generated H2. Accordingly, we postulated that oxidation and hydrogenation took place simultaneously over the iron oxide catalysts bearing zirconium or cerium. In this study, the formation of H2 was confirmed on cracking with the iron oxide catalysts, and hydrocarbon gases increased (Fig. 3). It was thought that zirconium and cerium oxides in the iron oxide catalysts suppressed the oxidation of the tar and promoted its hydrogenation. Ce(2.5)–Zr(7.5)–Al(7)–FeOx had the highest hydrogenation activity of the catalysts used in this study, while an excess of cerium, zirconium, and aluminium, such as in Ce(10)–Zr(10)–Al(20)–FeOx, was too oxidative, so that the production of these chemicals decreased. As shown in Fig. 3, the iron oxide catalysts containing zirconium or cerium seemed to cause oxidation and hydrogenation simultaneously. A previous study reported that the lattice oxygen of an iron oxide catalyst was consumed by decomposing petroleum residual oils over the surface of the catalyst [3]. To maintain the activity of the catalyst, an iron oxide matrix that is losing lattice oxygen must be supplied with active oxygen species, such as by the catalytic effect of zirconia, which dissociates H2O into active hydrogen and oxygen species. The active oxygen species generated by zirconia should compensate for the lattice oxygen that was lost from the catalyst. A more highly dispersed zirconia in the iron oxide matrix enhanced the capability of supplying active oxygen species to the matrix. A short supply of active oxygen species led to the transformation of the iron oxide matrix from a hematite structure to a magnetite structure, resulting in deactivation of the catalyst. Cerium appeared to supply insufficient active oxygen species to the iron oxide matrix, because, on the basis of the results of preliminary experiments using atmospheric residue as a feedstock, the Ce–FeOx catalyst assumed a magnetite structure after cracking. Thus, it was difficult to compensate for the lost lattice oxygen of the catalyst bearing cerium because that the catalyst had a magnetite structure and lost its activity. We postulated that the cerium in the iron oxide matrix had low dispersion relative to that of zirconium. As mentioned above, the main cause of the deactivation of an iron oxide catalyst is a structural change in the iron oxide matrix. We evaluated the deactivation of the catalysts used in this study by examining their X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Fig. 4 ). No peaks of cerium, zirconium, or aluminium oxides were detected because of their dispersion into the iron oxide matrix, and only peaks derived from iron oxides were observed. Both Zr(10)–Al(7)–FeOx and Ce(2.5)–Zr(7.5)–Al(7)–FeOx maintained a hematite structure after cracking for 2h, while the FeOx was transformed into a magnetite structure. The addition of zirconium to the catalyst bearing cerium prevented the structure of the catalyst from transforming from the hematite structure into the magnetite structure. To maintain the activity of an iron oxide catalyst bearing cerium it was essential to add zirconium to the iron oxide matrix. As Zr(10)–Al(7)–FeOx and Ce(2.5)–Zr(7.5)–Al(7)–FeOx maintained the hematite structure, we concluded that the catalysts maintained their activities. However, the structure of the iron oxide catalyst bearing an excess of cerium, zirconium, and aluminium, i.e., Ce(10)–Zr(10)–Al(20)–FeOx, was transformed into the magnetite structure. Hydrogenation over an iron oxide catalyst seemed to play an important role in the increase of chemicals and decrease of heavy tar in the liquid product during catalytic cracking. Tar fragments that are decomposed over the catalyst are prone to polymerise, because the fragments are unstable. A zirconium-bearing iron oxide catalyst produced active hydrogen species over the catalyst in a steam atmosphere [3]. Reportedly, cerium has the ability to decompose H2O, producing hydrogen [8]. In our study, the catalysts containing cerium or zirconium generated active hydrogen species and produced more hydrogen and hydrocarbon gases than FeOx (Fig. 3). Hydrogenation stabilised the fragments, so that Ce(2.5)–Zr(7.5)–Al(7)–FeOx increased the total yield of chemicals and enhanced the net fragmentation of the heavy tar to a greater extent than the other catalysts. In cracking with an iron oxide catalyst, the redox cycle of iron oxides, F e 2 O 3 ↔ F e 2 O 3 − a + a 2 O 2 ( 0 < a < 1 / 3 ) , requires active oxygen species generated from H2O by cerium or zirconium because lattice oxygen was lost via partial oxidative decomposition. Fig. 5 shows the variation in the yields of the commodity chemicals with the partial pressure of steam. The ability of Ce(2.5)–Zr(7.5)–Al(7)–FeOx to produce monoaromatic hydrocarbons including phenols was similar regardless of the partial pressure of steam. However, the higher the partial pressure of steam was, the more ketones were produced; the yield and selectivity of ketones depended on the partial pressure of steam. As the partial pressure of steam decreased, a magnetite structure appeared (Fig. 6 ). As a result, Ce(2.5)–Zr(7.5)–Al(7)–FeOx lost its activity at a steam partial pressure of 29kPa. Steam prevented Ce(2.5)–Zr(7.5)–Al(7)–FeOx from losing its activity, although it required a high partial pressure of steam to remain active. A future study should examine how to reduce the amount of steam consumed in cracking using iron oxide catalysts bearing cerium. We demonstrated that iron oxide catalysts containing aluminium, zirconium, and cerium could produce commodity chemicals from Loy Yang coal tar at a cracking temperature of 773K and ambient atmospheric pressure in a steam atmosphere. The addition of zirconium or cerium to the iron oxide catalyst enhanced its ability to produce commodity chemicals and the decomposition of the initial heavy tar. No structural change in Ce(2.5)–Zr(7.5)–Al(7)–FeOx was observed, and the catalyst maintained activity during catalytic cracking at a steam partial pressure of 87kPa. The iron oxide catalyst containing zirconium and cerium required a high partial pressure of steam. In conclusion, the combination of cerium and zirconium was effective at improving the activity of iron oxide catalysts.
abstract: Iron oxide catalysts containing metal promoters were tested to evaluate whether they could be applied to the coal tar obtained from the pyrolysis of Loy Yang coal. Catalytic cracking of the coal tar in a steam atmosphere was conducted in a fixed-bed reactor at 773K and ambient atmospheric pressure. For iron oxide catalysts containing cerium, zirconium, and aluminium, the total yield of monocyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, phenols, and ketones exceeded 40mol-C% on a tar basis, while about 97wt.% of the initial heavy tar was decomposed. The combination of cerium, zirconium, and aluminium improved the activity of the iron oxide catalyst. Moreover, the addition of steam to the tar vapour increased the yield of ketones and made the catalyst more durable.
title: Production of chemicals by cracking pyrolytic tar from Loy Yang coal over iron oxide catalysts in a steam atmosphere

full_text: The CO2 reforming of CH4 (Eq. (1)) or dry reforming (DRM) is as an efficient way for the CH4 and CO2 valorisation [1–4]. In the produced syn-gas the H2/CO ratio is equal to that suitable for the synthesis of oxygenated hydrocarbons and synthetic fuels. (1) CH 4 + CO 2 → 2 H 2 + 2 CO Δ H 298 K 0 = 247 kJ mol − 1 To date, the DRM industrial implementation is mainly impeded by the co-occurrence of the reverse water gas shift reaction (RWGS) (Eq. (2)), the catalyst deactivation and/or reactor plugging due methane cracking (Eq. (3)) and the Boudouard reaction (Eq. (4)). (2) H 2 + CO 2 → CO + H 2 O Δ H 298 K 0 = 41 kJ mol − 1 (3) CH 4 → C + 2 H 2 Δ H 298 K 0 = 75 kJ mol − 1 (4) 2 CO → C + CO 2 Δ H 298 K 0 = − 172 kJ mol − 1 To achieve suitable CH4 and CO2 conversions, DRM is operated at high temperature (generally above 700°C). In that condition, the Boudouard reaction is thermodynamically unfavoured and the RWGS is suppressed by the low CO2 concentration. While, the deposition of carbon may not be completely avoided affecting the long term operation of the catalysts therefore, there is a pressing need to increase the catalysts resistance toward carbon deposition. Ni-based catalysts are so far the most active, but also highly prone to carbon formation, because, together with the ability to activate the CH bond, Ni has a high affinity to carbon [5]. In the search of active and stable Ni-based catalysts, several approaches have been explored: a) the stabilization of small Ni particles which have low catalytic activity toward the CH cracking [6,7]; b) the addition of another metal resulting in the formation of less C-sensitive alloys or in the increase of Ni dispersion [8–10]; c) the use of supports which may activate CO2 and promote the gasification of carbonaceous deposits, such as those containing MgO [11], CeO2, and La2O3 [12]. Among bimetallic systems, best performances are shown by those containing a small amount (695°C). Likely, the Ru addition slightly promoted the reduction on Ni at lower temperature in comparison to the monometallic catalyst. The amount of hydrogen reacted in TPR experiments and the reduction degree are reported in Table 2 . NiO and RuO2 species were completely reduced to the corresponding metals at 800°C. Hydrogen chemisorption was studied by H2-TPD. H2 was released in two temperature ranges as shown in Fig. 2b. In agreement with relevant literature [31,32], the peaks at T<550°C have been assigned to hydrogen atoms desorbed from metallic surface (Type I), whereas, at higher temperature the desorption of hydrogen atoms located on the metal subsurface or on the support took place (Type II). Therefore, only peaks located in the low temperature range have been taken into account for the metallic surface area S (m2 g−1 of metal) and metal dispersion DTPD (%) calculation as reported in Table 2. On Ni- p catalyst, Type I hydrogen desorbed giving rise to a single peak, whereas, on Ni-Ru- p a doublet was observed. The amount of desorbed hydrogen was larger for Ni-Ru- p , being more than twice the value measured on Ni- p . Furthermore, since the Ru concentration was very low, the mean square section of the metal aMet in the bimetallic catalysts was basically the same of Ni. Moreover, the Ni quantity was very similar in both catalysts, thus the calculated metallic surface area and metal dispersion followed the same trend of the amount of desorbed hydrogen. TEM images of reduced samples and the corresponding particle size distributions are shown in Fig. 3 . Table 2 reports the parameters derived from morphological analysis ( d ¯ nm , d ¯ av nm , D TEM (%)). The γ-Al2O3 support showed plate like particles arranged in a mesoporous structure in agreement with BET results. The metal particles were of nanometric size and hemispherical shape. The mean particle size of Ni- p and Ni-Ru- p were in agreement with the XRD analysis. The composition of the metallic particles was checked by EDS nanoprobe as reported in Fig. S1 (Supplementary material). Some particles consisting of Ni and some others consisting of Ru based alloy were revealed on bimetallic catalysts. This feature suggests a low miscibility of Ni0 and Ru0 in agreement with recent literature [19]. Being the Ru loading quite low, a high dispersion of Ru nanoparticles was expected, whereas Ru rich particles showed a grain size comparable to that of Ni0 particles. The D TEM (%) values were 2.3 and 3.3% for Ni- p and Ni-Ru- p samples, respectively. For Ni-Ru- p sample the values obtained by TEM and H2-TPD analysis were very similar (2.9 and 3.1%) while in the case of Ni- p the different values obtained (3.3 and 1.4%) with the two techniques can be explained taking into account the strong interaction between the smaller Ni particles and the γ-Al2O3 support that may cause the coverage of a part of the particles by the support phase and therefore not accessible to H2 gas as reported by other authors [33,34]. The catalytic activity results of the powdered catalysts are reported in Fig. 4 . The reactants conversions rapidly increased by increasing temperature because the methane dry reforming is an highly endothermic reaction (Fig. 4a). The Ni-Ru- p catalyst was the most performing with a CH4 conversion ranging from 10% at 600°C up to 40% at 800°C whereas Ni- p showed a maximum CH4 conversion of 19% at 800°C. The CO2 conversions were higher than methane conversions (Fig. 4a) and the H2/CO ratio was lower than unity for both catalysts (Fig. 4b), indicating the occurrence of the reverse water gas shift reaction (RWGS) as side process. The H2/CO ratio increased with increasing temperature and the most selective catalyst was Ni-Ru- p at 800°C showing an H2/CO ratio equal to 0.8. It has already been reported that the RWGS is close to equilibrium in the 425–575°C range as result of fast reaction rate with respect to the reforming reaction [4]. On performing catalysts, such as Ni-Ru- p , the large amount of produced H2 yielded H2/CO values close to unity. Reaction rates were calculated from conversion values below 20% by Eq. (12) and the corresponding TOFs were calculated normalizing the reaction rate to the number of active metals site obtained from H2-TPD measurements, which determined the metal surface area accessible to reactants, and reported in the Arrhenius plot of Fig. 4c. The TOF values steeply decreased with decreasing temperature for Ni- p catalyst in comparison to those of Ni-Ru-p, showing Ni-Ru-p catalyst the highest activity per metal site at temperature lower than 700°C. The apparent activation energies (Ea) for CH4 and CO2 disappearance were calculated from the slope of the interpolated data. On Ni- p , Ea CH4 was 75.2kJmol−1 whereas on Ni-Ru- p the activation energy was much lower, being 57.6kJmol−1. On both catalysts, Ea CO2 was slightly lower than Ea CH4 due to the occurrence of the RWGS that shows an activation energy lower than that of the dry reforming reaction. Thus, as a consequence, CO2 was easily activated by H2 in the whole temperature range. In particular Ea CO2 values were 61.7 and 54.3kJmol−1 for Ni- p and Ni-Ru- p catalysts, respectively. It is important to note that the percentage difference between the Ea CO2 values of two catalysts (about 12%) was lower than that of Ea CH4 values (about 23%) due to the presence of RWGS and according to the lower H2/CO ratio of the Ni- p sample (see Fig. 4b). A wide range of Ea values has been reported in the literature [4]. Ea is influenced by various parameters, most importantly: space velocity [1], temperature range [35] and nature of the metal/support [11]. For Ni based catalysts, the most frequently reported value for Ea CH4 is about 58.6kJmol−1, which coincides with activation energies for CH4 dissociation on Ni (110) and Ni (111) [4,36]. The Ea CH4 obtained on Ni-Ru- p catalyst was in good agreement with literature data, suggesting that the Ru does not alter the rate-controlling step in the reaction sequence, likely, methane activation. The catalytic activity trend of catalysts could be primarily ascribed to the different ability of Ni and Ru active sites to chemisorb methane and to activate the CH bond moreover in agreement to the metallic surface area S (m2 g Met ) measured by TPD (Table 2). The stability as a function of time on stream was measured at 800°C (Fig. 4d). The Ni-Ru- p catalyst showed the highest initial CH4 conversion (43%), twice the value of Ni- p catalyst. The Ni- p catalyst was unstable during time on streams and its CH4 conversion decreased by ≈50% after 10h (from 23 to 10%) otherwise, Ni-Ru- p only slightly deactivated. Generally, the deactivation of catalysts during time on stream can be due to coking, metal sintering or formation of catalytically inactive NiO and Ni-spinel phases. To clarify those reasons and the different stability behaviours, the used catalysts were cooled to room temperature in inert gas flow (He) and analysed by complementary techniques as reported in the following. TEM images of the used catalysts are shown in Fig. 5 . No evidence of filamentous or encapsulating carbon deposits were observed on Ni- p catalyst (Fig. 5a) confirming the absence of graphitic carbon shown by XRD in Fig. S2 (Supplementary material). Since carbon balance was in the 96–98% range during the entire catalytic test, no massive carbon deposition was expected, suggesting that the rapid deactivation was not due to coking. TEM also showed that Ni particles did not sinter during TOS, the initial dimensions were maintained smaller than those of the bimetallic samples (not deactivated under the same experimental conditions). On the other hand, the intensity of Ni0 XRD peak clearly decreased in the used catalyst as shown in the inset of Fig. S2. All those features demonstrate that during catalyst operation CO2 oxidized Ni0 reducing the number of Ni0 active centres and leading to a rapid deactivation. The deactivation of Ni/Al2O3 catalyst by formation of NiAlOx “surface spinel” phases during steam reforming [37] or partial oxidation [38] of methane have been already reported by several authors and may appear at temperature as low as 600°C. Although CO2 is a mild oxidant in comparison to H2O or O2 (steam reforming or partial oxidation), Ni0 supported on Al2O3 is reported to be oxidized to Ni2+ in presence of highly concentrated CO2 atmosphere [39–41]. To confirm that the deactivation was due to Ni0 oxidation, XPS analysis was performed on Ni- p sample after calcination, reduction and TOS. The Ni2p signal collected on the calcined sample was fitted with a single pair of spin-orbit components, as reported in Fig. S3 (Supplementary material section) together with the respective shake up satellites (broader curves). The spin-orbit component of higher intensity 2p3/2 was taken as reference, and always found at about 855.4–855.9eV, typical of oxidized nickel (Ni(OH)2 on the nanoparticles surface) [42]. For the reduced and used Ni- p catalyst, a second contribution was observed at lower BE values (Ni2p3/2 =853.3), indicative for metallic Ni [43]. However, the spectral contribution arising by Ni(OH)2 on the samples surface [44] was still intense, about 80% of total Ni2p signal, due to the samples exposure to the atmosphere during preparation and previous to introduction in the UHV XPS measurement chamber. From the semi-quantitative analysis of Al2p, O1s and Ni2p signals the Ni/Al atomic ratio was calculated, on the calcined sample Ni/Al was 0.08, after reduction Ni/Al decreased to 0.02 due to the formation of metallic nanoparticles however on the used catalyst Ni/Al increased back to 0.03 suggesting that some Ni was interacting with Al2O3 forming Ni2+-spinel phases spread over the surface. Fig. 5b reports the TEM analysis of Ni-Ru- p used catalyst. Some carbon filaments were formed during TOS and showed a diameter of 10–70nm, thick walls and open ends. The metal particles were grafted to the γ-Al2O3 matrix and no metal particles appeared encapsulated by the carbon filaments indicating that carbon was deposited mainly according to a base growth mechanism. The presence of carbonaceous residues was consistent with the high catalytic activity and the low activation energy of methane, even though the carbon balance was close to 100% during the catalytic run indicating that carbon filaments were formed at a low rate. Moreover, the absence of encapsulated metal particles on the filaments tip was responsible for the high stability during TOS. Finally Ni-Ru- p catalyst is not deactivated by Ni oxidation because of the beneficial effect of ruthenium on keeping the surface reduced. The detailed investigation of powdered catalysts identified the Ni-Ru as the one showing the highest catalytic activity and the best stability toward oxidation, hence it was chosen to be deposited on the monolithic support. The slurry used to coat the cordierite monoliths remained stable over time without phase separation for at least one week, indicating that at low pH~3–4 the alumina particles were well dispersed by electrostatic repulsive forces [45]. The dry gain of the γ-Al2O3 increased almost linearly with the number of coating and with good reproducibility as shown in Fig. S4 (Supplementary material). The Ni (wt%) loading depends on the amount of the γ-Al2O3 layer, the solution concentration and the number of impregnation steps. Impregnating twice with a solution of 0.5M Ni and 5M urea, catalysts with the proper Ni content (10wt%) and species comparable to those present in the powders were obtained. The SEM micrographs of the coated monolith are reported in Fig. 6a and b. The catalyst was homogeneously distributed along the channels and the pores of micrometric dimension of the cordierite substrate were completely covered by the catalyst layer. The coating was thicker (~30μm) at the edges of the channels and lower along the channel walls (Fig. 6b) and some cracks are also visible. Thus, the catalytic layer was highly porous ensuring a proper gas diffusion. N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms confirmed that the catalyst layers were mesoporous (IV type, H2 hysteresis loop) with a pore size distribution (~10.4nm) equal to that founded on the powdered catalysts. The surface areas of the coated monoliths (~7m2 g−1) were one order of magnitude larger than that of the uncoated cordierite (0.3m2 g−1), and normalizing the values by the catalysts weight, the BET (~96m2 g−1) were comparable to that of the powdered catalysts (~108m2 g−1). XRD analysis of finely grounded monolith samples showed only the peaks of cordierite due to the very small amount of catalyst coating, as shown in Fig. S5 (Supplementary material). To assess the nature of the nickel species formed upon wet impregnation of the γ-Al2O3/Ru-γ-Al2O3 layers on the coated monoliths H2-TPR and H2-TPD analysis were reported in Fig. 7a and b. In Fig. 7a both Ni- M and Ni-Ru- M samples showed a main reduction peak corresponding to Ni-β1 species in the temperature range 515–530°C, furthermore, the reduction of Ni-γ species was clearly distinguishable for Ni- M sample at 750°C; differently, Ni-Ru- M sample showed a very low amount of Ni-γ species with negligible hydrogen consumption. Moreover, Ni-Ru- M showed a small peak at 213°C corresponding to RuOx reduction. The overall results are comparable to those of powdered catalysts. Fig. 7b shows that the H2 desorption of Ni-Ru- M was substantially larger than that of Ni- M, in accordance with the behaviour of the corresponding powdered catalysts. In the low temperature range, the Ni-Ru- M catalyst did not clearly show two distinguishable peaks. Thus, the physic-chemical properties of monolithic catalysts are comparable to those of powdered ones. The catalytic activity as a function of time on stream at 800°C is reported in Fig. 8a. Ni- M catalyst showed a low initial activity with a CH4 conversion of 25% that increased during the first 40h reaching a 60% of conversion. The activity remained stable for further 20h then, suddenly decreased down to 25%. Ni-Ru- M catalyst showed a different behaviour: a very high initial CH4 conversion of 78% that decreased during the first 40h reaching the same 60% conversion value of Ni catalyst and then remained stable up to 70h. The activation of the Ni- M catalyst during the first 40h of operation can be explained by the presence of NiAlOx inactive phase, which requires a long time to be completely reduced. The reduction process continued in the reactor and consequently the number of Ni0 active sites increased and the catalytic activity improved. This behaviour was just the opposite of Ni- p catalyst and could be due to the different contact time and is currently under investigation in our laboratory. In the case of Ni-Ru- M catalyst, Ru kept the Ni completely reduced in presence of reactants and therefore the initial methane conversion was much higher. On both Ni- M and Ni-Ru- M catalysts the CO2 conversion was higher than CH4 conversion due to the RWGS. Therefore, H2/CO ratio was lower than unity accordingly to the change of catalytic activity during time on stream (see Fig. 8b). Particularly, at the beginning of the catalytic test the H2/CO ratio of Ni-Ru- M was considerably higher than that of Ni- M catalyst. However, at steady state, having comparable performance, both monoliths showed almost the same H2/CO ratio (~0.85). To clarify the reason of the different stability, a morphological analysis of both used monoliths was performed using FE-SEM. In Fig. 9a, Ni- M catalyst showed carbon filaments with the typical bamboo like morphology along the channels. Some Ni nanoparticles were detached from the γ-Al2O3 support and were clearly visible on the tip of the filaments (tip grow mechanism). On Ni-Ru- M catalyst shown in Fig. 9b, the Ni nanoparticles were hardly visible even though well dispersed on the support. The carbon filaments seemed to be formed by a base grown mechanism clearly laying on the catalytic layer. EDAX analysis was used to evaluate the quantity of carbon deposited along the channels. The carbon amount (C wt%) decreased from inlet to outlet on both monoliths and was substantially lower on Ni-Ru- M . In Table 3 the C wt% values in three different positions along the channel (inlet, mid, outlet) are reported. The difference of C wt% between the two monoliths was larger at the outlet: ~ 20% on Ni- M and ~3% on Ni-Ru- M , respectively, whereas at the inlet this difference is smaller (~35 and 22%, respectively). Therefore, FE-SEM and EDAX analysis clarified that the deactivation of the sample Ni -M after 60h of time on stream was due to the greater amount of C. Moreover these results may be useful to engineer the monolith by using a zone coating of a specially designed catalyst (i.e. with a higher content of precious metal) where there is a greater tendency to form carbon residues. From the investigation on Ni and Ni-Ru unstructured catalysts the following main conclusions can be drown: - Ru had a very low miscibility with Ni and does not promote the metal dispersion; - Ni catalyst deactivated at high GHSV due to the formation of Ni2+-containing inactive phases; - Ru promoted catalyst (Ni-Ru), free from chloride, was remarkable active and stable, only few carbon filaments were formed after 12h of operation; - the role of Ru was to maintain the catalysts surface reduced. Ru had a very low miscibility with Ni and does not promote the metal dispersion; Ni catalyst deactivated at high GHSV due to the formation of Ni2+-containing inactive phases; Ru promoted catalyst (Ni-Ru), free from chloride, was remarkable active and stable, only few carbon filaments were formed after 12h of operation; the role of Ru was to maintain the catalysts surface reduced. The reactant conversions of Ni-Ru deposited on cordierite monolith were higher in comparison to packed bed reactor (unstructured catalyst) due to the larger contact time. The initial conversions of Ni-Ru monolith were much more higher than the corresponding monometallic Ni, further confirming the positive effect of Ru on maintaining Ni reduced. Ni monolith required a long time to reach steady state condition and rapidly deactivated due to carbon formation. While, Ni-Ru monolith remained stable after reaching steady state condition. Morphological analysis confirmed that a large amount of carbon filament was deposited on monometallic Ni mainly by a tip growth mechanism, whereas on Ni-Ru, only few carbon filaments were deposited with a base growth mechanism primarily in the inlet region. Thus, Ru behaved as an efficient and cheap promoter of Ni based catalyst. Finally, from the distribution of carbon deposits on monolith, a zone coating may further increase the operating time of the reactor. The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from BioplanarSOFC funded by Fondazione Cassa di Risparmio di Trento e Rovereto. Supplementary figures Image 1 Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.fuproc.2016.12.015.
abstract: Dry reforming (DRM) is as an efficient way for CH4 and CO2 valorisation because the produced syn-gas has an H2/CO ratio equal to that suitable for the synthesis of oxygenated hydrocarbons and synthetic fuels. The development of Ni (10wt%) based structured and unstructured catalysts promoted by a small amount of Ru (0.5wt%) for DRM has been investigated. Unstructured catalysts were prepared by wet impregnation method and a combination of wash coating-wet impregnation methods was used for cordierite monoliths. Samples were characterized by XRD, BET, H2-TPR, TEM, FE-SEM, XPS techniques and the catalytic activity for DRM was evaluated in the temperature range 600–800°C. The catalyst stability was followed at 800°C during time on stream. Ru promoted catalyst (Ni-Ru) was remarkable active and stable whereas Ni catalyst deactivated due to the formation of Ni2+-containing inactive phases. Ni-Ru monolith was initially much more active than monometallic Ni stating the positive effect of Ru on maintaining Ni reduced. Reaching steady state condition, Ni rapidly deactivated due to carbon formation, whereas Ni-Ru monolith remained stable confirming that Ru behaves as an efficient and cheap promoter of Ni for DRM.
title: Ni supported on γ-Al 2 O 3 promoted by Ru for the dry reforming of methane in packed and monolithic reactors
