full_text: Ethylene is an essential material for the petrochemical industry. Currently, it has been mainly produced by thermal cracking of petroleum or natural gas. Bioethanol from biomass fermentation is an attractive alternative feedstock instead of petroleum as chemical feedstock. Therefore, ethanol dehydration to ethylene has attracted more and more attention. In recent years, some catalysts have been tested for the ethanol dehydration to ethylene, such as γ-alumina [1], heteropolyacid catalysts [2] and zeolites [3–5]. Among them, HZSM-5 zeolites are considered as a promising catalyst because of its good performance of lower reaction temperature and higher ethylene yield [6–8]. However, deactivation caused by coke deposition easily occurred on HZSM-5 zeolites for ethanol dehydration because of its smaller pore size and strong acidic properties. In order to avoid the coke formation during the process of ethanol dehydration, it is necessary to reduce the acidity and enhancing molecular transport over HZSM-5 zeolites. In order to enhance the molecular transport over HZSM-5 zeolites, nanocrystal HZSM-5 zeolites were synthesized to shorten the microporous path length [9,10]. In addition, post-treatment procedures of HZSM-5 zeolites are taken to create extra-porosity [11,12]. For example, Bi et al. [9] investigated catalytic performance in the bio-ethanol dehydration to ethylene over nanoscale HZSM-5 zeolite catalyst. Gayubo [11] studied the behavior of alkali-treated HZSM-5 zeolites for bioethanol dehydration to hydrocarbons. Steam treatment is the most common method to adjust the acidity of HZSM-5 zeolites for tailoring catalytic properties [13–16]. Moreover, the dealumination of HZSM-5 zeolites by steam treatment is a suitable methodology to create a secondary pore structure. Therefore, steam treatment improves the stability of HZSM-5 zeolites by reducing the acid amount and limiting diffusion. Zhao et al. [13] reported that the acid amount and the acid strength of HZSM-5 zeolites were decreased after hydrothermal treatment combined with acid leaching. Triantafillidis et al. [14] investigated the effect of the dealumination on the structural and acidic characteristics of HZSM-5 zeolite. Datka et al. [15] studied the effect of steam treatment on catalytic performances of HZSM-5 in n-heptane cracking. In general, the studies about steam treatment mainly discussed the acidity change of HZSM-5 before and after steam treatment process. However, the effects of the pore structure changes after steam treatment on catalytic stability of HZSM-5 are still unclear, especially for the reaction of ethanol dehydration to ethylene. In this paper, a commercial HZSM-5 was treated with pure steam at temperatures of 400–500°C to investigate the effects of the steam treatment on the mesoporosity development of HZSM-5 zeolites and on the catalytic stability in the reaction of ethanol dehydration to ethylene. The commercial HZSM-5 zeolites (SiO2/Al2O3 =25, crystal size: 2–3μm) were purchased from Catalyst Plant of Nankain University. Steam treatment of HZSM-5 zeolites was performed in a fixed bed reactor. The HZSM-5 zeolites were heated in a nitrogen flow to a specified temperature (400°C, 450°C, 500°C and 550°C). The samples were steam treated at the temperature for 6h in pure steam flow (WHSV=13.8h−1). Finally, the samples were cooled to room temperature in a nitrogen stream before the reaction. The samples from steam treatment were denoted as HTx (where x is the steam temperature). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a D/max-2500 X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation, and instrumental settings of 40kV and 40mA. The scanning range was from 5° to 50° at the rate 10°/min. The relative crystallinity is calculated according to the aggregate intensities of the three peaks at 2θ of 23.07°, 23.28°and 23.90°. The three peak intensities of the parent HZSM-5 zeolites were considered to be 100% crystallinity. Inductively coupled plasma (ICP) optical emission spectrometry was used for the determination of the Si and Al content in each sample after the steam treatment. The measurements were performed with a JA1100 inductively coupled plasma quantometer. N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were recorded on a Micromeritics Tristar-3000 volumetric apparatus. The total surface area was calculated according to the BET isothermal equation, and the micropore volume and external surface area were evaluated by the t-plot method. Mesopore distribution was obtained according to the BJH method. NH3-TPD experiments were carried out on a home-made equipment. The sample of 100mg was pretreated at 500°C in Ar flow (40ml/min) for 1h, and then cooled down to 40°C. The adsorption of pure NH3 was performed at 40°C. When the saturated adsorption of NH3 is achieved, the system is swept by the Ar (40ml/min) for 1h. The curves of NH3-TPD were recorded under Ar flow by heating from 40°C to 600°C at the rate of 5°C/min. IR spectra of adsorbed pyridine were recorded using a Nicolet-510P apparatus. The samples were pressed into thin wafers and evacuated in situ at 400°C for 2h, then cooled to room temperature, and successive pyridine was dosed until saturated adsorption on the samples. Finally, the system was evacuated at different temperature and pyridine-adsorbed IR spectra were recorded. Thermogravimetric (TG) analysis technique of samples after reaction was carried out to monitor the amount of coke deposition, recorded by the NETZSCH STA 409 PC equipment. It was heated from room temperature to 800°C at a rate of 10°C/min under air flow. Ethanol dehydration was tested in a fixed bed reactor with an inner diameter of 6mm under atmospheric pressure. A stainless steel tubular reactor was placed into a temperature programmed tubular furnace. After steam treatment of HZSM-5, 1.0g catalyst was placed into the middle of the reactor. The mixture of ethanol and water (20 (v) % ethanol) was injected into the reactor under nitrogen carrier gas. The weight hourly space velocity (WHSV) of ethanol was 2.37h−1. After reaching steady state conditions, the reaction products were analyzed using a gas chromatograph (GC-9560, Shanghai Haixin GC) with GDX-103 packed column, N2 carrier gas and FID detector at oven temperature of 120°C. The ethylene selectivity (SE) and the conversion of ethanol (XEtOH) were adopted to estimate the catalytic performance. SE is defined as the molar ratio of ethylene to the total converted ethanol. The selectivity to diethyl ether (SDEE) is defined as the molar ratio of diethyl ether (side product) to the total converted ethanol. XEtOH is defined as the molar ratio of converted ethanol to the total injected ethanol. Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of the samples before and after steam treatment. The XRD patterns of all the samples exhibit typical MFI phase and no additional phase appears, suggesting that the structure of HZSM-5 zeolites is not destroyed during the process of steam treatment. It can be seen from Fig. 1 that the peak intensity of 2θ=23–25° of HT400 sample decreases a little in comparison with those of the parent sample. The crystallinity of treated samples decreases with increasing treatment temperature. The relative crystallinity values of HZSM-5, HT400, HT450, HT500 and HT550 samples are 100%, 98.6%, 82.4%, 76.7% and 57.3%, respectively (see Table 1 ). The decrease in relative crystallinity is mainly due to the partial dealumination during the process of steaming [17,18]. It can be seen from Table 1 that the molar ratio of SiO2/Al2O3 increases after steam treatment. With the increase of the treatment temperature, it does not change significantly. It is reported that the steam treatment led to the removal of partial aluminum result in an increase in the ratio of SiO2/Al2O3. The insignificant change of the molar ratio of SiO2/Al2O3 with increasing temperature is caused by the extra framework of Al species (EFAl) formed during steam treatment which is not leached out of the zeolites [19]. The N2 adsorption isotherms of all samples are shown in Fig. 2 . The isotherm of the parent HZSM-5 represents I-type curve as a result of its microporous nature. A small hysteresis loop of the parent HZSM-5 suggests that mesopores exist in parent HZSM-5, which is mainly caused by the aggregated small crystals and leads to intercrystalline porosity [20]. A hysteresis loop of the steam treated samples appears in the relative pressure range of 0.45–1.00, indicating the formation of new mesopores in the zeolite structure after the steam treatment. The generation of new mesopores may be due to the structure rearrangement and secondary pore formation of HZSM-5 during the course of steam treatment [21]. With the increase in steam treatment temperature, the hysteresis loop gradually becomes obvious. It indicates that the more mesopores are generated. Fig. 3 shows the corresponding pore size distribution calculated by the adsorption branch with BJH model. It can be seen that the steam treated samples show a wide BJH pore size distribution range 12–45nm, confirming the presence of mesopores. The increasing treatment temperature leads to an increment in the mesopore volume. It may be attributed to the removal of partial framework aluminum from the zeolite lattice during the steam treatment. With the steam treatment temperature increasing, the surface areas (SBET), micropore area (Smicro) and micropore volume (Vmicro) decrease, and the average pore diameter enlarges (see Table 2 ). When the steam treatment temperature increase to 550°C, the micropore area and volume decrease dramatically, which may be the result of hydrolysis of the SiOAl bonds and the removal of partial aluminum from the zeolite framework. Table 2 shows that both the external surface area (Sext) and mesopore volume (Vmeso) increase and reach their maximum for the HT500 sample. However, the decrease of that is observed in HT550. In brief, new mesopores can be developed over the HZSM-5 zeolites by steam treatment at 400–500°C. And the steam treatment of 550°C leads to the partial destruction of the zeolite framework, which is disadvantageous not only for the micropores but also for the formation of mesopores. Fig. 4 shows NH3-TPD patterns of the parent and the treated HZSM-5 catalysts. For the parent HZSM-5 catalyst, NH3 desorption temperature below and above 300°C correspond to weak acid sites and strong acid sites, respectively [22]. As shown in Fig. 4, compared with the parent HZSM-5, the steam treated samples exhibit lower desorption temperature of the strong acid sites, indicating the weakened strengths of acid sites after the steam treatment. When the steam treatment temperature is higher than 450°C, strong acid sites on samples decrease drastically, resulting in strong suppression of the coke formation and improvement of catalytic stability. The reduction in the acid sites may be due to the framework dealumination during the steam treatment of catalysts. As mentioned above, the steam treatment of samples leads to an obvious decrease in acid site quantity and strength. Generally, the significant decrease of strong acid sites on sample is attributed to the decrease of the framework Al. During steam treatment, tetrahedral-coordinate Al in zeolite framework hydrolyzes to generate aluminum hydroxide, which leaves the zeolite framework, leading to the decrease of strong acid sites. Evidently, the steam treatment is an effective way to decrease the acid amount and acid strength. To further understand the effects of the steam treatment on the acidity property of HZSM-5 zeolites, the pyridine-adsorbed IR measurements were carried out. Table 3 gives quantitative information about the total acidity and the ratio of Brönsted acid to Lewis acid (B/L) of the catalysts. The values of B/L are calculated from the integrated area at 1540 and 1450cm−1 which is the equation of 1.88AB/1.42AL [23]. It is observed that the B acid sites decrease obviously and the L acid sites increase slightly after steam treatment from Table 3. The decrease in the strong B acid sites originates from the removal of the partial framework Al (FAl) and the decrease of the amount of acidic bridging hydroxyl (SiOHAl) groups [16]. In addition, the B/L decreases notably after the steam treatment. For example, the B/L is 3.3 over HZSM-5 sample, while it is 1.4 over the HT500. The decrease of the B/L indicates that the loss of B acid sites is greater than that of L acid sites after the steam treatment. It may probably be the result of the dealumination and the formation of some L acidic external framework aluminum during the steam treatment. It should be noted that the change tendency of the B/L was in agreement with other researchers [24,25]. The effect of reaction temperature on the ethanol dehydration over steam treated catalysts was studied. Fig. 5 shows the conversion and selectivity of the parent and the steam treated HZSM-5 catalysts as a function of reaction temperature. Clearly, the reaction of ethanol dehydration is incomplete and ethanol conversion is lower at lower temperature. With the increasing reaction temperature, the conversions of ethanol and the selectivity to ethylene increase continuously over the different catalysts. The parent sample has the highest strong acid sites and exhibits the highest initial activity, while the steam treated samples show the less catalytic activity than that of the parent sample. The high reaction temperatures are propitious to the ethylene formation via intra-molecular dehydration, while the low reaction temperatures favor intermolecular dehydration to produce diethyl ether. Therefore, the selectivity to ethylene increases with increasing temperature, while the selectivity to diethyl ether decreases. At relatively high temperature (255–270°C), the selectivity to ethylene over the parent HZSM-5 begins to decrease due to the formation of higher hydrocarbons, while the selectivity to ethylene over steam treated catalysts enhances markedly due to the sharp decrease of strong acidic sites of steam treated HZSM-5 (see Fig. 4). Actually, the strong acidic sites play a key role in the conversion of ethanol to higher hydrocarbons, which can be easily form coke over the catalyst [26,27]. The product distribution at the optimal reaction condition for all samples was given in Table 4 . Table 4 shows that the product includes ethylene, diethyl ether (DEE) and higher hydrocarbons. Among them, ethylene is the main product. Table 4 also shows that higher hydrocarbon is not produced over HT500 catalyst and the amount of higher hydrocarbon over other steam treated samples is less than that over the parent sample. It indicates that the steam treatment strongly suppressed the formation of higher hydrocarbon. In a word, the steam treatment at different temperature can strongly influence the catalytic performance. The stability of catalysts (except the HT550°C) of ethanol dehydration to ethylene was studied at the optimal reaction condition. The results show that the parent HZSM-5 catalyst has poor stability, i.e. the conversion of ethanol and the selectivity to ethylene all decrease with reaction time (see Fig. 6 ). However, the steam treated catalysts show effective catalytic activity and much higher stability. For example, the conversion of ethanol and the selectivity to ethylene over HT500 are all higher than 95% even at 350h reaction time (see Fig. 6). It can be concluded that the steam treated catalysts exhibit higher catalytic stability than the parent HZSM-5 does. Generally, the stability of catalyst is associated with the catalyst deactivation. The deactivation on HZSM-5 should be attributed to the accumulation of coke deposition on the catalyst [28], which blocks the access of the reactants to the inner active sites uncovered in the channel. Usually, the amount of coke deposition over acidic zeolites is proportional to its strong acidity. The strong acidity on the surface has direct influence on the reaction stability of catalysts. For the parent HZSM-5, more strong acid sites lead to the quick deactivation of the catalyst. Thus the conversion of ethanol and the selectivity to ethylene decrease rapidly as the reaction goes on. It is reported that the stability of catalysts could be improved by eliminating the strong acid sites in the zeolites [29]. For the steam treated HZSM-5, the decrease in the acidity and the B/L suppresses the coke deposition, resulting in the relief of the coverage of active sites and channel blockage. Therefore, the deactivation of the steam treated catalysts is obviously delayed. Meanwhile, the increase of mesoporoes after steam treatment can favor the diffusion of reactants and products, thus increasing the turnover frequency of each active site and decrease the possibility of coke formation. Dogu [30,31] reported that such bidisperse pore size distribution of the catalysts was highly advantageous to catalytic reactions due to their much less diffusion resistance effect. In fact, the coke can be formed inevitably during the reaction of ethanol dehydration to ethylene. When the small amount of coke was formed, it can deposit on the active sites in the mesopores, which cannot hinder the feed or products diffusing from or to the active sites. The results also show that the coke was formed in the reaction procedure and tends to deposit in the mesopores (see Table 5 ). The newly created mesopores in steam treated samples can accommodate part of coke deposition resulting in the suppressing of coke deposition in its inherent micropores to some extent. It is consistent with Gayubo, who [11,12] reported that the mesopores introduced by alkali treatment over HZSM-5 attenuate deactivation of the catalyst by coke formation. To further demonstrate the reason for the high stability over steam treated HZSM-5 zeolites, the spent HT500 catalyst was investigated in comparison with the spent HZSM-5. The TG analysis was performed to study coke deposition on the spent HZSM-5 and the spent HT500 catalyst after reaction. As seen in Fig. 7 , the total weight loss up to 800°C shows a similar trend, i.e. 8.4wt.% loss and 9.8wt.% loss for the spent HZSM-5 and HT500, respectively. Actually, the weight loss below the temperature of 150°C is attributed to the physically adsorbed water in the porous materials. The weight loss in 200–550°C was attributed to desorption of the light coke, and the weight loss in 550–800°C is attributed to the burning of heavy coke [32]. The amount of coke deposition over the spent HZSM-5/120h is more than that over the spent HT500/350h catalyst. To clearly show the result, the amount of coke deposition per hour of reaction time is calculated. It is about 0.0117wt.% over the spent HZSM-5/120h at 550–800°C and decreases to about 0.0017wt.% over the spent HT500 catalyst. In addition, the amount of coke deposition per hour of the spent HZSM-5/120h and the spent HT500/350h at 200–550°C are 0.030wt.% and 0.005wt.%, respectively. Obviously, the amount of coke deposition over the spent HZSM-5 is higher than that over the spent HT500. It indicated that the steam treatment reduced coke deposition, which could be responsible for the improvement of catalyst stability. The pore characters of the fresh and the spent HZSM-5 catalysts were studied by the N2 adsorption–desorption technique, and the quantitative results were listed in Table 5. As shown in Table 5, the large amount of coke deposition is found on the spent HZSM-5, which blocks the catalyst channel during the reaction. On the parent HZSM-5 sample, the mesopore area (Sext) of 84.1m2 g−1 and the mesopore volume (Vmeso) of 0.112cm3 g−1 decrease down to 10.7m2 g−1 and 0.026cm3 g−1 after the reaction, respectively. The decrease of Sext and Vmeso is caused by the coke deposition. The channel blockage by coke deposition over the spent HZSM-5 restricts the access of reactants/intermediates to the internal active sites on catalyst. Moreover, the loss of micropore area (Smic) and the loss of micropore volume (Vmic) of spent HZSM-5 is 39.9m2 g−1 and 0.023cm3 g−1, respectively, while the loss of Smic and the loss of Vmic for spent HT500 is 20.8m2 g−1 and 0.006cm3 g−1. It clearly demonstrates the reduction of micropore blockage over HT500 zeolites. Due to the reduction of micropore blockage of the spent HT500 sample, most active sites are still accessible to the reactants and intermediates. Consequently, ethanol conversion over HT500 sample is much higher than that over HZSM-5 sample. The loss of micropore area and mesopore area of the spent HZSM-5 is 39.9m2 g−1 and 73.4m2 g−1, while that over the spent HT500 is 20.8m2 g−1 and 49.5m2 g−1, respectively. The higher loss of mesopore area over catalysts suggests that the coke precursors or coke produced during the reaction procedure tends to deposit in the newly created mesopores. So, the newly created mesopores may accommodate part of coke deposition, consequently reducing the formation of coke deposition in its inherent micropores to some extent. Hence, the stabilities of HZSM-5 zeolites can be relatively improved by steam treatment. Differences of the acid amount of the HZSM-5 and HT500 before and after reaction are shown in Fig. 8 by the NH3-TPD profiles. Compared with the fresh catalyst, the amount of the acid sites of spent catalyst decreased obviously, especially for the strong acid sites. This may be attributed to the coke deposition on the strong acid sites preferentially. The peak position belonging to strong acidity shifts from 428°C to 402°C, while the peak assigning to weak acidity shifts from 182°C to 171°C, indicating that the strengths of acid sites weaken after reaction. While the amount of acid sites over the spent HT500 decreased is less than that over the spent HZSM-5, especially for the strong acid sites, it might be related to the introduction of mesopores by the steam treatment. The decrease in the acidity, especially the strong acid sites, suppresses the coke formation. The coke deposition amount per hour over spent HT500 is 0.0068wt.%, while it is 0.0417wt.% over spent HZSM-5. This is in agreement with the TG results shown in Fig. 7. The steam treatment is an effective method to tune the acidity and the pore structure of the HZSM-5 zeolites. It reduces both the total number of acid sites and the B/L, and changes the pore structure from the single micropores to the coexistence of micropores and mesopores over the HZSM-5 zeolites. Catalytic tests for ethanol dehydration revealed that the steam treated catalysts exhibit good coke-resistance and stability than the parent HZSM-5, especially the HT500 catalyst. The analyses of the coke depositions over the spent catalysts indicated that the HT500 catalyst contained less coke deposition than that over the parent HZSM-5 and facilitated the formation of light coke. The newly created mesopores may accommodate part of coke deposition, consequently suppressing the formation of coke deposition in its inherent micropores to some extent. Thus, the improvement in the catalytic stability does not only simply correspond to the catalyst acidic properties but also correspond to the pore structure as well.
abstract: The effect of steam treatment on HZSM-5 catalysts was analyzed during ethanol dehydration to ethylene. The parent and the steam treated HZSM-5 catalysts were characterized by various techniques. The results showed that the steam treatment created new mesopores on HZSM-5 zeolites by increasing the treatment temperature, which resulted in the coexistence of micropores and mesopores. The total amount of acid sites and the Brönsted/Lewis ratios of HZSM-5 zeolites decreased after steam treatment, especially for the strong acid sites. The steam treated catalyst at 500°C (HT500) exhibited excellent catalytic stability and good selectivity for ethanol dehydration to ethylene. The improvement in the catalytic stability can be attributed to lower acidity, lower ratio of B/L and newly created mesopores after steam treatment. The analyses of the coke depositions over the spent catalysts indicated that the HT500 catalyst contained less coke deposition than that over the parent HZSM-5 did. The newly created mesopores on catalyst during the steam treatment may accommodate part of coke deposition, suppressing the formation of coke deposition in its inherent micropores to some extent.
title: Effect of steam treatment on catalytic performance of HZSM-5 catalyst for ethanol dehydration to ethylene
