full_text: Residue hydrotreating technology (RHT) can effectively remove heteroatoms and improve the quality of hydrocarbons in residual oils, thereby increasing the selectivity and yield of high-value products in the downstream processes, e.g. fluid catalytic cracking (FCC), delayed coking (DC) and low sulfur bunker fuel blending [1–3]. For fuel-oriented refineries, RHT is widely exploited to couple these downstream processes to produce transportation fuels and other products, such as gasoline, diesel, low-sulfur bunker fuel, low-sulfur coke, and so on, and it has become one of the most effective processes for heavy oil upgrading [1,4–7]. However, with the improvement of fuel efficiency and the rapid development of non-fossil fuel vehicles, the growth driving force of oil demand is gradually shifting from transportation fuels to chemicals [8,9]. Thus, RHT needs to be improved to support the transition from fuel-oriented refinery to chemical-oriented refinery by coupling with deep catalytic cracking (DCC) process, which has become a preferred technology to produce light olefins (e.g. ethylene, propylene and butene) in large quantities [10]. It was showed that the yield of light olefins of DCC unit increases with the contents of alkanes, one-ring to four-ring cycloalkanes and alkylbenzenes in its feedstock, and in order to maximize the yield of light olefins, the upstream RHT process must undergo a high efficiency in heteroatoms removal and aromatics saturation [10]. Actually, for RHT process, a hydrodesulfurization (HDS) rate of ca. 95% might be required for its downstream DCC process. However, the statistical HDS rate of industrial RHT units is much lower, typically 80%–90%. So far, due to the poor reactivity of sulfur compounds in residual oils, it has always been a huge challenge to realize such a high HDS depth meanwhile maintaining a long-term operation cycle for RHT. To meet this challenge, a lot of efforts has been made, such as feedstock selection [11], catalyst development [12], catalyst grading technology improvement [13], process development and optimization [1,2], and more fundamentally, structure analysis of refractory sulfur compounds [7,14], reaction mechanism investigation [15,16], and so on. Unfortunately, although these efforts are very effective to improve the deep HDS processes of gasoline [17–19], diesel oil [20–24] and vacuum gas oil (VGO) [25–27], it seems quite difficult to achieve a very deep HDS of residual oils [10,28], which is probably attributed to the difficult balance between the hydrotreating severity and the operation cycle [1,2]. But anyway, before realizing a deep or ultra-deep HDS process of residual oils, a deep insight into the physicochemical properties of the most refractory sulfur compounds and a systematic understanding of their reactivity under RHT process should be first carried out [14]. It is a great challenge to trace and identify the sulfur compounds in residual oils due to the massive coexisting molecules with various extraordinarily complex molecular structures [5,29,30]. Fortunately, ultrahigh-resolution Fourier transform ion cyclotron resonance mass spectrometry (FT-ICR MS) itself or combined with prior fractionation schemes or methods can obtain detailed information about the heteroatom-containing compounds in petroleum samples [14,16,26,27,31–44]. By virtue of these analysis methods, the evolution of sulfur compounds during different processes of heavy oils, such as VGO [26,34], atmospheric residual oils [37,38,41–43], residual oils [7,14,39,44], vacuum residual oils [35,40], has been studied by qualitative or semi-quantitative characterization methods. However, most of these studies were limited in scopes where the processing of heavy oils was carried out under conventional operation severity, generally leading to a lacking of wholly and accurately understanding about the sulfur evolution, especially during critical or extreme operation conditions, e.g. an ultra-deep HDS process of residual oils (i.e. HDS rate reaches up to above 95%). In fact, the evolution and molecular structures of refractory sulfur compounds during RHT process have been studied recently by the authors' group [14]. It was showed that the S1 are the refractory compounds during the RHT process, and their reactivity is greatly weakened by both the increasing aromaticity and the decreasing hydrotreating severity. Therefore, it is reasonable to suspect that the reactivity order of sulfur compounds obtained in a conventional RHT process should not be probably suitable to a deep or ultra-deep RHT process. Moreover, in the previous work where the HDS ratio were controlled from 53% to 80% [39], it was concluded that the key refractory sulfur compounds are those with double bond equivalent (DBE) equal to 9–12. Whereas considering the lower reactivity of the higher DBE sulfur compounds during RHT processes with lower HDS ratios, the above conclusions are questionable and should be revised by direct and clear evidences achieved from the much deeper RHT processes. Besides, a more specific molecular structure of the most refractory sulfur compounds is still lacking due to the overly complex CID information of conventional hydrogenated residues. Herein, in order to get a more fundamental and better understanding of HDS chemistry during RHT process and thereof obtain a deep insight into the molecular structure of the most refractory sulfur compounds, six samples obtained from six ultra-deep RHT processes with HDS ratio from 91.7% to 99.2% were chosen to be further studied by virtue of atmospheric pressure photoionization (APPI) FT-ICR MS combined with CID FT-ICR MS with narrow isolation windows. Based on the semi-quantitative information and the detailed microstructure of the six samples, the evolution and molecular structure of sulfur compounds during a deep RHT process were well discussed. The feedstock (denoted as XTAR), catalyst grading system, schematic diagram of RHT process and detailed operation conditions of the six RHT tests have been reported in the previous work [10], which are also available in Table S1–S3 and Fig. S1 in the Supporting Information. The hydrogenated samples analyzed in the present work were operated under temperatures from 370 °C to 395 °C. The key properties (i.e. density, asphaltene and sulfur content) of the feedstock and the six hydrogenated samples named from P1 to P6, together with the effect of reaction temperature on the HDS ratio are list in Table 1 . Before FT-ICR MS analysis, the feedstock XTAR and its six hydrogenated samples were dissolved in toluene (HPLC grade, Fisher Chemical, distilled prior to use) to obtain seven solutions of 0.5 mg/mL, respectively. Then the resulting solutions were analyzed using a 15 Tesla Bruker SolariX XR FT-ICR spectrometer with APPI source in the positive mode. During the analysis process, the prepared samples were injected into a metal-coated silica capillary by a syringe pump and sprayed at a rate of 360 μL/h. In the ionization source, nitrogen (99.999%) was used as the drying gas and nebulizing gas at a flow rates of 2.0 L/min and 1.0 L/min, and the gases were heated to 200 °C and 400 °C, respectively. All samples were analyzed for acquiring both broadband mass spectra and CID mass spectra. In both mode, the RF amplitude of the octopole was 350 Vpp, and the voltage for trap plate were 1.7 V. The RF amplitude of the collision hexapole was 400 Vpp in broadband mode and 1400 Vpp in CID mode. For acquiring broadband mass spectra, the time-of-flight was 1.2 ms, and the mass detection range was 150–1500 Da. For acquiring CID mass spectra, the time-of-flight was 0.8 ms, and the mass range was 100–1000 Da with the width of the isolation window was 0.5 Da (M ± 0.25 Da). Ions were accumulated for 2.0 s in the hexapole collision cell prior to their introduction into the ICR cell. Helium (99.999%) was employed as the collision gas with the collision voltage ranging 0–30 V. In all experiments, each spectrum was collected with 128 co-added scans and 8 M size. For the obtained mass spectra, the peaks with a relative abundance greater than six standard deviations of the baseline root-mean-square (rms) noise (6σ) were selected for the data processing. Molecular formulas (CcHhNnOoSs, the subscripts represent the numbers of atoms of each element) in the broadband spectra and the fragment ion formulas (in the form of [M-H]+) in the CID spectra were assigned according to the accurate m/z values, and the relative error was limited below 1 ppm. Double bond equivalent (DBE) was calculated as DBE = c − h/2 + n/2 + 1. All data were processed by the home-made software. The scaled relative abundances were calculated by dividing the compound relative abundances by the maximum relative abundance of each fraction. The sulfur pseudo-contents were obtained by multiplying relative abundances by the sulfur content in the sample. The pseudo-HDS rates were calculated based on the sulfur pseudo-contents. In the previous work [10], with the help of the processed low-viscosity feedstock, the high-performance catalysts and the used severe operation conditions, six high or ultra-high RHT processes were carried out, and the sulfur contents of the six deeply‑hydrogenated samples together with the corresponding HDS rates are listed in Table 1. As shown, with the temperature increasing from 370 °C to 395 °C, the sulfur contents of the six hydrotreated samples were reduced from 0.243 wt% to 0.023 wt%, with the corresponding HDS rate from 91.7% to 99.2%, which is much higher than the generally reported value in industry area or in research area (typically 80%–90%), thus laying the foundation of unravelling the evolution of refractory sulfur compounds, and especially the molecular structure of the most refractory sulfur compounds during RHT process [2,7,14]. Furthermore, some studies have pointed out that [14,26,37], during the hydrotreating process of heavy oils, the compounds of S1O1, S2 and S3 classes were very easily to be removed, and actually the key refractory sulfur compounds were the S1 class compounds remain in the hydrotreated products. However, considering the high abundance of S2 class compounds in the feedstock and they may be transferred to S1 class compounds during RHT process, the present study mainly focused on the physicochemical properties of S1 and S2 classes compounds in the feedstock and S1 class compounds in the six deeply‑hydrogenated residues. Other sulfur compounds such as S3, S1O1, S1N1 and S1O2 classes were not discussed in the present work due to their very limited abundance in the feedstock and especially the six deeply‑hydrogenated residues [10]. Fig. 1 shows the plots of DBE versus carbon number for the S1 and S2 classes in the feedstock XTAR. It could be seen that the S1 and S2 classes exited widely in the feedstock, with DBE ranges of 3–31 and 7–30, carbon number ranges of 19–67 and 20–61, respectively. While in the six deeply‑hydrogenated residual oils, all the S2 class compounds were removed, and both the DBE and carbon number ranges of the S1 class compounds narrowed sharply, as shown in Fig. 2 . Furthermore, the DBE and carbon number ranges of the S1 class compounds became narrower with increasing the hydrotreating severity. In the most severely‑hydrogenated residue (P6, HDS rate = 99.2%), the DBE and carbon number ranges decreased to 9–16 and 26–44, respectively, and the identified sulfur compounds became sparse. Moreover, the dominated DBE and carbon number in the deeply‑hydrogenated residues were almost unchanged. The key data of the afore-mentioned changes are summarized in Table 2 . Very interestingly, these observations are similar to an investigation of VGO deep HDS process [26], but different from some investigations of conventional RHT processes [14,37,40], in which the plots of DBE versus carbon number usually move to the top right for the hydrogenated samples. This quick overview indicates that during deep RHT process, the reactivity of S1 class compounds are not only determined by their molecular structure, but also closely related to the hydrotreating severity. In addition, the most refractory sulfur compounds in residual oils probably have similar molecular structure. This quick overview indicates that the inconsistent reactivity order of sulfur compounds in the literature [14,26,37,40] should probably relate to the different hydrotreating depths among these researches. In addition, the most refractory sulfur compounds in residual oils probably have similar molecular structures, which can be determined by the direct and clear information from ultra-deep HDS process, as discussed in the following sections. It is a good choice to investigate the HDS reactivity of the S1 class compounds in residual oils by lumping numerous sulfur compounds into smaller families of pseudo-components for the reason that, it is simple enough to avoid building a reaction network for huge number of sulfur compounds, but complex enough to track the reactivity of sulfur compounds with similar structures. Adopting similar lumping method used by other researchers [14,26], the S1 class sulfur compounds in the residual oils were divided into six families according to DBE values, as shown in Table 3 . To be mentioned, during the DBE-based HDS kinetic analysis, the pseudo-contents of S1 families in the feedstock XTAR were calculated as the sum of S1 families and half of the S2 families by assuming all the S2 families were transferred to S1 families during deep RHT process. While during the calculation of pseudo-conversion HDS rate, the formation of each sulfur family from other sulfur families was not considered due to the reason that there was not enough information to build a complete HDS reaction network. Similar approaches were also adopted in the some former researches [14,26]. In addition, considering the fact that each sulfur family are consisted of numerous compounds with different reactivity, the reactivity of sulfur families were usually referred to their average value. Fig. 3 shows the charts of pseudo-contents of S1 families identified for the feedstock XTAR and the six deeply‑hydrogenated samples. It can be seen that a large range of sulfur families were contained in the feedstock, and their pseudo-contents decreased in the following order: benzothiophenes (BT) > dibenzothiophenes (DBT) > benzonaphtothiophenes (BNT) > 5-plus ring thiophenes (5R-p) > 5 ring thiophenes (5R) > thiophenes (T). While in the deeply‑hydrogenated residues, the pseudo-contents of the most sulfur families decreased greatly. In addition, the T family were not identified in all deeply‑hydrogenated samples, while the BT family and the 5R-p family were sequentially removed to close zero with increasing HDS severity. Specially, in the most severely‑hydrogenated sample, only DBT, BNT and 5R families were identified, and their pseudo-contents followed an order of DBT > BNT > 5R. In other words, during deep RHT process, the sulfur families may be removed to close zero according to the following sequence: T, BT, 5R-p, 5R, BNT and DBT. More detailedly, although the beneficial degree were different, the pseudo-conversion HDS rate order of sulfur families stayed unchanged with the total HDS rate increased from 91.7% to 99.2%, as shown in Fig. 4 . At a total HDS rate of 91.7% and 99.2%, the pseudo-conversion HDS rate orders were T > BT > 5R-p > 5R > BNT > DBT, and T ≈ BT ≈ 5R-p > 5R > BNT > DBT, respectively. Similar phenomena was observed in a VGO HDS process with a HDS rate of 93.4%–98.9%, where the typical pseudo-conversion HDS rate order is T > BT > (DBE > 15) > BNT > DBT [26]. However, it was observed that during conventional RHT processes, the T, BT, DBT, BNT, 5R and 5R-p families were all identified in the hydrogenated samples [14,37], and their reactivity decreases in the orders of ((T > BT) or (BT > T)) > DBT > BNT > 5R > 5R-p due to different operating severity [14]. These conflictions may due to both the molecular structure of sulfur compounds and hydrotreating conditions. On the one hand, increasing molecular weight and aromaticity, as well as alkyl substitutions near the sulfur atom may decrease the reactivity of sulfur compounds due to the increased diffusion resistance and/or the reduced intrinsic reactivity [14,18,21,25,38,46]. On the other hand, during HDS processes of petroleum, the direct desulfurization (DDS) route is deeply inhibited by the increasing steric hindrance [29], which indicates that the hydrogenation (HYD) route may play a more important role in deep RHT process due to the high steric hindrance of sulfur compounds in residual oils. It seems that during conventional RHT process, due to the limited reactivity of aromatic saturation, the decreased reactivity order of sulfur compounds is determined by the increased order of molecular weight and aromaticity, except for the T and BT families under low hydrotreating severity, which may due to the fact that thiophenes could be an intermediate product of other multi-aromatic-rings thiophene compounds during the sulfur evolution [14,45]. While during deep RHT process, the severe hydrotreating conditions greatly change the relative reactivity of sulfur compounds. Firstly, due to the low steric hindrance and the high hydrogen pressure (17 MPa) which is more beneficial to the removal of themselves than BT and DBT families [14,45], T family are the most active sulfur compounds during deep RHT process, followed by BT family. Secondly, the high severity of deep RHT process increase the removal of multi-aromatic-ring thiophenes greatly through the HYD route. It was point out that the more aromatic rings, the easier it is for the sulfur compounds to be adsorbed on the active site of the catalyst and undergo hydrogenation reaction [46]. While the sulfur compounds containing more naphthenic-ring structures are easier to be removed due to the decreased steric hindrance [14]. Thus, the reactivity of DBT, BNT, 5R and 5R-p families are consistent with the order of aromatic saturation. The combined effect of these aspects is that during RHT process, the reactivity of sulfur compounds may varied with the increasing hydrotreating severity. During the conventional and the severe hydrotreating conditions, the reactivity of sulfur compounds may decrease in the orders of T > BT > DBT > BNT > 5R > 5R-p and T > BT > 5R-p > 5R > BNT > DBT respectively. In addition to the dominate effect of aromaticity, carbon number also have an important impact on the reactivity of sulfur compounds. To understand this, the evolution of S1 class as a function of carbon number before and after deep RHT process is shown in Fig. 5 . It can be seen that after deep RHT process, the carbon number distribution range become narrower with increasing hydrotreating severity. While in conventional RHT processes, the plots of S1 class relative abundance versus carbon number usually shift right to some extent for the hydrogenated samples [14,37]. These conflictions may due to the following reasons. Firstly, the low carbon sulfur compounds, which usually are less alkylated, are more easily removed during hydrogenation processes at any severity due to their lower steric hindrance. Secondly, the high carbon sulfur compounds, which usually are more alkylated or have more naphthenic and/or aromatic rings, may be reduced through the breaking of side chain bonds or the HYD route [14,37]. Thus, during conventional RHT processes, the carbon number distributions of refractory sulfur compounds move to the high carbon side due to the less intense of side chain bonds breaking and aromatic saturation. On the contrast, during deep RHT process, the corresponding distributions narrow to smaller range due to the higher intense of side chain bonds breaking and aromatic saturation. Furthermore, Fig. 6 shows the pseudo-conversion HDS rates as a function of alkyl-substituted carbon number by assuming the skeletons of S1 class with DBE = 9, 12, 15 and 18 have carbon number of 8, 12, 16 and 20 respectively. As illustrated, the pseudo-conversion rate of the S1 class remained in the severely‑hydrogenated samples decreased with increasing alkyl-substituted carbon number due to the increasing steric hindrance [39]. In addition, the S1 class compounds with higher and especially lower carbon numbers were removed more easily with increasing DBE in the more severe hydrotreating conditions. On the one hand, the sulfur compounds with high DBE are easier to undergo aromatic saturation reactions. On the other hand, the sulfur compounds with high DBE may have fewer alkyl-substituted carbon number and more alkyl-substituted positions, both of which lead to a lower average carbon number of alkyl side chains, resulting in lower steric hindrance caused by alkyl substitution. Based on the above results, it can be concluded that, during RHT process the T family usually have the highest reactivity due to their lowest steric hindrance, followed by BT family. While the DBT, BNT, 5R and 5R-p families usually with high steric hindrance caused by the skeleton and/or alkyl substitution should mainly be removed by the HYD routine. Thus, during the conventional hydrotreating condition, the decreased reactivity order of sulfur compounds is determined by the increased order of molecular weight and aromaticity due to the limited reactivity of aromatic saturation. While during the severe hydrotreating condition, which usually have high reactivity of aromatic saturation, the increased reactivity order of DBT, BNT, 5R and 5R-p families is determined by the increased order of aromaticity and the decreased order of alkyl-substituted carbon number. As a result, during the conventional and severe hydrotreating conditions, the reactivity of sulfur compounds may decrease in the order of T > BT > DBT > BNT > 5R > 5R-p and T > BT > 5R-p > 5R > BNT > DBT respectively. To go further, the molecular structure of the most refractory sulfur compounds was analyzed by CID FT-ICR MS with narrow isolation windows. It has been shown that during deep RHT process, the most refractory sulfur compounds are DBT family, followed by BNT family. In detail, the dominate DBE for the refractory sulfur compounds in the most deeply‑hydrogenated residual oil was 10–12, as shown in Fig. 7a. In the previous work [39], ignoring the lower reactivity of the higher DBE sulfur compounds during conventional RHT process, it was concluded that the key refractory sulfur compounds were those with DBE = 9–12 due to their dominate abundance in the hydrotreated products, and the key refractory sulfur compounds deduced from the results of CID were those alkyl(C2)-substituted DBTs. To check these conclusions more carefully, Fig. 8 shows the pseudo-conversion rates of S1 class with DBE = 9–14 for the six deeply‑hydrogenated samples. It can be seen that the pseudo-conversion rates of the S1 class with DBE = 9 were relative high in all samples, and in the most severely-hydrotreated sample, the S1 class with DBE = 10–12 shared similar low pseudo-conversion HDS rate, while the rest S1 class shared similar high pseudo-conversion HDS rate. Considering the fact that the pseudo-contents of S1 class with DBE = 10–12 were also the highest in the most severely-hydrotreated sample, it could be concluded that the most refractory sulfur compounds during deep RHT process are those with DBE = 10–12, followed by DBE = 9. This interesting result is similar to, but not exactly the same with the HDS processes of diesel and VGO, where alkyl-DBTs especially alkyl-DBTs with substituents at the 4 and 6 positions are or were supposed as the most refractory sulfur compounds [18,21,26,46]. Furthermore, it is surprising that under such a high HDS rate of 99.2%, the reactivity difference of the sulfur compounds with DBE = 10–12 was negligible, indicating that composition of the most refractory sulfur compounds during RHT process is much more complicate than those in diesel and VGO HDS processes. The reason for these observations may need to find from the molecular structure of the most refractory sulfur compounds. It was concluded that the sulfur compounds containing more naphthenic-ring structures are easier to be removed [14]. Thus, considering the severe operating conditions of deep RHT process, it is reasonable to speculate that the skeletons of the most refractory sulfur compounds in the deeply‑hydrogenated residues are mainly constructed by aromatic rings, as illustrated in Fig. 7a. Moreover, Fig. 7b shows that the carbon number distribution range of the refractory sulfur compounds in the most severely‑hydrogenated sample was 26–44, and the corresponding dominated carbon number range was 36–38, which indicates the most refractory sulfur compounds may also have similar alkyl side chains. If the above conjecture can be confirmed, a more specific molecular structure can be obtained for the most refractory sulfur compounds during deep RHT process. It has been shown in Fig. 1 that the dominate relative abundance of the refractory sulfur compounds in the most severely‑hydrogenated sample had a m/z of about 530–536. Therefore, the isolation windows selected for the CID FT-ICR MS analysis of residual oils were 530.2 ± 0.25 Da, 532.2 ± 0.25 Da and 534.2 ± 0.25 Da, which corresponded to the molecular formulas of C37H54S1 (DBE = 11), C37H56S1 (DBE = 10) and C37H58S1 (DBE = 9) respectively. Plots of DBE versus carbon number of the fragment ions from the S1 class in the feedstock and the most deeply‑hydrogenated sample are shown in Fig. 9 , in which the size of the parent ion bubbles (red) is reduced by 75% to match the size of the fragment ion bubbles (blue). In addition, some possible molecular structures are suggested as the precursors of the fragment ions for the feedstock and the most severely‑hydrogenated sample. Fig. 10 shows the plots of relative abundance versus DBE of the fragment ions from the S1 class in the feedstock XTAR and the six deeply‑hydrogenated samples. From Fig. 9 and Fig. 10, it could be seen that after the fragmentation of the feedstock, the most abundant fragment ions still had the same DBE as the parent ions, which were attributed to the dealkylation of the condensed aromatic sulfur compounds [33,34]. In addition, there were a lot of fragment ions with DBE between 5 and the DBE of the parent ions, which were attributed to the fragmentation of the naphthenic rings in the parent ions [33,34]. These observations confirm that both the condensed aromatic and naphthenic-aromatic sulfur compounds are exited in the feedstock [33,35]. After the fragmentation of the deeply‑hydrogenated residual oils, the fragments with the same DBE as the parent ions were more dominate, while the fragment ions with lower DBE than the parent ions were more rare for C37H54S1 and C37H56S1 or even absent for C37H58S1, indicating that the naphthenic-aromatic sulfur compounds are removed more greatly than condensed aromatic sulfur compounds during deep RHT process, and the skeletons of the most refractory sulfur compounds are mainly constructed by aromatic rings. However, it is interesting to noticed the abundances of the fragment ions with lower DBE than the parent ions were not always reduced with increasing hydrotreating severity, which may due to the partially hydrogenated of sulfur compounds with higher DBE. Fig. 11 shows the plots of relative abundance versus carbon number of the fragment ions from the S1 class in the samples P1 and P6, which with HDS rate of 91.7% and 99.2% respectively. As shown, the carbon number distribution of the fragment ions from the S1 class in P1 was continuous, indicating the sulfur compounds remained in P1 are numerous. While for the most severely‑hydrogenated sample, the carbon number distribution of the fragment ions from S1 class became sparse, indicating the sulfur compounds remained in P6 were very limited and may have similar alkyl side chains. Fig. 11 also propose the alkyl-substituted carbon numbers for the fragment ions from the S1 class in sample P6. It is noticed that there was no alkyl(C1)-substituted DBT, and the most abundant sulfur compounds were alkyl(C2-C3)-substituted DBTs, indicating that there are multi-substituted alkyl side chains on the skeletons. In addition, deduced from the carbon number distribution of the fragment ions, these alkyl side chains may mainly have a carbon number of 4–9. Furthermore, these alkyl side chains may possibly occupy the substitution positions near the sulfur atom, which would decrease the reactivity of the sulfur compounds due to increased steric hindrance. For instance, the 4 and 6 substitution positions for DBT. With these consideration, some possible molecular structures of the most refractory sulfur compounds during deep RHT process are illustrated in Fig. 9. Herein, different from the existing research results, the most refractory sulfur compounds during deep RHT process had dominate DBE range of 10–12, followed by 9, and dominate carbon number range of 36–38. Furthermore, the skeletons of the most refractory sulfur compounds mainly consisted of 2–3 aromatic rings and 0–1 naphthenic/cyclohexene ring, whose substitution positions near the sulfur atom may possibly be occupied by alkyl side chains with carbon number of 4–9. With these molecular structure information, it seems that during the most severe hydrotreating condition, the addition of a aromatic/ naphthenic/cyclohexene ring over DBT molecular would decrease the HDS reactivity. A reasonable assumption is that the contents of sulfur compounds with DBE = 10–12 may enrich due to partially hydrogenated of sulfur compounds with higher DBE, and these partially hydrogenated sulfur compounds may still have poor reactivity due to the two aromatics near the sulfur atom are probably still not saturated, resulting in significant steric hindrance caused by these additional rings and their alkyl side chains. While the sulfur compounds with DBE = 9 hydrogenated from higher DBE are more reactive for the reason that the two aromatics near the sulfur atom will be partially hydrogenated, resulting in reduce steric hindrance. This assumption was supported by the DBE distribution of the fragment ions from the S1 class in the deeply‑hydrogenated samples, as shown in Fig. 10, in which the DBE of the fragment ions may lower than their parent ions for the S1 class with DBE = 10, 11, while the DBE of the fragment ions from the S1 class with DBE = 9 remain the same with their parent ions. Finally, it is interesting to notice that the most refractory sulfur compounds identified in the deeply‑hydrogenated samples were all island type compounds with side chains. While recent researches [47–50] indicated that APPI FT-ICR MS may over support island model for the reason that asphaltene aggregates and archipelago type compounds have relatively poor ionization efficiency, and asphaltenes actually are composed of island and archipelago structure, though the dominant structure (island or archipelago) depends upon their sources. In the present research, firstly, as shown in Table 1, most asphaltenes were removed and only 0.1– 0.3 wt% asphaltenes were remain in the deeply‑hydrogenated samples. Secondly, as shown in Fig. 10, the DBEs of the CID fragments were unchanged or reduced less than 7 compared to their precursors. In consideration of 7 DBE unit constituting maximumly naphthalene, we attributed the refractory compounds in the samples were mainly island type or naphthene-containing island type rather than archipelago type. In addition, recent researches [42,50,51] using gel permeation chromatography inductively coupled plasma high-resolution mass spectrometry observed two kind of refractory sulfur compounds in the hydrotreated samples. Among them, the high molecular weight compounds are usually archipelago type compounds or island type compounds with side chains, which may have tendency to aggregate and bring difficulties to hydrotreat at supra-molecular and physical level. However, as the present research mainly focused on the molecular nature and chemical structure of the refractory sulfur compounds, the dominant role between side chain steric hindrance and nanoaggregate for the reactivity of refractory sulfur compounds should be figured out in the future researches. To get a more fundamental understanding of HDS chemistry during RHT process and thereof obtain a deep insight into the molecular structure of the most refractory sulfur compounds, the evolution of sulfur compounds in XTAR oil during six deep RHT processes with HDS rates from 91.7% to 99.2% were traced by the virtue of APPI FT-ICR MS and CID FT-ICR MS with narrow isolation windows combined with HDS kinetic analysis. Different from conventional RHT process, it was found that during deep RHT process, the reactivity of sulfur compounds decreased in an order of T > BT > 5R-p > 5R > BNT > DBT, in which the increased reactivity order of DBT, BNT, 5R and 5R-p families is determined by the increased order of aromaticity and the decreased order of alkyl-substituted carbon number. In addition, the most refractory sulfur compounds during deep RHT process had dominate DBE range of 10–12, followed by 9, and dominate carbon number range of 36–38. Furthermore, the skeletons of the most refractory sulfur compounds mainly consisted of 2–3 aromatic rings and 0–1 naphthenic/cyclohexene ring, whose substitution positions near the sulfur atom may possibly be occupied by alkyl side chains with carbon number of 4–9. The present work meanwhile reveals the reactivity order of individual sulfur compounds and its key influencing factors, as well as the molecular structure of the most refractory sulfur compounds during deep RHT process, which may provide wholly and accurately understanding about the sulfur evolution, and finally shed a light on the rational design of residue hydrotreating catalysts and the directional optimization of process conditions and catalyst gradation technology to realize a deep hydrogenation process of residual oils, which is beneficial to provide high-quality feedstock for the downstream FCC or DCC process. We confirm that the manuscript has not been published previously by any of the authors and is not under consideration for publication in another journal at the time of submission. No conflict of interest exits in the submission of this manuscript, and the manuscript has been approved by all authors for publication. There are two corresponding authors in this work. W. Han is responsible for the design of the manuscript structure and discussion, while Z.H. Deng is responsible for the investigation, conceptualization and original draft. Lishun Dai is responsible for the design and support of the research program. Xinheng Cai is responsible for the characterizations and results analysis. Xinpeng Nie and Qiang Fang are responsible for the HDS experiments. Hong Nie is responsible for guide of the program.
abstract: A deep insight into the molecular structure of the refractory sulfur compounds during residue hydrotreating process was realized by controlling the hydrodesulfurization (HDS) rate from 91.7% to 99.2%. The reactivity and the molecular-level evolution of the sulfur compounds were characterized by APPI FT-ICR MS and CID FT-ICR MS combined with HDS kinetic analysis. It was found that during deep RHT process, the increased reactivity order of sulfur families is mainly determined by the increased order of aromaticity and the decreased order of alkyl-substituted carbon number. Furthermore, the most refractory sulfur compounds have dominate double bond equivalent (DBE) of 10–12, followed by 9, and carbon number range of 36–38, and their skeleton typically include 2–3 aromatic rings and 0–1 naphthenic/cyclohexene ring, whose substitution positions near the sulfur atom may possibly be occupied by alkyl side chains with carbon number 4–9. This work reveals the reactivity order of individual sulfur compounds and its key influencing factors, and especially the molecular structure of the most refractory sulfur compounds, which may provide a deep understanding about the sulfur evolution.
title: Towards a deep understanding of the evolution and molecular structures of refractory sulfur compounds during deep residue hydrotreating process

reaction_catalog.transformation: sulfur compounds removal from residual oils

full_text: Steam reforming (Eq. (1)) and dry reforming of methane (Eq. (2)) are useful methods for the production of synthesis gas from natural gas; such synthesis gas can be utilized for hydrocarbon, methanol, and dimethyl ether production, and for oxo-syntheses. (1) CH 4+H 2 O→CO+3H 2 ΔH298 0=206 kJ/mol (2) CH 4+CO 2→2CO+2H 2 ΔH298 0=247 kJ/mol One of the problems of methane reforming to synthesis gas is the heat requirement since the reaction is highly endothermic, as shown above. In contrast, partial oxidation of methane (Eq. (3)) is a totally exothermic reaction as described below. However, it has been reported that the synthesis gas is produced by a two-step path consisting of highly exothermic methane combustion (Eq. (4)) to give H2O and CO2, followed by the endothermic reforming of methane with H2O (Eq. (1)) and CO2 (Eq. (2)) [1–7]. (3) CH 4+1/2O 2→CO+2H 2 ΔH298 0=−36 kJ/mol (4) CH 4+2O 2→CO 2+2H 2O ΔH298 0=−803 kJ/mol In this case, the reaction rate of methane combustion is so high that the combustion proceeds near the inlet of the catalyst bed, and therefore the temperature becomes very high within a very small thickness [8,9]. On the other hand, the temperature in the catalyst bed for methane reforming becomes lower. It has been reported that this causes a large temperature difference of the catalyst bed in partial oxidation of methane [6,8–12]. This hot spot in the catalyst bed is the one of the main difficulties in operating the partial oxidation. In addition, Dissanayake et al. [2] have reported that the catalyst bed was divided into three parts in the partial oxidation of methane over Ni/Al2O3 using a fixed bed reactor. Several researches on the partial oxidation and reforming of methane to synthesis gas using the fluidized bed reactor have also been reported [13–19]. It has been claimed that the high rates of heat transfer and the stability of the operation were obtained by using the fluidized bed reactor. The effects of CO2 and H2O addition on partial oxidation of methane and the effects of O2 addition on CO2 and/or steam reforming of methane have been investigated [20–24]. When the endothermic CO2 reforming of methane was combined with the exothermic partial oxidation of methane, it has been observed that the hot spots in the catalyst bed were reduced significantly [20,21]. In this reaction, it is possible to achieve a thermoneutral reaction by manipulating the CH4/CO2/O2 ratio [22]. Furthermore, little or no coking was found in the combination process [23], though coke formation is a serious and general problem in CO2 reforming of methane [25]. It has been reported that the heat transfer between exothermic oxidation and endothermic reforming is responsible for the better performance of the system [26]. In the autothermic conversion of methane to hydrogen, when two catalyst functions are available on the same support, optimal performance was achieved [26]. In the case of Ni catalyst, Ni is oxidized under the presence of oxygen as reported previously [2]. Oxidized Ni species shows activity for combustion, but it has no activity for reforming. Maintenance of metallic surface is thought to be very important when methane combustion and reforming proceed simultaneously on the same catalyst surface. In contrast, the metallic state is more stable for noble metals like Pt than Ni even under the presence of oxygen [27]. Recently it has been reported that the temperature profile of the catalyst bed of Pt/Al2O3 in methane reforming with CO2 and O2 is much flatter than that of Ni/Al2O3 [28]. In addition, Pt-Ni/Al2O3 catalyst prepared by the sequential impregnation method with small amount of Pt (0.45×10−5 mol/g-cat) exhibited very flat temperature profile in CH4+CO2+O2 reaction [29,30]. In this article, we investigated the effect of metal loading on Ni/Al2O3 and Pt/Al2O3 on the temperature profile in steam and dry reforming of methane with O2 addition. Supported monometallic Pt and Ni catalysts were prepared by the impregnation method. The precursors of Pt and Ni were H2PtCl6·6H2O (Soekawa Chemical) and Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (Soekawa Chemical), respectively. The Al2O3 (Aerosil, Aluminium Oxide C, 100 m2/g) was used as the support material. The catalyst was prepared by impregnating the support material with the aqueous solution of precursor. After removal of the solvent by heating, catalysts were dried at 393 K for 12 h, and then calcined in air at 773 K for 3 h. Furthermore, the catalysts were crushed and sieved to granules with 60–100 mesh after each sample was pressed into a disk at 600 kg/cm2. Loading of Ni is 1.5×10−4 mol/g-cat (0.9 mass%), and 4.5×10−4 mol/g-cat (2.7 mass%), and these catalysts are denoted as Ni(10)/Al2O3 and Ni(30)/Al2O3, respectively. Loading of Pt is 1.5×10−4 mol/g-cat (2.9 mass%), 1.5×10−5 mol/g-cat (0.3 mass%), 4.5×10−6 mol/g-cat (0.09 mass%), and 1.5×10−6 mol/g-cat (0.03 mass%), and these catalysts are denoted as Pt(10)/Al2O3, Pt(1)/Al2O3, Pt(0.3)/Al2O3 and Pt(0.1)/Al2O3, respectively. As a reference, we used a commercial steam reforming Ni catalyst G-91 (TOYO CCI, composition: 14 mass% Ni, 65–70 mass% Al2O3, 10–14 mass% CaO, and 1.4–1.8 mass% K2O). All the catalysts are reduced at 1123 K for 0.5 h before the reaction. Steam and dry reforming of methane with oxygen was carried out in a fixed bed flow reaction system under atmospheric pressure. The illustration of the reactor is shown in Fig. 1 . A quartz tube (outer diameter: 8 mm, inner diameter: 6 mm) was used as the reactor, and a thin quartz tube (outer diameter: 3 mm, inner diameter: 1.5 mm) was used as the thermocouple-well. This reactor is equipped with two thermocouples. The thermocouple outside the quartz reactor (denoted as wall temperature) is connected to the thermo-controller. The temperature of the catalyst bed (denoted as bed temperature) is measured by the other thermocouple in the reactor. This thermocouple can be moved from the inlet to the outlet through the catalyst bed. In the figures of temperature measurement, the position of the catalyst bed corresponds to 0–4 mm and the temperature of −5–0 and 4–8 mm was also measured. In our experiments, we have measured the catalyst activity and temperature profiles under the various partial pressure ratio (CH4/H2O/O2/Ar and CH4/CO2/O2/Ar) and the detail of the condition is listed in each result. The total pressure was 0.1 MPa. 0.07-g catalysts were used for each experiment. Contact time W/F (W/g=catalyst weight, F/mol h−1=total flow rate of the introduced gases) was in the range of 0.13–0.4 gh/mol. The wall temperature was controlled at 1123 K. Water was introduced by a microfeeder. The effluent gas was analyzed with an FID gas chromatograph(GC) equipped with a methanator for CH4, CO, CO2 (column packing: Porapak N) and with a TCD GC for H2 (column packing: active carbon). An ice bath was set between the reactor exit and a sampling port in order to remove water from the effluent gas for GC analysis. Research grade gases (CH4, CO2, O2) were purchased from Takachiho Trading, and were used without further purification. Conversion of oxygen in the experiment was 100%. The CO2 conversion can be calculated by {1−(the amount of CO2 effluent)/(CO2 introduced)}. When the formation of CO2 due to methane combustion is larger than CO2 consumption due to methane reforming, CO2 conversion is below zero. The activity test was carried out for 30 min, and all the catalysts under all the reaction conditions described here were stable. In all the experiments, the conversion of O2 was 100%. In addition, the gas phase homogeneous free radical reaction between methane and oxygen did not proceed at all under our reaction conditions. This is based on the result that methane and oxygen conversions were zero, and CO and CO2 formation was not observed at all when we used the quartz sand in stead of catalysts [28]. Conversion and H2/CO in steam reforming of methane (CH4+H2O reaction) over various catalysts are listed in Table 1 . The steam reforming activity of the commercial steam reforming Ni catalyst G-91 and Pt(10)/Al2O3 was almost the same level, however, Ni(10)/Al2O3 had lower activity than G-91 and Pt(10)/Al2O3. As reported previously, the activity of Ni(10)/Al2O3 in dry reforming of methane was almost the same as that of Pt(10)/Al2O3 [28]. Therefore, it is thought that a part of nickel can be oxidized in steam reforming of methane because the loading of Ni is rather small (0.9 mass%). It has been reported that the catalyst reducibility is much dependent on Ni content. This tendency has been clearly observed in the systems of NiO–MgO solid solution [31], and this can be interpreted by the interaction between Ni and support materials. On the catalyst with lower Ni loading, Ni can be highly dispersed and can obtain the stronger interaction. On the basis of the result, the effect of oxygen addition to steam reforming of methane was investigated. Conversion and H2/CO in CH4+H2O+O2 reaction over various catalysts are listed in Table 2 . The temperature profiles of the catalyst bed in the reaction over various catalysts are shown in Fig. 2 . The temperature profile of G-91(Fig. 2a) indicates that the bed temperature of the inlet (position: 0 mm) was very high and the temperature of the position (−5–0 mm) was also higher than the wall temperature. When the quartz sand was use in place of catalysts as a reference and CH4/H2O/O2=40:40:20 was introduced to the reactor, methane combustion did not proceed at all and the increase of the bed temperature was not observed at all. In this case, the bed temperature (1123±2 K) was almost the same as the wall temperature (1123 K). In addition, it is suggested that the increase of the temperature of the position (−5–0 mm) is related to the heat transfer from the catalyst bed. Methane conversion on Ni(10)/Al2O3 was lower than on G-91 and Pt(10)/Al2O3 in CH4+H2O+O2 reaction. This is because the activity of CH4+H2O reaction on Ni(10)/Al2O3 was lower than that on G-91 and Pt(10)/Al2O3. On the catalyst with low reforming activity, the bed temperature is high at all the positions of the catalyst bed. The temperature profile of the catalyst bed in CH4+H2O+O2 reaction over Pt(10)/Al2O3 is characteristic. Although the activity of Pt(10)/Al2O3 was almost the same as that of G-91 as shown in Tables 1 and 2, the temperature profile of Pt(10)/Al2O3 was much different from that of G-91. On Pt(10)/Al2O3, the temperature of the catalyst bed inlet drastically decreased with the increasing W/F, and the inlet temperature was almost the same as the wall temperature. Under the presence of oxygen in the gas phase, nickel exists as Ni2+. The Ni ion does not have the reforming activity [32], though it has high combustion activity. Only methane combustion proceeds over G-91 near the inlet of the catalyst bed and the methane reforming can proceed over the catalyst after oxygen is consumed. The exothermic and the endothermic reaction zones are separated in the catalyst bed. In this case, the temperature at the catalyst bed is much higher than the wall temperature. However, since the oxygen affinity of Ni is much higher than that of Pt [27], Pt metal is much more stable than Ni metal under oxygen atmosphere. It is thought that the Pt can exist as Pt0 even in the presence of oxygen under the reaction condition. Therefore methane combustion and reforming can proceed simultaneously on the same catalyst surface. Much shorter distance between the exothermic and the endothermic zones on Pt(10)/Al2O3 can be realized in this case than that on G-91. The flat temperature profile in the catalyst bed represents the simultaneous proceeding of methane combustion and reforming. The behavior of the bed temperature under various conditions over Ni and Pt catalyst with various metal loadings was investigated in CH4+CO2+O2 reaction. Conversion and H2/CO in CH4+CO2+O2 reaction over various Ni catalysts are listed in Table 3 . The temperature profiles of the catalyst bed in the reaction over various Ni catalysts are shown in Fig. 3 . The order of the catalytic activity is as follows: G-91≫Ni(30)/Al2O3>Ni(10)/Al2O3. The bed temperature over G-91 was lower than that over Ni(30)/Al2O3 and Ni(10)/Al2O3. This is due to high reforming activity of G-91. In the case of the catalyst with lower reforming activity, the distance between the endothermic and exothermic zones becomes longer. Conversion and H2/CO in CH4+CO2+O2 reaction over various Pt catalysts are listed in Table 4 . The temperature profiles of the catalyst bed in the reaction over various Pt catalysts are shown in Fig. 4 . The order of the catalytic activity is as follows: Pt(10)/Al2O3>Pt(1)/Al2O3>Pt(0.3)/Al2O3>Pt(0.1)/Al2O3. The profiles of the bed temperature over Pt(10)/Al2O3 and Pt(1)/Al2O3 were very similar. Especially, the temperature at the bed inlet at W/F=0.4 gh/mol was almost the same as wall temperature, and this indicates the overlap of the exothermic zone with the endothermic zone. In contrast, the temperature at the inlet became high over Pt(0.3)/Al2O3 and Pt(0.1)/Al2O3 since the activity of these two catalysts was low. This indicates that the overlap of two reaction zones can be realized over the Pt catalyst with high catalytic activity. Fig. 5 shows the relation between the temperature at the catalyst bed inlet and W/F in CH4+CO2+O2 reaction. The activity of G-91 was almost the same level as that of Pt(1)/Al2O3, however, the temperature of G-91 was higher than that of Pt(1)/Al2O3. Furthermore, the activity of Ni(10)/Al2O3 and Ni(30)/Al2O3 was similar to that of Pt(0.1)/Al2O3. This indicates that Pt is more suitable and active element for CH4+CO2+O2 reaction than Ni because Pt catalyst with 1/100 Pt loading gave the same activity level as Ni catalyst. From these comparisons, it is found that Pt(1)/Al2O3 and Pt(10)/Al2O3 catalysts are more effective in CH4+CO2+O2 reaction than Ni and Pt catalyst with lower loading. Fig. 6 shows the partial pressure dependence of the catalyst bed temperature over various Ni catalysts. Conversion and H2/CO in the reaction over various Ni catalysts are listed in Table 5 . In these experiments, the partial pressure was changed on the basis of the addition of the reforming gas (CH4/CO2=10:10) to the combustion gas (CH4/O2=10:20). Under the reaction condition of CH4/CO2/O2/Ar=10:0:20:70, only the methane combustion proceeded, and CO and H2 were not formed at all. Therefore, CO2 conversion and H2/CO cannot be described in Table 5. In this case, the bed temperature became very high and the maximum is located at the catalyst bed inlet. The bed temperature decreased with the addition of more reforming gas. This is because the reforming gas causes the endothermic reaction. However, the temperature of the catalyst bed inlet did not decrease so much by the addition of the reforming gas. This phenomenon was significantly observed over Ni(10)/Al2O3 and Ni(30)/Al2O3 under CH4/CO2/O2/Ar=30:20:20:30 and 40:30:20:10. The order of CH4 conversion is as follows: Ni(30)/Al2O3>Ni(10)/Al2O3>G-91, although the conversion level is not so different over these three catalysts under these reaction conditions. Fig. 7 shows the partial pressure dependence of the catalyst bed temperature over various Pt catalysts. Conversion and H2/CO in the reaction over various Pt catalysts are listed in Table 6 . The bed temperature decreased more drastically with the addition of more reforming gas than that of Ni catalysts. This phenomenon was observed more significantly on Pt(10)/Al2O3 and Pt(1)/Al2O3. In contrast, this was not observed on Pt(0.3)/Al2O3 and Pt(0.1)/Al2O3. Especially, the temperature of the catalyst bed inlet is almost the same as the wall temperature over Pt(10)/Al2O3 and Pt(1)/Al2O3 under CH4/CO2/O2/Ar=40:30:20:10. This strongly suggests that the exothermic heat by methane combustion and endothermic heat by methane reforming is balanced. The combustion of one molecule of methane (ΔH=−803 kJ/mol) and the reforming of three molecules of methane (ΔH=247×3 kJ/mol) can proceed simultaneously under this partial pressure conditions. The order of CH4 conversion under these reaction conditions is as follows: Pt(10)/Al2O3>Pt(1)/Al2O3>Pt(0.3)/Al2O3>Pt(0.1)/Al2O3. In order to balance the reaction heat, methane and CO2 conversions must be high because oxygen conversion is always 100%. In order to obtain high conversion enough, the loading amount of Pt must be high enough. Fig. 8 shows the relation between the temperature at the catalyst bed inlet and the partial pressure in CH4+CO2+O2 reaction. The conversion level of G-91, Ni(10)/Al2O3 and Ni(30)/Al2O3 was higher than Pt(0.3)/Al2O3 and Pt(0.1)/Al2O3. However, the temperature at the catalyst bed inlet over the Pt catalysts was lower than that over Ni catalysts. It is characteristic that the temperature decreased more by the addition of the reforming gas to the combustion gas over Pt(10)/Al2O3 and Pt(1)/Al2O3 catalysts than that over other Pt and Ni catalysts. Fig. 9 shows the model scheme of the effect of oxygen addition on steam and dry reforming of methane over Pt(10)/Al2O3 and Pt(1)/Al2O3, and Ni catalysts. Only methane combustion proceeds over Ni catalysts at the inlet of the catalyst bed and the methane reforming can proceed after oxygen is consumed. The exothermic and the endothermic reaction zones are separated on Ni catalysts. The reaction heat of the combustion can promote the reforming reaction only at higher W/F. One can interpret that the promoting effect is caused by the short distance between the combustion and the reforming zones. When the loading amount of metal is changed, both activities of methane combustion and reforming are influenced. The activities increase with the loading amount. However, the reaction rate of methane reforming much lower than that of methane combustion, and therefore, the effect of loading amount on the activity of methane reforming is more significant than that of methane combustion. Our results can be qualitatively explained by tendencies of the reforming activity. In contrast, the methane combustion and the reforming can proceed simultaneously in the same zone on the Pt catalysts with higher loading. Much shorter distances between the exothermic and the endothermic zones can be realized in this case, and it can give efficient heat transfer. Effect of oxygen addition to steam and dry reforming of methane over Pt/Al2O3, Ni/Al2O3, a commercial steam reforming Ni catalyst (G-91) on the temperature profile of the catalyst bed was investigated. In CH4+H2O+O2 and CH4+CO2+O2 reactions, it is interesting that the temperature at the catalyst bed inlet over Pt(10)/Al2O3 and Pt(1)/Al2O3 catalysts was much lower than that over Ni(10)/Al2O3, Ni(30)/Al2O3, G-91, Pt(0.3)/Al2O3 and Pt(0.1)/Al2O3. This indicates that the combustion and reforming zones overlap in the case of Pt(10)/Al2O3 and Pt(1)/Al2O3 catalysts. On other catalysts, the zones are separated. In terms of the energy efficiency in the syngas production by methane reforming, it is thought that the overlap is very effective. A part of this research has been supported by the Japan National Oil Corporation.
abstract: The effect of oxygen addition to the steam and dry reforming of methane on the temperature profile of the fixed catalyst bed was investigated over Pt/Al2O3, Ni/Al2O3 with various metal loadings and commercial steam reforming Ni catalyst. The temperature profile of the catalyst bed was much dependent on the kind of metal and its loading. Especially the Pt/Al2O3 with higher loading than 1.2×10−5 mol/g-cat gave the flat temperature profile and high methane conversion. From the profiles of bed temperature measurements under various reaction conditions, one sees that the distance between the combustion and reforming zones becomes very short. This is related to the high reducibility of Pt metal itself, and Pt maintains metallic state in the combustion zone even under the presence of oxygen. In this case, the heat of combustion can be supplied to the reforming reaction effectively. On the other hand, Ni/Al2O3 and commercial steam reforming catalyst gave high temperature at the catalyst bed inlet, and this indicates that the reaction zones exist separately.
title: Effect of oxygen addition to steam and dry reforming of methane on bed temperature profile over Pt and Ni catalysts

full_text: FUEL PROCESSING TECHNOLOGY ELSEVIER Fuel Processing Technology 50 (1997) 139-152 The nature of the synergistic effect of binary tetralin-alcohol solvents in Kansk-Achinsk brown coal liquefaction Peter N. Kuznetsov " * , Jan Bimer b, Piotr D. Salbut b, Evgeny D. Komiyets a, Ludmila I. Kuznetsova a, Colin E. Snape Institute ofhernistry and Chemico-Metallurgical Processes, 42 K. Marx Str.. Krasnoyarsk 660049, Russia b Institute of Organic Chemistry, 44 Kasprzaka Str., Warszawa 01-224, Poland ` University of Strathclyde, Thomas Graham Building, 295 Cathedral Str., Glasgow GI IXL, UK Received 10 January 1995; accepted 9 May 1996 Abstract Kansk-Achinsk brown coal hydrogenation and swelling in tetralin, in low molecular alcohols, in other solvents and in binary mixtures were studied. Tetralin was found to be the most effective liquefaction solvent, but methanol and ethanol were the active ones in coal swelling. Synergistic effects were observed when the mixtures of tetralin and methanol or ethanol were used for liquefaction and swelling. The effect of binary solvents was shown to be due to the ability of alcohol components to cause brown coal to swell improving the availability of the fragments of coal matter for the reactive hydrogen donor tetralin molecules.1997 Elsevier Science B.V. Keywords: Liquefaction; Swelling; Solvent; Hydrogen donor 1. Introduction The complex interactions of the solvent stream during coal liquefaction are important but as yet are imperfectly understood. It is widely accepted that at the initial stage coal is thermally decomposed to lower molecular fragments which are dissolved and stabilized by the solvent. It has been recognized that partially hydrogenated aromatic compounds play an important role in stabilising the coal-derived fragments through hydrogen transfer[1-3]. The ability of solvent to carry hydrogen and to solvate and/or to swell the ` Corresponding author. 0378-3820/97/$17.00 1997 Elsevier Science B.V. All rights reserved. PIIS0378-3820(96)01047-8 I-40 l'A. ktt-twtw rl a l.,. f uwl i'roc cssttt~ 7erltttnln~ 50 r / yy'J /.iy I ?_' coal structure is also beneficial for liquefaction. Many pure hydroaromatic compounds - tetralin is a good example - may he suitable for coal liquefaction from some viewpoints, but less well suited, if one is speaking strictly from the viewpoint of physical solvation. Recognizing that the ideal solvent must have a blend of several favourable properties. many groups have investigated solvents with various combinations of suitable traits. In many cases it has been shown that beneficial effects arose when favourable traits were combined in a single solvent [4-11]. The effects are widely invoked but the phenomena proved to be very complex. Although many properties have been identified as important to the performance of liquefaction solvents it remains a difficult problem to separate and determine the true significance of each factor individually. Experience of the operation of liquefaction units has demonstrated the beneficial effects of adding heavy coal-derived liquids to conventionally used distillate recycle solvents [4-6]. Polynuclear condensed aromatics have been identified as the effective solvent compounds found in these high boiling fractions[7,8]. Derbyshire [7] and Kamiya et al. [3,9] made it clear that these compounds can easily accept hydrogen as hydrogen shuttlers and subsequently donate a hydrogen atom to a free radical. The solvating ability is also a valuable property of these compounds. Aromatic hydrocarbons are not unique in their ability to participate in hydrogen shuttling and solvating reactions. Polar compounds such as phenols have long been considered as beneficial for liquefaction of coal [10-17]. High-rank coals were shown to be much more responsive to phenol content than lower rank coals. Kamiya et al. [ 12] suggested that phenols, particularly those with electron donor substituents, such as methoxy, may physically solvate the transition state during ether homolysis, thus increasing the rate of reaction. Recently [ 18-21 ] aliphatic alcohols have been shown to be effective solvents for coal liquefaction. Alcohol action is considered to be related to hydrogen donor [ 18,19,22] and alkylating [23-25] ability. Significant benefits were observed by using mixtures of methanol or ethanol with hydrocarbon solvents[26-3 I]. Synergistic effects were found to depend on the rank of the coals [26,29,30]. Shishido et al. [29] believe that the beneficial effect of ethanol-toluene mixtures in the supercritical extraction of coal is related to the ethanol molecules or reactive ethanol-derived species which attack coal altering its structure by adding to coal or abstracting hydrogen from it. The authors consider this mechanism to differ from that for toluene-tetralin extraction in which the radicals abstract hydrogen atoms from tetralin. Ouchi et al. [25] proposed that coal dissolution on ethanol treatment can be attributed to the cleavage of ether linkages in the coal and to alkylation. Iino et al. [26] believe that the beneficial effect of methanol and ethanol with tetralin could be related to the acceleration of coal swelling, improving contact between coal constituents and tetralin during liquefaction. Recently [21,31] we have investigated brown coal liquefaction using methanol, ethanol, isopropanol and tetralin as the solvents. In the medium of individual solvents, the highest coal conversion was observed with tetralin. This means that hydrogen donor ability of solvent molecules was the most important factor influencing coal liquefaction. An indication of the hydrogen donor ability of ethanol and isopropanol has been P.N. Kuznetsoc et al. / Fuel Processing Technology 5011997) 139-152 141 provided using the deuterium isotope technique [22,32]. The alkylation of coal and coal products by alcohols was monitored quantitatively using the mechanistic model developed [23,24]. In liquefaction with the binary tetralin-alcohol mixtures having various methanol or ethanol contents. the conversion and product yields were found to increase to maximum values and then decrease with increasing alcohol content [31 ]. It was important to understand the role of donating and alkylating properties of solvent constituents. With this purpose a thorough analysis of maltene composition as a function of solvent composition was made using a numerical model [31 ]. The composition was found to be quantified by the model of the additive process incorporating a) coal hydrogenation by a hydrogen donor and b) alkylation of the aromatic coal fragments by alcohols: \ \ Coal + Hydrogen - Liquid ~ + Dehydrogenated (1) donor produces donor CoalO + Hydrogen C2H50H- Liquid / + Dehydrogenated (2) /\ 1 donor products donor z H Furthermore, this quantitative analysis led to the conclusion that ethanol and tetralin gave the same liquid products from coal via reaction 1. This means that they functioned in the same manner in hydrogen donation. Methanol failed to exhibit the hydrogen donating property under the process conditions: it alkylated coal and coal products. However, its beneficial effect on coal conversion was comparable with that of ethanol. Whereas coal conversion first rose and then fell with increasing alcohol content in the solvent mixture, the degree of alkylation rose steadily. The reason for the enhancement in coal liquefaction owing to alcohol action was not established at that time because the study has not been sufficiently advanced for any definite conclusion. However, it was also our opinion, in agreement with other authors[26,30] that the principal reason could be related at least in part to the ability of alcohols to swell and disrupt the non-covalent cross-links within the coal structure. The object of this study was to provide evidence of the swelling nature of the synergistic effects of low aliphatic alcohols on brown coal hydroliquefaction in hydrogen donor tetralin solvent. Coal liquefaction studies were generally performed using conventional batch autoclave units. Although these units provided an adequate description of the conversion process for long residence times, they did not permit the initial reaction period to be separated and monitored with accuracy. Attempts have been made in this study to overcome this problem by using a reactor into which coal slurry could be charged after the reactor has been heated to the nominal reaction temperature. Consequently, detailed l'_ l'..1'. Ku-nism r rit. i / uc h m c.%wnI c chnolo; ~ 7U ( l y1): l b9 1 ?- monitoring of the first few minutes of the reaction could be possible in this case. Studies using similar reactors have been reported by other groups [33-36]. 2. Experimental All experiments were conducted with Borodino brown coal from the Kansk-Achinsk Basin. Prior to use the coal sample was ground to a particle size between 0.15 and 0.08 mm and dried. Reagent grade tetralin, various alcohols (methanol, ethanol, n-propanol, isopropanol, n-butanol, cyclohexanol), tetrahydrofuran (THF) and pyridine (Py) were used as the solvents. The volumetric swelling measurements were performed according to the conventional procedure [37]. Ground dried coal (2 g) was placed in a glass tube (9 mm o.d.) and centrifuged for 3 min at 5000 rev min-'. The height of coal was measured as h, An excess solvent (6 ml) was added and the tube was shaken. then centrifuged and the height (h,) was measured at various times after centrifuging till equilibrium was attained. The volumetric swelling ratio (Q,) was calculated Q, = h,/h, The formal kinetics of coal swelling at the early stages were evaluated on the basis of an equation of the form [37,38]: (Q, - I)/(Q - 1) = kt", where t is time, Q, is the solvent swelling ratio at t, Q is equilibrium swelling ratio, k is a swelling rate constant incorporating characteristics of the coal network and the solvent, and n is a diffusional exponent which reflects the mechanism of solvent transport within the network. Coal liquefaction was carried out at 380 in a 250 ml stirred autoclave. Methanol, ethanol, tetralin and mixtures of tetralin with methanol, ethanol and THF were used as the solvents in these liquefaction experiments. In typical runs the dry coal sample (6 g) and solvent (120 g) were charged into the autoclave at ambient temperature. After air removal, the autoclave was pressurized to 3 MPa with hydrogen and then heated. The heating period was within 30 min, the reaction periods at 380 were 5 and 30 min. When the reaction time had elapsed the autoclave was cooled. The time required for the autoclave temperature to decrease to 250 was within 15 min. The reaction products were recovered from the autoclave and exhaustively extracted with hexane (maltenes + gas) and then with benzene (asphaltenes). The coal conversion was evaluated based on the quantity of benzene-insoluble residue. To assess possible effects of swelling and heat-up periods on coal liquefaction behaviour the autoclave was designed so it could be operated in a normal or rapid-injection mode. For this reason the stirred autoclave was fitted with a piston accumulator, capable of rapidly injecting the coal slurry into the previously heated autoclave. In these experiments, autoclave was charged with tetraGn or with binary solvent (100 g) and hydrogen (3 MPa) at ambient temperature. Then it was heated to reaction temperature with stirring. After thermal stability has been attained, the prepared slurry of coal (5 g) with a solvent (tetralin, ethanol. or binary solvent. 20 g) was charged into the accumulator. After air removal, the accumulator was pressurized with hydrogen to 8 MPa then joined to the autoclave and then the slurry was injected rapidly. The time required for the quantitative injection was less than 3 s. The minimum time of coal P.N. Ku-netsor et al. / Fuel Processing Technolug50119971 139-152 143 contacting with the solvents before the reaction was within 4 min. After the injection, the temperature in the autoclave dropped by approximately 15. The desired reaction temperature was generally attained within 40-60 s. The reaction time was 5 min. Analytical measurements were performed using ultimate, proximate, oxygen-containing group and solid-state '3C NMR analyses. The content of hydroxylic groups was determined by acetilation and the content of carboxylic groups by reaction with calcium acetate and titration of liberated acetic acid. 13C NMR measurements were performed using the combination dipolar decoupling and magic angle spinning with cross-polarization (CP/MAS) and single-pulse excitation (SPE) techniques [39]. The analyses were performed at 25 and 50 MHz on a Brucker MSL 100 spectrometer. For CP, contact times were varied between 0.5 and 10 ms. Tetrakis(trimethylsilyl)silane was used as an internal standard to determine the proportion of carbon observed in the sample. 3, Results 3.1. Coal characterization Taking into account that the synergistic effect of solvents was greatly influenced by coal properties [26,29,30], the detailed characterization of the brown coal sample used was performed. Ultimate and proximate analyses data are presented in Table 1. The contents of hydroxylic, carboxylic and non-acidic (calculated by difference) groups were 6.9%, 3.5% and 13.8%, respectively (Table 2). Fig. 1 shows 25 MHz SPE and CP/MAS "C NMR spectra. Table 2 compares the values for the fractions of spz hybridized carbon atoms ,f,,' derived by SPE and CP/MAS techniques. Differences in the f,; values at the contact time of 7 ms were insignificant (0.75-0.76). In this case, the aromaticity fd, i.e. the ratio of aromatic atoms to total carbon atoms was 0.63 which is in good agreement with the values derived by IR spectra Q', = 0.64) [40] and by empirical evaluations based on the elemental analysis data [41], but differed from that derived by CP/MAS "C NMR (f;~ = 0.71) for this coal [42]. Using the SPE method 80 10% of carbon atoms were observed in the sample. 3.2. Coal swelling The swelling behaviour of Kansk-Achinsk brown coal in different solvents and in the binary mixtures with tetralin was found to be very specific (Table 3, Fig. 2 and Fig. 3). Of the alcohols, methanol, ethanol and also n-propanol were the most effective swelling Table l Analytical data on Borodino brown coal from the Kansk-Achinsk Basin Proximate analysis (wt%) Ultimate analysis (wt% daf) Ad vdd.1 C H N S Od'f 6.2 48.1 - 69.8 - 4.8 0.8 0.4 24.2 I-l-l P Kle:lrer.snre 0.79 1.2 Ethanol 1.65 >_ 0.56 1.1 n-Propanol 1.60 0.27 (1.8 Isopropanol 1.25 - 0.3 n-Butanol 1.30 - 0.3 Cvclohexanol 1.30 - 0.3 Tetrahydrofuran 1.65 0.14 0.8 Pyridine 2.10 0.35 1.4 Tetralin 1.20 - 0. On the other hand, coal swelling in Py and in THF with large and comparable molar volumes was also great but quite different. This reflected the difference in the abilities of Py and THF to disrupt intermolecular cross-links. Table 3 gives the values of the ratios of (Q - 1)/V, where V is the molar volume of a solvent. The ratios are proportional to the total number of solvent molecules taken up by the coal at equilibrium. In other words, they may reflect the number of cross-links disrupted. The greatest extent of cross-link disruption was observed for Py solvent ((Q- I)/V value was 1.4 X 10--) indicating its high reactivity in disruption of different kinds of cross-links. Methanol and ethanol reactivity ((Q - I )/ V = 1.1 - 1.2 X 10-= ) were less than that of Py but more than that of THF ((Q - I )/ V = 0.8 X l0-` ). Isopropanol. n-butanol and cycloxehanol uptakes were much smaller ((Q - I) / V = 0. 3 X 10 ). In tetralin mixtures with 40% methanol or ethanol. coal swelled to a greater extent than in tetralin or alcohol separately (Fig. 2). In the mixtures having various ethanol contents, the swelling increased to a maximum at 20-40% ethanol in tetralin and then decreased with increasing ethanol content (Fig. 3). The increase in coal swelling was a much steeper function at low ethanol content, indicating a strong dependency upon the polar nature of the solvent. Tetralin-isopropanol and tetralin-THF mixtures failed to show material swelling effects (Fig. 2). In general, the results obtained for binary mixtures agree with those of earlier authors [26.431. 3.3. Coal liquefaction Table 4 shows the effect of alcohol additives on the coal conversion with the H-donor tetralin solvent at 380 and at 5 min and 30 min reaction times in a stirred autoclave with slow heating mode. The addition of 17%n ethanol to tetralin increased the conversion from 40.1 to 61.6% at 5 min reaction time and from 51.1 to 79.2% at 30 min reaction time. Interestingly, the HS + gas yield (low molecular products) increased dramatically (from 34.4 to 62.1 %) while the asphaltene yield changed a little (from 16.7 to 17. I %). Thus blending not only affected coal conversion but, more significantly, the yield of low molecular matter. Kuznet,sor et al. / Fuel Processing Technolo,4n, 50 (7997) 139-152 147 The same improvement in coal liquefaction following addition of methanol to tetralin was observed. Preswelling of coal in alcohols at ambient temperature during a prolonged period before the reaction had very little effect on liquefaction reactivity. The data in Table 4 also demonstrate a slight beneficial effect of THF on coal liquefaction. In the experiments just described, coal swelling took place during the charge and heating periods. In order to separate the swelling effect on coal liquefaction of tetralin contact time from that of the alcohol-induced swelling of coal the tests were performed using the same stirred autoclave equipped with a device for rapid injection of coal slurry into the preheated autoclave with the solvent. In these experiments, the reaction time was 5 ntin, the minimum time of coal contact with the solvents before the reaction was within 4 min. During 4 min, the extent of coal swelling in ethanol at room temperature reached 40-50% of the equilibrium value. The swelling in THF was insignificant for this time. The results on the hydrogenation of coal charged into the preheated autoclave are displayed in Table 5. Several trends are evident on close inspection of the data. 1. The liquefaction of coal in tetralin alone was little influenced by whether the coal was brought to the reaction temperature quickly or slowly (38.2%, Table 5 and 40.1 %, Table 4, respectively). 2. The addition of ethanol failed to result in improvement in the conversion of coal which was injected into the preheated autoclave with tetralin alone (36.9%o, Table 5). 3. Also. there was no promoting effect when coal was injected with ethanol (or THF) alone (34.2% or 36.1%, respectively). 4. As the coal-ethanol contact time was increased (from 4 to 10 min and to 24 h) the extents of conversion differed only slightly from that produced with tetralin alone (34.2-42.8%n and 38.2%, respectively). 5. Beneficial effects occurred only when the coal was injected into the preheated autoclave with the ethanol -tetralin mixture (47.5%r), preswelling in this mixture Table 5 Coal liquefaction with tetralin and with binary solvents in a preheated autoclave at 5 min reaction time No. Liquefaction solvent Solvent for injection Coal contacting %r daf of coal into with injecting Coal HS+gas BS-H1 preheated autoclave solvent conversion 1 Tetralin, 100%o Tetralin. 100% 4 min 38.2 - - 3 Tetralin, 8Y/c+ethanol, 17%c Tetralin, 100% -4 min 36.9 30.8 6.1 3 Tetralin, 83%+ethanol, 17% Ethanol, 100/c 4 min 34.2 21.5 12.7 4 Tetralin, 8317c+ethanol, 17% Ethanol, IOO1Ic 10 min 42.8 - - 5 Tetralin, 8314,+ethanol, 17 Ethanol, IOO% 24 h 40.1 26.5 13.6 6 Tetralin, 83(71+ethanol, 17 Tetralin. 83% 4 min 47.5 39.3 8.2 +ethanol, 179( 7 Tetralin, 83"r +ethanol, 1791 Tetralin. 8391 24 h 52.8 48.0 4.8 +ethanol. 17111 8 Tetralin, 601/1 +ethanol, 4091 Tetralin, 60 24 h 59.9 51.1 8.8 + ethanol. 4017 9 Tetral, 83Qc+THE, 17 91 THE. l00 10 min 36.1 - - 14S l'. 1''. l~lt:n~,ur rl 'il.
