More significantly. the HS + gas yield increased dramatically from 21.5-30.8%o to 48.0%c. In fact, the conversion of coal preswollen in tetralin-ethanol mixture was close to that measured in the conventional heat-up mode autoclave (52.8%, Table 5 and 61.6%, Table 4. respectively). 6. As the alcohol content was increased from 17 to 40% the conversion and HS + gas yield increased up to 59.9% and 51.1%, respectively (Table 5). 4. Discussion Coal liquefaction is generally considered to begin with thermal rupture of linkage bonds with the resulting formation of free radical species. The key to the liquefaction is to `cap' these radicals by hydrogen addition before they recombine to form large fragments with a higher molecular weight. The brown coal is particularly susceptible to such condensation reaction primarily owing to the high content of the reactive oxygencontaining groups and the absence of some internal solvent having good H-transfer and H-donor properties. Coal is known to be a highly porous material of a macromolecular nature. Non-covalent interactions in its matter contribute greatly to the formation of thin porous structure. Therefore, reagent accessibility to the reactive sites, particularly in the initial stages of liquefaction, can be diffusion limited or steric restricted. The swelling in the appropriate solvent can result in disruption of non-covalent cross-links expanding pore structure and increasing the mobility of its fragments. Thus, the swelling makes it possible for a small amount of solvent to cause large changes in the behaviour of coals, since their response is sensitive to cross-link density. The data generated from the swelling measurements have shown the macromolecular structure of Kansk-Achinsk brown coal to be highly cross-linked. The main portion of the network volume is unavailable for most of the solvents. Only small polar molecules of methanol and ethanol were shown to be able to penetrate readily brown coal structure, solvate its fragments and disrupt intermolecular cross-links. Practically, the same number of cross-links were disrupted by small methanol and ethanol molecules ((Q I )/ V values were l .1-1.2 X 10- - ). Less cross-links were disrupted by n-propanol ((Q - 1)/ V= 0.8 X 10-' ). This may mean that some portion of cross-links which have been available for methanol and ethanol were unavailable for n-propanol owing to the network steric effect. The main portion of the network volume was unavailable for isopropanol and other alcohols with larger molar volumes ((Q - 1)/V= 0.3 X l0-' ). THF disrupted a smaller number of cross-links ((Q - 1)/ V = 0.8 X 10-- ) than did methanol and ethanol. The greatest number of cross-links were disrupted by pyridine solvent ((Q- 1)/V= 1.4 X 10- -) indicating its high reactivity to disrupt different kinds of non-covalent interactions. These data can be interpreted in terms of the rigidity of the swollen networks. In particular, they appear to mean that the brown coal network swollen in ethanol and methanol was more expanded and flexible than in other alcohols and in THF owing to a lower cross-link density. It seems clear that loosening the network rigidity made it P.N Kuznetsor et al. / Fuel Processing Technology 50 (1997) 139-152 149 possible for large molecules such as tetralin to diffuse in and cause further coal swelling. This could be the reason why binary tetralin-methanol and tetra] in-ethanol mixtures manifested a considerable swelling effect, but tetralin-isopropanol and tetralin-THF did not. The results obtained for coal liquefaction in tetralin using a conventional stirred autoclave have shown the great beneficial effect of small methanol and ethanol additives. The general trend of conversion with alcohol addition was the same as in our previous study with a rotating autoclave [31]. The important observation here is that good mixing (with stirring) increases the beneficial effect and that large amounts of alcohol are not necessary in this case to obtain significant improvement in the conversion and in the yield of low molecular matter. A decrease in the extent of improvement was observed as the quantities of alcohols were further increased [31]. The same trend occurred in the swelling of coal in binary mixtures (Fig. 3). These findings could be interpreted to mean that alcohols functioned mainly as a polar reagent to promote the swelling and partial disruption of non-covalent cross-links. Special attention should be given to the important experimental finding, which was obtained in the stirred autoclave with rapid heat-up mode injection. Namely, the ability of brown coal preswollen in ethanol alone to produce low molecular substances seems alone to be not enhanced; it was suppressed markedly as compared with that for the coal preswollen with tetralin alone. This can be seen from a comparison of the product yields for run 2 with runs 3 and 5 (Table 5). One can see that the yield of low molecular products decreased from 30.8 to 21.6-26.5% but the yield of high molecular matter (asphaltenes) increased from 6.1 to 12.7-13.6% when the coal was preswollen with ethanol alone as compared with that with tetralin alone. The great beneficial effect on coal destruction to low molecular products was observed as soon as it had been preswollen with the ethanol -tetralin mixture. The reactivity increased with increasing the contact of coal with this mixture before the reaction. Thus, these experiments showed, in fact, that it is not the coal swelling itself but the distribution of the tetralin molecules within the coal matter that is the predominant factor affecting the reactivity of coal for destruction to low molecular products, i.e. the diffusion of tetralin within the coal was a critical controlling step. The reason for this behaviour of coal could be understood to mean that the competitive reactions of coal destruction to low molecular products and condensation of coal-derived fragments, reactive oxygen-containing species in the first place, do take place under liquefaction conditions. A proportion of these reactions may be governed by the properties of the macromolecular network and the availability of an H-donor solvent. In preswollen coal, tetralin molecules can come closer to the reactive sites improving contact between thermally formed free radicals and tetralin-derived hydrogen. This prevents regressive reactions of radical fragments and provides the high yield of low molecular products. On the other hand, under the H-donor deficient environment the regressive reactions may increasingly prevail as the flexibility of the network increases. It is reasonable to assume that the flexible network could permit the large coal-derived radical to re-arrange and contact another large radical which may not have been previously (i.e. in an unswollen rigid network) accessible. They could recombine to produce a new pattern ISQ l'.:1-. hlw-nrtw~ , I nl, . I rn l Prru c,smr"' It,,hnnin,-. ~U I ic~cl;'i li(t/,= more refractory than that in the parent coal thus suppressing the reactivity for destruction. The proposed arguments are strengthened by our recent data on the effects of the chemical pretreatments of coals on their liquefaction reactivity [44,45], In particular, brown coal was methylated with (CH , ), SO, to eliminate the strong ionic and hydrogen bonding intermolecular interactions. incorporating carboxylates with polyvalent metal cations and different kind of hydroxyls. The behaviour of methylated coal in the hydroliquefaction with H-donor tetralin and with non-donor methanol solvent in the presence of ZnCI, as a catalyst was quite different. A great increase in the liquefaction reactivity after methylation was observed in tetralin medium. In contrast, a detrimental effect occurred when the non-donor methanol was used for the liquefaction of the same coals. Finally, it is of significance to consider why coal preswollen in the tetralin-ethanol mixture was somewhat less reactive in the preheated autoclave that in the conventional heat-up mode autoclave (run 7, Table 5 and run 2. Table 4). One of the possible explanations for this feature may be in terms of a further increase in the flexibility of the swollen coal network induced by thermal activation during the preheating period. That is, once the cross-links are partially disrupted by solvent swelling the thermal activation can provide the enhanced flexibility for relaxation of coal fragments thus improving further the tetralin penetration coal network. The latter could be the reason why THF produced a slight beneficial effect on liquefaction in a conventional autoclave, but no beneficial effect on liquefaction in the preheated autoclave was induced by THF preswelling during the short period (10 min) at ambient temperature. However. the effect of prolonged reaction time due to heating period can not be excluded fully in the case of the tetralin-alcohol solvent though no effect was observed in tetralin alone. 5. Conclusion The study has demonstrated evidence for the swelling nature of the beneficial alcohol effect on the liquefaction reactivity of brown coal with a H-donor tetralin solvent. The major implication of this study was that the distribution of H-donor solvent within the brown coal structure is a controlling factor in the early stages of liquefaction. This means that the diffusion mechanism plays an important role in the H-transfer reaction. Acknowledgements The authors thank Dr. N. Belskaya and Dr. G. Sukhova for assistance in the experimental work. We are very grateful to the referees who reviewed the manuscript with great care and made valuable comments and suggestions. Financial support from the Krasnoyarsk Regional Science Foundation Grant 3F 0041, is also acknowledged.
abstract: Kansk-Achinsk brown coal hydrogenation and swelling in tetralin, in low molecular alcohols, in other solvents and in binary mixtures were studied. Tetralin was found to be the most effective liquefaction solvent, but methanol and ethanol were the active ones in coal swelling. Synergistic effects were observed when the mixtures of tetralin and methanol or ethanol were used for liquefaction and swelling. The effect of binary solvents was shown to be due to the ability of alcohol components to cause brown coal to swell improving the availability of the fragments of coal matter for the reactive hydrogen donor tetralin molecules.
title: The nature of the synergistic effect of binary tetralin-alcohol solvents in Kansk-Achinsk brown coal liquefaction

full_text: rate constant at reference temperature (mol/g h barΣni) model parameter for water corrective term acetaldehyde 1-butanol total number of compounds reactor diameter density-functional theory diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy residual activation energy (kJ/mol) ethanol mole flow rate of compound k (mol/h) jacobian matrix of parameters reactor length exponent of the water corrective term reaction order total number of tests number of degrees of freedom number of reactions in the kinetic model number of parameters total pressure (bar) partial pressure of compound k (bar) reaction rate (mol/h g) ideal gas constant (8.314 J/mol K) objective function Student's t-distribution reaction temperature (°C or K) covariance matrix of model parameters mass of catalyst (g) water mass content in ethanol feed (wt%) weight hourly space velocity (h−1) reactions experiments compounds parameters significance level standard deviation model parameters stoichiometric coefficient 1,3-Butadiene is an important feedstock for the manufacture of several chemicals and materials such as styrene-butadiene rubber, polybutadiene rubber, acrylonitrile butadiene styrene resins, styrene butadiene latex, adiponitrile and nitrile rubber [1–3]. Presently, 1,3-butadiene is a by-product of ethene production by steam cracking of naphtha and, therefore, its production is not sustainable. New environmental policies [4,5] have fostered the search for new routes in order to replace fossil-oil derived products with bioproducts [6–8]. For the production of 1,3-butadiene, catalytic conversion of bioethanol into 1,3-butadiene raises as an attractive route since bioethanol is largely available and it can be cost-efficiently produced from a variety of renewable biomass and wastes [1,9]. Hence, in the last decade, the research of more selective and stable catalysts through a better understanding of reaction mechanisms, the required acid/base feature of the catalyst, the impact of preparation methods and the effect of reaction conditions has significantly increased [3,10]. Unfortunately, despite the great advance in catalyst development, the literature on kinetic models of the conversion of ethanol into 1,3-butadiene is scarce [11], and more research is necessary for efficient design of industrial reactors. In the literature, several catalytic pathways have been proposed for the conversion of ethanol to 1,3-butadiene. Gruver et al. [12], after studying the conversion of ethanol over aluminated sepiolite, proposed that 1,3-butadiene was generated by the reaction of ethene with acetaldehyde via a Prins-like mechanism, preceded by the formation of acetaldehyde and ethene by ethanol dehydrogenation and dehydration, respectively. However, no experimental evidence supporting this route has been reported to date. Cavani et al. proposed a different reaction pathway over MgO [13] and MgO-SiO2 [14] catalysts. Based on results from catalytic tests, DFT calculations and in situ DRIFTS experiments, they proposed that acetaldehyde, formed by dehydrogenation of ethanol, reacts with a surface carbanion, formed by the deprotonation of β‑carbon of ethanol, to directly produce crotyl alcohol that further dehydrates into 1,3-butadiene. Taifan et al. criticized this reaction pathway since, by DFT calculations, they found that the surface carbanion on MgO would preferably form ethene rather than 1,3-butadiene [15]. Furthermore, by in situ DRIFT experiments and DFT calculations, Taifan et al. proposed that over MgO-SiO2 ethanol was converted into 1,3-butadiene via the Toussaint-Kagan pathway [16], which is explained below. Qi et al. has recently proposed a reaction pathway over a Zn-Y-DeAlBEA catalyst, produced by introducing zinc and yttrium into a dealuminated beta zeolite [17], which has common features with that proposed by Cavani et al. in the sense that 1,3-butadiene is formed by reaction of coadsorbed ethanol and acetaldehyde to produce crotyl alcohol, which finally dehydrates into 1,3-butadiene. They proposed a reaction mechanism based on the structure of the catalyst and kinetic studies and derived an expression for the rate of production of 1,3-butadiene, but the expression was not tested against experimental rate data. Probably, the most supported catalytic pathway from ethanol to 1,3-butadiene is the Toussaint-Kagan pathway (or aldol-condensation pathway), which comprises the following consecutive reactions: (i) ethanol dehydrogenation into acetaldehyde, (ii) self-condensation of acetaldehyde to crotonaldehyde, (iii) conversion of crotonaldehyde into crotyl alcohol by Meerwein-Ponndorf-Verley-Oppenauer (MPVO) reduction with ethanol, and, finally, (iv) crotyl alcohol dehydration into 1,3-butadiene [11,18–24]. This reaction pathway is not free of criticism: some authors have remarked that important intermediates of this pathway, such as crotonaldehyde and crotyl alcohol, are either not detected or present in a very small concentration in the reaction products, which points that they do not play an important role in 1,3-butadiene production. Other authors state that the reason for this is the fast rate of conversion of these intermediates, which precludes their accumulation [11]. Except for the reaction pathway proposed by Gruver et al., the conversion of ethanol into 1,3-butadiene can be described with two reaction steps: a first step in which ethanol is dehydrogenated into acetaldehyde and a second step in which the mixture of acetaldehyde and ethanol reacts to produce 1,3-butadiene. When both steps take place over a single catalyst, we refer to the Lebedev or one-step process, while if each step takes place independently over different catalysts, we refer to the Ostromislensky or two-step process. According to the recent review by Pomalaza et al. on reactions and catalysts for the conversion of ethanol into 1,3-butadiene [11], the reaction pathways proposed in the literature may co-exist as complementary pathways to 1,3-butadiene or one of them may prevail under specific reaction conditions. The main point of disagreement between the different reaction pathways is how the CC bond the between ethanol and acetaldehyde is formed, which results in the C4‑oxygenated precursor of 1,3-butadiene. They state that even for the Toussaint-Kagan pathway, the most accepted, there are important disagreements and lack of understanding concerning the molecular-level mechanisms of the reactions, particularly on the CC coupling step (the aldol-condensation of acetaldehyde). The reasons are that the molecular-level mechanism varies depending on the type of catalyst and the high instability of the surface intermediates hinders its determination by observation. This lack of knowledge has precluded the development of mechanistic kinetic models for the conversion of ethanol into 1,3-butadiene and explains why there is a scarcity of studies on the kinetics of the one-step conversion of ethanol into 1,3-butadiene. Next, these studies are briefly commented. Bhattacharyya et al. [18], in 1967, investigated the reaction mechanism over a ZnO/Al2O3 catalyst by co-feeding ethanol and some intermediates. The results pointed to an aldol-condensation pathway. They established that the acetaldehyde condensation to crotonaldehyde was the rate-limiting step and estimated the apparent activation energy for the overall conversion of ethanol into 1,3-butadiene. Later, in 2014, Tret'yakov et al. [25,26] proposed the only formal kinetic model of a one-step catalyst (K2O/ZnO/γ-Al2O3) published so far. The Toussaint-Kagan pathway was modelled as two consecutive reactions: (i) dehydrogenation of ethanol to acetaldehyde and (ii) reaction of two molecules of acetaldehyde with hydrogen to produce 1,3-butadiene and water, thus, supposing that hydrogen was the reducing agent of crotonaldehyde intermediate instead of ethanol. Prins condensation (coupling of ethene and acetaldehyde to 1,3-butadiene) and dehydrogenation of side-product butene, assumed to be formed from ethene dimerization, were also considered possible routes for 1,3-butadiene production. The formation of other side-products was also included in the model: diethyl ether, from ethanol dehydration, and butanal, from acetaldehyde and ethene. Unfortunately, the rationale for the proposal of the reaction network was not presented. Every reaction was assumed to occur in consecutive elementary reactions over the different active sites of the catalyst. The rate-limiting step for each reaction was supposed to be the one where CH bonds were broken. The coefficients of the proposed kinetic equations were estimated by fitting the experimental rates of reaction from catalytic tests in a tubular reactor. Some unusual results were obtained, such as the apparent zero reaction order of the 1,3-butadiene formation steps [11]. Besides, neither the goodness of fit was reported, nor statistical analysis and validation of the model was performed. More recently, Bharadwaj et al. [27] proposed a multiscale modelling approach to separate transport limitations from chemical reactions in order to extract intrinsic kinetic parameters in heterogeneous catalytic systems, enabling the study of the effect of physical properties of the catalyst on its performance. They applied that approach to the conversion of ethanol to 1,3-butadiene over a 1Ag/4ZrO2/SBA-16 catalyst proposing a simplified two-step reaction scheme where a first reaction accounted for the overall conversion of ethanol to 1,3-butadiene and the second reaction for the deactivation of fresh active sites. The rate constants of the first and second reactions were independently determined by fitting experimental data of steady-state conversion of ethanol in short-duration catalytic test and deactivation data in long experimental runs, respectively. Due to the simple reaction scheme, the kinetic model cannot be used to predict the selectivity of the catalyst at different reaction conditions. A kinetic model of the conversion of ethanol into 1,3-butadiene should account for catalyst deactivation, the effect of reaction conditions (temperature and pressure) and impurities in the ethanol feed in order to be used for the design of an industrial process. Regarding the latter, water is the main impurity in ethanol feedstock (the Lebedev commercial process used crude bioethanol (~15 wt% water) as feedstock [28]) but it is also the main side product in the conversion of ethanol into 1,3-butadiene. The content of water in the reactor inlet stream is a key design variable. On one hand, a high water content allows using cheaper aqueous ethanol feedstock and reduces the cost of removing water from unconverted ethanol, but on the other hand, water has an undesirable effect on the performance of one-step catalysts, reducing ethanol conversion while promoting unwanted ethanol dehydration to ethene and diethyl ether [14,29–32]. These effects have also been observed in our previous studies on ethanol conversion into 1,3-butadiene over a hemimorphite-HfO2/SiO2 catalyst, prepared as a physical mixture of the zinc silicate hemimorphite (Zn4Si2O7(OH)2·H2O) and HfO2/SiO2 [33], whose 1,3-butadiene carbon yield is one of the highest reported (~70%) [11]. The reduction of ethanol conversion was ascribed to the blocking by water of Zn2+ Lewis acid sites of the hemimorphite, which are active for ethanol dehydrogenation, while promotion of ethanol dehydration was attributed to the transformation by water of the Zn2+ sites to Brønsted acid sites. Our results also revealed that water inhibited the generation of heavy compounds, formed by successive aldol-condensation reactions of acetaldehyde. These heavy compounds are related to catalyst deactivation. The inhibition of heavy compounds was ascribed to blocking by water of Hf4+-related Lewis acid sites, active for aldol condensation reactions, resulting in slower catalyst deactivation [33]. Although the effect of water content in the ethanol feed on catalyst performance is important, none of the few kinetic models published so far has considered it. In an effort to contribute to the development of a viable industrial process for the one-step conversion of ethanol into 1,3-butadiene [34], the aim of this paper is to build, for the first time, a kinetic model of a one-step catalyst that accounts for the effect of water as ethanol impurity on the catalyst performance. For this purpose, the results of previous studies on the reaction pathway in the conversion of ethanol over the hemimorphite-HfO2/SiO2 catalyst (in short, HM-HfO2/SiO2) [35] and the effect of water and reaction conditions on the catalyst performance [33] were used. A simplified reaction scheme comprising eight reactions, eight chemical species and three lumps was proposed based on the reaction network reported for the HM-HfO2/SiO2 catalyst by Cabello et al. [35]. A power-law kinetics was assumed for each reaction and corrective terms were introduced to account for the inhibition or promotion of certain reactions with water. Experimental data from previous studies on the conversion of ethanol to 1,3-butadiene over the HM-HfO2/SiO2 catalyst [33] were used for the regression and validation of the kinetic model. The kinetic parameters of the model were estimated by fitting the experimental data based on the maximum likelihood principle. For this purpose, differential mole balance equations in the tubular reactor for the chemical species and lumps needed to be solved. Starting from an initial guess of the kinetic parameters, an iterative loop was performed where the values of the kinetic parameters were estimated by an optimizer and then statistically analyzed, the least significant parameter was removed, and a reduced set of kinetic parameters was returned to the optimizer for further estimation. This loop was performed until all parameters retained in the model were significant. Finally, an analysis of residuals and assessment of prediction of the kinetic model on a validation dataset was performed. Details of the methodology are explained next. The synthesis of the HM-HfO2/SiO2 catalyst was presented in a previous work [35] and it is briefly summarized here. First, the HM and HfO2/SiO2 components were separately synthesized and then physically mixed in the appropriate proportions to achieve the desired nominal metal composition (3.0 wt% Hf, 9.3 wt% Zn). The resulting fine powder mixture was pelletized, crushed and sieved. The size fraction between 0.3 and 0.5 mm was used for the catalytic tests. For the regression and validation of the kinetic model, catalytic data from our previous studies on the determination of the reaction network in the conversion of ethanol over the HM-HfO2/SiO2 catalyst [35] and the effect of reaction conditions and water content in ethanol on the catalyst performance [33], were used. For the study on the effect of reaction conditions (weight hourly space velocity (WSHV), temperature (T)) and water mass content in ethanol (Wc)) a design of experiment was performed where different levels were selected for T (340, 360, 380 °C), WHSV (1.12, 3.6, 6.1, 8, 9.8 h−1, calculated as mass flow of ethanol feed (water-free) divided by the mass of catalyst) and Wc (0, 3.75, 7.5 and 15 wt%). In those studies, short-duration catalytic tests were carried out so that catalyst deactivation was negligible, which was checked by comparing catalyst performance at the beginning and the end of each test. That way, the proposed kinetics in this manuscript does not account for catalyst deactivation. Information on the catalytic tests regarding reaction conditions and inlet mole flow rates to the reactor, as well as product outlet mole flow rates, can be found in Tables A.1 and A.2, respectively. In order to estimate the kinetic parameters, 48 catalytic tests were randomly selected (estimation data) while the rest of the tests (test number 7, 19, 22, 24, 29, 33, 39, 50, 51 and 56) were used to validate the model (validation data). The carbon balance error in the catalytic tests was below 10%, except for the reaction conditions at high T and low WHSV, where the high formation of heavy compounds raised the error up to 20%. A simplified reaction scheme (reactions 1 to 8) was proposed from the detailed reaction network reported for the HM-HfO2/SiO2 catalyst by Cabello et al. [35], which was elucidated by analysing kinetic curves and feeding reaction intermediate compounds. The reaction scheme accounts for the formation of the main (acetaldehyde, 1,3-butadiene, hydrogen and water) as well as minor (diethyl ether, ethene, 1-butanol, butenes, heavy compounds and other oxygenated compounds) reaction products observed in the catalytic tests. The proposed reaction scheme does not represent the molecular mechanism on the catalyst surface, just the overall reactions from ethanol. With this simple reaction scheme, it is mathematically possible to describe the complex reaction system, as it will be shown later. Reaction 1 C 2 H 5 OH → C 2 H 4 O + H 2 Reaction 2 C 2 H 5 OH + C 2 H 4 O → C 4 H 6 + 2 H 2 O Reaction 3 2 C 2 H 5 OH → C 2 H 5 2 O + H 2 O Reaction 4 C 2 H 5 OH → C 2 H 4 + H 2 O Reaction 5 2 C 2 H 5 OH → C 4 H 9 OH + H 2 O Reaction 6 C 4 H 9 OH → C 4 H 8 + H 2 O Reaction 7 13 2 C 2 H 4 O → C 13 H 10 O + 11 2 H 2 O + 5 2 H 2 Reaction 8 2 C 2 H 5 OH → C 4 H 8 O + H 2 O + H 2 The production of 1,3-butadiene from ethanol over the HM-HfO2/SiO2 catalyst was found to occur through the Toussaint-Kagan pathway [11], which was modelled as two consecutive reactions: (i) ethanol dehydrogenation to acetaldehyde (reaction 1) and (ii) reaction of ethanol and acetaldehyde to 1,3-butadiene and water (reaction 2). This second reaction is an aggregate of the last three reactions in the Toussaint-Kagan pathway (self-condensation of acetaldehyde to crotonaldehyde, reduction of crotonaldehyde to crotyl alcohol with ethanol and crotyl alcohol dehydration into 1,3-butadiene). The reason of this simplification is that the concentration of crotonaldehyde and crotyl alcohol was very low in the reaction products, which precluded an accurate modelling of these intermediate steps. It should be mentioned that reactions 1 and 2 could also be used to model the production of 1,3-butadiene in the case that it occurred through the reaction of adsorbed ethanol and acetaldehyde molecules, as proposed by alternative reaction pathways, since the stoichiometry of the reaction steps is the same [13,14,17]. Regarding diethyl ether and ethene, it was experimentally found that they were formed by ethanol dehydration [35], so their formation was modelled with reactions 3 and 4, respectively. The reaction pathway of 1-butanol from ethanol over the HM-HfO2/SiO2 catalyst was not experimentally studied, but the authors believe that 1-butanol is formed by a Guerbet reaction of ethanol [36], because the catalyst is active for aldol-condensation. Since the first step in the Guerbet reaction is ethanol dehydrogenation, already modelled as reaction 1, an additional reaction, the overall formation of 1-butanol from ethanol (reaction 5), was added to the reaction scheme. Between these two reactions, or any linear combination of them, the kinetics of the Guerbet condensation of ethanol is simply described, an approach successfully applied by Riittonen et al. [37]. On the other hand, it was experimentally verified that 1-butanol dehydrated to butenes (1-butene, cis-2-butene, trans-2-butene, and isobutene) over the catalyst. This transformation is modelled with reaction 6, where butene isomers were lumped to simplify the modelling. Other lumps were also used to group numerous minor compounds. Thus, compounds with more than six carbons (mostly aromatic) were lumped as heavy compounds. These are believed to be formed by consecutive acetaldehyde aldol-condensation and further dehydrogenation and cyclization reactions [38]. For the sake of simplicity their formation from acetaldehyde was modelled with reaction 7. Diphenyl ketone (C13H10O) was chosen as a representative compound for heavy compounds since it was identified as a reaction product [35] and its molecular formula is close to that of the aggregate of all the products considered as heavy compounds. Finally, many minor oxygenated compounds (ethyl acetate, acetone, butanal, 2-ethyl-butanol, etc.) were formed over the catalyst from numerous side reactions. Modelling of all these side reactions is impracticable, so these compounds were lumped as oxygenated compounds, whose average molecular formula is close to that of butanal (C4H8O). Both ethanol and acetaldehyde are involved in the formation of the oxygenated compounds. By adding the overall formation reaction of butanal from ethanol (reaction 8) to the reaction scheme, and together with reaction 1, the formation of oxygenated compounds from ethanol and acetaldehyde was indirectly described. Due to the present lack of knowledge of molecular-level mechanism of this type of catalysts, as well as the large number of unknown side reactions and intermediates, no attempt was made to model the mechanism of the proposed reactions. Thus, a more practical approach was followed, and a power-law kinetics was assumed for each reaction. As commented in the introduction, water inhibits ethanol dehydrogenation (reaction 1) and aldol-condensation reactions (reactions 2, 5 and 7) but promotes ethanol dehydration (reactions 3 and 4). Thus, a corrective term (1 + a i P H2O ) mi was introduced in the power-law kinetics, according to Eq. (1), to correct the effect of water on the rate of those reactions: (1) r i = A i e − Ea i R 1 T − 1 T ref ∏ k = 1 C P k n ki 1 + a i P H 2 O m i i = 1 . … NR where for each reaction i, r i is the reaction rate in mol/(gּ h), A i is the rate constant at the reference temperature (Tref = 360 °C) in mol/(g h barΣnki), Ea i the activation energy in kJ/mol, nki the reaction order of reactant k in reaction i, T the temperature in Kelvin, Pk the partial pressure of reactant k and PH2O is the water partial pressure along the reactor. The corrective term (1 + a i P H2O ) mi includes a i (bar−1) and m i as fitting parameters, being m i  ≥ 0 for reactions 1,2,5,7 and 8, m i  ≤ 0 for reactions 3 and 4, m i  = 0 for reaction 6. The corrective term (1 + aPH2O)m in the case of reactions 1,2,5,7 and 8 resembles the adsorption term of a Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetics. This is on purpose since, from our previous work [33], it is known that the active sites of the catalyst for those reactions are blocked by water adsorption. In this regard, other authors have successfully modelled the kinetic inhibitory effect of water for other reactions and catalytic systems with that type of corrective term [39]. It should be noted that the choice of such corrective term it is also mathematically justified because the inhibitory effect of water cannot be simply modelled in a power-law fashion with a negative reaction order, since it would lead to infinite rates of reaction at the inlet of the reactor for an anhydrous ethanol feed. The kinetic equations for reactions 1 to 8 are shown in Eqs. (2) to (9). (2) r 1 = A 1 ⋅ e − Ea 1 R 1 T − 1 T ref P EtOH n 1 1 + a 1 P H 2 O m 1 (3) r 2 = A 2 ⋅ e − Ea 2 R 1 T − 1 T ref P EtOH n 2 ⋅ P Ac n 3 1 + a 2 P H 2 O m 2 (4) r 3 = A 3 ⋅ e − Ea 3 R 1 T − 1 T ref P EtOH n 4 1 + a 3 P H 2 O m 3 (5) r 4 = A 4 ⋅ e − Ea 4 R 1 T − 1 T ref P EtOH n 5 1 + a 4 P H 2 O m 4 (6) r 5 = A 5 ⋅ e − Ea 5 R 1 T − 1 T ref P EtOH n 6 1 + a 5 P H 2 O m 5 (7) r 6 = A 6 ⋅ e − Ea 6 R 1 T − 1 T ref P BuOH n 7 (8) r 7 = A 7 ⋅ e − Ea 7 R 1 T − 1 T ref P Ac n 8 1 + a 7 P H 2 O m 7 (9) r 8 = A 8 ⋅ e − Ea 8 R 1 T − 1 T ref P EtOH n 9 1 + a 8 P H 2 O m 8 In the kinetic model, the independent variables are temperature and partial pressures while Ai, Eai, njk, ai, mi are the parameters to be estimated (39 parameters). The parameters of the kinetic model were estimated fitting experimental data of the catalyst performance (section 2.1) by applying the principle of maximum likelihood, which under the following assumptions, is equivalent to minimizing an objective function [40,41]. Thus, assuming that errors in the observations are independent, normally distributed, with constant variance for each dependent variable, and that the covariance between dependent variables is negligible, the resulting objective function is the sum of the squared of residuals (e) divided by the variance of the experimental error. In the objective function (Eq. 10), the residuals were calculated as the difference between the experimental (F kj ) and estimated ( F ̂ kj ) mole flow rate at the reactor outlet for each compound k along all experiments j; σkj is the standard deviation of the experimental error of the mole flow rate of the compound k in experiment j, calculated from, at least, three measurements; N is the number of experiments, and C the number of compounds. Since C = 11 and the estimation dataset comprises N = 48 catalytic tests, the total number of experimental data points used in the fitting were 528, resulting in a ratio of 13.5 experimental data points per parameter to be estimated. (10) S = ∑ k = 1 C ∑ j = 1 N F kj − F ̂ kj 2 σ kj 2 = ∑ k = 1 C ∑ j = 1 N e kj 2 σ kj 2 The mole flow rate of each compound at the reactor outlet was calculated by integrating a set of mole balance differential equations in the reactor (Eq. (11)), where W is the mass of catalyst, F ̂ k the mole flow rate of compound (or lump) k, υki is the stoichiometric coefficient of each compound k in the chemical reaction i and ri the rate of reaction i. (11) d F ̂ k dW = ∑ i = 1 NR υ ki r i k = 1 … C In Eq. (11), two simplifications were imposed: i) ideal plug flow for the experimental laboratory scale reactor since both the ratio between the length (L) and the diameter (D) of the catalyst bed and the axial Peclet number are much greater than 1 [42]; ii) internal and external mass transfer limitations are absent and the controlling step is the chemical reaction. The latter was confirmed for every catalytic test by studying the Mears criterion and the Weisz-Prater criterion for external and internal mass transfer limitation, respectively, as explained by M.A. Portillo et al. [43] and briefly summarized in the Supporting Information (SI). Thus, the use of a pseudo-homogeneous reactor model is justified. Starting from an initial guess of the kinetic parameters, an iterative loop was performed to estimate the kinetic parameters. First, an optimization was performed where the kinetic parameters were searched in order to minimize the objective function (Eq. (10)). This optimization was carried out in two steps. In the first step, a Nelder-Mead direct-search algorithm was used to find a point close to a minimum, while in a second step an interior-point algorithm continued the search from that point to the minimum. The parameters estimated by optimization were then statistically analyzed and the least significant parameter for a given confidence level was removed. The optimization was again performed for further estimation, but with a reduced set of kinetic parameters. This loop was performed until all parameters retained in the model were statistically significant, and there was no need to remove any of them. With this strategy, the use of an excessive number of parameters, and therefore, overfitting, was prevented, improving the generalization capability of the model. In the optimization, the reaction orders were bounded between 0 and 3 since higher reaction orders are unusual. To establish whether the calculated parameters were statistically significant (different from zero), after each optimization a hypothesis test was conducted for every kinetic parameter (θr) (Ho: θr = 0 versus Ha: θr ≠ 0), where a parameter is significant if the following condition (Eq. (12)) is met: (12) P − θ ̂ r − 0 SE r 1 − α r = 1 … p being θ ̂ r the estimation of the kinetic parameter under consideration, SEr the standard error of the estimated parameter, p the number of model parameters, tNdf the t-student distribution with Ndf degrees of freedom (Ndf = C·N-p), pval the p-value and (1-α) the significance level (set to 95% or α = 0.05). Thus, a parameter θ ̂ r is statistically different from zero if pval< α. From the parameters which were found to be non-significant after each optimization, the one with the largest p-value was removed from the model. When all parameters were found significant, the estimation loop finished, and confidence intervals (CI) for the remaining model parameters were calculated according to Eq. (13): (13) CI θ r = θ ̂ r ± t Ndf , 1 − α / 2 ⋅ SE r r = 1 … p where tNdf, 1- α/2 is the t-student variable defined for Ndf degrees of freedom and a confidence interval (1-α/2). The standard error of each parameter (SEr) was calculated from the diagonal elements (vrr) of the covariance matrix of the model parameters, Vθ (Eq. (14)). This covariance matrix was calculated from the Hessian matrix Hθ [44] using the Gauss-Newton approximation of the Hessian, according to Eq. (15): (14) SE r = v rr r = 1 … p (15) V θ − 1 = 1 2 H θ ; H θ ≈ 2 J T ⋅ J where J = ∂ e kj ∂ θ p θ ̂ All calculations were carried out using Matlab® software. The integration of the differential equations along the reactor was accomplished with the ode45 routine. For the minimization of the objective function, the fminsearch function, which implements the Nelder-Mead direct-search algorithm, and the fmincon function, which make use of an interior-point algorithm and allows setting bounds for the parameters, were used. The lsqnonlin function was used to obtain the Jacobian matrix (J) at the minimum by the finite difference method. It should be noted that, to facilitate the estimation of the kinetic parameters, it is of paramount importance to start the optimization algorithm from a good initial guess of the kinetic parameters. To get a good initial guess of the kinetic parameters, a simplified method was carried out that assumes constant molar flow along the reactor so that the system of differential equations (Eq. (11)) can be transformed into an algebraic system, and the kinetic parameters to be estimated by non-linear regression. Details of this simplified method are explained in a previous work by M.A. Portillo et al. [43]. The estimated kinetic parameters are shown in Table 1 , while the comparison for each compound between the experimental mole flow rates and those predicted by the model is shown in Fig. 1 . The fitting is good, not only for the most abundant compounds at the reactor outlet (ethanol, acetaldehyde, water, hydrogen, and 1,3-butadiene), but also for most of the minor ones (ethene, diethyl ether, 1-butanol, and oxygenated compound lump). Approximately 90% of the points lie within the ±20% error bands. This result indicates that the chosen reaction scheme includes the most important reactions occurring over the catalyst. Only the fitting of the lumps butenes and heavy compounds is not so good. An obvious reason is that it is difficult to predict an aggregation of multiple compounds that are formed through different reactions by using only one reaction, particularly in the case of heavy compounds. This problem is magnified when trying to model the effect of water on their formation: the kinetic model slightly overestimates the formation of heavy compounds for an aqueous ethanol feed while underestimates it for an anhydrous ethanol feed. An additional difficulty is that there is a relatively large error in the quantification of the heavy compounds in the catalytic tests, which distorts the material balance and therefore, the fitting. In order to validate the assumptions of the regression model (that the errors follow a normal distribution and have a constant variance [45]), the residuals were analyzed. The standardized residuals are normally distributed (Fig. B.1) as they fairly follow the normal-distribution line, so it can be considered that the normality hypothesis is also fulfilled. The homoscedasticity hypothesis (constant variance) was validated by plotting for each compound the standardized residuals against predicted values (Fig. B.2). The standardized residuals of the main compounds (ethanol, acetaldehyde, hydrogen, 1,3-butadiene, water, diethyl ether, and ethene) do not follow any trend and also no change is observed in their spread around the zero line as one moves from left to right along the plots. Therefore, the homoscedasticity hypothesis is fulfilled for those compounds. On the other hand, the spread of the residuals of the lumps butenes and heavy compounds follow a downward linear trend. Although for these minor compounds the homoscedasticity hypothesis is violated, it is fulfilled for the major compounds and therefore the model is accepted. The validation of the kinetic model was performed by testing it against the experimental data that were not used for the fitting (validation data). Fig. 2 compares the predictions of the model against the validation data. The distribution of the points in the parity plots of Fig. 2 for each compound is like that in Fig. 1, consequently the kinetic model has not been overfitted. Again, most of the points lie within the ±20% error bands, except for butenes and heavy compounds, thus the generalization capability of the model is acceptable. From the above assessment, it can be concluded that, overall, a good prediction capability of the kinetic model is expected except for some lumps (butenes and heavy compounds). However, these lumps usually comprise less than 10% of the product stream on a mole basis, so only a small error is expected when estimating the overall performance of the catalyst with the help of the kinetic model. Therefore, the kinetic model can be a useful tool in the design of an industrial process for the one-step conversion of ethanol to 1,3-butadiene since it will allow to analyze the impact on process performance of reaction conditions, the use of aqueous ethanol as feedstock as well as the degree of water removal from unconverted ethanol. In the estimation of the kinetics parameters, corrective terms of reactions 3 and 4 (ethanol dehydration to diethyl ether and ethene, respectively), and reaction 8 (formation of heavy compounds from acetaldehyde) were removed from the model as they were found not significant in the statistical analysis. Regarding reactions 3 and 4, their corrective terms were expected to account for the increase in the formation of ethene and diethyl ether when using an aqueous ethanol feed, whose underlying cause was the transformation of Lewis acid sites into Brønsted acid sites by water. The removal of these terms makes us believe that the role of Brønsted acid sites is minor in that matter and that the larger generation of ethene and diethyl ether achieved with aqueous ethanol can be explained by the inhibition of ethanol dehydrogenation (reaction 1), which results in more ethanol available to be converted into ethene and diethyl ether. The inhibition of ethanol dehydrogenation by water can also be the reason why the corrective term of the reaction 8 is unnecessary, because the resulting lower availability of acetaldehyde reduces the formation of heavy compounds. There is only one kinetic model for a one-step catalyst available in the literature which was developed by Tret'yakov et al. [25,26] for a K2O/ZnO/γ-Al2O3 catalyst. There are important differences between that model and the one reported in the present work in terms of the proposed reaction network and kinetic equations. Regarding the reaction network, Tret'yakov et al. did not perform, to the best of our knowledge, a study of kinetic curves or of any other nature to elucidate it, so it is unknown the rationale behind it. One of the main differences in the reaction network when compared to our work is that they considered Prins condensation and dehydrogenation of butenes as routes for 1,3-butadiene, in addition to the Toussaint-Kagan pathway. This is debatable since dehydrogenation of butenes is thermodynamically unfavored [3] while Prins condensation is thermodynamically less favorable than the Toussaint-Kagan pathway [36]. The importance of these two routes relative to the Toussaint-Kagan pathway in describing the production of 1,3-butadiene over their catalyst is unknown, since their relative contributions were not reported once the kinetic model was regressed. On the other hand, in the present work, the production of 1,3-butadiene was well modelled only with the Toussaint-Kagan pathway, which was demonstrated to occur over the catalyst [35]. In both works, the Toussaint-Kagan pathway is modelled as two consecutive reactions: (i) ethanol dehydrogenation to acetaldehyde and (ii) conversion of two molecules of acetaldehyde to butadiene and water in the presence of a reducing agent (ethanol in the present work and hydrogen in that by Tret'yakov et al.). Regarding the latter, while there is a consensus on the role of ethanol as reducing agent, it is currently under debate whether hydrogen gas can also act as reducing agent. ZnO can perform the heterolytic dissociation of hydrogen, so it could explain why Tret'yakov et al. considered hydrogen as reducing agent but not why they excluded ethanol as such [11]. Regarding the kinetic equations, Tret'yakov et al. [25] distinguished three distinctive active sites of the catalyst where the reactions take place. For each reaction, they derived a kinetic equation by assuming a mechanism and a rate-limiting step. An unusual feature of the kinetic model by Tret'yakov et al. is that the reaction order of the butadiene formation steps is zero. Also, the model cannot account for the severe effect of water on the performance of one-step catalysts [14,28,30–33] since water adsorption was not modelled in the kinetic equations. On the other hand, in the present work, a straightforward approach was followed by making use of a power-law kinetics due to the present lack of knowledge of the molecular-level mechanism for one-step catalysts [11]. As explained before, the kinetic model successfully accounts for the effect of water in the reactions by introducing a corrective term in the kinetic equations, and, unlike the model by Treat'yakov et al., the rate of 1,3-butadiene production was found to increase with acetaldehyde and ethanol partial pressures. Finally, the validity and accuracy of the model by Treat'yakov et al. cannot be compared with that presented in this work, since they did not report any statistical analysis. More kinetic studies of one-step catalysts are necessary in the literature, particularly using an aqueous ethanol feed, due to the lack of research in this subject. A kinetic model for the conversion of ethanol to 1,3-butadiene over a hemimorphite-HfO2/SiO2 catalyst has been developed, comprising eight reactions, eight chemical species and three lumps in order to account for the formation of the main and minor reaction products formed over one-step catalysts. The novelty of the kinetic model is the consideration of the effect of water content in ethanol on catalyst performance, which is a cornerstone feature if the kinetic model is intended to be used for designing industrial processes using aqueous ethanol as feedstock. The former is achieved by introducing a corrective term in the power-law kinetics of each reaction whose rate is affected by water. Experimental data on the effect of water and reaction conditions on the performance of the hemimorphite-HfO2/SiO2 catalyst were used for the regression and validation of the kinetic model. By using statistical analysis, only significant parameters were retained in the model. The results show that the model can predict well the effect of reaction conditions and water content in ethanol on the formation of major and most minor compounds, except butenes and heavy compounds. Thus, the use of lumps simplifies the modelling, but limits the accuracy of the model. The modelling approach to build the kinetic model is expected to be valid for any other one-step catalyst. Such kinetic model will help to design more efficient processes for production of 1,3-butadiene from bioethanol as it is able to analyze the impact on process performance of reaction conditions, the use of aqueous ethanol as feedstock as well as the degree of water removal from unconverted ethanol. Further work is necessary to improve the model to account for the decay in catalyst activity by coke deposition. In addition, a more accurate modelling of the formation rate of heavy compounds and butenes could be achieved at the cost of a much more complex reaction scheme. This work has been carried out in the framework of the project “Biobutadiene production from bioethanol” (CTQ2015-71427-R) funded by the Spanish Ministry of Economy, Industry and Competitiveness (MINECO) through the European Regional Development Fund (ERDF).
abstract: A kinetic model for the one-step conversion of ethanol into 1,3-butadiene over a mixed hemimorphite-HfO2/SiO2 catalyst has been developed, which, as a novelty, accounts for the effect of water content in ethanol on the performance of one-step catalysts, which is important when designing industrial processes. The model considers the formation of the main reaction products (acetaldehyde, water, hydrogen, 1,3-butadiene, ethene, diethyl ether and 1-butanol) as well as numerous minor products, grouped into three lumps (butenes, heavy compounds (C6+), and oxygenated compounds). A network of eight reactions is used to describe this complex reaction system. The rate of each reaction is modelled using a power-law kinetics with a corrective term to capture the effect of water on certain reactions. Experimental data on the effect of water and reaction conditions on the performance of the hemimorphite-HfO2/SiO2 catalyst were used for the regression and validation of the kinetic model. The results show that the model can predict well the effect of reaction conditions and water content in ethanol on the formation of major and minor compounds, except for butenes and heavy compounds. The modelling approach to build the kinetic model is expected to be valid for any other one-step catalyst.
title: Kinetic modelling of the one-step conversion of aqueous ethanol into 1,3-butadiene over a mixed hemimorphite-HfO2/SiO2 catalyst

reaction_catalog.reaction_name_reported: ethanol and acetaldehyde to 1,3-butadiene and water

steps.method_details: separately synthesized HM and HfO2/SiO2 components
physically mixed HM and HfO2/SiO2 in appropriate proportions
pelletized the fine powder mixture
crushed the pelletized catalyst
sieved the crushed catalyst to size fraction between 0.3 and 0.5 mm

full_text: As petroleum reserves are diminishing and environmental pollution is aggravating, alternative fuels are becoming increasingly important [1]. Among these, biodiesel (fatty acid methyl esters or FAME) is a promising alternative fuel to conventional fossil diesel, and it has been produced by the transesterification of vegetable oils with short chain alcohol (usually methanol and ethanol) in the presence of a catalyst [2]. Compared to conventional diesel from petroleum, biodiesel is technically and economically more competitive because of its renewability, biodegradability, low emission profiles, high flashpoint, excellent lubricity and superior cetane number [3]. In addition, the use of biodiesel has the potential to reduce both the levels of pollutants and potential or probable carcinogens [4]. The transesterification of vegetable oils or animal fats to biodiesel by chemical catalysts, especially in the presence of a strong basic solution, such as sodium hydroxide and potassium hydroxide, has been widely used in industrial production of biodiesel. Such basic solutions can transform triglycerides to their corresponding fatty acid methyl esters at higher yield and in lesser time than the transesterification achieved by acid catalysts, even at a lower temperature. At the end of the reaction, the catalysts should be neutralized with the weak acid or removed with a large amount of hot water. However, separating the catalyst from a mixture of reactants and products is technically difficult [5]. Moreover, natural vegetable oils and animal fats usually contain small amounts of free fatty acids (FFA) and water, which can have significant negative effects on the transesterification of glycerides with alcohols, and also hinder the separation of fatty acid esters and glycerol due to saponification of FFA [6]. Compared with the basic solution, solid base catalyst is much more preferred due to better recovery and recycling. It can catalyze the transesterification of low quality oil or fat in the presence of FFA and water. However, the catalytic efficiency of conventional heterogeneous base catalyst is very low and needs to be improved. Various types of catalytic materials have been exploited to improve the transesterification of glycerides. Alkaline earth metal oxides，such as calcium oxide [3,7,8] and calcium methoxide [9] have been shown to have excellent catalytic activity and stability for the production of biodiesel. However, they can absorb H2O and CO2, and form slurry with products, which can cause an increase in the viscosity making it difficult to separate the product. Mg–Al hydrotalcite catalysts [10] have lower surface areas and base activities. Compared with the above two kinds of catalysts, supported-solid base catalysts are easier to prepare, such as Eu2O3/Al2O3 [11], La/β zeolite [12], KI/Al2O3 [13], Na/NaOH/γ-Al2O3 [14]. During the preparation of such catalysts, the mechanical intensity and surface area of the carrier can be adjusted to obtain different basic intensities and activity sites. The supported solid base catalysts are excellent catalysts for the transesterification of triglyceride, but a higher transesterification temperature is needed. Low-cost catalysts that still retain the advantages of a supported base catalyst should be exploited to simplify the preparation process. In this study, sodium silicate (Na2O·nSiO2) was first used for the transesterification of soybean oil to biodiesel. In addition to high catalyst activity, sodium silicate also has other similar characteristics as supported-solid base catalysts. In previous researches, sodium silicate was used as starting materials to synthesize γ-zeolite [15], NaY zeolite [16], and NaX zeolite [17]. Sodium silicate was exploited herein for its ability to catalyze the process of transesterification using an easy calcination technology. The properties of calcined sodium silicate were characterized by means of TG-DTA, Hammett indicator method, XRD, SEM, BET, IR and FT-IR. The reaction process was optimized and the tolerance ability of calcined sodium silicate to water and FFA in oil, as well as the recycling of catalyst was also investigated. Sodium silicate (containing 19.3–22.8wt.% of Na2O), dehydrated methanol and oleic acid used in this study were obtained from Kermel Corp., Tianjin, China. Refined soybean oils were purchased from Fuhong Corp., Liaoning, China. Sodium silicate was first characterized by Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential thermal analysis (DTA), which were carried out by a TGA/SD T851e (Mettler-toledo, CH) under air flow at a heating rate of 10K/min up to 900°C. To prepare the catalyst, sodium silicate was first dehydrated at 200°C for 15–20min, and then calcined in a muffle furnace at 200°C to 500°C for 1h to 5h. To improve the catalytic activity, the calcined sodium was triturated and passed through a 120 mesh sieve. The basic strength of calcined sodium silicate was determined by means of Hammett indicators, and the basicity of the catalyst was measured using the method of Hammett indicator-benzene carboxylic acid titration [10]. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was conducted using Cu Kα (λ =0.154nm) as a radiation source in an automatic X-ray Diffractometer (Rigaku Denki, JPN). The samples were scanned in the range of 2θ =5–60° at a scanning speed of 2°/min. Scans were collected by a MDI XRD diffractometer control and data acquisition system (MDI, US). Structural examination of the catalyst by scanning electron microscope (SEM) was performed on a JSM-5600LV (JEOL, JPN) operating at an accelerated voltage of 20kV. The BET surface area of the prepared catalysts was measured with AUTOSORB-1-MP (QUANTACHROME, U.S.). IR spectra (SHIMADZU, JPN) and FT-IR spectra (EQUINOX55, GER) were applied to characterize the structural change of the catalysts, with a scanning range from 400 to 4000cm−1. The catalytic reactions were carried out in a 250mL three-necked round-bottomed flask equipped with a reflux condenser. The flask was charged with 30g soybean oil and immersed in a thermostat-controlled water-bath. After achieving the temperature setting, a relevant amount of methanol and freshly prepared catalysts were added to the flask to start the transesterification. After reacting for 60min, samples were withdrawn for composition analyses using gas chromatographic (GC). The catalysts and the excess methanol were recovered by centrifugation and vacuum distillation, respectively. Transesterification was carried out at 30–70°C for 60min. The molar ratio of methanol to oil ranged from 3:1 to 9:1 while the amount of calcined sodium silicate varied form 0.5wt.% to 5.0wt.% of the soybean oil (30g). The tolerance of calcined sodium silicate to water and FFA was compared with non-calcined sodium silicate under optimal reaction conditions using soybean oil with different initial water contents (0.5wt.%, 1.0wt.%, 2.0wt.%, 3.0wt.%, 4.0wt.% and 5.0wt.%) and oleic acid (0.5wt.%, 1.0wt.%, 1.5wt.%, 2.0wt.%, 2.5wt.%, 3.0wt.%, 4.0wt.% and 5.0wt.%), respectively. The calcined sodium silicate was recovered by centrifugation after one batch of experiment, and directly used for the next transesterification under the optimal reaction conditions. The composition of the reaction mixture was determined by GC (GC-14B, Shimadzu, Japan) with capillary column of SE-30 (50m×Φ0.25mm×0.4μm). To detect the yield of biodiesel, purified biodiesel produced with 1wt.% sodium hydroxide was first analyzed using an internal standard (heptadecanoic acid methyl ester) prior to being used as external standard. The column temperature was 240°C, the temperatures of the injector and detector were 290°C. The transesterified products can be calculated using the following equation: (1) Y = Α s × Μ e Α e × Μ s × 100 % where Y is the yield of biodiesel; A s and A e are the total peak areas of FAME produced by sodium silicate and sodium hydroxide, respectively; M s and M e are the masses of FAME produced by sodium silicate and sodium hydroxide, respectively. The DTA and TG profiles for sodium silicate samples are shown in Fig. 1 . The only endothermic DTA peak ranged from 20°C to 200°C, and TG curve indicated that the mass loss for the catalysts was 41%. No mass loss occurred in the range of 200–900°C suggested that the bound water molecules were removed during drying. In order to obtain the optimal calcination conditions, especially calcination temperature and calcination time, sodium silicates were prepared at 200°C, 300°C, 400°C and 500°C for 1–5h. The basicity of each catalyst was tested and the catalytic activity of each catalyst was investigated by transesterification of soybean oil with methanol under the same conditions (Fig. 2 ). As shown in Table 1 , under the same calcination time, the total basicities first increased and then decreased with increases in calcination temperatures. This result was similar to that obtained with solid-base catalyst described by Xie et al [18]. The effect of calcination temperatures on basicity with H 0 in the range of 15.0–18.4 paralleled its effect on catalytic activity for the transesterification of soybean oil (Fig. 2). The catalyst calcined at 300°C for 2h had the highest total basicity. But optimum catalyst was obtained by calcined at 400°C for 2h, which gave the highest bacisity with H 0 in the range of 15.0–18.4, and the best catalytic activity for transesterification. This could be due to the stronger basicity as represented by H 0 in the range of 15.0–18.4 (Table 1), which also indicated that higher basicity could result in the higher conversion. The XRD diffraction patterns of sodium silicate (Fig. 3a) showed that the structure of sodium silicate was mainly assigned to Na2SiO3 hydrated with 9 molecules of H2O. If sodium silicate was calcined at 400°C for 2h, most of the combined water molecules would be removed and assigned to anhydrous Na2SiO3 (Fig. 3b). The calcination treatment caused the enhancement in intensity of some diffraction peaks, as illustrated by the crystalline phase of catalyst becoming more regular. The presence of water molecules and different structure of sodium silicate before and after calcination may result in different catalytic behaviors in the transesterification. Images of sodium silicate calcined at 400°C for 2h taken by SEM showed that it had accumulated large number of agglomerates on the surface (Fig. 4 ). These agglomerates were loosely attached, and the spaces between agglomerates were 1–5μm (Fig. 4b). Such structures are favorable to the entry of triglyceride and methanol, so the basic sites of internal surface can also be exploited for the transesterification reaction. The BET surface area of the calcined sodium silicate prepared at 400°C for 2h was 5.91m2/g, while the titration of basic groups showed that the density of the basic site was 16.16mmol/g. The low surface area and high density of basic site indicated that most of the basic sites were in the interior of the solid catalyst [23]. This allowed more glyceride to diffuse into the interior of the catalyst. The effect of calcination on the molecular structure of sodium silicate was analyzed by IR spectroscopy. The IR spectra of non-calcined sodium silicate and sodium silicate calcined at 400°C for 2h are shown in Fig. 5 . The spectra showed an absorption band at ∼1000cm−1 , which could be attributed to the Si–O–Na stretching. After calcination the intensity of Si–O–Na stretching peak was reduced along with a low-frequency shift and a new Si–O–Si stretching peak appearance (at ∼890cm−1), indicating that the SiO4 4− tetrahedral changed from the state of low polymerization to a state of high polymerization. As the amounts of Si–O–Si increased and the lattice structure of the catalyst became more regular, the corresponding absorption intensity for Si–O bending at ∼470cm−1 and Si–O–Na stretching at ∼1000cm−1 became weaker. During the process of polymerization change, the volume fraction of free water can be reduced as the surface area of ion groups in the catalyst became larger. Furthermore, the calcination process dehydrated most of the adsorbed water on the catalyst surface. Therefore, the intensity of water peaks (between 2800 and 3400cm−1) was decreased. Calcined sodium silicate in the range of 0.5–5.0wt.% of soybean oil was used as catalyst to investigate the effect of the catalyst content on transesterification of soybean oil. The yield of biodiesel increased from 53% to about 100% as the catalyst content was increased (Fig. 6a). However, there was no substantial increase in the yield of FAME when the catalyst content increased from 3.0wt.% to 5.0wt.%. The yield of biodiesel was 96% at 3.0wt.% catalyst to soybean oil mass ratio. The catalyst content was chosen for optimization of transesterification was 3.0wt.%. The effect of temperature on transesterification was investigated by carrying out transesterfication at different temperatures (30, 40, 50, 60 and 70°C, respectively) were for 60min. The yield of biodiesel increased with increases in temperatures, reaching nearly 100% at 60 to 70°C (Fig. 6b). Considering the biodiesel yield and energy economy, it is best to choose 60°C as the reaction temperature for the transesterification of soybean oil. The transesterification performed with a methanol/oil ratio of 3:1 to 9:1 (Fig. 6c) indicated that the conversion of soybean oil to FAME increased with increasing molar ratios of methanol to oil. After 60min of transesterification, the maximal yield of FAME was obtained at the molar ratio of 7.5:1 and 9:1. The optimal methanol/oil ratio was determined to be 7.5:1, since at higher ratio (9:1), stable emulsions would form under higher content of methanol, leading to complicated separation and purification of biodiesel [24]. At the end of the reaction, the excess methanol could be recovered by a vacuum distillatory, and recycled in latter transesterifications. The mixing played an important role in transesterification because soybean oil, methanol and calcined sodium silicate are immiscible. When the calcined sodium silicate was added to the reactants, small bubbles were formed, as reported by Kim et al [14]. It was necessary to stir the mixture to enhance the contact between reactants and basic activity sites of the catalyst. The effect of stirring speed on biodiesel yield was investigated at the stirring rate of 100–300rpm. The maximal yield of biodiesel was 97% at 250rpm (Fig. 6d). A high stirring speed was beneficial to the mass transfer of reactants. However, it also increased the vibration of the mechanical rotor which led to the loss of methanol. This also suggested that the methanol/oil ratio had more effect on transestirification than the stirring speed. Key properties of refined biodiesel obtained from soybean oil that are in accordance with European specification standard are shown in Table 2 . The kinematic viscosities of soybean oil and the obtained FAME were 22.9mm2/S and 4.2mm2/S, respectively. This difference was due to the significant decrease in average molecular weight for the product after the transesterification. Compared with other reported solid catalysts, calcined sodium silicate exhibited higher catalytic efficiency as shown in Table 3 . For example, the transesterification catalyzed by calcined sodium silicate could gave higher biodiesel yield at a lower temperature, shorter reaction time and lower molar ratio of methanol/oil compared with MgO [19], Al–Mg hydrotalcite [19], KNO3/Al2O3 [20]and montmorillonite KSF [21]. Although the reaction temperature for ion-exchange resin is lower, high amount of catalyst (20–40wt.%) was shown to increase the production cost of biodiesel [22]. CaO has been recently investigated for use as a popular solid base catalyst. However, certain amount of water (2.03wt.%) should be present in raw feedstock in order to obtain the best catalytic activity [8]. The average price of CaO and sodium silicate in China are ￥500/ton and ￥1000/ton, respectively. To produce one ton of biodiesel, 80kg of CaO (dosage of 8wt.%) or 30kg of sodium silicate (dosage of 3wt.%) would be needed to catalyze the transesterification. The corresponding costs of the two catalyst would amount to ￥40 and￥30 for CaO and sodium silicate, respectively. This cost analysis method has ignored the advantages of sodium silicate in terms of reaction temperature, methanol dosage and reaction time. When taken into accounts these advantages, sodium silicate has an economic advantage over CaO in biodiesel production. In the conventional transesterification of soybean oil for biodiesel production，the presence of water has a significant effect on the yield of biodiesel. Using soybean oils containing various water contents the synthesis of biodiesel catalyzed by non-calcined sodium silicate was compared to that catalyzed by calcined sodium silicate. For the synthesis catalyzed by non-calcined sodium silicate, little variation in the yields of biodiesel was observed for water content less than 1.0wt.% of soybean oil (Fig. 7 ). As water content increased, the yields clearly decreased, down to 44% when the water content was 4.0wt.% of the soybean oil. In contrast, the transesterification catalyzed by calcined sodium silicate displayed excellent tolerant ability to water contained in oil. The yield was maintained at 98% even when the water content was 4.0wt.% of soybean oil. This property of calcined sodium silicate made it more superior to the conventional alkaline catalyst reported by Ma et al. [25], in which the apparent yield of 17% was obtained when 0.9% of water was added. Calcined sodium silicate was suitable for catalyzing the transesterification of raw oil materials which may not be substantially anhydrous. The tolerance of calcined sodium silicate to water is related to its special crystal and porous structure. In the presence of high amounts of water, a sequential hydration will occur in three steps [26]: (2) ≡ Si − O − Na + H 2 O → ≡ Si − O − H + OH − ≡ Si − O − Si ≡ + OH − → ≡ Si − O − H + ≡ Si − O − ≡ Si − O − + H 2 O → ≡ Si − O − H + OH − As a result, Si–O–Si bridges would be hydrolyzed and H4SiO4 monomers are sequentially released. Such series of reactions not only produce OH−, but also avoid the formation of soap. The FFA content is a key parameter for determining the applicability of vegetable oil for transesterification. The transesterification of soybean oil with methanol catalyzed by non-calcined sodium silicate showed a decreased tendency as more FFA was added (Fig. 8 ), whereas calcined sodium silicate exhibited stable tolerance to FFA when the FFA content was below 2.5wt.% of soybean oil. The transesterification catalyzed by a homogeneous base in the presence of FFA resulted in a low biodiesel yield as reported by Ma et al. [24], because the formation of soap consumes the base catalyst and reduces the catalytic efficiency, causing an increase in viscosity of the reactants and a decline of product yield [27]. The starting materials used for base-catalyzed transesterification should have low acid values, i.e., low FFA contents. Lowering the acid values of oil will consequently increase the cost of biodiesel production. After the transesterification was complete, the samples were recovered by simple decantation. The remaining catalyst in the reactor was used to catalyze the next batch of transesterification. It is difficult for non-calcined sodium silicate to be reused in the transesterification of soybean oil, because non-calcined sodium silicate can be hydrolyzed to form H4SiO4 monomers. The latter will conglutinate the solid catalyst and affect the mass transfer, and eventually leading to a decline in biodiesel yield. The biodiesel yield was reduced to 56% when non-calcined sodium silicate was reused for 3 times (Fig. 9 ). This was because non-calcined sodium silicate could become hydrolyzed in the transesterification reaction, leading to the loss of basic sites during the separation process to recover the catalyst after one batch of reaction. For calcined sodium silicate, 94% of the biodiesel yield was obtained even after 5 recycling. The FT-IR spectra of fresh calcined sodium silicate and calcined sodium silicate recycled for 5 times are shown in Fig. 10 . The absorption intensity of Si–O–Si stretching at 710cm−1 was decreased, indicating that the content of Si–O–Si bridges had decreased. The responding absorption intensity of Si–O bending at 1025cm−1 and Si–O stretching at 960cm−1 showed the opposite trend, which can be explained by the fact that some Si–O–Si bridges were broken during the recycling process of the catalyst. The intensity of Si–O–H absorptions at 889cm−1 and ∼3500cm−1 increased, indicating that the catalyst absorbed some water molecule after 5 recyclings. Calcined sodium silicate can be recovered by simple centrifugation after reaction because it is immiscible with oil, methanol and FAME. The result of catalyst recycling also showed that the catalytic activity of the solid catalyst became more stable after calcination. Sodium silicate was first used in the transesterification of soybean oil to biodiesel. A calcination temperature of 400°C and calcination time of 2h were found to be the optimum conditions for preparing sodium silicate-based solid base catalyst. The catalytic activity observed in transesterification depended more on the basicity of the basic sites present in the catalyst. The maximum biodiesel yield could be achieved with 3.0wt.% of sodium silicate catalyst, a molar ratio of methanol to soybean oil of 7.5:1, and a reaction temperature of 60°C for 60min with stirring at 250rpm. The calcined sodium silicate could tolerate 4.0wt.% water or 2.5wt.% FFA contained in soybean oil. Although the calcined sodium silicate achieved higher activity as a homogenous base in the first batch of reaction, a considerable loss in catalytic activity was observed when the catalyst was recycled for more than 5 times. As a low-cost solid base catalyst, the sodium silicate can also be used as adsorbent to adsorb FFA and purify biodiesel, as well as in the transesterification of crude oil containing a certain amount of water and FFA.
abstract: This paper examined the use of calcined sodium silicate as a novel solid base catalyst in the transesterification of soybean oil with methanol. The calcined sodium silicate was characterized by DTA-TG, Hammett indicator method, XRD, SEM, BET, IR and FT-IR. It catalyzed the transesterification of soybean oil to biodiesel with a yield of almost 100% under the following conditions: sodium silicate of 3.0wt.%, a molar ratio of methanol/oil of 7.5:1, reaction time of 60min, reaction temperature of 60°C, and stirring rate of 250rpm. The oil containing 4.0wt.% water or 2.5wt.% FFA could also be transesterified by using this catalyst. The catalyst can be reused for at least 5cycles without loss of activity.
title: Calcined sodium silicate as solid base catalyst for biodiesel production
