full_text: With the depletion of fossil fuel sources as well as the global warming issues, the utilization of biomass is getting increased attention as a potential source of renewable energy. Among them, biomass gasification has attracted huge interest by producing a gas rich in H2 and CO used for fuel production, or methanol and Fischer-Tropsch synthesis [1,2]. Unfortunately, producing gas from this process usually results in unacceptable levels of tars, which can cause operational problems in downstream processes — by blocking gas coolers, filter elements and engine suction channels. Moreover, tars may deposit on the surface of the catalysts used in the downstream process (reforming, shift and methanol or Fischer-Tropsch synthesis), deactivating them. Most applications also require removal of at least part of the dust and tar before the gas can be used. Hence, the tar removal is a key issue for a successful application of biomass generated gases. The catalytic steam reforming is one attractive technique for tar removal. Several kinds of catalysts were developed and applied in this process, such as mineral, Ni-based and noble metal catalysts. Calcined dolomites are the most widely used nonmetallic catalysts for tar conversion [3]. They are relatively inexpensive and considered disposable. Calcined olivine also exhibits biomass tar conversion activity similar to that of calcined dolomite [4,5]. The observed total tar conversions of 63% over calcined dolomite and 46% over olivine was found [4], and more than 80% naphthalene conversion was observed under the calcined olivine (in air oven at 900 °C). In summary, these inexpensive minerals such as dolomite, calcined olivine and magnesia have been already used as bed material in industrial application such as modern CFB gasifier. But the activity and catalyst life of these mineral catalysts also should be improved. Ni based catalysts such as Ni/Dolomite, Ni/olivine, Ni/Al2O3 are another important and have been applied extensively for biomass gasification tar conversion [6,7] because of their high tar destruction activity, along with the added advantages of catalyze the reforming of light hydrocarbons, thus improving the syngas content in the produced gas. But two main limitations for Ni catalysts should be stressed: firstly, commercial Ni catalysts are usually designed for use in fixed bed applications and are not robust enough for fluidized bed applications, therefore are not used as primary, in-bed catalysts, but as secondary catalysts in separate fixed bed reactors operated independently to optimize performance for tar conversion; second, Ni catalyst quickly deactivate in biomass gasifier conditions, which leads to limited catalyst lifetimes. Ni catalyst deactivation is caused by several factors. Carbon formation is regarded as one main problem for deactivation of Ni-based catalysts, because during biomass gasification process, complex hydrocarbons steam reforming reactions are included, which involves a risk of carbon deposition. Deactivation by H2S and alkali metals are another one. Product gas formed from biomass gasification process also some inorganic (H2S, HCl, NH3, alkali metals) impurities and particulates, which is also a strong poison concentration for nickel catalysts and blocks nickel active sites. So the combination of high temperatures, the presence of hydrocarbons, high-pressure steam and sulfur containing compounds creates a severe environment for nickel catalysts. Several attempts have been made to promote the long-term stability of steam-reforming catalysts by addition noble metal or oxide such as Ru [8–10], WO3 [11,12], Pt [13–15], Rh [16–18]. A catalyst consisting of Ru (5%) dispersed on 15% MgO/Al2O3 carrier exhibits high activity and selectivity on steam reforming of the aqueous fraction of bio-oil, as well as satisfactory stability with time on stream, and only a small part of the deposited carbon on the catalyst surface was found [8]. Newly-developed Ni/MgO–CaO catalyst doped with WO3 showed high naphthalene reforming activity and exhibited superior resistance to coking [11]. However, Ru, Rh, Pt are prohibitively expensive [19], which results in no practical for use as industrial catalysts. Mayenite (Ca12Al14O33) was previously developed and used in our group, and exhibited good catalysis and high-performance on hydrocarbon combustion reforming because of the high oxygen restored property, for example the amount of ‘free’ oxygen restored in Ca12Al14O33 reaches 0.30% at 700 °C [20]. Recently we discovered mayenite showed excellent carbon and H2S resistance property as Ni support because of its special ‘free’ oxygen restored structure [21]. Ni-based catalyst (Ni/mayenite) was successfully developed by impregnation method with nickel nitrate hexahydrate in this study, which exhibited a high tar steam reforming activity, as well as its excellent resistance to deactivation by carbon deposition ability. Ni/CaO x /MgO1−x as the popular catalysts for hydrocarbon reforming was selected to compare with our catalyst. The efficiency of the catalyst was evaluated on different operation conditions (reaction temperature, S/C ratio and space-time). Higher conversion and CO selectivity were resulted by this catalyst. Kinetic parameters were calculated for optimized catalytic system and special attention was paid to catalyst stability, especially linked to carbon formation. Mayenite (Ca12Al14O33) was prepared by the mixture Ca(OH)2 and Al(OH)3 in stoichiometric ratio, grinding in Planetary Ball Mill apparatus (Pulverisette-7, Fritsch, Japan) for 4 h at speed of 300 rpm, then calcined at 1000 °C over 4 h in air environment, followed by crushing and sieving to obtain particle sizes between 212 and 425 μm. This mayenite will be referred to initial mayenite. Ca(OH)2 was bought from Wako Pure Chemical Industries Ltd., purity >98.0%, Al(OH)3 was bought from Kanto Chemical Co. Inc., purity >95.0%. The structure of mayenite, Ca12Al14O33, was investigated by neutron powder diffraction up to 1323 K. It has been described previously as a calcium-aluminate framework, in which 32 of the 33 oxygen anions are tightly bound, containing large cages, 1/6 of them being filled randomly by the remaining ‘free’ oxygen [22]. The schematic of mayenite were shown in Fig. 1 . Each AlO4 is three dimensionally linked with one another by sharing corner oxygen atoms, ‘free’ oxygen is located in micro-pores of mayenite. Ni/mayenite (5% wt.) catalyst was prepared by impregnation method. Mayenite (212–425 μm) was put into nickel nitrate hexahydrate solution, stirring for 1 h, then catalysts were dried, molded, calcined, at 800 °C for 4 h, using a temperature increasing rate 3 °C.min−1, lastly crushed and sieved to 212–425 μm. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) of mayenite (Fig. 2a) and Ni/mayenite (Fig. 2b) showed holes of diameters less than 1 μm, a deposit of almost spherical uniform grains (probably Ni or NiO) with size between 0.1 and 0.3 μm could be observed and hid completely the surface of mayenite from Fig. 2b. The nickel metal concentration of the final catalyst measured by EDS is 4.7% wt (nominal: 5%) after reduction by H2 (98.0%, CaO was bought from Kanto Chemical Co. Inc., purity >98.0%, nickel nitrate hexahydrate was also from Kanto Chemical Co. Inc. Toluene was selected as the biomass tar model compound. Experiments were carried out at atmospheric pressure in a fixed-bed quartz reactor (10 mm i.d.) placed in a furnace, the temperature is controlled by thermocouple. The catalyst bed was held by quartz wool in the uniform temperature zone. All the catalysts were initially activated under the reduced gas mixture (<5% H2 diluted by Ar) for 1.5 h at 650 °C, then tested at the given operation parameters. For the feed gas mixture, Argon was used as the dilution gas, toluene was introduced by permeater (GasTec PD-1B) carried by Ar gas, being the toluene concentration around 2000 ppm in the feed (25 °C). Water was pumped by syringe into a vaporization furnace, and mixed with toluene/Argon gas. All the pipelines were kept around 120 °C. The reactant and products gases were analyzed simultaneously by two on-line gas chromatographs. Toluene, benzene and other organic compounds were analyzed by GC-FID with a silicone OV-1 2% (2.0 m⁎3.0 mm I.D.) packed column. The products gases (H2, CO, CO2, CH4) were analyzed by GC-TCD with a packed carbon sieve column (2.0 m⁎3.0 mm I.D.). To determine the catalytic activity of the Ni/mayenite, the toluene conversion in the fixed bed was studied, from the measured toluene contents in the gas stream inlet and exit. Toluene conversion, H2 yields and the selectivity of CO2, CO, CH4, benzene were calculated with the formula (Eqs. (1)–(4)). Toluene conversion, X c, can be defined as Eq. (1): (1) X c = C T , i n − C T , o u t C T , i n × 100. C T,in and C T,out : Toluene molar flow rate of the inlet and outlet gases. Hydrogen yield, Y H 2 (Eq. (2)), is expressed as the percentage of the stoichiometric potential corresponding to the total conversion of toluene according to the reaction (6). (2) Y H 2 ( % ) = [ C H 2 ] o u t 18 [ C T , i n − C T , o u t ] × 100. The carbon containing products (CO, CO2, CH4) are calculated by the selectivity S i (Eq. (3)), which are defined as the ratio of the amount of carbon in the product i to the amount of carbon in the reacted toluene. (3) S i = [ C i ] o u t 7 [ C T , i n − C T , o u t ] × 100. [C i ]: molar flow rate i =CO, CO2, CH4 The selectivity of benzene S B is calculated by Eq. (4). (4) S i = [ C B ] o u t [ C T , i n − C T , o u t ] × 100. [C B]: molar flow rate of benzene in the products. Many parallel and consecutive reactions can take place during steam reforming. The final products can be regarded as the results from the competition of these reactions. Different S/C, operation parameters, and catalysts will results in different products. These gases yield evolutions with the different S/C ratios, temperature etc parameters can be explained by the reactions involved in toluene steam reforming. The reactions are shown as follows: Steam reforming: (5) C 7 H 8 + 7 H 2 O → 7 CO + 11 H 2 Δ H ( 800 C 0 ) = 927.2 kJ ⋅ mol − 1 (6) C 7 H 8 + 14 H 2 O → 7 CO 2 + 18 H 2 Δ H ( 800 C 0 ) = 688.5 kJ ⋅ mol − 1 . Water–gas shift (7) CO + H 2 O = CO 2 + H 2 Δ H ( 800 C 0 ) = − 34.1 kJ ⋅ mol − 1 . Dry reforming (8) C 7 H 8 + 7 CO 2 → 14 CO + 4 H 2 Δ H ( 800 C 0 ) = 1166.0 kJ ⋅ mol − 1 Hydrodealkylation (9) C 7 H 8 + H 2 → C 6 H 6 + CH 4 Δ H ( 800 C 0 ) = − 50.9 kJ ⋅ mol − 1 . Methane steam reforming (10) CH 4 + H 2 O = CO + 3 H 2 Δ H ( 800 C 0 ) = 225.7 kJ ⋅ mol − 1 . Other reactions such as carbon formation reaction by toluene decomposition and cracking also are included in the process. In order to study the catalytic activity of the Ni/mayenite on the effect of operation parameters (reaction temperature, S/C ratio and space-time), toluene steam reforming was performed with the Ni/mayenite. For all experiments before steam reforming reaction, the catalyst is reduced by H2 (<5% diluted by Ar) for 1.5 h at 650 °C. The nickel metal concentration of the final catalyst measured by EDS is 4.7% wt. (theory: 5%). Experiments of steam reforming of toluene were carried out during a temperature range of 500–800 °C for a S/C ratio of 2.1 and space-time (w cat/F toluene) of 8.9 kgcat·h·m−3 at 25 °C. No catalyst deactivation was observed after 24 h of time on stream. The results are shown in Table 1 . From the results, below 600 °C, it can see toluene conversion and hydrogen yield increased quickly with reaction temperature. And trace benzene and CH4 compounds (<1%) were observed in the products in addition to H2, CO and CO2, toluene was nearly totally decomposed. Above 600 °C, nearly total toluene conversion to H2, CO and CO2 was available. Above 700 °C, a decrease in the hydrogen yield and CO selectivity with a corresponding increase in CO selectivity is observed, due to the reverse equilibrium of water gas shift reaction (Eq. (7)). High temperature favors high yield H2 because of endothermal nature of the reforming reactions. Results were compared to those obtained with mayenite alone (Table 2 ). The latter showed almost no activity (<5%) until 650 °C, a moderate (34%) at 850 °C, and finally a lower activity. And also for mayenite, additionally to CO, CO2, and H2, significant selectivity towards benzene (22% at 850 °C) are observed. For toluene steam reforming, Świerczyński [23] has also observed significant selectivity to benzene (6%) and polyaromatics (14%) on olivine. These results illustrated the importance of nickel in the selectivity and efficiency of catalyst during tar reforming. Toluene conversion, H2 yield and CO2 selectivity were compared with those from Ni/CaO x /MgO1−x (Figs. 4–6 ), and also our previous results [21]. It found that Ni/mayenite exhibited higher activity than Ni/CaO x /MgO1-x especially at low temperature (below 650 °C), nearly same activity as the NiO/mayenite [21] and also resulted in higher H2 yield for the whole process. Ni/mayenite resulted higher CO2 selectivity compared to that of Ni/CaO x /MgO1−x , and the possible reason lies in the “free oxygen” structure in the mayenite, which shows higher oxidative property. Comparing with results from Bona [24], Ni/mayenite resulted higher CO selectivity and nearly same H2 yield with Ni/Co/Al at the S/C and temperature, but it should be mentioned that they were obtained from the bench-scale and fluidized-bed, our results were from small-scale and fixed bed. Study of the effect of the S/C ratio on toluene steam reforming was performed for Ni/mayenite at 450 and 500 °C, we used 50 mg of Ni/mayenite mixed with SiC, then fixed in the reactor as a constant length (8 mm). The space-time is w cat/F toluene =8.9 kgcat·h·m−3 at 25 °C, and the steam flow rate was changed to achieve the desired S/C ratios. The results were shown in Table 3 . In the range of S/C ratios studied, from 1.2 to 4.7, an obvious influence on toluene conversion was observed. The conversion of toluene increased from 36.6 to 48.3% at 450 °C, and from 49.6% to 65.1 at 500 °C, thus it can be stated that high S/C ratio resulted in high toluene conversion. These results are in concordance with values reported by our previous study [21], but exhibited higher activity than Ni/mayenite (prepared by mixing mayenite with NiO without water). High S/C ratio usually is not encouraged in industrialization process, because excess steam in gas products will reduce the low heating value (LHV) of the fuel. In order to provide helpful information for implementing the industrialization of the process, the influence of S/C ratio on products yield and selectivity was studied at 800 °C, Table 4 . From the results, it was demonstrated that H2 and CO2 yields increased when the S/C ratio increased, but the CO yield decreased for S/C ratios from 1.1 to 6.0. During these experiments, S/C values matched with those applied in industrial applications. The results of the effect of the S/C ratio accord well with those reported by Bona [24]. Higher S/C ratio favors higher carbon conversion, results lower CO selectivity and CO2 selectivity. These gas yield evolutions with the S/C ratio can be explained by the reactions involved in toluene steam reforming (Eqs. (4)–(9)). Methane steam reforming is significantly affected by the S/C ratio, when this ratio has a value of 4.9, a very small amount of CH4 is produced. The increase in the CO selectivity at the same time less H2 and CO2 yield can be explained by the water–gas shift reaction. The water–gas shift reaction produces H2 and CO2 at high values of S/C ratios, a high steam partial pressure push the water gas shift equilibrium toward hydrogen formation. However, the partial pressure of the organic compound in the gas stream is lower due to the dilution as the S/C ratio rise. All of those affect the apparent kinetic constant, making the reaction rate constant in the S/C ratio. The influence of the space-time on toluene conversion and selectivity of carbon containing products at different temperatures are showed in Figs. 7 and 8 . Ni/Mayenite (4.7%, particle size 212–425 μm) was mixed with appropriated amount of silicon carbide (having the same particle size of the catalyst), then fixed in the reactor as a constant length (8 mm). The space time was varied by changing the amount of catalyst, at a given feed rate, with constant feed composition and temperature. A similar series of experiments was performed at different temperatures. The comparison of these results suggested that raising the temperature allowed reduction of space-time at the same conversion. For all temperatures studied, an increase in space-time led to an increase in CO2 selectivity and H2 yield, formed from CO because of the water gas shift reaction. The above discussion highlighted excellent steam reforming activities of Ni/mayenite above 600 °C. To confirm the practical applicability of the promising catalyst, long-term durability test was conducted at the condition of 800 °C, space time of 8.9 kgcat h m−3 at 25 °C and inlet toluene concentration of 2000 ppm, S/C=1.15 in 3 days. The results showed Ni/mayenite maintain more than 98% of toluene reforming for more than 76 h at 800 °C. Beside, the conversion exhibited no tendency to decrease over 76 h. The results also proved our previous experiment, which also showed no obvious deactivation over 60 h for NiO/mayenite [21]. The quantitative analysis of coke amount on the used catalyst was also performed with the TG analyzer. The sample (around 50 mg) was heated from 30 to 1000 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C·min−1 in air. The weight loss convincingly due to the combustion of deposited carbon less than 0.1% was observed. The possible reasons were analyzed in our previous study [21]. The “free” oxygen in the special structure of mayenite (Ca12Al14O33, Fig. 1) was regarded as the reasonable possibility. In the mayenite structure, 32 of the 33 oxygen anions are tightly bound, containing large cages, 1/6 of them being filled randomly by the remaining ‘free’ oxygen. So the oxygen radicals can be separated from the oxygen atoms the frameworks, and exist in the form of O2 −, O2 2 −. Ca12Al14O33 can be expressed as Ca12(Al14O32)O. The super oxide radicals in the cages will transfer to nickel site to gasify the surface carbon on nickel metal to CO, which is the reasonable explanation for the excellent performance of Ni/mayenite. The “theoretical” amount of O2 −, O2 2 − in mayenite is 1.17% wt. These ‘free’ oxygen structures during reductive (reaction with H2) and oxidative (reaction with O2) environment were measured by Raman Spectra, and shown in Fig. 9 , from which it can be justified that the “free” oxygen structures can combust with reductive material and also be restored after reaction with oxygen. The disappearing and restored behavior of molecular oxygen anions was also confirmed from TG measurements, reaction with hydrogen decreased the weights of mayenite, and reaction with oxygen increasing the weights [20]. In this study, heat and mass transfer resistances are absent. The reactor has been considered as plug flow without gas expansion (constant flow rate through it). The apparent rate constant and the apparent activation energy for toluene steam reforming can be expressed: (11) − r t = k C T n C H 2 O m . The before mentioned experimental results and literature data showed that there is an influence of water concentration on conversion of toluene. But in this condition, the disappearance rate of toluene (Eq. (11)) in a binary reaction with H2O can be expressed as a pseudo-order reaction with respect to only toluene concentration. And also the decomposition rate of tar can be assumed to the first-order, as widely accepted in the literature data [25,26], then the Eq. (11) can be expressed as: (12) − r t = k a p p C T Under plug flow conditions, the apparent rate constant can be calculated as: (13) k a p p = [ − ln ( 1 − X c ) ] t t = W v 0 . The temperature dependency according to Arrhenius's Law can be represented as: (14) k a p p = k 0 , a p p e − E a p p R T . To find the activation energy, experiments are performed in a temperature arrange of 400–600 °C with gas composition (toluene 2000 ppm diluted by Argon, S/C: 2.1). The effect of temperature on toluene conversion was shown in Fig. 10 , then apparent activation energy for toluene conversion over Ni/mayenite (4.7% wt.) was calculated from Fig. 11 and shown in Table 5 . These values are in concordance with values reported our previous report Ni/mayenite prepared by solid mixing method without water (82.06 kJ·mol−1) [21], and also Narvaez [26] for tar steam reforming on commercial nickel-based catalyst (BASF G1-25S), performed in air gasifying agent (72±12 kJ·mol−1), and 84±6 kJ·mol−1 over calcined dolomite. One new catalyst support for biomass tar steam reforming was developed, and showed excellent carbon formation resistance ability for activity ingredient — Ni metal because of the high oxygen restored property. Then new nickel-based catalyst based on the support (Ni/mayenite) was prepared, the steam reforming experiments were conducted using toluene as biomass tar model compound, to confirm that the new catalyst exhibited higher activity. The influence of the catalyst preparation parameters and operating parameters (reaction temperature, steam to carbon ratio and space time) on activity and selectivity was examined. The result confirmed that the new developed catalyst exhibited higher toluene reforming activity and long-time durability comparing to commercial-like catalysts: Ni/Ca x /MgO1−x and our previous sample, also exhibited superior resistance to carbon poisoning. The reason to account for such excellent property is the ‘free’ oxygen in the special structure. The Ni/mayenite catalyst is effective and relative cheap, which may be lead to reduction in the cost of hot gas cleaning process. The apparent rate constant and the apparent activation energy for toluene steam reforming over Ni/mayenite was calculated assuming a first order reaction with respect to toluene, activation energy of 80.24 kJ·mol−1 was coincident with literature data. The authors gratefully acknowledge the postdoctoral fellowship No. P08408 along with Grant-in-aid No.20-08408 awarded from Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS).
abstract: Mayenite (Ca12Al14O33 or 12CaO.7Al2O3) was previously developed and applied as Ni support for biomass tar steam reforming in the absence and presence of H2S by our group because of its high oxygen restoring property in the structure [C. Li et al., Appl. Catal., B. 2008]. In this study, catalyst Ni/mayenite (mayenite as support) was prepared by impregnation method with nickel nitrate hexahydrate. Experiments were tested in a fixed-bed reactor, toluene as a tar model compound. The influence of the catalyst preparation and operating parameters (reaction temperature, steam to carbon ratio and space time) on catalyst activity and products selectivity were studied, and a long-time evaluation (more than 76 h) also exhibited excellent resistance to coking. These results were compared to these obtained by commercial-like catalysts: Ni/CaO x /MgO1−x and our previous NiO/mayenite, showing that Ni/mayenite exhibited excellent property for biomass tar reforming, with higher H2 yield than that of Ni/CaO x /MgO1−x , and higher CO selectivity than that of NiO/mayenite. For kinetic model, the first order reaction used for toluene with activation energy of 80.24 kJ.mol−1 was coincident with literature data.
title: Development of new nickel based catalyst for biomass tar steam reforming producing H2-rich syngas

common_conditions.feed_or_medium: toluene (2000 ppm diluted by Argon), H2O
common_conditions.other: catalyst reduced by H2 (<5% diluted by Ar) for 1.5 h at 650°C before testing

full_text: The rapidly increasing global population and economic growth over the past few decades have resulted in a corresponding increased demand for electricity, transport and agricultural products based on fossil-fuels. The increased emission of greenhouse gases, mainly as carbon dioxide, derived from fossil-based industries has caused severe problems in environmental pollution and global warming. Within Thailand, the Royal Thai government recently proposed an Alternative Energy Development Plan (AEDP2015–2036) to focus on the development of a low-carbon society by promoting the use of renewable energy and resources, such as solar, wind, hydro, bio-energy and bio-fuels [1]. Among these, bio-fuels and especially biodiesel are potential candidates for widespread use as an alternative transport fuel owing to its biodegradable nature, sustainable renewability and high cetane number. Specifically, biodiesel has lower exhaust emissions (unburned hydrocarbons and particulate matters) than petroleum-based fuels, which is in accordance with the more earnest environmental emission regulations [2,3]. The commercial production of biodiesel is usually as fatty acid methyl esters (FAMEs), formed via the transesterification of vegetable oils with an excess amount of methanol and catalyzed by homogeneous alkali bases under a mild reaction condition (60–80 °C at atmospheric pressure) [4,5]. However, the resulting biodiesel usually has a poor oxidative stability and cold flow properties, limiting its application to being blended with petro-diesel. In general, fuel properties are associated with the structural features of the starting oil or fat, such as the chain length, degree of chain branching and the nature of individual FAME with various numbers of carbon double bonds (CC). Those biodiesels with a high content of polyunsaturated FAMEs have a low oxidative stability, whereas those containing a high content of saturated FAMEs have poor cold flow properties. To obtain a high-quality biodiesel with a balanced oxidation stability and cold flow property, upgraded biodiesel enriched in monounsaturated FAMEs is expected to be suitable for most practical applications, especially high blend fuels [5–7]. Partial hydrogenation of FAMEs in the presence of transition metal catalysts has been studied as a means to decrease the level of CC bonds within the fatty acid chains to form a limited level of saturated FAMEs [8,9] In addition to the degree of hydrogenation, the isomerization of cis-monounsaturated FAME to its thermodynamically stable trans-configuration during the hydrogenation has to be averted due to its higher crystallization point, which results in worse cold flow properties [9,10]. Recent studies have demonstrated that the supported palladium (Pd) catalysts are able to perform hydrogenation in the oil phase due to their high hydrogen adsorption capacity, and to provide a higher catalytic performance than other active metal catalysts, such as platinum, rhodium, rubidium and nickel [9,11–13]. Another considerable and widely studied strategy for improving the catalyst performance in the hydrogenation process is to develop outstanding catalyst supports. The preparation of a mesoporous supporting material with different acidity and pore structures has been investigated recently to further tune the size and surface electronic property of the supported metal catalyst. Mesoporous silica materials, especially SBA-15, are extremely attractive because of their excellent characteristics of a high surface area, high surface-to-volume ratio, highly-ordered porous framework, tunable and uniform mesoporous size, super hydrothermal stability and high capacity to functionalize its surface [14–16]. For example, SBA-15 with its high surface area and narrow pore size distribution contributed greatly to a high Pd dispersion [17], while the well dispersed metal nanoparticles (NPs) on the SBA-15 support promoted a higher affinity towards hydrogen (H2) molecules [16]. Nevertheless, the lack of an acidic environment on the pure siliceous SBA-15 limits its utilization in a variety of chemical applications. To overcome this problem, the incorporation of zirconium (Zr) into the SBA-15 framework (Zr-SBA-15) to create potential Lewis acid properties has been extensively applied in many acid-catalyzed reactions, such as oxidation, hydroxylation, transesterification and Fischer–Tropsch synthesis [18–21]. However, the Zr-SBA-15 material has not been broadly applied in the partial hydrogenation of biodiesel. Chen et al. [8] researched the performance of Pd catalysts with various mesoporous supports, SBA-15, Zr-SBA-15 (Zr/Si molar ratio = 0.05) and commercial γ-Al2O3, for upgrading the oxidation stability and cold flow properties of palm oil-derived biodiesel using a continuous fixed-bed reactor. The hydrogenation activity of Pd/SBA-15 and Pd/Zr-SBA-15 were quite high and analogous due to the ultra-small Pd NPs with electron-deficient surfaces. With the higher acidic environment of Pd/Zr-SBA-15, the level of trans-monounsaturated FAME produced was slightly greater than that with Pd/SBA-15. Accordingly, the outstanding features of a Zr-SBA-15 support together with its optimum level of acid properties makes it an interesting candidate for enhancing the performance of Pd catalysts in the partial hydrogenation of soybean oil (SO)-derived FAMEs (SO-FAMEs). In this study, the motivation was to improve the characteristics and acid properties of Zr-SBA-15 by Zr incorporation into the silica framework of SBA-15. The Zr-SBA-15 materials were prepared by direct hydrothermal synthesis using a Zr/Si molar ratio range from 0.01–0.11. The reactions were performed in a semi-batch reactor at 100 °C and a 4 bar H2 atmosphere. The hydrogenation activity was examined in terms of the turnover frequency (TOF), while the selectivity was observed in terms of the ratio of trans- to cis-monounsaturated FAMEs (trans-C18:1/cis-C18:1 ratio) at low and high conversion levels of diunsaturated FAMEs (C18:2). X-ray diffractometry (XRD), nitrogen (N2) adsorption-desorption, carbon monoxide (CO) pulse chemisorption, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), temperature programmed desorption of ammonia (NH3-TPD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were employed to characterize the crystallinity phases, textural and structural properties, metal dispersion and size, acid properties and chemical states of the catalysts. The chemical functional groups and FAME compositions of the feed SO-FAMEs and the partially hydrogenated (PH)-biodiesels were analyzed using attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) and gas chromatography with flame ionization detection (GC-FID), respectively. In particular, the fuel properties, including the oxidative stability and cold flow properties, were determined according to the standard test methods [22, 23]. A nonionic triblock copolymer Pluronic P123 (EO20PO70EO20, M w = 5800), which was utilized as an organic structure directing agent, was obtained from Aldrich. Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 98%) and ZrOCl2·8H2O, which were used as the silica source and the zirconium precursor, were purchased from Fluka and Acros, respectively. Hydrochloric acid (HCl) was bought from Quality Reagent Chemical (QRëC). Palladium ammonium chloride (Pd(NH3)4Cl2·xH2O), used as the Pd precursor, was acquired from N.E. Chemcat Corporation, Japan. The SO used as the starting material for transesterification, was bought from the Thai Vegetable Oil Co. Ltd. Methanol (analytical reagent grade) and potassium hydroxide (KOH; 85%) were purchased from RCI Labscan Limited, whilst n-heptane (analytical reagent grade) was obtained from Fisher Scientific. Other chemical reagents, including sodium sulphate anhydrous (Na2SO4; 99%) and sodium chloride (NaCl) were bought from Ajax Finechem Pty. Ltd. The parent SBA-15 sample was synthesized by a hydrothermal process as previously reported [24]. In a typical synthesis procedure, a solution of P123 (1 g), deionized water (40.6 g) and 2 M HCl solution (40 g) were prepared in a sealed bottle at 35 °C. After that, TEOS (2.1 g) was added dropwise into the prepared solution and maintained at 35 °C for 24 h to give a milky solution mixture with a molar ratio of 0.017 P123: 1.0 TEOS: 7.9 HCl: 221 H2O. This was then transferred into a 125-mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and aged at 100 °C for another 24 h. The dispersed particles were harvested by filtration, washed with a large amount of deionized water, dried at 100 °C overnight and then calcined in air at 500 °C for 12 h at a 1 °C/min heating rate. The Zr was incorporated into the synthesized SBA-15 by direct hydrothermal treatment as previously reported [25] and described in detail [26], using various molar ratios of Zr/Si (0.01 to 0.11). The Zr-incorporated SBA-15 supports were denoted as xZr-SBA-15, where x refers to the Zr/Si ratio. In this synthesis method, P123 (1 g), deionized water (40 g), NaCl (0.59 g) and a different amount of Zr precursor were firstly homogeneously mixed in a sealed bottle at 35 °C. TEOS (2.1 g) was then gradually dropped into the prepared solution mixture and stirred altogether at 35 °C for 24 h, forming a milky mixture with a molar ratio of 0.017 P123: 1.0 TEOS: 0.01–0.11 ZrOCl2.8H2O: 221 H2O: 1.0 NaCl. The mixture was transferred into the Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and aged at 90 °C for another 24 h. The white-colored particles was recovered by filtration, washed with a large amount of deionized water and subsequently dried at 50 °C for 16 h. Finally, the white-fine powder of xZr-SBA-15 material was acquired after calcination using the same heating condition as the Section 2.2.1. The Pd/SBA-15 and Pd/xZr-SBA-15 catalysts were prepared by incipient wetness impregnation using a Pd loading level of 1% by weight (wt%). The synthesized supports were first dried in an oven at 110 °C overnight to remove any residual adsorbed water. The aqueous solution containing the Pd precursor was then impregnated into the dried support under vacuum for 24 h, and the catalyst was then dehydrated using a vacuum oven at 110 °C for 6 h. To eliminate any adsorbed organic compounds, the catalyst was calcined at 300 °C for 3 h under an oxygen flow of 500 mL/min and afterwards for 2 h under a H2 gas flow of 100 mL/min. Crystallinity and composition of the crystalline phases of the catalysts were examined by XRD analysis. The small-angle X-ray diffraction patterns were recorded on a Rigaku TTRAX III with an angular 2θ-diffraction in the 0.5–3.0° range, whereas the wide-angle X-ray diffraction patterns were obtained using a Rigaku DMAX 2200 HV at a scan speed of 1°/min over a 2θ range of 10–80°. The Cu Kα radiation with a wavelength of 1.541 Å was utilized as the X-ray source, and was generated by a ceramic X-ray tube at 50 kV and 300 mA for the small-angle region, and at 40 kV and 30 mA for the wide-angle measurement. The analysis was performed by placing the finely-powdered samples onto glass holders, which were then set at the center of the diffractometer. Textural properties of the catalysts were examined using a Quantachrome Autosorb–1 MP surface area analyzer. Each catalyst sample was degassed at 250 °C for 12–16 h under a high vacuum condition (≤10 μm Hg/min) to remove any volatile species adsorbed on the sample surface. The specific surface area (SBET) was calculated by the multi-point Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method. The total pore volume (VTotal) was obtained from the adsorbed N2 after complete condensation at a relative pressure of about 0.995. The pore size distribution (DP) was calculated on the basis of desorption branches of nitrogen isotherms using the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) method. Metal dispersion and metal particle size were analyzed by CO pulse chemisorption using an Ohkura Riken R6015 instrument. The catalysts were placed in quartz tube and reduced under a H2 atmosphere at 300 °C for 4 h, then purged with helium (He) and cooled down to room temperature. Afterwards, the pretreated catalysts were pulsed by a 10% (v/v) CO in a He flow until no more CO was adsorbed. The metal dispersions were calculated using a CO/Pd stoichiometric ratio of 1. The micro-structure and micro-texture of catalyst samples were characterized by TEM using a JEOL JEM-2100 model instrument. Moreover, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was applied for elemental identification. Typically, tiny amounts of powder samples were dispersed in ethanol and then placed in an ultrasonic sonicator for 30 min. A drop of the sample solution was added onto a carbon-covered grid, dried at room temperature overnight and finally kept in a desiccator in order to evade any moisture. Surface acidic sites of catalysts were determined by NH3-TPD using a Thermo Finnigan TPDRO 1100 series instrument with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD). The catalyst samples were first pretreated with N2 at 300 °C for 1 h, cooled to room temperature and then purged with NH3 for another 30 min. The NH3-TPD patterns were collected under He at a flow rate of 30 mL/min by heating from 40 to 900 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C/min. Surface elements and chemical states of catalysts were identified by XPS, recorded on a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD photoelectron spectrometer equipped with a magnetic immersion lens and charge neutralizer. The analyses were performed under a high vacuum condition (≤5 × 10−7 Torr). A monochromatic Al Kα (hv = 1486.6 eV) source operated at 15 kV was employed as the X-ray radiation source. The recorded spectra were calibrated using the binding energy peak of C 1s at 284.6 eV. The SO (180 g) was added to a 500-mL three-necked round-bottom flask and heated to 60 °C. Methanol (61 g) and 1.8 g KOH (1 wt% compared to SO) were prepared by homogeneous mixing and then added into the heated SO (9:1 methanol: SO molar ratio) and maintained at this temperature for 1 h. The solution was then transferred into a separation funnel to allow phase separation of the glycerine and biodiesel overnight. The lower phase (glycerine) was removed and the upper phase (biodiesel) was extracted with distilled water at 60 °C several times in order to remove the excess methanol, KOH and possible soap. Finally, the obtained biodiesel (SO-FAMEs) was kept in a sealed-glass bottle containing anhydrous Na2SO4 to adsorb the residual water. Partial hydrogenation of the SO-FAMEs (from Section 2.5) to yield the PH-biodiesel was performed in a 300-mL stainless-steel semi-batch reactor at 100 °C and a H2 pressure of 4 bar. Firstly, the desired amount of reduced catalyst (0.5, 0.75 and 1 wt% compared to biodiesel) was put into the reactor containing 130.4 g of the SO-FAMEs. The reactor was then purged with N2 to eliminate any residual air, followed by H2 at a flow rate of 150 mL/min, maintaining the H2 pressure at 4 bar. To obtain a perfect mix between the feed SO-FAMEs, catalyst and H2, a stirrer was applied at around 500 rpm. The temperature was gradually increased to the set point, and subsequently the first hydrogenated SO-FAMEs was instantly collected. Subsequent biodiesel sampling was performed every 30 min during the 4-h reaction time. The chemical functional groups of the SO, the feed SO-FAMEs and PH-biodiesel products were analyzed by ATR-FTIR using a Thermo Scientific iD7 ATR accessory for the Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. A drop of liquid samples was placed onto the monolithic diamond crystal plate. The IR spectra were recorded in the range of 3500–650 cm−1 at a 4 cm−1 resolution with 64 scans. The fatty acid composition of the feed SO-FAMEs and the PH-biodiesel were determined using a Hewlett Packard 5890 Series II GC-FID equipped with an HP–88 (100 m × 0.25 mm × 0.2 μm) fused-silica capillary column with He as the carrier gas at a flow rate of 70 mL/min. Each sample (2 μL) was injected into the oven (155 °C) with an injector and detector temperature of 200 °C and 230 °C, respectively, with a split ratio of 75:1. After a 20-min isothermal period, the temperature was increased to 220 °C at 2 °C/min and held at 220 °C for 10 min. Oxidative stability of the feed SO-FAMEs and the PH-biodiesel products, corresponding to the induction period (IP; h), was measured by the Rancimat method according to EN 14214 [22] using a Metrohm 743 instrument. The feed SO-FAMEs and the PH-biodiesels (after the complete reaction) were examined in heating blocks at 110 °C under an air flow rate of 10 L/h. During this time, the volatile compounds, which were the byproducts of FAME degradation, were released and recorded by the conductivity cells. The infection point of the conductivity vs. time curve designates as the IP (h). The cold flow properties, used for determining the operating capability of fuels, are normally evaluated as the cloud point (CP) and pour point (PP). The CP, which is the temperature at which wax crystals start to cloud the fuel, was assessed following the ASTM D2500 protocol [23]. The PP, which is the lowest temperature at which a fuel loses its flow characteristics, was investigated according to ASTM D97 [23]. The tests were performed using a CPP 5Gs automated CP and PP analyzer. The SO-FAMEs and the PH-biodiesel samples were put into the test jar and the temperature was continuously decreased. These characteristics were reported on screen to one decimal place. The small-angle and wide-angle XRD patterns of the Pd/SBA-15 and Pd/xZr-SBA-15 catalysts are illustrated in Fig. 1 . In the small-angle region, three conspicuous peaks centered at 2θ = 0.90°, 1.56° and 1.80°, corresponding to the (100), (110) and (200) reflections, were observed in the XRD pattern of Pd/SBA-15, indicating the existence of well-ordered hexagonal arrays and the two-dimensional channel structures of p6mm symmetry [8,16,18,27]. For Pd/xZr-SBA-15, however, the intensity of all the diffraction peaks decreased and slightly shifted to a higher 2θ value, indicating a decrease in the ordered level of structure as well as in the mesoporous diameter. Consequently, the regular interspace distance between the walls of the mesoporous channels (d 100) and unit cell parameter (a 0) were also decreased (Table 1 ). As previously reported [26], the diminishing intensity of these peaks in all the synthesized xZr-SBA-15 supports was due to the increasing Zr/Si ratios, and so the existence of Pd did not destroy the structure of the support materials. For the wide-angle region, the characteristic peaks were determined according to ICDD reference standards, PDF-2/Release 2012 RDB. All diffraction profiles demonstrated a broad peak located at around 2θ of 22°, attributed to the typical peak of amorphous silicate materials (DB 00-001-0649) [8,18]. Meanwhile, no peak that correlated with Pd and/or Zr was detected, which was due to the high metal dispersion (low density) and/or small Pd or Zr NP size on the prepared supports [8,28,29]. To affirm the high Pd dispersion and the nano-sized Pd particles, CO pulse chemisorption analysis was utilized (see Section 3.1.3). The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution curves for the Pd/SBA-15 and Pd/xZr-SBA-15 catalysts are presented in Fig. 2 . The isotherms of the SBA-15 samples featured hysteresis loops with sharp adsorption and desorption branches, indicating a narrow mesopore size distribution. All samples exhibited representative type IV isotherms, according to the classification of IUPAC, with a well-defined hysteresis loop in a relative pressure (P/P 0 ) range of around 0.4–0.8, suggesting the presence of ordered mesopores in the frameworks with narrow pore size distribution [20,27,30]. These results are in accord with the XRD profiles. The alternation of the hysteresis loop shapes was distinctly noticed for the Pd supported on xZr-SBA-15 materials, which was due to the influence of the Zr incorporation rather than that of the Pd loading. The addition of Zr aided in the formation of P123 micelles in a self-generated acidic environment, resulting in the well-ordered Zr-SBA-15 materials. However, the increased Zr/Si molar ratio (>0.07) caused a distortion in the structure as well as a partial blocking of mesopore channels, leading to the decreased pore diameter distribution, which has been described previously [26]. The textural properties of the catalysts are summarized in Table 1. The impregnation of Pd on the synthesized supports resulted in a slight decrease in the SBET and VTotal in comparison with the pure supports (not shown but reported in [26]). Surprisingly, the DP of the catalysts was hardly altered from that of the SBA-15 and xZr-SBA-15 supports, accounting for the high dispersion level and diminutive size of the Pd NPs. As a result, it could be epitomized that the blockage of the pore mouths by the Pd particles was negligible. The Pd dispersion level (%) and particle sizes for all the catalysts are tabulated in Table 1. The Pd/SBA-15 catalyst clearly had the highest Pd dispersion level and smallest Pd NPs at 40% and 1.22 nm, respectively, due to having the largest support surface area [17,31,32]. When Zr was introduced into the synthesized materials, the Pd dispersion level was reduced 1.29- to 1.54-fold to around 26–31%, with correspondingly larger Pd NPs (1.58–1.88 nm) than in Pd/SBA-15. This was due to the lower surface area of the xZr-SBA-15 supports and/or metal-metal attraction [8,17,33,34]. These CO pulse chemisorption results were supported by the absence of the metal crystalline phases in the XRD patterns (Section 3.1.1) as well as the high surface area of catalysts obtained from N2 adsorption-desorption analysis (Section 3.1.2). The TEM photographs of the Pd/SBA-15 and Pd/xZr-SBA-15 catalysts are displayed in Fig. S1 in the Supporting Information (SI). It is important to note that the well-ordered mesoporous structures with long cylindrical channels of catalysts were well preserved after the metal impregnation [15,20], albeit part of the pore channels of the supports was destroyed with a high Zr/Si molar ratio, especially for the Pd/0.11Zr-SBA-15 catalyst. As clearly seen in these images, the pore walls (dark region) were broader as the Zr/Si molar ratio increased, while the pore channels (clear region) narrowed, indicating the decreased DP. In the same way as the XRD profiles and N2 adsorption-desorption results, the deterioration of the highly-ordered structure of the catalysts were clearly associated with a large Zr incorporation and not the Pd impregnation. Apart from the ordered channels, the appearance of tiny dark spots that were well-dispersed on the supports, which were the Pd NPs (active sites), was evidently noticed. To confirm the presence of Pd NPs, TEM-EDS analysis was applied (SI, Fig. 2S). The EDS spectra of all the catalysts except for Pd/SBA-15 showed the characteristic signals of Si, O, Pd, Zr and Cu, which were attributed to the silicate framework of the support, metal active sites, incorporated heteroatoms and the copper grid, respectively. Thus, the presence of Pd and Zr NPs was confirmed even though no diffraction peak was revealed in the XRD patterns. The NH3-TPD profiles of all the support materials and catalysts are displayed in Fig. 3 . As noticed, the distinct peak below 160 °C, which was assigned to the NH3 physisorption on the materials' surfaces, was observed for all the samples due to the NH3 adsorption at room temperature (~30 °C; Section 2.4) [25]. As increased the Zr/Si molar ratio, the tail of this peak was gradually increased in the desorption range of 160–300 °C, which was due to the formation of new peak correlated wih ZrOSi and/or ZrOH groups with weak to medium strength. This was also substantial evidence that the acidity of the xZr-SBA-15 was higher than that of the parent SBA-15. At temperature above 300 °C, a broad peak ascribed to the dehydration condensation of silanol functional group (SiOH) was evident in the SBA-15 support because of abundance of surface silanol. Nevertheless, this peak gradually became smaller as the Zr/Si molar ratio increased, due to its better condensation resulting from the salt effect of Zr4+ species on P123 micelles. In the case of Pd catalysts, the desorption profiles were analogous to that of their corresponding supports, especially for the presence of the peak at around 160–300 °C, indicating the existence of weak to medium acid sites. In accordance with the NH3-TPD results, it was evidently epitomized that the Pd/xZr-SBA-15 catalysts were more electron-deficient than the Pd/SBA-15. To measure the actual acid sites, the NH3-TPD profiles only in the range of 160–300 °C were determined. The density of acid sites for all the supports and catalysts are summarized in Table S1 (SI) and Table 1, respectively. In the case of the SBA-15 support, no acidity was detected due to the absence of acidic species. The density of acid sites was close to 0.04 mmol/g for 0.01Zr-SBA-15, and then increased to a maximum of 0.60 mmol/g as the Zr/Si molar ratio increased to 0.11. The substitution of the larger atomic size Zr4+ ions (0.084 nm) into the smaller atomic size of Si4+ ions (0.026 nm) in the framework caused the difference between the bond length of ZrOSi and SiOSi, resulting in the higher acidity [35]. When Pd was impregnated onto the synthesized SBA-15 and xZr-SBA-15 materials, the number of total acid sites of all the catalysts was slightly diminished compared to the pure synthesized supports, which was due to the location of Pd that covered the disclosed Zr species on the supports [36–38]. The surface elements and chemical states of the Pd catalysts were investigated by XPS. To examine the surface elements, the survey scan (wide-scan) of the XPS was applied. The interesting peaks of O 1s, Zr 3p and Pd 3d were visualized in most of the Pd catalysts (SI, Fig. S3), supporting the successful Zr incorporation and Pd impregnation. As for the Pd/SBA-15 and Pd/0.01Zr-SBA-15, the characteristic peak corresponding to Pd 3d and Zr 3p was hard to discover in the wide-scan spectra, presumably due to the well-dispersed and diminutive-sized metal NPs on the catalyst surface. However, the presence of these metals was justified by CO pulse chemisorption, TEM-EDS and high-resolution XPS (narrow-scan) analyses. For the other catalysts, it was noteworthy that the intensity of the Zr 3p signals increased as the Zr/Si molar ratio of the synthesized supports was increased. To more thoroughly examine the chemical states of the catalysts, narrow-scan XPS analysis was performed. The XPS spectra with deconvolution for the O 1s, Zr 3p and Pd 3d are illustrated in Figs. 4 and 5 , respectively. For the chemical states of O 1s, the XPS spectrum of Pd/SBA-15 (Fig. 4A) was curve-fitted into two identical peaks centered at a binding energy (BE) of 533.6 eV and 531.2 eV, which were attributed to the SiOSi (amorphous SiO2 support) and M–O (in this case PdO) bonds, respectively [39,40]. The latter peak arose from the charge-transfer process of the Pd metal and oxygen, contributing to the existence of an identical peak at a lower BE. As for the Pd/xZr-SBA-15 catalysts (Fig. 4B–G), another characteristic peak was clearly observed at a BE of around 531.3–532.7 eV, which was ascribed to the bridging oxygen atoms in the silica framework as SiOZr bonds [28,41,42], indicating the Zr incorporation into the silica frameworks. In addition, the identical M–O peak, could be assigned to both PdO and ZrO due to the presence of Zr species. The chemical states of Zr 3p and Pd 3d were also examined (Fig. 5), where the XPS spectrum of Pd/SBA-15 (Fig. 5A) obviously showed the two doublets of Pd 3d. As expected, the main doublets at a BE of around 335.6 eV and 340.9 eV were identified to Pd0 3d 5/2 and Pd0 3d 3/2, respectively, due to the fact that the catalysts were reduced prior to application in the partial hydrogenation. Meanwhile, the other doublets centered at 337.8 eV and 344.8 eV were assigned to PdO 3d 5/2 and PdO 3d 3/2, respectively, suggesting that the Pd NPs might be exposed to atmospheric oxygen during catalyst storage and/or preparation prior to characterization [43–45]. Noticeably, two other doublets of the distinctive Zr 3p peaks were also detected in the XPS spectra of all Pd/xZr-SBA-15 catalysts (Fig. 5B–G). The main doublets appeared at around 333.1–333.9 eV and 347.4–348.3 eV and were specified to Zr4+ ions in both the framework and as excess ZrO2 particles [46,47]. According to the literature [26], it could be epitomized that most of the Zr4+ ions were located in the silica framework rather than on the catalyst surfaces, albeit the amounts of ZrO2 clusters, which were placed nearby the superficial areas of xZr-SBA-15 materials, increased with increasing Zr/Si molar ratios. The other small doublets at a BE of about 329.0–329.6 eV and 344.3–345.4 eV were assigned to sub-oxide of Zr species, resulting from the reduction stage in the catalyst preparation (Section 2.3) [48]. The ATR-FTIR spectra of the commercial SO and the synthesized SO-FAMEs (from Section 2.5) in the range of 3500–650 cm−1 are depicted in Fig. 6a and b. The characteristic peaks of the ATR-FTIR spectra were identified with reference to previous reports [49–51]. For both SO and SO-FAMEs, no adsorption band was observed in the 3500–3000 cm−1 region associated with OH bonds, suggesting the absence of water molecules or unreacted methanol in these oil samples. Also, the band located at a wavenumber of 3008 cm−1 was ascribed to the CH stretching vibration of the cis-CC bond (alkene groups), indicating that the samples contained unsaturated FAMEs with a cis-configuration. The bands at 2922 cm−1 and 2853 cm−1 were detected in both the SO and SO-FAMEs and were ascribed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the CH alkane groups, respectively. More specifically, these two bands suggested the presence of methyl (CH3) or methylene groups in the ester chains of the SO-FAMEs. The strongest peak absorbed at 1741 cm−1 was attributed to CO groups with a stretching mode of vibration, indicating the appearance of esters in both the SO (glyceryl ester) and SO-FAMEs (methyl ester). Thus, conversion of triglycerides in the SO to FAMEs was achieved [49,51]. Asides those absorbance regions, the ATR-FTIR spectrum in the range of 1500–900 cm−1 is well known as the fingerprint region that corresponded to the actual chemical discrimination between SO and its biodiesel [49]. In this region, the vibration bands of the bending of CH3 and/or CH2 aliphatic groups were exhibited at 1460 cm−1 and 1377 cm−1 for SO as well as at 1460 cm−1, 1435 cm−1 and 1361 cm−1 for the SO-FAMEs. In particular, the peaks at 1377 cm−1 and 1435 cm−1 were assigned to the asymmetric stretching of OCH2 for the moiety of mono-, di- and triglycerides and asymmetric stretching of OCH3 for methyl ester, respectively. In addition, the absorbance bands related to the stretching vibrations of CO and COC for SO were revealed at 1236 cm−1 and 1098 cm−1, while they were located at 1244 cm−1, 1195 cm−1, 1169 cm−1, 1120 cm−1 and 1016 cm−1 in the SO-FAMEs. The characteristic peaks that appeared at 1169 cm−1 and 1016 cm−1 corresponded to the CO symmetric and anti-symmetric stretching vibrations, respectively. Importantly, the band located at 1196 cm−1 in the spectrum of the SO-FAMEs, attributed to O–CH3 stretching, confirmed the presence of FAMEs (biodiesel). The other two peaks at a wavelength of 966 cm−1 and 722 cm−1 were ascribed to the trans- and cis-configuration of CH, respectively. Noticeably, the peak at 966 cm−1 was very weak in the ATR-FTIR spectra of both SO and SO-FAMEs, which conformed well to the fact that the natural occurrence of the cis-isomer in oil is more favorable than that of the trans-isomer [52]. To determine the yield and type of FAMEs in the biodiesel derived from soybean oil, GC-FID analysis was then performed. The synthesized SO-FAMEs had a proximate analysis of 99.1% FAME components, including methyl linoleate (C18:2, 50.23%), cis-methyl oleate (cis-C18:1, 25.36%), methyl palmitate (C16:0, 11.60%), methyl linolenate (C18:3, 5.60%) and methyl stearate (C18:0, 3.91%). Most of the geometric isomers were in the cis-configuration and no trans-isomers were observed in the SO-FAMEs, consistent with the ATR-FTIR spectra results. A high degree of polyunsaturated FAMEs (C18:3 and C18:2) causes a substandard oxidative stability in the biodiesel owing to their higher autoxidation rate, which for C18:3 and C18:2 are 98-fold and 41-fold, respectively, higher than that for C18:1 [53]. Accordingly, it is required to decrease the level of unsaturated fatty acid chains without increasing the saturation level by partial hydrogenation of the FAMEs. The amount of catalyst was previously specified at only 1 wt% of the feed biodiesel, a level at which it could transform most polyunsaturated FAMEs to C18:1 in a limited reaction time of 4 h. In this present study, where a new type of support (SBA-15) was established that was expected to have a higher catalytic performance, the catalyst loading level was studied in the range of 0.5–1 wt% of the feed SO-FAMEs. The partial hydrogenation of SO-FAMEs was performed with stirring at 500 rpm for 4 h at 100 °C and 4 bar of H2 at a 150 mL/min flow rate. The alteration in the FAME compositions of the obtained PH-biodiesels, in terms of the C18:0, cis-C18:1, trans-C18:1, C18:2 and C18:3 levels, with different catalyst loading levels during a 4-h hydrogenation reaction are illustrated in Fig. 7 . Almost all of the C18:3 and C18:2 contents were rapidly diminished, with the cis-C18:1, trans-C18:1 and C18:0 levels being substantially increased in the 4-h reaction. That the level of C18:0 was minimal reflects the principle of the partial hydrogenation of unsaturation. The maximum cis-C18:1 level at all catalyst loading levels was produced at 2 h of the reaction and then decreased continuously thereafter leading to an accelerated rise in the C18:0 content. Likewise, the conversion of polyunsaturated FAMEs to the highest level of trans-C18:1 was evident at a reaction time of around 2.5–3 h, but afterwards deep hydrogenation was performed. Therefore, a 2-h reaction was appropriate as a compromise between a high catalytic conversion without increasing the C18:0 content but keeping the highest cis-C18:1 formation. These results were in good agreement with the ATR-FTIR spectra (Fig. 6), in which the absence and presence of vibration bands at 3008 cm−1 and 966 cm−1, corresponding to the alkene groups and trans-configuration of –CH3, respectively, were clearly observed after partial hydrogenation with different catalyst loading levels. Meanwhile, the reduction of the absorbance band at 722 cm−1, which was attributed to the cis-configuration, was perceived as well. To determine the suitable amount of Pd/SBA-15 catalyst in the reaction, a comparison of both the trans-C18:1 selectivity, estimated from the trans-C18:1/cis-C18:1 ratio, and the C18:2 conversion level in a 2-h hydrogenation reaction was evaluated (Fig. 8 ). As expected, at higher catalyst loading levels, a larger selectivity towards trans-C18:1 and a higher C18:2 conversion level were obtained [54]. Hence, the 0.75 wt% Pd/xZr-SBA-15 catalyst was selected for further application in this hydrogenation process, based upon a compromise between the catalyst selectivity and activity. From this result, it was epitomized that the addition of Pd onto the outstanding SBA-15 support enhanced the catalytic activity and cis-C18:1 formation within a 2-h reaction, allowing a smaller catalyst loading level and shorter reaction time than previously reported [5,55]. The Pd/xZr-SBA-15 catalysts were examined for their catalytic behaviors in the partial hydrogenation of the SO-FAMEs under the same conditions as that used for the Pd/SBA-15 catalyst (Section 3.3.1), except at a catalyst loading level and total reaction time of 0.75 wt% and 2 h, respectively. The hydrogenation activity of all the catalysts was considered in terms of the TOF, derived using Eq. (1), which when compared at the conversion levels of C18:2 were around 10% and 80%. (1) TOF h − 1 = Converted C 18 : 2 × Amount of biodiesel g × MW of C 18 : 2 − 1 metal loading × metal dispersion × W catalyst g × MW of metal − 1 ÷ time The alterations in the FAME composition at a low (10%) and high (80%) C18:2 conversion level are exhibited in Table 2 , while the TOF and trans-C18:1 selectivity at both conversion levels for all catalysts are shown in Fig. 9 . At a C18:2 conversion level of around 10%, the FAME composition in the PH-biodiesel was only slightly altered from that in the feed SO-FAMEs, where the C18:3 and C18:2 contents were diminished, and the amount of C18:1 was increased with only a limited level of C18:0 formation (Table 2). As noticed in Fig. 9, the lowest TOF (around 4279 h−1) was presented by Pd/SBA-15. The TOF values increased with increasing Zr/Si molar ratios of the synthesized supports up to 0.07, and then slightly decreased thereafter at higher Zr/Si molar ratios. With respect to the increased TOF, the major reason for this phenomenon was the change in the acidity of the supports through the incorporation of Zr atoms, leading to electron-deficient surfaces of both supports and Pd0 and so, as a result, the adsorption of H2 and unsaturated bonds (electron-rich) of FAME molecules on the catalysts was augmented leading to an enhanced hydrogenation of the adsorbed FAME molecules, as described by Scheme 1 [44,56,57]. However, the decreased TOF levels detected in catalysts with higher Zr levels (Pd/0.09Zr-SBA-15 and Pd/0.11Zr-SBA-15) may have resulted from the steric hindrance of the strongly adsorbed-reacted FAME molecules on the catalyst surface. Another possibility was the reduced textural properties (Table 1), especially the decreased DP, compared to the other catalysts, which would lead to a more difficult diffusion for the FAME molecules through the pore channels of the supports, in which the Pd0 active sites were located, and hence a longer reaction time than that for the Pd/0.07Zr-SBA-15 catalyst. Analogously, the reduction in the DP during the synthesis of the SBA-15 materials with high Zr loadings, which contributed to their lower catalytic conversion, was previously proposed by Yusoff and Abdullah [58]. At an 80% C18:2 conversion level, the levels of TOF for almost all the catalysts were lower than those in the corresponding 10% C18:2 conversion level, presumably due to the lower amount of both basic CC bonds in the FAME molecules as well as unoccupied Pd0 active sites of the catalyst. This could suggest that the influence of the electron-deficient catalyst surfaces might abate, and so the time required for obtaining a high C18:2 conversion level was quite long. As evident in Fig. 9, the trends in the TOF changes in most catalysts at an 80% C18:2 conversion level were similar to those at a 10% C18:2 conversion level. More specifically, a slight increase in TOF for the Pd/0.01Zr-SBA-15 and Pd/0.11Zr-SBA-15 catalysts was evident and the highest TOF level was acquired from the Pd/0.07Zr-SBA-15 catalyst. This phenomenon could be explained separately for each catalyst. For Pd/0.01Zr-SBA-15, the increase in TOF might arise from the gradual but continuous adsorption and desorption of FAME molecules on the catalyst surface, allowing access to other FAME molecules without any hindrance from the reacted FAME molecules. In contrast, the insignificant increase in TOF for Pd/0.11Zr-SBA-15, compared to the other catalysts at an 80% C18:2 conversion level, might result from the abundance of Zr sites on the catalyst that enhanced the adsorption possibility of unreacted unsaturated FAMEs. In spite of having the highest Zr loading level, its TOF was a little lower than that of Pd/0.07Zr-SBA-15, as previously described, suggesting that the approach of other FAME molecules was blocked by the adsorbed-reacted molecules located at the high electron-deficient surface of the Pd/0.11Zr-SBA-15 catalyst, together with the worse physical properties. In brief, the optimal physicochemical properties, i.e. the amount of Zr sites and fine textural properties, were the important factors to achieve a high TOF in this hydrogenation reaction. The trans-C18:1 selectivity at a 10% C18:2 conversion level was very small and equivalent (~0.10–0.12) for all the catalysts, which resulted from the low isomerization from cis-C18:1 to trans-C18:1 at the beginning of the reaction. In addition, it was specifically observed that the lowest trans-C18:1 selectivity (around 0.10) was found with the Pd/0.07Zr-SBA-15 catalyst. However, at an 80% C18:2 conversion level the trans-C18:1 selectivity was significantly larger than that at a 10% C18:2 conversion level, which was due to the fact that the trans-configuration is produced together with the hydrogenation reaction. The selectivity towards trans-C18:1 was insignificantly increased with increasing Zr/Si molar ratios of the support materials. The stronger adsorption of the basic CC bonds of FAME molecules onto the acid catalyst surfaces would result in a higher level of the trans-isomer [59]. The fuel properties (oxidative stability, CP and PP) of the feed SO-FAMEs and the PH-biodiesels are tabulated in Table 3 . The polyunsaturated FAMEs contents are important determinants in the oxidative stability of biodiesel. The feed SO-FAMEs had an abundant polyunsaturated composition and so an oxidative stability of only 2 h, which would be considered as a substandard biodiesel according to the European Standard (EN14214:2012) and Thai standard, by which liquid fuel has to resist oxidation for at least 8 h [60] and 10 h [52], respectively. The polyunsaturated FAME contents were reduced by the partial hydrogenation, leading to a better oxidative stability. With respect to the catalyst loading level compositions in a 4-h reaction, the amount of CC bonds in the FAME molecules was markedly hydrogenated to saturated, where higher catalyst loading levels gave larger proportions of C18:0 in the obtained PH-biodiesel, and consequently a better oxidative stability. Nevertheless, the cold flow properties of the PH-biodiesels were considerably worsened (CP of ~25–27 °C and PP of ~24–26 °C). With respect to the various xZr-SBA-15 supports, the reaction time was reduced to 2 h in order to decrease the level of C18:0 formation, in order to obtain a compromise between the oxidative stability and cold flow properties of the resultant PH-biodiesel. As described previously, the incorporation of Zr into the parent SBA-15 together with suitable physicochemical properties, which were provided by the 0.05Zr-SBA-15 and 0.07Zr-SBA-15 materials, promoted the acceleration of the basic FAME molecule adsorption on the Pd catalyst surface. As a result, the hydrogenation activity was improved and consequently enhanced the oxidative stability above that of Pd/SBA-15. In the case of the 2-h hydrogenation, the oxidative stability of the PH-biodiesels was decreased compared with the 4-h reaction, being in the range of 32–60 h (4- to 7-fold higher than the European standard), whilst the CP and PP were improved to around 6–10 °C and 3–6 °C, respectively. To apply these PH-biodiesels in cold climate countries, other techniques, such as the addition of chemical additives, blending with other diesel fuels and winterization, would have to be used to improve their cold flow properties [61,62]. The influence of the Pd/SBA-15 catalyst loading level (0.5, 0.75 and 1 wt%) was investigated in the partial hydrogenation of SO-FAMEs to improve the oxidative stability of the biodiesel. The highest saturated FAME content was produced with 1 wt% catalyst in a 4-h hydrogenation reaction, giving an excellent oxidative stability but a substantially worsened CP and PP. To compromise between these fuel properties, partial hydrogenation was then performed for 2 h with 0.75 wt% Pd/xZr-SBA-15 catalyst. The modification of the SBA-15 support by the incorporation of Zr atoms (Zr/Si molar ratios of 0.01–0.11) was also examined for their catalytic performance in this reaction. All the Pd/xZr-SBA-15 catalysts had a higher TOF at 10 and 80% C18:2 conversion levels compared to the Pd/SBA-15 catalyst. This was due to the physicochemical properties of the catalysts. The electron-deficient catalyst surfaces, ascribed to the existence of Zr species, caused an enhanced adsorption of H2 and basic unsaturated FAMEs, and consequently an improved hydrogenation activity. Nevertheless, increasing the Zr/Si molar ratio up to 0.07 resulted in a much stronger adsorption of reacted FAME molecules on the catalyst surface, leading to stearic hindrance of other FAME accessibility. Also, the fine textural characteristic, i.e. pore width, was the necessary determinant for providing the high catalytic performance. The decrease level of polyunsaturated FAMEs through their partial catalytic hydrogenation could markedly improve the oxidative stability of the PH-biodiesels, even though the CP and PP were inevitably worsened. The authors acknowledge the contributions and financial support of the following organizations: the 24th Year PPC Fund for Ph.D. Program; the Ratchadapisake Sompote Endowment Fund; and Center of Excellence for Petrochemical and Materials Technology, Chulalongkorn University for fellowship support to Ms. Chachchaya Thunyaratchatanon. This research was supported by the Government Budget (GRB_APS_49_59_63_08) and by Chulalongkorn University (CU-GES-60-04-63-03), Thailand. We are also much obliged to the Bangchak Corporation P.C.L., and Renewable Energy Laboratory, National Metal and Materials Technology Center (MTEC), Thailand for instrumental analysis support. Supplementary material Image 1 TEM micrographs and EDS spectra of all catalysts, total acid sites of pure SBA-15 and xZr-SBA-15 supports and wide-scan XPS spectra of catalysts. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fuproc.2017.02.007.
abstract: Partial hydrogenation of soybean oil (SO)-derived biodiesel, as fatty acid methyl esters (FAMEs), over mesoporous palladium (Pd)/SBA-15 materials was examined in a semi-batch reactor. The amount of Pd/SBA-15 catalyst was varied from 0.5–1% by weight (wt%) of the SO feedstock. The selectivity of saturated FAMEs (C18:0), which contribute to an excellent oxidative stability but undesirable cold flow properties, over the Pd/SBA-15 catalyst at different wt% was poor after a 4-h reaction period. To improve the catalytic performance, zirconium (Zr) species were incorporated in the SBA-15, resulting in Zr-SBA-15 with tunable acidity and pore properties. Partial hydrogenation of SO-FAME over 0.75 wt% Pd/xZr-SBA-15 catalysts (where x is the Zr/silicon (Si) molar ratio and ranged from 0.01 to 0.11) was performed for 2 h. The catalytic performance and the selectivity towards trans-monounsaturated FAMEs (trans-C18:1), in terms of the turnover frequency (TOF) and trans-C18:1/cis-C18:1 ratio, respectively, were considered at 10% and 80% conversion levels of diunsaturated FAMEs (C18:2). The incorporation of Zr into SBA-15 resulted in an enhanced TOF due to the greater adsorption of both H2 and the basic double bonds of FAME molecules on the electron-deficient catalyst surfaces. The TOF for the Pd/xZr-SBA-15 catalysts increased as the Zr/Si molar ratio increased up to 0.07, and then slightly dropped at higher Zr/Si molar ratios, presumably due to the hindrance of adsorbed-reacted FAME molecules with strong adsorption and the worsening of physical properties, i.e. pore width (DP). The selectivity towards trans-C18:1 was directly correlated with the Zr/Si molar ratio. Since the strong adsorption of FAME molecules took place on the catalyst surface, the high level of trans-C18:1 was consequently generated. The oxidative stability of the partially hydrogenated biodiesels was improved by more than four-fold compared to that of the starting SO-FAMEs.
title: Catalytic hydrogenation of soybean oil-derived fatty acid methyl esters over Pd supported on Zr-SBA-15 with various Zr loading levels for enhanced oxidative stability

reaction_catalog.transformation: partial hydrogenation of polyunsaturated FAMEs (C18:2, C18:3) to monounsaturated FAMEs (cis-C18:1, trans-C18:1) and saturated FAMEs (C18:0)

TEOS added dropwise into prepared solution
transferred into Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and aged
harvested by filtration and washed with deionized water
dried at 100°C overnight
calcined in air

transferred into Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and aged
recovered by filtration and washed with deionized water
dried at 50°C
calcined using same heating condition as parent SBA-15

aqueous solution containing Pd precursor impregnated into dried support under vacuum
dehydrated using vacuum oven
calcined under oxygen flow
reduced under H2 gas flow

lower phase (glycerine) removed, upper phase (biodiesel) extracted with distilled water several times
obtained biodiesel kept in sealed-glass bottle containing anhydrous Na2SO4 to adsorb residual water
