full_text: Data will be made available on request. Biomass, as a promising clean and renewable resource, can be converted into biofuels and value-added biochemicals [1–4]. Fast pyrolysis is an efficient method for converting lignocellulosic biomass into products that can be used as liquid fuels or feedstocks for chemical production [5–7]. However, the drawbacks of unprocessed bio-oil limit its high value utilization. When used as a fuel, raw bio-oil has drawbacks including high-water content, high oxygen concentration, low heating value, strong acidity and corrosiveness [8]. Moreover, when used as feedstock for high value-added chemicals, bio-oil has the problem of the complex composition and low content of each component, thus making it difficult to separate and extract. Unprocessed bio-oil comprises over 300 oxygen-contained compounds in various categories, such as acids, esters, alcohols, and phenols which requires further refinement. The refining methods of bio-oil mainly include emulsification, hydrogenation, catalytic cracking, and so on. Catalytic cracking is a refining process under normal pressure, mild temperature [9]. The catalytic cracking of bio-oil using zeolite molecular sieve catalysts can help decrease the oxygen content while improving selectivity for the bio-oil refining process, which is conducive to improving the quality and achieving high value utilization. HZSM-5, one typical type of zeolite catalyst, has been studied extensively. Agarwal et al. [10] synthesized different crystal size HZSM-5 catalysts and used these zeolites to upgrade Kraft lignin derived bio-oil. They found that the reduction in crystal size of these catalysts could improve the catalytic performance significantly. Among these catalysts, HZSM-5-375 had the best performance for its small crystal size and large acid site content. In order to further understand the reaction characteristics and mechanism of upgrading bio-oil and to achieve directional utilization. Relevant studies have been conducted on model compounds to represent small chain-like molecules or other oxygenated cyclic compounds in bio-oil. Chen et al. [11] studied the catalytic cracking of different model compounds of bio-oil over HZSM-5 by a self-designed fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) equipment to analyze the product distribution of typical components in the process of catalytic cracking of bio-oil. Among all model compounds they used in experiment, acetic acid, an abundant component in bio-oil, had the lowest carbon yield of liquid products and the highest carbon yield of olefins. Liu et al. [12] chose phenol, guaiacol, and other model compounds to demonstrate that electrolytic hydrogenation and deoxygenation can upgrade lignocellulosic biomass-derived oil with high efficiency. The reaction time of catalytic cracking is extremely short, so the intermediates are difficult to be detected experimentally. So far, density functional theory (DFT) has been a convenient approach to investigating the reaction mechanism of biomass and biomass-derived chemicals [13–15]. Lu et al. [13] combined DFT, ESP and experiments of fast pyrolysis to explore the phenolics-assisted hydrogen transfer mechanism for lignin pyrolysis. Chiu et al. [14] studied the hydrodeoxygenation (HDO) of guaiacol on Ru (0001) using a DFT method and get reaction pathways of the formation of benzene. Liu et al. [15] studied furan and acetaldehyde reactions over HZSM-5 with computational and experimental methods, and the result showed that the mixture of furan and aldehydes could help reduce the catalyst deactivation. The use of DFT calculations to study reaction mechanisms is beneficial to reveal the characteristics and mechanism of catalytic cracking of bio-oil. However, no comparison of HZSM-5 zeolite and its metal-modified catalyst is made from a computational aspect. It is helpful to better understand key intermediates on the catalyst surface and the synergistic effect of zeolite pore and acid site by choosing guaiacol and acetic acid as model compounds of crude bio-oil. Additionally, some insight on how to further design highly-efficient zeolite catalysts may be expected. The purpose of this research is to elucidate the bio-oil refining mechanism of catalytic cracking on zeolite catalysts. In this paper, guaiacol and acetic acid were selected as the modeling compounds of bio-oil, representing oxygenated aromatic with two important oxygenated functional groups and oxygenated chain-like small molecules, respectively. With a combination of micro-reactors experiments and DFT calculations, the catalytic cracking characteristics and mechanisms of guaiacol and acetic acid under the action of HZSM-5 catalyst and metal-modified Zn/HZSM-5 catalyst are illustrated with the reaction pathway and experiment results. All calculations based on the first-principles DFT calculations were completed by using the Dmol3 module [16,17]. Geometry optimizations of the reactants, products as well as bond dissociation energy, bond order, adsorption energy, and transition state search were performed with the generalized gradient corrected approximation (GGA) treated by the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) exchange-correlation potential with the double-numerical plus polarization (DNP) basis set. The catalytic cracking experiments of guaiacol and acetic acid each with zeolite molecular sieves were carried out in gas-solid phases, so the solvent model is not required in the calculation. In this work, BDE (bond dissociation energy) was used as the energy barrier to analyze the preliminary and subsequent reaction path of guaiacol since it is difficult to find the transition state of homolytic reaction. BDE equation is: (1) E B R − X = E R + E ZP R + E X + E ZP X − E RX − E ZP RX where, E B, E, and E ZP are bond dissociation energies corrected by zero–point vibration, total energy of molecular, and zero-point energy, respectively. RX represents the dimer while R and X represent the free radicals produced during the reaction. R–X represents the bond connecting R and X. The Mayer bond order can describe the relative strength of bonds in a molecule; the smaller the bond order, the more unstable the bond is and the more likely bond breakage occurs. In this work, bond orders are used to compare the changes in chemical bond strength before and after catalysis. Catalytic reactions begin with adsorption, where the reactants are chemisorbed on the catalyst surface before a catalytic reaction can occur. The adsorption energy is calculated as: (2) E ads = E ad + sub − E ad − E sub where, Eads is the adsorption energy, E (ad+sub) is the total energy of stable configuration after adsorption, E ad and E sub are the total energy of the adsorbate and adsorbent, respectively. If the adsorption energy is negative, it means that adsorption can occur. In this work, the adsorption energy was used to calculate the suitable adsorption sites of guaiacol and acetic acid on the surface of HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5 catalysts to provide stable adsorption configurations for subsequent catalytic reaction pathways calculations. HZSM-5 zeolite molecular sieve consists of microporous channels of SiO4 and AlO4, whose 10-ring channels along the b-axis of crystallography intersect with the 10-ring orthogonal channels along the a-axis, with the 10-ring channels meaning that the channels consist of 10 Si or Al atoms. The MFI topology is imported from the Materials Studio 8.0 database, and its basic structural unit consists of 12 Si/Al atoms with different spatial environments, labeled T1 ∼ T12. In the repeating structural unit, the 34 T cluster model is intercepted, which contains a complete straight and a complete sinusoidal channel to reflect the pore structure confinement effect [18–20]. Relevant studies have shown that the intersection of these two kinds of channels is the most likely position for the replacement of Al atoms with Si atoms in HZSM-5 [21,22], and after tetravalent Si takes place of trivalent Al, an H proton will balance the zeolite charge to make it electrically neutral [23]. This phenomenon results in the formation of Bronsted acid sites between the O-centered Si and Al atoms in the zeolite backbone, which makes HZSM-5 exhibit high catalytic activity. In order to prevent the destruction of the pore structure and the zeolite skeleton, the Al atoms and their coordination atoms are loosely optimized and the other atoms are fixed during the geometry optimization. The steps for constructing the computational model of the Zn/HZSM-5 molecular sieve catalyst are similar to those of HZSM-5, except that the Si atom at the intersection of the straight and sinusoidal channels is replaced with a Zn atom, and one H proton is added to each of the two O atoms connected to Zn to balance the charge. Fig. 1 shows the model diagrams of HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5 zeolite molecular sieve 34 T clusters. Guaiacol (>99%), acetic acid (>99%), and Zinc acetate dihydrate (>99%) were purchased from Aladdin Co. The catalyst HZSM-5 zeolite molecular sieve was purchased from Nankai University Catalyst Co. The catalysts were placed in a muffle furnace and calcined in an oxygen atmosphere at 600 °C for 6 h before the micro-reactor experiments. Zn/HZSM-5 catalyst was prepared with an ion-exchange method by placing HZSM-5 in 0.5 mol/L zinc acetate dihydrate solution, maintaining a liquid-solid mass ratio of 10:1 [24,25]. The mixed solution was refluxed for 8 h, followed by filtration, washing, and drying at 120 °C for 8 h. The above process was repeated twice to ensure sufficient ion exchange [26]. Then the catalyst was calcined at 550 °C for 4 h. The catalyst obtained after calcination is the Zn/HZSM-5 catalyst. Catalytic cracking experiments of guaiacol and acetic acid over HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5 were carried out in a micro-reactor, and a schematic diagram of the micro-reactor is shown in Fig. 2 . A certain mass of zeolite catalyst is fixed in the lower part of the reactor through quartz wool. Before the experiment started, the interior of the reactor was purged with 100 mL/min of N2. The initial temperature was 25 °C at room temperature, and the reaction temperature was 600 °C, and then kept constant. Guaiacol (acetic acid) was injected from the upper of the reactor through a microinjection pump, then vaporized into the catalyst bed with a mass air velocity of 11.2 /h. The feeding time was 20 min, and the reaction continued with N2 purging at 100 mL/min for another 10 min to finish. The liquid product was collected by a three-stage condenser with ethanol, which was placed in ice water, and the liquid product was volumized to 100 mL with ethanol. A gas bag was used to collect gas products for analysis. The experimental device in this research has the same configuration of the fixed bed reactor for the experiment for pyrolysis in other research [27], and the differences in the size of our device will be discussed in detail later. The catalysts before and after loading were characterized by BET and Py-IR to determine the differences in specific surface area, pore size distribution, and acid site distribution between them. The specific surface area and pore size distribution of zeolite catalysts were determined by nitrogen adsorption at −196 °C using a Micromeritics, ASAP 2020 specific surface and pore size analyzer. The acid site types (B acid and L acid) and corresponding acid amounts of each type were determined by using NicoletiS50 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Py-IR). The result of the characterization of the catalyst is shown in the supplementary materials. The liquid-phase and gas-phase products were analyzed by gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS) coupler (Agilent, model 7890A-5977B, USA) and GC-2014 gas chromatograph (SHIMADZU, Japan), respectively. Mass air velocity and selectivity were calculated as shown below: (3) Weight hourly space velocity WHSV = feeding rate mg / h catalysts mass mg (4) Selectivity s = peak area of special product peak area of all product × 100 % Fig. 3 shows the liquid product selectivity when guaiacol was pyrolyzed alone and when HZSM-5 or Zn/HZSM-5 was added. It can be seen that when guaiacol was pyrolyzed alone, the main liquid products were phenol compounds such as phenol, salicylaldehyde, o-cresol, and o-ethylphenol. Since the addition of HZSM-5 as the catalyst, non-phenolic products such as toluene and coumarone appeared with a selectivity of 4.38% and 2.66%, respectively, phenol selectivity increased from 20.34% to 30.89%. Salicylaldehyde, o-cresol, and o-ethylphenol selectivity decreased, indicating that the HZSM-5 catalyst promoted the removal of non-phenolic hydroxyl functional groups. When the catalyst was Zn/HZSM-5, a new product, benzene, appeared with a selectivity of 8.15%. The total yields of benzene and toluene increased from 0% for guaiacol pyrolysis alone to 1.56% for catalytic cracking with HZSM-5 and 2.19% for that with Zn/HZSM-5. The increase in the number of L acid site of the Zn/HZSM-5 promotes the aromatization rate and improves aromatics yield. Table 1 shows the yields of gas products and the percentage of the volume for the pyrolysis of guaiacol alone and the catalytic cracking over HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5. The main gas product of guaiacol pyrolysis was CO with a yield of 10.07%. When HZSM-5 was added, it promoted the removal of non-phenolic hydroxyl groups such as methyl groups on the side-chain of the benzene ring, so the selectivity of salicylaldehyde decreased significantly. A slight decrease in the selectivity of o-phenol and o-ethylphenol was detected, and the removal of side-chain methyl groups also increased the methane yield from 2.52% to 2.86%. When the catalyst was Zn/HZSM-5, the selectivity of o-cresol and o-ethylphenol decreased compared to the HZSM-5, and the methane yield increased from 2.86% to 3.02%. Total volume percentages of ethylene and butene decreased from 0.076% in HZSM-5 to 0.065% in Zn/HZSM-5. Additionally, the increase of CO in guaiacol gas products deserves further discussion, in other research about the experiment of guaiacol. Catechol, a main product in catalytic conversion, is a typical aromatic compound [27,28]. Its selectivity does not show in all three conditions of the experiment. With the same experimental temperature of 600 °C, about 20% yield of catechol is detected from the catalytic conversion of guaiacol over HZSM-5(25) [27]. But there are serval differences in detail, the inner diameter of the quartz tube is 20 mm, while in this research, the inner diameter is 5 mm. With respect to the operational condition, the WHSV is 11.2 h−1 in this paper, which is bigger than that of the early research. The bigger WHSV means the slower input of reactant, and the smaller inner diameter of the quartz tube, both of which may promote more complete pyrolysis and catalytic cracking, and thus increasing the yield of CO in the gas product. Based on the free radical reaction mechanism, the preliminary cleavage pathway of guaiacol is shown in Table 2 . The R5 path corresponds to the removal of H atom in methoxy, and R6 ∼ R9 paths correspond to the removal of the H atom at different positions on the benzene ring, respectively. According to the results of the BDE calculation, it can be seen that R4 has the smallest BDE of 249.44 kJ/mol, followed by R2 and R3 with 380.46 kJ/mol and 458.84 kJ/mol, respectively, which means the methyl group on the side-chain is the easiest one to be removed from guaiacol, followed by the H atom on the phenolic hydroxyl group of the side chain and the methoxy group on the benzene ring. BDE of CH bonds at different positions on the methoxy are approximately equal, both are smaller than the energy required for the removal of the H atom on the benzene ring. It is noteworthy that R1 which corresponds to the break of the hydroxyl group on the benzene ring, has the maximum BDE of 528.51 kJ/mol, which is greater than the energy required for the removal of the H atom on the benzene ring, so the phenolic hydroxyl group on the benzene ring is difficult to be removed. This result is consistent with the phenomenon that the products of guaiacol pyrolysis alone are all phenolics (Section 3.1). Combined with the results of the main products of guaiacol pyrolysis alone, the subsequent reaction pathways are calculated as well. Their reaction pathways are shown in Fig. 4 . And energy barriers of these reaction pathways could be found in supplementary materials, when the side-chain methyl group is removed, the remaining group has high activity and combines with the free H radical to produce catechol. Since this reaction occurred instantaneously, no transition state was searched. For the ring-opening reaction of catechol to produce olefin and CO, its energetic profile will be discussed later in Section 3.5. In another reaction pathway, hydrogen radical is coupled to the C atom where the OCH3 also locates in. And then the intermediate product converts to phenol via TS2 by de-methoxylation with an energy barrier of 120.04 kJ/mol. After the removal of the H atom from the hydroxyl group, the remaining active group undergoes intramolecular H transfer and rearrangement reactions, with a reaction energy barrier of 91.03 kJ/mol and 135.06 kJ/mol for TS4 and TS5, respectively. and then the removal of the H atom from the active group lead to the formation of salicylaldehyde via TS6 with the energy required for bond breaking of 106.99 kJ/mol. Additionally, since CH4 is detected in the gas product, the reaction pathway for the conversion of o-cresol and o-ethylphenol can be found by transition state search. The figure above and the figure of reaction energy barrier in supplementary materials have shown that the formation of o-cresol and o-ethylphenol undergoes a series of reactions with a relatively low energy barrier. Therefore, catechol, salicylaldehyde, and phenol are the main products of guaiacol pyrolysis, these products may be further decomposed or combined with free active groups to form o-cresol and o-ethylphenol under certain reaction conditions. (See Fig. 5.) Catalytic reactions begin with adsorption. In this section, a calculation for the adsorption energy of guaiacol on both catalysts is performed to provide a stable initial adsorption configuration for the subsequent reaction path. The active site is located at the intersection of the catalyst straight channel and sinusoidal channel, where the guaiacol molecule is placed to calculate the adsorption energy. The side-chain of the benzene ring in guaiacol contains a hydroxyl (–OH) functional group and a methoxy (–OCH3) functional group. The H proton at the acidic site of the catalyst is more aggressive than the electron-rich O atom. Adsorption energies of the O atom in the hydroxyl group and the O atom in the methoxy group when interacting with the H proton are calculated. Fig. 9 shows the adsorption configurations of guaiacol in the pore of HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5. Ads1 and ads2 correspond to the adsorption configurations when the O atom in hydroxyl and methoxy interact with the H proton in HZSM-5, respectively, while ads3 and ads4 are the adsorption configurations when interacting with the H proton in Zn/HZSM-5. The adsorption energy of ads1, ads2, ads3, and ads4 are −55.38 kJ/mol, −58.47 kJ/mol, −56.2 kJ/mol, and − 62.24 kJ/mol, respectively. Results show that ads2 and ads4 are stable adsorption configurations for guaiacol in HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5, respectively, i.e., the O atom in methoxy interacts more strongly with the H proton at the acidic site and the adsorption is more stable. Table 3 shows the bond lengths, bond angles, and Mulliken charge distribution patterns of guaiacol, before and after adsorption. As can be seen from the table, both the bond length increase and the bond angle decrease in Si-Oa-Al/Zn indicate a tendency of proton Ha transfer from the catalyst surface to O atoms in methoxy, making O atoms easier to protonation. In both zeolite skeletons, Ha has hydrogen bonding interaction with Ob and Oc atoms. The distance of Ob-Ha and Oc-Ha on the surface of HZSM-5 is 2.418 Å and 2.409 Å, respectively, before adsorption, then increase to 2.563 Å and 2.411 Å after adsorption. The distance of both on the surface of Zn/HZSM-5 increased from 2.520 Å and 2.466 Å to 2.534 Å and 2.529 Å, respectively. The results indicated that the adsorption weakens the hydrogen bonding interaction, and stronger hydrogen bonding between Ha and O atoms in guaiacol methoxide makes the adsorption more stable. The calculation result of Mulliken charge shows that the Ha charges at the active sites of both zeolites increased after adsorption, while the Oa atoms connected to the Ha atoms in the zeolite skeleton and O1 atoms in the methoxy of guaiacol decreased further in charge, which indicated that there is a tendency for Ha to break away from Oa, the effect of the protonation affect the C1-O1 bond connection in the methoxy. The charge of C1 atom decreased from 0.026e to −0.07 e and − 0.046 e, respectively, before adsorption. The charge changed from positive to negative values indicates a tendency for the break of C1-O1 bond. The calculation results of the above-mentioned adsorption energy, the bond length and other parameters show that there is a tendency for the Ha protons at the catalyst hydroxyl group to detach from the catalyst skeleton and bind to guaiacol. When guaiacol is pyrolyzed alone, it is easy to remove methyl or methoxy to generate phenolic compounds such as catechol and phenol. This section will explore the mechanism of the catalytic cracking of guaiacol over HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5. The bond order of the demethylation and de-methoxylation of guaiacol when pyrolyzed alone and adsorbed on HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5 catalysts were calculated, respectively, i.e., the bond order of the linkage bonds O1-C1 in Fig. 6 , based on the results of the bond order calculations, it can determine whether these two catalysts had a weakening effect on the linkage bonds, which in turn promoted the break of the bond. The bond order calculations showed that the bond order of guaiacol O1-C1 bond was 0.9068. When the catalyst is HZSM-5 or Zn/HZSM-5, the bond orders of O1-C1 reduced to 0.8879 and 0.8815, respectively, both catalysts reduce the O1-C1 bond order in some extent. Between them, the weakening effect of Zn/HZSM-5 is stronger. The free radicals generated by the removal of methyl and methoxy from guaiacol combine with H radicals to produce catechol and phenol. The reaction pathways and energy barriers for the generation of catechol and phenol from guaiacol over two catalysts are calculated by transition state to search and the results are shown in Fig. 6. The results show that when HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5 catalyzed the cracking of guaiacol, energy barriers of catechol generation reactions are 184.65 kJ/mol and 157.42 kJ/mol, respectively, which are lower than the BDE of the break of methyl of 249.44 kJ/mol for guaiacol pyrolysis alone. Fig. 7 shows the liquid product selectivity when acetic acid is pyrolyzed alone and when HZSM-5 or Zn/HZSM-5 is added. It can be seen that when no catalyst is involved in the reaction, most of the acetic acid is not decomposed and only a small amount of acetic acid is decomposed to form ethyl acetate and acetic anhydride. No aromatic ring structure is generated at this time. When the catalyst is HZSM-5, only a small amount of acetic acid was not decomposed. The catalyst promoted the decomposition of acetic acid, the selectivity of ethyl acetate increased, and unstable acetic acid is converted into ethyl acetate with more stable properties, which is conducive to improving the stability of the final product. Moreover, aromatic products like toluene and p-xylene appeared with a selectivity of 6.36% and 1.85%, respectively. When the catalyst was Zn/HZSM-5, the acetic acid was completely decomposed and the products are ethyl acetate and benzene, and the selectivity of benzene was 10.37%. Table 4 shows the distribution of acetic acid gas products in all three situations. When acetic acid pyrolysis alone, basically no gas products are generated because a large amount of acetic acid is not decomposed. When the catalyst was HZSM-5, the CO and CO2 yield was 11.87%, indicating that HZSM-5 effectively achieves the removal of oxygen-containing functional groups and the increase of total volume percentages of ethylene and butene. When the catalyst is Zn/HZSM-5, the CO and CO2 yield increased significantly to 59.18%, while the total volume percentages of ethylene and butene decreased. The carboxyl functional group in acetic acid contains a CO bond and a hydroxyl, the adsorption energy when the O atom in the CO bond and the O atom in the hydroxyl interacts with the H proton are calculated, respectively. Fig. 8 . shows the adsorption configurations of acetic acid in HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5. Ads1, ads2 correspond to the adsorption configurations when the O atom in CO bond and the O atom in hydroxyl interacts with the H proton in HZSM-5, respectively. Ads3 and ads4 are the adsorption configurations when both interact with the H proton in Zn/HZSM-5. The adsorption energy of ads1, ads2, ads3, and ads4 are −76.34 kJ/mol, −54.44 kJ/mol, −66.64 kJ/mol, and − 47.57 kJ/mol, respectively, the results showed that all the configurations achieved the adsorption of acetic acid in the catalyst pore structure, among which ads1 and ads3 are the stable adsorption configurations of acetic acid in the pore structure of HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5, respectively. That means the O atom in the CO bond interacts more strongly with the H proton at the acidic site, and the adsorption is more stable. Table 5 shows calculated results of the bond lengths, bond angles, and Mulliken charge distributions before and after the adsorption of acetic acid. The increase of Oa-Ha bond length and the decrease of Si-Oa-Al/Zn bond angle both indicate a tendency of Ha proton transfer from the catalyst surface to the carbonyl group, making the carbonyl group more easily to protonation. Before adsorption, the bond lengths of Ob-Ha and Oc-Ha on the surface of HZSM-5 are 2.418 Å and 2.409 Å, respectively, which increase to 2.443 Å and 2.507 Å after adsorption. While on the surface of Zn/HZSM-5 the bond lengths of both increase from 2.520 Å and 2.466 Å to 2.642 Å and 2.513 Å. It shows that the adsorption of acetic acid weakens the hydrogen bonding interaction between Ob and Oc, and the stronger hydrogen bonding interaction between O atoms in the CO bond in the acetic acid and Ha makes the adsorption more stable. From the Mulliken charge results, both catalysts' Ha charge increase after adsorption, while Oa and O1 charge further decrease and C1 charge increases, indicating that Ha had a tendency to break away from Oa and the effect of protonation affects the formation of C1 O1 bond. H1 atomic charge increases from 0.109 e to 0.157 e and 0.149 e, respectively, so H1 is more prone to electrophilic attack on electron-rich atoms. Jared et al. [29,30] gave the reaction path of acetic acid pyrolysis by quantum chemistry, their research showed that acetic acid generates vinyl ketone through a two-step decomposition pathway, and the first reaction step is the rate control step. Ketene is an important intermediate in carboxylic acid cracking reactions. Fig. 9 . shows the reaction pathway for the generation of vinyl ketones from acetic acid over the HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5. The reaction starts with adsorption, and this process is accompanied by the formation and weakening of hydrogen bonds. When a stable adsorption configuration is formed, O1 atom in the acetic acid is protonated by the Ha atom at the intersection of the straight and sinusoidal channels of the catalyst, the C1 O1 bond is protonated, and the Ha atom is transferred from the catalyst to the acetic acid to form hydroxyl. At this point, the H1 atoms in the acetic acid are transferred to the zeolite to form the bridge hydroxyl group, and the product of this step is 1,1-ethanediol. The reaction energy barriers are 138.09 kJ/mol and 128.46 kJ/mol over HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5, respectively, both barriers are lower than the reaction energy barrier of 283.83 kJ/mol for the formation of 1,1-ethanediol by intramolecular hydrogen transfer of acetic acid. Under the restriction, the transition state TS1 adsorption configuration within the pore channel of both catalysts is planar, this configuration makes the adsorption more stable. The second reaction step is the dehydration of 1,1-ethanediol to form the ethylene ketone intermediate, the atoms in the zeolite do not participate in this reaction step, and the reaction energy barriers are 164.91 kJ/mol and 145.61 kJ/mol for HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5, respectively. Their energy barriers are not significantly lower than those for the cleavage of acetic acid alone. Since the Bronsted acid site of HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5 can provide Ha protons to participate in the reaction, it makes the reaction rate control step shift to the dehydration reaction. Both reaction energy barriers of the reaction over Zn/HZSM-5 are lower than those of the HZSM-5, which promoted the reaction and the conversion of the alkenone intermediates. And since the energy barrier of dehydration of catalytic cracking is higher than that of the first step, it can be concluded that the use of zeolite catalyst changes the rate-determining step change to the second step. Many scholars have detected the presence of acetyl cations on the catalyst surface when catalytic pyrolysis of carboxylic acids was performed [31], and acetyl cations may be generated from alkenones. It is hypothesized that there is a reaction path of vinyl ketone in the catalyst pore as shown in Fig. 10 ., i.e., the vinyl ketone is first converted to acetyl cation under the action of the catalyst, and the acetyl group is further decomposed to form CO and methanol salt. When the reaction occurs, the vinyl ketone is first adsorbed at the acidic sites of HZSM-5 or Zn/HZSM-5 and subsequently protonated by the influence of H1 protons at acidic sites. The distances between H1 and C2 atoms decreased from 1.766 Å and 1.910 Å to 1.217 Å and 1.101 Å, respectively, indicating that the CH bond is formed during the protonation reaction and the vinyl ketones are converted to acetyl cations with protonation reaction energy barriers of 8.79 kJ/mol and 12.75 kJ/ mol, this step of the reaction is more likely to occur in the presence of both catalysts. Subsequently, the CC bond in the acetyl cation is broken, generating CO and methyl radicals. During the protonation, H1 atoms leave the zeolite skeleton leading to the vacancy at the skeleton intersection, and the distance between C2 and Oa atoms in the HZSM-5 catalyst is reduced from 2.522 Å to 1.490 Å by the effect of this vacancy, and the distance between these two atoms in Zn/HZSM-5 is reduced from 2.448 Å to 1.476 Å, which shows C2-Oa bond is formed. In this step, the reaction energy barrier is high, 304.50 kJ/mol and 349.57 kJ/mol for HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5, respectively. The reaction between ketene and methoxide can fully reflect the reaction characteristics between the CO bond and CC bond, its reaction path is shown in Fig. 11 . The reaction begins with the adsorption of vinyl ketone on the methanol salt surface, and the distance between C2-C3 was shortened from 4.140 Å and 4.261 Å for the adsorbed state of ads to 1.543 Å and 1.544 Å for intermediate product P1 under the action of HZSM-5-CH3 and Zn/HZSM-5-CH3, respectively, indicating the production of intermediate product propionyl cation. The energy barrier of this reaction is 163.27 kJ/mol and 151.19 kJ/mol, respectively. The detachment of the methyl cation leads to the vacancy at the acidic sites of the zeolite backbone, thus making propionyl cation unstable. Under the effect of both catalysts, the distances between C1 and C2 atoms increased from 1.381 Å and 1.369 Å for intermediate P1 to 3.433 Å and 3.343 Å for intermediate P2, respectively, and the increased bond lengths indicate that CC bonds are broken to form CO. Meanwhile, the distances between C2 and Oa are shortened from 2.871 Å and 2.747 Å in P1 to 1.541 Å and 1.497 Å in P2, respectively, and then form ethyl cation. The energy barriers of this step in the presence of HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5 with vacancy are 120.43 kJ/mol and 98.77 kJ/mol, respectively, much lower than those of acetyl cation decomposed to produce CO and methyl cations are 304.50 kJ/mol and 349.57 kJ/mol, respectively. Therefore, the main pathway of CO formation is the decomposition of propionyl cation. The ethyl cation generated in the previous step combined with the O atom in the zeolite skeleton to form the unstable intermediate product R3. The H atom in R3 is easily transferred to the zeolite skeleton to form the bridge hydroxyl group, the energy barriers of this step were 139.11 kJ/mol and 131.59 kJ/mol, respectively. The distances between H and Oa are shortened from 4.103 Å and 4.462 Å for the intermediate product R3 to 1.004 Å and 0.990 Å for the product P3, respectively, indicating the formation of bridge hydroxyl groups. The conversion from R3 to P3 is a reverse reaction process of ethylene protonation [32]. The reaction process of ethylene ketone and methanol salt is divided into three steps, firstly, they combine to form propionyl cation, then propionyl cation decomposes into CO and ethyl cation, and finally, the H atom in the ethyl cation is transferred to the zeolite skeleton to form a bridge hydroxyl group and generate ethylene at the same time. The energy barrier of these three reactions shows that Zn/HZSM-5 has a more obvious catalytic effect compared with HZSM-5. Based on the above computational and experimental results, the reaction mechanism of guaiacol and acetic acid pyrolysis and catalytic cracking over HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5 can be demonstrated. The structure-property relationship of the zeolites in bio-oil upgrading can be further understood, according to the bond order calculation result of guaiacol (Fig. 6), O1-C1 bond order is smaller than that of guaiacol pyrolysis alone. Moreover, the energy barrier of the catalytic cracking of guaiacol over HZSM-5 or Zn/HZSM-5 to produce catechol is lower than the BDE of methyl of guaiacol (Section 3.2.1). This result explains the reduced selectivity of non-phenolic hydroxyl contained products such as salicylaldehyde, o-cresol and o-ethylphenol in the case of catalytic cracking of guaiacol (Section 3.1). Compared with HZSM-5, the conversion of guaiacol to phenol and catechol was further promoted by Zn/HZSM-5, which indicates that the distribution of acidic sites in zeolite after Zn loading is more favorable for the reaction. For the reaction of aromatic compounds, recently, many scholars have pointed out that the aromatic structures in phenolic compounds will break to form inorganic gases and C1–C5 hydrocarbons, and the C1–C5 hydrocarbons are important intermediates in the decomposition of phenolic compounds to form aromatic hydrocarbons [27,33,34]. Fig. 12 shows the calculation result of the BDE of guaiacol, catechol and phenol, The calculation result of BDE shows that though the BDE required to break the phenolic hydroxyl group on the phenol is the lowest among the three phenolics, it is still as high as 506.53 kJ/mol, indicating that all phenolics are difficult to generate aromatics by removing phenolic hydroxyl groups. Fig. 13 shows the reaction pathway of the ring-opening of catechol. The catechol finally produces 1,3-butadiene and CO, the energy barriers of all reaction steps of the catechol ring-opening reaction are low, the highest energy barrier is 239.91 kJ/mol, which is much lower than the energies of 513.82 kJ/mol and 507.49 kJ/mol required for the removal of phenolic hydroxyl groups from catechol. Therefore, compared to the direct removal of the phenolic hydroxyl group, catechol is more likely to generate olefins and CO by ring-opening reactions. Yang et al. [33] carried out an experimental study of catalytic cracking of catechol over HZSM-5, and the experimental results showed that CO was the main gaseous product of catechol pyrolysis, while the products of the ring-opening reaction are olefins and CO. The experimental results pointed out that the C1 ∼ C5 hydrocarbons products are consumed in large quantities during the catalytic cracking process, indicating that the generated olefins enter the hydrocarbon pool in the catalyst pore, and then undergo oligomerization, cracking, cyclization and other reactions under the catalytic effect of acidic sites to finally produce aromatic hydrocarbons. As for the acetic acid, the energy barrier of intramolecular hydrogen transfer to 1,1-ethanediol is significantly reduced. During the protonation of ketene (Section 3.4.2), the energy barrier of the formation of methoxide is 304.50 kJ/mol and 349.57 kJ/mol for HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5 catalysts (Fig. 10), respectively. This indicates that the acetyl cation is difficult to decompose to form CO and methoxide, which will lead to the enrichment of acetyl cation and alkenone intermediates in the catalyst pore. What's more, previous computational studies show that the alkoxide exists on the zeolite [35,36], and the weak interaction between the methoxide and the bridge oxygen makes it difficult to be observed by experiment. However, a pervious experiment [37] showed that the products of the catalytic cracking process indicating that the ethylene generated by the reaction of ketene and methoxide will enter the hydrocarbon pool in the catalyst pore, and eventually produce aromatics (Section 3.4.4). Based on the above analysis, it's not difficult to find that the deoxygenation reaction of these two model compounds of bio-oil can be favored by HZSM-5 and metal-modified Zn/HZSM-5 catalysts. Proper acid strength and sites are important to improve the aromatic yield, and the Zn loading of HZSM-5 increased the number of Lewis acid sites from 69.66 μmol/g to 123.25 μmol/g (Supplementary materials). From the comparison of the experiment result of catalytic cracking of guaiacol, Zn/HZSM-5 further promoted the process of aromatization by helping the removal of non-phenolic hydroxyl groups and thus increased the selectivity of benzene in the liquid products. For the catalytic cracking of acetic acid over Zn/HZSM-5, since the selectivity of both toluene and p-xylene is 0, the catalyst after Zn loading makes the hydrocarbon pool in its pore structure different from HZSM-5 and changes the selectivity of aromatic hydrocarbon, indicating that Zn/HZSM-5 could effectively reduce the oxygen content in the carboxylic acid pyrolysis products, facilitate the olefin aromatization reaction. Though the calculation result in this work is uninvolved in subsequent reactions like oligomerization and cyclization, the current results of the experiment and theoretical calculation could still provide a conceptual guide for the catalytic cracking of the refinement for bio-oil. Further research is deserved for investigating the effect of hydrocarbon pool in zeolite catalyst to perfect the complete reaction path of cyclization and other reactions. In this paper, guaiacol and acetic acid were selected as model compounds for bio-oil, respectively, and the catalytic cracking characteristics and mechanisms of two typical bio-oil model compounds over HZSM-5 and metal-modified catalyst Zn/HZSM-5 were investigated by a combination of micro-reactor experiments and DFT calculations. The following results have been obtained: (1) Both HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5 can promote the removal of non-phenolic hydroxyl functional groups from guaiacol. The computational results are consistent with the experimental results, which suggested that the use of zeolites reduced the selectivity of products containing non-phenolic hydroxyl functional group. (2) Both catalysts could promote the conversion of acetic acid to vinyl ketone and calculation results indicating that the decomposition of propionyl cations is the main pathway for the generation of ethylene and CO. Zn/HZSM-5 is more effective in promoting intramolecular hydrogen transfer, which is consistent with the results shown in the experiment. (3) The loading of Zn increased the number of L acid sites, and thus increased the yield of aromatic compounds in the catalytic cracking of guaiacol and acetic acid. The catalyst with Zn loading facilitates the aromatization of phenolic or chain-like small molecule substances. Both HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5 can promote the removal of non-phenolic hydroxyl functional groups from guaiacol. The computational results are consistent with the experimental results, which suggested that the use of zeolites reduced the selectivity of products containing non-phenolic hydroxyl functional group. Both catalysts could promote the conversion of acetic acid to vinyl ketone and calculation results indicating that the decomposition of propionyl cations is the main pathway for the generation of ethylene and CO. Zn/HZSM-5 is more effective in promoting intramolecular hydrogen transfer, which is consistent with the results shown in the experiment. The loading of Zn increased the number of L acid sites, and thus increased the yield of aromatic compounds in the catalytic cracking of guaiacol and acetic acid. The catalyst with Zn loading facilitates the aromatization of phenolic or chain-like small molecule substances. Huiyan Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision. Chenjun Yang: Investigation, Data Curation, Writing-Origin Draft. Yujie Tao: Writing-Review & Editing. Min Chen: Formal Analysis, Data Curation, Investigation. Rui Xiao: Conceptualization, Writing-Review & Editing.
abstract: The unstable oxygenates of raw bio-oil limit its high-value utilization, which renders the refinement process necessary. Catalytic cracking is one of the promising refining methods and HZSM-5 was found to be an effective catalyst. To investigate the reaction mechanism of catalytic cracking of the oxygenated components in bio-oil, guaiacol and acetic acid, are employed for the catalytic conversion over HZSM-5 and metal modified zeolite Zn/HZSM-5 in a self-designed micro-reactor. Herein, the mechanism of pyrolysis and catalytic cracking was calculated by density functional theory (DFT). For guaiacol, the selectivity of salicylaldehyde decreased from 57.41% to 41.84% and 40.31% over HZSM-5 and Zn/HZSM-5, respectively. In the catalytic cracking over Zn/HZSM-5, the yield of aromatic hydrocarbon increased. More specifically, benzene appeared in the liquid product. For acetic acid, the removal of the oxy-functional group was further promoted by Zn/HZSM-5, the as-formed ketene intermediate continues to react with zeolite catalysts, generating acetyl cation, and finally formed CO and ethylene. Zn/HZSM-5 has a better effect on promoting the reaction of ketene and methoxide since the energy barrier of the rate-determining step in this reaction decreased from 163.27 kJ/mol to 151.19 kJ/mol. Demethoxylation and dehydration were considered to be favored by Lewis acid sites.
title: Catalytic cracking of model compounds of bio-oil: Characteristics and mechanism research on guaiacol and acetic acid
