full_text: Rapidly growing global population (~7.89 billion in the year 2021) and better living standards are leading to an increase in demand for energy in various sectors like transport, agriculture, as well as power generating industries [1,2]. As per the report of the Government of India's Petroleum Planning and Analysis Agency, high-speed diesel consumption will be about 40% high among petroleum products and will further increase by leaps and bounds [3]. Challenges like fluctuating crude prices, environmental problems, and a noticeable reduction in conventional energy reserves are a big concern. This calls for an intensified research interest in the area of an alternative, sustainable and renewable fuel which needs to be energy-efficient, affordable, and has identical properties to that of petro-diesel [4]. Recently, biodiesel being renewable, non-toxic, environment-friendly, and biodegradable in nature has attracted enormous attention among researchers across the globe as an alternative to petro-diesel. Animal fats and vegetable oils are the chief feedstocks for biodiesel production via transesterification [5]. Glycerol is a byproduct of transesterification method accounting for 10% (mass basis) of the biodiesel production found numerous applications in personal care, pharma, food and cosmetic industries as well as used lubricant, plasticizer [6]. According to India's national Biofuels-2018 policy, a 5% biodiesel blending in petro-diesel is targeted by the year 2030 [7]. However, it has achieved only 0.14% blending of biodiesel till the year 2019 [8]. This motivates the need for increasing the production of biodiesel to meet the desired expectations. The consumption of first-generation feedstock which comprises the non-feasible option of edible oils leads to global food insecurity [9]. Thus, the trend has shifted towards the utilization of waste resources as an optional feedstock by which non-edible vegetation-based oils are selected as second-generation feedstock [5]. The biodiesel produced from second-generation feedstock is a promising option as the physicochemical properties of the product obtained are similar to that of petro-diesel [10]. Availability and the cost of potential feedstock are the main constraints to increase biodiesel production. Across the globe, a huge volume of wastes from cooking oil (WCO) is generated during food preparations every year with the largest WCO being produced by countries such as Europe (4 Mt) and USA (0.75 Mt) [11]. In year 2019, 0.065 Mt. of WCO was used for the production of biodiesel in India [12]. According to the Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI), around 2.2 Mt. of WCO can be recovered for the production of biodiesel by the year 2022 [13]. This huge resource of WCO can be utilized to achieve the target of 5% blending by 2030 [14]. Even though WCO is available in abundance, the presence of water and other metal impurities (sodium in particular added to food can create rapid chemical deactivation of the catalyst) are one of the major challenges in using WCO as a feedstock for biodiesel production [4]. Furthermore, the biodiesel produced from WCO has poor oxidation stability (5.8 h), high kinematic viscosity (4.54 cSt at 38 °C), and poor cold flow properties [high cloud (−8 °C) and pour point (−11 °C)] in comparison to petro-diesel because of the presence of saturated and unsaturated fatty acid esters [15]. The increased concentration of linoleic and linolenic acids majorly contributes to a rise in oxidation stability [16]. Cold flow properties are affected by the type of alcohol used in the transesterification process [17]. Improvement in physicochemical properties is an important key area in the biodiesel production industry [4]. Thus, to enhance the physicochemical properties and overcome the challenges mentioned above, a blending of oils using another appropriate potential oil prior to transesterification has been recently reported by researchers as shown in Table 1 and Table A (i-iii) of Annex A. Potential feedstocks available in India for biodiesel production are Jatropha curcas [18], Madhuca longifolia [19], Pongamia pinnata [20], and Ricinus communis [21]. Among them, castor oil (R. communis) has garnered greater attention as this non – edible oil crop grows in abundance (about 12,600 km2 cultivation) under diverse weather conditions worldwide [22]. Globally, around 1.8 Mt. of castor oil is produced per year [23]. India produced 1.84 Mt. of castor seeds in the year 2019–20 and is globally leading in castor seed and oil production [24]. It is also a front runner in the international market in castor oil business [25]. Castor seed production in India is likely to grow in the forthcoming years [24]. Castor oil cannot be consumed directly by humans and cattle as it contains poisonous toxins like ricin, ricinine, and other allergens [26]. Nevertheless, castor oil has wide applications such as lubrication, coating and is used in plastics, other polymers, and cosmetics [27]. However, in contrast to other oils, castor oil has major challenges because of its seven-time higher viscosity and its affinity to water. Published studies showed that the biodiesel produced from castor oil has a viscosity in the range of 13.4–18.6 cSt which is quite higher for a fuel as per ASTM standards [28,29]. This high viscosity is due to the higher (~85–90%) fraction of ricinoleic acid present in castor oil [30] (refer to Table A of Annex A). Since the availability of castor oil is restricted to specific regions and has other vital applications, the whole of castor oil cannot be utilized for biodiesel production. Moreover, a large amount of feedstock is required to achieve at least a 1% blending target with petro-diesel. In 2018–19, ~70 Mt. of biodiesel was procured by various oil marketing companies in India, and yet, the target was not been achieved [31]. Thus, to cope with the requirement of such a high amount of feedstock for biodiesel production, blending WCO with castor oil in an appropriate proportion seems to be the most effective and viable option at present. It will help in (i) solving the problem of depending on the availability of feedstock to a certain extent, (ii) reducing the overall cost of biodiesel production using raw castor oil (RCO), and (iii) improving the physicochemical properties of the produced biodiesel. It is seen that for the blends of castor oil and waste fish oil, physicochemical properties such as viscosity reduced from 205.11 mm2/s to 39.88 mm2/s, density from 0.9983 g/mL to 0.9514 g/mL, and acid value from 0.8 to 0.51 mg KOH /g in comparison to parent oils [32] (refer to Table A of Annex A). A reduction in physicochemical property, especially the viscosity of blended castor oil biodiesel from 15.54 mm2/s to 4.59 mm2/s is also reported in literature [33]. Furthermore, improvements in physicochemical properties of the blended oil biodiesel such as oxidation stability (114.2 h) [34] and calorific value (41.13) MJ/kg [4] are also reported. Apart from the low-cost feedstock used for blending, biodiesel production technology is equally imperative to reduce the overall cost of the product. When the conventional stirring technique is used longer reaction times are needed for biodiesel production (as heat is transferred via conduction in oil from the hot plate) and also an extended duration of product separation and purification is required [35]. The rate of transesterification depends on the heat transfer effectiveness which is difficult to achieve in the conventional stirring technique. Literature summary of biodiesel produced from blended oils using the conventional stirring technique is given in Table A (i) of Annex A (provided in supplementary data). Although biodiesel produced from blended oil has shown improvement in physicochemical properties, it is observed that this technique consumes excess reaction reagents like methanol/oil ratio (8:1 to 12:1), catalyst amount (1 w/w %), and even temperature (60 °C). Other major drawbacks are the longer reaction time (about 30–90 min) and product separation time (about 8–12 h), which make it a high energy-consuming process. The disadvantage in convectional technique of mechanical stirring method (MS) is that it depicts sluggish and ineffective heating at elevated operational conditions of stirring speed and temperature that leads to the consumption of excess energy and consequently increases the biodiesel production cost [36] and produces lower biodiesel yields,. In order to overcome these limitations associated with conventional method, process intensification (PI) techniques are used with added advantages such as better performance, higher yield, reduction in reaction time, post processing ease as compared to the conventional procedure [37]. Many researchers have reported the use of (PI) techniques (membrane reactor [38], reactive distillation [39], hydrodynamic cavitation [40], ultrasonic cavitation [41,42], microwave irradiation [43,44]) for biodiesel production. Individually microwave irradiation technique has the excellent heating capability, whereas ultra-sonication removes the immiscibility limitation between reactants. Table A (ii-iii) shows literature summary of the independent uses of microwave and ultrasound irradiation techniques for the transesterification of blended oils (refer to Annex A of supplementary data). Individually, these PI techniques are effective in biodiesel production but the power applied (W) for the preparation of biodiesel is higher [i.e. ultrasound (500 W) [43] and microwave (850 W) [44]]. The application of the combined PI techniques can effectively maximize the benefits in contrast to the individual techniques. The studies shown in Table 1 demonstrate the application of simultaneous MW + US irradiations results in a synergistic effect that enhances the biodiesel yield. The technique requires a shorter reaction time which results in energy and cost savings [45,46]. It is worth noting that the production of biodiesel imposes stricter norms for not only efficiency and selectivity, but also for production time, safety, cost, sturdiness, and a positive impact on the environment [45]. The application of microwave and ultrasound irradiation for biodiesel production using WCO has been reported in literature [47,48], whereas in the case of castor oil there is a limited number of studies reported using these techniques [49]. The main reason could be the high viscosity of oil, which restricts the operation of these individual techniques. Although literature (refer to Table 1) shows the successful application of the conjoint MW + US techniques, but they have never been applied for WCO and castor seeds [29,45,50]. Also the application of the combined MW + US effect on blended oils for biodiesel production has not been explored extensively till date. Thus, for WCO and castor as blended oils, this combined technique is expected to be promising, efficient, and environmentally acceptable for biodiesel production. In general, the results from Table A (i-iii) indicate that the blending of raw oils makes them the most suitable feedstock for biodiesel production (refer Table A of Annexure A). A blending of oils improves the physicochemical properties of the blended oil feedstock and the biodiesel produced, and this is one of the most important criteria required for the commercial application of biodiesel. Also, as per Table A (i-iii) in all the reported studies the ‘blended oil ratio’ is considered as a fixed variable and selected arbitrarily for transesterification. KOH as a catalyst is prominently used in the studies shown in Table 1 and Table A (i-iii) because the reagent mixture of KOH-methanol/oil leads to strong polarity which increases the efficiency of the heating medium whereby the biodiesel yield also increases. The application of statistical optimization provides a holistic assessment of the process which has been reported in limited studies [4,44,51]. Though, kinetic analysis for blended oil systems using a conventional stirring process has been reported in the trace, the same has not been explored so far using an MW + US process [33]. Response surface methodology (RSM) is an established tool to inspect the effects of the different independent variables on the dependent variables to determine the optimum process conditions. Unlike the conventional experimental procedures, RSM minimizes the number of experiments needed, reduces the time, saves material, and cost of a process [52]. The Box-Behnken-based experimental design procedure is a verified technique to determine the influence of the process variables on biodiesel yield and it is adopted in various published articles [44,53–55]. The novelty of the present experimental work is that for the first time conjoint MW + US reactor via the appropriation of the oil mixture of RCO and WCO is applied to produce biodiesel. Being a significant process variable, the influence of the blended oil ratio on biodiesel yield is studied in detail for the first time. The prime objectives of this work are: (a) to study the application of the conjoint effect of MW + US to optimize biodiesel yield using a wide range of blend ratios of RCO and WCO blend, (b) optimization of process variables (which includes a blended oil ratio as a unique process variable along with methanol/oil ratio, KOH loading, reaction time) for transesterification reaction using Box-Behnken based experimental design procedure and the determination of kinetic rate parameters, and c) quantitative improvement in physicochemical properties of biodiesel produced by blending of oils. Moreover, utilizing the analysis of variance (ANOVA) the reliability of model prediction was verified. The transesterification results obtained via the conjoint effect of MW + US were compared with the conventional stirring technique as well as an individual microwave and ultrasound irradiation. Kinetic modeling was performed and the rate of the kinetic parameters of the MW + US system was estimated along with the energy analysis. The physicochemical properties of the biodiesel were determined and discussed in detail. WCO was obtained from a local cafeteria in Gandhinagar and RCO was collected from a local market in Ahmedabad, Gujarat. Methanol (99% pure, AR. grade) was procured from M/s Merck India; potassium hydroxide tablets (99% pure, AR. grade) from M/s Fisher Scientific, India, and n-Heptane & n-Hexane (≥ 99.5% pure, GC Grade) and FAME standard (viz. palmitic acid ester, linolenic acid ester, stearic acid ester and oleic acid ester) from M/s. Sigma Aldrich, India for GC method. Three different blended oil ratios of RCO and WCO were prepared on weight basis (viz. 50RCO50WCO: 50% RCO + 50% WCO; 70RCO30WCO: 70% RCO + 30% WCO, and 90RCO10WCO: 90% RCO + 10% WCO; v/v) to examine the blending effects on the fatty acid profile as well as for biodiesel preparation. The homogeneity of the prepared blend mixtures was verified by keeping them at room temperature for 24 h and it was found to be physically stable (refer to Fig. A1 in Annexure-A1). Blended oil properties including the moisture content was measured by Karl Fisher titrator (model: μAquaCal10; M/s Analab, Vadodara); density at 25 °C determined using digital density meter (model: DMA 4500 M; M/s Anton Paar, Germany); kinematic viscosity according to ASTM standard D445 was found with Brookfield viscometer (model: LVDV2T; M/s Brookfield USA) at 40 °C, the acid value was assessed via potentiometric titration (model: 877 Titrino Plus; M/s Metrohm, Switzerland). (Refer to Table B in Annexure–A2 for accuracy detail). Individual oil compositions and their blends were determined using Gas Chromatography (GC) - Mass Spectrometry (MS) (Model: Agilent 5975C; M/s Agilent Technologies, USA) connected to a flame ionization detector (FID) using an HP-5MS capillary column. The acid value was found to be within permissible limits (< 4 mg KOH/g) (refer to Table 3 for acid values of RCO, WCO, and their blends). Thus, they were considered for a one-step process for biodiesel conversion. The experimental design was performed using, Design-Expert® software (version 10) for RSM supported with Box-Behnken design. In Table 2 , the process variable levels given for the combined MW + US reactor are selected based on the initial laboratory experiments performed. Similar conditions are reported in literature as mentioned in Table 1 for the conjoint microwave + ultrasound technique. As per literature methanol/oil ratio (A) in the range of 6 to 10; KOH loading (B), w/w%: from 0.3 to 0.8 [45], reaction time (C), min: of 10 min was found to be sufficient to perform transesterification reaction [46]. Blended oil mixture ratio (D) in the range of 50:50 to 70:30 was used for experimentation [4,33]. Based on these analyses, three levels for each variable were decided as shown in Table 2. The reaction temperature was fixed at 50 °C [50,70] for all runs since it is observed from previous studies [11,41] that 50 °C is the ideal temperature for such reactions. The Box-Behnken procedure was used to examine individual and interactive responses of the process variables on yield. This design uses fewer experimental runs and generates higher-order response surfaces [52]. Transesterification reaction was performed in a borosil glass flask (250 mL) using a hybrid reactor (MW-ER-02; Lab-Kits, China) having the capacity to provide simultaneous supply of ultrasound at fixed power (40 kHz, 50 W) and microwave energy with adjustable power (2450 MHz, 10–800 W). The reactor is equipped with a sidewall microwave heating element and an ultrasound transducer (OD: 50 mm) at the bottom. An energy meter is used to measure the electric energy consumed during the experimentation. Preheated 50 mL of blended oil and methanol plus KOH solution were mixed in the flask to initiate the reaction. The reactants were simultaneously irradiated with 50 W power of both MW and US. The mixture after the removal of glycerol was subjected to methanol recovery and was three times rinsed to remove traces of KOH using deionized water. The final product was heated at 80 °C to remove traces of moisture. The experimental runs to validate the model predicted optimized conditions were triplicated. Fig. 1 shows the schematic flow diagram that illustrates the method of producing biodiesel from blended oils. The optimized data obtained using MW + US were further compared with mechanical stirring and individual microwave and ultrasound irradiation techniques. The ester contents in the biodiesel samples were estimated using GC–MS. The samples were examined using a method developed in the test center on GC–MS set-up; (Agilent 5975C; Agilent Technologies, USA) coupled with flame ionization deterctor (FID) using a HP-5MS capillary column. For each run, a split ratio of 1:50 was maintained. The oven temperature was increased step-wise from 45 °C to 250 °C and then held for 10 min. The biodiesel yield was then calculated as per Eq. (1) [70]: (1) % Yield BD = Weight of Biodiesel Produced × % FAME from GC Weight of Oil × 100 where, “Weight of biodiesel produced” is the biodiesel obtained during a specific run, “% FAME from GC” gives the indication of the purity of the biodiesel produced, and “weight of oil” is the initial quantity of oil taken for a run. A second-degree polynomial expression was obtained based on the regression performed on the biodiesel yield data as represented by Eq. (2) with all the four factors and their interaction as variables. The equation contains linear, square, and interaction terms of each variable which describes the nonlinear nature of the factors on the yield. (2) Biodiesel Yield % Y = β 0 + ∑ i = 1 n β i x i + ∑ i = 1 n − 1 . ∑ j = i + 1 n β ij x i x j + ∑ i = 1 n β ii x i 2 + ε where ‘Y' is the predicted response or the output (dependent variable), xi and xij are the coded independent variables, β0 is the intercept term, βi is the linear effect, βii is the squared effect and βij is the interaction effect, ε is the value attributed to uncertainty, and i and j are the variable index numbers. Regression analysis examines the significance of the individual process variables and using Eq. (2) the optimized variables were calculated. The importance of the model was assessed using ANOVA within a 95% confidence interval. The various mathematical parameters required to judge the model as a proper fit are F-values, p-values, coefficient of determination (R2), adjusted coefficient of determination (adj. R2), and adequate precision. F-value is used to judge the significance of the multiple correlation coefficients. Test statistics are calculated and compared with their probable value (p-value) for accepting or rejecting a hypothesis [52,71]. RSM is a design tool used to optimize the process conditions and estimate the effect of the individual variables and their interactions on the response under analysis. This tool is applied to fit a complete quadratic polynomial model through DOE and to produce two and three-dimensional charts. These plots were analyzed to study the effect of single and interaction of each factor [72]. Optimized RCO + WCO-biodiesel properties were evaluated and compared with petro-diesel and biodiesel produced from individual oils. Physicochemical properties of biodiesel like density, kinematic viscosity (a procedure similar to blended oil analysis), flash point using closed cup Pensky – Martens equipment (model: PMA 5; M/s Anton Paar, Germany) as per ASTM (D93) method, heating value was obtained using a Bomb calorimeter (model: C200, M/s IKA, Germany) as per ASTM (D240) method. Oxidation stability was obtained using Biodiesel Rancimat (model: 893 Professional; M/s Metrohm, Switzerland) as per ASTM D6751 method. Refractive index was measured using an Abbe refractometer (model: AX-00652; M/s Bellingham Stantey Ltd., UK) it follows the ASTM D1747–09 method. Using GC free glycerin and total glycerin content in blended oil biodiesel sample produced under optimized condition were estimated as pre ASTM D6584 method. Carbon residue in blended oil biodiesel sample was measured following ASTM D4530 method. The main functional groups present in RCO, WCO, RCO + WCO blend, and biodiesel were obtained by applying the Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy technique. The viscosity and density of oils and their blends were estimated and are shown in Fig. 2 (a and b). As seen in Fig. 2, a reduction in the density and the viscosity of RCO was observed which is attributed to the blending effect with WCO for all the three blended oil ratios. The main reason is that the blending of oils causes dilution of RCO (with WCO) which reduces both the properties. Also, WCO has a lower viscosity and density in comparison to RCO. This property reduction helps in easing of the processing of blends for a better reaction. The variation in viscosity with an increase in temperature is seen in Fig. 2a. It was observed that the viscosity of RCO, WCO, and blended oils reduces with temperature. The viscosity of RCO at 30 °C is 465 cP and is found to be highly sensitive to temperature. The reduction in RCO viscosity is 82% when the temperature is increased from 30 °C to 60 °C. The viscosity of WCO is 71.04 cP which is 6.5 times lower than RCO at 30 °C and reduces by 62% with an increase in temperature up to 60 °C. Variation in the viscosities of blend oil is within the limits of RCO and WCO for a given temperature. Here it is important to note that for the 90RCO10WCO sample the viscosity reduction compared to RCO is 41% and 30% respectively at 30 °C and 60 °C. At 60 °C, the viscosity of the 50RCO50WCO blend is 31% higher when compared to WCO. The density of WCO, RCO, and blended oils decreases linearly with an increase in temperature. In each case, a negligible reduction of 2% in density was observed at the temperatures ranging from 30 °C to 60 °C. For the 50RCO50WCO blend, the trend in reduction in density is linear and similar to RCO. Since the properties of the blended oils demonstrate strong functions of blending proportions, blending ratio was selected as a unique process variable for the first time for optimization studies. Fig. 2 (a and b) shows that at 50 °C, values have decreased to a large extent [viscosity reduces by 71% for RCO, 50% for WCO, 66% for 50RCO50WCO, 64% for 70RCO30WCO, and 63% for 90RCO10WCO; whereas density is reduced by 1.4% for RCO, 1.1% for WCO, 1.4% for 50RCO50WCO, 70RCO30WCO, and 90RCO10WCO], thus making this an ideal temperature for carrying out the reaction. This variation in viscosity and density may be due to the change in the fatty acid composition of the blended oil ratio in comparison to the parent oil as seen in Table 3 . A further rise in temperature, reduces both the property values; however it also reaches the boiling point of methanol, which is not desirable in transesterification reaction. Despite WCO having a high acid value, it does not affect the blend due to the dilution effect with RCO. Saponification value estimated for the raw oils (RCO and WCO) and their blends are listed in Table 3. These values for raw oils are in good agreement with reported in literature [97]. Saponification value is a measure of the chain length of altogether fatty acids in the oil sample as triglycerides. The higher saponification value specifies lower fatty acid average length and lighter mean molar mass of triglyceride and vice versa. As expected trend is seen in the current case, RCO is having lower saponification value and higher molar mass than the WCO. The same can be confirmed from fatty acid composition obtained from the GC analysis. With increase in the RCO composition in the blends, saponification number approaches close to the RCO value, however the WCO fraction being less its influence on overall value can be also seen. Also with increasing the RCO fraction the molar mass approached towards the neat RCO. Further, it is to be concluded that oil blending is providing an advantage of redistribution of lower and higher length of fatty acids which can influence the final properties of produced biodiesel. The acid value of the blended oils shown in Table 3 have undergone change with the blended oil ratio. In the 50RCO50WCO blend ratio, the acid value is observed to be higher than RCO, because WCO has a higher acid value and the blending of RCO in oils has resulted in a decrease in values which is as expected. Over all the acid values of the blended samples are in trend as per blend ratios. The reported acid values for the blend are found to be well below the prescribed range of 3–5 mg KOH/g for alkali-catalyzed transesterification [73] (refer to Table 3). The estimated moisture content in RCO, WCO and the prepared blends was found to be less than 0.05%. As seen in Table 3, blended oils exhibit a change in their fatty acid profiles as compared to the parent oil profile. In the case of RCO ricinoleic acid is the major unsaturated fatty acid and acids like palmitic acid, stearic acid, oleic acid, and linoleic acid are present in minor quantities. WCO acid profile is also shown in Table 3. The fatty acid composition for RCO and WCO is similar to those published in literature by various researchers [27,74]. This variation in the blended oil mixture may be due to the chemical reaction between the hydroxyl groups of ricinoleic acid and the acids of WCO during stirring. Similar results are observed in various published literature [32,33] where the fatty acid composition changes after blending in comparison to the parent oil. For the combination of four process variables and three levels, twenty-seven factorial experimental data (having three located at the center of the space and the remaining twenty-four at the midpoint of each edge) were decided based on Box-Behnken procedure as shown in Table 4 . Table 4 also shows the experimental yield corresponding to these runs along with maximum and minimum yields which are 93.38% and 69.25% respectively. Measured yield data were used to perform regression analysis and the second-order polynomial equation was developed as the best fit. The results of ANOVA as shown in Table 5 were obtained from the regression model to determine the significance of each process variable involved in the Box-Behnken model. Based on the magnitude of the p-value (less than 0.05), the significance of the variables was determined [75]. As per ANOVA analysis in Table 5, linear coefficients such as Methanol/oil ratio (A), and KOH loading (B) have positive effect, whereas, time (C), and blended oil ratio (D) show a negative effect on biodiesel yield. Interaction terms AB, AC, and CD have negative effect, whereas, terms AD, BC, and BD show a positive effect on the response over the % of biodiesel yield. The p-values which are less than 0.0001 indicate that the present model is significant. The observed F-value of the model is 23.15 and a p-value less than 0.0001 shows that the model is significant and in agreement with the experimental data. The “lack of fit” parameter has an F-value equal to 8.94, which is found to be insignificant because the probability of the F-value found due to noise is 0.1048 (greater than 0.05). Thus, the experimental results fit with the regression equation with reasonable accuracy. R2 is found to be 96.43% which means that the model is unable to describe only 3.57% of the experimental yield data thereby confirming high accuracy between the experimental data and the predicted data. This value of adj. R2 (adjusted coefficient of determination) was determined to be 92.27%, which signifies the validity of the present model as observed in literature [44]. The adequate precision value in this study is 16.96 which proves that the model is desirable since the value is greater than four. The pareto chart is given in Fig. 3 (a) shows the significance and the insignificance of the four individual process variables along with their relative interactions with one another. The significant terms influencing biodiesel yield are A (linear effect of Methanol/oil ratio), C (linear effect of time), and D (linear effect of blended oil ratio); A2 (2nd order effect of Methanol/oil ratio), B2 (2nd order effect of KOH loading), C2 (2nd order effect of time) and D2 (2nd order effect of blended oil ratio); and AB (interaction between Methanol/oil ratio and KOH loading), AD (interaction between Methanol/oil ratio and blended oil ratio), BC (interaction between KOH loading and time) and CD (interaction between time and blended oil ratio). As observed from Fig. 3(a) the process variables, i.e., the blended oil ratio (D) is relatively the most significant term and except the quadratic term BD all the interactions involving variable D are significant. This demonstrates that the process variable D is a significant variable in modeling whereas, the same has been arbitrarily selected as a constant parameter in literatures seen in Table 1 and Table A (i-iii) of Annexure A. The regression model (obtained via ANOVA) establishes the relationship between the independent process variables and biodiesel yield as shown in Fig. 3 (b). The model has good stability as all the predicted data are entirely in the range of ±5% error band. Fig. 3(c) indicates the relationship between the residuals of yield and their normal distribution along a reference line. The model for biodiesel production, which is second order in nature, is described by scattered data spread randomly as shown in Fig. 3(d). Thus, the present statistical model shows good consistency for the production of biodiesel process. Perturbation graphs identify priority of each factor and its effect while the others are kept constant. The response is drawn by varying one factor at a time over its prescribed range while keeping the remaining factors as constant values at their middle level. Perturbation curves, shown in Fig. 4 , describe the sensitivity of the process variables viz.: A (Methanol/oil ratio), B (KOH loading), C (time), and D (blended oil ratio) used in the biodiesel production process. The steepness of the slope of a particular variable decides the dominant effect of the same over the yield. As depicted in Fig. 4, in the plot between the lower level (−1) and mid-level (0), variable A shows the most dominating effect as it has the steepest slope of the biodiesel yield followed by the remaining variables B, D, and C respectively. On the other hand from the middle level (0) to the higher level (1), the process variable D has prominent effect followed by the variables C, A, and B. It can be interpreted from the plot that when Methanol/oil ratio is in the range of 6 to 8:1 the yield changes drastically and from 8 to 10:1 range the effect of excess Methanol/oil ratio is not significant. The plot also shows that time (C), increased beyond the intermediate level (10 min) causes a reduction in the biodiesel yield. This is due to the conjoint effect of microwave + ultrasound because beyond the optimum time, the system starts increasing the temperature of the reactants resulting in the evaporation of methanol [47,76]. Similarly, with the blended oil ratio, the biodiesel yield is about 93% when the blended oil ratio is between 50RCO50WCO and 70RCO30WCO but it drops drastically to 81.15% at 90RCO10WCO blended oil ratio. This shows that a higher fraction of RCO tends to reduce biodiesel yield for the selected variable range. Unlike other published studies (mentioned in Table 1 and Table A of Annexure A) where the blended ratio is taken as a constant in an arbitrary manner, the present study demonstrates that it is the most sensitive variable between 0 (50RCO50WCO) and + 1 (90RCO10WCO) having a significant impact on the yield. As seen in the ANOVA Table 5, the process variable B (KOH loading) is an insignificant variable, and the same can be seen in the perturbation graph as the slope for the variable B is almost flat throughout the process. This may be because the amount of catalyst selected for the reaction is sufficient since both the process intensification techniques operate simultaneously. A detailed study of the individual and the interaction effects of the process variables on biodiesel yield is provided in the following section. The influence of the individual and the interaction of terms (methanol/oil ratio, KOH loading, reaction time, and blended oil ratio) on biodiesel yield is investigated and discussed below for better understanding. Fig. 5 (a-d) shows the plots of the effects of the individual process variables on biodiesel yield which is produced by having one variable factor while the other three are fixed at zero levels (i.e. middle level). In the current study, four process variables viz. Methanol/oil ratio (A), KOH loading (B), time (C), and blended oil ratio (D) are considered. Methanol/oil ratio (A) varies from the lower level (6:1) to the higher level (10:1) (as seen in Fig. 5a), whereas KOH loading (0.55 (w/w) %), reaction time (10 min), and blended oil ratio (70RCO30WCO) were set at intermediate levels. The least RCO + WCO-biodiesel yield of 83.53% is observed at 6:1 whereas the highest biodiesel yield of 93.10% is observed near the intermediate level (8:1). A appropriate amount of methanol/oil ratio is essential to increase the solubility of the KOH catalyst and assist in the forward rate of reaction [44]. In the conjoint system of MW + US, the reaction is carried out in a closed vessel which prevents the evaporation of methanol. Thus, it was observed that an excess methanol ratio (more than 10:1) hampers the purity of biodiesel since methanol is soluble and causes a reverse reaction. A recently reported study confirmed that excess methanol/oil ratio (12:1) is not desirable in the conjoint effect (MW + US) [50]. Excess methanol causes difficulty in separation and also, purification of methanol from glycerol is difficult as glycerol is miscible in methanol. Insufficient methanol/oil ratio affects the biodiesel yield as it reduces the rate of reaction that is required to the triglycerides into biodiesel. Short-chain alcohols like methanol have strong polarity with microwaves. Thus, it becomes an active absorption media giving an added advantage for transesterification reaction as it converts the absorbed energy into heat [46,77]. The US-assisted transesterification can be achieved at a lower molar ratio because the powerful disturbed turbulence produced by US waves in the reaction medium intensifies the process of transesterification reaction [11]. Solubilization of the KOH catalyst is facilitated by the presence of methanol which in turn enhances forward reaction [44]. A similar result was reported while using an equal fraction (w/w) of castor oil and waste fish oil with an 8:1 methanol/oil ratio to produce 95.2% biodiesel yield [32]. However, for a blend of WCO and beauty-leaf oil, 70% (vol%) of methanol/oil ratio is required to produce 96.84% of biodiesel [4]. The effects of KOH loading are witnessed in plot Fig. 5b when KOH loading varies from the lower level (0.3%) to higher-level (0.8%) while maintaining the methanol/oil ratio (at 8:1), reaction time (10 min), and blended oil ratio (70RCO30WCO) at the intermediate level. The highest predicted biodiesel yield of 92.89% was obtained at 0.58% of KOH loading which was reduced to 88.96% at 0.3% and 90.9% at 0.8% catalyst wt% respectively. An insignificant effect of KOH wt% on biodiesel yield was observed. A proper quantity of the catalyst is required as it produces a sufficient number of free ions required for the reaction. In turn, it reduces the activation energy of triglycerides and enhances the transesterification reaction. Catalyst loading of 1% was used for castor oil transesterification in MS-equipped studies [78]. A maximum biodiesel yield of 95.20% was obtained with a 0.5% of KOH catalyst using mechanical stirring for an equal proportion of castor oil and raw fish oil. However on increasing it further up to 1.25% reduced the biodiesel yield [32]. Due to the effect of the conjoint technique catalyst, wt% requirement is reduced which significantly lowers material and purification requirement [79] costs when compared to the existing biodiesel production techniques. It is reported that higher catalyst wt% enables quicker forward reaction but impurities lead to soap formation [45]. The use of blended oils in a conventional stirring technique shows that an excessive use of a catalyst (more than 2%) leads to soap formation and a backward shift in reaction [62]. Excessive amounts of catalyst in transesterification reaction can also lead to emulsion formation which ultimately increases the viscosity of the biodiesel by forming gels [43,80]. The biodiesel yield in the plot Fig. 5c increases in the lower (5 min) and intermediate (9 min) levels achieving a maximum yield of 93.07% at 8.89 min. The yield decreases when the time is increased to 15 min. Longer reaction times result in reactant degradation, evaporation of methanol and a reduction in the biodiesel yield by reversing the reaction [50,81]. The reaction is incomplete at a lower time and there exists unreacted oil as observed in the mixture. With higher time duration the temperature in the reaction medium increases causing excessive evaporation of methanol whereby a reduction in biodiesel yield is observed due to the initiation of backward reaction [81]. These observations were similar to studies conducted by various researchers [50,81]. Studies published show that a higher period of about 8 h is required for biodiesel production using a mechanical stirring method with an 83.41% of yield even at 65 °C with castor oil as the feedstock [27]. Thus, the reaction time is reduced to approximately 9 min using the conjoint effect of MW + US. This is because the ultrasound contributes to proper and uniform mixing of the reactants whereas microwave enhances the chemistry of transesterification reaction [82]. In the present study, reaction time is observed to be lower in comparison to those stated in the literature (refer to Table 1 and Table A of Annexure A) [45,50]. This is because, in this study, the power of both MW and US was selected at 50 W each and the batch size was kept at 50 mL. The effects of the various blended oil ratios on biodiesel yield are shown in Fig. 5d. Higher biodiesel yield (greater than90%) is observed in the region where the blended oil ratio is between the lower level (50RCO50WCO) and the middle-level (70RCO30WCO), and the yield near 60RCO40WCO is 93.00%. The yield slightly reduces to 92.81% when it reaches the middle level value. Furthermore, a sharp drop to 81.25% in the biodiesel yield was observed when the blended oil ratio was increased to a higher level (90RCO10WCO). This can be attributed to the increase in the RCO quantity in the blend that the required process variables were deficient to complete the reaction. In comparison to 60RCO40WCO, the biodiesel yield is slightly lower at the blended oil ratio of 50RCO50WCO. This may be because the methanol/oil ratio (8:1) is higher for 50% WCO present in the 50RCO50WCO blend and the excess amount of methanol may dissolve the biodiesel produced. As seen in Fig. 3, at 50 °C the viscosity and the density of the blended oil 70RCO30WCO remain high, thus making the reaction difficult and reducing the RCO + WCO-biodiesel yield. It is observed that the sensitivity of the biodiesel yield is the highest when the range of the blended oil ratio is above the middle value (0 level) when compared to the other process variables. As reported the castor oil biodiesel yield up to 93% is possible only if process variables are as high as 20:1, 1.3%, 65 °C, and 90 min (methanol/oil ratio, KOH loading, reaction temperature, and reaction time respectively) [83]. Thus, a blended oil ratio ranging between 50RCO50WCO and 70RCO30WCO is an ideal proportion to produce a higher yield with a reduced amount of process variables as concluded from the present study. The influence of the singular process variables is critical to understand the impact on the yield of biodiesel. Besides this, understanding the influence of the interaction of the process variables on biodiesel yield is equally important. Therefore, two-dimensional interactive contours of predicted biodiesel yield (%) are plotted by keeping the central-level value constant. These interactive graphical representations offer a deeper understanding of the reaction. The middle-level values [A: 8:1, B: 0.55%, C: 10 min, and D: 70RCO30RCO] are kept constant while drawing the contours. Fig. 6a shows the significance of the collaborative effect of methanol/oil ratio and KOH loading on biodiesel yield. It is seen that the contour has an oval shape of the yield profiles, which indicates the possibility of strong interactions among these process variables. It is also observed that the methanol/oil ratio has a more dominating effect in comparison to KOH loading on biodiesel yield (refer to Fig. 5). When the methanol/oil ratio ranges from 7:1–10:1 and KOH loading from 0.3 to 0.8%, the profiles have more than 90% biodiesel yield. Below the middle level of methanol/oil ratio and KOH loading of 8:1 and 0.55% respectively the biodiesel yield is found to be low (74.03%). On the other hand, when the methanol/oil ratio increases above the middle level and KOH loading is lower, biodiesel yield increases up to 93.12%. Thus, the collaborative effect of methanol/oil ratio and KOH loading shows more than 92.87% of yield in the range of 8 to 9.06:1 and 0.42 to 0.62% respectively. Beyond this feasible contour, the yield profiles decrease gradually in magnitude. Fig. 6b shows the interaction plot between the methanol/oil ratio and the blended oil ratio. It can be seen from the plot, that when the blended oil ratio is increased beyond 70RCO30WCO the biodiesel yield % reduces drastically even with a higher methanol/oil ratio. Thus, it shows that RCO needs higher values of process variables for the completion of transesterification reaction, and the same is confirmed in published literature [27]. Fig. 6c shows the interaction between KOH loading and reaction time. The plot shows that increasing KOH loading from 0.3 to 0.8% is ideal for producing greater than90% biodiesel yield. However, the biodiesel yield reduced with increase in time. Similarly, Fig. 6d shows the interaction with blended oil ratio and reaction time which demonstrates the fact that increasing the reaction time up to 15 min for a blend having more than 70RCO30WCO blended oil ratio could produce only 73.9% biodiesel yield Thus, the optimized predicted process variables for a maximum biodiesel yield of 92.81% are methanol/oil ratio of 8.42:1, KOH loading of 0.52 wt%, reaction time of 9.04 min, and blended oil ratio of 61RCO39WCO. Validation of the optimized process variables was done by conducting the experiments thrice and producing an average 93.12 ± 0.24% of experimental biodiesel yield. The same optimized variables were tested on two different temperatures with ±10 °C variation wherein it was found that the RCOWCO biodiesel yield reduced by 2% at 40 °C and 0.5% at 60 °C. Similar studies reported in literature have shown a reduction in biodiesel yield at a temperature lower or higher than 50 °C [70,84]. Further, biodiesel obtained using only RCO and WCO as feedstock found to have biodiesel yield of 78% and 91% respectively under the similar operating conditions of blended oils as mentioned above. It can be observed that individual oils has lower yield values when compared with the blended oil biodiesel. In comparison with the optimized composition the biodiesel yield is higher for biodiesel obtained from blends than raw oil. From optimization studies, it can be summarized that the conjoint effect of MW + US (yield 93.12%) is a superior approach under optimized conditions which is in contrast to the individual effect of MW (yield 50.23%) or US (81.23%) and conventional mechanical stirring (yield 34.25%) for producing biodiesel using blends of RCO + WCO for obtaining maximum yield. It is further evident from the Fig. B1 and the discussion given in Annexure-B in supplementary data for a comparative study of techniques. In consideration of the optimum process conditions, reaction kinetics was studied. The three temperatures selected were ± 10 °C of the optimum temperature, i.e. 40, 50, and 60 °C. Samples were collected at specified time and they were immediately quenched to freeze the reaction. Samples were later examined for kinetic parameter estimation. Pseudo-first-order kinetics was considered as reported in most of the studies on blended oils with castor oil [33,85] because of the presence of excess methanol. The hypotheses were considered and the equations used for the kinetic study are similar to those reported in Sharma et.al [11]. The details of the assumption and the equations used for the kinetic analysis are mentioned in Annexure-C in supplementary data. As seen in Fig. 7a, the estimated values of the transesterification reaction rate constant (k) were found to increase with temperature and equation fitting has an R2 value greater than95% (refer to Eq. C.2 Annexure-C). Such a higher value of rate constant ‘k’ of the conjoint technique is attributed to the powerful conjoint effect of MW + US which generates micro-emulsion due to the efficient mixing of methanol and oil. The activation energy for the blended oil biodiesel using the MW + US system was calculated using the Arrhenius equation (refer to Eq. C.3 in Annexure-C) which was found to be 12.13 kJ/mol as shown in Fig 7. A reduction in activation energy is due to a higher rate constant and the faster rate of reaction is due to the conjoint effect of MW+ US irradiation. Microwave irradiation aids in providing the proper temperature for transesterification reaction and ultrasound provides intimate mixing of the immiscible reactants [69,82]. Using the optimized data mentioned in Section 3.3 and assuming pseudo-first-order kinetics, reaction kinetics parameters were calculated for the conventional stirring process. Analysis showed that the ‘k’ value in the case of conjoint technique was 5 to 8 times faster in comparison to conventional stirring method. The activation energy calculated for the MS technique was found to be 30.18 kJ/mol. Considering pseudo-first-order kinetics, the activation energy calculated using Arrhenius equation for the transesterification of castor oil using conventional stirring process is reported to be in the range of 21.95 to 38.38 kJ/mol [21,28]. Till date, there is no reported data on the kinetic study of castor oil using MW and US either as an independent system or a combined one. These results explain that the activation energy of the MW + US process is 2.5 times lower than conventional stirring. This lower activation energy is due to (i) higher collision among the reactants due to microwave effect as it activates dipole oscillation and (ii) a high mass transfer rate resulting in the homogeneity of the reaction mixture due to the ultrasound effect which leads to faster reaction. Moreover, the stable emulsion produced among the immiscible reactants due to low-frequency ultrasound paves way for a consistent transesterification reaction. Hence, it can be concluded that lower activation energy reported for MW + US in the current work than those reported in literature for conventional stirring illustrates the effectiveness of the MW + US technique for a blended system in enhancing the reaction rate. Energy consumed depends on the type of power supplied to the reactor i.e. the combination of MW + US and the remaining power. The remaining power is related to energy consumption that involves oil heating, methanol recovery using a rotary evaporator, and biodiesel drying post washing to separate water traces. Equipment power was mainly divided into three categories (i) nominal power: power input to the set - up is the nameplate power capacity of the equipment, (ii) delivered power: measured directly using energy meter (refer to Eq.D1 in Annexure-D), and (iii) dissipated power: calculated using a standard calorimetric method [91]. Percentage efficiency is measured at a nominal power of 100 W for MW + US (50 W each for MS and US) [92] (refer to Eq.D2 in Annexure-D), and the parameter that shows the mass of biodiesel as a function of energy is specific energy consumption (SEC) [93] (refer to Eq.D3 in Annexure-D). Details of the equations used to calculate the energy terms are mentioned in Annexure-D. Table 6 shows the energy consumption of the conjoint MW + US assisted process and conventional stirring using the optimum process variables. The delivered energy was calculated directly from the energy meter. It was found that the delivered energy was reduced by 1.6 and 2.5 times in comparison to nominal power. Also, the power dissipated was reduced even further to 91 and 19.5 times of nominal power for MW and US systems. Delivered and dissipated power were reduced by 4.5 and 27 times respectively in comparison to the nominal power of 100 W. The obtained specific energy consumption (SEC) values show that the conjoint technique is more favorable since it has the lowest value in terms of the performance parameters and selection of the particular process [94]. The results of the present study can be extended further by revising the specifications of the conjoint MW + US reactor. Research at pilot scale using all process variables is required prior to industrialization. The possible variations in conjoint reactors can be performed towards (i) the experimental assessments of large-scale processes, (ii) the manufacturing of reactors for pilot-scale study, and (iii) detailed economic and environmental analyses. As observed in Fig. 8 , the spectra of the produced biodiesel exhibit several unique absorption features that are not observed in the spectra of any of the neat oil and blend mixtures. The concentrated peak at 2924 cm−1 is assigned to the CH stretching of the strong carbonyl group. The strong peak corresponding to the wavenumber of 1741 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching of the CO bond [4]. New absorptions peaks visible at 1173, 1199, 1366, and 1437 cm−1 respectively are the typical FTIR features of biodiesel [95]. A decrease in intensity of the spectra over 1000–1800 cm−1 indicates high methyl ester content in the sample [4]. The presence of –CH2 or –CH3 functional group is ascertained by the absorption peak corresponding to a wavelength of 1437 cm−1. The absorption band at about 3443 cm−1 is attributed to the – OH group connected in the fatty acid chain of castor oil and the same is seen in the blends and biodiesel formed [96]. Table 7 provides details of the estimated fuel properties for blended RCO + WCO-biodiesel (61RCO39WCO), single oil-based biodiesel viz. RCO biodiesel and WCO biodiesel with the specified ASTM diesel, and biodiesel standards. For comparison purposes, biodiesel from single oil feedstock such as RCO and WCO was prepared under optimal conditions (refer to Section 3.3) as mentioned in Table 7. Fuel density plays an important role and governs a few key parameters such as the volume of fuel injection, injection timing and injected fuel spray pattern. The high fuel density indicates the injection of more amount of fuel. The measured density of RCO + WCO-biodiesel produced was found to be 1.05 times lower as compared to that obtained from RCO-biodiesel. The kinematic viscosity of the fuel plays an important part in deciding the behavior of fuel spray, mixture formation, and the overall combustion process. The viscosity of the RCO + WCO-biodiesel prepared was reduced by 2.15 to 2.3 times when compared to RCO-biodiesel. As a result, it is expected that the prepared blended oil biodiesel will improve injection and the atomization process when compared to the biodiesel prepared from RCO alone. One of the important variables that is typically used to evaluate the cold flow properties of biodiesel and also governs the quality of a fuel is the pour point [32]. RCO + WCO-biodiesel has a pour point value of ~8 °C which is higher than diesel however, it falls within ASTM standards. This indicates that the biodiesel produced from blended oils is also suitable for use in cold weather. The addition of additives may further enhance the cold flow properties of the biodiesel. The calorific value and the refractive index of RCO + WCO-biodiesel are near to the values given for gasoline-diesel. The calorific value is improved by 6.5–12.4% using blended oil biodiesel when compared to biodiesel produced from independent oils. This variation can be attributed to the varied saturated and unsaturated acid chemical composition of the raw oils. This is similar to a few studies like those of Dias et al. [58], Ong et al. [51] (Refer to Table 1 and Table A). The free glycerin and total glycerin content in blended oil biodiesel sample produced under optimized condition were estimated using GC as pre ASTM D6584 method and found to be 0.016% wt. and 0.21% wt. respectively. These properties are found to be within the acceptable limit as per ASTM standard. Carbon residue was found to be 0.030% wt. (as per ASTM D4530 method) and also found to be within the permissible limit. Based on the above property analysis, it can be confirmed that the blended RCO + WCO-biodiesel produced using the conjoint effect of MW + US-assisted transesterification improves the physicochemical properties of biodiesel. As discussed above, physical properties such as the calorific value and kinematic viscosity of blended biodiesel are the main beneficial properties that improved significantly as compared with biodiesel from the individual oils. Current research also addresses the issue and benefits of waste management by effective utilization of WCO and RCO that will help to increase the production of biodiesel. Thus, dependency on a single feedstock can be significantly reduced. This blending approach needs to be strategically implemented for overall economic benefit. Energy analysis of conjoint PI technique shows overall energy savings and optimization studies provided insight on resource optimization including the correct proportions of raw materials to be used when compared to the individual oil systems and individual PI techniques as well as a conventional method for biodiesel production. Because of these outcomes, the current method proves to be an economical option as fuel quality is also enhanced providing better engine performance. Further, various industries manufacture large-scale ultrasound equipment viz. (i) M/s. Hielscher ultrasound technology, Germany manufactures a series of large-scale probe-type ultrasounds for industrial-scale applications (4000–16,000 W; 20KHz) that can handle flow rates ranging from 1 to 50 m3/h specifically for biodiesel application [98] and (ii) M/s. Sonomechanics Inc., USA has developed a continuous large-scale probe-type ultrasound generator (3000 W power) that can process volume starting from 1 L with no upper limit [99]. Large-scale microwaves are also available viz. (i) M/s. Tianshui Huayuan Pharmaceutical equipment technologies Co. Ltd., China manufactured large-scale microwaves capacity up to 500 L (total power of 6000 W) [100], and (ii) M/s. SAIREM Inc., France manufactures continuous flow microwaves (power of 75 kW) that can process up to 200 kg of biomass per hour [101]. Such various large-size ultrasound and microwave reactors are available in the market. These can be used in the conjoint process with suitable modifications for scale-up studies and industrial applications. Biodiesel was successfully prepared using an appropriate blend ratio of RCO and WCO by the simultaneous application of microwave + ultrasound irradiation along with potassium hydroxide as a catalyst. • The blended oil ratio is the most significant process parameter among the considered ones and the optimum value for the same was successfully established during this study. • Operating variables were optimized using the RSM-based Box-Behnken model. Conjoint irradiation improves biodiesel quality by mixing the reactants uniformly and which provides a higher yield as it overcomes the mass–heat transfer resistance. • The pseudo-first-order model was observed to be the best fit for kinetic analysis and showed significant improvement in activation energy in comparison to the conventional process. • Energy analysis showed that the conjoint MW + WE effectively dissipated 84% more power compared to conventional stirring thereby making the process energy efficient. • Finally, the improvement in the physicochemical properties of the blended oil biodiesel for raw castor oil and waste cooking oil indicates a positive effect of blending. The blended oil ratio is the most significant process parameter among the considered ones and the optimum value for the same was successfully established during this study. Operating variables were optimized using the RSM-based Box-Behnken model. Conjoint irradiation improves biodiesel quality by mixing the reactants uniformly and which provides a higher yield as it overcomes the mass–heat transfer resistance. The pseudo-first-order model was observed to be the best fit for kinetic analysis and showed significant improvement in activation energy in comparison to the conventional process. Energy analysis showed that the conjoint MW + WE effectively dissipated 84% more power compared to conventional stirring thereby making the process energy efficient. Finally, the improvement in the physicochemical properties of the blended oil biodiesel for raw castor oil and waste cooking oil indicates a positive effect of blending. Thus, the use of blended oil is a potential feedstock for biodiesel production using conjoint microwave and ultrasound. Anvita Sharma: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, data acquisition and interpretation, Validation, Investigation, Writing-Original draft preparation, Formal analysis. Pravin Kodgire: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing-Review & Editing, Visualization, Formal analysis, revising the manuscript critically for important intellectual content, Supervision, Approval of version of manuscript, funding acquisition. Surendra Singh Kachhwaha: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing-Review & Editing, Visualization, Supervision, revising the manuscript critically for important intellectual content, Funding acquisition, Approval of version of manuscript,
abstract: Current study investigates a novel simultaneous application of microwave and ultrasound irradiation (MW + US) via appropriation of raw castor oil (RCO) and waste cooking oil (WCO) blends for transesterification aiming on process optimization, reaction kinetics, and energy analysis. Applying Box-Behnken design, effect of process variables viz., methanol/oil ratio, KOH loading, reaction time, and blended oil ratio of RCO + WCO on biodiesel production was analyzed. First time, blended oil ratio as a process variable is studied and found to be most significant variable. The optimized conditions observed were methanol/oil ratio: 8.42:1, KOH loading: 0.52 w/w%, reaction time: 9.04 min, and blended oil ratio RCOWCO: 61RCO39WCO with experimental and predicted yield of 93.12 ± 0.24% and 92.81% respectively. First-time reaction kinetics study performed on RCO + WCO blended oils exhibited that reaction follows pseudo−1st-order kinetic with 2.5 times lower activation energy as compared to mechanical stirring process. Conjoint technique showed better energy dissipation with significantly reduced specific energy consumption. The physicochemical properties of RCOWCO biodiesel were found comparable to gasoline-diesel and international biodiesel standards. In comparison to castor oil biodiesel, blended oil biodiesel physicochemical properties improved significantly (esp. calorific value improved by 6–12%) and can be used as a renewable fuel to blend with petro-diesel.
title: Biodiesel production with enhanced fuel properties via appropriation of non-edible oil mixture using conjoint ultrasound and microwave reactor: Process optimization and kinetic studies
