full_text: Starting with coal and subsequently petroleum oil and natural gas, fossil fuels has ushered in an unprecedented era of material prosperity and technological advancement for human development. Today about 80% of global energy demand is produced from fossil fuels. The extensive utilization of fossil fuels during various industrial manufactures has led to global warming, which is primarily triggered by excessive emission of carbon dioxide. To date, the catalytic dry reforming of methane (DRM) using CO2 as an oxidizing agent has been proposed as one of the most promising technologies to reduce and utilize CO2 [1–4]. In addition, DRM can produce syngas (H2 + CO) with a desirable ratio (H2/CO ratio  800 °C), the initial activity over nickel catalyst easily decline due to the aggregation of catalytic Ni particles and the deposition of carbon [12]. Several important works revealed the relative contribution of CH4 and CO2 molecular species towards carbon formation over DRM catalysts. Transient isotopic experiments indicated that the accumulated carbon over the Rh/Al2O3 catalyst was mainly derived from the CO2 molecule [13–15], while over Ni-based catalyst, the carbon deposition was caused by CO disproportionation (2CO → C + CO2) and/or methane decomposition (CH4 → C + 2H2) [16,17]. The stable and inactive carbon without interacting chemically with nickel could progressively encapsulate the nickel particles, hindering the access of the reacting gases to the active surface. Thus, its accumulation closely followed the irreversible deactivation rate [18]. Over the last decade, the problematic deactivation has provoked extensive researches on designing carbon-resistance nickel catalyst for DRM process. By far, lanthanide-group elements have been the most utilized promotes for DRM catalysts [19–22]. This is mainly due to the fact that lanthanides can enhance the basicity of catalysts and hence strengthen dissociative CO2 adsorption on the catalysts surface which can expedite the removing of surface carbonaceous species. Pan et al. investigated the promotion effect of Ga2O3 onto Ni/SiO2 catalyst on CO2 adsorption [23]. They analyzed the states of adsorbed CO2 by means of Fourier transformation infrared spectroscopy and found that CO2 was linearly or physically adsorbed on the SiO2 catalyst, whereas CO2 was activated into carbonate and bicarbonate species on the SiO2–Ga2O3 catalyst. This resulted in the enhanced resistance of Ni/SiO2-Ga2O3 catalyst towards carbon formation during DRM reaction. Generally, neodymium (Nd) is widely employed in magnetic and optics materials [24,25]. Recently, the neodymium-promoted Ni-based catalysts had emerged as an important research topic as its amazing performance in catalyzing DRM reaction. Choudhary et al. [26] reported the perovskite-type CoNdO x catalyst with Co/Nd molar ratio of 1.0 showed not only high CH4 conversion (92.3%) and H2 selectivity (95.5%) but also high stability against the catalyst deactivation under harsh reaction conditions. They asserted that the high resistance towards coke formation for the CoNdO x catalysts was ascribed to the strong interaction between Co and Nd2O3. He et al. [27] analyzed the promotional effect of Nd on Ni/SAB-15 catalyst and found the addition of Nd to Ni/SBA-15 catalyst facilitated the reduction of NiO particles, which contributed to the outstanding DRM catalytic performance and strong resistance to carbon deposition. However they reported that Nd modification unexpectedly did not tune the acidic-basic properties of the supports [27]. In our previous work [28], Ni particles were impregnated over Nd-promoted ordered mesoporous silica (MS) support and the effect of doping Nd was studied. The catalytic conversions of CH4 and CO2 for Ni/Nd-MS at 700 °C were higher than the catalyst without Nd modification. Our results highlighted the merits of Nd that could improve Ni dispersion, increase exposed basic sites and strengthen nickel-support interaction. Very recently, Liu et al. confirmed that Nd-incorporation into Ni/Mg-Al-O catalyst significantly enriched Ni dispersion and active surface oxygen amount, which eventually contributed to the excellent DRM catalytic performance and strong anti-coking ability [29]. On the other hand, the stable structures of catalysts play a vital role in DRM catalytic performance as well. The traditional supports such as ordered mesoporous silica and alumina (OMA) can enlarge the specific surface area and inhibit the growth of metal particles larger than the pore size as a result of confinement effect, however the collapse of ordered structure is often observed during high temperature reaction conditions. Ni/Nd-MS catalysts displayed higher initial methane conversion and better sintering resistance, nevertheless it exhibited sharp deactivation due to the collapse of order mesoporous framework [28]. Tada et al. focused on investigating the reasons behind deactivation for Ni/SBA-16 catalyst and suggested that the stable mesoporous structure was an important factor that determined the long-term stability of supported nickel catalysts [30]. They evidenced the partial decomposition of the ordered SBA-16 framework after the long-term DRM test due to the presence of steam which is the byproduct of reforming reaction [31,32]. The unstable silica supports led to inevitable aggregation of the Ni particles: small Ni particles with the diameters of 5–8 nm and considerably larger Ni particles of 10–30 nm. This result was in agreement with previously reported Ni catalysts supported on SBA-15 [33,34] and MCM-41 [35]. These studies clearly showed the limitation of using mesoporous support for the efficient DRM. Recently Cerium (Ce)-doped-Ni(OH)2 have been reported in DRM application. The enhanced catalytic performance was attributed to the unique structure of 10 nm Ni nanoparticles stabilized within CeNi x O y [36]. The above observation led us to conjecture that without using the regularly ordered support, nickel oxide and neodymium oxide could be directly deposited each other. Starting from the literature previously presented, this study aimed at determining the influence of crystalline arrangement on the DRM performance, which has been previously ignored or not considered important. Herein, this work elaborated on producing the novel Ni x Nd1-x catalysts for DRM reaction. By regulating the Nd and Ni loadings, the crystalline of metallic nickel were controlled. Various characterization techniques such as nitrogen physisorption, X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), temperature programmed reduction (TPR), hydrogen chemisorption and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were conducted to identify the nanostructure and to understand the advantages of the optimized Ni x Nd1-x catalysts. The probable reasons, e.g., pore structure, coke deposition and metal sintering for the catalyst deactivation were investigated in detail using thermogravimetric (TG) analysis, XRD, nitrogen physisorption and TEM. The Ni x Nd1-x catalysts were synthesized through co-precipitation method. Typically, nickel nitrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O) and neodymium nitrate (Nd(NO3)3·6H2O) were dissolved in deionized water to fabricate a 0.1 mol/L (total metal ion concentration) solution with certain molar ratio (Ni/Nd = 1:9, 3:7, 5:5, 7:3, 9:1). 0.4 mol/L NaOH was added dropwise into the above solution until the final pH reached 12. The slurry was aged under vigorous stirring at 40 °C for 1.5 h, then refluxed at 85 °C for 24 h. After that, the obtained precipitate was filtered, washed with deionized water, dried at 110 °C overnight, and then calcined at 850 °C for 4 h using a heating rate of 6.7 °C/min. The catalysts were designated as Ni1Nd9, Ni3Nd7, Ni5Nd5, Ni7Nd3, and Ni9Nd1. Nd2O3 was prepared by the same process without using nickel nitrate. The actual nickel and neodymium content in the calcined Ni x Nd1-x catalysts were detected by means of 7700× (Agilent) inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). The crystalline phases of the reduced and spent catalysts were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) employing a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker). Cu K α (λ = 0.154 nm) was utilized as radiation source and X-ray tube was set at 40 kV and 40 mA. XRD patterns were recorded from 20° to 80° in 2θ scattering angle with a step size of 0.02° and a counting time of 0.2 s/step. Joint Committee on Powder Diffraction Standards (JCPDS) files were used to confirm the crystalline phases and the Scherrer equation was applied to calculate the average crystallite size. The catalyst was prepared as thin layer on a polymethyl methacrylate sample holder. The textural property of the reduced and spent catalysts were determined through nitrogen physisorption experiments at −196 °C on a Tristar II 3020 surface area and porosity analyzer (Micromeritics). In order to eliminate water or gas impurities, the catalysts were thermally treated under vacuum at 150 °C for 6 h prior to the measurements. The specific surface areas were obtained by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method at a P/P 0 range from 0.05 to 0.30. Pore size distribution curves were displayed by the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method according to the adsorption branch of the corresponding nitrogen isotherm. Total pore volume (V p) was determined by the adsorbed volume at a relative pressure of 0.99. Surface morphology and lattice fringe of the reduced and spent catalysts were acquired by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) employing a JEM-2100F (JEOL) microscope operating with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Before the analysis, the catalyst was dispersed ultrasonically in ethanol and a drop of suspension was disposed on a copper grid (300 mesh) coated with holey carbon film. A high-angle annular dark field detector (HAADF) was used to capture HAADF scanning TEM micrographs (HAADF-STEM). STEM was combined with energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) using an XFlash 5030 spectrometer (Bruker) to gather the distribution maps of chosen elements. The reduction behavior of the calcined catalyst was analyzed by temperature programmed reduction (Hydrogen-TPR) experiments on an AutoChem II 2920 chemisorption analyzer (Micromeritics). Hydrogen consumption was measured by a thermal conductivity detector (TCD). Prior to the measurement, 50 mg of the Ni x Nd1-x catalyst was placed in a quartz U-tube reactor and degassed at 300 °C for 0.5 h in argon stream (30 mL/min). When the reactor cooled down to 50 °C, 10% H2/Ar was switched with a flow rate of 30 mL/min, and the temperature was raised from 50 to 800 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C/min. The amount of H2 consumed was calibrated with a standard CuO powder. Hydrogen chemisorption was carried out using the same apparatus described in the TPR measurement. 100 mg of the calcined Ni x Nd1-x catalyst was reduced by treating with a 10% H2/Ar flow at 800 °C for 0.5 h, and the reduced catalyst was subsequently cooled down to 50 °C in Ar flow. The hydrogen pulse chemisorption was then measured at 50 °C by introducing several pulses with a known volume of 10% H2/Ar gas, and the measurement was terminated when no further uptake of hydrogen was detected. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the reduced catalysts was detected by an ESCALAB 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific) equipped with a monochromatic Al K α (hυ = 1486.6 eV) X-ray source. The binding energy scale of the spectrometer was adjusted with respect to the adventitious C 1s peak fixed at 284.8 eV. The amount and nature of carbon residual on the spent catalyst was analyzed by differential thermal analysis (DTA) and thermo gravimetric analysis (TG) with a Q600 SDT apparatus (TA Instruments). The spent catalyst was heated under flowing air with a flow rate of 40 mL/min from ambient temperature to 1000 °C with a heating ramp of 10 °C/min. The catalytic DRM tests were carried out in a continuously operated flow system at atmospheric pressure in a vertical quartz reactor of 6 mm inner diameter. The effluent gas was analyzed by an on-line gas chromatograph (GC9800, Shanghai Kechuang Chromatograph Instruments Co., Ltd) equipped with thermal conductivity detector (TCD). For the catalytic tests, the prepared catalyst powder was pressed and crushed; the catalyst granules with particle size in the range of 180–250 μm were selected. 0.1 g of the sieved catalyst, without being diluted in an inert material, was packed between two pieces of quartz wool and put in the center of the reactor. The reaction temperature was measured using an axial thermocouple located at the outlet of the catalyst bed. Initially, the catalyst was reduced in-situ in a pure hydrogen flow at 800 °C for 30 min. Afterwards, the dry reforming reaction was carried out with an equimolar mixture of methane and carbon dioxide with the total flow rate of 80 mL/min (gas hourly space velocity (GHSV) = 48,000 mL/g·h). Due to the configuration of the experiment setup and the endothermicity of the DMR reaction, a cold spot along the fixed-bed reactor was observed and the actual temperature in the catalyst bed deviated from the set temperature. During our activity measurements, the center of the catalyst bed was approximately 725 °C when the reaction temperature was set to 750 °C. The practical Ni and Nd loadings in the calcined catalysts were accorded with the theoretical values, indicating that Ni2+ and Nd3+ were deposited stoichiometrically (Table 1 ). Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm and pore size distribution of the reduced catalysts clearly showed type IV isotherm with H3 hysteresis loop for Ni1Nd9, Ni3Nd7, Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 catalysts (Fig. 1 ), revealing the typical mesoporous structures of these catalysts [37], whereas the reduced Ni9Nd1 catalyst exhibited no mesoporous structure. With the increasing of Ni loading, the hysteresis loop became larger and the condensation step apparently shifted to lower P/P 0 value, and the pore diameter correspondingly became smaller. Ni3Nd7 displayed a flat porous distribution extending to 120 nm, yielding a largest average pore size (D p: 69.3 nm) and the second lowest S BET value of 8 m2/g. Ni7Nd3 presented a narrow mesoporous distribution at 25 nm. Despite of its largest pore volume (V p: 0.12 cm3/g), this catalyst showed the largest S BET value mainly owing to its smallest D p (18.2 nm). Ni5Nd5 exhibited a wide mesoporous distribution till 100 nm, with two fold bigger D p than Ni7Nd3. In the case of Ni9Nd1, although it exhibited a similar D p with Ni1Nd9, a rapid decline in V p induced a quick shrinkage in surface area. Notably, only Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 remained typical mesoporous structure after reduction. The mesopores could enlarge the specific surface area and improve the metal dispersion, which could provide an efficient contact space between reactant gases and catalysts [38,39]. XRD patterns for the reduced catalysts evidenced the reduction of NiO (Fig. 2 ). The observed diffraction peaks at 21–23°, 28–32°, 42–47° corresponded to the crystal planes in nickel neodymium oxide (NiNd2O4) (JCPDS PDF #21-1274) and neodymium oxide (Nd2O3) species (JCPDS PDF #21-0579). A set of well-formed peaks at 2θ values of 44.5°, 51.8° and 76.4° in reduced Ni9Nd1 confirmed the high crystallinity degree for active nickel metal phase (JCPDS PDF #04-0850). The full-width at half-maximum of Ni peaks at 44.5° increased from Ni1Nd9 to Ni9Nd1, implying the increase of Ni crystal size from 16.5 nm in Ni3Nd7 to 20.2 nm in Ni9Nd1 (Table 1). It should be noticed that the strongest peak of NiNd2O4 originally at 31.9° moved to the lower angle (31.4°). Actually, with the increase of nickel nitrate dosage, more nickel oxide were produced, as confirmed by the gradually growing diffraction peak at 44.5°. Due to the partial lack of Ni2+ to form NiNd2O4, Nd3+ ions could replace the Ni2+ site and thereby, altering the stoichiometric composition of NiNd2O4. This occupation of Nd3+ (98.3 pm), which has a larger ionic radius than Ni2+ (69 pm) resulted in the enlarged lattice constant, correspondingly shifting the diffraction peak to the lower diffraction angle. The reducibility of Ni2+ species in the catalyst controls the formation of Ni0 as active site for dry reforming of methane. H2-TPR was employed to investigate the reducibility of Ni2+ species in the calcined Ni x Nd1-x catalysts (Fig. 3 ). Reducibility was discussed in terms of temperatures where the peaks were observed. Pure NiO could be reduced to metallic Ni species from 240 °C to 410 °C with a peak maxima at 385 °C [40]. For pure Nd2O3, no apparent reduction peak was detected below 800 °C (result not shown), verifying that all reduction peaks of the Ni x Nd1-x catalysts could be absolutely attributed to the reduction of Ni2+ relevant species. The reduction behavior of the Ni x Nd1-x catalysts depended on the molar ratio of Ni/Nd. Ni1Nd9 displayed one reduction peak around 360 °C, which could be assigned to the facile reduction of bulky NiO particles. In the case of Ni3Nd7 catalyst, there was a strong reduction peak at 335 °C with a shoulder peak at 413 °C. This higher temperature peak was related to the NiO species having weak interaction with Nd2O3 species. The other three catalysts displayed broad reduction peaks above 500 °C. This behavior came from the strong interaction between the dispersed Ni2+ species and Nd2O3 support after calcination at high temperature. Clearly, according to TPR profile of Ni5Nd5 catalyst, three kinds of nickel oxide existed, including free NiO, Ni2+ possessing weak and strong interaction with Nd2O3. The much lower and wider reduction zone in Ni7Nd3 catalyst indicated the ratio of Ni2+ possessing strong interaction with Nd2O3 in Ni7Nd3 was higher than that in Ni5Nd5. The higher nickel loading in the catalyst remarkably changed the reducibility behavior. A major reduction peak at much higher temperature (718 °C) in Ni9Nd1 catalyst implied that Ni2+ having strong interaction with Nd2O3 dominated the nickel species. The reduction degree (Fig. 3) calculated based on the overall experimental H2 consumption was in the range of 40–50%, indicating that the reduction process was not complete. The low reduction degree was related to the formation of the spinel phase, as spinel phase was hard to be reduced at 800 °C. Parallel results regarding the composition of the Nd x Ni1-x catalysts were obtained from XPS measurement. All the reduced Ni x Nd1-x catalysts presented main peaks of Ni 2p 3/2 at about 852.4 ± 0.4, 854 ± 0.4 and 856 ± 0.4 eV, with a shake-up satellite peak for Ni2+ species at around 861 eV (Fig. 4 a1-e1). The signal at circa 852 eV could be assigned to metallic Ni [41], while the small peak at 853.6–854.4 eV was comparable with those reported for Ni2+ [42,43], which was ascribed to local screening from lattice oxygen adjacent to the Ni 2p core hole. The high peak located at 856 ± 0.4 eV corresponded to the nonlocal screening effects in Ni2+ [44,45]. This peak exhibited a good symmetry, thereby indicating the presence of one single type of Ni2+ species. According to non-complete reduction in the H2-TPR profiles, this band at 856 eV was attributed to Ni2+ ions in the network of NiNd2O4 spinel phase, which was difficult to reduce under 800 °C even in pure hydrogen flow. On the basis of the fact that the characteristic band of Ni2+ were more intense than those of Ni0 in all the reduced catalysts, the predominant presence of the spinel was confirmed. A careful examination of the O 1s core level indicated more complex situation at the catalyst surface. As for the oxygen region (Fig. 4 a2-e2), the small peak at 528.6 eV for Ni1Nd9 and 529.2–529.8 eV for both Ni3Nd7 and Ni5Nd5 catalysts represented for oxygen anions in neodymium oxides [46] and the binding energy at 530.5 eV denoted the oxygen ions in nickel oxides [47]. The component at 532 eV could be ascribed to Nd and Ni hydroxyl groups because the earth oxides were very hygroscopic when exposed to the atmospheric conditions [48–50]. Consistent with the XPS analysis, TEM images revealed the presence of Nd2O3, Ni and NiNd2O4 (Fig. 5A) on the reduced Ni x Nd1-x catalysts. The distinct lattice fringes with the spacing of 0.32 nm in Ni1Nd9 could be assigned to (2 2 2) plane of Nd2O3 (JCPDS PDF #21-0579). This lattice fringes implied the aggregation of Nd2O3 particles during the reduction process. Although the specific surface area of the reduced Ni1Nd9 was higher than that of Ni3Nd7 and Ni9Nd1 (Table 1), nickel in Ni1Nd9 might be covered or encapsulated by the aggregated Nd2O3, thus leading to a lower surface nickel exposure on the Ni1Nd9 surface (Table 1). The well-defined lattice fringes in the reduced Ni3Nd7, Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 catalysts could be assigned to the (1 0 3) plane of NiNd2O4 (JCPDS PDF #21-1274). HAADF images (Fig. 5B) showed that the Nd species uniformly distributed in the Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 catalysts, while the Ni species mainly aggregated into Ni particles and distributed in the edge. As for the reduced Ni9Nd1 catalyst, in the area marked with a red circle (Fig. 5A) the lattice spacing was 0.20 nm, which corresponded to the facet (1 1 1) of metallic Ni phase (JCPDS PDF #04-0850). These nickel particles were cladded by amorphous neodymium oxide (Fig. 5B). The outer neodymium oxide prevented the contact of feed gas with Ni9Nd1 catalyst surface and thus was not conductive to the catalytic performance. The STEM images also revealed that the size of the aggregated nickel particles in Ni5Nd5 (11.7 ± 2 nm) and Ni7Nd3 (12.5 ± 3.3 nm) were smaller than in Ni9Nd1 (22.0 ± 3.9 nm). The results of XRD measurements were in accord with these findings. Based on the results in TEM images, it could be inferred that the Ni/Nd molar ratio affected the composition of the Ni x Nd1-x catalysts: when the Ni/Nd molar ratio was in the range of 3/7 to 7/3, NiNd2O4 predominantly and abundantly existed; when the Ni/Nd molar ratio was below 3/7 or beyond 7/3, either Nd2O3 or metallic nickel became the main phase. Different morphologies in the reduced Ni x Nd1-x catalysts from another aspect reflected the level of the reduction degree obtained from TPR profiles. Ni1Nd9 catalyst with the lowest nickel loading was easily to be reduced, however, in the reduced Ni1Nd9 catalyst, Nd2O3 gradually encapsulated the Ni species and made the remaining Ni2+ difficult to reduce, leading to its reducibility only 50% (Fig. 3). As for the Ni3Nd7, Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 catalysts, because of the formation of NiNd2O4, their reducibility (41–45%) was lower than that of Ni1Nd9. In the case of Ni9Nd1 catalyst, due to the higher Ni loading, some part of larger size of NiO could be easily reduced, contributing 52% to the reducibility. However, the remaining nickel species in stronger interaction with support was difficult to be reduced. Using high GHSV and absent inert gases such as nitrogen and argon during DRM reaction was much closer to the industrial situation and high GHSV is also favorable to eliminate the external mass transfer limitations [51–53]. Hence, a relatively high GHSV of 48,000 mL/g·h were employed in this study. In order to obtain intrinsic catalytic performance, the internal mass transfer (e.g., intra-particle diffusion) was examined over the Ni5Nd5 catalyst with three particle sizes (150–180, 180–250, 250–380 μm) (Table S1). The reactions were carried out at constant gas flow rate (80 mL/min) and constant reaction temperature (750 °C). The conversions of methane and carbon dioxide were almost not changed. Thus, the effect of intra-particle diffusion could be ignored at the present reaction conditions. The catalytic performance of the reduced Ni x Nd1-x catalysts in dry reforming of methane were studied at 550–800 °C (Fig. S1). At 550 °C, less than 10% of CH4 was converted over Ni1Nd9, possibly owing to the low surface Ni (Table 1). For the Ni3Nd7 catalyst, the conversions of CH4 and CO2 were 13.1 and 17.9%, respectively. The slightly higher conversion of CO2 compared to that of CH4 possibly resulted from the reverse water gas shift reaction between CO2 and formed H2 (RWGS: CO2 + H2 → CO + H2O ΔH°298 K = 41 kJ/mol) [54]. Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 showed the similar high catalytic performance with Ni3Nd7 because of their high surface Ni. However, the conversions of CH4 and CO2 declined to 6.8 and 13.9% for Ni9Nd1 catalyst. Though the surface Ni of Ni9Nd1 (18.6 μmol/g-cat.) was twice as high as that of Ni3Nd7 (9.2 μmol/g-cat.), the surface area for Ni9Nd1 was the smallest (0.73 m2/g), which reduced the exposed Ni surface for the reaction. Due to the endothermic characteristic of methane dry reforming, with increasing reaction temperature both CH4 and CO2 conversions increased monotonically. Over the most active Ni5Nd5 catalyst, at 800 °C, 52.1% of CH4 and 57.6% of CO2 were converted. The distinct difference of CH4 conversion among five catalysts was at 750 °C, following the trend of Ni5Nd5 > Ni7Nd3 > Ni3Nd7 > Ni1Nd9 > Ni9Nd1. This trend was generally in parallel with the increment of Ni amount on the catalyst surface (Table 1), indicating that more active metallic nickel particles available on the catalyst's surface would favor the DRM reaction. The values of H2/CO deviated from unity especially at low temperatures regardless of the catalysts, which could be ascribed to the thermodynamic effect of RWGS reaction. At a relatively low reaction temperature, there existed a high unconverted CO2 concentration which favored the RWGS forward reaction thus leading to a lower H2/CO ratio as well as higher CO2 conversion than CH4 conversion [55]. The long-term catalytic stability was carried out at 750 °C for 24 h over the Ni x Nd1-x catalysts (Fig. S2). Thermodynamic equilibrium conversions were calculated by HSC Chemistry software that gave the equilibrium vapor pressures of the possible gas phase species (CH4, H2O, CO2, CO, H2) using the Gibbs free-energy minimization procedure, and the calculations were obtained without allowing carbon deposit formation. The actual CH4 and CO2 conversions were below those corresponding to thermodynamic equilibrium, which were respectively 83.4 and 90.4% under 750 °C. This meant that the catalytic activities were governed by the intrinsic properties of the catalysts. Evidently, CH4 conversion and CO2 conversion achieved by using Ni5Nd5 or Ni7Nd3 as the catalysts were significantly more stable than those achieved by Ni1Nd9, Ni3Nd7 and Ni9Nd1 catalysts. CH4 and CO2 specific activity (Fig. 6A and B) were calculated using Ni surface loading which were determined by H2 chemisorption (Table 1). Notably, a six times increase in exposed surface nickel from 1.54 (Ni1Nd9) to 9.2 (Ni3Nd7) μmol/g-cat. resulted in a nearly five times decrease in both CH4 specific activity (from 3.4 to 0.7 mol·mmol−1(Ni)·min−1) and CO2 specific activity (from 4.7 to 0.94 mol·mmol−1(Ni)·min−1). Although further increase in surface Ni to 36 μmol/g-cat. (Ni5Nd5) brought about a significant decrease in the initial specific activity, CH4 specific activity became more stable. The most stable and satisfied result was obtained for Ni7Nd3 catalyst with CH4 and CO2 specific activities of 0.28 and 0.35 mol·mmol−1(Ni)·min−1, respectively. The lowest specific activity was observed over Ni9Nd1 catalyst during 6 h on stream, which might be caused by the low surface area and large nickel particles leaded by amorphous Nd2O3 species after reduction. Apparently, the specific activity was irrelevant with the nickel loading in the catalysts. In the case of stability, Ni1Nd9 catalyst exhibited severe deactivation over 6 h of time on stream (Fig. 6B). Evident deactivation was also observed in Ni3Nd7 (Fig. 6A), with the decline from 0.71 to 0.23 mol·mmol−1(Ni)·min−1 and from 0.94 to 0.33 mol·mmol−1(Ni)·min−1 during 24 h reaction for the CH4 and CO2 specific activities, respectively. The obvious deactivation of Ni1Nd9 and Ni3Nd7 was probably due to the weak interaction between Ni and Nd2O3 species, as evidenced by the H2-TPR profiles. In contrast, Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 displayed the remarkable activity and stability during 24 h reaction, with CH4 specific activity slightly descending from 0.21 to 0.18 mol·mmol−1(Ni)·min−1 and from 0.28 to 0.26 mol·mmol−1(Ni)·min−1, respectively. Their good stability correlated well with the results of N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and H2 chemisorption. In comparison with 8 m2/g for Ni3Nd7, the surface area increased two times to 15 m2/g for Ni5Nd5, and 3.5 times to 29 m2/g for Ni7Nd3 catalyst. This result indicated that high surface area and more surface nickel were conductive to the catalyst stability. The surface Ni species in Ni5Nd5 (36 μmol/g-cat.) and Ni7Nd3 (24 μmol/g-cat.) catalysts were appropriate because of the well-distribution of Ni species active sites distributed over a large surface area. On the other hand, the effective mesopore size in Ni5Nd5 (D p: 40.3 nm) and Ni7Nd3 (D p: 18.2 nm) (Fig. 1) increased the accessibility of active sites and mass transport. The excellent stability of these two catalysts during the reforming reaction was also related to the small metal size [56,57] and strong metal-support interaction [58], which could effectively keep control of carbon formation and sintering during DRM. The ratio of H2/CO (Fig. 6C) mostly followed the semblable tendency with CH4 specific activity. When nickel loading increased from Ni1Nd9 to Ni3Nd7, an obvious increase from 0.63 to 0.70 was observed, which was in correspondence with the variation in their surface nickel (Table 1). Gradual decrease in H2/CO with the ongoing reaction for Ni1Nd9 and Ni3Nd7 were in accordance with respective descending CH4 and CO2 specific activity. With the continuous increase of surface nickel to 24 (Ni7Nd3) and 36 μmol/g-cat. (Ni5Nd5), H2/CO ratio reached the highest value at 0.73–0.75, suggesting a high H2 selectivity and formation rate over these catalysts. However, after further increasing nickel loading until Ni9Nd1, the H2/CO ratio dropped to 0.6 and displayed an ascending trend with 6 h of time on stream. This was due to the higher reaction rate of methane decomposition (CH4 → C + H2 ΔH°298 K = 75 kJ/mol) than that of RWGS. An appropriate ratio of Ni/Nd at 5/5 and 7/3 were fundamental to achieve high activity and stability due to the enhancement of the metal dispersion and interaction of NiO or Ni2+ species with the support. Once the Ni/Nd mole ratio exceeded the optimum range, Ni species surface was covered by thick amorphous Nd2O3 after reduction treatment, which restrained the contact of Ni species with feed gases and hence induced poor activity and stability. Pore structure [28], coke-deposition [59,60] and metal sintering [14] were usually the main reasons for the catalyst decay under DRM atmosphere. In order to gain more information about the probable reasons for the deactivation, the spent Ni x Nd1-x catalysts were characterized by TG analysis, XRD, nitrogen physisorption and TEM. Deposited carbon over the spent catalysts after 24 h DRM reaction at 750 °C was quantified by means of TG-DTA analysis (Fig. 7 ). TG curves contained three segments: the slight weight loss below 200 °C, the weight increment in 200–400 °C and the weight loss between 400 and 1000 °C (Fig. 7A). The weight decrease in the initial part was ascribed to the desorption of moisture and other gases [61–63]. The weight increase in the second part could be assigned to the oxidation of nickel [63] and appeared proportional to the nickel content in the catalyst. The mild decrease in the high temperature was assigned to the oxidation of deposited carbon [64,65]. In the case of spent Ni9Nd1, the obvious and continuous weight gain from 575 up to 1000 °C was due to the oxidation of large amount of metallic nickel. Because of the oxidation of nickel or carbon deposits on the Ni x Nd1-x catalyst surfaces, the exothermal effect could be shown in DTA curves (Fig. 7B). The evident positive peaks (exothermic peaks) between 300 and 350 °C (black vertical line) corresponded to the oxidation of nickel on the spent Ni x Nd1-x catalysts [52]. The tiny exothermic peaks over the spent Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 catalysts at 520 (blue vertical line) and 400 °C (pink vertical line), respectively, could be ascribed to the oxidation of carbon. According to the study of Zhang et al. [66], the exothermic peak above 430 °C was attributed to the oxidation of the graphitic carbon, which was difficult to remove during DRM reaction. Apparently, the higher temperature (>430 °C) peaks in Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 appeared in low intensity, indicating the less graphitic carbon on these two catalysts. Although the carbon deposition increased with the nickel loading in the Ni x Nd1-x catalysts (Table 2 ), the spent Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 produced less inert carbon and realized the best carbon resistant ability than the other catalysts, thus no significant decrease in their activity were observed. On the other hand, the noticeable exothermic peak at 480 °C (red vertical line) over Ni3Nd7 catalyst confirmed the combustion and/or oxidation of deposited inert carbon, which was responsible for the deactivation of the nickel-based catalyst in the DRM reaction [61,67]. In the case of Ni9Nd1 catalyst, the serious carbon deposition (7.01 × 10−2 g C/g-catalyst) obviously contributed to the continuous decrease of catalyst activity in term of conversions in the 6 h. The mean Ni crystal sizes of the used Ni x Nd1-x catalysts calculated from XRD patterns were in the range of 14.4–20.5 nm (Table 2). Comparison of the reduced (Table 1) and spent catalysts showed no significant change in the particle size. This indicated that five catalysts did not suffer from severe sintering during the DRM reaction. The porous structures in the spent Ni x Nd1-x catalysts changed to different extent (Fig. 8 ). Except for Ni9Nd1, the other four catalysts exhibited type IV isotherm with H3 hysteresis loop (Fig. 8A). In Ni1Nd9, Ni3Nd7 and Ni9Nd1 catalysts, the surface area (S BET) and pore volume (V p) decreased upon reaction, whereas the average pore size (D p) showed an increasing trend. Specifically, D p of the spent Ni1Nd9 (99.7 nm) was four times larger than the reduced one (23.7 nm), S BET correspondingly sharply decreased to 0.34 m2/g. These three catalysts showed a serious reduction in surface area, which could be attributed to carbon deposition and sintering, however since XRD showed no increase in nickel particle size after reaction (Table 2), the major contribution might be from the carbon deposition. When merely correlated carbon deposition amount with the catalytic stability, the low amount of carbon did not always lead to the smaller activity loss in terms of the CH4 and CO2 conversions. For example, Ni1Nd9 showed the lowest carbon deposition with only 3.89 × 10−3 g C/g-catalyst (Table 2) however it deactivated quickly within 6 h. This result suggested that the accumulated carbon species was an important but not the sole factor to cause the deterioration of catalytic stability. Although the carbon deposition on the spent Ni1Nd9 and Ni3Nd7 were less than that on Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3, the produced carbon on the former two catalysts led to the blockage of small pore channels. Differently, the carbon deposition on the spent Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 catalysts did not decrease the surface area. Compared with the reduced counterpart, the spent Ni7Nd3 preserved the same S BET (29 m2/g) and V p (0.12 cm3/g), and the spent Ni5Nd5 even displayed larger S BET (21 m2/g) and V p (0.13 cm3/g) (Fig. 8B). This result suggested these two catalysts generated carbon deposition that were not enough to cover small mesopore channels, and the porous nature of the generated carbon contributed some micropores to maintaining the surface area [68,69], as evidenced by the decrease in D p and increase in V p in the case of Ni5Nd5 catalyst. The morphology of the spent Ni x Nd1-x catalysts occurred the dramatic change after DRM reaction (Fig. 9 ). Compared with the reduced catalyst, the lattice fringe of the spent Ni1Nd9 became uniform, the lattice spacing of 0.32 nm was attributed to the (2 2 2) plane of Nd2O3 (JCPDS 21-0579). Ordered lattice fringe was observed as well in the spent Ni3Nd7 with the lattice spacing of 0.28 nm, which was ascribed to (1 0 3) plane of NiNd2O4 (JCPDS 21-1274). Interestingly, the structures of the spent Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 catalysts occurred the obvious deformation. Apart from NiNd2O4 phase, the metallic nickel phase (JCPDS 04-0850) with the lattice spacing of 0.20 nm was evidently observed, indicating that the nickel segregated from NiNd2O4 phase during the reaction. The segregated nickel for Ni5Nd5 was more notable than Ni7Nd3 catalyst, consequently, the excessive Ni exposed area was obtained in Ni5Nd5. Owing to the nickel segregating from the NiNd2O4 phase on the spent catalyst surface to become the active site, Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 showed the best reaction stability among all Ni x Nd1-x catalysts. As for the spent Ni9Nd1, the size of nickel particles observed in the TEM with the lattice spacing of 0.20 nm (JCPDS 04-0850) and the amorphous neodymium oxide still existed. TEM images implied the morphology of the spent Ni x Nd1-x catalysts occurred reconstruction when the catalysts contacted with feed gases at high temperature, e.g., Nd2O3 and NiNd2O4 phases were respectively integrated in the spent Ni1Nd9 and Ni3Nd7; the distinct Ni particles along with NiNd2O4 were segregated in the Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 catalysts; the Ni particles were apparent in Ni9Nd1. In this work, the stable CH4 and CO2 conversions over Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 catalysts were likely due to the segregated nickel on the NiNd2O4 phase during the DRM reaction. This particular phase could continuously provide more nickel active sites for the DRM reaction. Only upon reduction by hydrogen which was carried out in-situ before the reaction, the reduction degree for the Ni x Nd1-x catalyst was in the range of 40–50%, indicating half of the Ni2+ was not reduced. This was because of the hard reduction of NiNd2O4 phase. The initially reduced nickel crystallites were the initial active sites for the DRM reaction. The spinel NiNd2O4 phase was presumably much less active than metallic Ni particles towards the dry reforming reaction. As the reaction proceeded, more nickel particles and NiC intermediates would also act as the active sites like in the Ni/gamma-Al2O3 catalyst [70]. From this point of view, Ni and NiNd2O4 phases (Fig. 9) were indispensable for obtaining the stable reactivity. Their coexistence could be considered as the Ni0 supported on NiNd2O4. The strong interaction between Ni0 and NiNd2O4 support stabilized the catalyst and prevented the inert carbon deposition. These results were consistent with previous result discussed by Mohamed et al. [71]. They found in the high temperature reduction, Co was strongly anchored onto the Co−Al spinel, and the strong metal-support interaction of Co and Co−Al spinel stabilized and prevented the sintering of Co particles, enhancing the efficiency for dry reforming reaction. In the case of Ni9Nd1, more Ni species easily induced carbon deposition and led to the serious decline in the surface area, thus decreasing the catalyst stability. In the cases of Ni1Nd9 and Ni3Nd7 catalysts, Nd2O3 and NiNd2O4 were the main phases, respectively. These two catalysts yielded quick deactivation because of the lack of enough Ni0 species to activate methane molecule. Even if some trace of Ni0 was produced during the reduction, they were trapped by Nd2O3 and NiNd2O4 quickly during the DRM reaction, as shown by the TEM images for the spent catalysts. Therefore, the control of Ni and NiNd2O4 in a well balance in Ni x Nd1-x catalysts could inhibit the inert carbon deposition and favor the methane activation, thereby promoting the catalyst stability. Ni x Nd1-x catalysts with different Ni/Nd molar ratios (Ni/Nd = 1:9, 3:7, 5:5, 7:3 and 9:1) were successfully synthesized via co-precipitation method. Different Ni/Nd ratio in Ni x Nd1-x catalysts significantly affected the structural properties and catalytic activity as well as the stability in dry reforming of methane (DRM). The following conclusions were drawn based on this study: (1) Ni/Nd molar ratio affected the formation of neodymium nickel oxide on the reduced Ni x Nd1-x catalysts: when the Ni/Nd molar ratio was in the range of 3/7 to 7/3, NiNd2O4 predominantly and abundantly existed; when the Ni/Nd molar ratio was below 3/7 or beyond 7/3, either Nd2O3 or metallic nickel was the main phase. (2) During DRM reaction at 750 °C, CH4 conversion for five catalysts followed the sequence of Ni5Nd5 > Ni7Nd3 > Ni3Nd7 > Ni1Nd9 > Ni9Nd1. The highest catalytic activity of the Ni5Nd5 catalyst in syngas production was due to more active metallic nickel particles (36 μmol/g-cat.) available on the catalyst's surface. (3) Carbon deposition was not the sole factor to cause the deterioration of DRM stability. Except for Ni9Nd1, in which deactivation was caused by carbon formation (carbon deposition: 7.01 × 10−2 g C/g-catalyst), the detailed deactivation reasons for other catalysts varied. After DRM tests at 750 °C for 24 h, carbon deposition followed the order: Ni9Nd1 > Ni7Nd3 > Ni5Nd5 > Ni3Nd7 > Ni1Nd9. The good carbon-resistance ability for Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 catalysts was attributed to the effective mesopore size (average pore size: 40.3 and 18.2 nm for Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3, respectively) which accommodated the deposited carbon without clogging the pore structure. In addition, the larger surface area and pore volume facilitated the accessibility of reactants to well-distributed Ni sites, which fulfilled the pre-requisites for preserving the stable reactivity. Whereas, in Ni1Nd9 catalyst, Ni species was covered by thick amorphous Nd2O3 after reduction treatment, which limited the contact of Ni species with feed gases, thus leading to poor stability. (4) With different molar ratio of Ni/Nd, the morphology in Ni x Nd1-x catalysts occurred rearrangement during the DRM reaction. In Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 catalysts, the segregated Ni0 on NiNd2O4 improved the interaction between Ni0 and NiNd2O4, which minimized carbon deposition and enhanced the catalyst stability. Ni/Nd molar ratio affected the formation of neodymium nickel oxide on the reduced Ni x Nd1-x catalysts: when the Ni/Nd molar ratio was in the range of 3/7 to 7/3, NiNd2O4 predominantly and abundantly existed; when the Ni/Nd molar ratio was below 3/7 or beyond 7/3, either Nd2O3 or metallic nickel was the main phase. During DRM reaction at 750 °C, CH4 conversion for five catalysts followed the sequence of Ni5Nd5 > Ni7Nd3 > Ni3Nd7 > Ni1Nd9 > Ni9Nd1. The highest catalytic activity of the Ni5Nd5 catalyst in syngas production was due to more active metallic nickel particles (36 μmol/g-cat.) available on the catalyst's surface. Carbon deposition was not the sole factor to cause the deterioration of DRM stability. Except for Ni9Nd1, in which deactivation was caused by carbon formation (carbon deposition: 7.01 × 10−2 g C/g-catalyst), the detailed deactivation reasons for other catalysts varied. After DRM tests at 750 °C for 24 h, carbon deposition followed the order: Ni9Nd1 > Ni7Nd3 > Ni5Nd5 > Ni3Nd7 > Ni1Nd9. The good carbon-resistance ability for Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 catalysts was attributed to the effective mesopore size (average pore size: 40.3 and 18.2 nm for Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3, respectively) which accommodated the deposited carbon without clogging the pore structure. In addition, the larger surface area and pore volume facilitated the accessibility of reactants to well-distributed Ni sites, which fulfilled the pre-requisites for preserving the stable reactivity. Whereas, in Ni1Nd9 catalyst, Ni species was covered by thick amorphous Nd2O3 after reduction treatment, which limited the contact of Ni species with feed gases, thus leading to poor stability. With different molar ratio of Ni/Nd, the morphology in Ni x Nd1-x catalysts occurred rearrangement during the DRM reaction. In Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 catalysts, the segregated Ni0 on NiNd2O4 improved the interaction between Ni0 and NiNd2O4, which minimized carbon deposition and enhanced the catalyst stability. Xiaoqing Yuan: Investigation, Writing - Original Draft. Baitao Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing-Review & Editing, Funding acquisition. Bin Li: Investigation, Methodology. Xiujun Wang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition.
abstract: The stable nickel catalyst for CO2 reforming of methane (DRM) is an indispensable requirement for boosting the efficiency of methane conversion to synthesis gas. Herein, without using the regularly ordered porous support, neodymium-promoted nickel catalysts were synthesized by co-precipitation method (denoted as Ni x Nd1-x catalysts, Ni/Nd = 1:9, 3:7, 5:5, 7:3 and 9:1). By regulating the Nd and Ni loadings, the nickel species in the reduced Ni x Nd1-x catalysts could be controlled. When the Ni/Nd molar ratio was in the range of 3/7 to 7/3, NiNd2O4 predominantly existed on the catalyst surface. Once the Ni/Nd mole ratio exceeded this range, Ni species surface was covered by thick amorphous Nd2O3. The activity during DRM was studied as a function of temperature (550–800 °C) and time on stream (750 °C, 24 h). Although carbon deposition increased with the nickel loading, the produced carbon did not block the porous structure in the spent Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 catalysts. A fraction of the nickel segregating on the spent catalyst surface to become the active site ensured Ni5Nd5 and Ni7Nd3 catalysts having the most active and stable catalytic performance. The activity results correlated well with the higher surface area of the starting Ni x Nd1-x catalysts, the smaller Ni crystallite sizes, and structural reconstructions during the DRM reaction.
title: Performance and stability in CO2 reforming of methane over neodymium nickel oxide composite catalysts

steps.method_details: nickel nitrate and neodymium nitrate dissolved in deionized water
NaOH added dropwise into solution until pH reached 12
slurry aged under vigorous stirring
refluxed at 85 °C
precipitate filtered and washed with deionized water
dried at 110 °C overnight
calcined at 850 °C for 4 h

0.1 g of sieved catalyst packed between quartz wool in reactor
catalyst reduced in-situ in pure hydrogen flow
