full_text: In the world today, only less than 12% of the total plastic produced is recycled [1]. However, in the European Union (EU) environmental legislation requires that the recycling capacity reaches 55% by the year 2030. Accuracy in this regard reveals the high potential of recycled plastic wastes [2]. Due to reasons such as cheapness [3,4], ease of use [3,5], availability [6,7], variety [6], wide deformation capability [4,7], etc., the use of plastic materials is very popular in the world [8]. Meanwhile, due to the lack of a proper mechanism, every year worrying amounts of wasted plastics are going for landfilling or polluting nature [9]. The landfill is commonly used as a simple method of all categories of waste utilization [10], but directives in the EU are extremely the effort to substitute them whit other suitable replacements [9]. It has been reported [11] that more than 50% of the plastics produced in the world are landfills without any recycling after just one use. In recent years, efforts have been made to recycle plastic waste, of which pyrolysis is one of the most successful [are 12]. Pyrolysis is one of the efficient processes in which in an inert atmosphere, heat causes the thermal decomposition of materials [13]. It should be noted that one of the main benefits of pyrolysis is its unique ability to convert low-energy materials into products with high energy content [3,14]. Another reason for the tendency towards pyrolysis is its ability to perform at low temperatures (500 ° C) [9]. Pyrolysis of plastic materials results in the production of three phases of products [7], which are carbon residues in the reactor [4], a mixture of organic liquids, and flammable light gases. The liquid product obtained from pyrolysis includes a mixture of paraffins [3,14], olefins [3,5,14], naphthenes [6] and valuable aromatics [3,4,13]. Therefore, it can also be used as a feedstock for the petrochemical industry [13]. On the other hand, if separated, it is possible to use produced aromatics in the chemical industry [15], pharmaceuticals [16], paint manufacturing, pesticide synthesis [17]. From another perspective, organic liquids produced from the plastic pyrolysis contain compounds that are very similar to the conventional fuels in the transportation industry. For this reason, they can be used as a reliable substitute for fossil reserves [1,12]. The boundless need of the current growing world has led to the depreciation of oil [18,19] and gas resources to produce the fuels required in the vast transportation industry. On the other hand, the high cost of investing and operating in the oil and gas industry has added to the difficulty of producing liquid fuels [20]. Pyrolysis of plastics can be the key to overcoming these problems, because at very low cost, process equipment, cheap and abundant feed, have the potential to produce liquid fuels. In addition, pyrolysis investment costs are lower than other chemical recycling processes of plastics such as gasification [4]. Due to the importance of this issue, good research has been done on a laboratory and even industrial scale using various reactors. Among the reactors used in the pyrolysis process are the following: batch [9], semi-batch [6], continuous [12,21], fixed bed [3,5], fluidized bed [4,8] and conical spouted bed reactors. Among the types of reactors used, fixed bed reactors are considered a good choice for research applications due to their ease of operation and low operating costs. In addition to the above advantages mentioned for fixed bed reactors, in laboratory research, these reactors can operate with small amounts of feed and also operate and control reaction conditions in them is easy [22]. Also, to better understand the pyrolysis process and to obtain a suitable product, several parameters such as temperature [3,6,7,9,13,23], pressure [4], reaction time [3,4,14], heat transfer rate [7] and addition of biomass [9] to feed has been investigated by researchers. One reliable method for studying the kinetic parameters of pyrolysis is the TGA method. Kim and Oh [23], after extensive research on polyethylene TGA using a variety of different kinetic models, eventually proposed two models, the dynamic model and the Friedman model, as the most accurate models. Based on the results obtained from TGA and DTG, Das and Tiwari [6] determined the appropriate temperature range for the pyrolysis reactor and then determined the suitable conditions by examining the properties of the obtained liquid fuel. After working with TGA and with the help of several kinetic methods, Duque et al. [7] showed that temperature stress reduces the activation energy of polyethylene pyrolysis. Sun et al. [3] Categorized the products formed during pyrolysis into three groups: alkanes, alkenes, and aromatics, and found that increasing residence time visibly led to an increase in aromatic production. Mechanical studies by Liu et al. [14] Show that rising temperatures produce lighter compounds, which will result in more ethylene and propylene in the gas phase. Kassargy and colleagues [12] in a continuous reactor with polyethylene pyrolysis could produce high-quality fuels such as gasoline and diesel with high thermal value after separating the liquid phase sections. The use of catalysts in the pyrolysis process while reducing the pro time increases the quantity and quality of the liquid product. Various compounds have been used as catalysts in the pyrolysis of polymeric wastes, including alumina, silica aluminacess, and zeolites. Zeolite Y as a strong solid acid heterogeneous catalyst, using stable three-dimensional channel cavities with Fujasite structure [24], due to having cavities with a size of 0.76 nm has a high ability to break large molecules [25]. One of the main challenges in loading metals on the support surface is the lack of molecular distribution of particles, which is usually very difficult to overcome. Disruption of this distribution and its conversion to bulk distribution can cause the catalyst to agglomerate, which can increase the hotspot by intensifying the activity of the catalyst in a point, which is one of the factors reducing the activity of catalysts. In recent years, it has been reported [26] that the use of compounds obtained from the pyrolysis of organic metal frameworks leads to the molecular distribution of active sites on the surface of the catalyst. Another advantage of using this group of compounds is that they have a carbon skeleton that causes uniform surface reactions on the catalyst. The outcome of the literature review reveals that the research on the energy recycling for pyrolysis of polymers is very limited and not comparable to other topics in this field. Mahari et al. [27] with the simultaneous pyrolysis of HDPE and used frying oil (UFO) could identify up to 18% of combustible gases that can be used for gas turbines to generate electricity. It has been reported [28] that the use of microwave radiation in the pyrolysis system reduces energy consumption. Lam et al. [29] in co-pyrolysis using a batch reactor display that energy management and the use of microwave radiation can reduce the cost of diesel fuel by 52%. Cortazar et al. [30] were able to dramatically increase hydrogen production in the gas phase by changing the parameters under autothermal conditions. Hydrogen gas, due to its more calorific value, creates a high potential for energy recovery of the system by combustion of the gas phase [31,32]. Many studies in the literature deal with catalytic and thermal pyrolysis of various polymers. This attractiveness is evidence of the importance of the chemical recycling of these materials. The researchers give a parts indication of the probable discussions and practical methods, conditions and chemical reactor designs, kinetic of reactions, demonstrating the variety of target feed and quality of products in the pyrolysis process. Although some parameters, such as temperature and reaction time, have been frequently investigated by many researchers, the effect of some parameters, such as reactor loading and heavy polyethylene density as feed, and energy consumption in the process have not yet been studied in an organized manner by design of experiment. Inappropriately, the absence of a modest predictive functional model of pyrolysis is a hindrance to the development of applied methods of conversion. In an attempt to overcome this issue, the design of the experiment makes it possible to understand the general behavior of the system stylistically the way with the help of statistical and mathematical functions. It is also a parameter density that has different values for distinct HDPE samples and can be easily measured, so it is considered a useful operating parameter. Moreover, there is a deep lack of comprehensive research using the design of experiment to investigate the effects of density, reactor loading, and temperature on liquid fuel products. It is safe to say that the effects of feed density change have not been addressed in the previous research studies. The reason for this concealment can be attributed to the proximity of the values related to the density of different types of polyethylene. For example, for the two commercial types of PE4000 and PE35000 polyethylene manufactured by Sigma-Aldrich, the density of the polymer is 0.920and 0.910(g/mL −1), respectively, but for these two samples, the number-average molecular weights are 1700 and 7700 (g/mol −1) [4]. It is observed that only 1% change in density caused 353% change in number-average molecular weight. The important conclusion is that density is a very sensitive parameter and small changes in it may drastically alter the fundamental and molecular properties of polyethylene. However, measuring the molecular mass of a polymer is a difficult and costly task and may add to its difficulties in industry and manufacturing units. In contrast, pyrolysis behavior of the polymer can be observed by using density as a parameter that is simple, practical, and inexpensive to measure. No comprehensive study has been done on the reactor load, and this shortcoming is still felt. If the effect of the reactor load is determined, it can lead to the operation of the reactor with the highest possible capacity and economically. Temperature is a parameter that all researchers agree on its dramatic effects on upgrading processes such as pyrolysis, gasification, and reforming [33]. In addition, no detailed studies have been conducted on the importance of energy recovery in the pyrolysis process of plastics. In this study, based on thermodynamic calculations, there is a comprehensive discussion about the use of heat from the combustion of flammable gases leaving the reactor and also the sensible heat of this phase for energy recovery in the process. The results show a significant reduction in total energy consumption for the production of liquid fuels relying on energy recycling. Finally, the optimal product obtained from thermal pyrolysis was pyrolyzed by a new catalyst in which the compounds obtained from MIL-53 (Cu) pyrolysis were loaded on the surface of zeolite Y, which resulted in the production of high-octane number gasoline with a sharp reduction of heavy compounds. Pyrolysis experiments were performed using two of the most widely used high density polyethylene samples purchased from the local market in Tehran, Iran. The main feature of selected polymers is their widespread use in the food, home and packaging industries, which has led them to be among the components of municipal waste plastic. The first sample has a density of 959 (kg/m 3) and is considered light while the second, heavier sample has a density of 967 (kg/m 3). To better understand the type of feed, the physical characteristics of the two species used in this study are given in Table 1 . In this table, MFI represents of the melt flow index. In the case of two polymers of the same type, a higher value of this index actually indicates a higher density of the molten polymer. At first glance, the range of feed densities may seem narrow, but it should be noted that the same range of changes has had significant effects on some of the properties of the polymer. For example, with increasing feed density from 959 to 967 (kg/m 3), the MFI parameters of the samples changed from 3.5 to 5.0, which is an increase of 42%. According to the MFI definition, increasing it will have a great impact on the fluidity of the feed inside the reactor and thus facilitate thermal cracking processes. The size of the polymer spherical a particle used in the experiments for both species of feed was about 4 mm. The copper nitrate (Cu(NO 3)2. 3H 2 O,>98%), terephthalic acid (C 6 H 4(COOH)2,>98%,), N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) anhydrous (HCON(CH 3)2,>99.8%) and Dichloromethane (CH 2 Cl 2,>99.8%) used to synthesize MIL − 53 (Cu) were purchased from Merck. The sodium aluminate (NaAlO 2,>99%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH,>98%) and sodium trisilicate solution (Na 2 O(SiO 2) x . xH 2 O) were used to prepare zeolite Y, all of which were purchased from Merck. The ammonium nitrate (NH 4 NO 3,>98%) was used for cation exchange was purchased from Merck. The nitrogen (99.9%, Iran Gas, Iran) was used as the carrier gas in the reactor system. Process feed may contain small amounts of volatile substances due to factors such as moisture absorption or other organic matter. Some impurities may also enter the structure at low boiling points during polymer production processes. In order to remove such volatile materials, the process feed was heated in a laboratory oven for 3 h at 105 ° C before entering the reactor. Metal organic framework MIL − 53 (Cu), was synthesized by hydrothermal procedure based on reported at our previous work [15]. In a typical method, a mixture with the proportions of 1 mmol Cu(NO 3)2. 3H 2 O: 1 mmol C 6 H 4(COOH)2 with the suitable amount of DMF was prepared. The final mixture was stirred at 70 ° C for half an hour. After this time, before the temperature of the mixture decreased, all of it was transferred to a stainless-steel autoclave with Teflon coating and heated at 100 ° C for 24 h. Finally, the formed solution was washed three times with DMF and given one day each time, and the same thing was repeated three times with dichloromethane to remove any impurities from the final product. Zeolite Y was synthesized in an autoclave reactor by solvothermal method. The advantages of this method include the production of catalysts with high crystallinity and homogeneity of structure. In this method, sodium aluminate solution will be the source of aluminum and sodium trisilicate solution will be the precursor of silicon in the zeolite structure. Deionized water was also used for solution, and sodium hydroxide was used to provide the alkalinity of the reaction medium. All chemicals used in this synthesis were used directly without any additional purification. The amount of starting material required for the synthesis of zeolite Y was adjusted so that the molar ratio of the components in the final product is equal to the following: (4.62Na 2 O : 2Al 2 O 3 : 10SiO 2 : 180H 2 O). The resulting mixture is stirred thoroughly for one hour to obtain a uniform gel, which was after that aged for 24 h at room temperature. The resulting gel is then transferred to an autoclave with a Teflon inner coating and placed at 100 ° C for 24 h to complete the reaction and form Y zeolite crystals. The product was then washed with deionized water and then vacuum filtered by the Buchner funnel. The washing process continues until the pH of the product reaches a number in the range of 8 to 9. At this stage sodium form, zeolite is formed. However, this form of zeolite Y does not have much catalytic activity and must be converted to hydrogen in the future. For this purpose, each gram of zeolite was mixed with 10 mL of 1 M ammonium nitrate solution for 24 h, and after this time, the resulting slurry was filtered in the vacuum by the Buchner funnel, and the filtering process continued until pH 9 was reached. In this way, sodium ions are exchanged with ammonium ions, and this exchange is repeated four times. In the fourth stage, the obtained product is dried for 24 h at 120 ° C and then calcined at 550 ° C for 2 h at the time and temperature suggested by Yang et al. [34]. To prepare the final catalyst, a mixture of zeolite Y and MIL − 53 (Cu) was poured into 100mL of deionized water, and the resulting mixture was refluxed for 12 h with mixing at 100 ° C. The ratio of zeolite to MOF is such that after pyrolysis, it contains 10% of the residue obtained from MOF. Finally, to ensure uniformity, the mixture was irradiated with ultrasonic waves at a frequency of 37 kHz for 30 min. The uniform product was dried at 120°C and finally pyrolyzed at 500 ° C in an atmosphere at a nitrogen flow rate of 200 mL/min. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the synthesized catalysts were determined using Holland Philips Xpert diffractor (CoK α  = 0.178897 nm) radiation source to determine the purity of samples and their crystallinity over 0 o  < 2θ < 90 o . The samples were calcined for 6 h at 400 °C before X-ray diffraction analysis. The surface morphology of the sample was investigated by scanning electron microcopy (SEM) using a TESCAN Vega Model microscope equipment. Elemental analysis of raw materials as well as Y zeolite was performed using an X-ray fluorescence (XRF) equipment (PW2404 calibrated with Philips analytical X − Ray B. V. standards). The catalyst Brunauer-Emmet-Teller (BET) pore volume and surface area were measured by the nitrogen gas adsorption-desorption isotherms analysis at 77 K by BELSORP-mini II. However, before the BET test, the zeolite catalyst was heated to 400 °C for six hours to degas. Thermal pyrolysis of different types of the HDPE was performed to produce liquid fuel in a tubular fixed bed reactor, the details of which are shown in Fig. 1 . The volume of the reactor is such that 100 g of feed can be loaded inside each test. By placing quartz wool on both sides of the feed, its position in the middle of, the reactor is fixed. With accurate temperature measurements, it was found that the temperature distribution in the middle of, the tubular reactor was quite uniform. The importance of same temperature distribution is that it is necessary that the entire mass of the feed is always at the same temperature so that the behavior of the reactor and feed can be properly analyzed [35]. Reactor heating is done by a furnace equipped with a PID temperature control system, which has also been used in previous research [36]. The high accuracy and efficiency of the PID controller has led to its recommendation by researchers [9]. To begin each run, after loading the feed into the reactor, the inert nitrogen gas is first passed through the reactor bed at a flow rate of 100 ml/min for 30 minutes. Based on pre-testing and applying changes on the carrier gas flow rate and measuring the yield of the liquid product, a value of 100 was considered appropriate and this amount has also been reported by Zhang et al. [37]. The presence of carrier gas flow during pyrolysis has been evaluated as effective [9]. This is essential because it ensures that no air remains in the reactor. In previous studies [35,36,38], we have shown that the use of inert carrier gas prevents the re-sedimentation of some semi-heavy compounds in the hot space of the reactor and the formation of undesirable products. Nitrogen has been accepted by some researchers as a suitable carrier gas [39,40]. It should be noted, however, that some researchers have used other carrier gases, such as carbon dioxide. The use of carbon dioxide leads to pyrolysis towards gasification, which reduces the required temperature of the reactor [37,41]. The reactor temperature is then set to the desired value, and the reactor is switched on. The inert input gas is preheated in the initial part of the reactor until its temperature reaches the temperature of the reactor bed by preheating. This gas plays the role of the carrier of manufactured products. All products enter the cooling section of the system. In this part condensed all products with a boiling point above 0 ° C and form an organic liquid product. Nevertheless, light combustible gases, which include paraffin, olefins, and hydrogen, enter the flare after leaving the cooling section. The heating rate in the reactor is a constant value of 20 ° C/min and the same amount has been applied in some other studies [6]. This heat transfer rate for pyrolysis reactors has also been reported in other articles [3]. The liquid product obtained from the reactor is stored in containers without air penetration to prevent the evaporation of its volatile components. In order to analyze the compounds in the gas phase, a sample of non-condensed gases was collected in a particular bag before entering the flare. Collecting reactor exhaust gases in special bags is a common practice today [9,38]. Also, the reactor control system is equipped with a device that can determine the power consumption for each run by measuring the electric current consumed in the furnace. In fact, the main goal of this study is to increase the production of liquid fuel, the amount of which is calculated by the Eq. 1. (1) Liquid Fuel wt % = Mass of Fuel Mass of Polymer Feed × 100 However, to check the energy consumption of the reactor, the amount of gas produced must also be determined. For this purpose, the Eq. 2 is used. (2) Mass of Gas wt % = Mass of Polymer Feed − Mass of Fuel − Mass of Coke × 100 At the end of each run, the remaining coke values were collected and weighed at the bottom of the reactor. The coke formed was so small that it could be ignored without reducing the accuracy of the calculations. Non-condensable gases from polymer pyrolysis at the reactor outlet were collected in a special bag immediately after each test. The gas samples were then injected into a gas chromatography (Varian CP3800) with two columns packed and capillary (J & W CP − Sil 8 CB (30m × 0.53mm)) and Two detectors of flame ionization detector (FID) and thermal conductivity detector (TCD). Light hydrocarbons, including C 1 to C 4 are analyzed by FID and non-hydrocarbon gases such as H 2 and N 2 are analyzed by TCD. Gas chromatography mass spectrometry (GC MS) analysis was accomplished with a Varian-Staturn 2200 consisting of a gas chromatograph coupled to a mass spectrometer with an ion trap analyzer. To calculate the concentration of various compounds found in liquid fuel, based on a standard method, the area under the peak in the analysis is used. In order to better understand the process, elemental analysis for light and heavy feeds has been performed by the CHNSO Thermo Quest − Fash EA 1112 analyzer. The proximate and elemental analysis for two types of the polymer are shown in Table 2 , and They have also been compared with some of the results reported in the literatures [7,9,11,12,42]. The development of experimental design methods have led to be more accurate analyzes of process behavior [43,44]. Among the different experimental design methods, the surface response methodology is more desirable due to its high accuracy and efficiency [45]. A central composite design (CCD) design has been used to model reaction behavior in this process [46]. The answer predicted by the model is the Y in Eq. 3, which will depend on the independent variables. The importance of this equation is that it is quadratic and therefore, well capable of modeling response behavior [47]. β 0 is a constant number that can be positive or negative, and β i is the coefficient of linear variables that are expected to be significant for the main variables in the design. The significance of the parameters is evaluated by the p-value obtained from the analysis of variance (ANOVA). β ii is coefficient that describe the magnitude or smallness of nonlinear parameters. Also, β ij is important number because if it is significant, that indicate the interactive sentences between the variables. In the latter equation, ϵ is the error of the model, which is, in fact the same component that causes the results of the prediction by the model to be slightly different from what has been achieved in practice. However, the analysis of variance is also equipped with powerful tools for measuring the accuracy of the model, one of which are R 2 and R adj 2, which are described in the following sections. (3) Y = β 0 + ∑ i = 1 k β i x i + ∑ i = 1 k β ii x i 2 + ∑ i ∑ j β ij x i x j + ϵ i < j In order to better understand the operating conditions governing the process, a summary of the parameters along with the level values based on the RSM is given in Table 3 below. It should be explained that levels −1 and + 1 are the values that were initially selected. However, the levels +2, 0 and − 2, which are the forward, center and backward points, respectively, are determined by the RSM. Nevertheless, all five levels are operational points where reactor testing has taken place. Thermal analysis of polymer samples was performed using a TG 209 F1 Iris thermogravimetric analyzer (NETZSCH) device in which the inert nitrogen gas at a flow rate of 60 ml/min was used to prevent the oxygen reaction with the materials obtained from the polymer decomposition. In addition to thermal gravimetry analysis, useful derivative thermal gravimetric diagrams can also be obtained by this device. Correspondingly, the heating rate in the range of 10 to 50 ° C/min is set for each sample. Successful models have so far been presented for the kinetic process of the pure or mixed polyethylene pyrolysis process. In this section, an attempt is made to determine the activation energy of pyrolysis reaction for two light and heavy feeds from polymer by using the kinetic model. Comparisons between reactive activation energies can indicate the severity of pyrolysis and for this purpose, a dynamic model has been used. What is agreed is that the thermal decomposition of solids such as polymers and biomass, follows [7] Eq. 4. (4) dα dt = k T f α In Eq. 4, α is the same degree of reaction conversion, f(α) is the reaction model, and k(T) is the constant temperature-dependent rate constant, which can also be expressed as the Arrhenius equation. The Arrhenius form of the reaction rate constant will be expressed as Eq. 5. (5) k T = Aexp − E RT In the latter equation, A is the frequency factor of reaction (s −1), R is the universal gas constant, T is the absolute temperature of reaction (K) that is equal to 8.314 (Jmol −1 K −1) and E is the activation energy kJmol −1. For all kinetic studies of chemical reactions [14], the conversion equation will be Eq. 6 [23]: (6) dα dt = A 1 − α n exp − E RT In Eq. 6 n refers the overall reaction order. The rate of heat transfer, or β (Ks −1), is defined according to Eq. 7: (7) β = dT dt Combining Eqs. 6 and 7 will lead to a beneficial relationship such as Eq. 8 [4]: (8) β dα dT = 1 − α n Aexp − E RT The dynamic model states that by combining the recent equation with the theory of collision [23] and rewriting the equations, finally drawing Lnβ versus 1/T will lead to a straight line [4,7]. The slope of this line is −E/R and the frequency factor can be calculated using the slope of the line, and the intercept [23]. Pyrolysis is presently a current technique to energy content recovering of utilized polymers. From an engineering point of view, change in the conditions can modify the pyrolysis product constituent distribution, But, from the precise molecular perspective, the mechanism of this process has not yet been fully elucidated. Polyethylene is also considered as a process feed because recent findings suggest that the polyethylene family alone accounts for more than half of the plastics produced in the world [4]. TGA is a methodical way that examines the weight loss of a substance with increasing temperature over a period of time. Fig. 2(a) examines the effect of the change in heat transfer rate for heavy polymer feed in the temperature range of 650 to 850 ° C. In this figure, the vertical axis is the same as the weight fraction, which is equal to the difference in conversion from one, and the horizontal axis is the absolute temperature. The difference between the curves is in the heating rate, which varies from 10 to 50 ° C/min. The heating rate is one of the basic components in the thermal analysis of organic matter [9]. As the temperature of the polymer rises, the material first melts, but a further increase in temperature causes the chemical bonds to break and the sample to heat up. In the absence of air and when the sample is heated in an inert nitrogen atmosphere, the pyrolysis process takes place instead of burning. Therefore, what happens will be quite similar to the transformations that occur in a pyrolysis reactor. Thermal decomposition for all samples occurs first with an increasing slope, and after passing through an extreme, the slope of the graph decreases and eventually becomes horizontal. In fact, pyrolysis will start from the point of change of the initial slope [7]. Behavioral patterns are almost the same for all curves, but increasing the heat transfer rate will delay the thermal decomposition. Another benefit of this form is that it can determine the appropriate temperature range for the reactor. Since the heating rate in the pyrolysis reactor used in this study is 20  min / ° C, the temperature range of the reactor is based on the suitable TGA curve in the Fig. 2(a). According to the diagram, the temperature range is in a window from 702 to 779 K or (429 to 506) ° C. This temperature range is exactly in the temperature range of the pyrolysis reactor of this study, that is, we will have: (415 < 429 < 506 < 585) ° C. The results clearly indicate that the temperature range used to design the experiment in this study was chosen to be completely accurate. The only difference between Figs. 2(a) and 2(b) is that Fig. 2(b) is drawn for a lighter polymer feed. The pattern of change is similar for curves with different heat transfer rates. The key point in Fig. 2(b) is that using lighter feed will cause the decomposition to begin at lower temperatures. This means that the pyrolysis reactor can use lower density polymers to reduce the operating temperature more economically by consuming less energy. According to a recent figure, for light feed, when the heat transfer rate is 20 ° C/min, the decomposition of the polymer begins at 691 K (418 ° C) and ends at 767 K (494 ° C). In the case of light feed, the results are in the temperature range of the pyrolysis reactor. However, what is obtained from the results of the two polyethylene feeds with two different densities is very similar, which is due to the fact that they have the same ethylene-like backbone [6]. According to Fig. 2(b), light feed, decomposition begins at 418 ° C, which is slightly different from the temperature reported by Liu et al. [5] 410 ° C. In studies TGA, Kim and Oh reported a species of heavy polyethylene, similar to that shown in Figs. 2(a) and 2(b) based on the dynamic model [23]. Although DTG cannot provide accurate information about the reaction mechanism, it can help determine the kinetic parameters of the reaction [23]. Fig. 3(a) shows the DTG curves for heavy, high-density polyethylene at thermal transfer rates from 10 to 50 ° C/min. In this figure, the vertical axis indicates the derivative of conversion to time, and the horizontal axis represents the absolute temperature. Therefore, the temperature change range is the same as in fig. 2 and is between 650 and 850 K. The vertical axis also takes values from 0 to 0.035 s −1. In Fig. 3(a), with increasing temperature, the values derived from the derivative of conversion to time with a low slope have an upward trend, but then the slope of the graph suddenly increases sharply. The chart after that reaches a point at the maximum and then decreases with the same trend faster than what it climbed. The reason for this is the asymmetry of each curve, which is plotted for the corresponding curve with a heat transfer rate of 20 ° C/min vertical lines perpendicular to the maximum point at 761 K (488 ° C) . Carefully in the two visible areas on either side of the vertical red line, the asymmetry on both sides is recognizable. As the heat transfer rate increases, the corresponding values of the maximum point of the curves shift to the right of the graph, and instead for each maximum point, the conversion derivative decreases over time, and the curve widens. The peak numbers on these curves show the temperature at which the most polymer sample is decomposed. To put it more clearly, increasing the heat transfer rate in a polymer causes the maximum thermal decomposition to occur at a higher temperature. A very similar trend for DTG with heat transfer rates between 5 to 20 ° C/min has been observed by Duque et al. [7]. It is very important that the temperature at which the maximum thermal decomposition occurs is in the temperature range of the reactor, which is true for the reactor used in this study (415 < 488 < 585) ° C. In addition, after calculating the maximum temperatures using what has been said in the previous sections, it is possible to calculate the reactive activation energy based on the dynamic model, which will be described in the next section. The pattern of changes in Fig. 3(b) is like to Fig. 3(a), except that lighter feed is used for DTG. It is observed that the use of low densities polymer causes that for similar rates of heat transfer, the temperatures corresponding to the maximum decomposition are shifted to lowering values. Thus, for the heat transfer rate of 20 ° C/min, the maximum point for the reduction in feed density has been shifted from 761 to 750 K. Based on Fig. 3(b) for the heat transfer rate of 10 ° C/min, the result differs by less than 2% from what Das and Tiwari [6] reported in almost the same conditions. However, the cause of the maximum point displacement is due to the special structure of this polymer. This means that as the number of connected branches in the main chain increases, they cause the polymer density to decrease. In general, branching has been reported to be the main cause of reduced polymer thermal stability [6]. The maximum temperatures obtained for any of the feed extracted in the previous sections from Figs. 3(a) and 3(b) are used in this section to calculate the activation energy as well as the frequency factor of reaction. In Fig. 4 , the vertical axis of the natural logarithm is the heating rate velocity, and the horizontal axis corresponds to the inverse values of the absolute temperature. As explained in the kinetic model section, based on the dynamic model, the slope of the straight line passing through these points will be equal to −E/R, and the frequency factor will be calculated with the intercept from the origin of this line. Clearly from Fig. 4 it can be seen that for heavy feed energy pyrolysis activation is equal to 300.984 kJ/mol and for light feed it is 273.570 kJ/mol. Table 4 lists the data on activation energy as well as the frequency factor reported by some researchers for polyethylene pyrolysis [14,23]. The closeness of the results obtained from Fig. 4 with the table values indicates the accuracy of this research. From what has been explained, it is clear that a higher density polymer has a higher activation energy for pyrolysis than a lower density polymer. This means that manufacturing a product using it requires higher temperatures. The calculations made, and the results obtained will be more useful when they agree to produce liquid fuel in a laboratory reactor, which will be discussed in the following sections. In polyethylene pyrolysis, the three products formed are liquid fuel, combustible gases and residual coke at the bottom of the reactor. Coke formation usually occurs when pyrolytic catalysis is performed, mainly due to the presence of active acid sites on the catalyst surface. Coke formation, on the other hand, is strongly dependent on process temperature and reactor diversity. In this study, it was observed that the amount of coke produced at the end of the process is so small that in most cases, it is less than 0.5% of the product, so it can be ignored without disturbance in calculations. Similar results have been reported in the absence of carbon solids in the reactor by Mastral et al. [8] In polyethylene pyrolysis. Therefore, only liquid fuels and combustible gases are considered in the distribution of products. Fig. 5 shows the distribution of the product according to consecutive runs in the form of a bar chart. In order to increase the accuracy of the design of experiment, five central repetition points are considered, which are shown in Fig. 5 in the form of a hatching. The proximity of the responses to all five repetition points, whether for liquid fuel or for the gas phase, indicates a relative low error in the experiments. The result of the liquid fuel product was additional evaluated using response surface methodology for the purpose of recognize trends in the production process. For this purpose, three of the parameters were selected, which were the reactor temperature (°C), density of the feed (kgm −3) and loading of reactor (%). For the better analysis of these parameters two of the most applicable results were selected as the valued responses, liquid fuel and gas yield. The effect of feed density and reactor temperature on the liquid fuel efficiency is illustrated in Fig. 6a . In this figure, the x-axis represents the temperature in the range of 415 to 585 °C, and the y-axis denotes the density of HDPE as the third most consumed plastic in the world [5] in the range of 959 to 967 kg/m 3. Liquid fuel efficiency on the z axis based on weight is also shown. Generally, increasing the process temperature from minimum values to the maximum operating margin will initially increase the efficiency of the liquid fuel production and then reach a maximum. This observation agrees with what has been reported in other studies that an increase in temperature to less than 500 ° C due to the production of ling chain hydrocarbons with more than 20 carbons, increases the production of liquid fuel [6]. However, the continued increase in temperature will always decrease the liquid fuel efficiency in Fig. 5a. It is clear that for every density of heavy polyethylene feed, a maximum of liquid fuel production efficiency will be found, but the key point is that with the knowledge of feed, the maximum efficiency will go from 89.1 to 95.3% upward trends. Therefore, the optimum value of the objective function will occur at temperature 509 °C and for the heaviest feed, corresponding to the efficiency of 95.3% for producing liquid fuel. Despite the complexity of the process, it can be generally stated that lowering the reactor temperature always results in a reduction in liquid fuel production efficiency, which is considered to be undesirable. 3D plots were drawn that were defined by the regression model to exhibition the belongings of the independent changeable parameter on the response of the process [45]. As it is illustrated, in Fig. 5a, the maximum geometric location of the procedure from the lowest to the highest density will correspond to temperature changes from 491 to 509 ° C. This interval corresponds to the data related to the high temperature of the process obtained from Figs. 2(a) and 2(b). The agreement between the TGA observations and the results of the experimental design shows that the achievements are correct. As a parameter applicable to the reactor, the feed load in the reactor and its interactions with temperature are examined in Fig. 6b . Accordingly, growing the temperature from its minimum value to the highest reactor temperature first increases the production of liquid fuel and then decreases it again after exceeding a certain maximum value. A similar trend can be understood for changes in the amount of loading and its effects on liquid fuel in the fig. A more detailed study of the recent figure shows that the highest amount of liquid fuel has been produced in an area, the temperature being close to the same temperature in the TGA study in the previous section, at about 509 ° C. To justify the behavior of temperature, it should be stated that the growth of temperature in the reactor based on TGA charts first increases the fracture of polymer bonds and converts it into a product by increasing the conversion. By the time the conversion reaches 100%, all the polymer has decomposed, and from this point on the reaction will be mainly between free radicals. Obviously, this does not mean that there are no free radicals at lower temperatures. Rather, at lower temperatures, free radicals cause a variety of reactions by attacking polymer chains. In other words, in these conditions, free radicals and unchanged feed play a role, but at higher temperatures, there will be no trace of feed. In agreement with this justification, Liu et al. [14], using molecular dynamics as well as laboratory findings, proposed mechanisms homolysis and recombination for the decomposition of polyethylene into lighter components, as shown in Fig. 7. It is further stated that mechanism β scission produces the lighter compounds. On the other hand, some products do not tolerate high temperatures and rising temperatures will cause them to decompose and turn into gas. Ultimately, these changes significantly reduce liquid fuel production at high temperatures. The olefins produced can also be converted to aromatics by the Diesel Alder mechanism [21]. In agreement with the above, Mastral et al. [8] proposed a similar mechanism based on the reaction of free radicals. In polyethylene thermal pyrolysis, hemolytic chain cuts always occur in completely random situations. In the sense that along the main chain in order to form free hydrocarbon radicals, chain failure can occur on any of the carbons. The free radicals created to stabilize and become a product can react with each other or attack stable molecules that have not yet been broken down, causing them to decompose and eventually turn into lighter compounds [4]. Due to the lack of oxygen in the feed formulation of the process and the impossibility of its penetration during the process, it is quite to be expected that the liquid fuel produced also lacks oxygenated compounds, and this group of fuels is considered to be of high quality [9]. Therefore, by ensuring the quality of the produced fuel, the design of experiment is tried to increase the quantity of production. Regarding the quality of liquid fuel produced in similar processes, about 46 MJ/kg have been recorded for the calorific value of fuel, which puts this achievement in the category of fuels such as diesel [6]. Additionally, for light cuts in liquid fuel, Kassargy et al. [12] Reported an octane number equal to base gasoline, 89.9. Zhao et al. [4] in polyethylene pyrolysis identified a significant percentage of light aromatics and therefore, the liquid fuel produced will be of good combustion quality. It can be said with certainty that temperature is the most challenging parameter during chemical processes. Therefore, its role is always considered as one of the effective factors in reactions [2]. However, there are other important components involved in pyrolysis that have been less studied, including the reactor loading percentage. However, Sun et al. [3] Performed several experiments to establish a direct relationship between reactor loading and residence time. They assessed the increase in residence time as moderate as the increase in liquid phase production efficiency, which is in full agreement with what is seen in Fig. 6c . The interaction effect of density as an illustrative of the weight of the feed and the percentage of loading as a representative of the amount of fullness of the reactor in Fig. 6c is plotted in three dimensions. The important thing to keep in mind is that for almost any amount of loading, increasing the density does not lead to significant changes in the amount of liquid fuel. It is as if each of these two parameters is independent of each other, which means that there is no interaction. This approach is fully consistent with the data in Table 5 , which considers the p-value for L × D to be 0.104. To evaluate the accuracy of the proposed equation by DOE, experimental data on liquid fuel are plotted against the values predicted by the model. Also, the straight line has adjusted all the data, the slope of which indicates the goodness of the model in Fig. 8. The response surface methodology was applied to explore the investigational data found from the CCD (central composite design). One of the advantages of this method is that it finally facilitates the analysis of process behavior by providing a mathematical equation and an ANOVA table [45]. (9) Liquid Fuel = − 111531 − 9.91916 × T − 25.8364 × L + 237.771 × D − 0.00156455 × T 2 − 0.0221409 × L 2 − 0.126991 × D 2 + 0.00293775 × TL + 0.0118794 × TD + 0.0275219 × LD The dependence of the amount of liquid fuel extracted from the reactor, and the operating parameters are described by Eq. 9. The independent variables in this equation are the same as the operating parameters of the reactor, namely the temperature, loading and density of the feed. In this equation, linear and nonlinear coefficients as well as interaction coefficients between the parameters are seen. The data extracted from the analysis of variance are shown in Table 5, some of the parameters of which have been used in previous sections. With the help of this data, in addition to the value of each component, it is possible to judge the effectiveness of each. According to the analysis of variance (ANOVA), the significance of all parameters involved in the reaction can be determined [45]. The effects of temperature and feed density in polyethylene pyrolysis on the yield of combustible gases produced in Fig. 9a are shown. Accordingly, for all values of density, increasing the temperature first reduces the gas production and after passing the temperature range of 500 ° C, the gas production increases. A similar downward trend has been reported [6] for temperatures below 500 ° C, which is consistent with the results of the graph. In contrast, increasing the density at constant temperatures has milder effects on improving gas efficiency. In other words, temperature change is a more key parameter than density for changes in gas yield. The validity of this statement will become clearer when the data for the p-value entered in Table 6 for temperature, and density are compared. Accordingly, the p-value for the temperature is 0.001 and for the density is 0.005, which means that the temperature is more effective for that parameter due to the lower p-value. Data from Table 5 also show that the p-value for T 2 is smaller than D 2, which means that the temperature behavior is much more nonlinear than the density. This result is entirely consistent with Fig. 9a, where temperature changes are almost curved. Based on what can be seen in Fig. 9a, increasing the temperature from 500 ° C upwards again causes an increase in gas production. In a similar process, Henus et al. [42] reported the results of agreement with this study that the amount of gas increased when the temperature rises above 500 ° C. Mastral et al. [8] also considered the increase of this parameter as the key factor in growing the production of combustible gases at high reactor temperatures. In a similar conclusion to Fig. 9a, Zhao et al. [4] observed that at temperatures above 500°C, an upward trend in temperature causes a noticeable increase in flammable gases. In order to better understand the nature of the process and the possibility of intelligently selecting the operating conditions of the reactor, the effects of temperature and polymer loading in Fig. 9b have been plotted. As it can be seen, gas production at lower temperatures is more intense, which reduces the load on its intensity. The reason for this trend is that at low temperatures due to the incompleteness of feed pyrolysis, heavy products that do not evaporate at low temperatures remain in the reactor and over time its bonds are broken and converted to gas products. Since the maximum production of the gas phase is against the goals of liquid fuel making, it is better to have the operating conditions of the reactor at high temperatures and loadings to minimize gas yield. Feed density actually indicates the compactness of the polymer and is therefore, an important feature, and obviously, unlike some parameters such as pressure, measurement and changes in it is very easy. An illustrative picture of the interaction between loading and feed density are shown in Fig. 9c, which also displays the gas efficiency in the vertical axis. According to this figure, for all values of density, with decreasing load, the gas efficiency increases sharply, which can be attributed to the fact that the products formed in the reactor experience more hot space before leaving the reactor, which causes the breakage chemical bonds to weaken. In Fig. 9c, the loading behavior is distinctly more nonlinear than the density function, as the p-values written in Table 6 obtained from the analysis of variance are evidence of this observation. From a statistical point of view, the p-value for the nonlinear loading function that appears in Fig. 7 as a three-dimensional curve trend is equal to 0.003, which indicates the significance of this parameter. However, for nonlinear behavior of density at a constant temperature, the p-value according to the Table 6 is 0.009, which indicates that it is less effective than L 2. The analysis of gases produced in the process of high-density polyethylene pyrolysis, which is finally ignited on the flare, is drawn in Fig. 10, in which the vertical axis is the mole fraction of gas, and the horizontal axis is the operating temperature of the reactor. The gas phase analysis includes alkanes, olefins and hydrogen, all of which are combustible and have a high thermal value [42]. In some studies, it has been suggested that flammable gases be used to optimize the energy consumption of the reactor in order to improve pyrolysis [10,12]. Due to the large amount of the olefins, the gas phase also has the potential to be used as a raw material for polymer manufacturing industries. On the other hand, due to the lack of sulfur compounds that are considered catalyst toxins [22], the gas phase can be applied as feed for catalytic reforming units with steam [48] or carbon dioxide [49] in order to produce hydrogen or syngas. Certainly, the latter two compounds are among the most widely used gases [50,51]. Some of the gases identified by other researchers have also been reported to include methane [4,8,13,52], ethane [4,8,13], butane [4,9], propane [9], ethylene [4,8,13,14,52], propylene [14,52], butylene [9] and hydrogen [13,14,42]. Although hydrogen has also been identified in the gas phase analysis, it has not been considered in Fig. 8 because it accounted for less than 1%. In some sources, similar results have been written for temperatures close to those used in this study. That is, measuring the percentage of hydrogen in the analysis of gases indicates that it is negligible [8]. Tests performed by Miscolczi [9] also show a small amount of hydrogen in the gas phase. Based on a semi-experimental model, Green and Sadrameli [13] evaluated the most abundant compounds in gas obtained from polyethylene pyrolysis of one and two carbon compounds (methane, ethane and ethylene) which is in full agreement with what is shown in Fig. 10. After experimental studies, it was found that the relationship of the gas phase analysis of the density and loading of the reactor is very weak, but instead the temperature strongly changes the gas analysis. In other words, density and loading change the gas yield, but they do not have much effect on the composition of the gas phase, and in contrast, the temperature changes the yield of the gas and its composition at the same time. Therefore, in order to better evaluate the molar components of the gas phase, the density and loading were adjusted to the average values of the reactor operating conditions. In Fig. 8, propylene and ethane are the most abundant gases, which is consistent with what Liu et al. [14] reported using molecular dynamics. Obviously, Das and Tiwari [6] have informed similar results in experimental researches. Fig. 11 shows the findings obtained in the laboratory for gas efficiency on the horizontal axis and what is predicted by the quadratic model on the vertical axis. The slope of the fitting line of these points indicates the accuracy of the model used. So, it can be said that the accumulation of points around the straight line will add to the goodness of the model. (10) Gas Yield = 82913.9 + 4.58835 × T − 0.459560 × L − 174.260 × D + 0.00170048 × T 2 + 0.0248977 × L 2 + 0.0920518 × D 2 − 0.00259025 × TL − 0.00649688 × TD − 0.000609375 × LD The Eq. 10 describes mathematically the gas yield behavior that is related to linear, nonlinear terms, and dual interactions with temperature, density, and reactor loading. The significance of this equation is that it is possible to predict the values of gas yield in the proposed operating range without performing experiments, and therefore, can be used for large-scale pyrolysis reactors. The analysis of variance as a very powerful statistical tool in the analysis of various scientific experiments has been welcomed by many researchers [53]. For gas yield, the results of variance analysis for all parameters, including linear, nonlinear, their interactions and also for the whole experiment are given in Table 6 that some of the data of which have been used in the previous sections. The p-value for the regression is 0.002, which indicates the significance and accuracy of the full proposed model. The main goal of this study was to maximize liquid fuel production, but also the amount and composition of the gas phase were determined. Good research has been done so far to produce liquid fuel, but comprehensive results have not yet been provided for the gas phase. Carefully in the resulting gas compounds can be found that due to the abundance of olefins can be used in units with olefin feed. In this regard, Somoza-Tornos et al. [52] have developed a process for the production of ethylene to such an extent that the price of olefin produced from ethylene obtained from naphtha based is less reported. Due to the ease of production of olefins with the pyrolysis of waste plastics and low investment costs, this method is preferable to the thermal cracking of hydrocarbons [54] to produce olefins [55,56]. In most cases, the product obtained from thermal pyrolysis does not have the necessary quality for use as fuel, in which case the use of a suitable catalyst can improve the product. So far, various catalysts have been used to promote the products of pyrolysis of polymer wastes, including Y and ZSM-5 zeolites, as well as alumina and alumina silica. Furthermore, some metals such as iron and nickel have been loaded on the support to improve the catalyst performance. Until now, to increase the capabilities of the catalyst by adding metal, it was only possible to load an oxide or a special combination of metal on the surface of the catalyst. Recently, it has been discovered that by pyrolyzing organic metal framework in an inert atmosphere, its organic ligands are converted to graphitic carbon that contained metal nanostructures derived from metal cores [26]. In this study, with a special technique, instead of loading a metal oxide, a set consisting of two oxides with different capacities as well as pure metal was loaded on the surface of the Y zeolite catalyst, which gave particular properties to the catalyst. Fig. 12a compares the two X-ray diffraction patterns, the first being the MIL − 53 (Cu) in which the 2θ is changed from 5 to 85°, the result being consistent with what is reported in the reference [15, 57]. The second pattern in this figure is related to the product obtained from MOF pyrolysis in which different compounds have been identified. It has been reported that if the pyrolysis of MIL − 53 (Cu) in air, its metal content is converted to divalent CuO. In this study, instead of air, MOF was pyrolyzed in a stream of inert nitrogen gas, which instead of CuO, various compounds such as CuO, Cu 2 O and Cu were produced, which is indicated by the position of the peaks in Fig. 12a. Fig. 12b also compares the two X-ray diffraction patterns for the process catalyst to ensure the presence of active metal compounds on the zeolite surface. In the latter figure, the first pattern is related to zeolite Y, which is very similar to what has been reported. Consistent with the diffraction spectrum exposed, in sum, the peaks at 2θ= 6.176, 10.066, 11.823, 15.589, 18.547, 20.258, 23.517, 26.914, 30.591,31.249,32.292 and 33.929o assigned to (111), (220), (311), (331), (333), (440), (533), (642), (660), (751), (840) and (664) planes approve the cubic faujasite construction of zeolite Y with the JCPDS no. 00–038-0240. The observed X-ray diffraction analysis is consistent with the results reported by Zhu et al. [58]. However, the peaks visible in the second pattern clearly indicate that the deposition of CuO, Cu 2 O and Cu on the zeolite surface has been successfully accomplished, with more precise details of the position of the peaks in the figure. X-ray fluorescence test determines the atomic amount of silicon and aluminum in synthetic zeolite, the ratio of silicon to aluminum is 2.44, which is considered an appropriate value.
