abstract: Pyrolysis of plastics is an economical method to recover valuable fuels from waste sources. In this study, the response surface methodology is adopted to model the behavior for the pyrolysis of high-density polyethylene (HDPE). The three operating parameters of temperature in the range of 415 to 585 ° C, feed density in a window between 959 and 967 kg/m 3 and reactor loading range from 23 to 57% were selected as the main variables. Using the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) study for different heat transfer rates, the range at which the feed is fully converted was determined, thus ensuring that the correct operating temperature range was selected. By drawing difference thermogravimetry (DTG) curves and using a dynamic model, the activation energy for converting heavy and light feeds was determined to be 300.984 and 273.57 kJ/mol, respectively. Analysis of the experimental results using the (Central Composite Design) CCD method shows the ease of pyrolysis of light feed, which is agreed upon with less activation energy. As the main goal of this study, the effect of parameters on the liquid fuel production was investigated, and it was turned out that under optimum conditions, the amount of liquid fuel production will be 95.3%. For the optimal conditions, a temperature of 500 ° C, feed density is 963 kg/m 3 and reactor loading rate of 40% were determined. Analysis of the exhaust gases also showed that propylene and ethane are the most abundant components of this phase. Furthermore, with the help of analysis of variance, the proposed model to produce liquid fuel and gas yield was evaluated as a significant and the accurate model. Finally, under the optimized conditions, by using a new MIL-53 (Cu) derived
title: Energy recovery from high density polyethylene plastic via pyrolysis with upgrading of the product by a novel nano MIL-53 (Cu) derived@Y zeolite catalyst using response surface methodology

procedure_catalog.name_reported: synthesis of MIL-53 (Cu) by hydrothermal procedure

steps.method_details: prepared a mixture with proportions of 1 mmol Cu(NO3)2.3H2O: 1 mmol C6H4(COOH)2 with suitable amount of DMF
stirred the mixture
transferred mixture to stainless-steel autoclave with Teflon coating and heated
washed solution three times with DMF and three times with dichloromethane

procedure_catalog.name_reported: synthesis of zeolite Y by solvothermal method

steps.method_details: adjusted starting materials to molar ratio 4.62Na2O:2Al2O3:10SiO2:180H2O using sodium aluminate, sodium trisilicate, deionized water, and sodium hydroxide
stirred thoroughly for one hour to obtain uniform gel
aged gel for 24 h at room temperature
transferred gel to autoclave with Teflon inner coating and placed at 100 °C for 24 h
washed product with deionized water and vacuum filtered by Buchner funnel until pH 8-9

procedure_catalog.name_reported: conversion of sodium form zeolite Y to hydrogen form

steps.method_details: mixed each gram of zeolite with 10 mL of 1 M ammonium nitrate solution for 24 h
filtered slurry in vacuum by Buchner funnel until pH 9
dried product
calcined product

procedure_catalog.name_reported: preparation of final catalyst by mixing zeolite Y and MIL-53 (Cu)

steps.method_details: poured mixture of zeolite Y and MIL-53 (Cu) into 100 mL deionized water and refluxed with mixing
irradiated mixture with ultrasonic waves
dried uniform product
pyrolyzed dried catalyst precursor
heated zeolite catalyst
calcined samples

procedure_catalog.name_reported: inert gas purging before pyrolysis run

steps.method_details: passed inert nitrogen gas through reactor bed

common_conditions.other: heating rate 20 °C/min, nitrogen carrier gas

point_conditions.other: feed density 967 kg/m³, reactor loading not specified

common_conditions.other: thermogravimetric analyzer, nitrogen atmosphere, heating rate 20 °C/min

full_text: Data will be made available on request. Researchers are currently exploring advanced tunable two-dimensional (2D) materials with well-defined pores and controlled structures for next-generation green energy harvesting [1–4]. Therefore, renewable energy, storage and conversion technologies that are environmentally friendly, low-cost, and high in energy density have received substantial attention these days [5–19]. The increased contents of CO2 in the environment causes profound ecological consequences, among which global warming is the most serious threat. Therefore, cry of the day is to decrease CO2 content in the atmosphere and convert it into valuable products. In this connection, attempts have been made to convert CO2 into a variety of valuable products using electrochemical and photochemical approaches [20–25]. The conversion of CO2 into formic acid would be crucial as formic acid being a valuable commodity with numerous industrial applications [20,22]. Although attempts have been made by researchers to convert CO2 into formic acid but the thermal stability of CO2 is a big obstacle which economically can be made feasible through the use of a suitable catalyst [26]. Single-atom catalysts (SACs) may resolve this issue, which possesses extraordinary stability and better electrocatalytic performance. In addition to their superior performance, SACs are cost-effective and have shown high activity and selectivity [22,27–34]. However, SACs tend to cluster on the substrate due to the high surface free energy. Using a suitable support throughout the synthesis and reaction process may help avoid SAC aggregation and improve electrocatalytic efficacy [35,36]. Recently, reported 2D materials (e.g., metal and metal-free nitrogen, phosphorus, and boron-doped carbon) have been used as electrocatalyst in many valuable conversions and even still modifications are made to improve their catalytic functions [4,6,7,37–42]. Among the attempted modifications of 2D materials, the introduction of nitrogen atoms is the most successful as it results in the formation of a perfect covalently bonded carbon framework. Various techniques have been reported on nitrogen-doped graphene [43] and graphene oxide [44], but these methods have poor control over nitrogen content and its oxidation state [45,46]. Furthermore, bottom-up strategies have been rarely used to construct a highly nitrogenated 2D material with a well-defined pore size [47]. These materials are fascinated to be an ideal host for incorporating single metal atoms into such materials. Moreover, these materials are suitable for CO2RR, ORR, and OER reactions, as they are considered green and cheaper when coordinated with non-noble metals [2,4,20,33,48]. Non-precious metal anchored materials have the vital advantage of being less costly, safer, and highly sustainable electrocatalysts as compared to Ir, Ru, and Pt (noble metals) [49–51]. The 2D metal and COF also have been used extensively for CO2RR [3,52]. However, their low graphitic degree causes poor ion-electron transport [53]. In contrast, most of the electrocatalysts for CO2RR described to date are based on transition metal oxides supported on carbon materials to facilitate energy transfer. Though the expansion of metal-based electrocatalyst has lately engrossed substantial courtesy, the usage of well-defined nitrogenated 2D supporting material for single atoms as CO2RR electrocatalyst has not been reported to date. Hence, we design a new 2D material with high nitrogen content called N-COF (nitrogenated two-dimensional material) synthesized through direct condensation of hexaaminotriphenylene hexahydrochloride (HATP) and hexaketocyclohexane octahydrate (HKH). From condensation of these two monomers together under solvothermal environments, pristine N-COF was synthesized as a black powder in 94% yield. DFT and experimental approaches were used to investigate N-COF as a suitable host material with well-organized nanopores and equally dispersed nitrogen atoms (Co-N-COF), for electrochemical conversion of CO2 into formic acid. Furthermore, the structural, electronic, and catalytic properties of N-COF and Co-N-COF were also explored. Both N-COF and Co-N-COF exhibited a small bandgap of 1.80 and 0.67 eV, respectively which shows that both of these materials have tremendous potentials to be used in other fields besides catalysis, e.g., batteries, semiconductors and energy storage devices [54–56]. The schematic diagram of N-COF and Co-N-COF synthesis have been shown in Fig. 1 whereas, their detailed structural characterization have been discussed in the supplementary information. Ethane-1,2-diamine, ethyl acetate, benzophenone imine, hexaketocyclohexane, tetrahydrofuran, sodium ter-butoxide, cobalt chloride hexahydrate, and various other chemicals used in this study were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. High-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) images were obtained by a Tecnai model F–20 microscope. The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by a Rigaku Ultima III X-ray diffractometer. Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms of the composites were tested at 77.15 K by a Micrometrics ASAP 2020. Prior to the adsorption, samples were outgassed at 150 °C for 6 h under vacuum (10−6 Torr). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and density functional theory (DFT) were used to calculate the specific surface area and pore size distribution. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted on an ESCALAB250 instrument at the base pressure of 1 × 10−9 mbar, whereas the X-ray source was Al Kα. A 10 ml Pyrex tube was charged with HATP (40 mg, 0.0073 mmol), NMP (2 ml), hexaketo cycloheaxane (23.5 mg, 0.0073 mmol) and 0.1 ml of 17.4 M acetic acid. The reaction mixture was degassed through freeze–thaw cycles (three cycles), sealed under vacuum, and heated at 120 °C for 3 days. Subsequently, the mixture was cooled to ambient temperature and the black crystals were collected by centrifugation, washed with THF, methanol, and water under Soxhlet (washing for 12 h), and dried under vacuum for 24 h to obtain N-COF with a 95% isolated yield. About 100 mg of N-COF crystals were suspended in 250 ml methanol and suplemented with 20 mg of cobalt acetate hexahydrate. The resultant mixture was stirred for 48 h reflux at 100 °C, followed by filtration, and washing with 1 M HCl, acetone, and ethanol. Subsequently, the material was heated at 300 °C for 1 h under a constant flow of argon gas. Afterward, the material was washed again with 1 M HCl, acetone and ethanol and dried overnight at 100 °C in a vacuum. The resulting black crystals were characterized by different instrumental techniques. The elemental composition of Co-N-COF and N-COF was investigated using a combination of Elemental analysis (EA) and Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectroscopy (ICPMS), where experimental analysis were found very close to the theoretical analysis as shown in (Table S1 and 2), respectively. The higher hydrogen and oxygen contents as compared to the theoretical calculations may be due to the trapping of small molecules (moisture, oxygen, etc.) between the interlayers and within the pores of N-COF. Similarly, the composition of elements in sample Co-N-COF was confirmed using XPS and elemental analysis. According to XRD measurements and structural simulations, N-COF has a layered crystal structure with high crystallinity (Fig. 2a). A sharp peak at 5.8° can be linked to [100] facet, which is part of the hexagonal lattice. Similarly, the peak at 26.3° associated to the [001] facet and corresponds to π-π stacking with 3.39 Å interlayer distance. The structure of N-COF material exhibited high stability when tested at 500 °C and 800 °C (Fig. S2). The simulated PXRD spectrum (purple curve) and experimental spectrum (blue curve) (Fig. 2a, b) were found to be consistent. N-COF is extremely crystalline, as revealed in high-resolution transmission microscopy (HRTEM), which reveals a d-spacing of 3.36 Å (Fig. 2c), that is nearly same as that of the experimentally determined value from the XRD results (3.39 Å, [001] facet). HAADF-STEM image of Co-N-COF further confirms the uniform distribution of Co+2 in the COF material (Fig. 2d). Additionally, as with N-COF, the XRD pattern of Co-N-COF have exhibited the retention of all peaks even after the incorporation of cobalt in the pores, which indicates that the structure still maintains its pristine nature (Fig. S3). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of N-COF indicated the construction of phenazine bonding with typical bands at 1365, 1436, and 1512 cm−1 (Fig. S4). In the meantime, the vibration bands (3313 and 3384 cm−1) related to the NH2 group of HATP and the CO (1676 cm−1) functional group of HKH have been disappeared. Nevertheless, while the FT-IR spectra of N-COF showed distinctive bands of phenazine linkages, signals ascribed to the NH2 group of HATP and the CO unit of HKH remained intact. 13C cross-polarization magic angle spinning (CP/MAS) NMR of N-COF demonstrated peaks of aza-aromatic rings at 110, 129, and 140 p.p.m. The peak at 176 p.p.m. belonging to carbonyl carbon indicating the presence of carbonyl group in N-COF structure. The 13C NMR spectra of Co-N-COF are comparable to that of the N-COF, representing that the framework after coordination with cobalt has retained its pristine state (Fig. S5). Furthermore, the N-COF and Co-N-COF as examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), reveals the existence of C1s, N1s, and Co2p of N-COF without any other impurities (Fig. 3b). Moreover, N-COF shows obvious peaks at 285.13 and 286.23 eV, representing CC and CN, correspondingly (Fig. 3a). In addition, the characteristic band of N1s for the K-edge appeared at 399 eV, signifying the existence of nitrogen-atoms in a sp 2-hybridized fashion for Co-N-COF (Fig. 3c). Two high-pitched set of signals represents the coordination of Co atoms with N-COF, the Co 2p3/2 (780.3 eV) and 2p1/2 (796.1 eV), validating the presence of cobalt as Co+2, which is comparable with commercial cobalt-porphyrin material (Fig. 3b). As determined by nitrogen sorption isotherms at 77 K, the N-COF is microporous, with pores ~0.98 nm in diameter (Fig. 4a). Whereas the BET (Brunauer-Emmett-Teller) surface area of N-COF is 586 m2/g. The BET specific surface area of N-COF materials increases up to 2743 m2/g after being pyrolyzed at 1100 °C, however this has been decreased to 1137 m2/g after cobalt atom's coordination, resulting in pores with a diameter of 0.66 nm. During pyrolysis the pore size remained the same because the increment of specific surface area is due to the increase of the degree of crystallinity arising from removing the edges groups (NH2 and CO) by pyrolysis, and the fixed the aromatic rings have led to the invariable pore sizes. Furthermore, the experimentally measured pore size and BET specific surface area were verified via density functional theory (DFT). Theoretically measured specific surface area and pore size of N-COF is 2673.721 m2/g and 1.0 nm, while for Co-N-COF it is 1202.588 m2/g with pore size 0.64 nm respectively which is consistent with experimentally measured BET specific surface area and pore sizes. The detail of the theoretically (computational studies) measured surface area of N-COF and Co-N-COF are given in supplementary information. Moreover, the reduction in BET surface and pore sizes with Co coordination is due to the fact that cobalt cover the surface area in the pores and reduce the overall pore sizes by occupying the space inside the pores of N-COF as shown in Fig. 4(a and c). The DFT study further revealed that the bandgap of Co-N-COF has decreased to 0.67 eV due to the synergistic effect of structural features and Co-coordination, whereas the bandgap of N-COF is 1.80 eV as shown in Fig. 4(d, f). The enriched Co2+ cations, remarkably reduce the electronic bandgap of Co-N-COFs and favour the transfer of electrons from the top of the valence band to the bottom of the conduction band, which facilitates the absorption and activation of CO2 leading to improved CO2RR activity. CO2 is adsorbed weakly on N-COF materials with adsorption energy of −0.12 eV as shown in Fig. S6. On the other hand, CO2 adsorbed strongly on Co site and Co-N site of Co-N-COF with adsorption energy of −0.66 and − 0.31 eV respectively. Another interesting feature of adsorbed CO2 is that the angle between between C and oxygen atoms is tilted from 180° to 145.2° and 131.43°, while the distance between each CO bond distance enlarges to 1.27 and 1.30 Å compared with the gas phase which is 1.18 Å. The elongation in CO bonds and O=C=O angle tilting clearly indicates that the adsorbed CO2 is being activated by Co-sites and Co-N sites in Co-N-COF materials which have further enhanced electrocatalytic activity as indicated in Fig. 5 . Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of the N-COF sample have shown exceptional thermostability up to 1000 °C with around about 20% of weight loss (Fig. S7). The CO2RR to formate activity on Co-N-COF electrocatalyst was mainly tested in a standard H-type cell with three-electrodes with 1.0 M KHCO3 solution used as electrolyte. The catalyst was consistently dropped on the carbon cloth with mass loading around 0.7 mg cm−2. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and gas chromatography (GC) were used to detect liquid and gas products, respectively (Fig. S8). Owing to the incorporation of single atomic cobalt inside the COF achieved a 200 mA cm−2 reduction current at −0.65 V vs. reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE), while the N-COF required −0.80 V vs. RHE to supply the same quantity of current (Fig. 5a). With gradual increase in potential, the formate FE of Co-N-COF increased up to the highest value of 97% (Fig. 5b) maintained above 90% for a wide voltage window ranging from −0.75 to −1.05 V, in an acute difference with that of N-COF sample. Moreover, Co-N-COF attained a remarkable current density of up to 480 mAcm−2 at −1.05 V, whereas still keeping a better formate selectivity (Fig. 5a), with a minimum quantity (<4%) of H2 and (<3%) CO side products found (Fig. 6a, b). Approximately, formate selectivity was 90%, attaining a partial current of 446 mAcm−2 for formate in neutral pH (Figs. 5c, 6c). As shown in (Fig. 5d), whereas pure formic acid solutions were obtained (Fig. S8), at ~1.35 V onset cell voltage was examined to attain 22.2 mA cm−2 current density. In contrast, by increasing the cell voltage, the cell current density was quickly amplified to a multiple of 100 mA cm−2, while at the same time maintaining a high concentration of formic acid above 70% FE with the accompanying 501.23 mA cm−2 enormous cell current density (Fig. 5e). In addition to excellent activity (formate partial current densities ~446 mA cm−2), selectivity (highest Faradaic efficiency ~97.4%), and stability (100h), the Co-N-COF electrocatalyst showed high purification of formic acid solutions (~ 100 wt%) (Fig. 5f). Furthermore, even with a higher current density of 711.2 mA cm−2, the selectivity of formic acid began to display a noticeable decline, perhaps owing to the CO2 mass transport constraint in addition to the congruently amplified HER side reaction (Fig. 6d). Furthermore, Co-N-COF and N-COF have shown no sign of degradation after the 100 h of test. Deconvoluted XPS spectra after test have shown that the material is intact and still active to perform its function, as the main peaks for Co and pyridinic nitrogen still remained extant in the material (Fig. S9). HR-TEM image have also shown no sign of material posing or degradation after cyclic performance (Fig. S10). XRD peaks also remained at their original position however, spectra is less sharp compared to pristine material before test (Fig. S11). Hence, further confirms the stability and robustness of Co-N-COF material even after 100 h of catalytic test. Moreover, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) have also clarified that Co-N-COF displays better electrical conductivity due to the reduction of bandgap after cobalt loading into the framework and got better mass transport, as can be seen from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (Fig. S12). When compared to the Co-N-COF sample, N-COF have exhibited significantly higher impedance than the Co-N-COF sample. Additional, EIS was used to verify the characteristics of the electrode/electrolyte interaction. We were able to acquire the Nyquist plots for Co-N-COF at an open circuit voltage, which resulted in the lowest interfacial charge transfer resistance (Rct) for this compound. These findings suggest that an additional fast electron transfer during the CO2 reduction method is extremely likely over the Co-N-COF based electrode surface, which is consistent with the previous findings [57]. A high rationally designed precursors for N-COF have been discoveted, which can act as the model catalyst to coordinate Co, for CO2 electrochemical reduction (CO2RR) to formic acid with good catalytic activity and high selectivity at high current density. An exceptionally well-ordered N atom network linked covalently to the carbonic framework, has given a crystalline appearance to the structure of the synthesized catalyst. The structure of N-COFs materials was stable even at 1000 °C. DFT study further revealed that the bandgap of Co-N-COF has decreased to 0.67 eV due to the synergistic effect of structural features and Co-coordination, whereas the bandgap of N-COF was found to be 1.80 eV. In addition to excellent activity (formate partial current densities ~446 mA cm−2), selectivity (highest Faradaic efficiency ~97.4%), and stability (100 h), the Co-N-COF electrocatalyst showed high purification of formic acid solutions (~ 100 wt%). Furthermore, the Co-N-COF exhibited lower selectivity for CO2 conversion to CO which was ~3% whereas for HER it was ~4%. The new 2D MOF material proposed in this work can be employed in a variety of other applications, comprising energy storage devices, supercapacitors, and fuel cells. Dr.Sajjad Ali and Dr. Rashiq Iqbal proposed idea. Dr. Sajjad Ali drafted the manuscript, and revised it. Prof. Liang Qiao, Prof. Haiyan xiao and Dr. Rashid Iqbal revised the manuscript. All authors give a significant contribution to the project. After the revision, the authors have approved the final version of the manuscript.
abstract: Designing covalent organic frameworks (COFs) with suitable characteristics could meet the distinctive requirements of various applications, such as catalysis, energy conversion, and molecular-sensing devices. It is indispensable to realize the apt functionalization and modification of COFs, mainly by introducing the heteroatoms to their copious pores and distinct structures. Herein, we designed nitrogenated COFs (N-COFs) with well-ordered nanopores and nitrogen-atoms, using density functional theory (DFT) and experiments. The N-COFs provided a uniquely coordinated environment for a single cobalt atom anchored between two nitrogen-atoms, which enables efficient CO2-reduction to formic-acid. In N-COFs catalyst, the N-atoms network is covalently linked to the carbonic-framework, providing the structure a crystalline nature. Moreover, N-COFs material is stable even at 1000 °C. DFT analysis revealed that the bandgap of Co-N-COF decreases to 0.67 eV owing to the synergistic effect of structural features and Co-coordination, while the bandgap of N-COF was 1.80 eV. The excellent catalytic-activity with formate partial current-densities ~446 mA cm−2, selectivity with highest Faradaic efficiency ~97.4%, and stability of 100 h was delivered by the synthesized catalyst. Moreover, Co-N-COF electrocatalyst showed high purification of formic-acid solutions (~100 wt%) and lower selectivity for CO2 conversion to CO, which was ~3%, and for HER it was ~4%.
title: Cobalt coordinated two-dimensional covalent organic framework a sustainable and robust electrocatalyst for selective CO2 electrochemical conversion to formic acid

reaction_catalog.reaction_name_reported: CO2 electrochemical conversion to formic acid

steps.method_details: charged HATP, NMP, hexaketo cycloheaxane, and acetic acid in a Pyrex tube
degassed through freeze-thaw cycles
sealed under vacuum and heated
cooled to ambient temperature, collected black crystals by centrifugation, washed with THF, methanol, and water under Soxhlet
dried under vacuum
suspended N-COF crystals in methanol and supplemented with cobalt acetate hexahydrate
stirred for 48 h reflux
followed by filtration, and washing with 1 M HCl, acetone, and ethanol
heated under a constant flow of argon gas
washed again with 1 M HCl, acetone and ethanol
dried overnight in a vacuum
catalyst was consistently dropped on the carbon cloth

results.value: layered crystal structure with high crystallinity
results.notes: sharp peak at 5.8° linked to [100] facet, peak at 26.3° associated to [001] facet

results.value: main peaks for Co and pyridinic nitrogen still remained extant
no sign of material posing or degradation
peaks remained at their original position
results.notes: spectra less sharp compared to pristine

common_conditions.other: catalyst dropped on carbon cloth
catalyst dropped on carbon cloth

full_text: As a greenhouse gas, methane plays an important role in the greenhouse effect with a global warming potential (GWP) 25–34 times that of CO2 [1–4]. Sources of methane in the atmosphere can be divided into anthropogenic sources and natural emissions, and coal mining is the principal source of methane emissions from human activities. With methane emissions reaching 0.68 billion tons in 2019 [5], China has been the world's largest methane emitters [6], resulting in a significant waste of clean energy. The effective control of methane emitted by coal mining is of great significance for China to save energy and meet environmental protection commitments. The methane released by coal mining is usually divided into three types according to the concentration [7]: high concentration methane (HCM, 30–100 vol% CH4), low concentration methane (LCM, 1–30 vol% CH4) and ultra-low concentration methane (UCM, also called ventilation air methane, VAM,  2), the oxygen-depleted combustion condition (0.1 < O2/CH4 < 2) and the oxygen-deficient combustion condition (0 < O2/CH4 < 0.1). The rate-limiting steps of methane catalytic combustion of each condition are significantly different. Thus, we can explain the change of X methane and S c with the following description: (1) At the beginning of oxidation (0–40 s after the start of oxidation), atmosphere in the reactor is an oxygen-deficient/depleted condition for CH4 because the vast of O2 is consumed by reduced OC, and the methane catalytic reaction rate is completely controlled by O2. Only a small amount of O2 is involved in the reaction with methane, so X methane is at a low level in this stage. (2) With the rapid oxidation of OC, atmosphere in the reactor becomes an oxygen-depleted/rich atmosphere for CH4. Sufficient O2 reacts with CH4 at the active site on the OC surface, and the rate-limiting step becomes the dehydrogenation of CH4. Thus, X methane and S c could gradually reach 100% in the process. This is a particularly encouraging finding that all groups of simulation gases with different concentrations of methane can be completely converted at near one minute after oxidation. This proves that CLCO could be adapted to all kinds of conditions with UCM. Fig. 4 and Table 2 shows the effect of reaction temperature and particle size on X methane and S c . The trends of the two indicators with oxidation time are generally consistent with the results in the previous section. Unexpectedly, some anomalous data are found at a temperature of 750 °C in Table 2. Firstly, X a, methane of OC with 106–170 μm at 750 °C (red data group in Fig. 4a) is only 79.64%, which is 15.60% ~ 17.88% lower than the other groups. It means that OC with this particle size may not convert methane completely. It is well known that the bed thickness is directly related to the particle size, and the bed thickness of small particle size tends to be thinner at the same mass. The air velocity of thinner bed thickness is higher than that of the thicker at the same flow rate. The high air velocity means that the gas-solid contact time becomes shorter, leading to methane being blown out of the reactor without reaction, which is one of the reasons for the low X a, methane of the small particle size OC. In addition, we note that the experimental groups for the other two particle sizes at 750 °C are also slightly lower compared with that at higher temperatures. It is generally believed that the catalytic oxidation of methane includes the following processes [29,41]: (1) CH4 would be adsorbed at the surface of oxidized OC and dehydrogenating to CH3 ⁎ (⁎ indicates in adsorption state). (2) The released H⁎ combines with surface O to form a hydroxyl group (OH⁎). (3) With further dehydrogenating, the next released H⁎ from CH3 ⁎combines with OH⁎ to form a H2O⁎ molecular. OV is generated on the surface because the desorption of H2O⁎ molecular takes away O atom. OV is an active site for adsorption of O2, which could split O2 into two O atoms to fill the OV or adsorb by CH2 ⁎. (4) CH2O⁎ repeats above process until complete dehydrogenation to CO2/CO. The first process is the step that requires the most energy for the entire reaction, while temperature is one of the most important sources to provide the energy for reaction. The increase in temperature improves the activity of OC and reduces the activation energy of the reaction. Therefore, satisfactory results are achieved in both experimental groups at 850 °C and 950 °C. Then, S a, c of OC with 170–280 μm, 280–355 μm at 750 °C (dark green and blue data groups in Fig. 4b) are 84.86% and 83.86%, respectively, which is a significant decrease compared with the other groups. This represents a certain percentage of CO produced in the generated gas. The phenomenon could be explained by the finding of Tang et al [42] Their works found that the desorption energy of adsorbed CO is −0.26 eV, implying that the process could occur spontaneously without additional energy. Whereas about 0.39–0.52 eV energy barrier needs to be overcome for oxidation of adsorbed CO. Obviously, the desorption of CO is easier to occur at low temperature. Besides, it is observed that S a, c gradually decreases with increasing particle size at 750 °C, which may mean that there is a relationship between particle size and S a, c . Compared with larger size particles, smaller particles generally have a larger total surface area at the same mass, and total surface area would decrease as the particle size increases. The surface of OC serves as a place for methane catalytic oxidation and provides the active site for methane catalytic oxidation. Therefore, particles with a larger total surface area would have a better catalytic effect. Overall, these results illustrate that AR at 750 °C is not suitable for UCM as oxidation gas technology because OC of different particle size ranges do not achieve satisfactory results at this temperature. To examine the effect of CLCO on the oxidation behavior of OC, a control group (grey data group in Fig. 5a) was set up which used air as oxidation gas. The variation of oxygen concentration with experiment time under different experimental conditions is shown in Fig. 5a. The result of control group shows that no O2 is detected within 50 s after the start of the oxidation reaction, and then it gradually rises to pre-set values. Similar phenomena were observed under other experimental conditions, respectively. Besides, the ratio of consumed O2 to the entering CH4 is 1:2, which is in conformity with the complete oxidation of CH4 (R1). To verify our judgment more clearly, variation of each gas concentration for an experimental group in the oxidation process with CH4 = 1.0%, CO2 = 0.0%, O2 = 20% is shown in Fig. 5b. The whole process is assumed as the following stages: (I) It takes a certain amount of time for the gas to flow due to the length of the pipeline, so no gas is detected in the first stage. (II) CH4 and H2 are detected almost simultaneously and rise rapidly to a peak. Variation of H2 concentration curve is similar to that of CH4, indicating that the cracking reaction of methane (R7) is the main source of H2. At the same time, there is a slight upward trend in the H2 concentration curve, indicating that there are other pathways for H2 production (R3, R5). A slow increasing trend of CO and CO2 illustrates that the catalytic oxidation of methane is not significant at the beginning and becomes progressively more intense with time. However, large amount of unreacted CH4, little CO and H2 generated prove that all these reactions are not the dominant reactions in this stage. Therefore, the only reasonable explanation is that O2 is consumed by the reduced state of OC (R8). This explanation is strongly supported by the following lines of evidence: with the progressive involvement of Fe2O3 and O2 in reaction R1~R5, the concentration of H2, CO and CO2 are gradually increasing during the oxidation. The observation confirms the speculation presented in Section 3.1 that the majority of O2 is consumed by the reduced state of OC. (III) CH4, H2, and CO start to gradually decrease to zero, which means that partial oxidation of OC is complete, and CH4 could be gradually oxidized. (IV) The appearance of O2 is observed in this stage, which represents that oxidation of OC has been completed. Only R1 and R4 exist in the reactor at this time. The conjecture about this process will be further validated in the next section. A controlled oxidation time method for the reduced OC was used to investigate the changes of OC during oxidation using CLCO technology. The fluidization gas was immediately switched from low-concentration methane to N2 when the specified oxidation time reached. Meanwhile, the electric furnace was set up for rapid temperature reduction to ensure that OC remains unchanged in the envisaged oxidation state. OC was removed from the reactor for characterization when cooled to room temperature. The oxidation time was chosen to 0 s, 20 s, 30 s, 40 s and 600 s according to the actual test results of the oxidation process. The phase composition of the sample particles was identified by XRD (D8 Advance, Bruker) and Raman (HR800, HORIBA LabRAM). XRD spectra of 170–280 μm OC at 950 °C with different oxidation time is shown in Fig. 6a. It is showed that there are only phases of Fe3O4 and Al2O3 contained in reduced OC (Corresponding to stage I in Section 3.3.1). The presence of Fe2O3 is detected in the sample after 30 s of oxidation (Stage II), while the characteristic peak of Fe3O4 is very blurred after 40 s (Stage III). After 600 s of oxidation (Stage IV), the sample contains only Fe2O3 and Al2O3. It can be seen from Fig. 6b that there are Raman shift at 300 cm−1, 538 cm−1 and 665 cm−1 of oxidation 20 s, corresponding to the characteristic peak of Fe3O4 according to documentation retrieval [43,44] and at 412 cm−1 corresponding to the characteristic peak of Al2O3. The Raman shift at 233 cm−1, 302 cm−1, 420 cm−1, 626 cm−1 and 1353 cm−1 of oxidation 30s corresponds to the characteristic peak of Fe2O3 [45–47]. XRD and Raman spectra verified the plausibility of the hypothesis on the four stages in oxidation process and confirmed that the oxidation process of OC was ultra-fast and basically completed within 40s in this work. Combining the results of the previous studies, we note that 40s after the start of oxidation is a critical point. The OC has essentially completed oxidation at this point, and then the flue gas analyzer starts to detect the presence of O2, and X methane and S c increase rapidly. From above findings we realize that although both Fe3O4 and Fe2O3 may be catalytic for CH4, Fe3O4 will first adsorb O2 for oxidation in an oxygen-rich atmosphere, and formed Fe2O3 will provide the active site for the catalytic oxidation of CH4. Twenty OC particles were randomly selected from each sample to acquire mechanical strength as measured by FGP-1 digital dynamometer, respectively. The changes in mechanical strength of freshly prepared OC with different particle sizes, and OC with 170–280 μm at different oxidation time and OC with different particle sizes at different reaction temperatures are systematically compared by box-plot in Fig. 7 . One interesting finding is that the mechanical strength of OC is generally around 2–5 N except for a few extreme points, which is strongly related to the particle size. The mechanical strength of larger size OC (270–355 μm) is 29.23% higher than small size (106–170 μm). This value is reduced to 28.71% after several reactions which could be considered to remain stable. According to these data, we can infer that large size OC has better resistance to attrition during fluidization. Another important finding is that the mechanical strength of OC gradually increases with the increase of oxidation time until it is close to the strength of freshly prepared. In other words, the mechanical strength of Fe3O4 is lower than that of Fe2O3, which may be one of the reasons why the attrition rate of reduction is greater than that of oxidation reaction. This finding is consistent with previous studies [48]. All DFT calculations were carried out by Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) exchange correlation function of the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) as implemented in CASTEP [49,50]. The electron–ion interaction was described by ultra-soft pseudopotentials (USP) [51]. With an energy cutoff of 400 eV, Monkhorst-Pack grid k-points separation of plane wave basis set was 0.04 for bulk optimization and surface relaxation. Geometry optimization was performed using the Broyden Fletcher Goldfarb Shanno (BFGS) method. The convergence criteria of self-consistent field (SCF) tolerance, energy tolerance, maximum force tolerance, maximum displacement tolerance was 1.0 × 10−6 eV/atom, 1.0 × 10−5 eV/atom, 0.03 eV/Å, 0.001 Å, respectively. As is shown in Fig. 8 , the calculated lattice parameters of Fe2O3 were a = b = 4.921 Å, c = 13.414 Å and Fe3O4 (a = b = c = 8.216 Å), in good agreement with the experimental measurement Fe2O3 (a = b = 5.035 Å, c = 13.747 Å) and Fe3O4 (a = b = c = 8.394 Å) [52,53]. Fe2O3 (001) and Fe3O4 (111) surface are modeled using (2 × 1) and (1 × 1) periodic slabs, respectively. The 15 Å thickness vacuum layer is set to eliminate the interaction between the adjacent slabs. For Fe2O3 (0 0 1), the bottom five layers of atoms are fixed and the top two layers fully relaxed. In addition, the bottom six layers of atoms of Fe3O4 (1 1 1) are constrained and the top two layers fully relaxed. The adsorption energy is defined as follows: E ads = E surf + adsorbate − E surf − E adsorbate where E surf+adsorbate is the total energy of the substrate together with the adsorbate, E surf is the total energy of bare substrate, and E adsorbate is the total energy of adsorbate. Stable adsorption structures and adsorption energy of the possible reaction in oxidation process were studied, as is shown in Fig. 9 . In configuration RA, CH4 interacts with the surface Fe3O4 with an adsorption energy of −3.8 kJ/mol, which illustrates that the adsorption of CH4 and Fe3O4 (1 1 1) is weak and belongs to physical adsorption. In configuration RB, CH4 adsorbs on the Fe atom to form CFe and HFe bonds with bond lengths of 2.276 Å and 1.878 Å, respectively, and the adsorption energy is −44.35 kJ/mol. O2 chemisorbs with the surface Fe atom in configuration R3 to form FeO bonds with bond lengths of 1.937 Å and 1.745 Å, respectively. Besides, the bond lengths of OO bonds become significantly longer from 1.240 Å to 1.358 Å, which indicates that O atom is activated. The adsorption energy of RC is −282.19 kJ/mol, which is an extremely strong adsorption. It means that oxidation process occurs spontaneously without energy barrier due to the high reactivity of surface Fe atom. The electron differential density of CH4/O2 adsorption on the Fe2O3 (0 0 1)/Fe3O4 (1 1 1) surface is shown in Fig. 10 . As seen in Fig. 10 (a), there is almost no significant electron migration with slight loss of electron on the surface of Fe atom and the CH bond. This confirms that the bond between Fe3O4 and CH4 is not formed. Obvious electron transfer between C-Fe/H-Fe bridge is observed in Fig. 10 (b). The electrons lost by Fe atom move to form bonds between C-Fe/H-Fe, and electron transfer occurs near H atom. In Fig. 10 (c), the most notable is the intense electron transfer between OFe bridge site, which is much larger than that in RA and RB. Comparing the results of adsorption energy and electron differential density, we find that RA is not occurring during oxidation because the ability of Fe3O4 to adsorb O2 is much higher than that of CH4. With both O2 and CH4 presented in the surface, Fe3O4 will adsorb O2 for oxidation reaction instead of reacting with CH4. Numerous O2 will be consumed during this process, resulting in the limitation of the catalytic oxidation of CH4. Once OC oxidation is completed, CH4 can be catalytically oxidized normally. The catalytic oxidation pathway of CH4 in CLC process has been extensively studied [42,54], therefore, it will not be discussed in this work. And a comprehensive review of methane conversion can be found elsewhere [27,55]. Schematic diagram of oxidation process of CLCO is illustrated in Fig. 11 . The finding clearly indicates that (1) oxidation of OC has the highest priority, and (2) completely oxidized OC participates in the catalytic oxidation of CH4 as a catalyst. This is consistent with the conclusion obtained from the experiment. With numerous types and complex composition, VOCs created by human activities are one of the main causes of environmental problems, which include alkanes, olefins, halogenated hydrocarbons, aromatic hydrocarbons and other organic substances. The feasibility of using UCM as an oxidizing gas has been verified in previous work. Considering that both methane and VOCs are carbon-containing organics with similar physicochemical properties, decomposition of VOCs using CLCO is theoretically feasible. In this case, propene, a typical representative of VOCs, was chosen as oxidation gas for exploration experiment. With reaction temperature at 950 °C and OC with 170–280 μm, the composition of the simulated VOCs gas was 0.5% C3H6, 20% O2 and balanced by N2. Other settings remained the same as the previous experiments. Similarly, air is used as the oxidizing gas as a control group, which oxygen concentration is shown as blue dashed data in Fig. 12 . Unsurprisingly, there is not much effect on the oxidation behavior of OC using C3H6 as oxidation gas. The presence of O2 is detected almost at the same moment between two samples and rises rapidly to the pre-set value. It can be found that the ratio of consumed oxygen to the concentration of C3H6 introduced is close to 4.5 when the oxygen concentration stabilized, which is exactly in line with the reaction equation of complete catalytic oxidation of propene. Although the propene involved in the reaction is completely converted to CO2, the conversion of propene reaches a maximum of 94% and then slowly decreases to about 88.5% after 600 s. The present experimental study proves that CLCO is a potential technology with a wide range of applications and can effectively dispose VOCs. Further investigations are needed to improve the activity of OC for complete conversion of VOCs and gain a better understanding of the process. In this paper, the conversion performance of simulation gas with different compositions were experimentally studied in a laboratory-scale fluidized bed reactor under different temperatures. The adsorption energy and electron differential density of each reaction in the oxidation process were calculated by DFT. Besides, the feasibility of coupling VOCs with CLCO was explored. These experiments confirm that CLCO is not only feasible in disposing ultra-low concentrations methane, but also effective in treating VOCs. Nevertheless, a more catalytically active OC needs to be further investigated, otherwise it is difficult to achieve complete conversion of VOCs. The work provides a potential method for the treatment of UCM and VOCs. Conclusions can be summarized as following: (1) The oxidation of reduced OC is the main reaction at the beginning of oxidation stage. A large amount of O2 is consumed in this process, which limits the catalytic oxidation of CH4 and results in a low level of X methane and S c . The low conversion process usually lasts around a few tens of seconds depending on the reactor device. Therefore, it is recommended to extend the residence time of OC in AR appropriately when using CLCO technology to achieve complete conversion. (2) Satisfactory results cannot be achieved at a reaction temperature of 750 °C with three particle sizes of OC, while ultra-low concentration methane can be completely converted to CO2 with the temperature above 850 °C. (3) Both experiments and theoretical calculations reveal that the adsorption of Fe3O4 with O2 is greater than that with CH4. In other words, Fe3O4 would not further reduce with CH4 in the presence of O2. OC first plays the role of a carrier for the delivery of lattice oxygen in oxidation process, and then functions as a catalyst for the catalytic oxidation of methane. The oxidation of reduced OC is the main reaction at the beginning of oxidation stage. A large amount of O2 is consumed in this process, which limits the catalytic oxidation of CH4 and results in a low level of X methane and S c . The low conversion process usually lasts around a few tens of seconds depending on the reactor device. Therefore, it is recommended to extend the residence time of OC in AR appropriately when using CLCO technology to achieve complete conversion. Satisfactory results cannot be achieved at a reaction temperature of 750 °C with three particle sizes of OC, while ultra-low concentration methane can be completely converted to CO2 with the temperature above 850 °C. Both experiments and theoretical calculations reveal that the adsorption of Fe3O4 with O2 is greater than that with CH4. In other words, Fe3O4 would not further reduce with CH4 in the presence of O2. OC first plays the role of a carrier for the delivery of lattice oxygen in oxidation process, and then functions as a catalyst for the catalytic oxidation of methane.
abstract: Ultra-low concentration methane (UCM) from coal mines is difficult to be combusted or oxidized, and is usually emitted directly into the atmosphere, resulting in the waste of numerous clean energy and the deterioration of the greenhouse gas effect. In this study, a concept was presented as chemical looping catalytic oxidation (CLCO). The influencing factors on methane conversion and CO2 selectivity were explored on a laboratory-scale fluidized bed, and oxidation processes were investigated by theoretical calculations. The results suggest that Fe3O4 would not further reduce by CH4 in the presence of O2 and CLCO is effective and feasible with around 95% methane conversion and near 99% CO2 selectivity of all experimental group above 850 °C. The oxidation of CLCO can be divided into two processes: (1) reduced oxygen carrier (OC) first adsorbs oxygen and be oxidized to achieve the transfer of lattice oxygen and (2) OC functions as a catalyst for the catalytic oxidation of methane after complete oxidation. Besides, exploratory experiments show that CLCO is also effective in treating VOCs with near 90% propene conversion rate.
title: A novel concept for ultra-low concentration methane treatment based on chemical looping catalytic oxidation
