full_text: Fossil fuels are the most fundamental driving forces for the rapid technologic development and economic growth. Oil continues to be the primary source of energy for transportation and as a feedstock for chemicals. Methane constitutes the main component of natural gas. Its ready availability and abundance make this compound an attractive feedstock for use in our predominantly oil based chemical industry. It also has potential to produce valuable chemical intermediates through reforming reaction [1,2]. Besides, natural gas is the least carbon-intensive of the major energy sources, emitting up to 60% less CO2 emissions than coal when used for electricity generation. In this context, the shift toward natural gas will carry tremendous benefits for the environment and consumers [3,4]. Carbon dioxide reforming of methane, namely, dry reforming of methane (DRM), is a method of producing synthesis gas (mixture of hydrogen and carbon monoxide) [5,6]. Synthesis gas is conventionally produced via the steam reforming reaction. In recent years, international concerns over the contribution of greenhouse gases on global warming have increased interest in the replacement of steam as reactant with carbon dioxide. Thermodynamically, DRM is favored at high temperature (above 700 °C) and atmospheric pressure [7,8]. Therefore, efficient catalysts and suitable catalytic processes are required to make DRM viable. Currently, the general tendency of the catalyst development is the application of transition metals as an active ingredient. Among them, nickel-based catalyst is recognized as the most appropriate candidate due to its reasonable catalytic activity and affordable price [9,10]. Nevertheless, the deactivation of this kind of catalyst caused by particle agglomeration and/or coke formation is inevitable [11–13]. Particular effort has been invested into the research and development of efficient and steady reforming catalysts [14,15]. Addition of secondary metals to Ni-based catalysts has been studied with relatively promising results [16,17]. From the economic viewpoint, the easily accessible transition metals such as Cu [18,19], Fe [20–22] and Co [23] have been widely applied to alloy with nickel. These three kinds of metals have similar electronic configurations and chemical properties with nickel, thus, their incorporations can readily generate Ni-based bimetallic alloy that exerts synergetic effect [24,25]. Jae-Hee Lee and co-workers [26] reported that appropriate Cu amount could suppress the carbon deposition of Cu-Ni/Al2O3 and enhance its stability. More recently, Tomishige et al. [27] verified that an appropriate Cu composition (25–45%) doped in Ni/Mg(Al)O catalyst could inhibit CH4 dissociation and promote CO2 dissociation, and subsequently suppressed the coke deposition. Concerning the NiCo bimetallic catalyst, Bian et al. [28] and Zhou et al. [29] confirmed that the addition of Co into Ni/SiO2 and Ni/SBA-15 catalyst enhanced the metal-support interaction, suppressed metal sintering and carbon deposition, resulting in a significantly stable catalytic performance for DRM. Takanabe et al. [30] recently provided the evidence using in-operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy that the negligible amount of deposited carbon over CoNi catalyst was likely due to balancing the oxidative (CO2, H2O) and reductive (CH4, CO, H2) species reactions on the CoNi surface. Due to good redox properties, Fe is a suitable option as a co-catalyst. Nanosized nickel ferrite catalyst NiFe2O4 promoted the DRM reaction even at low temperature [31]. A study of LaNi(1−x)Fe x O3 catalysts to obtain synthesis gas showed that the addition of iron to perovskite LaNiO3 could control the reversible migration of nickel from the structure to the surface [32]. Especially recently, Fe-modified Ni catalysts has held wide vision of research. Several studies have indicated that the formation of FeO helped to mitigate coke formation through its reaction with the surface carbon under dry reforming condition [33–35]. When MgAl mixed oxides were used as support, the migration of FeO at the surface of Ni-Fe/Mg x Al y O z significantly reduced the coke formation and improved stability of NiFe catalysts through a Fe2+O/Fe0 redox cycle [36]. Subsequently, a series of NiFe redox catalysts with different Ni/Fe ratios was designed for traditional steam reforming of methane and the equimolar NiFe catalyst was exemplarily described to be catalytically active. It presented outstanding cycling stability, owing to the advantages of the highly reactive Ni species for CH4 activation, Fe species for water splitting, and NiFe alloy in the reduced catalyst for catalytic CH4 decomposition [37]. Contrast with the conventional support, substantial researches have demonstrated that well-ordered mesoporous supports (micro- and mesoporous silica [38] and organized mesoporous alumina [39]) can effectively control metal particle size and improve the dispersion of active metal. The ordered mesopore with narrow size is beneficial to anchor the active particles within the channel, thus the metal agglomeration can be restrained. Among the ordered mesoporous materials, ordered mesoporous alumina (OMA) with strong mechanical strength and high thermal stability up to 1000 °C was a very potential support in DRM [40,41]. For example, mesoporous nickel-alumina composite catalyst synthesized by the evaporation-induced self-assembly (EISA) method showed superior catalytic performance to those prepared by the conventional impregnation method in DRM reaction, probably ascribable to the strong metal-support interactions and highly nickel dispersion [42]. Incorporation of Pd into Ni/OMA catalyst exhibited higher activity than the monometallic (Pd/OMA, Ni/OMA) due to the formation of NiPd nanoalloy [43]. Mg-modified mesoporous NiAl catalyst presented robust resistance to coke deposition and high catalytic performance in the coupled reforming reaction, which was associated with the stronger basicity [44]. More importantly, confinement of the nickel active phase within structured alumina pores helped minimizing Ni0 sintering during reaction, thus preserving reactivity with time on stream [45–47]. Considering the confinement of ordered mesoporous alumina and synergetic effect of bimetal, in this study Ni-based bimetallic ordered mesoporous alumina catalysts (MNiAl, M = Fe, Co, or Cu) were synthesized via a “one-pot” evaporation-induced self-assembly (EISA) method. Reactivity comparison of FeNiAl, CuNiAl, CoNiAl, and NiAl suggested that the incorporation of Fe improved the catalytic activity for DRM. Hence, several catalysts with constant nickel loading and different Fe loadings were used to investigate the effect of the Fe:Ni ratio upon catalyst activity and stability during DRM. The promoting effect of the Fe and the deactivation of FeNiAl catalysts during DRM were addressed by performing X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and scanning transmission electron microscopy-energy dispersive X-ray (STEM-EDX) microanalysis. The bimetallic Ni-based ordered mesoporous alumina catalysts (MNiAl) were synthesized using a “one-pot” evaporation-induced self-assembly (EISA) method [48]. The amount of Ni was fixed at 10 wt% relative to aluminum oxide and the molar ratio of M/Ni was 0.5. In a typical synthesis procedure, 1.0 g of (EO)20(PO)70(EO)20 triblock copolymer (Pluronic P123 from Sigma-Aldrich, 5800 g/mol nominal molecular weight) and stoichiometric amount of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O were dissolved in 10 mL of anhydrous ethanol at room temperature (25–35 °C). Subsequently, a mixed solution including 10 mL of anhydrous ethanol, 1.6 mL of nitric acid (67 wt% aqueous HNO3), 10 mmol of aluminum isopropoxide, and secondary metal nitrate precursor (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, or Cu(NO3)2·2.5H2O) was added into the above nickel salt solution. The resulting mixture was kept in vigorous stirring for 5 h, and then dried at 60 °C for 60 h to evaporate the solvent. The final gel was further calcined in a muffle over at 750 °C for 5 h (ramping rate of 1 °C/min, static condition, in air). x-FeNiAl catalysts with other Fe/Ni molar ratios (x = 0.3, 0.7, 0.9) were synthesized using the same method. For comparison, FeAl (the loading of Fe was 6.7 wt%) and NiAl (the loading of Ni was 10 wt%) monometallic catalysts were also synthesized. The actual nickel and iron content in the calcined catalysts was evaluated by an Agilent 240FS AA flame atomic absorption spectrophotometer (AAS). Prior to measurement, the catalysts were completely digested via a mixture of aqua regia and hydrofluoric acid, and then diluted with 5% hydrochloric acid to a certain concentration. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the calcined, reduced, and spent catalysts were detected on a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker) using a Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 0.154 nm) over the scattering angle from 20° to 80° at a 0.02° step with the acquisition time of 0.2 s. Low-angle measurements for the pure alumina support (OMA) and x-FeNiAl catalysts were performed on SmartLab diffractometer (Rigaku) in the 2θ = 0.5–3° region at a 0.01° step size with the acquisition time of 0.4 s. Surface morphology and active particle distribution for the catalysts was detected by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a JEM-2100F (JEOL) electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The elemental mapping was examined by scanning transmission electron microscopy-energy dispersive X-ray (STEM-EDX) microanalysis with an XFlash 5030 spectrometer (Bruker). Before measurement, the catalyst was dispersed in absolute ethanol using a moderate sonication, then 10 μL of the solution was dropped on double copper grids covered with a carbon membrane layer. Nitrogen physisorption was performed on a Tristar II 3020 surface area and porosity analyzer (Micromeritics) at −196 °C. Prior to the measurement, the catalysts were degassed at 150 °C for 6 h to eliminate water or gas impurities existing in the solid pores. The specific surface area (S BET) was determined via the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) equation in the relative pressure (P/P 0) range of 0.06–0.30. Total pore volume (V p) was calculated from the adsorption isotherm at a relative pressure of 0.995. The pore size distribution was calculated using Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) theory according to the adsorption branch of nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms. The H2 chemisorption was conducted on an AutoChemII 2920 chemisorption analyzer (Micromeritics). Before the measurement, the catalyst was reduced at 800 °C for 1 h under 10% H2/Ar flow, and subsequently purged with Ar flow at 800 °C for 30 min and cooled down to 50 °C. The H2 pulse chemisorption was measured at this temperature by introducing several pulses of 10% H2/Ar until no further uptake of H2 was detected. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of the reduced and spent catalysts were recorded on an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher) equipped with a monochromatized Al Kα (hν = 1486.6 eV) X-ray source. Binding energies of all profiles were corrected to the adventitious C 1s peak at 284.8 eV. In the case of the reduced catalyst, the reduction was carried out ex-situ before the XPS measurement. The reduced and spent catalysts were transferred in air to the XPS analysis chamber. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential thermal analysis (DTA) were carried out on Q600 SDT Simultaneous thermal analyzer (TA Instruments) to determine the nature and amount of carbon deposited over the spent catalysts. The catalyst (about 6 mg) was located in an alumina crucible and heated in flowing air atmosphere (40 mL/min) from room temperature to 800 °C with the heating rate of 10 °C/min. Methane reforming with CO2 reaction was conducted at atmospheric pressure in a vertical fixed-bed, continuous flow quartz reactor (i.d. 6 mm, o.d. 8 mm, length: 450 mm). 0.1 g catalyst with particle size of 180–250 μm was sandwiched between two pieces of quartz wool and positioned in the middle of the reactor. The reaction temperature was detected by a nickel-chromium/nickel-silicon thermocouple covered with a suited quartz thermocouple well (i.d. 1.5 mm), which was placed at the outlet of the catalyst bed. Activity measurements were performed at 700 °C with a feeding gas of CH4/CO2 (molar ratio = 1:1) at a total flow rate of 40 mL/min (gas hourly space velocity (GHSV) = 24,000 mL/gcat·h). The flow rate of each feed was monitored by the mass flow controller (D07-7B/ZM, Beijing Sevenstar Electronic Co., Ltd.). Prior to the experiment, all catalysts were reduced in situ at 800 °C for 30 min with a hydrogen flow of 60 mL/min. An ice-water trap was located between the reactor outlet and the sampling port to remove the gaseous water generated via the reverse water gas shift (RWGS) reaction. The total flow rate of the produced gas leaving an ice trap was measured by using a soap film flowmeter, and the gas composition was analyzed online by a gas chromatograph (GC9800, Shanghai Kechuang Chromatograph Instruments Co., Ltd) equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a stainless steel column filled with TDX-01. The catalytic reforming of methane with carbon dioxide over bimetallic nickel-based MNiAl catalysts (0.5-CuNiAl, 0.5-FeNiAl, 0.5-CoNiAl and NiAl) was evaluated under typical reaction condition (Fig. 1 ). CO2 conversion was higher than that of CH4 and the H2/CO molar ratio was less than unity, which indicated the concomitance of reverse water-gas shift (RWGS) reaction (CO2 + H2 = CO + H2O) [49]. Additionally, the thermodynamic calculation was performed using HSC Chemistry 6.0 Software by employing the Gibbs free energy minimization method. When the simultaneous contribution of the DRM and RWGS were considered and formation of solid carbon was ignored, the thermodynamic equilibrium CH4 conversion, CO2 conversion and H2/CO ratio at 700 °C were 72.5%, 82.3%, and 0.87, respectively (red dotted lines in Fig. 1). The catalytic performance of MNiAl catalysts with corresponding conversions of CH4 and CO2 during 6 h reaction were listed in Table S1. 0.5-FeNiAl exhibited the highest catalytic activity with a CH4 conversion of 60% at the beginning of the reaction, and the initial catalytic activity for MNiAl catalysts followed the order of 0.5-FeNiAl > NiAl > 0.5-CoNiAl > 0.5-CuNiAl. In terms of the stability, the deactivation rate decreased with the following sequence: 0.5-CuNiAl (1.18% h−1) > 0.5-FeNiAl (0.72% h−1) > NiAl (0.27% h−1) > 0.5-CoNiAl (0.22% h−1). The change in deactivation rate was probably related to the nature and amount of carbon deposition (Fig. 2 ). The DTA curves exhibited two kinds of peaks: positive peak (exothermic peak) and negative peak (endothermic peak) [50]. All the spent catalysts presented a negative peak around 55 °C and a strong positive peak between 250–350 °C, which were assigned to the desorption of the moisture and the oxidation of metallic species, respectively [51]. The positive peak between 100–250 °C indicated the oxidations of amorphous carbon [52]. This peak in 0.5-FeNiAl and NiAl was slightly higher than 0.5-CoNiAl and 0.5-CuNiAl, which was in line with their higher initial catalytic activity. Significantly, 0.5-CuNiAl catalyst showed a strong positive peak around 600 °C, suggesting the formation of inert carbon [53]. Based on the exothermic weight loss above 400 °C in the TGA profiles (Table S1), 0.5-CuNiAl possessed higher carbon formation rate (ca. 11.4 mg-C/g-cat·h) as compared to that of NiAl, 0.5-FeNiAl, and 0.5-CoNiAl catalysts (ca. 4.3, 4.5, and 3.2 mg-C/g-cat·h, respectively). This result was in accordance with the aforementioned deactivation rates. The most of carbon deposition over 0.5-CuNiAl catalysts were less-active carbon species, which was hardly oxidized and hence leading to a rapid deactivation. Notably, 0.5-FeNiAl presented similar carbon deposition with NiAl, however the former catalyst showed preferable catalytic performance. Therefore, a series of x-FeNiAl catalyst with different Fe content (x = 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9) was chosen for further study in order to investigate whether there was any synergetic effect of FeNi on improving the catalytic activity and stability of FeNiAl catalyst. The elemental composition of Ni and Fe were both about 1 wt% less than the nominal values (data in the parentheses) (Table 1 ) which was due to the inevitable loss during the synthesis process. Even so, the molar ratio of Fe/Ni in each catalyst matched well with the set value. N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the calcined alumina supports (OMA) and x-FeNiAl catalysts displayed that all catalysts showed type IV isotherm with H1-type hysteresis loop (Fig. S1Acalcined), which confirmed the presence of mesoporous architecture with ordered cylindrical pores [54,55]. The distinct capillary condensation step at a P/P 0 range of 0.7–0.9 indicated that the great majority of the adsorption occurred within the ordered cylindrically shaped mesopores [56]. The corresponding pore size distributions curves of these materials derived from the adsorption branch were given in Fig. S1Bcalcined. All the catalysts showed a unimodal mesoporous size distribution and the peaks of the curves were all located in the range of 7–12 nm. However, the sharpness of the capillary condensation step decreased gradually with the increase in Fe/Ni ratio. The slight loss of surface area (106–113 m2/g) and pore volume (0.23–0.29 cm3/g) were observed in all Fe-containing catalysts compared to Fe-free catalyst (137 m2/g, 0.37 cm3/g) (Table 1). The dramatic decrease in specific surface area for 0.9-FeNiAl (96 m2/g) implied that the long-range ordered mesopores were destroyed with the excess Fe loading. This result indicated that the presence of Fe3+ within a controlled concentration would not significantly affect the formation of mesophase during EISA process. Wide-angle XRD patterns of all calcined catalysts were shown in Fig. 3 . For OMA (ao curve in Fig. 3), three characteristic peaks at 37.6°, 45.8° and 67.0° were observed, which was assigned to γ-Al2O3 (JCPDS 10-0425). With the incorporation of nickel and/or iron (b–e curves in Fig. 3), (4 0 0) and (4 4 0) diffraction peaks slightly shifted to lower angles compared with OMA, proving the formation of NiAl2O4 spinel phase (JCPDS 10-0339) [57,58]. Additionally, no corresponding diffraction peaks of iron species was observed in x-FeNiAl catalysts, indicating all the iron species were highly dispersed, or existed in the amorphous form. More to the point, the addition of Fe had negligible impact on the crystallization degree and crystalline sizes, as evidence by no apparent change in the intensity of the diffraction peaks for NiAl2O4 with different Fe loading. The presence of clear mesostructure in OMA and x-FeNiAl catalysts was proved by low-angle XRD patterns (Fig. 4 ). All materials showed a strong diffraction peak at 0.7° and a weak peak around 1.3°, which were attributed to the (100) and (110) planes, respectively [48,59]. This result suggested that the incorporation of the Fe and/or Ni incorporation did not strongly affect the 2D hexagonal ordered structure (p6mm symmetry) of the original OMA framework. However, the intensities of (1 0 0) and (1 1 0) diffraction peaks decreased gradually with Fe loading. When x ≥ 0.7, the diffraction peaks shifted to higher angles and the intensities simultaneously decreased, indicating that the mesoporous structure slightly collapsed and some pores were shrunk by the excess Fe loading. All calcined x-FeNiAl catalysts maintained the ordered mesoporous morphology with an obvious alignment of the cylindrical pores and majority of metal particles were inside the framework and embedded in the wall (some dark spots representing the nanoparticles) (Fig. 5 ). For the catalyst with higher Fe/Ni ratio (x ≥ 0.7), in spite of the presence of ordered hexagonal structure, some ordered mesopores were destroyed, which illustrated that ferric salt precursor could disturb the process of EISA and lead to a formation of slightly disordered mesostructure. With further increase in the Fe/Ni molar ratio to 0.9, some worm-like morphologies were observed (Fig. 5e), which confirmed that excess incorporation of ferric salt could destroy the EISA process. Similar results were also reported by Zhao for mesoporous NiO-Y2O3-Al2O3 materials [60]. The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of all reduced x-FeNiAl catalysts presented type IV isotherms with H1-type hysteresis loops and the pore distribution remained narrow after reduction at 800 °C (Fig. S1Areduced, Breduced). This result demonstrated that all the catalysts possessed preferable thermal resistance. However, compared to the calcined counterparts, the intensity of the pore size distribution for all reduced materials slightly attenuated (Fig. S1Breduced). This behavior could mainly be ascribed to minor thermal contraction of the alumina framework during harsh reduction treatments [61]. Furthermore, it was noticeable that compared to the calcined NiAl, the surface area for reduced NiAl decreased by 35% (from 137 to 89 m2/g) and the pore volume dropped by 38% (from 0.37 cm3/g to 0.23 cm3/g) (Table 1). This was because some particles in NiAl catalyst migrated from the channels to the external surface and/or agglomerated. However, this decline was not significant for reduced x-FeNiAl catalysts, when compared to their calcined individuals (12–20% and 13–20%, respectively). This indicated that the incorporation of Fe could enhance the thermal stability of mesostructure to some extent. Wide-angle XRD patterns of reduced catalysts presented no NiAl2O4 spinel phase (Fig. 6 ). However, a phase transition to crystalline γ-Al2O3 phase along with the appearance of Ni0 was observed, revealing that the reduction of NiAl2O4 spinel occurred. Similar results were also reported by Jabbour [61] for one-pot synthesis of meso Ni-Al2O3 materials. Reduced monometallic NiAl catalyst exhibited three distinct diffraction peaks at 44.6°, 51.8°, and 76.4°, which was belonged to metallic Ni species (JCPDS 04-0850). Interestingly, the above peaks slightly shifted to lower angles with the Fe incorporation and the corresponding peaks centered at 44.2°, 51.5°, and 75.9° were related to FeNi3 alloy phase (JCPDS 38-0419) [62–64]. Due to the overlap between the Ni0, FeNi3 and γ-Al2O3 peaks, it was difficult to determine the average particle size of metal nanoparticles. Moreover, with the increase in Fe content, the intensity of FeNi3 diffraction peaks increased, suggesting that increasing Fe content facilitated the formation of FeNi3 alloy. Additionally, the bulk compositions of NiFe alloy in the reduced x-FeNiAl catalysts presented in Table S2 were determined using Vegard's rule [65]. The Fe/Ni molar ratios were 0.23, 0.34, 0.48, and 0.71 for 0.3-, 0.5-, 0.7-, and 0.9-FeNiAl catalysts, respectively. Obviously, the Fe/Ni molar ratio increased with the Fe loading and was lower than their nominal values. This result indicated that the Fe related species only partially existed in the form of FeNi alloy, while the remainder might exist in other forms. TEM images of the reduced x-FeNiAl catalysts (x ≤ 0.7) reserved the ordered mesoporous structures (Fig. 7 ). Metallic nanoparticles (very small dark spots on the grey alumina matrix) homogeneously located on the porous structure, and they exhibited similar particles size of approximately 7 nm. However, the reduced 0.9-FeNiAl catalysts presented “worm-like” appearance (Fig. 7e) without clear pore arrangements. Such shape was contrasted with the organized mesoporous network of the x-FeNiAl catalysts (x ≤ 0.7) and revealed a partial structural damage when doping excess Fe. In the partially structural damaged 0.9-FeNiAl catalyst, most of the metallic dispersed on the external surface with an average particle size around 9 nm. A possible explanation for this phenomenon was that the partially destroyed framework weakened the confinement effect of pore wall and led to the migration and agglomeration of metal particles. The surface chemistry information of reduced x-FeNiAl catalysts was detected by XPS analysis. The surface compositions (atom percentage Atom%) were calculated from the peak areas of Ni, Fe, Al and O elements (Table S3). It was worthy to mention that the bulk compositions of Fe/Ni in the reduced x-FeNiAl catalysts (Table S2) were slightly lower than their calcined forms (Table 1). However, the Fe/Ni molar ratios on the surfaces of the reduced x-FeNiAl catalysts were higher than their bulk compositions, suggesting that the surface Fe species was richer after the reduction treatment. The Ni 2p 3/2 XPS spectra of all the reduced catalysts as well as the deconvolution results were showed in Fig. 8 . Over all, the locations of Ni 2p 3/2 peaks were centered at 852.6 eV, 855.60 eV and accompanied by a shake-up satellite peak at circa 861.6 eV. According to the previous classification [66,67], the binding energy of Ni 2p 3/2 around 856 eV was ascribed to the Ni2+ species strongly interacted with Al2O3 supports, while that at 852.8 eV was assigned to metallic Ni species. This result indicated that Ni0 and Ni2+ species coexisted on the surface of reduced NiAl and x-FeNiAl catalysts. In reduced x-FeNiAl catalysts, the surface Ni0 gradually decreased with the increasing molar ratio of Fe/Ni (Table S3), indicating the reduction of Ni was inhibited with the addition of Fe. The XPS spectra of Fe 2p 3/2 for the reduced catalyst (Fig. 8) indicated iron species existed in several oxidation states. The 706, 710 and 712 eV could be assigned to Fe0, Fe2+ and Fe3+ species, respectively [68]. Clearly, Fe species mainly existed in two states (Fe2+ and Fe3+) on the surface of x-FeNiAl catalysts (in the range of 710–713 eV). However, there was also a small amount of Fe0 species, and the surface Fe0 decreased with the increasing of Fe/Ni ratio (Table S3). Moreover, the central binding energy of Fe 2p 3/2 (713 eV) in the reduced FeAl (a2 in Fig. 8) shifted to lower values in x-FeNiAl catalysts, and reached a minimum when x ≥ 0.7 (712.0 eV). This result indicated that Fe and Ni in the x-FeNiAl catalysts might have reacted with each other to form the FeNi3 alloy after reduction (as shown in XRD patterns). H2 chemisorption experiment (Table S2) revealed that 2.0 and 22.5 μmol/gcat of hydrogen were chemisorbed on FeAl and NiAl catalysts, indicating that H2 was chemisorbed mainly on Ni sites [36]. In addition, the quantity of chemisorbed H2 decreased with an increasing Fe/Ni ratio, suggesting the surface Ni0 decreased with Fe/Ni ratio. This result was in agreement with previous XPS results (Table S3). The catalytic methane reforming with carbon dioxide for x-FeNiAl catalysts was carried out at 700 °C for 24 h. The catalytic performance of x-FeNiAl catalysts with corresponding conversions of CH4 and CO2 were also listed in Table S1, including initial conversions and the deactivations during 24 h reaction. The intrinsic catalytic performance of CH4 consumption rate, CO2 consumption rate and H2 selectivity were normalized by the surface Ni0 content (Fig. 9 ). FeAl catalyst without Ni component was not active for DRM, the conversions of CH4 and CO2 were <1% (not shown). All FeNiAl catalysts exhibited better catalytic behavior than NiAl catalyst, indicating that the presence of FeNi3 facilitated the DRM reaction. The initial conversion of CH4 and CO2 gradually increased with Fe content and reached a maximum for the 0.7-FeNiAl catalyst. According to wide-angle XRD pattern of 0.7-FeNiAl catalyst (curve d in Fig. 6), the intensity of FeNi3 alloy diffraction peaks was stronger than that of other FeNiAl catalysts, confirming that there was more FeNi3 alloy formed in the 0.7-FeNiAl. Therefore, the difference of activity was probably related to the content of FeNi3 formed after reduction treatment. In spite of highly initial activity, remarkable deactivation of all FeNiAl catalysts was observed within 24 h reaction compared to monometallic NiAl catalyst, which indirectly demonstrated that the Fe addition indeed improved the initial catalytic performance independent on the additive amount however could not enhance the stability. To obtain the information of porous structure, the spent catalysts were analyzed by means of N2 adsorption-desorption and TEM. From sorption data (Fig. S1Aspent, Bspent), only a slight textural collapse was noticed after 24 h reaction, and all the spent catalyst still exhibited a well-defined type IV isotherm, which were similar to those of the reduced catalyst. Additionally, the tiny loss in surface area and pore volume were detected (Table 1), and the corresponding pore size distributions remained unchanged. This revealed a satisfying structural resistance of the organized alumina-based frameworks in spite of the drastic conditions of reaction. In line with the conclusion from Fig. S1Aspent, Bspent, sintering during the 24 h exhausting tests could be ignored since the morphologies of all the catalysts almost unchanged (Fig. 10 ). It was clear that the uniform channel system and its ordered hexagonal organization were well preserved, and only a slight increase of metal particle sizes (2–3 nm) could be observed thanks to the thermally stable mesoporous framework. It can be deduced, based on the above discussion, that in the current catalysts, the contribution of the catalyst deactivation from porous structure and sintering were negligible. In DRM, carbon deposition is another critical factor for the deactivation of Ni-based catalysts [69,70]. The carbon species primarily originated from the methane cracking reaction, which could release H2 and form CH x species. In most cases, the oxidation rate of CH x by either CO2 or surface oxygen was lower than the formation rate. As a result, the carbon deposition on the catalysts was inevitable [71]. To quantify the amount of carbon species on the spent catalysts after reaction at 700 °C for 24 h, thermal gravimetric analysis was performed under air atmosphere and the results were summarized in Fig. 11A. The weight loss below 100 °C was derived from thermal desorption of moisture and the curves of all the catalysts experienced a rise in the region from 250 to 380 °C due to the oxidation of the metallic species [72,73]. Two distinct weight-loss stages at 100–250 °C and above 400 °C could be ascribed to the oxidation of amorphous carbon and less-active carbon species, respectively [74]. It was evident that the 0.70-FeNiAl presented the lowest carbon formation (total weight loss: 3.8%, curve d in Fig. 11). However, there was no obvious difference in the amount of less-active carbon over all spent catalysts, while the formation of amorphous carbon reached a minimum in 0.7-FeNiAl, which indicated that the suitable Fe doping could promote the remove of amorphous carbon and subsequently exhibited preferable higher catalytic performance during the DRM reaction [75]. Besides, all catalysts only exhibited tiny graphitic carbon or whisker carbon deposition (<1%) at high temperature (above 600 °C). In addition, the corresponding DTA profiles (Fig. 11B) showed that all the catalysts presented a negative peak around 55 °C, which was assigned to the desorption of the moisture. There were also two weak exothermic peaks were observed between 100–250 °C and 250–380 °C, which were related to the oxidation of amorphous carbon and the oxidation of the metallic species on the spent catalysts, respectively [76]. Interestingly, the peak at high temperature attributed to the oxidation of graphitic carbon was not obvious, implying no inert carbon formed on the x-FeNiAl catalysts. In addition, the amount of carbon deposited on all the spent catalysts (Table S1) gave similar carbon formation rates (<1 mg-C/g-cat·h). This result revealed that the ordered mesostructure in the catalysts prepared by EISA method could effectively improve the coke-resistance performance and also indicated that the carbon deposition was not the reason for catalysts deactivation during DRM reaction. In accordance with TG analysis, the XRD patterns of spent catalysts showed no diffraction peak of carbon species (Fig. 12 ). The XRD data of spent NiAl catalyst (curve a in Fig. 12) did not reveal any change in the structure compared to the reduced one, indicating that the Ni particles was stable under DRM conditions. With respect to spent FeNiAl catalysts, the intensity of the FeNi3 alloy peak decreased and the peaks were slightly shifted to higher angles after reaction, implying that the partial FeNi3 alloy species probably dealloyed and formed Ni metal particles. That was to say, the amount of FeNi3 alloy gradually decreased during reaction, and Ni monometallic particles increased. Therefore, the catalytic performance gradually decreased under these circumstances. More to the point, among the x-FeNiAl series catalysts, this phenomenon was obviously observed in the 0.7-FeNiAl catalysts (curve d in Fig. 12) corresponding to its rapid deactivation during 24 h activity measurements. Accordingly, the composition of the Fe/Ni ratio in the spent catalysts (Table S2) decreased obviously compared with the corresponding reduced catalysts, confirming that the FeNi alloy species was dealloyed under the DRM conditions. On the other hand, STEM-EDX analysis for the spent 0.7-FeNiAl catalyst further revealed the isolation of Ni and Fe particles (Fig. 13 ). For the reduced catalyst (Fig. 13a1), most of the Ni (red) and Fe (green) particles were in the alloy phase (partial yellow) and they were homogeneously dispersed on the catalysts surface. In the spent catalyst (Fig. 13b1), there also were some visible FeNi alloy particles, however, some of the Fe (green) particles were individually dispersed on the support surface, instead of close to Ni (red) particles. The surface chemistry information of spent x-FeNiAl catalysts detected by XPS analysis was summarized in Table S3. Compared with reduced catalysts, the surface Fe species increased and Ni species decreased, especially for 0.7-FeNiAl catalyst. This phenomenon indicated that the Fe species probably dealloyed from FeNi3 alloy and then migrated to the catalyst surface, which covered the active component Ni species and increased the Fe/Ni ratio, eventually resulting in the decline of catalytic activity during 24 h DRM reaction. Fig. 14 displayed the Ni 2p 3/2 XPS spectra of the spent x-FeNiAl catalysts. Ni 2p binding energies at 853.6 eV (Ni0) and 856.5 eV (Ni2+) shifted slightly to higher values compared with the ones observed in reduced catalysts. Similarly, the Fe 2p 3/2 binding energies also shifted to a slightly higher value. More to the point, the Fe 2p binding energy around 708 eV for spent x-FeNiAl catalysts (x ≥ 0.7) was absent. Furthermore, the amount of Fe2+ and Fe3+ oxide increased with the increasing of Fe/Ni ratio (Table S3) and the surface O species correspondingly increased, suggesting the Fe0 species in reduced catalyst was totally oxidized to Fe2+ or Fe3+. For 0.7-FeNiAl catalyst, this oxidation during the DRM reaction might be another reason for its rapid deactivation. Nickel-based bimetallic catalysts were synthesized using one-step EISA method, and their catalytic performance were preliminary evaluated upon methane reforming with CO2 with a molar feed ratio of CH4/CO2 = 1:1 at 700 °C under atmospheric pressure. In the light of preferable performance and enhanced potential of the FeNiAl catalysts, the effect of iron doping on the textural structure and catalytic performance of FeNiAl catalysts was further investigated. The following conclusions could be obtained from this study: (1) FeNiAl catalyst showed better reactivity than CoNiAl and CuNiAl catalysts. Serious carbon deposition over CuNiAl catalyst led to its lowest reactivity and poorest stability. (2) The catalytic performance of x-FeNiAl series catalysts was found to be substantially better than Fe-free NiAl catalyst. The formation of FeNi3 alloy during the reduction was beneficial for DRM reaction. A Fe:Ni molar ratio equal to 0.7 (nominal) was found to be the optimal promotion as it increased the initial activity of the catalyst. (3) x-FeNiAl catalysts with low Fe-loading (x ≤ 0.7) exhibited preferable thermal stability after reaction at 700 °C for 24 h. Although FeNiAl catalysts presented high initial activity in DRM, they suffered from rapid deactivation. However, no obvious sintering of active metals and carbon deposition were found for the spent catalysts. As evidenced by STEM-EDS and XPS analysis, the partial dealloying of FeNi3 alloy during DRM was considered the most important factor contributing to this deactivation. FeNiAl catalyst showed better reactivity than CoNiAl and CuNiAl catalysts. Serious carbon deposition over CuNiAl catalyst led to its lowest reactivity and poorest stability. The catalytic performance of x-FeNiAl series catalysts was found to be substantially better than Fe-free NiAl catalyst. The formation of FeNi3 alloy during the reduction was beneficial for DRM reaction. A Fe:Ni molar ratio equal to 0.7 (nominal) was found to be the optimal promotion as it increased the initial activity of the catalyst. x-FeNiAl catalysts with low Fe-loading (x ≤ 0.7) exhibited preferable thermal stability after reaction at 700 °C for 24 h. Although FeNiAl catalysts presented high initial activity in DRM, they suffered from rapid deactivation. However, no obvious sintering of active metals and carbon deposition were found for the spent catalysts. As evidenced by STEM-EDS and XPS analysis, the partial dealloying of FeNi3 alloy during DRM was considered the most important factor contributing to this deactivation. This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21673079, 21872056), Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (2016A030313518) and Guangzhou Municipal Science and Technology Project (201804010116). Supplementary material Image 1 Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fuproc.2019.05.033.
abstract: The technologic route of efficiently converting methane into higher-value liquid-phase products that are easily transported have led to an increased industrial interest in the dry reforming of methane (DRM) to produce syngas. However, the nonprecious-metal catalysts suffer from a stability issue caused by coke formation. In this work, by evaporation-induced self-assembly (EISA) method, secondary metals (M = Fe, Co, or Cu) were doped into Ni-based ordered mesoporous alumina catalyst (MNiAl). The difference in catalytic behaviors for these catalysts in DRM could be explained in term of carbon deposition. CuNiAl catalyst displayed the lowest reactivity and poor stability, while FeNiAl was catalytically active. The influence of iron content (molar ratio of Fe/Ni ranging from 0.3 to 0.9) on the catalytic performance for x-FeNiAl were further investigated. Increasing the amount of iron in the catalysts affected both the chemical and activity properties. All x-FeNiAl catalysts showed high surface area and ordered mesoporous structure at Fe/Ni = 0–0.7, whereas the mesoporous structure was destroyed at high Fe/Ni molar ratio (=0.9). Iron displayed a positive role in nickel-based catalysts for DRM by virtue of the formation of FeNi3 alloy species during the reduction treatment. 0.7-FeNiAl catalyst was the most active for reforming with regard to the initial activity. By comparison of STEM-EDX, XPS and XRD results for reduced and spent 0.7-FeNiAl catalysts, the deactivation after 24 h DRM reaction was associated with the dealloying of FeNi3 under reforming conditions, rather than change in porous structure and sintering of the active metal. This study demonstrated the importance in manipulating the stability of FeNi3 that are relevant to DRM conditions.
title: Catalytic performance of iron-promoted nickel-based ordered mesoporous alumina FeNiAl catalysts in dry reforming of methane

steps.method_details: dissolved P123 triblock copolymer and Ni(NO3)2·6H2O in anhydrous ethanol
added mixed solution of ethanol, nitric acid, aluminum isopropoxide, and secondary metal nitrate precursor to nickel salt solution
inputs: secondary metal nitrate precursor (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, or Cu(NO3)2·2.5H2O)

steps.method_details: kept mixture in vigorous stirring
dried to evaporate solvent
calcined in muffle oven

per_catalyst_overrides.variation_notes: Uses Cu(NO3)2·2.5H2O as secondary metal precursor with M/Ni molar ratio of 0.5.
Uses Fe(NO3)3·9H2O as secondary metal precursor with M/Ni molar ratio of 0.5.
Uses Co(NO3)2·6H2O as secondary metal precursor with M/Ni molar ratio of 0.5.
Uses Fe(NO3)3·9H2O as secondary metal precursor with Fe/Ni molar ratio of 0.3.
Uses Fe(NO3)3·9H2O as secondary metal precursor with Fe/Ni molar ratio of 0.7.
Uses Fe(NO3)3·9H2O as secondary metal precursor with Fe/Ni molar ratio of 0.9.
Uses Fe(NO3)3·9H2O as secondary metal precursor without Ni, with Fe loading of 6.7 wt%.
