full_text: In recent decades, the conversion of methanol to hydrocarbons (MTH) over ZSM-5 zeolite has been developed to synthesize gasoline, olefin and aromatics etc. which are usually produced by conventional petrochemical technology [1,2]. In this conversion process, the tri-dimensional intersecting channel system of ZSM-5 with uniform straight channels (5.6 Å × 5.3 Å) and zig-zag channels (5.5 Å × 5.1 Å) provides it a high shape selectivity for aforesaid products [3–8]. However, the purely microporous channels of ZSM-5 also cause slow diffusion of bulky reactants and products, resulting in the formation of coke which would block the microchannels and cover the active sites, and eventually led to the rapid deactivation of ZSM-5 [9–13]. To overcome the diffusion limitations of molecular in ZSM-5 catalyst, decreasing its crystal size is an important choice [14–17]. It was pointed out in many reports that nano-ZSM-5 exhibited a better catalytic stability than micro-ZSM-5 in MTH reaction [18,19]. The hugely shortened diffusion path of nano-ZSM-5 guarantees the rapid diffusion of macromolecular, especially the coke precursor, outside the microchannels and reduces the coke deposition in micropores, thus improving the catalytic stability [20–22]. Wang et al. found that the nano-ZSM-5 zeolite with the crystal size of 250 nm showed the highest stability, with a lifetime above 170 h, whereas the micro-ZSM-5 with the crystal size of 2 μm only had a lifetime of about 30 h in the conversion of methanol to aromatics [23]. Creating mesopores in the bulk of nano-ZSM-5 can further shorten the diffusion path and reduce diffusion limitations [24–26]. Compared with the template method and acid dealuminum, alkali desilication is an efficient and simple method and has been widely used to introduce mesopores into nano-ZSM-5 [27–30]. However, Al has an inhibited effect on desilication due to the repulsion between AlO4 − and OH−. The existence of Al distribution gradient in ZSM-5 led to the uncontrolled desilication, thus the formed mesopores are usually uneven [31]. Specially, the richer Al distribution in exterior brought this part a better tolerance to alkaline treatment. Groen et al. [32] reported that the mesopores were difficult to be introduced into the ZSM-5 with low SiO2/Al2O3 (Si/Al  50). Furthermore, alkali desilication usually destroys the framework structure and decreases the crystallinity of ZSM-5. Lu et al. [33] found that the relative crystallinity of commercial ZSM-5 decreased to 88% after alkali treatment using 0.25 M NaAlO2 solution. Choi et al. [34] introduced various mesoporosities into a commercial ZSM-5 using NaOH solution with different concentration (0.1–0.5 M) and found that the peak intensities of XRD patterns of the obtained ZSM-5 is gradually weakened with the increase of concentration of NaOH. This structure destruction of ZSM-5 is not conductive to reflect its function on shape selectivity. Therefore, the synthesis of mesoporous nano-ZSM-5 with high crystallinity structure is still a challenge. In our previous work [35], mesoporous nano-ZSM-5 was successfully synthesized by adding Al source into tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH) solution during the desilication-recrystallization of silicate-1. The formed hollow structure with rich mesopores in shell for nano-ZSM-5 endowed it a large external surface area of 121 m2 g−1. Meanwhile the silicate-aluminum skeleton structure of ZSM-5 zeolite could be directly constructed during the desilication-recrystallization process and the acid amount of prepared nano-ZSM-5 reached 0.70 mmol g−1. This desilication-recrystallization method was obviously contrary to the traditional alkali treatment, in which the synthesized ZSM-5 was post-treated by desilication and the silicate-aluminum skeleton structure was partly destroyed. It was worth noting that during the desilication-recrystallization process, the additive amount of Al source (NaAlO2) directly affected the amount and strength of acid sites for prepared ZSM-5. In addition, the introduced Na+ also has an effect on the mesopore quality in the shell of hollow ZSM-5. In this study, in order to further investigate the influence of the NaAlO2 on the pore structure and acidity of ZSM-5, we synthesized a series of highly mesoporous hollow nano-ZSM-5 zeolites with adjustable pore structure and acidity by modulating the additive amount of NaAlO2 during the desilication-recrystallization of nano-silicate-1. The distinct Na+ content caused by the change of NaAlO2 amount led to the different degrees of competitive adsorption of Na+ and TPA+ on the zeolite surface, resulting in the various pore structures of the obtained zeolites. The acidic properties of obtained ZSM-5 were also diverse due to the variable Al content in synthesis system. The pore structure and acidity of ZSM-5 were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), N2 physisorption isotherms, ammonia temperature programmed desorption (NH3-TPD), inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES), solid-state magic angle spinning nuclear magnetic resonance (MAS NMR) and Py-IR. These highly mesoporous nano-ZSM-5 catalysts were applied to MTH reaction to study the effect of their pore structure and acidity on catalytic performance. The silicalite-1 zeolite was firstly synthesized through our previously reported method [35]. The crystal size of silicate-1 was controlled to be 200 nm (named as S200) by adjusting the H2O/SiO2 to 23 and the molar composition of final gel was 1 SiO2:0.27 TPAOH:8 EtOH:23 H2O. For preparation of series nano-ZSM-5 catalysts with different SiO2/Al2O3 ratios of 400, 200, 100, 50 and 25 (based on the feed ratio in synthetic solution), NaAlO2 with different amount (0.041 g, 0.083 g, 0.166 g, 0.331 g and 0.663 g) and 4 g S200 were added into 40 ml 0.3 M TPAOH solution. In typical, the mixture was hydrothermally treated in an autoclave at 443 K under stirring conditions for 72 h. The obtained solid product was collected by centrifugation and dried overnight at 373 K, following by calcination in an oven at 823 K for 6 h to remove TPA+ species. As-prepared Na-ZSM-5 was ion exchanged with 0.8 M NH4NO3 aqueous solution at 353 K for three times to obtain H-form zeolite. Finally, the H-ZSM-5 product was obtained after centrifugation and dried at 100 °C overnight, following by calcination in the static air at 823 K for 6 h to remove NH4 +. This nano-ZSM-5 was abbreviated as HS200-400, HS200-200, HS200-100, HS200-50 and HS200-25, respectively. For comparison, a zeolite was also prepared by desilication-recrystallization of S200 without the addition of NaAlO2, which was denoted as HS200-+∞. XRD patterns were recorded on a Rigaku D/Max 2500 equipped with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54439 Å) in the 2θ range of 5–50° with a scanning rate of 8° min−1. TEM images were obtained by JEM-2100F to investigate the morphology of zeolites. Nitrogen physisorption was measured in a Beishide 3H-2000PS2 apparatus at 77 K after the sample was degassed under vacuum at 523 K for 4 h. NH3-TPD was performed at a Micromeritics Autochem II 2920 analyzer. ICP-AES was conducted on an Autoscan16 TJA to measure the mass fraction of Si and Al. 29Si and 27Al MAS NMR spectra were carried out using a 600 MHz Bruker Avance III NMR spectrometer. Py-IR was used to obtain the ratio of Brønsted to Lewis acid sites (B/L ratio) of samples with a Bruker Tensor 27 spectrometer. Thermal gravimetric (TG) analysis was conducted to obtain the amount of coke formed in the deactivated ZSM-5 on a STA449F3 analyzer at a heating rate of 10 K min−1 from room temperature to 1073 K in air. The catalytic performance of ZSM-5 catalysts in MTH reaction was evaluated in a tubular fixed-bed reactor. The ZSM-5 catalyst (0.5 g, 80–100 mesh) was mixed with silica sand (2.5 g, 40–60 mesh) and then was loaded in the center of the reactor. Prior to the reaction, the mixture was pretreated at 723 K for 100 min in a N2 flow. Then, the reaction temperature was cooled down to 673 K and maintained under N2 atmosphere (35 cm3 min−1). Subsequently, the methanol was pumped at a rate of 0.085 ml min−1, then gasified at 513 K in a preheater and mixed with N2, keeping a weight hourly space velocity (WHSV) of 8 h−1. The MTH reaction was performed under the conditions of temperature 400 °C and pressure 1 MPa. The obtain product were analyzed by off-line analysis on a ZHONGKEHUIFEN GC-7820 equipped with TCD detectors and a SHIMADZU GC-2014C equipped with a FID detector. XRD results (Fig. 1 ) showed that all the samples exhibited characteristic peaks in ranges of 7–10° and 22.5–25° corresponding to the typical MFI structure [36]. The relative crystallinity was obtained by calculating the ratio of the total area of the three peaks in ranges of 22.5–25° for every sample to that for the parent S200, and the calculated results are shown in Fig. 1b [37]. The relative crystallinity of HS200-+∞ prepared without the addition of NaAlO2 was 104.2%, indicating the recrystallization of leached silicon species and more perfect MFI structure for HS200-+∞. While the relative crystallinity of other five ZSM-5 zeolites synthesized with addition of NaAlO2 slightly decreased with increasing the aluminum content, but were all high and >97%. XRD results revealed the sufficient recrystallization of S200 under different NaAlO2 additive amount and the production of highly crystalline ZSM-5 using this desilication-recrystallization method. In comparison, the relative crystallinity of the mesoporous ZSM-5 samples prepared by conventional alkali treatment was much lower than parent ZSM-5. Stein et al. [38] synthesized mesoporous ZSM-5 zeolites through desilication and found that the relative crystallinity of ZSM-5 treated by NaOH solution decreased to 53%. Gil et al. [39] also observed a reduction of crystallinity from 100 to 67% for ZSM-5 after desilication using 1.0 M NaOH solution. This desilication-recrystallization method has an advantage in preparing mesoporous ZSM-5 with high crystallinity. TEM images of the prepared zeolites showed that the crystals of parent S200 were monodispersed and exhibited regular coffin-like morphology (Fig. 2a). After desilication-recrystallization of S200 with the absence of aluminum source, the obtained HS200-+∞ presented a hollow structure with an intact smooth shell (Fig. 2b), which was due to the double effect of surface protection and structure template role of TPA+ [35]. During the synthesis process, the external surface of S200 can be partly protected because of the adsorption of TPA+ with large molecular size [40], so the internal silicon species were preferentially dissolved. The leached silicon species then recrystallized on the surface of S200 under the template role of TPA+ [41]. This continuous desilication and recrystallization led to the formation of large void with thin shell for HS200-+∞. On this basis, the aluminum source (NaAlO2) was added for the recrystallization with leached silicon species and five ZSM-5 zeolites (Fig. 2c–g) were synthesized. Interestingly, the obtained nano-ZSM-5 possessed various morphology because of the different NaAlO2 additive amount. As shown in Fig. 2c–e, hollow structures were still formed when small amount of NaAlO2 (SiO2/Al2O3 ≥ 100) was added. Thus formed shells were rough and there were a little of debris around the surface. This was due to that parts of the sites on external surface of zeolite was occupied by Na+, resulting that parts of TPA+ were not attached to the surface of solid crystal, but existed in a free state around it and induced the formation of single ZSM-5 crystal. It was concluded that the more Na+ attached on the S200 surface, the more TPA+ in a free state. This led to the more silicon species recrystallized around them, generating more debris. In view of this problem, more TPA+ could be added into the crystal to increase its competitive adsorption, aiming at improving the recrystallization on the external surface of crystal and decreasing the debris quantity. While for the ZSM-5 samples synthesized with large amount of NaAlO2 (SiO2/Al2O3 ratio ≤ 50), more Na+ can be attached on the S200 surface, leading to an enhanced inhibition for the skeletal growth and thus the formation of mesopores in the shell of hollow structured HS200-50 and HS200-25 (Fig. 2f, g) [41]. In particularly, the larger amount of Na+ in HS200-25 endowed it more shell pores than in HS200-50. However, the created mesopores in HS200-25 were small pores which were close to the inner shell and the connectivity of these mesopores was lower than that of HS200-50. The pore characteristics were analyzed by N2 physisorption. As shown in Fig. 3a S200 exhibited a type I isotherm, corresponding to microporous material, while H4-shaped hysteresis loop at relative pressure (P/P0) ranged from 0.40 to 0.99 existed in the isotherms of the other zeolites obtained by desilication-recrystallization of S200, no matter with or without NaAlO2 addition [42]. This verified the TEM result and affirmed that mesopores were indeed produced in the crystal after the desilication-recrystallization of S200. It was worth noting that the hysteresis loop of synthesized ZSM-5 samples enlarged gradually with the increase of NaAlO2 additive amount, indicating an increasing mesoporosity for ZSM-5 zeolites. Textural properties of above samples are further summarized. As listed in Table 1 , for parent S200, the value of external surface area (Sext) and mesoporous volume (Vmeso) were only 56 m2 g−1 and 0.26 cm3 g−1, respectively. After desilication-recrystallization without the addition of NaAlO2, the Sext and Vmeso of prepared HS200-+∞ were similar with S200, and were 66 m2 g−1 and 0.36 cm3 g−1, respectively. Interestingly, for other five ZSM-5 zeolites synthesized with the addition of NaAlO2, the Sext and Vmeso were both significantly enhanced. When the SiO2/Al2O3 ratio decreased from 400 to 50, the Sext and Vmeso of obtained ZSM-5 increased from 70 to 133 m2 g−1 and from 0.39 to 0.59 cm3 g−1, respectively. However, further increasing the NaAlO2 additive amount and the SiO2/Al2O3 was 25, the Sext and Vmeso of prepared HS200-25 reduced to 105 m2 g−1 and 0.50 cm3 g−1, respectively, even lower than those of HS200-100. This was attributed to its low mesoporous connectivity. Among all samples, the HS200-50 possessed the maximal Sext and Vmeso, demonstrating that moderate amount of NaAlO2 could promote the synthesis of highly mesoporous ZSM-5 zeolite. Considering the template role of TPA+, it is possible for us to adjust the relative content between Na+ and TPA+ to optimize the pore structure of nano-ZSM-5 and the related work is underway. The acidity of this nano-ZSM-5 was investigated by NH3-TPD. As shown in Fig. 3b, NH3-TPD profiles of all samples exhibited two desorption peaks in the temperature ranges of 423–593 and 593–823 K, corresponding to the weak and the strong acid sites, respectively [43,44]. Furthermore, three peaks represented weak, moderate and strong acid sites were obtained by the Gaussian fitting of these two peaks, from which we could compare the acid strength by peak temperature and calculate the amount of these three acid sites through the peak areas [45]. After recrystallization of S200 under the addition of NaAlO2, obvious acidity was built for as-prepared ZSM-5 catalysts. The total acid amount and the acid strength of these samples gradually increased with increasing the NaAlO2 additive amount (that is, decreasing SiO2/Al2O3 ratio from 400 to 25), and the total acid amount reached 0.91 mmol g−1 when the SiO2/Al2O3 ratio was 25. This indicated that to some extent in this work, the more the Al additive amount, the larger the total acid amount of the obtained ZSM-5 zeolite prepared by desilication-recrystallization. Obviously, HS200-25 prepared under the highest NaAlO2 additive amount possessed the largest acid amount and strongest acid strength, which was consistent with the work of Li and coworkers. They prepared a series of ZSM-5 with different SiO2/Al2O3 ratios using hydrothermal synthesis method, and they found that the acid amount of these zeolites increased with decreasing SiO2/Al2O3 ratio [46]. In comparison, using this desilication-recrystallization method can not only synthesize the ZSM-5 with high acidity, but also introduce mesopores into the crystals. Such one-step synthesis was conducive to maintaining the acid and mesopores structure at the same time. In order to investigate the acid distribution, we defined “w/t” and “s/t” as the ratio of weak and strong acid to total acid, respectively. As listed in Table 2 , the value of w/t gradually increased from 30.77 to 57.18 when the SiO2/Al2O3 ratio decreased from 400 to 25, while the value of s/t reduced from 53.85 to 33.23. This result is in agreement with that of literature. Lasa and coworkers [37] showed that the weak acidity/strong acidity ratios of commercial ZSM5 reduced from 2.04 to 0.70 with increasing SiO2/Al2O3 from 30 to 280. They found that when the Al content increased, the obtained ZSM-5 tend to form strong acid sites, which would weaken the interactions between neighboring sites and cause a high proportion of weak acid sites. The bulk SiO2/Al2O3 ratio results (Table 3 ) revealed that when the NaAlO2 additive amount was low, the bulk SiO2/Al2O3 ratio of obtained ZSM-5 zeolites was lower than that of synthetic solution. Especially for HS200-400 zeolite, its bulk SiO2/Al2O3 ratio was 156.7, much lower than feed ratio of 400. However, when the NaAlO2 additive amount increased, the prepared HS200-25 and HS200-50 presented higher bulk SiO2/Al2O3 ratios than that of synthetic solution, which were 37.4 and 52.6, respectively. To explain the difference between the bulk SiO2/Al2O3 ratios of ZSM-5 zeolites and their SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratios, the pH values of synthetic solution before and after crystallization were measured by pH meter. As shown in Fig. 4a, it can be seen that when the SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratio decreased from 400 to 25, the pH value of synthetic solution before the crystallization reduced from 13.13 to 12.60 while the pH value after crystallization was increased from 12.12 to 12.48. The change of pH value reduced from 1.01 to 0.12, revealing that the alkali consumption during the desilication-recrystallization process gradually decreased with decreasing SiO2/Al2O3 ratio. This change was consistent with the trend of solid yield. As shown in Fig. 4b, the solid yield increased from 60.0 to 86.5 with the SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratio decreased from 400 to 25. The low alkali consumption amount during the synthetic process reduced the loss of silicon species from silicalite-1 crystals. Besides, with the increase of aluminum content, the actual utilization of aluminum decreased gradually. This was another reason for the above results. Lasa et al. also found that the ratio of the measured strong acid amount to theoretical acid amount reduced with increasing the aluminum content [37]. The deconvolution of 29Si MAS NMR spectrum (Fig. 5 ) results in four peaks centered at −112 (±1) and −115 (±1) Si(4Si,0Al), −106 ppm Si(3Si,1Al), and −102 (±1) ppm SiOH [47]. It can be seen in Fig. 5a, the amount of SiOH and Si(4Si,0Al) sites for parent S200 were 7.82 and 92.18%, respectively. As for HS200-400, the amount of SiOH and Si(4Si,0Al) sites were all decreased, while a peak around −106 ppm responding Si(3Si,1Al) sites occurred and its amount was only 4.79% (Fig. 5b). This revealed that the recrystallization was indeed occurred under the addition of NaAlO2, during which the silicon species in SiOH and Si(4Si,0Al) of S200 crystal were leached out by OH− and then recrystallized with Al source under the template of TPA+ to form Si(3Si,1Al) sites of ZSM-5 zeolite. It was noteworthy that when the SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratio reduced from 400 to 25, the amount of Si(4Si,0Al) sites for obtained ZSM-5 decreased from 93.11 to 81.23% while the amount of Si(3Si,1Al) sites increased from 4.79 to 16.38% (Fig. 5b–e). This indicated that more Si(4Si,0Al) sites transformed to Si(3Si,1Al) sites with the decrease of SiO2/Al2O3 ratio. This result was consisted with the phenomena observed from NH3-TPD and ICP-AES. 27Al MAS NMR spectra were carried out to analyze the chemical environment around aluminum. As shown in Fig. 6a, the spectra of all samples exhibited a strong peak at ~53 ppm corresponding to tetrahedrally coordinated aluminum and a weaker peak at ~−0.5 ppm assigned to octahedrally coordinated aluminum, which were represented the framework Al (FAl) and extra-framework Al (EFAl), respectively [48]. For each sample, we calculated the peak areas and obtained the relative amount of FAl and EFAl. Interestingly, when the SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratio reduced from 400 to 100, the FAl content of obtained ZSM-5 was increased from 82 to 94% while the EFAl content decreased from 18 to 6%. This was due to that the increased Al additive amount promoted the embedding of Al into the skeleton structure to some extent. However, further increasing the Al content (decreasing the SiO2/Al2O3 ratio from 100 to 25), the peak at ~−0.5 ppm enhanced gradually and the EFAl content increased from 6% to 19%, illustrating that the FAl in ZSM-5 zeolites prepared with high NaAlO2 additive content (low SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratio) was more likely to convert to EFAl, which was in line with the study of Primo et al. [49]. They demonstrated that EFAl species were more expected to occur when the Al3+ amount of the zeolite was high. Simon and coworkers revealed that the EFAl content of commercial HZSM-5 increased from 3 to 9.5% when the SiO2/Al2O3 ratio decreased from 80 to 30 [44]. Py-IR spectra were carried out to study the information of Brønsted and Lewis acid sites. As shown in Fig. 6b, two peaks at around 1456 and 1546 cm−1 were attributed to Lewis acid sites and Brønsted acid sites, respectively [50]. After the Gaussian function fitting and integration of spectra, the content of LAS and BAS for these samples are summarized in Table 3. As can be seen, the BAS and LAS amount of HS200-400 were only 28.5 and 25.0 μmol g−1, respectively. Further enhancing the NaAlO2 additive amount, the BAS amount was sharply increased to 123.3 μmol g−1 with the decrease of SiO2/Al2O3 ratio from 400 to 25, while the LAS amount was slightly increased from 25.0 to 62.9 μmol g−1, respectively. Besides, the ratio of Brønsted/Lewis acid sites (B/L) also increased from 1.1 to 2.0 when SiO2/Al2O3 ratio reduced from 400 to 25. This was related to the increased more Al embedded into skeleton structure during the recrystallization process. As shown in Fig. 7a, except for HS200-25, other ZSM-5 possessed good stability and showed complete methanol conversion (100%) during the first 60 h reaction period. After that, a decrease in methanol conversion was observed in these samples, and HS200-50 and HS200-100 had similar downtrend. However, when increasing SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratio from 100 to 400, the reduction of methanol conversion became more and more obvious. For HS200-25, the methanol conversion decreased from 99 to 90% only within 29 h. This was due to that its large acid amount (0.91 mmol g−1) and strong acid strength led to the high reaction activity and generate a large amount of coke precursor to form coke in a short reaction time. For the production of liquid hydrocarbon, the yield of liquid hydrocarbon for nearly all samples firstly increased and then decreased after a period of maintenance (Fig. 7b). In comparison, the catalytic performance of these catalysts was very different and more exact results are shown in Table 4 . Due to the rapid deactivation of HS200-25, its lifetime and total production of liquid hydrocarbons were only 29 h and 27.2 g g−1 cat, respectively. While for HS200-50, the large external surface area of 133 m2 g−1 and slightly less acid amount of 0.66 mmol g−1 made it a superior performance. Its total production of liquid hydrocarbons and the lifetime were 101.0 g g−1 cat and 132 h, respectively. It is worth noting that for HS200-100, although its external surface area was a little lower than HS200-50, the appropriate amount of acid sites of 0.43 mmol g−1 brings it a similar lifetime of 125 h with HS200-50. However, increasing SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratio from 100 to 200, the total production of liquid hydrocarbons and the lifetime of obtained HS200-200 decreased to 76.2 g g−1 cat and 94 h, respectively, which was due to the declined external surface area of 78 m2 g−1 and acidity of 0.23 mmol g−1. Further increasing SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratio to 400, HS200-400 exhibited smaller Sext of 70 m2 g−1 and lower acidity of 0.13 mmol g−1, resulting that its total production of liquid hydrocarbons and the lifetime further reduced to 72.2 g g−1 cat and 84 h, respectively. The catalytic lifetime for ZSM-5 was influenced by their external surface area and acid amount. As presented in Fig. 7c, on the whole, the increased external surface area of ZSM-5 was found to contribute to the improvement of lifetime, although the synchronously increased acid amount would accelerate the reaction and cause the generation of coke precursor. However, for HS200-25, an excess of acid sites could lead to a high rate of coke formation. Importantly, the more location of mesopores in the interior of shell was not conducive to the diffusion of coke precursor for the existence of microporous layer in the exterior shell, resulting in the rapid deactivation of catalyst. This indicated that the ZSM-5 with proper acidity, large external surface along with uniform mesopores in the bulk phase will possess long catalytic lifetime in MTH reaction. The liquid hydrocarbons product during the reaction time from 12 to 22 h was collected and then analyzed. As shown in Fig. 8a and Table S1, after desilication-recrystallization of S200, the various acidity of ZSM-5 prepared with different SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratios presented great effects on the product selectivity. When the SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratio decreased from 400 to 25, the corresponding yield of aromatics for the obtained samples increased from 38.93 to 56.45%. This was due to that the enhanced acidity especially the strong Brønsted acid sites was conducive to the cyclization and dehydrogenation reactions to form aromatics species [51,52]. Furthermore, the olefins selectivity decreased from 10.67 to 4.48% with decreasing SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratio from 400 to 25. Therefore, a high SiO2/Al2O3 ratio (that is a low acidity) is preferred for the production of light olefins, which is widely reported in the literature for methanol to olefins (MTO) [53,54]. This work provides a possibility for the preparation of ZSM-5 with long lifetime and high selectivity for MTO and MTA reaction. During the process of desilication and recrystallization, the pore structure of ZSM-5 catalyst could be optimized by regulating the relative contents of Na+ and TPA+. Therefore, the ZSM-5 with large external surface area can be obtained to provide the high diffusivity for the large molecule in micropores. On this basis, by adjusting the aluminum content could control the acid properties of ZSM-5. Eventually, the ZSM-5 with low acid amount could be directionally prepared for MTO reaction, while the ZSM-5 with large acid amount for MTA reaction. As shown in Fig. 8b and Table S2, BTX distribution on all ZSM-5 were different and the xylene showed the highest selectivity in BTX products followed by toluene, benzene. With the SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratio decreased from 400 to 25, the yield of BTX and xylene on the obtained samples increased from 9.51 to 22.75% and from 7.40 to 16.53%, respectively. The changed trend of the yield of BTX products was similar with that of xylene and was also increased with reducing SiO2/Al2O3 ratio. Also as shown in Table S2, the zeolite with the strongest Brønsted acid sites (HS200-25) had the biggest ratio of BTX/Aromatics. Furthermore, the enhanced external acid sites with decreasing of SiO2/Al2O3 ratio accelerated the isomerization of PX [55], resulting in a decreased PX/X ratio from 28.11 to 16.03. As shown in Fig. S1 and Table S3, all catalysts showed a wide carbon number distribution ranging from C3 to C12 and the differences among them were determined by their pore structures and acidic properties [56,57]. As shown in Table S3, the carbon number distribution on these samples was exactly similar. When the SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratio decreased from 400 to 25, the selectivity of light hydrocarbons (C4-C6) of obtain ZSM-5 was slightly reduced from 38.10 to 36.79% while the selectivity of heavy products (C7-C11) was slightly increased from 61.15 to 62.63%, respectively. Although the ZSM-5 samples with low SiO2/Al2O3 ratio possessed large external surface and was in favor of the production of light hydrocarbons, the large acid amount and high acid strength of these samples was benefited to the cyclization and dehydrogenations of reaction intermediates reaction, which were prone to the production of heavy products. The content of coke for the spent catalysts after MTH reaction were measured by TG analysis and the results are shown in Fig. 9a and Table 4. For HS200-50 and HS200-100, the introduction of mesopores provided them an enlarged external surface and a shorten diffusion path length. The coke precursors formed inside the micropores could diffuse to the external surface more easily, resulting in more coke formation on the external surface [11]. So the lifetime of catalysts was prolonged along with the increase of the coke content to some extent. Especially for the sample HS200–50 with the longest lifetime, the coke content reached 0.22 g g−1 cat, suggesting its excellent diffusion property due to the fabrication of hollow structure and mesopores in its shell. As shown in Table 4, the coke formation rate of HS200-50 and HS200-100 were also similar and were 1.70 × 10−3 and 1.85 × 10−3 g g−1 cat h−1, respectively, which was due to that the fabrication of mesopores or hollow structures on ZSM-5 slowed down the coke formation. It is noteworthy that although a hollow structure with rich mesopores in its shell (Fig. 2g) was introduced into HS200-25, its lifetime was only 29 h. This is due to the maximum coke formation rate of 4.57 × 10−3 g g−1 cat h−1 caused by the largest acidity. When increased the SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratio from 100 to 200 and 400, the obtained HS200-200 and HS200-400 possessed lower external surface area. It was more difficult for the coke precursor in micropores diffusing to the external surface. This type of internal coke was more likely to lead to the catalyst deactivation by covering the acid sites and blocking the micropores. So the lifetime of HS200-200 and HS200-400 were only 94 and 84 h, respectively and the coke content were only 0.11 and 0.12 g g−1 cat, respectively. Besides, these two samples presented lower coke formation rate of 0.15 × 10−3 and 1.48 × 10−3 g g−1 cat h−1, which were related to their low acidity. The spent catalysts were also characterized by N2 physisorption analysis. As shown in Fig. 9b, compared with fresh catalyst, the N2 adsorption quantity and the hysteresis loop of spent catalyst obviously reduced, indicating that their total pore volume and mesopore volume significantly decreased. The variation of textural properties between fresh and spent catalysts is listed in Table 1. It can be seen that the textural properties of spent catalysts (Smicro, Smeso, Vmicro, Vmeso) indeed decreased due to the coke deposition. The change of pore volume (Fig. 9c) showed that the value of Vmicro and Vmeso for spent catalysts all decreased, and more decrease was found in the value of Vmeso. We defined “Rmeso” and “Rmicro” as the proportion of the reduction of micropore and mesopore volume to the reduction of total pore volume, which represented the loss degree of micropores and mesopores after the reaction, respectively. As shown in Fig. 9d and Table S4, the value of Rmeso were higher than the value of Rmicro as a whole, which showed that most of the coke in these spent samples were deposited in mesopores. For the ZSM-5 zeolites with high SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratio (≥200), the single hollow structure of HS200-400 and HS200-200 brought them low external surface area of 70 and 78 m2 g−1. The low mesoporosity caused low coke deposition amount in mesopores, resulting in a small Rmeso value of 0.57 and 0.58, respectively. When the SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratio decreased to 100, although HS200–100 still exhibited hollow structure, its external surface area increased to 114 m2 g−1. So more coke deposited in mesopore and the Rmeso value increased to 0.67. Further decreasing the SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratio to 50, mesopores were introduced into the shell of HS200–50 and its external surface area reached 133 m2 g−1. The production of shell pores could further shorten the diffusion path length of molecules in micropore, which allows the coke precursors formed inside the micropores quickly diffused to the external surface through the introduced mesopores. Therefore, ZSM-5 possessed high coke capacity and a high Rmeso value of 0.76. However, when the SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratio was 25, the external surface area decreased to 105 m2 g−1, resulting that the Rmeso value decreased to 0.71. In this study, a series of hierarchical hollow nano-ZSM-5 catalysts were synthesized by desilication-recrystallization of silicalite-1. Through modulating the additive amount of NaAlO2 during the desilication-recrystallization, the SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratio was controlled to be 25, 50, 100, 200 and 400, thus as-prepared ZSM-5 catalyst possessed adjustable pore structure and acid properties. When the SiO2/Al2O3 ratio was high (SiO2/Al2O3 ratio ≥ 100), ZSM-5 sample possessed simple hollow structure, while the mesopores were created in the shell of ZSM-5 with decreasing the SiO2/Al2O3 ratio to 50. However, further decreasing the SiO2/Al2O3 ratio to 25, the mesoporosity of obtained ZSM-5 sample was reduced because of the enhanced competitive adsorption of Na+ on surface with TPA+. It was found that the total amount of acid sites of these ZSM-5 samples gradually increased from 0.13 to 0.91 mmol g−1 with decreasing the SiO2/Al2O3 ratio from 400 to 25, and both their acid strength and the Brønsted acid amount increased. These highly mesoporous nano-ZSM-5 catalysts were applied to MTH reaction to study the effect of their pore structure and acidity on catalytic performance. We found that strong acidity of ZSM-5 promoted the aromatization reaction, and with the decreasing of SiO2/Al2O3 ratio, aromatics selectivity increased. Whereas, for the sample with SiO2/Al2O3 ratio of 25, the too strong acidity brought it the maximum coke formation rate of 4.57 × 10−3 g g−1 cat h−1, resulting in its rapid deactivation. The sample with SiO2/Al2O3 ratio of 50 exhibited largest external surface area of 133 m2 g−1 and greatest diffusion ability, in addition to the proper acidity of 0.66 mmol g−1, endowing it a longest lifetime of 132 h in MTH reaction. This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21606160, No. 21878207), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2018YFB0604901), the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (No. 201701D221039, No. 201701D121025) and the Key Research and Development Project of Shanxi Province (International Science and Technology Cooperation Program) (201803D421011). Supplementary material Image 1 Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fuproc.2019.106122.
abstract: Introducing mesopores into nano-ZSM-5 zeolite could further shorten its micropore length and significantly enhance its catalytic stability in methanol to hydrocarbons (MTH) reaction. In this work, a series of hierarchical nano-ZSM-5 were synthesized by desilication-recrystallization of silicalite-1. Through adjusting the NaAlO2 additive amount, SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratio was controlled to induce the structure evolution of ZSM-5. Hollow structured nano-ZSM-5 was synthesized at high SiO2/Al2O3 feed ratio, while mesopores were further created in the shell of hollow nano-ZSM-5 with low SiO2/Al2O3 ratio. The acid amount of ZSM-5 gradually increased from 0.13 to 0.91 mmol g−1 and the acid strength also enhanced with decreasing the SiO2/Al2O3 ratio from 400 to 25. The strong acidity promoted aromatization reaction and the sample prepared with a SiO2/Al2O3 ratio of 25 showed the highest aromatics selectivity of 56.45%. Under suitable SiO2/Al2O3 ratio of 50, synthesized nano-ZSM-5 possessed large external surface area of 133 m2 g−1 and proper acidity of 0.66 mmol g−1, endowing it long lifetime of 132 h. The adjustable pore structure and acidity achieved by controlling the Si/Al ratio during the desilication-recrystallization of silicalite-1 provides possibility for the directional preparation of ZSM-5 with long lifetime and high selectivity for other MTH reaction.
title: Si/Al ratio induced structure evolution during desilication-recrystallization of silicalite-1 to synthesize nano-ZSM-5 catalyst for MTH reaction

full_text: Fossil fuels are one of the most important forms of energy used by humans [1]. The growth of population and technological expansion have increased the use of this type of energy in the present century [2,3]. Fossil fuels naturally contain large amounts of sulfur compounds. These materials include thiols, thioethers, cyclic and aromatic sulfur compounds such as thiophene and its derivatives [1,4]. The presence of these compounds in transport fuels (such as gasoline and diesel) causes the release of sulfur oxides (SOx) into the atmosphere [5]. From the reaction of these sulfur oxides with water vapor of the air, the acid rain is produced, which can cause damage to soil, forests, and the ecosystem in general [6]. Due to environmental concerns, laws have been introduced in recent years that reduce the amount of sulfur in transportation fuels to 99% of molybdenum atoms are well dispersed at the support surface, and <1% may be inside the pores and texture of the bulk. In the sample with 7.5% Mo content, this ratio has decreased and reached about 0.95. In the sample with 10% Mo content, the ratio of experimental to theoretical surface density is even smaller and reached about 0.898, which means that the dispersion of molybdenum atoms at the surface is relatively reduced, and the accumulation of molybdenum atoms at the surface or in the texture of the bulk has increased. The TEM images of the nanorod-OMS-2 and Mo(x wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 (x = 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10) catalysts are shown in Fig. 7 . Fig. 7 (a), for the nanorod-OMS-2 sample, shows a good nanorod structure. Figs. 7 (b, c, d, and e) clearly shows spherical molybdenum oxide particles dispersed on the surface of nanorods, which with the increasing of molybdenum percentage in the catalyst samples, the number of these particles has also increased, and these results are completely consistent with the XRD and SEM analysis. To evaluate the redox properties of the synthesized catalysts and to study the effect of molybdenum loading on the reducibility of catalysts, H2-TPR analysis was carried out, and origin pro-2016 software was used for the separation of H2-TPR peaks. Fig. 8 shows the TPR curves for the nanorod-OMS-2 and Mo(x wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 catalysts after calcination with different loading of molybdenum in the range of 2.5–10 wt%. According to Fig. 8(a), the TPR profile of the nanorod-OMS-2 sample has three reduction peaks with maximum temperatures (Tmax) of 311, 408, and 465 °C. The first peak at Tmax of 311 °C relates to the reduction reaction of MnO2 to Mn2O3. The second and third peaks of Fig. 8(a), at Tmax of 408 and 465 °C, correspond to the reduction reactions of Mn2O3 to Mn3O4 and Mn3O4 to MnO, respectively [44–46]. As shown in Fig. 8(b, c, d, and e), in H2-TPR profiles of Mo(x wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 samples, five reduction peaks can be observed that all peaks have overlapped with each other and have separated by using origin pro software. According to Fig. 8(b), the H2-TPR profile of Mo(2.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 sample, the first peak at Tmax of 289 °C relates to the reduction of octahedral molybdenum oxide (Mooct +6 to Mooct +4) and a second peak at Tmax of 409 °C corresponds to the reduction of MnO2 to Mn2O3. Also, the third and fourth peaks at Tmax of 527 and 577 °C corresponds to the conversion of Mn2O3 to Mn3O4 and Mn3O4 to MnO, respectively. The values of these maximum temperatures (Tmax) have increased from 311 to 409 °C, 408 to 527, and 465 to 577 °C compared to the nanorod-OMS-2 sample, which could be due to the interaction between the octahedral manganese oxide (support) and molybdenum oxides species. In addition, the fifth peak (Tmax of 599 °C) corresponds to the simultaneous transformation of octahedral (Mooct +4 to Mooct 0) and tetrahedral (Moth +6 to Moth +4) molybdenum oxides species [19,20]. Also, according to Fig. 8(b, c, d, and e), with increasing of the molybdenum loading in the range of 2.5–10 wt%, the Tmax of all reduction peaks has shifted to higher temperature values, which indicates the interaction between support, and molybdenum oxide has become stronger and a chemical bond between the manganese and molybdenum oxides species has been probably established at the catalyst surface. In H2-TPR profiles, the area of the peaks corresponds to the amount of hydrogen consumption. Therefore, they show the relative amounts of molybdenum and manganese oxide species on the surface of the catalyst. The origin pro-2016 software has been used to separate the peaks and estimate the area under profiles, and the values of these peaks areas are reported in Table 2 . According to the published results [19], the molybdenum oxide species with stronger support- metal interaction in ODS reaction have more catalytic activity. So, for MoOx/nanorod-OMS-2 samples, the area ratios of the fifth peak (with higher Tmax and strong interaction of Mo-support) to the first peak (with lower Tmax and weak interaction of Mo-support), MoHigh/MoLow, were calculated and presented in Table 2. According to Table 2, the value of the MoHigh/MoLow ratio for Mo(2.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 sample is 5.18. With increasing the molybdenum loading, this MoHigh/MoLow ratio also has increased, and for Mo(7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 sample has reached to 6.15, in other words, it has increased by about 19%. However, in the sample of Mo(10 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2, this ratio has decreased. Therefore, it can be predicted that in the catalyst of Mo(7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2, where more molybdenum species with a strong interaction are distributed on the catalyst surface, it may be more active in the desulfurization reaction. The NH3-TPD technique was used to evaluate the acidic properties of the synthesized catalysts. The NH3-TPD profiles of nanorod-OMS-2 and Mo(x wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 catalysts with different loading of molybdenum (from 2.5 to 10 wt%) after calcination were displayed in Fig. 9 and their results are summarized in Table 3 . In these profiles, the area of the peaks indicates the number of acidic sites, while the shape and position of the peaks represent the acidic strength of the sample. Furthermore, we can also classify acidic sites as strong, medium, and weak according to the maximum temperatures of the peak. According to published articles, NH3 desorption peaks with maximum temperatures in the range of 100 to 230 °C, 230 to 370 °C, and above 370 °C indicate weak, medium, and strong acidic sites, respectively [47]. Fig. 9 shows three weak desorption peaks for the nanorod-OMS-2 sample in the ranges of 150–250, 320–400, and 450–550 °C, which correspond to weak, medium, and strong acidic sites, respectively. In Fig. 9, Mo(2.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 catalyst shows two peaks. The first peak is at a maximum temperature of 497 °C that overlaps with the peak corresponding to the nanorod-OMS-2 sample, and the second peak with high intensity can be observed at a maximum temperature of 619 °C. In addition, with increasing the molybdenum loading in Mo(x wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 samples, the intensity and the maximum temperature of the second-high intense peak have also increased from 619 to 650 °C. Therefore, it can be concluded that with increasing the percentage of Mo in the Mo(x wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 catalysts, the amount, as well as the strength of acidic sites, has increased (synergistic effect). This increase in the strength of acidic sites of the catalysts may be due to the increase in the Mn-O-Mo non-equivalent bond strength at the surface, as shown in the H2-TPR analysis. Many studies have shown that strong acid catalysts can be suitable for the oxidative desulfurization process [1,48–50]. Therefore, we can expect that Mo(x wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 catalysts will perform well in the desulfurization process. The oxidative desulfurization of DBT in isooctane as a model fuel over the synthesized catalysts was studied exclusively to evaluation of the catalytic activities, the effects of molybdenum loading, reaction temperature, Oxidant/Sulfur (O/S) molar ratio, catalyst amount, kinetic measurements, and results of these studies are as follows: Fig. 10 shows the profiles of the oxidative desulfurization of DBT in isooctane in the presence of H2O2 as an oxidant agent, nanorod-OMS-2, and Mo(x wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 samples with molybdenum loading of 2.5–10 wt%. All reactions have performed at a temperature of 65 °C for 2 h with 0.1 g of the catalyst, 10 mL acetonitrile, and 10 mL of the DBT solution in isooctane with the concentration of 1000 ppm, and the different molar ratio of H2O2. In all the reactions, the percentages of sulfur removal have been calculated from Eq. (1). According to Fig. 10, in the presence of nanorod-OMS-2, the percentage of sulfur removal is around 60%. However, the reactions in the presence of molybdenum-containing catalysts show relatively high values for conversion rate compared to the nanorod-OMS-2 sample reaction. According to Fig. 10, by increasing the Mo loading from 2.5 to 7.5, the desulfurization rate increased to 99%, and the sulfur content in the final product decreased from 1000 to 10 ppm. However, the rate of desulfurization in a catalyst containing 10% molybdenum has decreased. It seems that with the dispersion of molybdenum oxide on the surface of nanorod-OMS-2, the number of catalytic active sites and consequently the conversion percentage has increased so that in the Mo(7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 sample, dispersion of molybdenum oxide is maximum and the highest conversion percentage has obtained. However, in the 10% sample, the conversion percentage also decreased due to the accumulation of molybdenum oxide particles on the external surface of the support and the reduction of catalytic active sites. Therefore, the catalyst containing Mo loading of 7.5% is more active among the catalysts in Fig. 10 and is the optimal sample. Evaluation of the characterization results of the synthesized Mo(x wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 catalysts show that these samples exhibited suitable surface area, pore volume with the micro-mesoporous structures, and high dispersion of molybdenum oxides species on the nanorod-OMS-2 surface. In addition, it was found that there is a strong interaction between the nanorod-OMS-2 (support) and the molybdenum oxide species. Furthermore, it was found that strong acidic sites are created on the catalyst surface due to the Mn-O-Mo non-equivalent bonds. In catalyst Mo(7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2, in addition to the mentioned properties, the value of the MoHigh/MoLow ratio is also higher compared to other samples, and more molybdenum oxide species with a strong interaction were distributed on the catalyst surface. A change in the characteristic properties of the catalyst should cause the observed difference in the activity. Therefore, the Mo(7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 catalyst among the synthesized samples is a suitable catalyst for oxidation desulfurization reaction. Reaction condition: 0.1 g catalyst with the grain size of 230–200 mesh; 10 mL of DBT in isooctane solution with a sulfur concentration of 1000 ppm; H2O2/ DBT molar ratio 8; 10 mL acetonitrile (as extraction solvent); reflux temperature 65 °C; reaction time 2 h, the stirring rate of the reaction mixture 650 rpm. Table 4 shows the oxidation desulfurization reaction with O2, H2O2, or TBHP over the various tested catalytic systems. The Mo(7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 catalyst has a proper performance compared to other catalytic systems [5,20,23,24,26,27,49,51–56] and has provided a better conversion percentage in mild conditions and is a suitable system for desulfurization reaction. In the following sections, the effect of effective factors on the catalyst performance in the presence of Mo(7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 catalyst is investigated. In liquid-phase reactions, the catalyst amounts, catalyst grain size, stirring rate of the reaction mixture, reactants, and solvent, are effective parameters in diffusion study. In this study, ODS reaction was performed at various stirring rates at 450, 550, and 650 rpm to study the effect of external mass transfer. All reactions were carried out at 65 °C for 2 h with 0.1 g Mo (7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 catalyst with a grain size of 230–200 mesh, 10 mL solvent (acetonitrile), and 10 mL solution of 1000 ppm DBT in isooctane, O/S molar ratio of 8. The results showed that the conversion (%) is low in a stirring rate of 450 rpm, and the conversion (%) has increased with increasing stirring rate. The stirring rates in the range of 550–650 rpm have a low effect on the conversion (%) of DBT. Therefore, all reactions were carried out at 650 rpm. To investigate the influence of catalyst content on the desulfurization percentage, a series of catalytic oxidation experiments were performed in different amounts of Mo (7.5 wt%) /nanorod-OMS-2 catalyst in the range of 0.01 to 0.15 g while other reaction conditions remained constant. Fig. 11 (a) shows that the oxidative desulfurization reaction is forcefully dependent on the catalyst mount, and the conversion percentage increases with the catalyst amount. In fact, as the catalyst amount increases, the population of available active sites for the desulfurization reaction also increases. According to Fig. 11 (a), for the catalyst amount of 0.1 g, the conversion percentage has achieved to 99, and the amount of sulfur in the model fuel has decreased from 1000 to 10 ppm. However, by increasing the catalyst amount from 0.1 to 0.15 g, no significant change was observed in conversion percentage because the conversion (%) has reached its limit, and most of the DBT has been oxidized. In fact, in heterogeneous catalytic reactions, the mass transfer phenomenon and the surface reaction affect the reaction rate. In the presence of Mo (7.5 wt%)/ nanorod-OMS-2 catalyst, the desulfurization reaction occurs mostly on the external catalyst surface, and the internal diffusion has a very small contribution to the reaction rate. By increasing the stirring rate, the mass transfer on the external surface of the catalysts is improved. Under these experimental conditions, the surface reaction is the limiting of the progress of the reaction when the catalyst amount is <0.1 g, and with a stirring rate of 650 rpm, external diffusion does not limit the reaction rate even at a catalyst amount above 0.1 g. Therefore, in this reaction system, the proper values for the catalyst amount and stirring rates are 0.1 g and 650 rpm, respectively. To evaluate the effect of O/S molar ratio on the conversion (%) of desulfurization reaction in the presence of catalyst Mo(7.5%)/nanorod-OMS-2 with Oxidant/Sulfur molar ratio of 2–10, an experiment was performed, and the results are presented in Fig. 11 (b). The reaction was performed with 0.1 g of catalyst at reflux temperature (65 °C), 10 ml of solvent (acetonitrile), reaction time of 2 h, an appropriate amount of oxidant (H2O2), and a fixed amount of solution DBT (10 mL, 1000 ppm DBT in isooctane). According to Fig. 11 (b), by increasing the O/S molar ratio, the conversion percentage has also increased. It has reached the maximum value (99%) in the ratio of 8, so that in the higher ratio of O/S (10), this value has remained constant. The lowest O/S ratio (O/S = 2) resulted in 64.2% sulfur removal and the highest O/S ratio (10) produced 99% conversion. Fig. 11 (b) shows that the sulfur removal (%) increased up to 99% without any inhibitory effect with an O/S ratio of 8, which is greater than the theoretical amount. The kinetics of the desulfurization reaction of DBT with a surplus of H2O2 on the Mo(7.5 wt%)/ nanorod-OMS-2 catalyst was studied, and the conversion (%) of DBT was measured at temperatures of 25, 40, 55, 65 °C and plotted versus to time Fig. 12(a). The ODS reactions were performed using 10 mL acetonitrile, O/S molar ratio of 10, 0.1 g catalyst, and a reaction mixture stirring rate of 650 cycle/min in a glass flask. Samples of 0.3 mL were taken from the reaction mixture at a regular time out and analyzed by GC. According to Fig. 12(a), the conversion (%) at low temperatures is relatively low, so conversion (%) of DBT is only 65% at 25 °C and after 1 h time. However, at high temperatures, the catalytic activity has also increased so that the conversion (%) of DBT has reached 99% at 65 °C and after 1 h of reaction time. The rate Eq. [57] can be written as follow: (3) r = k DBT n H 2 O 2 m Where k is the rate constant, DBT stands for Dibenzothiophene, n and m are the orders of Dibenzothiophene and H2O2, respectively. If n = 1 and using a surplus of H2O2 concentration, the integrated Equation can be written as: (4) Ln 1 − X = kt where X is the conversion fraction of DBT after time t. The experimental data of Fig. 12-(a) were used to plot Fig. 12-(b) in which the values of Ln (1 -X) versus time at different temperatures are shown. Fig. 12-(b) confirms Eq. (4) and gives a proper linear relationship between Ln (1 -X) and reaction time, which shows a pseudo-first-order dependence on DBT, and from the slope of the plots, the apparent rate constants (k0) were determined. In order to regression analysis of the experimental data related to Fig. 12-(b), Origin pro-2016 software was used, and the results of these analyses are shown in Table 5 . According to Table 5, it can be concluded that the oxidation desulfurization reaction of Dibenzothiophene in the presence of excess H2O2 over the Mo(7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 sample good conforms to the pseudo-first-order kinetic model. In Fig. 13 , the variation of the Ln k0 vs. 1/T (Arrhenius plot) was drawn, and Ea (apparent activation energy) was determined 18.9 kJ mol−1. The oxidation process of DBT to the corresponding sulfoxide and sulfone, in the presence of Mo (7.5 wt%)/ nanorod-OMS-2 catalyst, can be explained by an acceptable mechanism (Fig. 14 ) according to the literatures [58–61] and our characterization results. First, the metal peroxide intermediates (manganese hydroperoxide and hydro peroxymolybdate) are formed in the presence of H2O2 by the “nucleophilic attack” mechanism (stage I). Then, the manganese hydroperoxide and hydro peroxymolybdate species undergo the reversible removal process of a water molecule to produce the monoperoxo and diperoxo species (stage II). Due to the higher electron density of the sulfur atoms, the DBT oxidation must be preceded by the nucleophilic attack of the sulfur on a peroxo group of the di- or monoperoxo species to generate sulfoxide (DBTO) (stage III). Subsequently, the sulfoxide undergoes further oxidation by peroxo species to produce the corresponding sulfone (DBTO2) (stage IV). Thus, nanorod-OMS-2 exhibits dual behavior in this catalytic system. The presence of OMS-2 as support helps to withdraw electron density from Mo atoms and gives a higher electrophilic character to the molybdate species. Nanorod-OMS-2 also has the oxidation ability of DBT to DBTO and DBTO2 with forming manganese hydroperoxide species in the presence of H2O2. The study of stability of heterogeneous catalysts is a significant factor, because it indicates the operating conditions and the lifetime of the catalyst. In this study, the effect of reusability and leaching and lifetime has been investigated. Also stability of catalyst structure was evaluated by XRD and Ft-IR techniques. Recycling potential of Mo(7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 catalyst was evaluated according to the following method; after completion of the oxidation reaction in each experiment, the solid catalyst was filtered, washed with acetonitrile followed by ethanol, dried at 250 °C for 10 h and then used again in the next run. In Fig. 15 , the results of Mo(7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 catalyst recycling for five cycles are shown. The results showed that the catalyst efficiency has decreased from 99% to 97% during five cycles. The ICP technique was used to determine Mo% in the spent catalyst. After 5 cycles, only 1.5% of Mo was lost. Therefore, in the reaction conditions, the desired catalyst is stable and the catalyst reusability is proper. Since the oxidative desulfurization (ODS) process takes place in the liquid phase and the solid catalyst is contacted with the liquid phase for a long time, there is a possibility that the main elements of the catalyst will leach from the solid phase. Also, the ions introduced in the liquid phase may catalyze the reaction as a homogeneous catalyst. Therefore, it is necessary to perform the leaching test. We have selected the Mo(7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 catalyst and using DBT solution in isooctane as a model fuel for the leaching study. The leaching of the catalyst was tested by filtering the catalyst during the reaction and studying the catalytic activity of the filtrate. In a typical experiment, 10 mL of model fuel was added to 0.05 g of catalyst and 10 mL acetonitrile. Afterward, the oil bath was heated to the 65 °C. Upon reaching thermal equilibrium, the hydrogen peroxide as oxidant (O/S of 8 M ratio) was added to the reaction mixture. The reaction mixture in a biphasic system was refluxed for 20 min at 65 °C and sulfur removal percentages of 78% was obtained. Then the reaction mixture was filtered, filtrate solution was refluxed for next 40 min and sulfur removal percentages of 79% was obtained. For comparison, another experiment was carried out by refluxing of initial reaction mixture for 1 h without catalyst filtration; the results showed 97% sulfur removal. Also the content of Mn and Mo of spend catalyst was measured by ICP technique and no appreciable loss of these elements was detected. Therefore, these results demonstrate that the no leaching of Mo or Mn species from solid catalyst during liquid phase reaction takes place and catalyst is stable. To evaluate the structural stability of the Mo(7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 catalyst, the catalyst was tested in the presence of acetonitrile, DBT solution in isooctane, the hydrogen peroxide, and in the reflux temperature (65 °C) for 24 h. The catalyst was then separated from the reaction mixture and washed several times with acetonitrile and acetone, dried overnight at 120 °C, and the XRD and FT-IR spectra were taken from the catalyst sample. In Fig. 16 A and B, The FT-IR spectra and XRD patterns for the fresh and used catalyst are compared. According to Fig. 16 A and B, after using the catalyst, no change in its crystal structure is observed and the catalyst is structurally stable. The above results confirmed that the Mo(7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 catalyst is highly stable and recyclable for the ODS reaction, which makes it promising for practical application in oxidative desulfurization (ODS) process. To determine the effectiveness of the proposed catalyst in the ODS process, sulfur removal from the real diesel fuel was studied on the Mo (7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 catalyst. The performance of diesel oil desulfurization, under obtained optimum conditions (0.1 g of catalyst, 65 °C, O/S = 8, 650 rpm, 2 h), was evaluated using a fuel with an initial sulfur content of 2810 ppm. Acetonitrile (MeCN) was used as the extraction solvent at all stages of the ODS reaction. Due to the different types of sulfur compounds in real diesel fuel, total sulfur analysis was used instead of GC. The amount of sulfur in real diesel fuel was determined by the KR-17040 X-ray fluorescence sulfur analyzer instrument. In the real diesel sample, approximately 96% of the sulfur was removed following the first ODS process. The results showed that the ODS process using the Mo (7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 catalytic system is an effective method of producing green diesel. In this work, a new high-efficiency two-component catalytic system was developed for the oxidative desulfurization process. Briefly, nanorod octahedral manganese oxide molecular sieve supported molybdenum oxide catalysts (MoOx/nanorod-OMS-2) with different loading of molybdenum oxide and micro-mesoporous structure were successfully synthesized by impregnation method and for the first time, and their catalytic activities evaluated for ODS reaction of DBT solution as a fuel model. This study showed that the combination of nanorods-OMS-2 with tunnel micropore structure and molybdenum oxide creates strong acidic sites due to the strong interaction between molybdenum oxide -OMS-2 and synergistic effect between Mn and Mo species. It was found that there are two types of active sites in this catalytic system (a) manganese oxide active sites (Mn2+, Mn3+, and Mn4+ ions) located on nanorod-OMS-2 surface and (b) molybdenum oxide active sites (Mooct 6+, Mooct 4+, Moth 6+ and Moth 4+ ions) that to be well have dispersed as molybdenum oxide nanocrystals on the external surface of the nanorod-OMS-2. In addition, it was found that the activity of catalysts is dependent on the strength of acidic sites, dispersion of molybdenum oxides species on the support surface, and the composition of the mesoporous – microporous structure. The percentage of desulfurization in the presence of an optimal catalyst (Mo (7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2) and H2O2 as the oxidant has reached 99%. Also, no leaching of Mo or Mn species from solid catalyst during liquid phase reaction takes place and the optimal catalyst is really heterogeneous system, highly stable, and recyclable for the ODS reaction, which makes it promising for practical application in oxidative desulfurization process. For future work, the study of synthesis of Mo-OMS-2 catalyst by incorporation method is under consideration.
abstract: Oxidative desulfurization(ODS) is an effective method for obtaining fuels that contain ultra-low sulfur. The purpose of this paper is to prepare a novel and effective bifunctional heterogeneous catalytic system for the desulfurization process by using nanorods cryptomelane octahedral manganese oxide molecular sieve(nanorod-OMS-2) and molybdenum oxide as active components. The MoOX/nanorod-OMS-2 catalysts were synthesized with different molybdenum loadings. The catalytic activities were evaluated for oxidative desulfurization of dibenzothiophene (DBT) in the presence of H2O2 and acetonitrile. Results of characterization analyzes showed that the synthesized catalysts contain the conventional surface area, pore-volume, mixed micro-mesoporous structures, and the molybdenum oxides species highly dispersed on the nanorod-OMS-2 surface. A synergistic effect was observed between Mn and Mo species which created strong acidic sites. In addition, the activity of catalysts is dependent on the composition of meso-microporous structures and the presence of strong acidic sites due to the interactions between the Manganese‑molybdenum oxide species. In optimum condition over the Mo(7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 catalyst, the percentage of sulfur removal was achieved to 99%. The kinetic of ODS reaction was studied, and the pseudo-first-order rate law was obtained with respect to DBT. The reusability of Mo(7.5 wt%)/nanorod-OMS-2 was studied and the efficiency of catalyst has decreased from 99% to 98% during three cycles. The effects of oxidant/sulfur molar ratio, reaction time, reaction temperature were studied, and the optimum values were 8:1, 2 h and 65 °C, respectively.
title: Novel synthesis of highly dispersed molybdenum oxide over nanorods cryptomelane octahedral manganese oxide molecular sieve (MoOx/nanorod-OMS-2) as a high performance catalyst for oxidative desulfurization process

full_text: Alternative energy resources, such as coal-derived liquid fuels and shale crude oil, are of general interest due to the depletion of crude oil [1,2]. Coal liquefaction technology has drawn attention because of the large and widely distributed coal reserves compared to other hydrocarbonaceous sources [3]. However, the oxygen content in coal-derived liquids may exceed 10 wt% and is much higher than that of crude oil [4]. Reduction of the amount of organic oxygen in coal-derived liquids to the level present in crude oil will enhance the fuel stability and combustion performance [5]. In recent years, attempts to catalytically deoxygenate coals and coal derivatives have been reported [6–8]. Some organic oxygen in coal are present in the form of carbonyl, carboxyl, ether, hydroxy, and methoxy groups, but other organic oxygens are incorporated into heteroaromatic rings [9,10]. From previous studies on deoxygenation of model compounds, we propose three major catalytic deoxygenation pathways according to the position of oxygen in alkyl aromatics molecules, which are shown in Supplementary Data. Different catalytic processes are needed to remove organic oxygen located inside and outside aromatic rings [11,12]. Bulk elemental analysis is a direct way to determine the content of oxygen during coal conversion [13]. However, further details like the position of oxygen in molecules are still lacked, which limit the evaluation of relationships between molecular structure and the corresponding reactivity. Therefore, the development of a method for the characterization of organic oxygen inside and outside aromatic rings is crucial for further understanding of the catalytic processes. Identification of oxygen in alkyl groups or heteroaromatic rings can be realized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy [14], X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy [15], and nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy [16]. However, these analytical methods cannot reveal structural details of unknown oxygen-containing compounds in very complex mixtures. Mass spectrometry (MS) with high resolution has been employed to determine elemental compositions of compounds in liquid fuels and/or soluble portions generated from solid fuel resources [17–19]. In high-resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS), orbitrap MS has a higher resolving power than time-of-flight MS, and more affordable compared to Fourier transform ion cyclotron resonance MS. At the mean time, orbitrap MS is sufficient for adequate characterization of petroleum fractions under consideration [20]. However, the positions of oxygen and other heteroatoms inside or outside of aromatic rings of compounds in catalytically treated coals cannot be adequately differentiated by the above methodology. The in-source ion fragmentation method and HRMS have been employed in this work to evaluate aromatic core structures of compounds in coal fluids after catalytic deoxygenation. The coal sample, NiO/Mo2O3-ZSM-5 (catalyst) and cyclohexane (solvent) were mixed in an autoclave and allowed to react at 300 °C with hydrogen to obtain a coal filtrate (F1, 12.3 wt% of yield). A supported catalyst on ZSM-5 was reported to effectively crack the macromolecular network in coal under mild conditions (300 °C in cyclohexane) [21]. Details of the experiments are listed in Supplementary Data. The same experimental procedure was repeated without the catalyst to acquire another coal filtrate (F2, 8.2 wt% of yield). The results of bulk elemental analysis for F1 and F2 are listed in Table S4. Both filtrates were ready for the mass spectrometric characterization. A high resolution linear quadrupole ion trap/orbitrap mass spectrometer (LTQ-Orbitrap XL, Thermo-Fisher Scientific, Waltham, USA), was used in the analytical work. The operation parameters of MS are also listed in Supplementary Data. Extractable components in the mass range from 50 up to 1000 Da were analyzed. In-source collision-activated dissociation (ISCAD) was realized at a collision energy of 100 V. Thermo Xcalibur Roadmap software Data Analysis 2.2 was used for peak selection and molecular formula assignment. Atmospheric pressure chemical ionization (APCI) and carbon disulfide as the ionization reagent have been demonstrated to efficiently ionize aromatic compounds, generating only stable molecular ions and no or minor fragment ions [22]. Therefore, this method is suitable for the analyses of soluble portions from coal that predominately contain alkyl aromatic compounds. High-resolution mass spectra were recorded for the ionized compounds to determine their elemental compositions. Soluble components in the cyclohexane filtrates with mass range from 50 upto 1000 Da were analyzed. Zoom insets of the mass spectra from HRMS have been introduced to offer evidences of the data quality and the sample complexity [23]. In this work, a mass scale-expansion of orbitrap mass spectra for soluble fractions without and with catalytic deoxygenation near 272 Da was provided in Fig. 1 . After catalytic treatment, the relative abundances of oxygen-containing compounds decreased and aromatic hydrocarbons increased. Shown in Fig. 2a are the approximate relative abundances of compounds representing various heteroatomic classes in the coal filtrates. Elemental compositions of the organic compounds belonging to different classes are represented by general chemical formulae. OxNy refers to compounds containing C, H, N and O (subscripts x and y provide the number of oxygen and nitrogen atoms per molecule, respectively.). Ox class contains C, H and O, and CH refers to compounds containing only C and H. Sulfur-containing compounds were not identified due to low abundance and/or ionization efficiency. Six major classes were observed for the sample that had not been catalytically treated and five major classes were left after catalytic deoxygenation. The relative abundances of O2 and O3 classes decreased from 37% and 15% to 18% and 6%, respectively. Further, an enhanced relative abundance of O1 class was observed after catalytic deoxygenation. This is likely due to partial deoxygenation of the compounds in other Ox classes (x = 2, 3 or 4), inducing the formation of molecules with a reduced number of oxygen. In general, the total relative abundance of Ox classes decreased because of catalytic deoxygenation and in turn the relative abundance of hydrocarbons (CH) increased. Similar results are also exhibited in the bulk elemental analysis for F1 and F2 as listed in Table S4. Plots of the double-bond equivalence (DBE) as a function of carbon number for compounds of the O1 class detected in the coal filtrates are shown in Fig. 3 . DBE represents the degree of unsaturation and cyclic structures in the compounds while carbon number correlates loosely with the extent of cracking of the macromolecules in coal [24,25]. Although the relative abundance of O1 class has increased after catalytic deoxygenation (Fig. 2a), the number of O1 compounds has decreased by 36% (from 189 to 121) upon deoxygenation (Fig. 3), and the average DBE value and carbon number have decreased after deoxygenation. Aromatic cores connected by alkyl chains are regular molecular structures in coal [26], which are also known as archipelago structures [27]. The removal of molecules with the highest carbon numbers may relate to the cleavage of alkyl chains connecting aromatic cores, suggesting the existence of archipelago structures [28,29]. Compounds in Fig. 3b are generally produced from catalytic deoxygenation of compounds in O2, O3 and O4 classes (Figs. S2, S3 and S4, respectively), and some of them are the left compounds of O1 class due to the limit of catalytic efficiency. The weighted mean DBE values for compounds in O2 to O4 classes before catalytic treatment are between 12.8 and 14.9 (Table S2) but the value decreased to 10.3 (O1 class) after deoxygenation, indicating that hydrogenation and/or cleavage of alkyl-bridges between aromatic moieties (archipelago structures) had taken place. The weighted mean carbon numbers before catalytic reactions range from 21.1 up to 23.3 for O2 to O4 classes (Table S3) but the number decreased to 17.4 (O1 class) after catalytic treatment, which suggests that some cracking of macromolecules, such as cleavage of alkyl chains in coal molecules, had occurred [30]. In Figs. 3, S2, S3 and S4, it is interesting to observe that the compositional space of O containing species, before and after catalytic treatment, shifts toward higher DBE ranges while increasing oxygen number per molecules. Similar phenomena were also observed in previous reports [27,31].The possible reason is that more carbon atoms are needed to form an organic molecule containing a higher number of oxygen atom, and an increased number of carbon atom can bring an increased number of aromatic ring to some extent for compounds in coal. Therefore, an increase number of O atom in coal molecules can be indirectly correlated to a higher aromaticity. While above experiments provided useful general information for the coal filtrates, they could not be used to identify the locations of oxygen atoms in or outside of aromatic rings. Alkyl groups can be removed from the molecular ions of alkyl aromatic compounds by collision-activated dissociation (CAD) [32], which should allow the determination of whether the oxygen atoms are in alkyl chains of aromatic compounds or part of the aromatic system. When CAD is performed in a quadrupole ion trap, relatively small amounts of energy are deposited into the ions and typically only one alkyl chain can be cleaved upon one CAD event [33]. It follows that one must carry out multiple dissociation steps for a complete dealkylation of the molecular ion of an aromatic compound with several alkyl chains [33], which is undesirable for the analysis of complex mixtures. However, ISCAD, which is accomplished via regulating the potential between the ion-transfer region and the skimmer, has been demonstrated to enable deposition of much larger amounts of energy into ions. ISCAD was demonstrated capable of performing near-complete dealkylation by cleavage of multiple alkyl chains from ions of alkyl aromatic compounds in one isolation/fragmentation event [34,35]. This approach was employed here to obtain structural information for compounds in coal fluids, which can allow the evaluation of oxygen and nitrogen atom distributions (within aromatic conjugation system or on alkyl chains) and reveals new details on aromatic core structures. Relative abundances of major classes of aromatic core ions (based on ISCAD) are exhibited in Fig. 2b. Compared to ionized whole molecules with evenly distributions in Fig. 2a, most of aromatic cores belong to CH class. After the catalytic treatment, the relative abundance of O1 class decreased and that of CH class increased due to deoxygenation reactions on aromatic cores. Plots of DBE versus carbon number after ISCAD for ionized compounds derived from O1, CH and NyOx classes in the coal fluids are shown in Figs. 4 , S5 and S6, respectively. As all alkyl chains (with the exception of a few methyl or methylene groups) have been cleaved off from the molecular ions upon ISCAD, the plots show only the ionized aromatic cores. For example, Fig. 4 shows the one oxygen atom-containing aromatic cores detected for untreated and treated coal fluids. Based on these data, it can be concluded that catalytic deoxygenation removed the oxygen atom from most compounds containing one oxygen atom in their aromatic cores. This likely involved ring-opening reactions. For O2 aromatic cores (Fig. S7) in coal filtrates, there is no such structure after catalytic deoxygenation, indicating the involvement of ring-opening reactions. Hence, this treatment removed most of the oxygen atoms that resided in aromatic cores. As the weighted mean carbon number of compounds in all classes decreased from 16.1 to 14.3 for the aromatic cores upon catalytic treatment, ring-opening reactions must have occurred during this treatment, which is in agreement with the observed decrease of weighted mean DBE number from 12.2 to 10.8 (Fig. 4). Fig. S5 indicates that ~47% of the aromatic cores in the CH class had been converted to something else via fragmentation or other reactions upon catalytic treatment. However, this percentage increases to 76% for compounds in the O1 class (Fig. 4) and 100% for O2 class (Fig. S7), indicating a high selectivity of ring-opening reactions for the aromatic cores of compounds containing oxygen in their aromatic cores. Comparing Figs. 3 and 4, the average number of compounds per DBE significantly decreased upon cleaving off the alkyl chains. The average number of compounds per DBE is 7.4 and 3.0 in the O1 class (Figs. 3a and 4a) for catalytically untreated sample without and with ISCAD fragmentation, respectively. After the catalytic treatment, the corresponding values are 6.1 and 1.3 (Fig. 3b and 4b). A shorter homologous series upon ISCAD, for the catalytic treated sample, could be induced by the loss of saturated moieties [29]. On the other hand, oxygen atoms in the alkyl chains were predominantly lost upon ISCAD. The relative abundance of heteroatomic classes is 75% and 60% (in Fig. 2a) before and after catalytic treatment, respectively. However, these values decrease to 22% and 11% for heteroatomic cores without and with catalytic deoxygenation, respectively (Fig. 2b). Heteroatomic compounds with heteroatoms in alkyl chains can be indirectly inferred via the difference between Fig. 2a and b. ISCAD and mass analysis at a high resolving power were applied in the characterization of compounds in untreated and catalytically treated coal filtrates. Oxygen atoms located inside vs. outside of aromatic rings can be distinguished by comparing mass spectra before and after ISCAD. Therefore, the ability of catalytic reactions to remove oxygen from inside or outside of aromatic rings could be evaluated. The decrease in the relative abundance of compounds accompanied by decrease in DBE values and carbon numbers observed for Ox class suggests that catalytic treatment reduced the number of oxygen-containing heteroaromatic rings.
abstract: Catalytic deoxygenation of coal enhances the stability and combustion performance of coal-derived liquid fuels. However, determination of the selectivity of removal of oxygen atoms incorporated in or residing outside of aromatic rings is challenging. This limits the ability to evaluate the success of catalytic deoxygenation processes. A mass spectrometric method, in-source collision-activated dissociation (ISCAD), combined with high resolution product ion detection, is demonstrated to allow the determination of whether the oxygen atoms are in aromatic compounds reside outside of aromatic rings or part of the aromatic system, because alkyl chains can be removed from aromatic cores via ISCAD. Application of this method for the analysis of a subbituminous coal treated using a supported catalyst revealed that the catalytic treatment reduced the number of oxygen-containing heteroaromatic rings but not the number of oxygen atoms residing outside the aromatic rings.
title: Tandem mass spectrometric evaluation of core structures of aromatic compounds after catalytic deoxygenation
