full_text: Crude oil plays the leading role in the global supplies of fuels and energy. Vacuum residue (VR) makes the heaviest fraction obtained from vacuum distillation of crude oil. VR of Ural crude contains mainly hydrocarbons with boiling points above 520 °C. Moreover, it contains about 3% sulphur and 0.5% nitrogen in the form of heteroorganic compounds, and minor amounts of organometallic compounds, predominantly nickel and vanadium compounds. The use of VR in/as heavy furnace oil fractions is much limited since it is responsible for considerable environmental pollution. As heavier and heavier crudes have to be processed, refineries face the problem of increasing production of VR; these volumes nowadays amount to 25 wt.% of the feed on average. Thus, the efficiency of refineries is highly dependent on availability of processes which convert VR and yield low-boiling products. VR can be converted into low-boiling fractions in coking, visbreaking, cracking and hydrocracking processes. Hydrocracking of VR offers additional volumes of high-quality motor fuels and therefore many researchers strive for further improvements in that process [1–9]. Hydrocracking of VR is far more difficult than hydrocracking of crude distillates since VR contains considerable amounts of asphaltic and resinous components. Those substances are thermally unstable and they would crack down readily. The resultant decomposition products undergo secondary condensation to produce coke, hence they are coke-forming components [10–12]. Most contaminants present in crude oil (sulphur, nitrogen and heavy metals) are bounded to asphaltic and resinous substances, chiefly to asphaltenes. Within the compounds considered, asphaltenes are the ones with the most complex chemical structures and highest molecular weights. These are principally condensed polycyclic aromatics, inclusive of heterocyclic compounds. Dealkylation and dehydrogenation of asphaltenes take place as a result of cracking and these reactions are favourable for the coke-forming process [13–15]. The coking problems faced at VR hydrocracking made the researchers analyse the composition and properties of asphaltenes contained in VR [10,16–18].The propensity of oil products to coking is measured by the content of Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR). In order to reduce the volume of by-product coke, the VR cracking process is operated in the presence of hydrogen and specific VR hydrocracking catalysts. VR hydrocracking has been intended to convert vacuum residue into low-boiling hydrocarbons and to remove sulphur, nitrogen and heavy metals from the hydrocracking products at the same time, having in mind the need to minimise the amount of coke formed in the process. The heavy metals removed from the feed and the produced coke will be deposited on the catalyst and they will lower its catalytic activity [19,20]. The catalyst hence needs to be replaced continuously and for that reason the process is usually carried out in ebullated or fluidised catalytic bed reactors [5,21]. The metals like Mo, Ni, Fe and Co, and more frequently their combinations like Ni–Mo, are usually employed as VR hydrocracking catalysts [12]. Adding to their catalytic performance, their second but equally important function is to be cocking inhibitors [4]. Hydrocracking of VR is usually organised as a one-step or two-step process. The product can be recycled to improve conversion of VR. VR hydrocracking is a very difficult process and it was implemented in the industry only after many years of research, and after solving numerous process-related problems and developing new process equipment [22–27]. The most popular and well-established commercial VR hydrocracking processes are: H-Oil, LC-finning and BOC Unibo [28]. This paper presents the effects from process parameters adopted for one-step VR hydrocracking, wherein the suspension Ni–Mo/Al2O3 catalyst was used, on VR conversion to lightweight products and on quality of those products. The volume of produced lighter fractions and catalytic performance in the hydrotreatment process made the evaluation criteria for the catalyst selected for tests. The hydrocracking experiments involved the vacuum residue of Ural crude (initial boiling point: 405 °C; content of: 538°C+ residue — 88.4 wt.%, sulphur — 2.54 wt.%, nitrogen — 0.53 wt.%, asphaltenes — 5.24 wt.%, CCR — 15.8 wt.%; and heavy metals: V — 150 ppm, Ni — 45 ppm). Ni–Mo/Al2O3 was employed as a catalyst; it was crushed and sieved, and its <0.06 mm fraction was used in tests. Its specification has been presented below: specific volume — 0.64 cm3 g−1, specific surface area — 260 m2 g−1, average pore radius — 3.7 nm, porosity — 67%, bulk density — 1047 kg m−3. The surface area (BET) and the porous structure of the catalyst were investigated by means of the nitrogen adsorption and desorption method at the temperature of −196 °C (BET), with the use of the NOVA 2200 Quantachrom equipment. The porous structure and porosity were studied with the use of the mercury porosimetry method and Pascal 440 tester. The flow-through test stand, made of stainless steel, was employed for the investigations of the VR hydrocracking process. The arrangement of that equipment was shown in Fig. 1 . The experimental reactor makes the principal element of the stand. Its volume is 0.6 dm3 and its design makes the liquid circulate. That is a flow reactor with internal circulating pipe [29], and hydrogen is both a reacting substance and a mixing agent at the same time. The VR hydrocracking process was studied at the temperature of 410–450 °C, under the pressure of 12–20 MPa, in the presence of the Ni–Mo/Al2O3 catalyst which had been ground to reduce its grain size down to below 0.06 mm. The vacuum residue was initially mixed with 1–10 wt.% of catalyst at 150 °C. That mixture was then fed from mixer 1, by means of pump 2, to reactor 4, at the liquid hourly space velocity (LHSV) of 0.25–0.75 h−1. Compressor 3 was employed to force hydrogen to reactor 4, at the pressure of 12–20 MPa and at the gas hourly space velocity of 2500 h−1. The reaction mixture was then transferred to separator 5 wherefrom the liquid product with the catalyst in it was drained periodically. The gaseous products and unconverted hydrogen were released to atmosphere through cooler 6 and flow control valve 7. The product gases were periodically sampled for analysis. The liquid product was subjected to filtration at 50 °C in order to separate the catalyst. The product with no catalyst in it was then subjected to fractional distillation to produce the following fractions: gasoline, diesel oil, vacuum gas oil (VGO) and final VR. The distillation cuts were analysed for their sulphur contents, while asphaltenes and CCR were determined in VGO and VR. In order to calculate the amount of C1–C4 hydrocarbons formed in the process, the volume of the reactor outlet gas was measured and the concentration of gaseous products from VR cracking was analysed in the gas stream. It is most advantageous from the viewpoint of thermodynamics and kinetics to operate the VR hydrocracking process under isothermal conditions. Those conditions can be provided in a laboratory model of VR hydrocracking bubbler-type reactor with internal circulation. That reactor operates in a similar way as the H-Oil reactor with the ebullated bed does [23]. • The content of C1–C4 hydrocarbons in gaseous products was determined by the chromatographic method: Perkin–Elmer gas chromatograph, FID detector, Chromosorb P+25% Dimethylsulfolane, 6 m×3 mm i.d. column. Nitrogen was used as a carrier gas, and the temperature was programmed from 30 to 100 °C. • The liquid products obtained from the VR hydrocracking process were subjected to atmospheric distillation, according to the Polish Standard PN-81/C-04012. That treatment yielded the gasoline fraction with the boiling point below 180 °C. The atmospheric distillation residue was subjected to further distillation under vacuum as per the method provided in ASTM D 1160-95. The diesel oil fraction with the boiling range of 180–370 °C was subjected to further distillation under 10 mm Hg vacuum, the vacuum gas oil (VGO) fraction with the boiling range of 370–538 °C was subjected to further distillation under 1 mm Hg vacuum, and 538°C+ vacuum residue (VR) stream was produced. • The Fisons Instruments EA-1108 analyser (acc. to ASTM D 1552-1990) was employed for elemental analysis CHNS–O of the raw material and the liquid products obtained from the hydrocracking process. The content of Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR) was found in accordance with ASTM D 189–88. The content of asphaltenes was determined with the use of the UOP 614–80 method (extraction with n-heptane and toluene at 80 °C). Asphaltenes are defined as heptane insoluble — toluene soluble materials [16]. • Chemical compositions of the liquid products (gasoline and gas oil) were determined by the chromatographic method: HP 6890 gas chromatograph, FID detector, ChemStation). The individual components were identified by means of the GC/MS method: HP 5890 Series II gas chromatograph, MSD 5973 Network mass detector, ChemStation). • Density of the raw material, liquid products and distillation fractions obtained from those products was determined by the hydrometer method, according to Polish Standard PN-EN ISO 3675:2004. The content of C1–C4 hydrocarbons in gaseous products was determined by the chromatographic method: Perkin–Elmer gas chromatograph, FID detector, Chromosorb P+25% Dimethylsulfolane, 6 m×3 mm i.d. column. Nitrogen was used as a carrier gas, and the temperature was programmed from 30 to 100 °C. The liquid products obtained from the VR hydrocracking process were subjected to atmospheric distillation, according to the Polish Standard PN-81/C-04012. That treatment yielded the gasoline fraction with the boiling point below 180 °C. The atmospheric distillation residue was subjected to further distillation under vacuum as per the method provided in ASTM D 1160-95. The diesel oil fraction with the boiling range of 180–370 °C was subjected to further distillation under 10 mm Hg vacuum, the vacuum gas oil (VGO) fraction with the boiling range of 370–538 °C was subjected to further distillation under 1 mm Hg vacuum, and 538°C+ vacuum residue (VR) stream was produced. The Fisons Instruments EA-1108 analyser (acc. to ASTM D 1552-1990) was employed for elemental analysis CHNS–O of the raw material and the liquid products obtained from the hydrocracking process. The content of Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR) was found in accordance with ASTM D 189–88. The content of asphaltenes was determined with the use of the UOP 614–80 method (extraction with n-heptane and toluene at 80 °C). Asphaltenes are defined as heptane insoluble — toluene soluble materials [16]. Chemical compositions of the liquid products (gasoline and gas oil) were determined by the chromatographic method: HP 6890 gas chromatograph, FID detector, ChemStation). The individual components were identified by means of the GC/MS method: HP 5890 Series II gas chromatograph, MSD 5973 Network mass detector, ChemStation). Density of the raw material, liquid products and distillation fractions obtained from those products was determined by the hydrometer method, according to Polish Standard PN-EN ISO 3675:2004. Conversion figures for VR as well as sulphur, asphaltenes and CCR, were calculated by balancing the contents of those components in the feed and in the hydrocracking products in the following equation: C i = 100 · ( m feed · x i feed − ∑ m i · x i ) m feed · x i feed where: C i — conversion for component “i”; m feed — weight of feed (VR); m i — weight of component “i”, x i feed — weight fraction of component “i” in feed, x i — weight fraction of component “i” in products. The effects of parameters adopted for the VR hydrocracking process on the conversion levels of feed as well as sulphur compounds, asphaltenes and CCR contained in the feed, and on fractional and chemical compositions of the obtained products, were studied within the following ranges of variability: reaction temperature — 410–450 °C, hydrogen pressure — 12–20 MPa, liquid hourly space velocity (LHSV) — 0.25–0.75 h−1, and catalyst concentration (C CAT) — 1–10 wt.%. The findings were presented in Tables 1–8. The VR hydrocracking process is operated to maximise the yield of fractions which are lighter than VR, and to subject the hydrocracking products to hydrofining, under process safety conditions. The principal function of the catalyst employed in the studied VR hydrocracking process is to eliminate the condensation reactions of highly unsaturated compounds produced when cracking the VR material. The condensation reactions are responsible for the formation of coke in the reactor, or in the reaction product collection system, and hence for process disturbances. Another important function of that catalyst is to promote the hydrofining reactions of hydrocracking products, inclusive principally of hydrodesulphurisation reactions. The employed Ni–Mo/Al2O3 system is a bi-functional catalyst which catalyses both cracking and hydrogenation reactions. The fission function in this catalysts is provided by its support, i.e. Al2O3. Its splitting activity is dependent on acidity of its acid sites since they control the reactions which follow the ionic mechanism, i.e. for example cracking of C–C bonds. The support employed for this catalyst is slightly acidic and its splitting capacity is moderate. The hydrogenation function is the duty of transition metals Ni–Mo dispersed over the supporting surface. The hydrogenation reactions catalysed by those metals follow the free-radical mechanism [30]. The effects from VR hydrocracking process parameters on conversion of feed and on conversion of sulphur compounds and asphaltenes contained in the feed, as well as on CCR, were presented in Table 1 . The effect from the catalyst concentration of conversion figures for VR, sulphur, asphaltenes and CCR was studied within the catalyst concentrations of 1–10 wt.%, at the reaction temperature of 430 °C, at LHSV of 0.5 h−1 and at pressure of 16 MPa. The presented data reveal that conversion of VR is constant in practice and it reaches about 62% over the studied range of catalyst concentrations. Hence, the conversion figure turns out to be slightly dependent only on the catalyst concentration (within the tested range of concentrations). This could suggest that the VR cracking reactions are predominantly thermal C–C cleavage reactions. The effect from catalyst concentration on conversions of asphaltenes and CCR is quite small, too. When the catalyst concentration is increased from 1% to 10%, conversions of asphaltenes and CCR increases by 4% only. Cracking of asphaltenes is also chiefly a thermal process, and the coking potential of VR is strongly dependent on the concentration of asphaltenes as they are the most important coke-forming components. The effect of catalyst concentration is clearly visible for the hydrodesulphurisation process. Hydrodesulphurisation is a catalytic process which is strongly dependent on the catalyst activity specification. When the catalyst concentration is increased from 1% to 10%, sulphur conversion goes up from 70% to 88%. It is known from literature reports that the amount of produced coke increases when the hydrocracking catalyst activity drops down. In our laboratory tests, no increased production of coke was observed when the catalyst concentration was lowered within 10–1 wt.%. The catalyst residence time in the reaction zone was too short under our experimental conditions, so no drop in catalytic activity could be noticed and the volume of produced coke was too small to be measurable. Since the effect of catalyst concentration on VR conversion was found negligible within our process parameters, the concentration of 1 wt.% was adopted for our further study on hydrocracking of vacuum residue. The temperature effect on conversion of VR, sulphur, asphaltenes and CCR was studied at the catalyst concentration of 1 wt.%, at hydrogen pressure of 16 MPa, and at LHSV of 0.5 h−1. A nearly rectilinear increase of VR conversion is observed for increasing temperature over the whole range of temperature programme. The conversion figure rises from 27.8% at 410 °C to 92.7% at 450 °C. Hence, process temperature strongly impacts the conversion of vacuum residue. The change in the conversion of asphaltenes and CCR with the increase in temperature follows the profile for VR, and the only difference is that conversion figures for asphaltenes and for CCR are lower by about 10%. The influence of temperature on hydrodesulphurisation is much inferior, which results from the characteristics of the catalyst used in the studies. Sulphur conversion at 410 °C equals to about 52%, while the higher temperature of 450 °C moves it up to 91%. That makes a prove for high activity of catalyst in the desulphurisation process over a wide span of temperatures. The highest values were obtained for the conversion of sulphur, lower values – in particular at low temperatures – were available for VR conversion, while asphaltenes and CCR offered the lowest conversion figures for the process parameters adopted in our research programme. Those findings correspond with the activation energy values for individual reactions which combine to form the VR hydrocracking process with the use of the studied catalyst; the activation energy values were specified by the authors in their earlier paper [31]. The activation energy value determined for the hydrodesulphurisation reaction is relatively low and it amounts to 37.4 kJ/mol. That speaks for high activity of the catalyst in the hydrodesulphurisation reaction, and also for a relatively low temperature effect on that reaction. The activation energy value is much higher for the VR hydrocracking reactions (86.9 kJ/mol), and it is especially high for the reactions which reduce the contents of CCR and asphaltenes in the hydrocracking products (109.4 and 135.4 kJ/mol, respectively). Those figures make evidence for lower catalytic activity and lower reaction rates experienced for those reactions. Hence, conversion data for VR, asphaltenes and CCR at moderate temperatures will be lower, and those reactions will be more sensitive to higher temperatures. The experiments intended to determine the effect of feed space velocity on the course of the VR hydrocracking process were conducted under the following conditions: reaction temperature — 430 °C, pressure —16 Pa, and catalyst concentration —1 wt.%. As comes from the presented data, lowering the liquid space velocity (increase of mean residence time for the liquid in the reactor) strongly increases the conversion figures for asphaltenes and CCR, while conversion of VR is affected to a much lower degree. Even lower effects from liquid hourly space velocity are observed on the conversion figure for sulphur. Thus, when the liquid space velocity is changed from 0.75 h−1 (mean residence time of 1.33 h) to LHSV of 0.25 h−1 (residence time of 4.0 h), sulphur conversion increases from 61% to 85%. Under the same experimental conditions, when LHSV is lowered from 0.75 h−1 to 0.25 h−1, conversion of VR increases from 41% to 89%, conversion of asphaltenes increases from 19% to 83%, and that of CCR from 17% to 81%. When the effects of reaction temperature within 410–450 °C (for LHSV=0.5 h−1) are compared to those of LHSV within 0.75–0.25 h−1 (for temperature=430 °C), those effects on the conversion of asphaltenes and CCR are much similar. The effect of temperature on sulphur conversion is yet considerably higher, and that on VR conversion is even higher. The effects of hydrogen pressure on the course of the VR hydrocracking process were investigated at the reaction temperature of 430 °C, liquid space velocity of 0.5 h−1, and catalyst concentration of 1 wt.%. When the hydrogen pressure is increased from 12 to 20 MPa, conversions of asphaltenes and CCR go up by about 10%. The effect of hydrogen pressure on the sulphur conversion is much lower — the same pressure growth results in the rise of the conversion value by 3% only. That situation is rather unexpected since hydrodesulphurisation is a catalytic process which is strongly dependent both on catalytic activity and on hydrogen pressure [32]. The limited influence of hydrogen pressure on hydrodesulphurisation of hydrocracking products could result from that fact that the experiments were carried out at a high excess of hydrogen because of the need to maintain good agitation in the reactor employed in our laboratory tests. The hydrogen flow was 50 times higher than the stoichiometric consumption calculated for the hydrogenation reactions. Increased hydrogen pressure, however, gives lower VR conversion by 3%. That is a result of secondary reactions of cracking products (polymerisation, alkylation, hydrogenation), and the rates of those reactions increase with increased pressure, yet no coke is formed. The effects of process parameters on the composition of VR hydrocracking products, defined by shares of fractions obtained from distillative separation of the reaction products, were presented in Table 2 . When the reaction temperature increases, the share of the heaviest fraction, i.e. VR, clearly declines. That fraction, despite the same initial boiling point as the feed, has a different chemical composition and somewhat different physical–chemical properties than the feed. When the reaction temperature is increased, within the studied scope, the shares of the most lightweight fractions: gas and gasoline, grow up a little, but stronger for higher temperatures. The weight share of the gasoline fraction in the products is close to that of gas fraction and their ratio changes slightly only when the parameters are changed. The effect of temperature on the yield of diesel oil fraction is much more distinct. The yield of diesel oil fraction amounts to 11.5% at 410 °C, and it then increases to 44.2% at 450 °C. The yield of VGO fraction reaches its peak value of 34.3% at 430 °C, and then it decreases to 25% at 450 °C. The reason is that more VGO undergoes cracking to lighter fractions at those conditions than is produced from VR cracking. The VGO fraction is not of any less value than the motor fuel fractions. It can be fed to the catalytic cracking plants for the production of low-sulphur fractions of motor fuels. It is also applicable as reduced-sulphur content fuel oil. When the liquid hourly space velocity is reduced within the scope of 0.75–0.25 h−1, conversion of VR clearly increases, which is reflected by higher shares of all hydrocarbon fractions which are lighter than VR. Increased temperature and mean residence time in the reactor improves the yield of the most valuable fractions, i.e. gasoline, diesel oil and vacuum gas oil, and also increases the production of the gas fraction, which is not so desirable. The effect of feed residence time in the reactor on conversions of fractions lighter than VR, within the studied scope of parameters, is similar to that of temperature. The hydrogen pressure within 12–20 MPa affected the shares on individual hydrocarbon fractions in the products obtained from hydrocracking of VR slightly only. The increasing hydrogen pressure was observed to reduce the amount of gas products, while the shares of higher-boiling fractions increased somewhat. That behaviour of the system results from secondary reactions (polymerisation, alkylation, hydrogenation) of cracking products, and the rates of those reactions increases to follow the increasing pressure. The catalyst concentration over the studied scope of 1–10 wt.% had a small effect only on the shares of individual hydrocarbon fractions in the VR hydrocracking products. However, the increasing catalyst concentration was responsible for a small growth in the production of lighter fractions. The VR hydrocracking process is accompanied by hydrodesulphurisation reactions. In order to characterise the depth of hydrodesulphurisation of VR hydrocracking products as a function of reaction temperature, pressure, feed space velocity and catalyst concentration, Table 3 presents the sulphur contents in individual hydrocarbon fractions separated from the VR hydrocracking products and refers them to the sulphur content of the hydrocracking feed. As can be seen, only small amount of sulphur is transferred unconverted to motor fuel fractions. Thus, the light hydrocarbons produced from VR hydrocracking, which fall within the gasoline fraction, contain 0.02–0.14 wt.% S only, while the diesel oil hydrocarbons contain 1.06–4.09 wt.% S introduced in the feed. The VGO fraction contains 2.81–8.30 wt.%, and the secondary VR fraction — 5.04–40.19 wt.% S present in the feed (primary VR). Those figures confirm good performance of the Ni–Mo/Al2O3 catalyst employed in the VR hydrocracking process, which turned out active in the desulphurisation processes at the same time, in particular for hydrodesulphurisation of lighter hydrocarbons. When the effects of investigated VR hydrocracking parameters on sulphur conversion in the products (Table 1) and on sulphur contents in individual fractions (Table 3) are compared, it is apparent that the depth of hydrodesulphurisation in the VR hydrocracking products is principally influenced by the reaction temperature, LHSV (i.e. residence time), and also catalyst concentration, while the effect from the hydrogen pressure is small. That conclusion is unexpected since the literature reports declare the level of desulphurisation to be dependent predominantly on catalytic activity and hydrogen pressure. Quite a small effect from hydrogen pressure on hydrodesulphurisation of VR hydrocracking products can be accounted for by the fact that the hydrocracking and hydrodesulphurisation reactions take place at such a high excess of hydrogen that the diffusion of hydrogen to the catalyst, within the adopted range of pressure 12–20 MPa, is not a conversion limiting factor, hence the hydrogen pressure does not control the rate of hydrogenation processes. The excess hydrogen adopted for the studied process model is necessary to maintain process hydraulics, i.e. chiefly fluidisation of catalyst. The internal circulation model adopted for our laboratory reactor provides – at high hydrogen flow rates – intense circulation inside the reactor, i.e. perfect agitation of reacting materials. Diffusion of reacting substances to the catalyst is quick, hence the reaction rate can be high, which is in particular important for the hydrogenation reactions. Hydrogenation reactions are exothermic ones. It is the hydrodesulphurisation reaction to be chiefly responsible for the total exothermic effect of the studied process. The reactor model employed in our studies provided advantageous operating conditions which were nearly isothermal, which is especially desirable for the VR hydrocracking process since excessive cracking of VR is experienced at higher temperatures and the process yields too much coke deposited in the reactor. Table 4 shows the contents of C1–C4 hydrocarbons in gas products obtained from VR hydrocracking. The scatter of shares for methane, ethane, propane and butane is nearly equimolar in the gas fraction, and any change in parameters gives no change in practice in the ratio of components of the gas fraction. The volumes of methane and ethane formed during the cracking and hydrocracking processes catalysed by carbonium ions are much lower than the volumes of propane and butane, and the catalytic mechanism for carbonium ions generates considerable shares of iso- compounds, both in C4 fraction and in light gasoline. Production of equimolar amounts of C1–C4 hydrocarbons and small shares of C4 isomers in the studied process is evidence of the fact that the cracking process is not catalysed by carbonium ions, hence it is predominantly thermal cracking. Running the VR hydrocracking process within the parameters which offer deep hydrocracking of VR, like reaction temperature of 450 °C, or feed space velocity of 0.25 h−1, results in the production of gas fraction between 10 and 20 wt.%. That is disadvantageous since the basic purpose of VR hydrocracking, besides hydrodesulphurisation of VR and its cracking products, is to yield considerable volumes of liquid fractions which are suitable for blending motor fuels and heating fuels. Production of considerable volumes of vacuum gas oil (VGO) is also desirable since it can make a feed to the catalytic cracking plants to be further converted to additional volumes of motor fuels. Tables 5–8 present the effects of process parameters on chemical composition and quality of liquid fractions obtained from distillative separation of VR hydrocracking products. Table 5 illustrates the effects of the studied parameters on the composition of gasoline fraction obtained from distillative separation of VR hydrocracking products, inclusive of the content of sulphur present in that fraction. The yield of gasoline fraction is changed by process parameters within 2.2–12.4%. Irrespective, however, of the adopted values of parameters, the shares of individual components of gasoline fraction vary within a narrow range only. Thus, the content of olefins changes within 1.0–1.7 wt.%, of naphthenes within 8.7–11.9 wt.%, of aromatics within 12.9–16.4 wt.%, and that of paraffins within 70.7–77.4 wt.%. The VR hydrocracking process produces also isoparaffins which were detected and analysed in the gas, gasoline and diesel oil fractions. The contents of paraffins as specified in Tables 4–6 are total values for n-paraffins and isoparaffins. Olefins are present in the gasoline fraction at the level above 1 wt.% despite high hydrogen pressure and the presence of hydrogenation catalyst. The contents of aromatics are higher within the studied parameters than those of naphthenes. When the reaction temperature is increased, the content of aromatics decreases due to hydrogenation, and the contents of naphthenes and paraffins increase at the same time, with the growth for paraffins becoming predominant at 450 °C. That results from the fact that the rates of hydrogenation reaction of aromatics to naphthenes, and subsequent hydrogenation reaction of naphthenes to paraffins, are higher than the rates of the aromatics-forming VR cracking reactions and subsequent reactions which are responsible for the production of aromatics. Following the expectations, also increased pressure resulted in lower aromatics content in the gasoline fraction. The composition of gasoline fraction obtained from distillation of VR hydrocracking products is much advantageous for gasoline blending, yet the produced volumes of that fraction are not satisfactory. When more severe VR hydrocracking parameters are employed, the volume of gasoline fraction is higher but the volume of produced gas surpasses that of gasoline. The sulphur content in the gasoline fraction varies within 0.031–0.095 wt.%, depending on the adopted VR hydrocracking parameters. That fraction needs to be subjected to further hydrodesulphurisation before it can be used in gasoline blending. The effects of process parameters on chemical composition of diesel oil obtained from distillative separation of VR hydrocracking products have been provided in Table 6 . The situation for diesel oil is much the same as that for gasoline fraction and the shares of its individual components – for the studied scope of VR hydrocracking parameters – vary within a narrow range only. Thus, the content of paraffins changes within 69.8–72.4 wt.%, of olefins within 0.8–1.1 wt.%, of naphthenes within 3.4–5.9 wt.%, of aromatics within 21.5–24.8 wt.%, and that of sulphur within 0.17–0.40 wt.%. The comparative analysis of compositions for those two fractions show that the diesel oil fraction has higher contents of both aromatics and sulphur. The effect of process parameters on the content of aromatics is not big, and increasing temperature and pressure reduce the sulphur content to a limited degree only. The situation in which compositions of individual fractions obtained from distillative separation of VR hydrocracking products are not much different from each other results from the fact that the product composition is principally controlled by cracking of high molecular weight compounds contained in VR to compounds with smaller molecules, i.e. for example cracking of polycyclic aromatics to more simple aromatic structures. Hence, the obtained cracking products contain similar shares of aromatics, irrespective of cracking severity, i.e. irrespective of process parameters. The importance is much lower for the hydrogenation reactions which convert unsaturated compounds to saturated ones, inclusive of converting olefins to paraffins, and which are responsible (to a smaller extent) for hydrogenation of aromatics to naphthenes and naphthenes to paraffins, with the latter reactions requiring more severe process parameters. Chemical compositions of both gasoline fraction and diesel fuel fractions obtained from the VR hydrocracking process make them useful as components of motor fuels. However, they should be subjected to hydrofining, in particular to desulphurisation and denitrogenation, before they can be utilised as a blendstock [33]. Removal of nitrogen compounds from the diesel oil fraction in the process of its hydrofining is harder to do than removal of sulphur compounds — more active catalyst and more severe hydrofining parameters are required. Yet, that removal is necessary since the alkaline organic nitrogen compounds, if left in the diesel oil fraction, make diesel oil darken during its storage. The nitrogen content increases for higher boiling points of oil fractions, and the ratio of alkaline nitrogen to total nitrogen reaches 0.5 in heavy fractions with boiling points above 315 °C. Organic compounds of alkaline nitrogen are responsible for poisoning of hydrotreatment catalysts, and these catalysts are usually difunctional catalysts [19,20]. Further volumes of good quality gasoline fraction and diesel oil fraction can be produced by catalytic cracking of VGO fraction, and for that reason VGO fraction makes a precious product of VR hydrocracking. The effect of hydrocracking parameters on the quality of VGO fraction obtained from distillation of hydrocracking products has been shown in Table 7 . The quality of VGO has been determined by its contents of sulphur, nitrogen and CCR, the last parameter defining susceptibility of hydrocarbons to coking. The sulphur content of VGO fraction falls  within 0.32–0.77 wt.%, nitrogen content within 0.35–0.64 wt.%, and CCR content within 0.13–0.43 wt.%. Although the sulphur and CCR contents in VGO fraction go down for more severe VR hydrocracking parameters, the nitrogen content increases for deeper conversion of VR. Deeper cracking of asphaltenes does not produce lower molecular weight heterocyclic compounds of nitrogen. More active catalysts are required for the hydrodenitrogenation process than for hydrodesulphurisation and hydrodemetallisation processes. Since the catalyst used in our VR hydrocracking process was not sufficiently active for denitrogenation, the VGO fraction nitrogen content increased. However, high nitrogen content in VGO fraction (0.35–0.64 wt.%) does not form any critical limit for the use of that fraction as a feed to the catalytic cracking plant operated to produce motor fuels [34]. The content of CCR in VGO is low and it amounts to 0.2 wt.% on average. That is considered disadvantageous when the fraction is to make a feed to a catalytic cracking plant since that process is favoured by a high content of coke-forming components in its feed stream. The VGO fraction can also be utilised directly as desulphurised heating oil. The effects of process parameters on the quality of secondary VR, which is left after distillation of VR hydrocracking products, have been presented in Table 8 . That quality was assessed on the basis of sulphur and CCR contents. Exactly as expected, the sulphur content in the vacuum distillation bottoms decreased at higher temperatures, at higher pressures and at lower LHSV in relation to its level in the feed by at least 40% and it amounted to 1.11–1.60 wt.%. Hence, hydrocracking of VR allows for considerable removal of sulphur from VR hydrocracking products, even from the heaviest fraction. Increased hydrogen pressure also reduces the CCR content in the residue after distillation of VR hydrocracking products. On the other hand, reduced LHSV, and in particular increased VR hydrocracking temperature, result in increased CCR content in the secondary residue obtained after distillative separation of the VR hydrocracking products. The CCR content for secondary VR is 19.8–38.1 wt.%, so it is higher than the CCR content in the feed (15.8 wt.%), for the whole range of parameters. 1. Based on the research on simultaneous vacuum residue hydrocracking and hydrotreatment of hydrocracking products, the catalyst Ni–Mo/Al2O3 employed in the hydrocracking tests was found to be a moderately active VR cracking catalyst, while it turned out efficient in hydrofining processes, inclusive of hydrodesulphurisation. 2. That catalyst eliminates coking because of condensation reactions of highly unsaturated compounds formed in the VR thermal cracking process — no coke was found in the reaction products. 3. Hydrocracking of vacuum residue makes it possible to produce significant volumes of light fractions with composition advantageous for blending motor fuels; 27.2–42.2 wt.% of motor fuel fractions could be obtained at 430 °C with VR conversion to distillates (b.p.<538 °C) of 61.6–88.7 wt.%. 4. VR hydrocracking at 430 °C yielded 20.2–38.1 wt.% of vacuum gas oil, which can be subjected to FCC or hydrocracking to produce further volumes of motor fuel fractions. 5. Research on the effects of process parameters on VR hydrocracking revealed that the catalyst concentration and hydrogen pressure had little effect on VR hydrocracking and on lowering the asphaltenes and CCR contents in the products. 6. VR hydrocracking as well as asphaltenes and CCR contents in the products were strongly affected by reaction temperatures and liquid hourly space velocity (LHSV), i.e. residence time. 7. Since hydrotreatment of VR hydrocracking products takes place simultaneously with the hydrocracking process itself, quality of those products becomes superior — sulphur conversion of 70–85 wt.% becomes available at 430 °C. 8. Hydrotreatment of products is most affected by the reaction temperature and LHSV, while the effect of catalyst concentration is lower, and hydrogen pressure has no effect in practice over the studied scope of parameters. 9. The process parameters were found to have low impact on the compositions of fractions obtained from VR hydrocracking. 10. VR hydrocracking offers motor fuel fractions with too high sulphur and nitrogen contents. These components are responsible for colour increase during storage, hence they must be removed in the hydrotreatment process of so produced fractions. Based on the research on simultaneous vacuum residue hydrocracking and hydrotreatment of hydrocracking products, the catalyst Ni–Mo/Al2O3 employed in the hydrocracking tests was found to be a moderately active VR cracking catalyst, while it turned out efficient in hydrofining processes, inclusive of hydrodesulphurisation. That catalyst eliminates coking because of condensation reactions of highly unsaturated compounds formed in the VR thermal cracking process — no coke was found in the reaction products. Hydrocracking of vacuum residue makes it possible to produce significant volumes of light fractions with composition advantageous for blending motor fuels; 27.2–42.2 wt.% of motor fuel fractions could be obtained at 430 °C with VR conversion to distillates (b.p.<538 °C) of 61.6–88.7 wt.%. VR hydrocracking at 430 °C yielded 20.2–38.1 wt.% of vacuum gas oil, which can be subjected to FCC or hydrocracking to produce further volumes of motor fuel fractions. Research on the effects of process parameters on VR hydrocracking revealed that the catalyst concentration and hydrogen pressure had little effect on VR hydrocracking and on lowering the asphaltenes and CCR contents in the products. VR hydrocracking as well as asphaltenes and CCR contents in the products were strongly affected by reaction temperatures and liquid hourly space velocity (LHSV), i.e. residence time. Since hydrotreatment of VR hydrocracking products takes place simultaneously with the hydrocracking process itself, quality of those products becomes superior — sulphur conversion of 70–85 wt.% becomes available at 430 °C. Hydrotreatment of products is most affected by the reaction temperature and LHSV, while the effect of catalyst concentration is lower, and hydrogen pressure has no effect in practice over the studied scope of parameters. The process parameters were found to have low impact on the compositions of fractions obtained from VR hydrocracking. VR hydrocracking offers motor fuel fractions with too high sulphur and nitrogen contents. These components are responsible for colour increase during storage, hence they must be removed in the hydrotreatment process of so produced fractions.
abstract: The laboratory findings were presented for a single-stage hydrocracking process of vacuum residue (VR) of Ural crude. The Ni–Mo/Al2O3 catalyst in suspension was used in tests. The amounts and quality of the obtained products were related to process parameters. Hydrocracking of VR was carried out in a continuous flow reactor, at the temperature of 410–450 °C and pressure of 12–20 MPa, at the liquid space velocity (LHSV) of 0.25–0.75 h−1, and at the gas space velocity of 2500 h−1. The studied catalyst Ni–Mo/Al2O3 was found moderately active in the VR cracking process, while it turned out an efficient catalyst for the hydrofining processes. The catalyst concentration and hydrogen pressure had no effect on hydrocracking of VR, and hydrofining of cracking products was affected slightly only over the studied scope of parameters. The reaction temperature and LHSV exerted dramatic effects on hydrocracking of VR and on the asphaltenes and CCR contents, while their effect on hydrofining of cracking products was much lower. The temperature of 430 °C resulted in VR conversion to distillates (b.p.<538 °C) of 61.6–88.7 wt.% and in sulphur conversion of 70–85 wt.%.
title: Effects of parameters in Ni–Mo catalysed hydrocracking of vacuum residue on composition and quality of obtained products

full_text: Converting CO2 to high value-added chemicals and fuels is an attractive prospect since it may realize the reuse of CO2 on a large scale and provide an alternative to fossil feedstocks [1]. By hydrogenation, CO2 can be directly converted to methane, methanol, olefins, dimethyl ether, etc. In particulat, the hydrogenation of CO2 to methane (CO2 methanation) is of importance from an industrial viewpoint. With the existing commercial infrastructure, methane can be used to produce syngas by steam reforming, generate electricity and heat via combustion, and serve as an alternative to liquid petroleum or gasoline in vehicles [2]. Moreover, producing H2 by water splitting with renewable electricity for CO2 methanation is believed to be an economical and feasible route to establish a sustainable carbon-based cycle. Therefore, CO2 methanation can also be applied to store hydrogen energy and renewable electricity. CO2 methanation is exothermic in nature with a high kinetic barrier [3], making it challenging for industrial application. Supported noble metals and nickel catalysts have been investigated for this reaction [4]. Although noble metals have excellent catalytic performance, their limited reserves, high price, and deactivation by CO poisoning restrict their commercial applications [5]. Cost-effective Ni-based catalysts with excellent catalytic activity are more promising for CO2 methanation in large-scale application [6]. However, it is difficult to achieve a satisfactory activity at low temperatures for Ni-based catalysts due to their high activation energy. When high reaction temperature is applied, problems such as poor catalyst stability, extra energy consumption, and side reactions would appear [7]. Therefore, developing Ni-based catalysts with excellent low-temperature activity is of great significance and has received considerable attention [8]. It is well known that catalytic behavior is affected by several factors [9]. Ni dispersion, metal-support interaction, concentration of basic surface sites, and catalyst redox properties are all related to catalytic performance [10]. Generally, metallic Ni is considered as the active site for H2 dissociation in CO2 methanation. Small Ni particles can provide more active sites and create more surface defects, reducing the activation energy for H2 dissociation [11]. To obtain Ni-based catalysts with small Ni particles, different preparation methods have been developed. For example, Dong et al. prepared Ni-phyllosilicate catalysts with a three-dimensional network structure via the hydrothermal reaction of 3D-SBA-15 and soluble nickel salts, and small-sized Ni particles ( Ni-7.5Ce > Ni-2.5Ce > Ni-0Ce > Ni-5Ce-IMP > Ni-0Ce-IMP. Thus, the COM-IMP method endows the catalysts with an improved low-temperature activity. The best performance is achieved on the Ni-5Ce catalyst, which shows a great increase at 260 °C. Besides, the temperature has little effect on the CH4 selectivity as shown in Fig. 4b. The catalyst with excellent CO2 conversion also exhibits high CH4 selectivity. The large Ni particles contribute to the formation of CO. Moreover, CO from the reverse water gas shift reaction is the only by-product in this work. The durability of catalysts prepared by the COM-IMP method was further investigated. The results are shown in Fig. 4c and d. All catalysts display a relatively stable performance at 300 °C. The best catalytic performance is achieved on the Ni-5Ce catalyst, which exhibits excellent performance even at 250 °C. The Ni-0Ce catalyst without CeO2 shows the lowest CO2 conversion (35%) and CH4 selectivity (85%). The catalyst performance is also compared with the reported works shown in Table 2 . Undoubtedly, the Ni-5Ce catalyst prepared by the facile COM-IMP method with low-cost materials in this work shows comparable low-temperature activity to other reported materials. The XRD, TGA, and BET results of the spent catalysts after a 50-h reaction are shown in Fig. 5 and Table 3 . There is no noticeable change in Ni diffraction peaks for all catalysts in Fig. 5a. The Ni crystal size is almost the same as the reduced catalysts, suggesting that there is no apparent Ni agglomeration after a 50-h reaction. This result agrees well with the steady catalytic performance displayed in Fig. 4. The amount of carbon deposits was determined by TGA and shown in Fig. 5b. The weight loss below 200 °C can be ascribed to the desorption of H2O and other gases, while the oxidation of metallic nickel causes the slight increase at 200–350 °C. The further weight loss at higher temperatures is caused by the elimination of coke species. Less than 1% of carbon deposition is formed for all catalysts, indicating the outstanding anti-coking ability. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the spent catalysts indicate the well-maintained mesoporous structure of catalysts. The slight changes in the textural properties of catalysts may be caused by the little carbon deposition. The adsorption ability of catalysts for H2 and CO2 was studied by H2-TPD and CO2-TPD, and the results are presented in Fig. 6a and b. Hydrogen can be adsorbed on the metal Ni sites and the subsurface layer. The desorption peak below 300 °C is attributed to the hydrogen adsorbed on metallic Ni, whereas the hydrogen located in the subsurface layers needs a high desorption temperature. The spillover hydrogen also leads to a high desorption temperature [40]. In this work, the Ni-0Ce catalyst exhibits a larger H2 adsorption capacity than the Ni-0Ce-IMP catalyst, as a result of the small Ni particles as well as the larger subsurface layer. Moreover, it can be found that the H2 desorption amount increases with CeO2 content. As the Ni crystal size of these catalysts is similar, the difference in H2 adsorption capacity is likely caused by CeO2. Reimers et al. found by DFT simulations that the H2 molecule can be slightly physisorbed onto the CeO2 surface, and the strongest interaction can occur at the oxygen vacancies on CeO2(111) [41]. Werner et al. also confirmed that oxygen vacancies on CeO2(111) are crucial for its interaction with H2 by both experimental and theoretical results [42]. The oxygen vacancies on CeO2 are confirmed by XPS in this work. As shown in Fig. 6c, with the increase in CeO2 content, the surface Ce content increases, while the lowest peak for the Ni-5Ce-IMP catalyst may be due to the poor dispersion of CeO2 as a result of the IMP method. The u, u”, and u”’ peaks in Fig. 6d correspond to Ce4+, whereas the u0 and u’ peaks located at 899.9 and 903.6 eV indicate Ce3+, suggesting the oxygen vacancies [43]. Hence, it can be speculated that with the increase in CeO2 content, more oxygen vacancies are generated, which endows the catalyst with more H2 adsorption sites. The CO2-TPD results are shown in Fig. 6b. At least three CO2 desorption peaks with different intensities are distributed in the temperature range of 100–600 °C [44]. These peaks can be classified into weak, moderate, and strong basic sites, corresponding to the desorption temperature at 100–200 °C, 200–400 °C, and 500–600 °C [32]. The weak basic site has been proved crucial for the CO2 methanation reaction at low temperatures [45]. In this work, the Ni-0Ce catalyst shows an obviously increased CO2 adsorption capacity than the Ni-0Ce-IMP and Ni-5Ce-IMP catalysts. It is reported that CO2 can adsorb on the support or on the sites located close to the metal-support interface region with formation of suitable carbonate, hydroxycarbonate or carbonyl groups, which are then transformed to methane with hydrogen on Ni crystallite [46]. As SiO2 is inert, CO2 can adsorb on the metal-support interface on the Ni-0Ce catalyst. The small Ni particles facilitate the generation of more metal-support interface, contributing to the CO2 adsorption and activation. Besides, the catalysts with CeO2 promoter show larger CO2 adsorption capacity at low temperatures (200 °C). Moreover, the CO2 adsorption capacity increases with CeO2 content, which can be explained by the oxygen vacancies. The oxygen vacancies can lead to bent CO2 adsorbates [47], which can react with the OH group to form bicarbonate species [48]. Bian et al. also believed that the oxygen vacancy was the active site for formate formation [49]. Therefore, the increased CO2 adsorption capacity can be attributed to the larger metal-support interface and the oxygen vacancies. Although the largest H2 and CO2 adsorption capacities are obtained on the Ni-7.5Ce catalyst, the best catalytic performance is achieved on the Ni-5Ce catalyst. As is well known, metallic Ni provides the active sites for H2 dissociation, while the oxygen vacancies located at the Ni-O-Ce interface promote the CO2 activation [50]. A proper ratio between both types of sites is necessary to achieve a high catalytic activity as reported [5]. In this work, excess CeO2 (Ni-7.5Ce) may lower the Ni reducibility as a result of the high oxygen mobility throughout the ceria lattice [51], which is confirmed by the H2-TPR result. CeO2 is an effective promoter to enhance the low-temperature activity of Ni-based catalysts. It is reported that H2 can also dissociate on CeO2 by homolytic cleavage at two surface lattice oxygen atoms of oxides, forming two lattice -OH groups. Moreover, two surface metal ions can be reduced concomitantly (H2 + 2M2x+ + 2O2− → 2O2−H++2 M(2x-1)+) [52]. In other words, Ce4+ can be reduced to Ce3+ (250–500 °C [53]) during the reduction process, generating oxygen vacancies which are located at the Ni-O-Ce interface between Ni and CeO2 [54]. The oxygen vacancies can enhance the CO2 dissociative adsorption through easier redox interconversions [10]. Moreover, the larger H2 adsorption capacity results in the increased H2 coverage of the catalyst, facilitating the generation of formate species as intermediate in CH4 production [55]. In this work, a series of Ni/CeO2/SiO2 catalysts for low-temperature CO2 methanation was synthesized via a facile and effective impregnation-combustion method. As a result of the “combustion reaction” during the calcination process, highly dispersed Ni particles with an average particle size of about 6 nm are obtained. The increased Ni active sites determining the H2 dissociation result in enhanced low-temperature catalytic performance. When CeO2 is introduced to the Ni/SiO2 catalyst, the reducibility of Ni species is improved as a consequence of the Ni-O-Ce interfaces which are easily reduced. Moreover, due to the redox properties of CeO2, oxygen vacancies can be formed and participate in the reaction process, which is conducive to CO2 activation. However, it is found that more CeO2 content shows the reverse effect, because it lowers the reducibility of Ni species, as confirmed by H2-TPR. With the synergistic effect of small Ni particles and optimal amount of CeO2 promoter, the best low-temperature activity of 63% CO2 conversion and 99% CH4 selectivity at 250 °C is obtained for the Ni-5Ce catalyst. In contrast, the catalysts prepared by the IMP method (Ni-0Ce-IMP and Ni-5Ce-IMP catalysts) show no catalytic activity at 250 °C. TheNi-0Ce catalyst with small Ni particles exhibits less than 10% CO2 conversion and 80% CH4 selectivity due to the absence of CeO2. In addition, the Ni-5Ce catalyst also displays good anti-sintering and anti-coking abilities. Therefore, the results in this work provide a new way to prepare efficient Ni-based catalysts for CO2 methanation via a facile method with simple procedures and cheap materials. Yan Xu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Hongri Wan: Methodology, Investigation. Xihua Du: Conceptualization, Methodology. Bing Yao: Validation, Funding acquisition. Shuai Wei: Validation, Funding acquisition. Yan Chen: Conceptualization, Validation. Wenchang Zhuang: Validation. Huamei Yang: Validation, Funding acquisition. Limei Sun: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Xumei Tao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Peng Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
abstract: CO2 methanation is of great significance to CO2 utilization. However, it is challenging to prepare Ni-based catalysts with superior low-temperature activity via a simple method. In this work, a highly active Ni/CeO2/SiO2 catalyst is developed by a combustion-impregnation method, and the synergistic effect of active sites for H2 and CO2 activation are studied. The research results show that highly dispersed Ni particles (6 nm) are obtained by this method with an instantaneous reaction. As a result of the increased number of Ni active sites, the catalyst exhibits improved catalytic activity. Moreover, addition of CeO2 promoter further improves the low-temperature activity. First, it enhances the catalyst reducibility, creating more Ni active sites for H2 dissociation. Second, the surface oxygen vacancies formed on CeO2 facilitate CO2 activation. Therefore, the best performance of 63% CO2 conversion and 99% CH4 selectivity at 250 °C is achieved on the Ni-5Ce catalyst with small Ni particles and optimal amount of CeO2 promoter. With higher CeO2 content, Ni-7.5Ce catalyst displays decreased catalyst reducibility and catalytic activity. Therefore, it can be concluded that a proper ratio between both types of sites is crucial for CO2 methanation.
title: Highly active Ni/CeO2/SiO2 catalyst for low-temperature CO2 methanation: Synergistic effect of small Ni particles and optimal amount of CeO2
