full_text: Data will be made available on request. The utilization of lignocellulose biomass for the production renewable fuels and chemicals is increasingly adopted as one of the effective solutions to the energy crisis and environmental pollution arisen from huge fossil resources consumption worldwide [1,2]. As the main components of lignocellulosic feedstock, cellulose and hemicellulose have long been investigated to produce a panel of useful chemicals, while lignin is less concerned until the past few years. Lignin has a high energy density and low oxygen content as compared to cellulose and hemicellulose, which is thus an appealing material to produce hydrocarbon products, in particular the aromatics [3–5]. By looking into the chemical structures of lignin, phenylpropane units are randomly connected by CC bonds (e.g. β-1, β-5 and 5–5 linkages) and CO bonds (e.g. α-O-4, β-O-4 and 4-O-5 linkages) to form a three-dimensional polymer structure, with the latter taking the predominant position [6,7]. Thus, selective cleavage of the CO bonds is the prime way leading to the production of lignin based value-added products. To depolymerize lignin into the monomers, several inspiring technologies have been developed, including pyrolysis, oxidation, hydrogenolysis and hydrothermal liquefaction [8], by which various aromatic products with diverse functionalities can be attained, accordingly. Among these technologies, hydrogenolysis of lignin is the most reported one to produce high yields of value-added monophenolic compounds (e.g. phenols, guaiacol) [9]. Molecular hydrogen is generally employed as the hydrogen source for this reaction, featuring good atom-economy and adjustable pressure [10]. However, potential risks are still concerned in the storage, transportation, and usage of high-pressure H2 [11]. Catalytic transfer hydrogenation (CTH) is then proposed as an alternative protocol by sacrificing the H atoms from hydrogen source for hydrogenation, which not only greatly reduce requirements for the equipment, but also allow the reaction to proceed in a more controlled manner [12]. Representative examples are formic acid, alcohols, alkanes, ammonia borane and hydrazine [13,14], among which alcohols are the most frequently reported ones owning to their wide availability, cost effectiveness and environmental friendliness. The CTH of lignin using alcohols has been extensively investigated in literatures. Both primary and secondary alcohols can serve as the hydrogen donor for the conversion. For example, Xiao et al. [15] reported the use of supercritical methanol for the depolymerization of a lignin model compound 2-(2-methoxyphenoxy)-1-phenyl ethanol at 300 °C in 4 h in the presence of Cu/CuMgAlOx catalysts, offering 100% yield of ethylbenzene and 96% yield of guaiacol at a complete conversion of substrate. Hossain A et al. [16] reported that a Ru/C catalyst was active for the CO bond cleavage in 2-phenoxy-1-phenyl ethanol with ethanol as a hydrogen source at 280 °C in 4 h, offering 27% and 36% yields of ethylbenzene and phenol, respectively. It's worth noting that harsh reaction conditions are usually required for CTH reaction when using primary alcohols, which is due to the high activation barrier of deprotonation of the CH of primary alcohol [17]. In contrast, secondary alcohols are more readily to sacrifice the Cβ-H at mild condition, serving as a more reactive hydrogen donor for CTH reaction. A series of supported metal catalysts based on Ni, Ru, Pd, and Pt [18] have been successfully applied to the CTH depolymerization of lignin and its dimers with α-O-4, β-O-4 and 4-O-5 linkages, significantly advancing lignin conversions technology. Albeit that, more efficient and eco-friendly catalytic systems are still highly desired to push the lignin conversion forward. Skeletal metals are a class of metal catalysts prepared by etching of metal alloys [19]. Unlike supported metal catalysts, this type of catalysts are self-supported, and possess a large number of low-coordinated active metal surface and rich pores within the structures. Raney Ni is such a type of catalysts prepared from NiAl alloy in alkali solutions, which exhibits high reactivity in catalysis applications [20]. For instance, Rinaldi et al. [21] reported the use of Raney Ni for the upgrading of lignin based model phenols, dimers and bio-oil to cyclohexanols/arenes at mild reaction conditions. The remarkable performances in the CTH reactions had demonstrated the great potential of skeletal catalyst for lignin upgrading. They later subjected the catalyst to the depolymerisation of real organosolv lignin (poplar pellets), and selectively achieved aromatics or saturated alcohols under different reaction conditions [22]. Unfortunately, harsh reaction conditions (>250 °C, 70 bar H2) were indispensable for the efficient depolymerization. Apart from that, research efforts were predominantly devoted to Raney Ni catalyst for lignin conversions, leaving the other potential skeletal metal candidates largely unexplored. Considering the unique catalytic properties resulted from the metal types, we herein reported the use skeletal CuZnAl catalysts for the CTH of lignin, with Cu serving as the key metal for catalysis process. Cu catalysts have long been reported with high reactivities in the CTH reactions for biomass utilization using alcohols as hydrogen donors [23] and are also capable of catalysing the CO bond cleavage of lignin [15]. Thus, a series of catalysts prepared under different conditions were characterized and compared in the reactions. Three typical lignin-model dimers were subjected to the 2-PrOH dependent CTH reaction systems over skeletal CuZnAl, offering good monomer yields and selectivities under mild reaction conditions. The reaction parameters, influence of water on the reaction efficiency and product distribution were systematically investigated. Finally, the conversion of organic-solvent lignin was also successfully demonstrated in the paper. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of using skeletal CuZnAl catalyst for the depolymerization of lignin to the value-added monomers, paving a way toward the sustainable lignin utilization. Devarda's alloy (99%), NaOH (99%), Cu powder (99%), Cu2O powder (97%), CuO powder (99%), Raney Ni (99%), Phenol (≥99.5%), cyclohexanol (≥98.5%), ethylbenzene (>99%), benzene (99%), guaiacol (>99%), 2,6-dimethylphenol (99%), 1,4-cyclohexanediol (99%), resorcinol (>99%), p-xylene (99%), 4-ethylanisole (98%) and naphthalene (>99%) were purchased from the Macklin chemical company (China). Isopropanol (99%, 2-PrOH) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (China). CuAl alloy was purchased from Yixing Chenfeng alloy Material Co, Ltd. The lignin model dimers were either purchased from the chemical companies or synthesized in the lab, and the synthetic procedures were supplied in the supporting material. All reagents used in this study were analytical grade and used directly without purification. The skeletal CuZnAl catalysts were prepared according to the reported procedures with slight modification [24]. Typically, a 6.25 M NaOH solution (5 g NaOH in 20 mL H2O) was prepared and placed in an ice-water to maintain the temperature around 5–10 °C. Then, the Devarda's Alloy (CuZnAl alloy, 5 g) was gradually added to the solution over 20 min under vigorous magnetic stirring. The etching process released a lot of H2, which was evacuated immediately. After the desired etching time, the powder was filtered, and washed with deionized water until the filtrate was neutral. The final catalyst was either stored in deionized water or further washed by the organic solvent and stored in it. The as-prepared skeletal catalysts were named as CuZnAl-x (0.5, 1.5, 2.5), where x referred to the etching time in hours. The equations between metals in catalyst and sodium hydroxide were listed below: Al + H 2 O + NaOH → Na Al OH 4 + H 2 ↑ Zn + H 2 O + NaOH → Na Zn OH 4 + H 2 ↑ The CuAl catalysts were prepared from the CuAl alloy using the same method as that for CuZnAl-x catalyst. The CuZnAlOx catalysts were synthesized via the traditional coprecipitation method by using Na2CO3 as precipitator. A certain amounts of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, and Al(NO3)3·6H2O were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water filled in a three-neck flask to make a total metal ion concentration of 2 mol/L. An aqueous solution mixture of sodium carbonate (2 mol/L) was then added dropwise at 60 °C under vigorous stirring until the pH value reached 9.0. The resulting precipitate was aged in the mother liquor at the same temperature overnight to form the hydrotalcite-like compound (HTLcs). The product was washed with deionized water at room temperature and dried at 100 °C overnight. The CuZnAl oxide catalysts were then obtained by thermal decomposition of the HTLcs precursor in air at 600 °C at a rate of 2 °C/min for 3 h. Then, the CuZnAl oxide catalysts were reduced by 20 vol% H2 in N2 at 280 °C for 3 h [25]. Power X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the catalysts were performed using a Bruker D8 advance X-ray power diffractometer using Ni filtered Cu Kα radiation source, the scanning speed was 10o/min with a scan range of 10-80o at 30 kV and 15 mA. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was studied using a HitachiSU8010 field emission scanning electron microscopy, coupled with an Energy Dispersive spectrometer (EDS). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected using a JEM-2100F electron microscope. The specific surface area of catalyst was measured from N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms by using automated gas sorption analyzer (ASAP2460) with BET (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller) equation. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) was examined using an ESCALAB-250 spectrometer. The element composition of alloy was analysed by Inductively Coupled Plasma Optical Emission Spectrometer ICP-OES 730. HSQC NMR experiments were carried out on a Bruker AVANCE 600 spectrometer. The lignin dimer model compound 2-Phenoxy-1-phenyl ethanol (α-OH-PPE, 0.25 mmol), 2-PrOH (2 mL) and CuZnAl catalyst (100 mg) were charged into a 25 mL pressure-tube under N2. The reaction was conducted at 130 °C, 800 rpm for 12 h. After the reaction, the reactor was cooled to room temperature and the mixture was filtered to remove the catalyst. The filtrate was analysed using a gas chromatograph employing naphthalene as an internal standard. Yields were defined as a total molar basis, and the defined equations for the conversion of α-OH-PPE and the yields of corresponding products were listed as followed:. (1) Conversion % = n r / n t × 100 % (2) Yield of product % = n p / n t × 100 % Where n r is the mole of reacted substrate; n p is the mole of product; n t is the total mole of substrate feed. The catalytic conversion of birch organsolv lignin was perform in a 30 mL pressure-tube with N2 atmosphere. Typically, the reactor was charged with 500 mg catalyst (wet weight), 200 mg of dried lignin, and 4 mL 2-PrOH. The hydrogenolysis reaction was carried out at 800 rpm and heated to 160 °C for 24 h. After reaction, the reactor was cooled to room temperature and the mixture was filtered to remove catalyst. The organic products were extracted with ethyl acetate and analysed by GC–MS and GC. The yield of the aromatic monomers (YAM) was calculated using the following equation: (3) YAM wt % = W A / W F × 100 % (4) Y Bio − oil wt % = W Bio − oil / W F × 100 % Where W A is the weight of aromatic monomers after the hydrogenolysis of lignin; W F is the weight of feed lignin; Y Bio−oil is the yield of bio-oil; W Bio−oil is the weight of bio-oil. The morphological features of the as-prepared catalysts were firstly characterized by SEM technology. For the unetched CuZnAl alloy, it exhibited smooth surface from the SEM images (Fig. S1). After etching, the catalyst exhibited increased porous structures as the etching time extended from 0.5 h to 2.5 h (Fig. 1a–c), indicating that Al metal was gradually etched after reacting with NaOH. This phenomenon could also be observed in the TEM images, where more roughness surface and rich holes were gradually formed along with the etching time (Fig. 1d–h, Fig. S2). Typical crystalline lattice fringes with the lattice distances of 0.203 nm and 0.200 nm were found in the CuZnAl-1.5 catalyst (Fig. 1f–g), which were attributed to the Cu (111) and Cu2O (111) planes, respectively [26]. This result suggested both Cu0 and oxidized Cu species were co-existed on the catalyst surface. The oxidized Cu species was probably generated from the oxidation of Cu0 during catalyst preparation conducted under air. EDX elemental mapping of representative CuZnAl-1.5 catalyst demonstrated the presence of homogeneously dispersed Cu, Zn and Al elements (Fig. 1i–m). The oxygen element was also captured, which was originated from the metal oxides or the etched Al species. To further gain insight into the chemical compositions of the catalysts, XRD characterization of CuZnAl-x (0.5, 1.5, 2.5) catalysts were conducted. As shown in Fig. 2a, there were several sharp diffraction peaks of the Devarda's Alloy without etching, which could be ascribed to the CuAl2 alloy species (Zn was only 5 wt% of the total alloy) [24]. After etching, new diffraction peaks at 2θ = 43.3o, 50.4o and 74.1o were clearly observed and strengthened as the etching time prolonged, while the peaks belonged to CuAl2 alloy decreased. These peaks were attributed to the (111), (200), and (220) planes of Cu0 species [24], respectively, suggesting that more Cu0 sites were exposed as the Al metal was gradually etched. Whereas, no oxidized Cu species were detected, possibly due to their amorphous states or lower resolution of the test machine. N2 adsorption-desorption characterizations exhibited typical type IV isotherms for all the three catalysts (Fig. 2b), which suggested that mesoporous pores were created during the etching process. This would benefit the mass transfer of the reactants from the reaction solution to the active sites on catalyst surface. XPS characterization of the CuZnAl-1.5 catalyst further disclosed the chemical states of the metals in the catalyst (i.e. Cu, Zn and Al). As shown in Fig. 3a, the Cu 2p3/2 peak could be deconvoluted into two peaks with the binding energies (BE) of 934 and 932.3 eV, which were ascribed to the Cu2+ and Cu0/Cu+ species, respectively [27]. The Cu0/Cu+ content decreased from 83% to 53% for the catalyst etched with a longer time, while that of Cu2+ increased from 17% to 47%. These results suggested that Cu metal was gradually oxidized during the preparation process as the etching time extended. The deconvoluted peaks of Al 2p at 72.8, 74.3, 75.1, and 76.1 eV (Fig. 3b) were respectively assigned to Al, Al(OH)3, AlOOH, and Al2O3 species [28]. These oxygenated Al species generated from the reaction between Al metal and the alkali solution added a lot of acid-base sites to the skeletal catalysts, which may bring distinctive catalytic properties as compared to the sole metal. The contents of the Al species were relatively steady regardless of the etching time, which might be caused by the continuous dissolution of Al species in solution as the etching proceeded, always leaving the newly etched Al surface. Finally, the Zn 2p spectrum of CuZnAl presented the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 peaks at 1021.6 and 1044.7 eV (Fig. 3c), respectively, characterized by a fixed energy gap 23.1 eV [29]. This result indicated the metallic form of Zn in the as-prepared catalysts. On the basis of the above results, it can be induced that Cu0 was the main species of the CuZnAl-x catalysts with varied oxidized Cu species upon the etching time used, Al maintained a relatively constant Al oxygenated surface while Zn was still in its metallic form after the etching process. Table 1 summarized the metal compositions and the textural properties of the unetched catalyst and the as-prepared catalyst of different etching times. The unetched CuZnAl alloy possessed negligible BET surface area and pore volume (Table 1). The pore size was probably originated from the surface of the alloy. The original composition of metals in CuZnAl was 50%, 5% and 45% for Cu, Zn and Al, respectively. Along with the increased etching time, the content of Cu significantly increased, reaching up to 77.9 wt% for CuZnAl-2.5, while that of Al decreased. The change of Zn content was probably caused by the leaching of Zn during the catalyst preparation. All the three CuZnAl-x (0.5, 1.5, 2.5) catalysts possessed comparable BET surface areas, with CuZnAl-1.5 having the largest pore volume of 0.13 cm3/g and average pore diameter of 14.38 nm. The etching process gradually dissolved Al metal and generated pores from surface to inside of CuZnAl alloy, however, the over-etching might cause the collapse of the catalyst structure, as revealed by the decreases in pore size and volume of the catalyst etched for 2.5 h. These results suggested the etching time affected the textural properties of the catalysts, which might in turn alter their catalytic performances. The CTH of lignin model dimer α-OH-PPE was firstly studied in the presence of 2-PrOH solvent over different catalysts at a mild condition of 130 °C. As shown in Table 2 , the unetched CuZnAl alloy showed no reactivity toward the CTH reaction (Table 2, entry 1), indicating that there were no active Cu sites on the alloy surface. By using the etched catalyst CuZnAl-0.5 under identical reaction condition, the reaction proceeded and offered an impressive ethylbenzene yield of 88%, cyclohexanol yield of 52% and phenol yield of 36% (Table 2, entry 2), indicative of the active metal sites formation after the alloy was etched. The ethylbenzene and cyclohexanol were originated from the deep hydrogenation of the depolymerized products, and at the same time, acetone was formed as the side product from 2-PrOH dehydrogenation. By increasing the etching time to 1.5 h, the reaction with CuZnAl-1.5 catalyst achieved the highest yields of depolymerized products, i.e. 97% ethylbenzene and 96% cyclohexanol (Table 2, entry 3). However, gradual decreases in the product yields were detected when using the catalysts etched with longer time (CuZnAl-2.5 and CuZnAl-3.5) (Table 2, entries 4–5). According to our previous work on the CuZnAl catalysts [30] and the above characterization results, the increasing oxidized Cu surface was largely responsible for the decreased activities of the catalysts. To verify this hypothesis, the effects of Cu species on the reactions were investigated. Commercial Cu, Cu2O and CuO powders were respectively subjected to the model reactions. Unfortunately, the reactions failed to proceed to yield the target products for all these three catalysts (Table 2, entries 6–8). The lower reactivity might be caused by the inert metal oxide surface of these powders, which was easily formed during the preparation and storage stages and inhibited the exposure of the active sites. This was verified by the XPS characterization of the Cu powder (Fig. S3). Alternatively, we subjected the optimized CuZnAl-1.5 catalyst to air passivation (Table 2, entry 9), after which the oxidized catalyst only provided inferior catalytic performance in the model reaction. This result further confirmed the crucial role of the Cu0 surface in CTH reaction. Previous research had indicated the good reactivity of Ni metal in hydrogenating activity [31], however, by applying Raney Ni catalyst in our reaction, only moderate product yields were obtained (ethylbenzene yield of 88% and cyclohexanol yield of 89%), indicative of the distinct reactivities of the skeletal CuZnAl catalyst and skeletal Ni catalysts (Table 2, entry 10). To further reveal the role of the counterpart metal Zn in the catalyst, we also prepared the CuAl-1.5 catalyst using the same preparation method and subjected to the reaction. However, only 59% yield of 1-phenylethanol and 69% yield of phenol were obtained (Table 2, entry 11). The phenol was not hydrogenated in the reaction system, suggesting the lower hydrogenation ability of CuAl-1.5 catalyst. This result also implied that Zn metal might participate in the reaction to contribute to the high reactivity of the catalyst, which will be discussed later. Finally, the reaction couldn't proceed without the catalyst, suggesting that the catalyst was indispensable for the hydrogenation reaction (Table 2, entry 13). With the optimal condition in hand, we then turned to investigate the catalytic performances of the CuZnAl-1.5 with varied reaction conditions. The reaction temperature was primarily studied as it remarkably affected the reaction efficiency and product distribution (Fig. 4a). When the reaction temperature was 100 °C, the CTH of α-OH-PPE could achieve a full conversion, but generating considerable amounts of the intermediates or, such as 2-phenylethyl phenyl ether (PPE), phenol, acetophenone, and 1-phenylethanol. By increasing the temperature from 100 °C to 130 °C, all the intermediates were gradually converted and the target products (ethylbenzene and cyclohexanol) reached the highest yields of 97% and 96%, respectively, and remained almost unchanged thereafter. The carbon balance at 130 °C was slightly higher than that at 140 °C. These results showed that the elevated reaction temperature was favoured to promote the deep hydrogenation of the reaction intermediates. In consideration of carbon balance and energy consumption, the reaction temperature was set at 130 °C for further investigations. The influence of catalyst loading on the reaction was then investigated by varying the CuZnAl-1.5 loading from 20 mg to 140 mg while the other parameters remained unchanged (Fig. 4b). Since the catalyst was stored in water to prevent air oxidation, a wet weight was used to normalize the catalyst usage (Table S2). The actual weight of dry catalyst was calculated to account for 55–60% of the wet weight, and the active Cu dispersion of the CuZnAl-1.5 was 66.1% based on the dissociative adsorption of N2O characterization (Table S3), according to which the actual Cu sites was calculated to be 28% of the total wet catalyst. Lower catalyst loadings led to incomplete substrate conversions and the intermediate phenol were not efficiently hydrogenated. As more catalyst was employed, both the reaction conversion and intermediate hydrogenation were promoted until reaching the highest level at 100 mg of catalyst loading. Noteworthy was that excessive catalyst loading didn't affect the reaction too much, which suggested that the products (ethylbenzene and cyclohexanol) were relatively stable under the hydrogenation conditions. Thus, a minimum usage of 100 mg was sufficient to achieve a good reaction efficiency. To figure out the reaction pathway, the reaction evolution was traced using the CuZnAl-1.5 catalyst (Fig. 4c). The substrate α-OH-PPE was quickly converted in the first 2 h of reaction time, accompanying with the massive production of 1-phenylethanol and phenol. This result indicated that the β-O-4 linkage of α-OH-PPE was easily cleaved as the reaction started. During this stage, PPE and acetophenone were also gradually formed, but in small amounts. As the reaction proceeded, all the four compounds (1-phenylethanol, phenol, PPE, and acetophenone) were gradually converted in different rates, offering the steady production of ethylbenzene and cyclohexanol as the final products. On the basis of that, all the above four compounds were the possible intermediates of the conversion. A clear reaction network can be envisioned that the reaction mainly proceeded via the β-O-4 bond cleavage to generate 1-phenylethanol and phenol. 1-Phenylethanol was either dehydrogenated into acetophenone or directly hydrogenolyzed to ethylbenzene, and meanwhile, phenol was hydrogenated into cyclohexanol. The acetophenone could be reversely hydrogenated into 1-phenylethanol and subsequently converted to ethylbenzene as well. A secondary route might also be anticipated that α-OH-PPE was directly hydrodeoxygenated into PPE, whose β-O-4 linkage was then cleaved to generate ethylbenzene and phenol, and finally hydrogenated to yield the same products as the main route. Finally, the influence of sacrificing alcohol on the reaction performance was also studied. A series of alcohols with varied carbon chains were compared in the model reaction, including the primary alcohols (e.g. methanol, ethanol, 1-propanol) and the secondary alcohols (e.g. 2-propanol, and 2-butanol) (Fig. 4d). The catalytic performances with secondary alcohols were far better than those with primary alcohols. This was in line with the previous work that secondary alcohols were more readily to be dissociated to transfer their hybrids for hydrogenation. Among the secondary alcohol tested, 2-PrOH exhibited the highest reaction reactivity than other tested alcohols, which had also been well acknowledged in the reported literatures. Since the skeletal CuZnAl catalysts were stored in water to prevent them from air oxidation, the CTH reactions using these catalysts inevitably brought a certain amount of water into the reaction system. To disclose whether the water affected the reaction efficiency or product distribution, a set of control experiments using the catalyst stored in alcohols, as well as the verifying assays, were symmetrically designed and carried out. At the beginning, three CuZnAl-1.5 catalyst samples were prepared and stored in water, EtOH and 2-PrOH, respectively. After subjecting them to the model reaction in 2-PrOH with a short reaction time of 0.5 h, distinctive α-OH-PPE conversions were obtained from the reactions with the catalysts following the order of CuZnAl-1.5(H2O) (61%) > CuZnAl-1.5(2-PrOH) (57%) > CuZnAl-1.5(EtOH) (45%) (Fig. 5a). This result was unexpected as a small amount of water seemed to significantly promote the reaction. On the other side, the negative impact of EtOH on the reaction was predicted, since EtOH might competitively adsorb on the catalyst surface with 2-PrOH and suppressed the dissociation of H from 2-PrOH. To figure out the role of water, extra water (100 μL) was respectively employed to the reactions with CuZnAl-1.5(2-PrOH) and CuZnAl-1.5(EtOH) catalysts. An obvious enhancement in α-OH-PPE conversion was observed for the reaction with CuZnAl-1.5(2-PrOH) (from 57% to 78%), while that remained unchanged with CuZnAl-1.5(EtOH). These results further confirmed that the water addition had a promotion on the α-OH-PPE conversion in sole 2-PrOH solvent. In spite of that, the similar product selectivities of the reactions made it difficult to distinguish the influence of water on the reaction network, thus motivating a further inspection on water effect. The lignin dimers (α-OH-PPE, PPE) and intermediates (1-phenylethanol and phenol) were respectively subjected to the reaction employing different amounts of water over the CuZnAl-1.5(2-PrOH) catalyst. For both lignin dimers, steadily increasing trends in reaction conversions were noticed when the water amount was increased from 0 to 60 μL (Fig. 5b–c). However, gradual decreases in reaction conversion were also detected for the reactions using the intermediates when more water was employed (Fig. 5d–e). The two opposite trends clearly delivered a message that the water addition had a positive effect on lignin dimer conversion, but a negative effect on the depolymerized product/intermediate conversion. To get insight into the underlying mechanism for the above phenomena, we designed two sets of experiments to explore the role of water. According to the reported literatures, water could serve as the acidic species by releasing the H+ under thermal reaction condition [32], which was exactly a key promoter to the CO bond cleavage. To test this hypothesis, we used p-toluenesulfonic acid in place of water to the α-OH-PPE conversion reaction, and were delight to obtain a growth trend of the conversion along with the acid used (Fig. 5f) [33]. This result indicated that the water employed in the lignin dimer conversions might act as the role of acid, which provided the protons to facilitate the CO bond cleavage. On the other side, the addition of water negatively affected the CTH conversion of intermediates (as mentioned above), which was probably caused by the decreased reactivity of the catalyst in the presence of water. To verify that, we carried out the CTH of simple styrene over CuZnAl-1.5 (2-PrOH) catalyst with and without water (Fig. S4). Results clearly showed that the addition of water suppressed the catalytic reactivity of CuZnAl-1.5 catalysts, and further validated our hypothesis. On the basis of the above results, it can be concluded that water could suppress the reactivity of CuZnAl catalyst in the CTH reaction, thus leading to the inferior catalytic performance in intermediate conversions. Nevertheless, it was capable of promoting the CO bond cleavage of the lignin dimers by acting as the acid source. The promoted effect obtained in the lignin dimer conversions was an integrated result of the promotion effect from CO cleavage and the suppression effect of reduced reactivity of the catalyst, as a result of water introduction. Based on the previous experiment results, several possible reaction routes were proposed accordingly, which can be summarized as follows. 1) α-OH-PPE was hydrogenolyzed by breaking the β-O-4 linkage to produce 1-phenylethanol and phenol, which were further hydrodeoxygenated and hydrogenated to ethylbenzene and cyclohexanol, respectively. 2) α-OH-PPE underwent the hydrodeoxygenation (HDO) reaction to yield PPE, which was then broken the CO bond to yield ethylbenzene and phenol, and further hydrogenated to cyclohexanol. 3) α-OH-PPE was dehydrogenated into 2-phenoxy-1-phenylethanone (α-CO-PPE), which was then converted to acetophenone and phenol through CO bond cleavage, and finally converted to ethylbenzene and cyclohexanol under hydrogenation reaction condition. The former two routes had been confirmed by the trace of the reaction as revealed in Fig. 4c. However, we cannot exclude the third one as acetophenone was also detected in the reaction system. Thus, we next turned to the controlled experiments to provide more information on the reaction routes. By subjecting each two of the three substrates (α-OH-PPE, PPE and α-CO-PPE) in equivalent mole loading to the model reaction with a short reaction time of 1 h, three sets of competitive reactions were conducted to provide insights into the reactivities of the substrates in terms of reaction conversions and product distributions. For the reaction with α-OH-PPE + PPE substrates (Eq. (1)), a higher α-OH-PPE conversion of 52% was obtained as compared to that of 10% for PPE, which was in line with our previous reaction profile (Fig. 4c) that α-OH-PPE was more reactive toward the CO bond cleavage and further confirmed the promotion effect of the hydroxyl group. It's worth noting that a small amount of α-CO-PPE was detected (0.6% yield), which suggested that the dehydrogenation of α-OH-PPE did occur in the reaction, but in negligible level. For the reactions using α-OH-PPE + α-CO-PPE (Eq. (2)) and PPE + α-CO-PPE (Eq. (3)), both of them provided higher conversions of α-CO-PPE, indicative of its lower CO bond energy as compared to those in α-OH-PPE and PPE. An interesting phenomenon was noticed that considerable amount of α-CO-PPE was converted to α-OH-PPE (20% yield), which was reasonable as the ketone group was prone to be reduced in the CTH reaction system. By combining the results from these three equations, in combination with the aforementioned reaction profile, we may induce that direct CO bond cleavage of α-OH-PPE was the major reaction pathway, accompanied by its hydrodeoxygenation to PPE before further CO bond hydrogenolysis reaction as the minor pathway. Although α-CO-PPE was the most reactive substrate among the three to undergo CO bond cleavage, the dehydrogenation of α-OH-PPE to α-CO-PPE was thermodynamically unfavored in the presence of hydrogen donor (2-PrOH) and metal catalyst. On the basis of that, a schematic reaction pathway was proposed accordingly, as shown in Scheme 1b. With the optimal reaction conditions in hand, we then investigated the applicability of the catalytic method in the conversion of different lignin model compounds, including β-O-4, α-O-4, 4-O-5 ether linkages. As shown in Table 3 , dimers with β-O-4 ether bond were successfully converted to the corresponding aromatics under the optimized reaction condition with slight modification of reaction temperature. For 2-phenoxyacetophenone and PPE, an elevated reaction temperature of 150 °C was necessary to ensure the full substrate conversions and > 90% yields of ethylbenzene and cyclohexanol products (Table 3, entries 2–3). This result clearly demonstrated that the hydroxyl group on α position of CO ether bond had a promotion effect to facilitate the CO bond cleavage [15]. When the methoxyl group was presented on the phenoxyl unit of the dimer, the ether bond was also efficiently cleaved, but the phenolic product guaiacol couldn't be hydrogenated into the saturated alcoholic products (Table 3, entry 4). This was due to the chemically inert nature of guaiacol that unreadily to be hydrogenated under mild condition [34]. By increasing the reaction temperature to 160 °C, dimer 1e was effectively converted to the aromatics, among which a certain amount of phenol was obtained through the demethoxylation of guaiacol (Table 3, entry 5). For dimers 1f and 1 g with two ortho- methyl groups on phenoxyl unit, they were converted to the corresponding aromatics without over hydrogenating products (Table 3, entries 6–7). For α-O-4 dimers 1 h-1 k, they were all effectively cleaved at 150 °C to yield the corresponding aromatic or alcoholic products, demonstrating the good applicability of our method for this type of lignin ether linkage (Table 3, entries 8–11). Finally, the 4-O-5 dimer 1f was converted at 160 °C to produce high yields of benzene and cyclohexanol, while that of dimer 1 m offered the saturated alcohol products under the identical reaction (Table 3, entries 12–13). On the basis of that, our method had been demonstrated with good efficiency in the cleavage of the typical CO bonds in lignin under relatively mild condition. After the depolymerisation of the lignin model dimers, we next applied the catalytic method to the conversion of the organosolv lignin (BOSL) extracted from the birch wood powder [35]. The procedure was illustrated in Fig. 6a (detailed information was presented in supporting material). The depolymerization reaction was conducted in 2-PrOH solvent over the CuZnAl-1.5 catalyst at 160 °C for 24 h. After the reaction, the solvent was removed by vacuum distillation and a 75 wt% bio-oil was obtained. By subjecting to GC–MS analysis, a series of aromatic phenols were identified. The quantitative analysis of the aromatic compounds afforded a total of ~56.1 wt% known product yields, including arenes (benzene, toluene, 6.1 wt%), phenol and cyclohexanol (3.0 wt%), guaiacols (10.2 wt%), and the syringols (36.7 wt%) (Fig. 6b). The two-dimensional HSQC NMR was used to characterize the structure changes of the BOSL before and after the reaction over CuZnAl-1.5 catalyst (Fig. S5). The signals of β-aryl ether (β-O-4), phenylcoumaran (β-5) and resinol (β-β) were detected in BOSL, which significantly decreased after the reaction, suggesting that most of the linkages were cleaved during the hydrogenolysis reaction. This result further confirmed the successful depolymerization of lignin, but at the same time, revealing that some of the lignin polymer remained unconverted during the reaction, which might constitute the unknown products after the reaction [35]. These results clearly demonstrated the good applicability of the CuZnAl catalyst system in the depolymerization of real lignin. The result was also compared the reported literatures dealing with the depolymerization of real lignin using the CTH method and found that both the product yield and the reaction condition were very competitive among the most efficient systems (Fig. 7 , Table S1), making CuZnAl catalyst a potential candidate for industrial production. For industrial applications, the productivity is an important parameter of catalyst, which can be simply referred to catalyst lifetime. To unravel the recycling ability of the CuZnAl-1.5 catalyst, we recycled the catalyst from the batch reactor to investigate its recycling performance. Each time after the reaction, the catalyst was filtered and successively washed by 2-PrOH and water to remove the organic residues and water soluble impurities. The resulted solid was directly subjected to the model reaction under standard reaction condition. To monitor the changes of catalyst reactivity, the reactions were conducted with moderate conversions. As shown in Fig. 8 , the catalyst maintained stable activity in the first 3 recycling runs, offering comparable reaction conversions and product selectivities. However, the catalyst's reactivity gradually decreased in the 4th and 5th runs, which was probably caused by the deposition of reaction residues such as humin, coke and undepolymerized lignin. To regenerate the catalytic activity, the spent catalyst was further etched with NaOH solution for 10 min and its reactivity was recovered in the 6th run. Also, the reusability of catalyst was tested in real lignin conversion (Fig. S6), the catalyst also exhibited good recycling performances, further demonstrating the good stability of the catalyst. These results indicated the good stability of the skeletal CuZnAl-1.5 catalyst in the CTH reaction with 2-PrOH, holding great potential for practical use in industry. In summary, we have developed a facile method for the depolymerization lignin with the skeletal CuZnAl catalysts in 2-PrOH via the catalytic transfer hydrogenation method. The catalysts were conveniently synthesized from commercially available Devarda's alloy via the alkaline etching method, among which the CuZnAl-1.5 exhibited the highest reactivity in the mild conversion of lignin model dimer α-OH-PPE to ethylbenzene and cyclohexanol with up to 97% and 96% yields, respectively. A wide range of α-O-4, β-O-4 and 4-O-5 lignin model dimers were also successfully depolymerized into small molecules with high efficiencies. The depolymerization of organosolv lignin from birch wood over CuZnAl-1.5 achieved a 56.1 wt% yield of aromatic products, including the arenes, phenols, cyclohexanol, guaiacols and syringols, which was among the highest level reported using CTH systems for lignin conversion. The etching time was found to significantly affect the catalyst surface composition and structure, which in turn led to varied catalytic performances. The water addition had a promote effect on cleavage of lignin CO bonds, but slightly inhibited the catalyst reactivity in CTH reaction. Experimental results suggested that the reaction mainly proceed via the CO bond cleavage of the dimers assistant by the protonation from water, and the resulted intermediates were further hydrogenated/hydrodeoxygenated to yield the target products. The current work provides a new solution to the mild depolymerization of lignin to value-added products, which would significantly advance the lignin valorization toward industrial production. Yao-Bing Huang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ji-Long Zhang: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation, Visualization. Xuan Zhang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xue Luan: Investigation. Hao-Ze Chen: Investigation, Software. Bin Hu: Software. Li Zhao: Methodology. Yu-Long Wu: Investigation. Qiang Lu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.
abstract: Selective cleavage of the aromatic CO bonds in lignin is a crucial step for lignin valorization to produce value-added chemicals, however, the development of efficient catalysts for mild conversions is still challenging. Herein, non-precious skeletal CuZnAl catalysts were firstly reported for the depolymerisation of lignin dimers and real organosolv lignin via the catalytic transfer hydrogenation (CTH) method. Typical lignin dimers, including α-O-4, β-O-4 and 4-O-5 types, were all effectively converted to the corresponding aromatics and alcohols in isopropanol under mild reaction condition. Furthermore, birch organosolv lignin was also well depolymerized into monophenols in 56.1 wt% yield. Experiments revealed that the water brought by the wet catalyst effectively promoted the CO bond cleavage, but slightly decreased the catalyst reactivity toward hydrogenation in isopropanol. Reaction evolution profiles and control experiments suggested that three reaction pathways were included, with the CO ether bond cleavage and subsequent hydrogenolysis taking precedence over the dehydrogenation or hydrodeoxygenation routes. This work provides an economical and environmentally-friendly method for the selective cleavage of lignin and lignin model compounds into value-added chemicals, which holds great promise in industrial application.
title: Catalytic depolymerization of lignin via transfer hydrogenation strategy over skeletal CuZnAl catalyst

full_text: Coal is the most abundant fossil fuel in the world. However, the combustion of coal containing high sulfur causes serious environmental damage such as acid rain because of emission of sulfur dioxide [1]. Sulfur is present in coal mainly in three forms: pyritic, organic and sulfate sulfur. The organic sulfur is the integral part of the coal matrix, whereas pyritic sulfur is present in coal as mineral matter. There are several physical–chemical methods employed to eliminate the organic or inorganic sulfur fractions of coal by floatation, oxidation and reduction with chemicals [2]. Nevertheless, these physical methods cannot remove the pyrite when these sulfides are finely dispersed in coal matrix. Furthermore, although the chemical methods can remove organic and inorganic sulfur from coal, which will also produce hazardous secondary products and lead to loss of partial combustible matter [3,4]. Biodesulfurization offers a clean alternative method to remove sulfur from coals, in which the microbes can catalyze the biochemical reaction in an aqueous medium resulting in the oxidation and dissolving of the sulfur content into sulfate [4,5]. Among of them, the mesophillic microbes, mainly A. ferrooxidans and A. thiooxidans have been frequently applied to remove the pyrite from coals [4,6,7]. However, in the last decade, limited literatures reported that the thermophilic microbes were used to eliminate the sulfur from coal [8,9]. Recently, A. caldus has been reported as the dominant sulfur-oxidizing thermophilic bacterium accounting for the relatively high percentage in bioleaching pyrite at 45°C [10,11]. To our knowledge, it has never been used in coal's biodesulfurization process. It is well known that the attachment of cells on the surface of mineral is reported to be involved in the oxidation of sulfide minerals, which depends not only on the interfacial process between the cells and mineral surface but also the biochemical properties of the cells. Furthermore, it has been reported that the surface properties of bacterial cells are significantly influenced by the growth conditions [12–14]. Hence, a better understanding of the surface properties of functional bacteria under different conditions is helpful to illustrate the interfacial interaction between cells and mineral surface [15,16]. The bio-oxidation of pyrite has been widely investigated and a possible mechanism has been proposed: the stepwise oxidation and the formation of surface-bound thiosulfate, and then oxidized via tetrathionate and other polythionates, finally to sulfate [17–19]. Many investigators agreed that the precipitation of mineral phases during the pyrite oxidation process may affect the efficiency of sulfur removal [20,21]. Besides the reported jarosite, the sulfur-rich layers may also deposit onto the surface of pyrite, thus preventing the contact of the microorganisms and hindering the leaching process [21–23]. The object of this study is to investigate the coal's biodesulfurization and the coal pyrite's bioleaching by thermophilies A. caldus cells and the interfacial interaction between cells and coal pyrite surface with the approaches of zeta-potential, FT-IR spectroscopy, SEM and sulfur K-edge XANES. The coal sample used in the experiment was collected from Indonesia area named MTWL-7 and the total sulfur of it was 2.15%, which was sterilized at 120°C for 20min. The pyrite powders used in the experiments were picked up from the coal samples and the component was analyzed with XRD (data not shown). A. caldus derived from the acid hot spring of Yun-nan province, south east of China was cultivated in the Starkey basal salt medium [24] supplemented with sulfur powder, pyrite, coal and thiosualfate, and the initial pH of culture were 2.5, 2.5, 2.5 and 4.8, respectively. The culture of A. caldus was grown in 250mL flasks containing 100mL medium and incubated at 40°C with 170rpm shaking. Bacterial cells were cultured until late exponential phase and harvested by the following procedure: the cultures were filtered three times through filter paper (pore size 7–10μm) to remove the suspended solid particles and then the filtrates were centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 15min, and the cell pellets were washed thrice in diluted sulfuric acid (0.1mol.L−1) in order to remove any trapped ions, finally all the samples were freeze-dried and stored at −20°C for zeta-potentials and the FT-IR test. The biodesulfurization processes were carried out in flasks with a volume capacity of 100mL in 250mL of basic salt medium, 10% w/v pulp density, particle size of −65 Tyler mesh, the initial cell concentration was 1.0×106 cells.mL−1, processing time of 30d. Every four days, the pH values, and concentration of iron of biodesulfurization system were determined. At the end of the experiments, the coal samples were prepared with the method mentioned in the literature [25], then the total sulfur and the relative sulfur contents in the coal samples were measured [26]. For leaching pyrite, A. caldus cells were inoculated into 250mL flasks containing 100mL sterilized culture medium and 3g pyrite (the initial cell concentration was 1.0×106 cells.mL−1, the particle size of pyrite was −200 Tyler mesh), and were then incubated at 40°C with 170rpm shaking, processing time of 40d. Every four days, the pH values, and concentration of iron of bioleaching system were determined. On the other hand, a 10mL sample was taken from the flasks and centrifuged at 4000rpm for 10min. The solid fractions were prepared with the same process as in our previous report [21] and analyzed by FT-IR and SEM and XANES in order to establish mineralogical transformations of pyrite. Triplicate leaching experiments were performed under identical conditions. Parallel experiments (without cells; but the same mixing culture medium and pyrite) were prepared as sterile control. Cells' concentration grown on pyrite containing media was monitored by using a counting chamber; the total iron ions were measured with atomic absorption spectrometer (WFX-110) and the ferrous concentration was determined with the method of potassium dichromate titration. Total sulfur in coals was determined by using sulfur analyzer (CTS-3000). For the SEM observation, a 100μL culture solution (containing mineral powder) was fixed with formaldehyde (final conc. 2.5% V/V). The fixed samples were then dehydrated with ethanol, conductive coated, and then finally introduced into the chamber of the SEM (JEOL JSM-6360 LV) for mineral surface analysis. The zeta-potential measurements of cells were determined by a Zeta-Potential & Size Distribution Analyzer (Delsa 440SX) at a specified pH value. The cells' zeta-potential measurements were conducted on bacterial suspensions of 1.0×108 cells.mL−1 with ionic strength of 10−3 molL−1 KCl. FT-IR spectra of modified and original pyrite and prepared cells were obtained using a Fourier transform spectrometer (Nicolet 380, Thermo Nicolet) with diffuse reflectance attachment. X-ray absorption spectra were performed at the same conditions as in our previous studies [27]. All the data were calculated with the same program as in our previous report [27]. A series of typical sulfur compounds (zinc sulfate, thiosulfate, potassium tetrathionate, pyrite, jarosite and elemental sulfur) were selected as the sulfur
abstract: In the present work, the acidophilic and thermophilic strain Acidithiobacillus caldus was used for biodesulfurizing coal and bioleaching coal pyrite. The surface characteristics of cells and minerals and the mineral transformation during the coal pyrite leaching process were investigated combined with zeta-potential, FT-IR spectroscopy, scan electronic microscopy and X-ray absorption near edge structure spectroscopy (XANES). The results showed that the coal pyritic desulfurization with A. caldus was about 47% and the total desulfurization was 19%. After processed by cells, there was clear corrosion on the pyrite surface. Coal pyrite and elemental sulfur-grown cells had more hydrophilic functional groups than thiosulfate-grown cells. During the coal pyrite leaching course, the elemental sulfur was the main sulfur speciation but no other secondary mineral components were detected.
title: Biodesulfurization of coal with Acidithiobacillus caldus and analysis of the interfacial interaction between cells and pyrite

full_text: Data will be made available on request. Biomass has emerged as an essential feedstock for the production of energy, platform chemicals, and natural materials [1]. Accordingly, the use of biomass shows great promise for addressing global energy supply, combating climate warming, and developing local economies [2,3]. Furfural, a crucial biomass-derived platform molecule [4], can be used to synthesize chemicals such as furfuryl alcohol [5], 2-methyltetrahydrofuran [6], γ-valerolactone [7], and maleic acid [8] through hydrogenation, esterification and oxidation allowing it to play a key role in reducing global dependence on fossil fuel resources [9]. However, the conventional furfural-production processes are subject to issues such as equipment corrosion, catalyst-recovery problems, and extensive byproducts [10]. As a result, the development of low energy consumption and recyclable production processes has become a key goal for the sustainable development of the furfural industry. Zeolites are widely regarded as ideal catalysts for furfural production owing to their superior hydrothermal stability, excellent shape-selective catalytic properties, and solid acidity [11]. The utilization of zeolites to catalyze the transformation of xylose or lignocellulose into furfural has been widely studied and reported, but the furfural yields are generally lower than 55% [12], as shown in Table 1 . As the research progresses, the passageway of xylose conversion to furfural was revealed, divided into Lewis acid-catalyzed isomerization of xylose into xylulose and subsequent Brønsted acid-catalyzed dehydration of xylulose into furfural [13]. Based on this catalytic mechanism, some modified zeolite catalysts were investigated for the conversion of xylose to furfural, including sulfonated zeolites [14], ion-exchange zeolites [15], phosphorylated zeolites [16], and dealuminized zeolites [17]. Moreover, considering the differences between various zeolites in terms of selectivity for furfural, the selection of a suitable zeolite and a simple modification method has become more critical. Y zeolite is one of the most widely used catalysts in the petrochemical industry [24], and its well-developed pore network and high surface area allow xylose molecules to associate easily with the acidic sites on its surface. However, The Y zeolite has not been widely studied for the conversion of xylose to furfural, and the modified Y zeolite is also a worthy direction to explore. Suitable dealumination treatment of zeolites can impart it with a rich secondary pore structure and suitable acid center density. This increase in mesopores allows the product molecules to leave the inner surface of the Y zeolite rapidly, avoiding resinization and carbon accumulation, ultimately providing higher activity and selectivity [17,25]. Zeolites modified with metal ions can also show improved catalytic performances [26,27]. Certain metal ions can provide plenty of strong Lewis acid sites after loading the solid acid catalyst. Lewis acids can facilitate the conversion of xylose to xylulose intermediates, providing a foundation for the next step of Brønsted acid dehydration. Furthermore, zeolites modified by metal ions are more selective and economical in comparison with those modified by other methods [28]. Specifically, Cr3+ constitutes a good active-metal center and has been demonstrated as a particularly effective Lewis acid site for promoting furfural production from xylose [13,29]. The reaction systems are also extremely important for furfural production. Biphasic reaction systems consisting of water and organic solvents have proven to be desirable systems for furfural production [30–32]. Previous reports have shown that DCM/H2O, 2-MTHF/H2O, MIBK/H2O and CPME/H2O systems are superior to butanol/H2O systems in terms of phase separation and furfural yield (Table 2 ) [33]. Furthermore, extraction solvents such as toluene, MIBK, and tetrahydrofuran are commonly used for organic products selection and separation in biphasic systems, but they are all petroleum derivatives and exhibit some toxicity. However, n-butanol, which can be obtained directly by hydrolytic fermentation of biomass feedstocks, is more economical, environmentally benign, and non-toxic compared with the organic solvents above, and it is thus more appropriate for renewable biomass conversion and furfural production [34]. In this work, a simple and scalable process (dealumination and ion exchange) was used to obtain a modified HY zeolite, and its performance in the biphasic water/n-butanol system for the conversion of xylose to furfural was assessed. NaCl promoted phase separation and reduced the resinization of furfural. The modified catalyst Cr-deAl-Y increased the furfural yield by 25.6% over the pristine HY. Poplar and corncob hydrolysates were chosen as reaction substrates to evaluate the catalytic activity of Cr-deAl-Y in biomass xylose solutions, and 75.6% and 66.8% furfural yields are obtained, respectively. Furthermore, after the reaction was finished, n-butanol, NaCl, and the catalyst could be effectively recovered from the catalytic system. The recovered catalysts maintained excellent catalytic performance over five cycles. Sulfuric acid (≥98.0%, analytical reagent), n-butanol (≥99.5%, analytical reagent), NaCl (≥99.5%, analytical reagent), Oxalic acid (≥99.8%, analytical reagent) and CrCl3·6H2O (≥98.0%) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology (Shanghai, China). Xylose (≥99.0%) was acquired from Shandong Fu Tian Pharmaceutical (Shandong, China). HY zeolite (Product Number: 045866; SiO2:Al2O3 molar ratio 5.1:1; surface area 769 m2/g) was supplied by Alfa Aesar. Corncob and Poplar sawdust were obtained from a farm in Hebei Province, China. All commercial reagents were used as received without further purification. Dealumination and ion exchange methods were used to modify the HY zeolite as follows: Initially, 5 g of HY zeolite was added to 100 mL of aqueous sulfuric acid of a certain concentration, stirred in a water bath at 80 °C under standard atmospheric pressure for 2 h, filtered, and washed until the filtrate was pH neutral. The concentrations of sulfuric acid for dealumination were set to 0.05 M, 0.10 M, and 0.15 M. The obtained zeolites were dried at 110 °C for 2 h. Then, the most active dealuminized zeolite was transferred to 100 mL of CrCl3 solution and stirred for 4 h at room temperature. The concentrations of CrCl3 solution used for ion exchange were set to 5 mM, 10 mM, 20 mM, and 30 mM. After the reaction, the modified zeolite (Cr-deAl-Y) was filtered and washed with deionized water until the filtrate was free of chloride ions, and it was dried at 110 °C for 4 h. The metal contents of the samples were determined by ICP-OES analysis. The crystal structures of the powder samples were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD; Rigaku Ultima IV), scanned in steps of 2°/min in the range 5°–40°. The morphological characteristics of zeolites were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM; ZEISS MERLIN Compact, Zeiss, Germany). The micrographs of the pore structure were obtained at a transmission electron microscope (TEM; FEI TalosF200x). The BET (Brunauer-Emmett-Teller) surface areas and pore structures of the catalysts were evaluated by N2 adsorption-desorption analysis. Before analysis, samples were degassed at 200 °C under a vacuum for 8 h. Specific surface area (SBET) was determined using the BET model. Micropore volume and micropore area were calculated using the Harkins-Jura model. Mesopore widths were determined using the Saito-Foley model. The acidic sites of the HY zeolites before and after modification were identified by NH3-temperature-programmed desorption (NH3-TPD), using an AutoChem1 II 2920 instrument. Pyridine-adsorption infrared spectroscopy (Py-FTIR) analysis was conducted on a BRUKER-27 FT-IR instrument with a wavenumber accuracy of 0.01 cm−1 at 2000 cm−1. Corncob and poplar sawdust were ground to a 40–60 mesh size before being roasted until completely dry. In the reactor, 10 g of dried corncob (or poplar) and 200  mL of a 0.5% oxalic acid solution were added, uniformly mixed and heated to 150 °C for 30 min of reaction. After the reaction, the residue and xylose solution was separated by filtration. To fully hydrolyze the xylose-oligosaccharides, the hydrolysates were rehydrolyzed (treated with 4% sulfuric acid at 121 °C for 60 min). Then NaCl was added to the fully hydrolyzed solution until saturation and filtered again to give the target biomass xylose solution. The catalytic reaction was carried out in a 500 mL reactor at a stirring speed of 300 rpm. Xylose (1.0 g), water/n-butanol (50 mL/75 mL), and a certain amount of modified catalyst was charged to the reactor. Before the reaction, the reactor was purged with N2 for 5 min to expel air. The reactor was heated up at a rate of 4 °C/min, and the timing started when the reaction temperature was reached. After the catalysis was completed, the reaction was terminated by cooling the water. Furfural was determined using an XDB-C18 column with a column oven temperature of 30 °C, while xylose was determined using an Aminex HPX-87H column with a column oven temperature maintained at 60 °C. Acetonitrile/water (15/85 v/v, flow rate 1 mL/min) and H2SO4 solution (5 mmol/L, flow rate 0.6 mL/min) were used as the mobile phases for the XDB-C18 and Aminex HPX-87H columns, respectively. Furfural yield, furfural selectivity and xylose conversion were calculated as follows: Furfural yield = moles of furfural produced moles of starting xylose × 100 % Furfural selectivity = moles of furfural produced moles of xylose reacted × 100 % Xylose conversion = moles of xylose reacted moles of starting xylose × 100 % The target catalyst was obtained by a two-step modification. Dealumination is a typical method for zeolite modification, and the acid concentration affects the performance of the resultant modified zeolite. As shown in Fig. 1 , the dealuminized zeolite shows the highest activity for a sulfuric acid concentration of 0.05 mol/L, at which the furfural yield reaches 58.6%. An excessively high acid concentration decreases the catalyst activity, and this may be attributed to the destruction of the zeolite crystalline structure. In addition, it can be seen that the loading of Cr3+ can significantly improve the zeolite's catalytic activity, and the catalyst shows optimal catalytic activity for a Cr3+ concentration of 5 mmol/L. This result may be attributed to the strong Lewis acidity of metal ions [35], which not only lowers the barrier of xylose isomerization to xylulose, it also accelerates the dehydration of xylulose to furfural. As the Cr3+ concentration increases from 5 mmol/L to 30 mmol/L, the catalyst activity gradually decreases and the furfural yield drops from 76.4% to 60.9%. By ion exchange, Cr3+ migrates from the supercage to the small cage inside zeolite crystals in the form of hydrated ions. It interacts with the skeletal oxygen atoms in the small cage and resides in the zeolite as chromium‑oxygen tetrahedral coordination [36,37]. The loaded Cr3+ inhibits the skeletal dealumination of zeolite under hydrothermal conditions and increases the stability of the modified zeolite in catalytic reactions. In Scheme 1 , a potential chemical pathway for the modified zeolite catalyzed conversion of xylose to furfural is depicted [26,38]. The XRD patterns in Fig. 2 show the changes in zeolite structure upon modification. There are clear characteristic peaks for HY zeolite, which is characterized by strong reflections at 6.35°, 15.8°, and 23.7° [39]. The XRD pattern of deAl-Y zeolite after sulfuric acid dealumination has the same characteristic diffraction peaks as the original HY zeolite, indicating that the zeolite has a stable crystal structure and that its basic structural properties do not change upon dealumination, which is consistent with previous studies [26]. Analogously, there are no metal crystallization peaks after the introduction of the chromium, indicating that the metal is uniformly distributed in the zeolite during the loading process or present in the zeolite as amorphous structures. With the increase in sulfuric acid concentration, the diffraction peaks in the XRD patterns of the dealuminized zeolites weakened significantly (Fig. S1). The introduction of excess Cr3+ caused a similar weakening of the main diffraction peaks in the modified zeolites. HY zeolite has an octahedral morphology characteristic of the FAU family of zeolites. The SEM image in Fig. 3 shows the HY zeolite with distinct flat edges and a smooth surface. The modified deAl-Y and Cr-deAl-Y zeolites have similar morphologies to that of pristine HY. This indicates that dealuminization and metal doping have no adverse effects on the crystalline structure of pristine HY zeolites, which is consistent with the XRD results. Crystals of zeolite particles of different sizes are arranged into larger aggregates, and this may give rise to the disordered mesopores dispersed inside or around the zeolite crystals. In order to observe the microstructure of the modified zeolite samples more accurately, TEM images of different samples were obtained (Fig. 4 ). There are no obvious mesopores detected in the HY zeolite samples, but a large number of regularly arranged microporous pore channels can be found. The increase in mesopores facilitates the access of xylose molecules to the inner surface of zeolite, which results in better binding to acidic sites. In contrast, on the rough grain surface of the dealuminized zeolite samples, some irregular pores with larger pore sizes can be observed. After the loading of Cr3+ (Cr-deAl-Y), the structure of deAl-Y zeolite is almost unaffected. Fig. 5 shows the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the catalysts. The pristine HY zeolite shows a type I isotherm, which is a typical microporous material. However, the deAl-Y and Cr-deAl-Y catalysts show hysteresis loops at high relative pressure and possess the properties of mesoporous materials. The results of N2 adsorption-desorption analysis and ICP-OES quantification of the catalyst samples, including BET area, mesopore volume, average pore size, and metal loading, are summarized in Table 3 . Both dealumination and ion exchange decrease the specific surface areas of the zeolite catalysts, which may be due to the substantial removal of non-skeletal aluminum atoms and pore blockage by metal ions. However, a significant increase in mesopore volume and average pore size are observed for the deAl-Y samples, which is attributed to the formation of open pores and the connection of micropores during the dealumination process. After the loading of metal ions, a decrease in pore volume and average pore size is observed, which confirms the homogeneous dispersion of Cr3+ in the zeolite pore channels, as is consistent with the XRD results. The acid strength of the catalysts was examined by NH3-TPD method. As shown in Fig. 6a, the curve of the HY zeolite shows two distinct absorption peaks at around 240 and 420 °C, corresponding to the weak and moderate acid sites [40]. The decrease of acidic center after the dealumination of HY zeolite may be due to the removal of aluminum outside the skeleton and aluminum inside the skeleton. The ion-exchanged Cr-deAl-Y zeolite presents a clear peak around 505 °C, indicating that introduction of Cr3+ increases strong acid sites in Cr-deAl-Y zeolite. Py-FTIR was used to identify the acid type of the zeolites, all curves in Fig. 6b possess Brønsted and Lewis acid sites. Dealumination decreases the Lewis acid content of HY zeolite remarkably (Table 3), which is reflected in the weakening of the intensity of Lewis acid peak around 1450 cm−1 in the image [15]. Thus, despite the decrease in total acid sites, the increase of strong Lewis acid sites gives it a good acidic center. NaCl lowers the solution pH and provides H+ for the acid-catalyzed dehydration of xylose [41]. Therefore, we investigated the effect of NaCl concentration on furfural yield in the water/n-butanol system. As shown in Fig. 7 , the furfural yield increases continuously with the increase of NaCl concentration. The maximum furfural yield of 60.9% is obtained when the NaCl solution reaches saturation, and it is only 36.8% without NaCl. Furthermore, the introduction of NaCl can improve the partition coefficient of furfural in n-butanol as well as decrease the reaction time and byproducts formation. At the initial stage of the reaction, the xylose conversion is accelerated by NaCl. The ability to effectively recycle the components in a system is a crucial determining factor for its broad development prospects [40]. As shown in Fig. 8 , the organic and aqueous phases are separated after the reaction. The separated organic phase is distilled under vacuum and moderate temperature (30–50 °C) to obtain colorless n-butanol and furfural (containing some impurities). Then the furfural is transferred to the high-temperature distillation device to further concentrate, and obtain the more pure yellowish furfural. The NaCl in the aqueous phase can be recovered by drying. Having determined the optimum NaCl concentration of the aqueous phase, we investigated the effects of other reaction conditions on the furfural yield. As shown in Fig. 9a, furfural yield increases with reaction time from 10 to 30 min, with all samples achieving >95% xylose conversion. Furthermore, it is interesting to observe that the acceptable furfural yield of 64.7% is achieved in only 10 min. A shorter reaction time means lower energy consumption and less economic cost. Thus the reported catalyst has significant advantages over most previously reported catalysts. Xylose conversion reaches 100% at 50 min. This also indicates that the Cr-deAl-Y catalyst can adequately bind to xylose and induce its conversion. With the further extension of reaction time, the furfural yield decreases slightly and the organic phase color darkens (Fig. S2), which may be attributed to the resinization of furfural [16]. Then, we optimized the temperature of the furfural formation system. As illustrated in Fig. 9b, lower temperatures do not provide enough energy to promote the conversion of xylose, and the highest yield of furfural (77.5%) is achieved at 180 °C. Furthermore, xylose conversion approaches 100% as the temperature increases. The temperature profile shows a similar trend to the time profile, with excessively long reaction times and high reaction temperatures leading to increased byproducts production and decreased yield (Fig. S2). The byproducts can block the pores of the catalyst, which decreases its activity. The main byproducts resulting from the long reaction time may be humins, which are formed by the aggregation of furfural in the organic phase. And the main byproducts at high temperatures may be humins formed by xylose and furfural in the aqueous phase [42]. As shown in Fig. 10 , the furfural yield is only 18.9%, when no catalyst is added. However, the addition of Cr-deAl-Y significantly increases the yield of furfural. Thus, it is confirmed that the zeolite plays the most crucial catalytic role in the process of furfural formation and that NaCl acts as a vital auxiliary agent. With an increase in catalyst/xylose ratio, both furfural yield and xylose conversion increase. At a ratio of 2:4, the furfural yield reaches its maximum of 77.5% and the xylose conversion reaches 99.7%. When the dosage of Cr-deAl-Y is increased further, the furfural yield slightly decreases and the xylose conversion remains largely unchanged. This may be because excessive catalyst loading provides too much acidity to the system, which accelerates the resinization of furfural and thus decreases its yield [43]. In the two-step process of furfural production, the xylose obtained in the first step often contains a large number of soluble impurities, such as hydrolyzed-lignin phenols and hydrolyzed-cellulose sugars. Such impurities may have an effect on the catalysis in the second step. Accordingly, we prepared poplar and corncob hydrolysates separately by the method of oxalic acid hydrolysis. The complete hydrolysates and their compositions are shown in Fig. 11 . Under the optimal conditions, 75.6% and 66.8% furfural yields are obtained for poplar and corncob, respectively. The impurities in the biomass feedstocks decrease the catalyst activity slightly, but a desirable catalytic effect is still observed. This indicates that the modified zeolite Cr-deAl-Y has good practical application value for biomass xylose. The separated catalyst (recycled-Y) was washed in deionized water and ethanol, respectively, followed by drying at 110 °C. After drying the catalyst, its performance was re-evaluated. This procedure was repeated five times. As shown in Fig. 12 , the catalyst shows excellent catalytic performance for the first cycle, and the furfural yield only decreases by 3.8% compared to that achieved with the fresh catalyst. In the next four cycles, the furfural yield decreases to some extent, but it remains at or above 66.1%. Xylose conversion also shows a slight decrease from 98.7% to 95.4%. This may be caused by the decrease in average pore size and specific surface area of the catalyst (Table S1). Comparing the catalyst morphologies before and after reaction, the surface structure (Fig. S3) does not observably change, which may be a reason for the stable catalysis. However, the partial loss of the Cr appears to have no significant effect on the catalytic performance of the Cr-deAl-Y catalyst [16]. This may be attributed to the fact that the modified catalyst retains large pores (3.79 nm) containing sufficient active sites, and the molecular diameter of xylose is 0.68 nm [44]. Therefore, even the loss of Cr and the slight decrease in the average pore size does not excessively hinder the diffusion of xylose molecules and their contact at the active centers. A modified zeolite Cr-deAl-Y catalyst for efficient conversion of xylose to furfural in a green, recyclable, biphasic water/n-butanol system was obtained by dealumination and ion exchange. Dealumination gives the catalyst a developed pore structure and ion exchange provides it with a strong acid center. It achieved 77.5% furfural yield and 99.7% xylose conversion in a short reaction time. The components of the reaction system were effectively recovered. Furthermore, Cr-deAl-Y was found to be effective for the hydrolysis of biomass xylose from lignocellulosic feedstocks. In recycling tests, the Cr-deAl-Y catalyst maintained excellent performance over five cycles. Thus, our study identifies a modified zeolite catalyst for xylose conversion that overcomes the problems encountered with conventional zeolites and conforms to sustainable development and green chemistry ideas, thus providing an attractive route for biomass conversion. Ye Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yanan Dai: Investigation, Visualization. Tianhao Wang: Data curation, Validation. Mengling Li: Data curation, Software. Ying Zhu: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Liping Zhang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. None. This research was supported by Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China (2019YFB1503803-01-05). Supplementary material Image 1 Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fuproc.2022.107472.
abstract: Limited furfural yields and reagent waste are often the critical problems of conventional zeolite catalytic systems. Consequently, in the present study, an efficient solid acid catalyst (Cr-deAl-Y) containing Cr was prepared from HY zeolite via dealumination and ion exchange. The modified catalyst possesses more strong acid sites and a more developed pore structure. Cr-deAl-Y was used to catalyze the conversion of xylose into furfural in the water/n-butanol system. The maximal furfural yield was 77.5% and xylose conversion reached 99.7% after reaction at 180 °C and 30 min. After the reaction is accomplished, all components of the catalytic system can be recovered, realizing the sustainable utilization of reagents. Moreover, the catalyst cycling performance and the catalytic performance of hydrolyzed biomass xylose were evaluated under optimal conditions.
title: Efficient conversion of xylose to furfural over modified zeolite in the recyclable water/n-butanol system
