full_text: Our excessive reliance on fossil fuels and the environmental concerns associated with their consumption has compelled us to search for an alternative renewable energy resource [1–10]. Interestingly, cellulose, which is the most abundant source of biomass, has gained a lot of attention in recent years, for the production of valuable chemicals and fuels [1,6–8,11–16]. Moreover, since cellulose is inedible, it can be used as a potent carbon neutral feedstock at an industrial scale, without competing with the food supplies [1,13,17,18]. Owing to its significant structural stability through β-1,4-glycosidic bonds, cellulose is insoluble in water which makes it highly reluctant for efficient transformation [1,7,18–21]. Thus, it requires pre-treatment for breaking strong glycoside bonds which will improve the accessibility of the catalytic sites towards glucose units in the cellulose structure [17,22,23]. Among several pre-treatment routes, acid treatment although is promising, yet is not free from toxicity, corrosiveness and complexity of the reaction environment [15]. However, ball-milling is an attracting alternative that shows prompt degradation of cellulose crystallinity and exposed the active components like glucose monomers for further reactions [12,23,24]. Cellulose hydrolysis to glucose is the key and most elegant step for hydrolytic hydrogenation route [15,19,25]. However, the major challenge is the stability of highly reactive glucose molecules, which has a high probability of humin formation via coking reactions [14,25]. Therefore, it is highly desired to convert glucose to valuable chemicals using hydrogenation strategy. Interestingly, cellulose/glucose conversion to hexitols (sorbitol, mannitol) and ethylene glycol has shown a major paradigm in academia and industrial research. Specifically, sorbitol is quite useful as an artificial sweetener and ethylene glycol is essential in pharmaceuticals, textiles and automobiles [12,26,27]. Industrially sorbitol is produced via hydrogenation of starch derived glucose and ethylene glycol is obtained from fossil-derived ethylene [28]. Therefore, the major focus of this study is to produce hexitols and ethylene glycol in one pot using renewable cellulose as feedstock. Conversion of cellulose involves initial hydrolysis to glucose, followed by hydrogenation/hydrogenolysis/retro-aldol condensation of glucose to sorbitol and ethylene glycol [10,12,16,26,29–31]. Precious metals (Ru, Pt, Pd) are well-known for its hydrogenation ability while acid- catalysts are required for the hydrolysis, hydrogenolysis and retro-aldol condensation reactions [9,22,26,29,30,32–38]. Ru-supported catalysts are found promising for cellulose conversion to hexitols as reported by Lazadiris et al. [34], Pang et al. [29] and Deng et al. [22]. Lazadiris et al. [34] used multifunctional support containing sulfonic acid functionalized Ru/C that converts cellulose to hexitol in high yield (44.5%). Furthermore, Pang[29] developed an interesting catalyst comprising RuSn alloy on activated carbon which proved better efficiency than Ru/C itself. They showed that Sn promotes glucose conversion through an alternative pathway involving retro-aldol condensation and isomerization reactions [29]. Among other transition metals, Deng et al. [22] proved Ru is promising for cellulose hydrogenation reactions. Apart from carbon-supported catalysts, BEA zeolite was also explored in cellulose conversion to sorbitol, as reported by Negoi et al. [26]. However, in most of the cases, it shows low stability and poor durability of the catalyst due to inadequate metal-support interactions. Simultaneously, the hydrolysed product of cellulose may suffer from poor stability in presence of such catalytic systems. As a result, glucose can be easily converted to other degradation products instead of the desired ethylene glycol [25]. Also sulfonic acids are labile and may create hazardous issues on environment through segregation from the support [35,36]. All such limitations may require a thorough understanding in developing stable catalysts for cellulose conversion reactions. Compared to sulfonic acid, W-based acid catalysts can be an interesting alternative for the hydrolysis reactions [30,31,39]. Ribeiro et al. [30] proved that with increasing W content in RuW/CNT, ethylene glycol yield was significantly improved through the cleavage of CC bonds of cellulose. Liu et al. [31] used WO3/C in combination with Ru/C and showed that WO3/C promotes the cleavage of CC bond and cellulose hydrolysis to ethylene glycol in high yields. In continuation to such research, we herein explore W-based heteropolyacids (HPA) as an initial hydrolysis catalyst for cellulose conversion. HPA have been reported for the synthesis of hexitols from cellulose with Ru/C [28,40–42]. Although a good catalytic activity towards hexitol was reported, the major challenge was the recovery of HPA as it is water-soluble [43,44]. Nevertheless, recrystallization process can be utilized for the recovery of HPA but it shows low recovery (~50%) [41]. Moreover, another problem of HPA is its low surface area.[45]. To overcome these issues, supports like zirconia have been reported earlier, which helped in easy recovery of the catalyst [43,45]. The overall catalytic activity of the HPA/zirconia catalyst is enhanced owing to the higher surface area provided by the zirconia support [43,45]. However, cellulose conversion based on HPA-support interactions to execute the product selectivity is still lacking. The present study describes easy heterogenization of several HPAs (phosphotungstic acid, silicotungstic acid, phosphomolybdic acid) on zirconia to investigate their catalytic activity on a pre-treated cellulose. Owing to significant interaction, HPA/zirconia composite shows enormous stability and durability in executing the initial, yet important hydrolysis step. This research thus shows an elegant approach containing multifunctional catalysts for the one-pot conversion of large biomass molecules to useful chemicals and feedstocks. Microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. Phosphotungstic acid (PTA), silicotungstic acid (STA), phosphomolybdic acid (PMA), zirconia (ZrO2), Ru/C (5 wt.% Ru), Rh/C (5 wt.% Rh), Pd/C (5 wt.% Pd) were procured from Sigma Aldrich. HPLC grade water was obtained from Merck. Sorbitol (≥98%), mannitol (≥98%), ethylene glycol (99.8%), glucose (≥99.5%) were procured from Sigma Aldrich, USA. MCC was pre-treated with planetary ball mill to decrease the overall crystallinity of MCC. In a typical experiment, 5 g of MCC was charged in a stainless-steel pot along with the tungsten carbide balls (70 balls with a diameter of 3 mm each). After that, the atmosphere inside the pot was made inert by purging and filling argon gas to avoid oxidation of the charged sample. Then, the ball milling was done under different rotation speeds (300 rpm, 600 rpm and 900 rpm) for different times (4 h and 8 h). The samples were denoted as MCC1 (MCC ball-milled at 300 rpm/4 h), MCC2 (MCC ball-milled at 600 rpm/4 h), MCC3 (MCC ball-milled at 900 rpm/4 h), MCC4 (MCC ball-milled at 900 rpm/8 h). Powder X-ray diffraction was used to investigate the MCC crystallinity of the ball-milled samples. XRD patterns were recorded using Rigaku Miniflex 600 (CuKα X-ray source, 40 kV, 15 mA) and the diffractograms were measured in a 2θ range of 5 to 40°, with a scanning speed of 4°/min. The crystallinity index (CrI) was calculated using the following equation (Eq. 1) reported in the previous literature [46]. (1) CrI % = Ic − Ia Ic x 100 where Ic is the crystalline peak intensity at 2θ = 22.6° and Ia is the amorphous peak intensity at 2θ = 18°. We measured FTIR using ATR mode of Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrometer. The spectra were recorded in a range of 500 to 4000 cm‐ −1 with 64 scans. Before analysis, the samples (untreated and ball-milled MCC) were dried in vacuum oven at 100 °C for 24 h. Morphology of the ball-milled sample was measured through FESEM analysis using JEOL JMS-7800F prime at different magnifications. For FESEM analysis, a well-dried sample was mounted on the aluminium stubs by using conductive carbon tape and sputter coated under vacuum with platinum. All the HPA namely PTA, STA, PMA supported on ZrO2 catalysts were synthesized using wet impregnation method based on our previous study [43]. The HPA loading was fixed at 40 wt.% with respect to ZrO2 in all the synthesized catalysts and maintained as 2:3 by weight. Initially, HPA and ZrO2 were taken separately in beakers. Then, ZrO2 containing beaker was placed on a magnetic plate and stirred continuously at 500 rpm. Aqueous solution of HPA was added dropwise to the ZrO2 solution and the resulting mixture was aged for 24 h. After that, the water from the mixture was removed using rotary evaporator and the resulting catalyst was dried at 100 °C using vacuum oven for 24 h. Finally, the composite was heated at 300 °C in a muffle furnace at 5 °C/min ramping rate for 5 h. The calcined catalysts, denoted as PTA/ZrO2 (40 wt.% PTA on ZrO2 support), STA/ZrO2 (40 wt.% STA on ZrO2 support) and PMA/ZrO2 (40 wt.% PMA on ZrO2 support) were used for investigating cellulose conversion reactions. The composite catalysts were characterized by XRD, ATR-FTIR, Raman spectroscopy, NH3-TPD, FESEM-EDX. XRD patterns were recorded in a 2θ range of 5 to 70° with a scanning speed of 4°/min. ATR-FTIR spectra were recorded in a range of 600 to 1200 cm1 with 64 scans. Renishaw plc micro-raman spectrometer was used for recording the raman spectra. Argon Ion laser at 514 nm with 20 MW power was used as the exiting source. The spectra were measured in a range of 150 to 1050 cm−1. NH3-TPD was done using Micrometrics ChemiSorb 2720. Initially, the sample was degassed at 200 °C for 3 h under helium flow (30 mL/min). Then, ammonia: helium (5:95) gas mixture was passed on the sample at 100 °C for 1 h followed by flushing of the sample with helium at 100 °C for 1 h to remove the physiosorbed ammonia. Finally, NH3-TPD was done within 50 to 1100 °C at 10 °C/min ramping rate and desorbed ammonia was measured using TCD detector. FESEM-EDX (JEOL JMS-7800F prime) was done to determine the dispersion of HPA on ZrO2 support along with the elemental distribution. All the reactions of MCC to hexitols (sorbitol, mannitol) and ethylene glycol were performed in a high-pressure batch reactor (Parr instruments company, Illinois, USA, series 4848) made up of Hastelloy material equipped with a temperature sensor, pressure gauge, gas supply lines and a chiller. In a typical reaction, first the reactor was fed with 0.5 g MCC, 0.25 g HPA/ZrO2, 0.125 g Ru/C and 100 mL water. After that, the reactor was filled with 100 bar N2 and left for 2 h to ensure leak-proof operation. Then, N2 was purged in the reactor for several times to remove air from inside. Finally, the reactor was fed with 50 bar H2 and the reaction was performed at the desired temperature (180 to 240 °C) for 5 h under continuous stirring (750 rpm). After the completion of the reaction, the reactor was gradually cooled to 25 °C using the chiller system connected to the reactor. The product mixture was then filtered to separate the unreacted MCC and catalysts from the liquid products. To understand the ball-milling effect on the product yield, different feeds namely MCC1, MCC2, MCC3, MCC4 were fed to the reactor. Moreover, the catalytic performances on the product yield of different HPAs (PTA, STA and PMA) on ZrO2 (PTA/ ZrO2, STA/ZrO2, PMA/ZrO2) as well as the effect of various precious metal (Ru, Rh and Pd) on carbon (Ru/C, Rh/C, Pd/C) were studied. During recycling tests, the catalysts were separated using filtration, washed thrice with water, dried in vacuum oven overnight at 100 °C and then used for the next run. Hydrogenation of glucose were performed in the same high-pressure batch reactor. In a typical experiment, 0.5 g glucose, 0.25 g PTA/ZrO2, 0.125 g Ru/C and 100 mL water was fed to the reactor. A similar procedure of N2 leak-test and purging was followed to ensure inert atmosphere for the reaction. The reactor was fed with 50 bar H2 and the reactions were performed at 200 °C for 5 h with a stirring speed of 750 rpm. To study the effect of different precious metal supported on carbon catalysts on the product yield, the reactions were also performed using Rh/C and Pd/C catalysts and the results were compared with the MCC conversion runs. The liquid products were analyzed with HPLC (Agilent 1200 infinity series) equipped with autosampler, RI detector and Hi-Plex Ca column (300 × 7.7 mm). HPLC grade water (100%) was the mobile phase at a flow rate of 0.6 mL/min, column temperature was 85 °C and the injection volume was fixed at 2 μL. The calibration curves of pure standard solutions of sorbitol, mannitol, ethylene glycol at different concentrations were plotted to quantify the products. MCC conversion and the product yield were calculated using the following equations (Eq. 2–4) (2) MCC conversion % = solid residue mg − catalyst mg initial MCC fed in the reactor mg x 100 (3) Hexitol yield % = weight of hexitol in the product mixture mg initial MCC fed in the reactor mg x 100 (4) Ethylene glycol yield % = weight of ethylene glycol in the product mixture mg initial MCC fed in the reactor mg x 100 MCC possesses robust and crystalline framework, which requires stringent condition for hydrolysis. In order to make its hydrolysis facile, MCC was pre-treated by a ball milling machine at 300–900 rpm for 4–8 h. Thus, ball milling technique disintegrates cellulose into smaller fragments enriching into degradable constituents [34,47,48]. Under extensive ball milling conditions (higher rotation and higher time), CrI and crystallite size of MCC were drastically reduced, as shown in Table 1 . Under optimized condition (900 rpm for 8 h), CrI was decreased from 88.9% of untreated MCC to 6.5%, which necessarily signifies the breaking of crystalline structure followed by an increase in amorphous characteristics. Thus, the reduction in CrI indicates the cleavage and weakening of hydrogen bonding network in cellulose through a rigorous ball-milling process, which could possibly alleviate the desired products (hexitols, ethylene glycol) through hydrolysis of easily accessible glucose monomers by the catalysts. XRD pattern of the ball-milled MCC was compared with that of untreated pristine MCC and the results are shown in Fig. 1a. The diffraction peaks at 2θ = 15.3, 22.6 and 34.5° corresponds to the lattice planes of 110, 200 and 004, respectively, of the cellulose [49–51]. As seen from XRD, the ball-milling pre-treatment reduces crystallinity of the MCC and higher amorphous characteristics was developed in the treated MCC which indicates quite loss of crystallinity in the processed cellulose. Additionally, intensity was also reduced in the treated cellulose as compared to pristine cellulose. This result clearly signifies that ball milling is quite effective for diminishing the rigid wall structure of cellulose. Reduction in crystalline phases and the contemporary structural changes of the pre-treated cellulose was further confirmed by ATR-FTIR analysis (Fig. 1b). All the samples showed absorption peaks at 3350, 2900, 1430, 1373 and 897 cm−1 associated with the native cellulose structure [52,53]. Moreover, new absorption peaks were not observed in the ball-milled samples compared to the untreated MCC indicating that generation of new functional group did not take place during the milling process [54]. The absorption band at 3350 cm−1 corresponding to the OH stretching vibration became stronger with the increase in the ball-milling intensity. The probable reason could be the cleavage of inter- and intra- molecular hydrogen bonding network in the structure of cellulose, which resulted in higher content of free hydroxyl groups [53,54]. The absorption peak at 1430 cm−1 are due to the CH2 bending which is also called the crystallinity band [46,52]. Moreover, the reduction in the peak intensities and disappearance of some peaks at wavelengths of 1430, 1337, 1320 and 1112 cm−1 is indicative of the reduction in cellulose crystallinity with the ball-milling as reported in the previous literature [55]. The absorption peaks at 2900, 1640 and 1373 cm−1 corresponds to the CH stretching, OH bending and CH in-plane deformation bending bands, respectively, typically present in the cellulose structure [46,53]. Absorption peak at 897 cm−1 is the characteristics peak of β-glycosidic linkages in the cellulose structure, which was seen increased in intensity with ball milled cellulose (Fig. S1) [46,53]. The morphological changes of the ball-milled MCC framework is as shown in Fig. S2. It shows that unlike pristine MCC (100 to 168 μm particles), ball milling reduces the size. Thus, the ball milled MCC shows ~4.6 to 25.4 μm of particles which are ~7 to 37 times smaller than pristine MCC. It has been reported earlier that the ball-milling breaks the cellulose crystallites along the direction perpendicular to the axis of the crystallite, which is confirmed by the decrease in the length of the particles [46]. Thus, the ordered crystalline structure of the MCC was disrupted due to the ball-milling pre-treatment which is well-supported by the XRD, ATR-FTIR and FESEM analysis, signifying the reduction in the tensile strength and the crystallites of MCC can be well accomplished via adopting the advanced ball milling technique [52]. Powder XRD diffraction of HPA, ZrO2 support and the HPA/ZrO2 composite catalysts (PTA/ZrO2, STA/ZrO2, PMA/ZrO2) are as shown in Fig. 2a. ZrO2 shows major characteristic peaks at 2θ values of 24, 28.1, 31.4, 34.4 and 49.2° corresponds to lattice planes of (011), (111),(111), (020), (022), respectively, of the monoclinic phases of ZrO2 (JCPDS no. 81–1314) [43,56,57]. Moreover, the peaks at 2θ values of 30.1, 35.1, 50.5, 60 and 62.7° corresponds to lattice planes of (101), (110), (200), (211) and (202), respectively, of the tetragonal phases of ZrO2 (JCPDS no. 81–1544) [43,56]. XRD patterns of the PTA/ZrO2, STA/ZrO2, PMA/ZrO2 catalysts also showed diffraction peaks close to 2θ of ZrO2 support. However, minor peaks at 10.4, 17.4, 18.3, 21 and 25.9° were detected in the synthesized catalysts, corresponding to the diffraction peaks of HPA, which correlates the coordination of HPA with the ZrO2 support [43]. Acidity of the synthesized catalysts were determined using NH3-TPD technique. As can be seen from the NH3-TPD profiles (Fig. 2b), there are two major acidity regions, the first region corresponding to the weak acid sites (75 to 450 °C) and the second region representing the strong acid sites (730 to 1080 °C). Two broad peaks at 240.8 and 876.5 °C were present in the PTA/ZrO2, which indicated the presence of weak and strong acidity in the catalyst. Similar peaks were present in STA/ZrO2 at 233.7 and 858.4 °C. In PMA/ZrO2, the broad peaks at 282.2 and 837.8 °C showed the presence of weak as well as strong acidity in the catalysts. The quantitative data for the weak, strong and total acidity of the synthesized catalyst is given in Table 2 . Overall, the total acidity of the synthesized HPA supported on ZrO2 catalysts was found to be in the order PTA/ZrO2 > STA/ZrO2 > PMA/ZrO2, which is consistent with the reported literature possibly due to the decreasing interaction with the support ZrO2 [58,59]. Strong interaction between PTA and ZrO2 enables highest brønsted acidity in PTA/ZrO2. ATR-FTIR spectra of the parent HPA (PTA/ STA/ PMA) is shown in Fig. 3a-c. The keggin structure of the parent PTA was confirmed by the ATR-FTIR analysis which showed characteristic peaks at 1075 cm−1 for PO stretching, 975 cm−1 for W=Oterminal stretching, 887 cm−1 for W-Ocorner-W stretching and 792 cm−1 for W-Oedge-W stretching [60,61]. Similarly, the parent STA showed peaks for SiO stretching (1015 cm−1), W=Oterminal stretching (978 cm−1), W-Ocorner-W stretching (914 cm−1) W-Oedge-W stretching (784 cm−1) that confirms the keggin structure of STA [43]. The characteristic peaks at 1058 cm−1 for PO stretching, 960 cm−1 for Mo = Oterminal stretching, 872 cm−1 for Mo-Ocorner-Mo stretching and 786 cm−1 for Mo-Oedge-Mo stretching confirms the keggin structure of the parent PMA [62,63]. Interestingly, it shows a change in the FTIR stretching among PTA/ZrO2, STA/ZrO2 and PMA/ZrO2 as compared to the parent HPA, signifying that HPA may be strongly interacting with oxygenated supports. The oxygenated surface of zirconia may favour electronic interactions with HPA and promotes brønsted acid of the supported HPA, which is in line with NH3-TPD results. Further, the result confirms that the keggin structure of HPA remains intact on the ZrO2. To further confirm the keggin structure in the heterogenized catalysts, Raman spectra were recorded (Fig. 3d-f). Parent PTA showed characteristic bands overlapped at 1007 cm−1, 990 cm−1 corresponding to the symmetric and asymmetric W=Oterminal stretching, respectively [57,64]. The Raman shift at 915, 540 and 219 cm−1 corresponded to the W-Ocorner-W stretching, W-Oedge-W stretching and WO stretching, respectively, in the PTA. [64] Raman shifts at 1005, 978 and 926 cm−1 corresponding to the symmetric W=Oterminal stretching, asymmetric W=Oterminal stretching and W-Ocorner-W stretching, respectively, were observed in parent STA. Moreover, the shifts were also observed at 545 and 225 cm−1 corresponding to W-Oedge-W stretching and WO stretching, respectively [65]. In case of PMA, the raman shifts at 985, 955, 921 and 245 cm−1 corresponding to symmetric Mo = Oterminal stretching, asymmetric Mo = Oterminal stretching, Mo-Ocorner-Mo stretching and MoO stretching, respectively, were detected [66,67]. Raman spectra of the ZrO2 support shows characteristic bands at 181, 222, 310, 337, 382, 474, 499, 540, 559, 620, 636 cm−1 corresponding to the monoclinic phase in ZrO2 (Fig. S3) [57,68]. Moreover, the raman band in the range 261 to 270 cm−1 was also observed that corresponds to the tetragonal phase of ZrO2 (Fig. S3) [68]. Thus, the results are consistent with the XRD confirming the presence of both monoclinic as well as tetragonal phases in the ZrO2 support. Fig. 3d-f shows the Raman spectra for the heterogenous catalysts (PTA/ZrO2, STA/ZrO2, PMA/ZrO2). Some additional bands at 337, 382, 474 and 620 cm−1 corresponding to the ZrO2 were also detected indicating some possible weak interaction between the parent HPA and ZrO2 support. Additionally, a difference in Raman shift between the parent HPAs and the support modified HPAs is clearly noticeable, demonstrating that a significant stabilization between HPA and ZrO2 support might be possible due to the interaction among them. As reported previously [43,69], HPA and ZrO2 shows considerable acid-base interaction through hydrogen bonding between hydroxy of ZrO2 and brønsted acid sites of HPA. Furthermore, the bridging oxygen sites among HPA and ZrO2 also shows covalent interactions. The morphology of the synthesized catalysts was determined using FESEM-EDX technique and the results are as shown in Fig. S4. The ZrO2 support showed a morphology of spherical nanoparticles and the elemental mapping confirmed the uniform distribution of Zr and O elements. Similar morphology was observed in case of HPA supported on ZrO2 catalysts indicating that the doping on HPA did not alter the structure of the catalyst support. Moreover, the elemental mapping showed that the HPA were uniformly dispersed on the ZrO2 support. Fig. S4 shows the homogenous distribution of Zr, O, W, P elements on the PTA/ZrO2 catalyst. Similarly, the elements Zr, O, W, Si and Zr, O, Mo, P were finely dispersed on the STA/ZrO2 and PMA/ZrO2 catalysts, respectively. To disrupt the robust structure of the MCC, ball-milling was done under different conditions. The crystallinity of the MCC reduced significantly after the ball-milling, which enhanced the conversion of MCC to the desired products (hexitols, ethylene glycol) as can be seen in Fig. 4 . Even when reactions of the untreated MCC was done in the absence of catalyst, 36% conversion of MCC along with hexitols and ethylene glycol yield of ~1% and 5%, respectively, was achieved. According to the earlier reports, at higher temperatures (~200 °C or above), water has the ability to produce proton (H+) that could catalyze the hydrolysis of cellulose [11]. Furthermore, when the reaction was accomplished in the presence of PTA/ZrO2 and Ru/C catalyst, a significant increase in the MCC conversion from 36% (without catalyst) to 73.2% was observed. Moreover, the yield of hexitols (6.7%) and ethylene glycol (10.1%) were also improved with a composite mixture of HPA/ZrO2 and Ru/C, thus indicating a positive role of the catalyst in MCC conversion. Interestingly, the ball-milling pre-treatment showed a positive effect on the conversion and product yield and the conversion of MCC increased with increasing in the pre-treatment severity (Table 1). Thus, MCC4 shows highest conversion (~100%) of cellulose as well as highest yield (24.2% hexitols and 31% ethylene glycol) of the products that accomplishes from the easy hydrolysis of amorphous-like cellulose. Optimized reaction conditions while combining several hydrolysis and hydrogenation catalysts were shown in Table 3 . Initially, to understand the role of Ru/C, the reaction was performed in the absence of PTA/ZrO2. Although ~100% conversion was achieved, the yield of hexitols (17%) and ethylene glycol (11.2%) was lower. However, when the experiment was done in the presence of PTA/ZrO2 along with Ru/C, the products hexitols and ethylene glycol yields were significantly improved by 24.2 and 31%, respectively. The results clearly demonstrated a tandem hydrolysis vis-à-vis hydrogenation/hydrogenolysis/retro-aldol condensation for the conversion to cellulose to hexitols and ethylene glycol. Generally, PTA/ZrO2 facilitates hydrolysis of cellulose to glucose and further hydrogenation was promoted by Ru/C. Apart from Ru/C, hydrogenation ability was also compared with Pd/C and Rh/C in the presence of PTA/ZrO2. Interestingly, all the precious metal catalyst shows ~100% conversion of MCC4 and the product yield was necessarily improved with increasing d-band width of the precious metals. As suggested by Negoi et al. [26], increasing d-band width of the corresponding metal promotes selective hydrogenation of cellulose towards hexitols. DFT analysis suggests that Ru possess higher d-band width followed by Rh and Pd [70]. Hence, our result is consistent and follows the sequence; Ru/C > Rh/C > Pd/C (Table 3) [26]. As a proof of concept, when glucose was exclusively used instead of cellulose, all the catalyst shows higher hexitol yield at the cost of ethylene glycol, which clearly signifies that glucose was the most possible intermediate for this one-pot tandem reaction. Among all the hydrogenation catalysts, Ru/C in combination with PTA/ZrO2 shows highest yield of hexitol from glucose which is in accordance with d-band structure (Table 3). To understand the effect of acidity on the product yield, different ZrO2-supported HPA, were investigated for the conversion of MCC4 along with Ru/C. In this regard, PTA/ZrO2, STA/ZrO2 and PMA/ZrO2 catalysts were studied. Among these, PTA/ZrO2 gave the highest yield of hexitols and ethylene glycol as compared to other HPA/ZrO2 (Table 3). The activity was in the order of PTA/ ZrO2 > STA/ZrO2 > PMA/ZrO2 which in agreement with the acidic characteristics as observed from NH3-TPD results. The results clearly indicate that higher acidity of PTA/ZrO2 helps in hydrolysis of MCC4 to glucose. Moreover, it also promotes the formation of ethylene glycol by further hydrogenolysis/retro-aldol condensation in presence of ZrO2-supported HPA. These investigations clearly suggest that PTA/ZrO2, Ru/C is the most active catalyst for considering further experiments. We also investigated a significant temperature dependency of the reaction on the product yield, as shown in Fig. 5a. When the reaction was performed at 180 °C, lower yields of hexitols (7.1%) and ethylene glycol (6.5%) were observed. However, at higher temperature (200 °C), a significant increase in both hexitols (24.2%) as well as ethylene glycol (31%) was seen and beyond 200 °C, although hexitol yield was slightly improved to 26.2%, but interestingly ethylene glycol yield was more (40%). The probable reason for the better improvement in the ethylene glycol yield compared to the hexitols at higher temperature might be the higher activation energy required for glucose retro-aldol condensation to produce ethylene glycol compared to the lower activation energy for glucose hydrogenation [25]. Nevertheless, when the temperature was further increased to 240 °C, a decrease in the hexitols yield (10.4%) and ethylene glycol yield (31%) was observed. The results indicate the products are not thermally stable at higher temperatures and they can further degrade to form smaller molecules via polyol hydrogenolysis followed by coking of the substrates [1,18,25,27]. Therefore, 220 °C was considered to be the optimum temperature for the one pot conversion MCC to hexitols and ethylene glycol in high yields. According to the principles of green chemistry, to make the overall process sustainable and commercially viable, it is important to recycle and reuse the catalysts. The major issue of the HPA is its recyclability as these catalysts are readily soluble in water and could be easily leached out from the support due to inadequate metal-support interaction [71]. Thus, solid catalysts containing HPA should avoid such deactivation through strong interaction with supports. The covalency between the keggin structure of HPA and zirconia shows enhanced stability as well as better brønsted acidity of the HPA. Thus, HPA might be stable on zirconia to provide a better durable catalytic system which we observed from the following recycling tests. All the recycle runs were conducted at 220 °C for 5 h. After the reaction, the PTA/ZrO2 and Ru/C catalysts were easily recovered using filtration. The recycled catalyst showed good catalytic activity for three recycle runs (Fig. 5b). Although complete conversion of cellulose (~100%) was observed in all the recycle runs, a slight reduction in the product yield was observed i.e., the yield was decreased from 26.5% (hexitols) and 40% (ethylene glycol) for fresh run to 23.5% (hexitols) and 34.8% (ethylene glycol) for third recycle run. Heterogeneity of the catalyst was proved through hot-filtration test based on the previous reported literature [72]. Regarding this, we separate the catalyst after 2.5 h through filtration. The filtrate was used instead of the catalyst and we further extended the reaction for another 2.5 h. We observed no change in yield after 2.5 h and both ethylene glycol (20%) and hexitol (10%) yields were the same. This experiment clearly signifies high stability of HPA/ZrO2 under the present reaction conditions. Therefore, the possible reason for reduction in the catalytic activity might be due to some loss of catalyst during the work-up procedures (filtration, washing with water, drying overnight at 100 °C in vacuum oven). Wang et al. [73] reported Ni/ZnO to be effective for the conversion of MCC to hexitols and ethylene glycol. Although 19.1% yield of ethylene glycol was achieved at 245 °C for 2 h, the hexitols yield was low compared to the present study (Table 4 , Entry 1). Moreover, a significant loss in the catalytic activity was observed for the recycled catalyst (11.3% ethylene glycol yield after fifth run), due to metal leaching. Similar results were reported by Baek et al. [74] (Table 4, Entry 2) which showed the deactivation of the catalyst (Ni/W/SiO2-Al2O3) in the cyclic tests due to the oxidation of W and metal leaching. The total polyol yield decreased from 32.1% (first run) to 11.3% (third run). Lazaridis et al. [34] used Ru/AC-SO3H for the conversion of cellulose at 180 °C. Even after long reaction time of 24 h, lower yields of ethylene glycol and hexitols, were reported (Table 4, Entry 3). Ribeiro et al. [30] reported the bimetallic RuW supported on carbon nanotubes (CNT) for the hydrolytic hydrogenation of cellulose to ethylene glycol. The catalyst was active giving ethylene glycol yield of 34.8% but the hexitols yield was low (Table 4, Entry 4). In another study, the same group [75] reported the combination of Ru supported on nitric acid modified CNT with W/CNT for cellulose conversion. The acidity of the oxygen-surface groups of the CNT enhanced the ethylene glycol yield (41.2%) which is comparable with the present study. However, the hexitols yield was significantly lower compared to the present study (Table 4, Entry 5–6). Thus, the comparative results of the present study with the reported literature (Table 4) shows our developed catalytic system to be highly effective for the synthesis of both ethylene glycol and hexitol in high yields, in a one pot way. To understand the reaction pathway for one pot hydrolytic hydrogenation of MCC to hexitols and ethylene glycol, a series of reactions were performed under different conditions. The cellulose hydrolysis to glucose is the first step in MCC conversion, which is an acid-catalyzed reaction (Fig. 6 ). When the reaction was performed at 200 °C for 5 h in the presence of Ru/C, both the desired products hexitols (17%) and ethylene glycol (11.2%) were produced. It was confirmed from the blank run that water has the ability to produce H+ at higher temperatures, which might catalyze the cellulose hydrolysis to glucose. Further, the glucose hydrogenation reaction took place in the presence of Ru/C catalyst to produce hexitols. The production of ethylene glycol can take place via two pathways: (I) retro-aldol condensation of glucose to glycol aldehyde followed by hydrogenation to ethylene glycol or (II) hydrogenation of glucose to sorbitol followed by hydrogenolysis to ethylene glycol (Fig. 6). Interestingly, when both Ru/C and PTA/ZrO2 were used in the reaction, an increase in the yields of both hexitols (24.2%) and ethylene glycol (31%) was observed. The results indicated the positive role of PTA/ZrO2 in hydrolysis of cellulose to glucose, which was further converted to hexitols via hydrogenation over Ru/C. However, the yield of ethylene glycol was almost thrice when the combination of catalyst (Ru/C and PTA/ZrO2) was used compared to the Ru/C. The results indicated that the ethylene glycol might have produced via retro-aldol condensation of glucose catalyzed by PTA/ZrO2 to produce intermediate glycol aldehyde via CC bond cleavage. It has been reported earlier that the tungsten can catalyze the cleavage of CC bond in glucose [30,31,76,77]. The glycol aldehyde was further hydrogenated by Ru/C catalyst to produce ethylene glycol. Nevertheless, comparatively lower improvement in the hexitols yield (from 17% to 24.2) with the addition of PTA/ZrO2 in Ru/C is also indicative of the transformation of formed hexitols to ethylene glycol via retro-aldol condensation route. To further confirm this hypothesis, reaction was performed with glucose as feed using Ru/C and PTA/ZrO2. As expected, direct hydrogenation of glucose led to the production of sorbitol which was stable under the given reaction conditions and therefore, lower yield of ethylene glycol was observed (21%), which indicated that the transformation of sorbitol to ethylene glycol via hydrogenolysis route is less viable, in accordance with the reported literatures [25,77,78]. This study shows successful immobilization of HPA on zirconia as solid catalyst along with other hydrogenation catalysts for one-pot conversion of raw cellulose to value-added products like hexitols and ethylene glycol in high yields. A conventional ball-milling technique reduces high crystallinity of cellulose and improves the product yield. We found Ru/C and PTA/ZrO2 provides an effective heterojunction for ~100% conversion of cellulose with highest yield of hexitols (26.2%) and ethylene glycol (40%). The present catalytic system is efficient in-terms of product yield and reusability than other contemporary solid acid catalysts. The activity and well stability of PTA/ZrO2 comprises due to strong interaction between HPA and zirconia support which was consistent from our experimental results. Mechanistically we show, the hydrolytic hydrogenation is successfully accomplished through glucose as a stable intermediate, thereby avoiding the formation of soluble humin polymers or 5-hydroxymethylfurfural via glucose side reactions. Overall, this research could be interesting for future development of multifunctional catalytic system for one-pot conversion of cellulose to renewable fuels and additives.
abstract: Catalytic valorization of biomass has gained a significant global attention in recent years. Cellulose is an interesting bio-renewable feedstock, which can be utilized to replace the fossil fuel derived chemicals to attain a sustainable bio-based economy. Our research shows a one-pot conversion of cellulose through hydrolytic hydrogenation using heteropolyacid (HPA)/zirconia and Ru/C catalyst. The developed catalytic system was found to be highly active towards selective production of hexitols and ethylene glycol. A significant improvement in the catalytic performance towards cellulose conversion was observed when the cellulose was pre-treated using ball-milling (900 rpm, 8 h), as the ball-milling significantly reduced the crystallinity index of cellulose, which decreased the recalcitrance of the cellulose structure. Almost complete conversion (100%) of cellulose was observed when the reaction was performed at 220 °C for 5 h giving hexitols and ethylene glycol yields of 26.2 and 40%, respectively. Based on a series of experiments, a possible reaction pathway was proposed which suggested heteropolyacid/zirconia catalyzed hydrolysis and retro-aldol condensation reactions, accompanying by hydrogenation with Ru/C. Interestingly, HPA immobilized on the composite catalyst shows enhanced stability and recyclability, demonstrating the utilization of a heterogeneous system for tandem conversions of biomass to renewable fuels and feedstocks.
title: High yield synthesis of hexitols and ethylene glycol through one-pot hydrolytic hydrogenation of cellulose

steps.method_details: HPA and ZrO2 were taken separately in beakers
Aqueous solution of HPA was prepared
ZrO2 solution was stirred continuously at 500 rpm, and aqueous HPA solution was added dropwise
The resulting mixture was aged for 24 h
Water was removed using rotary evaporator, and the resulting catalyst was dried at 100 °C in vacuum oven for 24 h
The composite was heated at 300 °C in a muffle furnace at 5 °C/min ramping rate for 5 h
5 g of MCC was charged in a stainless-steel pot
Tungsten carbide balls (70 balls with a diameter of 3 mm each) were added
The atmosphere inside the pot was made inert by purging and filling argon gas
Ball milling was done under different rotation speeds (300 rpm, 600 rpm, 900 rpm) for different times (4 h, 8 h)
Catalysts were separated using filtration and washed thrice with water
Catalysts were dried in vacuum oven overnight at 100 °C

full_text: The European Union (EU) Directive on renewable energy sets ambitious targets for all Member States, such that the EU will reach a 20% share of energy from renewable sources by 2020 and a 10% share of renewable energy specifically in the transport sector [1]. Diesel engines are widely popular in Europe, accounting for roughly 50% of the car market there. As a substitute for fossil diesel, biodiesel has received considerable attention in many countries, such as Europe, North America, Brazil and India [2]. Biodiesel, a mixture of monoalkyl esters of fatty acids, is a low-emission fuel made from renewable resources and waste lipids [3]. The use of biodiesel has not expanded in developing countries mostly due to the high production cost which is associated with the expensive high-quality virgin oil feedstock [4–6]. Therefore, low value lipid raw materials and more efficient production processes must be used in order to make biodiesel more competitive. Biodiesel production is normally obtained from refined oil, due to the fact that the most common technology to produce biodiesel is based on a homogeneous catalyst such as sodium hydroxide. However, in order to use less pure raw material, newer technologies are still needed [7,8]. In fact, low value lipids are characterized by high free fatty acid contents which hamper the conventional homogenous basic catalytic systems. The effect of the oil acidity on the Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME) yield obtained by homogeneous catalysis has been studied by several researchers and, therefore, it is well documented in the literature [3,9,10]. The acidity of the raw lipid material promotes the partial neutralization of the catalyst, thus decreasing the transesterification reaction rate and the FAME yield. Commonly, in homogeneous catalysis, biodiesel production from high acidity oils is carried out using a two step process: first the fatty acids are esterified using acid catalysts, and further on the triglycerides are transesterified using basic catalysts [11] which increases the operating costs. Acid catalytic systems are not sensitive to the acid content of the lipid but the reaction is slow thus requiring high temperature and pressure [12,13]. In order to achieve a more efficient production process for biodiesel using oils with high free fatty acid content, the transesterification of rape seed oil (RSO), acidified with oleic acid, was studied using basic heterogeneous catalysts. Taking into account the excellent catalytic performances for the alcoholysis of vegetable oils reported in the literature [14–17], CaO was chosen. In fact, this material is cheap, not harmful for the environment and the catalytic activity can be improved by the water usually present in the low value lipids [18]. Actually, Ca(OH)2 seems to be the active phase [19]. The methanolysis tests were carried out over commercial CaO material. The as received chalky-white lime, with a broad granulometric distribution (20mgKOH/g) the catalyst becomes amorphous and inactive due to soap formation. Actually, the amount of formed soap increases when oil acidity increases. The use of WFO showed that biodiesel can be produced from low value raw materials using inexpensive basic catalysts, such as lime, even without previous neutralization of the free fatty acids or water removing. It can be processed mixed with semi-refined oil with no significant loss of the catalytic performances. Lime catalyst seems to be stable even to process inexpensive lipid materials with significant acidity.
abstract: Biodiesel is a renewable fuel suitable to replace fossil fuel which can help to mitigate the carbon footprint of the transportation sector. Its price is still noncompetitive mainly due to the high price of raw materials (vegetable oils) and the costs of the processes. In order to find an efficient basic heterogeneous catalyst to replace the nowadays industrial caustic catalyst, a cheap CaO material was used to produce biodiesel using semi-refined rape seed oil (RSO). For the same catalyst the catalytic activity was tested with oleic acid acidified oil in order to simulate the use of low price materials with high activity. A linear decay of the Fatty Acid Methyl Ester (FAME) yield was observed, when oil acidity increases due to catalyst neutralization. The XRD and TG data showed that catalyst deactivation can be related with catalyst amorphization what can be related with soap formation. The catalytic tests with acidic waste frying oil (WFO) showed that biodiesel can be produced over CaO catalyst from low value raw materials without significant deterioration of the catalytic performances.
title: Effect of the oil acidity on the methanolysis performances of lime catalyst biodiesel from waste frying oils (WFO)

full_text: Fuel Processing Technology, 17 (1987) 169-186 Elsevier Science Publishers B.V., Amsterdam -- Printed in The Netherlands 169 Support Studies for the U-GAS Coal Gasification Process Part I: Fluidization ANIL GOYAL, AMIR REHMAT, TED M. KNOWLTON, DENNIS LEPPIN, RICHARD T. WAIBEL and JITENDRA G. PATEL Institute of Gas Technology, 3424 South State Street, Chicago, IL 60616 (U.S.A.) (Received July 6th, 1986; accepted in revised form March 25th, 1987) ABSTRACT This is the first part of a series of papers providing the details of support studies conducted as part of the development of the U-GAS fluidized-bed ash-agglomerating coal gasification process. The fluidized-bed gasifier in the U-GAS process behaves much like a conventional well-mixed bed, having a uniform bed composition and temperature in the bulk of the region, but it exhibits some departure in a small localized area confined to the proximity of the central spout, where a somewhat higher temperature is maintained for producing ash agglomerates. Extensive cold-flow experimental studies have been conducted to predict the solids discharge rates and agglomerates classification from the venturi/classifier system in the gasifier; these studies have resulted in a generalized analytical expression in terms of operating velocities, gas properties, and venturi/ classifier configuration for the gasifier scale-up. Large-scale U-GAS gasifiers can have single-cone or multiple-cone grid configurations. X-ray cinematographic studies have been conducted in a program at University College London to com- pare the fluidization behaviors in the one-cone and three-cone grid configuration systems. The objective of this test program was to visually examine the solids circulation and study the bubble dynamics in the fluidized beds with these different configurations. INTRODUCTION Fluidized-bed reactors have been widely used in the process industry because of their inherent advantages, including good mixing, high rates of heat and mass transfer, mechanical sturdiness, and capability of continuous operation. This technology has been extensively applied to the processing of coal to pro- duce either medium-Btu gas or synthesis gas for chemicals. In recently devel- oped fluidized-bed coal gasification processes, such as the IGT U-GAS process, the Westinghouse process, and the NKK process, an ash-agglomeration tech- nique is utilized [ 1-3]. The incentive for using this technique is the combi- nation of the inherent advantages of a conventional fluidized-bed gasifier with the ability to obtain high carbon conversion by discharging low-carbon-con- 0378-3820/87/$03.50 1987 Elsevier Science Publishers B.V. 170 tent material from the gasifier. A conventional fluidized bed is not capable of discharging low-carbon-content ash from the bed. The restraint results from the necessity to maintain a relatively high ratio of carbon to ash in the bed to get high reaction rates and a stable, nonslagging operation. Due to the well- mixed nature of the fluid bed, the discharge would ordinarily be expected to be of the same composition as the bed and would thus be rich in carbon, as in the Winkler process. By using the ash-agglomeration technique, however, a con- tinuous and selective discharge of low-carbon-content material is obtained from the bed under nonslagging conditions. This technique enables the gasifier to achieve a carbon conversion efficiency as high as that of slagging types of fixed- bed and entrained-bed gasifiers. The U-GAS process, an advanced single-stage fluidized-bed coal gasification process, has been developed by the Institute of Gas Technology ( IGT ) to pro- duce a low- to medium-Btu fuel gas from a variety of feedstocks including highly caking, high-sulfur, and high-ash coals. The gas produced is environ- mentally acceptable and economically attractive. A 24-ton-per-day pilot plant has been in operation in Chicago since 1974. Recent tests conducted with dif- ferent feedstocks have achieved overall coal utilization exceeding 98% with long-term continuous steady-state operation. Extensive pilot-plant testing has established process feasibility and provided a complete data base for scale-up and design of the first commercial plant. The details of the process develop- ment have been presented in several publications [ 1,4-8 ]. The heart of the process is a single-stage fluidized-bed reactor that accom- plishes five important functions (Fig. 1 ): It decakes, devolatilizes and gasifies coal, and agglomerates and separates ash from bed char. In the bed, coal reacts with steam and oxygen (or air) at a temperature of 1750-2000 (950-1100 o C, depending upon coal characteristics), producing a mixture of CO, CO2, H2, and CH4. As the coal gasifies, the ash concentration of the particles increases and the particles agglomerate into roughly spherical particles, which are then selec- tively removed from the bed. The fluidizing gas enters the reactor in two areas: through the gas distributor plate, a sloping grid at the bottom of the bed, and through the ash-discharge device, consisting of a venturi/classifier system, located at the center of the distributor plate. The ratio of oxygen to steam in the two gas entry streams is such that a greater oxygen-to-steam ratio is main- tained to the ash discharge device. By this mechanism, a higher temperature is maintained in the bottom of the bed in the central zone, wherein ash parti- cles selectively stick to each other at the incipient softening temperature of the particles. The agglomerates grow until they can no longer be supported by the gas rising through the venturi/classifier. They are removed and discharged from the bed through this classifier into a water-filled ash hopper, where they are then withdrawn as a slurry. In addition to the pilot-plant operations, several support studies have been conducted on various aspects of the U-GAS process to aid in the scale-up of CRUSHED COAL~ FEED FE LOCJ HOPP RAW PROOUCT GAS 171 STEAP STEAk AGGL ASH AGGLOMERATES SLURRY Fig. 1. Schematic drawing of the U-GAS gasifier. the pilot-plant gasifier and the design of large U-GAS gasifiers: (a) cold-flow tests to understand the mechanism of agglomerates separation from the bed, (b) X-ray cinematographic studies to understand fluidization characteristics of the bed with different mechanical configurations, (c) combustion tests to determine the combustion characteristics of the gas generated in the gasifier, (d) ash chemistry to improve understanding of ash-agglomeration principles, (e) laboratory studies to develop information for preventing ash deposition in the cyclone, (f) steady-state and dynamic computer modeling to predict the performance of the gasifier and obtain scale-up information, (g) thermobal- ance tests to develop kinetic information, (h) bench-scale tests to determine the operating variables affecting the formation of ash agglomerates, and (i) environmental characterization to determine the environmental impact of the process. The details of ash chemistry studies were published earlier [ 9,10 ]. The details of laboratory studies on the subject of ash deposition in the cyclone have been 172 adequately covered by Tsao et al. [ 11 ], and Mason et al. [ 12,13 ]. The fluidi- zation aspects, which include cold model studies and X-ray cinematographic studies, are presented here, in Part I. The combustion characteristics are cov- ered in Part II. COLD MODEL STUDIES A basic concern in scaling up the gasifier from pilot-plant size to a large commercial size is the ability to produce sufficient quantities of ash agglom- erates and the ability to discharge these agglomerates (without excessive loss of carbon). These are directly related to the design of the grid and the ash discharge section of the gasifier. For the upper section of the gasifier, conven- tional fluidized-bed principles are applicable, and considerable past industrial experience is available. However, since this is the first development of its kind, very little specific knowledge or information is available for scaling up the grid and the ash-discharge section. The cold model studies have been conducted to aid in understanding the hydrodynamics of the grid/venturi region and to develop an engineering design model of the ash-discharge system that will aid in its scale-up. The experiments were designed to correlate the ash-discharge rate to gasifier design and operating parameters such as the venturi and clas- sifier diameters, venturi velocity, grid velocity, gas density, bed height, and others. Equipment description Figure 2 is a schematic diagram of the closed-loop test system used for the pressurized cold model (PCM) studies. The details of the main test vessel are shown in Fig. 3. The bottom section of the PCM vessel was similar to the one used in the pilot-plant gasifier. The grid was an inverted 45-degree truncated cone. Venturi diameters of 4.5, 6, and 8 inches were tested during this investi- gation. Gas flows to the venturi section and the grid plenum were individually controlled, metered with orifice plates, and recorded. Nitrogen was used to pressurize the system and to replace losses at the compressor, thereby main- taining a constant vessel pressure. Nitrogen leaving the test vessel passed through a cyclone and two filters in series for removal of any entrained partic- ulates; then it returned to the compressor for recycling. A 4-foot-diameter feed hopper was used for feeding the material into the test vessel. A 2-foot-diameter hopper was used to collect the solids discharged through the venturi/classifier section. A lockhopper of the same size, located below it, allowed the discharged material to be removed at atmospheric pressure. 173 I IST-STAGE FILTER I 2NO-STAGE FILTER CYCLONE VENT ~CYCLONE RECEIVER VENTq'~-rH BYPASS i CONTROL VALVE T T ROTARY SCREW COMPRESSOR LOCK HOPPER PSV EL CLASSIFIER PNEUMATIC CONVEYING LINE LOADING HOPPER N2 DISCHARGE HOPPER Fig. 2. Schematic drawing of the closed-loop system for pressurized cold model studies. Experimental procedure The material was loaded into the feed hopper using the low-pressure pneu- matic conveying line and loading hopper. While the system was being pressur- ized with nitrogen to the nominal test pressure, the recycle compressor was started with the bypass valve in the open position. Once the system was at 174 4 f~ C)O *1 L2ft 7in i - I ft 9-3/4m 6m } i,, tf? J I f~ 3m. 9 bn T I ff 10-3/8=n. Fig. 3. Schematic drawing of the test vessel for pressurized cold model studies. pressure, an appropriate compressor discharge pressure was set, and predeter- mined minimum velocities were established through the venturi section and the grid to prevent material from falling through them during loading opera- tions. The material was then fed into the test vessel from the feed hopper. When all the material had been transferred to the test vessel, the grid and venturi velocities were quickly adjusted to their planned test values. Any material that had collected in the discharge hopper during solids loading or the setting of test velocities was discharged before the actual venturi/clas- sifier discharge samples were collected. A programmable controller and timer were used to control the automated hopper discharge system, with the number and duration of sample periods set prior to the test. Samples from the bed were 175 also collected using solids sampling pots located at different levels in the vessel. After all discharge samples were collected, the venturi velocity was decreased to allow all remaining bed material to be quickly discharged through the hoppers. Data analysis Post-run data analysis consisted of calculating the venturi and grid super- ficial gas velocities, the measured bed density and initial height, the venturi/ classifier discharge rates, and the various agglomerate concentrations. Two methods were used to determine the concentration of agglomerates in the samples. The first method, a fluidization segregation technique, was based on the difference in particle densities of the char and the agglomerates, approximately 80 and 150 lb/ft3 (1280 and 2400 kg/m3), respectively. A representative por- tion of the sample to be analyzed was first divided into narrow-size fractions by screening. After each fraction was completely fluidized, the velocity was slowly decreased to zero, which allowed the denser particles (agglomerates) to defluidize and accumulate at the bottom of the bed. The boundary between the char and the agglomerates was then determined by visual examination, and the char was vacuumed off the top and weighed. This technique revealed not only the overall agglomerate concentration but also the particle-size distribu- tion of the char and the agglomerates. This cumbersome and time-consuming procedure, however, prevented sample analysis from keeping pace with the test program. Thus, the fast-ash method of destructive analysis was investigated; this is the method that is used to rapidly determine the ash content of bed samples in the U-GAS pilot plant. For the fast-ash method, the average ash content of the char and agglomer- ates must be known to infer the agglomerate content of the samples from their ash content. Four samples - three from the venturi discharge and one from the final bed - were separated with the fluidization segregation technique described above. The ash content was determined for each portion of separated char and agglomerates as well as a portion of each original sample. The high ash con- centrations of the agglomerates indicated that the fluidization technique could correctly identify agglomerates. There was no assurance, however, other than that obtained by visual examination, that the char portions were agglomerate- free. The ash contents of the separated char and agglomerates were averaged to 32% and 98%, respectively. With knowledge of the average ash content of any sample, the percentage of agglomerates in it could then be calculated. The overall percentage of agglomerates calculated by the fast-ash method agreed closely with that determined by the fluidization segregation technique and was therefore used because of its ease and speed of analysis. Table 1 gives the ranges of the various operating conditions used and the 176 TABLE 1 Ranges of operating conditions and experimental results for pressurized cold model studies Venturi diameter (in.) 4.5 6 8 Total number of tests System pressure, psiaa Venturi velocity, ft/s Classifier velocity, ft/s Bed heightb, ft Overall discharge rate, lb/min Agglomerates in bed, wt% Agglomerates in discharge, wt% 19 39 30 30 -45 29 - 75 30 -75 13 -40 11 - 51 16 -35 4 -25 7 - 30 5 -25 2.4-3.3 1.8- 4.0 1.1-3.9 0 -66 1 -117 0 -93 22 -62 40 - 81 31 -64 39 -90 61 - 96 48 -99 a 1 psia = 6.895 kPa. bHeight of the bed above the grid cone. test results obtained in this study. Approximately 90 tests were conducted with different sizes ofventuri and classifier, but their diameter ratio was maintained the same as the one used in the hot U-GAS pilot plant. The extensive analyses of data obtained in these experiments led to a hypothesis that the net rate of material discharged from the gasifier is deter- mined in two steps. In the first step, the venturi is viewed as a device that determines the rate at which uniformly mixed bed material passes into the classifier. This venturi discharge rate was found to be primarily dependent on the rate of momentum of the gas through the venturi and the size of the venturi. The second step occurs in the classifier below the venturi. In the classifier, the actual rate of material discharged and the classification of the material are determined. The latter seems to be related to the terminal velocity. In the classifier, those particles entering the classifier that have a terminal velocity greater than the gas velocity in the classifier pass through the classifier and are discharged. The particles that have a terminal velocity lower than the clas- sifier velocity are carried back up through the venturi and into the fluidized bed. Thus, in essence, the venturi discharges the uniformly mixed bed material into the classifier, and the classifier separates agglomerates from the char particles. This terminal velocity mechanism is supported by the various test results with different venturi/classifier sizes investigated here. Using this model for the classifier, the venturi discharge rates (My) were calculated for different runs (step 1 above) using the measured discharge rates of agglomerates, par- ticle-size distributions of char and agglomerates, their calculated particle ter- minal velocities as a function of particle diameter, and agglomerate content of the bed and discharge material.
