Effect of venturi diameter on the calculated venturi discharge rates for gas mass velocity (#gVv) --5 lb/(ft2 s) [24.4 kg/(m2 s)]. were plotted against the venturi gas mass velocity (pgVv), as shown in Fig. 4. The slopes of the regression lines for each venturi size were about the same, averaging -2.5. Figure 5 shows the effect of the venturi diameter on the ven- turi discharge rate at a constant venturi gas mass velocity. The regression slope of 2.03 indicates that Mv is directly proportional to the venturi area. The over- all relationship between these four variables was determined by plotting Mv versus the quantity (pgVv)-2.s Dv2 (Fig. 6). The equation of the resulting regression line (correlation coefficient = 0.984) is given by: Mv =89.3 (pg Vv) -2.s Dv2 where, Mv = venturi discharge rate into the classifier (lb/min), pg = gas den- sity (lb/ft3), Vv=superficial gas velocity through the venturi (K/s), and Dv = venturi diameter (in.). Note that Mv is the calculated venturi discharge rate of bed material and not the measured overall agglomerates discharge rate. The above equation clearly shows that a smaller venturi can discharge material at the same rate as 178 1000 800 VENTURI / SIZE, in.~: / 6OO 0 6 .-~ 400 [3 8 E 4-1/2 ff zoo ~ t00 ,~ 80 I .~ 6o ~ 40 I.- Z w > 20 10 I I I L L I IJl I I l I I I 111 0.1 0.2 0.3 0.4 0.6 0.8 I 2 3 4 5 6 7 8910 20 (p~Vv)-z.5 (Dr)z Fig. 6. Relationship among venturi discharge rate, venturi gas velocity, gas density, and venturi diameter. a larger venturi if the venturi velocity is decreased. If there is a minimum veloc- ity in the classifier that is required to achieve the desired degree of classifica- tion, this would set a lower limit on the venturi velocity and would result in the minimum venturi size for discharging a specific amount of material at the desired classification. The effect of classifier length on classifier operation was also determined. Three different classifier lengths (8, 28, and 49 inches) were tested at varying venturi velocities. The results show that, at a constant venturi velocity, clas- sification improves with increasing classifier length; however, it tends to sta- bilize with the increased classifier length indicating that the terminal velocity approach is a reasonable one. An investigation was carried out earlier in a semicircular atmospheric-pres- sure cold model employing a similar type of venturi device to determine its discharge characteristics. The study was conducted using acrylic molding pow- der that simulated bed char together with alumina spheres that simulated agglomerates. The details of this study are given by Leppin and Sahay [ 14 ]. X-RAY CINEMATOGRAPHIC STUDIES The configuration used in the 3-foot-diameter pilot-plant gasifier is a conical grid that has a single ash-discharge (venturi/classifier) system located in its 179 center, hereafter referred to as the "one-cone" design. A preliminary scale-up study indicated that there might be some advantages in designing a grid con- taining multiple ash-discharge systems in a large-scale gasifier. The main advantage of this configuration (hereafter referred to as the "multiple-cone" design) is the smaller scale-up ratio for the venturi section. The main disad- vantage is its complexity in both design and control. The one-cone design has a larger scale-up ratio but retains the basic geometric similarity to the pilot plant and involves a relatively simpler design. A test program was conducted at University College London to compare the fluidization behaviors in the one-cone and three-cone configuration systems using X-ray cinematography. Features of a fluidized bed, such as the bubbles and the location of dense-phase solids, can be identified by using this X-ray technique. The objective of this test program was to visually examine the solids circulation and study the bubble dynamics in the gasifiers with these different configurations. Equipment description Two small-scale grids (one-cone and three-cone designs) and one common fluidized-bed and freeboard region were fabricated for this program. Figure 7 is a schematic drawing of the fluidization equipment. Figure 8 shows the details of the fluidization column with the one-cone grid. The main vessel consisted of a 5-inch-ID and 13-inch-long Plexiglas tube with a detachable aluminum 45 degree conical bottom (grid). A second Plexiglas tube with a 7-inch ID and 13 inches in length, having a 5-inch-diameter base opening, was joined with the main vessel and connected to a disengaging drum to collect entrained solids. The aluminum bottom was connected to the venturi system made of carbon steel. An 80-mesh screen was placed at the bottom of the conical grid to elim- inate any possibility that material could drop into the venturi system. In the one-cone system the fluidizing gas (air) entered the main column through the grid and a venturi/classifier inlet. In the three-cone system, the fluidizing gas entered the main column through the grid and three venturi/classifier inlets. Experimental procedure Forty-two test runs were conducted - 21 with the one-cone grid and 21 with the three-cone grid - using char from western Kentucky No. 9 bituminous coal, char from Wyoming subbituminous coal, and a mixture of coal agglomerates and powder acrylic as bed materials. All tests were conducted at atmospheric pressure. The X-ray beam was passed through the bed horizontally, and pho- tographs were taken for about 10 seconds ( in each test) at a rate of 44 frames/s with an exposure time of 0.2 to 0.8 millisecond. Table 2 gives the ranges of operating conditions used in this study. -
abstract: This is the first part of a series of papers providing the details of support studies conducted as part of the development of the U-GAS fluidized-bed ash-agglomerating coal gasification process. The fluidized-bed gasifier in the U-GAS process behaves much like a conventional well-mixed bed, having a uniform bed composition and temperature in the bulk of the region, but it exhibits some departure in a small localized area confined to the proximity of the central spout, where a somewhat higher temperature is maintained for producing ash agglomerates. Extensive cold-flow experimental studies have been conducted to predict the solids discharge rates and agglomerates classification from the venturi/classifier system in the gasifier; these studies have resulted in a generalized analytical expression in terms of operating velocities, gas properties, and venturi/classifier configuration for the gasifier scale-up. Large-scale U-GAS gasifiers can have single-cone or multiple-cone grid configurations. X-ray cinematographic studies have been conducted in a program at University College London to compare the fluidization behaviors in the one-cone and three-cone grid configuration systems. The objective of this test program was to visually examine the solids circulation and study the bubble dynamics in the fluidized beds with these different configurations.
title: Support studies for the U-GAS coal gasification process. Part I: Fluidization

reaction_catalog.transformation: coal reacts with steam and oxygen to produce CO, CO2, H2, and CH4

full_text: Pyrolysis-gasification technology is considered one of the most promising methods for solid waste treatment, which is safe and efficient, and can achieve harmless and resource utilization of solid waste. However, this process usually produces unacceptable tar components [1]. Tar is a kind of complex liquid mixture with high aromatic hydrocarbon, which has viscosity and toxicity. It needs to be removed to avoid clogging and damaging downstream pipes or equipment [2,3]. In terms of tar removal substances, it is well known that transition metal has outstanding ability to remove tar among many substances [4,5]. Nickel (Ni) is one of transition metals and show excellent catalytic capacity in catalytic reforming of tar [6]. Many researchers have studied the catalytic activity of Ni in the catalytic process [7–10]. In terms of tar removal methods, it has been proven that steam reforming of tar is an effective technique for tar removal because this approach can effectively convert tar into useful gases (such as H2, CO, CO2, and, CH4). Therefore, excellent tar removal effect can be achieved by using nickel catalyst and steam reforming method in the study of tar removal [11–13]. In recent years, the research of nickel and solid waste synthesis catalyst is more popular because of the advantages of cost-effective and green properties [14]. Pyrolytic char has the porous structure and a high specific surface area via pyrolytic from solid waste, which can be used as a valuable catalyst carrier for tar reforming [15–18]. The catalytic activity of nickel supported on pyrolysis chars (wood pallet char [19], coal char [20] and rick husk char [21]) have been proved to be excellent. In Zhang et al.'s [22] review paper, the nickel-based catalysts used in catalytic reforming of biomass tar in recent years are summarized, and comprehensively described the research work of pyrolytic char-based Ni catalysts. Typically, Ashok et al. [23] reported that solid waste incinerator bottom ash can be used as a carrier for the preparation of metal catalysts after simple treatment, and their study proved that by using NaOH treatment, bottom ash can be developed as an effective catalyst for catalytic reforming of tar. It is proved that the catalytic performance of hydrothermal treatment catalyst is better than raw catalyst. Daorattanachai et al. [24] had synthesized the Re/char and Ni/char catalysts using sewage sludge, the prepared catalyst has good stability, and the H2 yield decreased by only 5% after 18 h. Hu et al. [25] showed that pine sawdust char supported nickel catalyst in steam gasification and the results indicated Ni promoted the transformation of tar by carbothermal reduction and hydrogenation reduction. Many studies have shown that the combination of pyrolytic char and Ni can lead to catalysts with good catalytic performance. However, to the best of our knowledge, in the catalytic cracking reaction of tar, the deactivation of char-based catalyst is an important factor, and how to effectively reduce the deactivation phenomenon is a key scientific problem. In our previous paper [26], we found that solid waste peat is a good catalyst carrier. Peat is treated to form peat char, which is rich in silica. In the process of preparing metal catalyst, silica can effectively combine with metal substances to form stable active sites and prevent the loss of metal active substances. Thus, the peat char-based catalyst can effectively alleviate the deactivation phenomenon. If the carrier peat is also used in the preparation of tar reforming catalyst, a good catalytic effect will also be achieved. Further study on the interaction between peat char and nickel can effectively improve the activity and stability of the catalyst. Moreover, as a by-product of pyrolysis and gasification, solid waste carbon can be continuously produced and utilized, which is conducive to the realization of renewable resources. Therefore, the aim of this study was to develop a cheap and stable catalyst for tar reforming, and to verify and analyze the implementation. To facilitate fundamental research, a simplified model tar, toluene was selected [27]. To further improve the catalytic performance, the surface structure of peat char was increased by KOH and CO2 activation methods. Moreover, the effects of residence time (τ) and steam-to‑carbon ratio (S/C) on the catalytic reaction were studied, and the conversion, the molar ratios of H2, CO, CO2 and CH4 in the generated gas were investigated under different conditions. Waste peat was collected from Changbai mountain region (China). The active substance l nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O, AR, 98%) and the lye potassium hydroxide (KOH, AR, 98%) were supplied by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd., China; the organic solvents dichloromethane (AR, 99.5%) and toluene (AR, 99.8%) were supplied by Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China; the cylinder gas carbon dioxide (CO2, purity >99.99%) and nitrogen gas (N2, purity >99.999%) were purchased from the Guangzhou Shengying Gas Co. Ltd., China. The peat is dried to evaporate the moisture (oven, 24 h, 108 °C), and then calcined in a tubular furnace to produce peat char (PC) (800 °C, 2 h, N2 atmosphere). The as-synthesized PC was then ground and screened. PC was activated by KOH or CO2 activation methods. Specific physical activation and chemical activation methods have been described in detail in previous articles [3]. Finally, the material obtained by the KOH activation method was labeled as APC, and the material obtained by the CO2 activation method was labeled as CPC. In this paper, the composite catalyst was prepared by wet impregnation method, the catalyst Ni/PC was prepared as an example: weigh 4 g of NiCl2·6H2O dissolved in 100 ml of deionized water and stir well; then add 10 g of PC and stir in a magnetic stirrer at 500 rpm at room temperature for 6 h. After mixing, put the mixed solution into the oven for drying with 24 h at 105 °C, and the resulting solid is called the precursor. The precursor is calcined twice in the atmosphere of nitrogen, and the conditions of calcination are consistent with those of pyrolysis, the product after calcination is the catalyst, denoted as Ni/PC. By a similar approach, we obtain the catalysts Ni/APC and Ni/CPC. The prepared catalyst is placed in a drying dish for standby use. Thermal field emission (analytical) scanning electron microscopy (Japan's Hitachi, SU-70) was used for the analysis of Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) images and Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) of the samples, which were sprayed with gold prior to testing. The samples were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD; X'Pert Pro MPD, PANalytical, D8 Advance) in the range from 10° to 80°, all powder samples coupled with Cu Kσ radiation and a step size of 0.02° was used. Jade 6.5 software was used to process the data. The surface structure of the sample was analyzed by automatic specific surface and aperture analyzer (Quantachrome, ASIQMO002–2), the sample was measured for N2 adsorption-desorption at −196 °C and was degassed in a glass adsorption tube (90 °C, 24 h, N2 atmosphere) prior to measurement. The experimental instruments are self-developed small assembly equipment. The reactor consists of feeding zone, vaporization zone, reaction zone and product separation zone. Before the reaction begins, the catalyst is placed in a quartz tube supported by quartz cotton in a tubular furnace, and the device is sealed. The inert gas nitrogen (100 ml/min) is then purged with 20 min to remove the air from the reactor. After the washing, the reactor and vaporization began to heat up, set the vaporization temperature at 200 °C and the reactor temperature at 650–850 °C depending on the reaction requirements. When the temperature of the reactor reaches the set temperature, the toluene and water enter the vaporization under the action of the injection pump and peristaltic pump, the flow rates of toluene and water are 0.1 ml/min and 0.36 ml/min respectively, then enter the reactor in the form of a gas for the reaction. The reactants undergo a catalytic reforming reaction under the action of a catalyst, and the product gas and the reactive toluene are separated by a gas-liquid process with ice water. The toluene not involved in the reaction is absorbed by the gas-washing bottle in the cold water bath, which contains a certain amount of dichloromethane, and the generated gas is collected by the air bag after the gas-washing bottle. Using the gas chromatograph (Agilent 7890A) detected contents of generated gas (H2, CO, CH4 and CO2), and the unreacted toluene was determined by liquid chromatograph (Agilent 1290–6540). The toluene conversion (C) was estimated using Eq. (1) (1) C = m tol , in − m tol , out m tol , in 100 % where mtol, in was the amount of toluene injected, mtol, out was the amount of unreacted toluene. The residence time (τ) and steam-to‑carbon ratio (S/C) used in the experiment were calculated as follows: The value of τ was estimated according to Eq. (2) (2) τ = S ∗ h f N 2 where S is the area of the catalysts, h is the height of the catalyst bed, fN2 is the flow velocity of nitrogen. The value of S/C was estimated according to Eq. (3) (3) S C = m H 2 O M H 2 O 7 ∗ m tol M tol where mH2O is the weight of the injected water, MH2O is the molar mass of water, mtol is the mass of the injected toluene, and Mtol is the molar mass of the injected toluene. The mol% of H2, CO, CO2 and CH4 were estimated using Eq. (4) (4) mol % i = % i % co + % H 2 + % CO 2 + % CH 4 100 % where i represents H2, CO, CO2 or CH4, the %H2 , %co, %CO2 and %CH4 are the corresponding gas chromatograph test results. Fig. 1 shows the SEM images of (a) PC, (b) APC and (c) Ni/APC. PC particles have a blocky massive structure with few holes. After activation by KOH (show in (b)), APC displays an irregularly lamellar and has many holes in its surface structure. KOH activation is a common chemical activation method of carbon materials. The carbon consumed in the activation process mainly generates potassium carbonate. When the material is calcined again at high temperature, the steam of potassium metal is constantly squeezed into the layers formed by carbon atoms for activation, so that the product has a large specific surface area [23]. Fig. 1 (c) shows the morphology of the Ni catalyst, the surface of catalyst Ni/APC presents a large number of homogeneous nanoscale particles, and covers the whole surface of the catalyst, thus forming a large number of pores on the surface. According to the ruler unit, the surface particle size is approximately 100 nm. Table 1 lists the elemental compositions of the chars and catalysts. PC contains large amounts of C, O and Si, as well as small amounts of various metals (Al, K and Ca). The KOH and CO2 activation treatments had no significant effect on elemental composition. When Ni is added to the peat char to form catalysts, the content of Ni in the catalyst is 6.52%–7.83%, which indicated that metallic matter was successfully inserted into the char framework. Table 2 shows the surface structure data of the chars and catalysts. The results show that the surface area of pure PC is 81.97 m2/g, the pore volume is 87.67 mm3/g, and the pore radius is 118.31 nm. The surface structure data of activated PC were significantly improved after using the CO2 or KOH activation method. The surface area increased from 81.97 m2/g to 213.64 m2/g and 221.06 m2/g. Both KOH activation and CO2 activation can effectively increase the pore structure on the surface of carbon materials, resulting in an increasing trend of three kinds of data, which provides more space for the subsequent loading of nickel active substances [28]. In the prepared catalyst, the surface area showed a significant decrease from 81.97 m2/g, 213.64 m2/g, and 221.06 m2/g to 60.65 m2/g, 128.86 m2/g, and 110.78 m2/g for Ni/PC, Ni/APC and Ni/CPC, respectively, were found. The decrease of pore structure data is due to the interaction between the functional groups on the PC surface and nickel, which forms new materials and covers the surface of the catalyst, resulting in the covering of some pores. At the same time, the average pore radius data show that the surface structure of a series of catalysts prepared is dominated by macropores. Some researchers have found that in the water vapor reforming reaction, mesopores and macropores have good elasticity and activity, which can effectively prevent a large amount of carbon accumulation [29,30]. Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns of the chars and catalysts. The several intense and sharp characteristic peaks at 2θ = 26.6° and the small peaks at 2θ = 22.0°, 50.2°, 59.9° and 68.0° could be identified as amorphous silica. The peaks at 2θ = 20.0°, and 39.0° were corresponding to the characteristic peaks of C. In the XRD patterns of catalysts Ni/PC, Ni/APC and Ni/CPC, there are three peaks different types were observed. It can be seen that the peaks at around 44.2°, 51.7° and 28.6° were typical characteristic for NiSi, Ni and AlNi2Si, illustrating that the active substances exist in the form of Ni, NiSi and AlNi2Si on the surface of catalysts. The formation of AlNi2Si structure means that the SiO2 and Al in the carrier can effectively combine with the active substance nickel and form a more stable new substance. This result also indirectly indicates that the preparation method of the catalyst in this paper is feasible and effective. The change of catalyst material structure can cause the difference of surface morphology, and this result is in good agreement with SEM analysis. Fig. 3 shows the catalytic performance of the catalysts Ni/PC, Ni/APC and Ni/CPC. Fig. 3 (a) show the effect of three different catalysts on toluene conversion and producing gas composition, for the tar conversion, it can be found that the Ni/APC (95.3%) had better catalytic performance than Ni/PC (92.6%) and Ni/CPC (94.8%). For the produce gas content, the mole percentage (mol%) of H2 was the largest, followed by that of CO. The maximum mol% of syngas (H2 + CO) was 89.2% for the Ni/PC catalyst, 94.7% for the Ni/APC catalyst and 91.6% for the Ni/CPC catalyst. It is well known that a slightly higher syngas yield was attained by the Ni/APC catalyst. In this scenario, the mol% values of CH4 and CO2 decreased by less than 5% and 10%, respectively. It is especially pointed out that the Ni based peat char catalyst had better selectivity for syngas. The variation trend of main gas composition (H2, CO, CO2 and CH4) with reaction time was analyzed to further explore the gas composition selectivity of the Ni/PC, Ni/APC and Ni/CPC catalysts, as showed in Fig. 3 (b), (c), (d). Specifically, for the catalyst Ni/APC (Fig. 2 (c)), the maximum mol% of H2 and CO were 68.5% and 26.2% at the beginning of the reaction. As the reaction time increased, the mol% of H2 and CO gradually decreased while the mol% of CH4 and CO2 showed the opposite trend. In the reaction, the mol% of H2 and CO were reduced by 5.8% and 10.6% after 150 min, respectively. This phenomenon means that the catalyst becomes less selective for H2 and CO as the reaction time increases. Gradually deactivated catalysts are more likely to produce CH4 and CO2. Compared to the Ni/APC, the catalytic activity of catalysts Ni/PC and Ni/CPC were relatively poor. As shown in Fig. 3 (b) and (d), the mol% of H2, CO, CO2 and CH4 showed the same trend change as catalyst Ni/APC for Ni/PC and Ni/CPC, but with a slight reduction in H2 and CO. This result illustrated that the Ni/APC catalyst had the best stability and excellent H2 and CO gas selectivity, the catalytic performance and selectivity of the catalysts followed the order Ni/PC  2.5), similar to the results reported for other catalysts. Fig. 4 (b) shows the effect of residence time (τ) on conversion, as τ increased from 0.3 s to 0.8 s, the conversion of all three catalysts gradually increased, and toluene conversion reached a maximum at 0.6 s, 0.5 s and 0.6 s for Ni/PC, Ni/APC and Ni/CPC, respectively. It's worth noting that there was no obvious increase in conversion when the residence time was prolonged to 0.8 s, illustrating that a long residence time (slow flow velocity) is not necessarily conducive to the reaction. By comparing the residence time and steam-to‑carbon ratio, it can be seen that the residence time has a greater influence on the results. In the experimental study, finding a suitable residence time will be beneficial to the efficient removal of tar [31]. Reaction temperature is also one of the important factors affecting tar removal. Fig. 5 summarizes the variation in toluene conversion with reaction temperature. It was found that for the three catalysts, when the reaction temperature increased from 650 °C to 850 °C, the toluene conversion increased gradually. Note that as the temperature increases from 750 °C to 850 °C, the increase in conversion rate is minimal. The result illustrated that higher temperature leads to catalyst sintering, sintering results in the collapse of the surface structure, which affects the fracture of toluene at the active site, resulting in the increase of toluene conversion is not obvious [32]. In tar reforming, the deactivation of catalyst is mainly caused by the carbon deposition on the surface of catalyst. In order to understand the carbon deposition of used catalyst, we performed the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential thermal analysis (DTA) analysis on the fresh and used catalyst Ni/APC. The used catalyst Ni/APC is the catalyst that has undergone the reaction in Fig. 3. The experimental condition is that the temperature rises to 800 °C in air atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C/min, and the result is shown in Fig. 6 . The fresh and used catalyst Ni/APC catalyst exhibits a significant peak of weightlessness between 450 and 600 °C, the weight loss of fresh catalyst and use catalyst is about 28% and 35%, and the weight loss ratio of the used catalyst increases by 7%. The weight loss and exothermic peak corresponds to the oxidation of carbon species deposited [33,34], the number of active sites decreased and the catalytic performance decreased due to the carbon deposition on the surface of the catalyst. It can be seen that the amount of carbon deposited at the end of the reaction accounts for about 7% of the mass of the catalyst. In order to more intuitively observe the carbon accumulation on the surface of the used catalyst, we conducted SEM test on the used catalyst. Fig. 7 shows a comparison of the surface morphology of the fresh catalyst and the used catalyst. The surface of the fresh catalyst showed rough round nanoparticles, and the round particles on the used catalyst surface became smooth, and various irregular carbon accumulation groups were formed on the surface of the round particles. It can be seen that the carbon deposition is not uniformly distributed on the surface of the used catalyst, but in relatively concentrated clusters. In the water vapor reforming reaction, the surface of the catalyst is continuously scoured due to the addition of water vapor, which may be an important factor causing the surface particles of the catalyst to become smooth. At the same time, carbon deposition is inevitable on catalyst due to deep dehydrogenation and aromatics condensation reaction. The rate and amount of carbon deposition are related to many factors, such as the properties of raw materials, reaction conditions, etc. How to avoid the deactivation of catalyst caused by carbon deposition is the key problem in catalytic reforming reaction, and will be the focus of future research in this paper. In this paper, catalyst Ni/APC showed excellent catalytic performance, and tar conversion rate and gas production results were compared with the reported catalysts of the same type, as shown in Table 3 . The catalyst Ni/APC were compared with the same type of solid waste carbon-based nickel catalysts, including single-support (sludge char) catalysts, dual-support (wood and food char) catalysts and typical nickel catalysts. It can be seen that compared with other nickel catalysts, the peat char based nickel catalysts in this paper have obvious advantages in producing syngas. It also has a very significant aromatic tar removal capability. The peat char-supported Ni catalysts by ourselves was applied to the catalytic reforming of toluene using a laboratory reaction unit. The experimental results show that peat char is a good carrier and can combine with nickel effectively, and the prepared catalyst has excellent catalytic performance. The catalytic performance of the catalysts followed the order Ni/PC < Ni/CPC < Ni/APC, The optimal catalytic efficiency was 95.3% under reaction conditions of 850 °C, steam-to‑carbon ratio of 3:1 and residence time of 0.5 s. The catalyst also exerted a positive effect on the syngas (CO + H2) production with the maximum molar ratio reaching up to 94.7 vol%. The results demonstrated that the peat char can be used as a novel catalyst carrier for toluene removal and syngas production.
abstract: The paper aims to investigate an effective method of catalytic reforming of toluene using peat char-supported Ni metal to achieve clean gas production. Utilizing KOH or CO2 activation to increases the surface area and pore volume of peat char during catalyst preparation. The chemical and structural properties of catalysts is explained based on SEM, EDS, XRD, BET method. Meanwhile, the influence of reaction temperature, residence times (τ), steam-to‑carbon ratios (S/C) in catalytic reaction and the molar ratio of CO, CO2, H2, CH4 in clean gas were investigated. The results indicated that SiO2 structure plays a significant role in nickel-based peat char catalyst, and the catalyst exhibited high catalytic performance. Conversion efficiencies of toluene can reach about 95.3% using Ni/APC catalyst at residence time of 0.5 s, steam-to‑carbon ratio of 3:1, temperature of 850 °C. The catalyst also exerted a positive effect on the syngas (CO + H2) production with the maximum molar ratio reaching up to 94.7 mol% under the optimized conditions. The high catalytic activity was mainly attributed to the structure of AlNi2Si and NiSi on the catalyst surface. Therefore, the cost-effective peat char-supported Ni catalysts could be used for aromatic tar conversion.
